ITINERARY write by fynes moryson gent. first in the latin tongue and than translate by he into english a itinerary write by fynes moryson gent. contain his ten year travel through twelve dominion the first part. the first book chap. i. of my journey from London in England to stand hamburg Lubeck Luneburg my return to hamburg and journey to magdeburg leipzig Witteberg and the neighbour city in Germany be a student of Peter-house in Cambridge and enter the eighteen year of my age I take the degree of bachelor of art and short after be choose fellow of the say college by queen Elizabeth mandate three year expire from my first degree take in the vniverfitie I commence master of art and within a year after by the favour of the master and fellow I be choose to a vacant place of privilege to study the civil law then as well for the ornament of this profession as out of my innated desire to gain experience by travel into foreign part to which course my parent have give consent some few year past upon my first declare of my inclination to the say profession upon the privilege of our statute permit two of the society to travel I obtain licence to that purpose of the say master and fellow in the year 1589 be then full 23 year old and present leave the vniversiy I go to London there to follow some study fit to enable i in this course and there better teach and these study the visit of my friend in the country my go to Oxford to take the same degree I have in Cambridge and some opposition upon new deliberation make by my father and friend against my journey detain i long in those part than I purpose at last in the begin of the year 1591. and upon the first day of May I take ship at Liegh distant from London twenty eight mile by land and thirty six 1591. by water where Thames in a large bed be carry into the Sea thence we set sail into the main and the eight day of our sail the merchant fleet of sixteen ship be disperse by a fog and tempest two Dunkirk pirate follow our ship till by God mercy the fog be clear after some few hour and two of our ship upon our discharge of a great piece draw towards we the pirate despair leave to pursue we that they be pirate be apparent since as we for trial turn our sail they likewise fit themselves to our course so as we though fly yet prepare ourselves to fight till God thus deliver we the nine day towards night we fall upon a island call the holy-land vulgar Heiligland and not dare to enter the river ell before the next morning we strike all sail and suffer our ship to be toss too and fro by the wave all that night which mariner call lie at Hull this island have only one port capable of some six ship in the form of the moon decrease and lie open to the east on the north side be a great rock and the rest of the shore be all of high cliff it be subject to the duke of Holste and by that title to the king of Denmark but the inhabitant be so poor as they yield no other tribute than stone for the duke building it be in circuit some three mile and have about one hundred family the ten day we enter the river Elue and land at stand this be a ancient stand city and one of the empire free city and one of those sea-towne which from the privilege of traffic with their neighbour be call free city vulgar hansteten but of late be become so poor as they have sell the privilege of coin money and some like right to hamburg till the english merchant remove their seat of traffic from hamburg to stand it begin late to grow rich not without the envy and impoverishment of the hamburger in the dutch inn I pay for each meal four Lubeck shilling and a half and in the english inn eight penny English in the great wind and trouble stream of ell which ebb and flow as high as Luneburg certain booye be lay to show the channel and shoal of the river and the maintain of each of they cost 40 pound yearly and of all a thousand pound at the least at the common charge of stand and hamburg but after frost begin they be take up and reserve to the next spring of old when stand flourish this charge belong only to it take some contribution of the other city for the same this free city have then choose the bishop of breme for their protector and have but small scatter revenue to the value of ninety pound sterling by the year but the soil be so fertile as they milk their cow thrice each day of late the hamburger have in vain attempt by naval force to forbid the arrival of the english at stand who as they have grieve have their seat with they as well with exaction as with forbid they free exercise of religion so now sometime by lair treaty sometime by force they labour to draw back unto they those of stand have by privilege the preemption and choice of rhenish wine pass by they this city may be make strong if the work they have begin be perfect the field of the north and east side may be drown and because the high hill towards the west and south though somewhat distant seem to threaten danger they have on those side raise a high and broad wall of earth fasten on the outside with willow in which place a armoury for all munition be build but the gate of the city for ridiculous ostentation of strength be furnish with artillery of stone paint over the territory without the city belong on the west side to the bishop of bream and on the east side to the earl of Scbeneburg and the duke of holst from stand to hamburg be five mile in a wagon hire for five Lubeck shilling each person we pass two mile then cross the ell not without danger in respect of the shallow place and present storm we hire another wagon for four Lubeck shilling each person and through thick wood pass the other three mile to hamburg the passage by water to hamburg have be much easy especial for a stranger and a boat daily pass from stand thither in some three hour space if the wind be not contrary wherein each man pay three Lubeck shilling for his passage but all passenger without difference of condition must help to row or hire one in his stead except the wind be good so as they need not use their oar beside that the annoyance of base companion will easy offend one that be any thing nice hamburg be a free city of the empire and one of they which as I say be call hamburg hansteten and for the building and populousness be much to be praise the senate house be very beautiful and be adorn with carve statue of the nine worthy the exchange where the merchant meet be a very pleasant place the haven be shut up with a iron chain the city be compass with a deep ditch and upon the east and north side with a double ditch and wall water be bring to the city from a hil distant some english mile by pipe of wood because those of lead will be break by the ice and these pipe be to be see under the bridge whence the water be convey by they unto each citizen house the territory of the city extend a mile or two and on one side three mile out of the wall it have nine church and six gate call by the city to which they lead it be seat in a large plain and a sandy soil but have very fat pasture ground without on the south side and some part of the west it be wash with the river Elue which also put a branch into the town but on the north and somewhat on the east side the river Alster run by towards stand and fall into the ell the street be narrow except one which be call Broad-street vulgar breitgasse the building be all of brick as in all the other seabordering city lie from these part towards Flanders and all the beauty of the house be in the first entrance have broad and fair gate into a large Hal the low part whereof on both side be use for a Warehouse and in the upper part lie to the view of the door the chief householdstuff be place and especial their vessel of english pewter which be keep bright make a glitter show to they that pass by so as the house promise more beauty outward than they have inward here I pay each meal four Lubeck shilling and one each night for my bed the citizen be unmeasurable ill affect to the english to who or to any stranger it be unsafe to walk out of the gate after noon for when the common people be once warm with drink they be apt to do they injury myself one day pass by some that be unload and tell of billet hear they say these word wirft den englanders kopf that be cast the ten at the head but I and my companion know well their malice to the english for the remove their traffic to stand be content silent to pass by as if we understand they not hence I go out of the way to see Lubeck a imperial city and one of the above name hans-towne be ten mile distant from hamburg each of we for our coach pay twenty Lubeck shilling and go forth early we pass through a marish and sandy plain and many wood of Oakes which in these part be frequent as wood of fir be in the upper part of Germany and have go six mile we come to a village call Altslow for the situation in a great marish or boggy ground where each man pay for his dinner five Lubeck shilling and a half our dutch companion contribute half that money for drink after dinner in the afternoon we pass the other four mile to Lubeck in the space of four hour and until we come within half a mile of the town we pass through some thick wood of oak with some fair pasture between they for the german use to preserve their wood to the uttermost either for beauty or because they be so huge and frequent as they can be consume when we come out of the wood we see two fair rise hill and the three upon which Lubeck be seat on the top of this three hill stand the fair church of saint Mary whence there Lubeck be a descent to all the gate of the city who situation offer to our eye a fair prospect and promise great magnificence in the build the city be compass with a double wall one of brick and narrow the other of earth and broad fasten with thick row of willow but on the north side and on the southeast side there be no wall those part be compass with deep ditch full of water on the southeast side the water seem narrow but be so deep as ship of a thousand ton be bring up to the city to lie there all winter be first unlade at Tremuren the port of the city lie upon the baltic Sea to this port one mile distant from Lubeck we come in three hour each man pay for his coach five Lubeck shilling and four for our dinner and return back the same night to Lubeck the building of this city be very beautiful all of brick and it have most sweet walk without the wall the citizen be curious to avoid ill smell to which end the butcher have a place for kill their beast without the wall upon a run stream water be bring to every citizen house by pipe and all the brewer dwell in one street have each of they his iron cock which be turn the water fall into their vessel though the build of this town be of the same matter as that of the neighbour town yet it be much prefer before they for the beauty and uniformity of the house for the pleasant garden fair street sweet walk without the wall and for the citizen themselves who be much commend for civility of manner and the strict execution of Iustice the poor dwell in the remote-street out of the common passage there be a street call the fun haussgasse that be the street of five house because in the year 1278. it be all burn except five house since which time they have a law that no man shall build of timber and clay except he divide his house from his neighbour with a brick wall three foot broad and that no man shall cover his house with any thing but tile brass or lead the form of this city be like a loze thick in the midst and grow narrow towards the two end the length whereof be from the gate call burke port towards the south to millen port towards the north. we enter the town by holtz port on the west side to which gate Hickster port be opposite on the east side it be as long again as broad and two street breitgasse that be Broad-street and konnigsgasse that be Kings-street run the whole length of the town and six other street make the breadth and if you stand in the midst of any of these street you may there see both the end thereof here I pay each meal four Lubeck shilling have my bed free for a quart of rhenish wine five Lubeck shilling and as much for sack neither do I remember that ever I have a more pleasant abide in Germany either for the sweetness of the place the courtesy of the people or my diet the citizen be very courteous to all stranger who the law extraordinary favour above the native so they only abide there for a time and be not inhabitant neither be they less friendly to the english though they complain of injury so they call they offer they by we at Sea this city have many thing worth the see there be ten fair church whereof one be use for a armoury of all munition for war saint Mary cathedral church vulgar kirk be fair than the rest where there be a fair and artificial clock in the top whereof be a picture whereof both the ear of the head be see which painter esteem a master work in the porch thereof be three marble pillar each of they thirty foot long of one stone only one of they be piece for one foot but the image of the virgin Mary in this church and of Christ crucify in burk Kirke be think work of singular art for which they say a spanish merchant offer a mass of money I will confess true that myself behold the virgin statue all of stone do think it have be cover with a gown of white buffin and that be altogether unskilful in the grave art yet I much admire the workmanship without millen port there be a conduit of water which serve all the town the more notable because it be the first of that kind which since have be disperse to London and other place on all side out of the town there be sweet walk especial towards Jerusalem so they call the passion of Christ grave in diverse pillar where also be a pleasant grove under the shade whereof rope-maker and like artificer use to work the canon of the cathedral church have great privilege and as it be a absolute power over themselves and of old they have a gate of the city free to themselves to go in or out at pleasure till the citizen find how dangerous it be to the maintain of their freedom from any subjection upon a good opportunity when the emperor come thither do of set purpose lead he into the city by that gate where fall on their knee they beseech he that it may be brick up and never more open he be the last man that ever shall enter thereat from Lubeck we take our journey to Luneburg be ten mile distant and the first night we lodge in a village call millen where a famous oulenspiegell who we call owly-glasse have a monument erect he die in the year 1350. and the stone cover he be compass with a grate lest it shall be break and carry away piecemeal by passenger which they say have once already be do by the german the townsman yearly keep a feast for his memory and yet show the apparel he be wont to wear this country be barren and sandy ground full of thick wood of Oakes by the way in Kasborough castle they say that a duke of inferior Saxony lie imprison by the emperor command his brother govern the dukedom charge with great debt by his prodigality but his village hereabout be possess by the hamburger and lubecker by right of mortgage we pass the ell twice the coachman pay for himself his coach and each one of we a Lubeck shilling and beyond the ell the ground be somewhat more fertile at millen I pay for my supper four Lubeck shilling and a half the next day we come to Luneburg which by the citizen for defence of their Luneburg liberty be strong fortify for it be one of the free imperial city but the duke of Luneburg challenge a superiority over it the wall build of earth be high and broad and the ditch very deep the building be very fair especial that of the senate house and almost all the house be of brick they have two large market place and the street be broad but very filthy and full of ill smell the city itself be almost of a round form be seat in a valley but have mountain near it on the west side and further off on the east a high mountain call Kalkberg hang over it on the north side in the top whereof be a strong castle which the citizen have get into their hand some threescore year before my be there not far from the city be a monestary call Luna whereof some say the town be name other say it have the name of the river run by it now call Eluenau of eleven river run into it which history testify to have be call Luna of old but other prove both the river and the town to have have their name of the idol Isis bear two horn of the moon which be keep in the castle upon Kalkberg and worship by the people among the thing best deserve to be see be the fountain of salt and the house wherein salt be boil over the gate whereof these verse be write behold of fine salt this fount do store afford by the almighty grace and free gift from above the mountain bridge and fountain be thy gift o lord for which let we increase in piety and love to all the poor round about and to all the citizen for their private use salt water be free give and they say this fountain once lose his virtue when they deny to give water to the poor every one give the porter a small reward when he come in not when he go out as otherwhere be use for this be proper to the german that they will be pay ere they begin to work as if they have do the profit of this salt fountain be divide into diverse part some to the city some to the duke of Luneberg who howsoever he be so call yet have no power over the city but only over the country some belong to the monastary and diverse earl have their part whereof some boil not the salt in their own name but set it out to other there be fifty two room and in each of they eight leaden pan in which eight ton of salt be daily boil and each ton be worth eight flemish shilling in the say monastery within the town they show a table of gold which Henry Leo duke of Saxony take from Milan and place here and it be fasten to the altar be more than a ell and half long and about three quarter broad and little or nothing thick than a french crown they show also four cross of pure gold which they say a certain queen once take from they but present fall lunatic neither can be cure until she have restore they in the open street some monument be set on the wall in honour of certain citizen who die in a night tumult when the duke hope to surprise the city I say that the senate house be stately build in which they show to stranger many vessel of gold and silver of a great value and quantity for a city of that quality from Luneburg I return to hamburg whither I and my company may have have a coach for 4. dollar but we mislike the price hire a waggon for three Lubeck shilling each person to Wentzon three mile distant from Luneburg here the duke of Lunebergs territory end to who each man pay a Lubeck shilling for tribute myself only except who have that privilege because I go to study in the university here each man pay two Lubeck shilling for a wagon to the ell side be one mile and the same day by water we pass other three mile to hamburg not without great noy someness from some base people in the boat for which passage we pay each man three Lubeck shilling let i admonish the reader that if when we take boat we have only cross the ell we may have hire a wagon from Tolspecker a village to hamburg be three mile for two dollar among six person be at hamburg and purpose to go up into mizzen because I have not the language I compound with a merchant to carry i in his coach and bear my charge to leipzig for ten gold gulden the first day have break our fast at hamburg we pass seven mile over the heath of Luneburg and lodge in a village in our way we pass many village of poor base house and some pleasant grove but all the country be barren yet yield corn in some place though in no plenty the second day we come to a little city Corneiler through a country as barren as the former and towards our journey end we pass a thick wood of a mile long the three day we go seven mile to Magdenburg which be count six and twenty mile from hamburg and this day Magdenburg we pass a more fertile country and more woody and they show i by the way a hill call Bockesberg famous with many ridiculous fable of witch yearly meeting in that place this city of old call Parthenopolis of Venus Parthenea be now call Magdenburg that be the city of virgin and for a inland city be very fair and the german speak much of the fortification because mauricius elector of Saxony besiege it a whole year with the emperor Charles the five his army yet take it not howbeit I think that not so much to be attribute to the strength of the city as to the distract mind of the besieger who in the mean time solicit the french king to join with the dutch prince to free Germany from the emperor tyranny and the french army be once on foot himself raise force against the emperor the form of this city be like a moon increase the bishopric thereof be rich and the margrave of Brandeburg his elder son do then possess it together with the city and territory by the title of administrator in which sort he also hold the bishopric of hall and he lie then at wormested a castle not far of in the market place there be a statue erect to the emperor Otho the great founder of that city and Munster write of another statue erect to Rowland which I remember not to have see in the senate-house they show a singular picture make by one Lucas a famous painter dead some thirty year before where also be the picture of that monstrous german with all the dimension of his body who not long before be lead about the world to be show for a wonder this man I have not see but in this picture I can scarce reach the crown of his head with the point of my rapier and many of good credit tell i that they have see this man sister half a ell high than he in the church that lie near the market place there be a font of great worth and a lute paint with great art the cathedral church of saint Maurice be build by Otho the great very sumptuous where his wife lie bury in the year 948. and the inscription be that she be daughter to Edmund king of England there they show one of the three vessel in which our saviour Christ turn water into wine at Cana in Galilee there be in all ten church but the above name be the fair hence we go fourteen mile to leipzig be a day and a half journey through fruitful corn field and a country full of rich village the merchant with who I go bear my charge from hamburg I may have hire a coach to leipzig for six person those of Nurnburg bear eight for 24. dollar and if a man go thence to Luneburg he may easy light on a coach of return at a less rate so that in respect of the cheapness of victual in these part no doubt I give the merchant too much for my charge in this journey leipzig be seat in a plain of most fruitful corn ground and full of rich village leipzig in a country call mizzen subject to the elector duke of Saxony and the country lie open to the eye in a most ample prospect only one wood can be see in this large plain the street be fair the market place large and stately and such be the chief house build of free stone four roof high there be a convenient conduit of water in the suburb lie towards prage the ditch be dry the wall of stone threaten ruin neither may the citizen fortify the town nor use red wax in their public seal nor wind a horn in their night watch as other city do these and other privilege be take from they in the year 1307. when they kill their duke in saint Thomas church out of this city they have as many city in Germany have a beautiful place to bury their dead call gods-aker vulgar gotts-aker where the chief citizen buy place of burial proper to their family round about the cloister and the common sort be bury in the midst not cover with any build here I find this epitaph the numeral letter whereof show the year when the party die and like epitaph be ordinary find through Germany this city have a university and in the year 1480. the student of prage remove hither to fly the hussite war but at this day the university be much decay by reason that Wittteberg lie near have better conveniency for the scholar live from hence I take my journey in the afternoon to Witteberg and come that night to a village four mile distant through a wood so large as we can not pass it in two hour beyond which the ground be barren till we pass the river Elue which run by Witteberg all the length of it from the east to the west but be somewhat distant from the town the next day we pass four mile to Witteberg which have his name of Wittekindus the first christian duke of Saxony and be seat in a plain sandy ground have Witteberg on the north hill plant with vine yield a sour grape plentiful yet they make no wine thereof one street lie the whole length of the town be all the beauty thereof and in the midst of this street be the cathedral church and a fair market place in which the senate house be build and near the west gate be the duke church it be proverbial say that a man shall meet nothing at Witteberg but whore student and swine to which purpose they have these two verse Witeberga sue have Witeberg no swine if no whore be nor Phoebus train I pray you what be there whence may be gather that the citizen have small traffic live only upon the scholar and that the street must needs be filthy in the study of doctor this inscription be in latin here stand the bed in which Luther gentle die see how much they attribute to Luther for this be not the place where he die neither be there any bed yet suffer they not the least memory of he to be blot out Luther be bear at Isleb in the year 1483 and certain die there in the house of count Mansfield where after supper the seventeen of February he fall into his usual sickness namely the stop of humour in the orifice of his belly and die thereupon at five of the clock in the morning the eighteen of February in the year 1546. the say count and his countess and many other be present and receive great comfort from his last exhortation yet from his sudden death the malicious jesuit take occasion to slander he as if he die drunken that by aspersion on his life and death they may slander the reformation of religion which he first begin these man after their manner be to conjure a unclean spirit out of a man in prage give out that he be free from this spirit for the time that Luther die and that when he return they examine he where he have be that time and the spirit shall answer that he have attend Luther Philip melancthon bear in the year 1497. die 1560. and both these famous man be bury and have their monument in the duke church at Witteberg which be say to be like that of Jerusalem and in that both of they be round I will not deny it but I dare say they differ in this that Jerusalem church have the chancel in the midst with ally to go round about it whereas the chancel of this church be at the east end of it the witteberger tell many thing of Luther which seem fabulous and among other thing they show a aspersion of ink cast by the devil when he tempt Luther upon the wall in s. Augustine college beside they show a house wherein doctor Faustus a famous conjurer dwell they say that this doctor live there about the year 1500. and have a tree all blast and burn in the adjoin wood where he practise his magic art and that he die or rather be fetch by the devil in a village near the town I do see the tree so burn but walk at leisure through all the village adjoin I can never hear any memory of his end not far from the city there be a mountain call the mount of Apollo which then as of old abound with medicinable herb in a village near the town there be yet many token that the emperor Charles the five encamp there I live at Witteberg the rest of this summer where I pay a Gulden weekly for my diet and beer which they account apart and for my chamber after the rate of ten gulden by the year I hear that since all thing be dear the scholar use to pay each week a dolor for their diet and a dolor for chamber and wash hence I take my journey to Friburge that I may see the funeral of christianus the elector three of we hire a coach all this journey for a dolor each day with condition that we shall pay for the meat of the horse and of the coachman which cost as much more and this we pay because we have freedom to leave the coach at our pleasure though we return with it to leipzig to which if we will have tie ourselves we may have have the coach for half a dolor a day the first day we go six mile to Torge through sandy field yield corn and we dine at belgar a village where each man pay five grosh for his dinner and by the way they show we a village call itzan where Luther make his first sermon of reformation Torge be a fair city of mizzen of a round form fall each way from a mountain and seat on the west side of ell it have a stately castle belong to the Torg elector duke of Saxony who be lord of leipzig Witteberg and all the city we shall pass in this journey this castle be wash with the river Elue and be build by John Fredricke elector in the year 1535. it have a wind way or plain stair by which a horse may easy go to the top of the castle the passage be so plain as the ascent can scarce be discern the hall chamber and gallery of this castle be very fair and beautiful and adorn with artificial picture among which one of a boy present flower be fair than the rest also there be a picture on the wall of one Laurence Weydenberg a sweitzer make in the twenty year of his age in the year 1531 show that he be nine foot high in the church there be a monument of Katherine a nun which die 1552. and be wife unto Luther the village Milburg be within a mile of this city in the way to dresden where the elector Frederick be take prisone by Charles the five in the protestant war the lake near the city be a mile in circuit for the fish whereof the citizen pay 500 gulden yearly to the elector of Saxony and they fish it once in three year and sell the fish for some 5000. gulden the bear of Torge be much esteem through all mizzen whereof they sell such quantity abroad as ten watermil beside windmill scarce serve the town for this purpose from Torge we go six mile to mizzen in our coach hire as aforesaid and we dine mizzen each man for five in the village star and we pass through goodly cornehil and fair wood of fir and birtch the city mizzen be round in form and almost all the house be build on the fall side of mountain which compass all the city open towards the east where ell run by duke Frederick surname the wise and duke George surname papistical be bury in the cathedral church here I pay six grosh every meal the city be subject to the duke of Saxony have the same name with the whole country in which it lie hence we go three mile to dresden in a coach hire as aforesaid and pass through sandy and stony hill some fruitful valley of corn and two wood of fir whereof there be many near dresden whither be come I pay six grosh for my dinner this city of dresden be very fair and strong fortify in which the elector of dresden Saxony keep his court have be forty year pass only a village when the first stone of the wall be lay there be hide a silver cup guild a book of the law another of the coin and three glass fill with wine the ceremony be perform with all kind of music and solemnity the like ceremony be use when they lay the first stone of the stable the city be of a round form seat in a plain run between two mountain but some what distant and the house be fair build of free stone four or five roof high whereof the high roof after the italian fashion be little raise in steepness so that the top of the house appear not over the wall except the elector castle build betwixt the north and west side and the church tower build between the west and east side in this tower the watchman dwell who in the day time give notice by flag hang out what number of foot or horse be come towards the town to which tower they ascend by two hundred seventy stair and in the top two demiculuerin be plant we enter on the east side through old dresden be wall about and so pass the ell compass the wall of new dresden on the east side by a bridge of stone have seventeen arch under which half the ground be not cover with water except it be with a flood upon the bridge we pass three gate and at the end enter the city by the four where the garrison soldier write the name of those that come in and lead they to the inn where the host again take their name the city have but two little suburb the citizen be then as busy as bee in fortify the city which the elector than make very strong the ground rise on all side towards the town and the new city have four gate welsh-thore siegeld-thore new-thore and salomons-thore and be compass with two wall between which round about there be a garden from which man may ascend or descend to it at each gate over the outward wall there be a cover or close gallery private to the elector who therein may compass the town unseen he have use the best wit of Germany and Italy in this fortification wherein he have spare no cost the wall be high and broad of earth who foundation be of stone and they be on all side furnish with great artillery yea in that time of peace the street be shut with iron chain at eat time and all night the elector stable be by much the fair that ever I see which I will brief describe in the first court there be a horse-bath into which they may bring as much or little water as they list and it have 22. pillar in each whereof diverse arm of the duke be grave according to the diverse family who arm he give the same court serve for a tilting-yard and all exercise of horse-manship and there be also the horseleech shop so well furnish as if it belong to a rich apothecary the building of the stable be four square but the side towards the duke palace be all take up with two gate and a little court yard which take up half this side and round about the same be little cuboord peculiar to the horseman in which they dispose all the furniture fit for ride the other three side of the quadrangle contain some 136. choice and rare horse have only two other gate lead into the city market place opposite to those gate towards the court. these horse be all of foreign country for there be another stable for dutch horse and among these chief horse one name Michael Schatz that be Michael the treasure be say to be of wonderful swiftness before each horse nose be a glass window with a curtain of green cloth to be draw at pleasure each horse be cover with a red mantle the rack be of iron the manger of copper at the buttock of each horse be a pillar of wood which have a brazen shield where by the turn of a pipe he be water and in this pillar be a cuboord to lay up the horse comb and like necessary and above the back of each horse hang his bridleand saddle so as the horse may as it be in a moment be furnish above this stable be a gallery on one side adorn with the statue of horse and their rider with their complete armour fifty in number beside many armour lie by the wall on the other side be a gallery have forty like statue and thirty six sledge which they use in mizzen not only to journey in time of snow but also for festival pomp for in those city especial at Shrovetide and when much snow fall they use to sit upon sledge draw with a horse furnish with many bell at the foot of which sledge they many time place their mistress and if in run or sudden turn the rider or his mistress slip or take a fall it be hold a great disgrace to the rider some of these sledge be very sumptuous as of unpurified silver as it come from the mine other be fair cover with velvet and like stuff above the forepart of the stable towards the market place be the chamber wherein the elector feast with ambassador in the window of the first chamber or stove be a bay window towards the street be a round table of marble with many inscription persuade temperance such as be these Demat. be not drunken in youth or age or no more than may care assuage again gluttony kill more than the sword yet I dare say that notwithstanding all these good precept few or none ever rise or rather be not carry as unable to go from that table twelve little marble chair belong to this table and the pavement of the room be marble and close by the table there be a rock curious carve with image of fish and creep thing this rock put forth many sharp pinnacle of stone upon which the vessel of gold and silver be set forth at the feast and when the drink be at hot the statue of a horseman by work of great art come out of the rock and present each stranger with a huge bowl of wine which he must drink off for his welcome without expect that any shall pledge he in the next chamber belong to this stove be a bedstead of marble and both have hang of gold leather there be another chamber and another stove like these and above they in the uppermost loft there be many little room whereof one be furnish with spear another with saddle and among they I remember one which in the pummel gild head with eye continual move and in the hinder part have a clock the rest be furnish with sword shield helmet and feather among the sword every prince have his own which the successor use not to wear and there be one belong to the electorship when he exercise his office as marshal of the empire there be another sword have in the hilt two little pistol here I see lay up a iron chain in which they say that duke Henry the father of Manrice the first elector of this family shall have be hang in the low country who escape bring the same with he and lay it up here for memory after the funeral of christianus return from Friburg to this town I find only fifteen of those choice horse in the stable all the rest have be give to prince come to the funeral the duke palace in dresaen be build by mauricius part of the city wall and the gate be build by Augustus who do also lay the foundation of this stable but christianus the elector perfect the wall of the city with the close gallery over they and build this famous stable set this inscription upon the wall in latin christianus duke of Saxony heir to Augustus the elector of happy memory and imitator of his virtue cause this stable to be build and the yard adjoin to be fit for tilt and military exercise the present age etc. the armoury at dresden be no less worth the note wherein be arm and all kind of munition for seventy thousand man but of late it have be somewhat empty by a expedition into France undertake by the duke at the solicitation of the count of Turin ambassador for Henry the four king of France the duke be at great charge in keep garrison soldier at dresden and officer as well for the stable as the armoury in these part and no where else in Germany they use boat of a hollow tree drive not by oar but by battledore whereof I see many upon the ell as likewise water mill swim upon boat and remove from place to place the like whereof be since make at London by a Dutchman but become unprofitable by the ebb and flow of Thames at dresden I pay seven grosh a meal hence in our coach hire as aforesaid we pass four mile to Friburg through Friburg fruitful hill and mountain of corn but few or no wood and here we pay each man fix a meal this city be of a round form compass of all side with mountain have many fault or cave under it by which the citizen enter and go out of the city by night to work in the silver mine yet have the city two wall and two ditch but altogether dry it have five gate and four church among which saint Peter church be the fair the elector have his castle in the city and in the church as I remember of saint Peter wherein the duke use to be bury mauricius have a very fair monument of black marble raise in three pile whereof each be deck with divet statue of white marble and alabaster whereof two belong to mauricius the one in posture of pray the other arm and recein a deadly wound two monument be begin but not then perfect for Augustus and christianus the territory of Friburg abound with silver mine whereof some and fine hundred fathom deep some seven hundred and some nine hundred and after each thirty fathom the earth be support with great beam of timber lest it shall fall and from each of these building wind stair of wood be make to descend to the bottom the citizen live of these mine and grow rich thereby whereof the elector have his proper part and use to buy the part of the citizen the workman use burn lamp under the earth both day and night and use to work as well by night as by day and they report that come near the pure vein of silver they be often trouble with evil spirit these workman go out to the mine by night through the cave under the city and be call back from work by the sound of a bell they come in the same way the water which the workman use spring in a mountain a arrow shoot from the town whence fall to a low mountain it be convey by hollow tree to fall upon the wheel of the mill so as a little quantity thereof drive they these mill draw the water up out of the mine for the depth of forty fathom whence it run in pipe towards the city when they try and purify the silver first with water they wash away the red earth than they beat the mettle with a hand hammer and thus break they cast it into the fire which they make in the open air lest the workman shall be stifle with the fume of the brimstone then they melt the mettle six time by a fire make of whole tree in a little house adjoin then in another house they sever the mettle from the earth with a then again they beat the mettle with a hammer drive by a mill and thus beat they wash it upon three clothes hang slopewise and the pure silver stick to the uppermost cloth this do they melt it again six time and the best of the dross be lead and silver the rest copper and this silver and lead be again melt the lead fall from the silver like dust the fire wherein they try this silver be so hot as it consume the brick furnace in three day the workman beside their hire use to be reward for expedition of the work the mean by which they find silver be very strange be by a rod which vulgar they call chassel-wand or the divine rod which they carry in their hand and when they go over silver they say the rod bend or break if it be straight hold and there be not above seven man in this city which have skill of this mystery the way be plant with tree to direct passenger lest as they go to the city they shall fall into the mine for as they report it have often happen that the citizen themselves have perish in this sort and the like mischance happen late to five workman though skilful in the way these mine of Friburg be first find in the year 1180 yield to this day rich vein of silver there be other mine of silver near these since that time discover namely at Schaneberg find in the year 1470 at Anneberg find 1510 in joachimus valley find 1526. for this elector of Saxony have many mine of silver which lest any shall think to belong of right to the emperor it must be observe that the prince elector have regal power under the emperor grant by privilege in the law of the golden bulla by which they have right to all mine find in their own territory after I have see the elector christianus his funeral at Eriburg the ceremony whereof shall be describe in the proper place I return to dresden in a coach hire as above say and so from dresden to mizzen but from mizzen I return not to Torge but take another way to leipzig be ten mile which we go in one day four mile to three to Wortson where we dine and thence three mile to leipzig all through plain and fruitful corn field I spend this winter at leipzig that I may there learn to speak the dutch tongue the grammar whereof I have read at Witteberg because the mizzen speech be hold the pure of all other part in Germany here each student use to bay for his diet a Gulden weekly beside beer for which every man pay according to his drink some less some more most beyond measure for the citizen have no beer in their house but one kind which be very small and buy the better kind as that of Torge which the rich sort usual drink from a public house where it be sell by small measure to the profit of the senate beside the scholar pay several for their bed and chamber myself lodge with a rich citizen and for diet bed and chamber pay weekly a Doller and a half chap. 2 of my journey from leipzig to prage in Bohemia to nurnberg Augspurg ulm Lyndaw Costnetz in Germany Scaphusen Zurech baden and bazell in Sweitzerland be to take my journey to prage in the end of the year 1591. after the english account who begin the year upon the twenty five of march I return again to dresden from whence I write this letter concern my journey to a friend lie at leipzig honest m. know that after I part from you at Torg by good hap and beside my expectation I light upon a coach go to dresden with which good hap while I be affect and haste to hire a place therein I have forget to pay for my coach for the day before but when we be ready to go remember my error and entreat my consort to stay a while for i I run back to the inn as speedy as the parasite Curculio in Plautus and find not the coachman there I give the money to the servant of the house before witness and so return to the coach all sweat with haste there I find that dunghill rascal the coachman have my gown on his back I lay hold of the garment as if I know it and he hold it as fast as a pledge for his money I be enrage that he shall use i so when I have deal honest with he draw my sword and make know that I have pay the money bid he lay down the gown upon his peril I have almost draw a rabble of coachman on my back but they forbear i in this heat for you know they be not apt to quarrel in the morning but if I have thus provoke they in the afternoon be warm with drink sure they will have run upon i though they have be naked will you know the companion of my journey I be alone among a coach full of woman and those of the elector duchess chamber for sooth which you will have say to have be of the black guard it be a comedy for i to hear their discourse now declaim against caluenist now brawl together now mutual with tear bewail their hard fortune and they fall into all these change while the wind blow from one and the same quarter be anything light than a woman and lest the flock of goose shall want matter sometime they charge i to be a Caluenist sometime a jew and I answer merry that if any of they be but a consul wife I will satisfy they for my religion at eight a clock in the night the horse be spend myself weary and only their tongue untired we come to a village call Derwaldhan where we shall lodge we enter a kind of barn myself not without sigh Lipsius shall here have have no cause to complain of stink beer brown bread and often shake hand no man return salutation to we the woman my companion draw out victual they have bring to eat I be fast to that hour with great fear and tremble of heart expect that at least they will give i some raw bacon or dry pudding but they think nothing less at last I desire a egg or two for my supper the servant answer that the old woman be in bed and that he know not the mystery whether any egg be in the house or no. if the comical poet Saturio have be here he will have fall into a sound to be brief the woman take compassion on i and I without blush be content to eat of free cost and make they know that I be no jew for I make no religion to eat what be before i the next morning before the daystar arise I be walk in a meadow what do you bless yourself with a cross sure I be no less sleepy than I be but he be soon apparel that have a dog bed in straw yet this straw be clean which be no small favour and when I give the servant a mizzen for his pain he be astonish as if he have never see a whole before so as he forget to thank i only show it to the stander by as if I have deserve another burden of fresh straw the woman virgin man and maid servant all of we lay in one room and myself be lodge further from the stove which they do not for any favour though contrary to their opinion I be glad of it delight more in sweet air than the smoke of a dunghill my companion laugh at i for babble dutch in my sleep sure reason command i wake have not power over i in sleep to hinder i from revolve the pleasant passage of the day past on Saturday the same day I come to dresden from whence five passenger be new go for prage in a coach but after three day expectation I have find new consort and to morrow be Wednesday and the eight of march I begin my journey to Bohemia while I despair of consort I be determine to go in a boat'vpon the ell and have now buy clean straw in which I mean to triumph alone when by good hap turn from the river to the inn I light upon this commodity of a coach which have free i from the annoyance of the water and mariner embrace in my name our common friend g. b. and of my love host family let not a whelp go unsaluted farewell honest m. and return i love for love from dresden the seven of march 1591. myself and four consort hire a coach for 14. dollar from dresden to prage the first day we go three mile to Gottleben a village where we pay five bohemian that be six white each man for his dinner half the way be on the west side of the river Elue in a fertile plain than we pass the ell and travel through mountain yet fertile and a baggy wood after dinner we go two mile to a village where we lodge through stony mountain without any wood and in the mid way there be a wooden pillar which divide the territory of the saxon elector from the kingdom of Bohemis the second day we go two mile through stony mountain bear not one tree to Ansig a little city where we pay for our breakfast four bohemian grosh the same day we pass three mile in a straight between rock lie upon the ell and two short mile through fruitful corn field to Wedin lie upon the river which run a little below into the ell but be here at this time so deep by a flood or melt of snow as our coach in come to the bridge of Wedin by the bank side take water the three day we pass a mile to a village call Welber or Welberg through fruitful hill of corn without any wood and there each man pay 5 bohemian grosh for his dinner in the afternoon we go three mile for the most part through fruitful hill of come the rest through rock and mountain plant with vine and so come to prage through which the river Molda run but be not navigable on the west side of Molda be the emperor castle seat on a most high mountain in the fall prage whereof be the suburb call kleinseit or little side from this suburb to go into the city a long stone bridge be to be pass over Molda which run from the south to the north and divide the suburb from the city to which as you go on the left side be a little city of the jew compass with wall and before your eye towards the east be the city call new prage both which city be compass about with a three call old prage so as prage consist of three city all compass with wall yet be nothing less than strong and except the stinch of the street drive back the turk or they meet they in open field there be small hope in the fortification thereof the street be filthy there be diverse large market place the build of some house be of free stone but the most part be of timber and clay and be build with little beauty or art the wall be all of whole tree as they come out of the wood the which with the bark be lay so rude as they may on both side be see Molda in the winter use to be so freeze as it bear cart and the ice thereof be cut in great piece be lay up in cellar for the emperor and prince to mingle with their wine in summer which i think can neither be savoury nor healthful since neither the heat of the clime nor the strength of the bohemian wine be small and sharp require any such cool one thing they boast to be proper to that kingdom that the ell Molda and all other river water Bohemia have their spring head in the same in public inn they demand some six bohemian grosh for a meal yet do they not common give meal at a ordinary rate as they do through all Germany but what meat you require that they dress and the servant buy all thing out of door after the manner of Poland make a reckon of the expense myself have my diet with a citizen very convenient for a dolor and a half weekly I do here eat english oyster pickle and a young bohemian come in by chance and taste they but not know the price desire the merchant to give he a dish at his charge which contain some twenty oyster and find they very savoury he call for five dish one after another for which the merchant demand and have of he five dollar the dearness no less displease his mind than the meat have please his taste as you pass over Molda from the suburb kleinseit into the city there be a hand of stone as it be cut off upon the gate of the city signify to stranger that whosoever draw a sword there or upon the bridge lose his hand and the like hand there be to the same purpose on the senate house in the town the emperor have two enclosure wall about which they call gardaine one of which be call Stella because the tree be plant in the figure of star and a little fair house therein be likewise build with six corner in form of a star and in this place he keep 12-cammel a indian ox yellow all over rugged and hairy upon the throat like a lion and a indian calf and two leopard which be say to be tame if such wild beast may be tame they be of a yellow colour spot with black the head partly like a lion partly like a cat the tail like a cat the body like a greyhound and when the huntsman go abroad at call they leap up behind he sit upon the horse like a dog on the hinder part be so swift in run as they will easy kill a hart. I remember that a gentleman of Hungary walk in the street with i and observe a picture on a wall of a german protect a hungarian from a Turk with this inscription not for thy sake but my own take this occasion to complain much of the emperor and relate many thing wherein his prodigality and other error have overthrow Hungary and have do nothing less than ease their afflict fortune in the church Emaus this inscription be grave  the tenor of king Alexander the great his etc. we Alexander the great of king Philip the founder of the grecian empire conqueror of the persian meades etc. and of the whole world from the east to west from north to south son of great jupiter by etc. so call to you the noble stock of the sclavonian and to your language because you have be to i helper true in faith and valiant in war I confirm all this tract from the north to the south part of Italy from i and my successor to you and your posterity if any other nation be find there let they be your slave date in our city of Alexandria new found by we upon the great river of Nilus the twelve of the goddess Minerva witness be aethra and the 2 prince who we appoint successor to we die without heir also there be these verse show by numeral letter the antiquity of prage university from the year 867. though the college there about ruin scarce show any shadow of this university the numeral great letter show the year this universal school be found here in the church of the emperor castle these thing be to be see a fair chapel name after the emperor sister marry to the french king and crown queen of the french another chapel belong to the baron call the great family of the kingdom next to the baron of rosenberg which chapel be proper to they for burial and be dedicate to St Andrew the apostle in the emperor church be a monument of rodolphus the second then live emperor build of white marble and compass with grate of iron in the same place lie bury Charles the four in the year 1378. Wenceslaus in the year 1419. Ladislaus in the year 1459. Ferdinand the four in the year 1564. Maximilian the second in the year 1577. all be archduke of Austria and emperor and George a bohemian and king of Bohemia to all these be one monument erect and that of small beauty in the same church be the monument of a bishop who be the queen confessor be cast into Molda because he will not reveal her confession to her husband Wenceslaus they do so reverence the monument of this bishop since make a saint by the pope as they think he shall die with shame that pass by it without reverence in old prage towards the south and upon the east side of Molda there be a old palace where they show a trap-door by which the queen be wont to slide down into a bath where she use to satisfy her unlawful lust in the same place be grave the leap of a horse no less wonderful than byard fabulous leap the house of kelley a famous english alchemist be of old a sanctuary and build for a order of friar upon the gate whereof these verse be write have favour and this house through old king clemency free from all law no threat respect dare not fright they with slavery who under God Vrabsky protect in the senate house the city arm be paint be a castle with three tower over and two lion argent lange gules be the supporter and these verse be write upon the arm Praga who so these tower arm to old prage give give lucky sign of future happiness for as the tower over top the wall most brave so prage thou do surpass thy fame no less not far from prage they say that the crown of the kingdom be lay up in Karlsteine castle at prage in Bethlem church they show a pulpit in which John hus use to preach at the first reformation of religion I live at prage some two month and be to depart from thence I will have go to Vienna the famous fort against the turk but my desire to see nurnberg and Augspurg so prevail as I leave that journey which by chance happen better than I imagine for be call back into the low country as hereafter I shall relate I pass thence through Poland into Italy because France be shut up by the civil war and I ever shun to go twice one way and so have the opportunity more fit than the former to see Vienna now for their sake who may pass from prage to Vienna give i leave to remember that in this way their be a village call Chassel some nine mile from prage where the famous captain of the bohemian call Ziska lie bury who do lead the hussite valiant and be ready to die wish they to make a drum of his skin ominate that the sound thereof will be so terrible to the enemy as they will run away such confidence have be in arm as be dead he think to terrify his enemy in the begin of the year 1592. I take my journey from prage to nurnberg 1592. be six day journey the first day after dinner we go four mile partly through rocky mountain partly through a fruitful corn plain and lodge at berawn where a loaf of bread worth two three part of a creitzer be as big as a threepenny loaf in England by reason of the abundance of corn in that country and here each man pay for his supper fourteen creitzer this city belong to the family of the pople the second day we go four mile to Zudermont all through mountain and grove and two great wood yet reasonable fruitful in corn and by the way we see the city bodily and the city spill the fair of that kingdom next to prage both belong to the emperor and two castle belong to the baron popeles and here each man dine for sixteen creitzer after dinner we go two mile to pilsen half the way through wood where be a little city Ruchtsan and half through hill and plain fruitful in corn almost the whole country of Bohemta be hilly and rich ground for pasture and corn and here we sup each man for twenty three creitzer the three day we go three mile to Kladen through pleasant hill of grove pasture and corn where each man dine for eighteen creitzer after dinner we go three mile to Frawenberg through high mountain and great wood have no great store of corn in all which territory the city and village acknowledge the emperor for their supreme lord as he be king of Bohemia for this kingdom be not divide as other be into province and country but into nobleman territory here we pay each man eight creitzer for our supper and twelve for wine the four day we go a mile and a half to a little river divide Bohemia or Boemerland from Germany through rocky mountain and many wood of tall fir tree fit to make mast for ship then we enter a country belong to that elector Palatine of the Rhine which elector be call vulgar the phaltz-grave and we come within half a mile to weithawsan where each man dine for eighteen creitzer after dinner we go two mile in the phaltzgraves country through woody mountain and one mile in the landgrave of Leytenberg his country through fruitful corn field and lodge at Shenhutton where each man pay six creitzer for his supper and thirteen for wine for in these part they drink no beer as before but wine and that at a low price than other where whether it be native or foreign yet no man must wonder that we spend more in wine than meat all my consort be dutchman the five day we go in the phaltzgraves country four mile to amberg through fruitful hill of corn and some few wood and this city belong to the phaltzgrave be seat in the upper palatinate after dinner we go in the marquis of Anspach his country who be also call the burggave of nurnberg two mile to hous-coate a village where each man pay six Batzen for his supper the six day we go three mile pass by erspruck a city subject to the nurnberger and many village belong to divet lord and a fort in the mid way call schwang belong to seventy two lord and be then by course in the phaltzgraves keep for all these lord keep the same by course for three year the first and great part of the way be through fruitful hill of corn the rest through sandy pasture and a wood of a mile length we dine at a poor village each man for six Batzen after dinner we go two mile to nurnberg through sandy corn field and pass by many house and garden of the citizen whether they use to come out of the city sometime to recreate themselves the wood which we pass in the morning lay on our left hand towards the south as we enter the city on the east side and not far from the city turn itself and run far towards the south the city of nurnberg seat in a barren sandy ground yet be very rich by the nurnberg citizen industry for as common few be rich in a fertile country either because have enough for food they be give to idleness or because abundance make they prodigal so the nurnberger plant in a barren soil by their subtle invention of manual work and cunning art draw the riches of all country to they the river bengett run by the city but be not navigable nor bear any the least boat this river run from the east where we enter the town towards the gate lauff-thore and so compass the suburb towards the south where divide into two bed it enter the city and come out again at the west wash the city wall on the east side the margrave of Brandeburg besiege the city at the command of Charles the five therefore on this and the south side beside a dry ditch and two stone wall compass the whole city diverse bulwark be build upon the wall on all side as you come up to the city the earth rise and almost at every gate there be a long suburb upon the wall there be many tower distant one from the other some 1000 ordinary walk pace and the whole circuit of the city be less than a german mile among the say tower three be strong than the rest and furnish with artillery the first be on the east side near the gate lauff-thore the second be on the south side under the gate fraw-thore and on the same side be the gate spittle-thore the three be on the north side under the gate new-thore and on the same side be another gate call burk-thore there be a castle call burk which by Nero the emperor be of his name call Noricum castrum it be certain that this castle stand in the time of Charles the great and the city be of itself not ancient be think to have have his name of this castle old latin name on the west side be the gate haller-thore so call of he that cause it to be build where be a pleasant walk thick shadow with tree where the citizen use to walk for pleasure the city be absolute of itself be one of the free city of the empire and i think the chief or at least second to augsburg sure it may perhaps yield to augsburg in treasure and riches of the city but it must be prefer for the building whichis all of free stone six or seven roof high I speak of the whole city of augsburg for one street thereof be most beautiful and some palace there be fit for prince of which kind nurnberg have none the tower which I say be of old call Noricum castrum hang over the city which be seat in a plain have no mount near it and be of a round form the say tower be compass with a dry ditch very deep upon the wall whereof they a spaniard blood there sprinkle who undertake to betray the castle to Charles the five as also the print of a horse foot in memory of a wonderful leap from the castle side to the other side of the bridge the senate house lie under the side of this castle or tower as it be under the shield of Aiax and under the same house and under the earth be the public prison the armoury be build on the south side of the town and be open to no man without consent of the senate which in all other city of Germany be ready show to stranger and in that armoury by the citizen report they have 400. great piece of artillery with great store of all munition the city have also a granary which be so large as diverse year provision for corn may be lay up therein it have churches whereof only four be use for prayer and preach and in one of they lie bury zebalemus-king of Denmark who first convert the city to christian religion near the church of saint Laurence be the golden fountain so call of the beauty and magnificence and it distil water out of twenty leaden pipe near the church call Frawenkirk be another fair fountain guild over and compass with a iron grate it be unlawful to walk in the night without a torch or a candle and lantern in the inn they give no beer at the table but diverse kind of wine and a large diet if not delicate for which every man pay six batzen a meal and beside for his chamber or lodging which he may have private to himself three creitzer by the day in the almshouse out of gift by the last testament of those that die they maintain great number of poor people and in one of they twelve old man apart and in another twelve old man and as many old woman whilst I live at prage and one night have set up very late drink at a feast early in the morning the sun beam glance on my face as I lie in bed I dream that a shadow pass by tell i that my father be dead at which awake all in a sweat and affect with this dream I rise and write the day and hour and all circumstance thereof in a paper book which book with many other thing I put into a barrel and send it from prage to stand thence to be convey into England and now be at nurnberg a merchant of a noble family well acquaint with i and my friend arrive there who tell i that my father die some two month past I list not write any lie but that which I write be as true as strange when I return into England some four year after I will not open the barrel I send from prage nor look on the paper book in which I have write this dream till I have call my sister and some friend to be witness where myself and they be astonish to see my write dream answer the very day of my father death I may lawful swear that which my kinsman have hear witness by my brother Henry whilst he live that in my youth at Cambridge I have the like dream of my mother death where my brother Henry lie with i early in the morning I dream that my mother pass by with a sad countenance and tell i that she can not come to my commencement I be within five month to proceed master of art and she have promise at that time to come to Cambridge and when I relate this dream to my brother both of we awake together in a sweat he protest to i that he have dream the very same and when we have not the least knowledge of our mother sickness neither in our youthful affection be any whit affect with the strangeness of this dream yet the next carrier bring we word of our mother death be as I have say certify of my father death at nurnberg and think not fit to go on my journey into Italy and yet be loath to return into England before I have finish my purpose voyage I take the middle counsel to return into the low country that in those near place I may dispose of my small patrimony for in England gentleman give their young son less then in foreign part they give to their bastard and so may leave the same in the hand of some trusty friend yet lest I shall loose the opportunity of see augsburg mean to return some other way into Italy I resolve to go from hence to augsburg and then to cross over the west part of Germany and so to pass along the river Rhein into the low country to augsburg be two day journey and a half I hire of the city carrier in who company I go a horse for two dollar as I remember the merchant of nurnberg and augsburg give pension to eight of these carrier daily pass between those city beside the profit they make of letter and other thing they carry by horse the first day after breakfast we ride one mile in a thick wood and another mile through sandy corn field somewhat woody both in the territory of the nurnberger and four mile more in the territory of the margrave of Anspach to Blinfield where each man pay for his supper and horse meat six batzen the second day we ride four mile to Monheime through a wood of juniper full of black berry and barbery at the end whereof be a free city call Wassenberge and after through fruitful hill and valley of corn all the territory except the free city belong to the marshal of the emperor not of the empire when we come almost to our journeys end the carrier have a guide give he according to custom for thief use to lie by that way Monheime belong to the phaltz-grave of newburg be of the family of the phaltzgrave of Rhein and there we pay each man for his dinner and horse-meat thirty four creitzer which make eight batzen and a half and there we taste juniper wine which I never remember to have taste else where after dinner we ride two mile and a half through fruitful hill of corn and a small wood of Okes though all the wood of upper Germany be common of fir bear green leave all winter as those of inferior Germany towards Denmark be all of Okes. by the way we pass a monastery grant to the phaltzgrave of Rhein by the emperor and a free city of the empire call Donaward of the two Rivers Danow and werd meet there and there we pass by bridge the Danow run by the city then we ride to Weschendorff two mile and a half more through fruitful field of corn and pasture the country belong to the fugare citizen of augsburg and to diverse other lord the castle of this place belong to the say fugare who be rich and famous for their treasure and though they have princely revenue and the title of baron yet still be merchant here each man pay for his supper and horse-meat 8. batzen and a half the three day in the morning we ride three mile to augsburg through a fruitful augsburg plain of corn without the wall whereof on the east and north and some part of the south side the field be drown with water and man pass to the city by causy for on these side the ground lie low but on the west side be all the beauty of the city where the house be seat upon a hill and there be a place for the merchant to meet call the berle and likewise the senate house in the street weingasse so call of the wine cellar there also be many palace stately build of the fugare and other citizen all the building be of free stone six or seven roof high but in other part it be more poor build of timber and clay on this west part of the city be the gate call and the ditch be dry as they be round about the city the wall be of stone which be on all other side narrow be on this side broad for upon the wall of this side there be little house build for five hundred garrison soldier to dwell in with their wife and family which place be vulgar call die schwang here the soldier keep watch each three day by turn and each of they have for pay six gulden by the month and there be a market place whether the soldier upon any difference use to challenge each other on this west side be another gate vulgar call der einlasse by which passenger be receive into the city by night when the gate be shut and this their entrance be so curious admit as many stranger desirous to see the fashion suffer themselves of purpose to be lock out at night and willing give a reward to the soldier let they in when they receive in those that be shut out diverse gate be open and no man be see to open they be present shut on the back of they that come in then they be thus shut as it be in a prison a box be put down to they in which they cast a reward which do the watchman out of win dove behold each man that be to enter and so be safe from all treason let they pass by the last gate into the city on the south side there be two gate and on the east side the gate and a little gate call holeblatten-thorelin on the east side the river Werda and the brook Lecca run towards the north in three arm divide the city and the suburb on that side be more strong fortify then other where the city join with the suburb be of a round form and in the suburb on this side the fugare have build many house to be let for a gulden yearly to the poor that be papist on the north side the wall be high build and the ditch be deep fill with water and there be four other gate this side of the city lie low the field without be as I say all drown with water here each man pay for each meal six or seven Batzen I pay for two meal and my horse meat twenty three Batzen this city have a very fair conduit call the stately workmanship vulgar Statlich Kunst and the fountain of the water be ten mile from the city there be ten very fair church in the cathedral church it be write in golden letter that the bowel of Otho the emperor be bury there also there be a curious picture of Christ pray in the garden whilst his disciple sleep and upon a very fair clock be three statue of the three king of Colen so they call the wise man of the east and these carry about by a circle of iron worship Christ when the clock strike in the senate house where the imperial parliament vulgar call reichs-tagen have often be hold I find nothing to answer the magnificence of this city only on the gate this be write wise man build upon the rock fool upon the sand for I say this part of the city on the west side be build upon a stony hill in the jesuit church the altar be of silver gild over and another altar of Christ nativity be curious paint like the bark of tree augsburg call of the vandal for distinction from augsburg rauracorum in Sweitzerland be divide from the vandal by the brook Lycus and be of old a city of Rhetia now be reckon the metropolitan city of Suenia vulgar Schwaben and be say say to have be build by the son of japhet six hundred year before Rome be build of old they have a yearly feast to Ceres and now upon the same day they have a fair and for the fruitfulness of the soil the city give a sheave of corn for their arm this city be utter destroy by attila king of the hun and when he be dead be rebuild again it be a free city of the empire which be vulgar call retchs-statt and as other free city it be govern by senator there be many alm house for the poor and one wherein four hundred be nourish by rent of land and house give to that house of old by good man the city be seat upon the northern mouth of the Alps in a fruitful plain of corn and pasture and hill full of game for hunt and it may be gather how populous it be by that a german author write that in a year when no plague reign 1705 be baptize and 1227 bury be constant in my purpose take at nurnberg to go from Auspurge to the west part of Germany and so into the low-countries yet I will remember the reader that he shall find the journey from Augspurg to Venice describe in my voyage from stand to Venice and thence to Jerusalem from Augspurg I ride to ulme and thence to Lindawe and all the way hire my horse for six or seven Batzen a day pay for the day in which my horse return and hire footman to bring they back and bear their charge the first day I ride four mile through the territory of the fugar and the bishop of and one mile in the territory of the archduke of Austria of the house of inspruch in a mountainous country full of wood of juniper ash oak and beache to burg where each man pay for his dinner and horsemeat eight Batzen in the afternoon I ride four mile to ulme through a fruitful plain of corn enter the city we pass by a bridge the river Danow which though run in vlme a plain yet have a most violent course so as boat carry down the stream use to be sell at the place wherethey land it be very difficult to bring they back again yet some bark of burden be sometime draw back by the force of horse myself have see ten horse draw one bark but they use a great number according to occasion some thirty or more as they report and he that ride on the horse near the bark be call wage-halse that be neck venturer because hce and the horse be often draw under the water till the other horse draw they out again this river have four great water fall whereof the great be at struddle eighteen mile from Vienna which be hardly to be pass except it be in a flood and the multitude of bridge be very dangerous for boat by reason of the violent stream and especial because the mariner be many time drunken or negligent they use for a charm to sprinkle their draw horse with water and use with continual loud cry to make they draw this navigation be very necessary that the low Oestreich be fertile may supply the upper be barren with wine and corn Munster write of two fall of this river one below lintz where the water make a terrible noise beat upon the rocky bed the other at grin where the water make a dangerous whirlpool of unsearchable depth vlme be seat in Schwaben or Suevia as Augspurge be and have his name of elm tree Charles the great build a monastery in this village which in time grow to a city and under the emperor Fredrick the three buy their freedom of the monk the building thereof be of wood and clay the order of knight call in latin Teutonicus be in old time of great power and have yet a house in this city it have one stately church in the yard whereof the mount Olivet be curious engrave it have a fair senate house and the armoury have such store of ordinance and all munition as it yield not therein to the proud city in Germany the write table make in this city be famous for their goodness and be thence carry into foreign part the diet of the inn of this city seat in a most fertile soil be very plentiful both in meat and banquet where each man pay for his dinner seven Batzen in this country they drink nothing but wine as they do in all upper Germany but it be sharp and the mass or measure be sell for three Batzen when we be at dinner a tumbler come in and be admit to show his cunning he stand upon his head and drink a measure of wine which seem strange to the beholder after dinner we ride a mile in a pleasant valley upon the Danow which we pass and ride two mile further in the like fertile plain which be very large and by all man much commend for the fruitful pasture thereof and so we come to baltr subject to a little free city call Bubery and here each man pay five Batzen for dinner and three for horse-meat next morning after a mile ride we come to the city Bubery and ride two mile further to waldshut through woody mountain and corn valley which be so boggey as many time we have almost stick fast the country belong to the archduke of the family of inspruck and here we pay each man nine Batzen for dinner and horsemeat after dinner we ride two mile through a woody
