country to Ravenspurg a free city seat between mountain whereof one hang over it and the river ache run by it in a narrow bed so as the water fall from the mountain very swift do many time overflow to the great damage of the country and from the mountain many wooden pipe convey water to the city in these part be many alm house for those that be infect with leprosy who may not come near the passenger but do beg of they a far off with the sound of a wooden clapper here each man pay for his supper and horse-meat twelve Batzen and a half the first day of May we ride three mile one through a wood the rest through a plain of corn and enclose pasture and hill plant with vine to the city Lindaw by the way we see the house of count montfort and pass the river arba by a bridge which do often overflow the plain do great hurt and there we pay half a creitzer tribute to the say count for each man we be now come out of Suevia and have ride two mile in Algoia and on the left hand towards the south we discover the mouth of the Alps which in this place be call Spliego Lindaw be a free city of the empire which freedom it buy in the year 1166 and it be almost a I and seat in the lake call acronius vulgar Bodensea be Lindaw join to the continent by a bridge of stone on the north-east side where it have only one gate call burg-thore by which we enter on this side the field be very pleasant and plant with vine and near the bridge there be a rampire so old as they say it be erect by the heathen before any christian be hence the city lie in length towards the south west partly on the west and altogether on the south side lie the lake Bodensea that be upper sea close by the house of the citizen where they descend by stair to take water beyond the lake be most high mountain which be then cover with snow and at the foot of the high mountain there be a tower which they report to be build by Hannibal near which be the place famous by the defeat of hasdrubal on this side the city they show a stone whence they say the saint call Aurelia pass the lake woe to they that believe at one step the river breget fall most violent from the say mountain whence also the Rhine descend in a narrow bed and may be pass with horse and foot when it overflow not both these run into the lake and the Rhine till it come out of the lake again lose the name there be but one church in the city beside a ruin monastery the citizen draw their water to seethe their meat and mingle it with wine not out of the lake but from Wels. three consul choose for life and twenty senator govern the city beyond the lake in the territory of the arch duke of Austria be a bath of great virtue and a monastery build by Otho the second with great privilege upon a vow he make be in great danger when he pass the lake and there murderer have a sanctuary there be much salt make in the city and carry thence to other part here I pay each meal six Batzen they drink altogether wine whereof the measure of the old be give for eleven creitzer the new for nine of which measure twenty make some twenty two english beer quart by ill fortune I be here force to exhibit a petition to the consul both in the dutch and latin tongue the event whereof I will set down after the petition the form whereof in english be this after the dutch fashion ever tedious in their style or title honourable provident good wise lord and consul I humble desire you to respect my cause as of a stranger far from his friend and bereave of his money by deceit thus my case stand be at nurnberg and purpose thence to go to bazell there to study I deal with a merchant that he will exchange my money thither retain only so much as will plentiful serve i for my expense thither there I meet with m. b. a citizen of Lindaw who tell i that the gold gulden of Rhine be not to be spend in these part without loss so as I find he acknowledge by the carrier of this city then be there and by many nurnberger for the son of a senator in this town be induce to deliver he some gold gulden to be pay i here in french crown and we come in company together to this town when I see many principal citizen gratalate his return I be induce to deliver he the rest of my gold gulden which I have keep for the expense of my journey upon his promise to exchange they into french crown so as in all he be to pay i thirty two french crown want six creitzer for twenty seven of which french crown and thirty six creitzer I take his bill at nurnberg but the rest I deliver he here upon his bare word here I expect his payment eight day and when I be instant with he to put off the payment no long he be steal out of the town and his brother give i no hope of payment be not so noble as to ponder the case right or to have any due fecle of my state be in this case not able without money to go on my journey or endure the delay of a suit in law against he here all my hope be in your just help which fail i I know not what course to take therefore I desire earnest of your worthiness to assist i and give expedition to my cause that I may be deliver by your goodness my debtor while he live in the public inn with i use i with all courtesy but find himself disinherit by his father late dead and so despair of mean to pay i he be now flee to the monastery beyond the lake be a sanctuary for wicked-person and bankrupt he that know the honesty of the dutch will not much marvel that I be thus deceive by a Dutchman who I know not to be taint with foreign vice but it grieve i to be thus-scorned by fortune and it afflict i beyond measure that I shall be force to spend the time dear to i then gold in follow the law at Lindaw which I hope to employ in my study at Bazell therefore not to be want to myself I hire a horse and make this cozenage know to the archduke officer desire he to exclude my debtor from the privilege of the monastery but this dutch gentleman find i to speak latin ready take i for some schoolmaster and despise both i and my cause so as I return to the city weary and sad have obtain no favour but a better star shine there on i for the consul that day have determine in court that my debtor horse shall be seek out and deliver to i and the lawyer and clerk be so courteous to i as neither they nor any other will take the least reward of i though I press they to receive it then my debtor brother be loath the horse shall be carry away pay i my money and I glad take my journey thence towad bazel this integrity of the dutch magistrate which especial in the city of the reform religion have find by many testimony I can sufficient commend and courtesy of the dutch towards stranger I have think good in this place thankful to acknowledge upon the lake vulgar bodensea that be upper sea I pass by boat four mile to Costnetz and pay for my passage three Batzen between this upper sea and the low sea vulgar vnden-sea this city Costnetz lie on the bank costnet lengthwise and be subject to Ferdinand of inspruch archduke of Austria who base son have also the bishopric of that city which be famous by a council hold there whither John hus be call with the emperor safe conduct in the year 1414 yet be there condemn of heresy and burn on the west side of the city within the wall in the monastery call barfussen cloister be the tower wherein he be imprison and without the wall on the left hand as you goeout be a fair meadow and therein a stone upon the highway to which he be bind be burn the same year 1414 in the month of july where also his fellow jerom of prage be burn in September the year follow both their ash be cast into the lake lest the bohemian shall carry they away the senate-house in which this council be hold be of no beauty when the emperor Charles the five besiege this city it be yield to the hand of Ferdinand king of Bohemia and brother to Charles who make the citizen peace for they here each man pay eight Batzen a meal and for wine between meal eight creitzer the measure hence I go by boat two mile to Styga and pay for my passage two Batzen we take boat at the end of the lake close by the city where the Rhine come again out of the lake and take his name therein lose do run in all narrow bed and when we have go by water some hour and a half we enter the low lake call vnden-sea near Costnetz be a island call little Meinow and in this low lake be another island call Reichnow of the riches the monastery thereof have of old so much land as the monk be send to Rome use to lodge every night in their own possession this island be say to bear nothing that have poison so as any such beast die present in it and in the monastery be some relic of saint mark for which as they say the venetian have offer much money writer report that of old a monk thereof climb up a ladder to look into a huge vessel of wine and be overcome with the vapour fall into the same with a great bunch of key in his hand and that short after this wine be so famous as prince and noble and many sickly person usual send for the same the cause of the goodness be not know to proceed of the putrify flesh till the vessel be empty the key and the friar bone be find therein the monk till then think that their fellow have secret go to some other monastery of that order yet the dutch in my company report that this happen in a monastery not far off call Salmanschwell by the way be a stately palace belong to the fugare of augsburg on the east-side out of the wall of Styga lie woody field on the westside the island horue and pleasant hill full of vine and corn in this city the bishop of Costnetz have his palace who be lord of the two land Meinow and Reichnow and have very large possession in these part mingle with the territory of other lord and this city be upon the confine of Germany and Sweitzerland hence I pass by boat two mile to schaffhausen and pay for my passage two schaffhausen Batzen the swistnes of the Rhine make the mile seem short and this river again lose his name in the say low lake and when it come or rather violent break out of it then resume it again this city be one of the confederate canton of Sweitzerland not far from this city on the south side in the river Rhine be a great fall of the water over a rock some fifty cubit downward pass with huge noise and end all in foam and for this cause the bark be force to unlade here and to carry their good by cart to the city and from the city to embark they again which yield great profit to the city by taxation impose on the good which must necessary be land there on both side the river as we come to this city be pleasant hill plant with vine and fair pasture with sweet grove the city be round in form and be wash with the Rhine on the south side and upon the bank of the river within the town be a pleasant green where the citizen meet to exercise the shoot of the harquebus and crossbow where also be a Lynden or Teyle tree give so large a shade as upon the top it have a kind of chamber board on the floor with window on the side and a cock which be turn water fall into a vessel through diverse pipe by which it be convey thither for wash of glass and other use and here the citizen use to drink and feast together there be six table for that purpose on the same south side be a monastery with wall and gate like a little city it have the name of (@) that be a sheep or schiff that be a ship and that be a house as of a fold for (@) or ride for ship here I pay for each meal six batzen for the better understanding of my journey from Schafhusen to Zurech I will prefix a letter which I write to that purpose from bazel to the right worshipful master doctor John Vlmer in those few hour I stay at Schafhusen you have make i your servant for ever I remember the hour of our conversation which for the sweetness thereof seem minute to i I remember the good office you do towards i a stranger with gentleness if not proper to yourself yet proper to your dutch nation now as soon as I have pitch my tent at bazell for i think I be one of the nomads every day change my dwell I think fit to give you account of my journey whereof I be sure you desire to know the success I will ever be rule in doubtful case by the counsel of so wise and so good a friend as yourself but you shall never more persuade i to take a journey on foot which I find unprofitable for my health and purse the other day after dinner by your advice I take my journey on foot and with more sigh than pace come in four hour with much pain to the little city eglisaw and come to the inn they offer i meat but I do nothing but so cry out for my bed as you will have say I be the elder son of sloth to be brief they be slow to satisfy my desire I fling myself on the feather bed without sheet and so at ease sup on the old fashion with the cloth spread on the bed you give i a pleasant companion to guide i the way who make it seem short with his good discourse and be very diligent to serve my occasion next morning early by twilight we begin our journey have the gate open for a little reward you may think that I be fresh after my night rest for a new journey but I rise early only to be out of my pain by nine of the clock in the morning we come to the fair city Zurech which seem fair to i at the end of my tedious walk present I run into my chamber and with like importunity as before obtain my bed to be make the Mercury you give to guide i bring i meat plentiful and there we dine together then because he be to return to Schafhusen that night I give he a Gulden for his pain beside pay his charge and fifteen creitzer of free gift each of we go to our task he as I think to go I to sleep since I have begin I will tell you historical all circumstance so you promise i not to reprove he but rather to salute and thank he in my name which if you refuse I conjure you on all love to read no further after four hour behold this honest man with a garland of rose on his head a glass and pot in his hand and well arm with drink enter my chamber I be half asleep wipe my eye as if I have see a vision till he drink to i and importune i to pledge he make i know it be no apparition we drink a whole hour together then in good earnest he go away and do nothing to my knowledge more wise then that be drunken he leave his reckon of forty eight creitzer to be pay by i who be sober neither Ceres nor bacchia can for twenty hour draw i from bed but the next morning I deliver your better to master doctor s. who use i very courteous and present i with a book he have late print so this comedy be end only I will add the epilogue here at Bazell I can get for no money the book of Repub. which you commend unto i at last I find it in a friend study who esteem it as the apple of his eye yet I so prevail with he as he let i have it upon my faithful promise that because I mean to blot the same with note I shall procure he a new book wherein if you do not disengage i by send the same unto i I shall forfeit the small credit of a stranger farewell good sir and I pray you let we not suffer this spark of our love to go out but rather with often write let we set all the coal on fire again farewell from bazell the 24. day of May 1902. from Schafhusen I take my journey on foot as be above write and go half a mile in the territory of that city and a mile and a half in the territory of the count of (@) sweitzer the land of Zurech lie on the south side from we and so we pass through mountain yield corn and plant with vine and through wood pasture and a large valley of corn and in four hour space for the mile of Sweitzerland be so long as they reckon the journey of horse or foot by the hour and not by the mile come to eglisaw and enter the same pass the river Rhine by a bridge where I pay two creitzer for tribute and there I sup for five Batzen the next morning I go two mile on foot in six hour space to Zurech through Zurech a long wood and hill of corn which they say be often blast with hail and through woody mountain and hill of enclose pasture with store of vine plant near the city which be one of the canton of Sweitzerland have on the west side the lake call Zurechsea and the brook Limachus have his head eight mile further on the same side ruune into this lake and after come out divide the city into two part call the great and the less city have three bridge for passage the great where of the merchant use for their meeting the foresay lake be three mile long and have on each side pleasant hill plant with vine the foresay brook near the city bear only small boat and be all take up with water mill but above towards the Alps be deep and below near baden run into the Rhine the street of Zurech be narrow and the house build of timber and clay and the city itself be seat upon and between hill which on the east side of the brook grow high from it on the northwest side be a pleasant mountain and a fair meadow for shoot with gun and other exercise wherein be a fair Lynden or teyle tree yield large and sweet shadow where the citizen meet to recreate themselves and to feast together the arm of the city be a man and woman call Falix and without the city on the south be the foresay lake and beyond it the Alps cover with snow on the west side be a plain and the mountain far off but on the north and east side the mountain lie near the citizen have a custom that when they go forth against the enemy they place the minister or pastor in the front or where they may partake the danger and there be a place two mile from the city towards where a famous preacher and reformer of religion be kill in the field here I pay each meal six batzen I ride three mile to baden in three hour and so hire my horse as beside the price of six or seven batzen the day I pay as much for the day in which he return baden and also pay the hire and charge of one to bring he back most part of our way be in the territory of Zurech through hill of corn and vine and a plain of enclose pasture enter the city we pass the brook Limachus by a bridge the suburb be build upon the ascent of a mountain and the city on the top of it where there be a castle upon a rock of old very strong but now ruinate on the north side descend into a valley by the brook on the right hand or upon the east side within a musket shoot lie the bath which be famous for medicine and be in number thirty seat on each side the brook which divide they into Bethora the great and the little in the great diverse bath be contain under one roof of a fair house and without the gate be two common to the poor these water be so strong of brimstone as the very smoke warm they that come near and the water burn those that touch they of these one be call the marquis bath and be so hot as it will scald off the hair of a hog many have no disease but that of love how soever they feign sickness of body come hither for remedy and many time find it woman come hither as rich attire as if they come to a marriage for man woman monk and nun sit all together in the same water part with board but so as they may mutual speak and touch and it be a rule here to shun all sadness neither be any jealousy admit for a naked touch the water be so clear as a penny may be see in the bottom and because melancholy must be avoid they recreate themselves with many sport while they sit in the water namely at card and with cast up and catch little stone to which purpose they have a little table swim upon the water upon which sometime they do likewise eat these bath be very good for woman that be barren they be also good for a cold brain and a stomach charge with rheum but be hurtful for hot and dry complexion and in that respect they be hold better for woman then men. the inn be wont to pay tribute to the three canton of baden brucke and bazell but now baden alone make great profit of they by the great concourse of sickly person and the parliament of the canton common hold there I pay for my diet six Batzen a meal from hence I hire a horse at the same rate as before and pass through woody mountain come in three hour ride to the city brucke by the way I pass the brook Russee which run from into these part and the boat be draw by a cable run upon a wheel by reason of the water swift course where I pay for my passage six creitzer and when I come near Bruck I pass the brook ara by a bridge pay one creitzer for tribute and here I pay five batzen a meal from hence hire a horse as before I ride in two hour space to hornsea through steep mountain and a wood of oak by the way lie Kingsfeld that be kingly field a monastery so call because widow queen and king forsake their sceptre and inferior prince be wont of old to enter into this place for the solitary profession of religion in the same cloister of old live the friar of saint Francis order in the build on the right hand as you come in and the nun of saint Clara on the left hand and both come to the same chapel the friar to the body of the church and the nun to close gallery above look out and hear through grate the emperor Albertus be kill by his nephew in the year 1380. at santbacke three mile distant this monastery be build for his memory though his bone be bury at spire the revenue of this monastery grow in time to be yearly forty thousand gulden which be now appropriate to the common treasure of the sweitzer canton Leopold duke of Austria lie here bury Ferdinand of insprucke one of the archduke of Austria be lord of Hornesea all the rest of the territory from Schafhusen to this place belong to the canton of Sweitzerland and here I pay six batzen a meal hence I hire a horse as before and ride in five hour to Rheinefeld through fruitful hill of corn have on all side woody mountain in sight here again I pass the Rhine and pay two rap for my horse and myself footman pay but one the Rhine pass by with a violent course and wash the town on the east side here I pay six Batzen a meal hence hire a horse as before I ride in two hour space to bazell through a fair plain of corn and pasture lie upon the Rhine have on all side woody Bazell mountain in sight and near the city be most pleasant field plant with vine to the which field the territory of the say archduke extend on the east side of the Rhine I enter by little bazell seat in a plain on the east side of Rhine and so pass by a bridge of wood into the great bazell seat upon pleasant hill on the west side of Rhine this city of old be one of the imperial free city but now be join to the canton of Sweitzerland and be build in the year 3 and 2 have the name of a basilisk slay by a knight cover with crystal or of the word Pasell which in dutch signify a beat path or of the greek word (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) as a kingly city the less bazell be of old build by a archduke of Austria in prejudice of the great and after be sell to it for thirty thousand gulden be incorporate thereunto the great have many cave under the hill and suffer a great earthquake in the year 1346 at which time the palace near the cathedral church fall into the Rhine and another earthquake in the year 1356. wherein 180. person be kill all the people fly out of the town Eugenius the pope hold a council in this city the year 1431. the bridge of wood join the little and great city divide by the Rhine be broad enough for two cart to pass at once and towards little bazell six arch be of stone but towards great bazell where the Rhine tun most swift eight arch be build of wood that they may be more easy repair and upon any war from Germany more ready break down this city be of the form of a half moon I mean the great city reckon the less for a suburb and be seat upon diverse hill on the west side of the Rhine embrace between the two horn the lesser city seat in a plain on the east side of Rhine on the west side of the great the emperor of Habspurg besiege the city and on this side something towards the north within the wall be a most pleasant green for walk call peter in this place be the armoury of the city and the tribunal of justice and some fair house of private man and a most pleasant shade of tree among which be a oak who bough be in circuit one hundred forty walk pace and from the root be more than twenty such pace in length forward and they report that the emperor Manimilian the second sup under this oak and give 2000 gulden to keep and trimic on the east side of great bazel the Rhine run by it from the south to the north and the yard of the cathedral church lie over the river and have a lynden tree which give very pleasant shade have seat under it and along the river the course of the Rhine be very swift but not so violent as that of the Danow yet the watermen of bazel as those that dwell upon the Danow sell their boat at Sirasburg which they weekly carry thither and can bring back against the stream of the Rhine and so they return home on foot always remember that this river be at the broad between bazel and strasburg pope Pius the second found a Vniversine at bazel in the year 1459 and give it all the privilege of Bologala in Italy in the public inn man pay six Batzen a meal but the student have their diet with doctor and citizen for some eight dollar a month myself diet with the overseer of the college and pay two gulden a week for stranger may hire chamber in the college and the overseer willing admit they to diet with he in the cathedral church this be write upon the sepulchre of Hotoman a famous civil lawyer he bear in France lie bury here his last name live every where in the same place lie Oecolampadius bury to who Luther oppose himself when in the first reformation of religion he violent break down the image and he have this inscription in latin m. John Oecolampadius by profession a divine most skilful in three language first author of the reform religion in this city and the true bishop of this church excellent in sanctity of doctrine and life be lay under this short stone in the year of war lord m. d. 42 in the same place lie bury Erasmus with this inscription in latin to desiderius Erasmus a most great man carry way who imcomparable learning in all kind of art join with like wisdom (@) come shall admire and celebrate Boniface Jerome Frobenius Nichol bishop heir and extertitort of his last will and testament to their patron of happy memory which by his write he have get and so long as the world stand shall retain for the repose of his mortal body have lay this stone he die the four of the ides of july be now seventy year old in the year of our lord mdxxxvi these two verse be write upon the tomb of Lodovicus Pontanus Plato Cato Tullius over Vermes here lie Plato Cato Tully for his art life and eloquence worm do feed upon his body his soul to heaven be mount hence there be also the monument of henricus Glarianus and of bishop Hatto who the emperor Charles the great send ambassador to Irene empress of the east in this city a stone be show call the hot stone vulgar heisteine upon which the consul and diverse other be behead who have conspire to betray the city if the clock strike false have not prevent and deceive both they and the enemy lie in ambush without the city and expect a sign to be give they at the hour appoint and for this cause or as other say to hasten the council hold in the senate house the clock to this day strike one when it shall strike twelve near the stair of the senate house be a old statue on foot arm but without a sword bear a sceptre clad with a loose gown with a bird sit on the helmet and have this inscription Hono and l. l. f. l. n. l. Pron Planci Cos Imper: and Ter 7 triumph Raetis Edem Saturni f. in Italia in Galliam deed lugdunum in rauracorum fine transducta Senatus auct of Lucius Munatius the son of Lucius grandchild of Lucius great grandchild of Lucius surname Plancus be consul general and thrice one of the seven precedent of the holy banquet who triumph of the rhaetian build Saturnus temple with the spoil divide the land in Italy at Benevento deduce colony into Gaul to lion and about bazel the city of bazel deduce of the most warlike nation of the alemans into the territory of the rauraci or basilian by authority of the senate procure this statue to be consecrate and here set in the year of Christ mdlxxx in the house where the doctor and other graduate take their degree be the bone of a anatomy for the magistrate use to grant the body of some man execute to serve this use and it have this inscription kill for our wickedness we live by other here and vessel be of honour as of shame we be chap. 3 of my journey from bazel to strasburg to Heidelberg to frankfort to Cassiles to Brunswick to Luneburg to hamburg to stand to breme to oldenburg and to Emden the last city upon the confine of the empire I go by boat to strasburg fourteen mile and in six hour upon the swift Rhine we pass six mile to brisake a city subject to Ferdinand of inspruch archduke of Austria but of the village oft intermix some be subject to the margrave of baden and some to diverse bishop all the territory on the west side of Rhine almost to strasburg gate be subject to the say archduke or the bishop of bazel or the duke of Wirtenberg the boat come down to brisake and Neoberg pay tribute and send forth two mariner take from thence two other in their stead at brisake each man pay two rap for pass the bridge which be shut up with a iron chain and keep by watchman lest any boat shall pass without touch there I say before that the boat and bark come down be sell at the end of the way because they can be bring up against the stream be seat upon a round and high mountain and though it be improbable that there shall be any want of water so near the foot of the Alps yet this city have a fountain where water be sell and a certain price be give for the water of every beast we pass the other eight mile to strasburg the same day in eight hour be help with the same swiftness of the Rhine which be oft divide by the way make many little land the bridge of strasburg over the Rhine be more strasburg than a musket shoot from the city on the east side thereof the bridge be of wood and have threescore five arch each distant from the other twenty walk pace and it be so narrow that a horseman can hardly pass by a cart it lie open on both side and it be build of small piece of timber lay a cross which lie loose so as one end be press with any weight the other be lift up with danger to fall into the water it be like they build no strong bridge either because they have try that the swift course of the Rhine will easy break it down or because in the time of war it may be good for they to break it in which case it be far great charge to rebuild it with stone then with wood the Rhine lie thus far off from the city the boat be bring up to the same by a little channel the brook of bress and Elb pass through many street of the city and fill all the large ditch thereof with water the city be very well fortify have high wall of earth the bottom whereof be fasten with stone and the side with tree plant on the same on the west side towards France be the gate weissen-thore and rheinethore on the east side towards the Rhine be the gate croneberg-thore at which though it be out of the way for the jealousy of neighbourhood the french must enter and at no other on the east side be the butcher gate call metsiger-thore on the same side be the cathedral church the circuit of the cty be three hour walk the building and church be fair and high of free stone most of the street be narrow but those divide by water be broad i pay six Batzen a meal and for wine extraordinary three Batzen the measure many thing in this city be remarkable the steeple of the cathedral church be most beautiful and number among the seven miracle of the world be begin in the year 1277 and scarce finish in twenty eight year in the build of one gate thereof they say three king treasure be spend in who memory three statue be there engrave the church be cover with lead which be rare in Germany where the chief church be cover with brass grow in the country the brazen gate of this church be curious carve the clock thereof be of all other most famous be invent by conradus in the year 1571. before the clock stand a globe on the ground show the motion of the heaven star and planet namely of the heaven carry about by the first mover in twenty four hour of Saturn by his proper motion carry about in thirty yeeere of jupiter in twelve of Mars in two of the sun Mercury and Venus in one year of the moon in one month in the clock itself there be two table on the right and left hand show the eclipse of the sun and moon from the year 1573 to the year 1605. the three table in the midst be divide into three part in the first part the statue of Apollo and Diana show the course of the year and the day thereof be carry about in one year the second part show the year of our lord and of the world the equinoctial day the hour of each day the minute of each hour easter day and all other feast and the dominical letter the three part have the geographical description of all Germany and particular of strasburg and the name of the inventor and of all the workman in the middle frame of the clock be a astrolabe show the sign in which each planet be every day and there be the statue of the seven planet upon a round piece of iron lie flat so as every day the statue of the planet come forth that rule the day the rest be hide within the frame till they come out by course at their day as the sun upon Sunday and so for all the week and there be a terrestrial globe and the quarter and half hour and the minute be show there be also the skull of a dead man and two statue of two boy whereof one turn the hourglass when the clock have strike the other put forward the rod in his hand at each stroke of the clock moreover there be statue of the spring summer autumn and winter and many observation of the moon in the upper part of the clock be four old man stutuae which strike the quarter of the hour the statue of death come out each quarter to strike but be drive back by the statue of Christ with a spear in his hand for three quarter but in the four quarter that of Christ go back and that of death strike the hour with a bone in the hand and then the chime sound on the top of the clock be a image of a cock which twice in the day crow aloud and beat his wing beside this clock be deck with many rare picture and be on the inside of the church carry another frame to the outside of the wall wherein the hour of the sun the course of the moon the length of the day and such like thing be set out with great Art beside in the city there be a fair house in which citizen and stranger at public meeting or otherwise use to feast their invite friend near the gate be the armoury vulgar Zeighauss which abound with ordinance and all munition they have a theatre for comedy and a tower to lay up their treasure call penny tower vulgar phennigthurne they say this city be call argentina in latin of the word because the roman of old lay up their treasure here and Strassburg in dutch of the word strass that be way and burg that be a city as be build where many way lead to many province I have almost omit one remarkable thing namely the fair house of the cannon call Bruderhoff that be the court of the brother I hire a coach for a dolor my person from Strassburg to Heidelberg be sixteen mile the first day after dinner I go four mile to Leichtenou through a plain all compass with garden and orchard and pay six batzen for my supper the next morning we go four mile to Milberk through a sandy and barren plain the margrave of Turloch uncle to the margrave of baden be lord of this village by the way thither we pass a fort of the margrave of baden where tribute be pay for all merchandise bring out of Sweitzerland and France and these frequent tribute be gather by the dutch prince upon pretext that they free the way from thief to which purpose when the fair of Franckfort draw near they send out certain reyter that be horseman vulgar call geleyte which conduct the merchant and their good out of the frontier the say fort include the high way with rampire lest any shall pass without pay tribute beside by the way we may see the city of baden towards the south seat upon a mountain after dinner we go three mile to the village Graben through a sandy ground but somewhat less barren the margrave of Turloch be lord of this village the three day in the morning we go five mile to Heidelberg through sandy field but fruitful in corn all lie in a plain as the rest of the way from Strassburg hither and near Heidelberg we pass a great wood of Oakes full of great head of Heidelberg red dear which lie still by the way and will not stir for our cry or fear of our coach wheel but seem to know their privilege all hunt be forbid upon high penalty Heidelberg be compass with high mountain on the south east and north side but towards the west beyond the city and a long suburb be the sole suburb in the town the mountain lie open this suburb be long than the city and they both lie in great length from the east to the west and they both consist almost of one street and be build in the plain though compass with mountain on the southeast side there be a fair and pleasant market place and not far thence a very high mountain call Konigstull that be kingly seat upon the middle ascent whereof be the castle in which the phaltz-grave of the Rhine hold his court and upon the top of this mountain be the ruin of a old tower blow up with gunpowder from this mountain on the south side run cave under the earth to the western part of the mountain of goat upon which mountain be a tower call trotz-keys as if it be build in despite of Caesar and it be worth the see for the antiquity and building have no gate but be enter by the cave under the earth and be build with lime temper not with water but wine incredible durable at the time when the emperor make war against the phaltz-grave besiege this city in the valley under this mountain of goat towards the city be a pleasant walk of the sweetness call the philosophical way in the ditch part the city and suburb be a place for the exercise of shoot with the musket and crossbow on the north side of the city the shallow and unnavigable river Neccar run in a plain by the city between it and the mountain from the east to the west and in the same course have run more than a mile fall into the Rhine and though it be shallow yet sometime it overflow the bridge of the city as it do in the year 1565. pass into the town on the north side you pass a bridge build of wood upon arch of stone to a pleasant walk upon the bank of the river between the mountain and the water and from thence you go up to the holy mountain vulgar Heiligberg and some say this city have his name of that mountain but other say it have the name of the dutch word Edelberg that be noble mountain other of a colony of roman be heathen will have it at first call Heidenberg that be the mountain of the heathen Vpou this mountain be the ruin of a church of great antiquity first dedicate to Mercury and since make a monastery and near the same be a ruin cloister of nun as common their nest be not far distant and there be a passage under the earth from one cloister to another this be a most high mountain and have a thick wood the city of Heydelberg by reason it be compass with mountain have a very unhealthful air which make funeral very frequent therein but the water be hold very healthful in the inn they ask seven batzen the meal but the student have their diet in citizen or professor house for two gulden or one dolor weekly and the fame of the professor draw many student at this time to this university there be to my remembrance but one church use for prayer and preach and there be a monument with this inscription in latin  Viglius lay this to the memory of Agricola bear in Friestand he die in the year 1485 ihe 28 of October he live 42 year and two month there be another epitaph to this Agricola make in verse by Hermolaus Bar patriarch of he mar more frisij envious fate under this stone have close the frison joy Rodulph Agricola by who all praise on german be impofde that Italy or Greece have to this day while I live here the rest of this summer I make a journey of pleasure to see the city lie upon the west side of the Rhine and hire a horse after the wont price at Heydelberg I ride two mile and a half to the rheine side and then half a mile further to the city of spire where the imperial chamber be hold in which court spire the chief difference of the empire be judge and the elector themselves or any absolute prince under the empire may be call thither to trial of law the city be build in a plain on the west side of Rhine and have more antiquity than beauty or magnificence here I pay eight batzen each meal from hence I ride one mile to the city of Wormz famous for many imperial parliament hold there of old and by the way we pass Frankendale a little city new and very fair build which place Casimire the elector give unto the fleming of late who then have build many fair brick house there and then compass it with a wall and Casimire take upon he the tutorship of his nephew against the will of the lutheran who reject he as a caluinf take some of these fleming to guard the castle of Heidelberg the building of Wormz show great antiquity and Wormz want not magnificence where I pay feven Batzen a meal this territory on the west side of the Rhine be very fruitful and yield the best rhenish wine so call of the Rhine by which they grow from hence again I pass the Rhine and return to Heydelberg then I take my journey to Franckfort fair the first day I pass four mile to bentzon have hill on my right hand towards the east plant with vine and field set with root and upon my left hand towards the west a fair and fruitful plain and here I pay seven Batzen for my supper the second day in the morning I pass four mile in the territory of George landgrave of Hessen to arhelygen through woody mountain plant with some vine and a plain for one mile sandy but the rest good pasture we pass by Dormstat where the say landgrave hold his court and there each man pay six Fenning tribute at Arheligen I pay six Batzen for my dinner in the afternoon I pass some three mile to Franckfort through a sandy plain and a wood of oak and beech and by the way they show we a strange leap of a stag which be chase do leap over a cart if you may believe they load with hey Franckfort be a free city of the empire famous for the elector meet there to choose the emperor and for two yearly fair as also for many parliament of the Franckfort empire hold there and it be call Franckfort upon the Maene to distinguish it from another city of the same name build upon the brook Odera and name thereof for the river Maene run from the east to the west divide the great city from the less call the saxon house vulgar sachsen-hausse and between they be a bridge of stone upon four narrow arch both the city be govern by the same senate and law and have the fame name either of Francus rebuild it or of a ford for passage of the francke or French the city be compass strong with a double wall and upon the east side be the gate where be the jew street who be permiteed to dwell in this famous mart-towne and suck the blood of christian by extortion there be another gate call on the north side of the city be the gate Brickenport and a large place for a horse fair on the west side be the gate of stranger vulgar welsh-thore so call because the french enter that way it be very strong and without the gate there be a very pleasant walk upon the bank of Maene among vineyard and meadow with sweet grove on the south side the Maene run by divide as I say the new city from the old in the new or less city call the saxons-house be a house of old belong to the teutonike order of knight which by old privilege be to this day a sanctuary for bankrupt and manslayer so they be not wilful and malicious murderer but they enjoy this privilege only for fourteen day so as when the time be near out or upon any opportunity during the time they use to steal out and return after a hour begin a new to reckon again the fourteen day a little before my come thither a certain bankrupt of Colen enter the same for a debt of twenty thousand gulden on this side some ground without the wall belong to the city but on other side it have almost no land without the wall the city be of a round form seat in a large plain the street be narrow and the house build of timber and clay the foundation of some be of stone in the inn they ask seven or eight batzen a meal but merchant and many stranger use to hire a chamber and buy their meat of the cook from hence to Hamburge I and four other hire a coach for fifty dollar and beside be to pay for the coachman diet for here first the coachman condition to be free from pay his diet vulgar Maulfrey that be free for the mouth whereas in other part our coachman pay for themselves always understand that at the time of the fair coach be set dear than any time else the first day after breakfast we go three mile to Freideburge through corn field set with cabbage and root and by the way we pass a village belong to the count of Hanaw Freideburge be a free city of the empire and the building be of timber and clay here each man pay seven batzen for his supper and for his part of the coachman supper the second day in the morning we go three mile to Geysen through fruitful hill of corn Philip landgrave of Hessen leave three son William of Cassiles who Maurice his son succeed and be now live and Lodowick of Marpurg and George of Dormstat this territory belong to the landgrave Lodowick for all the brother in Germany have the same style of honour and he be also at that time lord of this city geysell which be fortify with wall of earth and deep ditch but the building be base of timber and clay and for the most of mere dirt these verse be write upon the gate of the city prince Philip captive not by war but art this work of strength be then demolish in country love prince Lodowick for his part rebuild it seventy nine year finish ruin repair be for a prince hand from which disaster Christ shield Hessen land here I pay six Batzen for my dinner and my part for the coachman in the afternoon we go three mile through high stony mountain and wood of oak to Kirnham belong to the landgrave Lodowick who court at Marpurg lie a mile from thence all of we at supper drink six measure of wine beside beer and from henceforth we pay several for meat and drink and at this time each of we pay ten Weissenfenning for both together the three day we pass three mile to dress through high mountain with wood of oak and many fruitful valley of corn and each man pay with his portion for the coachman four Weissenfenning for meat and as much for wine this territory belong to landgrave Maurice of Cassiles after dinner we pass three mile to fester through high mountain full of oak wood and enter the city seat upon a mountain by a bridge of stone upon which side great store of water fall from the mountain the house be of timber and clay each one for the most part have a dunghill at the door more like a poor village than a city but such be the building of the city in Hessen the house of village be of mere dirt and thatch here each man pay for his meat and old wine and his part for the coachman a ort Doller or four part of a Doller the four day we pass three mile to Cassiles a city where the landgrave Maurice Cassiles hold his court all our way lie through fruitful hill of corn the city be strong fortify with wall of earth and deep ditch but the house be base build like the rest in Hessen Phellip his grandfather build the castle and William his father the wall for my dinner and my part for the coachman I pay the four part of a Doller in the afternoon we pass two mile through woody mountain to Myndaw in the territory of the duke of Brunswike who be also lord of the city the river visurgis run by it over which there be a bridge of stone upon five arch here each man pay for himself and his part for the coachman seven maria-groshen for meat and as much for wine the beer of this territory be very bitter and like a potion make one laxative the five day we pass three mile and a half through mountain for half the way and the rest through corn field most fruitful and dine at Norton each man pay five batzen and a half after dinner we pass two mile and a half to a poor village through a like fruitful plain of corn and by the way we pass Namerton a city belong to the duke of Brunswick in this village each man pay five maria-groshen the six day we pass two mile to the city zeason through hill and field of corn the build of the city be of mere clay cover with thatch but our diet be plentiful and each man pay six maria-groshen for himself and his part for the coachman after dinner we pass three mile to a poor village through woody mountain yet fruitful of corn and pasture and through a great fen and here each man pay seven maria-groshen the seven day we pass three mile to Brunswike through a fruitful plain of corn end a large fen set with willow tree near the city many field as we come beside Brunswike the corn be set with cabage and root and within a mile of Brunswike we leave on the right hand towards the south the city Wolfenbieten where the duke of Brunswike keep his court and though he be so call of a old title yet he be not lord of Brunswike which be a free city of the empire seat in a plain all the territory round about it be most fruitful in corn the city be of a quadrangle form and in circuit contain two mile be hold great than nurnberg and less than Erford it have high wall of earth fasten with willow and be very strong have the wall on some side double and otherwhere treble beside that it have a woody valley between deep ditch fill with water and be compass with the river within this wall and river be five city distinguish by privilege but unite by law the first seat towards the west be call Altstat that be old city have almost at the entrance a fair market place and near it the cathedral church call Martinstifft the second lie towards the north be call Newstat that be new city the three lie towards the east be call imsacke the four lie towards the south be call Imhagen and the five which be build first of all and lie also towards the south be call altweg that be the old way this city of old be the metropolitan city of Saxony and have the name of bruno and the dutch word vuick signify a village it have twelve church whereof two have the steeple cover with lead which be very rare in Germany be hold to be magnificent the rest be cover with tile one except which to my remembrance be cover with brass which be with they be less esteem and the house be build of timber and clay in the yard of the cathedral church there be the statue of a very great lion which the emperor Henry the first surname lion erect there from Branswike I go to Luneburge and the first day in the morning pass four mile to a certain village through a sandy plain and fenny wild ground and by the way we pass getherne a village where the duke of Luneburge lord of this territory have a castle and he hold his court some five mile off at sell. here each man pay for his dinner five Lubeck shilling in the afternoon we pass five mile to a country house through like fenny and woody wild ground see but one village in the way and here each man pay for supper three Lubeck shilling next morning we pass four mile to a village Empsdorff through like ground and here each man pay for dinner five Lubeck shilling the coachman part be reckon for I former say that hire a coach from Franckfort to hamburg we be tie to pay for the coachman diet himself pay for his horse-meat as common they do after dinner we pass three mile to Luneburge through a soil as barren as the former where each man pay for himself and his part of the coachman supper eight Lubeck shilling I speak nothing of the city which I have describe before but go on with my journey the next morning we pass three mile to Wintzon through a fenny ground and wood of oak yield some corn but spare and here our coachman pay a Lubeck shilling for his coach to the duke of Luneburge who territory end here then we pass a mile further to Bergendorff and by the way our coachman pass over the ell pay a Lubeck shilling to the officer of the city of Lubeck and hamburg to which city this territory be subject and govern by they in course the soil whereof after the passage of the ell be more fruitful the field be full of corn and ditch of water plant with willow here each man pay six Lubeck shilling for our dinner in the afternoon we pass three mile to Hamburge have on the left side towards the west fair pasture and on the right hand towards Humburge the east wood of oak and fruitful hill of corn from hence I pass by boat with a fair wind in three hour to stand and pay for my passage three Lubeck shilling these thing I brief set down have describe these city before from stand I write this letter to Francis Markham a english gentleman who I leave at Heidelberg noble sir I glad take this occasion of witness my love to you which in a word I have do omit all ceremony as yourself have give i example only for my promise sake I will trouble you with the short relation of my journey when we part at Eranckfort you know I have for companion of my journey two fleming poor merchant of linen cloth and a dutch rider and a bookebinder of Denmark I come first to the coach take the most commodious seat which these my worthy companion forsooth take in ill part yet neither their murmur nor rude speech can make i yield the place to they we pass through Hessen to Brunswike which journey since you purpose to take I advise you to pass as soon as you can that you may be out of your pain and come to more pleasant country for there you shall have gross meat sour wine stink drink and filthy bed and be not the way free from robbery and the people courteous I know not what other inconvenience may happen to a stranger in any passage your diet shall be for most part of coal word which be so strange to i and so hard of digestion as it great trouble i and wrought upon my body like physic at Brunswike I see a lamentable sight which I dare scarce relate to you know your tenderness in those case yet for promise sake I must tell you that I see a very fair maid of fifteen year marry to my host a old ohurle of seventy year be not discourage I will tell you a merry accident who will have think that my companion have dissemble so long their malice to i that now it may break forth with more bitterness you know Brunswike be a free city of the empire and one of those which for privilege of traffic upon these coast be call hans-steten here out of custom passenger come at first to enter traffic use to give the wine to the old merchant to which custom gentleman for sociablenes have submit themselves so as the custom be almost grow into a Law now for this purpose salt be put about the table for all to swear whether they be free or no I confess that I have not yet pay for my freedom yield myself to their censure to be brief after they have fine i some can of wine and with many ceremony have make i free it remain that he who they have choose to be my godfather make a grave oration with some rude jest after their fashion shall instruct i with some precept how to recover this expense one of my companion easy take this charge upon he and after many circumstance he conclude in this manner you be a Englishman and because your country man love to sit easy and to fare delicate I advise you that both at table and in coach you be careful to take the best place which if you be diligent to perform you shall be soon satisfy for this expense by chance my place then at table be between the coachman and his servant for you know the dutch be not curious of place and little regard stranger in that kind but I know where my gentleman shoe wring he namely in that I have choose my place in the coach and thus I answer he sir I take thankful your grave counsel and will make use of it but i think it be too general make no distinction of degree for if I have gentleman to my companion who be not willing overcome in courtesy I shall rather yield they place but if I fall into base and clownish company I will not fail to make use of your counsel the gentleman at table smile and so we end this ceremony with a health hence I pass to Lunchurg and so to hamburg where the people after dinner warm with drink be apt to wrong any stranger and hardly endure a Englishman in the morning when they be sober therefore without any stay I pass hence to stand it be strange how the people rail on englishman in these part for that which we call war at sea and the royal navy that they term robbery and pirate ship neither have they the patience to hear any justification or excuse you see what toy I write rather than I will leave you unsaluted and if you use not like freedom to i farewell friendship so I take my leave from stand the first of October 1592. from stand I pass to Emden and for the better explain of that journey give i leave to prefix the follow letter out of the due place be write from Emden and direct to AEgidius Hoffman a gentleman of Flaunders my dear friend student at Heidelberg noble AEgidius the letter you give i to deliver at breme have produce a comical event such may all the passage be of our love which you shall understand in a word when in my purpose journey I come to stand more tire with the base companion I have than the way it happen while I spend some day there with my friend every man speak of spanish thief vulgar call freebooter who steal out of their garrison upon the low-countries lay in the village and upon the highway by which I be to pass in my journey to Emden from which city a merchant be new arrive who terrify i more than all the rest affirm that in one day he have fall thrice into these cutthroat hand and though he be of a neutral city yet have pay many dollar for his ransom add that they inquire curious after englishman promise reward in the village to any man shall give they notice when any such pass I know not what counsel to take there be no less danger from the pirate of dunkirk if I pass by sea especial in a ship of hamburg no other be in the harbour and they be like to betray i out of malice to our nation beside the weather be very tempestuous and not like to change therefore my obstinate purpose to see the city upon this coast make i resolve to go by land so I buy a old Brunswick thrum hat and make i a poor dutch suit rub it in the dust to make it seem old so as my tailor say he take more pain to spoil it then to make it I buy i linen stocking and discolour my face and hand and so without cloak or sword with my hand in my hose take my place in a poor waggon I practise as much as I can pythagorical silence but if any ask i who I be I tell he that I be a poor bohemian and have long serve a merchant at leipzig who leave i to dispatch some business at stand and then command i to follow he to Emden if you have see my servile countenance my eye cast on the ground my hand in my hose and my modest silence you will have take i for a harmless young man many pleasant event happen to i thus disguise wherewith I will not trouble you only one I be tie to impart to you when I come to breme I be doubtful what to do with your letter I think not to deliver they but keep they till a fit time or at least to send they by a messenger but in so do I shall have break my promise to you have lose the fruit of your recommendation and the opportunity to see your mother and sister without hope hereafter to see they then I think to deliver they and because I be disguise in base apparel to confess who I be and wherefore so disguise but when I look my face in a glass I can not for shame take this course at last I resolve to deliver they and to say I be servant to myself wherein I lie not for I have ever too much obey my own affection and that my master meaning to pass from stand by sea for fear of the abovesaid danger have send i by land with command to stay for he at Leyden to be brief I go to your mother house where a servant open i the door to who I give your letter but when he scarce look at i will have lock the door I take my letter again say I have promise to deliver they with my own hand and so I enter with he and give they into the hand of your mother and sister who inquire much after you and so much after my master as I may perceive you have make friendly mention of i in your letter they entertain i with much courtesy be thus disguise for my own servant and when I go away your mother will needs give i six batzen to spend neither will any refusal prevail but I must needs take they so I set a mark upon these piece leave I shall spend they and be not out of hope ere I die to show they to you to the purpose at the door I meet your brother who I have see at frankfort and be not a little afraid leave for all my disguise he will have know i let it not trouble you that I tell you another merry accident I have in the same city of breme disguise as I be I go to the house of doctor desire to have the name of so famous a divine write in my stemme-booke with his mott after the dutch fashion he see my poor habit and a book under my arm take i for some beg scholar and speak sharp unto i but when in my master name I have respective salute he and tell he my request he excuse his mistake and with all courtesy perform my desire I will trouble you no long but hope by some good occasion to embrace you and tell you all the other passage of my journey in the mean time I go forward to Leyden in Holland you as you do ever love i and as my soul live and farewell from Emden the twenty one of October 1592. i pay twenty four stivers for my passage eleven mile in a waggon from stand to breme and the first day after breakfast we pass three mile to Ford a poor city subject to the bishop of breme through wild fenny and woody ground the town be seat in a fen have a long pave causey to pass unto it and the gate be open to we by night each man give the porter two Lubeck shilling and by the way in a village ' each man pay six Fenning for his person at Ford the bishop of breme have a castle strong fortify with rampire of earth and deep ditch full of water and here each man pay for his supper three Lubeck shilling and a half the waggoner take i thus disguise as former I have say for a poor bawre say these word to i in dutch pack high that be ho good fellow help here to carry this pack I answer yea most willing and smile lay my shoulder to the burden and groan deep but help he very little next morning early by moon light we pass on three mile through large and wild wood to a country house and by the way my companion fall in talk of english affair so foolish as my laughter though restrain have often betray i if twilight have not keep i from be see their ignorance great shorten my way with the pleasure I take in their answer to some such question propound by i whereof myself have many time be force to give a account to other by the way they show i a hill call Meineidig of certain false witness of old sink there into the ground at this country house each man pay for his breakfast three Lubeck shilling and a half then from six of the clock in the morning till nine we pass five mile to breme through a heath and many huge wood of oak have towards the south a fen of ten mile length which of the vastness and wildness be call the devil fen by the way within a mile of breme each man pay half a Sesling tribute to the officer of the city and from thence we pass a wind pave causey to the very city man may also pass from hamburg to breme by water this city be one of the imperial free city and of they which upon this sea-coast breme be call hans-steten for freedom of traffic and it be very strong fortify with high wall of earth and deep ditch fill with water beside that the citizen may drown the fenny field almost round about at pleasure the building of this as also of the neighbour city be partly of brick partly of stone and very fair but the street here be filthy the city be five mile distant from the sea and the river visurgis run from the south east to the north west by the south west side of the city run all the length of the same on the north east side the wall of earth be broad and there be three fair gate with strong rampire upon the south west side be compass all with fen there be no wall in the further angle or corner towards the north west where the city grow narrow there be a strong fort build and the gate be within a island beyond which lie a plain of fair pasture osen-bridge lie not far hence from which town great quantity of narrow linen cloth be bring into England at breme I pay half a Doller for dinner supper and breakfast and a stiebkin or measure of wine extraordinary they have here also the custom of make stranger free and the same ceremony of give salt to swear by and I confess that I be not free commit my fine to their censure hope they will deal better with i for my poor disguise habit but it save i nothing the chief man say to i in dutch Gutt gesell must gedult es here deise beyde that be good fellow thou must have patience it be all one whether thou be a servant or a master this custom touch both alike after dinner take my journey from breme we pass a mile upon a stony causey call Steinweck that be stony way and there each man pay to the officer of breme a quarter of a stiver then enter the territory of the grave that be count of oldenburg we pass a mile through fair pasture compass with ditch of water to a village where each man pay a Sesling to the count and to this place each man pay for his wagon five groat here when my companion have drink their fill and have sleep a while in the straw as myself do upon a bench to shun the stink heat of the stove we hire another waggon for three mile pay fifteen groat and that we may more secure pass we take our journey at midnight through a heath of huge wood of oak and come to Oldenburge early in the morning before the gate be open the city be build of mere clay but the count castle be build in a round form oldenburg of stone with deep ditch of water over which they pass by a draw bridge and both the castle and the city be strong fortify here we have english beer the goodness whereof make my companion speak much in honour of England and of the queen with much wonder that she be a virgin be so victorious against the spaniard till in this discourse they all fall fast asleep after breakfast the next morning we have hire a waggon for eighteen groat pass four mile in the territory of the say count and one mile to Stickhausen in the territory of the count of Emden who have a castle there then because we can get no waggon in this place we go one mile further on foot which be very long and myself have some gold gulden in my shoe which I can not remove without suspicion the way be very irksome to i and we come to a country house but we find good cheer each man pay for his supper seven groat myself sit last at the table by reason of my poor habit pay as much as the best and feed on the worst but I have more mind of my bed then of my meat and one of my companion after supper have straight boot when I have teach he to pull off one by the help of a staff for recompense of my counsel desire i to pull off the other which be disguise as I be I can not well refuse the next morning we hire a waggon for eleven stiver and pass along mile to leer a town subject to the count of Emden who dwell not far off at dunort a strong castle our way through a fen be so deep as the waggon wheel be pull off we go good part of the way on foot here we understand that the spanish freebooter call by the english malcontent lie at Aurick another castle of the say count and be load with booty have take a bark by force to pass over the Emsz. these cutthroat use at this time to range out of the spanish garrison upon the low-countries and to spoil all passenger in these part which they do with more confidence because the count of oldenburg be offend with the citizen of breme permit these thief to rob they who be also very malicious against those of breme because they have late take thirty four freebooter and behead they altogether have set up their head upon stake beside the count of Emden have be late drive out of Emden by the citizen in a tumult about religion do permit these freebooter to lie in his country and spoil the merchant of that city the chief captain of the free-boaters then lie at Aurick be Hans jacob a notable rogue and very malicious to the english who he use to spoil of their very apparel and to handle they cruel mock they with these english word I can tell and swear that he will make they tell both of themselves and of their country man pass that way some few day before he have take four english woollen clothes and many flemish linen clothes which they divide by the length of a ditch in stead of a better measure and we be glad to hear that in this division they fall at variance for when this Hans I acob will have stop a part for the chief captain of the garrison the rest cry out in dutch woollen zwey Stelen far selb that be we will rather cleave thy lean pate in two shall we steal for other and hang for ourselves and they use many reproach against he and their chief captain say in dutch Finstu be bringt habt dir pfaffen hauptleinte hund. kost mund: that be find thou aught bring it i hang thou take that to thou the Jew priest captain and dog earn their live with their mouth but these cutthroat howsoever they have pass the Emsz yet mean present to return and have their spic in every town and village I return to my journey while we lie at leer for a night a doctor of the civil law see i walk in the garden and think my servile habit not fit for contemplation command i to draw water for his horse give i no reward present but only a nod yet after when he have drink with his friend go out he say to i Knecht door that be sirrah drink you what be leave after supper have expect a bed almost till midnight the maid at last tell i I must lie upon the bench but after while I be wash my foot which the gold in my shoe have gall she espy my silk stoking which I wear under my linen run to her mistress and procure i a very good bed this effect please i well but I be afraid of the cause by which lest I shall be discover I haste away early next morning i pay here for my supper and breakfast fifteen stivers and give the servant one for his pain he will have restore it to i seem by my habit to have more need thereof then himself all this night and the next day great store of rain fall and the wind be so tempestuous as we can not pass by water neither will my companion hire a waggon beside that the way be at this time so dirty as no waggon can pass it notwithstanding since now only two mile remain of my dangerous journey and I think no thief will come out in such rain I resolve to go on foot with my companion to Emden be two mile but of unspeakable length and difficulty to pass in the high way we have three passage one upon the top of the bank lie upon a arm of the sea or rather upon the river Emsz run into the sea and in this passage the tempestuous wind be like to bear we over and blind we with drive salt water into our eye beside that we go over the shoe in dirt the second passage be on the side of the bank from the water somewhat fair than the other but in that most troublesome that we be force continual to lean upon a staff which every one have in his hand lest be not stay with the staff we shall fall into the low way which be intolerable dirty the low way or three passage in the bottom of the bank further from the water be for the passage of wagon but the field round about be overflow in winter this passage be now (@) (@) in this way we pass a very long mile from the little city leer to the village Aldernsea from seven of the clock in the morning to twelve we come out at first ten companion in this journey but at the very come out (@) six of they leave we despair to pass against a contrary wind in a foul rainy day and their foot stick fast in the dirt and they mock at our obstinacy in go within a while myself be wet to the skin and my shoe at every step be almost to the off so as I be force to bind they on with four point neither do any of we look back at his fellow to help he if he can not follow and if I shall have fall into the sea I be confident none of they will have come back to succour i after we have go half a mile one of our four companion be a young man with a black beard and able body will not go one foot further though he have but one stiver in his purse and be force to borrow money of we that he may stay in a poor alehouse when we come to Aldernsea the freebooter spy come to the inn and gape upon we so as though I be wet to the skin yet I dare not pull off any thing to dry sest my inward garment better than my upper shall betray my disguise neither dare I call for wine and spend free lest they shall think I have store of money each of we pay seven stivers for his dinner here another of our companion leave we be so tire as he go to bed without eat one bit so as now I have only one companion leave call Anthony a man of little stature and a citizen of Emden we so be free of this dangerous journey go forward and as we come out of the village the freebooter spy come close to we and behold we narrow but see we all cover with dirt they take we for poor man and a prey unfit to be follow we gather up strength go on till at last we be so weary as have no strength to choose our way we cast away our staff and go almost up to the knee in dirt in the low way at last have go one mile as i think wondrous long from one of the clock in the afternoon to five we come to Emden where myself enter the gate can not stand till the soldier write our name but have lie down on the ground if they have not give i a seat now be out of all danger of the freebooter in give my name I write myself a Englishman the stander by not a little wonder that I have put myself to this dangerous passage and true this journey if it be free from all danger yet the ill diet and lodging will yield trouble enough for which I appeal to Lipsius who have pleasant write of the entertainment in West-Phalen and oldenburg the city of Emden lie in the utmost border of the empire and be only divide by the river Emsz from the unite province of netherlands and by a inland sea from west freezland be one of they the country about Emden abound with village and from a tower at goricome a man may see at once upon a day twenty two wall town not far from this city near (@) be the place where the duke of Aluna defeat the force of Lodowick of Nassaw his dutchman refuse to fight except they be first pay all the field about Emden be drown in winter and the city lie upon the sea for want of fresh water they dress most of their meat with rain water the air be very unhealthful but the city be fair build of brick and the citizen be very courteous on the south side the river Emsz wash the city with his salt stream on which side be the haven and the citizen be say to have some three score ship of a hundred ton a piece and some six hundred bark of their own in the churchyard on this side many piece of ordinance be lay towards leer and dunort the count fort and the like be lay upon the haven and some place of advantage for the city have no wall on this side on the west side beyond the water lie marish ground to the mouth of the sea and upon this side be a strong old castle on the north side the city be compass with a wall of earth and deep ditch full of water and there be two strong gate belgar-port and newport without which the field be fenny on this side there be a passage by boat to the suburb on the east side where the field without the town be fair pasture in summer but all overflow in winter and upon the rampire of the wall be many windmill the city be of a round form if it be not somewhat long from the east to the west at Emden they pay ordinary six stivers a meal three stiver for a quart of english beer eleven stivers for a quart of spanish wine thirteen stivers a quart of rhenish wine and seven stivers for french wine myself pay for supper and breakfast twenty three stivers chap. 4 of my journey from Emden in Germany to Leyden in Holland and through the unite province of the low-countries on Thursday the twenty seven of October in the year 1592. I take ship after dinner at Emden be to sail ann. 1592. into west Freesland one of the unite province and pay for my passage ten stivers the same night we cast anchor near Vrspenhurn a fort seat beyond the Emsz and belong to the state of the unite province and beyond this port towards Plaunders on the same side of the Emsz lie the territory of the city groan seat in a island rich in pasture and at this time govern by a spanish garrison which the citizen have willing receive though the state after besiege this city and drive out the spaniard and unite the city to the rest as we lie at anchor two little land lay on the north side one subject to Emden the other to groan and beyond they lay the german Sea on Friday we set sail with a scant wind and towards night be leave upon a flat vulgar call get where the water forsake we we walk out of the ship upon the sand compass round about with the sea till the same flow back again our ship float on Saturday we set sail again and towards night rest upon a like flat expect the flood three of these flat be in this inland sea and there be two like flat in the inland sea beteene Freesland and Holland at last we land on Sunday in Freesland at the village Anjou lie near the sea shore whether we hire a sledge for eight stiver and be draw thither over the ice and snow after we have dine for twelve stiver each man whereof more than half be reckon for drink we go in a skeut by water in four hour space one mile to Dockam and each man pay for his passage four stiver and a half we can not Dockam pass by waggon the high way be then drown nothing be more pleasant nothing more quick than sea-voyage if a man may promise himself a good wind and a reasonable gale but through contrariety of wind and tempest they common prove tedious this small voyage which afflict we four day may have be pass in six hour if the wind have favour we and this hope of a short passage cause we to make no provision of victual so as the bark be govern by one mariner and a boy who have nothing but cheese and musty bread to live upon and so can not much relieve we each hour of these four day seem a year unto we Dockam a city of west Freesland little in circuit be in two place divide with water which at this time over flow into the very house the wall be strong with rampier of earth and the house here as in all these part of netherlands be build of brick here I pay for my supper twenty stiver eat at a ordinary but the company sit at the fire and drink after supper all use to be divide equal whether a man drink or not the first of November we go by water in six hour space two mile to Lewerden have on each side the water fertile pasture and pass by two fort and each man Lewerden pay for his passage three stiver the city be fair and well fortify and William count of nassau cousin to count Maurice and governor of Friesland have his residence in the same the street be large and divide with water and the house be fair build of brick the city have no suburb and be of a round form but the water divide the street slow or not at all move be in this city as almost in all other of these province subject to stink in the midst of the city there be a dam tolet in water at pleasure which in this place and two mile further be salt in taste passenger enter the city leave their sword with the guard of soldier and receive they back when they
