go out of the town the village hereabout pay yearly contribution to the spanish garrison of groan leave they shall break in and spoil they here they say the first sermon of reform religion be make in the monastery of the jacobinet and here I pay for my supper fourteen stiver from Lewerden we go by water from eight a clock in the morning to five in the Froniker afternoon two-mile to Froniker a university of Friesland late renew and one mile to the city Harlingen and we pay six stiver for our passage enter this city we leave our sword with the guard of soldier who restore they to we when we Harlingen go away it be a little city and lie in length from the east to the west but be somewhat more narrow towards the north where the house be thin build on the west and north side lie a arm of the sea come out of the german sea and here enclose with the continent and land on the south and east side without the gate be fair pasture in a large plain I lodge in a house the chief host of the city who either despise England and englishman or too much respect his master of Friesland give i such entertainment as I take he for one of the old pict for have place his gentleman of Friesland at one table he call i to the second and see that I take it in ill part lest I shall no less dislike my lodging he entreat a gentleman of Friesland to admit i partner of his bed but I hear the gentleman condition with he about the cleanness of my body and linen for very scorn will not trouble his worship but choose rather to lie upon a bench and it be most ridiculous that this host excuse himself to i as have for country sake make bold with i who he have never see before I pay for my supper and breakfast with wine thirty stiver and one of my consort drink no wine pay sixteen whereof nine be for beer from Harlingen I go by the say inland sea vulgar call Zwidersea fourteen mile to Amsterdam and pay eight stiver for my passage some of our passenger go only to Enchusen pay five stiver for by covenant between the city the ship must land their passenger at Enchusen and there receive such new passenger Enchusen as they find and one ship at least be bind daily to make this passage from the say Harlingen a city of Friesland we pass in four hour sail to Enchusen a city of Holland which be fortify with a wall of earth and strong rampier and lie in length from the north to the south the haven lie on the east side and the new city be then build towards the west side this city lie between the mouth of the german sea and Amsterdam another city of Holland and in the begin of the war take part with the prince of Orange force Amsterdam by stop all supply of victual to yield to the say prince have make short stay here we take ship again and sail from a clock in the evening to twelve in the night in the same inland sea we enter the river Tay where we cast anchor till four in the morning and then set sail pass one mile in that river before six of the clock and land at Amsterdam five street of this city be divide with water the river Tay flow like a large and calm sea on the north side where be a safe port the traffic be great in this Amsterdam city and at Midleburg since the passage to Antwerp be stop upon the haven lic a field or market place call Campplata where the citizen use to behold their friend go to sea and return home from this place towards the south lie warmerstrat a long and large street between two river which part of the city be call the new ditch the merchant in summer meet upon the bridge and in winter they meet in the new church in very great number where they walk in two rank by couple one rank go up and another go down and there be no way to get out of the church except they slip out of the door when in one of those rank they pass by they on the east side of the city there be a wall of stone high than the city have a pleasant walk upon it in the same place be house for exercise of shoot in and crossbow beyond this wall there be another of earth and between these wall the new city be plot out where of few house be then build but since I hear it be full finish likewise on the south and west side there be two like wall and between they the plot of the say new city in which many fair house be then build the field on all side without the gate be fenny and drown with water do make the city more strong but for this cause they say the foundation of the house be lay in water cost as much or more as the house themselves the river amster of which and the word dam the city be name run from the south through three lake enter this city and pass through it fall into the river Tay on the north side the city have five gate which be shut at dinner and supper though the danger of the war be far from they there be two church in which they have two sermon each second day and four on Sunday the city lie in length from the north to the south but add the plot of the new city it be of a round form the street be narrow and the build of brick with a low roof show antiquity they have two almshouse call Gasthausen that be house for stranger which be of old monastery one of these house build round be a cloister for nun wherein sixty bed at this time be make for poor woman disease and in another chamber thereof be fifty two bed make for the auxiliary shoulder of England be hurt or sick and in the three room be eighty one bed make for the hurt and sick soldier of other nation to which soldier and sick woman they give clean sheet a good diet and necessary clothes with great cleanliness and allow they physician and surgeon to cure they and most of the city in these province have like house here I lodge with a Englishman and pay for dinner and supper twenty stiver and for a guest invite to supper ten stiver and for three pint or chopine of spanish wine twenty one stiver from Amsterdam I go in a boat three mile to Harlam and pay for my passage four stiver we have not pass far from Amsterdam when we come to a dam shut out the flow of the sea for the water be salt thus far though the ebb and the flow of the sea can hardly be discern at Amsterdam for the depth of the river Tay and because inland sea show little ebb or flow our boat be lift over this dam by rope and so let fall into the water on the other side for which the mariner pay tribute there be another dam for great bark and as by these dam they let in water to the land at pleasure so they have other dam at Torgay to let they out again into the sea when the land have too much water from hence we have the seashore all the way on the north side not far distant and on both side of the water in which we pass be fair pasture part with ditch of water the river run from Amsterdam from the east to the north do turn near Harlam towards the south and divide the city which on all side be compasy Harlam with navigable water on the north side near the gate jans-port don Frederick son to the duke of Alun pitch his tent in a meadow when he besiege the city with the spanish force and much spoil those part beat down gentleman fair house dwell frequent in that part with his artillery play into this street have the name of the knight of saint John on the same side be two other gate sayle-port and cruyse-port and without they towards the sea be half a mile distant be very fair pasture but there be no river nor ditch that lead from the city to the sea for these province have only three passage to go to sea one between Rotherodam and Bril a fort of Zealand the second at Vlishing another port of Zealand and the three from Amsterdam between two land call the fly and shell we come from Amsterdam to Harlam enter the city by the gate kleine holt port on the east side where the very almshouse be beat down in the aforesaid siege of the spaniard and the wall then beat down be not yet rebuild on the south side be the five gate grote-holt port the street whereof be the fair next that of the knight of saint John on this side be a wall of stone but at this time they be build another very strong wall beyond it of earth in the new-street be the house for exercise of shoot and another old house for the same use and one marketplace sweet shade with tree and a second marketplace of good length for the sell of cattle likewise on this side another part of the spanish army lay and destroy a most pleasant wood of which the gate and street have the name and they report that the spaniard take the city use great cruelty to all but especial to the garrison of the english soldier the history witness that three hundred be behead and more than two hundred drown in the lake call harlam-mere on the west side the city be compass with a wall of earth and there be fair pasture betweeve the city and the Sea among the church that which be call the great be the fair and our lady church vulgar call vnserfraw kirk be the next in beauty all the sea coast of Holland be a sandy down in which be great store of coney this city make great store of linen clothes and have some five hundred spinster in it the water here as most of these city stand and little or nothing move be subject to stink so as they be force to fetch water for brew by boat here I pay for supper and my part of wine twenty stiver and for my dinner without wine thirteen stiver about a mile from the city be a very sweet hil call Weligheberg whether the bride use to walk and there take their leaf of the virgin and in the mid way towards almer be another hil where the count of Holland be wont to be consecrate in the marketplace over against the palace they show the house of one Laurence John who they brag to be the first inventor of the press for print and they show two bell of the brass of Corinth which they say be bring from Pelusium a city in Africa upon the Nile from Harlam we hire a waggon for eight stiver and come five mile in five hour space to Leyden our waggoner bait his horse in the mid way but stay Leyden very little in the way we have on all side fair pasture and pass by the lake or mere of Harlam lie towards the south and the sea bank within sight towards the north. the high way in these province seem to be force and make by art be sandy and very dry though all the pasture on both side be compass with frequent ditch of water at the gate of Leyden the man go out of the waggon and only woman may be carry into the city lest as I think the wheel of the load wagon shall break the brick pavement of the street hence I return present to Amsterdam that I may receive money send i by exchange so I hire a waggon for eight stiver my part from hence to Harlam and by the way I observe that the wagon have past more than half the way must have the way give they by all the wagon they meet because their horse shall in reason be most weary at Harlam I pay for supper bed and breakfast twenty five stiver hence I go by waggon and pay for my part of it sixteen stiver for three mile to Amsterdam and there receive my money return to Harlam draw over the snow and ice which have plentiful fall on a sledge for which I pay four stiver and I observe many mark set up in the field to direct the way to passenger from Harlam I return to Leyden where I lodge in a Frenchman house for intend to bestow all my time in the french tongue till by letter I shall dispose Leyden of my estate in England and there be a famous university in this city I find no abide fit for i then this I pay for my diet and chamber in this Frenchman house three and fifteen stiver weekly but in the common inn they pay ten or fifteen stiver a meal according to the quantity of beer they drink and ordinary twenty stiver or more if they drink wine Leyden be so call of the word de dunen that be lie by the down so they call the sandy bank of the sea as the english do likewise in Kent Leyden be of a round form or perhaps somewhat long from the east to the west where the Rhine pass by it it be a city of much beauty the house be very fair build of brick and be uniform the church be cover with long slate as they be almost through all holland and among the street one be much fair than the rest in the midst whereof be a piece of ground rail in where the merchant meet many street be divide with water which be pass by wooden bridge and in deed if a man dig two foot in any part of Holland he shall find water I say that the Rhine pass by this city yet do it not fall into the sea but lose itself in many stand ditch of water in this low part of the continent towards the northwest about a mile from the city there be the end of a ditch dig of old from the very city and vulgar call Malgatt because the citizen spend much treasure in a vain hope to make a haven for ship and a navigable water to come up to the town for the heap of sand daily cast up by the sea fill the place up where they think to have make the haven as fast as they can dig it yet be it long before they will cease from this ill advise work notwithstanding salt water come under the earth from the sea into this ditch and they carry the same unto the city to make salt thereof upon the same seashore towards the north and like distance from the city be a village call Catwicke seat upon mountain of sand on the main sea upon the same shore further towards the north be a place where they say the roman of old have a armoury the ruin whereof some musket shoot from the shore more or less appear as the wind cover they with sand or blow from another quarter drive away the sand and so lay they open hereabout they say that many coin of the roman be oftentimes dig up and near the hoch-landish church be a monument build by Caligula the emperor which now belong to a gentleman of that country upon the north side of this city the village Warmond and Nortwicke lie upon the aforesaid down but the city have no gate that direct lead to they Leyden have five gate Regenspurgport on the west side which lead to Harlam and to Catwicke and white port which lead to hage between which gate there be a low water-gate of iron grate for boat to pass in and out near white port lie a house where they exercise shoot with the piece and crossbow on the south side be the gate kow-port lead into the pasture upon the east side be the gate hochwertzport more fortify than any of the rest and it lead to Vberden Gonda and to Alphen there be another gate Zillport which lead ro Vtretcht whither you pass by water or land the foresay street which I say be the beauty of the town lie from the west to hochwertzport on the east side and be call breitstrat that be Broadstreete in the spring time of the year 1593. purpose to see the city of the unite province an. 1593. I hire a wagon for six stiver and go from Leyden to Delft three mile in three hour space through corn field and rich pasture and have go two three part of the way we pass over the water that run from Leyden to Delft in all these part the high way have ditch on both side and be very plain sandy and very dry be daily repair by the country people by the way be a mill in which they make oil of rape and line seed mingle with walnut shell and they have many such mile in those part not far of at Voberg the history write of a holy grove famous for a conspiracy against the roman the city of Delft lie in length from the north to the south and the falr street call corne-mart lie the same way here as in all the city of these part Delft the building be of brick but the house of Delft be more stately build and seem to have more antiquity than other where in the new church be a monument of the prince of Orange the poor that ever I see for such a person be only of rough stone and mortar with post of wood colour over with black and very little erect from the ground near the church be a large marketplace and within a little island the senate house be build the haven be on the south side the prince of Orange dwell here in a monastery and use to eat in a low parlour whence as he ascend the stair into the chamber a wicked murderer give he his death wound who fly by a back door be after take in the city and put to a most cruel but most deserve death the countess of buren daughter to this say prince now live in this monastery with her family here I pay for one meal for myself and a guest invite by i and two pot of rhenish wine three gulden and five stiver when the spanish army most press the unite province the prince of Orange then lie here to shun a great mischief from the spaniard break down the bank of the sea and let in the water which do much hurt to the country but save they from the spaniard who with great fear haste away give great reward to those that guide they to the firm continent at Delft be about three hundred brewer and their beer for the goodness be call delphs-english but howsoever they have brewer and the very water out of England they can never make their beer so much esteem as the english which indeed be much better by the carriage over sea to these part hence I go to Sluse so call of the dam to let water in and out and come thither in two hour pay for my waggon thirteen stiver which I hire alone for if I have light upon company we shall have pay no more between we hence I pass the river mase where it fall into the sea and come to Brill myself and two other pay twelve stiver for our passage but the bark be present to return and therefore not enter the port set we on land near the town whether we walk on foot Brill be a fortify town lay in pledge to queen Elizabeth for money she lend the state and it be then keep by four english company pay by the queen Brill under the government of the lord burrowes the town be seat in a island which be say to be absolute of itself neither belong to Zealand nor Holland on the north side the river mase run by on the east side be corn field and the river somewhat more distant on the south side be corn field on the west side be corn field and the main sea little distant here I pay for my supper and dinner twenty stiver and for a pot of wine eighteen stiver from hence I return by water to Roterodam in Holland and pay for my passage three stiver in the mouth of the river of Roterodam lie the city arseldipig and another call delphs-ile be the haven of Delft which be then a pleasant village but grow to a city and have be late burn by fire be fair rebuild Roterodam lie in length from the east to the west the haven be on the south Roterodam side be then full of great ship upon which side it lay open without wall have many fair house and a sweet walk upon the bank of the water neither be it fortify on the side towards the land nor seem to i able to bear a siege have low wall on the north and east side yet compass with broad ditch the street hoch-street be fair and large extend itself all the length of the city and lie so as from the gate at the one end you may see the gate at the other end and in this street be the senate house in the market place towards the west be the statue of Erasmus be make of wood for the spaniard break down that which be make of stone and the inscription thereof witness that he be bear at Roterodame the twenty eight of October in the year 1467 and die at bazel the twelve of july in the year 1531. in new-kirk-street there be the house in which Erasmus be bear wherein a tailor dwell at this time and upon the brickwall thereof these verse be write side the world Erasmus in this poor house bear with art religion faith do much adorn the same verse also be write in the flemish tongue and upon the brickwall be the picture of Erasmus upon the same west side be the house for exercise of shoot in the piece and crossbow the water of Roterodam and Delft be near the sea be more wholesome than the stand water within land here I lodge at a Englishman house and pay for my supper ten stiver for my breakfast two stiver and for beer between meal five stiver by which expense compare with that of the flemish inn it be apparent that stranger in their reckon pay for the intemperate drink of their dutch companion from hence I go by sea three mile to Dort in two hour space to which city we may have go great part of the way by waggon as far as Helmund but then Dort we must needs have cross a inland sea for the city be seat in a island have be of old divide from the continent of Holland in a great flood the form of the city resemble a galley the length whereof lie from the east to the west we land upon the north side lie upon the sea where there be two gate but of no strength on the east side be the new gate Reydike and beyond a narrow water lie fenny ground on the south side the ditch be more narrow yet the sea ebb and flow into it and upon old wall of stone be a convenient walk place on this side be the gate spey-port and beyond the ditch lie fenny ground on the west side be the gate feld-port and a like walk upon wall of stone and there be a great ebb and flow of the sea there be a great church build of brick and cover with slate be stately build with arch cloister and there of old the count of Holland be consecrate from this part the two fair street reydike-strat and wein-strat lie wind towards the north. turn a little out of the fair street reydike-strat towards the south lie the house for exercise of shoot in the piece and crossbow and there by be a very pleasant grove upon the tree whereof certain bird frequent which we call hearne vulgar call Adhearne or Regle and their feather be of great price there be a great penalty set on they that shall hurt or annoy those bird there be a house which retain the name of the emperor Charles the five and another house for coin of money for the count of Holland be wont to coin money at Dort as the count of Zealand do at Midleburg between the fair street reydike-strat and wein-strat be the haven for ship to be pass over by bridge and there be a market place and the senate house which have a prospect into both these street the house be high build than other where in Holland and seem to be of great antiquity this city by privilege be the staple of rhenish wine which be from hence carry to other city so as no imposition be here pay for the same the pot of rhenish wine be sell for twelve stiver for which in other place they pay eighteen or twenty stiver for three meal I pay here thirty stiver from hence I go by water to the state camp besiege Getrudenberg and Getrudenberg come thither in two hour space but the wind be very tempestuous we see a boat drown before we out of which one man only escape by swim who seem to i most wretched in that he over-lived his wife and all his child then drown the besiege city lie in the province of Brabant and the county of buren be the inheritance of the prince of Orange by right of his wife and in this month of june it be yield to count Maurice the spanish army lie near but not be able to succour it the sea lie upon this part of Brabant be of old firm land join to the continent till many village by diverse flood and seventeen parish at once by a famous flood be within less than 200. year ago swallow up of the sea and for witness of this calamity diverse tower far distant the one from the other appear in this sea and according to the ebb and flow more or less see do always by their sad spectacle put the passenger in mind of that woeful event and the hollander say that these flood cause the Rhine to change his bed as hereafter I shall show in the due place from count Maurice his camp at Getrudenberg I fail in six hour space to the island plate and at midnight put forth again fail in ten hour space to the island tarlot and from thence in three hour space to the city bergenapzome where bergenapzome we land by the way we see one of the aforesaid tower high above the water be a steeple of some parish church swallow up in the say deluge of which there be many like sad remembrance in this inland sea the channel lead to the city be call forcemer and have upon the bank many strong sort and in this channel lie a man of war to defend passenger from the border enemy this city be strong fortify and be scent in Brabant and have many castle of the enemy lie neerert and it be govern by a garrison of english not in the queen but in the state pay as ostend at that time be whereas Virshing and Brill pledge to the queen for money wear keep by english garrison in the queen pay and sir Thomas Morgan be at this time governor of this city at out entrance every man give his nameto the Guard without the city on the west side many acre of land be drown when the prince of Orange as I say let in the water to drive the spaniard out of those part which from that day to this can never be dry and gain again on this side I enter the city where be many poor house build in form of a lute neck which be add to the city almost of a round form make the whole city much like unto a lute on this side be three strong ravel and upon the neck of the say lute be the haven in the channel Forcemer which go no further into the land end in a mill make of purpose to keep the ebb water so as the ditch may always be full on the north side be the prison not unpleasant for situation and the english house and the house of the governor which of old belong to the count of Brabant between the gate wouldport and Stephenbergport which be both strong fortify the river some fall into the town whereof it have the name yet the channel be stop it seem here a stand water rather than a river towards the east the city be very strong fortify and there be the gate Boskport so call as I think of the word Bosco which in the italian tongue signify a wood for on this side without the gate be many wood and orchard till they be destroy in the war on this side be another ravelin of great length and beyond the fortification lie fair pasture but somewhat cover with water and from hence we may see Woudcastle scarce three english mile distant which be then possess by the spaniard on the south side be a new fort beyond a strong bulwark and a very strong counterscarp compass the city and from hence be of old a most pleasant walk under the shade of tree to the old castle some mile distant on this side in a pleasant grove be many such bird as I say to be at Dort vulgar call adherne much esteem for the feather they bear in their fore head and there be a penalty set on those that hurt or drive they away on this side also be the english church and upon this and the east side the prince of Parma in camp when he besiege this city there be in the midst of the city a triangular market place and from the sharp end thereof towards the west ravel run beyond the wall the house be build of brick and seem to be build of old the church have a very high steeple whence the watchman show the come and number of horseman by hang out white flag and of foot by red all the village hereabout though live under the Spaniard yet pay contribution to this garrison lest the soldier shall upon advantage break out and spoil they the citizen live of mannall art and the expense of the garrison from hence I sail to Midleburge and at one ebb of the sea pass in seven hour space to der-goese and at another ebb in four hour space to Armaren a city of the island walkern belong to Zealand and I pay for my passage six stiver from hence in half a hour I walk on foot to Midleburge the house whereof be stately Midleburg build and very high especial the new city and be all of brick as be the city of Holland and as be the house of Vlishing but some of these be stately build of free stone yet the street be somewhat narrow here I pay for my supper five stiver in the english house where the host be only bind to provide for the merchant and such gucst as they invite yet many time he admit english gentleman both to lodge and eat there the house lie in the street longdel and howsoever the merchant cat there yet they hire their lodging scatter in the city and refuse a abbey which the senator offer they to lodge therein perhaps out of fear lest in any civil tumult they may more easy be wrong if they shall all lie together this city be the staple of all merchandise except Rhenish wine for which by old privilege dorte be the staple therefore french and spanish wine be here sell much more cheap than other where because they be free of impost in this place and have great imposition lay on they be carry out to other city the form of the city be round save that on the east side the building of the new city be unperfect make it to have the form of a half moon though the plot thereof be round come from Armuren I enter on this east side by a very fair gate call the new gate where the water fall into the town pass to the burse where the merchant meet there be a public house for shoot the wall on this side as round about the city be of stone and be rather adorn then fortify with some tower and this wall be double upon the inner whereof compass with deep ditch many house be build on the west side without the gate almost half way to Vlishing be Rammakins castle keep by english soldier send from Vlishing to that purpose be a place of great importance because the channel go to Midleburge run within the command of their artillery on this south side be the haven and without the wall very fair pasture to the uttermost part of the island also on this side be a new haven make for ship in the winter time and the gate be call Rammakins port of the say castle on the west side you may see Vlishing a mile off and in a clear day the down of Kent in England on this side within the wall be a round market place and the senate-house of antic building and two gate call of the city to which they lead Vlishing port and longe-ville port on the north side be a abbey and pleasant walk and another public house for exercise of shoot this populous city have only two church either because the people be of many sect in religion and much occupy in traffic scarce the three part come to church or else because the people be much increase by stranger come to dwell in these part upon the stop of the passage to Antwerp in the civil war it be no wonder that the old church will not receive they the citizen may at pleasure drown all the field about they and this one and the chief island of Zealand call Walkerne contain wall city beside village but the air be repute unwholesome Midleburge be the chief place of traffic in Zealand as Amsterdam in Holland from hence I go in a long wagon cover with hoop and cloth to Vlishing a long mile and pay for my passage two blank ten english foot company one Vlishing hundred and fifty in each company under the government of sir Robert Sidney keep this strong town for the queen of England and under her pay be engage to she for money lend the state and the ten captain in course watch each three night the city be little and of a round form but very strong it have a narrow sea on the west side where upon the last confine of Zealand and the unite province be one of the three passage whereof I former speak to the main Sea on this side be the mountain of the mill where the soldier watch nightly and beyond the mountain be a dam to let in the sea at pleasure on the south side be the gate Waterport strong fortify lie upon the inland Sea on this side towards the north the sea flow into the town make one haven and towards the east another and divide the city into three part the old the new and the middle whereof any one be take by the enemy yet the other be fortify for defence beyond these haven or channel be a mountain lie over the city upon which the soldier keep guard day and night as they do likewise upon the bridge divide the city and upon other low hill at all the gate of the city and in prayer time at the door of the english church this church be on the east side and be common to the english and dutch at diverse hour between the high mountain and this church be the governors house belong of old to the count of Zealand and the public house for exercise of shoot but less pleasant than the like house be in other city on the same east side lie two way one to Rammakins castle the other to Midleburge on the north side the down of Kent in England may easy be see and there be the hospital or gasthouse for sick people and for sick and maim soldier of which a mountain thereby have the name on this and the east side be two mill to retain the water when the sea ebb that the ditch round about may always be fill and if need be to overflow the field these ditch be common a pike depth and can by no art or enemy be dry the citizen want good water have no well nor any fresh water but rain water keep in Cesternes the foresay number of soldier in the garrison be not sufficient to master the citizen only their courageous mind despise death keep the citizen in such awe as they dare not attempt to recover their liberty by force which they hope to obtain by peaceable mean and the unite province depend upon the opinion of the queen aid perhaps more than upon the aid itself so as either fail they be like to be a prey to the spaniard since that time I hear the garrison be diminish so as it seem the english have less strength to keep it if the state change their mind shall attempt to surprise it be invite by my english friend I spend nothing in this city hence I return to Midleburge on foot upon a pave causcy have on each side rich corn field and fair pasture with many orchard and in the midway a gentleman call Aldegondey famous for his wisdom have his castle wherein he dwell at Midleburge I pay six stiver for my supper and two for my bed and provide victual to carry by sea I pay for a loin of mutton twenty four stiver as also for my wash seven stiver and stay in the town two day I spend in all four gulden and four stiver I take ship at ten in the morning and between the island der-goese and the inland sea call Zurechsea I see two tower of village swallow in the foresay deluge and sail by the island plate and the island of Brill we pass certain booye direct to find the channel the next day in the afternoon I land at Roterodam in Holland and pay ten stiver for my passage thence I pass in two hour space by boat to Delft and pay two stiver for my passage thence in two hour space I pass to the hage by wagon and pay for my passage two stiver for which journey one man alone may hire a wagon for seven stiver at the hage count Maurice with his mother in law the countess of Orange bear the hage of the noble family of the chastillon in France and the general state of the unite province and prince ambassador have their residence which make i desirous to stay here a while to which purpose I hire a chamber for which for my bed sheet tableclothe towel and dress of my meat I pay twenty five stiver weekly I buy my own meat and live private with as much frugality as convenient I may I spend by the week no more than five gulden and a half though all thing be in this place extraordinary dear my beer in one week come to fourteen stiver and among other thing buy I pay for a quarter of lamb thirty stiver for a hen seven stiver for a pigeon four stiver for a rabet three stiver I remember not to have see a more pleasant village than this great part of the house be fair build of brick though many of they in by-streete be cover with thatch and some few be stately build of freestone the village have the form of a cross and upon the east side come in from Leyden there be a most pleasant grove with many wild walk like a maze and near the house be another very pleasant walk set round about with willow here be the public house for exercise of shoot in the piece and crossbow which have a sweet prospect into a large green plain where they use to spread linen clothes in the sun and here certain row of tree be plant yield a pleasant shade to they that walk therein one of the say row of tree call Vinareberg lead to a old castle of the count of Holland compass with a dry ditch in which count Maurice dwell but in the great hall thereof be many shop of merchant for small ware upon the wall of the say castle and upon the window of the church these word be write in latin to charles the five etc. to the most invincible Caesar Charles the five roman emperor the victorious desender of the catholic religion and Augustus the provisor of this house have place this in the year 1547. thereby be the statue of Charles the five kneel on his knee in the window be paint the arm of all the knight of the golden fleece the history of the country report the build of this palace to be wonderful in that the top of the hall be not join with beam but with arch but for my part I observe no great magnificence in the work the second of the foresay row of tree call Furholt lead to a gentleman house the fair and most stately build in this village in the midst of the hage lie the market place and the church on the south side be the water that leader to Delft and round about on all side without the village be fair pasture except the north-side where the sandy down of the sea lie near to the village in the church be a monument of count Albertus duke of Bauria and another of a count of Hanaw with diverse other which I omit as have no antiquity or magnificence while I stay at the hage I walk out in half a hour space to the village lausdune where I see a wonderful monument the history whereof print in a paper lausdune the earl of Leicester as they say have carry with he into England leave only the same in write hand the copy whereof I will set down first remember that two basin of brass hang on the wall in which the child whereof I shall speak be baptize the manuscript be in latin a follow so strange and monstrous thing I tell as from the world frame never befall he part amaze that mark it well the rest in latin be thus english Margaret wife to count of Henneberge daughter to Florence count of Holland and Zealand sift to William king of the roman and Caesar or govermour of the empire this most noble countess be about forty two year old the very day of preparation call paraseene about nine of the clock in the year 1276. this year fall in a lie and supersitious age bring forth at one birth three hundred sixty five child which be baptize in two basin of brass by Guido suffragan of Vtretcht all the male be call John and all the female Elizabeth but all of they together with the mother die in one and the same day and lie bury here in the church of lausdune and this happen to she in that a poor woman bear in her arm two twin the countess wonder at it say she can not have they both by one man and so reject she with scorn whereupon the woman sore trouble wish that the countess may have as many childen at a birth as there be day in the whole year which beside the course of nature by miracle fall out as in this table be brief set down for perpetual memory out of old chronicle as well write as print almighty God must be in this behold and honour and extol with praise for ever and ever amen from the hage myself and other consort hire a wagon for two gulden and pass to Leyden have on both side fair pasture fruitful corn field and some pleasant grove chap. v. of my journey out of the unite province by the sea coast to stand and Lubeck in Germany of my save to Denmark and thence to dantzk in Prussen and my journey through Poland to Padova in Italy in the end of the month of june and the year 1593. have now dispatch by letter all my business in an. 1593. England and have see the unite province I be in doubt by what way I shall return into Italy and have already pass the two way of Germany that by Augspurge and the other by the sweitzer and the way by France be then shut up by the civil war the common desire of traveller not to pass the same way twice but to see as many new country as their course will permit make i resolve to pass through the kingdom of Denmark and Poland and by the fortify city of Wien in Austria in which journey howsoever I shall go much out of my way and be like to endure many trouble yet I think nothing be difficult to a willing mind therefore I hire a waggon from Leyden to Vtrecht and pay for my part twelve stiver we pass three mile and a half in three hour by the village Alpha where the spaniard encamp when they besiege Leyden and by a little town call Gonda have on both side fair pasture but somewhat over flow and ditch set with willow and we come to a little village where the waggoner give his horse meat then in four hour space we pass four mile and a half have on both side fruitful corn field and like ditch set with willow and so we come to Vtrecht but a mile and a half before we come thither we pass out of the territory of Holland and enter the bishopric of Vtrecht which be one of the unite province not far from the city we see a cross set up for a monument of a bishop die in battle against the hollander I have almost forget the little city werden which they show we by the way and tell we that the form thereof be like the city of jerusalem which at that time I have not see and therefore mention this from their report rather than from my judgement the city Vtrecht be seat in length from southeast by east to northwest by west and upon the end at southeast by east be the gate where the Rhine V'tretcht enter the city at the other end nothwest by west be the ruin of a old castle which the spaniard keep before the war to bridle the city and there be two gate saint katherine-port and wert-port each of they have their suburb on the south-west side be wall of earth but the ditch be almost dry on the north-east side be the gate olske-port and there be three strong raveling one defend the other on this side be two street fair than the rest call new-graft and Altkirkhoffe and there be a pleasant walk well shade with tree upon the bank of the river in the midst of the city be the cathedral church have a fair tower and a bell which they report to be of eighteen thousand pound weight near to the same be the bishop palace wherein the bishop dwell before the union of the province but at this time there dwell the countess of Meurs who husband die in these war in the same part lie the market place and the senate house the house of the city be of brick and fair build but lose much of their beauty by be cover on the outside with board and they seem to have more antiquitic than the building of Holland there be thirty church but only three be use for divine service in saint Mary church which as I remember be the cathedral church these verse be write upon a pillar posterity hear this and to your child tell bull hide bear up this pillar from the low hell upon a second pillar this be write in latin a frison kill the bishop because he have learn of he be drink and betray by his son the art to stop a gulf in this place the year 1099. upon a three pillar this be write in latin the emperor Henry the four build this church to our lady because he have pull down another church at Milane dedicate to she and to my understanding they show i at this time manifest sign of the aforesaid gulf which these inscription witness to have be in this place here I pay for my supper twenty stiver and for my breakfast six stiver from hence I go to Amsterdam five mile in three hour space and pay for my passage in the waggon ten stiver for half the way on both side we have fair pasture and see many strong castle belong to gentleman near Vtrecht at the passage of a river each man pay a doit and before we come to the half way we pass the confine of this bishopric and enter the county of Holland then in the space of two hour and a half we come to Amsterdam have in our way on both side fair pasture on Friday in the begin of the month of july at five a clock in the evening I take ship upon the mast whereof be a garland of rose because the master of this ship than woo his wife which ceremony the hollander use and the sea be calm we pass eight mile to Enchusen where we cast anchor by the way we pass a shoal where our stern strike twice upon the sand not without fear of great mischief on Saturday we'sayeld between west Freesland upon our right hand towards the east and Holland upon our left hand towards the west and after ten mile sail come to the island fly which be of small compass and Fly consist of sandy hill have two village in it from hence they reckon twenty eight mile by sea to hamburg in Germany whether we purpose to go assoon as we cast anchor here the master of our ship go aboard the admiral of certain ship which use to lie here to guard this mouth of the sea with who he speak concern our passage to hamburg and deliver he letter command that our ship shall have a man of war to waste it this admiral lie continual in this harbour to guard this passage into the sea and he command nine ship which be upon all occasion to waste the hollander to hamburg and defend they from the dunkirker and all pirate but at this time there be not one of these man of war in the harbour and the admiral himself may not go forth so as for this cause and for the tempestuous weather we stay here all Sunday but upon Monday the wind be fair for we and contrary for the man of war that be to come in so that lose this wind we must have expect not without great irksomeness a second wind to bring in some of these man of war and a three wind to carry we on our journey the master of our ship carry six great piece and have some ten musket do associate himself with seven other little ship have only pike and sword and so more bold then wise resolve to pass to hamburg without any man of war this Monday morning we hoist sail but be calm at noon we cast anchor between the fly on our left hand towards the west and another little island shell on our right hand towards the east and lie here we may see two little bark hover up and down which we think to be fisherman and nothing less than pirate of Dunkirk here till evening we be toss by the wave which use to be more violent upon the coast but a fair wind then arise all our ship glad weigh anchor at which time it happen that the anchor of our ship break so as our consort go on but our master according to the naval discipline not to put to sea with one anchor return back to the harbour of the fly there to buy a new anchor all of we foolish curse our fortune and the star on Tuesday morning while we sad walk on the shore we may see our consort come back with tear sail and dead man and quarter of man lie on the hatch we behold this with great astonishment take boat to board they and demand the news they tell we that the little bark we see the day before be dunkirker have in each of they eighty soldier and some few great piece and that they have take they and spoil their ship of their chief and light good and have carry away prisoner to Dunkirk all the passenger and chief mariner after they have first wring their forehead with twine rope and with many horrible torture force they to confess what money they have present and what they can procure for ransom further with mourn voice they tell we that the pirate inquire much after our ship say that be it the bride with who they mean to dance curse it to be destroy with a thousand tun of devil and swear that if they have foresee our escape they will have assail we by day while we ride at anchor they add that they have leave no good but those they can not carry for weight and have change their ragged shirt and apparel with the poor mariner and indeed they have just cause to bewail the escape of our ship be lade with many chest of spanish rial whereof they be not ignorant use to have their spy in such place who for a share in the booty will have betray their very brother as we have just cause to praise almighty God who have thus deliver we out of the law of death so have we much more cause to bewail our rashness yea and our wickedness that we have strive yea and repine against his divine providence which with humble and hearty sorrow I confess to the glory of his sacred name in this island I pay for my supper and bed ten stiver for my breakfast and dinner eight stiver on Wednesday we have a most fair wind but the terror of our last escape make we stay in the harbour in the evening I go to lodge in the village and pay ten stiver for my supper and bed and there I see great store of all kind of shellfish sell for a very small price since this journey by sea have beside our expectation prove so difficult myself though I have see the city upon the sea coast of Germany yet prefer my safety before the charge and trouble of that way do resolve to pass to hamburg by land with which purpose when I acquaint my consort sudden all the passenger resolve to leave the ship and to go by land at which resolution the master of the ship storm but when each of we have pay they a Doller for our passage from Amsterdam to the fly they be well pacify myself and nine consort in my company hire a boat for thirty stiver each man pay three stiver and so upon Thursday in four hour space we sail three mile to Harlingen a city of west Freesland pass the aforesaid inland sea and the same day hire a boat for which each man pay six stiver we pass a mile to the city Froniker where be a university and pass by water through the midst of that pleasant little city we pass two mile further to Lewerden where we lodge and I pay for my supper ten stiver the next morning be Friday we pass in six hour space two long mile to Dockam and each man pay for his boat hire three stiver without delay in the afternoon we enter a bark to sail from west Freesland one of the unite province into east Freesland a province of the german empire but scarce one mile from the town we cast anchor to expect the flood and lie there we hear from the land great noise bark of dog cry of man and sound of bell which proceed from some spanish freebooter break out of groan to spoil the peasant all the next day we sail and in the evening for our better safety we cast anchor near a man of war whereof there be some appoint to lie in this inland sea to guard the friend of the state and early the next morning be Sunday we set sail and by the rise of the sun land in the island of Rotermere which be divide from the continent by this inland sea and have the main sea on the other side whence soon after we part and be put on land in east Freesland a province of the empire and pass one mile on foot come to the city of Emden I say that the state maintain some man of war in this inland sea and these use to send out in lesser boat some body of man to search the bark whether they be friend or not which body of man be vulgar call that be the hunt of a metaphor take from the hunt of dog for my passage from Dockam to Emden I pay ten stiver and here for supper and breakfast I pay twenty three stiver though the ordinary rate be but six stiver a meal without wine and for a pound of cherry I pay eight stiver in our journey to stand the first day we come in six hour space three mile to the village upon the confine of the county of Emden pass through fruitful corn field and fair meadow and be eight in the wagon we all pay fifteen stiver for the same from hence we go a mile through wild and fenny field to the village open and each four consort pay for their wagon three stiver and our wagon be drive by a woman here the grave or count of oldenburg have a castle and each man pay for his supper seven groat by night we pass four mile through a wild heath to oldenburg and four consort pay ten stiver for a wagon they have very little horse in these part to draw the wagon like to the way nag of Scotland the second day we pass two mile and a half in four hour space through a sandy heath ground and thick wood of oak and come to a village where each man pay for his dinner four stiver after dinner we pass more than a mile through a like woody heath and in three hour space come to Delmerhurst where the count of Oldenburge have a fair and strong castle though it be a poor village and here each man pay half a stiver to the count and for our wagon ten stiver the same day we pass a mile through sandy pasture and in three hour space come to breme where each man pay for our wagon four groat and for our supper five lubeck shilling from breme we pass four mile through wild field yield some little corn and thick wood and in six hour space come to a poor house where each man pay for dinner five lubeck shilling here those which carry any merchandise pay toll and one man have a pack which a man may carry on his shoulder pay four lubeck shilling for the same but all that go to study in university or be no merchant be free from this imposition after dinner we pass three mile in five hour space to fur where each man pay for his supper five lubeck shilling the next day from two of the clock in the morning to seven we pass three mile through a heath and wood of oak and come to stand where each man pay for his wagon from breme twenty two Lubeck shilling at stand I pay for my dinner in a dutch inn four Lubeck shilling and a half and for a steifkin or measure of rhenish wine half a dolor I brief pass over this journey upon the sea-coast of Germany because I former describe the same the one and twenty of july I pass in four hour space by boat five mile to Hamburge and pay for my passage by water three Lubeck shilling for my supper four and one for my bed early in the morning I pass six mile in six hour space through wild fenny field wood of oak and some few field of corn and come to the village Altslow seat in a bog whereof it have the name where I pay for my dinner five Lubeck shilling and a half give i leave to tell you a ridiculous toy yet strange and true at Hamburge gate lead to Lubeck we find a dog that follow we and some passenger of credit assure i that for many year this dog have lie at that gate and every day without intermission watch the first coach that come forth have follow the same to this village Altslow be the bait place at noon and after dinner have return back to Hamburge gate with another coach come from Lubeck for coach pass daily between those city after dinner we pass four mile in four hour space through hill more thick with wood but in many place bear good corn and come to Lubeck for my place in the coach this day I pay twenty lubeck shilling and this night for my supper and bed I pay six lubeck shilling here I buy the fourteen book of Amadis de Gaul in the dutch tongue to practice the same for these book be most eloquent translate into the dutch and fit to teach familiar language and for this book I pay eighteen lubeck shilling and for the bind four and for a map of Europe to guide i in my journey I pay fourteen lubeck shilling also I pay for a measure of rhenish wine five lubeck shilling and as much for a measure of spanish wine from lubcke I pass two mile in three hour space through fruitful hill of corn and some wood of oak to the village Tremuren and pay for my coach the four part of a Doller which notwithstanding use to be hire for five lubeck shilling and for my supper I pay four lubeck shilling I former show that this village be the haven where the great ship use to be unlade and from thence to be carry upto lie at Lubeck in the winter here I take ship to sail into Denmark upon the baltic sea so call because it be compass by the land as it be with a girdle this sea do not at all ebb and flow or very little after it have pass in by the straight of Denmark be more than twenty four mile long so as upon the shore of Prussen Muscaw and Suetia this sea seem little to be move and many time be freeze with ice from the shore far into the sea and the wave thereof once stir with the wind be very high neither be the water of this sea any thing so salt as otherwhere so as the ship sail therein do sink deep at least three span then in the german ocean as manifest appear by the white side of the ship above water when they come out of this sea and enter the say ocean and this will not seem strange to any who have see a egg put into salt pit and how it swim be bear up with the salt water the master of the Lubeck ship in which I pass to Denmark give i beer for four lubeck shilling for which the dutchman and dane drink more large pay but one lubeck shilling more and every man have provide victual for himself I pay for my passage twenty four lubeck shilling and give four to the mariner from Lubeck they reckon twenty four mile to falsterboaden and from thence seven mile to Coppenhagen so call as the haven of merchant we leave upon our lefthand towards the south a little island call munde and as I remember the three day of August land at Drakesholme be one mile from Coppenhagen whether I pass in a wagon through some pasture and barren corn field and near the city I pass over the Coppenhagen haven from one island to another I pay for my wagon three lubeck shilling at our entrance of the city on the east-side be the king castle where the court lie especial in winter time on this side the city lie upon the sea and there be the say haven as likewise on the north-side the sea be little distant from the city when I enter the gate the guard of soldier examine i strict and the common people as if they have never see a stranger before shout at i after a barbarous fashion among which people be many mariner which be common more rude in such occasion and in all conversation the city be of a round form in which or in the king castle I observe no beauty or magnificence the castle be build of freestone in a quadrangle the city be build of timber and clay and it have a fair market place and be reasonable well fortify here I pay for three meal and breakfast eight lubeck shilling and as much for beer the king at this time lie at Roschild purpose short to go into the dukedom of holst where he have appoint a meeting of the gentleman at Flansburge to receive their homage there which upon Roschild old piviledge they have refuse to do unto he in Denmark therefore I go four mile in four hour space through a wild hilly country to Roschild so call of the king fountain and myself and one companion pay twenty lubeck shilling for our wagon and though it be the month of August yet the wind blow strong from the north and from the sea I be very cold as if it have be then winter Roschild have a bishop and though it be not wall have the title of a city but well deserve to be number among fair and pleasant village here they show a whet stone which albrecht king of Suetia send to Margaret queen of Denmark despise she as a woman and in scoff bid she to whet her sword therewith but this queen take the say king prisoner in that war and so hold he till death here I pay seven danish shilling for my supper in the chancel of the church be a monument of black and white stone for this queen Margaret and her daughter and the dane so reverence this queen as they have here to show the apparel she use to wear in this church be the sepulchre of the king whereof one erect by Frederick for christianus his father be of black marble and alabaster curious carve have his statue kneel before a crucifix and hang round about with sixteen black flag and one red have see the king and the courtier myself and my companion next day return to Coppenhagen each of we pay for the waggon ten Lubeck shilling and here I pay for my supper six Lubeck shilling and three for beer from hence I pass by sea four mile in five hour space to elsinure and pay for my passage eight Lubeck shilling and for my supper eight danish shilling and because I be to return hither to take ship for Dantzke I pass the next morning three mile in four hour space through hill of corn but somewhat barren and wood of beech to fredericksburg and have but one companion with i we pay for our waggon thither and so to Coppenhagen each of we twenty two Lubeck fredericksburg shilling here the king have a palace and a little park wall in where among other foreign beast be keep some fallow dear transport hither out of England the twenty four year of queen Elizabeth reign i pay for my dinner four danish shilling and as much for beer in the afternoon we pass five mile in six hour through barren field of corn and grove of beech and hasel-nut to Cappenhagen and by the way we see a cross set up in memory of a waggoner who have drink too much drive his waggon so fast as he overturn it on the side of a hill and himself break his neck the waggoner have a appoint place at Coppenhagen where they have a stable for their horse for two danish shilling a night but themselves buy hay and oat and the next day by noon they must return to their dwelling though they go empty when no passenger be to be find from hence myself and one companion hire a waggon for twelve Lubeck shilling each of we to elsinure be five mile whither we come in five hour fetch many circuit upon the sea coast this be a poor village but much frequent by seafaring man by reason of the straight sea call the sound where the king of Denmark have lay so great imposition elsinure upon ship and good come out of the baltic sea or bring into the same as this sole profit pass all the revenue of his kingdom in this village a strong castle call Croneburg lie upon the mouth of the straight to which on the other side of this narrow sea in the kingdom of Norway another castle be opposite call Elsburg and these castle keep the straight that no ship can pass into the baltic sea or out of it have not first pay these imposition they say there be another passage between two land for all the kingdom of Denmark consist of little land but the same be forbid upon penalty of confiscation of all the good and they report that three ship in a dark fog pass this straight without pay any thing but after this be make know to the king minister at the return of the say ship all their good be confiscate in respect of the dane scrupulous and jealous nature I do with great difficulty put on a merchant habit and give a great reward than the favour deserve obtain to enter Croneburg castle which be build four square and have only one gate on the east side where it lie upon the straight above this gate be a chamber in which the king use to eat and two chamber wherein the king and queen lie apart under the fortification of the castle round about be stable for horse and some room for like purpose on the southside towards the baltic sea be the large road for ship and upon this side be the prison and above it a short gallery on the west side towards the village be the church of the castle and above it a very fair gallery in which the king use to feast at solemn time on the north side be the prospect partly upon the island and partly upon the narrow sea which reach twenty four mile to the german ocean and because great store of ship pass this way in great fleet of a hundred more or less together this prospect be most pleasant to all man but most of all to the king see so many ship whereof not one shall pass without add somewhat to his treasure on this side lie two chamber which be call the king of Scotland his chamber ever since his majesty lodge there when he woo and marry his queen the hang thereof be of red cloch and the chair and stool cover with the same but they say that the rich furniture be lay up in the king absence the haven will receive great number of ship and it have Croneburge castle on the north side the castle of Elsburg on the east side and Zealand the chief island of the kingdom on the west side and the island wheen on the south side to which island the long straight or narrow sea lie opposite towards the north lead into the german ocean this island wheen be a mile long and not altogether so broad have only one grove in it this solitary place king Fredrick father to christianus now reign give to a gentleman call Tugo-Brahe for his dwell who be a famous astronomer live here solitary at this time and be say to have some church live for his maintenance and to live unmarried but keep a concubine of who he have many child and the reason of his so live be think to be this because his nose have be cut off in a quarrel when he study in a vniversitiy of Germany he know himself thereby disable to marry any gentlewoman of his own quality it be also say that the gentleman less respect he for live in that sort and do not acknowledge his son for gentleman king Frederick also give this learned gentleman of his free gift many and very fair astronomical instrument and he live in a pleasant island wherein no man dwell but his family want no pleasure which a contemplative man can desire beside the aforesaid instrument this gentleman have a very fair library full of excellent book and a like fair still-house beside not far from his house he have a little round house of great beauty in which he do exercise his speculation the cover thereof be to be remove at pleasure so as lie with his face upward he may in the night time full behold the star or any of they in this little house all famous astronomer be paint and the follow verse be add each to the picture to which they belong hero o timochare salve God save you worthy old ay greet thou more than many venturous to mount the star and show they unto we Hipparche and thou Hipparchus thou do measure even the course of sun moon and all star of heaven Ptolomee Ptolemy thou to pass old age reach the number and the orb do better teach albategne thou somewhat yet to mend do strive but be prevent by thy end alphonse these get by pain and study thou by gold Alphonsus with such man to be enrol Copernice Copernicus thou old say saw do doubt thou make heaven stand and earth turn round about in the best place this gentleman Tugo brahe have set his own picture with the follow verse with old rule and my own the star I place which aftertime as it deserve shall grace many instrument be there place by he which himself invent and he have make a solemn dedication of the house to the age to come with earnest prayer that they will not pull down this monument the dane think this island wheen to be of such importance as they have a idle fable that a king of England shall offer for the possession of it as much scarlet cloth as will cover the same with a Rose-noble at the corner of each cloth other tell a fable of like credit that it be once sell to a merchant who they scoff when he come to take possession bid he take away the earth he have buy the great revenue exact in this straight have give occasion to these and the like fable and in truth if either the king of Suetia or the free city of Lubeck have the possession of this island and be fortify therein they may easy command this passage and extort what they list from the merchant pass that way and perhaps conquer the part adjoin but the possession thereof be altogether unprofitable for any prince who territory lie out of the sound the entrance whereof be forbid by the two foresay strong castle but lest I shall be as foolish as they I return to my purpose and first give i leave to mention that there lie a city not far distant in the kingdom of Norway which be call London as the chief city in England be call upon Sunday the twenty six of August in the year 1593. I take a english an. 1593. ship here to sail into Prussen have first buy for my victual half a lamb for twelve danish shilling thirty egg for six shilling and some few pot of spanish wine for forty two danish shilling with some other small provision from elsinure to dantzk they reckon eighty english mile assoon as we be come out of the harbour we see two ship sail two contrary way and yet have both a forewind which sometime happen upon the shore as mariner know for of these two contrary wind the one be airy which hold when you be go into the main the other be from the earth and in short time fail at the very shore which event we present see with our eye one of the ship go fair on his course the other cast anchor the english ship in which I go be call the Antilope be of one hundred fifty tun or thereabouts and one master bodley be the master thereof who show i manifest sign where his ship in two place have be strike with lightning the first whereof pass into the pump and rend it but come to the water be by the nature thereof carry upward and come out at the top of the pump make two little hole then pass to the great mast rend it and make a great cranny therein from the hatch to the top the second strike the top of the say main mast and again rend it in such wise as it will scarce bear sail till we may come to dantzk where the best mast be sell at a good rate the first day we sail in the baltic sea some five mile with a scant wind and cast anchor near copenhagen with a fair wind and good gale mariner usual sail some three dutch league in a hour on Monday early we sail along the shore three mile to Falsterboden on Tuesday early we sail eighteen mile to the island Brentholm and upon our left hand see the land in two place and there sound with our plummet sand of amber stick thereunto the same day by noon we sail the length of that island and upon Wednesday by three of the clock in the morning have sail thirty mile we pass by rose-head be a promontory (@) dantzk on thursday by eight of the clock in the morning have sail eighteen mile we come to a land call rettell and enter the port of Meluin where the water be scarce two fathom deep our ship draw one fathom and a half the entry be narrow and there be many booye float upon shoal and sand and the weather be calm we be draw in by a boat with oar in like case ship use to draw themselves in by the cast and weigh of anchor with great labour and flow riddance of way from kettell we pass ten mile and come to the port of Meluin Meluin in the aforesaid entry of the river on the right hand towards the west we see Dantzke seat not far from the sea shore where it have a haven but not so safe as this and towards the north-east in the same place a channel run up to konigsberg the court of the duke of Prussen the port of Meluin be searce ten foot deep but our ship pass through the mud like a plough upon land this port be a little distant from the city on the north-side where we enter by a fair large street call martgasse lie thence towards the south prussen of old be subject to the order of the teutonicke knight but by agreement make between the king of Poland and the margrave or Marques of Brandeburg master of the say order part of the province be give to the say marquis and his heir with title of duke under homage to the king of Poland with condition that for want of heir male it shall return to the kingdom of Poland and the other part be then unite to the say kingdom but Dantzke and Meluin remain free city acknowledge the king of Poland for their protector for which cause they give he many custom and permit his officer to abide in the city and receive the same Meluin be a little and fair city late compass with new wall and at this time grow rich by the english merchant have their staple in the same they give good fare for four a meal and he that pay for two meal in the day may beside have meat or drink between meal at pleasure without pay any thing the same evening we land at Meluin our mariner stay in the ship entertain other english mariner come aboard and according to their custom give they a piece when they depart it happen that the piece be of iron brake and therewith cut the cook off by the middle and rend all the prow of the ship the english merchant at Meluin have no preacher though the citizen give they free exercise of religion so that how so ever they excuse in by reason that learned preacher can hardly be draw to come so far for mean to live yet I think they not free of blame in this point because our merchant further distant in Asia and live under the turk empire find mean by their bounty to have learned preacher neither indeed do I ever observe in any other place Italy except that our merchant want preacher where they hold their staple from Meluin I go ten mile in one day to Dantzke and we be only two comfort Dantzke pay each of we a Doller for our Coach in the morning we go six mile and by the way pass the river beget come out of the river vistula where our coachman pay three grosh to have his coach carry over a dam beyond this river we enter the territory of the king of Poland and pass all this way through fruitful come field and rich meadow and pasture in a country abound with town and village we dine in a village where we two by covenant pay for our coachman spend each of we eigh in the afternoon we pass the rest of the way one mile in the-king territory where we pass another dam of the river vistula and three mile to Dantzke in the territory of the same city the king of Poland at this time be at the port of Dantzke call der mind a english mile from the city expect a wind to sail into his kingdom of Suecin and have with he his queen and many lady and courtier therefore desirous to see the king and the queen with their train I walk the next morning to this port which be bar with a mountain of sand so as the ship must unlade in the road before they can enter this haven neither be any village build there but only one inn in which the king (@) with all his train but beyond the water there be a strong castle of a round son from hence after dinner I return on foot to Dantzke the next day the king have a good wind but before this as those of the romish religion be very superstitious the king and the queen be of the house of Austria while sometime they think Monday sometime Friday to be unlucky day have lose many fair wind the city of Dantzke be a very fair city and howsoever few age past they have not any house build of stone yet at this time many be build of freestone and the rest of brick with great beauty and magnificence be six or seven roof high and they have public garden for sport banquet and exercise which be very pleasant they have a very fair senate-house call Hoff that be the court and the citizen have a strange fashion to put off their hat when they pass by it from the market place be round in which the king of Poland lodge some day to the gate be rich engrave lie a very fair street call longgasse and lead up towards the mountain hang over the city the famous river vistula do not enter the city but pass by it on the east-side and run towards the north fall into the baltic sea but a little brook enter the city on the southside and run through it towards the north. there be a fair water conduit vulgar call wasserkun where by a mill the water be draw up into a cistern from whence they be carry by pipe into all the street and private house beside that many citizen have their private well the aforesaid brook drive many mill among which one for the grind of corn belong to the senate and it have eighteen room and bring into the public treasure every hour a gold gulden and another without any help of hand saw board have a iron wheel which do not only drive the saw but hook in and turn the board to the see the garner for lay up of corn call speiker be very fair and very many lie together in which the citizen lay up corn bring out of Poland and according to the want of Europe carry it into many kingdom and many time relieve fruitful province in time of casual dearth the queen of Poland come in a disguifed habit to see these garner and they have a law that no man may carry fire or a light candle into they in the church call Parkirk the resurrection of our lord be paint with great art and the same again be figure under a globe of glass which kind of paint be here in use this city compass with one wall contain three city govern by three senate out of which one chief senate be gather to govern the whole city and these three city be call Furstat that be the fore city and Alistat that be the old city and Reichstat that be the empire city the whole-city unite lie in length from the south to the north and upon the southside be Furstat where the foresay brook divide the city do enter and there be the aforesaid water conduit and without the wall a fair village or suburb call Scotland in which there be a sanctuary which offender may enter pay a gulden to the bishop and none but artificer and for the most part shoemaker d well in this suburb on this side and towards the east and north without the wall lie plain field which may be drown at pleasure upon the east-side within the wall be the aforesaid garner for corn on the westside without the wall great mountain hang over the city and upon they Stephen king of Poland encamp when he besiege the city which have for defence very high wall on the same side upon the north-side in a corner lie Altstat between which and Furstat on the southside lie the chief city Reichstat in the midst whereof be the aforesaid market place and a public armoury beside that great ordinance be plant upon the wall round about the city I say that from this market place the fair street longgasse lie to the gate between Reichstat and lie the foresay two mill to grind corn and see board both in my opinion very rare the city of Dantzke from the roman superstition have the same saint for protector of their city which England have namely saint George who they carry in their flag and banner and by the way let i remember that the state of Genea in Italy and the island of Chios vulgar Zio in the sea near Constantinople carry also the same saint in their flag at Dantzke I pay a meal and be to pass into Poland where good meat be not in all place to be have I carry some provision in the coach and pay for two hen five grosh and for each measure of wine all kind be of like price I pay ten grosh which measure be call a stoop and be somewhat big than the english guart the nine of September after the old style for the new style be use in Poland I take the nine of September after the old style for the new style be use in Poland I take my journey to Crakaw and we be four consort hire a coach for forty gulden the first day in the morning we pass five mile in five hour space through fruitful hill of corn and only one wood in that part of the dukedom of Prussen which belong unto the king of Poland and come to the city Diersaw by which the river vistula run after dinner we pass three mile through a wood and a fen to the village Zunzane inhabit by hollander who have dry the fen make the field much more fruitful and from thence the same night have pass the river vistula we go half a mile to a city belong to the Sborosky a family of gentleman the second day in the morning we go five mile through a wood and fruitful field of corn to a little city Colmersea where that day be a meeting of the neighbour gentleman if you except Crakaw and the great city the build in these part be poor be of mere dirt in the village and of timber and clay in the better town the house be cover with straw or tile of wood and the gentleman house be far distant one from the other and of no beauty after dinner we go four mile through fruitful field of corn to the city toarn hitherto we have give money to a Hollander one of our consort for the pay of our expense and now by his account each of we have spend three gulden and a half for he have provide wine and such thing which we can not find in village to be carry in our Coach in this city we sup at a ordinary and five of we pay two dollar the river vistula pass by this city and for pass the same by a bridge we pay for our coach two and then enter massovia a province of the kingdom of Poland the three day in the morning we pass three mile through a wood of fir and one mile through corn field and I have omit what I pay for my dinner in the afternoon we pass three mile through a wild plain and wood of oak to Britzoll where I likewise omit my expense the four day in the morning we pass five mile through corn field and wood of oak to Quodonab where I forget my expense I former say that four of we have hire a coach from Dantzke to Crakaw but the horse be but two be extreme weary so as one of our company have a letter to take post horse if so I may call poor jade pay a grosh for each horse every mile which letter be vulgar call podwoda-briefe I be easy induce to leave the coach and bear he company and they tell we that we have now pass more than half the way we agree so as we two who leave the coach shall pay each of we six
