gulden for the same but the Hollander overreach we for we have not go half the way and yet pay more than half the price which we shall have pay for the whole journey this do we two draw upon a sledge pass two mile through corn field that afternoon to a poor village and pay for our passage two the five day in the morning hire a country wagon and two horse at the same rate we pass two mile through field of hop and sandy field of corn and two wood of fir to Lonzchizcha and from thence five mile through wood and sandy field to another village and pay for our sledge half a each mile and for a horse for two mile six grosh and for three mile seven grosh here we invite two polack to dinner yet both together spend only four and a half for we and they for we have ten egg for a and all other victual very cheap after dinner we hire two horse and a country wagon for eight and pass four mile through a stony way and sandy field of corn to Peterkaw where the king have a castle and there we buy for ourselves flesh bread and beer for our supper at a very cheap rate and give one grosh to the hostess for dress our meat and for butter and fire she be very well content with it the six day in the morning we pass five mile and in the afternoon two mile with the same wagon for which we pay seven grosh and the same horse for which we pay fourteen grosh and we pass through wood of high fir tree and some few field of corn our meat we buy ourselves and as former our hostess dress it and we fetch our beer without door I remember we pay three grosh for a goose two for a partridge two for a loin of mutton and three for a pig they sell a bushel of oat for two which at thoarn they sell for six grosh in these part be great store of hop-yard after dinner we pass three mile through woody hill and corn field and pay for our horse and a country waggon twelve grosh the seven day in the morning we pass to a poor village four mile through fruitful hill of corn and many wood of fir and one of oak and we hire our country waggon with two horse for fourteen grosh further we go three mile to another village through hill of corn and a heath full of wood and pay for one horse and a waggon six grosh after dinner we pass to a village three mile through fruitful hill of corn and by the way we may see a stately gentleman house and gentleman hawk in the field and I remember not in all my long travel ever to have meet hawker or hunter in the field but only here and once in Bohemia in this village the king have a castle the eight day in the morning we pass forward with the same horse and waggon which we have in the afternoon before and go two mile to Pnecho and we pay for our horse and waggon fifteen grosh and give one to the waggoner here the king have another fair castle from hence we pass two mile to a village through mountain and corn field and pay for two horse and a waggon four and give to the waggoner procure our horse one grosh the same day we go three mile through little mountain of corn to Crakaw and pay for two horse and a waggon six here we lodge with the Crakaw Fleming consort of our journey and have our diet after the dutch manner and price fortify city be very rare in Poland they place their strength in their sword and horseman rather than in wall of all the city Crakaw be the chief where the king and his council reside it be seat in a plain have mountain on all side but somewhat distant and it be compass with two wall of stone and a dry ditch the building be very fair of free stone four roof high but cover with tile of wood for the most part it be of a round form but somewhat long from the east to the west in the midst of the city be a large market place quadrangular wherein be the cathedral church and in the midst of the market place be the senate house for the city about which be many shop of merchant upon the east side of the city be the king castle seat on a hill be fair and high build almost quadrangular but somewhat more long than broad and lie open on the south side without any build above the wall on the east side be the Chambersof the king and queen with gallery adjoin on the north side be a fair gallery some forty five walk pace long where they use to feast and dance on the west side be the chamber of the queen dowager from whence be private stair to the gate of the castle by which the french king henry the three steal away secret into France on the same side be a chapel in the which the king be bury upon the east side of this city where this castle be seat lie four suburb namely the jew little city and cagman which be divide by the river vistula from the other two call Stradam and the stew and Stradam belong to the city but the rest have their own magistrate and privilege towards the south and south-west lie the suburb Garbatz belong to the city which of late be burn in the civil war by the force of Zamosky one of the palatine and chancellor of the kingdom defend the election of Sigismond now king against Maximilian of Austria choose king by another party on the north side be the suburb biskop and clepart which have their own magistrate from hence be to take my journey for Italy I buy a horse for eighteen gulden and he that sell he according to the manner there use cause his bridle to be put on and so by the same deliver the horse into my hand i pay fifteen grosh for a pair of shoe fifty for a pair of boot nine for spur two gulden and a half for a saddle a gulden and a half for other furniture for my journey nine for stirrup eight grosh for four horse shoe and eight grosh for each bushel of oat a italian gentleman be to return into Italy buy likewise a horse and with this fair companion I take my journey the first day towards evening we ride two mile through fruitful hill of corn to a country house where I pay for my supper two for hay a and a half for a quarter of a bushel of oat two and give to the ostler half a the second day in the morning we ride three mile through wood of fir to a village where I pay for my dinner two for hay half a for the three part of a bushel of oat a and a half after dinner we ride one mile and a half through a great wood have the mountain of Hungary on our left hand and pass the river vistula we ride a mile through fenny field and wood of fir and beech and come to a little city Opsenson where I pay for my supper eight and for some three english pint of wine five for beer a and a half for a three part of a bushel of oat nine and for hay and straw a and a half and this city be subject to a gentleman of Poland the three day in the morning we ride three mile and a half through field somewhat overflow but fruitful in corn and a wood of fir to Plesua subject to the baron of Promnitz and seat in silesia a province of the dutch empire for after one mile ride we come out of Poland into the say province which be subject to the emperor as likewise moravia be by his right as he be king of Bohemia but in silesia they speak dutch and moravia have his own language little differ from that of Bohemia also in our way we pass the river vistula by boat and another arm thereof by a bridge here the baron of Promnitz have a castle wherein they reside and here I pay for my dinner six grosh for beer one for hay and a three part of a bushel of oat two grosh and for a measure of wine somewhat big than the english quart ten grosh after dinner we ride two mile through fruitful field of corn to a little city subject to the emperor not by large subjection but proper right to all the revenue of that territory as he be king of Bohemia and I pay for supper three for a three part of a bushel of oat with hay and straw five the four day we ride two mile in the morning be now enter into moravia where the mile be exceed long as they be in Bohemia and we pass through most fruitful hill of corn and some wood and come to a little village seat a little beyond the city belong to the duke of Tesch and here I pay for my dinner three grosh and as much for my horse-meat after dinner we rodetwo mile through hill and mountain fruitful of corn and some wood of oak for moravia be a pleasant country very fruitful and full of town and village and we come to Ostrenam where I pay for my supper three for beer two for my horse-meat four and a half the five day in the morning we ride three mile through fruitful hill of corn to the village being very pleasant and full of orchard and subject to a gentleman of that country by the way we pass on horseback two arm of the river Odera which have his head three mile distant here we dine with the preacher or minister of the town because the host of the inn be new dead and I pay for my dinner four for beer one and for horse-meat two afterdinner we ride two mile through most fruitful hill of corn to a pleasant village as all moravia be pleasant and fertile and I pay for my supper five and four for my horse meat the six day in the morning we ride three mile through fruitful hill of corn have woody mountain on both hand and in the midst of the way pass by the city Granitz and come to Leipny the city in these part be build with arch half over the street so as in the great rain a man may pass in the street under they with a dry foot and such be the build of this little city where in some thirty family of jew do dwell here I pay for my dinner four and for my horse-meat two grosh hitherto I mean grosh of Poland after dinner we ride a mile and a half in a pave way with corn field on both hand to the city Speron where I pay for my supper five I mean now and hereafter grosh of moravia and for my horse-meat three and here I pay for a orange two in this journey through Poland and from Cracaw to this place we have here the first bed have before lodge upon bench in a warm stove the seven day in the morning we ride two very long mile through most fruitful hill of corn and rich pasture to a village have by the way pass by the city of Creitzon wherein many jew dwell and by very many village and here I pay for my dinner three grosh and for my horse-meat one for a measure of wine like a english pint three creitzer after dinner we ride a mile and a half through most fruitful hill of corn to a place call the jew village beyond the episcopal city Vascon and I pay for my supper two and for my horse-meat three grosh and a half the eight day in the morning we ride two mile go much out of our way and pass through most fruitful hill of corn and pleasant vineyard to Nimsich and I pay for my dinner four for my horse-meat one and a half after dinner we ride half a mile through hill plant with vine to Tracht and I pay for my supper six for my horse-meat two and a half and for beer to wash my horse foot six creitzer the nine day in the morning we ride four mile to a village through hill of corn and in the mid way be the confine of Morania and of Austria into which we now enter here I pay for my dinner fourteen creitzer and three for my horse-meat the vintage be now at hand when we come from our inn in the morning we use to carry bread with we and so to break our fast with bunch of grape gather by the way after dinner we ride two mile through hill and mountain most fruitful of corn and wine to pasdorffe and I pay for my supper eighteen creitzer and nine for my horse-meat the ten day in the morning we ride two mile and a half through hill of corn and many wood to a village not far from vlrich-kirke and here I omit my expense after dinner we ride two mile and a half through a very large plain fruitful of corn and pasture with many pleasant wood and compass round about with mountain and come to Vienna vulgar call Wien near the city on the north side the river Danow run by from the east to the west three arm whereof close Vienna together with some ground between which many time be overflow we pass by three bridge whereof one have twenty nine arch the other fifty seven and the three fifteen each of those arch be some eighteen walk pace long between the second bridge and the three next to the city be a pleasant grove and good part of the ground under the bridge be many time dry but when the river rise it do not only fill the all bed but overflow the field on both side at the gate of Wien each man pay for his horse two pochanel and when we come to the inn the host send our name write to the magistrate Wien the metropolitan city of Austria be a famous fort against the turk upon the confine of Austria which if they shall once gain their horseman may sudden spoil the open country of Bohemia and Morauta and good part of Salesia the city be of a round form and upon the north side there be a ascent to it upon a hill otherwise without the wall on all side the ground be plain except the west side where mountain lie a good distance from the city and upon that side the sultan of the turk encamp upon the hill near the gallows when in the time of the emperor Rodulphua he besiege the city or rather come to view it with purpose to besiege it the next summer the street be narrow but the building be stately of free stone two tower of the church be curious engrave the like whereof be not in Germany except the tower or steeple of Strashurg the common report be that two chief workman have great emulation in build they and that one have finish his tower find mean to break the neck of the other lest his workmanship shall excel that he have do one of the tower some three year past be shake with a earthquake and indeed the house of this city be many time shake therewith and they have a prophecy of old that this city shall be destroy with a earth quake it be dangerous to walk the street in the night for the great number of disorder people which be easy find upon any confine especial where such a army lie near as that of Hungary govern by no strict discipline Ernest as and Mathias archduke of Austria and brother to the emperor do at this time lie here both in one house and do eat at one table and in the time of their meal it be free for stranger and other to come into the room I stay three day a Wien to ease my weary horse and I pay each meal twenty four creitzer for oat the day and night eighteen and in like sort for hay six creitzer from hence we take our journey for Paduoa in Italy and the first day after dinner werode six mile in a plain of vineyard pasture and corn field with some wood to a village where I pay fifteen creitzer for my supper and eight for my horse the next day in the morning we ride four mile through a wild plain by the city and not far thence come to Newkirke where I pay twenty four creitzer for my dinner four for my horse and twelve for a measure of wine like our english quart henceforward we have no more beer but only wine set on the table after dinner we ride three mile through wood and mountain plant with vine and a rich valley of pasture and corn all in a stony soil to Schwatzen I observe that the horse we meet lade with wine have their nose cover which they say be do lest they shall be overcome with the vapour thereof this city be seat between most high mountain in a narrow straight hew out of a rock and shut up with a wall of stone here I pay for my supper twenty creitzer and for drink after supper vulgar schlaffdruncke that be sleep drink six creitzer and for the four part of a bushel of oat nine creitzer which before we have for four creitzer and a half and for hay and straw three creitzer the three day in the morning we ride two mile through woody mountain the ascent of one of they be half a mile and through rich pasture to Mor thus and I pay for my dinner eighteen creitzer and for my horse-meat five creitzer oat be dear here than before this day near spittle we pass out of Austria into styria after dinner we ride two mile through woody mountain yield good pasture to a village where I pay for my supper twenty four creitzer and twelve for my horse-meat the four day in the morning we ride four mile through mountain with pasture and wood and valley of corn to the city brucke where I pay for my dinner fifteen creitzer for my horse-meat five creitzer the three part of a bushel of oat be here sell for twenty four creitzer after dinner we ride in like way two mile to the city Lowen and I pay for my supper fifteen creitzer for three little measure and a half of oat fourteen creitzer for stable three creitzer and four for dregs of wine to wash my horse foot the five day in the morning we ride two mile in like way to a village where I pay nine creitzer for my dinner and four for my horse meat after dinner we ride in the like way and over mountain cover with snow three mile and a half to a village not far from which Charles of gratz archduke of Austria uncle by the father side to the emperor and father to the queen of Poland late marry to king Sigismond be of late bury in a monastery near Knettelfeld in this village I pay fourteen creitzer for my supper and twelve for my horse-meat the six day in the morning we ride one mile in like way to judenburg that be the city of the jew and I pay fourteen creitzer for breakfast then we ride five mile in a stony way through high mountain to newen-markt and I pay eighteen creitzer for my supper and fifteen for my horse-meat in this country of styria many man and woman have great wen hang down their throat by drink the water that run through the mine of metal the seven day in the morning we ride two mile to the confine of styria and enter Carinthia pass by the city freysacke in which be a fair and strong castle seat upon a high mountain and so we pass one mile further to a village all our way have be very troublesome by reason of the stony mountain and narrow passage we have a coach in our company here I pay twenty four creitzer for my dinner and fourteen for my horse-meat after dinner we ride two mile in a plain compass with mountain to sternfield where I pay nineteen creitzer for my supper and sixteen for my horse-meat the eight day in the morning we ride one mile through a fruitful plain of corn to a pleasant city saint Voyte as in styria so here in Carinthia the man and woman have great wen upon their throat with drink the water that pass the mine here I pay for my dinner and supper forty eight creitzer and twenty four for my horse-meat for we stay here to rest our horse and every day we take short journey because we have a coach in our company which can hardly pass the straits and stony way of the Alps and in no other part of the Alps they use at any time to pass with coach but here very seldom in respect of the ill way the nine day we ride three mile through a fruitful plain of corn to feldkirken where I pay nine creitzer for my dinner and four for my horse-meat after dinner we ride about two mile by the side of a lake on our left hand towards the south beyond which lake Boleslaus king of Poland lie bury in a Monasbery who have kill a bishop warn he to amend his life do upon his own free will do penance there take the habit of a monk and serve in the same cloister as a lay brother to warm stove but the polacke say that the body of the dead bishop do many miracle whereupon with great expense of treasure they of late obtain at Rome to have he make a saint and so we come to a village where I pay twenty creitzer for my supper thirteen for my horse-meat and eight for drink after supper the ten day in the morning we ride about a mile through high and rocky mountain to the city villake by which the river draw run and here I omit my expense after dinner we ride three mile through high and rocky mountain and a narrow way and our coachman by the way show we upon the left hand towards the south a castle which of old belong to the governor of the province be now demolish and because money receive of the turk for treason be hide here they say that ever since ill spirit walk in that place in the village of Carinthia be a province of the dutch empire the country people speak Wendish or the tongue of the old vandal which I have like wise hear to be use in village near Angsburg and near Witteberg in Saxony and upon the shore of the baltic sea in Pomern and Meckleburge so as it seem that barbarous nation though scatter and lose their name yet still live in those place so we come to Altaporta that be high gate where I omit my expense the eleven day in the morning we ride a mile through high mountain and rocky and a narrow way to the village Trenise where the dutchman show a passport and we all have a like passport give we from the emperor his officer which we be to deliver to the venetian officer at Pontena leave either for suspicion of infectious sickness or any other cause they shall not permit we to enter into Italy in the say village I pay fifteen creitzer for my dinner and five for my horse-meat after dinner we ride two mile in a stony way between mountain to Pontena which the dutch call Pontafell and by the way there be a wall of stone between the mountain and a village call Chiusa where there be a gate shut up the high way upon which be write in italian L'Alpi Italia l'honer Contarini the enclosure or shut up the Alps close up the confine of famous Italy but can never enclose the honour of the wise Contarini the Contarini be a family of gentleman in Venice here the venetian soldier keep this passage require a benevolence of we which we willing give and out companion pay four venetian lyre for the four horse in their coach but we that be horseman pay no tribute here we have another passport to be show at Venzona I pay at Pontena thirty sol of Venice for my supper thirty five for oat and ten for hey and give i leave to remember that I have for the cold at Dantzke in the begin of September put on a woollen wasecoat be force now at the enter of Italy for great heat in the end of October to put off the same the twelve day in the morning we ride four mile mean dutch mile though we be now enter into Italy because my dutch companion so reckon they we now have enter the italian province frioly which the latin call forum because the legion use to be send from hence over the Alps and the venetian call that be country because the venetian flee from hence into the lake of Venice when attila king of the Hun invade Italy by this name acknowledge it to be their country from whence they original come the seat of the patriarch destroy by attila be of old famous but the venetian by the pope favour have draw the patriarch seat to Venice by the way we pass seven branch of the river Tagliamonti on horseback without boat the stream be so violent by the water fall from the mountain that it dazzle the eye if the passenger look upon the water for which cause we pass wary turn our eye from the water and have guidespass before we to try and show we the Ford by the way upon a bridge this be write in latin for the carry over of dutch merchandise by the stream of Ledra s. s. precedent of the province speedy build this bridge so we come through a plain somewhat wild or less fruitful to Spilenburg where I pay twenty one sol for my dinner eight for oat and four for hay after dinner we ride two dutch mile through wild stony field to sanuocate where I pay thirty sol for my supper thirty two for oat and ten for hay the thirteen day in the morning we ride three dutch or fourteen italian mile through wild ground and stony field of corn and near our journey end by many orchard and vineyard to konian where I omit my expense by the way we meet a gentleman in his coach draw with ox after dinner we ride two dutch or eight italian mile and in a wild field pass two branch of a river by a boat in which we sit on horseback and we pay sixteen sol for our passage and thence we come to a village where I pay forty sol for my supper twenty three for oat and ten for hay and come hither on allsoule evening which they keep with great superstition we can not sleep for little bell tinkle all night the fourteen day in the morning we ride six italian mile through fruitful hill of corn and by pleasant vineyard to Trevigi a city little in circuit but fortify and build of brick with arch hang over the street under which man walk dry in the great rain where I have omit my expense after breakfast we ride twenty two italian mile through a most pleasant plain in which we pass over a river and come to Paduoa here I sell my horse for twenty Paduoa silver crown which I buy at Crakaw for eighteen gulden and by the way I may have sell he for twenty six crown or more and from the place where I sell he may easy have hire a coach or horse to Paduoa but my foolish hope to sell he dear and desire to save the charge of hire a coach or horse keep i from sell he by the way whereof I repent when I come to Paduoa where horse-meat be very dear and the horse-coarsers find that I must needs sell he agree among themselves so crafty send i every day new buyer to offer i less than before they have offer as when I have keep he fourteen day I must have be force to sell my horse at their price if I have not find a english gentleman by chance who return into Germany give i twenty crown for my horse I stay all this winter at Paduoa in which famous university I desire to perfect my italian tongue where a student may have his table at a ordinary vulgar a la and his chamber for eight or at most for ten silver crown the month but few live after this fashion save the dutch and stranger new arrive and have not yet get the language but rather they hire a chamber which be to be have for a sequin or ten lyre the month or at a low rate the hostess be to find linen and dress the meat you buy my host have a large house with a fair court hire yearly for forty crown and with he myself and some dutch man lodge each have his chamber and plentiful diet for eight silver crown the month when I go to Venice I lodge with a hostess a old widow which have a house like a palace for which she pay two hundred crown yearly and there I pay for a chamber four silver crown by the month every man there buy meat at his pleasure which the hostess dress and find linen but that the price of thing may better appear it will not be amiss particular to set some price down for both city for howsoever stranger spend more in Venice then in Padnoa yet that be not by reason of diet but for the great price of chamber and extraordinary enticement to spend the price I will brief set down in some few particular because in the due place treat of diet I be to speak more large thereof it be the fashion of Italy that only man and the master of the family go into the market and buy victual for servant be never send to that purpose much less woman which if they be chaste rather be lock up at home as it be in prison again the small coin of brass be very helpful to the poor all victual be sell in small portion according to the small money yea the very spice which in the shop be put up in paper ready beat according to great or the very lest coin the italian be spare in diet but particular at Padnoa the market abound rather with variety than quantity of meat some hundred of turkey hang out to be sell for six or seven lyre each according to the goodness and this territory yield better corn than other part they have very white bread light and pleasant in taste especial that which be call pan-buffetto I remember I buy a pound of mutton for five sol and a half of veal for eight of pork for eight a fat hen for two lyre eight little bird for six sol a great and fat pigeon for two lyre a pullet for thirty five and sometime forty sol a eel after ten sol the pound krevise the pound three and sometime six sol a pike the pound seven or eight sol round cockle the hundred three sol the long which we call razor the hundred twenty sol the skalop which they call holy cockel twelve for a lyre Cheverns the pound four sol a plaise six sol tench the pound eight sol sausage the pound ten sol six egg eight sol butter the pound fourteen sol piacentine cheese the pound six sol and parmesan the pound ten or twelve sol a measure of salt for the table four sol rice the pound three sol ten snail four sol apple the pound two sol pear and warden the pound four sol chestnut the pound three sol dry grape the pound two sol sometime three almond the pound five sol six orange for one a pomegranate one oil the pound ten sol a secchio of wine thirty five sol or the pound thereof eight sol wax candle the ounce two sol and ten small wax candle twenty two sol other candle the pound sixteen sol or fourteen if they be little a choir of write paper five sol the hostess dress your meat in the bargain for your chamber and find you napkin tableclothe sheet and towel and either in your chest or her own will lay up the meat and very bread you leave more provident than any of our part will require and little boy attend in the market place with basket who for a sol will carry home the meat you buy and dare not deceive you though you go not with they I pay to my tailor for make a cloak four lyre and for my doublet and hose eight light to my laundress for make a shirt a lyre that be twenty sol for wash it two sol and for wash four handkerchief one and this shall suffice for particular expense the city Paduoa be build by Antenor a trojan and the heneti drive out of their country join themselves to these trojan these with joint force drive out the euganei from the fertile euganean hill near Paduoa where Hereules leave they and these heneti give to their posterity the name of venetian to who the colony of Tuscany join themselves than the french subdue all this province till at last they subject themselves to the roman and be make citizen of Rome the roman empire decline the visigothe under alaricus drive the chief citizen of Paduoa into the lake of Venice then attila king of the hun spoil Paduoa and the longobard burn it which be rebuild and flourish under the german emperor Acciolinus usurp the government thereof in the year 1237. but pope Alexander the four help by the venetian restore it to liberty in the year 1257. in the faction of the guelph and gibelline paduoa then and from that time have be subject to many prince of the scaliger and cararrians till about the year 1402. the venetian take the city which they hold to the year 1509. when the french king Lewis make they yield to the emperor but the venetian after two month recover it and to this day it be subject to they who send a magistrate call Podesta every fifteen month to govern it some say Paduoa be first call Antenoria as the heneti give the name of Venice to the country till after Antenor death the heneti call it paduoa of a city in their country whence they be drive other say it have the name from a greek word upon the fly of swan other say it be so call of the river Po call in latin Padus or of the territory lie beyond the Po the river give name to the territory and that to the city before it be destroy by attila it be seat on the east-side of the river medoacus but after it be build on the other side in a fenny soil where now the market place be but since it have be enlarge on both side the water be without the outmost wall seven mile compass and of a triangular form as it seem to i the first angle be on the north-side where be the monastery of the hermit of saint Augustine and the stately palace Areno in which the french king Henry the three be lodge when he return from Poland into France the second angle be towards the east where be the gate at which they take water to pass upon the river brenta to Venice the three angle be towards the south where be the monastery santo call of saint Anthony of Lisbon and the monastery of saint justina and these angle take away the old city be round on the westside upon the wall be build the old palace of the old city Paduoa be seat in a sweet plain have no tree near the city of old the wall be triple and now it be double the inner wall be some three mile in compass and be very high have a walk upon it round about with pleasant shade of tree where gentleman use to play at the balloon this wall compass round with the brent have fourteen gate with as many bridge of stone the river brent likewise compass the outward wall which be about seven mile compass and have six stately gate but this wall be nothing so strong as the other the river athesis divide the territory of Paduoa from that of Uerona and the river Po divide it from that of Ferrara two river of old call Medonci enter the city the great at this day call brenta fall from the Alps with the right hand branch run to Paduoa and with the left hand branch to Rosta and divide again into two branch one by the dirch Brentella be carry to Paduoa the great take the name Bachilio and near to the wall of Paduoa receive the water of Brentella increase with a branch of brenta these river enter the city and with diverse channel drive many mill compass the wall and not only make the field fertile but serve to carry all commodity abound here from hence to Venice and to bring from thence such thing as they want and beside do cleanse all filth of the stable and privy the air at Paduoa be very healthful and the building be with arch of stone hang over the street under which they walk dry in the great rain but the street be thereby make narrow and in the midst be dirty there be five market place in the first the gentleman and student meet and walk in the second herb be sell in the three corn in the four wood and in the five straw the aforesaid monastery of saint Anthony be inhabit by franciscan friar and be much fair than any other religious house the church whereof be of old dedicate to juno and after to the virgin Mary and at last to saint Anthony the pavement thereof be of marble and the build very stately have in the top seven globe cover with lead and three high tower the chapel wherein St Anthony lie be all of marble and round about it the miracle be engrave which they attribute to this saint at who feast day they use to present for great gift the hallow girdle of this St which they tie about their loin and attribute strange effect thereunto here be a statue of marble erect to Peter Bembus and in the large yard there be a horseman statue of brass which the senate of Venice erect to Gatta Melata in the church of franciscan minorite there be a statue erect to Roctha Benello a physician sit in his chair in the aforesaid monastery of saint justina the order of saint Benedict be first establish and from thence disperse into Italy and the church thereof be of old dedicate to concord and after be make the bishop church be endow with great rent these monk have a black habit and in the church they show the relic of the martyr saint justina of saint a greek who be say to have be saint Peter disciple and to have convert Paduoa and to have baptize saint justina when she suffer martyrdom and likewise of saint Maximus both bishop and protect saint of the city as also of saint Luke the evangelist bring by Vrius a monk from Constantinople but the venetian say the relic of saint Luke be with they Biondus write that here be a church dedicate to jupiter and the sepulchre of Titus Livius in the first court yard of this monastery the incredible miracle of saint Benedict be paint in the second I find this epitaph proper as d. m. young man though thou hast this stone desire thou to behold it then to read that be write here be lay the bone of the poet Pacunius this I will have thou know farewell d. m. a large and pleasant meadow lie before this monastery there be another of the benedictine in this city but those friar wear a white habit and live with more severe rule in the monastery of saint Augustine hermit before name be the sepulchre of the prince of the family Carraria the cathedral church be of old magnifical and to this day have twelve church under it within the city the marble chest contain autenors bone be find when the foundation of the almshouse be dig be then bring to the church of saint Laurence wherein be find a guild sword and latin verse in a barbarous style show that the letter a shall be fatal to the city which they say have prove to true by and Albertus under who the city be much afflict this chest be erect upon marble pillar at the door of the church and upon the wall these verse be write in latin famous Autenor Troy wall pull down henet and dardan remnant here do train expel the euganean build fair Padnoa town who this low marble house do here contain another epitaph of the same Antenor seem late write by the very name of the city and savour a transalpine wit give small credit to Livy or their fabulous antiquity Antenor padoaes founder lie here he be a traitor these he follow near the monument of the trojan horse of wood be keep in the palace of the capilist family whereupon they be call the capilist of the horse there be eighteen cloister of nun in the city and two of repent or illuminate woman so they call whore enter cloister about the midst of the city be a fair palace where the venetian Podesta or governor dwell the gallery whereof in which he sit to judge cause be very large and have a high arch roof hang by art not sustain by many pillar and the same be cover with lead and adorn with many picture of the famous painter Zoto and the length thereof be one hundred forty walk pace the breadth forty three pace there be the statue of julius Paulus doctor of civil law and of Peter Aponensis or and of Titus Livius and of the Hermitan place over the four door at the west end of this gallery be a monument of Titus Livius the historian carve within the wall and these verse be write upon the wall in latin at that paetria and great Livy thy country man have lay thy head and bone here with a ready mind thy country and Rome thou have famous make here bear while their great act thou have refine thy country give thou this if more it may here all in gold thou shall stand shine bright this Titus Livius die in the four year of the empire of Tiberim Caesar and in the sixty six year of his age not far from this monument stand a brazen image of the same Livy with this inscription in latin the bone of Titus Livy of Paduoa by all mortal man consent worthy by who pen true invincible the act of the invincible roman people shall be write beside they show in the city Titus Livius his house and this monument bone of he be bring thither from the monastery of saint justina the court where the senate meet lie near to the say gallery of this palace where there be astone which they call the stone of turpitude that be filthiness or disgrace whereupon debtor which disclaim the have of good to pay their debt do sit with their hinder part bare that with this note of disgrace other may be terrify from borrow more than they can pay they have a pest-house call Lazaretto and two like house for leper and one alsmes-house for the poor stranger another for orphan and a three for child cast out or leave in the street near the church of saint luria there be a well call the devil well which they say be bring into the street by art magic out of the court-yard of a gentleman deny water to his neighbour this city have little traffic though it lie very fit for the same because the venetian draw it all to themselves but gentleman of all nation come thither in great number by reason of the famous university which the emperor Frederick the second be offend with the city of Bologna plant herein the year 1222 or there about some come to study the civil law other the mathemeticke and music other to ride to practice the art of fence and the exercise of dance and activity under mostskilful professor of those art draw hither by the same reason and student have here great if not to great liberty and privilege so as men-slaiers be only punish with banishment which be a great mischief and make stranger live there in great jealousy of treason to be practise against their life the school where the professor of liberal science teach be seat over against saint Martin church and be of old a public inn have the sign of a axe which name it still retain the promotion of degree be take in the bishop hall near the cathedral church and the doctor be make in the chief church and there be eight college build for poor student of several province the athestine family of the duke of Ferrara and the honorian family of the tyrant Acciolinus and the carrarian family of their own prince have their beginning in this city as they write and they do no less triumph of diverse citizen bear here namely Marsilius a minorite who be a divine write learned in the year 1329 of the power of the pope and emperor defend the majesty of the emperor against the pope and julius Paulus a civil lawyer disciple to papinius and live in the time of Titus Livius and Francis Zabarella a civil lawyer die in the year 1417 and Peter a physician and ginger die in the year 1312 who body be to be burn for suspicion of his be a conjurer his concubine bury but his picture and his book be burn by the emperor command and Michael savonarola a physician and Titus Livius a famous historian and Lucius aruntius Stella and Caius Valerius Flaccus all poet celebrate by marshal and a stage-player of wonderful elocution die in the year 1542 and Thraseas Peto a famous soldier hate by Nero and Andreas Mantinia the best painter of his age be knight and die about the year 1517 and augustinus Zoto a painter with other famous man the second book chap. i. of my journey from Paduoa to Venice to ferraria to Bologna to Ravenna and by the shore of the adriaticke sea to Ancona then cross the breadth of Italy to Rome seat not far from the tirrhene Sea whosoever come into Italy and from whence soever but more especial if he come from suspect place as Constantinople never free from the plague he must bring to the confine a certificate of his health and in time of any plague he must bring the like to any city within land where he be to pass which certificate bring from place to place and necessary to be carry they curious observe and read this paper be vulgar call Bolletino and if any man want it he be shut up in the lazareto or pest-house forty day till it appear he be healthful and this they call vulgar far' neither will the officer of health in any case dispense with he but there he shall have convevientlodging and diet at his pleasure in the spring of the year 1594 the italian begin the year the first of january an. 1594. I begin my journey to see Italy and take boat at the east gate of Paduoa the same be draw by horse along the river brenta and have shoot two or three small bridge and pass twenty mile we come to the village Lizzafusina where there be a dam to stop the water of brenta lest in process of time the passage be open the marsh on that side of Venice shall be fill with sand or earth and so a passage make on firm ground to the city which they be careful to prevent and not without just cause have find safety in their isle when Italy be often overflow by barbarous people beside they say that this dam be make lest this fresh water shall be mingle with their salt water since all the gentleman of Venice fetch their fresh water by boat from thence the poor sort be content with well water here while our boat be draw by a instrument out of the river brenta into the marsh of Venice we the passenger refresh ourselves with meat and wine and according to the custom agree upon the price of our meat before we do eat it then we enter our boat again and pass five mile to Venice upon the marsh thereof and each man pay for his passage a lyre or twenty sol and for a horse more than ordinary that we may be draw more swift from Paduoa to Lizzafusina each man pay four sol but the ordinary passage be only sixteen sol we may have have coach but since a boat pass daily too and fro between these city most man use this passage as most convenient for the boat be cover with arch hatch and there be very pleasant company so a man beware to give no offence for otherwise the lumbards carry shirt of male and be arm as if they be in a camp be apt to revenge upon shameful advantage but common there be pleasant discourse and the proverb say that the boat shall be drown when it carry neither monk nor student nor courtesan they love they too well to call they whore the passenger be for the most part of these kind I remember a young maid in the boat cross herself whensoever a old woman look upon she fear she shall be a witch whereat the passenger often smile see the girl not only cross herself for fear but thrust her crucifix towards the old woman eye I say former that two Rivers Medoaci run through Paduoa and that the great by the name of brenta run to the village Lizzafusina be stop with a dam lest it shall mingle itself with the salt marsh of Venice and that also the lesser river by the name of Bachilio pass through Paduoa this lesser stream run thence into the ditch clodia and go out of it make a haven call the Chiozza which lie in the way from Venice to Farraria and there it divide itself into two stream and enter the salt marsh make the haven of Venice call Malamocco beside other river fall from the Alps through Frioli do increase these marsh which be salt by the tide of the sea though the same do very little ebb or flow in this mediterranean or inland sea and this haven Malamocco be very large and deep and be defend with a bank from the wave of the adriatic sea the description of Venice upon the west side of venice beyond the marsh lie the territory of Paduoa on the north side beyond the marsh lie the province Frioli on the south side beyond the marsh lie partly the firm land of Italy and partly the adriatic sea on the east side beyond the marsh lie the adriatic sea and the city consist all of isle compass round about with the say marsh a the great channel b the market place of saint mark seat in the first sextary of saint mark c the cathedral church of saint Peter the seat of the patriarch seat in the second sextary call Caestelli Olivolo d the three sextary on this side the channel call di Canarigio e the church of saint james lie near the bridge Rialto and be seat in the four sextary of saint Paul be the first of they beyond the channel the rest of the city be divide into two other sextary beyond the channel namely the five di Sta Croce and the six f the church of saint George the great g and the church of Santa Maria gratie both lie in the sextary Croce h the island Giudecca belong to the sextary di k the bank of the sea vulgar lido l the island Murana m the new Lazaretto n mazorbo w Chioza 10 Malamocco the haven within the sea bank a island z San' georgiod ' Alega in the way as we come from Paduoa to Venice a little island (...) a little island the henetian of Paphlagonia their king Palemon be dead at the siege of Troy join themselves to antener and possess these part after they have drive out the euganean call the country Venice and through their great virtue be make citizen of Rome and their chief man senator thereof but when attila king of the Hun invade Italy and the empire of the west be weaken do destroy the same the say Henetians come out of histria now call Frioli and from the territory of Paduoa and other italian come from adjoin part into certain land compass with marsh that they may be safe from those barbarian and about the year of our lord 421 begin to build a city which prove a safe retreat from the tyranny then continual oppress Italy in process of time by civil art grow incredible these land be in number sixty near adjoin and twelve more distant which be all join in one have make this stately city and the chief of they be call in the vulgar tongue Rialto Grado Heraclca and Caestello Olivolo the island Grado be of old the seat of the patriarch after that the Patriarchate of in histria be by the pope authority translate thither but now the seat of the patriarch be remove to Caestello Olivolo at first consul govern the city than tribune choose out of each i will one till the year 697 when the citizen abide in heraclea choose they a duke who dwell in the same i will after forty year they choose a tribune of soldier in stead of a duke with like authority as he have and at last in the year 742 meet in the island Malamocco they choose a duke again and remove his seat from heraclea to that island then Pipin reign in Italy about the year 800 the venetian demolish heraclea which be build again but never recover the old dignity be more notable in the seat of the bishop then in the number of citizen for most of the gentleman remove their dwelling into the island Rialto otherwise call Rivo either of the depth of the marsh or because it be high than the other land and thereupon call Ripa whereupon that island get more dignity than the rest the citizen in process of time join the sixty land lie near one to the other with some four hundred bridge of which island as be above say and of the twelve more distant this stately city consist then by common counsel the seat of the duke be establish in this island who build the stately palace which at this day we see and now a new dukedom arise out of these salt marsh of the sea from that time daily grow in dignity but the city be first call Kialto and after of the country from whence the citizen come be call Venetia or in the plural number Venetiae because many dukedom and province or many nation be join in one and at this day be vulgar call Venegia that the city be first call Rialto appear by old record of notary write in these word after the use of Venice in the name of eternal God amen subscribe in such a year of Rivoalto and in these word after the use of the empire in the name of Christ amen subscribe date at Venice this stately city build in the bottom of the gulf of the adriatic sea in the midst of marsh upon many land be defend on the east side against the sea by a bank of earth which have five or some say seven mouth or passage into the sea and be vulgar call lido and be so place by nature not make by art bend like a bow and reach thirty five mile and by the aforesaid passage the ship and the tide of the sea go in and out and the deep marsh whereof I have speak be make of these salt water and of diverse fresh water fall from the Alps and vulgar call ill Tagliamonts livenza la praac brenta Po adice and ill Bacchiglione on the west side the city be compass with marsh and after five mile with the territory of Paduoa on the north side with marsh and beyond they partly with the province Frioli partly with the aforesaid sea bank and upon the south side with many land wherein be many church and monastery like so many fort and beyond they with the firm land of Italy the city be eight mile in circuit and have seventy parish wherein each church have a little market place for the most part four square and a public well for the common sort use well water and rain water keep in cistern but the gentleman fetch their water by boat from the land it have thirty one cloister of monk and twenty eight of nun beside chapel and almshouse channel of water pass through this city consist of many land join with Bridges as the blood pass through the vein of man body so that a man may pass to what place he will both by land and water the great channel be in length about one thousand three hundred pace and in breadth forty pace and have only one bridge call Rialto and the passage be very pleasant by this channel be adorn on both side with stately palace and that man may pass speedy beside this bridge there be thirteen place call Traghetti where boat attend call gondola which be of incredible number give ready passage to all man the rest of the channel run through less street be more narrow and in they many bridge be to be pass under the aforesaid boat be very neat and cover all save the end with black cloth so as the passenger may go unseen and unknown and not be annoy at all with the sun wind or rain and these boat be ready at call any minute of the day or night and if a stranger know not the way he shall not need to ask it for if he will follow the press of people he shall be sure to be bring to the market place of saint mark or that of Rialto the street be very narrow which they pave with brick and beside if he only know his host name take a boat he shall be safe bring thither at any time of the night almost all the house have two gate one towards the street the other towards the water or at least the bank of the channel be so near as the passage by water be as easy as by land the public boat with the private of gentleman and citizen be some eight hundred or as other say a thousand though the flood or ebb of the salt water be small yet with that motion it carry away the filth of the city beside that by the multitude of fire and the situation open to all wind the air be make very wholesome whereof the venetian brag that it agree with all stranger complexion by a secret virtue whether they be bring up in a good or ill air and preserve they in their former health and though I dare not say that the venetian live long yet except they soon grow old and rather seem then true be age I never in any place observe more old man or so many senator venerable for their grey hair and age gravity to conclude the situation of Venice be such as the citizen abound with all commodity of sea and land and be not only most safe from their enemy on the land be sever from it by water and on the sea be hedge in with a strong sea bank but also give joyful rest under their power to their subject on land though expose to the assault of their enemy the city part in the midst with the great channel come in from the sea bank near the two castle be of old divide into six sextary or six part vulgar three on this side the channel and three beyond the channel the first sextary on this side the channel be that of saint mark for howsoever it be not the cathedral church yet it be prefer before the rest as well because the duke reside there as especial because saint mark be the protect saint of that ciry the body of which saint be bring hither by merchant from Alexandria this church be build in the year 829. at the charge of the duke justinian who die give by his last will great treasure to that use and charge his brother to finish the building which be lay upon the ruin of saint theodore church who former have be the protect saint of the city and the same be consume with fire in the year 976. it be more stately rebuild according to the narrowness of the place the merchant be charge to bring from all place any precious thing they can find fit to adorn the same whatsoever it cost the length of the church contain two hundred foot of Venice the breadth fifty the circuit 950. the building be become admirable for the singular art of the builder and painter and the most rare piece of marble Porphry Ophites stone so call of speckle like a serpent and like stone and they cease not still to build it as if it be unfinished lest the revenue give by the last will of dead man to that use shall return to their heir as the common report go there be stair of old to mount out of the market place into the church till the water of the channel increase they be force to raise the height of the market place on the side towards the market place be five door of brass whereof that in the midst be fair and the same with one more be daily open the other three be shut except the day of feast upon the ground near the great door be a stone paint as if it be engrave which paint be vulgar call ala Mosaica and upon this stone pope Alexander set his foot upon the neck of the emperor Frederick Barbarossa adore he after his submission the outward part of the church be adorn with 148. pillar of marble whereof some be Ophytes that be speckle and eight of they be Porphry near the great door which be high esteem and in all place about the church there be some six hundred pillar of marble beside some three hundred in the cave under ground above these pillar on the outside of the church be a open gallery bear up with like pillar from whence the venetian at time of feast behold any show in the market place and above this gallery and over the great door of the church be four horse of brass guild over very notable for antiquity and beauty and they be so set as if at the first step they will leap into the market place they be say to be make to the similitude of the horse of Phoebus draw the chariot of the sun and to have be put upon the triumphal ark of Nero by the people of Rome when he have overcome the parthian but other say that they be give to Nero by Tiridates the king of Armenia and be make by the hand of the famous engraver Lisippus these horse Constantine remove from Rome to Constantinople and that city be sack the venetian bring they to Venice but they take of the bridle for a sign that their city have never be conquer but enjoy virgin liberty and all the part of these horse be most like the one to the other yet by strange art both in posture of motion and otherwise they be most unlike one to the other above this gallery the image of saint mark of marble and like image of the other evangelist of the virgin Mary and of the angel Gabriel be place and there be a bell upon which the hour be sound for the church have his clock though another very fair clock in the market place be very near it the roof in form of a globe lie open at the very top where the light come in for the church have no window and the Papist church be common dark to cause a religious horror or to make their candle show better this be more dark than the rest I pass over the image of saint mark of brass in the form of a lion guild over and hold a book of brass likewise the artificial image of the doctor of the church and other I will pass over the image of the virgin Mary paint Mosaica that be as if it be engrave but that they attribute great miracle to it so as woman desirous to know the state of their absent friend place a wax candle burn in the open air before the image and believe that if their friend be alive it can be put out with any force of wind but if he be dead that the least breath of wind put it out or rather of itself it go out and beside for that I will mention that those who be adjudge to death offer wax candle to this image and as they pass by fall adore the same to conclude I will not omit mention thereof because all ship come into the haven use to salute this image and that of saint mark with piece of ordinance as well and more than the duke a merchant of Venice save from shipwreck by the light of a candle in a dark night give by his last will to this image that his heir for ever shall find a wax candle to burn before the same above the say gallery be little chamber in which they lie up piece of stone and glass with other material for the foresay paint Mosaica which be like to engrave and painter have pension from the state do there exercise that art high esteem in Italy the outward roof be divide into four globe cover with lead touch the inside of the church in the very porch thereof be the image of saint mark paint with wonderful art and the image of Christ crucify of he bury and of the four evangelist high esteem beside many other much commend for the say paint like engrave and for other workmanship and there be erect four great pillar of Ophites which they say be bring from the temple of Solomon at the entry of the door be a old and great sepulchre in which lie the duke Marine not far thence be the image of saint Geminian in pontifical habit and another of saint Katherine both paint with great art when you enter the body of the church there be the great altar under which lie saint mark in a chest of brass deck with image of silver guild and with plate of gold and image enamel and with the image of Christ sit upon a stately throne adorn with pillar of most white marble and many precious stone and curious engrave at the back of this altar there be another which they call the altar of the most holy sacrament make of the best marble with a little door of brass deck with carve image and with four pillar of alabaster transparent as crystal and high esteem and upon the same hang every day two lamp of copper but at the time of feast there hang two of pure silver moreover the organ be say to be the work of a most skilful artificer in the high gallery compass the church be the image of pope Pelagius under which be a place where the holy relic be keep which pope Clement the eight give to John Delphin knight one of the procurator of saint mark and ambassador at Rome for Venice namely a piece of a bone of Philip the apostle a piece of the cheekebone and four tooth of the martyr saint piece of bone of saint Bartholomew and saint Thomas forsooth of Canterbury and of the apostle saint Matthew and saint mark who body they say be lay in the foresay chest and part of the hair of the bless virgin and a piece of a finger of the evangelist Luke and a piece of a rib of saint Peter with many like which they show to the people to be adore certain day in the year above the altar of saint Clement these verse be write which show how they worship image in a more modest though superstitious age that which the image show be God itself be none see this but God here see in mind adore alone likewise these verse of the same author be in another place as thou Christ image pass fall the same before yet what this image signify not it adore no reason that it shall be God who essence stand material of stone formal of workman hand this image which thou see be neither god nor man but who it represent he be both God and man. at the entry of the chancel be the throne of the duke make of walnut-tree all carve above the head and when the duke sit there it be wont to be cover with carnation satin but now it be cover with cloth of gold give by the king of persia. there be two stately pulpit of marble with history carve in brass where they sing the epistle and gospel on the left hand by the altar of saint james be a place where if a man may believe it the body of saint mark by a crevice sudden break through the marble stone appear in the year 1094. to certain priest who have fast and pray to find the same the memory of the place where it be lay at the build of the church about 829. be utter lose I believe that the memory thereof be lose about the year 829. when superstition be not yet ripe but that it be find in the year 1094. that age be infect with gross superstition let he that list believe they themselves seem to distrust this miracle while they confess that the same body be most secret lay under the great altar and never since show to any man but once or twice and that after a suspicious manner to the foresay pulpit another be opposite where the musician sing at solemn feast and from whence the duke new create be show to the people and likewise the holy relic as they term they be show twice in the year the wall in the church be so cover with the best marble as the lime and brick can be see and these piece of marble with their spot and brightness be very beautiful whereof two be hold for admirable monument which be so join as they lively represent the image of a man here Marino first of all the duke hang his arm upon the wall who the other duke after he in number forty three have follow and there hang up their arm in the midst of the church hang a banner give by the citizen of Verona in token of subjection and two other for the same purpose give by the citizen of and Cremona the marble pillar setin cave under the church bear up the pavement which be make of piece of the best marble carve and wrought with little stone of chequer work very curious especial under the middle globe of the roof and near the great door and among the rare stone opposite to the singer pulpit they show one of such natural spot as it be esteem a jewel which by change of colour they say do show the change of weather moreover they show certain image carve by the direction of the abbot John joachim of old time whereof many show future event as that of two cock carry a wolf upon their back which they understand to be Lewis the twelve and Charles the eight french king cast Lodovico Sfortia out of his dukedom and in like sort to omit many other more hide that of the lion fat in the water and lean upon land which they understand to be the power of Venice by sea and the weakness by land beside they say the same abbot cause the image of saint Dominicke and saint Francis to be draw upon the door of the sanctuary long before they live and the title of saint be add to each of they but the name be not set upon the picture yet they both be paint in the habit of their order they show two like picture draw by direction of the say abbot whereof they understand one to be the last pope under who shall be one shepherd and one foul but they say it be unknown what the other signify before the new chapel of the bless virgin there be two little chamber whereof one be call the sanctuary in which their holy relic be keep the other be call the jewel house because the treasure of saint mark so they appropriate all public thing to saint mark be there keep and it be vulgar call Luogo groie that be the place of the jewel the procurator of saint mark keep this treasure and make no difficulty to show it to stranger of the better sort in this place I see the ducal cap vulgar ill corn or Beretta ducale which the duke wear at their creation be of inestimable value for the multitude and price of the jewel especial of a diamond upon the crown of the cap and a chrysolite sct in the midst I see two crown of king with twelve stomacher of pure gold set with rich jewel which the noblewoman wear at Constantinople before the turk take it and twelve other crown all of pure massy gold all which the venetian divide with the french have for their part when they take Constantinople in the year 1203. I see a saphire of extraordinary bigness and a diamond which the french king Henry the three give to this state when he return that way from Poland and two whole unicorn horn each more than four foot long and a three short and a little dish of a huge price with innumerable vessel which for price rareness and workmanship be high value they say that a candian thief take away this treasure which be keep with many door and bar of iron but that he restore it be betray by his fellow in a chapel of this church be a font of brass with a brazen image of saint John baptize and the altar thereof be of a stone bring out of Asia upon which they say Christ do sit when he preach at Tyrus but other say it be the stone upon which the patriarch jacob do sleep they show there the chair of the bless virgin of stone and two piece of marble spot with the blood of John Baptist and the marble sepulchre of duke Andrea Dandoli in the chapel of the cardinal zeno they show the rock strike by Moses and distil water and two precious piece of porphyry in the upper vestry they show the picture of the virgin paint by saint Luke hand and the ring of saint mark and his gospel write with his own hand and a piece of the cross of Christ and of the pillar to which he be tie and book cover with massy silver and candlestick chalice and many vessel of silver guild all set with little precious stone and the bishop mitre of great price and many rich vesture for the priest the chief priest of this church must be a gentleman of Venice and though he be no bishop yet the pope have give he great privilege and he be to be choose by the duke because the duke build this church whereupon it be ever since call the duke chapel this church of saint mark be not unworthy call the golden church for the rich ornament thereof especial for the image thereof paint like a work engrave for the workman do incorporate gold with little square piece of glass and guild the same over then break they in very small piece they lay they upon the picture among the parish church belong to saint mark be the chapel of saint Theodore where the inquisitor of religion sit thrice a week namely the pope nuntio and the patriarch a inquisitor by his place and at this time a dominican Friar and three senator choose by the senate likewise the little but most fair church of saint Germinian be seat in the market place of saint mark who priest according to the custom of Venice be choose by they that have unmoveable good in the parish and be confirm by the patriarch in which church the most notable thing be three image grave upon the great altar and the sepulchre of John Peter Stella great chancellor and the altar of Lodonito Spinello and the monument of james and Francis Sansovine famous engravers in the church of saint Mary Zebenigo the monument of Sebastian a philosopher and of Jerome Molini a florentine poet and the picture of the lord supper in the church saint Vitale the artificial statue of that saint on horseback in the church saint Angelo build by the family of the Morosmi the altar of the holy sacrament in the church of saint Fantino the architecture and among other image the head of a crucifix and the singular image of the bless virgin and saint John paint stand by the crosse. in the vestry of saint Fantino who monk use to accompany and comfort those that be execute the two altar and in the first of they the brazen image of the bless virgin and saint John and in the second the excellent marble image of saint Jerome in the parish church of saint Luke seat in the midst of the city amonument of four most learned man and another of Peter Aretine call the scourge of prince be the most remarkable thing the inquisitor worthy condemn the book of this Aretine for the filthiness of they howsoever they be yet common sell and the common report be that they also command his horrible epitaph to be blot out which be set in this church of saint Luke in these word gaice l'Aretin' Tusco mal' d'ogniun' four a che be nol' here lie the Aretine a poet of Tuscany who speak ill of all but of God excuse himself say I know he not of the same Aretine say Ariosto in the stately church of saint the marble image of saint jerem another of he and a three of saint Laurence and the great altar of pure silver be curious engrave and in the chapel of the holy sacrament the image of Mary Magdalen and in another chapel the image of saint Augustine pray among his monk and not far off two image of the monument erect to duke Francis Venerio all paint with great art and the altar of the bless virgn equal or to be prefer to the best in the city the altar of St Antony and two monument of duke all adorn with rare engrave and paint image and a fair pair of organ in the church of saint Bartholomew the picture of manna fall from heaven and the brazen image of Christ of the four evangelist ' and six angel in the church of saint giuliano many picture but especial that of Christ carry his cross and near the door another of saint Jerome and two marble image upon the altar in the church of saint Stephen rich with marble and pillar the marble image of the apostle with the pillar whereon they stand and the altar engrave with brass and the monument of james Suriani and another of Anthony Cornari with this inscription at the ash of Anthony passenger stand this be that Cornarius who of old teach the principle of nature and the god Antenor Athens be wont to admire after call home to his country grace with title and magistracy with his learning he make Venice happy these thing I say be in these church most remarkable the second sextary on this side the channel vulgar i will sestiero castello have the name of the castle Olivolo which seat towards the sea may seem to be divide from the city yet it be join thereto by a long bridge of old it be a city by itself and therefore the duke throne be establish in the island Rialto the bishop seat be make here who be invest by the duke and be consecrate by the patriarch of Grado till that be extinguish this be raise to the dignity of a patriarch in the year 1450. in the cathedral church of saint Peter this be write upon the chapel in latin who ere thou be that approach worship within these grate of iron the cross be enclose that be adorn with three hair of the beard of Christ with a nail the cup in which he drink to his disciple and with a piece of the true cross etc. this Patriarcall seat have two old pulpit of marble the monument of the bishop and patriarch which with the adjoin palace of the patriarch be the most remarkable thing thereof in the church of John Baptist in Bragola many curious picture the sepulchre of that saint guild over the image of Christ the picture of the less altar especial that of Christ baptize that of saint Helen that of Christ resurrection and the lively picture of Christ sit with his apostle at his last supper in the church of saint Mary this inscription be read vincentius Capellus most skilful in navigation and perfect of the galley noless praise of old who receive sign of honour from Henry the seven king of Brittany etc. there upon the great and very fair altar the image of the four evangelist and upon the top that of Christ resurrection and of two angel in the church saint Marina the statue on horseback erect by the senate to tadeo volpe of Imola and the great altar with the pillar of prophry in the church of saint leone the image of saint Jerome of Christ at supper with his disciple of John the evangelist and saint Michael all paint by the hand of most skilful workman in the church of saint Anthony four most fair altar in the second whereof the image of Christ and in the three rich with excellent pillar the history of ten thousand martyr paint and in the four the espousal of the bless virgin areal paint with singular art and a foot statue erect by the senate to victor pisanus in the church of saint Dominicke the library and picture of the altar in the church of saint Francis paola many thing give upon vow and hang upon the wall in that of saint Francis a very fair and stately church the altar of the chapel belong to the family Grimani and the picture and brazen image of the same and in 1571. also three horseman statue one to Leonardo de Prato knight of Rhodes another to nichola Orsino count of Pitiglia both erect in the church the three for great honour erect in the market place to Bartholmeo Coleoni of Bergamo for his good service to the state in their war all three erect by the senate also a foot statue erect by the senate to Deunys naldo a most valiant commander of their foot whereas the grave image of this church be of rare beauty they say that the chief of they be bring out of England after the death of queen Mary in the church of saint a parish church and yet the chief cloister of nun twice rebuild by the family two curious statue of marble of paros in the church of the holy sepulchre be a cloister of nun the sepulchre of Christ like that at jerusalem of ophite and like stone in saint Zachary a cloister of nun the pall of the virgin paint another like it in the chapel the sepulchre or altar under which the say saint father to John Baptist be lay and at the back of the great altar three sepulchre of Porphry and ophyt the stone of the great altar and the stately architecture of the church be the thing most remarkable and the same cloister have great revenue in general understand that the church be for the most part build of brick and some few of free stone though they be so cover with marble and like stone as the brick or free stone be scarce see in the inside in the priory of St John belong of old to the templary knight and now to the knight of Rhodes or Malta it be remarkable that the revenue thereof be great and that the priory be give by the pope which Paul the three give to the cardinal saint Angelo his nephew for so they call their bastard who Alexander the cardinal of Farness succeed yet not as cardinal but as knight of Malta and after he the pope give it to the cardinal Ascanio Colonna and the most remarkable thing in the church be the pall of the great altar the supper of our lord paint the picture of Christ speak with the woman of Samaria and that of Herod banquet when he give John baptist head to Herodia the greek church belong to this sextary build in Rio San' Lorenzo the almshouse saint Lazero feed four hundred or five hundred poor people for all that beg be send thither and they have many of these house these be the most remarkable thing in the church of this sextary the three sextary or six part of the city on this side the channel mean towards the gulf of Venice vulgar be call Ilsestiero Canaregio of the cane or pipe which they be wont to use in the build of ship in the church of the prophet jeremy build which I will set down leave any shall think incredible the like practice of papist against emperor and John the king of England in these word to jerom Savina a citizen of Venice prior of saint mary notable learn in good art but more renown for piety which he also show at his death towards his enemy who give he poison in the chalice at the lord supper by many argument of his charity he die in the year mdci also in the great school the same be witness in these word to jerom Savina wicked kill by poison give o horrible villainy in our lord supper etc. the four sextary or six part of the city and first of those beyond the channel mean towards the territory of Paduoa be vulgar call of the chief church San' polo in which church of saint Paul the most remarkable thing be these the picture of and the apostle upon pillar in the very fair market place of the same church of old a market be weekly hold and to the year 1292 the market be hold here on Wednesday and in the market place of saint mark on the Saturday but at this day none be hold here but both in the place of saint mark for the benefit of those that dwell there and that the house may be more dear let which belong to saint mark near the church of saint Siluestro the patriarch of Grado dwell till the bishop of castello Olivolo be make patriarch in the church of saint james of Rialto narrow but very fair the precious stone and the picture of great art and antiquity and the five altar in the church of saint Mary fair and great the belfry stately build the monument of the most famous painter Titiano two image of marble near of the great altar the epitaph of Francis Bernardo who be employ into England in his young year make peace between king Henry and the french king Francis which many great man have attempt in vain and for this brave act be knight by both the king these thing in this church be most remarkable the five sextary and the second beyond the channel of the chief church be call ill Sestiero Croce in which of the resurrection of Samson and especial of Christ sup with his apostle in the monastery of saint Helen give name to the island and found by Alexander Boromeo and be one of the fair in the city a cross of inestimable value in the church of saint Andrew Certosa the monument of Austin who hearten the confederate to fight be chief cause of the victory against the turk by sea in the year 1571. and while he live by fair and rough term keep the league unbroken which present upon his death be dissolve in the church of saint Nicholas deal liro the sepulchre of duke Dominicke Contarini rich with porphyry and ophyte stone and a well of fresh water lie very near the sea and have so full a spring as it serve all the ship and galley the almshouse of saint Lazerus be build for leper the old lazereto be a pest-house where the prior and physician have yearly fee to attend the sick not far from that be the new lazareto whither they be send who be suspect to have the plague but as soon as they begin to be sick they be send thence to the old lazareto and hither all suspect man be send to try their health which if they keep for forty day than they be set free these thing be in this sextary most remarkable the six sextary and the three and last beyond the channel be of the form of the island call in the church of the saint and the grave image and picture in the chapel of the holy sacrament in the cloister of saint Agnes the prioress bring up six virgin which be of ripe year be either marry or make nun and six more of good family send thither in their place in the church of
