saint Gregory there be a second monument erect to Anthony Bragadini traitorous slay by the turk at the take of Cyprus the island Giudecca belong to this sextary the chief church whereof be saint Eufemia it have nine other church the church of the jesuit be call saint Mary of humbleness and it have picture with great art the pal of the passion of Christ of the apostle Peter and Paul of Christ circumcise and of saint Francis and the great altar be one of the fair in the city in the church a singular pair of organ the image of the bless virgin four evangelist and Christ crucify and upon the altar of Christ crucify two stone shine like crystal which be esteem for jewel in the church of saint Mary of charity the rich chapel of San Saluadore in the most fair church of the Capuchin friar seat in the island Giudecca the image of brass and the fair screen of the great altar in the most fair church of saint Mary the great be a nunnery the rare picture of the great chapel in the church of the holy cross della Giudecca the monument of the cardinal Francis send ambassador to the Turk and nuncio to pope sixtus the five in the french court and here the rest of his family use to be bury the monastery of the convert be for whore repent another be build for orphan virgin the church whereof have rich screen of marble with brass image and in the same live some two hundred and fifty virgin of alm and by the work of their hand which come to ripe year be either marry or make nun these thing be in this sextary most remarkable the venetian have six fraternity or great school such as be also at Rome and the gentleman and citizen all give their name to one of they as in England at London the citizen have company into which the king queen and noble many time vouchsafe to be admit and in these school as it be in university they use to have exercise of religion the first of they be call saint Mary of charity after the rule whereof the rest be frame and the great guardian thereof be choose yearly and wear a scarlet gown with large sleeve which they call ducal sleeve and he have the title of Magnifito by privilege these school give dowry yearly to 1500. virgin and distribute among the poor much money meal and clothes for beside many gift by last testament daily give to those use each of the school have some five or six thousand ducat in yearly revenue and they be govern like common wealth in the say school the image of the apostle and the picture especial one of the bless virgin and another of the four doctor of the church be very fair in the school of saint John the evangelist the passion of Christ be wonderful figure and Philip the second king of Spain and his son Ferdinand and don John of Austria and other prince have be of this fraternity the three be of mercy the four of saint mark the five of saint Rocco pass the rest in ceremony and pomp and number of brother the six be of saint Theodore and each of these have his church and palace and precious monument and these be subject to the counsel of ten for there be many less school each art have his school and these be subject to the old justice and out of they when need be soldier be press it remain to add something of the magnifical build of this city and in the first place the market place of saint Mark be pave with brick and it consist of four market place join in one whereof two may rather be call the market place of the duke palace join to the church of saint mark the one be on the further side from saint mark between the palace and the great channel the other right before the palace towards the channel four hundred foot in length and some one hundred and thirty in breadth the three be before the church door of saint Mark and lie in length five hundred and twenty foot towards the church of saint Geminiano and have one hundred and thirty foot in breadth which may more proper be call the market place of saint mark the four be on the other side of the church towards the church of saint basso in this market place of four join in one be solemn spectacle or show and all procession make and there on Ascention day be the fair hold and the market on wednesday and Saturday there they use to muster soldier and there the gentleman and stranger daily meet and walk before the door of saint mark church be three piece of brass carve and for bigness like the body of tree upon which at festival day three rich banner be hang in sign of liberty or as other say for the three dominion of Venice Cypro and Candia under the tower of the clock fifty foot distant from saint mark church be a passage to and from this market place and this tower all cover with marble bear a remarkable clock which show the course of the sun and the moon daily and the degree they pass and when they enter into a new sign of the zodiac and above that the guild image of our lady shine place between two door out of one of which door only at solemn feast a angel with a trumpet and the three wise man of the east follow pass before our lady image and adore she and so go in at the other door above that there be a carve image of a lion with wing and upon the very top two brazen image call the moor which by turn strike with a hammer upon a great bell sound the hour the house opposite to the palace of the procurator of saint mark be call the house of the state and they belong to the church of saint mark and have some fifty shop under the arch of the upper roof where man may walk dry when it rain they yield great rent to the church opposite to these be the palace of the say procurator which be also in the say market place which I say to be more proper call the market place of saint mark and these be stately build sixty six foot high and the stone curious carve do not only adorn the market place but in summer give a pleasant shade to passenger beside that under the arch of they man may walk dry in the great rain and the shop under these arch yield great rent and under these palace out of four little street there be so many passage to and from the market place these palace be build at the charge of the state the nine procurator be to have nine palace for as yet they be not all build but in the mean time any palace fall void it be give to the elder of they that have none yet not according to their age but according to their election the steeple or belfry of saint mark distant some eighty foot from the church and set over against it be to be admire not only for the foundation strange lay under the earth but also for many other cause it be build four square each square contain forty foot and it be three hundred thirty three foot high of which foot the pinnacle contain ninety six and the wooden image of a angel above the pinnacle cover with brass and guild and turn with the wind contain sixteen foot it be adorn with high pillar of marble and with a gallery at the bottom of the pinnacle make with many pillar of brass and upon the pinnacle with great marble image of lion and from the top in a clear day man may see a hundred mile off the ship under sail and it bear four great bell whereof the great call la Trottiera be ring every day at noon and when the gentleman meet in senate with like occasion but when a new pope or duke be make all the bell be ring and the steeple be set round about with wax candle burn I go to the top of this steeple which have thirty seven ascent whereof each have fourteen less ascent by which the go up be as easy as if a man walk on plain ground at the contrive whereof I much wonder in the lodge of this steeple the four brazen image of Pallas Apollo Mercury and of peace and above they the figure of Venice with the dominion by sea and land and the image of Venus the goddess of Cyprus and of jupiter the king of Candia present themselves and near the great gate the image of the bless virgin and of saint John Baptist be high value right over against the duke palace in the foresay second market place of the palace be the library who building be remarkable and the architecture of the corner next the market place of the baker be hold by great artist a rare work and diverse carve image of heathen god and goddess in the old habit be no less praise as do by the hand of most skilful workman on the inside the arch roof curious paint and the little study of ivory with pillar of alabaster and rare stone and carve image in which a old breniary of write hand and much esteem be keep be thing very remarkable the inner chamber be call the study in which many statue and half statue twelve head of emperor and other thing give to the state by cardinal Dominicke Grimani be esteem precious by all antiquary and in this library be lay up the book which the patriarch and cardinal Bessarione give to saint mark that be to the state by his last will and the most rare book bring from Constantinople at the take thereof and otherwise gather from all part of Greece out of this library be a passage to the chamber of the procurator of saint mark before you enter they most fair statue and on the inside rare picture draw your eye to they not far from thence be two pillar the three whereof in take they out of the ship fall into the sea and can never be recover and they be of huge bigness for the erect whereof as a most difficult thing great reward be give to a Lombard and immunity be give to he by privilege for all that shall play at die under they since it be accustom that all condemn man be execute between these pillar which of old be put to death near the church of saint John Bragola and upon one of these pillar stand the brazen statue of saint mark under the form of a lion and upon the other stand the marble statue of saint theodor. the statue of saint George bear a shield in token that Venice rather defend itself then offend other since the right hand carry a defensive weapon behind the library be the mint house vulgar call la whereupon I think the gold coin of the venetian be call Zecchino in which house it be remarkable that there be no wood in any part thereof but for fear of fire it be all build with stone brick and bar of iron here the great statue of giant lift up their massy club as it be forbid the entrance and in the court yard the statue of Apollo hold wedge of gold in his hand to show that gold be make to grow in the bowel of the earth by the virtue of the sun be thing remarkable from hence on the leave hand be the market place which I say to be the first of the palace seat between the channel the palace and from hence on the right hand be the fish market in which as likewise in that of Rialto store of good fish be to be buy twice in the day the market place in which the say belfry and library be build be also adorn with the stately palace of the duke all cover with marble and most sumptuous in the carve image and picture and in the pillar of the arch walk on the outside the first stair towards the second market place of the palace and over against the say library be very stately and be vulgar call scala that be the stair of the giant so call of two huge marble statue of Mars and Neptune which the common people call giant but the palace have many other stair whereby man ascend thereunto opposite to the aforesaid statue be two other of Adam and Eve but not so great as they and not far from thence be a stone guild with a inscription which the senate place there in memory of the french king Henry the three who they entertain pass that way from Poland into France on the left hand be the chapel of saint Nicholas which be the duke private chapel hence you ascend into a large hall as they call it or a large gallery in the midst whereof the golden staite shine with gold and two marble image and rare picture on the left hand of the say stair be the passage to that part of the palace which be assign to the duke for his dwell and in the first chamber call the duke arm Sala the picture of Christ resurrection and another of he crucify be much praise though it have many other rare picture when you have ascend the golden stair your shall see four rare picture from thence the way on the left hand lead to the chancery where many chamber be adjoin proper to diverse counsel of state all adorn with grave image and picture of the best namely the chamber of the council rich in the paint of the arch roof that of the Pregadi have general rare paint and carve that call ja in which the secret write of the state be lay up the chapel of the college where the duke and the senator daily hear mass and it appear by a inscription that the antiquity be of old lay up there among the picture whereof that of Christ resurrection and the map of the territory of Venlce be much praise that of the council of Tenn in which the picture of the wiseman offer gift to Christ be much praise near the same be chamber in which many rich armour and rare monument be lay up and that of the great council one hundred fifty foot in length and seventy four in breadth adorn round about with rare picture namely on the side towards the foresay second market place of the palace the history of Frederlcke the emperor and of pope Alexander the three be cursory paint towards the foresay first market place lie between the palace and the channel the history of Constantinople take by the venetian and french be paint and the capitulation of the voyage make in the church and the rest of the saint in heaven be repute rare work the prison of old be under this palace of the duke but late a new house be stately build of the stone of ay for that use near the bridge della Paglia the four square market place of Rialto be compass with public house under the arch whereof and in the middle part lie open the merchant meet and there be also a peculiar place where the gentleman meet before noon as they meet in the place of saint mark towards evening and here to nourish acquaintance they spend a hour in discourse and because they use not to make feast one to another they keep this meeting as strict as merchant lest their frinship shall decay the goldsmith shop lie thereby and over against they the shop of jeweller in which art the venetian be excellent there be the palace of a gentleman who prove a traitor the state for his reproach turn the same into a shambles and some upper chamber to place of judgement the fish market lie by this shambles a great length along the bank of the great channel and in the same shambles and fish market as also in the like of saint mark great plenty of victual especial of fish be daily to be sold. a public palace stately build lie near the bridge of Rialto this bridge in the judgement of the venetian deserve to be repute the eight miracle of the world the old be pull down this new bridge begin to be build in the year 1588. and be scarce finish in three year and be say to have cost two hundred fifty thousand ducat it be build of the stone of istria upon one arch over the great channel and the ascent to the top have thirty six stair on each side and upon each side of these stair be twelve little shop cover with lead not to speak of the carve image of the bless virgin the angel Gabriel and the two protect saint of the city namely saint mark and saint Theodore thereby be a palace call Todeschi because the dutch merchant have it to their use the armoury build for all kind of arm and munition vulgar call Arsenale as it be the tower of the senate be compass with wall be in circuit more than two mile where some four hundred artificer be daily set on work about naval provision and they receive weekly for wage about one thousand two hundred ducat within the same be a several place to make cable and within the circuit hereof and no where else in the city they build ship and galley and there be always in the same about two hundred galley ready for service to conclude the state of Venice be not grow to full strength do in a hundred day space arm one hundred galley against Emanuel emperor of the east and no doubt their strength have every day grow great to this time in the say compass of the armoury lie a great boat call because it carry about the number of two hundred which boat have upon it a kind of chamber which use to be rich hang and cover over when in the same the duke and senator be carry by water at some time of solemnity especial at the feast of the ascension when of a old custom they go forth to espouse the sea by the ceremony of fling a ring into the same and to challenge the command thereof give they by pope Alexander the three the jew have a place to dwell in several call Ghetto where each family have a little house and all have one court-yard common so as they live as it be in a college or almshouse and may not come forth after the gate be lock at night and in the day they be bind to wear a yellow cap. though the city be seat upon little and narrow land in the midst of marsh and tide of the sea yet have it garden in great number and abound with rare herb plant and fruit and water conduit which with thecarued image and picture out of the gentleman courtesy may be see by any curious stranger the public library of special note be these s Giovanni and Paolo di San' Francisco di San' stefano di San' Georgio Maggiore and Sant' Antonio also private library may be find out by those that be curious and will be after the same manner easy show they and be indeed most worthy to be seek out for the rareness of many instrument picture carve image antiquity and like rare thing for the venetian being most spare in diet and apparel do exercise their magnisicence in these and the like delight and these precious monument they will with great courtesy show to any stranger or to any love antiquity which my self-found by experience morespecial at the hand of Sigr. Nicolao a gentleman dwell in the island give who most courteous show i and my friend though be altogether unknown to he some rare clock admirable carve image and a pair of organ have strange variety of sound the palace of gentleman be call house but be and worthy deserve to be call palace some hundred of they be fit to receive prince for howsoever this commonwealth at the first sound be tie by many law to mediocrity and the equality among the citizen yet pride have by degree seize upon the same the say palace have one door towards the land and another towards the water and most of they have garden the foundation be lay of oak in the water and the stone of istria be much esteem the flooer of the upper room be not board but plastre with lime temper with tile beat to dust the window be for the most part very large the great room lie almost altogether open to receive air but the lodging chamber have glass window whereof the venetian brag glass be rare in Italy where the window be for the most part cover with linen or paper and howsoever glass be common with we on this side the Alps yet it be certain that the glass maker of Venice dwell in the island murano have a more noble matter and thereof make much better glass than we can to conclude as I say the venctian be most spare in diet and apparel so not only in the build of their house but in the furniture thereof the general sort pass their degree and many of the gentleman use princely magnificence these be account the chief palace that belong to the procurators office near the church of saint Anthony the old palace which belong to the templary knight that of the family Gritti near the bridge della madonna that of Alexander Gritti near the market place of saint John bragora that of Dandoli near the bridge della Paglia the palace near saint Francis church which the senate buy and use to assign it to the dwell of the pope nuncio that of the duchess of Florence build upon the channel of the duke palace that of the Vetturi near the market place of saint Mary that of the patriarch Grimani near the Malipieri that of the family near the same that of Francis Priuli that of Lodowick that of the Capelli that of Peter that of those of Pesaro near the church of St. Benedict that of the loredani near saint Stephen church that of Zeni that of Contarini that of Silvester near the church of saint job. that of the Cornari near saint Paul church that of james Foscarini near the church that of the Michaeli near saint Lewis church that of Lewis Theophili near the church della the chief palace upon the channel be these that of the loredani that of the Grimani near saint Lucia that of delphini that of the Cornari near saint Maurice church and that of the Foscarini a old build but have the best prospect of all the rest in which the venetian entertain the french king Henry the three to conclude there be two rich palace in the island Giudecca one of the Dandoli the other of the in this famous city be twenty thousand family and three thousand of the gentleman and no age have be so barren which have not yield worthy man for marshal and civil government and learning of this city have be three pope Gregory the twelve Eugenius the four and Paul the second and many cardinal of which these be the chief Peter mark I Anthony John Amideus and in our age John Baptist Zeni and dominick Grimani also Peter Bembus be a venetian who pope Paul the three make cardinal here be bear Pantalean justinianus patriarch of Consiantinople when the french rule there and Venice have yield many most learned man Andrew Dandoli duke Francis Barbarigi Andrew who write the history of his time in heroic verse and many famous civil lawyer Lodowick Foscarini and jerom Donati and many rare engravers and painter Titiano Tenterotto and Belino and many commander in the war John Marino dominick the first provisor of military affair Andrew and Simion Dandoli and many more famous in all kind of virtue to the chief whereof I have say that the senate erect many statue and monument give i leave to add this of the family namely that among the most famous man who picture be in the chamber of public meeting before it be burn there be the picture of Barbaro and Marco and Autonia and that the same family have give three duke Dominico Marino and Michaele and three patriarch and twelve procurator of saint mark which number few family have attain only that of the Contarini that of the and that of the Grimani have a little pass it and that myself be at Venice find there eighty gentleman of this name let the reader pardon this observation which I make for the confonancy of that name with my own only differ in the place of a vowel for more gentle pronunciation which the italian speech affect yet these gentleman be of one family write their name somewhat diversity some writing in their own tongue other Moresini and in the latin tongue and Maurocenus of the hire of chamber and the manner of diet in Venice I have speak joint with that of Paduoa in the description of that city only I will add that this city abound with good fish which be twice each day to be sell in two market of saint mark and Rialto and that it spend weekly five hundred ox and two hundred and fifty calf beside great number of young goat hen and many kind of bird beside that it abound with sea bird whereof the venetian writer make two hundred kind and likewise abound with savoury fruit and many salt and dry dainty and with all manner of victual in such sort as they impart they to other city I will also add that here be great concourse of all nation as well for the pleasure the city yield as for the free conversation and especial for the commodity of traffic that in no place be to be find in one market place such variety of apparel language and manner that in the public inn a chamber may be hire for four sol a day but for the cheapness and good dress of meat most man use to hire private chamber and dress their own meat that in the dutch inn each man pay two lyre a meal that no stranger may lie in the city more than a night without leave of the magistrate appoint for that purpose but the next day tell they some pretend cause of your come to the town they will easy grant you leave to stay long and after that you shall be no more trouble how long soever you stay only your host after certain day give they account of you to conclude this most noble city as well for the situation free they from enemy as for the freedom of the commonwealth preserve from the first sound and for the freedom which the citizen and very stranger have to enjoy their good and dispose of they and for manifold other cause be worthy call in latin Venetia as it be that be come again from Venice to Farraria be eighty five mile by water and land and upon the three of February after the new style and in the year 1594. as the italian begin the year anno. 1594. the first of january and upon Wednesday in the evening myself with two dutchman my consort in this journey go into the bark which weekly pass betwixt Venice and Ferrara the same night we pass twenty five mile upon the marsh within the sea bank to Chioza or or to speak vulgar the better to be understand in ask the way a Chioza the first village on firm land or rather seat in a island where the ditch clodia make a haven the next morning in the same bark we enter the river and pass fifteen mile to the village and after dinner ten mile in the territory of Venice and eight mile in the dukedom of Fervaria to Popaci and upon Friday in the morning twenty two mile to Francoline where we pay for our passage from Venice thither each man three lyre and a half by the way on land upon both side the river we pass a pleasant plain and field of come divide by furrow in which furrow elm be plant and upon they vine grow up to the top such be the manure of Lombardy or the low part of Italy towards the west where the vine grow high yield not so rich wine as in the other part of Italy upon mountain and hill upon the side whereof the vine support with short stake and grow not high yield much rich wine by this way our bark stay many time in village where we have time to eat or to provide victual to be carry with we and we have a Ingistar or measure of wine something great than our pint for three sol of Venice we buy bread after the weight for they have loaf of all price in which a stranger can be deceive it be the fashion to see the meat in the kitchen and to agree of the price before you eat it which if you do not you shall be subject to the host insatiable avarice who take pleasure to deceive stranger and the price of the meat you may understand by the italian who you shall see buy of the same and if the decrenesse displease you you may carry dry fig and raisin and dine with they the price of bread and wine be certain but you must sup at your host ordinary if you will have a bed I say that we leave our bark at Francoline where we may have hire a coach to ferraria for which we three shall have pay twenty two bolinei but the way be pleasant to walk we choose rather to go these five mile on foot ferraria be a very strong city of Fiaminia and near the city the river Po divide itself have make a long and broad island which now be grow to firm land ferraria it be compass with wall by the exarch of the eastern emperor lord thereof and after it be subject to the bishop of Raucnna than it come into the hand of the prince of the family of Este the lawful heir whereof possess it to the year 1394 when it pass to the line of bastard these prince of Este be at first marquess and afterward be create duke and Hercules of Este be the three duke who live about the year 1550. at this day the family of Este be extinct the bishop of Rome have invade this dukedom the city seat in a plain be compass with a fenny bank and be of a triangular form the three corner be towards the north west and south on the south side the river Po do run of old but it have now leave his bed which be dry up to firm land but the less branch thereof run from Francoline to Chioza where it fall into the sea the great make many lake at Comatio yield the duke much profit by the fish of eel in the heart of the city be a large market place and join thereunto a little island in which the father of Hercules of Este build a stately palace call Belueder and in the market place before the door of the palace there be a statue sit in a chair erect to duke Burso and another of a horseman and of brass erect to duke Nicholas the street be broad and very dirty in winter and no less subject to dust in summer the house be build of freestone but according to the build of Italy be almost flat upon the top so as that upper roof have neither chamber nor window the house be not build one near the other but divide with most pleasant garden and disperse on the north side of the city without the wall the duke have a large park for hunt and to keep therein many strange beast there be two stately palace beside the duke one of the Bentivoli the other of Caesar nephew to duke Alfonso who be eighty year old beget he of a concubine and because the pope have intrude themselves into the succession of this dukedom which this holy mother the church daily gape to enjoy upon the want of lawful heir male the duke can not obtain by entreaty or any money to have the right of succession convey to this nephew and howsoever he be now eighty year old yet the opinion of his command in miltary affair make the common people report that the pope will grant the succession to his nephew upon condition he shall lead a army into Hungary against the turk but the event show that the pope think nothing less for this Caesar defend his possession fearful the pope have since that time and long before I write this invade this dukedom and unite it to the patrimony of saint Peter the circuit of the city be say to contain seven mile and in the same be a university little frequent and a fair college wherein the professor read the duke have two stable in the one one hundred twenty horse for coach in the other fifty for the saddle in the beneventane monastery be the sepulchre of the poet Ariosto bear in this city and it be of red marble with this inscription in latin to Lodowick Ariosto poet a patrician of ferraria Augustine Musicus to so great a man and so well deserve of he have cause this monument and image of marble to be erect at his own prote cost in the year of the lord m d 73 Alfonso the second be duke he live 59 year and die in the year of the lord m d 33 the eight of the ides of june the statue of marble be to the shoulder and be set over his tomb with those verse sale here Ariosto lie who pen still feast the civil ear on stage with comic jest who sayter scourge the foul sin of his time who sing the frantic worthy in sweet rhyme great duke fierce battle and their pensive care thus have one poet three crown to his share greek poet latin tuscans each scarce one of these attain he have all three alone in the monastery Certosa there be a round pinnacle the monument of duke Borso in the church of saint Mary of the angel be lay up some trophy of victory against the venetian which when a citizen of Ferrara show upon a time to a venetian in fashion of brag he answer pleasant and witty to my remembrance when you of Ferrara get this victory against we we take the country of Poleseno from you and though we be overcome yet we keep that to this day jerom Saucnorolla a friar be bear in this city who in a late age be of great fame and authority among the florentine and for some opinion of religion be burn by the pope here I pay thirty bolinei for a meal in the chief inn where we be well use when in base inn we have pay more with vile usage from hence they reckon thirty four mile to Bologna we go on foot three mile to the village torre and in the midst of the way we observe the old bed of the river Po which be now dry up from hence we hire a boat for four and four quatrine and pass in a broad ditch between high reed to a place call Mal' Albergo that be the ill lodging be nine mile and we understand there that four soldier be drown the day before in the say ditch by their own folly play and tumble in the boat we have now pass seven mile in the state of Bologno and lodging here each man pay for his supper sixteen the next morning a boat go from hence to Bologna but since they ask for each man passage twenty two bolinei and that the day be fair and the way very pleasant we choose to go on foot these eighteen mile to Bologna in the midway we come to a country inn where they demand excessive price for meat we for spare in the begin of our long journey and loath to be make a prey out of their opinion of our gluttony take bread and wine of they at the know price and dine with some provision we have with we namely one pound of raisin for which we have pay seven bolinei a pound of fig at the same price and a pound of almond at the same price buy at ferraria to this purpose after this refection we go the rest of our journey through pleasant field manure after the Lombard fashion before describe when we enter the gate of Bologna the soldier demand a courtesy of we which we glad give they perceive they will not search our portmanteau Bologna which otherwise by their office they may do this be a city of flaminia of old subject to the Exarchate of Ravenna till the eastern emperor be cast out of Italy by the conspire of the pope with the king of Lombardy and so the Exarchate be unite to that kingdom and short after the pope likewise conspire with the french king Charles the great against the king of Lombardy and divide Italy between they this city fall to the pope share howsoever they do not then attain the possession thereof or at least do not keep it long for afterward the city be subject to many tyrant sometime under the viscount of Milano and at last invade by the citizen thereof namely the family of Bentivoli under pretence to defend the common liberty till the pope about the time of the french king Lewis the twelve conspire with he to invade Italy do cast out the Bentivoli and by little and little reduce the citizen to obedience and use they to the church government do at last set over they his Podesta and a cardinal legate both stranger to govern they this populous city be of a round form and of great circuit but the wall be round about almost fall down the city be seat in a large plain and such be the whole territory only on the southside without the wall lie the apennine mountain which divide Italy almost in the midst all the length thereof even from Genoa to the further limit of the kingdom of Naples border upon he sea towards the east on the same southside be the school of the university and the monastery of saint Michael and the rich stately monastery of saint Dominicke in which be the sepulchre of the say saint curious engrave and of white marble and under a rich screen lie the body which they superstitious worship and they show the place where the saint give up his last breath their refectory or place where the monk eat be fair and large and the cellar of wine and their store thereof be so great as will better become the temple of Bacchus than a cloister of monk it have two fouresquare court yard with arch cloister to walk under and they be pleasant plant round about with cedar of which they especial esteem one plant by the hand of that saint who likewise with his own hand do beautify a well of water no less esteem by they their public library be much esteem for many book of write hand wherein they brag to have a bible write by the hand of esdras the building of this monastery be very stately and it have large gallery as well below under the arch as round about in the upperroofe here be a monument of Hans that be John son to the emperor Frederick the second and they have a place give by privilege to the dutch for burial the building of the city be antic and many house seem to have be build by the Lombard the foundation of the house be of free stone and the rest for the most part of brick build with arch cloister towards the street under which they walk dry in the great rain the palace of gentleman be build towards the street stately on the inside but with little show on the outside and they all seem to have be build of old the window be not glaze which the venetian brag to be proper to their city as a thing to be wonder at but they be cover with paper whereof part be oil over towards the west side of the city be a large market place two fork in which be a fair conduit of water with the image of Neptune and diverse goddess pour water out of their mouth and breast and all make of mettle in this market place be the senate-house vulgar call on the one side whereof be the court of judgement on the other the lodging of the governor at the very entry be a statue of brass erect to pope Gregory the thirteen a citizen of Bologna which appear by a inscription in the cathedral church and within the palace be a statue of white stone erect to pope Paul the three and another statue of a giant the stair of the palace be make wind and rise by little and little give so easy a ascent as a horse may go up without difficulty the like staite be at Ferrara in the duke palace and at Venice in the steeple of saint mark and at Torge a city of Germany within the palace be the statue of julius the second bishop or rather the god Mars of Rome engrave to his shoulder with aleane and longface upon the door of the palace be write in golden letter that the emperor Charles the five hold his court there when the pope put the imperial crown upon his head in the church of saint Petronius which church be of the old Lombard building and this saint be the protect saint of the city near the stately cathedral church of saint Peter be a house call the mountame of piety where poor man may borrow money free bring pawn to avoid the oppression of the Jew usury among the Lombard building there be a old tower call Asinelli build of brick with four hundred seventy two stair which they esteem one of the high in Europe from this tower without the gate all the field be full of palace and house at the gate of saint Francis be a pinnacle with this inscription the sepuich of who write the gloss upon the law son to Francis H we in the territory of this city be a medicinal water find in the year 1375. very famous throughout all Italy of which be proverbial say Chibene Porretta o i lo lo that be he that drink the water of Porretta either it burst he or else it cleanse he the stranger student here call the stately palace of cardinal Caup the sin of the dutch as build by the fine impose on they we stay in this city two day and be three consort hire a chamber each man for four bolinei the day the hostess give we linen and dress our meat and we pay for a eel by the pound five bolinei for they sell fresh water fish by the pound for a pike the pound four bolinei for three apple two quatrine for a pound of four for a pound of small nut four bolinei for a ingestar of wine a measure somewhat big than the english pint four bolinei for a wax candle six it be now the time of Lent and so we be force to eat only fish as the italian do in the territory of Bologna there be a place almost a island call Forcelli which be a island of old and historian witness that the Augustus Antonius and Lepidus here divide the world between they from Bologna the right way for Rome be direct to Florence which way I never pass dispose as I think my journey more commodious yet for the direction of other passenger it will not be amiss to set down the way from Bologna to Pianore be eight mile to Lograno six to Scaricalasino five to Caurez three to Fiorenzuola twelve to Scarperia ten to the bridge saint Piero two and to Fiorenza or two mile at Bologna we chance to light upon post horse be to return twenty mile to Imola and each of we pay five for his horse by the way towards the south be pleasant hill towards the foot of the apennine mountain on the left hand towards the north be field manure after the Lombard fashion before describe and we pass by the castle saint Petro the italian call the wall town which have no bishop seat by the name of castle when we enter the gate of Imola according to the custom we deliver our sword to a boy who be to carry they to our host that he may keep they till we go out of the town and here each of we pay two real for our supper and half a real for our bed the next morning we ride ten mile to the city Faenza through a sandy way and a barren soil yield some few vine grow upon tree and each man pay for his horse eight poli. from hence our right way to Ancona have be to Forli Cesena and rimini but our desire to see the old famous city of Ravenna make we go out of the way twenty Ravenna mile to the say city through a dirty way and fruitful field of corn and each of we pay seven for his horse Ravenna be a most ancient city who wall the emperor Tiberius either build or repair here of old be the harbour for the navy of Rome here the emperor of the east after the western empire be extinguish make the seat of his exarch after by the conspire of the pope and the french king Pipin and Charles the great all the city of this Exarchate fall to the pope share yet other write that the french king only add Tuseany to the patrimony of saint Peter and it be most certain that these city for long time do not acknowledge the pope for their lord till at last the pope in like sort conspire with the french king Lewis the twelve and Charles the eight have their aid to subdue these city and then pope julius the second by terror of his excommunication extort Ravenna and other city from the venetian and cast out the lord of other city the pope from that time be very skilful to fish in trouble water have get possession of all the territory from the confine of the state of Venice to ferraria Bologna and along the coast of the adriaticke sea to Ancona it be say that Ravenna stand not now in his old place for at this time it be some two mile distant from the sea but the soil thereof be most fruitful in corn and unfit to yield wine and it be rich in pasture the house be build of brick and flint stone and be so old as they seem ready to fall this city have be often take by enemy have lose all the ornament which it have from so many exarch and king of Lombardy and from the bishop thereof who be so powerful as they strive long time for primacy with the bishop of Rome on the north-side of the city lie the sea but distant from the same and without the wall be a wood of pine-tree and not far thence lie the ruin of a very old and most fair church saint Mary the round who roof be admirable be of one stone and in the same church be the rich sepulchre of the Lombard king theodoricus which the soldier pull down with the church to get the metal thereof on the east-side the sea lie some two italian mile distant where be the haven for ship so much speak of in the roman history where the navy of Rome do winter yet be it now neither convenient nor secure for ship neither indeed can any but very small boat come up to the town on the southside without the golden gate build by the emperor Claudius lie the ruin of a stately palace build by the same king theodoricus and likewise of the city Caesaria in a chapel of the cathedral church be a most rich font and they report that many kingly monument be of old in this church in the market place lie a vessel of Porphry a kingly monument which the citizen in the year 1564. bring from the foresay sepulchre of king theodoricus in the ruin church of saint Mary near the gate on the north-side in the monastery of saint Francis be the sepulchre of the poet Dantes with these verse in latin at subnix we conder be area in a poor tomb Dantes thou do lie here the place obscure make thou almost unknown but now a marble chest thy bone do bear and thou appear fresh as flower new blow Bembus with tuseane muse ravish give this to thou who they most cherish in the year 1483. the six of the kalend of june Bernar Bembus the praetor lay this at his own charge the strength merit and crown of the friar minorite covent s. v. f. and these verse be add in latin the monarchy god lake and Phlegeton I search and sing while my fate do permit but since my better part to heaven be go and with his maker among the star do sit ay Dantes a poor banish man lie here who Florence mother of sweet love do bear in the church of saint vitalis the pavement be of marble and the wall all cover with precious stone of many kind but unpolished as they be take out of the mine show great antiquity and magnificence and do not a little delight the beholder also there be certain image grave in some stone I know not whether by nature or strange art which be to be admire among which I remember one stone have the picture of a Turk in all the apparel they wear another the image of a monk in his habit another of a priest with his bald head and two other the one most like the foot the other the leg of a man there be a altar of alabaster and the church be of a round form who roof be paint a Mosaica like engrave of which kind of paint rare and much esteem in Italy I have speak before in the description of Venice in this church be a fountain of water which by virtue give it from this saint as they say be thrice drink off give remedy to the headache another church of saint geruasive be so join to this of saint vitalis as it seem to i but a chapel thereof and in this church also be the saint bury of who it have the name and there be also the sepulchre of Placidiae sister to the emperor honorius and of her son and daughter and of her nurse with her husband here we pay each man three for his supper from Ravenna we ride thirty five mile to the old city rimini namely ten to five to Ceruia five to cesnadigo and fifteen to rimini through wild fenny field and a great wood of pine-tree and by the sandy shore of the sea between which and the apennine mountain divide Italy by the length the valley be so narrow as we continual do see the snowy top of those mountain towards the south and for the most part do see together with they the adriatic sea towards the north. in the foresay castle cesnadigo the postmaster will have force we to take new post-horse if he that let our horse to we have not please he by the payment of some money for the post-horse be know by a list of fur they wear in their bridle and if a man ride into a town upon a post-horse he must either go away on foot or take another post-horse there for no private man dare let he a horse which make passenger loath to high post-horse of return though many time they may be have at good rate rather than he will return empty with they yet if a man will walk a mile or two he may easy hire a horse in other town which be frequent in Italy and let no man marvel that these prince favour the postmaster and innkeeper to the prejudice of stranger because in that respect they extort great rent from they by the way in the village Bel' aria each of we pay two bolinei for passage of a river the brook rubico now call Pissatello by this way to rimini do run from the west into the adriatic sea and there of old be a marble pillar with this inscription in latin here stay leave thy banner lay down thy arm and lead not thy army with their colour beyond this brook rubico therefore if any shall go against the rule of this command let he be judge enemy to the people of Rome etc. and hereupon it be that julius Caesar return out of France and first stop here and then after he have see some prodigious sign pass over this brook with his army utter word in latin to this effect let we go whither the prodigic of the god and the sin of our enemy call we the die be cast in the marketplace of rimini be a monument of the same Caesar yet remain where rimini word in the latin tongue be grave in a stone to this effect the consul of rimini do repair this pulpit decay with age in the month of November and December in the year 1555. under that be write Caius Caesar dictator have pass rubico here in the market place of rimini speak to his fellow soldier begin the civil war in the same marketplace of rimini be a pleasant conduit of water the city have no beauty and lie in length from the east to the west on the westside be a bridge build by the emperor augustas which they hold to be very fair towards the east be a triumphal ark build by the same Augustus with old inscription and a pinackle erect which show the flaminian way to Rome and the emilian way towards Parma I say that the pope territory extend this way as high as Ancona and these inhabiter of Marca be account a wicked generation the great part of the cutthroat and murderer disperse through Italy be bear in this country our host use we very ill demand of each of we a for our bed and three for our supper and when we desire a reckon demand for a little piece of a eel one and a half and for three little sole ten bolinei beside that by the aforesaid privilege he force we be postmaster to take horse of he at what price he list the next morning we ride fifteen mile to the castle where be a bridge divide the territory of the pope and the duke of Urbine then we ride to Pesaro ten Pesaro mile and each man pay for his and all our way be through fruitful hill and little mountain this city have a fair round marketplace and a pleasant fountain therein distil water at eight pipe the air be think unwholesome for which cause and the great plenty of fruit nothing be more frequent here than funeral in the month of August and the inhabitant seldom live to be 50 year old each of we pay a polo for our dinner call for what meat we like and agree first for the price from hence to Ancona eat forty five mile and we hire three horse for twenty five with condition that our guide vulgar call or Veturino shall pay for his horse-meat and bring they back again after dinner we ride five mile to the little city compass with high wall of flint and lie upon a hill-side towards the sea and subject to the pope where we do see a triumphal ark of marble curious engrave then we ride fifteen mile more to Senogalita a strong city and subject to the duke of Urbin by the way we pass the bridge Metro have fourscore arch and the bridge Marctta have fifty arch both build of wood and very low as serve to pass over little brook which notwithstanding by reason of the mountain be near do often overflow all this day journey be by the seashore on our left hand towards the north and fruitful hill of corn towards the south over which hang the apennine mountain whence many time the water discend violent by reason of the narrow valley between the sea and the say mountain it be proverbial say of the magistrate of Senogallia ill Podesta that be the governor command and do it himself whereby it seem he be little esteem the city be of a small circuit but very strong and the house be build of brick with a roof something flat after the italian fashion the inn be without the gate and so the more comodious for stranger who may come late and depart early which they can not do if their lodging be within the wall it be true that he who buy have need to sell for the duke extort great rent from the innkeeper he in like sort oppress the passenger for a short supper at a common table each man pay four or Poali a coin so call of pope Paul the next morning we ride fifteen mile to Fimesino and ten to Ancona have the sea on our left hand towards the north and fruitful mountain on our right hand towards the south Fimesino be a fort and belong to the pope but the inn without the gate belong to the duke of Urbine and again when you have pass the bridge all the territory to Ancona be subject to the pope the city of Ancona be compass with three mountain and have the form of a Anconae half moon on the north side be a mountain upon which the governor dwell and upon the east side be another mountain and upon the side of these two mountain the city be build to the valley and seaside towards the north. on the southside be the three mountain upon which be the castle call build in the same place where the temple of Venus stand and upon this side the city be narrow there be no house build upon the mountain but only in the valley upon the sea the pope have soldier in this castle and thereby keep the city in subjection for the citizen long defend their liberty and how soever they be subject to the pope yet secret choose their magistrate every year to the year 1532 at which time pope Clement the seven build this castle against the turkish pirate but beside he use it to bring the citizen in absolute subjection the street be narrow and the way ill pave with flint the haven be of a triangular form and be now very pleasant as of old it be of great fame for a most secure port yet it seem not to i capable of many or great ship perhaps it be of old fit to receive the roman navy of galley but since they have neglect to preserve it the emperor repair this haven and adorn it with a stately triumphal ark of marble which remain to this day about this haven there be pleasant walk and the place where the merchant meet call lie upon the sea be as sweet a open room as ever I see but narrow and nothing answerable for stately build to the exchange of London it be beautify with sweet picture among which one of a angel which look right upon you on which side soever you behold it be much esteem they have a proverb one Peter in Rome one tower in Cremona and one haven in Ancona for the excellency of they near the gate of the city to my remembrance on the east-side be a very sweet fountain pour water out of many head of stone at Ancona according to the custom of passenger we agree with a vetturine or letter of horse that each of we pay he fifty five he shall find we horse and horse meat and our own diet to Rome and to this end his servant follow we on foot after the fashion of the italian who ride slow and these servant be call or vetturals now we be to cross the breadth of Italy from the adriatic to the tyrrhene Sea the first day in the morning we ride fifteen mile to a little city call madonna di Loretto through fruitful mountain and pass a high promontory by the way be a altar with this inscription in latin o passenger go on merry etc. Gregory the thirteen have well pave the rest of the way the like inscription be in the ascent of the mountain upon which the little city Loreto stand for this way in a fruitful country of corn and a dirty soil be pave at the charge of the say pope a certain chamber have give beginning to this city and the church thereof than i. oreto which nothing be esteem more holy among the papist and because many gift of great price use to be give by vow to our lady of this church the city be well fortify against pirate who do once spoil the same and be like again to be invite by the hope of rich spoil to the like attempt if the town lie unfortify it be of little circuit and lie in length from east to the west so narrow as it have almost but one street in the breadth and all the house of this street be inn or shop of they that sell bead to number prayer on the east side after a steep descent of a mountain lie a valley of two mile and beyond that the sea on the north side to ward ancon though the sea be very far distant yet from this city seat upon a high mountain it may easy be see upon the door of this church famous for man superstitious worship these verse be write enter not here unwashed of any spot for a more holy church the world have not at the church door be a statue of brass erect to pope Gregory the thirteen as I walk about the church behold in a dark chapel a priest by his exorcism cast a devil out of a poor woman good lord what fence and true conjure word he use how much more skilful be he in the devil name then any ambitious roman ever be in the name of his citizen who he court for their voice if he have eat a bushel of salt in hell if he have be a inhabitant thereof sure this art can never have be more familiar to he he often speak to the ignorant woman in the latin tongue but nothing less than in Tully phrase and at last the poor wretch either hire to deceive the people or if that be more probable draw by familiar practice with the priest or at least affright with his strange language and cry confess herself dispossess by his exorcism in the body of the church a table of write hand in the greek latin and many other tongue be fasten to a pillar set down at large the wonderful history of the chamber in the midst of the church which I confess be less curious observe by i abhor from that superstition and hasten from thence as much as I may yet give i leave to set down the sum thereof out of the itinerary of Villamont a french gentleman this chamber or chapel say he be the very house in which the queen virgin of Nazaret be let the reader believe as he list woe to he that woe to he that believe bear bring up and salute by the angel foretell she of Christ birth and in which Christ be conceive and in which the virgin dwell after Christ ascension accompany with the holy apostle especial with saint John by Christ command which the apostle after the virgin death for the great mystery do here turn into a chapel consecrate to the sacrifice of Christ and dedicate the same and with their own hand make the great cross of wood now set in the window of the chapel and in which saint Luke make with his hand the picture and image now set above it let i add this chapel from a house become a chamber and of a chamber be make a chapel and it be build of brick and be thirty foot long twelve and a half broad in the chimney as Villamont say as yet remain the holy ash which no man dare take away and the altar also upon which the mass be sing be make by the apostle hand there be a room into which you first enter which be divide from the chapel by a iron grate for no man enter the chapel without leave but must say his prayer in the outer room yet leave be give to any that ask it Villamont add that he find by diligent search that this chapel be much reverence in the primitive church but the holy land be subdue by saracen then by turk he faith it happen in the year 1291. that this house be take up from the foundation by angel who in the night miraculous carry it to the sea shore of Sclavonia where it be make know to the people by the shine of the virgin image and then by a vision of a religious man the virgin herself make know the history to he he add the virgin oration wherein she give herself many title which in late age be first invent and she do so extol she own praise with her own mouth as he that read the old song of the bless virgin will cry out with the latin poet only change the name o how be she change from that virgin which so modest speak of herself Villamont add that messenger be send into Palestina who find this history to be most true yet this chapel do not long abide in Slavonia but the angel in the year 1294. take it up again and transport it to this sea coast of Italy where again it be make know by the shine of the image and many miracle daily do whereupon the chapel of the image be call madonna di Loreto that be our lady of Loreto and because thief lie in the wood do spoil stranger who daily come thither for devotion the angel as he say the three time take it up and set it down in a private possession of two brother who disagree in the division of the profit rise by the concourse of people the angel the four time take it up this image never rest till it come into the pope territory where it be not more helpful to other then profitable to the pope and church man and place it in this firm seat where now it remain after it be often visit by stranger pope Paul the second build a other stately church over it pope Leo the ten have first fortify the little city against pirate let i add that pope sixtus the five bear in this Marca of Ancona establish a bishop in this town and so make it a city Villamont relate the treasure of this church among the rest name certain map of city and mountain and the image of the twelve apostle a great crucifix candlestick and infinite vessel of silver image chalice cross of gold and many precious stone of huge value two cross make all of precious stone whereof one be give by the archduke of Austria and a heart of gold set with precious stone the gift of the duchess of Lorraine and a vessel of huge value which the french king Henry the three give with this inscription 3 franc. and pol. reg. Christianiss m. d. 84 o queen who with thy child the world have bless let not this king and kingdom childless rest of Henry the three of France and Poland most christian king in the year 1584. the arm of the kingdom be also set upon it thus far Villamont relate he remember no gift of great value than this of Henry the three yet with leave be it speak this king a very slave to the roman church obtain not his petition all these gift be give upon vow and myself do see in the outward reome of the behold how holy these wall of brick be which can abide the impure touch of marble chapel into which all be admit a galley under all all of beat gold give by the vow of the duke of Florence upon the recovery of his health Villamont add that this chapel be compass with a wall of white marble curious engrave but that this wall can never by any art be fasten to the chapel and that the chapel be also compass with twenty pillar bear the image often prophet and the ten sibyl he add that many miracle be here do and first give instance in the person of the marquis of baden in the year 1584. second he set it down for a maxim and prove it by a example that no man ever take any thing out of this church without great mischief betalling he and that the robber thereof be compel to restore as it be by infernal fury let i say true always reserve due reverence to the bless virgin to who the scripture teach such divine worship to be most unpleasing as the papist yield she I say let i with due reverence tell a truth myself and two dutchman my consort abhor from this superstition by leave enter the inner chapel where we do see the virgin picture adorn with precious jewel and the place to increase religious horror be dark yet the jewel shine by the light of wax candle when we be enter the priest courteous leave we to give we space for our devotion but when we come forth as the italian proverbial speak of the priest avarice every psalm end in glory be etc. as if they shall say all religion to end in profit it be necessary for we to cast alm into a iron chest behind the altar cover with a iron grate therefore my consort of purpose to delight the priest ear with the sound of money as with music do cast into that chest many brass quatrine but of small value and myself be last when my turn be to give alm do in stead thereof gather some ten quatirne of they which lie scatter upon the grate and get that clear gain by that idol God forbid I shall brag of any contempt to religion but since it appear that such worship be unpleasing to God and because papist will have all their miracle believe I will free say by experience that have get these few quatirne in such sort as I say yet after that God of his mercy preserve i in my long and dangerous travel and from that time to this day by his grace I have enjoy though no abundant yet a competent estate and more plentiful then in my former day the four miracle relate by Villamont for I omit the three be worth all the rest which he say hang up in this church write in the italian tongue and also print namely that a french woman possess with a devil come hither and be exorcize by a holy canon do answer that she have seven devil and he cast they out that the first call at his come forth blue out a torch and that the second be call Heroth the three Venteloth the four and while he make they all without torture or command to confess their own wicted act and while he omit the other three yet he do not omit that the four tell the priest thing unknown to the world namely that he show he the stone upon which the angel stand when he salute the virgin and likewise the place where the virgin at that time stand and that those place be afterward no less worship than the chapel itself this Villamont relate of these thing reveal by the devil give i leave to say that if the devil have be the great friend the church of Rome have he can not have tell a more profitable thing to it and that the roman church be not altogether ungrateful which believe the father of lie in so great a matter and do not so much as put he to his oath but they be wise to be of Ovid opinion why shall I not flatter my own desire I will end the rest in one word there be incredible concourse to this place from all part profess the roman religion neither be any man in the most remote part of Europe oppress with any calamity but he vow some gift to this image in this church I do see fifty banish man vulgar call Banditi who be banish for murder and such like crime and now have their pardon upon condition that for some year they shall serve the emperor in Hungary against the turk these man abhor in all Italy yet no doubt at this time very devout do make stiff vow to expiate their sin and to have happy return out of Hungary yet they hold their hand from give any large alm myself and my consort be all this day fast for it have be a unperdonable sin to have demand meat in our inn before we have be in the church and will have give open occasion to suspect our religion at last when we return to the inn our Vetturine give we our dinner the same day after a slight dinner we ride fourteen mile upon a causey pave with stone and wind about a mountain then through field abound with olive tree but have no vine and we come to the city where the pope legate lie and keep his chancery for this Marca of Ancona part of this province yield rich wine whereof they have only white wine in the inn the second day in the morning we ride twenty two mile to Poluertna through a pleasant way and fruitful field yield corn and olive and by the way near the city Tollentine be the confine of the Marca of Ancona and of the dukedom of Spoleto after dinner we ride ten mile to the castle Serevallo through stony and barren mountain the three day in the morning we ride sixteen mile to Fuligni through most stony and barren mountain which be call Apennine and divide the length of Italy and through a large plain plant with olive tree and compass about with mountain this city be build upon the ruin of the city forum Flaminium after dinner we ride ten mile to the city Spoleto through a firtle plain but stony yield together in the same field vine corn almond and olive tree and at the end of the plain this city be seat partly in a plain partly on the side of a mountain upon the top whereof be a strong castle build upon the ruin of a old amphitheatre to the which man pass over a bridge of stone uphold by twentyfour great pillar and join two mountain which have a deep valley between they but narrow and without water in the church of this castle they show a picture of the bless virgin paint with saint Luke hand of which kind there be a infinite number among the papist theodoricus king of the goth build a stately palace in the city which be ruin narses the eunuch governor of Italy under the eastern emperor do rebuild it the dukedom of Spoleto be subject to the pope who take it when he cast the eastern emperor out of Italy and after extort the grant thereof from the renew western emperor and the soil of this dukedom be most fruitful of corn wine almond and olive tree and of most sweet fruit of the wine marshal thus write falerna hib as if with Spoleto bottle once you meet say that Falerno must be not so sweet the four day in the morning we ride ten mill through stony and most barren mountain and five mile through a fertile plain wherein grow together corn vine and olive tree which tree I observe always to grow in stony ground which soil in Italy use to be more fertile than other and seven mile through a more fertile plain in the end whereof be the city narni who situation be altogether like that of Spoleto the italian tell i that the soil of this territory be make dirty with the sun and wind and dusty with rain which since I have find confirm by learned cosmographer on the southside of narni the river Negra fall with great noise from a steep rock and the friar Leandro who have best describe Italy affirm that the river Velino make a fen which Cicero number among wonderful thing and that this fen end in a lake of old call now vulgar call Lago pie and that between the run out of the water there be a fountain of Neptune which Pliny have describe and that this lake be the navel of midst of Italy and last that the water fall into the lake compass with mountain by steep descent make noise like the groan yell and sigh of infernal spirit from whence and by other argument he seem to prove plain that the verse of Virgil in the seven book of his AEneados be mean of this place and that other be deceive who think they mean by Tenaso in apulia especial since the valley Ansancti be in this place vulgar call Nesanto for Ansanto which signify on all side holy because they be fertile the verse of Virgil be these and Ansancti etc. and etc. italtes centre have great mount beneath a noble place which be far know by fame the Ansancti valley etc. a dreadful hole whereat fierce Dis do breath here may be see etc. after dinner we ride twelve mile to a little town lie beyond the river Tiber namely eight mile to the castle Otricoli through woody mountain and Valyes bear olive tree and corn together with those tree and from thence to the side of the river Tiber two mile in pasture field here we pass to the west side of this so famous river where of old the emperor Augustus build a stately bridge but now man and horse pass in a ferry-boat which be draw over with the force of man hand by a great cable fasten across the river and lest the boat shall be carry away with the swift stream a second cable be fasten across the river by post on each side high than a man and they have a three short cable to the one end whereof the boat be fasten and the other end have a strong wheel which be put upon the second high cable upon which the boat slip forward as it be draw with man hand by the first low cable for the bed of the Tiber be broad in this place and have his spring not far off among the high apennine mountain and fall thence with great force will carry away any boat row with oar but from thence the bed of the river grow narrow and be such at Rome as it scarce deserve the name of a brook and nothing answer the glorious fame which italian have give it who always extol their own thing to the sky hereupon it be necessary that when any store of rain fall or much snow sudden melt those water fall from the mountain shall overflow the field and the city of Rome itself as they have often do with great danger of the city the same be not far distant from this ferrey and these high mountain among which the river have his spring but from Rome it run in a narrow bed 2 mile to Ostia with a slow course and there end in lake the mouth of the haven be so stop as the least bark can pass to and from the sea here beyond our expectation our Veturine allege that he have agree with we to pay for our diet not for our passage of river by which captious trick each of we be force to pay two for our passage over the river of the foresay twelve mile to the little town where of I speak two mile remain which we ride and there lodge that might the five day in the morning we ride seventeen mile to Castel' through woody mountain and valy of corn in a way very dirty and slippery and here our Veturine tie to pay for our diet put a new trick upon we say that he will not dine but go on to Rome yet if we please to dine he will out of his duty stay for we otherwise be ready to finish the rest of his journey we smile at the knave craft and each of we pay two and a half for our dinner after dinner we ride thirteen mile to Rome of old the head-city of the world through wind hill and pasture and when we come to the first gate we do meet Rome many english man on horseback without boot be all priest go to madonna di Loreto I be much afraid lest some of they be scholar of Cambridge shall know i bring up in the same university neither be the hear of the english tongue or the sight of english man ever before so unpleasing to i from this first gate we ride in the way of Flaminius by the wind bank of Tiber and many cave under mountain and hill to the bridge call ponte-mole which unite the say way of Flaminius lie on both side the river and there we pass to the east-side of the say river Tiber and pass on the same way of Flaminius we enter the city by a large gate vulgar call porta and by a market place vulgar call before in which marketplace be the church of s. Mary chap. 2 of my journey to Naples and my return to Rome and of the description of both city of my journey cursory to Sienna Fiorenza Pistoia Lucca and Pisa and the description of the three last city defer the view of Rome till my return I think best to pass on present to Naples lest if I have stay long I may perhaps have be betray into the hand of the spaniard when I shall come thither for that kingdom be subject to the spaniard with who the english then have war beside that in like case delay brecd danger into which if I shall have fall I hope to escape with more ease and contentment when I have be at the further of my journey therefore according to the fashion I agree with a vetturine at Rome for forty four to give i a horse to Naples and to pay for my diet and horsemeat I say it be the fashion especial in way of danger and trouble to get meat that passenger shall agree with their Vetturine for their diet which if they do not they shall be subject to the fraud of host in such a journey and hardly get so good meat as they who daily pass be well acquaint in all place and in this tumultuary journey to Naples it be most of all necessary for stranger thus to agree with their Vetturine since the host be great extorter from all man and especial from stranger and it will be difficult for stranger not know the fashion of that hasty journey and of the country to provide for themselves when we go out of Rome our consort sudden in a broad street light from their horse and give they to the vetturine to hold and so go themselves to the holy stair vulgar call scale that they may there pray for a happy journey at which time myself and my consort slip into the next church and go in at one door and out at the other escape the worship of those holy stair and at fit time come to take our horse with the rest they say that these stair be the same which Christ ascend in pilate house at jerusalem and that they be from thence bring to Rome and indeed at jerusalem the place of they lie void so as I will in this mnch rather believe the roman then in the transportation of the chamber at lareto which they will have do by the angel and that often and at unseasonable time whereas in so many voyage into Palestine it be not difficult to bring these stair from thence yet they be of marble and very rich I will fain know how such a monument can be preserve when jerusalem be destroy and if they say they belong to that house of Pilate which they show at this day I dare be bold to affirm that the magnificence of these stair be nothing answerable to the poor build of that house the twelve of march we ride twelve mile to Marino a castle belong to the roman family of Colonna and we pass through a fruitful plain of corn have on our right hand towards the south the ruin of old Rome and the castle Tusculo where Cicero write his Tuseulane question not far from Palestrina of old call Preneste where Marius besiege by Scylla kill himself and we may often see the tyrrhene sea and have upon our left hand towards the north a antic conduit make of brick lie all the length of the way from Rome to the eastern mountain in which Marino be seat and from whence the water be so far bring to Rome and upon the same side have a new conduit build by pope sixtus the five when the pipe of the other be break but the same be much low and less magnificent than the other and upon this hand we have mountain not far distant Marino be of old call Marianavilla and from this castle the mountain which by the way we have on our left hand towards the north cross over to the tyrrhene sea towards the south shut up the large plain from Rome hither and these mountain plant with vine and have a sweet prospect into the same plain be very pleasant whereupon there be very many palace of roman senator build upon these mountain which lie high of the fresh air vulgar this place be call Frescada among these mountain in the village tivoli the decease cardinal Hippolito of Este build a palace and a wonderful garden which be ten mile distant from the city of Rome the passenger for the most part have scene Rome do in the cardinal time and yet many time do pass that way for it resemble a terrestrial paradise by reason of the fountain statue cave grove fishpond cage of bird nightingale fly loose in the grove and the most pleasant prospect in this castle Marino we make some stay to expect some passenger which be long detain at Rome by their business and the pope in this place give sixty horseman musqueter to accompany the carrier vulgar call Procaccia and to defend he from the spoil of banish man vulgar call Banditi and for this cause all passenger go in this carrier company neither dare any pass alone for these banish man lurk upon the confine of the pope state and the kingdom of Naples many time make excursion as far as these mountain to do robbery and the week last pass they have kill many passenger and have rob the carrier who do not only bear letter but lead many mule lade with good the chief of these banish man be the nephew so they call churchman bastard of the cardinal Caietano who have eight thousand crown yearly reut in these part be banish by the pope and he understanding that a roman gentleman pass with that carrier who have great friend about the pope and hope to make his peace by take he prisoner do for that cause assail that carrier and his guard till hear that the gentleman while they fight have escape to the next city he withdraw himself and his man into the mountainr this danger from banish man make the journey to Naples very trouble some and it be not safe nor lawful for any man to leave the company of this carrier so as the passenger rise before day and take horse and so sit all the day yet ride not abouc twenty mile for the slow pace of the mule and at noon they have no rest only when they have the inn in sight so as there be no danger of thief they be permit to gallop before that they may eat a morsel or rather devour it for as soon as the mule be past they must to horse again every man not only make haste for his own safety but the soldier force they to be go who be more slow than the rest to conclude the mule go a very slow pace it be very irksome to the passenger to rise before day and to follow they step by step have dine at Marino and our full company be come we together with our guard of horseman ride eight mile to Velitri through woody mountain infamous for the robbery of banish man and upon our right hand towards the south and towards the tyrrhene sea be a lake vulgar call Lago Nympao which the old roman delight with do difficult thing use to fill with sea water and therein to make naval fight one wood by which we pass be more dangerous than the rest where the pope maintain forty foot to assist the guard of horse till they have pass the same the descent of the last mountain near Velitri be two mile long yet pleasant by reason of the multitude of vine grow upon short stake which use to yield the rich wine be by writer call belitre a old city of the volsci and famous for the birth of the emperor Augustus and the dwell of the Octavian family the second day in the morning we ride thirteen or fourteen mile to Sermoneta and in the midst of the way our guard of horseleft we and their trumpet ask of every man a gift in courtesy which we glad give and there new horseman meet we
