take upon they our guard after dinner we ride eight mile to a little town and five mile to a old city which Livy callesh privernum yet other co'mographer write that the ruin thereof lie in a plain two mile off whereas this be seat upon a mountain yet grow to a city by the decay of the former be call privernum and vulgar Piperno we pass through woody mountain full of olive tree on the right hand and a fruitful plain of corn and many orchard of orange tree and like fruit on the left hand and among the mountain on the right hand the most remote be call of the famous witch Circe and it be a promontory hang over the sea where at this day they show the cup in which Ulysses drink the enchant potion and under the hollow cave of this mountain the turkish pirate lurk in the summer time and rob the christian the last five mile of our journey all the passenger and soldier be put before the carrier and his mule for than we turn out of the plain towards mountain on the left hand where as they say the banish man have the week before assail the carrier after we have dine the horseman leave we and certain foot do after guide we from one city to another the three day in the morning we have a guard of horseman and ride twelve mile to Terracina a old city so call in the time of the emperor Tiberius and we pass through a fertile plain of corn on the right hand towards the sea and stony hill full of olive tree on the left hand towards the land and many vineyard and ruin of house near the city after we have this morning ride two mile we pass by a old monastery call badia where they have a monument of saint Thomas Aquinas but his body be carry to the city Tolouse in France when the frenchman have the kingdom of Naples and after we have ride ten mile our guard of horse leave we and certain foot meet we conduct we other 2 mile in this way the water in many place at the foot of the hill do stink of brimstone but infinite laurel tree on all side refresh our smell Terracina in the flourish time of Rome be call anxur and it be seat upon a mountain as most of the foresay city be and it lie upon the sea which the land embrace like a half moon this city lie upon one horn thereof and the city caieta upon the other of which city the cardinal have name who do oppose himself to Luther the flood of the sea make great noise with strike upon hollow cave of rock a soldier come out of the tower of and demand of every man five which we pay though it be only due from they who have portmanteave with lock near this city we do see the ruin of a stately theatre after dinner we ride ten mile to the city fondi through a stony way be part of the old way of Appius and upon the right hand we have a plain towards the sea and upon the left hand rockey mountain towards the land where we pass by the city monticello at the midway the pope guard have leave we we come to two old ruin wall shut up the way and lie from the mountain to the sea this place call Sportelle divide the territory of the pope and the king of Naples and be keep by a garrison of spaniard I remember at our come back these soldier demand of the passenger a gift in courtesy and when some refuse it they stop their passage and only trouble they in the search of their carriage under pretence that they may carry some prohibit thing those soldier do accompany we to the city fondi I call the same and some other place by the name of city because they be city of old though now they be only village and have no other beauty but the ruin of age this old city be sack in the year 1534 by Barbarossa a turkish pirate it be seat in a plain have only a meadow and a field overflow between it and the sea and the house be build of flint and such little stone but it have most pleasant orchard of citron orange and lemon the orange tree at one time have ripe and green fruit and bud and be green in winter give at that dead time a pleasant remembrance of summer by our veturine spare our diet be daily very short and at Terracina we can not so much as get wine and here our supper be so short as we judge our vetturine good physician who persuade light supper the wine of fondi and Cecubo for the mount Cecubo be not far distant be much celebrate by the roman poet namely by Horace the four day in the morning we ride ten mile to Mola vulgar call Nola upon a pave causey between stony mountain be part of the way of Appius and through great wood of olive tree have by the way many orchard of orange and like fruit and enter near Mola into a more open air not only this village but all this sea coast be call Mola of the mile as I think drive by water fall from the mountain and it be number among the most pleasant place of Italy Mola be build upon the ruin of old which be to be see in the field round about it among these ruin be the house of Cicero who speak of his village where Scipio and Lelius come to recreate themselves and there be also the sepulchre of Cicero so as it seem he be kill by Anthony in this territory after dinner we ride eight mile through a wild field with low shrub upon a pave way till we come to the river Garigliano who narrow and deep stream we pass by boat and stay long about the put over of our horse our company be great and each horseman pay five for passage near this river we do see the ruin of a most fair theatre build of brick and flint and of another old and round theatre and of a conduit build of brick upon a 140 arch not far hence among huge and snowy mountain be the city which have the title of a dukedom and be of old call minturne after we have pass the river we ride seven mile to Sesso and three mile to a country house through a fruitful plain of corn have the tirrhene sea so near we as we may see it three or four time and because the other carrier come from Naples to Rome lodge with his consort a mile before we in the village Castellano we be force to lodge in this country house the five day in the morning at the begin of our journey we meet the say carrier with his consort and we ride eight mile to the village francolisse in a most pleasant way between hill of black clay like stone but a most fruitful country this village lie on the left hand of our way towards the land among very pleasant hill and the place be not far distant where Hannibal bring into straits by Fabius do escape by a stratagem tie fire upon the horn of Oxen. after we ride 8 mile to the most pleasant city Capua through a most sweet plain Capua call because it be laborious to the tiler but it be wonderful fruitful and abound with olive tree and vine plant upon elm here we dine not according to our covenant at our vetturine charge but at our own cost and each man have such meat as he choose and that as I think because the passenger be now out of danger and in a place abound with all dainty refuse to be diet at their veturine pleasure and choose rather to feast themselves as they list and in deed we have excellent cheer delicate wine most white pure bread and among other dainty I remember we have black olive which I have never see before and they be of a most pleasant taste here each of we pay two and a half for our dinner this city be new build but if you go out of the gate to saint Mary church towards Naples upon the south-west side of the town there you shall see a colossus and a cave and many monument of old Capua among the orchard the delicacy of which city be of old so famous as we read that the army of Hannibal grow effeminate thereby this new city have a castle upon the north-east side build upon the wall wherein be a garrison of soldier which keep the city in obedience and the river Vulturnus run upon the same side of the city which they pass with a bridge of stone near which there be a inscription that Philip king of Spain repair the way and build the bridge the city be of a little compass but strong and it have a fair senate-house and a fair church call Annonciata with a fair altar after dinner we have no guard neither be tie to accompany the carrier but it be free for every man to take his way and company or to ride alone at his pleasure so from Capua we ride eight mile to a new city otherwise call and of old call Attella whence be the old satirical comedy which be full of bawdry and be call Attellane and between this city and the mountain Veswius now call out of the way towards the land and near the castle Airola be the valley Caudine where Hannibal put the roman draw into strait disgraceful to pass under a pair of gallows which be call the Caudine gallow well know to all that have read Livy the same afternoon we ride further eight mile to Naples and all this way from Naples Capua to Naples be a most fruitful plain of corn and vine grow high upon elm tree according to the tillage of Lombardy one and the same field yield corn and wine and wood to burn but the other wine of this country grow upon hill and mountain and all the other fruit can be worthy praise we enter Naples on the east side by the gate of Capua where the vice-roies use to enter in pomp and this gate be stately build and upon this side the suburb be long and fair and the street of Capua within the wall be no less fair in which be the prison and because we be attire like frenchman the prisoner scoff at we and to my great marvel the citizen of good sort do not forbear this barbarous usage towards we the description of Naples and the of Avernus s bay t cento Camerelle five w the elysian field from the foresay part on the east-side of the city where we enter by the a gate of a Capua without the wall towards the land eight mile from the city lie d torre di Graco now call torre where Pliny writer of the natural history and admiral of the navey of Augustus be near the say sour choke with vapour while too curious he desire to behold the burn of the mountain Vesunius now call this mountain be most high and upon the top be dreadful where be a gulf cast out flame and while the wind enclose seek to break out by natural force there have be hear horrible noise and fearful groan there of the mountain abound with vine and olive and there grow the greckewine which Pliny call Pompey wine and of this wine they say this place be call Torredi Graco the great burn of this mountain break out in the time of the emperor Titus the smoke whereof make the sun dark burn up the next territory and consume two city pompeia and Herculea and the ash thereof cover all the field of that territory it break out again in the year 1538 with great gaprig of the earth and cast down part of the mountain the palace there take the name of the next village be call Pietra Biancha that be white stone which on the inside be all of marble deck with carve work in the very chamber and there be a image of a nymph sleep and lie upon a earthen vessel out of which great quantity of water flow and fall into marble channel wherein fish be keep as in pond this palace be build in the year 1530 by a counsellor to the emperor Charles the five at the foot of this mountain of old Decius the first of all the roman consul do by vow give himself for the army and at the bridge of the brook Draco the last king of the goth Teius be slay have three buckler all pierce with his enemy arrow on the same east side come back to Naples yet the say mountain lie northward you shall come to a stately palace which the king of Naples have build and call it Poggio real be not above a mile from Naples there of old be seat the city Paleopolis and it lie in a most sweet plain from the say palace the way lead right to the kingly b gate call porta real at which only the king enter in solemn pomp and from this gate right to the west lie a most fair and large street call Strada Toletana the way whereof on both side be raise with a fair and large pavement for man to walk upon and it have a fair marketplace when you come to the end of this street there be the church of saint c Clara call vulgar San' which be build by Agnes of Spain wife to king Robert where be artificial sepulchre of the say Robert come of the french king and of his wife Agnes and of other king and prince of the french family Durczzana and there in a chapel the monk day and night sing with a lamentable voice or rather groan for the rest of their decease soul in the church of saint dominick be a altar which they say cost some twenty five thousand crown and in the vestry lie the body of nine king in coffin of wood cover with pewter and have black velvet lay over they among these king be Alphonso the first king of Arragon and ferdinand his son and Ferdinand the second and in this place also the monk in like sort sing or rather howl rest to their soul they show a crucifix which they say do speak to Thomas Aquinas in this manner Thomas thou have write well of i what reward do they ask and that Thomas shall answer no reward Lordbut thyself only I have hear that saint Bernard know the fraud and imposture of the monk and not dissemble they when the image of the bless virgin do in like sort praise he do with much more piety and wisdom answer out of s. Paul 1. cor. 14. let woman be silent in the church for it be not permit they to speak not far thence be the public school of the university which the emperor Frederick the second found there in the most fair church of the monk of saint Olivet the image of Ferdinand the first and Alphonso the second be so lively engrave and do so artificial represent they as well in the bed die as upon their knee pray with the mourn of the bystander the horror of religion be increase with lamp continual burn as myself by chance pass by this chapel think I have fall among live prince not dead image and perhaps I have see a more sumptuous monument but a more beautiful do I never see in the little church of the hermitane friar saint John in Carbonara be a monument of Robert king of Naples and of joane the first his sister of white marble be a altar which the italian think the most stately monumenr of Europe but for my part I dare not prefer it to some in Germany nor to many in England nor to the monument of the turkish emperor many table be hang up by vow in this church there be a fair sepulchre of white marble erect to n. Caraccioli Martial of the kingdom I omit the most fair church of saint Mary of the preacher almost all of marble and the cathedral church call Episcopio and the church of saint Laurence upon the northwest and by-north part of the city be the fort call d s. cut out in a high rock yet the ascent thereunto be so easy as a horseman may well mount to the top upon this mountain top lie a plain in which this castle be seat which command the city though it be take by the enemy a little beneath be the monastery of the carthusian and upon pretence to enlarge that monastery the emperor Charles the five build this most strong castle to bridle the wont petulancy and inconstancy of the citizen and from thence there be a most sweet prospect as well into the city as to the bay of the sea towards the southside be the haven and beyond the f bay of Naples lie firm land for the sea come in from the west make this bay upon this side be a fortification for the safety of the haven which be call g and it drive off the wave of the sea and make the haven like a half moon and therein at this time be twenty galley and ten small ship the armoury lie upon the sea from whence the galley and ship and land force be arm and among other thing there be keep the rich armour yet without any ornament of gold of the french king Francis the first which he do wear when he be take prisoner at Pavia thereby lie a large market place in which be a fair fountain with many image cast out water also there be a tower where they set light by night to guide sea man into the haven in the say market place be a stone upon which many play away their liberty at dice the king officer lend they money which when they have lose and can repay they be draw into the galley for the spaniard have slave of both sex on the outside of the say or fortification upon the haven towards the west and near to the shore lie the most strong fort call l castello seat in a plain and build by Charles the first of Arion and so fortify by Alphonso the first king of Arragon as it be number among the chief fort of Europe the inward gate be most fair all of marble and it have a little foursquare hall in which the parliament be yearly hold and the viceroy weekly sit in judgement near this hall be a fair tower in which the kingly ornament be lay up namely a sceptre of gold with great diamond upon the top the sword with the haft and scabbard of gold adorn with precious stone the king crown shine with precious stone a golden cross a huge pot of gold set with precious stone great vnlcorne horn and the chief kind of precious stone further towards the west yet so near as the garden of the palace lie upon the ditch of this castle be the k viceroy palace which have a large and most sweet garden and delicate walk pave with diverse colour and engrave marble and in this garden be two banquet house whereof one be very stately build and have a sweet fountain close to the table continual pour out water also there be a delicate cage of bird wrought about with thick wire and it be as big as a ordinary stil-house delicate shadow round about wherein be many kind of sing bird as well of Italy as foreign country a little further within the water be the h castle of the egg build upon a rock by the norman which rock be of a oval form and give the name to the castle vulgar call Castel' del' vuono which at this day be ruinous and some say it be the palace of Lucullus but it be certain that the norman build it as they do also another castle which be old and call the capuan castle of the adjoin capuan-gate Naples be of old call Parthenope of one of the siren there bury who they write to have cast herself into the sea for grief that by no flattery she can detain Ulysses with she the citizen of old Cuma build Naples and leave it shall grow great to the prejudice of Cuma they pull it down again till at last oppress with a great glague upon the warn of a oracle they build it again and change the old name Parthenope call it Naples which in greek signify a new city it be seat at the foot of hill and mountain in length from the north-east to the south-west or rather seem to be triangular whereof two corner lie upon the sea and that towards the west be more narrow than the other and the three blunt corner lie towards the mountain upon the east-side there be pleasant suburb and upon the westside more large suburb but upon the north-side without the wall there be only some few eeeee scatter house build upon the side of hill the house of the city be four roof high but the top lie almost plain so as they walk upon they in the cool time of the night or at left in general the top be not much erect like other part of Italy and the building be of free stone and show antiquity but the window be all cover with paper or linen cloth for glass window be most rare in Italy and as it be proper to Venice it have three fair broad and long street namely Toletano Capuana and the rest be very narrow there be eight gate towards land and as many towards sea among which the capuan gate since the emperor Charles the five enter thereat be deck with monument and statue there be in this city very many palace of gentleman baron and prince whereupon the city be vulgar call Napoli gentile among these two palace be most stately one of the duke of which the king of Spain forbid to be finish the other of the prince of salerno there be four public house call in which the prince and gentleman have yearly meeting and there also be the daily meeting of the merchant almost every house have his fountain of most wholesome water near the market place be many inn but poor and base for howsoever the city abound with house where they give lodging and meat yet it deserve no praise for fair inn of good entertainment on all side the eye be as it be bewitch with the sight of delicate garden aswell within the city as near the same the garden without the wall be so rare delightful as I shall think the hesperide be not to be compare with they and they be adorn with statue laberinthe fountain vine myrtle palm cetron lemon orange and cedar tree with laurel mulberry rose rosemary and all kind of fruit and flower so as they seem a earthly paradise the field be no less fruitful bring forth abundant all thing for the use of man the king stable without the wall be worth the see for the horse of this kingdom be much esteem and if any man buy a horse to carry out of the kingdom he pay the ten part of the price to the King the city be seat upon the side of hill and by lie open to the south be subject to great heat and most part of the street be narrow so as in walk the heat be not to be endure and yet they can use coach one fashion please i beyond measure that at the end of many street they have chair vulgar call Napoli which those that be weary do enter and they be cover round about and only have window on the side he that be carry therein can be see of any and yet himself may see all that pass two porter carry these chair by two long staff fasten thereunto and lift they but little from the ground and so for a moderate price carry the passenger to any part of the city after I observe the same fashion at Genoa which be in like sort seat upon the side of hill and mountain and in city so seat I think this fashion very convenient the territory of Naples have many famous antiquity and wonderful thing to be see which that we may behold we go early in the morning on foot out of the southwest-side of the city and have pass long suburb and scatter house we come within a musket shoot to the mountain e Pausilippo which be wonderful pleasant aswell for the house and village build upon it as for the excellent fruit which it yield of all kind this mountain be hard to be ascend extend itself in good length from the sea towards the land so as the way will be very troublesome to pozzol either ascend the mountain or compass it have they not find a remedy to this inconvenience therefore the progenitor of these citizen which some attribute to Lucullus as they do all magnifical thing and other to one Bassus but Leander the cosmographer a witness without exception attribute it to Coccius a roman I say their progenitor with wonderful art and huge expense dig a passage under this mountain and so make a plain way to Pozzoli and those part this way Strabo call a cave and it be vulgar call la Napoli and serve this famous city in stead of a gate yet be it a musket shoot distant and always lie open and the foresay Leander witness that it be twelve foot broad twenty four high and two hundred long to which length if you add 500 foot more which at both end be dig but lie not cover as the rest but in open air this work may well be say to be a italian mile long myself observe that part of the passage under the mountain to be nine hundred and sixteen walk pace long and nine broad and the height I imagine to double the breadth yet be it in some place biger then in other and for the breadth it be certain that two coach or cart may pass together one by the other the entrance and the go out at the other end be like two gate and of old light come in by many hole or window from the top of the mountain but the fall of earth do by little and little stop this light and in the time of Seneca this passage be so dark as he compare it to a prison and at last the light be so stop by the fall of earth by nettle and shrub as there be no light at all till alphonso the first of Arragon king of Naples open two window towards the two end which only light it have at this day to direct passenger at the entrance of either end the opposite gate seem no big than a full moon and a man enter there will seem a little child it have no light in the midst but like twilight or the Ovidian light which be in thick wood and in the twilight of morning and evening passenger use torch and continual the carter or horseman when they pass by the midst of the cave use to give warn one to the other cry vulgar that be towards the sea or Alla Montagna that be towards the mountain according to the side on which they come before we enter this cave among other stately palace one vulgar call Merguilino build by james Sanazzarro a famous poet almost of our age and give by his last will to a religious house contain the sepulchre of a learned man upon which Bembus be say to have write these verse these relic deck with flower sincer we here in tomb as muse to Maro come most near upon the mountain of Pausilippo be the sepulchre of Virgil show in two place who Servius write to have be bury in this way near Naples and that these verse be write upon his sepulchre Parthenope deuce Mantuan bear Calaber dead i hold Parthenope who sing plough duke sheepefold or thus Mantua give i life Calabry death my grave Parthenope who sing pasture plough captain brave or thus Mantua life do lend to i Calabers lay i on death car my bone lie at Parthenope who sing sheep tillage feat of war and the best judgement hold that he be bury in the church of the friar regular canon at the entrance of the cave as you go from Naples and not in the church at the go out of the cave and though both place show the sepulchre yet these verse be in neither place but the inscription be wear out with age the monk report that there be a statue of brass upon his sepulchre which those of Mantua steal fro thence and indeed at Mantua they show such a statue whether steal from hence or no let they dispute when we have pass this cave we bend our way from the sea towards the land and come to the mountain m astrune be of form like a theatre compass a large plain Alphonso the first king of Arragon and Naples and his son Ferdinand use to invite the prince noble and people of the kingdom to hunt in this place turn the dog and beast they hunt into the valley himself and the noble man sit in a pleasant grove upon the top of the mountain and the people be scatter round about the mountain to behold the sport in the same plain compass with this mountain be the lake of m Agnano which be say to be without bottom and to have nothing in it but frog and at the foot of the inside of the mountain next to Naples there be a venomous cave vulgar call n grotta ' that be the cave of the dog because they try the poison by put dog into it this cave be some eight foot high and six broad and go some four pace under the mountain where a sign be set beyond which if any live thing pass it present die Pliny write that this cave be call Cherone a evaporate a pestilent air we give two to a woman dwell there for a dog to try the poison with it which dog we fasten to a long staff and so thrust he into the cave hold he there till he seem dead and be take our will not move for any blow we give it then according to the fashion we cast the dog into the aforesaid lake and when he be draw out he begin by little and little to move and at last be come to his sense run away as if he have be mad the common sort attribute this to the blessing give to the water by the saint of which it be name but nothing be more clear then that the sprinkle of any water will revive the spirit choke with any ill vapour beside many have try that live thing cast into that cave and hold there for long time than be usual can never be fetch again to life by this or any other water they report that a french gentleman of Tournan try to fetch a stone out of this cave beyond the aforesaid sign pay for his curiosity by unrecoverable death and that the french king Charles the eight command a ass to be thrust into this cave the beast can never be fetch to life again and that do John base son of the emperor Charles the five force a galleyslave to go into this cave and he fall dead force another slave to fetch he out who likewise fall dead and that he kill the three slave with his own hand because he refuse to fetch out his two dead fellow many cast frog into this cave and except they present leap back this vapour kill they which be say to rise out of mine of brimstone and other metal into the foresay lake they cast flax which will be sleep in that water in 14 hour though it lie usual two week in other water and this water though cold to touch yet seem to boil the earthquake and flame break out of these mountain by the vapour enclose give the poet occasion to feign that giant be bury under they not far hence be the wholesome bath vulgar call I d'agnano which kind of bath be very frequent in this part here lie the ruin of a great village of Lucullus and writer affirm that he bring the sea water into the foresay lake cut the passage through mountain here also be the ruin of the village of Cicero which retain the old name and the emperor Adrian die at bait be bury here and his successor Anthony here build a temple to he upon the top of a mountain near this place be a round field like a marketplace vulgar call o Solfataria which Strabo call forum and Pliny write that of old this place be call campi Flecrci it be of a ouall form somewhat more long than broad have 1500 foot in length and 1000 in breadth be compass on all side with mountain except the entrance lie towards Pozzoli all the earth be hollow and be beat with a man foot sound like a empty vessel and not only the earth by the brimstone be make yellow but it make our boot and shoe of the same colour with walk upon it yea when I cast a piece of silver upon the ground it be present make yellow and with no rub can be make white again in this ouall market place as I may call it there be a short and narrow ditch of water which be almost round and the water thereof boil as if fire be under it they say if any thing be cast into it that it will be sodden in short space but some part of it will be consume and Leander report that one cast four egg into it and present take three full sodden but the four be consume also he witness that this little ditch be not always in one place but in time work itself from one place to another in this circuit and yet be never great and that the old ditch be fill present with new matter a horseman can well come to this place and as the same Leander write a horse man come bold thither be swallow up into the hollow earth and that the strange heat of this water may appear one of the viceroy guard a dutchman and come hither according to their fashion to guide his country man my consort tell we that one of his fellow not long before come in like sort as he do to guide his countryman hither either be careless or rather as it be most probable have drink too much and not guide his foot well by chance stumble into this ditch and when one of his friend take he by the hand to pull he out that he pull of all the skin from his hand and that after better advice they pull he out with a cloak fling about he but that within few day he die neither can the physician give he any remedy or promise any hope of his life at the foot of the mountain there be a hole where the vapour with their own motion continual cast up little stone and stink smell but if any man move these vapour by a staff or any thing put into the hole the more they be stir the great stone they cast up yea flame of fire sometime there be some cottage near this place where they make brimstone and all these part smell of brimstone and if the wind blow from hence towards Naples the stink thereof may be smell thither on all side here be bath of wholesome water which of old be famous after we have pass huge ruin of old building we come at the foot of a mountain to the city p Pozzoli of old famous and call Puteolis to which all these ruin be say to have belong of old and it have the name of the latin word as also it have the present name from the italian tongue of the well which be frequent I say it have the present name of the italian word pozzo signify a well though some will have it name present of Puzzo which signify a stink because of the smell of brimstone in these part but the city be most ancient can have the old name of a italian word and it be certain that the roman prince of old use this part for the place of their recreation for the great sweetness of the country and the plenty of medicinal water whereupon they give it the first name other say that it be of old call but at this day it be call Pozzoli or Puzzoli here we dine and be force to give our sword to the host there be a great penalty set upon any that carry their arm the city have nothing worth the see but the old church first build to heathen idol and after by christian dedicate to saint Proculus and it have the name of the workman that build it grave upon it and there be show the bone of a giant of wonderful bigness the haven of this city be of old very commodious but by negligence be grow of no use here the sea enter between two mountain be of old call the creek of p bay of that city seat on the opposite shore or the creek of Pozzoli of this city Suetonius write that the emperor Tiberius consult about his successor and incline more to his true nephew thrasyllus the mathematitian shall answer that catus shall no more reign than he shall ride over the creek of bay wherefore Caius be emperor and hear of this divination not as other say in emulation of Xerxes who make a bridge over Hellespont nor to the end that with the fame of this great work he may terrify the rebellious german and Britan do build a bridge over this creek of the sea be about three mile long that he may thereupon pass from bay to Pozzoli of this bridge thirteen pile of brick may be see near the shore at Pozzoli and as many on the other side near the shore of bay and some of these pile have yet arch upon they but ready to fall and from these pile the inner part of the bridge be found upon two rank of ship fasten with anchor and cover over with a bank of earth to make the passage like the way of Appius the rest Suetonius add in these or the like word over this bridge he go to and fro for two day the first day upon a trap horse have his head adorn with a crown of oak leave and bear a hatchet a sword and a garland and a robe of cloth of Gold the next day in a coch-mans habit drive a coach draw by four famous horse carry before he Darius a child one of the pledge give by the parthian his praetorian soldier accompany he and his friend follow he in a coach etc. he that desire to comprehend the magnificence of this work must first know that the mediterranean sea be very calm have little or no ebb or flow and that this creek be yet more calm and that this bridge be build in the further part of the creek very near the land these thing consider if my judgement fail not there be great cause of wonder at the bridge build by the duke of Parma besiege Antwerp be in like sort build upon bark fasten one to the other and also at the bridge of London bear a great ebb and flow of the sea and build of free stone upon so firm a foundation as it bear many great and fair house upon it but whatsoever the magnificence be sure the vanity of this work be great to spend so much upon this bridge the way by land be not a mile long than by the bridge give i leave to digress so far as to remember that the territory of be not far from pozzol the wine whereof call be so much praise by Horace after dinner we go from to view the antiquity lie upon this creek and first we come to the labyrinth a build under ground which have the name of the multitude of room with such passage to and fro as a man may loose himself in they and here we have not only need of the thread of Ariadne but of light also to conduct we Leander think that all this building be to keep fresh water then we come to the amphitheatre be of a ouall form the inner part whereof be 172 foot long and 88 broad the building whereof be little ruin and Suetonius write that this be build for the play of Vulcan not far thence near the shore be a fountain of clear and sweet water flow plentiful out of the sea so that for a great distance we may with our eye distinguish the same from the sea water which Leander think to have be bring by pipe under the earth to these house of the old roman near this place be the ruin of many building now call Belgeimano which the emperor Tiberius be say to have build when hereturned with triumph from the german war between the rock that compass this sea be the way Attellane which lead those that pass to Rome to the way of appin and there be many bath for most of the water be medicinal near the lake of Avernus upon the side towards Pozzoli lie a mountain q which late break out of the earth where of old be the bath of Tripergola whence the dwelling in this part and this place be call Tripergola and here of old be many large and stately building but by reason of many earthquake and robery of pirate the house be long since forsake and at last in the year 1538 be swallow up by the earth for in that year upon Michaelmas day be a terrible earthquake in this place which break out with fire in great flame cast up stone with a great tempest of wind and darkness of the air so as the people think the world end be come and at this time the ash of this fire be carry by the wind to place twenty mile distant at last after seven day this confusion cease and then the aforesaid mountain break out of the bowel of the earth be first see be three mile high and at the bottom four mile compass upon the top of this mountain be a hole some fistie pace broad which towards the bottom grow more and more narrow where it seem round and of little compass have a clear water yet give a stink of brimstone and this hole be like a theatre make by art in the foresay fearful earthquake cause by the break out of the vapour enclose under the hollow earth many famous bath be lose and no more see not far hence be the mountain of Christ so call because they say that Christ with the squadron of the father pass this way when he ascend from Hell but the french gentleman Villamont worthy judge this to be fabulous and likewise the miracle of the crucifex here bear the mark of Christ yet do he give too much credit to the miracle of Loreto upon the shore of the creek of r bay lie the lake as Virgil say of the foul stink Avernus this lake be a natural haven but be not use because the haven of lucrinus be between it and the sea it be compass with high hill on all side but only where the sea enter on the southside at a passage fifty pace broad and the form of it be round and the hill that compass it now seem pleasant but of old be all cover with a thick wood which shut up the air and by the shadow draw many bird to it be think to be the cause that these bird stifle with the smell of brimstone fall sudden dead till the emperor Augustus cause the wood to be destroy and of the bird thus kill the lake be call Avernus for this smell of brimstone and the shadow of the foresay wood darken the lake and the black colour of the water and because the sun be shut out from the lake by the hill this lake be feign by the poet to be one of the lake of hell Leander write of a fountain here the water whereof no man will drink because they think it come from hell derive by the heat of Phlegeton whereupon a oracle be build here as in a place consecrate to Pluto and the cymerian live here in a cave enter this place when they have sacrifice to the god for the soul of the dead Leander also say that they use to sacrifice man in this place and name elpenor sacrifice by Ulysses for he understand Homer to mean this place and also misenus sacrifice by Aeneas though Virgil write that he die here some will have this lake to be the famous fen of Acheron of which Virgil write the dark fen of Acheron pour out this also affirm and show that this lake come from the infernal river Acheron so call as without joy but Leander show that Acheron feign by the poet to be a river of hell be a river of Calabria and that there be another river of that name in Greece they say that the water of this lake Avernus seem black because it have no bottom but Leander affirm that some by a long rope find the bottom to be three hundred and sixty fathom deep the hill that compass Avernus be very steep with a head long fall whereupon Virgil say the descent of Avernus be easy under the hill towards the west side be a cave which they call the cave of Sibylla of Cuma and among many room there be one in which she be say to have attend her devotion but Leander think this place to have be a sweat bath of this cave Virgil thus write horren Antrum and in Antrum etc. of dreadful Sibyl the far distant rite to the vast cave he go and after a huge den cut out in the euboyan rock vast side etc. whence rush so many voice Sibyl answer from these hill to the neighbour city they say the earth be all hollow with cave under it and that the cimmerian of old dwell under a hill towards the seashore and Leander think that cave to have belong to they and sure whether it belong to they or any old prophet or to the prophetess Sibilla or who work soever it be the wonderful art and huge expense therein do plain appear these cimmerian of old do lead stranger under the earth to the oracle and be digger in mine and repute to have the spirit of divination whereupon the king give they pension for reveal secret unto he these man never see the sun but come abroad only in the night whence be the proverb of cimmerian darkness and the fiction of the poet that they do lead stranger to the court of Pluto they write that these have deceive the king by false divination be by he destroy upon the hill of Avernus they show the ruin temple of Mercury and another temple of Apollo little break down Nero begin a ditch to be make from the lake Avernus to Ostia to avoid the trouble of go by sea from the say lake there be a sluice of old into the lake lucrinus by which when there be any flood of the sea the water pass out of lucrinus into Avernus lake but this be now stop since the foresay earthquake of Tripergula the lake lucrinus be so call in latin of the gain make by fish sold. Suetonius write that julius Caesar let in the sea to this lake as also into the other for the senate of Rome make great gain of the fish sell here till the sea do once break in with such force as the fish go out of these lake at the ebb of the sea do thereupon command Caesar to give remedy thereunto which he do raise bank against the sea at which time he make a passage for the fish out of one lake into another we give a clown three for lead we through the cave of Sibylla upon the sea shore lie the bath common call of Cicero which the physician call the bath of Tritoli of a latin word for rub the letter f be change into t and this bath lie near the ruin of the village of Cicero call his academy I know not whether this village or rather palace have the name of academy or no for I find in my note a village of Cicero in the way from Naples to Pozzoli and likewise the mention of this bath of Cicero and his academy near the lake of Avernus and Leander mention a village of he in both place but Villamont speak of a village near Pozzoli and of a palace in this place call academy and these differ not much from my note but other confound the village and the bath put both together so as write of these intricate cave under the earth myself be fall into a labyrinth wherein I have much rather die then go back to Naples for search the truth we enter this bath Tritoli and give a clown one Poalo for conduct we the passage to enter be straight and extend far under the mountain and there be a mark set which they say no man ever pass we do sweat extreme yet I desire to come to that mark till at last feel my spirit begin to fail i I be glad to return and to creep upon the earth where the air be more cold than above they say that this bath be very healthful and much frequent in the spring time and that Nero have of old a palace build over it near this lie the ruin of Baulos or Boaulia name of the ox steal by Geryon for here be the temple of Hercules and Servius expound Virgil say that Aeneas do here speak with Hercules Leander write that Hortensius do here make cistern wherein he keep his so much prise lampreyes Tacitus and Suetonius in the life of Nero make mention of this place for Agrippina mother of Nero pass by water from the village of Piso to this Baulos be of purpose and by the command of Nero put into a rot boat that she may be drown which boat split in the midst of the passage Agrippina perceive the intent and silent the being dark slip into another boat and so for that time escape but her waiting-maide be in great danger and cry out that she be mother to Nero find death by that name by which she hope to save her life be present strike into the water by one of the conspirator at last when wicked Nero resolve to kill his mother he invite she to a feast entertain her love on the sea shore and when she return out of show of duty attend she to this Baulos lie between the Misene promontory and the lake of ray but at the same time he command that she shall be kill and here under the earth we do see her sepulchre in a cave curious carve and one of the sine old monument I do ever see hence we pass to s bay a ancient city and for the sweetness prefer to Rome by Horace no place of Rome sweet bay do excel the situation of this city be most sweet but all the house near the shore be drown except the bath and the house upon the mountain be all ruin neither do any dwell here but some few poor and miserable people such as the husbandman of Italy be common yet these ruin show the pride and magnificence of that old time this city be say to have the name of a friend of Ulysses there bury here be the foresay ruin of Caligula his bridge which I say do lie on this side the creek here we do see the stately ruin of two senator house where the excellent picture do yet remain upon the high roof they show we a tree as they say turn into a stone and the ruin of the temple of Diana and Venus from hence we walk towards the mountain misenus and near the dead sea first we come to t a hill make hollow by the build under it which be vulgar call of the number of the room cento that be one hundred little chamber Leander say that it be a cistern to keep freshwater whereof the roman have great store in these part whether they come certain season of the year to recreate themselves and all this territory on both side near this creek or bay of the sea be so full of ruin palace temple and sepulcher as a man will say they be not several village but one great city this say building be large and four square and sustain by four rank of four square pillar into which we be let down at a hole in the earth round about the entrance there be many cell almost four square and of a unequal bigness part with entery wind about and because the building be intricate some think it be a labyrinth five the ruin of a stately building be opposite to this into which we descend by forty stair it have no window but all the light come in at cranny and it have four rank of foursquare pillar to bear up the arch roof every rank have twelve pillar and in all they be forty eight and each one be twelve foot distant from the other and twelve foot high to which if you add the high roof of the building the room be twenty five foot high which I behold not without be amaze at the magnisicence of the roman in these building this house be little break down and the plaster of the wall be so hard as I can not pierce it with my dagger and it be vulgar call it be certain that the roman of old bestow great charge in build place for the keep of fish and some think this be build to that purpose by Antonia the wife of Drusus other say by Hortensius but Leander say that it be build to keep fresh water and he with other writer do judge it a stately monument of the palace of Lucullus build near bay which he prove out of Plutarch who mention one palace of Lucullus in his foresay village for his summer dwell and another here near bay for his winter abode and Tacitus say that the emperor Tiberius foresee his death and often change place at last come to this place and here die it be a infinite work if I shall several describe the palace of Marius Caesar and Lucullus I will not omit that our guide I know not how credible show we certain round with field compass round with mountain and at this time plough which they say be the elysian field we be now come to the 10 Misene promontory which have the name of misenus friend to Aeneas bury here or rather by he sacrifice to the god at the lake Avernus as be aforesaid upon the top of this mountain be a tower of old call faro upon which a light be hang for a sea-mark under the mountain especial where it grow narrow and upon three side be wash by the sea there be so many house under the earth as the pillar thereof seem only to bear up the mountain and among they there be one call grotta Traconara of the wind passage therein which by the ruin now remain seem to have be a magnificent work and this Leander think to have be build to keep fresh water right opposite to this mountain be the a'n cape of Minerva and near that lie the island k Caprea or Capre easy to be see by the white and high cliff and famous by the cruelty and more than goatish lust of the emperor Tiberius when he withdraw himself out of the sight of the senate and people of Rome to live there in solitude this island have no haven neither can little boat land there whereupon be safe from pirate it be hold a place of pleasure in the time of Augustus the creek of the sea come in between these two foresay promontory be of old call sinus upon the side of the mountain Misene lie towards Cuma be a lake of salt water call the dead sea into which water fall our of the creek of rozzol and it be of old more large for Suetonius write that Augustus keep one navy in this lake and another at Ravenna to guard the upper and low sea and Tacitus write that his successor Tiberius keep two navy in those place at this day the lake be part from the sea with a bank some fifty pace broad and it be almost round in form and some two mile broad and Plutarch write that Lucullus make this lake to keep fish therein from this mountain Misene we walk upon the sea shore five very short mile and come to the ruin of the old city z Cuma build by the calcedon of the greek island Euboia and the old city in all Italy and it be say to have have the name of a good presage from the captain of the navy or a woman great with child of that name it be seat of old upon a hill near the sea shore and yet on the side towards the land the wall be stand but the daughter have devour the mother for the increase of Naples be the decrease of Cuma yet the ruin still remain and upon the top of the hill be the temple of Apollo of which Virgil write at Apollo etc. but good Aeneas high apollee tower etc. and there be yet a ancient temple partly ruin a triumphal arch be yet unbroken but some say the foresay temple be consecrate to Hercules of old Aristodamus do lead the force of Cuma and after his victory they make he their prince and Livy write that the proud be banish come to he and there die historian write that Drusus make a ditch from this shore towards Capua in the hill or mountain of Cuma there be a labyrinth under the earth and from this hill we do see diverse land near the Land the poet fable that in one of they call f Nisa the witch Calypso dwell that in the second call g Procida the giant Typheus be bury because of the flame that sometime break out of the earth the three be call the H island of saint Martin the four be call ay Ischia wherein the king of Naples have a strong castle to which the king flee for a time when the french king Charles the eight take Naples we walk along this shore of the sea to the tower call r be sixteen mile from Naples eight mile from bay and five mile from Cuma it be seat in a pleasant place and upon the westside have a lake call by the name of the tower and the river Vulturnus run into the sea on the southside the sea be near and upon the east and north side it have pleasant field and hill at this day there be no building stand but the say tower and a poor miserable inn to lodge passenger but among the shrub there be many ruin of house and of a bridge and this place be of old call Linternum whether Scipio the african retire into voluntary banishment to fly the envy of the ungrateful roman and there he build he a stately palace and a sepulchre in which he will be bury say that the ungrateful roman shall not have so much as his bone Livy in his twenty two book call Linternum a sandy soil beyond Vulturnus from Rome but Leander think that he speak this of the territory not of the place itself and that the rather because in his twenty three book he write that Sempronius the consul do lead the force to beyond Vulturnus and there do agree in the situation thereof with all writer and the sharp fountain like vinegar whereof Pliny write be find among these ruin which water he say make they drunken that drink thereof though other write that they have take it moderate without any such effect Pliny also write that this water moderate take have the virtue to cure the headache while Scrpio live here in solitude Livy and Plutarch write that certain bold and valiant pirate upon the fame of his virtue come to see the face and hear the word of so great a captain Livy in his thirty eight book write that he do see two sepulchre of Scipio this at Linternum and the other at Rome near the gate capena both deck with carve image and that these verse be write upon his tomb at Linternum Annibale Carthagine and Europa Africa quondam Hannibal foil Carthage sack and the empire enlarge thy ash in this marble lie who Europe or Africa never make retire how short a chest hold see man vanity Leander think that Scipio be bury in this place aswell because Livy write it as for the word of Scipio relate by Valerius Maximus that his ungrateful country shall not have so much as his bone and he think that the monument at Rome be either build by Scipio in the time of his prosperity or by his friend long after in memory of so worthy a kinse man as we walk from Cuma to Linternum we do see no memorable thing but take this journey only out of desire to see the monument of this famous man neither do we know the danger from banish man in this place who often resort to this poor inn yet for that cause this way from Naples to Rome more commodious than the other and therefore have postmaster appoint there for public affair have long be forsake by passenger this way to Rome be thus distinguish into mile from Naples to sixteen mile to Rocca fourteen to Fratta eighteen to ponte Curto ten to Capetano eight to Frusalone eighteen to three to Montone twenty two to la Ficha fourteen to Rome eight there be no house at Linternum but the foresay base inn and there we lodge and find not our supper answerable to the fruitfulness of Campania neither have we any bed and can hardly get clean straw which inconvenience be accompany with the fear to be surprise by the banish man so as we sleep not one wink that night here we do see two tower one compass with water and near the tower we do see the ruin of a stately palace which they say be the palace of Scipio and that he be bury there also we do see a pillar upon which be the arm engrave of the king of Spain and Naples and we do see the ruin of a bridge which show the old magnificence thereof but there be nothing to be see that may countervail the danger we have run our journey the day before from Naples to bay be very pleasant through most fruitful hill of corn and vinc but from Cuma to this tower the way upon the sea shore be wild and barren yet not far distant within land we may fee most pleasant and fruitful hill when we have pass a night without sleep at Linternum we return early in the morning to Naples by the same way we come but with a more right and there I make no stay because England then have war with Spain but take the next opportunity to return to Rome with the carrier after the same fashion I come hither and I pay to my Vetturine fifty two for my horse and horse-meat and my own diet from Naples to Rome and beyond my covenant to gratify he I be content to pay for my diet the first and last meal which I promise of my own free will yet shall have be force thereunto for otherwise he will have carry i fast to Rome and have give i slender diet at Capua be a plentiful place and I observe the other passenger to doe the like in these place where they be out of danger I pass over the journey which I have describe before and will only say in a word that we return to Rome where that I may stay with more security to see the antiquity Rome thereof it happen very fit that the cardinal Allan a Englishman have use to persecute the english come thither and therefore be ill speak of by they have change his mind since the english have overthrow the spanish navy in the year 1588. and there be now small hope of reduce England to papistry and therefore to gain his countryman love do not only mislike that they shall be entrap at Rome but do himself protect they though suspect for religion so they will seek his favour whereof I be advertise by the experience of other when I have in silence and through many danger see Naples subject to the king of Spain and be now return to Rome I present go to the say cardinal and after the fashion have kiss the hem of his vesture I humble desire that according to this his courtesy for which he be much honour in England he will receive i into his protection till I may view the antiquity of Rome he be of a goodly stature and countenance with a grave look and pleasant speech bid i rest secure so I can command my tongue and shall abstain from oftence only for his duty sake he say that he must advise i and for the love of his country entreat i that I will be willing to hear those instruction for religion here which I can not hear in England I submit myself to these condition and when after due reverence make I will have go away the english gentleman and priest there present overtake i in the next room among these be a Englishman a priest of Calabria who in my journey from Naples hither have be my consort by the way at the table and even in bed who I have often hear talk with the italian of english affair but more modest and honest then any man will expect of a Priest he take myself and one master warmington a english gentleman by the hand with a aftonished look do congratulate with i that I who have be his companion at bed and board and who he have take rather for any countryman be now become a english man all the rest commend my judgement in come to the cardinal and inquire after my lodging promise to be my guide in Rome and for country sake to do i a good office and so after mutual salutation I go from they I well know that such guide will be very troublesome to i for they according to the manner dispute of religion I must either seem to consent by silence or maintain argument full of danger in that place beside that to gratify they for their courtesy I must needs have run into extraordinary expense therefore have tell they my lodging I present change it and take a chamber in a vitling house in the marketplace close under the pope palace where I think they or any else will least seek i and so be free from that burden and yet secure in the cardinal promise protection I begin bold yet with as much haste as I possible can make to view the antiquity of Rome the description Farnese Rome be situate on the east side of Tiber may further be distinguish into three part seat on the west side of Tiber whereof the first be call ay i will borgo and it contain the pope palace compass with high wall by pope Nicholas the five and the garden thereof which of the fair prospect be call Beluedere and the library and the church of saint Peter in vaticano and the field or marketplace lie before the church and the strong castle saint Angelo all which be compass with wall by pope Leo the four and for a time this part be of he call Leonina but now it be call borgo the second part be call 2 trastevere that be beyond the Tiber and be call of old I anicolo of the mountain include therein and also be call the city of the man of Ravenna of the soldier which Augustus keep at Raucnna against Anthony and after place they here and because the air be unwholesome as the wind be that blow here from the south it be only inhabit by artisan and poor people and at this day it be compass with wall which seem ancient save that it lie open towards the Tiber and Rome and it be adorn with church and building but much sever one from the other the three part be call 3 Isola that be a island of Tiber which of old be call Licaonia of the temple of jupiter of Licaonta when Tarqvinius the proud be of old banish from Rome the people abhor to convert the good of such a wicked man to private use do make his ground a field for train of soldier and call it Campus Martius and the senate command the great store of his corn chaff and straw to be cast into the Tiber of which matter grow together they say this island first come after a temple be build in this island to Esculapius bring hither from Epidaurus in the shape of a serpent and the isle be consecrate to he be then make in the form of the ship that bring that serpent whereof there be a monument in the garden of saint Bartholomew namely a stone in the form of a ship with a serpent grave upon it it be a quarter of a mile in length and some fifty pace in breadth and it be full of stately church and house if you draw a line from the east-side of the mountain Capitolino 22 to the gate 4 lie towards the north and from the say mountain draw aline to the further part of the bridge upon the west side of the island of Tiber this compass may true be call Rome as at this day it be inhabit for the rest lie wild have only ruin and some scatter church and house and towards the south field of corn within the wall they say that Romulus do only build upon three mountain the palatine the capitoline and the celian yet other add the Esquiline and that he compass they with wall and that he build the gate Carmentalis so call of the mother of evander which lie under the capitol upon the right hand between the rock tarpeius and the river Tiber and be also call the curse gate of the 300 fabius which go out of the same to fight and be all kill in one day and that he build the roman gate lie near the mount Palatine towards the amphitheatre call and the gate Pandana so call because it be always open after seven mountain be enclose Rome have eight gate and after thirty four as Livy write and at last thirty seven gate at this day the first gate be call 4 lie on the east-side of Tiber towards the north which of the river be of old call Flumentana and of the way of Flaminius to which it do lead be call flaminia the second gate be call V Pinciana of a senator of that name and of old be call Collatina of a palace adjoin and it be a mile distant from the former gate the three gate be call 6 la Salarta of salt bring in that way and be of old call quirinalis of the temple or the mountain adjoin of the same name and also call of a mountain or as have no corner and also call Collina of a hill and it be less than a mile distant from the last name gate the four gate be call 7 la Pia of pope Pius the four who repair it and the way without it and it be more than half a mile distant from the last name gate at this day it be many time call saint Agnese of a church lie near it and it be of old call of oseyre grow there and also call Figulensis of potter dwell there and also call of old of a castle I will omit the gate of old call because almost no ruin thereof remain at this day the five gate be call 8 di s. Lorenzo of the church near it it be of old call tiburtina though other think that gate be near to Tiber on this side and esquilina of a place near it and Taurina of a bull head which still be grave upon it and it be a mile and a half distant from the last name gate the six gate be call 9 Maggiore and be of old call Nevia and labicana and praenestina the seven be call 10 dt s. Giovanni and it be of old call Caelemontana of a mountain and Quercotulana of a oak and Settimia and the eight be call 11 Latina of Latium to which it lead and be of old call and be more than a mile distant from the seven gate the nine from the eight more than half a mile distant be call 12 di s. Sebastiano of the church to which it lead and be of old call capena of the city or river of that name and also Camena of a church and Appia of the way which Appius the censor pave and Fontinale of the fountain and some write it be call trionfale for part of the triumph that do enter there and the brother of the Horatij escape in the fight against the brother Curiatij do return at this gate without the same be the sepulchre of Scipio the african whereof I speak describe Linternum near Naples where he will be bury far from his ungrateful country the ten gate be call 13 di s. Paolo of the church whither it lead and be of old call trigemina of the 3 Horatij go out there and call ostiensis as lead to Ostia where Tiber run into the sea and it be a mile from the (@) gate and as much distant from the river Tiber the eleven gate lie on the west side of Tiber in that part of the city which I say be call 2 trastevere and be distant from Tiber half a quarter of a mile be call 14 and be of old call portuensis as lead to the haven of Rome make by the emperor Claudius the twelve gate almost a mile distant from the former be call 15 di s. Pancratio and of old be call Aurelia of Aurelius the emperor or of the way Aurelia and of other call Pancratiana and it lie near the mountain the thirteen gate half a mile distant from the former be call 16 Settimiana of the emperor Settimius who name be engrave upon it and it be repair by pope Alexander the six some think this gate be call Fontinale other Festinale and it be the last gate in trasievere the fourteen gate be call 17 di s. Spirito and it be the first in that part of the city call ay borgo the fifteen gate be call in the map 18 but I find it call by writer deal Torrione and Posterula and to be repair by pope Nicholas the five the sixteen gate be call 19 be neete to the pope stable the seventeen be call 20 di Beluedere lie near the pope palace and 3 garden and it be call in some map Angelica and by other giulia of the pope Giulius the eighteen be call 21 di s. Angelo and delCastello of the castle s Angelo and it be of old call enea and more late di Cenello I pass over the gate call of old Trionfante where the great triumph do enter because no ruin remain thereof but only it be say to have be seat near the triumphal bridge 36 it remain to speak of the way lead to Rome which I will note with the letter of the gate lead to they and first I will only name the way that be within the wall the first la Suburra begin at the amphitheatre call Coliseo 20 and lead to the church saint Lucia in Orsia the second lie from the arch of Constantine 21 to the arch of Vespasian 22 through forum romanum 23 to the capitol 22 the three nuova do lead from the great palace in mount Palatine 23 to the bath of Antonius in mount Aventine 24 the four la trionfale do lead from the mount vaticano 29 to the capitol in the mount 22 Capitolino the five be in the Martius where be Traiano 10 the friar Leander describe Rome name twenty nine way within and without the wall namely 1. Appia 2. Latina 3. labicana 4. campana 5. praenestina 6. cumana 7. flaminia 8. Cassia 9 tiburtina 10. Collatina 11. nomentana 12. salaria 13. Emilia 14. Portuesen 15. Cornelia 16. Claudia 17. Aureliana and upon these way he relate many stately palace build out of the city now I will note the way without the gate by the same letter by which I have note the gate lead to they among these the most famous be the way of Appius call the queen of way most part of the chief triumph enter that way it begin at the gate of saint 12 Sebastian and be pave to Capua and then divide into two way that on the left hand lead to Brundisium and that on the right hand lead to Pozzoli and to Cuma have stately palate on all side and it have the name of Appius Claudius the censor in this way two mile from the city the roman build a temple in memory of Hannibal who encamp there be force to raise his siege with disgrace the way of be no less famous which lie from the pillar of Antoninus 11 to the gate 4 deal and do lead to rim. upon the adriatic sea and part of it be call Quincia and it be join with the way call Claudia and of old be call the large way where the way of Flaminius end there begin the way AEmilia make by his fellow consul Aemilius Lepidus lead to Bologna and pave to the very Alps yet there be another way of the same name near pisa. the way Collatina be without the gate five Pinciana the way salaria without the gate 6 salaria the way without the gate 8 saint Lorenzo the way praenestina without the gate 9 Maggiore on the left hand or east-side and the way labicana on the whereof I speak in my journey from Rome to Naples to conclude the way Latina be without the gate 11 Latina the way ostiensis without the gate saint 13 Paolo the way Aurelia without the gate 15 saint Pancratio which if I be not deceive be call also the way Vitelia pave from the mount janiculo to the Sea but who will not wonder that from the gate 19 the way shall lead into the valley of hell for so it be call close to the holy seat of the pope Rome be of old call of seven hill or little mountain contain within the wall namely capitolinus palatinus Auentinus Celius esquilinus and quirinalis hereof the first and chief be 22 capitolinus of old call Saturnius of the city Saturnia and tarpeius of the virgin Terpeia which betray her country to the sabine give they entrance at that place be for reward there kill by they and at last in the reign of Tarquin the proud it be call capitolinus of a head dig out of the ground at this day it be vulgar call Capidoglio it be divide into two part namely the and the rock tarpeius lie on the northside of the hill and it have sixty church whereof the chief be of old dedicate where the triumpher use to give thanks for victory and to offer rich spoil to jupiter and it be adorn with the stately build of the and with many stately palace of noble man the second mount be call 23 palatinus of Palantus grandfather to evander among many diverse opinion follow Virgil at this day it be vulgar call Palazzo Maggiore and it be a mile in circuit but be not at all inhabit and upon the side lie towards the Circus they show a little house in the place where Romulus dwell only preserve in memory of he and upon this mount catilina and Catullus and Cicero do dwell the three mountain be call 24 Auentinus of bird by who flight they use to prophecy or of the king Auentinus and it be of old call Romorio of a place in the top where they observe the flight of the bird and it have two mile in compass the four mount be 25 Celius of Celius king of Hetruria and be of old call Querquetulanus of a wood of Oakes and a little mountain be part of it be vulgar call where I shall show the church of saint John the evangelist to be seat upon this mountain be the house of Scipio the african near the church of saint George the five mountain be call 26 esquilinus or Esquilia of the guard of the praetorian soldier or of the fragment of meat cast there to feed hawk it reach from the marketplace of trajan to the bath of Dioclesian and the gate of s. of the mount Esquiline but all writer general reckon it among the seven hill and it have the name of the church of jupiter so call of the oseyer grow there and they say Marcus Crassus dwell upon this mountain the seven mount be call 28 quirinalis of the quirite or senator dwell there or of the spear of juno and it be also call at this day it be vulgar call cavallo there be two other mountain beyond the Tiber which be seat in Toscany not in Latium for Leander make all the place most near to Rome on this side to belong to Hetrurta the first of these mount be call 29 vaticanus of the cry of a infant or of a god of that name as Gellius write which god be the god of prophecy pope Leo the four compass this hill and the next field with wall and call it Leonina but at this day it be call borgo the other mount beyond Tiber be call 30 I of I dwelling and bury there and be now vulgar call Mortorio within the wall of Rome there be some other hill or little mountain but less famous the first be call 31 Pincius and vulgar the li reach to the gate salaria the second be call 32 Citorius and of old of the tribe descend from thence to choose magistrate as those that be suitor to be magistrate descend from the mount Pincius and go into Campus Martius the three mount less and more obscure be call 33 I or of the family Orsini who at this day have their palace upon it the four mountlesse and obscure be call 34 of earthen pot for the potter of old dwell there and the image and vessel of the temple be for the most part of earth and when the dead body be burn the ash be lay up in these vessel whereupon a heap grow to a hill and a hill to a Mount many do false think that it have the name of such vessel in which tribute be bring to Rome and then the vessel be break here of old eight bridge be build over Tiber among which be reckon pons Miluius vulgar ponte mole without the gate 4 more than a mile distant from Rome and near this bridge Constantine the great under the sign of the cross do overcome the tyrant maxentius also this bridge be famous for the night lust of Nero the second bridge be call 35 di Castel' Sant' Angelo and it be of old call Elius of the emperor Elius adrianus who build it but pope Nicholas the five build it as now it stand and set upon it the image of saint Peter with his key and of saint Paul with his sword the three bridge be call 36 vaticanus as lead to that mount and be also of old call of the triumph pass upon it and it be not lawful for the country people to enter that way but at this day only the ruin thereof be see the four bridge be call 37 ponte-sisto of pope sixtus the four who repair it it be of old call janiculonsis of that mount and Aurelius of the way of that name and it be build of marble by Antoninus Pius and after be decay be long call ponte Rotto that be the break bridge till the say pope repair it in the year 1475. and it be two hundred and fifteen foot broad and be build upon three arch of stone the
