Bartolomeo and it be opposite to the five bridge and join the island with that part of Rome call trastevere and of old it be call and cestius and it be sixty foot long have but one arch of stone the eight bridge at the foot of the mount Aventine be of old call 41 because it be build of wood in the war with the tuscans that it may be more easy break and repair and we read that the tuscans be victor have take Rome if Horatius Cocles have not defend the bridge till it be break down behind he which do he save himself by swim after that emilius Lepidus build this bridge of stone and call it emilius and when it be break with flood first the emperor Tiberius repair it and then Antoninus Pius build it very high of marble and condemn man be cast from it into the water this bridge be the first that be build over Tiber now be not to be see by any ruin Rome by the great power of the emperor and since of the pope have be long most famous and be first build in Latium upon Tiber fifteen mile from the tyrrhene sea as the greek write by Ascanius Eurilantes Romulus and Remus nephew to Aeneas or as other greek write by the or as other greek write by the son of Roma a woman of Troy marry to the latin king of the Aborigenes which son be Romulus and Remus or as xenagoras write by the son of Ulysses by Circe to omit many other opinion of the greek the latin historian do no less vary some say it be build by the son of Aeneas namely Romulus and Remus other say that Ascanius build Alba and Remus build Capua and Romulus build janiculum after call Rome but I omit these diverse opinion and will follow Leander the friar who say that Rome the daughter of the king in Italy build Rome the same year that Moses be bear and when the city have be long forsake for the unwholesome air of the fen adjoin that evander come from Arcadia into Italy seat himself upon the mount Palatine and build a city call Palantium of his city in Arcadia and he be dead that Hercules come with a army leave some of his consort here who build upon the mount of Saturnius after call capitolinus before the destruction of Troy for the unwholesome air Rome be again forsake that the albani begin to dwell there in cottage and seed their flock there for by the continual over flow of Tiber the field be make fenny and the air unwholesome but historian write that upon sacrifice make to God Vertuno these fen by little and little be dry up he add that amulius take the kingdom of the albani from his brother numiter who daughter rhea a vestal virgin be great with child by amulius Mars or any other bring forth Romulus and Remus and amulius leave they by Tiber to perish in the water but a she wolf say they and after faustulus overseer of all the flock and cattle of amulius take they home who come to ripe year kill amulius and restore their grandfather Numitor to his kingdom but themselves be desirous to build a city upon the mount Palatine at the foot whereof they have be cast out Romulus draw with a plough the circuit of the city of a quadrangular form in the 430. year after the destruction of Troy and in the year of the world 3211. he give mount Celius to be inhabit by Celius king of Toseany aid he against the sabine and then take by force the sabine woman and they make peace between they he give to Tatius and his sabine for their dwelling the mountain capitolinus and quirinalis and to his brother Remus the mount Auentinus and keep for himself and his man the mountain palatinus and esquilinus till the rest be dead himself alone become lord of all the seven rock be of old call seven hill have a pleasant plain between they and Tiber and this circuit be in form of a bend bow the Tiber stand for the string romu'us make the city four square but he be dead Ancus Martius enclose the mount beyond Tiber and Servius enclose other mount on this side of the Tiber six king reign two hundred forty three year in Rome and Torquine be banish it become a popular state wherein consul yearly choose do govern and eight hundred eighty seven consul in four hundred sixty four year by forty three battle obtain the empire almost of the whole world in the mean time the that be ten man rule for two year and the tribune for military affair have consular power rule forty three year and in the time of any difficult war a dictator be choose who with absolute power rule till that business be end and there be no magistrate for four year at last julius Caesar with the title of perpetual dictator invade the empire which be after divide into the oriental and occidental empire and the occidental be destroy by the incursion of barbarous nation the bishop of Rome by little and little cast their oriental lord out of Italy and erect a new occidental empire in France that they may invade the power of the roman emperor and of the heavenly jurisdiction upon earth under pretext of religion by a new monster of a roman wit draw from the supremacy of the apostle saint Peter Pliny in his time make the circuit of Rome twenty mile and vopiscus in the time of the emperor Aurelius make the circuit fifty mile but he join to Rome all the neighbour village at this day if you add to Rome the two part beyond Tiber call trastevere and borgo the circuit at the most be fifteen mile for other say thirteen or fourteen beside that a very great part of this circuit within the wall be not inhabit and the wall not withstand lie not upon their old foundation neither be build of that matter but as it please those who repair they among which belisarius governor of Italy under the emperor justinian build Rome demolish by the goth and make the circuit of the wall less and pope Adrian the first a roman the wall be fall build they as now they stand and many of his successor have since add new ornament to decay Rome but the old wall as appear by some ruin be build of four square stone the rest be of diverse building as it please the repairer and have a brick gallery to walk upon under which man may stand dry when it rain and they be ready to fall with age have many round tower which in like sort be ready to fall Rome at this day be trouble with the old overflow of Tiber by reason of the Tiber narrow bed not able to receive the water fall sudden from near mountain after great rain or melt of snow for memory whereof these inscription be upon the wall of the church of saint Mary Minerva in the year 1530. if I be not deceive for the first word be race out the ides of October Clement the seven be pope thus far come Tiber and all Rome have drown have we not from the virgin swift help find and there in another place this verse be write in the year MUD. thus far this muddy brook water do swell in each place be a red mark upon the wall how high the water ascend by which it appear mark the seat of the church that all the plain be overflow between it and the Tiber by reason of these flood and for that the city be build upon the cave of old Rome which make the foundation to be lay with great charge and also by reason of the vapour rise from the bath the air of Rome be at this day unwholesome the roman drink rain water and the trouble water of Tiber keep in Cesternes and they brag that it be proper to the water or Tiber the long it be keep to grow more pure sure stranger do not like that water howsoever the roman make a virtue of necessity do say that it be only make good to drink at Rome and no where else by the blessing of pope Gregory the great now be to describe the antiquity of Rome I will first set down out of order the seven church famous for the indulgence of pope which they say be build by the emperor Constantine the great then I will set down the rest in due order as they be seat and because I finish in haste the view of Rome in four day I will distinguish the antiquity into four day journey the first day be to visit these seven church by reason of their distance and the have we make I and my consort hire each of we a mule each man for two and we never find our error till the evening when we demand the way of a man of mean sort he reply thus with some anger what do you ride to heaven and we poor wretch go on foot without shoe to visit these holy church by this we find our error and be glad that we have pass that day without further danger in general these church be bare on the inside without any picture except some few about the altar this day we first ride to the chief church a dt s. Giovanni Laterano seat upon mount Celius and build by Constantine the great in his palace and it have a stately font in which that emperor be baptize and in the church there be four most fair pillar of brass the church be sustain by four row of brick pillar and there hang certain banner take from the french and near the door the pope Sergius the four and Silvester the second be bury in low monument in the church yard be old sepulchre and little pillar of marble near to this church lie those holy stair whereof I speak in my journey from Rome to Naples when our italian consort go to pray for a happy journey kneel without the grate but they that will have the grate open to pray there use to creep upon their knee from stair to stair and upon each stair to say a and maria. these stair be twenty six in number divide into three row and they be of marble vulgar call scale sante and be bring from the house of Pilate in Jerusalem it be not safe for he to inquire after relic who will not worship they yet to satisfy the curious I will set down the chief by beare-say here they show a tooth of saint Peter a cup in which saint John drink poison at the command of Demitian and have no hurt the cloth with which Christ dry his disciple foot the head of Peter and Paul the rod of Aaron the ark of the covenant the table at which Christ sup three marble gate of pilate house the image of Christ be twelve year old with the like part of these they say be bring from Jerusalem by the emperor Titus yet he be no Christian nor like to regard the monument of Christ one chapel of this church be call and be think to have be the chamber of Constantine neither may any woman enter it to conclude the place be show here in which many counsel have be hold and the pope long dwell here before the palace in the Vatican be build the church of b saint Peter in the mount vaticano join to the pope palace they say it be build by Constantine the great the pope have give full remission of sin to they that pray here upon certain day and like remission for certain year sin pray on other day and the remission of the three part of all sin pray there upon saint Peter even and you must understand that all these church have some like indulgence here they show the body of saint Simeon and saint jude the apostle and saint John Chrysostome and of pope saint Gregory the great and the head of saint Andrew and of saint Luke the evangelist and half the body of saint Peter and saint Paul and Christ face print upon the handkerchief of Veronica and the head of the spear thrust into the side of Christ and among many pillar bring from Jerusalem one upon which Christ lean when he do preach and cast out devil which yet have power as they say to cast out devil always understand that in Italy priest that cast out devil be most frequent neither be they want in any place where the papist can hide their imposture great part of these relic they say be send by the turkish emperor to pope innocent the eight but I omit these thing into which none but papist may safe inquire and return to the monument which lie open to every man view the chapel be most rich in which Gregory the 13 lie and the stately sepulchre of pope Paul the three have most fair statue the statue of saint Peter of brass place under the organ be of old erect to jupiter capitolinus in the court of the church for I can call it a churchyard the emperor Otho the second lie bury in a low sepulchre of Porphry there be a most fair pineaple of brass guild more than five cubit high which they say be bring hither from the monument of the emperor Andrian in the place where the castle of saint Angelo now stand as likewise the peacock be bring from the monument of Scipio the three church of St Paul be without the 13 gate of St Paul see the four church of saint c Mary Maggiore be upon the mount Esquiline I will omit hence forward the indulgence and relic lest I be tedious this church be adorn with forty pillar of marble the rich Lorenzo be without the 8 gate of that name in the way to tiburtina something more than a mile from the city and it be say to be build by Constantine the great he that go to this church every wednesday in a whole year shall deliver a soul from purgatory if the pope keep his promise it be adorn with a pulpit of white marble and most fair ophite stone and at the door with a sepulchre of saint eustacius of white marble curious carve and another sepulchre opposite to that the six church s. Sebastiano be without the 12 gate of that name more than a mile out of the city in the way of Appius here be a place call Catacombe and there be a well in which they say the body of saint Paul and saint Peter do lie unknown a long time and here be a way under earth to the church yard of calixtus where they say the christian lav hide in the time of persecution and that there be find 174. thousand which have be make martyr and that eight of these be bishop of Rome here on all side with amazement I behold the ruin of old building and the sepulchre of the emperor Aurelius be not far from this church the seven church d di s. we begin the view of Rome with the q pope palace seat in the part of the city call Il'Borgo which palace pope Nicholas the three build and Nicholas the five compass with wall and the palace be of great circuit and the stair be so easy that horse and mule may go up to the top of the mountain and with easy ascent and descent bear the pope carriage at the entrance there be three gallery one above the other whereof the two first be build by Leo the ten and Paul the three and the three and high by sixtus Quintus and they be all fair paint and guild upon these lie two large chamber and beyond they be a vast and long gallery of four hundred seventy and one walk pace in the midst whereof be the famous library of the pope in vaticano and therein be many inscription of the pope sixtus Quintus who repair it and it be adorn with many fair picture guild all over I do fee the several room thereof the first one hundred forty and seven walk pace long have three row of cubbard fill with book the second be thirty nine pace long and the three contain the book of great price lock up be twenty pace long pope sixtus the four build this library with the chapel of the palace and the conclave the wall of the chapel shine like a glass with precious stone where the pope sixtus Quintus command Michael Angelo to paint the day of judgement and the common report be that this pope promise this famous painter that he will not come into the chapel till he have finish his work yet by some cardinal persuasion that he break his promise and that the painter thereupon make the picture of the pope and the cardinal in hell among the devil so lively as every man may know they between this chapel and the conclave where they choose the pope lie a kingly gallery not un worthy call vulgar Sala which other call Sala conclave the wall of this gallery in like sort shine with precious stone and the pavement be of precious marble the arch roof all guild and at the upper end I wonder to see the Massacro of Paris paint upon the wall with the pope inscription great commend that detestable cruelty at the same upper end the foresay chapel as you come up lie one the left hand and the conclave on the right hand in which conclave the cardinal meet to choose the pope divide into several room but meet at a common table and when they have choose he they lead he into a chapel at the low end and near the door of the say kingly gallery and place he there upon a hollow seat of marble I know not whether this be the chair in which the sex of the pope be try but I be sure it be hollow with a hole in the bottom after they put a banner out of a high window and there make know to the people the name that the pope have choose and then his arm be hang up round about this chapel at the low end of the say gallery have the name of pope Paul the three of the family of Farnese and it be little and of a round form as I remember but it be beautiful beyond imagination the image of the apostle seem to be of silver and paradise paint upon the arch roof with angel fly be the work of Michael Angelo seem to i admirable upon the other side of the say library be the private gallery of the pope look into the garden 3 plague be in Rome this church be make one of the seven in the place of saint Sebastian with all the indulgence to it belong and it always carry that title here be two fair sepulcher whereof one be for a chancellor of Milan the other for pope sixtus the five sister son both the work of Andrew Sansovine the yard of this church have a obeliske almost as fair as that near saint John Lateran which pope sixtus the five also erect concern the church from this gate to the 22 mount Capitolino the church of saint Laurence in Lucinia that of saint Silvester that of the holy apostle that of saint Marcello and that of saint mark do all give title to cardinal near the church saint Silvester be the monastery convertite that be of harlot repent and become nun the church s. marry de Rione be a monastery that be of holy miserable woman and near that be another monastery that be of woman unfortunate marry and leave in want by their husband the foresay gate be repair and beautify by pope Pius the four near it lie the most sweere vineyard of pope julius the three and his pleasant fountain cast up water two else high not far thence be a triumphal arch erect to Domitian vulgar call Portogallo from this gate towards the v gate Pinciana and near the 17 church Trinita we do see the palace of the florentine cardinal Medicis who from a cardinal become the duke of Florence by right of succession and this palace be rich and stately the stair wind so artificial as it be a beautiful sight to look in a perpendicular line from the top to the foot and upon the stair be a fair statue of Apollo hence there be a gallery open on the side towards the garden fuil of beautiful image of lion a she-wolf a ram all of white marble with other image and very fair pillar and the first garden have only flower the second in the upper part have a sweet grove and the low part be full of fruit tree there be a fountain with a brazen image of Mercury upon it upon a mount call Pernasso be many image of white marble of Pegasus of the muse and one of Cleopatra fair than that I see in the pope garden with two image of Cerberus and another monster there be two large cistern of Porphery and in a chamber be the image of a satire a nymph and a gryphon last in the grove be stair pave with carve marble with figure of fish and there be a most fair statue of Europa sit upon a bull back the outside of the grove be all of fir tree which be green in winter but the inside have most pleasant walk among row of many other kind of tree in this grove be a most sweet arbour have four roof and as it be chamber one above the other the first whereof be twenty stair from the ground whence lie a most large and most fair gallery of stone under which be a most pleasant solitary walk between two wall all set with orangetree and like fruit the large way lead to the city gate Pia be pave by pope Pius the four and have on each side a pleasant walk and be also call Pia of the same pope as the 7 gate be which he build between this gate and the next of 8 saint Laurence be of old a large park in which the people of Rome keep beast to be hunt hence we go towards the mount quirinalis vulgar 28 monte cavallo and under the most pleasant vineyard of the cardinal of ferraria be many cave and old ruin and there be a field where in honour of 16 Flora as I say former describe that marketplace the harlot of Rome keep a yearly feast and dwell in the foresay cave use to run from thence naked into this field with unspeakable liberty of speech and gesture from hence pass a little lane we ascend to 28 monte cavallo which be so call of two horse of marble set there which they write to have be give to Nero by Mithridates king of Armenia the one wrought by Phydias the other by praxitile the common people hold one of they for Bucephalus and think that Alexander hold his bridle and that they be wrought by the foresay engravers in emulation one of the other but the far different age of Alexander and the say engravers make this opinion unprobable two man be engrave who hold the bridle of the horse as if they shall lead they and some think that they be make for two horse of Diomedes which do eat man flesh and be tame by Hercules the palace of Nero be near this mount and from thence they show some ruin thereof this palace as history do witness do reach from mount Celius to mount Palatine and to the further part of mount Esquiline and it be compass with a lake have within the circuit thereof meadow vineyard wood and park and all the house be guild and thereupon be call golden it shine with ivory and precious stone and the great hall thereof do move round like the world cast out flower and odour from hence Nero see Rome burn with joy and all this building be burn in the time of trajan the emperor and when Nero have finish this house he say that he begin then first to dwell like a man upon this say mountain of the horse the pope have a stately palace which a cardinal of Ferrara build and he be dead pope Gregory the thirteen seize upon it the stair be very fair each have his pillar and the ascent be most easy I think a fair gallery can hardly be see be one hundred and twenty walk pace long there be a chamber wherein pope the five die a second wherein ambassador be hear a three in which cardinal be choose the pope study be very pleasant and so be the garden have many fountain grove labyrinth a rock artificial distil water and many most sweet arbour moreover on this mount be the most fair fountain of pope sixtus the five call the happy for hither be the water bring from the stately conduit without the gate Maggiore in the way 9 praenestina reach many mile which be build by pope sixtus the five with imperial magnificence in the year 1587. and this fountain cast out water from the mouth of four lion of white marble likewise upon the same be the image of Moses strike the rock with his rod and there be two other mouth low to cast out water and it be all engrave with the say history of Moses descend from this mount we do see in a private gentleman house a horse of brass esteem at twenty five thousand pound sterling which Henry the second have place at Paris as they say it death have not prevent he hence towards the east we go forward towards the bath of Dioclesian and by the way at the foot of the mount of the horse we do see the bath of Constantine a man can sufficient wonder at the ruin of Dioclesian bath by which it seem they be of incredible greatness and they report that this emperor compel many thousand of christian to work upon this build for many year under the earth be gate and diverse passage of unknown extent upon these bath pope Pius the four in the year 1561 build the church saint Mary of the angel and with the consent of the people of Rome give it to the Carthusian friar in the room of this bath pope Gregory the thirteen in the year 1575. build a granary for corn and the say church of saint Marie be beautify with fair pillar thirty span compass and with exquisite picture especial those two near the altar of the emperor Maximilian and his empress hence we go to the church of saint c Marie Maggiore be one of the seven church and under this church upon a hill near saint Potentiana which give the title to a cardinal of old be the novatian bath and upon mount 26 Esquiline near the church saint Laurence in Palesperna give title to a cardinal be of old the olimpike bath and the say church of saint Marie be build where the temple of Isis stand high honour of the old roman pope sixtus the five bring hither in the year 1587. the foresay obeliske from the sepulchre of Augustus near 18 saint Rocco and it be the low and the least of all the obelisk upon this mount Esquiline be a place for burn dead body but the roman be offend with the smoke Augustus give that field to Maecenas who make most famous garden there hereabout lie the chapel of saint Luke peculiar to the painter and there be a grove consecrate to juno the church of saint Anthony be little but full of fair picture and in the vineyard of the hospital be the stately church of Diana and the famous church of Mars be build where that of saint Martin now stand which give the title to a cardinal as we go from mount Esquiline to the theatre of Vespasian we see by the way a rare monument build by the say emperor or his son Titus upon mount Esquiline it have nine cistern under the ground and be vulgar call set sale of seven several chamber where of each be seventeen foot broad twelve foot high and one hundred thirty seven foot long and have four door which so answer one another as a man may see all the room at once near the church of saint Clement be the wonderful theatre of Vespasian vulgar call Coliseo in which the people be wont to see the fight of fencer the hunt of wild beast and like game and it receive one hundred and ninety other say eighty five thousand person and the outside be of old round but the inside of oual form and as high as the top of mount Celius I observe the build now to be of brick and the foundation to be sixty walk pace broad and to have four row of seat one above the other and the inside from the foundation of the seat to be eight walk pace broad and one hundred thirty and five pace long the ruin of the like theatre but less build by Statilius lie near the church d San'Croce the foresay church of saint Clement give the title to a cardinal but the wall be all ruin and this part be not inhabit neither be the street pave from thence to the church d s. Croce in memory as they say of pope joane which be great with child and have go a folemne procession from a saint John Lateran to the say church of the d holy cross as she return this way amid her pomp be deliver of a child near to the say theatre of Vespasian lie the triumphal arch erect to Constantine the great when he have overcome maxentius and it be most fair and curious carve near that be the church of saint Marie the new which give the title to a cardinal and in the garden be a monument of the idol serapis the 22 triumphal arch erect to Vespasian and Titus when he come from the destruction of jerusalem have but one arch and be less than the former erect to Constantine but it be no less adorn with their action engrave near the say church of saint Marie the new lie the vast ruin of the famous temple of peace the roof whereof be rich engrave near this be a kind of gibbet which they call of old that be the sister rafter because when the Horatij three brother have kill the three brother yet only one of the Horatij remain alive he return into the city and find his sister weep for her private loss in a day of public joy kill she with his own hand and for show of justice be condemn to pass under this rafter without further punishment in respect of his desert in that battle upon mount palatine of old be a palace now call vulgar 23 Ilpalazzo Maggiore in which most of the emperor do dwell and upon the ruin thereof the decease cardinal Farnese have a pleasant vineyard and at the foot of this mountain near the chapel of saint Theodor they say that Romulus and Remus be cast forth and nourish by a she-wolf for they say that Tiber of old do run this way though now it have change the bed the church of saint Cosmo give the title of a cardinal and there the temple of Romulus be of old seat also the temple and palace of the emperor Marcus Aurelius and his empress Faustina daughter to Antoninus Pius be in this place in the same field of old call the 11 roman market-place I say before that the temple of the vestal virgin be seat near the church saint Marie Liberatrice which be also consecrate to Venus there also in the field call Vaccino three pillar be yet remain of the ruin of the marble gallery which Caligula build and those pillar be very high and not far from they be the lake or gulf into which Curtius cast himself for his country sake the triumphal arch erect to l. Septimius Severus for his victory against the parthian be curious engrave with that war and it be the fair arch next that of Constantine and it be seat at the foot of the mount capitoline in the market place call of old forum romanum for it begin at the foot of the 22 mount capitoline and reach to the foot of 23 mount Palatine where now be the church of saint cosmo. and therein be a monument erect call Ringhiera from whence oration be make to the people and it be probable that this be the place call where Cicero make his oration to the people for antiquary agree that it be in this marketplace in which also at the foot of the capitol of old be a pillar call which show the way to all the gate and whether soever a man will go there also at this day be a church with a brazen door where be the treasury of the people of Rome the gate whereof julius Caesar break and take from thence infinite treasure near this place be the statue of jupiter other say of the river Rhine upon the head whereof stand one of the foot of Domitian horse of brass and this statue be now call Marforio and it be of marble lie at length as upon a tomb as that 14 of Pasquin stand upright against a wall and upon these two image be fasten all the libel of Rome frame in manner of dialogue this statue of Marforio be of late take up by pope Clement the eight out of the valley and place upon this mount before the Capitol as appear by the inscription beside that the place be to be see whence it be take upon this mount 22 Capitoline of old be seat the famous Capitol and now there be the senate house the building whereof have no magnificence between two senate house be a yard in which lie the rock tarpeia from the top whereof condemn man be cast of old in the fore-yard of the Capitol be a horsemans statue of brass guild which be erect to the emperor Marcus Aurelius and for the engravers art be hold of inestimable price fron this place great part of Rome lie open to the eye in a sweet prospect the foresay statue be set in this place by pope Paul the 3 in the year 1538. thereby lie a foot statue of white marble and the fore say monument 9 which pope sixtus the five in the year 1590. remove to this place from the old monument erect to Marius there also be the foresay statue of 23 Marforio which be take away by the consul of Rome from the first place and place here by pope Clement the eight here also be the pillar of combat and a most pleasant fountain build by pope sixtus the four where lie two ancient image of white marble of two river Tigris and Nilus there be also three pillar bring from the temple of concord where the old senator of Rome do meet within the Capitol or senate house itself we do see many most fair antiquity namely statue erect to julius Caesar to Octavius to Augustus and to Marius seven time consul the image of Hercules of brass guild over which be dig out of the ruin of the temple of Hercules in the time of pope sixtus the four also the image of Hercules his son of jupiter of Minerva and of Ceres all of marble a head of brass upon which be engrave junius m. Brutus two statue of young man whereof one stand upright in the habit of a servant the other be naked have one foot above the other knee with a needle in his hand to pull a thorn out of it and both be of brass guild over and of admirable beauty the image of Aventine dig out of mount Aventine a brazen image of a she wolf which give suck to Romulus and Remus and it be make of fine impose upon usurer a brazen statue of Aeneas a brazen statue erect to pope sixtus the five for repress the banish man another of marble erect to pope Leo the ten we do see in the hall of judgement within this Capitol the statue of marble erect to pope Gregory the thirteen to pope Pavi the three and to king Charles make a senator of Rome upon the sight of these a gentleman tell we that by a law he be make infamous who shall make mention of erect a statue to any pope while he live in the foreyard of the Capitol we do see the fragment of a huge and upon the wall near the stair the triumph of Marcus Aurelins engranen in square marble stone there we do also see marble fragment dig up under the arch of Settimius upon which be engrave the name of consul dictator and censor and under the very porch the head of a emperor the pillar of naval fight the sepulchre of Settimius Alexander Severus and of his mother julia Mammea bring hither from the field of Fabricius the image of Minerva certain image of brass of the monster Sphynx the bone entomb of Agrippina niece to Augustus and wife to Germanicus the image of a lion devour another beast and certain ancient vessel to keep wine upon this mountain of old be sixty temple but of all other the temple of jupiter Maximus build by Tarquin the proud be most esteem by the old roman from this mountain we ascend by 128. broad stair of marble bring hither from the temple of Quirinus upon Canalle to the church of saint Maria ara build upon the ruin of the temple of jupiter Feretrius and of the palace of the emperor Augustus and upon these stair be two statue erect to the emperor Constantine and the church itself be adorn with many marble pillar and stone and a pulpit of porphyry and it have the name of a altar which they show there erect to the son of the virgin by Augustus who they say have read in the oracle of that a virgin shall bring forth a son the arch roof of the church be rich guild and here pope Pius the four consecrate the spoil take from the turk in the naval victory of he and his confederate the memory whereof be there keep by a inscription upon a pillar also the senate and people of Rome have here erect a table to pope Paul the three upon which be write in golden letter that while he be cardinal he erect here many ancient monument dig up in Rome at his own charge on the other side of the mount capitoline towards the west as I remember at the very foot of the mountain be the Tullian prison appoint for that use by the old king Ancus martin and Tullus in which they say the holy apostle Peter and Paul be imprison and at this day there be a church under the earth call saint Peter in career the 24 theatre of Marcellus son to the sister of Augustus be near the palace of the family Savilla not far thence towards the fish market be the of Octavia sift to Augustus but no ruin now remain thereof the 25 palace of the cardinal Farnese be seat in a plain be one of the fair in Rome which for the dignity of such a city have very few stately palace here I do see a admirable statue cut out in one stone of a bull tread a woman under his foot with the image of two brother stand upright and of a hunt dog with the figure of a high rock concern the church which we do see in this day journey the 23 church of saint Adrian in the market place call romanum or the church of saint Peter and between the 20 theatre of Vespasian and the church a s. Giovanni in Laterano the church of saint Matthew in the way towards c saint Mary Maggiore the church of saint Pietro in upon 26 mount Esquiline where be a sepulchre of white marble of pope julius the second adorn with fair statue namely one of Moses of the rare work of Michael Angelo the church of upon the same mountain the church of saint Vito in the church of saint Eusebius near the mounment call 9 Trofei di Mario the church of saint sear near c s. Maria Maggiore who chapel call Paradiso no woman may enter the church of saint Quirico the church saint Susanna upon 28 monte cavallo the church saint Vitallis all these church give the title of cardinal the church saint Costanza be a long mile out of the 7 gate Pis and be of old dedicate to Bacchus where be a most fair sepulchre and large of porphry ten ordinary span deep and fifteen long curious engrave with boy quaff and bear cup of wine as in a feast of Bacchus which some think to be the sepulchre of Constantia a virgin daughter to Constantine but the engrave like a feast of Bacchus seem more ancient and to be wrought by the heathen roman and it be vulgar call the sepulchre of Bacchus this church be of a round form and little but very fair and bear up with twelve row of marble pillar set in a round compass near this lie the church of saint Agnese have door of brass where the lamb be keep whereof the Pals of archbishop be make which the pope so dear sell at my first come to Rome I lodge in a public inn and pay three each meal then as I have write I haste my journey to Naples and after return to Rome I hire a chamber by the day and buy my own meat but my haste to be go make i not to note my particular expense only I remember that they who sell any thing in the market use to look into the hand of the buyer to see whether they bring silver or brass coin and thereafter make their price whereupon many show silver till they have buy and then pay in copper which the people dare not refuse and from hence it be that in a solemn pomp when the people fall on their knee before the pope and I think they only expect his blessing they all cry out holy father command that we may have white bread and that the gentleman may be force to take our brass quatrine so as it seem they refuse to receive they I scarce buy my meat one or two day in Rome for after I have be with cardinal Allen I former say that to shun the company of his englishman I change my lodging and myself and two dutch gentleman my consort take a chamber in a victual house close under the pope palace and each of we pay for our bed each night one or two and buy our meat in that house agree for the price before we do eat it and so be ready upon all event of danger and have no carriage to trouble we we hope if need be to escape yet still I rest much upon the cardinal promise and be free from englishman company and have two honest dutch gentleman for my consort both bear in the palatinate of Rhine where they profess the same religion as in England I do with more security set myself free to satisfy my curiosity in the view of Rome after be desirous to see the mouth of Tiber where it fall into the sea I go out of the gate of saint Paul and have the narrow bed of Tiber on my right hand pass twelve mile to Ostia through fruitful hill of corn and a vast wood at my journey end here be a strong castle seat in Latium and belong to the pope of Rome which pope Martin the five build and pope julius the second do more strong fortify but nothing remain of the stately building of that city but some poor house Strabo write that king Ancus Martius first build this city in a soil to which the river bring much dirt it be certain that of old the ship do cast anchor without the haven of the city and send their good to Rome in galley and bark draw up by a rope the haven be not so deep to bear they the citizen be free from tribute to make they dwell there the air be then as also at this time very unwholesome now the Tiber seem to end here in a lake and the water run in little channel under the pave high way the haven of trajan be a mile and a half from the town be broad enough but so bar with sand and like matter as no ship can come to it and only small bark sometime pass from Naples and near place to Rome and that very seldom the foresay haven be first build by Claudius then repair by trajan and call of his name of which Suetonius write thus in the life of Claudius he make the haven of draw a arm on the right and left side and make a bar at the entry where it be deep which bar that he may make more stable by art he sink the ship wherein the great obeliske be bring from Egypt and upon pile heap up place a most high tower like to the Pharo of Alexandria out of which light be hang out by night to direct the ship etc. when trajan repair this haven the city portuensis be build on the other side of Tiber towards the southeast in the soil of Toscany the bishop whereof be one of the seven cardinal assistant to the pope the branch of Tiber fall from and divide itself make a island compass with the sea on the other side in which be many ruin of stately house and great stone of marble this haven seem of no use and bar up for the security of Rome from any naval siege the only port that Rome have be call Citta'vecchia seat upon the shore of Tuscany between the foresay and Ligorno the chief haven of the duke of Florence in this haven of Rome of old call cellae and after ruin by the saracen and after build again the pope of Rome possess many place in the neighbour soil of Toscany do build a strong castle and call it citta Vecchia I return from Ostia to Rome the same day and pay five for my mule and two for my dinner I have purpose to see the famous garden of the cardinal of ferraria at tivoli whereof I speak in my journey to Naples but easter be now at hand and the priest come to take our name in our lodging and when we demand the cause they tell we that it be to no other end but to know if any receive not the cummunion at that holy time which when we hear we need no spur to make haste from Rome into the state of Florence only I have a obstinate purpose to see Bellarmine to which end have first hire a horse and provide all thing necessary for my journey to Sienna and have send away my consort to stay for i with my horse and boot at a inn in the suburb that I may more speedy escape if my purpose succeed not I bold go to the jesuit college and bellermine then walk in the field I expect his return at the gate the student tell i that he will present come back which fall out as they say I follow he into the college be attire like a italian and careful not to use any strange gesture yea forbear to view the college or to look upon any man full lest I shall draw his eye upon i thus I come into bellermine chamber that I may see this man so famous for his learning and so great a champion of the pope who seem to i not above forty year old be lean of body and something low of stature with a long visage and a little sharp beard upon the chin of a brown colour and a countenance not very grave and for his middle age want the authority of grey heir be come into his chamber and have make profession of my great respect to he I tell he that I be a french man and come to Rome for performance of some religious vow and to see the monument especial those which be live and among they himself most especial earnest entreat to the end I may from his side return better instruct into my country that he will admit i at vacant hour to enjoy his grave conversation he gentle answer and with gravity not so much swallow the praise I give he as show that my company shall be most please to he command his novice that he shall present bring i in when I shall come to visit he and so after some speech of courtesy he dismiss i who mean nothing less than to come again to he the very same hour at ten in the morning upon the Tuseday before Easter I come to my consort in the suburb and present we take horse after a short breakfast the way from Rome to Sienna be thus vulgar note a Borgetto six mile a Isole one a Bacchano six a Monterose five a four a Roneignone three a one a seven a seven a Bolsena seven a San' lorenzo four a six a recorseto a Paglia twelve a Scanciericho four a Buon' Conuento seven a saravalle four a San' Michaele de la Seala six a Sienna six in all from Rome to Sienna ninety six mile I will follow my italian consort in describe this journey who do not much differ from this vulgar number of mile each of we pay fifteen for his horse from Rome to Sierra and we have but one Vetturine who go with we on foot for the italian use to ride a slow pace and he be to bring back our horse and to pay for their meat and for his own the first day after breakfast we ride twenty two mile to Monterose through fruitful hill of corn and some wild barren field and wood at our journey end after we have ride five mile we come to a monument lie upon the high way and call the sepulchre of Nero in the place where he kill himself when the senate have condemn he and when we come to the little town Bachano I observe the mountain to compass it in the form of a theatre with a lake in the midst like the mountain Astrumo near Naples out of this lake come a brook call cremera upon the bank whereof three hundred fabius with five thousand of their follower of old be kill in one day Bacchano of old call Campagnano have the name of Bacchus and it give the name to the territory and to the wood near it which be infamous for robery Monterose be subject to pope Clement the eight then live his nephew so they call their bastard and though it be seat within the old confine of Hetruria yet now it be under the pope dominion here we sup at a ordinary vulgar all and each man pay four and all the company complain that so much be demand for our supper the host so thunder among we like the brag soldier as he soon make both stranger and italian glad to be quiet the second day in the morning we ride eight mile to Lago diVigo where we break our fast upon reckon vulgar all and each man pay eight baocchi after breakfast we ride fourteen mile to a little city have pass by the way the city viterbo where pope Celestine the three place a bishop and make it a city of old it be govern by prince bear in the town till upon civil dissension between the family of Gatti aid by the roman family Colonna and the family Magancesi aid by the roman family Orsini the pope bring the city into his subjection about the year 1446. a most high mountain lie near viterbo bear chestnut and a great wood of oak infamous for robbery where we do see many quarter of thief hang upon the tree the way to viterbo be through a fruitful plain of corn and beyond this mountain be store of olive tree and vine viterbo be of old call and it have 3 city within the wall but we pass sudden through it I observe nothing markable but a fair fountain in the marketplace the way from thence be through a fruitful plain of corn to the say little city Montefiaschoni seat upon a high hill at the foot whereof begin the lake of Bolsena and it be subject to the pope have no singular thing in it but the white and red Muskedine one of the most famous wine in Italy here we three consort have two bed for ten baochi and we sup upon reckon and each man pay two the three day in the morning we ride eight mile by the lake Bolsena through a plain of corn have woody hill of oak not far distant with store of chessenut and olive tree in this lake there be a island which the queen famous for her wisdom be kill by the command of the king of the ostrogothe and in the castle of Balsena they show a piece of bread consecrate for the lord supper which be in the hand of a priest not believe that it be the very body of Christ do shed blood as they say who have many such lie miracle then we ride six mile to the castle through a plain of corn where each man pay one giulio for his dinner upon reckon after dinner we ride through wild mountain bear little corn twelve mile to the brook Paglia run under the castle and divide the state of the pope and the duke of Florence and we ride further in the state of Florence four mile to a country inn as I think call Scancicricho where each man pay three poali and a half for his supper at a ordinary vulgar all have almost nothing but red herring and salad to supper the four day in the morning upon the last day of April after the new style in the year 1594 we 1594. ride thirteen mile to a country inn through high hill of corn and for the great part very fertile where each man pay seven baochi for his breakfast the same day we ride eighteen mile to Sienna through most pleasant hill and a fertile plain of corn with store of vine on each side and many palace of gentleman so they call their house build of freestone with a low roof and small magnificence and most frequent dwelling of husbandman we come to Sienna the Friday before Easter day and in a public inn each man pay three real for his supper the next day I go to Fiorenza for money and ride through wood and fruitful hill to the castle Poggio wall town be call castle and after through stony mountain bear corn and olive till I come to the village Tavernelle be seventeen mile from Sienna where I pay two real for my dinner upon reckon after dinner I ride fifteen mile to Fiorenza through stony little mountain bear great store of olive almond and chessenut and many poplar tree and towards our journey end store of cedar tree and we pass by innumerable palace of gentleman and a most fair monastery call Certosa and a desert rock upon the top whereof a eremite dwell all alone this territory yield great store of pine-tree the bough whereof be thick and round at the top but the rest of the tree have neither bough nor leaf and it yield a very great nut with very many kernel in one shell which be pleasant in taste and much use here in banquet by the way I do meet a dutch lady with her gentlewoman and manservant all in the habit of franciscan friar and not only go on foot but also barefooted through these stony way and because they be all aswell man as woman in friar weed though I look on they with some suspicion yet I know not their sex or quality till upon inquiry at Florence I understand that the duchess of Fiorenza or hear that some woman be pass by in friar apparel and think they be nun steal out of their cloister do cause they to be bring back unto she and so understand that upon penance impose on they by their confessor for the satisfaction of their sin they be enjoin to go in that friar habit barefooted to Rome whereupon she dismiss they with honour I forget to note what I pay for my horse from Sienna to Florence whether we come upon easter day and there I lodge in the dutch inn and pay three real each meal but I do not at this time view the city defer it till my return the next morning I take my journey to Pisa that by often remove I may shun all question of my religion into which they use more strict to inquire at this time of the year when they use to observe who receive not the sacrament for howsoever there be less danger of the inquisition in this state yet the duke use not and scarce be able to protect those that rash give open offence I think good thus wary to avoid these snare I go this journey on foot mean leisurely to see the next city so little distant one from the other as they be pleasant journey on foot especial in so pleasant a country the first day in the morning I walk ten mile to the castle Prato through the pleasant valley of the river arno. this pleasant castle or wall town be of a round form have at the very entrance a large market place wherein stand a fair cathedral church adorn with many stone of marble and here I pay twelve creitzer for my dinner in the afternoon I walk ten italian short mile to the city Pistoia through a most pleasant plain call the valley of Arno till after the manner of Lombardy bear Pistoia corn and wine in the same field all the furrow be plant with elm upon which the vine grow this city be seat in a plain and compass with mountain which on other side be somewhat distant but on the north-side hang over the same and here as likewise at Prato and Florence the street be pave with broad free stone most easy to walk upon and the cathedral church be stately build and the pavement be of marble curious wrought like the church of Sienna the city have the name in the latin tongue as also in the italian of a plague which invade the city when the troop of the rebel catilina be overcome flee thither who posterity be seat there have nourish a great plague by perpetual faction show thereby of what race they come desiderius king of Lombardy compass the city with a wall after the florentine about the year 1150 subdue this chief city of Hetruria under who government first the faction of the Neri and the Bianehi break out and defile the city with murder which be extinct the faction of the Cancellieri and the panzaditi begin which last almost to our time with incredible hatred and murder but of late time the duke of Florence publish a edict that upon great penalty no man shall wear any rose or other sign of faction which till that time they do bear upon the part of their body where they may most easy be see from hence I walk three mile through little mountain to saravale and two mile to Povanni where the plain begin to open and three mile to the brook Pescha where I pay a creitzer for my passage by boat and five mile to borgo through mountain full of chessnut and other nut tree and eight mile in the territory of Lucca through a large plain to a solitary inn call Moretta by the way I pay upon reckon on real and a half for my breakfast and in this inn I lodge and pay at a ordinary three real for my supper the next morning I walk one mile to Lucca the emperor Charles the four Lucca make this city free which have keep the liberty to this day govern by senator but live in perpetual fear of practice against this liberty from the great duke of Florence it be seat in a plain and strong fortify and compass with mountain on all side but somewhat distant and only lie open on the side towards Pistoia be two mile in compass and have about thirty four thousand inhabitant the strecte be narrow and pave with broad free stone most easy to walk upon the palace of the chief gentleman be build of free stone with a low roof after the italian fashion and they have many pleasant garden within the wall in the corner towards northwest by north be a strong castle near which lie the cathedral church stately pave with marble but very dark as most of the papist church be build either because they think darkness increase religion or to make it a excuse for their burn candle in the day there also lie the senate house and all the inn be in one street that they may more easy look into stranger for any practice against their liberty for which cause no man may wear any weapon in the city nor so much as a knife except the point be blunt these citizen first spread through Italy the art of make silk and weave it into clothes and by this traffic they have very rich family here I pay at a ordinary o real for my dinner and supper from Lucca I walk mile through a pleasant plain to the mountain of Pisa which divide the territory of those two city and it be very high and stoney yet be full of rosemary time and sweet smell herb and the passage of it be 2 mile long after I go 3 mile through fenny ground often overflow with the river Arno and come to pisa. Arno be a little river pisa. talling from the apennine mountain near Florence through which city it run and so pass through a most pleasant and fruitful plain to Pisa through which city it also run and by reason of the narrow bed and the near mountain of Pisa be subject to overflow upon any great rain so as with great hurt it drown the field of Pisa and those that lie from thence to the Sea Pisa of old famous for navigation be make free by the emperor Charles the four about the year 1369. but long after it be the second time sell to the florentine by Galeacius viscount of Milan then they practise with the french to recover their liberty when Charles the eight pass that way to conquer Naples till they be the three time subdue by the florentine since which time the family of Medici invade the liberty of Florence together with that of Pisa under the title of great duke of Florence which they hold to this day but when Pisa be thus bring in subjection to Florence many of the chief citizen choose rather to live at Venice and other place in perpetual banishment with their posterity then to be subject to the florentine Pisa be of old call Alpheo of the builder come from their dwelling near (@) a river in Greece the brook Arno run from the last to the west through Pisa seat in a plain and towards the north-west by north be a gate and a most fair cathedral church pave with marble curious wrought and have a most fair pulpit in which church near the high altar be the sepulchre of the emperor Henry the seven who and many german writer affirm to have be poison by a wicked monk of the order of the predicant at the communion of our bless lord supper upon this monument these word be write in Latin in this (@) (@) to be despise be contain the bone of Henry the seven count of Luceburg and after the seven emperor of that name which the second year after his death namely 1319. the twentyfive day of the etc. be bring to Pisa and with great honour of funer all lay in this church where they remain to this day the steeple be near the church but fever from it which seem to threaten the sal from the top to the bottom but that be do by the great art of the workman deceive the eye for it be as strong build as the church I ascend the same by two hundred and forty stair of marble in which ascent it have seven gallery on the out side not far then to be a yard use for common burial call the holy field vulgar Campo santo in which the emperor Frederick Barbarossa return from do lay great stone of that earth which he have use for ballast of his ship and they say that dead body lay there doe-consume in a most short time this yard be compass with a building all of marble which lie open like a cloister we call it a and the same be cover with lead very sumptuous have in breadth 56 pillar and in length 189. each distant from the other thirteen walk pace so as in my opinion this yard for burial be much more stately than that most fair yard for the same purpose which I former describe at leipzig in Germany call in dutch saker in this place be a sepulchre stately build of marble of diverse colour with this inscriptjoin Latin pope Gregory the thirteen bear at Bologna command this to be make so the most (@) the (@) lawyer John Buon' Compagno his brother by the father side decease in (@) the year 1544 at Pisa where he be chief professor of that Law here I do see another sepulchre with this inscription in latin to Matthew Curtius physician duke Cosmo make this at his own charge in the year 1544. at the west corner of the city be the armoury vulgar call l Arsenalo where they build and keep the duke galley which be there at this time no more than seven in number for the duke use to send out in summer time some two or three galley and seldom any more to spoil the turk which he may do more bold because the florentine have no cause to fear the turk since they use no traffic by sea but send out their silk and other commodity by strange ship and only take care to entertain those merchant well who bring they corn and victual by sea at the same west corner of the city be a bridge of brick over the river Arno build high in the midst with three arch under which the boat pass and towards the east there be three other like bridge near the first bridge be another gate of the city lead towards the sea and near the same be the most sweet walk that ever I behold it have in breadth some five row of tree on each side and a like distance of green grass between those tree but it reach in length many mile and out of the river Arno be draw two ditch which run all the length of it one upon each side so as the citizen in summer time use to take boat in Arno at their door with a a basket of victual and so many family of they pass by the ditch on both side the walk sit down a good distance the one from the other and there sup and converse with great pleasure on the south side of the city a strong fort lie near the wall and there be the three gate of the city in the midst of the city upon the bank of Arno be the palace of the duke of Florence and there be a statue erect to Ferdinando the duke then live who much favour this city in which he be bear not far thence be a little but most fair chapel all of marble build in the form of a thorn vulgar call Capella Jesus spina the pleasant seat of the city the courtesy of the citizen and my desire to converse with the professor of the university make i spend some day in this city where I pay by the day for my chamber and bed three creitzer and my host be tie to buy and dress such meat for i as I desire wherein I spend some three by the day and if I have purpose to stay long I may have live well at a more easy rate chap. 3 of my journey to Ligorno my return to Florence and to Sienna and the description of these city of my journey by land to Lirigi in which again I pass by Lucca and Pisa and by sea to Genea with the description of that city and my journey by land to to Milano to Cremona and to mantous with the description of the city and of my return to Padena I hire a horse for three from Pisa to Ligorna a haven of this state which Ftolomy of old call of which name there be another haven between istria and Dalmatia and Cicero call not far from Pisa I pass by boat the brook run from Lucca and pay two creitzer for pass my horse and one for myself then for three mile I ride upon a pave way and thirteen mile more through a plain of pasture full of many wood and lake which lake and fenny ground lie near Pisa make the air unwholesome some moneth of the year and the citizen much subject to sickness so after sixteen mile ride I come to Ligorno which be of old fortify by the pisans and those fortification be demolish by the genoe in the year 1297. but peace be make between they this place return under the command of the pisans and they be after subdue by the florentine this place come also into their hand and when the french king Charles the eight in the year 1494. restore the pisans to liberty this place follow their fortune till the pisans again be subdue by the florentine in the year 1509. this place also return to their subjection it be say to have the name of son to Phaeton Cosmo de Medicis duke of Florence begin to fortify again this ancient town and to measure out the circuit and the street thereof and duke Francis take upon he to go forward with this unperfect work and he be dead duke ferdinand his brother at that time live bring it to the form of a most strong fort and fair city and at this time the street begin to be replenish with house for the duke make this place as it be a sanctuary to offender upon who he use to impose for punishment either to dwell there for ever or at least for some year and to add one or more house to the build so as the city be now fair and populous but it be fill with citizen guilty of crime and of no civil conversation myself hear that they be such man perhaps out of prejudicate opinion do think their look barbarous which make i look more wary to myself and to those thing I have with i the city be seat in a plain somewhat long from the north to the south than it be broad from the east to the west and the sea lie upon it partly on the north and partly on the south and altogether on the west side and it have one tower on the north side and another one the south side reach into the sea out of which they hang light by night to direct sailor and between these tower full on the west side there be a haven for great ship further out into the sea and also near the city and compass with the wall thereof be two haven for galley and small bark the river Arno run from the east to the west pass by the city on the north side and there fall into the sea and at the corner on the north side be a strong fort. here I pay upon reckon two for my supper and as much next day for my dinner and return to Pisa by water I pay seven creitzer for my passage two week be thus spend I think good to return to Florence that I may receive my money which I have not receive before because for fear of the inquisition I only stay there easter day in the dutch inn therefore hire a horse for four I ride forty mile to Florence through the pleasant valley of Arno partly till after the manner of Lombardy where the same field yield corn wine and wood partly divide into sweet pasture by the way it happen that I break a bough of a mulberry tree to shade i from the sun and fall into the company of a honest gentleman he tell i I seem a stranger because I carry that bough since those tree plant in the high way belong to the duke who preserve they for silkworm and have impose a great penalty upon any that shall break a bough thereof so as if I pass with this bough through any village I shall be sure to be take and keep prisoner till I shall pay a great fine whereupon I present cast away this bough with many thanks to he for his gentle warning how Florence come to be subject to the house of Medici with the title of great duke I must hereafter show in the discourse of the florentine commonwealth at Florence vulgar Fiorenza this time I will set down as brief as I can the description of the city and field adjoin it be a most sweet city and abound with wealth the citizen be much commend for their courtesy modesty gravity purity of language and many virtue the city be ennoble with the duke court and with stately palace build within and without the wall and for the stately building and sweet situation it be worthy call Florence the beautiful vulgar Fiorenza it be say to have have the name florentia in latin either of the Fluentini a neighbour people of who they come or of the perpetual happiness into which it grow like a flower or of the citizen wealth and the city beauty flourish like a flower the pavement of the city be not of flint as at Rome nor of brick as at Sienna but of very broad and fair free stone the house after the manner of Italy be build with a low roof except the palace which be stately build of free stone engrave the window as in all the city of Italy except Venice alone be not glaze but either lie altogether open to take air or be cover with oil paper and linen cloth the street be most broad and have a open air in describe the city I will begin without the wall and first towards the north and east it be compass with pleasant hill plant with excellent fruit tree and lie in the form of a amphitheatre and behind they the high mountain of the apennine somewhat remove be instead of strong wall to the city also on the south side it have like hill and distant mountain but towards the west it lie open to the most pleasant Valy of Arno which Valy continue as far as Pisa and to the seaside on all side without the wall palace of gentleman be most frequent and house of citizen not distant above three or four close one from the other whereupon the emperor Charles the five behold the city and the country from a high steeple affirm that Florence be the great city in the whole world and when he perceive that the stander by (@) doubtful of his meaning he add that in good earnest he repute all the palace without the wall compass with hill and mountain as with wall to be within the circuit of the city it be seat as it be in the centre of Italy between the air of arezzo produce quick wit where Peter Aretine the poet be bear of a sharp wit though he abuse it wanton and the air of Pisa less pure and yield man of strong memory so as it have have by this temperature of air many citizen as well sharp to learn science as strong to retain they the river Arno run from east to west divide the city but into unequal part the far great part lie on the north-side and the less on the southside and the bridge to pass from one to the other be almost in the very midst of the city which be fair build yet be more magnify by stranger than it deserve it have little house upon it where with it be cover and upon each side be goldsmith shop which make small or no show at ordinary time but when the duke Ferdinando bring his duchess the daughter of the duke of Lorraine to the city at her entrance those shop be furnish with vessel of silver and many rich jewel yet borrow of the citizen to that purpose and howsoever some stranger may wonder at it yet they who compare that bridge with the bridge of London or those goldsmith shop with the daily show of the goldsmith in Cheapside shall find no cause to wonder thereat the bridge have two hundred twenty eight walk pace in length and be build upon seven arch there be three other bridge over Arno but far less in bigness and magnificence than the former for the river be shallow scarce cover the sand or stone though beyond the city it be increase with other water and be subject to overflow upon any great rain the city be of a round form and upon the wall thereof lie eight fort whereof the great and strong lie towards the south and the far great part of the city on the north side of the river lie in a plain but in the less part on the south side of the river the house towards the north-east be build upon the side of mountain and the dwelling be more scatter have many and large garden and in that part there be a place vulgar call ruinate that be the ruinous because the house have be often ruin by earthquake and there you shall find this inscription in latin duke Cosmo in the year 1533 forbid the house of this mountain to be rebuild which thrice fall by the fault of the soil near that place lie a lane unpaved in memory of a virgin that dwell there who a young man love who be bear of a family of a contrary faction to she between who many cruelty have be exercise and they mutual love each other and despair to get their friend consent for marriage and at last be impatient of delay resolve with what danger soever to meet together but it happen that the young man be to ascend into the virgin chamber by a ladder be surprise who to save the reputation of the virgin confess that he come to rob the house whereupon he be condemn to die and be lead to execution by the house where the virgin dwell she lay aside all shame come run out with her loose hair about her ear and embrace he confess the truth public with which accident both their parent be so move as lay aside all former malice they contract affinity and the young man deliver from the bond of the hangman be tie to she in the sweet bond of marriage and of this wonderful event the florentine think good to keep this memory for posterity the duke have two palace within the city whereof one be call Pallazzo pitti seat in this part of the city which a gentleman of Florence by name Lucca casa de pitti begin to build but fall into poverty and not able to finish it be force to sell the same to Cosmo de Medicis be great duke of Florence and short after convict of treason be behead this be the most stately palace in the city in the garden whereof call belueder be many most sweet shade among pleasant grove together with a pleasant cave and fountain they say that one mule do bring all the matter to this building in memory whereof these verse be write upon the picture of the say mule the litter these stone marble pillar wood do carry lead draw bear this mule so good the outward side of the palace be of freestone engrave and the ornament within be regal between the two chamber wherein the duke and the duchess lie apart be a very fair gallery and in the chamber of the duchess be a second bed most like she own for the duke when he please to lie there and there be a table wrought with silver and precious stone value at 3000 crown in the dine room be many fair statue and the figure of thirty cardinal choose at one time by pope Leo the ten be of the house of Medici in the very court be two great loadstone the strong fort call saint Meniato lie over this palace and indeed over all the city which be build by Alexander de Medicis nephew to pope Clement the seven and have late be keep by a garrison of two thousand spaniard as likewise another fort on the other side of Arno build in the time of the free state be likewise keep by a garrison of 100 spaniard for the duke of Medici advance to their dukedom by the emperor Charles the five do at first admit these garrison of spaniard under a italian captain either to show their confidence in Spain or to fortify themselves against the citizen who liberty they have invade but duke Ferdinand then live the family of citizen be now extinct or suppress who have live in the free state and can not endure subjection be now confirm in his dukedom have late effect that these spaniard shall yield the fort to he and depart the country upon the north-side of the river Arno and upon the bank thereof be a monument of a horse bury in the high way with this inscription in latin the bone of the horse of Charles Capelli venetian ambassador when the city be besiege in the year 1533. and these verse be add praise worthy horse of war thy thankful lord thou for thy merit do this tomb afford the city have diverse market-place 1. mercato Vecchio 2. s. Spirito 3. Croce 4. s. Maria 5. Plazza signoria which be the fair and large of all the rest and therein be the senator palace and many stately statua one of a virgin take by force and of the ravisher beat her keeper and tread he under his foot another of Hercules tread Cacus under his foot for the florentine bear Hercules in their great seal the three of David all which be of white marble the four of Perseus carry in one hand the head of Medusa upon his shield and tread the bulk of her body under his foot curious wrought in brass in the same marketplace be a most fair fountain set round about with fair statue of brass and in the midst thereof the statue of a giant and of three horse almost cover with water all wrought in white marble do power the water out of their mouth into the cistern in the corner of this market place be the senator palace so call because the senate be wont to meet there in time of the free state but now it be the duke palace and the second that he have within the city therein I see a cat of the mountain not unlike to a dog with the head of a black colour and the back like a hedgehog a light touch whereof give a very sweet send to my glove here they show we as they use to show to curious stranger the duke treasure as they call it namely vessel of gold and silver rose hallow by the pope which these prince hold for rich present many chamber and gallery have a sweet prospect upon the Arno and adorn with picture and statue notable for the matter art and price a most fair look glass a theatre for comedy one table of Porphery value at five hundred crown another of jasper stone value at four hundred crown a table then in the workman hand unperfect the jewel whereof they value at fifty thousand crown and the workmanship at twelve thousand crown moreover they show we the picture of the pope of the house of Medici rich sword and hat and a lather of silver to mount into the coach and many notable antiquity and certain bird of India with many other beautiful thing which they use to show to curious stranger and for the same expect some reward of they in courtesy among other thing I wonder to see there the picture of Flizabeth our famous queen but the duke of Florence much esteem her picture for the admiration of her virtue how soever the malicious papist have long endeavour to obscure her fame especial in those remote part who slander God turn to her great glory here they do show we the great duke study call studiol' duca in which we do see most fair picture two chest of crystal guild over diverse statue not of brass but of mix metal shine here like silver there like gold a cup of amber a little mountain of pearl wrought together by the hand of duke Francis a pyramid of pearl as they grow in oyster-shel two knife set with jewel and a three Indian knife a nail half turn into gold by Torneser a alchumist the other part still remain iron a piece of gold unpolished as it be dig out of the mine two picture of fleming whereof one be value at five hundred the other at eight hundred crown a clock of amber a piece of amber fall upon a lizard and retain the lively form thereof a stone call Vergoara that cure poison the head of a Turk all of pure gold a most beautiful head of a turkish woman a table of gold and of jasper stone and other jewel among which one emerald of a perfect green colour be high value be round and almost as big as a egg for they that keep it repute it worth one hundred thousand crown not far thence be a palace of the noble family of Strozzi and another of Alxeander great duke of Florence wherein be a ruin chamber in which certain conspirator kill he in the stately church of s. Maria (@) be many most fair statue and the pavement and outward part of the wall aswell of the church as of the steeple be of carve marble and here be keep three triple crown of three pope of the family of Medici the tower or steeple be very high and in the ascent be 3 gallery round about the outside and it be all stately build of marble have four hundred forty and nine stair to the first gallery in which be most fair and all of marble the emperor Charles the five sup when he come to Florence and from this gallery to the second be fifty nine stair and from thence to the three sixty eight stair where at the very top be a wonderful hollow globe of brass wherein 30 man may stand together and upright for it be high than any man and be vulgar call another most ancient church lie close to this which be of old dedicate to Mars and now be dedicate to s. John Baptist and it be of around form and have a most stately font with the door of mix mettle much rich than brass and cutious carve the like whereof they say the world yield not and therein be to be see the monument of pope John the twenty three depose at the council of Constantia who before his popedom be name Balthassar cossa the statue of the same pope of mix mettle and diverse other statue of the same mettle shine like gold to conclude in this little but most fair church be keep the monument of the Pisanes subdue namely a pillar a basket and a chain and other notable thing not far thence be the fair church call s. Maria upon the wall whereof this verse be write I will give fatling not lean sacrifice which verse they observe may by beginning with the word backward be a true verse and of direct contrary sense in this manner sacrifice lean not fatling give will 1. but the monk will not have it understand in the last sense unprofitable to their profession but rather in the first as if man be bind now to give the clergy their best good as they be of old to give the fat sacrifice to God in the church call L'Annonciata of the angel annunciation to the virgin the wall be round about hang with image of man and of man foot and arm some of wood some of metal which be offer to our lady upon vow by those that have recover health of body or have be cure of disease in those part and as it can be express how much the italian do attribute to the virgin so there be more concourse to this church then to any other in the church of saint Laurence they keep many relic with uncredible reverence and show they to the people to be adore in a chapel of this church be many fair statue of marble and one most fair erect to duke Alexander kill by treason by which he be make sit with a sad countenance his right hand lay to his mouth and his left hand rest upon his knee and there be two statue of giuliano Angelo and Michaele Angelo two famous painter and engravers in another chapel be low monument erect to duke Cosmo of Medici and to Laurence his son who build this church and to the duchess of Cosmo and to duke Francis and to his lawful son decease for he have a base son yet live after his brother Ferdinando have succeed he and also to Paulus the historian die in the year 1574. as I remember the monument of Cosmo have this inscription in latin by public consent to the father of his country in a stately chapel
