of this church be the library of pope Clement the seven the Medici full of rare greek and latin book but especial they show the commentary of julius Caesar a write bible map of Ptolemy draw with line of gold the a old manner of writing of the egyptians and the pandect in write hand bring from Pisa subdue I dare not say that this be the same library which be gather by Lorenzo of Medici in the year 1484. but I be most sure that I do see these book in one of they in this church and the foresay chapel be also a monument erect to duke Ferdinando then live near that be a garden in which we do see two ostrage and flax of India make most strong thread and the herb that yield this flax much like a artichoke but far great in the monastery of saint mark which duke Cosmo build for dominican friar be a chapel proper to the family Salviati for burial which be very rich with diverse colour marble image of brass and picture especial one rare picture of a red vale which seem two finger distant from the wall and in this monastery be another library of rare book the hospital of s. Maria be say to pass all other in Italy for all necessary to cure and nourish the sick and for orderly attendance whereto that purpose be ninety six bed in one room not far thence be two stable of the duke and in both of they be some thirty two horse of price the duke keep fierce wild beast in a little round house namely five lion five wolf three eagle three tiger of black and gray colour not unlike cat but much great one wild cat like a tiger bear leopard spot with white black and red and use sometime for hunt a indian mouse with a head like our mouse but a long hairy tail so fierce and big that it will easy kill one of our cat and wild boar and the keeper tell we that the duke and duchess with many gentleman come late to behold they sit in a gallery build round about the yard at which time certain man be put into little frame of wood run upon wheel to provoke these beast to anger which be let loose in the court-yard walk without offend one the other and to this end these man have many firework from which the most fierce of they do run away only the wild boar rush upon one of these frame wheel towards he and not only turn it over but rend out a board with his tusk so as all the company be afraid leave the man who lay therein shall perish upon the wall of the church s Croce be a monument of Arno overflowiug with this in scription in the italian tongue in the year 1333. the water of Arno overflow to this height and in the year 1557. to this yet high in this church be the sepulchre of Michael Angelo Bonoritio a most famous Engraver painter and bullder who bone be bring from Rome at the instance of duke Cosmo in the year 1570 and lay here it be most certain that he be most skilful in those art and of he the italian great boast and with all tell much of his fantastic humour namely that when he paint the pope chapel whereof I speak in describe the pope palace that he first obtain the pope promise that no man shall come in till the work be finish and understand that the pope have break this promise come in himself with some cardinal at the back door of the vestery that he be then to paint the last judgement do so lively figure the pope and the cardinal that tempt he among the devil as every man may easy know they but that be abominable which the roman of the better sort serious tell of he that he be to paint a crucifix for the pope when he come to express the lively action of the passion hire a porter to be fasten upon a cross and at that very time stab he with a penknife and while he be die make a rare piece of work for the art but infamous for the murder and that hereupon he be banish Rome and go to the court of the duke of Vrbino where he be entertain with much honour and they report also that when he be recall to Rome with pardon of that fault the duchess of Vrbino be bold upon her former acquaintance shall entreat he at his leisure to paint all the saint for she and that he to show that so great a task shall not be impose upon a workman of his sort shall satisfy this request or rather put it off with a rude and uncivil jest send she the picture of a man privy part most artificial paint and pray she to take in good part the father of all the Saines till he can at leisure send their picture in the house of John Bolena a Fleming and a excellent engraver I do see yet unperfect a horseman statue of brass fifteen else high the belly of the horse be capable of 24. man whereof four may lie in the throat and this horse be make as go in the high way put forward the near foot before and the far foot behind and stand upon the other two which statue be to be erect to duke Cosmo be value at 18. thousand crown also another foot statue of white marble which be to be erect to duke Ferdinand then live when I have see these thing within the wall and about the same I go out to near place to view other rarity and I and my two Dutch consort each of we hire a horse for a giulio and a half by the day early in the morning we go out by the plain lie on the west side and come to Pratoline the Pratoline duke famous garden seven mile from the city the conduit whereof for water if a man well consider he may just say of the garden of Italy as monster say of the town of Valesia that their water cost they more than their wine this garden be divide into two enclosure compass with stone wall in the upper enclosure be a statue of a giant with a curl beard like a monster some forty six else high who great belly will receive many man at once and by the same be the image of many all which cast out water abundant near the same be many pleasant fishpond and there be a cave under the earth lead three mile to the fountain of water from whence by many pipe the water be bring to serve the work of these garden there be a fountain which have the name of a labyrinth close by it and a fountain of jupiter and Iru distil water the fountain of the bear the fountain of Aesculapius and the fountain of Bersia I call these by the name of fountain vulgar call Fontana which be building of stone adorn with many carve image distil water and such be place in most part of Italy in the marketplace open and uncover but in this and like garden these fountain be wrought within little house which house be vulgar call grotta that be cave or den yet be they not build under the earth but above in the manner of a cave it remain I shall speak of the low garden which be much more beautiful than the upper for at the first entrance there be a palace of little compass but stately build be of a round form the midst whereof contain the great chamber large than the other room which round about the same be little but beautiful and rich furnish for private retreat from under all the stair of the palace and the pavement round about with the turn of a cock spout of water rise up in great force for in respect of the heat of the country they take great pleasure to wet the passenger in this sort under the palace there be a cave vulgar call la grotta Maggiore which and like conduit make as be abovesaid I will hereafter call fountain because they be so vulgar call in the say cave a head of marble distil water and two tree by the turn of a cock shed water abundant and a little globe be turn about by Cupid where the image of duck dabble in the water and then look round about they and in the midst of a marble table be a instrument which with great art and force drive water into any further part of the cave so many and so diverse be the cast of the water as the most wary man can escape wet where they make sport to betray all looker on in that kind near this and under the palace be a bath the wall whereof shine with glister stone and therein be a table of alabaster near this be a cave strong build yet by art so make as you will fear to enter it lest great stone shall fall upon your head and here by the turn of a pipe certain image of nymph be carry by the water out of the cave and in again as if they have life no water be see and in this cave seem ruinous be the most curious image of many beast that ever I do see in the next fountain with the turn of a cock the unseen water cause a noise like thunder and present a great shower of rain fall but among all the cave or fountain under the palace one be most fair and large at the one end whereof upon the turn of a cock by the same motion of water unseen the image of fame do loud sound a trumpet while the image of a clown put a dish into the water and take up water present it to the image of a tiger which dringe the same up and then move his head and look round about with his eye which be as often do as they please who have the skill to order the cock at the other end of that cave be the image of Syrinx with her finger half turn into reed and right against that be the image of Pan sit upon a stool with a wreathe pipe in his hand and Syrinx becken to pan to play upon the pipe pan put away his stool with one hand then stand on foot play upon his pipe and this do look upon his mistress as if he desire thanks or a kiss for his pain and then take his stool again and sit down with a sad countenance I know not that any place in the world afford such rare sight in this kind but lest I shall be tedious it shall suffice only to name the other image and cave as you go down from the palace you shall first see the cave of Aeolus another of Parnassus where with the turn of a cock a pair of organ do make sweet music and there be a head which together with the eye in move to and fro by the unseen water and there be a pleasant shade with many statue or image curious carve and there the duke do many time eat the three fountain be call that be the clown the four that be the fishpond where a duck of India have four wing do swim in the water the five la lavandara the laundress where the statue of a woman with the turn of a cock beat a buck turn the clothes up and down with her hand and the battledor wherewith she beat they in the water the six vulgar Caccioli contain vessel to keep the water cold the seven Del the eight grotta Copito and in this cave on all side be marble chair whereupon passenger willing sit after their walk but assoon as they light press some of the seat a pail of water fall upon his head that sit upon it beside the pavement be of marble and therein many stone be so place as light touch with a man foot they cast up water into his very face and eye there be also well wrought image of a serpent bite the finger of a man and of a toad creep to and fro and of a dragon head bow down to drink water which present it vomit up again the nine the satire the ten a woman with a vizard to conclude there be a large cage of bird make of wire and open to the air in which be bird of all kind and many country not only sing to delight the ear but of most pleasant and diverse colour to delight the eye return from this garden we ride to the duke palace call Petraia where at that time he hold his court such as I shall in due place relate and there we do see duke Ferdinando and his duchess daughter to the duke of Lorraine and the young prince and princess of the house of Medici walk into the garden thence we ride in our return to Florence to another palace of the duke call castello be two mile distant from Florence in the garden whereof we do see a fair oak call Quercetta to the top whereof we ascend by stair and there with the turn of a cock the water spring up on all side there be a fountain or a statue of a woman make of mix mettle rich than brass call vulgar di Bronzo and this statue shed water from all the hair of the head and there be seat which cast out water when they be set upon here in another cave be diverse image of beast of marble curious wrought namely of elephant camel sheep heart wolf and many other beast admirable for the engravers work here our guide slip into a corner which be only free from the fall of water and present turn a cock pour upon we a shower of rain and there with do wet those that have most wary keep themselves from wet at all the other fountain this garden be full of pleasant hill and shade of cypress tree and have three cistern of marble to keep water have now speak of all the duke palace within and near the city give i leave to relate by other report that the duke have another palace ten mile distant from Florence call Poggio which he build for the pleasure of hunt this day be thus spend we return to Florence and the next day we go out on foot by the south gate to the stately monastery of the carthusian call Certosa have in our company italian gentleman who cause we to be well entertain there and invite to dinner in their public refectory where we have great cheer of fish pastry and salad but no flesh which those friar never eat at least not public I make mention of this monastery in my journey from Sienna to Florence at which time those that do penance about easter flock thither in great troop and now our italian consort give we the mean to view the same the church be stately build and the seat of the chancel be of nut-tree they do show we the statue of saint Chrisostome to the middle of silver who relic also they keep and they show we one of the pot in which they say Christ turn water into wine in Cana of Galily whereof the papist show many also a statue of saint Dennis Areopagita of silver and like relic keep there these friar profess great austerity in religion and be tie to keep silence not pythagorical for some year but perpetual the lay-brethren except who do the manual work of the house they never eat flesh for such be their rule which if they break yet they do it not in the public place of eat the priest have sing mass do after it many time bow down his head and then fall prostrate on his face pray each friar have four cell or chamber and his private garden plant with fruit tree and therein a private well they have no bed but sleep upon straw and eat private in their own cell only eat together in the public room on the feast day so as they may easy in private break this vow of not eat flesh if they list to conclude they give large alm to the poor and thus by show of holiness get great riches from layman gift they think to deserve heaven by give they as the proverb be a pig of their own sow the seat of this monastery be very pleasant upon a hill or little mountain hence we return to Florence all the city of Italy have many house wherein stranger may hire chamber call Camere and in Florence there be only three or four public inn all in one street for daily passenger and three huose like college call for those that make long stay in the city wherein they may hire chamber for ten the month the host be tie after the manner of Italy beside their chamber and bed to dress their meat and find they linen I live after this fashion remember these rate of thing buy for a pound of almond vulgar di mandole one giulio a pound of great grape dry and call six creitzer two pigeon one giulio that be eight creitzer two apricott a quatrine a pound of mutton fourteen fifteen or sixteen quatrine a pound of lamb twelve quatrine two egg five or six quatrine a pound of raisin or less grape dry two baelli and of another kind call six baelli two hen forty or fifty sol two capon sixty sol two apple one quatrine and seven apple one baello a orange two quatrine two citrons one baello a pound of dry fig seven or ten quatrine a pound of the great reason or dry grape call twelve quatrny and the best kind eighteen quatrine a pound of Rice fourteen or fifteen quatrine a vessel call boccale of oil twelve crietzer or baelli be all one a pound of butter contain twelve ounce two each ounce be seven quatrine two ounce and a half of sugar five baelli a ounce of nutmeg six baelli a pound of walnut twelve quatrine two little fresh cheese call Recotti thirteen quatrine a fit proportion of any herb for salad one quatrine and little proportion of any spice one quatrine which proportion you may increase as you list and I be lodge in the Albergo of the golden key call (@) bevigliane pay for my chamber by the month twelve or real and moreover for salt at table five Crietzeri or and in these he that desire to live at a ordinary without trouble to buy his meat vulgar in shall pay for each meal two and if he stay long shall pay no more for two meal and they be wont to give a stranger his chamber and diet in these house for ten crown the month each crown be ten I be purpose to live in the state of Florence this summer especial desire to spend my time in learning the italian tongue repute the most pure in those part to which end I resolve to return to Sienna and live in that university but because many dutch and english gentleman live there which be of my acquaintance and solitary converse with the italian best fit my purpose I rather choose to live at the castle s Casciano be a pleasant seat and lie eight mile from Florence in the way to San' Casciano Sienna and because I live in a public inn and in a great highway and be short to pass through the duchy of Milan subject to the spaniard who then have war with England I do for the avoid of danger in that journey give out that I be a Dutchman but I stay here much long than I purpose for it happen at this time that the roman inquisitor pursue a english gentleman who have escape their hand at Rome do in stead of he cast another english gentleman into prison who then lie at Sienna and be not much unlike he and howsoever the first gentleman escape the second be short set fie yet this chance make inee make less haste to Sienna beside that I have my diet here at a easy rate spend not above on Giulio each meal and yet have such meat as I most desire neat dress and be diligent attend but especial the most please conversation of a gentleman dwell there call Nicolao Rocca make i most unwilling to leave that castle and the rather because he have make i acquaint with a learned kinsman of his Raphaele Columbano a florentine and I free confess that the courtesy and manifold virtue of this gentleman Nicolao Rocca then take such impression in i as I shall not only so long as I live dear love he and his memory but be glad to do any pleasure to his least friend or any florentine aswell for their general good desert as for his sake more special he be my companion in view the pleasure of this territory where among other thing I do see many delightful grove vulgar Boschetti net to catch bird garden for that purpose Vccellart all belong to the noble florentine family of Buondelmonte and guicciardini have palace near adjoin of which sport I shall more large speak in the due place treat of the italian exercise and to make the delight of my stay in this place more particular justify and to explain some event therein mention I will add two epistle which I then write of this subject the first from this place the second from Florence after I be depart from this castle and these be write in italian I will also turn into English Ill Sigr Sigr t. h. oss more a Pisa in Casaa messier t. a. Fiorenza a li and 1594 v. s. affett more f. m. to the noble gentleman m. t. h. a gentleman of England my most respect at Pisa in the house of master t. a. after I have send i unto you my eye and ear be ever attentive to receive your answer not without some grief that it be so long delay these bless thus in english letter so much expect be at last arrive which I open in as great haste as hungry man set down to table and fair be our fortune after patient expect at last we have get a man-child for so many and so diverse be the subject you propound as they seem to require a large answer and therefore I that be as much at leisure in the country as a hermit in the desert will enlarge myself with your patience to answer all the particular and first I will give you a account of the chance at Sienna know then that of late master w. m. howsoever he have public employ himself here in matter of state yet to satisfy his own humour will needs venture to go from Florence to Rome apparel like a Swisser and as it seem to i too much disguise his journey fall out well yet he be no soon return to Sienna and from thence by good adventure go towards Florence but a mandate come from the inquisitor of Rome to put he in prison at Sienna now it happen that master w. l. lie then at Sienna and be as you know high of stature and of cheerful countenance and have other mark of his beard and face be take by the scrgiamt and imprison by the inquisitor of Sienna where he carry himself with that discretion as they perceive they have mistake he set he at liberty which he now enjoy at Sienna but the mandate pass forward come to Florence where master w. m. have notice thereof in the evening about Marie time by a friend in court I say not with the duke privity present take he to his heel towards Paduoa in such haste as he seem to fly over the apennine without wing and now God be praise he be in safety for howsoever this chance import not we that be dutchman yet myself as a stranger be sorry for any disaduenture happen to other who be far from their friend and country I can hold from laugh when I imagine with myself what large step he make over the rockey mountain the country people will think that he hunt for kid not mark how often he look back for fear lest some roman greyhound shall lay hold on he behind the main doubt be that he think not himself safe where he may be betray for if he stay not by the way I dare assure you of his safety the roman hound have little skill to hunt in the state of Venice and none at all beyond the Alps now let he go on his happy voyage and I will tell you without jest that I be much astonish at this accident till I be restore by understand this happy event it be well for they that this happen in the state of Florence for otherwise the priest will have sift they like bran so much for they now for pastime let we talk a little of Orlando and that I may answer you from point to point first concern your excellent tuscan language see how there be never honey without sting bee neither be your love letter without their exception how soon a man may be blame be misunderstand shall I jest at you sure I never jest at my friend sometime I be merry with they and what will you have i do know no other subject of idle letter but complement and mirth if I think you have write this in earnest I shall take such pepper in the nose as I shall be ready to run my head against the wall but I be most certain you have pick this quarrel with i to make i angry with myself therefore I reply howsoever you take it that I be still amaze at your tuscan eloquence not that I think you pass Petrarch dante and Baccaceo and the other great master of that language but that of a novice you be become a doctor before I can perceive it have you not give i this taste thereof by your gentle letter so as you be in the wrong take my word and not my meaning and blame yourself for the trouble you have in answer i in the italian tongue since I understand from your own mouth that you will willing exchange such letter which for my part will be profitable to i give you occasion to correct my error and to impart unto i the eloquent phrase of Lasco and this I pray you do free with i and therein show i how much you love i be content with this answer otherwise I swear by no beggar I will praise you so much to your face as I will make you blush I go forward and come to your quip that I begin my letter with the word alas to show my eloquence and that you be ashamed to write to i for your rude style very good I find the irony content yourself that I glad yield to you in all point of wit so it may be lawful for i to equal you in love yet to revenge this frump and that you may not think I be daunt with your Toscan bravery I will attempt to cast that upon your own shoulder which you impute to i and this I think to do with ease since upon this one little word alas you have make so fair a discourse as you may just be envy by our great divine who upon the least subject be hold able to discourse from morning till night again i think you be somewhat too bold with the mountebank not care to make they your enemy into who hand if you fall without be well store with woe be to you for you know they be most skilful fencer with the tongue moreover the confidence of your skill in this tongue transport you to speak ill of the most gentle and delightful thing in the world namely of complement letter and to cure you of this error I will brief remember you that the thing of great use be most dear unto we the air that nourish we be most of all dear all man seek more for bread and wine without which they can live then for pheasant blackbird or quail such be letter of complement for in every gentle company there be more discourse of courtesy love and toy then of law or state matter and not to be tedious with many argument the art and wit of the orator be most show in the barrenness of the subject whereof he speak as in praise the ass dispraise liberal science and in like subject now for letter of business no man be so blockish that can easy dispatch they when he have tell the business and bid farewell all be do but if letter of complement be not beautify with invention they be dull and the writer deserve small praise it be true that secretary and scrivener tire with write of business have some reason to declaim against letter of complement but you be at leisure and live in a city yea in a university of Toscany shall say with i well fare letter of complement full of gracious word go too now and if you think I have not well fit you hereafter make yourself sport with our country eloquence towards the conclusion you give i many mastership or worship to do i pleasure and you remember i of it lest I shall not see it indeed you have now hit i just where my pain lie yet you know nothing be so cheap in Italy as mastership which be plentiful give to very porter so as if I love they well yet the very plenty of they will make they irksome write you to i without any speak of mastership in the three person which I will take for no disgrace ' but rather for a pledge of your love and for my part since these title be unpleasing to you I will hence forward send you no more of they except it be in exchange of those you shall send i in which case I will pay you with usury touch your affectionate offer of love to i I can express how I take they to heart but for the present I can return you nothing but word till occasion serve to witness my love in action and in the mean time I leave myself at the stake with you yea I give myself to you all that little I be worth do with i what you please keep i for your servant and if need he sell i to the turk what will you have more you will say also that this be write in jest yet you know that poet under feign word shadow the truth believe i except you will have i use furious protestation for I will and must be believe I speak in good earnest command i with security where I be good for your service for myself will free make bold with you as in effect you may see in the trouble I give you by the enclose and so I kiss your hand and also your cheek after the manner of Venice from saint Casciano this 23 of july 1594 the same as your brother f. m. to the noble Sigr. the Sigr. Nicolao Rocca my most respect at his house in saint Casciano or to his hand let this foolish business not to say worse be confine to merchant counting-house since it have make i not forget you which it can never do but to use too great delay in give you testimony of my kind remembrance of you now be ready to take horse for my journey to Paduoa I think good to write these few line unto you with condition that you send they not to be censure in the academy Crusea for myself be thus remove they must needs participate the confusion in which I be for the present be it possible that a brave gentleman like yourself shall fail of his promise I stand look with what security you will proceed with i to take it for a evidence of your love and expect many day I will say free not without some inconvenience to have the happiness to see you ere I go but since either by your forgetfulness or other reason best know to you this our meeting have not succeed and there be no more hope that we shall meet to reconcile this quarrel there be no other remedy but to make our peace at leisure by exchange of letter in which duty for my part I will not fail so long as I shall stay at Paduoa and when I shall be return into my country I will upon all occasion scour up that little toscane language which after my long journey through confusion of tongue shall be remain unto i to make it appear to you that howsoever my language be decrease yet my hearty love towards you shall evermore increase two thing lie heavy upon i first the burden of your courtesy wherewith you have load i as you best know and wherewith Sigr Raphaele have new charge i here by invite i friendly to his house by lead i to the monastery Certosa and by entartain i with unspeakable kindness which I take as do for your sake know my own small desert and yet I do not so much as thank you for it because I know such favour can not be repay with word the second thing which lie heavy upon i be that be thus bind unto you I be ashamed to have deceive you in one point which so much the more grieve i because this deceit have utter take from i all hope to express my love to you hereafter upon any happy occasion than which nothing shall be more please to i now at once to disburden myself and to clear you for be any long deceive by this paper have the privilege of masker vizard which never blush howsoever their master have cause to be ashamed I let you know that I be a Englishman and not a Dutchman as I have hitherto cause myself to be repute and lest you shall thereby doubt that you have cast your love upon a juggler or a man unworthy your so great favour know that for honourable respect I have thus conceal my country I be sure you know that the english have war with the spaniard so as either fall into the hand of the other shall be lawful prisoner upon ransom and I be within few day to pass through the Duchy of Milan do therefore think it no wisdom to make myself know especial lie in a public inn upon the beat highway which all man of those part daily pass I be confident that you will make good my excuse or at least pardon my error remember that I be your servant more than ever neither can be more you than I be and maintain the Englishman in your good favour by the same gentleness in which you vouchsafe it to i as a Dutchman for in whatsoever you shall command i as a Englishman my heart serve i to do you as faithful service as any Dutchman whatsoever and so embrace you thus far off I offer and recommend myself to you once for all and again I kiss your hand from Florence this ten of August 1594 your affectionate servant f. m. I have take my journey from saint Casciano to Florence that I may receive money and now upon a sudden occasion be to return to Sienna and from thence to Padova I hire a horse to Sienna but have omit what I pay for the same and so I return to Sienna by the same way I come namely to Travernelle fifteen mile and to Sienna seventeen which journey for other instruction I will particular set down to saint Casciano eight mile to Colmo four to Barbarino six to Puodibonzo six to Sienna five be in all thirty two mile the situation of Sienna be most pleasant upon a high hill and the form not unlike to a earthen vessel broad in the bottom and narrow at the mouth which narrow part lie towards the west where come from Florence you enter by the gate Camolea near the same be a fort wherein the great duke keep soldier and there without the gate be the church of saint Marie whether be great concourse of people for devotion from hence to the east gate lead towards Rome the street lie even and plain though the city be seat upon a mount and in this part towards the east the city be broad and from this gate a man may see the castle forty mile distant upon the confine of the state of the pope and the great duke between the say gate as it be in the centre of the city lie a most fair marketplace in the form of a oyster and lie hollow as the shell thereof be and there be a stately palace of the senate build when the city be free in the front whereof be a statue of mix mettle vulgar call which seem to be apparel have on the head a broad hat and this statue strike the hour of the clock on the southeast side within the wall lie a large field which be then sow with corn yet the city have few or no garden within the wall not far from the wall on the southside lie the cathedral church vulgar call and howsoever it be little it seem to i the fair church in Italy it have but one door to which you ascend by long and broad marble stair all the pavement be most beautiful of engrave marble adorn with image of the five sibyl and there be in this church some twenty image of mix mettle beside many other of marble the seat of the chancel be of walnut-tree curious carve and all the roof of the church be paint of sky colour and all set with star upon the inside and in the upper part of the church be the image of the pope wrought in stone to the shoulder set round about where between Gregory the four and adrian the second I wonder to see the head of pope joane with the inscription name she especial in a city so near Rome have note this at Sienna and after my come joane this monument as I hear be deface in the time of pope Clement the eight then live into England read the same I search history to see how they agree in this matter which the papist can hear with patience and I find in approve author that after the say Gregory the four succeed Sergius the second confirm by the emperor Lotharius in the year 844 then Leo the four die in the year 854 then pope joane set two year and few month and die in the year 856 then Benedict the three than Nicholas the great in the year 858 then the foresay Adrian the second in the year 867. and if any man ask why the head of sergin the second Leo the four Benedict the three and Nicholas the great be omit the head of pope joane shall stand between the head of Gregory the four and adrian the second I leave the curious search thereof to they that list dispute it as a matter nothing to my purpose can sudden give no other reason thereof then that I conjecture the say head be set on the other side of the church for at that time I be content to note the same without cast this doubt and so not search to satisfy myself therein but I dare bold affirm that myself and the two dutch-gentleman my consort do see this monument in this church near the door on the right hand as we come in and since that time I have confer with diverse worthy english gentleman who affirm that they do see the same in this church be two sepulchre one of pope Alexander the three the other of pope Pius the second and in a chapel of this church be a most fair font. from this church descend by slope and steep street towards the south-west wall you shall come to a most pleasant fountain call Branda without the south-gate of which the citizen proverbial say that if a stranger drink thereof he shall so love Sienna as he shall very unwilling and scarce at all depart from the city but the Florentincs in scorn of the Siennesi have a proverb Branda beve pazzo he that drink of the fountain Branda become a fool it cast out water by nine mouth of stone and there be three place where laundress wash and near the same be a pleasant grove on the same south side towards the sea some few mile from the town lie a fenny plain call maremma most fruitful in corn but infamous for ill air so as the place be forsake by all italian be till by the grison come down from the Alps to this place in winter time when the alphe be all cover with snow and in the spring time return back into their own country Sienna itself be much subject to rain so as a Spaniard come often thither in rainy weather do write or be say to have write to his friend for a wonder that it always rain at Sienna on the north-east side of the city two large field within the wall be sow with corn all the pavement of the street be of brick which last the long because there be neither cart nor coach but all burden be carry upon the back of ass there be a stately palace which pope Pius the second build who be a citizen of Sienna of the family of and there in the mount Olivet the passion of Christ be curious grave it be vulgar and true say that Sienna abound with fountain tower and fair woman there be no better place to live in through all Italy than the state of Florence and more special the most sweet city of Sienna the citizen whereof be most courteous and they have many public meeting of the young woman and virgin to dance where the door be open for any citizen or stranger beside Sienna be commend for the best language and in the same and in all the state of Florence man live safe from robbery and from the murder which be frequent in Lombardy add that they have delicate diet at Florence at a reasonable rate and in the rest of the territory at a very cheap rate our hostess at Sienna give we clean linen often change both at bed and board a large chamber a good bed a linen canopy oft change and do provide our meat very clean for which each man pay no morethen ten by the month we buy our own meat and I remember that the price of oil be twenty five lyre the barrel that I pay for as much wood as a ass will bear four baelli they have butter but not so good as in the valley of Arno and they sell it twenty two sol the ounce the magistrate set a price upon every thing to be sell in the market neither dare any man sell aught before his price be set and upon the butcher stall a bill be set of the price at which they must sell their meat so as a stranger can be deceive the price of wheat be 120. lyre the Moggio contain forty eight english peck and each lyre be a giulio and a half the tuscan hold ram stone fry for a great dainty which they call 1 a Granella and sell it after a giulio the pound at Sienna common they eat kid flesh for three the pound and a whole kid for four and a half mutton two and a half the pound a kids head three Bacon eighteen quatrine the pound cheese a giulio the pound little bird a quatrine a piece a vessel of wine contain thirty two and a half for ten and two the eighteen of November in the year 1594 I ride forty mile to Lucca and ten 1594 nou. 18. to pisa. if any man desire to know the right way from Sienna to Pisa I will set it down by relation of a friend who ride the same in a day and night without intermission be fifty mile the first seven through mountain thirteen mile through pleasant hill and thirty mile in a plain till after the Lombard fashion with corn and vine grow upon elm and he report the way to be most safe by night as well as day though a man be know to carry money but I return to my own journey from Sienna where I hire a horse to Lucca for fourteen and the first day in the morning I ride twenty two mile to Castell fiorentine in a narrow plain between fruitful hill of pasture and corn by the way I light at castle certaldo seat upon a mount that I may see the sepulchre of John Boccacio upon which these verse of his own invention while he live be engrave under this pile john bone and ash lie his soul at rest enjoy his labour hire bear at certald he study poesy Boccacio be the surname of his sire Pierius have add more in old Lombard letter in the year 1375 and upon the very sepulchre other write be race out under which this be write in latin Lactantio when he be Podesta or governor at certaldo for the people of Florence admire his pleasant wit and quick invention do for the renew of his memory erect this monument to he at his own charge and that by fine impose in the year 1500. also his statue without a beard carve in marble be set upon his tomb at castle Fiorentino I pay three and a half for my dinner and one giulio for my horse-meat after dinner I ride fifteen mile to ponte Capiano where every horse of carrier lade and of vetturine hire pay two to the duke which tax they say the duke impose to withdraw merchant from trade that way lead to Lucca all the way the field be till on the Lombard fashion with corn and vine grow upon elm before we have ride two mile I pass the river Arno and pay half a giulio for my passage at ponte Capiano I pay ten for my supper upon reckon and twelve for oat for my horse and eight for hay straw and stable room the second day in the morning I ride through the like way have mountain on my right hand towards the north seven mile in the state of Florence and six mile to Lucca in that free state this city I have describe before and here I pay for my dinner upon reckon two and a half after dinner I hire a horse for two and ride through like way in a fruitful plain five mile in the state of Lucca and then five mile more to Pisa pass into that state over a high mountain and the rest of the way lie through fenny ground this city I have describe before and here I pay for my supper three or real and because the passage by sea be more dangerous from Ligorno to Genoa I hire a horse to Lirigi for one or silver crown the first day I ride twelve mile through a open plain to and there pass out of the state of Florence into that of Lucca I ride eight mile through a thick wood where the quarter of thief be new hang up who few day before have rob and almost kill a Frenchman and then enter again the state of Florence I ride one mile to Pietra Santa and five mile more in the same state of Florence and one mile and a half in the state of Lucca and half a mile to Masso in the state of the prince of Masso who be a marquis of the family of Malaspina all this way be through a plain till after the manner of Lombardy with mountain of chessenut on the right hand have in all ride this day twenty eight mile in this city of Masso the postmaster stay we from go any further pretend to give we new horse because those we have be hire of his man at Pisa but the true cause be that we may lodge in his house that night to which my companion agree but myself be desirous to see the quarry of marble at Carrara take of he half the piastro I have pay at Pisa and so I leave my horse then turn out of the way I go on foot three mile to Carara through woody mountain abound with chessenut this town be subject to the prince of Masso and be famous for the marble which be much prefer before other as well for the exceed whiteness of some stone as for the length of pillar and table dig thence which madeit much esteem at Rome in the time of the free state and of the empire and by reason it lie near the sea the stone be more easy convey to Rome or else where in one of the quarry call Pianella I do see many stone dig out which be as white as snow and other quarry have vein of all colour and they sell as much marble as a ox will draw for twenty sol but if it be carve there the price be great according to the workmanship each quarry be proper to some private man and if any man dig in another man quarry they fine he at twenty crown or more according to the damage when I behold the beauty of man and woman in these part which seem to i great than in any other part of Italy I remember the patriarch jacob who lay party colour rod in the water trough when the ewe be in heat to make they bring party colour lamb and I think by the same reason and force of nature that they who dig these white marble may have a more beautiful race the inn of this town be base and only fit to entertain artificer and here I pay for a poor supper of herb egg and chessenut ten the second day in the morning I go forward in my journey on foot and that alone first three mile to the confine of the marquisate of Masso than four mile in the territory of Genoa to a strong fort belong to that state and seat in a plain and I pay for my passage over the river Magra two and after I go three mile over mountain abound with olive tree and the tree Lecha yield a fruit like the olive and so I come to Lirigi a haven town upon the tirrhene sea whence we be to sail by the shore to Genoa there we expect passenger and a good wind for some few day and at the same time there be the french cardinal of joyeuse who be to sail into France I pay each night four for my bed and eat upon reckon I spend less than two by the day they use to make this voyage to Genoa in little bark call frigate and a less kind of boat call feluce and each night to strike into some port upon the coast. I pass in a and pay three real for my passage the first day we sail in a short space from Lirigi to Wien a haven not far distant under a promontory at the entrance into this sea but the wind be high and our boat little and somewhat over load and the mariner themselves show no great confidence we dare not put forth to sail any further I pay a cavelotto that be four bolinei for my bed with a companion and six bolinei for my supper the second day the wind be somewhat calm we sail not without danger thirty mile to Sestri another haven this day be the day of saint Katherine the patroness of mariner who think that no man be ever drown that day but they observe that after that day the wind use to grow boisterous I will willing have go by land but this coast be all of high rock there be no good high way over they nor commodity for passage yet you can imagine a more fruitful and pleasant place than the narrow valley and hill lie upon the sea side only this coast lie upon the south sun breathe fire out of Africa be subject to great heat in summer time this territory do so abound with fruitful tree and flower as the market be furnish with they in the very month of December it yield noble wine namely lavernazza and in village call cinqueterre the wine call Christo that be the tear of Christ which be so pleasant as the jalians say that a Dutchman taste it do great lament that Christ have not weep in his country at Sestri we have delicate white bread and excellent wine as likewise in all this journey and all thing at a cheap rate and each man spend there nine the three day we sail ten mile over a arm of the sea to Porto Fino call of old the haven now they call it Fino for the goodness thereof on the east side of this promontory the sea be most calm but when we pass to the west side the wind be so high and the wave so trouble as we have almost be cast away and be by force drive upon the side of a rock where my consort trust to their crucifix vow and bead upon which they number their prayer and myself creep upon hand and knee with great difficulty first get to the top of the rock where be in safety the name of the haven come in my mind which answer to my christian name and thank God for my deliverance from this danger I be glad that I escape christen in this haven of my own name after my other consort climb to i and thence we go on foot ten mile by the twilight of the evening and moon light to a village where each man upon reckon pay six bolinei for his supper the next morning early before day break we go forward on foot our consort of Genoa often warn we to be silent for fear of thief and after we have go six mile we come by the break of the day to Genoa by the way we do see a village Genoa all ruin and they tell we that turkish pirate land sudden have spoil the same and burn it and have pull down the church and altar and among other prisoner have take away a most fair virgin from her bridegroom side who have marry she the day before the description of Genoa a the fort of the sea bank b statue erect to the builder thereof c the palace of Andreetta Auria without the wall upon the sea d the statue of Andrea Auria upon the wall e the new fort f the new street most stately build g the cathedral church k saint Mathewes church l the duke palace m the inner haven N the tower faro and the ruin of the fort call Briglia that be the bridle PPPP three gate of the city and the four lead to the haven on the north-east side where the sea lie p upon the city we enter and at the very entrance we do see two stately palace of Georgio Auria and a gentleman call Seba and six other palace but less stately Gtnoa be seat upon the side of mountain and hill decline from the high mountain on the westside towards the east and to the sea side upon the foresay north and north-east side werelong suburb and two gate and without the wall a river fall from the western mountain towards the east and so into the Sea on the southside be the outward haven in the form of a half moon upon the horn whereof towards the east lie the sea bank call Mola about 600. pace long which keep off the wave of the sea that beat upon the city on the east side and in the midst of this bank be a a fort build to detend the navy there also be certain statue b erect to the founder of the build and in the further corner of this haven towards the city be a m inner haven compass with wall where the galley lie under a cover binide near that be the armoury of the city and the chief galley in the port call la real the regal be about seucnty five walk pace long and they say that four hundred rower belong to the same at the other horn of the outward port towards the south-west be the N tower faro upon firm land keep by certain watchman who by night hang out light to direct the mariner at sea near that lie the fort Lahiigita that be the bridle which the french king Lewis the twelve fortify but the citizen expel the french out of the city demolish the same thence as you walk towards the city and before you enter into the gate lie c the stately palace of Andreetta Auria or Doria the building whereof the garden the stair to descend to the sea the banquet house and diverse open gallery be of kingly magnificence not far thence upon the wall be a d statue erect to Andrea (@) Aurta late admiral to the spanish fleet then you come to the p gate of the city and not far thence within the wall be p her gate lead to the inner haven where the galley lie not far thence be the most fair cathedral g church in which be a ancient monument of mettle dig out of the adioun valley which have a old inscription show the antiquity of the city-not far thence be the k church saint Matthew wherein the prinet of the family of (@) have long have their monument near that lie the l dukes palace not his private palace but public which be keep by a guard of dutchman who also have the keep of two of the strong gate of the city in the court yard of this palace be a foot ftatua arm and of white marble erect to the foresay Andrea Aurta by the senate with the title of father of his country because ne have late restore the citizen to their liberty and in a upper chamber call Sala be diverse statue in the habit of senator erect to Paulo Spinola to Eattista Grimaldo and Ansidio gri. s. c. on the west side without the wall be palace of gentleman almost innumerable and in the high part of the city be the new castle e most strong fortify which the citizen demolish to preserve their liberty a little low and within the wall be the new street vulgar f lie from the west to the north-east each house whereof be build with kingly magnificence neither do I think that any city in the world have so fair a street these house or rather palace may be see by stranger for the gentleman servant keep they willing show they to any desire that favour aswell in expectance of reward as for the honour of their master and country myself do see the palace of Giovan battista Auria the building whereof be very stately and the garden not only most pleasant but adorn with statue and fountain and in one of the chamber be the gentleman arm whereof some be of pure silver guild over the city have certain inner gate which always stand open and show that the circuit of the city be now much increase and upon these gate be chain of iron for remembrance as they say of their liberty once lose the whole circuit of the city except the Mola be five mile and save that the inner haven strike somewhat into the city it seem almost of a round form no doubt the city be of great antiquity which some say be build by Gianus king of Italy and of he have the name and that the promontory (@) be of old call the vineyard of Gianus the monument in the cathedral church witness that this city flcrish among the old city of Italy about 300. year before Christ incarnation other will have the city name of a latin word as the gate of Italy it be fortify towards the sea with all art and towards the land aswell by nature as art there be but one way to come to it and that over high and steep rock the street be narrow the palace be stately build of marble and the other house of free stone five or six story high and the window be glaze which be rare in Italy the street be pave with flint and the house of the suburb be almost as fair as within the city coral be fish in this sea towards Sardinia and Corsica land not far distant and the ounce thereof be here sell for three lyre now in the very month of December the market be full of summer flower herb and fruit whereof I shall speak more in the due place it be proverbial say of this city Montagne Mar' senza mori Genoa that be mountain without wood sea without fish man without faith woman without shame white moor Genoa the proud in good earnest they report that the merchant be not bind by writing make little account to break their promise and the french liberty of the woman make the italian judge they without shame and as Florence be call the fair for the building so I think Genoa be call the proud the chair call whereof I speak in the description of Naples be also in use here in which the citizen of both sex be carry upon two porter shoulder through the street lie upon the side of hill the chair be cover with a curtain draw and have glass window so as they may see all man and themselves be unseen beside in regard of the narrow street and the steep mountain on all side they use horse litter here in stead of coach the man in their feast dance and free conversation and the woman in their apparel come near to the french then any other italian here I pay one real by the day for my chamber and dress my meat which I buy myself all thing be at good rate in the city as in the country there be such store of fruit as they give a citron for a quatrine and two orange for a quatrine and to end in a word my diet here be for the manner and price not much differ from the same at pisa. they account ninety mile from Genoa to Milan which journey I go on foot willing expose myself to this trouble partly to spare my purse in the bottom partly to pass more safe in this disguise through the duchy of Milan subject to the spaniard who then have war with the English the first day after dinner I walk all alone seven mile to ponte by the bank of a river between stony mountain but frequent inhabit and I pay eight soldi for my supper on reckon and a cavellotto that be four bolinei for my bed the second day I go on foot eleven mile ascend all the way high mountain and tire with the difficulty of the journey only refresh with the hope of a easy descent from the mountain and be very hungry by the way I chance to meet with a beg friar of the order of saint Francis who have victual in his bag give i to eat but will receive no money for it say it be against their rule to handle any money thence I walk seven mile down those mountain in the territory of Genoa to Gavidon and four mile more through a plain and dirty way in the duchy of Milan to seravalle where I pay four cavellotti that be sixteen for my supper and my bed the three day in the morning I walk fourteen mile in a dirty way to Tortona where I pay one for tribute as all passenger pay and seven for my dinner upon reckon thence I walk after dinner in a dirty way five mile to ponte Curon and further in a way somewhat fair five mile to Voghera all this way in the duchy of Milan be in a most fruitful plain of corn with elm plant in the furrow and vine grow upon they and such be the way in all Lombardy and to the very city of Paduoa at Voghera I pay three real for my supper and bed and here by chance I sound a english merchant in the inn who talk rash do voluntary without be examine whence he be profess himself to be a Dutchman and myself in disguise poor habit sit at the low end of the table and speak to he in the dutch language he be force for want of the language to say that he be a Dutchman but bear upon the confine of France and know no no other language but the french whereupon I speak to he in the french tongue he have as little skill in that as in the dutch so as I may perceive that he dissemble his country and be not willing to press he as have be myself often force in like sort to dissemble my country do forbear to speak any more to he in the dutch or french tongue and we begin to discourse in italian wherein he have speak little before he utter these word that be irepented myself thereof whereas a italian will have say by which fillable add by he I present know he be a english man supper be end he perceive himself to have be thus press by a poor fellow sit at the low end of the table take i for a spy and fear I shall betray he and present go into the stable where he command his servant to saddle their horse that they may ride all night towards Genoa but I follow he and bold speak english to he he be soon content to stay all night and to take i in my homely apparel for his bedfellow have pass this night merry I hire a horse the four day for four cavellotti and ride eleven mile to bastia than I walk on foot seven mile to paula and be paula afoote-man I pay five for my passage over the river po. this journey hitherto be in a dirty way have plain field on both side till after the foresay manner of Lombordy and many rich pasture which be rare in all other part of Italy enter paula I pass a stately bridge build over the river (@) which run from the west to the east and after six mile fall into the river po. this bridge be two hundred walk price long and so broad as two cart may pass together and be build of stone and cover over the head with a roof with open air on the side and support with pillar the city lie in length from the east to the west and a new fair street divide it in the midst by the breadth from the south to the north. on the west side of this street be two market place one great than the other in the less be a (@) call of mix mettle vulgar Dibronzo which some write to have be make with art magic by the emperor anastasius for his own image and to haud be place by he upon the pillar of the soldier at Ravenna where he keep his court and after Ravenna be take by Charles the great that this image be to be carry into France be by the way leave here other will have it the statue of the emperor Antoninus for they be deceive who think it the statue of odoacer king of the Lombard who have another statue in this market place on the (@) west side of the foresay new street towards the north-side be the castle which John Galiacius first duke of milon build and the same duke library but almost void of book and in this castle lie a garrison of spaniard near that be the church of saint (@) in a Chappol whereof be a stately sepulchre in which they say the bone of that saint be lay be bring thither out of the island Sardinia and this sepulchre be of marble curious engrave and worthy to be seek out and behold there I do read this inscription write in latin upon another sepulchre the french king Francis the first be take by Caesar army near Pavia the fourteen of Febru (...) among other lord these be Lorraine Francis duke of Lorraine Richard de la Poole Englishman and duke of Suffolk banish by his tyrant king Henry the (@) at last Charles Parken of morley kinsman of the say Richard banish out of England for the catholic faith by queen Elizabeth and make bishop hereby the (@) of Philip king of Spain dead out of his small mean erect this monument to he etc. in a cloister of the same church be a sepulchre of this Charles Parken bishop decense in the year (@) there be another monument of (@) king of Lombardy and another of the bishop (@) (@) with this inscription in latin most (@) in the greek and latin who be consul be send hither into bamshment and with these verse have death (@) aught my goodness mount the sky great be my same my work live in man eye on the east side of the say new street and towards the north lie the church of saint Francis where be a monument of Baldus the civil lawyer and they show his head of a extraordinary bigness without the wall of the city on the north side be a piece of ground of some twenty mile circuit compa with a wall in many place break down vulgar call Barco that be the park which John Galiacius duke of Milan wall in to keep fallow dear hare and coney but at this day it be divide into pasture and plough field on the further side of this park from the city be the place where the french king Francis the first be take prisoner by the army of the emperor Charles the five not far thence be the monastery of the carthusian call Certosa where the build of the church the stone of marble the engrave the top cover with lead part of the great altar of alabaster high value the sepulchre of John Galiacius first duke of Milan and the revenue of the church exceed three hundred thousand crown by the year deserve admiration the building of the city be of brick and seem to be of great antiquity the emperor Charles the four in the year 1361 at the instance of Galiacius the second give this city the privilege of a university the king of Spain permit Jew to dwell here but they may not stay in Milan above twenty four hour this city be the seat of the king of Lombardy who old castle be to be see near the church of saint Michael after it be subject to the king of Italy and the be overcome it be subject to the emperor Otho the first by right of his wife and successive to the emperor with some show of a free city which freedom that they may more full attain they willing yield themselves in the year 254 to the archbishop of Ravenna after they be subject to usurp citizen who the viscount of Milan expel and so join this city to their state which together with the dukedom of Milan come to the spaniard hand in the time of the emperor Charles the five I lodge here in a fair inn but common to the base for it the hostess whereof be a masculine woman and by the night let in ruffian to drink I be not a little afraid of some violence to be offer i in my chamber whereupon I firm resolve with myself to lodge ever after in the best inn and of best fame especial in Lombardy infamous for murder and here I pay for my supper and my bed three real I go on foot from Pavia go forth at the nothwest gate twenty mile through Milano rich pasture to Milan call that be the great of the large circuit thereof the city have the name of Olanus a tuscan captain or the latin word that be half wool of those kind of stuff make in the city it be large populous and very rich seat in a plain as all Lombardy lie and that most fertile and by the commodity of a little river bring to the city by the french and almost compass the same it abound also with foreign merchandise of old it be the seat of many roman emperor but the history of the city be contain in the history of Italy I will only remember that the archbishop thereof long time challenge the primacy in the italian church never acknowledge the bishop of Rome for superior and that he crown the emperor with a crown of iron after the people of Milan have approve he that the king of the ostrogothe have the same crown set upon his head after his victory which crown they say be give in sign that the empire and the command of Milan be to be win by iron that the citizen of Milan be often rebel to the emperor that the viscount make of the city do by little and little subject the territory and the city with title of duke of Milan that the family of viscount be extinct in duke Philip about the year 1447 the duke of Orleans by right of their mother and Francis Sforza by the right of his wife challenge the inheritance of the dukedom but the emperor think the same to be fall back to his right that Francis Sforza be by the people first make captain of their force and then choose duke that the french king Francis the first defend the right of the duke of Orleans cast Sforza out of the dukedom in the year 1449. that the emperor Charles the sift cast out the french in the year 1521 first restore Sforza to the dukedom with some restraint of his power but he be dead invade the dukedom himself whereupon after many contention and battle it come to his successor the king of Spain of the family of Austria to who at this day it be subject the city be of a round for i and have nine gate the building show antiquity and the house be of brick and low build except some stately palace such as be that of the duke of the street be broad and the pavement of brick raise in the midst with broad stone when I come to the city on foot I make offer to enter at the gate call Genese on the south side but the guard refuse i as a footman to paste into the city and lest by my importunity I shall have make they look more narrow into my quality they be common expert man to find out any disguise person I go back into the suburb as it I will lodge there but as soon as I be out of sight I walk further towards the east compaise a great fen and so join myself to some citizen return from walk in the field I enter with they into the city by the next gate on the same south side which gate be call Lodovico and be only keep by one soldier a little brook within the wall compass the very centre of the city circular beyond which brook on the north-side within the wall not far from the gate Zobia be a large meadow wherein be no house for there be the most strong castle seat in a plain and keep by a spanish garrison into which no Frenchman may enter therefore I have get so difficult into the city restrain my curiosity from attempt to view this castle lest I shall rash expose myself to great danger further towards the north without the gate Renza be a large hospital for those that be sick of the plague have more chimney as they say than the year have day not tarre from the gate Genese be the church of s. Laurence which old be dedicate to Hercules by the emperor Erculeus bury in the same and it have a rare image of the virgin Marie and 16 stately marble pillar and the building be magnificent the emperor Theodosius be say to have give to s. Ambrose archbishop of Milan one of the nail wherewith Christ be fasten to the cross and the brazen serpent that Moses lift up in the desert the image of which serpent be of mix mettle vulgar call bronzo and they say that s. Ambrose leave these relic in the church of s. Tccla and of s. Ambrose and the altar under which the body of s. Ambrose lie be value at 28000 crown in the church Gratte belong to the be dictine friar not far from the gate Zobia be a stately throne and under it a unperfect monument which duke Lodovico Sforza purpose to have build for himself but the french cast he out of his dukedom and he die in France and in this monastery be a notable library and in the place where the friar eat the supper of our lord be paint with wonderful art in the little chapel of s. gottard be the sepulchre of that saint who name the mountain of the Alps do bear which be most famous for the difficult passage the great and stately cathedral church call be build all of white marble and support with some 100 marble pillar in which at this day they sing the mass of s. Ambrose differ from the roman mass and only agree therewith in the word of consecration from Milan to Cremona be account 52 mile and I make short stay at Milan for the danger of my abode there hire a horse to Cremona for a crown of gold want 8 soldi and ride out at the roman gate and bear the carrier company of who I hire my horse ride 12 mile the first day after dinner to Marignano through a plain country of rich pasture where I pay 3 real for my supper the second day I ride 30 mile to the castle Pizighitone through like rich pasture have by the way pay 24 soldi for my dinner and near my journey end 3 for my passage over the river Adda and at this castle 1 pay 30 soldi for my supper the french king Francis the first take prisoner by the emperor in the year 1525 be for a good space keep with much honour in the tower of this castle the three day in the morning 1 road ten mile to Cremona the family pallavicini at this day chief in the city when the empire Cremona of Rome decay make themselves lord of this city who viscount of Milan subdue and unite the city to the state of Milan and so by the say dukedom subdue by the spaniard it come into their subjection the cardinal Francis Sfondrato and Eusebius the writer of the ecclesiastical history be bear in this city the form of the city seem very like to a cardinal hat with broad brime and it be seat in a plain one mile distant from the river po. we enter this city by the narrow part lie towards Milan and there be a most strong fort build to keep the citizen in awe and keep by a spanish garrison and seat in a plain field wherein be no other building but the fort itself from hence go to the opposite and broad part of the city be a large and very fair market place near which be a tower or steeple of such height and beauty as the italian proverbial say one peter at Rome one haven at Ancona one tower at Cremona thereby note the excellency this tower be build of brick and have four hundred ninety and two stair in the ascent near the same be a statue of a giant who they say be overcome by Hercules the founder of the city and the citizen keep a feast once a year at which time with many ceremony they adorn this statue with rich robe near this tower and market place lie the stately cathedral church and the fair and rich monastery be that of saint dominick this city have many stately palace and the street thereof be broad and very pleasant here I pay thirty three soldi that be the four part of a Ducaton for my supper from hence to Mantua be forty five mile whether I hire a horse for five lyre the first day we ride twenty two mile where go out of the duchy of Milan and pass the river we enter the duchy of Mantua and then ride nine mile to Mercaria and by the way we pass the pleasant castle or rather city call Bozilia belong to julius Gonzaga be of the family of the duke of Mantua which castle be build with open cloister or arch towards the street under which the passenger walk dry in the great rain and such be the building of the city in this dukedom and in many neighbour place by the way also in a solitary inn I pay fifteen soldi for my dinner and at Mercaria I pay thirty four for my supper the second day we ride fourteen mile to Mantua through most fruitful field Mantua till after the manner of Lombardy and in a most dirty highway the history report that this city have the name of Manto the daughter of Tiresias it be seat in the midst of fen or lake make by the river Mencius the building be partly of brick partly of free stone and the street be large and clean the form of this city be round save that the foresay lake on the north and east-side enter into the city in the form of a half moon come from Cremona I enter Mantua on the west side by the gate Praedella where be a fair street call borgo on the same side towards the south be the gate Pistrella which lead to the duke stately palace call Teye seat some mile out of the city and compass with water where in the giant chamber I do see most fair picture and it be build in a quadrangle only two story high with a low roof after the manner of the build of Italy on the southside be the gate of (@) whence the way lie by the bank of the lake to a village call Petula two mile distant from the city in which they say that the famous poet Virgil be bear and show the house where his parent dwell partly on the north and partly on the east side the city be compass with lake which usual be cover with infinite number of water foul and from these lake there be a passage into the river Po and so by water to Venice on the north-east side be the gate of s. George whence between the two lake be a causey two hundred walk pace long and beyond it a bridge of stone five hundred pace long like to a fair gallery cover over the head and support with brick pillar have open window two pace distant one from the other then pass a drawbridge you come to another causey between the say two lake which causey be two hundred forty walk pace long before you come to firm land on the east side of the say bridge and within the city the duke stately palace lie upon the lake and to this palace join the cathedral church of saint Peter where also be a pleasant marketplace there lie the duke stable and in one of they be some hundred horse for the saddle and in the other as many for the coach and he have a three stable without the gate wherein be the like number of young colt on the north-side at the further bank of the lake be one only gate and a like bridge to pass into the suburb and there lic the way to the chief palace of the duke some few mile distant from the city call (@) the building whereof be only two story with a low roof and the chief chamber be hang with guild leather after the italian manner three skin whereof be common sell for a crown and the garden of this palace be exceed pleasant in the midst of the city Mantua be a large market place wherein the jew have their shop and sell all manner of ware for all traffic be in their hand grow rich by the poverty of the citizen and be so much favour by the duke as they dwell not in any several part of the city but where they lift and in the very marketplace neither be they force as in other part of Italy to wear yellow or red cap whereby they may be know but only a little piece of yellow cloth on the left side of their cloak so as they can hardly be distinguish from christian especial in their shop where they wear no cloak such be the privilege which the jew have get by bribe especial in the dutchey of Savoy through the unsatiable avarice of our christian prince near this marketplace be the large church of saint Andrew and the senate-house in which they show two statue of Cupid whereof one be ancient and of much great value than the other and a very long unicorn horn and a pair of organ of aliblaster beside jewel and vessel of gold and silver not farrethence be the three marketplace of Iustice to conclude at the gate of saint Francis church be the head of Virgil which the neapolitan say as in the description of that city I former say be steal from the sepulchre of Virgil upon the mount Pausilip in the palace call be another statue of Virgil sit at a table of brass as if he be write and crown with laurel I say former that there be a passage from the lake into the river Po and so by water to Venice and the duke to take his pleasure upon the water have a baot call because it will bear some two hundred and it be build in the upper part like a banquet house have five room with glaze window wherein the duke and his train do sit and these room be support upon a boat the mariner that row the same sit under the say room the first and large room whereof be fifteen walk pace long with bench on both side the second be eight pace long the three five and the four likewise five pace long the five be a gallery over the other room forty pace long and open to which they mount by stair out of the first room and this boat do not only much differ from our king barge aswell for the bigness as the rich furniture but also be flat in the bottom the water be still and calm on which it pass these room according to occasion have more or less rich hang when the duke either go out to disport himself or when he take any journey therein as oft he do it be unlawful to wear a sword without licence of the magistrate either at Milan Cremona Mantua or almost in any city of Italy only at Venice and Paduoa and the city of that state stranger may wear sword and only the wear of pistol or short gun be forbid at Mantua I pay three real each meal and be to depart thence I be force to take a bill of the customer by which they signify to the guard at the gate whether the passenger be to go on horseback on foot or by coach and what tribute he be to pay for which bill a footman pay 3. soldi another passenger six thus the prince of italy have small territory do not only burden their subject with tax but all stranger and strict take account from the exacter thereof be to go from hence to Paduoa we go out of the gate saint Ceorge and I hire a horse from Mantua to the castle for eleven lyre the first day we pass by a fort of the venetian most strong fortify upon the confine of that state which fort lie upon the river athesis and be call Lignaco and ride some twenty mile through a plain till after the manner of Lombardy to Monteguiara where I pay forty soldi that be two lyre for my supper the next morning I ride nine mile to the castle whence be the family of the duke of ferraria long flourish but now extinct from thence I pass by boat 15 mile to Paduoa and pay 22 soldi for my Paduoa passage this day when I return to Paduoa be the 14 of December after the new style in the year 1594 which city and the rate of victual there I have former describe chap. 4 of the sopulcher of Petrarch at Arqua of my journey to Vicenza Verona brescia and Bergnmo in Italy then pass the Alps to Chur Zurech solothurn Geneva and in my return thence to berna in Sweitzerland thence to strasburg in Germany and to Chalon to Paris to Rouen and to deep in France and final of my passagety (@) land to London in England while I expect the commodity of the spring for my journey home-ward I go to Venice to receive money there and retain a sufficient proportion in my hand I think to make over the rest to Paris by bill of exchange but France have be now long waste with civil war I can not find one merchant of Venice who have any the least traffic at Paris therefore persist still in my obstinate purpose to return by France I think at least to procure the change of this money to Geneva and so only out of my desire to see the city of Geneva I resolve again to pass over the Alps into Sweitzerland though I have former see those town then I buy a hungarian horse for twenty crown of a dutch gentleman new arrive in these part and while I prepare all thing necessary for my journey and expect a fit season of the year it come in my mind to see the monument not far distant of the famous poet Francis Petrarch and be willing to give my horse rest I go on foot with certain dutch gentleman thirteen mile to Arqua by the way we do see a most fair monastery praia and the bath of Abano the water whereof do boil with such heat as it will fetch off the skin be touch at Arqua be the sepulchre of Fetrarch of red marble spot and it have this inscription in latin to the worthy man f. p. a laureate poet his son in law Francis lus-debro of Milan for their inward conversation love affinitte and his succession leave this memory under that follow these verse Petrarchae sit virgin this stone do Francis petrarch bone enclose take my soul virgin spare it virgin son tire on earth in heaven let it repose then follow letter race out 1300 (@) 20 19 then follow the three inscription in latin with his image to Francis Petrarch Paulus admire his poem and succeed he in the possession of his house and field make this image in the year mdxcvii the ides of September there be also a fountain vulgar call the fountain of Petrarch upon which these verse be write Petrarcha this some god dwell here worship the sacred spring whence Petrarch drink heavenly rhyme do sing Petrarch dwell at Arqua and here in the same house wherein they say he dwell the history of petrarches life be paint where the owner of the house show we some household stuff belong to
