he and the very skin of a cat he love which they have dry and still keep here I do see his study a pleasant room especial for the sweet prospect and likewise a fair picture of Lucretia ready to die no situation can be imagine more pleasant then that of Arqua lie in the mouth of mountain abound with olive tree and open themselves upon a fruitful plain on the east and north side this plain yield nothing in pleasantness or in fruitfulness to that of Capua famous for the corrupt of (@) army but it be a (@) work to praise the euganian hill which so many poet and writer have magnify upon Friday the three day of march after the new style in the begin of the year 1593. according to the italian begin the year the first of january of the ar. 1595. end of the year 1594 according to the english begin the year upon the twenty five of march I turn my face to journey towards my dear country and the first day I ride eighteen mile to Vicenza through a most pleasant plain till after the manner of Lombardy where one and the same hold yield plenty of corn and have elm tree grow in the furrow which support the vine so that one field give bread wine and wood for to burn by the way my curiosity make i turn aside two mile out of the way that I may see a wonderful cave and a most pleasant parlour at Costoza in the house of Cesario Irento a gentleman of Vicenza the cave be large and fit to receive diverse band of soldier the parlour be call the prison of Aeolus god of the wind because there be certain mill which in summer time draw much wind out of hollow cave and disperse the same through all the chamber of the paliace refresh all that dwell there with a most pleasant cool air and upon this parlour this verse of Virgil be write Aeolus (@) cere Aeolus here in the wind prison reign the city of Vicenza be a fair city compass with a wall of brick but the building Vicenza howsoever it be very stately be not like to that of other city in these part in this one point namely that the second story of the house hang over the street and be support with arch give the passenger shelter from rain here I do see a theatre for play which be little but very fair and pleasant in the market place there be a stately palace and the monastery of saint Corona belong to the preach friar be fair build and have a rich library and the friar keep for a holy relic the thorn wherewith Christ be crown the city be subject to the venetian and be seat in a plain have mountain somewhat distant on the north and south side here I pay forty soldi for my supper and eighteen for three measure of oat call and for the stable so they call hay straw and the stable room and so I will hereafter call it I pay twenty here I hire a horse for fifty six for a footman that have attend i hither and be to return to Paduoa from Vicenza I ride thirty mile to Verona in a most pleasant plain till after the Verona manner of Lombardy lie on my left hand towards Italy far than I can see and have fruitful nil on my right hand towards the Alps abound with vine grow low upon stake and yield rich wine I enter Verona on the east side by the bishop gate call porta they write that the city be of old call Berona by the name of the founder thereof but the friar Leander of Bologna write that the city be build by the tuseans and have the name of the family vera and be after rebuild by the Galli cenomani this most fair city be build in the form of a lute the neck whereof lie towards the west on which side the river athesis run towards the east do not only compass the city but run almost through the centre of the body of this lute so as the less part of the body lie on the north side of the river the bank of athesis vulgar call (@) adice be join together with three bridge of stone and one of marble and be adorn on both side with many ruin of a old theatre and old triumphal arch the city be compass with a wall of brick and be seat towards the south upon the end of a large slony plain and towards the other side upon pleasant hill rise towards the distant mountain it be not build with the house cast out towards the street and support with arch to avoid rain as other city be in those part but the build of the house be stately and the cathedral church be remarkable for the antiquity as likewise the church of saint for the great beauty thereof and towards the wall the ground lie void of house as the manner be in strong town it have a pure air and be ennoble by the civility and ancient nobility of the citizen who be endue with a cheerful countenance magnificent mind and much incline to all good literature Verona be a free city under the empire about the year 1155 till the family of the grow great in the city about the year 1259 do by little and little invade the freedom of the city and make themselves lord over it at last Anthony Scaliger kill his brother Bartholomew partner with he of that lordship about the year 1381 be drive out of the city by viscount John the first duke of Milan and he be dead William Scaliger help by Francis drive the Garrilon of Milan out of the city in the year 1404. but the say Francis kill the say William by poison and the family of the scaliger be then so waste as scarce any one be to be find of that name the venetian take occasion by this detestable treason of the say Francis to make the city subject to they but their army be defeat by the french in the year 1509 by a composition make between the french king and the emperor Maximilian the city become subject to the say emperor till the venetian recover the same out of his hand in the year 1517 under who subjection the city to this day flourish in great abundance of all thing on the north-side of the city without the wall be the mountain Baldo hang over the city and famous for the great plenty of medicinable herb and upon the side of this mountain within the wall be no building but only a strong fort. on the south side lie the way to Mantua 23 mile distant and upon the same side lie the foresay stony plain five mile long and ennoble with many skirmish battle and victory in this plain the consul Caius Marius defeat the cimbri and odoacer king of the heruli who destroy the western empire be defeat by theodoricus king of the ostrogothe and the dutch emperor Arnolphus duke of Bavaria be defeat by Hugh of Burgundy then possess Italy upon the same south side within the wall be a fair market place and the palace of the venetian governor which governor in Italy be vulgar call Podesta and necre the wall on this side lie a stately monument of a old amphitheatre at this day little ruin vulgar call Harena and build by Luc: Flaminius though other say it be build by the emperor Octavius it pass in bigness all the old amphitheatre in Italy and the outside thereof be of marble and the inner side with all the seat be of brick it be of a oval form and the inner yard be sixety three walk pace long and forty eight broad where the low seat be most narrow whence the seat arise in forty four stair or degree howsoever other write that there be only forty two degree and they so arise as the upper be still of great circuit than the low and the shop of the citizen build on the outside under the say increase of the inner circuit have about fifty two walk pace in breadth which be to be add to make the full breadth of the inside it have eighteen gate and between every arch be very fair statue and the seat within the same be say to be capable of twenty three thousand one hundred eighty and four beholder each one have a foot and a half allow for his seat each one of we give two gagetti to the keeper of this monument Alboinus king of the Lombard be kill by his wife at Verona in the monastery of saint zeno be a monument erect to Pipin son to Charles the great and between this monastery and the next church in a church yard under the ground be the monument of queen Amalasaenta Barengarius king of Italy be kill at Verona and this city brag of two famous citizen namely the old poet Catullus and a late writer the territory of this city be most fruitful abound with all necessary for life and more special with rich wine particular the retian wine much praise by Pliny and prefer to the wine of by Virgil which the king of the goth be wont to carry with they as far as Rome it be of a red colour and sweet and howsoever it seem thick more fit to be eat then drink yet it be of a most pleasant taste the lake be much commend for the store of good carp and other good fish beside this territory yield very good marble here I pay forty soldi for my supper and sixteen for the stable that be for hay and straw and eighteen for three measure of oat certain gentleman bear i company from Paduoa to this city and be to return thither do here each of they hire a horse for three lyre and a half to Vicenza where they be to pay for their horse meat from hence I ride fifteen mile to the castle Peschiera build by the old lord of Verona and seat upon the lake Bennacus vulgar call Lago where they demand of i two quatrine for the passage of a bridge but when I show they my that be a paper witness that I be a scholar of Paduoa they dismiss i as free of all tribute and in like sort by the same writing I be free at Paduoa from pay six soldi and at Verona from pay eight soldi I ride from this castle seven mile to a village seat upon the same lake famous for the pleasant territory and the abundance of good fish and here I pay twenty soldi for my dinner and eight for my horse meat all my journey this day be in a most sweet plain rise still high with fair distance so as the ascent can hardly be see after dinner I ride eighteen mile to brescia which city flourish under thelold brescia ' emperor of Italy then be subject to the Lombard and tyrant king of Italy and they be overcome to Charles the great and french governor then to the western emperor of Germany and to the italian family of the and it obtain of the emperor Otho the privilege to be a free city of the empire till be waste by the faction of the Guelphi and Gibellini the a family of the same city make themselves lord thereof who the viscount of Milan cast out of the city and when Philip Maria duke of Milan oppress the city and will not be induce to ease the same of his great imposition they yield themselves in the year 1509 to the french king who have defeat the venetian army then by the french king agreement with the emperor maximiltan the city be give into the emperor hand who nephew the emperor Charles the five restore the same to the french king Francis the first who likewise in the year 1517 give the same into the hand of the venetian the most fruitful territory of brescia have mine of iron and brass and I think so many castle village and house so little distant the one from the other can hardly be find else where the brook garza run through the city which be of a round form and be seat for the most part in a plain and towards the north upon the side of a mountain where a tower be build which have many house adjoin and in this tower or castle the Venctian governor dwell who take a oath that he will never go out of the same till a new governor be send from Venice the city building be of brick the street be large and be pave with flint Boniface Bembus be a citizen of brescia and the brescian as also the citizen of Bergamo be in manner and custom more like the french their old lord than the other italian far distant from France and the very woman receive and give salutation and converse with the french liberty without any offence to their husband which other italian will never endure here I pay forty soldi for my supper and forty for four measure of oat and for the stable from hence I ride thirty two mile to Bergamo and as the territory in this part of Bergamo Italy lie upon the south sun which beat upon the side of the hill and mountain with great reflection of heat and upon the other side defend from the cold wind of the north and east by the interposition of the Alps be singular fruitful and pleasant so for the first twenty mile of this day journey they seem to i more pleasant than the very plain of Capua yield plenty of corn and of vine grow upon elm in the furrow of the land which elm be plant in such artificial row as the prospect thereof much delight the eye and the other twelve mile be yet more pleasant be till in like sort and towards my journey end yield most large and rich pasture the city Bergamo after the roman empire be extinct first obey the Lombard than the french and follow the fortune of brescia be sometime subject to the viscount of Milan and other prince invade their liberty which they have under the empire and other time be subdue by diverse of their own citizen and be oppress by the duke of Milan they yield themselves in the year 1428 to the venetian who army be deicate the next year by the french this city likewise submit itself to they and they be cast out of Italy it be subject to the sfortian family duke of Milan and they be extinct and the emperor and french king make war for the dukedeme of Milan this city in the year 1516 return under the power of the venetian who at this day enjoy the same in peace the city be seat upon a mountain upon the southside whereof a fort be build and under the mountain towards the east be two large suburb full of fair house and church near the market place in the church of saint Mary be a stately sepulchre of marble and in the monastery of the preach friar be a rich library these citizen speak the italian tongue but more rude than any other of Italy here I pay four lyre for my supper and horse-meat and twelve for my breakfast from hence I take not the right way to Geneva but decline to the way of Chur aswell because it be more safe from robbery as to be free from all danger by venture again to pass through the state of Milan when I come from Paduoa I be not curious to find out companion for this my long journey aswell because I hope to find some by the way as for that I be now use to converse with any christian stranger little care to be solitary by the way but deceive of this my hope to find company I pass all alone not so much as accompany with a footman over the high Alps which I think very few have do beside myself from Bergamo I ride nine mile to Trescher where I first enter the mouth of the Alps and thence I ride nineteen mile to lover pass by many very pleasant lake and by the way I pay sixteen gagetti that be thirty two for four horse shoe be to pass from hence over the steep and snowy Alps I cause my horse to be shod with eight sharp and three blunt nail for which I pay six and for my supper twenty eight and for three measure of oat twenty four and for the stable eighteen the second day I ride thirty two mile to the village Edoll through high mountain and there I pay three lyre for my supper and horse-meat the three day in the morning I ride ten mile to a village Auryga over a most high and steep mountain of the same name and now I begin to freeze for cold though before I enter the Alps I can hardly endure the heat of the clime hence I go forward one mile to a little brook which divide the territory of the vene ian and the Grysons which (@) free people confederate with the canton of Sweitzerland and five mile further to villa where I pay twenty six of Venice for my dinner and horse-meat and it be now the time of Lent they give we flesh to eat whereof I be glad as of a dainty I can not get in Italy neither will they gratify the italian their neighbour in provide any thing else for they so as they be force to eat flesh without any scruple of conscience which this people of the reform religion will little have regard after dinner I ride ten mile to Poschiano through a most pleasant valley compass on all side with mountain where I pay two berlinghotti or two lyre of Venice for my supper and breakfast for all passenger use to break their fast in go over the Alps and one berlinghotto for five measure of oat and for the stable the four day in the morning for twelve mile I ascend the mountain and after ride thirteen mile to Lasagna through a valley cover with snow where I pay four batzen for my supper and as much for my breakfast and six batzen for two measure of oat and two batzen for hay straw and stable room I former say that I buy a horse at Paduoa and he be a stone horse and those of the territory of Venice and all Lombardy use to ride upon mare which they put in the same stable with horse it happen at Verona that the ostler let my horse loose that the rascal may make himself sport with his cover of the mare which for that time I know not but after manifest find since ever after he be contrary to custom very troublesome to i with neigh and curvet when soever (@) (@) by any mare and in this day journey as when soever I pass the narrow way of steep mountain he be most troublesome to i so as this people of the Alps common use mare for their carriage whensoever I meet they I be force not without danger to light from my horse and though I hold he by the bridle yet he be so fierce as I can hardly keep he from fall down most steep mountain or from be drown in the snow which make i repent the buy of he though otherwise he be rich worth my money the building of the grison be of free stone but low and for three part of the year the house be cover with snow and the window thereof be glaze and large and for three part of the year they only open a little quarry of glass and present shut it again and all the window for the most part be continual cover with window of wood lest the heat of the stoave shall go out or any cold enter into the same the five day in the morning I ride twenty four italian mile which the grison account four mile to the town Lanzi and have pass three high mountain and after enter into a plain upon the next ascent of the mountain I may first discern the open of they towards the north and then begin to descend the Alps into Germany in this passage of the Alps I do many time observe mountain of snow to fall from the high mountain into the valley with such noise as if it have thunder and this noise many time preserve passenger from be over whelm with the same fall many time into the very high way out of the wood near Lanzi in the twilight of the evening I do hear more than a hundred wolf howl and because it be towards night I have hire a country churl to guide i unto the town who tremble for fear and desire i to make ready my Carbiner to shoot at they for he say nothing terrify they more than the smell of powder I wish he to be of good cheer because the wolf seem busy about a prey and the town be near at hand promise that I will not forsake he but if need be let he ride behind i but fear give he wing so as he go as fast as my horse can trot within short space we come to Lanzi where I pay sixteen batzen for my supper breakfast and horse-meat the six day in the morning I ride fifteen italian mile which the Grysons call two mile to Chur a city and the seat of a bishop through little mountain cover with snow the head of the river Rhine be distant from this city as far as a footman may go in half a hour and it lie towards the south the city lie in length from the church on the north-side towards the south and have spend a hour in view the same I ride further four mile of Sweitzerland through mountain cover with snow to Walstat where I pay five batzen for my supper and to gratify my dutch consort four batzen for drink after supper vulgar call schlaffdrunke that be the sleep cup and three batzen for my horse-meat the seven day in the morning I pass two mile I mean always the mile of the country by boat upon the lake Walsea that be the wall sea because it be compass with mountain and I ride two most long mile more over hill to the little city Rabesuele and for the passage of myself and my horse over the lake I pay seven batzen and for oat for my horse while I expect consort I pay three creitzer the foresay little city be confederate with the Sweitzar canton and here I pay eighteen batzen for my supper with extraordinary fare and my breakfast and horse-meat the eight day in the morning after I have ride four hour space for the sweitzer mile be so long and of so uncertain measure as they use to measure their journey by hour ride not by mile I wonder to hear that we have ride but one mile our way be through pleasant hill plant with vine grow upon short stake as the dutch use to plant they here we dine in a village and throughout all this territory I pay about seven batzen a meal after dinner have in three hour ride three mile my horse weary of this long journey without so much as a day rest begin to faint so as I be force in a village to give he some two hour rest and some provender and my way hitherto be through pleasant hill in like sort plant with vine on my right hand towards the east and by the side of the lake Zurechzea on the left hand towards the west and the pleasantness of this village seat among hill plant with vine on the east side of the say lake make i as willing as my horse to rest there the same evening I ride further one mile to Zurech which city I former describe in my former passage through Sweitzerland I former say that for the unpossibilitie to exchange my money from Venice to Paris I be force to exchange the same to Geneva for which cause and out of my desire to view that city famous for reformation of religion after some few day I take my journey thither turn out of my high way the first day in the morning through a way most pleasant for the variety of plain hill orchard wood and garden wherein I pass by a ancient castle of the count of Habspurg I ride in eight hour space to a village where I lodge and pay a frank and a half french money for my supper and horse-meat the second day in the morning through a plain heath wood and hilly ground for pasture I ride in four hour space to a village and there as in the rest of this journey I pay about seven batzen of dutch money for a meal after dinner through like way I ride in three hour space to solothurn a ancient city and one of the sweitzer canton call in latin and it have the solothurn name in both tongue as the tower of the sun or as consist only of tower whereof there be many one tower thereof be of great antiquity and upon it these verse in latin be write what be old among the Celts then Solidure nothing but treir who sister sure they will have this city build in the time of the patriarch Abraham the three day in the morning I ride in four hour space to Arberg by the side of a great river call be pass twice over it by two bridge after dinner I ride in four hour space to Morton through pleasant mile of corn and wood and pasture and by the side of the lake Mortonzra not far hence Charle duke of Burgunay be defeat by the sweitzer in the year 1476 and there in a field lie the bone of the soldier there kill the burgundian be thrice beat in one day and here in the last battle duke Charles also be kill the four day in the morning I ride in three hour to Bitterline through fruitful corn field and pasture and after dinner in four hour space I ride to milden and about the midst of the way do see the ruin of the ancient city auenza or which julius Caesar utter race and corn be now sow within the old circuit of the city whereof no memory remain but one ruinous tower and a statue but they say that the husbandman till the ground do many time dig up old roman coin of silver and gold not far thence towards the west lie the snowy mountain which divide the territory of Burgundy and Sweitzerland the five day in five hour space I ride to Losanna through mountain cover Losanna with snow and thick wood this city be subject to berna be one of the sweitzer canton but the citizen speak French it be seat on the north side of the lake of Losanna of old call Lacus (@) which be compass with mountain continual cover with snow which open themselves on the eastside towards Italy on the eastside of the city be the head of the river Rhodanus which full into this lake have so clear a colour as it seem not at all to mingle with the stand water of the lake from hence I ride by the west side of this lake and in two hour space come to which town be also subject to berna then I ride four mile in four hour space to Geneva have the sandy bank of the say lake on my left hand towards the east and most pleasant hill plant with Geneva vine on my right hand towards the west and by the way I do see a village ruin in time of war nothing there stand but a pillar erect in honour of the papist mass Geneva be seat on the south side of the lake right opposite to losanna seat at the north end thereof the east side of the lake lie towards Savoy and Italy and the west side towards France on which side also the high way lie into Sweitzerland the low part of which city vulgar be seat in a plain and the rest upon a Hill the building be fair and of freestone this city be confederate with some of the sweitzer Cantones and more strict with berna have defend the freedom of the citizen and the profession of reform religion for many year with great courage and piety and through many misery and practice to subduethem against the pretend right of the bishop and the duke of savoyes ambition and hatred he bear to the reform religion the low part thereof on the north side lie close to the south side of the lake where be a little haven for galley which they have build to keep free the passage of the lake and on the same side be a strong fort and there the river Rhodanus come out of the lake enter the city and run through the low part thereof have two bridge for passage the duke of Savoy who have long watch to surprise this city possess the east side of the lake but the city be careful not to suffer he to build any galley thereupon and upon the least rumour of build they arm their galley to burn the same therefore the way into Savoy lie upon the east south east side of the city in a plain between hill and mountain the city have build a fort of little circuit but great strength with fortification of earth some musket shoot without the wall upon that way and therein continual keep a garrison not far thence the river arba flow from the eastern mountain do beyond the city fall into Rhodanus at the south gate be a public churchyard for burial and a hospital or pest house which be both without the wall on the same side within the wall be a pleasant walk upon hill where of old a pillar be erect with this inscription to the emperor Caesar m. Aurelius Antoninus Pius by foelix Aug. great bishop with tribunal power consul etc. on the west side of the city without the wall little mountain lie not far distant may seem dangerous for the encamp of enemy save that on the one side they be compass with the territory of berne confederate with the city and on the other side with the river Rhodanus so as the enemy passage to they be very difficult this city be of old repair by the emperor Aurelius and julius Caesar make mention of this city in his first book of the gaul war so as the antiquity thereof can be doubt here I have great contentment to speak and converse with the reverend father Theodore Beza who be of stature something tall and corpulent or big bone and have a long thick beard as white as snow he have a grave senator countenance and be broad face but not fat and in general by his comely person sweet affability and gravity he will have extort reverence from those that lest love he I walk with he to the church and give attention to his speech it happen that in the church porch I touch the poor man box with my finger and this reverend man soon perceive my error who have use in Italy to dip my finger towards the holy water according to the manner of the papist lest the omit of so small a matter general use may make i suspect of my religion and bring i into danger of great consequence do now in like sort touch this poor man box mistake it for the font of holy water I say he do soon perceive my error and take i by the hand advise i hereafter to eschew these ill custom which be so hardly forget when I have take counsel with my friend if it be safe for i to go the right way from Geneva to Paris they be of great experience dissuade i from that journey which can not but be dangerous the peace be scarce conclude and the country be full of band of soldier return to their own home which council after I find good by experience the mother of fool and when they perceive that I be obstinate purpose to pass through France into England they advise i rather to pass into France by the dukedom of Lorraine which for the time be more free from the tumult of war who council I think good to follow so as I be now to return to Strassburg in Germany almost the same way I come thus after noon I leave Geneva and ride that day four mile to the second day in the morning I ride in two hour space to Losanna and in five hour space to milden where I pay eight batzen for my dinner and horse-meat after dinner in four hour space I ride to Bitterline and pay fourteen batzen for my supper and horse-meat the three day in the morning I ride one mile as they call it in four hour space to Morton and in three hour space to berne one of the sweitzer canton through sandy field of corn and many wood at Geneva many french gentleman and student come thither for the liberty of their religion do speak pure french and from that city all the people speak a barbarous french till I come near berne where they first begin to speak the sweitzer language be to describe berne give i leave first for traveller sake to mention what I have berne read in some author that in the territory of which I never view and who be earnest papist and so may just be suspect in like report there be a wonderful lake upon the bank whereof they say Pilate do once in a year walk anire in judge robe and that whosoever then see he do die the same year the most fair city berne have the name of bear in the dutch tongue because berthold duke of Zeringen be to build the city and go four to hunt think good to give it the name of the first beast he shall meet and kill and there be a wood of oak in the very place where the city be to be build the workman cut the same for the build of the city do sing this rhyme in dutch holtz alas muss heissen bern. wood let we willing cut thou this city must bern name be they write that the ground whereupon the city be build be of old call the sack and that the city thereupon be build in form of a sack this most fair city be not of any great antiquity for berthold the founder thereof die in the year 1175. it be build upon a little mountain yet seem to be seat in a valley because it be compass with great mountain the little mountain whereupon it be seat be narrow and the full breadth thereof be within the wall neither be it much long than the city lie in length from the west to the east in which length it have three fair and broad street and be fortify round about with the valley of this little mountain the house be uniform and very fair build of free-storie have the first upper room of the house cast out towards the street and support with arch under which they walk dry in the great rain round about this little mountain the citizen have their garden from the fall of the same to the low valley and upon the southeast by south be a most fair church and very pleasant for the light someness thereof and on every side there be a pleasant place for walk on the south side without the wall the river arba run from the west to the east and be pass by a bridge at the east end of the city whence it turn towards the north and so make the city almost a island here I pay fourteen batzen for my supper and horsemeate the four day in the morning I ride three mile in six hour space through fruitful field of corn and pasture to solothurn and by the way I observe a monument of the english defeat by the sweitzer with this in scription in dutch Ritterlich english guckler 1425 arm jucke that be the english juggler knightly beat in the year 1425 poor knave the english history make no mention of any war with the sweitzer Semler a sweitzer historian in his first book fifty four leaf write that Leopold duke of Austria draw the english against the sweitzer and that they do much hurt by waste the territory aswell of Austria as of Sweitzerland but that they be overcome in some battle do after the waste of these country return home in the year 1376 and this he call the first english war also Semler in his first book the leaf 273 write that the english as it seem call from the french war do at the instance of duke Leopold besiege Strassburg in the year 1365 but the emperor Charles the four come with a army against they that they retire but neither do the word of Semler agree since he call the first attempt the first english war and after mention another of former time neither do the year set down by Semler agree with the year of this monument neither seem it by the french history that the english have any leisure to make war upon the sweitzer in the year 1365 and less in the year 1376. to conclude it appear aswell by the english as french history that the english conqueror in France have so weighty a war lie there upon they in the year 1425 set down in this monument as it be not credible they can at that time turn their force any other way only the most approve french writer witness that the english and french have make a truce for eighteen month in the year 1443 it seem good to the leader on both side that the soldier hate rest and peace shall be draw out to some foreign war and that the english serve under Lewis the dolphin of France the say Lewis in the year 1444 make war upon the sweitzer kill therein 4000 of they but the victory be so bloody of this battle fight in the territory of bazel as he that have the victory lose some 5000 man of his own and that the emperor Frederick the three come against he he draw his man back for my part I leave the credit of this monument to be try by the consent of historian and return to my journey the five day in the morning I ride four mile to the town Ottmersea and in the afternoon through a stony plain of corn and some wood I ride four mile to besa the six day in the morning I ride five mile through the like plain to Gerzen and in the afternoon through a woody heath plain and towards my journey end through fruitful field of corn I ride four mile to Strassburg and in all this journey I pay about seven batzen for each meal from solothurne to Strassburg some reckon senentene mile other twenty two mile for the dutch reckon the mile diverse according to the length of they in their own country and in these part they use to distinguish their journey by hour ride not by mile not far from the foresay town besa lie the city Bazell which I have describe in my former journey through these part but to gratify those who love to search antiquity give i leave to say that Augusta rauracorum so call for distinction from Augusta vindelicorum a city of great antiquity and at this day become a poor village lie distant from bazell some mile towards the mountain jura and that near this ancient city be many old monument of the roman and many building under the earth which myself be less curious do not see and that the husbandman there dig up late a coin of gold and sell it for copper which be after value at nine crown of the old roman I say nothing of Strassburg which I have in the foresay place former describe only I will say that I have the good fortune there to find a french gentleman the governor of Monwick with his train in who company I ride thither (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) a fruitful plain of corn four mile to Sauerne in which city the papistical canon of Strassburg have long fortify themselves under the protection of the duke of Lorraine against their lord the senator of Strassburg and have appropriate to themselves great part of the revenue of that bishopric lie under their power after dinner I ride three mile through hill yet cover with snow to Villa concern my expense I spend each day little less than a french crown namely two francke for my supper and common three french sou for my breakfast and one frank for my horsemeate the second day I ride one mile to the confine of the empire and the dukedom Monwick of Lorraine and some three mile further to Monwick where so much salt be make as the duke of Lorraine yearly receive sixty thousand french crown for the same the three day through a dyrtie way and fruitful field of corn I ride five mile to the city Nanzi where the duke of Lorraine keep his court and when I be enter Nanzi the gate the captain of the guard draw towards i to know my name and country I not ignorant that the family of Lorraine usurp great power in France under the pretence to defend the roman religion bear no good will to the english at that time answer that I be a polonian he inquire many thing of the kingdom king and queen of Poland and perceive that I answer he direct he whisper something with some chief man of the guard about my confidence and so turn again to i bid i lift up my hand for so the french use to take oath I be much afraid lest I shall be force upon this oath to confess my country which I have dissemble but when I demand the cause he tell i that many place be infect by the plague I be to swear that I come not from any of they which to be free from my former fear I do glad assure he upon my oath the city be strong fortify save that the south side where the circuit of the city be enlarge be not yet compass with wall neither be the house on that side yet build the house be fair build of free stone the duke palace be build four square with a large inner court all of free stone and with a high gallery towards the say court-yard and there I have the opportunity to see the duke and the prince and the princess his child find not here any company for my journey into France which I hope to find at metz and think it not convenient to stay long than I must Metz. needs in a place for the time ill affect to the english I ride the four day eight french mile to Metz. in the time of the emperor Charles the five the french take this city from the empire and at this day it be hold for Henry the four king of France by a garrison of his man and every one now talk of peace make in France yet it be not then proclaim in these part nor upon any of the confine of France myself after few day stay find no consort for my journey into France be admonish by some honest gentleman in this city that this journey will be very dangerous to i in respect that the army be break up all France will be full through all part of scatter troop of soldier return to their own home but when they perceive that I be obstinate in my purpose to pass through France into England they persuade i at least to sell my horse and go on foot for they say the booty of a good horse will sure cause i to be rob by those who may perhaps let i pass quiet on foot disguise in poor apparel for they see i well mount will sure set upon i and twenty to one kill i aswell because they that rob in France do common kill they they rob as because they will imagine i to be a soldier either on the king or on the leaguer side and in that case if I be on their own side will kill i for fear of be force to restitution and if I be on the adverse part will think i well kill as a enemy beside that the marshal of the kingdom at the end of a civil war use such severity of justice to suppress all disorder as they will sure kill i lest I shall complain of they whereas if I pass on foot they be like either to let i go in peace or at least to be content with my money without offer further violence to i who they will judge to be of some base condition this their counsel I approve and howsoever unwilling sell my horse for sixteen french crown in this city I pay a frank each meal it be a fair city and well fortify and it be seat upon a hill in a fat soil fruitful of corn and the river mosella run from Nancey in a plain pass close by the west gate of the city where it be to be pass by a cover bridge be to take my journey towards Paris on foot I hire a poor man to guide i to chalons and to carry my cloak and my little baggage the first day after dinner we go two french mile by the bank of the say river through a dyrtie way and a country fruitful of corn but have no wood not so much as a tree and come to a poor village by the way I pass by Pontmolin where of old be a fair bridge to pass over mosella the ruin whereof at this day be call arch de joy in this my journey to Paris I spend each day for my diet about twenty four sou the second day we go two mile to Pont and five mile to toul where I pay two testoone and a half for a pair of shoe our way be very dyrtie through fruitful field of corn and we often hear the cry of the country people drive their cattle to fortify place upon the see of some scatter troop of soldier which make we much afraid and that not without just cause but God deliver we from this danger the three day I be so weary as I can not go on foot hire for one Frank two post horse return homeward for i and my guide to ride 2 mile and after 2 horse for three frank for we to ride three mile to saint aubine the four day I hire two horse for we for a french crown and we ride five mile to bar in a fair way through pasture and hill plant with vine yield a rich wine and fruitful field of corne. and this day we pass by the village Longeville which the country people have fortify in this civil war to defend themselves from be spoil by sudden invasion though otherwise they be each night force to lodge some troop or other have dine plentiful at bar and be refresh with excellent wine in the after noon we go four mile through the like way to the village ampton Cor where a french gentleman dwell who the same day have there proclaim the peace for now we have pass the confine of Lorraine and this be the first village of France in the province of champagne the five day be after the new style the first of May in the year 1595 we go on foot nine long french anno. 1595. mile to chalons at the end of the first two mile we come to the first house stand alone and call that be the red house inhabit only by a old woman who will give we nothing to eat or drink and in all the rest of the way we do neither see village nor house nor so much as a tree the field be plain and all the province according to the name be a champion country and seem apt to bear great store of corn but now in the time of civil war they lay unplough and the husbandman house be fall to the ground yea we can not find so much as water to quench our thirst so as my guide be force to drink the stand water lie in the cartrut of the high way and myself to quench my thirst do chew the crust of a brown loaf which he have give i whereby I keep my mouth moist cast the crust away when I have chew it we have now scarce enter France when sudden the mischief fall upon i which my friend at metz have foretell i when I have pass half this day journey I meet with some dozen horseman who captain demand of i my name and country I answer that I be a dutch man and the servant of a dutch merchant who stay for i at chalons whether I be then go he as it seem to i think it dishonourable to he if he shall himself assault a poor fellow and a stranger do let i pass but before I come to the bottom of the hill I may see he send two horseman after i who wheel about the mountain that I may not know they be of his company sudden rush upon i and with fierce countenance threaten death present their carbine to my breast I have no ability to defend i think good not to make any the least show of resistance so they take my sword from my guide and be content only to rob i of my money I former say that I can not find at Venice any mean to exchange my money to Paris the long civil war have bar the parisian from any traffic in foreign part and that I be force to exchange my money to Geneus this money there receive I have quilt within my doublet and when I resolve to go on foot to Paris I make i a base cover for my apparel which when they perceive they take from i the inward doublet wherein I have quilt the gold and though they perceive that under my basecover I have a jerkin and hose lay with gold lace yet they be content to take only the inner doublet and to leave i all the rest of my apparel wherein I do acknowledge their courtesy since thief give all they do not take beside they take not only my crown but my sword cloak and shirt and make a very unequal exchange with i for my hat give i another deep greasy french hat for it one thing in this misery make i glad I former say that I sell my horse for 16. french crown at metz which crown I put in the bottom of a wooden box and cover they with a stink ointment for scab six other french crown for the worst event I lap in cloth and thereupon do wind diverse colour thread wherein I stick needle as if I have be so good a husband as to mend my own clothes this box and this ball of thread I have put in my hose as thing of no worth and when in spoul i they have search my pocket they first take the box and smell the stink of the ointment they cast it away on the ground neither be they so frugail to take my ball of thread to mend their hose but do tread it likewise under their foot then they ride swift to their companion and I with some spark of joy in my great loss take up the box and ball of thread think myself less miserable that by the grace of God I have some money leave to keep i from beg in a strange country this tragedy thus act I and my guide very sad because he despair of my ability to pay he his hire go forward our journey he wonder that I be no more deject in the danger I have pass and for my miserable want of money think that I have never a penny leave who he do see so narrow search and yet perceive that I be in some sort merry at last we do see the city of challons not far distant and upon our left hand be a fair spring which have seven head to which we go to drink be both very thirsty here I put into the water the hat which the thief have give i by unequal exchange for i be greasy to the very top and deep according to the french fashion and fill it with water thrice drink it up greedy then I fill it the four time and break into it the crumb of the brown loaf the crust whereof have to that time keep my mouth with some moisture which I devour and think I have never eat better brewis but three day sickness of vomit and looseness make i repent this intemperance thence we go to chalons where my guide bring i to a poor alehouse and when I expostulate the wrong he do i he reply that stately inn be not for man who have never a penny in their purse but I tell he that I look for comfort in that case rather from gentleman than clown whereupon he willing obey i and with a deject and fearful countenance bring i to the chief inn where he cease not to bewail my misery and to recount my tragedy as if it have be the burn of Troy till the very host despair of my ability to pay he begin to look disdainful upon i the next morning when he be to return home and take his leave of i I pay he his hire which he neither ask nor expect think that I have not one penny and likewise pay my host for my supper and lodging he first begin to talk like a mad man and come to himself profess that he know not how I shall have one penny except I be a juggler or a alchumist or have a familiar spirit then confound between wonder and joy he begin to triumph with the servant and will not depart till he have first drink a quart of wine the building of chalons be low and base be of timber and clay and this city have no beauty but in the large marketplace and strong fort. on the west side chalons without the wall be pleasant land whether the citizen use to pass by boat and to walk there for recreation I former say that I spend in this journey some forty two sou by the day for my diet after which rate I pay here and if extraordinary I call for wine I pay two sou and a half for a measure little big than our english pint from hence to Paris I pass in a long waggon of Paris and pay two french crown for my place therein the first day we pass in like way to the former and in the same province of champagne fourteen mile to sizan and do scarce see two poor village by the way but I be tell that some half a mile out of the high way be the castle Chastilton whereof the admiral of France kill in the massacre of Paris and the gentleman of his family have their name the second day we be carry 12 mile to Nangi be as vet not free from the cry of poor people drive their cattle from troop of soldier but for my part I make the proverb true that the passenger have nothing sing before the thief yet be I not without fear of a great mischief than rob by the loss of my life have no money to redeem it from the cutthroat soldier the three day we be carry ten mile in champagne through a champion country lie waist and 4 mile more to Paris through a fruitful plain of corn and pleasant hill plant with vine this country wherein Paris be seat be compail with the river of seyne and matrona and orsa and be proper call the island of France the parisian have their name either from Paris of Troy or of the a people of Asia which Paris do accompany Hercules or of the temple of Isis neete they according to the greek language for the statue of Isis be at saint German till it be take away in the year 1514 and a cross be set up in the place thereof by the bishop of Molun the city have the name of Lutetia in latin either of dirt for the fen adjoin or in the greek tongue of mortar there dig out because all the floare be of plaster and the house plaster over and some say that it be of old call the city of julius Caesar who build great part thereof it lie in the elevation of the pole forty eight degree and the chief part thereof namely the island or great city be seat in a fenny ground for the river seyne have often overflow Paris and break down the bridge in the time of king Philip Augustus the water rise to the statue without the cathedral church of saint Mary on the north-side thereof as appear by a inscription also in the year 1373 for two month space they so overflow the city as they pass in boat the street of saint Denis and s. antoine to conclude omit many overflow mention in history it appear by a inscription in the valley of misery that in the year 1496 there be a great inundation the city of old be all in the island and when it can not receive the multitude increase the city be enlarge to both side of the continent and first that part of the city call than the three part call the university be esteem suburb till after they be join to the city for the king court and the city still increase with building so as the subburbe be great than the city whereupon king Charles the five give they the same privilege which the city have and compass they with wall whereof the ruin yet appear and new suburb be afterward build king Henry the second in the year (@) make a edict that the house unpersect shall be pull down and that no more shall afterward be build the river seyne run from the south and enter at the southside divide the city into two part the great part whereof towards the east and north lie low in a plain and be vulgar call the less lie towards the south and west upon a high ground be seat between hill and be call the university between those two part lie the three namely the island call the city which be seat in a plain and compass on all side with the river seyne run between the ville and the university and this part be of old join to the university with two bridge and to the ville with three bridge but now a six call the new bridge do moreover join the island aswell to the as to the university the part of the city call the ville be compass on the south and west side with the river seyne and upon the east and north side with wall rampier and ditch in the form of half a circle the second part of the city call the university be compass on the east and north side with the river seyne and upon the south and west side with wall which they write to have the form of a hat save that the long suburb somewhat alter this form for my part it seem to i that join with the island it have also the form of another half circle though somewhat less than the former the three part call the island or city be compass round about with the river seyne and upon the southeast side be defend from the flood of the river by four little land which be mark in the map with black ine and lie like rampire divert the stream from beat on the city to this island they pass on both side by bridge and in respect of the bishop palace and he king great palace it may be call the heart of the city the old wall of the be first of less circuit than now they be for new wall be build which also include the suburb and the inner wall be of unpolished stone the outer wall be of earth compail round about with ditch which near the river be broad and full of water but further off towards the north and east be narrow and altogether dry but the old wall be either demolish or convert to the support of private house the university be compass with like wall and because it be seat upon high ground the ditch be altogether dry and the wall of earth aswell of the ville as the university be so broad as three or four may walk together upon they and round about the city I mean the ville and university compass the island be many rampier upon the wall like so many fort the island or city be of old compass with wall wherewith the great palace lie towards the north at this day be compass Paris in general be subject to the king so as it have under he a peculiar jurisdiction and in spiritual matter it be subject to the bishop in the time of king Lewis the eleven one hundred and four thousand citizen be number able to bear arm in the year 1466 and king Charles the five in the year 1371 give the citizen the right and privilege of gentleman king Philip Augustus in the year 1090 make shiriffes to govern the city with consular authority and he give the city for arm a ship adorn with lily he pave the street with flint and compass the city with wall the parisian have raise many sedition the first in the year 1306 against rich man raise the rent of house the second with the king of Navarre and the english against the dolphin the three in the year 1383 against the king treasurer which Charles the six return with his army out of Flanders do severe punish the four between the faction of Orleans and Burgundy the five most pestilent and long with the guisian against the last king of Valois the building of the city be for the most part stately of unpolished stone with the outside plaster and rough cast and the house for the most part be four story high and sometime six beside the roof which also have glass window the street be somewhat large and among they the fair be that of saint Dennis the second saint the three saint antoine and the four saint Martin and in the island the way to these street be fair the pavement be of little but thick and somewhat broad stone but in the mean time the street of the either for the low situation or by the negligence of the citizen be continual dirty and full of filth the three part of the city namely the ville the island and the university be join together be of a round form which of all other be most capable save that the half circle of the be great than the other half circle which be compass as it be with the two horn of the former and the whole circuit of the city without the wall exclude the suburb be say to be of six mile the market place which be in the street be vulgar call carrefour as be foursquare and have passage to they on all side and they be eleven in number namely four of the butcher which upon a sedition raise by they be divide into four tribe the five the shambles upon the mount saint genovefa the six build for the poor which have no shop and for the woman which sell linen which be vulgar call lingeria well know for the cosinage of these linen seller the seven of the broker vulgar call frippery the eight and chief be in the island call marsh because of the fenny soil the nine be for fish of the river seat near the ten be the little bridge of saint German of the university the eleven be without the gate for hog there be fourteen fountain beside the fountain of the queen and that of the innocent build of stone the ville have eight hospital the university four and the island two the description of Paris a the gate saint antoine b the c the gate of the temple d gate of saint Martin e gate of saint Denys f gate mont-martre g gate saint Honore H new gate I k gate saint victoire l gate Marcell m gate of saint james N gate saint Michael o gate saint Germain p gate Bussia q gate Nella R cathedral church saint church saint Bartholomew T the great palace five Pont denostredame with Pont au change 10 Pont a'n petit pont z Pont saint Michael 20 Pont I will begin the description of the city with the first part thereof call which have seven gate from the south east to the northwest I will not speak of the old or inner gate of the old city which gate since the build of the new wall be call false gate as serve for no use only I will say that they be of the same number and so call as these new gate be and that king Francis the first for comeliness sake cause they to be demolish the first of these seven gate lie towards the southeast and be call a saint antoine by this gate I enter the city when I come from chalons and without this gate I do then see the king palace not far distant from Paris and most sweet for the seat and building call bois saint and then I pass the bridge call Calantoine be without this gate where the river matrona fall into the seyne and so enter Paris by the gate and the church and fair street of saint Antoin near this gate Francis the first build a fort as I come in on the left hand be the tower b call the well know by that name which be begin to be build in the year 1369 by hugho provost of Paris and he be condemn to perpetual prison for impute heresy it come to the king hand on the same side be the king store-house for brass ordinance near the monastery of the celestine in who church there be many marble sepulchre and among the rest one erect to Lewis of Orleans slay by the duke of Burgundy and to his duchess Valentina daughter to the duke of Milan by king Lewis the twelve with learned epitaph on the same side be the church of saint Paul the house of the queen the house of the provost of Paris the public senate-house and the place call the greve famous by the capital punishment of offender for in this part of the city call ville there be three place for the execution of justice the other two part have not one place namely this of the greve and that of the temple lie on the left hand of the gate call temple next adjoin to this and the three call Luparia lie on the left hand of the gate call the new gate and from these three place the dead body be carry out of the gate of saint Martin to be bury upon montfalcon and give i leave out of order to remember you that pierre remy treasurer and governor of France under king Charles the fair repair this montfalcon and that his enemy then write upon the gallow stand there this time in french Ence gibeticy ser pierre remy upon this gibet here you see Peter remy hang shall be and that according to the same he be in the time of Philip of Valois hang there for the ill administration of his office on the right hand as you come in by the same gate of saint Anthony be a place for tylt call Tournelles not far thence at saint Catherine church in the scholar valley be a inscription witness that a house be pull down to the ground for a arrow shoot into the church when the rector of the university be there at mass in the year 1404 there be at that time a great sedition raise between the city and the university about a scholar denle with dirt and that this house by permission of the university be build again in the year 1516. also as you come into this gate on the right hand in the monastery saint Anthony a dry crocodile be hang up which a french ambassador at Venice leave there for a monument in the year 1515. and there be a sepulchre of the daughter of king Charles be of black marble with their statue of white marble near that lie the church yard of saint John for public burial make in the yard of the house of Peter (@) which be as pull down to the ground in the year 1392 because the constable of France be wound from thence the second gate towards the east be the gate of the c temple near which be the fort call Rastillon on your right-hand as you come in and this fort or some other in this place be build by Francis the first on the lefthand as you come in be the house of the templary knight like a little city for the compass and from it this gate have the name and when this order of knighthood be extinguish their good be give to the order of saint John the church of this house be say to be build like that of jerusalem and there be the monument of bertrand and Peter prior of France and the table of the altar be curious paint and here Philip Villerius master of the knight of saint John be bury in the year 1532 to who a statue of white marble be erect the three gate be call d saint Martin and it lie towards the north-east without which gate be the suburb of saint Laurence so call of the church of saint Laurence the four gate be call e saint Denis and without the same be the hospital of saint Lazarus and the foresay mount falcon and when king Henry the four besiege this city he do much harm to the same from some high place without this gate on the left hand as you come into the broad and fair street of saint Denys lie a castle which they say julius Caesar build and the same castle be of old the chief gate of Paris whereupon marcellinus call the whole city the castle of the parisian and upon the right-hand be the nunnery of the daughter of God which use to give three morsel of bread and a cup of wine to condemn man go to execution not far thence be the large church yard of the holy innocent which king Philip Augustus compass with wall and there be many fair sepulchre and they say that body bury there be consume in nine day the five f gate lie towards the north and be call mont-martre so call of a mountain of the same name lie without that gate and have the name of martyr there execute and Henry the four besiege the city mount his great ordinance in this place the six g gate saint Honore have a suburb in which be the market place for swine flesh and upon the right hand as you come in hard by the gate be a hospital for three hundred blind man the seven H and last gate lie upon the seyne towards the northwest and be call the new gate and within the same about a musket shoot distance be the ay king palace which may be call the less palace in respect of the great seat in the island and this little palace be vulgar call let this palace have only one court yard and be of a quadrangle form save that the length somewhat pass the breadth and the building be of free stone seem partly old partly new and towards one of the corner the king chamber vulgar call pavillon be more fair build then the rest without the say new gate some half musket shoot distance be the king garden with the banquet house vulgar call tuilleries and now the civil war be end the king begin to build a stately gallery which shall join together this garden and the foresay palace of the king and I hear that this gallery be since finish and the hall join this gallery with the palace do pass the stately build of the rest of the palace be beautify with many stone of marble and of porphyry I say that this gallery lead from the palace over the wall of the city and the ditch thereof be near the river and so full of water and after be support with two or three arch reach to the same garden and all the way without the wall from the palace to the say garden be compass with wall on both side this gallery the garden seem to be so much increase on the left hand as you come into the foresay new gate lie the tower Luparia and Alencon house and Bourbon house and the coin house and upon the right hand the chief coin house lie upon the river seyne to conclude of the street of this part of the city call ville the chief be s. antoine the second of the temple the three s. Martin the four s. Denys the five mont Martre the six s. Honore all so name of their gate and the seven Luparia upon the bank of the river seyne and among all these the most fair be that of s. antoine s. Denis s. and s. Martine so call of their gate a. e. g. d. the second part of the city call the university have the river seyne on the east and north-side and be compass with wall on the south and west side and have seven gate the first k gate s. victoire lie on the south side upon the river and have his suburb with a stately monastery and from the hill adjoin to this gate the army of king Henry the four besiege the city much press the same have their cannon plant near the gallow on the right hand as you come in towards the river lie the tower Nella the upper the college of the cardinal the college of the good boy the college and the church of the Bernardines which pope Benedict the twelve build and the cardinal of telouse increase with a libraty and with maintenance for sixteen scholar to study divinity also there lie the house of Lorraine the great school of four nation the market place for river fish and the castle and the little bridge which the provost of Paris build to restrain the scholar walk by night in the time of king Charles the five the second gate be call l the of Marcellus or of the stew and it have a suburb where in the church of saint Marcellus bishop of Paris and canonize for a saint which Rowland count of Blois nephew to Charles the great do build peter lombardiu bishop of Paris be bury in the year 1164 and behind the great altar in a window be the image of Charles the great on the right hand as you enter the say port by the mount of s. genovefa lie the college the college the college of the dutch the college of Navarra and the college Marchieum and the college Laudunense and on the left hand the college of the Lombard the college Prellaum famous for Peter Ramus who be master of that college and be there kill in the massacre the three gate of m saint james lie on the south-west side where king Francis the first build a fort and without this gate be a suburb in which be a church yard of the monastery of saint Marie at the very entry whereof be a most ancient image of the virgin paint with gold and silver with a inscription upon it in the street of saint james the jesuit have their college till for their wicked act they be banish the city and kingdom and since their restitution I think they now enjoy the same on the right hand as you enter this gate lie the college Lexoviense the college of saint Michael or cenate the college which build in the year 1490 maintain certain poor scholar call the college of s. Barbera the college of rheine the school of decree the college bellovaccuse the college the college cameracense and the college carnovallense on the left hand lie the college of the bald man the college of Sorbona which Robert of Sorbona a divine and familiar with king saint Lewis do institute and the same in process of time become of great authority in determine question of divinity the college of master Geruasius a Christian the college Plexourense and the college Marmontense the four n gate of the university be call port Michael where Francis the first build a fort and before the gate be a monastery of the carthusian where a statue of black marble be erect to Peter and there be two statue of white marble without any inscription on the right hand as you enter this gate lie the college cluniacense the palace of the bath which they say be build by julius Caesar and be so call either of the bound of the tribute or of the bath of julian the the water whereof be draw from a village adjoin and the college of eighteen and upon the left hand the college of Hericuria the college of justice the college of the treasurer the college the college and the college Turonense the five gate on the west side be call o saint Germain and without the gate be a suburb all suburb be vulgar call Faulxbourg which be large and be pull down to the ground in the civil war and there king Henry the four lie encamp when he besiege the city in this suburb be the monastery of saint Germain not inferior to any in wealth and endow with great privilege and jurisdiction where the old king Childebert the second and ckilperit the four and Clotharius the second lie bury and there be a chest of silver the gift of king eudo. on the right hand as you come into this gate in the minorite cloister be the sepulchre of the queen and prince whereof one be of black marble with white statue be the fair my memory herein may fail i that there be another cloister of minorite without the gate of saint Marcellus also there lie the college and upon the left hand the house Rothomagensis the college of Burgundy the house of Rheims the college the college Praemonstratense and the college the six gate be call p Bussia and upon the right hand as you come in lie the college Anthunense and upon the left hand lie the house the seven and last gate of the university lie towards the northwest and be call q Nella and without the same be the meadow of the clerk on the right hand as you come in this gate lie the house Nella the college of saint Denis and the house of the Augustine wherein be the sepulchre and lively image of Philip Comineus and upon the left hand lie the low tower Nella and the western bank of the river seyne these be the fair street of the university the first of saint victoire the second of saint Marcellus the three of saint james the four of saint Germain the five of the celestine upon the bank of the river the six of the mountain of saint genovefa the seven of saint Michael and the eight of the Augustine upon the bank of the river seyne the three part of the city be the island compass round about with the river Seyn it have of old four gate upon the four bridge but seem to have have no gate upon the five bridge call Pont which in this description I reckon to be the three gate in the upper part of the island towards the southeast be a fenny market place call the marsh that be the fen. near that lie r the cathedral church of the bless virgin which king Philip Augustus begin to build in the year 1257 the foundation be before lay by a uncertain founder and it be repute the chief among the miracle of France it be support with one hundred and twenty pillar whereof one hundred and eight be less and twelve very great be all of free stone the chancel be in the midst of the church which have (@) walk pace in length and sixty pace in breadth and all the chancel be compass with stone wherein the history of the old and new testament be engrave it have forty five chapel in the circuit thereof which be shut up with grate of iron in the front it have two double door with fair statue of twenty eight king upon the side be four tower or belfreye thirty four cubit high the great bell call Marie require twenty four man to ring it and the sound thereof in fair weather may be hear seven league of in a chapel towards the south be the statue of king Lewis the fat and of his son Philip with the image of a hog because he die with a fall from his horse stumble upon a hog on the north side be a mark that the overflow of the river seyne pass the outward statue from that of Philip Augustus king Philip of Valois have get a victory against the fleming in the year 1328 offer his horse and armour to the bless virgin and give the canon a hundred pound yearly rend to who for that cause a horseman statue be there erect also there be a giantlike statue erect to saint Christofer in the year 1413 by Antony dessar knight in the low part of the island towards the north-west the church of s saint Bartholomew be seat which be build by king Philip the fair and after be turn from the king chapel to a monastery by king Lotharius in the year 973 and then become a parish church whereof the king in respect of the old palace be the chief parishioner and I think be so still it become most famous in that the bell of that church be sound upon the very day of saint Bartholomew in the year 1572 to give a sign to the regalist and guisian that they shall kill those of the reform religion who they have draw to the city under pretence of love and can not otherwise have overcome as they find by experience of their valour near that lie the king great t palace wherein the old king keep their court but it have since be use for the court of justice and plead of lawyer in the great hall hang up a dry crocodil or a serpent like a crocodil there be the paint image of all the french king from Pharamund there be a statue of a hart with the head and neck of gold set there in memory of the treasurer who in the time of king Charles the six turn the money in the exchequer into that form lest it shall be waste here be paint upon the wall near the tower upon the top of the stair of the great hall the image of earl of Longaville and overseer of the build of this palace under king Philip the fair with this inscription Chascun' suffisance iln' a rien be thou content with the good thou befall who have not enough have nothing at all this be speak like a philosopher but the same man under Lewis be hang for deceive the king and this his image be break and kick down the stair in the hall of the palace be a marble table at which king and emperor be wont to be feast the chamber of the palace where verbal appeal be decide be call the golden chamber and it be adorn with stately and fair arch roof carve and picture and there the image of a lion with the head deject and the tail draw in remember the pleader of their duty Lewis the twelve do build with regal expense this chamber and another call the chamber of account vulgar chamber compte in this palace the chapel build by saint Lewis lie upon a arch chapel which have no pillar in the midst but only on the side and they say that the true image of Christ and the bless virgin be upon the low door and in this chapel the relic be keep which Balduinus the emperor of Constantinople engage to the venetian and the king of France redeem out of their hand in the very hall of the palace round about the pillar be shop of small ware or trifle right against the gate of the palace stand the house of John Chastell which be pull down in memory of a young man his son bring up among the jesuit and a practiser of their wicked doctrine who attempt the death of king Henry the four do strike out one of his tooth I have say former that this island be join to the ville by three bridge and to the university by two bridge and at this time be join to they both by the six bridge the first v bridge towards southeast lead to the street of saint Martin and be call pont dame that be the bridge of our lady and it be build of wood in the year 1417 have threescore walk pace in length and eighteen in breadth and threescore house of brick on each side build upon it but this bridge in the time of Lewis the twelve fall with his own weight be rebuild upon six arch of stone with threescore eight house all of like bigness build upon it and be pave with stone so that any that pass it can hardly discern it to be a bridge the second bridge of the broker vulgar with Pont au change be support with pillar of wood the three bridge of the miller vulgar call 10 Pont lie towards the north-west and lead to the street of saint Denis which they say do fall and be rebuild within three year then past by these three bridge the island be of old join to the the four bridge lie on the other side of the island towards the south lead into the street of saint james and be call y pont that be the little bridge be rebuild or repair of stone by king charles the six the sifth bridge be call z saint Michael and lie towards the south-west side lead into the street of saint Michael and have a pleasant walk towards the foresay bridge of the miller on the other side of the island and build upon pillar of wood be repair in the year 1547 and adorn with brick house by these two bridge the island be of old join to the university since that time after the end of the civil war a new bridge have be late build on that side of the island which lie towards the north-west and it be call 20 pont that be the new bridge join the island both to the and to the university the chief street of the island be the very bridge and the (@) way lead to the cathedral church and to the great palace the church or the little city compass with wall in respect of the church of saint Denis the protect saint of the french be two little mile distant from Paris hither I go pass by the gate of saint Denis lie towards the north east thence I pass upon a way pave with flint in a large plain towards the east have mount falcon on my right hand whether I say that they use to draw the dead body of those that be behead in the ville and the next way to this mount be to go out by the gate of saint Martin and upon my left hand I have the mountain of the martyr vulgar call Mont Martre and the next way from the city to this mountain be to go out by the gate Martre upon this mountain they say that the martyr Dennis Areopagita and and be behead in the time of Domitian because they will not offer sacrifice to Mercury and they constant believe this miracle that all these three martyr carry each one his head to the village catula which now be call saint Dennis and I observe by the way many pillar with altar set up in the place where they say the martyr rest forsooth with their head in their hand and at last fall down at catula where this church be build over they and likewise a a monastery by king who also lie there bury and have a statue in the cloister of the monastery here be the sepulcher of the king among which that of king Francis the (@) be somewhat more stately than the other be of white marble with the statue of that king and his queen Claudia there bury with he that of Lewis the twelve and his queen be of white marble but less fair and the three erect to Charles the eight in a chapel of the church be of black marble with some statue of brass to conclude to every three or four of the rest of the king one poor monument be erect neither be these sepulchre of the king in my opinion any thing stately or answerable to the fame but at the entrance of the chancel the representation of Christ bury and of the three king or wiseman and of the shepherd and other there engrave seem to i who have no skill in that art to be of much art and beauty I have read other itinerary which relate that here be bell of most pure metal that the door be of brass guild over that the table of the high altar be of gold that here be a cross of gold offer by king Dagobert that the body of the martyr be lay in a coffin of gold that the roof of the church be partly of silver and that there be a crucifix of gold before the altar but I shall think that these old ornament be take away and not to be see at this day have view Paris I desire to see the french king Henry the four and his court and because I late have be rob aswell of my cloak as of my crown here I buy for some two french crown a old cloak among the broker in the market place call the frippery so I take my journey towards the court and go by boat upon the seyne which boat daily pass from Paris towards the south nine league to corbevile and four league to Melune have on both side pleasant hill plant with vine and I pay seven sou for my passage then I go on foot four mile over a mountain pave with flint to the king palace call fontain-bleau fontain-bleau that be the fountain of fair water beyond the same mountain
