this palace of the king be seat in a plain compass with rock and it be build with kingly magnificence of freestone divide into four court-yard with a large garden which be then somewhat wild and unmanured at this time the civil war be end the king begin to build a gallery the beginning of which work be very magnificent the next day after I have see the king I return on foot eight league to soon here I find post-horse return to Paris and hire one of they for twenty sou I ride eight mile through fruitful field of corn and pleasant hill plant with vine and so return to Paris enter by the gate of saint victoire in the university now my crown which I have save from the foresay thief be by little and little spend and I who in my long journey have never want money but have rather furnish other that want with no small sum be force to treat with unknown merchant for take money upon exchange but howsoever I have in other place deal with noble merchant yet here I find myself to be fall into the hand of base and costive merchant who perhaps have be deceive by english gentleman drive by want to serve in the war of France have not the least respect of i for my misfortune among thief nor yet for our common country it happen that at this time there be in Paris two english knighis brother namely sir Charles and sir Henry Davers who for a ill accident live then as banish man and to they I make my misfortune know who like gentleman of their quality have a just feel thereof especial for that they be acquaint with sir Richard moryson my brother and they will willing have lend i money but I will tell a truth well know these brother upon good bond be to have receive some thousand of crown a few month pass in the temple hall which be one of the inn of court of London for those that profess the english law this be make know by one of the debtor the queen confiscate those crown as belong to banish man whereupon these knight be to attend the french king to lion in his war upon Savoy be much drive to their shift to get money for that journey yet do they not cast off all care to provide for i but with great importunity persuade a starveling merchant to furnish i with ten french crown when I have receive they I spend some few day in refresh myself at Paris they account forty eight mile from Paris to Roan whether I go by boat and pay a french crown for my passage the first day we pass eighteen mile to Poissy a most fair and famous nunnery and towards the evening we pass by the king palace s german the next day we pass twenty league to andale and by the way pass by a bridge divide the county of France from the duchy of Normandy and do see the palace Galeon and a most fair monastery then we pass four mile by water to port s. antoine and one mile by land then we hire another boat in which we pass five league to Roan and I pay for this passage three sou this our way be by pleasant land have on both side pleasant hill plant with vine and fruit-tree the city of Roan be seat on the north side of the river seyne partly in a plain Roan partly upon side of hill the building be for the most part of freestone bring from the city cane and upon a hill towards the north without the wall the fort s Catiline be seat when king Henry the four besiege Roan and then the fort much annoy the quarter of the english auxiliary force but now this fort be altogether demolish concern expense of diet in these part I spend at Paris in the inn fifteen sou each meal and at Roan twelve sou and at some inn by the way fifteen sou but whosoever pay for his supper have nothing to pay for his bed but before the late civil war they pay no more at Roan then eight sou for a meal passenger who stay long in the city use to hire a chamber which at Paris be give for two french crown by the month if it be well furnish and otherwise for less they that at Paris hire a chamber in this sort use to buy their meat in cook shop and have agree for it the cook bring it to their chamber warm and with pleasant sauce and sure all thing for diet be cheap at Paris than they use to be at London and since they use to buy small piece of meat a solitary passenger shall in that respect spend the less other passenger agree with some citizen for diet and chamber which may be have at Paris in convenient sort for one hundred and fifty french crown by the year and at Roan for one hundred and twenty but before the last civil war it may have be have for one hundred or eighty and sometime for sixty french crown at Roan I now pay for my supper twelve sou and the next day eleven sou for my dinner the night follow we ride fourteen league to deep in a most pleasant way divide into enclose pasture yield great store of apletree not only in the hedge but also in the open field about midnight we take some rest and meat in a poor and solitaire inn of a village but with such fear as we be ready to fly upon the least noise from Roan to dieppe I hire a horse for thirty sou and in this last inn I pay twelve sou for my meat and five sou for my horse-meat dieppe be a pleasant city and the great part thereof especial la rue that be the great street be seat in a plain upon the haven but it be compass with mountain dieppe and be divide into two part by a arm of the Sea the great part of the building be of timber and clay like our build of England I have spend at Paris most part of the ten crown I there receive and when I come from Roan I perceive that I shall present fall into want of money be in these strait I go to the young painter one of the english post pass between London and Paris and now return in my company to London and to he in few word I make my case know who willing yield to bear my charge to London have i still in his company for a pledge at dieppe I pay fifteen sou for each meal and ten sou for my licence to pass over sea and five sou of gift to one of the officer and ten sou for my part of a boat hire to draw our ship out of the haven of dieppe after we have fail fourteen hour upon Tuesday the thirteen of May after the old style in the year 1595 early in the morning we land in England at Dover and I pay a french crown for my passage in the ship and six english penny for my passage in a boat from the ship to that port of bless England but we be fierce land when we be cite to appear before the mayor and his assistant where for my part the more poor I be in apparel the more froward I behave my self towards they as many good mind be most proud in the low fortune so as they begin to entreat i rude as if I be some popish priest till by chance a gentleman one of the mayor assistant ask my name and be familiar acquaint with my brother by private discourse with i understand that I have be rob in France whereupon he give his word for i unto the mayor and so walk with i to our inn there he show so much respect and love to i and after my refusal of money from he so frank give his word for i to the english post as he be not only willing to furnish i with what money I will but himself and the dutch gentleman my consort in that journey much more respect i though poor apparel than they have former do assoon as I come to London I pay the ten french crown due by my bill of exchange to the foresay french merchant and not only pay to the english post the money he have disburse for i by the way but give he six french crown of free gift in thankfulness for this courtesy at London it happen that in regard of my rob in France when I enter my sister house in poor habit a servant of the house upon my demand answer that my sister be at home but when he do see i go up the stair too bold as he think without a guide he not know i in respect of my long absence do furious and with threaten word call i back and sure will have be rude with i have I not go up fast than he can follow i and just as I enter my sister chamber he have take hold on my old cloak which I willing fling of to be rid of he then by my sister embrace he perceive who I be and steal back as if he have tread upon a snake the three book chap. i. of my journey to Stoade through the unite province of netherlands and upon the sea-coast of Germany then to Brunswick and the right way to Nurnburg Augspurg and inspruck in Germany and from thence to Venice in Italy and so by the mediterranean sea and the land thereof to jerusalem in which journey I slight pass over the place describe in my former passage those way from my tender youth I have a great desire to see foreign country not to get liberty which I have in Cambridge in such measure as I can not well desire more but to enable my understanding which I think can not be do so well by contemplation as by experience nor by the ear or any sense so well as by the eye and have once begin this course I can not see any man without emulation and a kind of virtuous envy who have see more city kingdom and province or more court of prince king and emperor than myself therefore have now wander through the great part of Europe and see the chief kingdom thereof I sigh to myself in silence that the kingdom of Spain be shut up from my sight by the long war between England and Spain except I will rash cast myself into danger which I have already unadvised do when I view the city and fort of Naples and the city of Milan and howsoever now be new return home I think the go into more remote part will be of little use to i yet I have a itch desire to see jerusalem the fountain of religion and Constantinople of old the seat of christian emperor and now the seat of the turkish Ottoman be of this mind when I return into England it happen that my brother Henry be then begin that voyage have to that purpose put out some four hundred pound to be repay twelve hundred pound upon his return from those two city and to lose it if he die in the journey I say he have thus put out the most part of his small estate which in England be no better with gentleman young son nor so good as with bastard in other place aswell for the english law most unmeasurable favour elder brother as let i bold say it for the ignorant pride of father who to advance their elder son drive the rest to desperate course and make they unable to live or to spend any money in get understanding and experience so as they be in want and yet more miserable by their gentry and plentiful education must needs rush into all vice for all wise man confess that nothing be more contrary to goodness than poverty my brother be partner with other gentleman in this fortune think this put out of money to be a honest mean of gain at least the charge of his journey and the rather because it have not then be hear in England that any man have go this long journey by land nor any like it except only master John wrath who I name for honour and more special he think this gain most honest and just if this journey be compare with other base adventure for gain which long before this time have be and be then in use and I confess that this his resolution do not at the first sight dislike i for I remember that this manner of gain have of old be in use among the inhabitant of the low-countries and the seacoast of Germany and so it be yet in use with they I remember that no mean lord and lord son and gentleman in our court have in like sort put out money upon a horserace or speedy course of a horse under themselves yea upon a journey on foot I consider that those kind of gain only require strength of body whereas this and the like require also vigour of mind yea that they often weaken the body but this and the like always better the mind I pass over infinite example of the former custom and will only add that earl lord gentleman and all sort of man have use time out of mind to put out money to be repay with advantage upon the birth of their next child which kind of gain can no way be compare with the adventure of long journey yea I will bold say it be a base gain where a man be so hire to that dalliance with his wife and to kill a man so he may get a boy as if he be to be encourage to a game of Olympus be lead with these reason I like his counsel and make myself his consort in that journey and I have now give out upon like condition money to some few friend when perceive the common opinion in this point to be much differ from i and thereupon better consider this matter and observe as a strange that have be long out of my country that these kind of adventure be grow very frequent whereof some be undecent some ridiculous and that they be in great part undertake by bankrupt and man ofbase condition I may easy judge that in short time they will become disgraceful whereupon I change my mind for I remember the italian proverb etc. that be the beauty of a harlot the strength of the porter and to omit many like music itself and all virtue become less prize in they who set they out to sale also I remember the pleasant fable that jupiter send rain upon a village wherewith whosoever be wet become a fool which be the lot of all the inhaitant except one man who by chance for dispatch of business keep within door that day and that when he come abroad in the evening all the rest mock he as if they have be wise and he only foolish so as he be force to pray unto jupiter for another like shower wherein he wet himself also choose rather to have the love of his foolish neighbour be a fool then to be despise of they because he be only wise and no doubt in many thing we must follow the opinion of the common people with which it be better regard only man to be foolish then alone to be wise I say that I do for the aforesaid cause change my mind and because I can not make that undo which be do at least I resolve to desist from that course only I give out one hundred pound to receive three hundred at my return among my brother and some few kinsman and dear friend of who I will not shame to confess that I receive so much of gift and lest by spend upon the stock my patrimony shall be waste I moreover give out to five friend one hundred pound with condition that they shall have it if I die orafter three year shall repay it with one hundred and fifty pound gain if I return which I hold a disaduantageous adventure to the giver of the money neither do I exact this money of any man by suit of law after my return which they willing and present pay i only some few except who retain the very money I give they deal not therein so gentlemanlike with i as I do with they and by the great expense of my journey much increase by the ill accident of my brother death and my own sickness the three hundred fifty pound I be to receive of gain after my return and the one hundred pound which my brother and I carry in our purse will not satisfy the five hundred pound we have spend though my brother die within the compass of the first year but I be force to pay the rest out of my own patrimony gentle reader I will no long trouble thou with these trifle only in the behalf of they who for a reasonable gain and upon long journey and not upon ridiculous adventure have put out their money in this sort give leave to i howsoever I desist from that course to add this all manner of attire come first into the city and country from the court which be once receive by the common people and by very stage-player themselves the courtier just cast off and take new fashion though somewhat too curious and whosoever wear the old man look upon he as upon a picture in arras hang for it be proverbial say that we may eat according to our own appetite but in our apparel must follow the fashion of the multitude with who we live but in the mean time it be not reproach to any who of old do be those garment when they be in fashion in like sort many dance and measure be use in court but when they come to be vulgar and to be use upon very stage courtier and gentleman think they uncomely to be use yet be it no reproach to any man who former have skill therein to conclude that I may not trouble you with like example which be infinite I say that this manner of give out money upon these adventure be first use in court and among the very noble man and when any of they show thereby extraordinary strength the most censorious approve it but when any perform a long journey with courage and discretion no man be find who do not more or less commend it according to the condition of the journey perform now in this age if bankrupt stage-player and man of base condition have draw this custom into contempt I grant that courtier and gentleman have reason to forbear it yet know not why they shall be blame who have thus put out their money in another age when this custom be approve a man may just say it be great injustice that our action shall be measure by opinion and not by reason but when a man leave any custom that have be approve leave he shall oppose himself to the common people a monster of many head the most envious have nothing whereat they may just carp and if any measure may be impose to detractor sure they must spare they who undertake long voyage full of great danger who do not put out their money in tavern or at feast to any man without distinction but dispose of their money with their friend upon reasonable adventure of gain which in absence they can otherwise dispose to profit final who be not rich by patrimony take these journey only for experience and to be enable to that expense do condition this reasonable gain I say the detractor must spare these and distinguish they from other who make cursory journey without any desire to better their understanding thereby and more from those who in these course rather make trial of their body strength then of their mind ability and most of all from those who expose themselves to the scorn of man by base and ridiculous adventure or that little differ from selfe-murtherer in undertake desperate action for gain in the same year 1595 wherein some few month past I return into England from my former journey I now set forth again towards jerusalem and upon the twenty 1595. nine of November after the old style I and my brother consort of my journey go by water twenty mile which be seventeen mile by land from London to Gravesend in a boat with two oar for which we pay two shilling six penny at last the wind serve we upon the seven of December in the evening we set sail at a ebb water and upon the eight of December in the afternoon have pass the river Thames we cast anchor upon the shore of England right before the village Marget then in the twilight of the evening we put to sea and the nine of December enter the narrow sea of Zealand upon our call a boat come out of Vlishing to we in which we go thither leave our ship which go forward to Midleburg and each man pay a dolor for his passage the eleven of December we walk on foot one mile to Midleburge where be invite by our friend as we be at Vlishing we sup on free cost the twelve day we pass by sea in two hour space to Armuren where we cast anchor not without fear of spanish pirate who come with small boat out of the castle Wouda do then many robbery upon this Sea the thirteen day in the morning we sail nine mile to the island plat where a man of war send out by the state against these pirate do lie at anchor under the guard whereof we also lay at anchor the night follow the fourteen day in the morning have always a fair wind we sail eight milc to delph haven in Holland where we leave our bark and each man pay twelve stiver for his passage and myself give three stiver to a mariner that have attend i from hence myself and three consort hire a wagon for 18 stiver to the hage where I pay for my supper a flemish guilden and seventeen stiver the fifteen day of December which after the new style be Christmas day that feast by the old style fall on the twenty five of December myself and three consort hire a wagon for two gulden for two long mile to Leyden from Leyden at a set hour every day boat pass to all the next city and we enter the boat that go for Amsterdam pay each of we 6 stiver for our passage first we pass five mile upon the lake call Harlam meare which lake be much subject to tempest our boat be then draw by force of hand over a dam into a channel of water in which we pass two mile and so come to Amsterdam the boat be thus draw out of the lake into the channel by a privilege grant to Harlam because that city have spend much money in the work for conveyance of water and this be grant to they to the end that this passage be shut up to bark of great burden the merchant ware pass by land shall be force to come by Harlam we lodge in a english merchant house at Amsterdam and pay eight stiver for each meal the seventeen of December we enter a little ship to sail out of Holland into west-freesland but be almost freeze in with ice after we have sail two day and a night with great danger through huge piece of ice and be the second night also compass therewith and have be force to lie at anchor a good space we hardly arrive the three day at horn in north holland which city be five mile distant from Amsterdam and each man pay ten stiver for our passage and two stiver for the use of a little cabin in the ship the ninteenth day we will needs go on foot two mile as long as ten english mile to Enchnsen because they ask four gulden and a half for a wagon and in the mid way it happen that we light upon a sledg which we 4 consort hire for 20 stiver and therein we be all carry but for my part I pay 5 stiver more for the carriage of my necessary and we can not sufficient marvel at our first set forth that the village shall be so frequent in such a obscure country as we can hardly see how they be part one from the other for half the way at least from hence we sail with a very fair wind in three hour space two mile to stavern a village seat in west Freezland and each man pay ten stiver for his passage four for his supper and four for beer the next day we pass on foot one mile of Freezland which mile be exceed long to warcome and we hire two clown for two gulden to carry our necessary which clown drink stout all the night we be force beyond our bargain to pay for their intemperance which wrong we can not avoid though we much repine at it early in the morning we pass by water one mile over a lake to Bolsworth and each man pay two stiver and a half for his passage and eight stiver and a half for his dinner in the afternoon we hire a boat for three mile to Lewerden and each man pay six stiver for his passage and thirty four stiver for his supper and breakfast with wine the next day in the morning we may have pass to groan in a common boat each man pay twelve stiver but because the covetous mariner have overload it and the wind be boisterous we four consort hire a private boat for seven gulden and a half the first day we pass by water five mile to Kaltherberg that be the cold inn with a very fair wind but so boisterous as we be in no small fear here each man pay twelve stiver for his supper and seven stiver for his drink while in good fellowship we sit at the fire after supper the next day we pass in the same boat two mile to groan in a great tempest of wind beside that in the midst of the lake we lose our rudder be thereby in great danger have not the wave of the water by God mercy drive it to we here we pay eight stiver each man for a plentiful dinner but without wine in the afternoon we pass by water two mile to Delphs i will and each man pay forty stiver for the hire of the boat and twenty four stiver for supper and breakfast and fire in our private chamber from hence we sail with a most fair wind in two hour space two mile to Emden the first city of the german empire seat in east Friezland and each man pay six stiver for his passage and as much for his dinner in the afternoon we pass in a boat hire for four gulden whereof each man pay ten stiver for his part three mile to a little city lyre and by the way pass by the fort in which the earl of Emden hold his court. we rest at lyre this night and the next day be Christmas day by the old style and each man pay sixteen stiver for each supper and eight stiver for one dinner the spanish garrison daily send out freebooters into these part with the permission of the earl of Emden for his hatred to the citizen of Emden who late have shut he out of their city and of the earl of Oldenburge for his hatred against the citizen of breme therefore we be here many passenger do at last obtain of the earl of Emden that we may hire his soldier to conduct we safe for some few mile to these soldier we give twenty one dollar yet when at the three mile end we come to the village Stickhausen and be now in the great danger they as hire to go no further will needs return till each of we give they a german gulden to conduct we only to the next village be the confine of the county of emdens and oldenburg to which they be persuade not so much by our prayer as by our reward and once by the way to make show of danger as it seem to i lest we shall repent we of the money we have give they they rush into some old house with such a noise as if they will have kill all they meet but no enemy appear and if they have lurk there I think they will not have fight with the earl soldier who favour they as on the other side if they have assault they I doubt whether our mercenary soldier will have lose one dram of blood for our safety as long as these soldier be with we we partly go by water each man pay 3. stiver for his passage and partly on foot they be now dismiss we go on foot a dutch mile in the territory of the earl of oldenburg to the village where each man pay four stiver for his dinner in the afternoon we be many consort hire diverse wagon pay for each of they twenty five stiver to oldenburg be four mile and we come thither by nine of the clock in the night and there each man pay fifteen groat for his supper and breakfast the second day in the morning we go four mile to Dolmenhurst and each man pay two copstuck for his wagon then six of we hire a wagon one mile to breme for three copstuck where each man pay fourteen lubeck shilling for his supper and breakfast from hence eleven of we hire two wagon to Stoade for which each of we pay thirty lubeck shilling the first day we go four mile to a poor village where each of we pay five lubeck shilling for our supper and have rest here somefew hour upon straw we go before the break of day three mile to Foard and make no stay there pass three mile further to Stoade hence five of we hire a coach for five dollar to Luneburg ten mile distant and the first day in the afternoon we go three mile to alte-klost that be the old cloister which be seat in the bishopric of breme and the next day one mile in the same bishopric and six in the duke of luneburgs territory and so we come to Luneburg but the horse be weary and the journey long we come so late in the night as we be force to lodge without the gate where each man pay five lubeck shilling for his supper three for his breakfast and five for beer the next day we enter Luneburge where each man pay four lubeck shilling and a half for his dinner from hence four of we hire a coach for seven dollar to Brunswick be sixteen mile distant the coachman pay for his horse meat and we for his own meat the same day in the afternoon we pass five mile in six hour space to Sawerburg where each of we pay for our own supper with our part of the coachman supper seven lubeck shilling the second day in the morning we pass four mile to where each man pay in like sort five lubeck shilling for our dinner in the afternoon we pass three mile to gethurne where the duke of Luneburg have a castle which the dutch call schlosse where for we and the coachman supper and breakfast we four english consort pay a dolor and twelve stiver the three day in the morning we pass four mile to Brunswick where in like sort we four pay for our own and the coachman beaver supper and dinner two dollar and a half from hence six consort of we hire a coach forthirty dollar to nurnberg forty eight mile distant and the coachman pay for his horse-meat and we for his own meat which hereafter divide among we I will reckon in our seureall expense the first day at ten of the clock begin our journey we pass through fruitful hill of corn one mile where we leave on our left hand Wolfenbeyten a city where the duke of Brunswick keep his court with a village belong to his brother the bishop of Ossenbruck and there we meet with certain of the duke horseman who keep the way safe from thief and at their request we bestow on they a ort or four part of a dolor then in the like way we pass two mile and a half to Rauchell where each of we pay for we and the coachman supper five silver groshen the second day in the morning we pass three mile and a half to Halberstatt where each man pay in like sort for we and the coachman dinner half a dolor our journey this day be through fruitful hill of corn not enclose and grove and wood in a fruitful and pleasant country the duke of Brunswick be call the administrator of the bishopric of Halberstatt and have the rent thereof after dinner we pass one mile to Ermersteben subject to the duke of Brunswick through a plain more pleasant than the former have no enclosure but be fruitful in corn and full of village where we four english consort pay twenty three silver groshen for our own and the coachman supper I have omit the quality of the soil in place which I have former describe which now I mention again because this be the first time I pass from Brunswick to Nurnburg the three day in the morning we pass in a dirty way but through most pleasant hill and fruitful of corn but have no wood nor so much as a tree two mile to Mansfield the count of man field well know captain in the war of the low-countries and Hungary have their name from this castle and village and this territory have beside some twelve castle be not sufficient to maintain the great number of they they be force to seek advancement by the war the citizen of Mansfield be of old very rich by the mine of this territory but at this time they have scarce bread to eat the count have engage these mine to the of augsburg and to some citizen of Nurnburg some of these mine yield gold but so spare as it scarce quit the cost other yield silver and the very mine of copper in each hundred yield twenty lot that be half ounce of silver of these mine some be two hundred clafter that be fathom deep under the earth and these mine of the mountain be bear up with pillar and building of wood under the earth lest it shall fall upon the workman they say that these mine yield yearly one hundred and forty thousand dutch gulden beside the expense at this time three officer live in the castle who receive the profit of the mine for those to who they be engage whereof one receive five part the other three the three only one part moreover here be wonderful stone in the quarry which break into never so many part have still the lively figure of diverse thing our host show we some of they which have throughout the figure of diverse fish in a lake adjoin this stone of one fish that of another and so of all the kind and he show we one that have throughout the figure of Christ crucify another that have the figure of the pope triple crown and another that have the lively figure of Luther sure the german be account no juggler nor liar and if they will have deceive we yet I can see how our eye shall be deceive see many of they break and still have the same figure this poor village yet pleasant for the seat be of old a city and at this time the house be cover as they be in many part of Germany not with tile of brick but with like piece of wood here each of we pay a quarter of a dolor for our own and the coachman dinner in the afternoon we pass a dirty way but through fruitful corn field four mile to Sangerhausen where each of we in like sort pay the four part of a dolor and a for our supper this be the first village of the province Thuring belong to the elector of Saxony the four day in the morning we pass through most pleasant and fruitful hill of corn adorn with some pleasant wood which in high Germany be of fir that be green all winter four mile in the territory of the elector of Saxony to a country inn where have nothing but egg for our dinner we pay joint ten silver grosh after dinner we pass in the same elector territory and through the like soil or country three mile and a half to a country inn where we have to supper a pudding as big as a man leg and gross meat and straw for our bed and joint pay fourteen grosh the five day early in the morning we passed-through the like way but more pleasant for the plenty of vine two mile to the city of erfurt where we four english consort with our coachman pay joint a dolor and twenty one for our dinner with sour wine of the country this city be seat in a plain and be a free city but not a imperial city and pay some tribute to the bishop of metz and to the saxon duke of Wineberg it be large be a dutch mile in compass but the house be poor build of timber and clay have the roof cover with tile of wood and they seem to be build of old it have forty two church but only sixteen be use for divine service namely eight for the papist among which be the two cathedral church under the power of the archbishop of metz and eight for the protestant or lutheran this be the chief city of Thuring and of old here be a university but time have dissolve it after dinner at the first go out of the city we ascend a very high mountain whereupon be a pleasant wood of fir then we pass by the beautiful little city of Armstat I call it beautiful for the seat in a fertile soil have dry and pleasant walk and for the plenty of fountain and groave and for the magnificence of the castle wherein the count of Schwartzburg keep his court final for the uniform build of the city which some fifteen year past be burn to the ground and be since rebuild and so come from erfurt we pass three long mile to the village blow subject to the count of Schwartzburg where joint we pay fourteen grosh for our supper the six day we pass three mile through woody mountain to the village Fraw-im-Wald that be our lady in the wood which village be subject to the say count and here we pay joint thirty seven grosh and a half for our dinner after dinner we pass three mile through mountain cover with snow and wood of fir to Eysfield subject to the saxon duke of coburg for this wood of Thuring vulgar call Thuringwald have many lord namely the elector of Saxony the saxon duke of Wineberg and coburg and the count Schwartzburg the duke of coburg have in this place a fair castle and we pay joint for we four and the coachman sixty four for our supper and breakfast the seven day in the morning we pass three mile over dirty mountain and fruitful in corn to coburg seat in the province of franconia they say this city be of old call Cotburg that be the city of dirt and the dirty street well deserve the name here one of the duke of Saxony call of coburg keep his court and our host tell we that his duchess for adultery be then brick up in a wall the place be so narrow as she can only stand and have no door but only a hole whereat they give her meat the building of the city be very base of timber and clay here we five pay sixteen grosh for our dinner in the afternoon we pass two mile to the town through fruitful hill of corn and in a most dirty way where we five pay forty nine for our supper and the town be subject to the popish bishop of bamberg the eight day we pass four mile to bamberg through a fruitful plain of corn and pleasant hill plant with vine and in a most dirty way this city be the seat of the bishop of bamberg by the way we pass by a ferry the river manus run to Franckfort here we five pay thirty seven grosh for our dinner in the afternoon we pass through a wood of fir in a sandy soil and then through fruitful field of corn and pleasant hill three mile unto a village subject to the margrave of Anspach from which a city subject to the bishop of bamberg be not far distant for the prince dominion in these part be mingle one with the other and here we five pay fifty five for our supper the nine day we pass three mile through a sandy and barren plain and wood of fir always green to a village subject to the say margrave where we five pay forty grosh for our dinner in the afternoon we pass three mile through the like way to Nurnburg and be now free from pay for our coachman each of we pay here six batzen each meal and four creitzer each day for our chamber this city I have former describe and so pass it over here we hire a coach be seven consort for twelve Dutch gulden to augsburg be nineteen mile distant the first day after breakfast we pass through Nurnburg wood two mile and in the say margraves territory who be of the family of the elector of Brandeburg four mile to Blinfield and each of we pay ten batzen for our supper and four batzen for a banquet after supper the second day in the morning we pass four mile to the city monheyme subject to the phaltz-grave of the Rhine and here each of we pay half a gulden for his dinner by the way in this morning journey we do see Weyssenburg a free but not imperial city protect by Nurnburg the margrave of Anspach lord of this territory have a fort build upon a mountain that hang over this free city which when he do build the citizen complain to the emperor and they say that how soever they bring the emperor mandate to desist from the work yet he will not obey the same the rest of our journey to monheyme be in the territory of the baron of Papenheym in a dirty way through many wood and in a village subject to that baron we have a guide and two fresh horse for our coach and for they our coachman pay six batzen after dinner we pass three mile through a wood and fruitful hill of corn to the city donwerd be a fair imperial city which I have former describe and here each man pay six batzen for his supper now we be come out of Franconio and begin to enter into Suenia the three day we pass three mile through fruitful field of corn and wood of fir to a village where each man pay eight batzen for his dinner in the afternoon we pass through likeway three mile to augsburg where each man pay seven batzen for each meal I pass over this city which I have describe before here we agree with the carrier of augsburg who by course that week go to Venice that each of we shall pay he seventeen crown each crown worth twenty two batzen and that he not only shall find every man a horse and pay for the horse meat but also shall pay for our diet and here I gain this by my dutch language that make the bargain with he for one of my countryman who can not speak dutch when we come to Venice and he have no money can not pay the carrier I have no remedy but to pay those crown for he to which the law will have compel i as the maker of the bargain if I have not rather choose willing to do it see how ignorance may sometime advantage and skill may prejudice a man we begin this journey in the afternoon and the first day through a heath and in the territory of the duke of Bavaria from the very gate of augsburg we ride six mile to a inn near Landsperg the second day in the morning through fruitful hill and wood of fir green at this time of the year we ride four mile to Schongaw and after dinner through mountain cover with snow four mile to amberg the three day in the morning we ride two mile to the village Warten and after dinner six short mile to Seyfeld and in the midst of the way a bridge divide the dukedom of Bavaria from the county of tyrall at Seyfeld there be a church build in memory of a gentleman swallow up by the gape earth as they say because be to receive the sacrament he demand in scoff a great piece of bread the four day in the morning we ride three mile to inspruck the chief city of tyrall subject to the family of Austria where be at the top of the Alps the mountain begin to open towards the south and our morning journey be in a pleasant plain between the high mountain pass this plain they show we upon a high mountain so high as we can scarce discern the thing they show though of great bigness I say they show we the statue of the emperor maximiltan proportionable to his body and a great crucifix erect by he upon this occasion one day when he hunt and wander from his company lose himself so as he have no hope to get out of those most thick wood and most high mountain there appear to he a man or as they say his good angel who lead he through wild vast wood till he come in safety and then vanish away in memory whereof they say the emperor erect these monument in this city of inspruck and in the cathedral church thereof be the sepulchre of the say emperor and there be many image partly of brass partly of marble erect inspruck to the archduke of Austria and eight of brass erect to the archdutchesses among they be the sepulchre of Philippina a citizen daughter of augsburg who the archduke Ferdinand late bury and lie in a chapel without any sepulchre as then erect to he take for his wife but with a coucnant as they say that her child shall not inherit as archduke of Austria from hence we ride in the afternoon between mountain descend into Italy and lie towards the south sun four mile and a half of most pleasant way to lueg the five day we pass the mountain bremer and ride two mile and a half to Sterzen subject to the cardinal of brixia one of the family of the archduke of Austria at Inspruck the mountain bremer be more than a dutch mile high yet nothing steep this way from augsburg to Venice be for the most part through pleasant valley in the midst of mountain and so wind over the mountain as the passage be very easy here my brother Henry fall sick I deal with the carrier that we pay he three guldin and a half for the common charge of his company supper he will there stay for we till the next morning and this likewise I obtain of our consort with more ease because they be to diet at the carrier charge the six day we ride three mile to a village not far distant from brixia where the say cardinal hold his court this way be all through woody mountain after dinner we ride three mile in the say cardinal territory through mountain and hill plant with vine and lie towards the south sun to Clausy the seven day we ride four mile in a straight way between rock with pleasant orchard of pomegranate and other italian fruit lie on both side to (@) which the dutch call pozen and after dinner we begin to leave the Alps on our back and ride three mile to newmart through wood of fir in a plain more and more enlarge itself and plant after the Lombard fashion with elm tree set in the surrowe of cornfield and vine grow up high upon the elm the eight day we ride by the bank of the river athesis to Trent three mile in the county Tyroll within the dutch empire and one dutch mile in Italy the city Trent be seat in length from a mountain on the east side to another on the west side and the river athesis which the dutch call esh run by the city Trent on the north side the city be famous by a late council hold there and the archduke of Austria at Inspruck have his officer there to gather tribute belong to he and the cardinal Madrucci lame of a arm and foot but repute eloquent have some privilege in this city for the administration of justice and otherwise for the command of the city belong to the family of Madrucci be gentleman after dinner we enter the mountain again where we walk over a way pave with flint which the dutch call plat and do lead our horse in our hand the way be very slippery and so we ride one most long dutch mile to Bersena the nine day we ride three most long mile to Bergo I mean dutch mile for I have no other companion but dutch who reckon the way after their own mile this day we pass infamous place for robbery especial one where 5 way lead to 5 woody mountain and here we do see thief be ourselves in safety they have no mean to come to we in a plain so cover with snow that our horse be upto the saddle therein and can hardly pass the high way after dinner we ride two most long dutch mile to the ten day we ride two dutch mile to Primolano the first village in the state of Venice but from the foresay confine of the empire to this place all the territory belong to diverse gentleman neither subject to the empire nor to the state of Venice but live free only acknowledge to hold their land from the archduke of Austria also beyond Primolane subject to the state of Venice there be a fort build between a narrow passage of two mountain which fort be very little but hang over the high way be build on the side of a mountain and this fort be keep by dutch soldier it have no gate but they that will go forth must be let down by a rope and they that will enter it must in like sort be draw up moreover before dinner we ride from Primolano one Dutch mile to Carpanella and at our set forth from Primolan we pass over a bridge under which be little water for the present but when the snow melt from the mountain or any great rain fall the water there be high and violent and some month past when the carrier of Augspurg and his consort can no way pass the same they tell we that a english gentleman impatient of delay and try to pass the same be drown and that his body be find after somefew day when it have be spoil of a gold chain and store of crown they be unknown who get this booty and that he be at first bury in a chapel but after take up by the priest and bury in the high way when they hear he be a Englishman and thereupon suspect he to be a heretic for the build of this bridge each of we pay two creitzer in the next village in our morning journey the way be narrow between mountain and we may see and hear mountain of dissolve snow draw with they huge stone to fall with great noise into the high way before we and into other adjoin valley after dinner we ride through a plane till after the manner of Lombardy three dutch mile and a half to the castle franco the twelve day we ride three dutch mile to the city Trevigi through a like plain after dinner for I have former describe Trevigi we ride two dutch mile or ten italian mile through a like plain to mestre from hence we pass by water to Venice be five mile first in a ditch each man pay one for his passage then in other boat over the lake wherewith Venice be compass each man pay three soldi for his passage I omit to speak any thing of Venice which I have former describe we be now to take our purpose journey into the turk dominion think it best first to go to Constantinople where the english ambassador give we a janizare our journey from Venice to jerusalem for our guide we hope the rest of our journey will be pleasant and void of all trouble for this janizare aswell for their wont faithfulness to those that give they wage as for the account he be to make of our safety to the say ambassador no doubt will have be a faithful guide to we but when we inquire of the way from Raguza to Constantinople by land all the post and messenger pass that way tell we that the war of Hungary make all those part full of tragedy and misery then we think to go by sea to Constantinople but when we hear that no ship will be have in three month at least that long delay be hateful to we too late after my return by experience in my journey and conference I find a three way namely by sea from Venice to Zant and from that island take the council of the english merchant there to Petrasso seat upon the corinthian creek of the sea in a province of Greece call by the turk Morea then to take a janizare from the english consul there reside and with he to pass by land to Athens and by sea in little bark from thence to Constantinople the four way be not unknown to we namely to go by sea to Cyprus as we do and in the same ship to have pass to Scanderona and there take a janizare from the english factor maintain there by our merchant of Haleppo to have pass under his conduct by sea to jerusalem or else to have go from Scanderona to Haleppo and there take a janizare of the english consul and obtain letter from the italian to recommend the care of we and our safety to the guardian of the monastery of the latin church in jerusalem which our merchant will easy have obtain for we to have pass from thence by land to jerusalem but when we impart at Cyprus this our purpose to the consort of our journey from Venice thither who be then hire a bark to pass from Cyprus to joppa whence they have but forty mile to jerusalem they be very desirous of our company and with great earnestness give we confidence that they will procure the say guardian and friar at jerusalem to do we all courtesy in their power and so persuade we to commit ourselves to their company and protection I will add for the instruction of other that the say janizare use to be hire for eight asper a day and if he take this charge of any man safety from a ambassador or any christian officer of account he will easy save a man more than his wage in govern his expense and keep he from those extortion which the turk use to do upon christian as also from all their injury but I return to the purpose we lie at Venice and while our health be yet sound and our crown unspent desire with all possible speed to finish our voyage into Turkey do by good hap light upon french consort for our journey namely two franciscan friar one Eremitan Friar and two honest young frenchman both citizen of Bloys in France and one of they a burgher son the other a notary of the city and last a Fleming or Dutchman citizen of Emden in east Freezeland this Fleming be a fat man bear to consume victual and he have now spend in his journey to Venice thirty pound sterling and here for his journey to jerusalem have already put into the ship full hogshead of wine and store of all victual when sudden he change his mind for fear of a great rheum wherewith he be trouble or be discourage with the difficulty of the journey and will needs return to Emden with purpose if he be to be believe to return the next spring to some place near jerusalem in a english ship which he think more commodious he profess that he have put much money out upon his return and since he be old and very sickly and after so long a journey and so much money spend will needs return home I can think that he ever undertake this journey again many papist think they must have the pope licence to go this journey and Villamont a french gentleman write that otherwise they incur the censure of the church and affirm that the pope write under his licence these word fiat quoth petitar that be let that be grant which be crave and under the remission of his sin fiat that be let he be make happy and he add that he be force to take as much pain and to spend as much and to use as much help of the pope officer for the obtain of these two suit as if he have be a suitor for a bishopric but I know many papist that have go from Venice to Jerusalem who either care not for this licence or never think upon it and how soever it may give some credulous man hope of full indulgence or merit sure it will serve they for no other use among our consort I never hear any mention thereof neither do the friar at jerusalem inquire after it when I first begin to think of undertake this journey it be tell i that each ascension day a venetian galley be set forth to carry pilgrim to jerusalem but it seem that this custom be grow out of use since few be find in these day who undertake this journey in regard of the turk impose great exaction and do foul injury to they for the very friar which every three year be send into those part to do divine duty to the Papist merchant there abide the friar former send be recall use to pass in no other than common merchant ship in the end of march we have the opportunity of a ship pass into Asia which at that time of the year be not rare this ship be call the less lion and the master who the italian call be Constantine Coluri a grecian as most part of the mariner be greek the italian abhor from be sea man concern diet some agree with the steward of the ship call Ilscalco and they pay by the month four silver crown each crown at seven lyre and I mark their table be poor screw for our part we agree with the master himself who for seven gold crown by the month pay by each of we do courteous admit we to his table and give we good diet serve each man with his knife and spoon and his fork to hold the meat while he cut it for they hold it ill manner that one shall touch the meat with his hand and with a glass or cup to drink in peculiar to himself he give we wine mingle with water and fresh bread for two or three day after we come out of any harbour and otherwise biscuit which we make soft by soak it in wine or water in like sort at first set forth he give we fresh meat of flesh and after salt meat and upon fast day he give we egg fish of diverse kind dry or pickle salad sod Rice and pulse of diverse kind oil in stead of butter nut fruit cheese and like thing also we agree that if our journey be end before the month expire a rateable proportion of our money shall be abate to we each of we for his passage agree to pay five silver crown of Italy and howsoever I think they will not have deny we wine or meat between meal if we have be dry or hungry yet to avoid trouble of they myself and my brother carry some flagon of rich wine some very white biscuit some prune and raisin and like thing and to comfort our stomach in case of weakness we carry ginger nutmeg and some like thing and for remedy against ague we carry some cool sirop and some pound of sugar and some laxative medicine also we carry with we two chest not only to lay up these thing but also that we may sleep and rest upon they at pleasure and two woollen little mattress to lie upon and four quilt to cover we and to lay under we which mattress and quilt we carry after by land or else we shall have be far worse lodge in the house of turk beside that many time we lie in the field under the starry canopy in stead of sheet we use linen breech which we may change at pleasure howsoever all nation may use their own apparel in Turkey yet the clothes of Europe and especial the short cloak be most offensive to they so as the wearer provoke they thereby to do he injury therefore myself and my brother buy each of we a long coat of as course stuff as we can find and a long gown of a course and rough freeze our sword daggers and european garment we leave in our chest with a flemish merchant lie at Venice to be keep against our return and howsoever he fall banckerout leave the city before that time yet our good be by the public officer lay apart and ready deliver to we at our return whereas we leave our sword at Venice know that no turk and much less christian carry any arm except when they go some journey and that we be not ignorant that howsoever christian may defend themselves from thief by the high way yet it be hard to distinguish between the turk violent extortion with the injury of they and the janizares by the high way and flatrobbery by thief and that whosoever shall draw a sword or a knife against these man or any Turk scoff and defpise he shall be sure to die a ill death by public justice which notwithstanding I know not how any man carry arm can have the patience to endure therefore since the turk journey in great troop be sufficient arm against thief and in all event be unfaithful fellow soldier to a christian join with they except the janizares who how soever they make a show of fear of thief that they may seem better to deserve their wage yet have seldom or never be assault by they for these and other reason we leave our sword at which reason it will be tedious to urge and choose rather unarm then arm to suffer injury which there can be avoid myself and my brother Henry who die this year in the month of july spend four hundred and eighty pound sterling in this journey from England to jerusalem and thence to Haleppo and in my particular journey after his death to Constantinople and return into England if any say we spend lavish let he know that my dear brother death and my necessary stay thereupon and a desperate sickness into which I fall upon grief very much increase the say expense add that I be press with these misery have now spend all the money we bring in our purse and then suffer great loss by the money take upon exchange to be pay in England and be weak after my sickness be force much to increase my charge by take a servant to wait upon i from which extraordinary mischief God deliver all that undertake this journey and yet I be deceive if the ordinary burden will not seem more then enough heavy to they but I return to the relation of my journey it be the mariner fashion that be to go to sea they will affirm they set sail present that the merchant and passenger may bring their good on board which do they will not easy take they out again though that ship after long delay shall go last out of the haven therefore we keep our good in our lodging still inquire after the Scrivano who dwell hard by we and when he profess serious that he will take ship the next day than we present ship our provision so on Friday the 19 of April after the new style in the year 1596 we together with the patron our master go aboard and the patron return that night to Venice but we lodge in the ship the patron 1596. have some monethspa promise i and my brother that we shall set our chest upon which we be to rest above the hatch hard by the stern where the ship be great we have commodity to set they in a place cover over the head but open on the side towards the prow and this place be close at the other end lie at the very door of the patron cabin where he sleep and lay his private good and this place seem to i very pleasant and fit to rest in since we be cover from rain and the wind blow common upon the stern while we be at sea for we sail common with a fore wind the wind be more constant in that sea at set season of the year then in our sea and for the time of our abide in haven and otherwise in that calm sea if the wind be contrary yet in summer time and in a clime so near the aequinoctial line we can receive no hurt but rather pleasure by their coolness beside be thus part from the mariner we be free from louse and all filthiness wherewith the frenchman our consort be much annoy who sleep under the hatch and that the rather because they wear woollen stocking we silk draw over with linen and they sleep in their apparel we only in our doublet and linen breech and stocking which doublet of we be line with taffety wherein louse can breed or harbour so as howsoever I wear one and the same doublet till my return into England yet I find not the left uncleanliness therein and give i leave to joy in my good fortune as the common sort speak namely that the taffata line of my doublet be of green colour which colour none may wear upon great danger but only they who be of the line and stock of Mahomet of who I can challenge no kindred yet it happen that by sleep in my doublet aswell by land as by sea no Turk ever perceive this my error neither do I understand by any christian no not by our english merchant at Haleppo in what danger I be for the same till I come to Constantinople where our english ambassador tell i of the strict law forbid the use of this colour and that a poor christian some few day before have be beat with cudgel at Constantinople and be hardly keep from be kill because ignorant he wear a pair of green shoestring whereupon I be yet in fear when all danger be almost past yet will I not cast off my doublet but only more wary keep the line from sight till I enter the greek ship wherein I pass thence to Venice and so be free from all danger I return to my purpose the patron of our ship as I say return to Venice but we stay in the ship to dispose all our provision fit for the journey the next day be the twenty of April after the new style the patron Scrivano and all the merchant come aboard and the follow night be clear our ship be draw out of Malamocco the venetian haven by little boat fasten to the ship by rope and make their way with oar for great ship use no sail to go out of this haven upon Sunday the 21 of April in the year 1596 be thus put to sea we set April 1596. sail with a fair wind then all fall on our knee we pray unto God for a happy voyage kneel above the hatch but pray every man private and silent to himself some write that in the ship of Venice they use to pray public in latin every day after the roman fashion and some day to celebrate mass but in this our ship the patron and most of the mariner be greek and only the Scrivano that be scribe with some merchant be italian and of the roman religion therefore every day a bell be ring at prayer time but each man pray private after his own manner there be beside in the ship many eastern christian of diverse sect and nation and turk and persian yea very indian worship the sun all which at the ring of this bell to prayer go under the hatch myself and my brother willing pray with they above the hatch after the foresay manner whereof we think no scruple of conscience to be make since greek pray with we as well as italian and french who difference in religion be well know to themselves so as this our private prayer be void of all dissimulation and we be glad that no profession of our religion be impose upon we in regard of our consort with who we be to go to jerusalem and of the italian who after our return may perhaps meet we in Italy prayer be end they use a ceremony which I like well for the sub-patron give the sign with his silver whistle all the mariner bareheaded and turn their face to the east cry with a loud voice Buon' Buon' that be a good voyage and the same sign give do cease and again cry so three time upon Tuesday the patron with the Scrivano stand by he stand upon the castle of the ship and make a solemn oration to the sub-patron and the mariner stand upon the low hatch who he admonish how they shall behave themselves and especial to refrain from swear blasphemy and sodomy under great penalty then he write the name of the mariner and give every man his charge and last turn himself to the passenger exhort they to behave themselves modest and I must true witness that the patron the Scrivano and the sub-patron use all passenger courteous yet so keep their gravitic as they have due respect at all time particular at the table where they do first set down other expect till they come than the friar do sit down and last the layman in due order neither do any sit or walk upon the high hatch save only they who do eat at the table of the patron but the rest and all the eastern people who he never admit to his table be on the middle hatch or at the prow upon Wednesday in the morning we do see upon the shore of Italy the mountain of Ancona which be two hundred italian mile distant from Venice upon Thursday the five and twenty of April we sail by the island or mountain Poma or Pamo seat in the midst of the gulf of Venice which be a high rock rise sharp at the top and uninhabited where in the autumn they take falcon and we sail by the island saint Andrea distant one hundred mile from Ancona on the north side and the shore of Italy on the south side and the same night we sail by the island Ischa and the next morning be Friday by the island Buso Aulto Catsa and towards the evening by the island Cazola Augusta and Palaofa for in this gulf of Venice be many land whereof the most be subject to Venice and the rest to Raguza and other lord and some towards the north-shoare to the great Turk here great store of Dolphines follow our ship and the voice of the mariner as they use to do and they play about we do swim as fast as if they have fly then we do see the island Liozena be all of mountain subject to Venice and inhabit by gentleman where the venetian have build a strong fort upon the haven for their galley and after five mile we do see the island Curzola subject to Venice and have a bishop and the wind be high we cast anchor near Curzola but the wind soon fall we set sail again from the fix and twenty of April to Thursday the second of May the southeast wind which the italian call Syrocco do blow very contrary unto we the three of May be Friday towards the evening we be drive upon the northern shore and do see the fort Cataro build on a mountain upon the continent against turkish pirate and distant eighteen mile from Raguza the chief city of Selavonia which be free yet pay tribute to the venetian and turk their powerful neighbour not far thence the turk also have a fort build against the venetian Raguza be some Raguza one hundred mile distant from the island Andrea and some four hundred mile from Venice upon Saturday we sail by the promontory of saint Mary on the north side and Otranto a city of apulia in Italy on the south side see they both plain for now we be pass out of the gulf of Venice into the mediterranean sea by this straight some sixty mile broad and some two hundred mile distant from Raguza here we do overtake a ship of Venice call Ragazona and that we may enjoy one another company the sea be calm for the time our ship be the less yet of some nine hundred ton be fasten to the stern of the other ship by a cable and towards the evening upon the greek shore towards the north we do see Vallona now we be come forth of the adriatic sea otherwise call the gulf of Venice which have in length some six hundred italian mile and the breadth be diverse sometime two hundred mile sometime less between Ancona and the opposite haven Valdagosta seventy mile and in the straight we now pass sixty mile broad on Sunday the five of may we do see the mountain Fanon and as I remember a island three mile distant from the island corfu and upon the greek shore beyond the island we do see the most high mountain call inhabit by the albanesi who neither subject to the turk nor venetian nor any other do upon occasion rob all and the venetian and the king of France and especial of Spain use to hire they in their war the six of may we sail by the promontory call the cape of corfu the description of which island I will defer till my return this way on Tuesday the seven of May we sail by the island Paro very near we and the island saint maura join by a bridge to the continent of Epirus and subject to the turk and the island Ithaca vulgar call by the italian compare also subject to the turk and famous for their king Ulysses and some four mile distant from the island Cepholania which towards evening we do see be distant some one hundred mile from corfu on Wednesday early in the morning we enter a narrow sea some two Cephalonia mile broad have Cephalonia the less on the north side and the great Cephalonia on the south side and we cast anchor near a desert rock where of old there be a university and many of we in our boat send with mariner to cut wood and take fresh water do go on land in the great Cepholania to refresh ourselves and to wash our body in the sea water but we dare not go far from our mariner lest the inhabitant of those woody mountain shall offer we violence both the land be subject to Venice and abound with wine and small currend and in time of war the inhabitant retire to a fort build there by the venetian to be safe from the turk the venetian every three year choose some of their gentleman to be send hither for governor and officer the same Wednesday the eight of May towards the evening we set sail and before dark night pass by the promontory call the cape of Cepholania and do see on the north side the land some ten mile distant where the navy of the pope king of Spain and venetian confederate have don John of Austria base brother to king Philip of Spain for their general obtain a noble victory in the year 1571 against the navy of the turk the christian hide there many of their galley that the turk come out of the gulf of Corinth now call the gulf of Lepanto may despise their number and so be more easy draw to fight in the mouth of the say gulf upon the west shore be the castle of Toran or Torneze seat in a province of Greece which the turk call Morea and in the bottom of the gulf Petrasso be seat in the same province and Lepanto in the province of Achaia and of these city this gulf of Corinth be in these day call sometime the gulf of Lepanto sometime the gulf of Petrasso in the city of Petrasso the english merchant live have their consul and they traffic especial for currand of Corinth near Cepholania great store of dolphin do again swim about our ship which they say do foretell that the wind will blow from that quarter whether they swim and the same day in the main sea great dolphin and in great number do play about our ship on Thursday in the morning we do leave on the south side the island zante subject zante to the venetian and seventy mile distant from Cephalania the description of which island I defer to my return this way and so we sail close by the shore of or Morea on the north side be almost a island join on the north side to Achaia by a narrow neck of land which many of old have attempt to cut and to make that province a island and it contain large county or territory of Greece we sail along the south side of this province do see the city Coron Modon and navarin and somewhat low towards the south be a little island call Strivalli which be barren and inhabit by grecian monk call Caloiri who come out to we in a boat to beg alm and the patron of our ship in honour of our lady or virgin Marie of Strivalli salute the island with some piece of ordinance upon Friday the ten of may we sail by the foresay city Modon seat in Greece and one hundred mile distant from the island zante the eleven day in the morning we sail by the promontory call the cape of Modon and within six hour sail be out of the sight of any part of Morea but in the evening we come to the cape of s. John the first promontory of the island Candia Candia distant some one hundred and fifty mile I always understand italian mile be now among the italian from Modon the foresay city of Morea and these high mountain of Candia be yet cover on the top with snow we sail on the south side of Candia and towards evening pass by the middle part of the island and the thirteen day by nine of the clock in the morning we sail by the cape of Solomon be towards the east the further part of Candia the description of which island I defer to my come back that way at this time our mariner aswell greek as italian be great offend with one of our french consort a lay man because at dinner time according to the negligent fashion of the french he turn the clean side of his trencher upward for of all man the mariner and of all mariner the greek and italian be most superstitious and if any thing in the ship chance to be turn up-side down they take it for a ill sign as if the ship shall be overwhelm otherwise I never observe that either the chief or inferior mariner ever use the least disrespect to any passenger be rather love and familiar to they in conversation and I remember that my brother Henry use to walk upon the high hatch the patron and Scrivano and other do with smile observe his fast walk and melancholy humour yet howsoever it be troublesome to they do only once and that courteous reprove he or rather desire he that he will have respect to the mariner who watch all night for the public safety and be then sleep under the hatch always understand that a man may not be so bold in another man house as in his own and may yet less be bold in a ship of stranger and that a unknown passenger must of all other be most respective and whereas mariner be hold by some to be thievish sure in the haven at the journey end where thief easy find receiver it be good to be wary in keep that belong to you but at sea no place be more safe than a ship where the thing steal be easy find and the offender severe punish on Sunday the nineteen of May we come to the first promontory of the island Cyprus Cyprus towards the west and after eight hour sail we come to the old city Paphos or paphia now call Baffo and the wind fail we and gentle breathe upon this castle of Venus we hover here all the next night gain little or nothing on our way this place be most pleasant with fruitful hill and be of old consecrate to the goddess Venus queen of this island and they say that adamant be find here which skilful jeweller repute almost as precious as the oriental a mile from this place be the cave wherein they feign the seven sleeper to have sleep I know not how many hundred year the twenty one of May towards the evening we enter the port of Cyprus call saline and the two and twenty day obtain licence of the turkish cady to go on land we lodge in the village Larnica within a monastery of european friar here some of we be to sail to joppa and thence to go by land to jerusalem do leave the venetian ship which sail forward to Scanderona the turk do conquer the island Cyprus from the venetian in the year 1570 and to this day possess it the chief city whereof be Nicosia seat in the midst of the island and Famogosta seat in the further part of the island towards the east the turkish bassa or governor use to choose Famogosta for his seat though Nicosia be the fair city because it have a good haven and a most strong fort which the venetian build the island lie two hundred and forty mile in length from the west to the east and have some eighty mile in breadth and six hundred mile in compass this island be say to be distant some four hundred mile from the island of Candia which be some two hundred and thirty mile long but I speak of the next promontory in both of they and from Venice some two thousand two hundred and twenty mile from Alexandria in Egypt some four hundred and fifty mile from Alexandretta at this day call Scanderona the haven of Caramania eighty mile from Tripoli of Syria ninety mile and from joppa a haven in Palestina about two hundred and fifty mile speak of the uttermost promontory on all side this island yield to no place in fruitfulness or pleasure be enrich with corn oil cheese most sweet porke sheep have tail that weigh more than twenty pound caper grow upon prick bush pomegranate orange and like fruit cane or reed of sugar which they beat in mill draw out a water which they see to make sugar with rich wine but gnaw or burn the stomach odoriferous Cypress tree whereof they make sier store of Cotton and many other blessing of nature near the promontory Del'Gatto so call of cat that use to kill serpent they take falcon which hawk the governor be command to send to Constantinople they sow corn in the month of October and reap it in April I know not how it come to pass that in this island of Venus all fruit taste of salt which Venus love well and I think that this be only proper to the place at which we land where they make salt till many islander affirm to i that the very earth the sweet herb the beast feed there and the fountain of water have a natural saltness the house be build after the manner of Asia of a little stone one roof high and plain in the top which be plaster and there they eat and sleep in the open air by the assistance of a venetian merchant seven of we hire a ship of a greek dwelling in Cyprus for twenty eight zechine to joppa now call jaffa or Giaffa with condition that he shall stay at joppa fifteen day to expect our return from jerusalem and shall thence carry we to Tripoli in Syria the most part of these zechine we leave in the hand of the venetian merchant to be deliver to the master of this ship at his return if he bring our testimony under our hand that he have perform all covenant with we for we also condition with he that he shall stay long than fifteen day at joppa if need be for our return we pay he a sequin for every day above fifteen which he shall stay there for we we may have hire a ship or bark for ten zechine direct to joppa without these condition of stay there and carry we to Tripoli and because the turkish governor of city use to impose great tribute upon christian drive into their haven and sometime by trick of fraud to bring they in danger of life only to spoil they of their money some of our consort will have add another condition that the master shall not carry we to any port but that of joppa have not the rest judge it unreasonable to tie he for performance of that which be only in the power of God according to the wind which may force he to take harbour myself do familiar know a english gentleman who short after come to Scanderona and there take ship to pass by this shore to joppa and so to jerusalem if a honest man have not forewarn he have by the treason of a janizare in the way be sell for a slave to the inland turk whence he be like never to be redeem be far remove from christian who only trade upon the coast and he be so terrify with this danger as he return into England without see jerusalem to which he have then a short journey only carry with he a counterfeit testimony and seal that he have be there because he have put out much money upon his return I former say that we lodge at Cyprus in a monastery whence be now to depart the friar of our company and also the layman give each of we eight lyre of Venice to the guardian of the monastery and one lyre to the friar that attend we in the name of gift or alm but indeed for three day lodging and diet upon Friday the twenty four of may we seven consort namely two franciscan friar one Erimitane Friar and two lay man all frenchman and myself and my brother hire a boat in the haven for four lyre of Venice to carry we to the cyprian bark we have hire and we carry with we for our food a cheese cost four asper a jar of oil cost six asper and a vessel of wine call somewhat big than a english barrel and full of rich wine but such as fret our very entrails cost one sequin and four of Venice and two turkish asper and egg cost twenty three asper beside biscuit which we bring out of the greek ship in twilight for the night use not here to be dark we set sail and be force to go back towards the west along the shore of Cyprus to the promontory call Capo Gatti that be the cape of cat that we may from thence according to the mariner experience fetch a fair wind so we sail that evening thirty mile of Italy I mean and the next day twenty mile to a village of Cyprus call where christian ship use to put in here we cast anchor and all the six and twenty day of May expect a wind which we get at midnight follow joppa be no more than two hundred fifty mile from Cyprus and may easy be run in two night and a day sail with a fair wind yet how soever the wind be most favourable to we we can see no land till Wednesday thetwenty nine of may at which time we find ourselves by the ignorance of the mariner to be upon the coast of Egypt near the city Damiata which we may see seat upon the bank of Nilus and they say it be some six mile from the Sea now our mariner see the shore know better to direct our sail and the night follow we lie at anchor near this shore upon Thursday we coast the land of the philistine and first do plain see the city Gaza and after thirty mile sail the city Ascolon near which we cast anchor for that night upon Friday be the last of May after two mile sail we enter the haven of joppa from hence we send a messenger hire for fourteen meidine to the subasha joppa of ramma entreat he that he will give we leave to pass to jerusalem and send we a soldier to protect we the foresay shore of the philistine seem to be a wild narrow and sandy plain near the sea with mountain pleasant and fruitful towards the east upon Palestine the city of joppa mention in the scripture have some ruin of wall stand which show the old circuit thereof but have not so much as any ruin of house only we do see the exactor of tribute come out of two
