all the day trouble with a weakness of body and because the great danger of our participate with they in their rite be like to be when we shall be lock with they in the church of the sepulchre which only remain to be see by we I make such use of this my brother and my own weakness as increase their opinion of his danger and myself take many occasion to make they think my sickness the great it happen that in the evening our consort burn with desire of return homeward appoint the next day for the visit of the sepulchre but I in respect of my own and my brother weakness desire to have it defer some few day till the very friar our consort impatient of delay and yet unwilling to incur the blame of leave we behind they make free offer to we of that which we most desire say that in their chamber within the church they have bed for we to rest upon and that they will provide we meat and all necessary which we can have stay in the monastery we glad take this condition and so be lock up in the church of the sepulchre upon Tuesday the eleven of june in the afternoon after we have satisfy our curiosity we lay we down upon the bed and only forbear meat for avoid of suspicion we rest there till Wednesday the twelve of june in the afternoon when we come forth and return to the monastery the thirteen day we have no thought but of make we ready for our return and the next day early in the morning we depart from jerusalem so as in all this time the friar themselves our consort have no leisure to be at a mass but only the Sunday when we be at Bethlehem and in the church of the sepulchre when upon pretence of sickness we rest on our bed otherwise we profess ourselves catholic as the papist will be call yet enemy to the king of Spain as the enemy of our queen and country and when our superstitious consort be now to leave jerusalem have gather great heap of stone from the monument to carry into their country and have receive of the guardian gift for great treasure holy bead Agnus and like trash we so refuse to take any such burden as still we bewail our mistortune that we be not to return the right way home as they do but to pass to Constantinople can not carry such relic with we leave they shall fall into some turk hand who may abuse they and when our consort at Bethlehem print the sign of the cross with ink and a penknife upon their arm so as the print be never to be take out we will not follow they in this small matter but excuse ourselves that be to pass home through many kingdom we dare not bear any such mark upon our body whereby we may be know beside it be some advantage to we that the frenchman our consort be of their king party and profess no less hate against Spain then ourselves to conclude the friar of our consort tell i and my brother that the guardian will make we knight of the sepulchre so we will crave that honour which be never grant to any but they that crave it in which case they offer to be intercessor for we I well know that they have offer this honour as they term it to a plebeian Frenchman our consort and have hear that the friar use this art to get money from pilgrim make no difference to who they give this title and for my part I never affect title think better to be of a interior condition with plenty then of high degree with want therefore I so answer they as give due thanks for their courtesy yet I profess that if I be worthy of that title I may not crave it nor receive it offer in respect of the oath impose military duty upon i and the profession of service to the king of Spain the public enemy of our country beside that I shall be tie thereby to hate and prosecute all of the reform religion which many of my friend and kinsman profess it be true that if we have have a janizare to guide and protect we we may have lodge in Terusalem with some christian who will have show we the monument without trouble the italian friar and it be well know that the great Turk give liberty to all religion but the other sect of christian be poor and these friar be full of money as well the christian as turk depend great upon they so as if they will they may easy have bring we into danger neither can we have have such convenient diet and lodging with any other as with they and howsoever by our merchant help we may have obtain letter from the italian merchant at Haleppo for our entertainment and good usage in this monastery yet since for the foresay reason we have at Cyprus commit ourselves to the protection of the friar our consort we have now no mean but honest dissemble to free ourselves from danger for it have be easy for these friar secret to have draw we into danger of life and we know that papist make no conscience or rather think it meritorious to use like practice against those of our religion and if they will not practice against our life yet we know that they may have cast we into many danger both here and in our return through Italy if we shall have provoke they to wish we ill therefore this our foresay dissemble may well be excuse especial since thereby we do not in any sort wound our conscience to my best remembrance now that this dissemble may prove more profitable and honest it behoove we thorough to know our consort and so to apply ourselves to they of which the two french layman be young and of no experience in the world and one of the friar be more simple than simplicity itself so as small art be require to deceive these the second friar have be a soldier in the war of France and have make himself friar after the peace only to escape the private revenge of some who friend he have kill for they never seek revenge of those that put on a religious habit and to gain this man love it be sufficient to use good fellowship towards he the three friar have a sharp wit join with the wisdom of experience so as all the rest choose he for their guide and to govern their expense and because he may easy incense our consort and the friar at jerusalem against we we think good to gain his good will by all good respect to he so as howsoever we be not ignorant to manage our own affair yet join ourselves to the rest we make he also governor of our expense to witness our confidence in his love and care not to bear some loss so we may bind he to we under the title of friendship which we easy effect with he be of a courteous disposition to conclude I do often experience his good will and howsoever I find he love and courteous to make we respect as well of our consort as the friar at jerusalem yet I perceive by many and clear argument that he think we to differ from he in religion for in particular when I refuse for the foresay reason and with due modesty the friar offer to make i knight of the sepulchre he smile say to i allude to my name in the french tongue etc. that be in truth you be crafry as your name import but I will endeavour to make the guardian interpret your excuse to the best beside his friendship it be no small advantage to we that our stay at jerusalem be so short as the time permit they not to inquire after our religion by the aforesaid be we free ourselves from all danger yet will I not advise any by imitate we to incur the like who can have the foresay better commodity of perform this journey since it will be hard for any so to disguise their condition who have not first have good practice and acquaintance with friar in Italy as myself have beside that all our skill be sharpen to great wariness by a late tragical example of other the memory whereof be daily and hourly before our eye for we behold upon the wall in the chamber where we lodge the name write of Henry Bacon and Andrew Verseline two english gentleman of Abraham Frederichson and Henry Vonwildt peterson Van narden two fleming who name be write there upon the fourteen of August 1595 and lay before we both sleep and wake warn we like so many prodigy or vision to take wary bead to our step these four come in company to jerusalem have be receive into this monastery and when they have see the monument within and near jerusalem they go to Bethlehem where it happen that upon a health drink by the fleming to the king of Spain which the english refuse to pledge they fall from word to blow so as two of they return wound to the monastery of jerusalem then these italian friar according to the papist manner who first make the sick confess their sin and receive the lord supper before they suffer physician or apothecary to come to they or any kitchen physic to begluen they I say the friar press they to confess their sin and so to receive the lord supper which when they refuse to do it be apparent to the friar that they be of the reform religion who they term heretic whereupon the friar begin to neglect they I will not say to hate they and while the two which be wound stay for recovery of their health and so detain the other two with they it happen that the three fall sick so as none have their health now but master Verseline who love and like a servant more than a friend provide all necessary for his companion master Bacon till at last himself also fall sick and be the first of they that die then within eight day space all the rest die either for that they be neglect by the friar which I think sufficient in that country to cast away any in their case or by their too much care namely by poison as some suspect for the friar have one of their order who be skilful in physic and have a chamber furnish with cool water sirop and other medicine most fit for that country when they be dead the friar give into the turk hand the body of the two fleming and master Verseline who have little store of crown which belong to the great Turk as heir to all stranger and the turk permit they to be bury upon mount Zion without the wall in the church yard proper to the christian of Europe but master Bacon over-living the rest and now see his life to depend upon the friar care of he show a novice Friar long bracelet of piece of gold twine about his arm and promise to give they all to he and great reward if he will go with he into England so as he will take care of he in his sickness he have persuade the young friar to go with he into England and to promise he faithful service there yet when this novice at his confession make this know and after verify as much to the guardian and chief friar I know not whether the hope of this booty make he die soon but I be sure he live very few day after and give i leave to tell the truth these friar either to gain his money which be due to the great Turk or for fear that inquisition shall be make by the turk after the cause of his death appear by manifest sign upon his body as other suspect and report I say these friar bury this gentleman in a yard of their monastery secret which if the great Turk or any of his magistrate have know no doubt they will glad have take this occasion to extort much money from the friar since by the like forge accusation they use sometime to oppress they the very turk have at other time themselves bury dead body within the circuit of the monastery and after cause they to be dig up as if they have be casual find and then cry that their ottoman be deceive put the friar to pay large ransom for redeem of their life and let no man wonder that these hungry governor of city and province in Turkey shall use like fraud to entrap christian as they do very frequent since they buy their office and many time be recall before they be warm in their seat if any man at Constantinople offer large sum for their employment so as this one province of Palestine and one city of jerusalem though have small or no traffic have have in one year space four Zaniacci the old be recall to Constantinople assoon as his successor have outbribed he there and this be one of the great mischief in this empire since starveling fly suck much more than those that be full gorge the foresay zaniacco be chief governor for military and civil affair of all Pallestine and lie at jerusalem in the house of Pontius Pilate his substitute or lieutenant be call catake who cast one of our consort for a time into prison because he complain of the turkish exaction and his own poverty the three magistrate be call cady who govern ecclesiastial matter and dwell in Salomon house as they call it at jerusalem near the yard of the old temple of the jew in which now a turkish Mosche be build and of this man we have our leave to enter the city and to see the sepulchre and be call before he we be command to put off our shoe he sit across legged like a tailor on the ground upon a Turkey carpet the four magistrate be call Agha who keep the castle of jerusalem and when we walk one evening on that part of the roof of our monastery whence we have the fair prospect into the city he send a messenger to command we to retire from behold the castle or otherwise he will discharge a piece of ordinance at we chap. 3 of our journey from jerusalem by land to Haleppo by sea to Tripoli in Syria by land to Haleppo and Scanderona and of our passage by sea to the island Candia upon Friday the fourteen of june in the year 1596 we go out of jerusalem and by the same way and in the same june 14. ann. 1596. manner as we come ride back to ramma deliver to our guide as many zechine as before to pay for the turkish exaction and to our for their ass which we have hire neither do any memorable thing happen to we by the way save that when we come near to ramma and by chance ride over the place of burial for the turk where some woman be then mourn for their dead friend they think it a reproach that we shall ride over their grave do with enrage countenance fling stone at we till we appease they by dismount from our ass the fifteen of june we come back to joppa where our guide give three meidine to a janizare that he will beat with a cudgel certain arabian who have offer we wrong by the way which he do ready and round then without delay we go aboard our little greek bark which according to our bargain at Cyprus stay here for our return for the master thereof be further tie to transport we from hence to Tripoli in Syria neither have he yet receive full payment for transport we hither the money be leave in Cyprus with a italian merchant who be to pay it he at his return if he bring a testimony under our hand that he have perform his bargain to we this condition we make provident and by advice of experience man for otherwise the master of our bark upon any profitable occasion will have leave this port before our return from jerusalem and we shall hardly have find another bark here in a place not much frequent with ship beside that the restraint of the money not to be pay but upon a testimony bring under our hand be a good caution that he shall not use we ill nor any way betray we the sixteen of june upon Sunday by twilight of the morning we set sail from joppa and coast the shore of Asia have the land so near we every day as we may easy distinguish the situation of the city and territory and first we pass by the city call Caesaria Philippi seat in a plain and twenty five mile distant from joppa which of old be a famous city but now for the most part ruin and become a infamous nest of turk moor and arabian here Christ raise to life the daughter of jairus and heal the woman which for twelve yeete have a flux of blood and here saint Peter do baptize the centurion Cornelius and saint Paul in the presence of foelix dispute with tertullus here Titus the son of Vespasian land when he come to destroy jerusalem cast great multitude antipatris of jew to wild beast to be devour in the right way to Tripoli antipatris be not far distant which Herod do rebuild and thither the soldier do lead saiint Paul by the command of the tribune lysias but we can not see this village next we do see the pilgrim castle now call Tortora then we sail by the promontory hang far over the sea of the mountain carmelus make famous by carmel the abode of the prophet Elias then we pass within sight of the old city Ptolemais after call Achon and Acri seat in a fair plain within a creek of the sea of Achon the same name and compass the city and such affair plain lie all along the coast from joppa to Tripoli this city be famous by the army of Europe pass to conquer these part and at this day it have a large circuit compass with wall and a commodious haven and be thirty five mile distant from Caesaria from hence fall twenty mile we pass by the city Tyrus then call sur the ruin whereof Tyrc witness the old magnificence the seat thereof seem most pleasant be build upon a low rock in the form of a peninsule which rock be part of a high promontory hang over the sea and it may appear how strong it be fortify of old by Curtius relate the difficulty with which Alexander the great take and subdue the same when we have sail some ten mile further we do see the ruin of sarepta where the prophet elea lodge with a widow in the time of a great sarepta famine after we have sail some twenty mile further we do see and pass by the city Sydon now call Saetta seat on the north side of a promontory and lie Sydon towards the west and south to the very sea side these most pleasant territory be inhabit by wicked people but God send we a fair wind by which we escape from they into who port if we have be drive they will have take all lust and unjust occasion to extort money from we if they do we no worse harm mention be often make in the holy seripture of Sydon Tire and these territory as well in the old as new testament the particular whereof I omit here first we do glad see the hill and high top of mount Lybanus be a very pleasant and fruitful mountain Lybanus the wine whereof be carry as far be Haleppo the castle Barutti be some two mile distant from the promontory of Saetta and it be seat upon the north-side of Barutti a hill hang over the sea here they say that saint George deliver the king daughter by kill a dragon and to this place as also to Tyre and Sydon there be great concourse of merchant who have their chief traffic at and especial at Haleppo from hence we pass ten mile to biblis then after ten mile sail we pass by biblis (@) and again after ten mile sail by a promontory which the italian call Capo Peso last we pass ten mile sail by a most pleasant plain and so upon Thursday the seventeen of june land at Tripoli of Syria so call for difference from Tripoli Tripoli in Africa the haven be compass with a wall and lie upon the westside of the city whereif be many little bark and some ship of marsiles in France the haven be fortify with seven tower whereof the four be call the tower of love because it be build by a italian merchant who be find in bed with a turkish woman which offence be capital as well to the Turk as christian if he have not thus redeem his life upon the haven be build many storehouse for merchant good and shop wherein they be fetch to sail the city of Tripoli be some half mile distant from the haven to which the way be sandy have many garden on both side in this way they show a pillar fasten upon a hill of sand by which they say the sand be enchant lest it shall grow to overwhelm the city likewise they show other pillar under which they say great multitude of scorpion be in like sort enchant which of old waste all that territory and they think that if these pillar be take away the city will be destroy by the sand and scorpion the length of the city somewhat pass the breadth and lie from the south to the north seat upon the side of a hill so cut by nature as it convey a brook into the street upon the west side of the city towards the south corner be a castle upon a high hill which the french man build of old to keep the citizen in subjection and therein the great Turk to the same end keep a garrison of soldier under his Agha or governor of the city upon the east side be two bridge over the foresay brook whence many pleasant fountain spring which run from the south to the north pass through the street of the city and then water the garden beyond this brook be fruitful hill and beyond the hill mount Lybanus lie so high as it hinder all further prospect which mountain be very pleasant abound with fruitful tree and with grape yield a rich wine upon the north side without the gate be many most pleasant garden in which they keep great store of silkworm for the turk sell their raw silk to the italian and buy of they the stuff weave thereof the building of Tripoli and of these part be like to that of Cyprus and jerusalem the street that lead to the way of Haleppo be broad the rest narrow and the air and water be unhealthful mount Lybanus as I former say be incredible fruitful and the plain of Tripoli reach ten mile be more fruitful than can easy be express bear great store of pleasant fruit whereof one among the rest be call amazza-franchi that be kill Frankes or french because the man of Europe die in great number by eat immoderate thereof the plain of Tripoli do of old yield two hundred thousand crown yearly to the count thereof as historian write and how soever the old traffic of Tripoli be for the most part remove to Damascus and Haleppo yet the city of Tripoli still yield four hundred thousand crown yearly to the great Turk it may seem incredible but it be most certain that here and throughout Syria they have sheep of such bigness as the very tail of they hang in many wreathe to the ground do weigh twenty five pound and many time thirty three pound a Christian who use to entertain the french do very well entreat we here and when I do see a bed make for i and my brother with clean sheet I can scarce contain myself from go to bed before supper because I have never lie in naked bed since I come from Venice to this day have always sleep by sea and land in my doublet with linen breech and stocking upon a mattress and between coverlet or quilt with my breech under my head but after supper all this joy vanish by a event least expect for in this part of Asia great store of cotton grow as it be upon stalk like cabbage as I former say in my journey from joppa to jerusalem and these sheet be make thereof do so increase the perpetual heat of this country now most unsupportable in the summer time as I be force to leap out of my bed and sleep as I have former do my host tell i a strange thing namely that in Alexandria of Egypt seat upon one of the mouth of the river Nilus there be a dovecote and that also at cairo or Babylon far within the land of Egypt there be another dovecote and because it much concern the merchant to have speedy news of any commodity arrive he assure i that they use to tie letter about the neck of the dove at Alexandria and so to let they loose which dove have former breed in the dovecote at Cayro do fly thither most swift and the keeper of they there take the letter they bring use to deliver they to the merchant this I believe not till I come to Haeleppo and tell it for a fable to the english merchant there they serious affirm the same to be true moreover the host of Tripoli tell i news from Constantinople namely that the greek have burn great part of the city which he think to be false and only invent to oppress they in other part and that the janissary have raise a great tumult against the subasha of the city who use great severity towards they by restrain they from drink wine and from keep harlot and that some one hundred of these seditious janissary be drown in the heaven and the rest be daily seek out to be punish moreover that Halil bassa the admiral of Turkey be part from Constantinople with sixty galley have take many greek and armenian christian by force to row in his galley beside that for want of mariner he have leave there twenty galley which be prepare to keep that narrow sea final that the great Turk be present to go with his army into Hungary but be not yet depart from the city now the frenchman our consort go aboard a ship of marsiles to return into France but myself and my brother be to go by land to Haleppo agree to give our Muccaro nine for two ass to ride upon and their meat and for three tribute call cafarri which he be to pay for we by the way come to some twenty meidine they call he Muccaro who let out mule ass or horse and they call he who conduct the merchant good moreover we be force to give a sugar-loaf to the value of a sequin to the governor of the city and a Piastro to the scribe or clerk of the city for the privilege to go without a janizare to conduct we so they pretend omit no occasion to extort from christian but we covenant not to pay the nine to our Muccaro till our journey be end only give one piastro into his hand for earnest and pretend that we will pay the rest at Haleppo where we be to receive money leave they think that we have store of crown with we shall practice any treason or oppression against we this Piastro we give he in hand to buy meat for his beast and the other eight we pay after at Haleppo and beside give he of free gift a sequin for his faithful service to we by the way we be to take our journey with the caravan go from Tripoli to Haleppo the turk call a caravan the company of merchant passenger and driver of load camel keep together for safety against thief and use to lodge in the open field for in Turkey they make journey in great troop neither do I ever see any ride alone but only a horseman of the army and that very rare upon Saturday the two and twenty of june we go out of Tripoli at the north gate and pass over a bridge of the foresay brook and from eight of the clock till noon we pass along the sea-shoare and over high mountain then over a untilled plain see not one village nor so much as the least house by the way then at last come to a little shade of figtree we rest there the heat of the day and feed upon such victual as we have while our Muccaro and the rest give meat to their beast at three of the clock in the afternoon we go forward in the like way and late in the evening we come to a village near which we lodge in the open field in a pleasant plot of grass near the bank of a river plant with some tree upon Sunday we rise early and for two hour space pass a promontory of the sea then turn towards the land we pass through wild and untilled hill and plain field and at noon we rest under the shadow of some bramble refresh ourselves with meat and sleep and give meat to our ass at three in the afternoon we go forward and pass by the castle huss in which some say job dwell and which they say be possess by the french while they have the kingdom of jerusalem huss also we pass by a monastery of saint George then possess by christian friar and seat in a pleasant valley yield tree of fig and olive and towards evening we encamp as I may term it in the open field at the foot of a high mountain they say job do of old possess this territory and that not far hence in the way lead to Damascus there be a city now call hemp and of old call huss which the christian inhabitant to this day call the city of job and the valley not far distant the valley of huss and the turk have build a Mosche or church in this city which they think to be build upon the very ruin of the house wherein job dwell and that his body be carry from hence to Constantinople other object that according to the scripture job can not dwell here because they write that he dwell among the idumean and be rob by the sabean I dare not affirm that he dwell here but I dare bold say that I know more than Socretes do even two thing whereas he know but one first that the arabian to this day make excursion into these part rob the caravans that go from Haleppo to Tripoli so as if job be alive and have a hundred thousand head of cattle they be as like to rob he here as in any other place second I know that we pass a sad night in this place and never have more need of job his patience then here for it happen that one of the woman which the leader of the caravan use to have for their attendance light a fire to make ready their supper by chance some spark or flame break out of the stone wherewith it be compass and set the dry herb of the field on fire which be neglect at the first do spread itself for a great compass whereupon the governor of the province dwell upon the mountain and behold the field on fire send to we one janisary arm only with a cudgel who fall upon the man of our Caravan be some hundred in number beat they with his cudgel till they fall upon the fire with the upper long garment they use to wear and so extinguish it in the mean time myself and my brother go aside lie out of his sight by the advantage of a high ground between he and we where we be astonish to see one man arm only with a cudgel to beat a hundred man and the very zantons or priest arm with sword and many calliver the fire be put out we think all safe join ourselves to the company again but soon espy our error for the janizare drive we all before he like so many calf to appear before the governor and satisfy he for this damage and if at any time we go slow he wheel his cudgel about his head and cry wohowe rooe present strike they that be next he my brother and myself treat with he by the way to give he a reward that he will dismiss we but when he give this warning we be the first to run from he with laughter to see our man thus drive like beast and commend to ourselves the honesty of the man who first give warning before he strike then present assoon as we do see the gentleman pacify we return again to he with our Muccaro to interpret our word and tell he that we be the servant of a christian merchant and have no good in the caravan nor any thing to do with they and offer he a reward so he will let we return for we know that the turk will take any occasion to oppress we as christian and that the governor will have deal worse with the caravan if he perceive that christian be with they thus we often flee from he when he give the say sign of anger for howsoever we offer he a gift yet we can not otherwise escape his blow and often we return to he be pacify offer he a gift to dismiss we which at last we obtain give he a sequin when we be dismiss we be in no less fear of some violence while we return alone and unarmed to the place where our baggage lay but go forward between hope and fear at last we come safe thither and there hide ourselves till our consort shall return who after a hour space return tell we that the chief of the caravan be the cause of the fire make have pay ten for the damage and the governor swear that if the fire have go over the mountain into the plain field of corn he will have hang we all upon the high tree on the top of the mountain this tragedy end we refresh ourselves with meat and sleep upon Monday early in the morning we set forward and spend eight hour in ascend the mountain which be very high but the way easy with many turn about the mountain which of itself without manure yield many wild but pleasant fruit seem to pass in pleasantness the best manure orchard upon the top of the mountain we meet some horseman of the army not without fear of some violence to be offer we till we understand that they be send out to purge the high way of thief they be arm with lance shield and short broad sword so as a man will have say they have be the knight of amades de Gaul neither be it unprobable that those fiction come from the horseman of Asia since we do see some mile from Tripoli a bridge call the bridge of Rodomont and a fountain near Scandarona call the amazon fountain and many like monument in these part when we have pass the foresay high mountain we come into a very large and fruitful plain of corn which be yet uncut-downe here we refresh ourselves and our beast with meat rest near a fountain for the turk require no better inn for their beast and themselves than a fountain of clear water after dinner we go forward in this plain and do see some village which in this vast empire be very rare and near one of these village we do sit down at night sup and rest in the open field upon Tuesday early in the morning we take our journey and for six hour pass in the same plain have not so much as the shadow of one tree and come to the city aman which in the scripture the second of the king the seventeen chapter be call Hamath be some three day journey from Damascus Hamath this city be of large circuit and pleasant seat upon two hill for the three hill of the castle have nothing but ruin have a river run by it and abound with orchard of palm and fruitful tree and near the same be six village in sight here we rest part of this day and the next night the master of our caravan have business in the city neither import it where we lodge for they have no public inn nor bed in any house nor cook but every man buy his meat and can dress it but to the end we may be ready to go early with the caravan in the morning most of we lodge in poor house of the suburb myself and my brother be to sleep in the yard upon our own quilt and the yard decline from the house to the bottom where our beast be tie we lay ourselves down upon the top of the hill but in the morning find ourselves tumble down between the foot of the ass and camel when I can not remember the english inn without sigh this city have great traffic and abound with necessary to sustain life and here our Muccaro buy for we sour curd vulgar call mish mash for two meidines a cheese for six three hen for three meidine twenty egg for four meidine cucumber for three milk for five which they call Harach and drink as large as wine for ten meidine four pound of wine for one sequin biscuit for the turk have no other bread but cake bake on the hearth for thirty meidine which thing we provide for our supper and to carry with we by the way yet may we have buy and do buy most thing by the way except wine and bread which be hardly find and must be carry by those that will have they the guide of our Caravan be detain here by his business most part of the next day be Wednesday and in the mean time it fortunate happen that a turkish bassa return with his train from his government and be to go our way rest here so as his company free we the rest of our journey from fear of thief upon Wednesday in the afternoon we setforward in the company of this bassa and journey all night in this plain wherein there be not the shadow of one tree and at eight of the clock the next morning we do sit down in the open field rest under the ruin of old wall here the janissary of the bassa inquire curious after the condition of i and my brother so as our Muccaro advise we to give they half a piastro which they receive promise to defend we from all injury but in the mean time they do so swallow our wine as when it be spend we be force to drink water to which we be not use upon Thursday at three of the clock in the afternoon we set forward and about midnight we come to the city Marrha where our Muccaro and diverse other pay each of they ten meidine for cafar or tribute and at the city gate a man be hang in chain also the next day we do see another impale that be sit and rot upon a stake fasten in the ground and thrust into his fundament and bowel upon Friday before day we set forward and pass a stony barren way but full of walnut tree upon which many bird do sit and sing we come in four hover space to a hospital which they call Caon and it be stately build of stone in a round form with arch round about the court-yard under which arch each several company choose their place to eat and rest both which they must do upon the ground except they bring table and bed with they neither be any victual there to be sell or dress but every man buy his victual in the village adjoin and dress it after his manner the same Friday at four in the afternoon we go forward and ride all night do upon Saturday early in the morning sleep a hour in the open field while meat be give to our beast then go forward we come by noon the same day be the nine and twenty june 29. Haleppo of june after the pope new style which I have follow hitherto be in company of italian and friar to the famous city of Haleppo where the english merchant live in three house as it be in college entertain my brother and i very (@) and George Dorington the consul of the english there lead we to the house wherein he live with other merchant and there most courteous entertain we with plentiful diet good lodging and most friendly conversation refuse to take any money for this our entertainment and howsoever we bring he only a bill of exchange for one hundred crown yet when we complain to he that we now perceive the same will not serve our turn he free lend we as much more upon our own credit yea when after my brother death myself fall dangerous sick and be force to go from those part before I can recover my health so as all man doubt of my return into England yet he lend i a far great sum upon my bare word which howsoever I due repay after my come into England yet I confess that I can sufficient acknowledge his love to i and his noble consideration of poor and afflict stranger the city Haleppo be say to have the name of Halep which signify milk because the province be most fruitful or of the word aleph as the chief city of Syria and to have be call of old aram Sohab mention the second of Samuel the eight chapter and three verse or at least to be build not far from the ruin thereof the traffic in this place be exceed great so as the good of all Asia and the eastern land be bring hither or to Cayro in Egypt and before the portugal find the way into east India these commodity be all bring from these two city and the venetian and some free city of Italy sol enjoy all this traffic of old but after that time the portugal trade in east India serve all Europe with these commodity sell they yea and many adulterate drug at what price they list cut off most part of this traffic from the italian at last the french king make league with the great Turk the merchant of marsiles be make partner of this traffic and in our age the english under the reign of queen Elizabeth obtain like privilege though great opposition be make against they by the venetian and french merchant and the Turkey company in London be at this time the rich of all other silent enjoy the safety and profit of this traffic understand that when I write this the traffic into the east indies be nothing at all or very little know to the english or fleming this city lie within land the port whereof call Alexandretta by the christian and Scanderona by the turk I shall hereafter describe the building of this city as of all house in Syria be like to that of jerusalem but one roof high with a plain top plaster to walk upon and with arch before the house under which they walk dry and keep shop of ware the city be nothing less than well fortify but most pleasant seat have many sweet garden the air be so hot as i think I sup hot broth when I draw it in but it be very subtle so as the christian come hither from Scanderona a most unhealthful place have the air choke with fen continual fall sick and often die and this be the cause that the english factor employ here seldom return into England the twenty man scarce live till his prenticeship be out he may trade here for himself the christian here and the turk at the christian cost drink excellent wine where of the white wine grow in that territory but the red wine be bring from mount Libanus moreover all thing for diet be sell at cheap rate and indeed the turk want not good meat but only good cook to dress it the english merchant can bear i witness that these part yield sheep whereof the tail of one wreathe to the ground do weigh some thirty or more pound in fat and wool in one of the city gate they show the sepulchre of saint George where the turk maintain lamp continual burn for among all the christian saint they only reverence saint George in a garden of the suburb I do see a serpent of wonderful bigness and they report that the male serpent and young one be kill by certain boy this she serpent observe the water where the boy use to drink do poison the same so as many of the boy die thereof and that the citizen thereupon come out to kill she but see her lie with her face upward as complain to the heaven that her revenge be just that they touch with a superstitious conceit let she alone final that this serpent have live here many age and be of incredible year moreover they show a well near to the city in which they report that a chest of treasure be of old cast so as it may be see by passenger and that some attempt to take it out be assault and affright with devil in this city myself and my brother Henry lie sick some few day but by the help of a jew physician we soon recover our health and for fear of want money and especial out of our desire to return home we make too great haste to begin our journey for Constantinople if we will have expect eight day the Cassenda so they call a troop of horseman guard the great Turk treasure be in that time to go for Constantinople in who company we may safe and swift have perform this journey namely in sixteen day whereas those who follow the slow pace of camel scarce arrive there in thirty day but this province be extreme hot in this time of summer and we be scant of money for our long journey all mention of long stay be most unpleasing to we moreover master George Dorington never to be name by i without mention of love and respect do at this time send a caravan that be camel load with good of his own to Constantinople and be to make a present to a cady return from his government to Constantinople that he will take his caravan into his protection and to pass in his company and love make offer to we to recommend we in like sort with his good to the same cady we be easy persuade to take this journey present in the company of his servant and of a courteous english merchant call master jasper tyant be then to go for Constantinople this our conclusion prove great to the loss of sir John Spencer merchant of London who good these be which master Dortngton send with we for my brother die by the way and the great Turk be heir to all christian and stranger die in his empire the turk either thought or fraudulent pretend that these good belong to my brother and so take they into the great Turk storehouse and keep they there till they have unjust extort good sum of money from master Dorington beside the great loss which be sustain by the servant and camel hire in vain be now to enter this journey we hire for seventy one a camel to carry our victual a amble mule for my brother and a horse for myself and so much we present give into the hand of our Muccaro with covenant that he shall pay for the meat of the beast moreover we present lay out one hundred and twenty for diverse necessary namely two long chair like cradle cover with red cloth to hang on the two side of our camel which chair the turk use to ride in and to sleep upon camel back but we buy they to carry victual for biscuit and a tent wherein we may sleep and for like provision but behold when all this money be lay out and the very evening before the day in which we be to begin our journey my brother Henry fall sick of a flux be amaze with this sudden chance we stand doubtful for a time what to do till the consideration of the great sum of money we have lay out and of the difficulty to get more make we resolve to take this fatal journey yet with this purpose when we come to Scanderona some four day journey distant to go no further except in that time he recover his health propound this comfort to our miserable estate that there we may have commodity of convenient lodging with a Englishman there abide factor for our merchant upon Thursday the last of june that I may now follow the old style take here the last of june from the english and general use in Turkey among the very christian howsoever hitherto I have follow the new style take it from the venetian ship in which I come and from the friar at my abode in jerusalem I say the last of june we go out of Haleppo pass over stony hill and by the village (@) where the jew say the prophet jeremy be bury then ride forward all that night at last we sit down at eight of the clock in the morning and pitch our tent near a village where I do see a pillar erect to Pompey and here we rest and refresh ourselves the heat of the day this kind of journey be strange to we and contrary to our health for we begin our journey at four in the afternoon to shun the heat of the day past and ride all night so as we not use to this watch be so sleepy towards the Sunnerise as we can not abstain from nod and be many time like to fall from out horse to which mischief we can find no other remedy then to ride swift to the head of the caravan and there dismount to lie down and slumber with our horse bridle tie to our leg one of we by course walk by we to keep we from injury and to awaken we when the last camel pass by lest we shall there be leave a prey to thief and we have some two hundred camel in our Caravan do in this sort pass the sleepy hour in the morning till seven or eight of the clock at which time we use to pitch ourtent and rest moreover this great afflict we that spend the morning till ten or eleven of the clock in pitch our tent prepare meat and eat we have no time to rest but the extreme heat of the noon day which so pierce our tent that we can no more sleep then if in England upon a summer day we have lie near a hot sea-coal fire and how soever we lessen this heat by fling our gown over our tent between the sun and we yet for my part I be so afflict with want of sleep and with this immoderate heat as I fear to fall into a lunacy what then shall a man think will become of my sickly brother in this case upon Friday the first of july towards evening we take up our tent sup while our Muccaro load our beast than we ride over mountain all night and the next morning again pitch our tent near a poor village and our Muccaro buy we some fresh victual in the village according to the manner of Turkey where the very city yield no inn upon Saturday towards evening we set forward and ride that night over a large plain and next day after sunne-rise we come to Antioch a city of Asia famous for the Patriarchate and by history sacred and Antioch profane upon the east-side and upon the top of a high mountain lie great ruin of the old wall and house whence the seat of the city decline to the plain on the west side in which plain our Caravan rest the heat of this day near the pleasant and large fountain of water wherein the scripture record so many to have be baptize together as first in this place the faithful have the name of christian this fountain have fair build and seem of old to have be very stately and here we pitch our tent in the midst of the garden of this plain within the wall for howsoever the ruin of the wall show that of old the circuit of the city be very large yet scarce the hundred part thereof be now fill with house upon the west side without the wall the city be all compass with a river and a great fen and upon the east-side with mountain which situation make it natural strong here first wretched I perceive the imminent danger of my most dear brother death which I never suspect till this day much less have any just cause to fear it a Turk in this Caravan trouble with the same disease of a flux go to the ground more than twenty time each night journey and yet live whereas my brother only three or four time descend from his mule to that purpose which fill we with good hope but here first I learn by miserable experience that nothing be worse for one trouble with the flux then to stop or much restrain the course thereof for my brother stop this natural purge by take red wine and marmelat experience man do attribute all too late his death to no other thing I can not hire a horselitter by any endeavour of our Muccaro nor for any price though I offer a incredible sum for that or like commodity to carry he and we think it very dangerous to stay here among the turk after our Caravan depart especial since Scanderona be but five and twenty mile distant where we shall have the commodity to lodge with a Englishman and so to get all necessary for his recovery therefore upon Sunday in the evening we put all our provision in one of the foresay cover chair or cradle carry by the camel and make my brother a bed in the other cradle where as we think he may commodious rest and I promise the Muccaro half a piastro for every time my brother shall descend from the camel to ease himself for we be to ride before with the horseman and he be now to come behind with the camel so we set forward and myself twice in the night and once towards morning leave the horseman and ride back to my brother to know how he fare and when he give i no answer I return to the horseman think that he sleep then towards morning I be so afflict with my wont desire of sleep as I think a hour rest worth a king ransom therefore myself and master jasper tyrant our love consort ride a good pace to the village byland byland where we be to pitch our tent that we may make all thing ready to receive he but within short space our Muccaro run to our tent and tell i that he have leave my brother ready to give up his last breath in the first house of the village seem to say to i go quick and hang thyself with all possible speed I run to this house embrace my die brother and confound with sorrow understand from his mouth how far the event of our night journey have be contrary to our hope for whereas myself advise he to leave his mule and lie in the chair upon the camel back he tell i that he be shake in piece with the hard pace of the camel and whereas I have offer the Muccaro half a piastro for each time he shall light to ease himself he tell i that he have often ask this favour of the Muccaro but can never obtain it he excuse himself by fear to be leave behind the caravan for a prey to thief and whereas the camel hinder part be high than the fore part I have lay my brother head towards the hinder part and raise it as high as I can with pillow and clothes for his better ease it happen which I be ignorant of the way can not foresee that we all the night ascend mountain his foot be far high than his head whereupon he tell i that most part of the night he have lie in a trance which be the cause that he can not answer i at such time as I come to inquire of his health thus mischief light upon mischief to make my wretched state most miserable why shall I use many word in a case from the remembrance whereof my mind abhor therefore I will say in a word my most dear brother Henry upon Monday the four of july after the old style the year of our lord 1596 and of his age the seven and twenty die in my july 4. 1596. arm after many love speech and the express of great comfort in his divine meditation the turk present snatch all thing that be he as belong to the great Turk yea myself cast his shirt with many other thing of good value and whatsoever I can see that be he out of the tent into the turk hand and as a man half out of my wit can endure to see nothing that may renew the bitter remembrance of he the turkish officer in the great Turk name seize upon all the good of sir John Spencer which master Dorington send with we as if they have belong to my brother neither can they be release without great bribe after the contrary be prove present I send for the english factor lie at Scanderona who scarce obtain with the pay of five zechine to have my brother body bury in the open field beside the janizares turk and moor come in several swarm to i in this miserable case threaten to hinder his burial or to dig he up after he be bury except I will satisfy their insatiable extortion and have not the foresay english factor take upon he to satisfy these people and take up my purse full of zechine which I cast among they in a rage sure for my part I have willing give myself and all that I have with i to they for a prey one thing above measure afflict i which I think job himself can not have suffer namely that while myself and my brother be in our last embrace and mournful speech the rascal multitude of turk and moor cease not to grin and laugh at our sigh and tear neither know I why my heartstring break not in these desperate affliction but I be sure from that day to this I never enjoy my former health and that this hour be the first of my old age towards the evening the same four day of july we descend with the say english factor take care to have our baggage carry from the mountain towards Scanderona Scanderona little distant from this place in the further northern part of the valley upon the seashore from hence jasper tyant our love consort in this misery return back to Haleppo but myself not know what to resolve nor have power to think of dispose myself remain at Scanderona in the english factor house the next night while I lie wake I hear multitude of wolf as I think howl upon the mountain of byland and in the morning I understand by the english merchant that a kind of beast little big than a fox and engender between fox and wolf vulgar call jagale use to range upon these mountain in troop and many time to scratch the body of the dead out of their grave whereupon I hire a ass to carry i and a janizare to accompany i and go to see the place of my brother burial from which part I think to hear those howl and there beyond my expectation I find that they have scratch up the earth almost to his body and the turk make no doubt but that these beast hide themselves from day light will according to their manner return the next night to devour his body therefore I hire many poor people to bring stone whereof I make such a pile round about his body as I preserve that prey from their curse jaw which do I return to Scanderona so call by the turk which the christian call Alexandretta this be a poor village build all of straw and dirt except the house of some christian factor build of timber and clay in some convenient sort and it lie along the sea-shoare for the famous city of Haleppo have no other haven the merchant here unload their good but themselves make haste to Haleppo stay as little here as possible they can and commit the care of carry their good thither upon camel to the factor of their nation continual abide here the pestilent air of this place be the cause that they dare not make any stay here for this village seat in Cilicia now call Caramania be compass on three side with a fenny plain and the four side lie upon the Sea in the way to Haleppo as I remember towards the east there be in this plain a fountain of clear water some mile distant from this village and howsoever all other water fall out of the fen be most unwholesome yet the goodness of this fountain be so much prize as the merchant use to carry their meat thither and eat there under a pleasant shade not far from this fountain there stand a old castle at the foot of the mountain which they call the castle of Penthesilea queen of the amazon on the same side beyond the fen be a most high mountain which keep the sight of the sun from Scandarona and be full of bog infect the fenny plain with ill vapour and beyond this mountain my dear brother lie bury on the other side towards the north as I remember in the way lead to Constantinople the like fenny plain lie and the mountain though more remote do bar the sight of the sun and the boggy earth yield ill vapour make Scanderona infamous for the death of christian on the same side Asia the less stretch itself into the sea towards the west and in the next shore thereof be a pleasant village now call bias which of old be call Tarsus Tarsus where saint Paul be bear be six mile from Scanderona and seat in the same province of Cilicia and abound with fruit silkworm and all thing necessary to sustain life Scanderona on the south side towards Palestina be also compass with the like fenny Scanderona plain but far more large then on the other side final on the west side towards the sea and Italy be a safe haven in the further part of the mediterranean sea towards the east and into this sea the prophet jonas be cast and preserve miraculous by a whale be in this part cast upon the shore they say that the owe of the sea do here much increase the malignity of the air yet the sea man use to sleep in their ship and seldom to come on land till the sun be rise above the mountain hide it and have draw up the ill vapour the foresay mountain of Cilicia be hold for part of mount Taurus which in Scythia be call Caucasus and in these part amanus I have former say that these part near the equinoctial line have seldom any rain but the earth be common moisten with the dew fall after sunset but while I stay here a great tempest fall of thunder hail and rain upon the seven of August and the rain do not fall by drop but by paileful as we read it fall but much more violent towards west India and near the equinoctial line and as no violent thing be perpetual so this tempest soon pass short after I come to this unhappy village Scanderona the grief of my mind cast i into a great sickness so as I who in perfect health have pass so many kingdom of Europe at this time in the very flower of my age first begin to wax old this sickness bring the first weakness to my body and the second proceed of another grief after my return into England take from i all thought of youthful pleasure and demonstrative teach i that the poet most true say that be care make gray-headed while I languish here in a last sickness it happen that upon occasion I look upon the two testimony give to my brother and myself at jerusalem of our have be there and I be not a little astonish to see that they be both at the same time cut out of the same skin of parchment and write with the same hand and ink yet that of my brother be in all part eat with worm when i be altogether untouched and after I do more wonder that to this day the same testimony give to my brother be no more eat with worm then at that time it be and i still remain unperished my foresay sickness be so vehement and so long that all man doubt I will never recover so as my friend in England after they have hear of my brother death be advertise within few week that myself also be dead but for my part though my nightly dream that I be walk in the cave and sepulchre of Italy may have somewhat discourage i and though I have no other physician than the barber-surgean of a ship yet can I never doubt of recover my health but my mind still presage that I shall return home yet when diverse time I begin to (@) and present by the heat of the clime and ill air of the place have be cast down again I resolve to follow their counsel who persuade i to try if the air of the sea will strengthen i therefore my dear friend master George Dorington have send i one hundred zechine for my expense the great sum of money which I have be all spend by the accident of my brother death and my sickness the particular of which expense I omit because in this grief and weakness I have no mind to note they only for a taste remember that I pay a piastro each day to a poor man who continual cool my heat with a fan master Dorington I say have send i money and I have provide all necessary Octo. 19 an. 1596. for my journey at last upon Thursday the ten of October after the new style and in the year 1596 I be carry aboard a french ship of marsiles partly by the help of porter partly in a boat be so weak as I can not stand this ship be call John Baptist and the christian name of the master be Simon with who I have covenant that I pay he thirty or ducat for myself and my servant he shall set we on land in some good haven of the island Candia and if it be possible in the chief city thereof call Candia and lie on the north side of the island whence I be now resolve to take my journey to Constantinople leave all thought of go by land upon Friday the eleven of October we sail prosperous but after the wind grow so contrary as we be drive to the south of Candia therefore the french mariner murmur against we as heretic cause their ill passage and there be no hope leave with those wind to set we on land at Candia the chief city the master of our ship send we in his boat with some few mariner which he least esteem that we may sail to land be fifty mile distant thus upon Thursday the three and twenty of October have sail eight the island of Candia hour in great danger towards the evening we land under a promontory of Candia where there be neither city village house nor cottage so as plenty of rain fall that night yet we be force to lie in a open boat where my companion or servant not know our danger sleep sound but myself dare never close my eye fear lest these mariner be marsilian who at that time little love the english shall offer we violence to gain our good this consort or servant of i be a english man and by profession a cook and be come into these part to serve master Sandy who be send from London to be the english consul at Haleppo as he pass from Constantinople thither die in Asia Minor of the same disease whereof my brother die and in the same month this servant be after his master death to return into England I take to attend i that I may by his company avoid solitude and mitigate some part of my sorrow he be no soon enter into the french ship but he present fall sick and not able to serve himself can not give i the expect comfort much less do i any service but great increase my charge spend all upon my purse and much trouble i have not himself the least skill in any foreign language so as he recover not till we come to Venice where be among christian I have small use of his help he be rather a burden then a comfort to i when I be to enter the french ship I lay in provision of hen egg damask prune and other thing but my languish stomach not desire not be able to digest any other then salt meat these provision fall to the share of my sick servant and myself be nothing but skin and bone as one that languish in a consumption my blood and humour renew with these salt meat can not but weaken my future health so as I have be always very lean after by decay of natural heat become very fat and have lose the retentive faculty of my stomach so as I continual cast all that lay upon it so soon as in the morning I come into the air I have no remedy against this weakness but the take of tobacco the french mariner who bring we to the shore of Candia part from we on Friday the twenty five of july after the new style early in the morning and when I the island of Candia have well reward they for their pain then first they show i above the wild rock call Calisminiones a monastery of the greek some three mile distant and call Santa Maria Ogidietra we be leave alone and stay there fast till noon at last espy and call to we two man pass by upon the mountain but they think we to be pirate flee away as fast as they can present behold my man come out of the wood and bring with he a ass which he have find there who persuade i to lay my baggage on that beast and so to walk soft towards the monastery I willing try my strength and lean upon our two sword for want of a staff and yet often fall go forward like a snail till despair of go further I fall upon the ground after a hour space a shepherd pass by and I show he gold and name monastery which word he understand he swift run to the monastery and tell the monk call by the greek caloiri our state and condition they present send a servant to we who in the italian tongue tell we the great danger wherein we shall be if we stay upon those mountain till night advise we to make haste to the monastery thus drive with fear and encourage by his company I try again to go forward and with great trouble pass one mile over the mountain for lean as I say on two sword and upon the passage of any steep mountain by reason of the lightness of my head creep upon hand and foot with great difficulty I go so far and now be not able to go any further no not to save my life behold a boy who come to water his assear fountain adjoin to who the servant of the monk give a piastro and so whether he will or no take his ass and set i upon it and so at last we pass the other two mile long than three english mile and come to the monastery the or monk receive we courteous and give we such victual as they have namely pomegranate olive bread and sharp wine which be no good meat for sick man have fast almost two day also they confer love with we but still desire we to keep aloof from they at bed time they give we a straw mat to lay upon a plastred floor for our bed but we be better provide of matterass and quilt of our own and though lie upon the ground yet sleep sound because we be in safety the italian in regard of their clime be very curious to receive stranger in a time of plague and appoint chief man to the office of provide for the public health call the place where they meet the office of health also without their city especial in the state of Venice they have public house call Lazaretti and for the most part pleasant seat whether passenger and merchant with their good must at their first arrival retire till the provider for health have curious inquire if they come from any suspect place or have any infectious sickness and here they have all thing necessary in abundance but may not converse or talk with any man till they obtain the grant of free conversation call la or if any man speak with they he must be enclose in the same house and because they stay forty day there for the trial of their health this trial be call Quarantana moreover they that go by land in Italy must bring a testimony of health call Boletino before they can pass or converse the venetian be more curious in this than any other use this trial when there be no plague I know not for what reason except it be that the city of Constantinople be seldom or never free of the plague whence many of their ship come or for that some mystery for the good of traffic or of the commonwealth lie hide under the pretence of this custom for no man dare enter the city and converse there till he have get licence of these provisor neither dare any merchant dispose of his good till they be bring to this house and there search by the officer if they see cause this preface I make because the island of Candia be subject to the venetian and the prior of this monastery will in no sort give we free conversation till by letter we have signify our state to the provisor of health reside at the chief city Candia and till they shall send some answer back unto we in the mean time they shut we up in a garden house where we have pleasant walk and store of orange and like fruit and the country people bring we partridge and many good thing to eat and my man have skill to dress they and the monk furnish we with such necessary as we can not otherwise buy we want here no conventence to make the time of our abode seem short but only good bed thus I be force to write this follow letter in the italian tongue and to send a messenger with it to Candia the chief city All'Illustr more and excel more Sigr Sigr Nicolao Donati Proveditore and Candia more sa Sig ria Illustr ma Fynes luome a li tree per volta Candia che in quel'paese i come San' Marco Patron'della nave Francese in (...) villa Isola lascio'soletti Diego ' present a San' maria Ogidietra dove i. frati Eccellenza in in quel' rinchiso in a part Constantinopoli dell' Illust more perch suoa eccell haver forestiero suoa che oltre eccell compassione con disagi mar' and priegho San' maria ogidietre d'ottobre an. 1596 Sig ria Illust ma Humil more re of these letter I receive the follow answer al mag co Sigr Sig fie mag co Sigr come fratello scritte 2d deal present all uso all ill more Sigr ss. ss. eccell mandate and fauorir'et in questaoccasione salute ca Mandiamo stradiotti dove and li seguitar' assicuration' off s. s. senza pratticar' altr te che and trouar' Christiani and and a detti stradiotti come Et. a. v. s. Candia Alli 20. a'ottobre all uso vecchio 1596. diu. s come lli li ' these letter follow translate into English to the most illustrious and most excellent lord the lord Nicholas Donati general Previsar and inquisitor in the kingdom of Candia my most respect most illustrious etc. your excellency may please to understand that I Fynes morison a Englishman with my servant the three of this month set sail from Alexandretta to sail into Candia and that those part be free from all infectious sickness as appear by my seal with the wont seal of saint mark the master of the french ship in which I pass break his covenant with i in that he do not land we in the haven of Candia the chief city of this kingdom but send we in his boat to the wild promontory Calisminiones land and leave we there in a place altogether disinhabit from thence we ask the way of the country people do with much trouble at last come to the monastery saint Maria Ogidietra upon the five and twenty of this present after the new style where the friar till they may know the pleasure of your excellency will in no sort give we liberty to converse but in the mean time have shut we up in a solitary garden house my journey lie further to Constantinople for the affair of the lord ambassador of England there abide wherefore I humble pray that your excellency will vouchsafe to have favourable respect of a poor stranger bear of a nation well affect to that of your excellency and that by your favour licence may be send i free to converse and to take my journey to the city of Candia since my solitary live here all delay and many other thing in this place be irksome unto i thus assure myself that your excellency will have compassion of his servant tire with many misfortune by sea and land I beseech God for the increase of your honour and so humble kiss your hand from San' maria Ogidietra this five and twenty of October after the new style in the year 1596 your excellency humble servant Fynes moryson the letter send i in answer thus follow to the noble gentleman master Fynes moryson dear to we as a brother noble sir dear to we in place of a brother by your letter date the five and twenty of this month after the new style and direct to the illustrious lord general and by his excellency send to our office we have understand your request and desirous to favour you in this occasion with due respect to the public health we have send you two horseman who shall guide you and your servant hither where you shall be courteous receive and shall not want the opportunity of a bark to finish your journey after we have take due order according to our office for the preserve of the public health therefore without converse with any man follow these guide we have send you and come hither with a cheerful heart as to christian and friend but fail not to follow the order which we have give to these guide whereof we doubt not and so tender ourselves to you from Candia the twenty of October after the old style in the year 1596 bring with you the testimony of your health you in place of brother the provisor for health this testimonial above mention I take from the venetian consul who know my disease free from all infection when I part from Alexandretta before know the necessity thereof the foresay two horseman be arrive which with great courtesy be send to conduct i I part from the monastery to go in their company to the city of Candia eight and thirty mile distant be to pass almost the whole breadth of this kingdom in the very middle part thereof the breadth of the island contain five and forty mile the length two hundred and thirty mile and the circuit as Ortelius write five hundred and twenty other say six hundred or seven hundred mile the ancient and modern writer reckon diverse this island be distant from the cape of Otranto in Italy five hundred mile other write five hundred and thirty from Alexandria in Egypt four hundred and fifty mile other write five hundred from the next shore of Africa two hundred and fifty mile from joppa in Palestine six hundred and sixty mile other write six hundred and forty from Tripoli in Syria seven hundred mile from the island Cyprus four hundred mile from Venice 1500 mile and from Constantinople seven hundred and twenty mile we begin our journey in the afternoon and as we ride our guide show we not far out of the high way the monument famous for the love of the king daughter Ariadne to Theseus call the labyrinth of Crete for so Candia be call of old the labyrinth and Saturn the first king thereof beget Radamanthus Minos and sarpedon of Europa the daughter of Agenor as they write also our guide tell we that not far out of the way to the city Candia there be a monument of the cave of Minos which the candian call the sepulchre of jupiter but my former adversity have take from i my wont desire to see antiquity so as we keep the high way and pass that day by a city of the jew lodge that night at a village not in any inn but in the very church upon straw and our own bedding be content with such victual as our guide bring we namely cheese fruit and good wine it be probable that if we have have free conversation we may perhaps have find good lodging in the village yet do we just doubt thereof because we can buy no better meat nor get any provender for our beast the next day in the morning we set forward and come to a pleasant village where we dine in a fair church but can get no meat for our horse except they will have eat pomegranate or like fruit the same day in the afternoon we come to the city of Candia where we stay at the city Candia the gate till we know the pleasure of the provisor for health they can not be ignorant that our sickness be free from all infection yet imagine as after I perceive that we shall be merchant and have some rich lewel they send we to the Lazaretto where in a week space when their spy according to their manner have inquire after our state and find that there be no hope of gain by our imaginary lewel and it then fall out that other merchant be land with good be to be lodge in our chamber at last the general Sigr Nicolao Donato call general for his command in the war and of health by the say office and chief inquisitor for religion which office be spare execute in the state of Venice yet be not the chief commander of the island for ill Sigr Marc' antonio Venerio be then lieutenant to the duke of Venice in this island with limit authority as the duke himself have I say this general provisour for the health send unto we a gentleman of that office Sigr Vicenzo who use we noble and courteous and the Scrivano that be clerk or secretary of that office call Sigr Giovanni Papadapolo with authority to give we free conversation these gentleman according to the custom such as the state of no passenger can be hide from they cause rope to be hang across our chamber and all thing we have yea our very shirt to be several take out and hang thereupon and so perfume they with brimstone to our great annoyance though they well know we have no infectious sickness which do they give we freedom to go into the city and wheresoever we will to the Scrivano I give a sequin desire he to take it in good part as the gift of a poor gentleman and nothing less than a indian merchant as they suspect this house call Lazaretto be build of free stone with cellar for the lay up of good and have pleasant walk both in the yard and garden and the keeper of this house have furnish i with a bed and all necessary and for the seven day past have buy we our meat in the city which he will likewise have dress but that my servant be a cook and for this service he have do I give he also a sequin then we go into the city and lodge with a italian who have often bring we meat and necessary to the Lazaretto and with he myself and my servant have convenient bed and plentiful diet for which I pay six lyre each day but the horseman who conduct we to Candia come often to i and for that service I give to each of they a sequin and by they I understand the price of the market for diet so as all the candian speak italian aswell as their natural greek and I find the rate of our expense to be excessive I determine to hire a chamber and to buy my own meat in the market but it happen that at the same time a english merchant land who be a factor to buy muskedines of Candia whereof and especial of red muskedint there be great plenty in this island and this merchant call Richard darson be well acquaint with the best course of live in Candia have hire a little house and a woman to dress his meat and at my entreaty he be content to give we a chamber in his house and to hire we two bed that so we may diet together where he use we very courteous and our diet be as plentiful as before at a far low rate divide our expense into three part whereof he pay one and myself two for my own and my servant diet there be at that time great dearth of corn so as white bread be hardly to be get though the italian make their meal for the most part of bread use to have it very white and good here we buy a bocale of rich wine contain two english quart and a half for a lyre of Venice a pigeon for 7 soldi a partridge for a lyre or 16 soldi a pound of veal for 7 of mutton for some 5 and we have plenty of fruit for a small price the beef in Italy use to be lean and be seldom eat and such beef they have here for by the law call Foscherini it be command that no man shall kill a beef till it be unfit to draw in the plough and to do like service here I pay four lyre for a pair of shoe the rest of my expense I omit for brenitie sake those suffice to give a passenger some guess at what rate he may live only I will add that the work of porter and labour man as well in Italy as here be have for small wage because there be great number of poor people and they abhor from beg so as one content a porter for bring your victual from the market when I go to jerusalem and sail by the island of Candia I make some mention thereof and I have now former set down the length breadth and circuit and the distance thereof from other province and have show that Candia be subject to the venetian and have also name the chief governor thereof for that time I will now brief add that this island be defend by a venetian garrison against the great Turk to who all the adjacent country be subject that it have great plenty of red muskedines where with England for the most part be serve that it have great plenty of all kind of corn of all manner of pulse of oil of all kind of flesh of cane of sugar of honey of cedar tree of all colour die of cypress tree where of many sweet smell chest be make and carry into foreign part and of all necessary for human life neither be any venomous beast find in this island but it have store of medicinable herb especial upon the famous mountain ida. the city of this island be of old one hundred and in the time of Pliny forty but at this day there be only three namely Canea at the west end of the island near which lie the fort Sonda with a haven capable of a thousand galley the second call Retbino by the italian seat on the southside of the island upon which side the italian add a four city call Settia and the three call Candia the metropolitan the city Candia city of the island which be fair and large build of stone with a low roof after the manner of Italy and the street thereof be fair and large it be strong fortify as need require by the venetian against the turk and to that purpose have a strong castle from this city a large and pleasant plain lead to the foresay cave of mine which the candian call the sepulchre of jupiter near which be the most famous mountain Ida which they hold to be seat in the midst of the island be high than any of the other mountain thereof and it abound with cypress tree final I remember that when I lodge in the monastery San' maria Ogidietra the Caloiri or monk who for
