the most part be unlearned and till the ground and labour like layman assure i that each measure of corn sow in their field the year past have yield ninety five measure chap. 4 of my journey from Candia partly by land partly by sea by the sea shore and by the land of the aegean sea Pontus and propontis to the city of Constantinople and of my journey thence by sea to Venice and by land to augsburg nurnberg and stand in Germany and of my passage over sea into England and of my journey through many several share of England Scotland and Ireland upon Monday the twenty of December after the old style December 20. at three of the clock in the afternoon we go aboard a little greek bark load with muskedines and with ton of lemon juice which the turk drink like nectar and with onion and ready to sail for Constantinople where I pay for my passage five zechine and as much for my servant the night follow be very bright with moonshine yet we stay all the night in the haven compass with wall either because the governor of the castle will not let the bark go forth till the master have satisfy he or because the master pretend that cause of stay have some business to dispatch the next morning early be the one and twenty of December we set sail and the same day we sail close by the island more than one hundred mile distant from Candia they report that this island and another of the same name both of little circuit be in our age cast up in the midst of the sea with a eruption of flame and of brimstone and that they be not inhabit but be common call the devil land because many ship cast anchor there and fasten their cable upon land have have their cable loose by spirit in the night and so suffer shipwreck or hardly escape the same the night follow we sail in the midst of many land which make that channel very dangerous and for my part I be more afraid of the danger because our candian merchant grow acquaint with a harlot in the ship be not ashamed to have the use of her body in the sight of the mariner that watch and much blame he for the same upon Wednesday the two and twenty of December we sail by the island paros celebrate by poet for the fine marble paros Naxos grow there and so we come to the island Naxos two hundred mile distant from Candia Naxos and the adjacent land have their own duke of old but now be subject to the Turk as the other land be for the most part and our mariner dwell in this island and land to see their wife we also land with they where I do see upon a hill like a peninsul near this chief village two marble image erect to Theseus and Ariadne here I observe that when any stranger or inhabitant land the beggar flock to the door of the house or inn where they eat and have former observe in the greek church at Venice that when they give their alm to beggar they not only suffer they to touch their garment with their lousy rag but also take they familiar by the hand I know not whether I shall attribute this fashion to their charitable affection in time of their bondage or to their seldom feast and the multitude of beggar in the evening we loose from Naxos and sail over a channel no less dangerous than the former for the multitude of land upon the three and twenty of December we pass close by the shore of the island Zio call Chios of old it be inhabit Zio by greek as the other land be and be famous for the pleasantness and fertility of the situation and soil it yield great store of Mastic and the country people keep flock of rain partridge as of hen other where they brag that Homer lie bury upon the mountain Helias and this island have saint George for their protect saint and bear his cross in their flag as England do here we may distinct see the shore of Asia in that part where of old the seven church stand not seven church far distant to which saint John write his revelation and the island Pathmos be not far distant where saint John live in exile towards the evening we cast anchor Pathmos near the island Metelene which be seat as Zio in the egean sea and be no less pleasant Metelene and fertile of old it be call Lesbos than issa and after Pelasgia and therein be bear Pythagoras the poet Alceus Antimenides Theophrastus Arion and and the famous woman poet Sappho Zio be distant one hundred and forty mile from Naxos and Meteline ninety mile from Zio the four and twenty of December be Christmas even after the old style use among the greek and in all Turkey early in the morning we weigh anchor and with a fair but gentle wind sail close by the shore where the city of Troy stand Troy of old seat in a plain and upon pleasant hill near the sea and at this day the ruin of Ilium the castle of Priamus be see upon a hill and the ruin of the wall in the plain yet show the circuit of the city the poet say true corn grow now where Troy once stand yet the plough field have very many ruin of building on the north side of these trojan ruin a neck of land lie towards the sea where they say the greek encamp and leave their fatal trojan horse right over against this land lie the island Tenedos scarce ten mile distant in the haven whereof we cast anchor for a Tenedos hour under a little castle and this Tenedos be sixty mile distant from Metelene from hence sail some eighteen mile we pass by two neck of land one of Greece on the westside the other of Asia the less now call Anatolia on the east-side and after twelve mile sail we enter the straight of Hellespont now call the two castle seste and abide the description whereof I will defer till my return this way the greek mariner have a custom here to demand a gift of all merchant and passenger in their ship for joy of their happy voyage and they say which I believe not that if any refuse they tie a rope to his foot and draw he up to the top of the mast till he yield to this custom but howsoever we all obey this ridiculous custom not to offend they who have use we well this channel run from the black sea call euxinus into propontis and so by Constantinople to these say two castle and from hence into the aegean sea from the north towards the south be always contrary to those that sail from the mediterranean sea to Constantinople especial after they enter this straight of the two castle and near Constantinople it run with such force towards the south as they that sail to the city whereof we have experience with the best wind yet sail very slow this violence of the channel be attribute to great river violent fall into the black Sea the foresay Christmas even we land at gallipolis a greek city seat in Thrace gallipolis have the name as it seem from the french and eight and twenty mile distant from the two castle on Saturday the five and twenty of December be Christmas day after the old style we set sail but the wind drive we back to the haven of gallipolis where be detain some few day though I stay in the ship for fear of some fraud from the turk yet once I go on land with our mariner the city lie in length upon the shore of propontis from the south to the north and it have without the wall towards the west great number of windmill the building be of slint or little unpolished stone one or two story high and the roof be low and tile not plain and plaster to walk upon as they be in Syrea and Cyprus and this roof be so low as it have no window so as the building of these part be very like those of Italy the haven be on the east side and upon the opposite shore of Asia towards the east be the ruin not far distant of nice a city of Bithynia famous for the holy council hold there of old upon Saturday the first of january we sail sixty mile in this straight of propontis to the island not without fear of turkish pirate the haven of camera be near we where the great Turk galley lie by the way they show i a castle towards the east upon the shore of Asia the less which they say stand upon the confine of the trojan dominion and thereof have the name to this day the island be so call as I think of themarble wherewith it abound the second of january we set sail from and be by contrary wind drive back as I think or little advance we come to the island Aloni some ten mile Aloni distant from and so call of the form of a yard in which ox use to grind corn or beat it small after the begin of the new year which the greek as most of Europe begin the first of januarie the first Wednesday be the 1. 1597. four of that month the grecian mariner have a custom retain from old time to baptize the sea as they term it which do they think the flood and wind to grow more calm then former the island Aloni have a port on all side compass with land and that very large and safe where while we pass some stormy day we hear of many bark and galley cast away while I walk here upon the shore a wild-headed Turk take my hat from my head be of the fashion of Europe not use there and have turn it and long behold it he say to use his rude word lend i this vessel to ease my belly therein and so girn fling it'on the dyrtie ground which I with patience take up these and like wrong of speech even threaten of blow I sometime endure in Turkey but never have the disaster to have any blow give i by any of they which many good christian notwithstanding have suffer and daily suffer and myself if they have fall to my share must have suffer with patience except I will by resistance have incur shameful and cruel death on Thursday the thirteen of january at last we set sail with a fair wind and after twenty mile sail we pass by the city Palormo seat upon the shore of Asia the less and famous for the white wine it yield the best that ever Palormo I taste and have sail ten mile further we sail by the city heraclea seat on heraclea the shore of Greece whereof in my return this way I shall have cause to speak more at large towards evening we think we be come to one of the corner of Constantinople call the seven tower yet by reason of the foresay swift channel run from the black sea full against we with a most fair wind we can not land in the haven of Constantinople till midnight have that day sail one hundred and twenty mile in all from the say isle Aloni this voyage be more tedious to we in that howsoever land we have sometime good diet yet while we be at sea we have no good victual in the ship for the greek mariner feed of onion garlic and dry fish one kind whereof they call Palamedes and the italian call Palamite and in stead of a banquet they will give you a head of garlic roast in the ash and pleasant call it a pigeon with this and biscuit they content themselves and these we be force to eat have omit to provide any dry or salt meat at Candia because we hope to find those in our bark and know that it be in vain to provide any fresh meat because they will not suffer a fire to be make in so small a bark wherewith we may dress they but after we have eat biscuit and dry fish we have a unknown comfort or help to digest they for in our private cabin we have the head of a tun of Muskedine lie under our head when we sleep in stead of a bolster and our ship be bind on the upper part of the side with bundle of reed to beat off the force of the wave we take one of the long reed find mean to pierce the vessel and get good wine to our ill fare and drink so merry that before we come to our journey end our former reed become too short so as we be fain to piece it with another have cast anchor as I say in the port of Constantinople behold as soon as day Constantinople begin to break many company of turk rush into our bark who like so many starve fly fall to suck the sweet wine each rascal among they beat with cudgel and rope the best of our mariner if he dare but repine against it till within short space the candian merchant have advertise the venetian ambassador of their arrival he send a lanizare to protect the bark and the good and assoon as he come it seem to i no less strange that this one man shall beat all those turk and drive they out of the bark like so many dog the common turk dare no more resist a soldier or especial a janizare than christian dare resist they and the sergeant of the magistrate have take some of our greek mariner though subject to the state of Venice to work for their Ottoman in gather stone and like base employment this janisary cause they present to be release and to be send again into their bark such be the tyranny of the turk against all christian aswell their subject as other so as no man sail into these part but under the banner of England France or Venice who be in league with the great Turk have their ambassador in this city and their consul in other haven to protect those that come under their banner in this sort send they a janizare to keep they from wrong so soon as they be advertise of their arrival myself lodge in the house of master Edward barton the english ambassador who give i a janizare to guide and protect i while I go to view the city round about the whole circuit whereof I go on foot and by boat in four hour space the form of the city be triangular and contain nine mile by sea towards the north and east and five mile by land towards the west I profess myself to have small skill in the art of geography yet will I adventure though rude to set down the form and situation of this city so plain as I doubt not but the reader may easy understand it howsoever in the same as in other city former describe I acknowledge that I use not the rule of the scale in the distance of place nor other exquisite rule of that art have no other end but to make the reader more easy understand my description the description of the city of Constantinople and the adjacent territory and sea the great line or wall show the form of the city and the single small line describe the teritory adjoin a in this tower they hang out a light of pitch and like burn matter to direct the sailor by night come to the city or sail along the coast out of the sea euxinus which they say be call the black sea of many shipwreck therein happen and this tower be sixteen mile distant from the city b here be a marble pillar erect upon a rock compass with the sea which they call the pillar of Pompey and therein many passenger for their memory use to engrave their name and here be innumerable flock of sea foul and of many kind wherewith he that be skilful to shoot in his piece may abundant furnish himself c here be the euxine or black Sea d e here lie two strong castle one in Europe the other in Asia some eight mile distant from the city build to defend the haven from the assault of the enemy by sea on that side and the garrison there keep search the ship come from the city that no slave or prohibit good be carry therein neither can any ship pass unsearched except they will hazard to be sink final the great Turk send his chief prisoner to be keep in these strong castle f here great ship use to cast anchor at their first arrival till they be unload and here again they ride at anchor to expect wind when they be load and ready to depart g all along this bank and the opposite side for a large circuit the great ship use to lie when they be unload and they lie most safe and close by the shore fastane by cable on land H here lie the old city build by the Genoesi of Italy call Gallata by the turk and Perah by the greek of the situation beyond the channel it be now account a suburb of Constantinople and be seat upon a most pleasant hill wherein for the most part live christian aswell subject as other and the ambassador of England France and Venice only the emperor ambassador must lie within the city more like a pledge of peace than a free ambassador and very few turk live here mingle with the christian the situation of Gallata as I say be most pleasant former the ambassador of England be wont to dwell upon the seashore in the plain and their palace be not far distant from this note k but master Edward barton the english ambassador at this time dwell upon the top of the hill in a fair house within a large field and pleasant garden compass with a wall and all Gallata be full of very pleasant garden and compass with pleasant field whereof some towards the land further from the sea be use for the burial of turk ay here a little creek of the sea be compass with wall and building within which the galley of the great Turk lie in safety and there be fit place to build galley and storehouse for all thing thereunto belong k here be the chief passage over the water call Tapano where a man may pass for two asper all along this sea bank lie very many great gun as upon the tower wharfe at London and here the fisher land and sell their fish l here the megarense of old build chalcedon a city of Bethinia famous for a council hold there by the ruin of which city Constantinople increase at this day there be only a village or rather some scatter house and it be common call Scuteri or Scudretta m here the great Turk mother then live have her private garden n hither the heir of the empire be send as it be into banishment under pretence to govern the province Bursia assoon as he be circumcise and so be make a Musulman that be a circumcise Turk first begin to draw the eye of the army and janizares towards he o here be the palace or court of the great Turk call by the italian seraglio and vulgar and it be of old the monastery of saint Sophia Mahomet the second first compass it with wall and the building together with the large and pleasant garden be some three or four mile in circuit I enter the outward court thereof by a stately gate keep by many janizares call Capigi of that office the court yard be large all compass with building of free stone two story high with a low and almost plain roof tile and without window after the manner of the build of Italy and round about the inside it be cast out with arch like the build of cloister under which they walk dry in the great rain and in this court be a large pulpit or open room where the great Turk vi to show himself to the janizares to satisfy they when they make any mutiny p here be a banquet house vulgar call chuske the prospect whereof be more pleasant than can be express behold four sea at once and the land on all side beyond they q here be the church of saint Sophia opposite to the court gate of old build by the christian after the form of Salomon temple and endow with the annual rent of three hundred thousand zechine now make a Mosche or mahometan church and howsoever the turk can endure that unwashed christian so call by they because they use not bath so continual as they do shall enter their Mosches or pass over their sepulcher yet myself enter this church with the janizare my guide trust to his power to defend i yet he will i first to put of my shoe and according to the turk custom to leave they in the porch where they be late till we return the church be of a round form and build of brick and support with fair pillar and pave with marble over which the turk lay mat to kneel and prostrate themselves more commodious upon they the roof be beautify with picture of that rich paint which the italian call Mosaica shine like enamel work which now by antiquity be much decay and in some part deface round about the church hang many lamp which they use to bury in the time of their lent call Beyram and every week upon Thursday in the evening and Friday all day which they keep holy after their fashion for their sabbath day round about the upper part of the church be large and most fair gallery and here I do see two nut of marble of huge bigness and great beauty moreover I do see the great Turk when he enter this church and howsoever it lie close to the gate of his palace yet he come ride upon a horse rich trap with many troop of his chief horseman stand in rank within the court of his palace and from the court gate to the church door between which troop on both side he pass as between wall of brass with great pomp and when a chaus or pensioner be on horseback do see i close by the emperor side he rush upon i to strike i with his mace say what do this christian dog so near the person of our great lord but the janizare who our ambassador have give i for a guide and protector repel he from do i any wrong and many janizares according to their manner come to help he the chaus be glad to let i alone and they bid i be bold to stand still though I be the second or three person from the emperor near this church be the stately sepulchre of Selimus the second and another sepulchre no less stately and new build for Amurath late decease where he lie with those male child round about he who according to the manner be strangle by his successor after he be dead not far thence be the market place have some one hundred marble pillar about it and adorn with a pyramid or pinnacle erect upon four globe and with a pleasant fountain of water together with other ornament leave as it seem by Christian emperor r the wonderful Mosche and sepulchre of Solyman number among the miracle of the World saint two house for the same use as the exchange of London where the merchant meet namely for the sell of fine ware but no way to be compare to the same for the build they be call the great and the less Bezestan and use to be open only certain day of the week and for some six hour at which time small and more precious ware be there to be sell as jewel semiter or sword set with jewel but common counterfeit piece of velvet satin and damask and the like and the market place be not far distant where captive of both sex be weekly sell and the buyer if they will may take they into a house and there see they naked and handle they as we handle beast to know their fatness and strength T here be a fort that be fortify with seven tower call by the turk jadicule and by christian the seven tower where a garrison of soldier be keep because the emperor treasure be there lay up and chief prisoner use to be keep there the treasure be vulgar say to be lay up there but the great Turk seldom go thither and since it be true that where the treasure be there be the mind I think it probable which I have hear of experience man that most of the treasure lie in the seraglio where the great Turk hold his courr five here be the ruin of a palace upon the very wall of the city call the palace of Constantine wherein I do see a elephant call philo by the turk and another beast new bring out of Africa the mother of monster which beast be altogether unknown in our part and be call Surnapa by the people of Asia Astanapa by other and Giraffa by the italian the picture whereof I remember to have see in the map of mercator and because the beast be very rare I will describe his form as well as I can his hair be red colour with many black and white spot I can scarce reach with the point of my finger to the hinder part of his back which grow high and high towards his foreshoulder and his neck be thin and some three else long so as he easy turn his head in a moment to any part or corner of the room wherein he stand put it over the beam thereof be build like a barn and high for the turkish building not unlike the build of Italy both which I have former describe by reason whereof he many time put his nose in my neck when I think myself further distant from he which familiarity of his I like not and howsoever the keeper assure i he will not hurt i yet I avoid these his familiar kiss as much as I can his body be slender not great but much high than the body of a stag or hart and his head and face be like to that of a stag but the head be less and the face more beautiful he have two horn but short and scarce half a foot long and in the forehead he have two bunch of flesh his ear and foot like a ox and his leg like a stag the janizare my guide do in my name and for i give twenty asper to the keeper of this beast w 10 here be two castle or fort divide by the Hollespont one seat in (@) the other in Asia whereof I make mention in my sail to Constantinople and of which I shall speak more large at my go from this city Constantinople build six hundred sixty three year before Christ be bear by (@) a lacedaemonian be first call till Constantine the great in the year of the lord 315 do rebuild it after it have be destroy by the emperor (@) and call it by his name the turk under the second first take this city in the year 1453 from the christian with destruction of great multitude of they and at this day it be call of the great circuit stimbal by the grecian and by the turk it be seat in Thrace also call Romania and be build in form of a triangle whereof two side towards the north and east lie upon two sea and the three side towards the west lie upon the continent of Greece for many cause this city be famous and in two respect may be just prefer to any other in the world know to we namely for the pleasantness of the situation and the largeness and safety of the haven the form thereof former set down do in part show the pleasantness of the situation but the fruitfulness of the field the sweetness and beauty of the flower and the variety and goodness of the fruit can sufficient be praise the fish in the winter season fly from the cold of the euxine or black sea falldowne in great number into the sea propontis pass close by the wall of the city and again in summer time not endure the heat of the mediterianean sea return back in like number the same way this city have a thousand pleasant creek of the sea within sight to conclude the country be no less pleasant than the inhabitant be wicked the haven will receive a huge number of ship and upon both the bank of the city and Gallata ship of 300 (@) or great once unload may so lie with their cable fasten on the land as they (@) from the ship to land without (@) (@) and for the excellency of the port the city itself be call the port by the turk and Ovid call it the port of two sea for the two channel of propontis and the euxine Sea of old the city have eleven gate call aurea Pargea romana Caligaria Harmagona Phara Theodosia and at this day the slender wall of brick towards the sea have thirteen gate not worth the name the wall towards the land be of brick and be say to have be much ruin in the year 1509 by a earthquake yet still on that side be three wall which be broad enough for a cart to pass of which the outmost towards land be little high than the foundation of the second nor that much high than the foundation of the three the field on that side be plain yet in like sort rise high and high as they be near to the wall of the city save that near the foresay palace of Constantine some hill lie without the wall this city as Rome be say to contain seven hill or mount within the wall whereof some to i seem imaginary but I will reckon they as they do and first begin with the hill upon which stand the ruin of Constantine palace the second have the stately Mosche or turkish church build upon the palace which of old belong to the grecian patriarch upon the three stand the stately Mosche and most rich build sepulchre of Mahomet the second with a hospital build by the same emperor where all turkish pilgrim have their lodging and diet free for three day and it have one hundred and fifty chamber build for the poor of the city and the yearly rent thereof be value at two hundred thousand zechine yea the court or seraglio of the great Turk pay each day a hundred asper to this hospital the sepulchre of Selimus take up the four hill and the sepulchre of Bajazet the five hill between the five and the six hill be the old palace of the great Turk which the Italiatis call seraglio vecehio where the concubine of the decease emperor and the present emperor sister and a great number of his concubinet for the fair and dear to he be take to live in his court be keep by eunuch within this old seraglio which be of great circuit contain many house and garden compass within one wall upon the six hill stand the foresay wonderful Mosche and sepulchre of Solyman note with the letter r last the seven hill contain the chief palace of the great Turk and the church saint Sophia now make a Mosche note with the letter o. q. the top of the sepulcher and Mosches be of a round form and cover with brass and the spacious garden of cypress and fir tree make show of more beauty and magnificence to the beholder from any high place or without the wall then in deed the city have the sepulcher be no doubt very stately build have upon the top one two or more round globe cover with lead or brass on the inside they seem like lightsome chapel with many window and they be build in a round form the dead emperor be lay in the midst or centre of the sepulchre in a chest or coffin raise some three foot from the ground have the tulbent which he wear upon his head in his life time lay upon his tomb be see forth with the jewel he most esteem which Tulbent be make of some twenty or more yard of pure and fine white linen fold in many fold in the form of a half globe next the emperor lie the sultana or empress in her coffin so they call his concubine mother of his heir and successor provide always that she have have a letter of dowry by which she be make his wife for otherwise she be not bury with he and round about the emperor and empress in coffin low than they lie the body of his male child which according to their manner be strangle by his successor assoon as he be dead and upon their coffin likewise their tulbent be lay several these child be lay in little coffin of Cypresle and this middle part wherein the dead body lie be compass with a grace so as between the body and the window there be a gallery round about which be spread with Turkey carpet and upon they the priest that keep the sepulchre do lie by night and sit cross legged by day neither be the room at any time without some of these keeper so as the emperor be attend even after death the building of the city have no magnificence be partly of a matter like brick but white and as it seem unhardened by fire partly of timber and clay except some few palace which be of free stone but nothing so stately build as may be expect from the pride and riches of the great turk chief servant and these house as those of the adjacent territory of Europe be build only 2 story high with a low roof without any window after the manner of Italy whereas the house of Asia have a plain and plaster roof to walk upon especial in Asia the great the street of this city be narrow and shadow with pentise of wood and upon both side the way be raise some foot high but of little breadth and pave for man and woman to pass the midst of the street be leave low and unpaved and no broad then for the passage of ass or beast load in many place of the street lie carcase yea sometime the body of dead man even till they be putrify and I think this uncleanliness of the turk who otherwise place religion in wash their body and keep their apparel especial their tulbent pure and clean be the chief cause that this city though most pleasant seat yet above all the city of the world be continual more or less infect with the plague they say that job famous for his piety and patience be bury in this city but I do not see his monument and think it probable that the same and all like christian monument be deface by the turk when they take the city the worthy english ambassador master Edward barton most courteous entertain i with lodging and diet so long as I stay in this city so as for they I spend not one Asper but I pass over the due praise which I owe to the memory of this worthy gentleman be hereafter to speak more of he I will only add that I attend he once to the great Turk court and when I have nothing satisfy my curiosity in view the city by occasion casual happen that he command a janizare to guide i round about the same till I have take full view thereof and with this guide the first day I view the foresay monument within the wall and the second day compass the city without the wall begin at the passage over the water call Tapano and note with the letter k and so pass by water in a boat vulgar call pyrame and hire for forty asper to the castle of the seven tower note with the letter T then pass by land to the palace of Constantine note with the letter v. and by the way as we pass by land a old woman meet we and take i for a captain to be sell demand my price of the janizare who for mirth entertain her offer to buy i and another gentleman servant to the ambassador who he have send to bear i company but because I be very slender and lean after my long sickness he can not induce she to give more than one hundred asper for i though she offer four hundred asper for the other gentleman in my company as the janizare tell i in the italian tongue when he have entertain this discourse with she to pass away the time in our long walk from the foresay palace of Constantine we hire a boat for eight asper and so by water return to the passage Tapano from whence we set forth have go by land and water the full circuit of the city be nine mile by water and five by land thence we return to the ambassador house where I give the janizare my guide fifty asper for his pain a venetian ship call the great lion be now ready to set sail from Constantinople to Venice which commodity of my speedy return I think good to take and have agree with the patron or master of the ship and be promise by the chief of the mariner who I have bind unto i with some curtisy that they will without fail call i before they be to set sail I pass the time in the sweet conversation of this worthy ambassador more secure than I shall have do till one evening I hear a great piece of ordinance discharge and thereupon suspect as indeed it fall out that this ship ready to set sail give this warn piece to call aboard the mariner and passenger and so I make all the haste I can to the water side but when I come thither see that my labour be in vain the ship be under sail and go out of the haven myself my servant and a gentleman the ambassador servant and send by he into England with letter to the queen from the great Turk be thus leave behind present take one of the ambassador janizares for our guide and upon the last day of February in the end of the year 1596 according to the english computation or in the begin of the year 1597 according to the computation of most kingdom begin the year ann. 1597. the first of january hire a boat call pyrame for one thousand asper to gallipoli in hope to overtake the great ship sail slow before it can pass the straight of Hellespont and the same evening in which the great ship set sail we in our little boat sail by the shore of Thrace forty mile to a town of Thrace not without great fear and far great danger of be cast away for when we find the little boat unfit to pass the great wave of the sea though much calm than other sea and therefore will our mariner to sail close by the shore he tell we that there be great danger from thief upon the shore then from the wave of the sea and so easy persuade we to imitate the prophet David commit ourselves rather to the hand of God by sail in the deep then into the hand of man by coast the shore when we have pass the dark night without sleep in this obscure harbour the next morning early be Tuesday and the first of march we sail twenty mile to Erylis seat upon the same shore of Thracia not without extreme Erylis danger of be cast away which we often and just fear and our janizare no less who either for fear or repentance of his sin shed abundance of tear Erylis be of old call heraclea famous know by have be the seat of the greek patriarch and many christian emperor here we leave the boat which we have hire at Constantinople have find it unfit for this passage but howsoever we have hire it to gallipoli yet the mariner will remit nothing of the covenant price from hence to gallipoli we hire for eight hundred asper a great bark call Cayke the second of march notwithstanding the rage of the wind and the wave we set sail and land at the island after fifty mile sail in which island I have former be and to the mention thereof former make let i now add that it be inhabit only with greek and these greek fear lest our janizare after their manner will pay they nothing for our necessary and he be a young unexperienced man and so not carry himself with such authority as other janizares do we can not get lodging nor diet in any house till at last ourselves promise to pay honest for all we take we be receive into a house where as we be wont we sleep upon our own bedding they have no convenient bed and we pay for two egg one asper for a five and twenty asper for our fire five and twenty asper and for the use of the house five and twenty asper as likewise in other place where we land we pay common sixteen asper or thereabouts each night for our lodging and the use of the house the three of march we set sail after midnight and have sail sixty gallipolis mile come before break of day to gallipolis and the same day hire a boat with two oar for two hundred asper we pass eight and twenty mile and find the great ship of Venice at anchor but not dare to go aboard in the night we sleep in our little boat fasten to the shore with no little inconvenience because it rain all night the four of march we give the janizare our guide three hundred twenty eight asper for his pain and to bear his charge back which be a small sum for so great a journey yet after some repine he be satisfy therewith because he serve the english ambassador then we go aboard the venetian ship call the great lion and when I remember that the ship wherein I sail from Venice to jerusalem be call the little lion I be stir up to give praise and humble thanks to the great lion of the tribe of juda who through so many danger preserve i in this voyage this venetian ship be force here to expect the pleasure of the turkish searcher and customer namely at the two castle upon the entry of the straight two castle of Hellespont whereof I make mention in my sail from Candia to Constantinople and in the description of that city have note they with the letter w and 10 for the ship that come from Constantinople use to be detain here some three day to the end that in case they carry away private man slave the master may have time to follow after they and in like case if they carry away any prisoner or offender the public magistrate may have mean to bring they back beside these searcher and customer look that they carry no prohibit ware neither can the ship nor any passenger be suffer to pass these castle except they bring the passport of the great Turk which the chief or bassa use to grant unto they thus when no ship without the knowledge of the chief can either pass these castle lead to the mediterranean sea or the two castle above lead into the euxine sea note with d e sure these four castle be the great strength of Constantinople by Sea I say that these castle where we find the venetian ship be in the description of Constantinople note with the letter w and 10 and they be now common call the castle of gallipolis but of old that note with the letter w be call Sestos be a city in Thrace in which the most fair Hero be bear and dwell and the other note with the letter 10 be call Abydos be a city of Asia the less in which Leander dwell famous for his love to Hero and these castle be divide by the Hellespont some two mile broad at least so narrow as Leander be say often to have swim over it to his beloved Hero the castle of Sestos more special be seat in a most fertile soil for Nairo the next adjoin town yield excellent wine and all necessary to sustain life plentiful howsoever the ship ought and use to be stay here for three day yet a very fair wind blow and all duty be perform the patron of the ship by a large gift to the officer sometime obtain leave to depart soon they say that each passenger by Pole pay here one sequin for tribute but perhaps this belong only to merchant for myself my servant and the english gentleman in my company have give between we one sequin to the substitute of the venetian bailiff so their ambassador be call we be dismiss upon their motion yet we moreover give forty asper to a janizare and fifty asper to a Chiauslar for the fee of their office it be unwholesome to sleep above the hatch of the ship at this time of the year though in summer time I make choice to sleep so when I sail from Venice to jerusalem we three namely myself the english gentleman and my servant give for each of we three zechine to the pilot to be partner with he in his cabin which by his office he have proper to himself in the castle of the ship and to the patron or master of the ship for our diet we pay each of we after the rate of five zechine and a half by the month as well at sea as in harbour and for our passage we joint pay ten ducat of Venice so as I still pay two part of three in all expense beside that we bring with we some hundred of egg and a vessel of excellent wine of Palormo which our ambassador at Constantinople give we upon Monday the seven of march after the old style use in Turkey by all christian and other in the afternoon we set sail and pass the straight of Hellespont and the same night sail by the foresay island of Tenedos this sea be call Pontus of the adjacent province of Asia the less name Pontus which province contain Tenedos Colchis famous by the old argonauticall expedition Capidocia and Armenia the eight of march early in the morning we do see the island Lemnos famous for a kind Lemnos of earth there dig and in latin call upon our right hand and the Metelene Zio Smirna island Metelene and Chios now call Zio and the city Smyrna upon the continent of Asia the less upon our left hand to omit Ephesus not far distant upon the same continent and be now enter into the aegean sea now call Archipelagus of fifty land stand like arch and not far distant one from the other which be call cyclades or sporades the nine of march have now sail eighty mile and be to sail by the island saint George of Skyra the wind be so contrary as we be force to strike sail and lie at hull that be toss to a fro by the wave the Andros same day we set sail and leave the island Andros one of the cyclades and the island Tyno subject to the venetian on our left hand or towards the east and the island Negropont lie close to the continent of Attica and right over against the ruin of famous Athens Athens on our right hand or towards the west the ten and eleven of march we sail 100 mile in the same sea full of land and sail by the island Gia and Makarone but towards night contrary wind rise high and we fear to be cast upon some shore of many adjacent land again we strike sail and lay at hull toss to and fro but make small or no progress the twelve of march early in the morning we set sail and sail by the island Milo of old call Miletum where saint Paul land act 20. 15 and a near island sdile of old call Delos and most famous for the oracle of Apollo and the promontory of Morea of old Delos call contain many province of Greece which promontory be call Capo the thirteen of march have sail one hundred and ninety mile we pass by the island Cerigo not subject to the turk as most of the land be but to the venetian who in a castle on the south side keep a garrison of soldier it be one of the cyclades seat at the entrance of the Archipelagus towards the south scarce five mile distant from Morea the foresay continent of Greece and some one hundred and fifty mile from Candia the chief city of the island Candia and be of old call Scotera also Porphoris of that precious kind of marble there dig and also Cytherea of which as her chief Cytherea seat Venus be often so call and to this day there be see the ruin of a temple dedicate to Venus and of a palace belong to Menelaus the husband of Helena from the thirteen to the seventeen of march the wind be so contrary or scant as we only sail one hundred and twenty mile and take harbour in the island zante subject to the venetian whereof I make mention in my voyage from Venice to jerusalem here some english merchant continual reside and the haven zante be commodious and most ship that trade in these sea use to put into this harbour the good that be diverse transport thence be vulgar but false esteem the native commodity of the island it have scarce sixty mile in circuit and the mountain round about upon the seaside enclose a pleasant and fruitful plain the haven be like a half moon increase and the chief town call zante lie in a little plain upon the innermost part thereof in length the building of the house be two story high with a tile but low roof without any window according to the build of Italy but be poor and base for the matter so as the only beauty of the town lie in the castle build at the east end upon a high hill be of a large circuit and contain many house and church within the wall thereof in which castle the governor call and the other venetian inferior magistrate dwell and give law to the people of that island the turkish pirate of saint Mauro in Morea have late set upon and take a huge venetian ship do lade seventeen of their little bark with the most precious good thereof namely clothes of gold damask and grogeran to the value of a thousand thousand zechine as the report go and set the ship on fire take away the mariner for slave and the very time of my be in this island seven turkish galley lay upon this coast and rob all the venetian ship fall into their hand so as howsoever they have peace then with the turkish Ottoman yet their ship dare not stir out of this haven whereupon they have now occasion to send out ship for corn the magistrate force the master of a english ship then harbour there to waste these ship though much against his will when there be some 20 venetian ship in the same haven whereof diverse be of 400 or 500 tun also at this time it happen that a spanish ship of Catolonia be drive into this harbour and the magistrate call our merchant before he will have force they to give caution that the english ship then lie there shall not assail the same though between England and Spain war have now long time be proclaim but they contest against this course as injurious to they yet can not be dismiss till they give their word that our ship shall not assail the same by day or night so long as it lay in this haven and this spanish ship for long time not dare to go forth at last upon occasion of venetian galley land there be waft by they and so escape upon the rob and burn of the foresay venetian ship by turkish pirate the venetian Baliffe so they call their ambassador lie then at Constantinople have obtain the great Turk mandate that these pirate galley be burn they shall present be send in bond to his court and this mandate be bring by a Chiauss or pencioner who come in the same ship with we whereupon the pirate be more enrage against the venetian do at this time take and spoil another venetian ship of some four hundred and fifty ton call the Sylvester and not content with the most rich booty do cast into the sea many mariner yield to mercy and can hardly be persuade by the intercession of turk passenger in the same ship to spare the life of some twenty chief mariner yet alive and to forbear the burn of the ship the italian of best judgement do not expect that the great Turk will due punish these outrage but rather be of opinion that himself and his chief will draw the great part of the prey to their own hand and that the turkish governor entice by like participation will endeavour to free these pirate and do their best to nourish they yea that this very Chiauss now send with a mandate to suppress they will be induce by bribe to return to Constantinople with relation that the pirate can not be find so they do as no doubt they will for a time hide themselves and in conclusion that the venetian after have spend much money in obtain new mandate for their apprehension shall find no other remedy but to repel force by force upon Wednesday the thirty of march after the old style in the begin of march 30. ann. 1597. the year 1597 we set sail but by contrariety of wind be again drive back into the haven of zante upon Friday the first of April after dinner again we set sail and the second of April sail by the island Cefalonia whereof I speak in our voyage Cefalonia corfu from Venice to jerusalem we cast anchor in the outward haven of the island corfu because the master of our ship be sick and this his sickness increase we set sail to return back to the chief city of that island the haven whereof we enter the six of April from zante to be seventy mile and from thence to corfu be 120 mile this island corfu inhabit by greek be very fertile yield plenty of fruit corn wine and currand and this haven be fortify with two fort cut out off a rock namely the old and the new fort which be more than a mile in circuit both be very strong and hold unexpugnable so as this island be worthy repute one of the chief key of Christendom the master of our ship have recover his health we set sail upon Tuesday the twelve of April and return again to the foresay outward haven of corfu island where a old woman a passenger die and her kinsman carry her body to be bury on Land here again we be force to stay the wind be contrary atlast upon the nineteen of April towards evening with a fair wind we joyful set sail and the twenty day we sail over the straight sea between Greece and the province of apulia in Italy upon Friday the two and twenty of April towards the evening have sail some two hundred mile from the say straight we be carry by the shore of the city Raguza with a fair gale of wind and have the Raguza wind be never so contrary yet our master know some late difference rise between the raguzean and venetian will not willing have land in that haven the blame of which difference some impute to the raguzean in that they favour the scocchi a neighbour people upon the shore of Sclavonia who be subject to the emperor and christian yet rob all kind of ship pass these sea and have late spoil and burn a venetian galley in the port of rovinge but other allege a more probable cause thereof namely that some venetian gentleman for some venereous in solency there commit have jate be slay in a tumult raguze of old call Epidaurus and the chief city of Selavonia be four hundred mile distant from Venice build at the foot of a high mountain upon the sea shore and have great traffic by those sea and huge ship which the king of spatne have often hire and join to their navy the government be popular and this city to the wonder of many do to this day maintain the liberty though it be seat between the very jaw of the two powerful state of the great Turk and venetian to one of which all other near town land and country be subject for the venetian be loath to drive they be christian to such despair as they may be force to yield themselves to the great Turk and the city be very strong fortify towards the sea whence the venetian can only assail they beside that they pay great custom of their traffic to the state of Venice for which reason that state attempt nothing against the freedom of the city again the turk know that if they shall besiege the city by land the citizen will with all their best movable fly into Italy by sea and receive also a great yearly tribute from the traffic of this city where the great Turk have his own officer call Chiaussagha to gather the same be also content not to molest they by war especial since they know that the pope the king of Spain and the state of Venice will assist the raguzeant against they and no way endure that the turkish Ottoman shall make himself lord of that haven upon the three and twenty of April towards evening we sail by the little island Augusta be yet of a good large circuit and populous and subject to the raguzean but the coast be dangerous for ship arrival by reason of the rock call the Augustine and by the little island Corsolavi some land in this sea be subject to the raguzean and some near to the northern continent have the great Turk for their lord but the rest be subject to the venetian and be very many in number but little and good part of they little or not at all inhabit the italian our consort tell we of a island not far distant call Pelaguza and lie near the continent of Italy upon the coast whereof the famous turkish pirate of Algiers a haven in Africa be late wont to hover and lie hide and make rich booty of the venetian and italian merchant upon Sunday the four and twenty of April we have in sight and little distant the little land Catza and lissa and in the afternoon on our left hand towards Italy the island Pomo and in the evening towards Dalmatia two land and upon the continent the city Zaga be some two hundred mile distant from Venice and the night follow we sail over a arm of the sea some thirty mile broad lie between Dalmatia and istria call Cornaro which we pass without any Cornaro appearance of danger though otherwise it be general repute so dangerous as the venetian offend with any mariner use this imprecation Maledetto Cornaro to have that be curse be the Cornaro for let thou pass upon Monday the five and twenty of April as we sail by the coast of istria one of the mariner age and as we think honest and of some authority among the rest private admonish i that I shall safe lock up our good in our chest leave the inferior mariner shall steal our shirt or any other thing they find negligent leave which they use to do especial at the end of any voyage upon Tuesday the six and twenty of April we cast anchor beyond pola in the continent of istria a city now ruin and upon the seven and twenty day we enter the haven of rovinge in istria subject to the venetian where the ship use to take a pilot for their own safety or else be tie so to do by some old privilege of that city here the provisor for health give we liberty of free conversation as they have former do at zante see no man in our ship to be sick or sickly and I do not a little wonder when I observe each second or three person of this city to halt and be lame of one foot which make i remember the citizen of islebe in Germany and in the province of Saxony where almost all the man have wry neck whereof I know the cause namely because they use daily to dig in mine with their neck lean on one side but of this common lament of the inhabitant in rovinge I can not learn any probable cause except it be the foul disease of lust reign in those part which I rather think likely because the lameness be common to woman as man now the sail in our great ship be like to be more trouble some dangerous and slow whereupon five of we join together do upon the thirty of April after the old style hire a boat of six oar for seven venetian ducat to Venice where we Venice arrive the next day towards evening and stay in our boat upon the wharf of the market place of saint mark till the provisor of health sit in their office near that place come unto we and after some conference understand that we and our ship be free of all infection or sickness give we free liberty of conversation we stay three day at Venice to refresh ourselves and pay each man three lyre for each meal in a dutch inn then have receive money of a merchant I go to the village mestre and there buy of dutchman new arrive in Italy two horse for myself and my man the one for thirty the other for twenty ducat these horse I sell at stand in Germany after my journey end at or about the same rate he that have the dutch tongue and either know the way of Germany himself or have consort skilful therein be to travel from stand or those part into Italy shall find more profit in buy a horse in those part of Germany for so he shall save great sum usual pay for coach and at the journey end or rather by the way towards the end of his journey may in Italy sell his horse with good profit in the village mestre each of we pay each meal fifty that be two lyre and a half from hence we take the right way to augsburg in Germany to nurnberg Brunswick and to stand a old city lie on the northern sea of Germany the particular of which journey I here omit have in my journey to jerusalem pass the very same way from stand to Venice so as it shall suffice to add some few thing in general within the confine of Italy each man of we pay for each meal forty and sometime fifty venetian soldi and for hay and stable for his horse common at noon four at night twelve and for ten measure of oat give each day to each horse fifty after we enter Germany each man pay each meal common twenty creitzer at inspruch twenty four and sometime twenty six creitzer for hay six creitzer a day or thereabouts and for ten measure of oat serve one horse for a day we pay fifty creitzer in the middle province of Germany each of we pay for each meal common sixteen creitzer that be four batzen and in the part upon the northern sea some four Lubeck shilling and from the city Armstat seat between augsburg and nurnberg to the say northern sea side we have a new measure of oat call hem one of which measure be sell for some ten Lubeck shilling and serve three horse for our bait at noon and another be almost sufficient for they at night from stand seat upon the german sea we pass in a boat to the outmost haven where we go abroad a english ship upon the four of july after the old style being Tuesday the six of july early in the morning we set sail and the eight of july we come upon the most wish land of England and cast anchor near orford a castle in Suffolk upon Saturday the nine of july after the old style we land at Gravesend and without delay with the night-tide pass in a boat to London where we arrive on Sunday at four of the clock in the morning the ten of july in the year 1597 july 10 an. 1597. our heart beingfull of joy that our merciful God have safe bring we thither this early hour of the morning be unfit to trouble my friend I go to the cock a inn of Aldersgate street and there apparel as I be lay i down upon a bed where it happen that the constable and watchman either be more busy in their office then need be or have extraordinary charge to search upon some foreign intelligence and see i apparel like a italian take i for a jesuit or priest according to their ignorance for the crafty priest will never have wear such clothes as I then do but after some few hour when I awake and while I wash my hand do inquire after my friend health dwell in the same street the host of the house know i dismiss the watchman that say to apprehend i and tell i how I have be thus mistake chap. v of the journey through England Scotland and Ireland he that desire to see the city and antiquity of England Scotland and Ireland let he read the chapter of the usual manner of all kingdom to journey and to hire coach and horse and also the chapter wherein these kingdom be geographical describe out of Camden or if he list rather let he read Camden himself of this point and last let he in the same last name chapter peruse the diet of these kingdom and the entertainment in inn touch the distance of place by mile first for England he shall easy find a little print book particular set down the same for Scotland I will brief set down my journey therein and for Ireland the city be rare and far distant he must have a guide who may without great trouble inquire they out only give i leave for the help of stranger to add this one thing namely how they be curious to search antiquity and loath to omit the light of thing worthy of observation may to this purpose best dispose of their journey which all other man may fit to their end and purpose first let they pass out of Normandy to Rhye a english haven in Sussex then let they visit such of the five kentish port as they please let they see Canterbury famous for the seat of the metropolitan archbishop then the castle of Quinborrough in the island of Shoppey and the regal navy then let they pass by Rochester a bishop seat the regal palace at Greenwich and Depford the naval storehouse and not far thence see the break rib of the ship wherein famous sir Francis Drake compass the world and so let they come to London when they have view the monument of London and Westminster and see the king court they may take a cursory journey to view such antiquity in Middlesex surry and Berkshire as upon the read of Camden they shall most desire to see and especial all or the chief palace of the King again from London they may take a cursory journey to see the university of Oxford and so by Worcester return to London in their journey to the confine of England and Scotland they may see the university of Cambridge and view the most choice antiquity mention by master Camden in Harfordshire Northamptonshire Lincolnsheire Yorkshire Durham and Northumberland myself upon occasion of business in the month of April and the year 1598. take a journey to these say confine namely to Barwick a town then very strong April ann. 1598. fortify by the english to restrain the sudden incursion of the scot and abound with all thing necessary for food yea with many dainty as salmon and all kind of shellfish so plentiful as they be sell for very small price and here I find that for the lend of sixty pound there want not good citizen who will give the lender a fair chamber and good diet as long as he will lend they the money be to return from Berwick I have a earnest desire first to see the king of scot Court so from hence I ride in one day forty mile to Edenborrow the chief city of that kingdom and in this say day journey after four mile ride I come to Aton a village where the lord of Hames dwell who family be powerful in those part after sixteen mile more I come to Dunbar which they say to have be of old a town of some importance but than it lay ruin and seem of little moment as well for the poverty as the small number of inhabitant after the ride of eight mile more on the left hand towards the west and something out of the high way the pleasant village hadrington lie which the english in the reign of queen Elizabeth take and keep against the french who draw over in the time of faction keep the town of Dunbar and fortify the same when I have ride five mile further I come to the ancient and according to the build of that kingdom stately palace of the l. seton beautify with fair orchard and garden and for that clime pleasant not far thence lie the village preston-graung belong to the family of the car powerful from these part to the very border of England within land after I have ride three mile more I come to the village fisherawe near which beyond a brook lie the village Musselborow in a stony soil famous for a great victory of the english against the scot on the left hand towards the west and something out of the high way the queen of scot than keep her court in the absence of the king at the village Dawkeith in a palace belong to the earl of Murray from the say village fishrawe I ride the rest of the way be four mile and so Edenborow in one day journey as I say come to Edenborow seat in lodovey of old call Fictland the most civil region of Scotland be hilly and fruitful of corn but have little or no wood this city be the seat of the king of Scotland and the court of justice be hold in the same of old according to the changeable fortune of war it be sometime in the possession of the scot sometime of the english inhabit this eastern part of Scotland till the english kingdom be shake with the invasion of the dane at last about the year 960. it become whole in the power of the scot this city be high seat in a fruitful soil and wholesome air and be adorn with many nobleman tower lie about it and abound with many spring of sweet water at the end towards the east be the king palace join to the monastery of the holy cross which king David the first build over which in a park of hare coney and dear a high mountain hang call the chair of Arthur of Arthur the prince of the britane who monument famous among all ballad-maker be for the most part to be find on these border of England and Scotland from the king palace at the east the city still rise high and high towards the west and consist especial of one broad and very fair street which be the great part and sole ornament thereof the rest of the side street and ally be of poor building and inhabit with very poor people and this length from the east to the west be about a mile whereas the breadth of the city from the north to the south be narrow and can be half a mile at the further end towards the west be a very strong castle which the scot hold unexpugnable Camden say this castle be of old call by the Britain castle meyn agnea by the scot the castle of the maid or virgin of certain virgin keep there for the king of the pict and by Ptolemy the wing castle and from this castle towards the west be a most steep rock point on the high top out of which this castle be cut but on the north and south side without the wall lie plain and fruitful field of corne. in the midst of the foresay fair street the cathedral church be build which be large and lightsome but little stately for the building and nothing at all for the beauty and ornament in this church the king seat be build some few stair high of wood and lean upon the pillar next to the pulpit and opposite to the same be another seat very like it in which the incontinent use to stand and do penance and some few week past a gentleman be a stranger and take it for a place wherein man of better quality use to sit bold enter the same in sermon time till he be drive away with the profuse laughter of the common sort to the disturbance of the whole congregation the house be build of unpolished stone and in the fair street good part of they be of free stone which in that broad street will make a fair show but that the outside of they be face with wooden gallery build upon the second story of the house yet these gallery give the owner a fair and pleasant prospect into the say fair and broad street when they sit or stand in the same the wall of the city be build of little and unpolished stone and seem ancient but be very narrow and in some place exceed low in other ruive from Edenborow there be a ditch of water yet not run from the inland but rise Lethe offspring which be carry to Lethe and so to the Sea Lethe be seat upon a creek of the sea call the Frith some mile from Edenborow and have a most commodious and large haven when Dessy a Frenchman do fortify Lethe for the strength of Edenborow it begin of a base village to grow to a town and when the french king Francis the second have marry Mary queen of the scot again the french who now have in hope devour the possession of that kingdom and in the year 1560. begin to aim at the conquest of England more strong fortify this town of Lethe but Elizabeth queen of England call to the succour of the lord of Scotland against these frenchman call in by the queen soon effect that the french return into their country and these fortification be demolish Erom Leth I cross over the Frith which ebb and flow as high as Strivelin to the village king-korn be eight mile distant and seat in the region or country call fife which be a peninsule that be almost a island lie between two creek of the sea call Frith and taye and the land yield corn and pasture and seacoale as the sea no less plentiful yield among other fish store of oyster and shell fish and this country be populous and full of nobleman and gentleman dwelling common compass with little grove though tree be so rare in those part as I remember not to have see one wood from the say village king-korn I ride ten very long mile to Falkeland than the king house for hunt but of old belong to the earl of Fife where I do glad see I James the six king of the scot at that time lie there to follow the pastime of hunt and hawk for which this ground be much commend but the palace be of old building and almost ready to fall have nothing in it remarkable I think to have ride from hence to saint Andrew a city seat in Fife and well know as a university and the seat of the archbishop but this journey be hinder I will only say that the bishop of saint Andrew at the intercession of the king of Scotland james the three be by the pope first make primate of all Scotland the same bishop and all other bishop of that kingdom have former to that day be consecrate and confirm by the archbishop of York in England likewise I purpose to take my journey as far as strivelin where the king of the scot have a strong castle build upon the front of a steep rock which king james the six since adorn with many building and the same have for long time be commit to the keep of the lord of eriskin who likewise use to have the keep of the prince of Scotland be under year and from thence I purpose to return to Edenborow but some occasion of unexpected business recall i speedy into England so as I return present to Edenborow and thence to Berwick the same way I come I add for passenger instruction that they who desire to visit the other county of England and Ireland may pass from Edenborow to Carlisle chief city of comberland in England and so between the east part of Lancashire and the west part of York and then through Darbyshire Nottinghamshire Warwickshire Staffordshire and Chesshire may take their journey to the city Westchester whence they shall have commodity to pass the sea to Dablin in Ireland and while they expect this passage they may make a cursory journey into Flintshire and Caernaruenshire in North-wales to see the antiquity thereof or otherwise may go direct to holy head and thence make a short cut to Dublyn in Ireland from Dublyn they may pass to see the city of the province monster whence they may commodious pass to the south part of Wales and there especial see the antiquity of Merlin and so take their journey to the west part of England may search the antiquity of these several county and easy find commodity to passeinto the west part of France and all this circuit begin at London may with ordinary favourable wind according to the season of the year be easy make from the begin of march to the end of September always I profess only to prescribe this course to such as be curious to search all the famous monument and antiquity of England mention in Camden complete description thereof chap. 5 of the manner to exchange money into foreign part and the diverse money of diverse part together with the diverse measure of mile in sundry nation most necessary for the understanding of the former journal the traveller most commodious course be to deliver into the hand of some merchant those kind of gold or silver coin which be of great value in those part to which he take his journey with covenant to deliver he by his factor the same both in the species or kind and in the number or to send they in that sort to he by a trusty messenger but the first course be not in use beside that it be a difficult task to find such diverse kind in any particular place except it be from the exchanger and usurer who use not to serve another man turn for profit or otherwise without their own gain and be most expert in such affair be like to draw all the hope profit to themselves and the second course threaten many danger by robbery by confiscation of the transmit coin and by the doubtful credit of the messenger particular in England and France he that be know to carry great sum of money about he shall run great danger to be rob and in England the law forbid any traveller upon pain of confiscation to carry more money about he out of the kingdom then will serve for the expense of his journey namely above twenty pound sterling as likewise in France the like law restrain the export of money allow a horseman from lion to Rome eighty crown from Turine to Rome fifty and no more for his expense all great sum find about he be subject to confiscation yet I confess that many wary traveller have export great sum out of England into France and out of France into England and thereby with these hazard have make great gain but upon the confine of Italy and the several principality thereof yea at the gate of each city in Italy most crafty officer so curious search into the breach of these paenall statute and so narrow pry into all man carriage never wink at any delinquent as there be no hope upon breach of the law to escape the penalty for these searcher be tie to more strict attention in this business that according to their office they may beware lest their prince be defraud of their tribute and since very jewel and the least toy carry about passenger be subject to frequent tribute according to the frequent principality these good fellow leave nothing unassay in the wish discovery of these fraud yea where they have no just suspicion of fraud yet cease they not to trouble passenger in this kind till they have extort some bribe or gift from they behold here a two horn danger as I may term it of the captious argument call dilemma which traveller can possible escape in Italy who carry about they jewel or great sum of money where they be in danger of confiscation if they hide they wary and of thief if they show they and pay due tribute for they for thief namely man banish for notorious crime lie continual upon the confine of prince very frequent in Italy and more special of the kingdom of Naples and of the pope territory in Germany Bomerland Sweitzerland the low-countries Denmark Poland and Turkey passenger may carry sum of money about they with more security neither have I there observe any great danger therein so that the passenger affect not solitariness and be so wary as not to boast of his plenty touch the diverse kind of coin to be transport I forewarn the reader that the gold coin of England and of France and aswell the silver as gold coin of Spain be in general spend abroad with great gain but even the gold and silver coin of other prince be rare currant out of their own dominion and can hardly be exchange among merchant without some loss again that he who export any gold coin must take care to have they of just weight for such he may disburse with gain but shall bear loss in other that be light because they want the help of their prince prerogative where no man can be force to receive they now I will brief show which kind of foreign coin be most value in diverse state in England the gold and silver coin of Spain and french crown be high value in Scotland the same coin and as well in Scotland as Ireland moreover the gold and silver coin of England be value for Germany at stand the english angel and all the gold coin of England France and Spain be most esteem but if you pass into the upper part of Germany you must for your expense there change these coin at stand into the rhenish gold gulden and silver dollar of Germany but at Vienna and the confine of Hungary the hungarian ducat be most currant in Bohmerland the rheinish gold gulden the silver dollar of Germany and the hungarian ducat in Sweitzerland first the french crown of gold than the gold gulden and silver dollar of Germany in Denmark the silver and gold coin of England at dantzk in Prussen and throughout all Poland the gold coin of England and the gold gulden and silver dollar of Germany and especial hungarian ducat in France upon the sea coast the angel and gold coin of England be currant but for your journey further into the land you must change they into french crown and the silver coin of that kingdom and the gold coin of Spain be very currant in all the city even within the land in the low country all coin be currant they be most cunning exchanger and want many thing yet draw to they abundance of all foreign commodity so as they have skill to draw gold out of the dung as he say of Ennius yet they most esteem the coin of England Spain and France in Turkey the gold zechine of Venice be most current and prefer even before their own Sultanones of gold the coin after they most esteem and to be spend with most gain be the silver rial of Spain which the italian call Pezzi d'otto and Pezzi piece of eight and piece of four and the turk call and half in Italy general the gold coin of Spain be spend with most advantage in the next place and more particular at Venice and Naples the french crown be esteem but in Italy more than any other place you must have care they be of just weight in general all gold coin may be put away with gain at Venice but they be in other part of Italy less value or not currant he that travel high must change they there into silver crown of Italy and lest the weight of they shall be burdensome and he shall chance to fall into the hand of thief call Banditi banish man he shall do well to carry no more about he then will suffice for the expense of his journey and to deliver the rest to some venetian merchant of credit take his bill of exchange or letter by which he may receive they in any other city or city as his occasion require but in respect of the foresay difficulty to export coin in that be in the kind the traveller shall do better who take the second and most usual course take care to have his money deliver to the exchanger or merchant by the hand of some trusty friend to be exchange over according to the exchange variable in respect of the time and the place to which it be to be make and send to he in foreign part for his expense by several sum and at set time of the year this exchange be so variable upon many usual accident as a constant manner of so unconstant a thing can be prescribe but the rate and course thereof may be inquire in the burse or public meeting place for the merchant for the alteration thereof be weekly make know particular to the merchant that by letter they present may certify the same to their several facter beyond the sea now this daily alteration of the exchange come partly of the quantity of money for the present to be exchange to the prince or merchant use and of the great or lesser number of those that desire money in exchange for when small sum be to be exchange either by the prince for payment of his army or by the merchant for their traffic and when many desire money upon exchange than the rate thereof be raise and the traveller make a motion that some of this house may be send to their lordship with request that the say Mr popham be a member of this house may forthwith be remand and restore to this house again which some think not needful to be do before the election and other again deny that he or any other can be choose speaker except he be present himself the clerk be command to read the say precedent again of choose Mr onslow in the say eight year of her majesty reign which say precedent see on Monday the 16 day of this instant forego and thereupon that course be agree upon to be follow the say Mr treasurer and other be send up to the lord to demand the restitution of the say Mr popham and bring answer again that their lordship have resolve he shall be send down the rather because he be a member of this house and this house possess of he before he be solicitor or have any place of attendance in the upper house upon relation whereof a motion be present make that it be not meet or convenient to choose a speaker by person that be not of the house and withal it be think of some that diverse person be new return in the place of other yet live be not or ought not to be account member of this house whereupon to avoid length of argument and the impediment of the election the say Mr treasurer by the assent of the house pronounce a admonition that all such as be new return in the place of other yet live shall forbear to repair to the house till their case be further consider then immediate Mr Anderson the queen sergeant at law and sir Gilbert Gerard knight her highness attorney general bring from the lord the say Mr popham her majesty solicitor general one of the citizen for the city of Bristol and restore he to this house as a member of the same and so depart and then be a motion make by Mr Lewkenor for prayer to be use before the election that it may please god both in that and in
