the residue of the proceed of this house to direct they with his holy spirit and a form of prayer be then read to the house by the Clerk and then afterward the house proceed to the election of a speaker the say Mr treasurer first speak do for his own part name and commend the say Mr popham allege many good reason and cause move he thereunto but still leave nevertheless liberty without prejudice to the residue of the house to name who they will or think good and thereupon the whole house with full consent of voice agree upon the choose of the say Mr popham who stand up and much disable himself in dutiful and reverend wise and allege for himself many reasonable cause and excuse beseech they humble to proceed to a new election whereof the house do not allow and so then be he forthwith by the say Mr treasurer and Mr controller bring up and place in the chair and order thereupon give that the house shall the next day assemble together both to understand her majesty pleasure for present of the speaker and also to determine of the case of the say person new return into this house in the place of other yet live on Thursday the 19 day of January the house again assemble the speaker elect sit in the chair the matter begin to be debate touch the say burgess of who question be make the day before and the case be open by Mr Norton a citizen of London to the effect follow viz. that there be member of this house absent in her majesty service as in embassage or in her affair in Ireland in who place new be return item some person be sick of durable disease as ague etc. and new be return in their place item one Mr Flowerden be the last session burgess for castle-rising in Norfolk and in the vacation be sick upon suggestion of which sickness a writ go to choose a new whereupon sir William Drewry be choose and return for castle-rising who now appear and Mr Flowerden also in the same vacation one Beamond a citizen for Norwich be sick of the gout upon suggestion whereof a writ go out to choose a new for Norwich Mr Flowerden be choose return and new swear for Norwich march 18 Saturday the question be whether such as be return in place of person sick or of person absent in the queen service be burgess and the old discharge Mr Norton think the old burgess remain and that the say cause of sickness and service be good excuse for their absence but no cause to remove they and to choose new and for this he allege diverse precedent as of doctor dale ambassador in France and of sir Henry Sidney deputy of Wales who have be former both of they member of the house of commons and absent by reason of both their say employment yet when their case be once make know unto the house and there question they be still retain as member of the say house and no new choose or admit but however although such absent member by reason of sickness or foreign employment may be remove yet that ought not to be do upon a suggestion in the chancery but by the judgement of the house of commons upon information thereof Mr sergeant Flowerden Mr Robert Snagg Mr Seintpoole and Mr Serjeant fleetwood controller argue to the contrary and say that in all these case new be to be choose and the old discharge and that it need not to have discharge by the judgement of the house but it suffice to make suggestion in the chancery and to procure a writ thereupon for a new election and to question this be to discredit the lord lie to be pay at sight at usance at half usance and at double usance which word usance be not english I take to be borrow of the italian word usanza signify a manner or custom the word at sight import present payment at half usance a fortnight after the date at usance a month at double usance two month and thus to he that go from London to hamburg in Germany it be all one whether his bill of exchange be pay at sight or at half usance since hce can hardly arrive there in less space than a fortnight but touch the exchange from London to Venice far distant by the word usance three month be signify and by double usance six month the turk empire be so far distant and the journey sare so uncertain as our merchant use no certain rate of exchange thither neither indeed use they to give any bill of exchange but only letter of credit to receive set sum of money or at large as much as the traveller shall want of which three kind of receive money in foreign part I shall hereafter speak by the foresay bill of exchange according to the foresay opportunity the traveller common lose and sometime gain for myself be familiar acquaint with a merchant who take up one hundred pound at London to be pay by his factor at stand in Germany which factor again take up the same hundred pound at stand to be pay by his master at London and at four month end he pay the same have by these bill of exchange make use thereof all this time without one penny loss but in general when great quantity of money be to be make over to any place from London the traveller shall lose after five in the hundred by the year and when small quantity be to be make over he shall lose after the rate of fifteen in the hundred by the year and ordinary he shall lose about the rate of ten in the hundred by the year by reason of the aforesaid uncertainty in receive money by bill of exchange as well by the slow send of they as by the delay which factor use to make in pay they as also by the usual negligence of the traveller friend who be to make over his money or by his want of ready money at the time I say for this uncertainty lest the traveller shall lose the season of the year fit to take journey by the expect of his money a three course of receive money in foreign part have grow in use namely that the traveller shall take with he letter of credit from some merchant of great trade to his factor to furnish he from place to place of money either according to his want or for a certain yearly sum I confess it be a more frugal course that he shall first pay his money at home and after receive it beyond the sea then that he shall first receive it there and after repay it at home but I will advise he to make over his money after the foresay manner by bill of exchange and withal to carry these letter of credit for abundant caution of all event so as upon any cross accident he may rather incur a small loss of money than the unrecoverable loss of time yet even in money take by letter of credit reckon the time of the repayment merchant use not to exact great gain than ten in the hundred by the year especial if they be honest man or have any bond of friendship with the traveller or his friend at home and be confident of repayment without any doubt arise either by the traveller sickness or by his friend ill keep of their credit it remain that I add somewhat in general out of my experience of the rate at which myself receive money by the foresay bill of exchange or letter of credit and first I will confess my negligent omission in note the rate of my exchange whereof as a matter of moment I much repent i but for this reason the reader must bear with i if I set the same down more brief than be fit for his instruction our of England into Scotland and Ireland a traveller shall have many opportunity to carry money that be in kind or to exchange they without any loss the exchange out of England to Stoade or Hamburge in Germany use to bear this rate for a pound or twenty shilling sterling to receive there five and twenty Hamburge shilling and six penny myself deliver forty pound in England and after the rate of twenty four hamburg shilling and eight penny for each english pound or at the rate of a imperial dolor value at four shilling six penny english I receive at Stoade forty nine hamburg pound six shilling and eight penny hereof I keep in my purse ten dollar that be two hamburg pound and fifteen shilling the rest I leave in a merchant hand who send i at diverse time to leipzig first nine and thirty dollar that be ten hamburg pound fourteen shilling and six penny and at another time threescore dollar that be sixteen hamburg pound ten shilling and the three time seventy dollar that be nineteen hamburg pound five shilling these join together with two shilling pay to the carrier for my letter make the foresay sum of forty nine hamburg pound six shilling eight penny receive upon bill of exchange for my forty pound first pay in London again I take up a stand from a english merchant seventy dollar upon my letter of credit and rate each dolor at four shilling eight penny english I give he a bill of sixteen pound sterling to be pay he by my friend in London from these part in Germany a traveller must carry with he the foresay kind of money most currant in Germany when he take his journey to the upper part of Germany to Bohemia and to Sweitzerland or the confine of Hungary the exchange out of England into the low-countries usual rate a english pound sterling first pay in England at four and thirty flemish shilling want two stiver to be pay after in the low-countries in Denmark traveller seldom make any long abode and the trade of our merchant be more rare in that kingdom want native commodity so as there be no usual exchange from London thither from London to dantzk in Prussen the exchange of a english pound sterling first pay in London use to be rate at four and twenty hamburg shilling and six penny to be pay there myself by letter of credit receive fifty dollar at dantzk and after the rate of four shilling six penny english for each dolor I give my bill for the payment of eleven pound five shilling english to be repay by my friend at London and at dantzk the same merchant for the same fifty dollar give i one and thirty hungarian ducat of gold and fourteen grosh in silver be the fit money for my journey to Crakaw in Poland and to Vienna in Oestreich or Austria out of England to Venice in Italy the exchange of four shilling and six or eight penny english use to be rate at a venetian ducat myself take no bill of exchange from England to Venice but have letter of credit to receive money of a venetian merchant to be repay in London upon my bill after the rate of four shilling three penny for each venetian ducat and at first be to take my journey for Rome and Naples I take up two hundred silver crown most fit for that journey which at Venice be rate at two hundred five and twenty ducat and nineteen grosh and I give my bill for three and fifty pound sterling twelve shilling and six penny english to be repay by my friend in London then I retain with myself as many of those crown as be necessary for my journey leave the rest in the hand of a venetian merchant who give i a bill to receive so many crown in that be in kind at Florence where I purpose to make my abode for some few month out of England into Turkey I former say that for the uncertainty of the journey upon the great distance of place there be no certain value of exchange neither use our merchant to send bill of exchange thither but to give letter of credit first to receive money there either at large according to the passenger want or for a certain yearly sum to be after repay in England upon the passenger bill and the merchant there for each sequin of gold of Venice deliver at Haleppo use to exact nine or ten shiliing english to be repay in London to the passenger great loss which he that will avoid may exchange his money to Venice and there receive zechine of gold or silver money of Spain to carry with he in that be in kind from London into France the exchange of six shilling english use to be rate at threescore french sou or three french pound which make a common french crown but a french crown in and of just weight be value there at threescore and four sou as in England a angel of gold be worth more than ten shilling silver among the exchanger though in expense it be out for no more than ten shilling and not only bill of exchange into France be give at the foresay rate for money first receive in England but he that have a merchant to his friend or acquaintance may easy compound to receive money first in France upon his letter of credit and to repay it in London after the rate of six shilling english for sixty french sou to this I will add two general caution most necessary for traveller first whereas in Germany and Italy the territory of absolute prince be frequent and of small extent and each of these prince do coin small piece of brass money it behoove the passenger to take heed that he spend each prince brass money within his territory or else that upon the confine he change they into brass money currant in the next territory which if he neglect the subject of the new prince howsoever they be neighbour to the former prince and may daily change these coin for their own yet they will not receive they without great gain they be of themselves little worth and only by the prerogative of each prince currant among their own subject second the passenger must take special care to leavea faithful friend at home to pay the bill ready which he send over to his merchant for so do he shall never want in foreign part at least among christian and know place of traffic yea out of his good report he shall be furnish with more money then be warrant by his letter of credit but on the contrary if his friend deny or delay payment he shall not have credit to borrow a penny upon his occasion more than that for which the factor shall have warrant by bill of exchange or letter of credit and if he fall into any misfortune he shall not find a friend to deliver he from penury and shame these thing be say in general nothing remain now but to set down the particular money of several kingdom and the value of they at the time when I live beyond sea which value be subject to change at the pleasure of each absolute prince and in this discourse I think most fit to begin with the money of England be more familiar know unto i be to write of the standard weight and value of english money I think fit of the diverse money of England first to give some few admonition to the reader first that the pure gold contain four and twenty carat in the ounce and four grain make a caract. second that the pure silver contain twelve ounce in each pound Troy weight and that Edward the first king of England keep the feast of Christ nativity at Barwich in the year 1300 do upon saint steven day decry the value of base silver money and after do altogether forbid the use of they and short after command sterling money to be coin so call of the easterling who first coin silver money of that standard which be of eleven ounce two penny weight three that the english pound as well of gold as silver mean the pound of the ballence not the pound of twenty shilling common spend contain twelve ounce Troy weight and that each ounce of silver be worth five shilling of the currant money and each ounce of angel gold be worth three pound five shilling or sixty five shilling of queen Elizabeth silver money and each ounce of crown gold be worth three pound or sixty shilling of the same coin four that the mintmaster give account before the queen examiner for the money they coin as well by the tale or number of the piece as by the sheer for it be not possible to coin money of the just prescribe weight yet the mintmaster be hold to have perform his contract with the queen for the standard prescribe by she so the silver be not more than 2 penny weight in the ounce heavy or light than her standard prescribe and in like sort for the coin of gold a certain proportion of some eight grain in the ounce be allow to the mintmaster in this account by the sheer five that 20 penny weight make a ounce and 24 grain make a penny weight now I return to the discourse in hand queen Elizabeth in the year 1600 contract with the mintmaster that of gold of the standard of twenty three carat three grain and a half he shall coin piece of angel half angel four part of angel piece of a angel and a half and of 3 angel now this angel be of three penny weight and 8 grain and this gold be common call angel gold also she contract with he that of gold of the standard of twenty two carat he shall coynepieces of twenty shilling and piece of ten shilling and piece of five shilling and the piece of ten shilling be three penny weight fifteen grain and this gold call crown gold be almost two carat base than the former and two carat after the rate of this standard be worth five shilling of queen Elizabeth silver last she contract with he that of silver of the standard of eleven ounce two penny weight he shall coin shilling half shilling four part of shilling and piece of two penny and of one penny and of half penny and the shilling be four penny or ninety six grain weight the same queen not long before her death reduce her silver to the standard of eleven ounce which be twopenny weight base than the former in each ounce and the mint office be say to have gain thereby one halfpenny in each ounce or about five in the hundred king james in the year 1604 publish a proclamation whereby new piece of gold be to be coin of a standard uniform to the standard of other nation for it appear by the proclamation that the gold coin of England be not of a just proportion between gold and silver according to the proportion use by all nation so as the english coin of gold be give in England for less than indeed they be worth it come to pass that they be transport into foreign part where they be esteem at high rate which mischief his majesty desire to take away by this uniform standard publish in the same proclamation for the better understanding whereof this follow table be join to the same it be to be remember that the pound weight english be twelve ounce Troy do over-poix the pound weight of Scotland four penny weight and my grain english whereupon this table be make to distinguish every several piece of gold and silver coin according to the true weight of both nation king james in the year 1609 contract with the mintmaster that of gold of the standard (@) three and twenty carat three grain and a half he shall coin piece of thirty shilling call Rose ryal piece of fifteen shilling call spur ryal and the foresay rose ryall be nine penny weight and five grain also he contract with he that of gold of the standard of two and twenty carat he shall coin piece of twenty shilling call unite piece of ten shilling call double crown piece of five shilling call Briton crown piece of 4 shilling call thistle crown and piece of 2 shilling 6 penny call half Briton crown and last that all these piece shall be proportion to the foresay table but the first standard of this year 1609 be light than the standard of the year 1600 by ten penny in each angel and the second standard of the year 1609 be light in like proportion than the second standard of the year 1600. moreover the goldsmith of this time say that of old a wedge of gold or any gold uncoined be bring into the mint be coin there for six silver shilling in each pound of gold whereas at this time the mint exact thirty silver shilling for the same whereupon the merchant carry their wedge for the most part into Flaunders to be coin and few of they be bring into England the goldsmith can not procure any of they for the exercise of their trade but be force to melt coin gold and silver for that purpose in the same year 1609 the king contract with the mintmaster that of silver of the standard of 11 ounce he shall coin diverse piece above mention in the former table according to the rule therein prescribe to conclude Caesar in his commentary mention brass coin of the Britain but the king of England have now for many age cast out of England all use of brass or copper money use none but coin of gold and silver and that of a pure allay of the diverse money of Scotland king james in like sort as he do for the english coin do also join to his foresay proclamation the foresay table of the scottish weight whereby the correspondency of the scottish money to the english and the just value weight and pureness thereof may be distinguish to which end I have also former join those table the scot also coin a silver money of 13 penny half penny and another piece of half the same value and both these piece of money be of the same pureness and value with the english silver and the scot of old call 20 english penny a pound as we in England call 20 silver shilling a pound and in like sort thirteen penny half penny english be by the scot call a mark as in England thirteen shilling four penny be so call also the scot have of long time have small brass coin which they say of late be take away namely Bahees esteem by they of old for 6 penny whereof 2 make a english penny also Placks which they esteem for 4 penny but 3 of they make a english penny also hard-head esteem by they at one penny halfpenny whereof eight make a english penny the irish history report that a bishop justice of Ireland under John king of England of the diverse money of Ireland do coin money in Ireland of the same pureness and weight with the English and the irish have a mint-house at the begin of queen Elizabeth reign but in our memory the irish have not enjoy any privilege of coin money but have continual receive they from the mint of England and for the most part of queen Elizabeth reign they have the same coin with the english save that the irish shilling be stamp with a harp the arm of the kingdom and be call harper be only worth 9 penny English but civil war have set all Ireland in a combustion the same queen more easy to subdue the rebel do take silver coin from the irish some few year before her death and pay her army with a mix base coin which by proclamation be command to be spend and receive for sterling silver money for no piece of gold be at any time express coin for the irish. and this base mix money have 3 part of copper and the four part of silver which proportion of silver be in some part consume by the mixture so as the english goldsmith value a shilling thereof at no more than 2 silver penny though they acknowledge the same to be worth 2 penny half penny at last the civil war be appease immediate before the queen death king james her successor in the year 1605 take away this mix coin and restore their old silver harper to the irish. moreover in the happy beginning of king james his reign the irish have the under write old coin which sir George Carey knight at that time lord deputy and yet continue treasurer at war for that kingdom do so gather up as at this day none of they be to be find these coin be thus call first they have silver groat call broad face groat which of old be coin for four penny though some of they be now worth eight penny also they have silver groat call crosse-keele groat stamp with the pope triple crown likewise coin for four penny but be of more value and these groat be either send hither of old by the pope or for the honour of they have this stamp set upon they last they have silver groat of like value call groat of the king of England then call that be lord of Ireland also they have groat so call of the king of England after they have the style of king of Ireland which be coin for four penny but by the mixture of copper be only worth two penny also they have white groat which be coin for four penny but of such base allay as nine of they be give for a english shilling they have little brass penny and penny of a second kind call harper be as big as a english shilling they have also brass farthing call smulkin whereof four make a penny last there be late find brass coin by plough up the earth who stamp show that the bishop of Ireland have of old the privilege of coin and of all these money aforesaid some be coin at London some at the mint at York and some at the mint at Bristol in England be to write of the diverse money of Germany I think fit first to set down some of the diverse money of Germany law of the empire about coin of money in the diet or parliament at augsburg in the year 1551. it be decree by the emperor together with the elector prince state the counsellor of those that be absent the ambassador and substitute that in the great piece of coin to that piece include which be worth six creitzer the mint-master of a mark of Colen pure silver shall make eight gold gulden and a half with half a creitzer the gold gulden be esteem at seventy creitzer make in silver ten gulden twelve creitzer and a half the silver gulden be esteem at sixty creitzer and that hereafter in the sacred empire the under write piece of money shall be coin namely the great silver piece and two half of the same answer in value to a gold gulden also piece of twenty creitzer twelve ten six three and one also that the state according to the condition of their country shall coin for common use certain piece of small money with penny and half penny that the rhenish gulden of the elector and the gulden answerable to they shall be worth seventy two creitzer and that all dollar be worth sixty six creitzer and so half dollar shall be admit by the counsellor but for the rest that they shall certify the emperor the true value of each to the end he may prescribe how each coin according to the value make by they shall be receive and spend or prohibit and leave the empire shall by fraud suffer loss in the carry out of uncoyned silver and bring in of foreign money it be in the mean time decree that no man shall carry out of the empire any uncoyned silver and that those who have the regal privilege of coin shall not fell the same to any other but useit themselves with this condition that hereafter of a silver mark of Colen weight they shall make ten silver gulden with twelve creitzer and a half the gulden be esteem at sixty creitzer so as in that sum there shall be find a silver mark of the say weight except always the charge of coin for the small piece of money and this to be do upon penalty of lose that privilege moreover it be decree that upon pain of burn all man shall abstain from clip and wash of coin or any abase of they with like fraud last it be decree that the state have the privilege of coin shall not hereafter upon penalty bring any dollar gulden grosh or half or four part of grosh to the mint except those who have mine of their own who be not sorbidden to coin as much gold and silver as they have in their own mine so they coin according to the foresay decree and that no other shall coin any other gold then according to the value and weight use by the emperor and the prince of the empire upon the Rhine in the Dieta at spire in the year 1557 it be decree that hereafter the stipend shall be increase to the assessor of the imperial chamber so as a Gulden have be give hitherto for 16. Batzen or sixty four creitzer shall hereafter be pay from the calends of April in the year 1558 for seventy seven creitzer likewise in the Dieta at augsburg in the year 1558 it be decree that the follow stipend shall be pay to the judge and assessor of that chamber namely that the judge be a earl or baron shall have 2000 gulden and if he be a prince his stipend shall be increase that a assessor be a earl or lord shall have seven hundred gulden a doctor licentiate or a gentleman shall have five hundred gulden a advocate in exchequer cause shall have three hundred gulden each Gulden be esteem at sixteen batzen till agreement be make for equal money in the empire the prince and diverse state and free city have from old time by the gift of emperor the privilege of coin the elector and prince of Austria do stamp their coin upon one side with their own image have the imperial apple over their head and upon the other side with their own arm but the coin of other prince and free city be stamp with the imperial eagle the coin that be not of just value be prohibit by imperial edict but the great gain which the prince and city make by their privilege be by the coin of small brass piece which piece be not of any value out of the territory where they be coin and can be spend upon the confine without loss as these privilege of coin be derive from the emperor so be they subject to the emperor ceusure while their power be yet unbroken for I find these word under the emperor name in the abstract of the imperial law vulgar call Keichs Abscheydt let every one which have the privilege of coin send their counsellor to i at nurnberg etc. in the mean time let coin cease in all place upon penalty of lose that privilege in the same book by a imperial edict with the consent of the prince and state in the year 1559 the weight and stamp of all coin be prescribe and it be decree that none shall coin more small money then for the necessity of their subject and that these money increase they shall present be forbid to coin any more by like edict diverse coin be either decry for the value or altogether take away and the bring in of foreign money and transport the coin of Germany be for the time forbid otherwise each prince may convert foreign money into the coin of Germany for the use of his subject the transport of uncoyned gold or silver into the low-countries be there forbid for the time great punishment be decree therein to be inflict on those that use fraud to abase the coin likewise it be decree that goldsmith shall not use any coin money in the exercise of their art except uncoin wedge be not to be have in which case they be restrain to melt no more coune than necessity require last therein decree be make that for the time none shall sell or lay to gage their privilege of coin and that hereafter like privilege shall not be grant to any without be subject to the prescribe law and that a silver mark shall be worth fourteen half ounce which weight the german call loath be half a ounce and that goldsmyths offend herein shall be punish according to the quality of the fraud now I will set down the diverse money of Germany with the several value of they and first I will forewarn the reader that most reckon of Germany be make by common silver gulden yet be there no such coin in the empire and these gulden be esteem at fifteen batzen in Germany near the value of three shilling four penny English also that in reckon of payment the german use to make they by mark of Colen and Lubeck yet be there no such money at all coin now I return to the purpose the gold Rhenish gulden of Germany be almost of the same standard with the crown gold of England but the difference of the value shall general hereafter appear the gold ducket of Hungary be of the pure gold of twenty four carat and it be two penny weight and six grain for I will apply all value to the english coin and in England they be worth seven shilling and two penny the silver Doller of the empire call Reichs Doller be of the standard of ten ounce or thereabouts and be eighteen penny weight sixteen grain and at this time in England a Doller be worth four shilling and five penny which of late before the reduce of our money be give for four shilling four penny the Phillips Doller be of the standard of nine ounce ten penny weight and it be a ounce half quarter weight and at this time in England it be worth four shilling ten penny at Stoade hamburg and Lubeck the gold Rhenish Gulden be worth eight and at Stoade and those part twenty silver mizzen grosh and a half and a four part of a grosh and the imperial Doller be worth four and twenty grosh the same rhenish gold Gulden be worth six and thirty Lubeck shilling and a half and the imperial Doller be worth three and thirty Lubeck shilling though in all reckon it be account but two and thirty shilling a common silver Gulden be worth eight and twenty Lubeck shilling a french crown of gold be worth four and forty a english angel of gold be worth two dollar with the four part of a Doller and two Lubeck shilling or otherwise it be worth twelve flemish shilling and four Lubeck shilling seven Lubeck shilling and a half make a english shilling sterling and six Lubeck shilling make a flemish shilling and likewise a shilling of hamburg the hamburger coin a piece of gold call a Portegue which be worth foure-pound and eight shilling of hamburg or three and thirty mark of Lubeck a mark be esteem for two shilling eight penny of hamburg at Stoade the silver shilling of England be worth seven stiver and the groat of England be of the same standard yet be currant for two stiver and a half because seven stiver in that money can not be divide into three equal part whereupon it fall out that he who buy any thing for a hamburg penny if he pay three english groat have a english shilling give he back and so have the thing buy for nothing at Emden upon the confine of the empire and the low-countries a at Emden silver Gulden of Emden be worth twenty stiver a imperial Doller five and forty which since that time be worth seven and forty a Doller of Emden be worth thirty stiver for prince and city coin gold and silver gulden which often in their value differ from the imperial as likewise dollar in that be kind differ in value from dollar as they be esteem in contract a french crown be worth three flemish gulden and six stiver now six stiver as also six Lubeck shilling make one flemish shilling at breme and oldenburg they have these small money at breme oldenburg and those part currant namely groat and piece of the stamp call Copstucks and a Doller be there worth four copstuck and a half ot five and fifty groat a french crown be worth six copstuck and one copstucke be worth ten stiver or twelve groat or there about a groat be worth little more than a english penny a Sesling be worth half a Lubeck shilling and they have also half sesling in these part at Brunswick a Doller be worth six and thirty Maria grosh which be of equal value with four and twenty silver mizzen grosh and also nine Maria grosh make eight at Brunswick Lubeck shilling the same Doller be worth eighteen spitz-groshen whereof each be worth two Maria Groshen here also I change six dollar into five rhenish gold gulden and nine grosh at magdeburg Leipzag mizzen and in all the electorate of Saxony and in the neighbour territory to the confine of Bohemia a Doller be at mizzen and those part worth sour and twenty silver Groshen which be as much worth as eighteen spitz-groshen or as six and thirty Maria Groshen a rhenish gold Gulden be worth seven and twenty silver Groshen and the silver Phillips Doller be of the same value a common silver Gulden be esteem at one and twenty silver Groshen a french crown at three and thirty a spanish pistolet at two and thirty a half Milreise at six and thirty the short and long crusado at five and thirty the hungarian ducat at thirty silver Groshen the rose noble be esteem at three dollar and a half the english angel at two dollar and little more than a ort or four part of a Doller and the silver grosh be worth more than two penny less than 2. halfpenny English and for the small coin a grosh be worth four dry and one dry be worth two dreyheller and one dreyheller be worth a pfen and a half and twelve pfen make a grosh and two schwerdgroshen make one schneberger in general through all the upper part of Germany a dolor be esteem at eighteen in upper Germany batzen a silver Gulden at fifteen a Phillip dolor at twenty a rhenish gold Gulden at twenty a french crown at four and twenty and a half a gold crown of Italy be esteem at Heidelberg and at Strassburg at four and twenty batzen at augsburg a silver Italian crown at two and twenty batzen and a half and the silver grosh of mizzen be carry out and currant in all Germany a rhenish gold Gulden through high Germany be worth seven and twenty silver mizzen grosh a silver Gulden there as in Saxony be esteem at one and twenty grosh the Batz be worth three english penny and four creitzer make a Batz four pfen make a creitzer and three creitzer make a zweluer and twenty zweluer make fifteen batzen which be a common silver Gulden more particular know that in all prince territory new and diverse small money be find at nurnberg eight pfund that be pound with twelve pfen make a silver Gulden thirty pfen make one pfund two hall make one pfen five pfen make one finfer seven pfen make a Maria grosh thirty six Maria grosh make a silver gulden in Austria two haller make one pfen four pfen one creitzer seven creitzer and a half make a shilling eight shilling make a silver Gulden at augsburg seven haller make one creitzer eight pfen make one bemish three creitzer make one plappart twenty plappart as also six and twenty bemish with 2 pfen make a silver Gulden in franconia six pfen whereof twelve make nine pfen of mizzen make one gnack in Suevia and Bavaria towards the Rhine three creitzer make one shilling or one plappart and twenty plappart make a silver gulden at Lindaw and from thence to Costnetz three pfen of Costnetz make one creitzer twelve pfen make a batzen and there be also half batzen from thence to Basil these money be spend before name at strasburg and Spira four pfen of the phaltz that be palatinate make one creitzer and at Heydelberg and so to francfort two strasburg pfen make one creitzer at francfort seven and twenty weispfen make a silver Gulden and eight haller make a weispfen at Wien that be Vienna and upon the confine of Hungary four pfen make a creitzer thirty pfen or seven creitzer and a half make a shilling and one shilling make a pfund or pound always let the reader understand that the value of these money be subject to change in diverse province and more at diverse time and let he know that a imperial in general Doller be now in Germany worth nineteen batzen which at my be there be give for eighteen batzen and that a Phillip dolor be now worth twenty two batzen which then be give for twenty and that a rhenish gold Gulden be now worth three and twenty batzen which then be give for twenty likewise that in the territory of the elector of Saxony a dolor be now worth six and twenty mizzen grosh and a half which then be give for four and twenty also that the great coin of Germany be now worth more in England than they be at that time in respect of our silver somewhat debase and if any object that our english coin of silver be now worth more in the low-countries than they be at that time when they be more pure let he know that all great coin as well of silver as gold as well foreign as domestical have since that time be increase in value in the low country yet he that will change a english angel into dollar or great silver coin in the low-countries shall gain no more at this time then former since now they esteem a dolor at seven shilling six penny which former be give for five shilling four penny and all the gain which this raise of the coin seem to promise a passenger be by change his great coin into stiver and small brass money which be of none or small value in themselves be despise by merchant who be to receive great sum but I will refer the change of the value of coin in the low-countries to the proper place and return to the value of coin increase in Germany wherein no man be to wonder that the great coin still remain of the old standard yet be give for more of their own small money then former they be since diverse reason thereof may be ready bring either for that the merchant be to receive great sum desire rather with loss to receive great coin then with gain to receive the base and brass coin or perhaps for that the small coin be now either more abase or altogether make of brass and most of all for that foreign merchant do more carry out the money of Germany for their ware than any native commodity thereof so as the increase value of coin in Germany turn only to the loss of stranger and to the gain of the empire Bohemia now long subject to the family of Austria have long hold the dignity of diverse money of Bohemta emperor do admit all the great coin of Germany in the same value as the german hold they and as well the bohemian as the hungarian in the year 1551 give consent that thenceforward their money shall be make agreeable to those of the empire in weight matter and value touch the small money in Bohemia and upon the confine of Hungary and Poland I remember that three potschandel or pochanel make one creitzer and nine creitzer with a pochanell make four weissgrosh and that thirty grosh of moravia or weissgrosh make a dolor and that in Bohemia they have a kind of grosh which answer in value to the grosh of Polonia last that merchant reckon two haller for a pfen and six pfen for a and sixty grosh for a shock and forty grosh for a mark the commonwealth of the sweitzer consist of diverse city and village diverse money of Sweitzerland and territory do also admit diverse money the privilege of coin be grant in common to Zurech Basil and Schaphusen and each of these city have his peculiar mint with officer to oversee that the money stamp with the mark of the city be of just weight and due mixture but each of these city have their peculiar money they of Basil with the neighbour town of Alsetz do coin a piece of money which of a crow stamp upon it be call reppen Muntz whereof five and twenty pound Troy weight call in latin assis make a common gold gulden esteem at sixty creitzer they of Schaphusen do coin money of the same mixture and value together with the usual money of the empire they of Zurech coin dollar and half dollar after the value of those in the empire but they have a less money of their own whereof forty pound Troy weight call in latin assis make a common gold Gulden they also coin Batzen whereof sixteen make a gold Gulden more particular to explain the value of these money six rappen of Basil make a plappart or three creitzer and twenty piappart or sixty creitzer make a common Gulden and as I former say in the discourse of german money from Lindaw to Costnetz three pfen of Costnetz make a creitzer now I add that the money of Schaphusen and Costnetz be spend to the confine of Schaphusen and the money of Basil be spend from thence to basil. at Zurech six pfen make a shilling worth a penny english and three pfen make a Sicherling two great finfer of Basil and one little finferlin make a batz of Basil and in like sort five finferlin make a batz and five finfer make two batzen and these money be spend to Strassburg and so to spire in Germany bern Frtburg and solothurn have a peculiar money whereof two and forty pound Troy weight in latin assis and twice four ounce in latin make a gold Gulden beside they and especial those of Solethurn in great quantity coin a piece of money which the sweitzer call Dickenpfenning and the french call testoome but it be less worth by the ten part then the testoone of France those of bern do first coin Batzen so call of a bear the arm of the city for the word Baren and also batzen signify bear in the sweitzer tongue and the city of Suevia imitate they draw the same money and word into Germany at bern sixteen batzen be esteem for a rhenish gold Gulden the money of be like to that of Basil but only six ounce Troy weight more base and fifty of these money make a rhenish gold Gulden as the french gold be spend with gain in Sweitzerland so in all place upon these confine of France the french silver coin call franc be common spend in the canton dwell scatter in village namely the Sweitz those of vnderuald the tugians those of Glarona and Abbatiscella a pound be a dolor in rhaetia or the grison the bishop and the citizen of Chur coin money and the abbot of Disent have a old privilege of coin and here sixty creitzer make a common gulden four Angster make a creitzer and twelve Angster make a Behmisch but in this province confine upon the state of Venice the lyre or berlingot and the gaget of Venice be vulgar spend and I remember that when I come out of the state of Venice into this province I spend crown of Italy and I find in my note that at Lasagna I change a silver crown for eight and twenty batzen but since seven batzen of Germany make two lyre of Venice and a gold crown of Italy be there give for eightlire I think that either it be a gold crown that I change or that the batzen of this province be of less value than those of Germany for a gold crown of Italy and the french crown be both of a value and I say before that at Strassburg I change each of these crown for four and twenty batzen yet to the contrary I find that pass forward in this territory of the grison I exchange at Lanzi a silver crown of Italy for seven and twenty batzen and that three batzen be there esteem at fourteen creitzer which in Germany be only worth twelve creitzer so as I be doubtful and can determine the value of the italian coin in the small money of the grison in the foresay imperial Dieta or parliament hold at augsburg in the year 1551. of the diverse coin of the low-countries these word be add to the imperial edict we will that this our edict shall be propound to our hereditary state of the low-countries and we will take care that they apply themselves thereunto as much as they possible can the state of the low-countries coin diverse piece of gold as archiducall angel and crown and the angel be of the standard of twenty two carat and of three penny weight seven grain also they coin gold lion call rider whereof each be worth four flemish gulden and a half the noble of gant be of the same standard with the french crown and in England it be worth thirteen shilling four penny they spend common Phillip dollar the value whereof I have set down with the money of Germany to conclude they coin any piece of which they can make gain yea the merchant report that they coin the great money of Spain England and all dominion stamp with the same image and arm and for such utter the same in their traffic with the indian but they coin little gold or silver of their own have a singular art to draw all foreign coin when they want they by raise the value and in like sort to put they away when they have get abundance thereof by decry the value and when their exchequer abound with any money and they be to pay their army than they increase the value thereof but have abundance of the same coin and be to receive their revenue than they decry the value thereof and while they thus raise the value of foreign great coin in their small money it fall out that they have plenty of gold and silver with the only loss of base stiver and brass money and indeed as well the art as industry of this nation have in our age become wonderful to all other nation for they have no wood yet by the commodity of their river and ditch they be become terrible to their enemy in the strength of their navy they have not corn to suffice their own want yet by fetch it from other part they relieve therewith all nation that want corn of late when they have no skill in traffic the italian trade at Antwerp do rash take their child to write their letter and be their cashier and they too late complain that when these child grow to age they do not only take all foreign traffic from they and send they back into Italy but also follow they thither and live disperse through all the city of Italy and spend at a low rate do also draw all that traffic to themselves in like sort they have no mine of gold and silver yet by their singular wit and rare industry do abound both with gold and slluer to conclude as they be most practical in all kind of business so be they most subtle in the art of the mint and money matter but I will return to the money of the low-countries at this day the english angel be esteem at five flemish gulden and two blank two english angel or twenty shilling be there worth sour and thirty flemish shilling want 2 stiver and a hundred pound flemish make sixty english pound sterling two english shilling be three shilling four penny flemish and make a flemish silver gulden twenty flemish shilling make a flemish pound twenty stiver make a flemish gulden six stiver make a flemish shilling two blank make one flemish stiver and a half four orkee or of brass make a flemish stiver a french crown be give for three flemish gulden and four stiver but in Holland only three stiver and in some place but two stiver more than three gulden a imperial dolor be give for forty five stiver a spanish pistolet for three flemish gulden and two or three stiver a gold Rhenish gulden for two flemish gulden and nine stiver and a Phillip dolor of silver for two flemish gulden and eight stiver or very little more the mark of Denmark be esteem at 16 Lubock shilling and two shilling of the diverse money of Denmark of Denmark make one Lubeck shilling and thirty three Lubeck shilling be give for a imperial Doller yet two and thirty of lubect or sixty four shilling of Denmark make a common Doller in contract the gold of England be common spend in Denmark and they esteem a english angel at two dollar and little more than the four part of a Doller and they esteem the english rose noble of that time at three dollar and a half which coin they have almost draw altogether into Denmark by the exact the same for the tribute of ship-mast and other merchandise pass the narrow straight of their Sea the polonian coin gold ducat of the same value with the hungarian ducat of the diverse money of Poland whereof I have speak among the money of Germany and these ducat at this day be give for seventy polish grosh which of late be worth no more than sixty five Venceslaus king of Bohemia be crown king of Poland about the year 1300 who first bring silver money into Poland namely bohemian groshen I mean those of silver not the white which to this day be currant in Crakaw and those part for before that time the polonian do traffic with little piece of uncoined silver and with exchange of skin and other commodity at this day the polonian aswell as the german make all contract by silver gulden but have no such coin stamp thirty polish grosh make a silver gulden and a dolor at this day be worth forty polish grosh at the least which not long since be worth no more than thirty five but to this day in contract thirty six polish grosh make a dolor howsoever a dolor in that be in kind be worth forty grosh at the least three pochanel make a creitzer and seven pochanel make a polish and bohemian groshen of silver at dansk in Prussia of old a province of Germany but late annex to the crown of Poland they coin hungarian ducat of gold as they do in Poland and they have two coin of gold call Milreis and half Milreis and I receive of a merchant there each hungarian ducat and each half milreis for a dolor and a half with one sesling and each milreis for three dollar and two sesling and thirty six polish grosh do there make a dolor but I remember that I do there change a hungarian ducat for fifty six polonian grosh which value pass the former about a and a half for a sesle (@) hamburg make a danish shilling and that be little more worth than a half polish the muscovite empire lie upon this province and therefore I will add a word of their coin they make all contract by a money call Rubble which be altogether imaginary for they have no such coin and it be esteem in England at thirteen shilling four penny sterling and in the muscovite money it be rate at thirty three altines and two diagoe and six single or three double diagoe make one altine the italian crown of gold and the spanish pistolet and double pistolet be of the diverse money of Italy in general there current be of the same standard allay and value with the after mention french crown save that the double pistolet contain two french crown the venetian zecchine be of the same standard fineness and value as the hungarian ducat above mention in the money of Germany the pope of silver and so likewise the be of the same standard fineness and value with the english six penny but the lyre of Venice be worth about nine penny english be of a little base standard the spanish coin of silver be currant in Italy and they be call Pezzi d'otto piece or rial of eight and they be of the standard of 11 ounce and two penny weight and be three quarter of a ounce and a half weight and in England each of they be worth four shilling four penny half penny this spanish money be two and twenty penny in each twenty shilling english more worth than the english silver all crown of gold be currant in Italy and all at one rate except the french crown which at Venice and Naples be esteem somewhat high than other though in all the other city of Italy it be more commodious to spend spanish pistolet or crown than french crown in general the italian silver crown give for seven lyre of Venice be worth almost five shilling english and the italian gold crown vulgar call give for seven lyre and about fifteen sol of Venice be worth almost five shilling six penny english and the gold crown vulgar call sole give for eight lyre and some odd sol of Venice be currant in England for six shilling to conclude great sum pay in little brass money be in Italy deliver by weight not by tale or number and more particular to explain the value of money at Venice a sequin of Venice be give for ten lyre and at Venice ten or twelve sometime more sol a double pistolet of Spain call Dublon ' be there give for seventeen lyre a french crown be give for eight lyre and eight or sometime ten sol a italian crown of gold be there give for eight lyre and some for seven lyre sixteen sol for the weight of Venice be heavy than in other part of Italy the light crown be less esteem the spanish piastro of silver be give for six lyre the silver ducat for six lyre and four sol the silver crown for seven lyre the for two lyre the thirteen the poalo and the giulio in other part of Italy be both of one value but here the giulio be give for six and four brass quatrine or for forty brass quatrine and the poalo be give foreight or for forty eight brass quatrine also he that change any crown shall have more gain if he change it into which be good for expense there then if he change it into because the be currant in other part but the only in the territory of Bologna at Pesaro and in the dukedom of Vrbino a gold crown be spend for twelve and fifty two brass quatrine make a at Pesara poalo but if you will change your gold crown into brass quatrine which be not (@) out of the territory you may have 182 quatrine for the crown which make fifteen which be a five part more than it be worth in silver at Ancona at Ancona and in the marci of Ancona a gold crown be give for eleven and a half or in the value of twelve if you receive it in brass quatrine and there sortie quatrine of brass make a giulio forty three make a poalo a silver crown be give for ten and if you receive brass quatrine for they will give seven more and ten make a poalo at Rome a gold crown be sometime give for eleven and a half sometime for twelve sometime for twelve and a half according at Rome to the abundance and want of gold and all gold crown be of one value and a silver crown be give for ten or and ten make one giulio or poalo and four brass quatrine make a baocco last at Rome more than any other where he that change crown into quatrine which can be spend out of that state shall in appearance make great gain but in the market those which sell use to look into the buyer hand and if he have quatrine therein they hold the thing to be sell at a high rate if he bring silver they sell cheap and I remember that the gentleman of Rome refuse to take quatrine for their rent the people when the pope come abroad fall on their knee before he in stead of ask his blessing do with humble cry crave a remedy for that oppression at Naples a gold spanish crown or a at Naples french crown of just weight be give for thirteen a italian gold crown for twelve and a half a silver crown for ten and nine make eight real or or and five make one and forty brass quatrine make one carlino ten quatrine make one sequin three quatrny one and two make one quatrine in the dukedom of Florence a gold crown be give at Fiorenza for twelve or half touch small money ten brass dener make a quatrine three brass quatrine make a five quatrine make a baello or creitzer which be a little coin of silver and eight baelli make a giulio or carlino rial 20 soldi make a lyre 2 make a susine and 3 dener of Mantua make a the great Turk coin a piece of gold call Sultanon' and it be of the same standard the diverse money of Turkey fineness and value with the hungarian ducat above mention among the money of Germany in Africa those of Barbary have a gold ducat common current among christian which be so rare in Turkey as I do not remember to have see any piece there of spend there neither have the african any mine of gold but they carry salt to ganger and thence bring this gold and this Barbary ducat of gold be of the standard of three and twenty carat and the four part of a grain and three penny weight want two grain and at this day in England it be give for nine shilling two penny sterling which of late be worth no more than eight shilling four penny sterling to conclude the gold sequin of Venice and the spanish piece of silver of four and eight real and the very silver of Venice be so common spend in Turkey as the gold and silver of Turkey seem despise or at least be seldom spend but because this vast dominion have large circuit it will not be amiss to name the money currant in diverse place and first I forewarn the passenger that in all place he be in danger who show his money but most of all among the turk where to be rich be more dangerous than the great crime can be object either in respect of the covetous judge in public or the ravenous nature of each man in private neither do any thing more provoke the turk to lay snare for the wary passenger so as howsoever it be not improvident do to carry sum of gold or silver by sea and in bark of christian yet I will advise a passenger to be wary how he show they among christian and much more among turk and advise he rather to make show to draw his money from a little purse hide his great store then in payment to pull it out by heap the greek island zante subject to the venetian have Venice at zante money and a gold sequin be give there for eleven lyre and two gaget likewise the greek island Candia subject to the venetian have the money of Venice at Candia where a gold sequin be give for eleven lyre and to the turk land there at a high rate the silver crown call piastro be there give for six lyre and about four and here I find a silver piece which I never find to be spend in the state of Venice namely a perper worth eight and eight of Venice the greek island Cyprus subject to the turk spend the venetian gold and silver at Cyprus lyre but receive not the piece of eight nor the lesser money of Venice neither be the venetian lyre currant any further than this island though perhaps they may be spend with some loss upon the coast adjoin at Cyprus the gold sequin be give for eleven lyre of Venice and for 120 asper of Turkey and the silver crown call piastro or a piece of eight real spanish be give for seventy asper and the gold Sultanon of Turkey be of the same value with the sequin of Venice yet the very subject more willing receive the zechine the turkish Asper be a little piece of silver which at Haleppo in Syria be worth some three farthing english and eight asper at Cyprus make one a turkish money which the italian call Seya be esteem at little more than six penny english and fifteen mad a sequin twelve mad a french or spanish crown ten mad a piastro or spanish piece of eight real and sixteen brass Mangouri make one silver Asper neither can any money of Cyprus be spend in Palestine without loss at jerusalem and through all Palestine and those part the gold zechine of Venice at jerusalem be more esteem as in all Turkey then any other piece of gold and the very turk more willing receive they then the turkish sultanon at jerusalem a sequin be give for five and forty meidine of cairo in Egypt the spanish piece of eight real call plastro be give for eight and twenty meidine and half a piastro at the same rate neither have they any asper there but these meidines only where of each be worth three asper so as the five and forty meidine give for the sequin be worth 135 asper and the eight and twenty meidine give for the be worth eighty four asper the gold crown of France and Spain be not spend here without loss but the silver ducat of Italy be commodious to be spend here at Tripoli in Syria and at Haleppo and in the territory adjoin the foresay silver asper at Tripoli be common spend the gold sequin of Venice be there worth ninety meidines and the spanish worth sixty but these meidines of Tripoli differ from the other of Cayro in Egypt for those of Tripoli be each worth one asper and a half but those of Cayro be each worth three asper at Constantinople all contract be make by asper howsoever the foresay piece of gold and silver be there also currant and in small at Constantinople contract they pay asper by the weight because they can easy number they but in great contract they reckon by ass load of asper as the english do by hundred and thousand pound last at Constantinople I exchange gold zechine each at 125 asper a french crown at one hundred asper and a dolor of Germany at 75 asper the gold french crown be of the standard of two and twenty carat and be two of the diverse money of France penny weight four grain and a quarter the silver piece call Quartfield d'escu that be the four part of a crown be of the standard of eleven ounce and be six penny weight four grain and be worth two Venice lyre or eighteen penny sterling English the piece of silver call Francke be of the standard of nine ounce ten penny weight and eighteen penny weight sixteen grain and be worth two shilling English the french crown be exchange for three francke or for four quart d'escn or for little more than four testoone for fourteen sou and a half make a testoon fifteen sou make a quart and twenty sou make a frank and sixty sou make a french crown and twelve denier make a sou yet a gold french crown in that be in kind be change for sixty five sou as in like sort in England a french crown be worth no more than six shilling and the english angel be worth no more than 11. shilling in common estimation yet he that bring a weighty a french crown in to the goldsmyths they will give he six shilling six penny for it and he that bring to they a old angel of gold they will give he 11. shilling and six penny or more for it and in the last civil war the value of the french crown be raise to 120 sou till the king reduce the same to the old value after the war compose the same king Henry the four since that time raise the value of gold crown to the end he may draw back his gold which be carry into foreign part myself pass through Lorraine before the french civil war be full appease do at Monwicke upon the confine of Lorraine and German exchange a french crown for four francke and nine and short after come to shallon exchange a french crown for no more than sixty sou so as I guess that either the francke of lord aine differ from the francke of France or that the tumult of the war and the make of peace short after make this difference of the diverse measure of mile through diverse part of the world fvrther be to write of the diverse measure of mile through the diverse part of the difference of mile the world it seem good to i to add the measure of mile vulgar receive namely that five Italian mile or three french or two and a half english make one dutch mile and that one dutch mile and a half make a mile of Sweitzerland it remain now that according to my own experience I shall speak something of the diverse kind of mile and in general this my opinion have respect to the difficult or easy passage of the way since even in England the mile seem and indeed be more short near London where the way be fair and plain and frequent inhabit as they seem and indeed be more long and tedious through the desert place of the north over mountain and through uninhabited and difficult passage the roman of old hold a thousand pace for a mile and such be the mile of Italy Italian a common english mile make one and a half italian but towards the north and in some particular place of England the mile be long among which the kentish English mile be a southern county be proverbial hold to be extraordinary long the irish mile among the english and the irish-english be answerable to the english irish. howsoever for the solitary and disinhabited way and many foard often overflow they be more troublesome to pass in like sort the mile of Scotland answer to the northern mile of England save that the frequent climb of mountain and the unbeaten way make they seem scottish long and indeed require more time for the passage Villamont a french gentleman in the book of his travel witness that one french French mile contain two italian mile the common german mile be for the most part in plain make more than three english or five italian mile but in some place the solitude of wood and the German ascent of mountain make the mile of Germany seem much long and Suevia extraordinary have long mile though it be a plain country the mile of Sweitzerland be over continual mountain be so long as passenger distinguish their journey more by the space of hour then by the distance or number of mile and I remember that find no horse to be hire I go on foot from Scaphusen to Zureth which journey I be go ten hour be account but four mile and in rhaetia among the grison upon the confine of Italy one mile be hold for six italian mile and upon the foot of the Alps towards the north one mile be account for seven mile and a half of Italy where have a good horse I can ride with a ordinary pace no more than one dutch mile in four hour space by which appear that the measure of mile be very uncertain among the sweitzer who for the most part reckon their journey by hour ride or go with a ordinary pace and not by mile the mile of Bohemia and moravia be no less tedious and I remember that my Bohemia self pass there on horseback do common ride no more than four mile in a day journey and howsoever the length of the sweitzer and bohemian mile may in part be attribute to the climb of mountain and bad way yet no such reason can be give for the mile of moravia which country be either a plain or little pleasant hill and the way fair and the country well inhabit the low-country mile be of a middle length between the german and french mile but in the very country of Holland they differ much one from another flemish since four mile of great Holland make six mile of little Holland and I remember that about the city Horn I esteem each mile long than three English also next to the holland mile those of Freesland be long than the rest a mile of Denmark be somewhat long than three english mile and answer to danish the common mile of Germany the mile of Poland general be like the mile of Denmark but they differ Polonian in length one from the other for I remember that in Prussia each day journey I pass by coach some seven mile and in middle Poland nine or ten mile but in upper Poland towards Germany I common ride on horseback no more than five mile or thereabouts each day in my passage from Crakaw to moravia in Russia among (@) the moscovite confine upon Poland a mile be call a fierce and answer to five italian mile or one common mile of Germany in Turkey those that guide christian have the italian tongue do in my opinion turkish number the mile to they much after the italian manner the rebellion of HUGH Earl of Tyrone and the appease thereof write in form of a journal part 2 book i. chap. i. of the induction or preface to my irish journal and a compendious narration how CHARLES BLOUNT lord Mountioy my lord and master of happy memory be choose lord deputy of Ireland and of this worthy lord quality as also of the counsel in by which he break the kebel heart and give peace to that trenbled State together with his particular action in the end of the year 1599 at my return from Scotland about the month of September in the year 1598. I retire myself to heal my dear sister faith mussendine house be situate near the south bank of Humber in the county of Lincoln in which place and my dear sister jane alingtons house near adjoin while I pass a idle year I have a please opportunity to gather into some order out of confuse and tear write the particular observation of my former travel to be after more delibrate digest at leisure after this year spend in country solace the hope of preferment draw i into Ireland of which journey be to write in another manner than I have former do of other country namely rather as a soldier then as a travele as one abide in camp more than in city as one lodging in tent more than in inn to my former brief discourse of the journey through England and Scotland I have of purpose add there out of my ordinary course the like of Ireland only for traveller instruction I be now to treat of the famous and most dangerous rebellion of Hugh earl of Tyrone call himself the oneale a fatal name to the chief of the sept or family of the oneales and this I will do according to the course of the former part namely in this place not write historical but make only a journal or bare narration of daily accident and for the rest refer the discourse of Ireland for all particular to the several head wherein each point be joint handle through all the dominion of which I have write only in this place for the better understanding of that which I principal purpose to write I must crave leave to fetch some short remembrance by the way of preface high than the time of my own be in Ireland in the lord Mountioy his government about the year 1169 not to speak of the kind of subjection which the irish be ann. 1169. write to have acknowledge to Gurguntius and some Briton king henry the 2 be himself distract with french affair give the earl of strangbow leave by letter patent to aid Dermot Morrogh king of Lemster against the king of meath and this earl marry Eva the daughter of Dermot be at his death make by he heir of his kingdom short after king Henry himself land at Waterford and whilst he abide in Ireland first Dermott Mac carthy king of Corcke and the south part of monster and Dunewald Obzian king of Limrick and the north part of monster then orwark king of meath and Roderick king of Connaght by singular privilege over the rest call the king of Ireland and the above name king of Lemster yet live do yield themselves vassal unto king Henry who for the time be salute lord of Ireland the title of king be first assume by act of parliament to king Henry the eight many year after in the say Henry the second reign sir John de courcy with four hundred voluntary english soldier send over do in five battle subdue Ulster and stretch the bound of the english pale as far as Dunluce in the most northern part of Ulster about 1204 John courcy of english blood earl of Ulster and Connaght do rebel and be subdue by Hugh lacy. about 1210 the lacy of english blood rebel be subdue by king John who after some three month stay return back into England where the lacy find friend to be restore to their earldom of Ulster about 1291 O-Hanlon and some Ulster lord trouble the peace be suppress by the english colony from 1315 to 1318 the scot make great combustion in Ireland to who many irish family join themselves and both be subdue by the english colony in the year 1339 general war be between the english colony 1339 and the irish in which infinite number of the irish perish hitherto Ireland be govern by a lord justice who hold the place sometime for few year sometime for many in the year 1340 John darcy a Englishman be make justice for life and the next year do exercise the place by his own deputy which neither before nor after I find to have be grant to any but some few of the royal blood about the year 1341 the english-irish or english colonies be degenerate first begin to be enemy to the english and themselves call a parliament write to the king that they will not endure the insolence of his minister yet most of the justice hitherto be of the english-irish or english bear in Ireland about the year 1361 Leonel duke of Clarence be make lord lieutenant of Ireland and sometime leave his deputy to govern it this duke be earl of Ulster and lord of Connaglit by the right of his wife come over with a army of some 1500 by pole and quiet the border of the english pale in low lemster hereformed the english-irish grow barbarous by embrace the tyrannical law of the irish most profitable to they which cause they likewise to take irish name and to vie their language and apparel to which purpose good law be make in parliament and great reformation follow aswell therein as in the power of the english for the leaven year of his lieutenancy and after till the fatal war of Turk and Lancaster house and hitherto most of the justice be english-irish about the year 1400 Richard the second in the eighteen year of his reign ann. 1400. come with a army of four thousand man at arm and thirty thousand archen full to subdue the irish but pacify by their submission and no act of moment otherwise do he return with his army into England after to revenge the death of the earl of March his lieutenant he come again with a like army but be sudden recall by the arrival of Henry the 4 in England during the say king reign Ireland be govern by his lord lieutenunt send from England and in the reign of hen. the 4 and hen. the 5 by justice for the most part choose of the english-irish only the lord Scroop for 8 year be deputy to Thomas the second son to hen. the 4 who be l. lieutenant of Ireland this I write out of the annal of Ireland print by Camden in which from the first conquest of Ireland to the follow war between the house of York and Lancaster in England I find small or no mention of the oneal greatness among the irish lord and I find very rare mention of any sedition in Ulster especial among the northern irish so as that province from the first conquest to these civil english war do thereby seem to have be one of the most peaceable and most subject to the English neither read I therein of great force or sum of money lend out of England into Ireland except voluntary and the cursary journey of king John and king Richard the second but rather that for the most part all sedition as well between the english-irish and the mere irish as between the english-irish themselves be pacify by the force and expense of the same kingdom during the say civil war between York and Lancaster for England most of the noble family be waste and some destroy whereupon the english irish which hitherto have valiant maintain their conquest now begin to repair into England partly to bear out the faction partly to inherit the land of their kinsman of who they be descend and the mere irish bold rush into the possession which the other have leave void in Ireland and from that time under the government of english lieutenant and deputy sedition and murder grow more frequent the authority of the english king become less esteem of the irish then in formertime and the english pale have sometime large sometime straight limit according to the diverse success of the irish affair at diverse time after the appease of the say bloody war I find some 1000 man send over by Henry the seven to suppress Perkin Warbeck a english rebel and 500 man send by Henry the eight to suppress the geraldine of english race rebel against he otherwise the say annal mention no great or general rebellion in Ireland especial such by which either much blood of the english be spill or much of our treasure exhaust till the happy reign of queen Elizabeth for in this only age religion rather than liberty first begin to be make the cloak of ambition and the roman locust to maintain the pope usurp power breathe every where fire and sword and not only make strong combination against those of the reform religion in all kingdom but be not ashamed to proclaim and promise heaven for a reward to such cut throat as shall lay violent hand on the sacred person of such prince as oppose their tyranny among which this famous queen be of great power and most happy in success against they they not only lest nothing unattempted against her sacred person and her crown of England but whither encourage by the blind zeal of the ignorant irish to popery or animate by a old prophecy he that will England win must with Ireland first begin do also raise two strong and dangerous rebellion in Ireland the one of the earl of Desmond and the other of the earl of Tyrone not to speak of the trouble make by shane oneale the easy settle whereof shall be only mention in the treat of tyrones ancestor howbeit the wont general peace seem to have continue till after the 19 year of the queen reign be 1577 at which time the lord of Conuaght and Ororke for their particular make a composition for their land with sir Nicholas 1577 Malby governor of that province wherein they be content to yield unto the queen so large a rent and such service both of labourer to work upon occasion of fortify and of horse and foot to serve upon occasion of war as it seem the popish combination have not yet wrought in they any alienation of mind from their wont awe and reverence of the crown of England touch the rebellion of Gerald earl of Desmond John Gerald the son of Thomas the rebellion of the earl of Desmond who progenitor of english race have long behave themselves valiant in subdue the irish have Kildare give he by king Edward the second with title of a earl and this family of the Fitz gerald or geralden as they be now call long flourish not only keep Ireland in obedience to the king but infest the sea coast of the welsh not yet unite to the crown of England and never raise arm against England till Thomas Fitz Gerald the son of Gerald Fitz Gerald earl of Kildare and lord deputy of Ireland under king Henry the eight who the king have call into England and there bring in question for his ill government hear by light and fall rumour that his father be execute rash take arm against the king invite the emperor Charles the five to invade Ireland which he in the mean time waste with fire and sword this Thomas and five of his uncle be short after hang the father be before dead of grief but queen Marie restore this family to honour and land though they never after recover their former dignity of these gerald most of the great lord in monster be descend though for diverse cause many of they have take other surname and particular the earl of Desmond Maurice fitzthomas a Geraldine be first create earl of Desmond by Edward the three of who posterity many excel in wealth virtue and honourable reputation far extend their power but james invade his nephew inheritance by force and impose heavy exaction on all depend upon he who son Thomas follow his father step be by the lord deputy behead in the year 1467 his son be restore and the earldom remain in his posterity till Gerald earl of Desmond in the year 1578 rebel against queen Elizabeth to who aid certain ann. 1578 band of italian and spaniard send by pope Gregory the twelve and Philip king of Spain land at Smerwic who besiege by the lord Arthur Grey than lord deputy in a fort they have build and call the fort deal over short after yield themselves in the year 1583 and be put to the sword as the necessity of that state and their manner of invade the land be then say to require and the earl of Desmond fly into the wood be there in a cottage kill and his head cut off be as they say betray by his own follower wherein the Ulster man challenge a honour of faithfulness to their lord above those of monster for in the follow war none of they can be induce by fear or reward to lay hand on their reverence oneale thus with a army of six thousand man whereof some fourethousand be new send over at diverse time this rebellion of Desmond in monster be soon appease the earldom of Desmond
