blackewater and 1500. common soldier whereof many be of the old company which have serve in Britain under general Norreys be slay in the field the yield of the fort of blackwater follow this disaster when the assault guard see no hope of relief but especial upon message send to captain Williams from our break force retire to armagh profess that all their safety depend upon his yield the fort into the hand of Tyrone without which danger captain Williams profess that no want or misery shall have induce he thereunto short after sir Richard bingham above mention late governor of Connaght and unworthy disgrace be send over to succeed sir Henry bagnol in the marshalship of that kingdom by this victory the rebel get plenty of arm and victual Tyrone be among the irish celebrate as the deliverer of his country from thraldom and the combine traitor on all side be puff up with intolerable pride all Ulster be in arm all Connaght revolt and the rebel of Lemster swarm in the english pale while the english lay in their garrison so far from assail the rebel as they rather live in continual fear to be surprise by they after the last year naval expedition out of England into the land certain old company of one thousand and fifty foot draw out of the low country be appoint to winter in the west part of England to these nine hundred and fifty new man be add this summer and the command of these two thousand foot and of one hundred horse be give to sir Samuel Bagnol who be appoint to go with they to Loughfoyle in the north of Ireland but after the defeat of blackwater they be countermand to go into Lemster to strengthen the queen force in the heart of the kingdom after the defeat of blackwater Tyrone send only Mac rory o more and one captain tyrel of english race but a bold and unnatural enemy to his country and the english to trouble the province of monster against who sir Thomas Norreys lord precedent oppose himself but assoon as he upon necessary occasion have withdraw his force to cork many of the monster man now first about October 1598. break into rebellion and join themselves with tyrones say force spoil the country burn the village and pull down the house and castle of the english 1598. against who especial the female sex they commit all abominable outrage and now they raise james Fitzthomas a Geraldine to be earl of Desmond which title have since the war of Desmond be suppress with condition that forsooth he shall be vassal to oneale the monster rebellion break out like a lightning for in one month space almost all the irish be in rebellious arm and the english be murder or strip and banish thus have inflame monster with the fire of rebellion and leave this sedition to be cherish and increase by this new earl of Desmond and other rebel of that province the Ulster force return back to Tyrone the infection which monster man have draw from the corrupt part in rebellion do more and more spread itself so as the old practice long hold by the arch-traitor Tyrone to induce they to a revolt now full attain their wish effect to the work whereof in the heart of the seditious there want not many strong motive as the hatred which the geraldine bear to those english undertaker of who I former speak in desmonds war which possess their ancestor land also the encouragement they receive by the good success of the rebel and no less the hope of pardon upon the worst event and to speak truth Munster undertaker above mention be in great part cause of this defection and of their own fatal misery for whereas they shall have build castle and bring over colony of english and have admit no irish tenant but only english these and like covenant be in no part perform by they of who the man of best quality never come over but make profit of the land other bring no more english then their own family and all entertain irish servant and tenant which be now the first to betray they if the covenant have be keep by they they of themselves may have make two thousand able man whereas the lord precedent can not find above two hundred of english birth among they when the rebel first enter the province neither do these gentle undertaker make any resistance to the rebel but leave their dwelling and flee to wall town yea when there be such danger in flight as great can not have be in defend their own whereof many of they have woeful experience be surprise with their wife and child in flight among the monster rebel be the viscount Mountgarret the earl of ormonds near kinsman and the baron of Cahir a butler and of the earl kindred both these pretend their discontent and malice against the say earl for cause of their revolt but more dangerous cause be suspect and royal force be quick oppose to the rebel bold attempt a general revolt be fear may you hold laughter or will you think that Carthage ever breed such a dissemble faedifragous wretch as Tyrone when you shall read that even in the midst of all these garboil and while in his letter to the king of Spain he magnify his victory beseech he not to believe that he will seek or take any condition of peace and vow constant to keep his faith plight to that king yet most impudent he cease not to entertain the lord lieutenant by letter and message with offer of submission this he do but not so submissive as before for now the gentleman be grow high in the instep as appear by the insolent condition he require Ireland be in this turbulent state many think it can not be restore but by the powerful hand of Robert earl of Essex this noble lord have from his youth put himself into military action of great moment so far as the place he hold in earl of Essex lord lieutenant court will permit and have of late year win much honour in some service by sea and land so as he have full possession of a superintendency over all martial affair and for his noble worth be general love and follow by the nobility and gentry in which respect the queen know he fit for this service he have long be a dear favourite to the queen but have of late lie so open to his enemy as he have give they power to make his embrace of military course and his popular estimation so much suspect of his sovereign as his greatness be now judge to depend as much on her majesty fear of he as her love to he and in this respect he may seem to the queen most unfit for this service but sure the earl be persuade that his hovour can not stand without embrace this action and since he affect it no man dare be his rival beside that his enemy glad put for ward this his design that they may have he at more advantage by his absence from Court. final the give ominous acclamation to his enterprise but the wise sort rather wish then hope happy effect either to his private or the public good in regard of the powerful enemy he leave in court whence all second be to come to he and of his own distract end though incline to the public good yet perhaps in aim at the speedy end of this war and some other particular not full concur with the same the earl of Essex when he first purpose to entertain the manage of the irish war advise and obtain that two regiment of old soldier shall be transport out of the low-countries into that kingdom namely these regiment land in Ireland before the earl come over and be then disperse by the earl into diverse regiment of new man to season they and to replenish they with sufficient officer the earl patent be grant with title of lord lieutenant and with more ample authority than many other lord deputy have former grant they for whereas other have power to pardon all treason felony and all offence except such treason as touch her majesty person her heir etc. and the counterfeit of money this exception be by the earl importunity leave out which he extort with wise providence since the lawyer hold all treason to touch the prince person and whereas other lord deputy have power to bestow all office except the chief reserve to the queen gift his lordship have power to bestow some of the chief and to remove all officer not hold by patent and to suspend such as hold by patent beside his lordship have power in many thing which never have be former give to any as to make martial law he be lord marshal of England and to punish the transgressor and to let the land of Tyrone and other rebel name to any person whatsoever and to their heir male reserve due rent to her majesty to command the ship already send and to be send into Ireland except the lord admiral be send forth to sea and commandment be give of join the say ship to his fleet and last to issue the treasure according to the two establishment with liberty to alter that which be sign by the lord in England with the advice and consent of the counsel of Ireland so as he exceed not the sum of the establishment he have a army assign he as great as himself require and such for number and strength as Ireland have never yet see the establishment be sign by the queen the four and twenty of march be the last day after the english account of the year 1598. it contain first the the establishment pay of the chief officer in the army the lord lieutenant general ten pound a shilling a day whereof the total in the year amount to thirteen thousand one hundred twenty seven pound sixteen shilling eight penny it contain further the pay of thirteen hundred horse divide into six and twenty band each band have a captain at four shilling a day a lieutenant at two shilling six penny a day a cornet at two shilling a day and fifty horseman each at fifteen penny a day whereof the total in the year amount to one and thirty thousand four hundred eight pound five shilling it contain further the pay of sixteen thousand footman distribute into one hundred and sixty band each band have a captain at four shilling a day a lieutenant at two shilling a day a ensign eighteen penny a day two sergeant a drum and a surgeon each at twelve penny a day and ninety four soldier and six dead pay allow to the captain at eight penny each by the day whereof the total in the year amount to two hundred twenty eight thousand two hundred forty six pound thirteen shilling four penny last it contain a extraordinary supply of six thousand pound to be allow by for spy guide messenger bark hire keep of prisoner building reparation reward and like charge the total of the establishment by the also consider that the thirteen hundred horse and sixteen thousand foot by new supply be make full twenty thousand the heavy burden of this year war in Ireland will appear the earl of Essex have in special charge from the queen to bend all his force against the chief traitor Tyrone and the Ulster rebel his confederate and withal to plant garrison at Loughfoyle and Balishannon to the end they may at the same time assail he and they at the back both which course his lordship have in all counsel persuade and often tax the omission of they thus with happy acclamation of the people who to so worthy a general in the head of so strong a army do ominate nothing but victory and triumph yet with a sunshine thunder happen as master Camden note for a ominous ill token this noble lord accompany with the flower of the english gentry and conduct on his way with many of the nobility take his journey from London towards Ireland in the end of the month of march and the beginning of the year 1599 and though cross an. 15999. with tempestuous weather wherein the earl of Kildare and some gallant gentleman accompany he in a little bark choose of purpose for speed be unfortunate cast away land within few day at Dublin where according to the manner of other governor he receive the Sword upon his lordship demand to be advertise from the counsel of the present state of that kingdom a collection thereof debate and agree upon in counsel and sign by the counsellor be present to his lordship the seventeen of Aptill be to this effect first for the province of lemster in the county of all the town and castle stand firm for the queen in the county of Lonford all the ofarrol be in rebellion except two chief man of that family and the castle of longford be hold by a english ward and horse the okennydaye five hundred foot thirty horse the burke in the lord burkes country two hundred foot four horse in the county of cork james fitzthomas the suppose earl of Desmond two hundred and five hundred foot in the county of Limrick Pierce Lacie with diverse sept have three hundred foot and fifteen horse in the county of kerrie the lord Fitz Morrice Thomas Oge John delahyde with other five hundred foot thirty horse in the county of Desmond call Oswyllivan bear and Oswillivan Moor country Dermod Mac Owen usurp the name of Mac Arty Moor have five hundred foot six horse in the county of Waterford the rebel have two hundred foot and ten horse in all the rebel of this province of monster be strong five thousand thirty foot and two hundred forty two horse this number the earl of ormond judge to be the least and think the horse one hundred more in number observe that all the city and port-towne and almost all the castle in this province of monster and many great lord and gentleman hold for the queen four and last for the province of connaught in the county of roscommen the castle of roscommen athlone Tulske boil and Ballinestawe be keep at her majesty charge and the rebel of diverse sept have five hundred foot fixtie horse in the county of sligo o connor sligo and diverse sept of rebel have three hundred foot and thirty horse and only the castle of Calony hold for the queen orworke in Leytrim call ororke country have six hundred foot sixty horse and not any castle be keep for the queen in the county of maio some three castle late hold for the queen but be think to be render up to Mac William who with his follower have six hundred foot sixty horse in the county of Galloway the town of Galloway of Atheurie and the castle of Milech hold for the queen but many sept of the country be in rebellion who have some four hundred ninety foot in the county of Clare the earl of thomonds brother who first be upon suspicion commit to prison by the say earl and after release with the Obryans and Mac Marres and other sept have six hundred foot fifty horse and not one castle be there keep for the queen in all the rebel of this province of connaught be strong three thousand and seventy foot two hundred and twenty horse and the rebel in all the four province be strong eighteen thousand two hundred forty six foot and two thousand three hundred forty six horse the earl of Essex in the month of April dispatch two letter to the lord in England by the first whereof he advertise they of this strength of the rebel and by the second that Tyrone have in counsel resolve first to hearten his confederate and strengthen they in their dependency on his protection then to make two head against the queen force the one in Ulster of some six thousand horse and foot under his own command and the other in Connaght of some four thousand horse and foot under odonnell command and further advertise their lordship that many in monster have take a solemn oath at a public cross in that province to be steadfast in their rebellion and that no traitor seek pardon but use such insolent behaviour as may well show they have no such thought that the mind of the very subject be so alienate from the english as well for religion as government as some who can bring one hundred horse and three hundred foot into the field upon private revenge will protest not to be able to serve the state with six horse or foot that every active borderer have a with the rebel and almost every one of the great in the state have some rebel or other to his client conclude that small or no assistance can be promise from the irish so as howsoever the queen army be great yet he dare bold say that the plaster will do no more than cover the wound after few day of rest good part of the english force be draw together this noble lord lieutenant give entrance to his first action from which the progress common receive a kind of ominous lustre or stain and therein he attempt not the head of the rebellion according to his own advice in England and the queen express command but be induce by some of the counsel in that state aim at their own private interest more than the public good to lead his force against some few rebel in monster where he take the castle of Cahir belong to Edward Butler baron of Cahir and make a great prey of the rebel cattle in those part he cast the terror of his force on the weak enemy who he scatter and constrain to fly into wood and mountain to hide themselves the fifteen of june while the lord lieutenant be yet in this monster journey he receive advertisement from a captain who he have employ by sea into the north to spy out tyrones action that two ship late come from Spain have put confidence in Tyrone who go from Dungannon to Loughfoyle about that business but they bring only munition not any treasure that Tyrone have give force to Brian Mac art son to art Mac baron that he may take pledge and watch over Neale Mac Brian who he suspect and have charge Mac Genis to do the like over Mac Cartan also suspect by he so as there be no possibility to parley with they according to the instruction give by his lordship that Tyrone keep his great pledge shane oneales son in a island within a strong fastness but as yet have neither gather at home nor receive from foreign part any treasure that both Tyrone and Odonnel have their agent in the out isle of Scotland to solicit the redshankes to assist they for pay that the king of Spain have promise they aid of man which they will not have land in Ulster but in some port of monster or at Galloway in Connaght that scot daily carry munition to they which traffic may be hinder by two galley with oar but no ship use sail can stop their passage that the gross of the northern rebel in Ulster and part of Connaght draw together will be nine thousand foot and one thousand four hundred horse that they be confident to draw the war into such a length as shall be unsupportable to the state of England to which end Odonnel have hire a mass of redshankes who be to be cess in Connaght and monster because Tyrone have deadly feud with some of the chief leader dare not trust they in Ulster beside that upon arrival of foreign treasure great multitude of those scot be like to flock unto they and to the same end Tyrone have make strong fastness or intrenchment as well upon the passage of Loughfoyle and Ballishanon where he leave force to resist the english garrison to be send thither as at the blackewater and Ballinemoyree himself purpose to meet the english army in the wood of Ballinemoyree between dundalke and the newry where he hope to make some of the best to drop and after to fall back at his pleasure to like fight of advantage which he have prepare at the blackewater so as the only mean sudden to break those rebel be to hire 4000 redshankes to break in upon they by advantage of their row boat into the heart of Tyrone between these intrenchment where they may easy take from he all his wealth consist in cattle and there entrench themselves and in despite of all tyrones force be supply with all necessary from the scottish land and indeed to this purpose the lord bourgh if he have not be prevent by sudden death have contract with those scot promise 4000 man for the first month pay 1200. pound the chief leader a colonel pay and certain captain the pay of a captain of 100 man after which month their sept be to be cess for their victual upon such country as they must have fight with the rebel for every morsel namely the son of Agnus mac connel with their sept upon the rout the glinne and north clandeboy who for the pretence they have to inherit that country will prosecute james mac Surley the possess thereof to the uttermost Donnel groom and his sept upon Yuogh be Mac Genis his country the Mac lane and their sept upon the south clandeboy and the Duffren all which sept be to put in pledge to the lord bourgh not to prey any under the queen protection and to depart the kingdom when his lordship shall please no long to make use of their service the five and twenty of june during the say monster journey the lord lieutenant write unto the queen this letter follow when this shall come to your majesty hand I know not but whensoever it have that honour give it leave I humble beseech your majesty to tell you that have now pass through the province of lemster and monster and be upon the frontier of Connaght where the governor and the chief of the province be with i I dare begin to give your majesty some advertisement of the state of this kingdom not as before by hearsay but as I behold it with my own eye the people in general have able body by nature and have get by custom ready use of arm and by their late success boldness to fight with your majesty troop in their pride they value no man but themselves in their affection they love nothing but idleness and licentiousness in their rebellion they have no other end but to shake off the yoke of obedience to your majesty and to root out all remembrance of the english nation in this kingdom I say this of the people in general for I find not only the great part thus affect but that it be a general quarrel of the irish and they who do not profess it be either so few or so false that there be no account to be make of they the irish nobility and lord of country do not only in their heart affect this plausible quarrel and be divide from we in religion but have a especial quarrel to the english government because it limit and tie they who ever have be and ever will be as absolute tyrant as any be under the sun the town be inhabit by man of the same religion and birth as the rest be so carry away with the love of gain that for it they will furnish the rebel with all thing that may arm they or enable they against the state or against themselves the wealth of the kingdom which consist in cattle oatmeal and other victual be almost all in the rebel hand who in every province till my come have be master of the field the expectation of all these rebel be very present and very confident that Spain will either so invade your majesty that you shall have no leisure to prosecute they here or so succour they that they will get most of the town into their hand ere your majesty shall relieve and reinforce your army so that now if your majesty resolve to subdue these rebel by force they be so many and so frame to be soldier that the war of force will be great costly and long if your majesty will seek to break they by faction among themselves they be covetous and mercenary and must be purchase and their jesuit and practise priest must be hunt out and take from they which now do solder they so fast and so close together if your majesty will have a strong party in the irish nobility and make use of they you must hide from they all purpose of establish english government till the strength of the irish be so break that they shall see no safety but in your majesty protection if your majesty will be assure of the possession of your town and keep they from supply the want of the rebel you must have garrison bring into they able to command they and make it a capital offence for any merchant in Ireland to trade with the rebel or buy or sell any arm or munition whatsoever for your good subject may have for their money out of your majesty store that which shall be appoint by order and may serve for their necessary defence whereas if once they be tradable the rebel will give such extreme and excessive price that they will never be keep from they if your majesty will secure this your realm from the danger of invasion assoon as those which direct and manage your majesty intelligence give notice of the preparation and readiness of the enemy you must be aswell arm and provide for your defence which provision consist in have force upon the coast inrole and train in have magazine of victual in your majesty west and northwest part ready to be transport and in have ship both of war and transportation which may carry and waft they both upon the first alarm of a descent the enrol and train of your subject be no charge to your majesty own coffer the provide of magazine will never be any loss for in use they you may save a kingdom and if you use they not you may have your old store sell and if it be well handle to your majesty profit the arm of your majesty ship when you hear your enemy arm to the sea be agreeable to your own provident and princely course and to the policy of all prince and state of the World but to return to Ireland again as I have show your majesty the danger and disadvantage which your servant and minister here shall and do meet withal in this great work of reduce this kingdom so I will now as well as I can represent to your majesty your strength and advantage first these rebel be neither able to force any wall town castle or house of strength nor to keep any that they get so that while your majesty keep your army in strength and vigour you be undoubted mistress of all town and hold whatsoever by which mean if your majesty have good minister all the wealth of the land shall be draw into the hand of your subject your soldier in the winter shall be caseful lodge and ready supply of any want and we that command your majesty force may make the war offensive and defensive may fight and be in safety as occasion be offer second your majesty horseman be so incomparable better than the rebel and their foot be so unwilling to fight in battle or gross howsoever they be desirous to skirmish and fight loose that your majesty may be always mistress of the champion country which be the best part of this kingdom three your majesty victual your army out of England and with your garrison burn and spoil the country in all place shall starve the rebel in one year because no place else can supply they four since no war can be make without munition and munition this rebel can have but from Spain Scotland or your own town here if your majesty will still continue your ship and pinnace upon the coast and be please to send a print proclamation that upon pain of death no merchant townsman or other subject do traffic with the rebel or buy or sell in any sort any kind of munition or arm I doubt not but in short time I shall make they bankrupt of their old store and I hope our seaman will keep they from receive any new five your majesty have a rich store of gallant colonel captain and gentleman of quality who example and execution be of more use than all the rest of your troop whereas the man of best quality among the rebel which be their leader and their horseman dare never put themselves to any hazard but send their kern and their hireling to fight with your majesty troop so that although their common soldier be too hard for our new man yet be they not able to stand before such gallant man as will charge they six your majesty commander be advile and exercise know all advantage and by the strength of their order will in all great fight beat the rebel for they neither march nor lodge nor fight in order but only by the benefit of their footmanship can come on and go off at their pleasure which make they attend a whole day still skirmish and never engage themselves so that it have be ever the fault and weakness of your majesty leader whensoever you have receive any blow for the rebel do but watch and attend upon all grosle oversight now if it please your majesty to compare your advantage and disadvantage together you shall find that though these rebel be more in number then your majesty army and have though I do unwilling confess it better body and perfect use of their arm than those man which your majesty send over yet your majesty command the wall town hold and champion country and have a brave nobility and gentry a better discipline and strong order than they and such mean to keep from they the maintenance of their life and to waste the country which shall nourish they your majesty may promise yourself that this action will in the end be successful though costly and that your victory will be certain though many of we your honest servant must sacrifice ourselves in the quarrel and that this kingdom will be reduce though it will ask beside cost a great deal of care industry and time but why do I talk of victory or of success be it not know that from England I receive nothing but discomfort and soul wound be it not speak in the army that your majesty favour be divert from i and that already you do boadil both to i and it be it not believe by the rebel that those who you favour most do more hate i out of faction then they out of duty or conscience be it not lament of your majesty faithful subject both there and here that a Cobham or a Kaleigh I will forbear other for their place sake shall have such credit and favour with your majesty when they wish the ill success of your majesty most important action the decay of your great strength and the destruction of your faithful servant yes yes I see both my own destiny and your majesty decree and do willing embrace the one and obey the other let i honest and zealous end a wearisome life let other live in deceitful and unconstant pleasure let i bear the brunt and die meritorious let other achieve and finish the work and live to erect trophy but my prayer shall be that when my soneraigne lose i her army may not loose courage or this kingdom want physic or her dear self miss Essex and then I can never go in a better time nor in a fair way till then I protest before God and his angel that I be a true votary that be sequester from all thing but my duty and my charge I perform the uttermost of my body mind and fortune abilitte and more shall but that a constant care and labour agree not with a inconstant health in a unwholesome and uncertain climate this be the hand of he that do live your dear and will die your majesty faithful servant Essex towards the end of july his lordp bring back his force into Lemster the soldier be weary sick and uncredible diminish in number and himself return to Dublin all that his lordp have do in this journey beside the scatter of the rebel weak troop be the take of Cahir castle and receive the l. of Cahir the l. roche and some other into her majesty protection who after his departure do either open fall again to the rebel party or secret combine with they while his lordp be in this journey some 600 man leave in the glinne by the unskilfulness of some young captain and soldier and the ill affection of some irish officer receive a disgraceful blow from the obirn whereupon his lordp now severe punish their fault disarm the soldier and execute the ten man call the captain to a martial court and discharge they and condemn to be shoot to death a irish lieutenant who have parley with the rebel and be think to have animate they then his lordp understand that the queen be much offend with this monster journey he cast in his letter the fault on the counsel of Ireland who advice by reason of their long experience in those war he think fit to follow at his first entry but withal give her majesty full assurance that he will present lead the army into Ulster against Tyrone himself yet these letter be scarce deliver when by other he signify a necessity of a journey into Ophalia and Leax near Dublin against the Oconnors and Omores who he break with ease himself lead some 1500 into Ophalia and send sir Christopher Blunt the marshal into leax with 1000 man under the command of sir Charles Percy and sir Richard moryson then at his return take a view of the army he find it so weaken as by letter sign by himself and the counsel there he desire a supply of 1000 foot out of England to enable he present to undertake the Ulster journey thus resolve to march northward he command sir Conyors Clifford governor of Connaght to draw his force up to belike that he may force Tyrone to send some of his force that way while he assail he on the other side sir conyers Clifford according march this way with one thousand four hundred foot by pole and the earl of southamptons troop of one hundred horse under the lead of captain John lephson with some other irish horse and come to the curlew mountain he leave the munition and carriage under the guard of the horse till he pass forward with the foot have try the passage he have not go far before Ororke and other rebel with he upon the advantage of wood bogges and a stony causey assail our man who at the first valiant repel they till the rebel find the munition our man have about they beginning to fail renew the charge with great fury than before at which time our man discourage with the want of powder almost all they have about they be spend and their store be behind with the carriage as also weary with a long march they have make before the skirmish begin to saint and take themselves to flight who the rebel pursue and kill some one hundred and twenty in the place among which the governor sir conyers Clifford and a worthy captain sir Alexander Ratcliff be lose beside as many more hurt whereof the great part recover and no doubt the rest have all perish if the horse have not valiant succour they for the lord of Dunkellyn who that day have most valiant behave himself send word to captain John jephson of their distress who present charge upon the causey and to the very skirt of the wood with such resolution as the rebel either think horse can not have serve there or expect advantage upon they in that boggy place stand gape on they and give way without any resistance for a good space in which our man have leisure to retire over a ford into the plain where the carriage be and thence to the abbey of the boil be very near the place afterward the rebel begin to charge our horse but their powder be almost spend captain jephson safe retire with the loss of some few horse in a consultation some be earnest to have march forward the next day but the lord of Dunkellin sir Arthur savage captain John jephson and many of the best judgement consider the governor be lose our troop utter dismay and Odonnel come down with all his force into those part think fit our man shall retire to their garrison so captain jephson all that night keep the ford while our foot in the silent night retire and in the morning when they be in safety he with the horse under his command go soft after they to the castle of athlone it be strange the rebel then present be but some two hundred and most of our man be old soldier how this defeat can be give but small accident in military affair be often cause of strange and great event for I have hear this mischance full attribute to a unorderly turn of the whole body of the van which though it be towards the enemy yet be mistake by some common soldier for a flight it cause a general rout in the mean time the foresay supply of one thousand foot be send out of England to the lord licutenant according to his and the counsel be request but few day after his lordship signify by his letter into England that he can do no more this season of the year then to draw thirteen hundred foot and three hundred horse to the border of Ulster whether he come about the ides of September and Tyrone two day together show himself and his troop upon distant hill to the english then Tyrone send hagan to the lord lieutenant to entreat a parley between his lordship and he which his lordship refuse answer that if Tyrone will speak with he he shall find he next day in arm in the head of the army the next day after a light skirmish one of tyrones horseman cry with a loud voice that Tyrone will not fight but will speak with the lord licutenant and that unarmed and both withdraw aside from the force the next day when his lordship march forward hagan meet he again and declare to he that Tyrone beseech the queen mercy and that he will vouchsafe to speak one word with he which grant he will in all humbleness attend his lordship at the Foard balla-clinch near the chief town of the county of louth his lordship send some before to view the Foard who find Tyrone there and he assure they that howsoever the water be something rise yet they may easy hear one another from each side his lordship be come thither Tyrone leave a troop of horse upon a hill not far off come down alone and put his horse up to the belly in the water with all humbleness salute his lordship stand on the other bank and there they pass many speech then Tyrone call his brother cormack Mac Gennys Mac Guire ever Mac Couley Henry Ouington and o Quin to the Foard the lord lieutenant have first call the earl of South-hampton sir George Bourcher sir Warham Sant Leger sir Henry Davers sir Edward Wingfeld and sir William constable to come down Tyrone very courtly salute each one and after short conference it be conclude that the next day commissioner shall meet to treat of peace and they make a mutual truce from that day for six week and so from six week to six week till the calends of May with caution that it shall be free to either side upon fourteen day warning first give to renew the war and if any of the earl of tyrones confederate shall not assent hereunto he leave they to be prosecute by the lord licutenant by this time the queen have receive his lordship last letter above mention signify that he can only for this winter draw to the confine of Ulster with one thousand three hundred foot and three hundred horse at which time to justify his resolution he send the judgement of the chief commander of the army subscribe with their hand date the one twenty of August that for that time more can not be enterprise for these reason that the army be unwilling to be draw towards Ulster so as many run away from their colour that many be sick that no plantation can be make this year at Loughfoyle nor any course take to divert tyrones force that the Connaght army be defeat that his lordship army have not above four thousand able man at the most that these be unable to stand against the rebel be six thousand shot and lie within strong intrenchment that much less any strong garrison can be leave in the north and a safe retreat make and last that those garrison if they can be leave there will more endanger the english be continual to supply they with victual in winter time then annoy the rebel her majesty be high offend that so royal a army maintain with her excessive charge have in six month effect nothing and now give no hope of any important service to be do against the rebel write a sharp letter to the lord Lieurenant and the counsel of Ireland as follow Elizabeth by the queen right trusty and right well belove cousin and councillor and trusty and well-beloved we greet you well have sufficient declare unto you before this time how little the manner of your proceed have answer either our direction or the world expectation and find now by your letter by Cuffe a course more strange if stranger may be we be doubtful what to prescribe you at any time or what to build upon by your own write to we in any thing for we have clear discern of late that you have ever to this hour possess we with expectation that you will proceed as we direct you but your action show always the contrary though carry in such sort as you be sure we have no time to countermand they before your departure no man counsel be hold sound which persuade not present the main prosecution in Ulster all be nothing without that and nothing be too much for that this draw on the sudden transportation of so many thousand to be carry over with you as when you arrive we be charge with more than the list or which we resolve to the number of three hundred horse also the thousand which be only to be in pay during the service in Ulster have be put in charge ever since the first journey the pretence of which voyage as appear by your letter be to do some present service in the interim while the season grow more commodious for the main prosecution for the which purpose you do importune with great earnestness that all manner of provision may be hasten to Dublin against your return of this resolution to defer your go into Ulster you may well think that we will have make stay if you have give we more time or it we can have imagine by the content of your own write that you will have spend nine week abroad at your return when a three part of july be pass and that you have understand our mislike of your former course and make your excuse of undertake it only in respect of your conformity to the counsel opinion with great protestation of haste into the north we receive another letter of new reason to suspend that journey yet a while and to draw the army into Ophalia the fruit whereof be no other at your come home but more relation of further misery of your army and great difficulty to perform the Ulster war then follow from you and the council a new demand of two thousand man to which if we will assent you will speedy Camden say only one thousand undertake what we have so often command when that be grant and your go onward promise by diverse letter we receive by this bearer now fresh advertisement that all you can do be to go to the frontier and that you have provide only for twenty day victual in which kind of proceed we must deal plain with you and that council that it be more proper to leave trouble themselves with instruct we by what rule our power and their obedience be limit and to bethink they if the course have be only derive from their counsel how to answer this part of they to train we into a new expense for one end and employ it upon another to which we can never have assent if we can have suspect it shall have be undertake before we hear it be in action and therefore we do wonder how it can be answer see yourattempt be not in the capital traitor country that you have increase our list but it be true as we have often say that we be draw on to expense by little and little and by protestation of great resolution in generality till they come to particular execution of all which course whosoever shall examine any of the argument use for excuse shall find that your own proceed beget the difficulty and that no just cause do breed the alteration it lack of number if sickness of the army be the cause why be not the action undertake when the army be in better state if winter approach why be the summer month of july and August lose if the spring be too soon and the summer that follow otherwise spend if the harvest that succeed be so neglect as nothing have be do then sure must we conclude that none of the sour quarter of the year will be in season for you and that counsel to agree of tyrones prosecution for which all our charge be intend further we require you to consider whether we have not great cause to think that the purpose be not to end the war when yourself have so often tell we that all the petty undertake in lemster monster and Connaght be but loss of time consumption of treasure and waste of our people until Tyrone himself be first beat on who the rest depend do you not see that he make the war with we in all part by his minister second all place where any attempt be offer who do not see that if this course be continue the war be like to spend we and our kingdom beyond all moderation as well as the report of the success in all part have blemish our honour andincourage other to no small proportion we know you can so much fail in judgement as not to understand that all the world see how time be dally though you think the allowance of that counsel who subscription be your echo shall serve and satisfy we how will you have deride any man else that shall have follow your step how often have you tell we that other which precede you have no intent to end the war how often have you resolve we that until loughfeyle and Ballishannon be plant there can be no hope of do service upon the capital rebel we must therefore let you know that as it can be ignorance so it can be want of mean for you have your ask you have choice of time you have power and authority more ample than ever any have or ever shall have it may well be judge with how little contentment we search out this and other error for who do willing seek for that which they be so loath to find but how shall that be hide which be so palpable and therefore to leave that which be past and that you may prepare to remedy matter of weight hereafter rather than to fill your paper with many impertinent argument be in your general letter savour still in many point of humour that concern the private of you our lord lieutenant we do tell you plain that be of that council that we wonder at your indiscretion to subscribe to letter which concern our public service when they be mix with any man private and direct to our counsel table which be not to handle thing of small importance to conclude if you will say though the army be in list twenty thousand that you have they not we answer then to our treasurer that we be ill serve and that there need not so frequent demand of full pay if you will say the muster-master be to blame we much muse then why he be not punish though say we may to you our general if we will that all defect by minister yea though in never so remote garrison have be affirm to we to deserve to be impute to the want of care of the general for the small proportion you say you carry with you of three thonsand five hundred foot when late weaugment you two thousand more it be to we past comprehension except it be that you have leave still too great number in unnecessary garrison which do increase our charge and diminish your army which we command you to reform especial since you by your continual report of the state of every province describe they all to be in worse condition than ever they be before you set foot in that kingdom so that whosoever shall write the story of this year action must say that we be at great charge to hazard our kingdom and you have take great pain to prepare for many purpose which perish without understanding and therefore because we see now by your own word that the hope be spend of this year service upon Tyrone and o Donnel we do command you and our council to fall into present deliberation and thereupon to send we over in writing a true declaration of the state to which you have bring our kingdom and what be the effect which this journey have produce and why these garrison which you will plant far within the land in Brenny and monaghan as other whereof we have write shall have the same difficulty second we look to hear from you and they joint how you think the remainder of this year shall be employ in what kind of war and where and in what number which be do and send we hither in write with all expedition you shall then understand our pleasure in all thing fit for our service until which time we command you to be very careful to meet with all inconvenience that may arise in that kingdom where the ill affect will grow insolent upon our ill success and the good subject grow desperate when they see the best of our preserve they we have see a writing in form of a cartel full of challenge that be impertinent and of comparison that be needless such as have not be before this time present to a state except it be do now with a hope to terrify all man from censure your proceed have it not be enough to have send we the testimony of the counsel but that you must call so many of those that be of slender experience and none of our counsel to such a form of subscription sure howsoever you may have warrant they we doubt not but to let they know what belong to we to you and to themselves and thus expect your answer we end at our manor of Nonsuch the fourteen of September in the one and forty year of our reign 1599 the lord lieutenant be nettle or rather gall with this letter resolve to lord instice leave Adam loftus the lord archbishop of Dublin and sir George Cary treasurer at war to govern the kingdom in his absence and present sail into England post to the court where altogether unlooked for he arrive the eight and twenty of September and present himself on his knee to the queen early in the morning be in her private chamber who receive he not with that cheerful countenance which she be wont to show he but after a brief conference command he to retire to his chamber and there to stay until he know she further pleasure from whence his lordship next remove be to the lord keeper house in state of a prisoner the list of the chief officer of the kingdom and the army and the disposal of the force make in September 1599 when the lord lieutenant leave the kingdom officer and governor lord lieutenant the earl of Essex lord precedent of monster void by the death of sir Thomas Norreys place of chief commissioner of Connaght void or provisional lieutenant of the army earl
