Totall of horse one thousand two hundred thirty one Totall of foot fourteen thousand four hundred twenty two the foresay lord justice be leave to govern Ireland upon the lord lieutenant sudden departure do easy rule the unwieldy helm of this kingdom so long as the sea be caline by the continuance of that truce former mention to be make between the lord lieutenant and Tyrone which be then conclude for six week and so from six to six week till the calends of May except either of they shall give fourteen day warn of their purpose to break the same but about the begin of December tyrones party enter into act of hostility the lord justice send sir William Warren to expostulate with he the cause of this breach he answer that he have not break the truce have according to the condition thereof give they fourteen day warning and that he have so do because the earl of Essex be imprison in England in who he have place all the confidence of his life and estate he be resolve not to rely on the council of that kingdom who have former deal deceitful with he therein final that he can not now renew the truce though he never so much desire it since he have already send Odonnel into Connaght and diverse of his confederate into other part to renew the war thus much their lordship advertise into England by letter full of diffidence profess that they fear the rebel will present assault the english pale likewise some ill affect to the earl of Essex advertise that among the rebel a common rumour be spread and that no doubt from Tyrone that England will short be in combustion within itself which increase the suspicion already conceive of the foresay conference have between the earl and Tyrone to the great prejudice of the earl be in durance now her majesty receive these advertisement and further understanding that the rebel daily increase in number and courage that the mere irish aspire to liberty and that the english irish if perhaps well affect yet be daunt by the ill success of the queen affair who great expense and royal army they have see vanish into smoke and be beside exasperate with a old grief to be exclude themselves from the government while english deputy be daily send to command they and have intelligence that Tyrone full of pride do every where boast himself as champion of the jish liberty and romish religion every where receive to his protection and cherish all seditious person help the weak with succour confirm the diffident with strong hope and that he be grow confident to root out the english government aswell by former success as by the succour of the king of Spain who already have send he some munition and a little money with brag promise of great supply and by the fair promise and large indulgence send from the pope with a crown of phoenix feather perhaps in imitation of pope Vrban the three who send John the son to king Henry the second than make lord of Ireland a little crown weave of peacock feather her majesty I say have these advertisement and find thereby that it be high time to make strong opposition to this rebellious monster make choice of Charles Charles blonnt l. Mountioy l. deputy blonnt lord Mountioy to be deputy of Ireland who her highness have the last year purpose to employ in that place at which time the earl of Essex though link in near friendship with he yet secret oppose this her majesty determination allege that the lord Mountioy have small experience in martial affair save that he have gain in the small time he serve in the low-countries add that he be too bookish and have too few follower and too small a estate to embrace so great a business so as the earl not obscure affect this employment himself to the end he may more strong confirm that dependency which all military man already have on he and his enemy willing give second to this his ambition that by his absence they may have better advantage to hurt he and to benefit themselves at that time the say earl easy draw this fatal government on his own shoulder which be one of the first step and not the least cause of his ruin be now to write of this honourable lord Mountioy my decease lord and master I do faithful profess and pray the reader confident to believe which I hope most easy to obtain of those who best know i that as in the duty of a servant I will not omit any thing I remember which may turn to his lordship honour so in my love to truth I will be so far from lie and flatter as I will rather be bold modest to mention some of his defect whereof the great worthy of the world can be altogether free to which I will only add that as I esteem lie and flattery by word of mouth among the live to be unfallible note of basenesle and ignorance so I judge these vice infamous and sinful when they be leave in print to deceive posterity since the first may detect falsehood by inquire the truth but the latter have no mean to rectify their misinform judgement thus I return to proceed in my former narration and first I will delineate after my best skill the true portraiture of this worthy lord body and mind than I will collect the counsel by which he tame this monster of rebellion and last I will descend by order of time to his lordship particular action but ere I take my pencil in hand to figure this noble lord person I must acknowledge my weakness such as I can full apprehend his complete worthiness and therefore desire that those of great judgement to discern the same will impute all defect to the unskilfulness of the workman and that with other to who his lordship be less know my rude pen may not derogate any thing from his due praise again give i leave to remember that which I receive from his mouth that in his childhood when his parent will have his picture he choose to be draw with a trowel in his hand and this Mot to rebuild the ancient house for this noble and ancient barony be decay not so muchby his progenitor prodigality as his father obstinate addiction to the study and practice of alchumy by which he so long labour to increase his revenue till he have almost full consume they now to the purpose let we observe how he fulfil this ominous presage in rebnild that noble house till by his untimely death the same be fatal eclipse again he be of stature tall and of very comely proportion his skin fair with little hair on his body which hair be of colour blackish or incline to black and thin on his head where he wear it short except a lock under his left ear which he nourish the time of this war and be weave up hide it in his neck under his ruff the crown of his head be in his latter day something bald as the forepart natural curl he only use the barber for his head for the hair on his chin grow slow and that on his cheek and throat he use almost daily to cut it with his scissors keep it so low with his own hand that it can scarce be discern as likewise himself keep the hair of his upper lip something short only suffer that under his hither lip to grow at length and full yet some two or three before his death he nourish a sharp and short pikedevant on his chin his forehead be broad and high his eye great black and lovely his nose something low and short and a little blunt in the end his chin round his cheek full round and ruddy his countenance cheerful and as amiable as ever I behold of any man only some two year before his death upon discontentment his face grow thin his ruddy colour fail grow somewhat swart and his countenance be sad and deject his arm be long and of proportionable bigness his hand long and white his finger great in the end and his leg somewhat little which he garter ever above the knee wear the garter of saint George order under the left knee except when he be boot and so wear not that garter but a blue in stead thereof above his knee and hang over his boot the description of his apparel may be think a needless curiosity yet must I add some few word thereof be cause have promise the lively portraiture of his body aswell as his mind the same can otherwise be so lively represent to the imagination beside that by his clothes some disability of his body to undertake this hard war may be conjecture and especial the temper of his mind may be lively shadow since the wise man have teach we that the apparel in some sort show the man his apparel in court and city be common of white or black tafetaes or satin and he wear two yea sometime three pair of silk stocking with black silk grogran cloak guard and ruff of comely depth and thickness never wear any fall band black beaver hat with plain black band a taffeta quilt waistcoat in summer a scarlet waistcoat and sometime both in winter but in the country and special keep the field in Ireland yea sometime in the city he beware jerkin and round hose for he never wear other fashion then round with lace pane of russet cloth and cloak of the same cloth line with velvet and white beaver hat with plain band and beside his ordinary stocking of silk he wear under boot another pair of woollen or worsted with a pair of high linen bootehose yea three waistcoat in cold wether and a thick ruff beside a russet scarf about his neck thrice fold under it so as I never observe any of his age and strength to keep his body so warm he be very comely in all his apparel but the robe of saint George order become he extraordinary well for his diet he use to fare plentiful and of the best and as his mean increase so his table be better serve so that in his latter time no lord in England may compare with he in that kind of bounty before these war he use to have nonrish breakfast as panadoe and broth but in the time of the war he use common to break his fast with a dry crust of bread and in the spring time with butter and sage with a cup of stale beer wherewith sometime in winter he will have sugar and nutmeg mix he feed plentiful both at dinner and supper have the choice and most (@) meat with the best wine which he drink plentiful but never in great excess and in his latter year especial in the time of the war aswell when his night sleep be break as at other time upon full diet he use to sleep in the afternoon and that long and upon his bed he take tobacco abundant and of the best which I think preserve he from sickness especial in Ireland where the foggy air of the bog and waterish foul plenty of fish and general all meat with the common sort always unsalted and green roast do most prejudice the health for he be very seldom sick only he be trouble with the headache which due and constant like a ague for many year till his death take he once every three month and vehement hold he some three day and himself in good part attribute aswell the reduce of this pain to these certain and distant time as the ease he therein find to the virtue of this herb he be very neat love cleanliness both in apparel and diet and be so modest in the necessity of nature as myself be at all hour but time of sleep admit into his chamber and I think his most familiar friend never hear or see he use any liberty therein out of the privilege of his private chamber except perhaps in irish journey where he have no withdraw room the tender use of his body and his dainty fair before the wary give Tyrone occasion upon hear of his come over to jest at he as if all occasion of do service will be pass ere he can be make ready and have his breakfast but by woeful experience he find this jest to be the laughter of Salomon foole. his behaviour be courtly grave and exceed comely especial in action of solemn pomp in his nature he love private retiredness with good fare and some few choice friend he delight in study in garden a house rich furnish and delectable for room of retreat in ride on a pad to take the air in play at shovelboard or at card in read playbook for recreation and especial in fish and fishpond seldom use any other exercise and use these right as pastime only for a short and convenient time and with great variety of change from one to the other he be undoubted valiant and wise he much affect glory and honour and have a great desire to raise his house be also frugal in gather and save which in his latter day decline to vice rather in greedy gather then in restrain his former bounty of expense so that howsoever his retiredness do alienate his mind from all action yet his desire of honour and hope of reward and advancement by the war yea of return to this retiredness after the war end make he hot embrace the force course of the war to which he be so fit by his wisdom valour and frugality that in short time he become a captain no less wise wary and deliberate in counsel then cheerful and bold in execution and more covetous in issue the public treasure then frugal in spend his own revenue and his care to preserve his honour and maintain this estate make he though cold entertain the like force course of a state counsellor at home after the war to the manage of which affair he be no less enable by the same valour wisdom and many other virtue have not the stream of his nature prevail to withdraw he from attend they further than to the only obtain of these his own private end but sure these disposition of nature beside other hereafter to be mention and these his private end make he of all man most fit for this irish employment wherein the queen and state long for a end of the war and groan under the burden of a unsupportable expense touch his affect honour and glory I may not omit that his most familiar friend must needs observe the discourse of his irish action to have be extraordinary please to he so that howsoever he be not prone to hold discourse with lady yet I have observe he more willing draw to those of this nature which the irish lady entertain he then into any other and as he have (@) that commendable yea necessary ability of a good captain not only to fight and manage the war well abroad but to write and set forth his action to the full at home so I have seldom observe any omission of like narration in he whereof he use to delate the more weighty serious and to mention the small at least by way of a jest touch his study or bookishness by some impute to he in detraction of his fitness to embrace a active employment he come young and not well ground from Oxford Vniver sity but in his youth at London he so spend his vacant hour with scholar best able to direct he as beside his read in history skill in tongue so far as he can read and understand the italian and french though he dare not adventure to speak they and so much knowledge at least in cosmography and the mathematics as may serve his own end he have take such pain in the search of natural philosophy as in diverse argument of that nature hold by he with scholar I have often hear he not without marvel at his memory and judgement to remember of himself the most material point the subtle objection and the sound answer but his chief delight be in the study of divinity and more especial in read of the father and schoolman for I have hear himself profess that be in his youth addict to popery so much as through prejudicate opinion no writer of our time can have convert he from it yet by observe the father consent and the schoolman idle and absurd distinction he begin first to distaste many of their opinion and then by read our author to be confirm in the reform doctrine which I be confident he profess and believe from the heart though in his innated temper he be not factious against the papist but be gentle towards they both in conversation and in all occasion of disputation and I will be bold to say that of a layman he be in my judgement the best divine I ever hear argue especial for dispute against the papist out of the father schoolman and above all out of the write word whereof some chapter be each night read to he beside his never intermit prayer at morning and night insomuch as I have often hear he with strange felicity of memory and judgement discover the papist false alleaging of the father and text or addition and omission in they and to urge argument strong and as much as beseem he scholarlike as well in discourse with jesuit and priest in Ireland more special at Waterford where he make the very seduce irish ashamed of they as upon diverse occasion with other papist his friend further in his nature he be a close concealer of his secret for which cause lest they shall be reveal and because he love not to be importune with suit a free speaker or a popular man can not long continue his favourite he be spare in speech but when he be draw to it most judicious therein if not eloquent he never use swear but rather hate it which I have see he often control at his table with a frown brow and a angry cast of his black eye he be slow to anger but once provoke speak home his great temper be most scene in his wise carriage between the court faction of his time he be a gentle enemy easy pardon and calm pursue revenge and a friend if not cold yet not to be use much out of the high way and something too much reserve towards his dear minion beside that the strength of his judgement make he so confident as they have more power in second his counsel then in divert or alter they to his servant he be mild seldom reprove they and never with ill word for his look of displeasure be sufficient to check they and the best sort near he do so well know he as they serve and observe he as much almost by his look as his word he make no servant partner of his secret further than his place necessary give he knowledge thereof neither can any of they lead he or if any do it be more by art to know his humour than power to sway they I can say that he be bountiful to they some of their place draw profit which can no more be stop than the miller can stay the drain of his water through his dam gate otherwise his gift to they be rare and spare so as if it be above a hundred pound it be no morsel for a servant yet still he keep their hope so green as may continue their diligence and at his death he give a thousand pound by will to be divide by his executor discretion among they they who have his care may easy season he with good or ill opinion of his servant and stranger by reason he drink in their speech without utter they only his judgement be excellent to discern the truth of the relation aswell out of the informer passion as observe the other action he keep his word in public affair inviolable without which he can never have be trust of the irish but otherwise in his promise he be delatory and doubtful so as in all event he be not without a evasion last in his love to woman for as wanton peace succeed bloody war so in the last period of his life after the irish war grief of unsuccessfull love bring he to his last end he be faithful and constant if not transport with self-love more than the object and therein obstinate this worthy lord Mountioy be he who know valour sound wisdom grave constancy and singular temper two old counsellor of Ireland well observe do on their death bed as it be by divine faculty pronounce to be the man by who tyrones fatal rebellion in which their thought and endeavour have long be weary be to be suppress if ever the english be to recover the helm of that government neither do their presage deceive we herein for like another Fabius he do by the ensue deliberate and slow counsel restore his country decline power 1. he entertain and cherish especial at his first enter the government when he be yet unskilful in the affair of that state and war all active spirit who endeavour he see like to be of good use in that great action and this he do rather with a please familiarity then with any large bounty 2. the heart of the english common soldier break with a currant of disastrous success he hearten and encourage by lead they wary especial in his first action be more careful that our man shall not be foil then that the rebel shall be attempt with boldness to this end also and that he may be ever at hand as well to encourage and direct they fight as to second they by any accident dismay he brave adventure his person more than in the opinion of military wise man a general shall ordinary hazard himself howsoever I must confess the nature of the irish fight maintain upon passage by sudden eruption of hide rogue do more expose the general to these danger than any other war and such be his forwardness as his lordship servant may without offence bold say they be a small part of this great action for howsoever we have neither stipend in the war with the soldier nor pension with they after the war end yet by reason of this our lord extraordinary forwardness to put himself into danger and for that the rebel use most common to assault-upon woody pace and difficult passage where every man must needs be in danger and they most who ride in the best troop it can not be but that we shall have our share in the adventure of our person and lest I shall sceme to arrogate that to myself and my fellow which be not due to we the event may clear this point since in this short war not to speak of many less danger my lord himself have his horse shoot under he his galloglasse carry his helmet have the same bruise with the graze of a bullet upon it yea his lordship very grayhound likewise use to wait at his stirrup be shoot through the body among his lordship chaplain doctor Lattwara be kill and mast. Ram have his horse shoot under he among his lordship secretary master cranmer be kill and myself have my thigh bruise with a shot I receive in my saddle among the gentleman of his lordship chamber master chidley have his horse kill under he master do be shoot in the leg and master saint John a gentleman attend near his lordship be kill I have hear a pleasant report of a general in our age who on the contrary be so spare to hazard himself as a gentleman his follower hear some marvel at a cold piece of service perform by his mast merry reply for his own excuse that he go to follow his general and not to go before he but I will bold say that if our noble lord follower do well attend his person they find danger enough without seek it but enough and I fear too much of this point I will now return to this worthy lord counsel by which he effect this great work 3. the rebel be swell to the height of pride by their full number and much more by continual success in their action he proceed in like sort with they as former with his own man at the first wary taste they with light skirmish yet he so prudent and brave pursue his attempt as he still carry what he atempt 4. the wise distribution of the force avail he much for first he plant garrison upon the chief rebel country as likewise he compass Tyrone on every side with they which keep the rebel at home so as they can not second one another for fear of lose their own good 5. and whereas other deputy use to make some two or three journey in a summer against the rebel and then do lead a great army with they and whereas this kind of service never tooko any good effect as well because the bruit thereof come long before to the rebel as because these great force can not long be keep together so as the rebel hear the bruit of any such journey take victual with they for certain day and assemble themselues-togeth do lie upon the bog and hard passage where without danger to themselves they be able to annoy the great army can be lead against they this noble lord mount-ioy on the contrary as I say by garrison keep they at home himself keep the field with some thousand foot and two hundred horse whereof myself have many time observe the great part to be english irish and not only be able to affront Tyrone himself special since the garrison lie upon his country draw towards he at the same time on all side together but also by reason of his singular secrecy in keep his purpose unknown and cast out false report of they to deceive the rebel have the opportunity to assail and spoil any one of the rebel on the sudden while he keep all the rest like dare lark in continual fear aswell of himself as of the garrison adjoin 6 again where other deputy use to assail the rebel only in summer time this lord prosecute they most in the winter be common five day at least in the week one horseback all the winter long this break their heart for the air be sharp and they naked and they be drive from their lodging into the wood bare of leaf they have no shelter for themselves beside that their cattle give they no milk in the winter be also waste by drive to and fro that they be thus trouble in the seed time can not sow their ground and as in harvest time both the deputy force and the garrison cut down their corn before it be ripe so now in winter time they carry away or burn all thest oar of victual in secret place whether the rebel have convey they 7 again he have a special care to cut down and clear the difficult passage that so our force may with more safety meet together and upon all occasion second one another 8 for protection and pardon the easy obtain whereof have former encourage the rebel aswell to enter into rebellion as to break their faith after submission in hope to be again receive to mercy although it be necessary for the state in this general rebellion like a mother to open her bosom to her child lest be drive to despair they shall plunge themselves into all mischief yet he never receive any to mercy but such as have so draw blood on their fellow rebel and be themselves make so poor as there be small danger of their relapse to which end he forbid all conference and parley with the rebel by pretence whereof many treacherous plot have former be draw by the false-hearted subject and many corruption have be practise by some covetous commander but to such as be receive to mercy that he may take away the diffidence they have long conceive of the state he keep his word inviolable 9 and whereas these rebellious people be by nature clamorous which make they tedious in complaint and also use great oppression under the shadow of justice which make they continual importunc the governor with petition which be sign by they give those irish lord a shadow of authority to oppress the people by show the governors hand and conceal the matter to which he subscribe this worthy lord deputy for their encouragement in the first use singular patience in hear their tedious complaint and for the second give they such delatory ansiver as may well hearten they in obedience but can no way strengthen their tyranny over the poor people 10 to conclude nothing further this noble lord more in his design than his singular temper not so much in secrecy and in sparingness of speech though many great captain have hinder their proceed by let fall rash speech as more special in court faction for he use in such sort the familiar love of the earl of Essex in his doubtful course as he not only keep he from entertain dangerous counsel so long as he live with he in England but demean himself towards his enemy with such moderation as he little provoke their envy yea rather gain a inclination of their good affection towards he so as they at this time govern all the affair in England be ready to give all possible second to his end which as I former show do aim at nothing but the speedy end of this war by these counsel this worthy lord restore the decline state of Ireland from the desperate term wherein he now find it I have before set down in the earl of Essex his government the power of the rebel through all the province of Ireland and have show that at his lordship leave that kingdom the same be nothing abate and from that time the rebel be the rebel strength in all part increal the mountain rebel in the county of Dublin than 480 foot 20 horse now by the go out of the Walshes and harrol be increase 100 in the county of Kildare the rebel then in number 220 foot and 30 horse be now more than double by the go out of the briminghams all the leynagh and many of other sept in the two county of Catherlough and wexford the rebel then 750 foot and 50 horse be now increase a hundred in the county of Ophaly five strong castle then hold by the english be now betray and above 468 foot and 12 horse than be in rebellion but now Mac coughlan be go out with 200 more and the oaoine with 100 more be now in rebellion in the county of meath the rebel be increase in number 150 by the delahide the rochfords hussy and daey and beyond the river capt. John o Roly have then 100 foot in her majesty pay well arm be now revolt in the county of westmeath the rebel then 140 foot and 20 horse be now increase 100 at least by the revolt of the elder son of William nugent second brother to the lord of Deluin with diverse of the pettye and daiton in the county of longford the rebel then 120 foot be now increase 180 so the rebel in Lemster be then in the whole number 3048 foot and 182 horse be now increase 1280 and make in all 4510. for the province of monster Tyrone in his present journey thither take pledge of almost all the irish lord and gentleman he number of the rebel be now there increase beyond estimation for the province of Connaght the rebel be increase three hundred by the revolt of o conner sligo beside the uncertainty of Tybot Long who have one hundred irish man in her majesty pay so as at this time I may bold say the rebellion be at the great strength the mere irish puff up with good success and blood with happy encounter do bold keep the field and proud disdain the english force great part of the english-irish be in open action of rebellion and most part of the rest tempofise with the state open profess obedience that they may live under the protection thereof but secret relieve the rebel and practise with they for their present and future safety among the english the worthy general of this age partly by this fatal war partly by the faction at home be so waste as the best judgement can hardly find out any man fit to command this army (@) hiefe the english common soldier by looseness of body the natural sicknosse of the country by the poverty of the war in which nothing be to be gain but blow and by the late defeat wherein great number of they have perish be altogether out of heart the colonel and commander though many in number and great in courage and experience yet by these consideration of the army weakness be somewhat deject in mind yea the very counsellor of state be so diffident as some of they in late conference with Tyrone have descend I know not upon what warrant to a abject entreaty for a short cessation not to speak of the general distraction of the heart of all man in England and much more of the soldier by the faction of this age between the worthy earl of Essex now imprison and his enemy able to ruin a great kingdom much more to divert the success of any great action and the general voice be of Tyrone among the english after the defeat of blackwater as of (@) among the roman after the defeat of Cannae thou know how to overcome but thou know not how to use victory to conclude not only the remote part but the very heart of the kingdom now languish under the contagion of this rebellion leax and Ophalia be possess by the o and the o conners and the glynne or mountainous country on the south-west side of Dublin be in the hand of the (@) and o (@) and more remote of the cavanaghs who nightly make excursion to the very gate of the city give alarm of war to the long ground senate and as it be to the chair of estate in this miserable estate be Ireland when the lord Mountiey like a good planet with a fortunate aspect begin to shinethereon who happy action I will now set down particular yet as brief as I can the ten of january towards the end of the year 1599 the lord of England signify an. 1599 by their letter to the lord archbishop of Dublin and sir George Carey treasurer at war which be then lord justice of that kingdom that from that day forward the entertainment due to they as lord justice shall cease and be confer on Charles Blount lord Mountioy who her majesty have make lord deputy and now Tyrone who hitherto have contain himself in the north only make short excursion from thence into the pale be proud of victory and desirous to show his greatness abroad resolve with his force to measure the length of Ireland and to the end he may by his presence strengthen and increase the rebellion in monster which in absence by practice he have raise under the religious pretence of visit a piece of Christ cross keep for a holy relic in the monastery of the holy cross in the county of tipperary he enter this journey about the twenty of januarie on the three and twenty the rebel of the Brenny meet he in the cavan from whence he march forward take the rebel of lemster in his company and lead with he some two thousand five hundred foot and two hundred horse leave the rest of his force and the gentleman of the north to guard those part the intent of his journey be to set as great combustion as he can in monster and so take pledge of the rebel to leave they under the command of one chief head this month of january her majesty sign that warrant which be vulgar call the great warrant for Ireland whereby authority be give to the lord treasurer and chamberlain of the exchequer in England that according to a establishment after sign by her majesty the first of February and to begin that day wherein the army be reduce to twelve thousand foot and one thousand two hundred horse they shall pay to the treasurer at war for Ireland such sum as shall be sign by six of the privy counsel of England the lord treasurer the principal secretary and the under-treasurer always be three of they second above the four thousand pound for extraordinary therein mention to pay he such sum as shall by the same be sign three to pay in like sort according to a establishment or list of officer and other not contain in the former establishment it not exceed yearly fifteen thousand pound which list be then to be sign by the lord of her majesty counsel four to pay in like sort diverse officer payable out of the revenue in case the revenue extend not to pay they five to pay in like sort all sum for reinforcing the army for leavyes of man for conduct transport and victual they at sea according to the rate of the first establishment the establishment sign by her majesty the first of February 1599 the lord deputy entertainment to be pay according to the list after follow which list be to be sign by the lord officer of the army lieutenant of the army threell sergeant twenty general of the victual ten four commissary of victual whereof three at six and the four at eight twelve colonel each at ten a provost Martial for Loughfoyle another for Ballishannon each at four shilling four thousand four hundred fifty three pound the pay of three hundred horse divide into six band each band consist of fifty viz the captain four shilling lieutenant two shilling six penny cornet two shilling and fifty horseman at eighteen penny a piece the pay of two hundred horse divide into four band each band consist of fifty viz. captain four lieutenant two six d. cornet two and fifty horseman at fifteen d. a piece the pay of seven hundred horse divide into fourteen band each band consist of fifty viz. captain four shilling lieutenant two shilling six penny cornet two shilling fifty horseman at twelve penny a piece twenty nine thousand two hundred threescore thirteen pound the pay of twelve thousand footman divide into 120 band each band consist of a hundred head viz. captain four s. lieutenant two shilling ensign eighteen d. two sergeant a drum and a surgeon at twelve d. a piece and fourscore fourteen soldier and six dead pay at eight d. a piece one hundred threescore eleven thousand one hundred fourscore and five pound extraordinary viz. for send of letter hire of bark for passage of packquet for gift and reward for espy from abroad or at home carriage of treasure victual or munition and the like etc. for a whole year four thousand pound two hundred eight thousand nine hundred and eleven pound the lord Mountioy hasten away from court do not stay for the lord sign of the above mention second establishment as a thing of ordinary course continue for many year with little or no alteration and be now in this journey towards Ireland the ten of February he write to master secretary from daintry entreat he that whereas her majesty not withstand the contrary opinion of all admit to that consultation have reduce the army to twelve thousand foot and that he find by letter from the counsel and other commander in Ireland a general concur in opinion that these force be not sufficient especial since the plantation of Loughfoyle and Ballyshanon garrison be present to be make and that Tyrone be now master of the field have lead his force in person as far as monster he will move her majesty to give he power to retain one or two thousand in list of those english which otherwise he be to cast the above mention second establishment or list of diverse officer and servitor not contain in the former establishment which list be sign by the lord the eleven of February the end of the year 1599 the lord deputy ordinary entertainment one hundred pound (@) (@) thirteen hundred pound to he for a band of horseman in his family four pound four shilling to he for fifty footman in his family eight penny a man the treasurer at war thirty five shilling the marshal at five shilling nine penny the master of the ordinance three and twenty shilling eight penny note that the above name as also the chief governor of province undernamed have beside in the army the command of a band of foot or horse or both diverse minister of the ordinance twenty five shilling two penny mustermaster two shilling eight penny five thousand three hundred seven (@) seven shilling eleven d. the lord President one hundred thirty three (@) his diet at ten pound a week and so five hundred twenty pound his guard of horse and foot at thirty shilling seven penny halfpenny chief justice one hundred pound second justice sixty six pound thirteen shilling four penny queen attorney thirteen pound six shilling eight penny clerk of the counsel twenty pound provost Martial two hundred fifty five pound ten shilling one thousand six hundred threescore seven pound eight shilling two penny half penny governor of ten s. for increase one hundred (@) chief Connaght justice one hundred pound for his diet forty pound clerk of the counsel twenty pound for his diet twenty pound provost Martial one hundred two pound thirteen shilling one penny halfpenny beside twelve horse out of the army five hundred sixty five pound three s. two penny halfpenny governor at four shilling four penny beside his entertainment Ulster as a colonel governor of Carickfergus and dundalke no entertainment but as colonel of the army threescore pound sixteen shilling eight penny governor of the queen county at six shilling eight penny provost Lemstor Martial of the army four shilling three penny provost Martial of Lemster five shilling seven penny and to both provost horse to be assign out of the army at the lord deputy discretion three hundred one pound two shilling seven penny warder in diverse province three thousand thirty one pound seven penny halfpenny pensioner forty four at four pound nineteen shilling two penny almsman fourteen at six penny irish a piece commissary of muster twenty at six shilling eight penny a piece three thousand one hundred twenty two pound five s. fix fourteen thousand fifty five pound four shilling eight penny halfpenny the same day this list be sign be the eleven of February the lord by their letter to the lord deputy be yet in England but new go from London and in his way towards Ireland appoint that the ship of Bristol which have transport one thousand two hundred foot from thence to Dublin shall there be stay to the end they may transport a thousand man which be to be send from Dublin to meet with three thousand more send out of England out of which the garrison of be to be strengthen and a new garrison plant at Loughfoyle the lord Mountioy lie at Westchester for a passage into Ireland and there receive notice that the imprison earl of Essex have sign a submission to the queen whereupon her majesty begin to be incline to show he mercy direct his letter thence the eighteen of February to sir Robert Cecil secretary of state therein avow that as his love make he interest in that noble earl fortune so he will thankful acknowledge from he such favour as he shall be please to show that distress earl withal protest that he will always be a free man and slave to no man humour but as he in this irish employment expect all favourable second from he according to his noble promise whereupon his hope chief rerely so he will ever be honest and thankful towards he in all occasion and upon these term all exchange of good office pass between this lord deputy and master secretary till the fatal death of that noble earl of Essex hereafter to be mention and the lord deputy participation of that ruin make he change his style and never to cease till he have confirm a near friendship between himself and the secretary at least as entire as greatness admit as hereafter shall be show the twenty six of February the lord deputy land in Ireland and there receive a new lord precedent of monster the sword and within few day by warrant out of England he grant her majesty letter patent to sir George Carew to be lord precedent of monster which place liad lie void some few month from the death of sir Thomas Norreys the 27 he receive advertisement from the earl of ormond general of the english force till his come over that Tyrone be in the west part of monster have about he not only his own force but those of the rebel of that province which be so great as he have not hitherto power to oppose they but now have gather all the queen force he can make purpose the next morning to set forward towards he the five of march his lordship receive advertisement from other part that Tyrone can not escape in his return to the north but either over the river Shanon which passage the earl of Thomond and Clanrickard may easy stop or by the westward border of the pale where if his lordship will draw his force to athboye Ballymore and athlone it be not possible for he to escape they that Tyrone have thus engage himself presume on the corruption of the state and little expect his lordship so sudden come over so as if his lordship forgive he this fault he be not like to catch he again in the like neither can any thing but want of intelligence make his lordship fail in stop the return of Tyrone and his force into the north. advise his lordship to be wary in credit intelligence which be common false and make of purpose and to expect that beside the know enemy and a confuse war he shall find a break state a dangerous counsel and false heart subject the eight of march the earl of ormond send advertisement that Tyrone purpose to pass the river Shanon that he have write to the earl of Thomond to draw towards he that they may oppose his passage but that his lordship can not perform his order by reason that the mayor of Leymricke will not afford he carriage for his victual that Tyrone in scatter troop and a cowardly manner hasten his return and that present day have march four and twenty mile without any stay that sir Warham send Leger and sir Henry power joint commissioner for govern of monster with the force under their charge have meet near cork with Hugh Mac gwter chief lord of fermanagh in the north and that in the encounter sir Warham send Leger and the say Mac Guire be kill that his lordship have burn all the town where the traitor may find relief and that they use the same course towards her majesty subject the same day the lord deputy receive further advertisement from monster that Tyrone be compass in by the earl of ormond on the one side and the earl of Thomond on the other and by the commissioner force on the three side who rule the province after the death of sir Thomas Norreys until a lord precedent shall be choose for he that be new send over be yet at Dublyn that the mayor of Lymbricke have commandment to lay ship and boat to hinder his passage by that haven as likewise the mayor of galway to interrupt his passage by sea and the earl of Clanrickard to stop his passage by land through Connaght so as how soever he be five thousand strong in able man beside many of base sort yet he be far from any second of Ulster man in who the chief strength of the rebellion consist and no way able to return thither his utter confusion be confident hope but these be only irish oftentation of service which seldom use to take effect and many time be not true intend as the sequel will show and lest the lord deputy shall expect faithful deal of the english irish subject in the other kind of service by supply the army of necessary the nobility and gentry of the very english pale the same day exhibit a petition to his lordship to prevent the opinion of disloyalty upon refusal of such supply by pretend of disabillitie upon the great spoil which aswell the rebel as the english soldier have make upon all the inhabitant the lord deputy have write a former letter to master secretary in excuse of not reduce the army from fourteen thousand foot to twelve thousand according to the new establishment aswell because the same be to begin the first of February which his lordship can not effect since he arrive not in Ireland until the twenty six of the same month as also because the army be present far divide the great part thereof be with the earl of ormond and for that whensoever they return the discharge company must present be reduce into some other or else so many man and arm shall be mere lose as the lord justice have late find by experience when determine to cast a company of one hundred and fifty be by pole a hundred of the old and best soldier with purpose next day to deliver they to other captain upon the divulge thereof only three of the whole company with their arm can be find to be so transmit to this letter former write and persuade that the two thousand might still be continue in pay his lordship receive the follow answer from her majesty date the fifteen of March. Elizabeth although we have upon your earnest request in who affection and duty we do repose trust and confidence yield to the continuance of fourteen thousand foot for some small time both because we conceive that according to your reason it will give good assurance to the plantation of Loughfoyle and the reduction of lemster and prevent the present terror which this proud attempt of tyrones to pass over all the kingdom have strike into the heart of all our subject and will increase if we shall present have abate our number yet must welet you know that we do expect at your hand and do determine that assoon as the present bruit be pass you shall diminish the same by little and little hereafter according to our first determination for we have have too good proof of that government as not to know and discern that all the mischief of our service have grow most by lack of discretion and order by vain journey whilst better opportunity have be lose by undiscreet carriage of all secret purpose by place captain of small merit or experience and which be above all by nourish the irish who be snake in our bosom whilst we hold they and when they be out do convert upon ourselves the experience and strength they have get by our make they to be soldier and therefore you shall understand now that although we have be content to grace some such as be of noble house and such other as have draw blood on the rebel with charge of company yet we find it now grow to a common opinion that it be as good to be a rebel as a subject for rebel find and feel it that they shall be hire even with whole company in our pay only to forbear do harm and not for have do any such service as may make they irreconciliable and further we see that other that be in pay in their own country be so far from do service on their neighbour that be out as when they tarry in for a show they be the chief mean under hand to help the rebel with such powder and munition as to our no small charge we put into their hand to be use against they in this point therefore we command you henceforth to be considerate and not to be induce to put such in pay as spend our treasure only to their own advantage upon this supposition or brag of they that they must run to the enemy if they be not entertain for when we consider the effect that be derive from our charge to have so many foot and horse of the irish entertain only to save their own cow and country we be of opinion so they go not with our arm to the rebel that it be better service for we to save our treasure then to pay for their body see they that live by the war better than they shall do in peace intend nothing less than our service and therefore we command you not only to raise no more when these shall be decay but to keep they unsupplied that be already and as they waste to casse their band for we can never allow of this entertainment of they whilst you be force to keep the 2000 man for our service you may keep the captain uncass but not give any warrant to they to supply their company with any more irish. we do also require you that you do seek by all mean possible where the irish be entertain to use their service as far from their own country as may be wherein we pray you especial to take care in the province of Connaght where there be so many irish band together and rather to draw some of they to serve else where and send english in their stead this shall serve for the present to answer your dispatch wherein we do write to you who we know to love our commandment more direct in this point of our desire to have our charge abate than we do to you and the counsel together because we will have they apprehend see you think such a opinion will be good that our army shall not be so soon abate as we hope you will wherein notwithstanding we do refer thing to your discretion who we will trust with the charge of 2000 man see we have commit to you our whole kingdom etc. his lordship in a second dispatch to master secretary have write that Tyrone have pass through the pale into monster with some one thousand five hundred horse and foot of such sort as so many of the queen worst man be able to encounter they be now in monster with a army of 4 thousand in reputation and be there attend by the queen army of three thousand foot and three hundred horse command by the earl of ormond so as only the dregs of the queen army be now near he out of which notwithstanding he hope to be able to draw one thousand five hundred foot and three hundred horse and therewith to make head against Tyrone at his return but in regard the plantation of Loughfoyle and ballyshannon be present command he whether sir Henry Dockura be to ship three thousand out of England and another thousand be to be ship from Dublin these one thousand be part of the man he be to draw against Tyrone be dare not lead they far from the sea and so may perhaps be force to loose good occasion of service whereas if these thing have be leave to his discretion he will have defer the plantation of Ballishannon to a time of more safety and with these one thousand man and their munition to be send thither will now attend Tyrone in his passage with so great advantage as he be not likely to find hereafter and if he escape will present have put himself in the head of the earl of ormonds troop to prosecute he into the north and will further have advise sir Henry Dockwra in his passage from Chester to Loughfoyle to descend at Carickfergus and thence to take five hundred old soldier leave so many new in their room to this dispatch his lordship receive the queen gracious answer by a letter date the sixteen of this month wherein allow his beginning and approve his reason the forbear present to plant Ballishannon and the order of Loughfoyle plantation and the dispose of garrison apt for the defence of such as in that case offer to return to due obedience be all free leave to his lordship discretion with promise to make good construction of his action be confident that they have no other object but loyali service the lord deputy have draw as many together as he can about to lie for Tyrone in his return out of monster receive advertisement the fifteen of this present that Tyrone hear of his preparation to meet he have leave a thousand Connaght rogue to assist Desmond and some eight hundred man with Richard Butler and have make captain tirrell chief commander of all the lemster rebel be steal out of monster with six hundred in his company and have pass the enney and so escape into the north. whereupon the lord deputy the same day write to the earl of ormond to send back from monster the force he have draw thither out of lemster and with all send he her majesty letter import thanks for the service he have do and her desire that he will still hold the place of lieutenant of the army in the acceptance whereof the lord deputy profess that he shall esteem himself much honour and will be ready after put off the person which now be impose on he with much contentment to be command by his lordship the same time the lord deputy advertise master secretary that his intelligence have be so bad not only in false report of tyrones purpose but also in the relation of the force he have with he to be far great than indeed they be by which intelligence of false heart subject to discourage the queen force the rebel use to prevail more then by fight and now hope to discourage he at his first come from any present attempt against tyrones return as in one and the same day he first hear together of tyrones look back out of monster come into Leymster and pass over the enney and the next day be assure of his escape he then receive the first intelligence the former letter of the eight of March be not till then deliver that ever come to his hand from the earl of ormond concern Tyrone who in this return have go further in three day then at his set forth in thirteen have in one day march twenty seven mile so speedy as he can not overtake any of his troop with the queen force though he march after he twenty mile in four hour add his purpose to make present head towards the north without which diversion (@) rebel the (@) to be plant at Loughfoyle be like to run a dangerous to tune and withal send some of tyrones mandate by which he summon the (@) of monster to appear before he and to join with he of which I have think good for the strangeness of the form to insert this one follow o Neale commend he unto you moorish Fitz Thomas o Neale request you in God name to take part with he and fight for your conscience and right and in so do Oncale will spend to see you right in all your affair and will help you and if you come not at oneale betwixt this and tomorrow at twelve of the clock and take his part oneale be not behold to you and will do to the uttermost of his power to overthrow you if you come not to he at further by Saturday noon from knock dumayne in calrie the four of February 1599 oneale request you to come speak with he and do give you his word that you shall receive no harm neither in come nor go from he whether you be friend or not and bring with you to oneale fitzgerald subscribe o Neale the seven of march the lord deputy be advertise that Tyrone return to Dungannon his house the fifteen day and bring with he out of monster four pledge of desmonds faith unto he that the earl of Clanrickard have swear so soon as the lord of Dunkellyn his elder son return out of England to take no long day than may next to join with Tyrone and enter into action so the irish term rebellion and that Tyrone have call the lord of the north together to consult about the opposition to be make against the intend plancation of the english garrison at Loughfoyle the twenty of March master secretary write to the lord deputy that the earl of Essex hitherto restrain in the lord keeper house have find the grace with her majesty to be send to his own house in London yet with a keeper for sir Richard Bakley have the guard of he with the key of the water-gate and street door and the earl have the freedom of the whole house with a dozen servant to attend he who may free go in and out at pleasure and the countess of Essex have liberty to come thither to he and the lord deputy still continue frequent to solicit the secretary favour to this noble earl many time enlarge himself so far as to justify the earl faithful endeavour in the main point of the late irish service about which he be most question insomuch as see the earl action in Ireland to be narrow sift he write not long after to the secretary express avow that if the earl of Essex have bring with he a far great army the estate of the year be as then it be and he come at that time of the year when he do yet during his abode there which be from march to September there can no other consequence have just be expect in that so short time but that the rebel move with the countenance and terror of the army shall general or for the most part have seek her majesty mercy and make their submission have be receive upon pledge to continue subject or else to have seek to have ruin they by plant strong garrison which in most place must have be do by a army and they be in several place and many circumstance beside require thereunto the effect thereof will have take up as much time as he spend here and though the terror of the army do not work the first effect be in the choice of the enemy until by the second course they may be constrain that the fault be in their disposition and not in the earl endeavour or power and though the garrison be not according plant that as well the shortness of the time as the counsel to which the earl be tie at that time may just clear he of that default chap. 2 of the lord deputy particular proceed in the prosecution of the rebel in the year 1600. the twenty four of march be the last day of the former year the lord deputy sign the follow list of the army to be a direction to the treasurer at war for the payment thereof from the first of April in the year 1600 so forward general officer for the army the earl of orn. and lord lieutenant of the army three pound sir Oliver divide into three regiment under the governor sir Henry Dockwra and the two colonel above name sir Matthew Morgan and sir John Bolles 4000 Carickfergus garrison foot foot 700 foot at the Newrie foot sixteen surgian that chief at fivel the other disperse in province and garrison at thirty or forty a piece the week and that the lord deputy doctor of physic at fivel the week and his chief chaplain to the same company and that the commissary of the muster raise from five to twenty at three s. 4 d. a piece be pay out of the checque which themselves raise and one of they follow the army in field in each province the rest be distribute to be resident in particular garrison have make distribution of the force for the present it remain I shall descend to the brief narration of the lord deputy particular counsel and action against the rebel about the begin of April it be determine in counsel by the lord deputy and the general assent of the counsellor that the islander scot shall be hire to serve against james sorley that Agnus pretend right to his country be the fit for that purpose and upon his refusal Mac Alaine be think fit to be entertain for this service that the number of scot shall be 1500 or 2000 at most that they shall not land till the end of August and remain in pay as occasion shall serve their pay be to each man a cow for a month or for the default of cattle fix by the day and that they shall land between the (@) and Oldenfleet except they think some other place fit two inhabitant of be appoint to treat with these scot and they be to have the l. deputy letter to the earl of Argile and to the queen agent in Scotland for the furtherance of this business but this council take no effect by reason the course be dislike in England in the same council it be propound how the army shall be employ till the lord deputy go into the field which in all probability can not be for some two month after and it be resolve to prosecute the rebel at one instant both on the border in the north and in Lemster for the north border 650. foot and 100 horse be to lie in garrison in dundalke 7 co foot and 50. horse at Ardee 400. foot and 50. horse at Kelles 1000 foot and 50. horse at the newry and a hundred foot at carlingford if Tyrone draw not to a head it be conclude these garrison be to infest the fewes Ferny obanlons country Mac Gonnis his country and other part of monaghan and the cavan if Tyrone draw to a head than it be conclude his own troop be like to spoil these country and our man send to Loughfoyle shall plant themselves with more case and short be able to spoil both Tyrone and odonnel country for Lemster a thousand foot and a hundred horse be to draw into Ophalia to build up the togh to victual the fort of Phillipstone and to spoil the connors Omoloyes and Mac Coghlins this do it be conclude these force shall pass into leex thereto attend direction or if that passage be difficult then to return the way they go and by the way to send for further direction and to further the last prosecution the o carol be command at the same time to innade the omoloyes and the lord of Delain and sir Francis shane be to meet and join with the lord Dunkellin in Mac coghlins country and thereto invade the neighbour rebel the three of April the lord deputy advertise master secretary that the queen have few subject in Ireland of any sort who have not either some kind of intelligence with Tyrone or have not frame their heart that way whereof the whole pale make sufficient overture by a petition late deliver and by their contestation at the counsel table that the old earl of Clanrickard at tyrones go into monster have take day with he till May next to declare himself on that party but that the lord of Dunkellin his elder soon hate by his young brother who the father esteem much above he give he great confidence of his firm allegiance who supect his father disposition that way have take occasion by repair to Dublin and after go for England to put himself as a gage and bridle to his father proceed conclude that (@) the lord deputy have take order for secure the castle of athlone but that all his hope of keep the province of Connaght in obedience be in the lord of dunkellins honesty neither be the lord deputy deceive in this worthy lord who (@) during his father life so from his death happen within few month to the end of the war serve the queen as noble valiant and faithful as any nobleman or gentleman in the army the lord deputy explain the danger of the irish commander and company yet for the time show the remedy to be more dangerous than the disease protest that her majesty can not take a more unprofitable way to satisfy the irish suitor then by give they company his lordship further advertise master secretary that upon tyrones retire out of monster into the north in manner of a fearful flight he the lord deputy have (@) (@) in the earl of ormond such company as be not appoint for monster and upon their arrival to athye have send sir Richard moryson to take possession of the government of leave and sir Oliver Lambert to lead and bring back the force send with victual to relieve the fort call Mariabourg of queen Mary seituate in leax otherwise call the queen county which fort be before in extreme (@) now he have supply for three month that he have employ Brimingham who have about that time submit himself to the queen mercy to put in some cow into the fort of Ophaly that he purpose to prosecute the rebel in lemster with one thousand foot and a hundred horse and to lodge the rest in garrison upon the north so as on the sudden he may divert Tyrone from resist our present plantation at Loughfoyle that he will present send a thousand old soldier from Dublyn to Loughfoyle and likewise with they such as be to lie in garrison at Ballishannon under the command of sir Matthew Morgan but that for some difficulty they can not yet be settle there yet lie at Loughfoyle in the mean time may do service and always be ready to be send thither that tyrones confederate be discourage at his fearful retreat into the north which can not have be great if he have be break with a army for after a unreasonable day march hear of the lord deputy draw towards he within one hour of his sit down he do present rise again at seven a clock in the night and be assault by some of our scatter band still march leave to the sword as many of his man as be engage and leave or lose all his carriage so as now almost every day the head of some rebel or other be send he and many service be of late do as therecovery of a prey by the garrison at the Naas with the kill of many rebel and the defeat of one hundred and forty rebel by sir Francis shane whereof forty five be kill and of they some fourteen with his own hand and the rebel of Lemster daily make mean to be receive to mercy only the town be the store of the rebel and stand so saucy upon their privilege as a sharp rod and strong hand be requisite to amend they for which cause his lordship advise that the castle of Lymerik may be repair to bridle that town which seem of more importance than any other city of that kingdom whatsoever that the despair rebel be by con raise to some hope by two ship late come into the north out of Spain which bring the rebel some munition and either assurance of great and present succour or Tyrone at least so use their come to his purpose as the rebel believe such aussrance be give beside many priest come in those ship of which one term himself the pope legate and Leger ambassador for the king of Spain and archbishop of Dublin give out that he be content to suffer death if he preach not in Dublyn before Michaelmas day whereupon the rebel begin to avow themselves the king of Spain subject and only the expectation of Loughfoyle garrison together with the doubt of these succour keep the very pale from the boldness to profess the same last his lordship vehement complain that her majesty by absolute command dispose of charge in that kingdom so as he can neither pleasure his own friend nor reward her majesty best servant yea that have already give the government of leax to sir Richard moryson a friend who he confess especial to love and who he will undertake to worthy in his profession as any of his time or any the queen have in that kingdom now by the lord letter signify her majesty pleasure he be force to his friend and his own disgrace to confer the place on another and in conclusion beseech her majesty in such recommendation to leave they somewhat to his choice promise to execute they or else to yield great reason to the contrary the six of April the lord deputy advertise master secretary that the earl of ormond be go from Dublyn to his country have make great compliment of affection to her majesty service yet it be apparent that either he be grow weak in judgement or worse affect to the queen service than be imagine in England affirm of certainty that in the last cessation he have thrice at least speak very long with Tyrone and at his last be in monster have once hear from he and in general that the subject be no better servant to her majesty then the rebel with who they daily practise and will give no assistance with body or good to her majesty service yea will no doubt quit their allegiance whensoever they may do it with safety that every rogue ask a company and if he have one than seek a regiment but that God bless her majesty army he hope short to give law to their irregular humour the province of monster as I former say be much confirm in rebellion by the earl of Tyrone his last journey into those part where he strengthen james affair of monster fitzthomas who by the northern rebel send thither from Tyrone be exalt to be earl of Desmond in the year 1598. and be by a nicke-name call the suggon earl he combine with Florence mac carty call by the irish Mac carty more a name great follow there and in like sort with most of the great man of those part encourage those who he find willing to persist take pledge of those he sususpect to be waver and burn and spoil those few who do absolute refuse to join with he as the lord barry with some other and at this time another accident seem of great consequence do much erect the heart of the rebel and dismay the subject of those part which I will brief set down sir George Carey have new receive letter patent to be lord precedent of monster and resolve present to repair to his charge depart from Dublin on his journey thitherward the seven of April and upon the nine come to kilkenny with the earl of Thomond in his company and one hundred horse to attend he where the earl of ormond tell they he have appoint to parley with some rebel of those part whereof owny Mac rory be the chief and desire they to accompany he the ten of April they ride out of kilkenny with some twenty horse of the earl of ormonds follower and some few other mount upon hackney his lordship refuse to have the lord president horse to guard he so they ride eight long mile to the place of meeting and the earl of ormond leave his company of two hundred foot two mile short of that place the rebel Owny come out of the wood with five hundred man well arm and leave his shot and the gross of his troop some caliever shoot distant from the earl come up to he with some choice pike after a hour spend and nothing conclude the lord precedent move the earl to return but he will first speak with the jesuit archer and the rebel call he his lordship reprove archer and call he traitor in the mean time the gross of the rebel have creep over the shrub and compass round the earl and his company which the lord precedent dislike pray the earl to return but as he turn about his hackney the rebel take he prisoner and owny Mac rory lay hand on the lord precedent but the earl of Thomond rush upon he with his horse make he leave his hold and they both escape by the swiftness and strength of their horse from the push of many pike wherewith the earl of Thomond be slight hurt in the back this treachery be say to be plot by owny and archer and very few other for if more have know it many thought that the earl have such spy and be so fear among the rebel as his lordship will have have notice thereof either for fear or love but there want not other who think the earl be willing surprise howsoever it be the rebel do he no hurt in his person only one of the earl man be slay five be hurt and fourteen take prisoner the lord precedent with the one hundred horse attend he and six hundred foot which he send for out of monster keep the unsettle humour of those part from present tumult where the earl true follower want their head and the ill affect now stand in no awe of his power be all at liberty the countess of ormond be much afflict with her husband misfortune and with fear of she own and her daughter estate for diverse pretend to be heir to the earl as sir Edward Butler his brother and in respect his blood be attaint sir Walter Butler the earl nephew and for other reason the viscount mountgarret and each of these be likely to seek to have the earl sole daughter in their hand beside that these controversy breed distract humour among the gentleman and other of those part the lord deputy hear hereof present dispatch sir George Bourcher to command in chief and sir christop saint Laurence to assist he in guard the countess her daughter and the earl house with the force appoint by the lord deputy for that service namely the earl company of foot 200. the foot company of sir Christopher saint Laurence 200. the earl troop of horse 50. horse of saint Laurence 25. sir George bourcher horse 10. yet the lord deputy conceive the earl surprise to be a evil more spetious than material see no reason why the counsel of the war shall stagger upon his well or ill do for whereas some be of opinion that he be willing take and will declare himself for Tyrone his lordship resolve that if he continue faithful his country may easy be defend if otherwise as easy waste since after the garrison shall be once plant at Loughfoyle and those part on the back of Tyrone he shall be able to spare force for any such service and whereas many think the news will much amaze the court of England his lordship on the contrary since neither the lord precedent nor himself deserve any imputation for this event the parley be contrive without the lord president privity and both contrive and execute without make himself acquaint therewith conceive it will make the army both better and more careful second out of England and whereas it be think that this accident will erect the rogue spirit which before begin to be deject and so hinder the submission of many his lordship know that they will never be faithful to the state till they can not subsist against it be of opinion that till they be bring into great extremity it will prove better that they shall stand out then come in his lordship the fifteen of April advertise master secretary of this accident and how he have send force to strengthen those part and have take special care for the safety of the earl daughter and heir and be loath sudden to give his opinion herein only profess to think it strange that one so full of regard to himself in all his proceed shall be so easy overtake then his lordship give confidence that if the butler declare themselves for Tyrone as soon as Loughfoyle garrison be plant at his back his lordship doubt not to be able to meet the lord precedent in kilkenny and with their joint force to subdue the rebel and set those part in obedience at this time the fort of Phillipstown in Ophaley otherwise call the king county be to be victual and only Mac rory with the o mores in Leax together with the o conners in Ophaly brag that the queen force shall not be able to victual it now by the emulation of one of our chief commander against another prefer before he and strengthen by the court faction of England the say commander have set out some weak company for this service to be lead by the other as in pre-eminence of his place but a near friend to the lord deputy conceive how much this first action success may add reputation or give a blemish both to his lordship and the army give notice thereof so as his lordship offer the same commander the lead of those company he refuse to go with they manifest the suspect emulation whereupon his lordship cause fourteen strong company to be allot and give the command of they in chief to sir Oliver Lambert who conduct the victual and lead the man with such judgement and valour as be strong fight with at the come off and especial at the go on yet they perform the service with great loss and discouragement to those proud rebel and the fifteen of April his lordship advertise master secretary of this good service the thirty of April the earl of ormond send to the lord deputy from the wood the condition only Mac rory demand under his own hand for his liberty which till then he can not get because Ony stay for tyrones and his confederate advice add a postscript of his lordship own hand that the letter be bring to he ready write neither be he allow any man of his own to write for he the insolent demand be these first that her majesty force shall be remove from leax and the garrison deliver to oney Mac rorye hand second that pledge shall be deliver he for caution that no garrison shall ever be plant there which do Ony and his follower will submit themselves three if pledge be not give than the garrison also in Ophaly shall be remove and every man leave to shift for himself the postscript require that upon such pledge deliver a general protection for six week shall be send to onye Mac rorye and all his friend in lemster whereupon answer shall be return who desire the benefit thereof but during the say time of the protection no force of her majesty shall be send against their confederate in Ulster and the north. the 5 of may the l. deputy draw into the north part to make Tyrone look towards he and so to give better facility to our man to settle themselves in garrison at Loughfoyle but before his departure from Dublin for the better govern and defend the pale his Lr. do by commission leave sir h. poor to command in all martial affair and some of the counsel to govern civil matter during his absence and stay some few day at Tredagh for the company which have victual phillipston and for the garrison of Kels and Ardee as also for victual he march to dundalke whence take that garrison also with he he pass the pace of the Moyry on whitsunday morning and so come to the newry where he understand that according to his opinion Tyrone turn his force from loughsoyle be come in great haste to Dungannon have raze the old fort of blackwater burn armagh and have draw his man into the strong fastness of Loughlurken where with great industry the rebel have make trench and fortify the place some three mile in length his lordship to the former end advance towards he on the 16 of May draw out of the Newrie and encamp in the way towards armagh with 1500 foot and some 200 horse and there have notice that the rebel inquire after the time the fight at the Moyry when the earl of Southampton and sir Oliver Lambert sergeant Maior be to come to the army and with all hear that the say earl and sergeant Maior be that day arrive at dundalke his lordship early in the morning on the 17 of May send captain Edward blany with 500 foot and 50 horse to secure their passage through the pace of the moyrye who march from the camp and so through the moyrye to the faghard from which hill to dundalke there be no danger there he make a stand and leave his foot in two squadron of 250 each himself with the horse pass to dundalke and tell the earl of the force the lord deputy have send to conduct he assure he further that his lordship with the rest of the army will meet he by two of the clock in the afternoon at the causey beyond the pace from which the whole pace have the name of the moyrye hereupon the earl have with he beside this convoy the foot company of sir Oliver Lambert and sir Henry Follyot and some 50 horse of voluntary gentleman march to the faghard where he command one of the two squadron above mention to march on and after that the carriage then his lordship with the horse follow after who the second squadron march and last of all the two foot company of sir Oliver Lambert and sir Henry Follyot captain Blany command the vanguard advance towards the foure-milewate be a ford all environ with wood in the midst of this dangerous pace call the moyrye and come within half a mile of the same they discover the rebel on both side in the wood whereupon the earl direct the vanguard to pass over the water and to make good the rise of the hill beyond it when these come within a musket shoot they perceive two hundred foot of the enemy lodge beyond the water in the most advantageous place then captain blany divide his man into three maniple send 60 on the right hand under captain Henry atherton and as many on the left hand under captain Williams his lieutenant and keep the rest in the midst with himself and so by the sergeant mayor direrection they give the charge in the mean time the lord deputy be on the hill beyond the pace have send his vanguard consist of two regiment the one under sir Charles Percy and the other under sir Richard moryson two colonel of the army to advance towards the pace and at this instant when captain Blany give on upon the rebel the say lord deputy vanguard appear on the left side within two musket shot after some volley on either side the rebel on the right hand and those right before captain blany quit those place and
