retire through the wood to the earl of southamptons rear so as captain Blany pass the water make a stand there as he be appoint to do till the carriage and horse shall be pass and now the lord deputy vanguard be come to the passage of the say water maintain a resolure skirmish with the rebel on the left hand and altogether secure the earl troop on that side therebel thus beat on both side leave some one hundred shot to skirmish with the lord deputy vanguard and all retire to the earl of southamptons rear and come desperate on our man both with horse and foot but sir Henry Follyot make a very good stand and sir Oliver Lambert fear leave our man shall be distress the more to encourage they take his colour in his own hand and together with some 30 of the earl of southamptons vangards best man send back to the rear hasten towards the assaliant to second the earl who at that time with some 6 horse do charge the assail rebel and beat they a musket shoot back still pursue they till they have spend their powder and throw their staff dart and innumerable stone recover the place where Tyrone stand himself with some 220 horse and 200 foot in sight beside a far great number hide in the wood which never come unto this fight when our man have thus gain much ground the earl command they to march towards the army and present sir Richard Wingfeild the marshal of the army of Ireland come to the with order from the l. dep. that since the repulse rebel be not like to give any second charge they shall continue their march follow his l ps. troop direct to the newry in this conflict 2 of our man be slay capt. atherton and mast. Cheut be shoot and some few hurt with sword and such weapon on the rebel side there be in all 1200 foot thus advantageous lodge and 140 horse and tirone himself confess that ten of his man die with over-travelling in this hasty march beside such as be kill who number can not certain be learn the 21 of May his Lp. be advertise from sir Arthur chichester governor of Carickfergus that the english send to plant at loughsoyle be safe land with small resistance and have take Newcastle belong to sir John Odogherty who country they have spoil and waste and that some of they send forth upon a draught have take good store of cow and kill some of odonnel people and that they be now busy in fortify about the Derric so as many of that country southward do pass their cow and movable into Scotland depend special upon the hope of spanish succour that Brian Mac art a rebel border on Carickfergus have leave his fastness of Kilultagh and now lie on the border of Lecale where he purpose to assail he the rather because he have send 200 man to assist Tyrone that diverse gentleman and other do daily fly from the rebel and resort unto he with their good to the number of 1200 cow and more will come but that he doubt their faithfulness that to free himself of the imputation to keep james Mac Surley a enemy till he have revenge on he his brother death he have employ colonel Egerton to invite that rebel to submission but receive only temporize answer whereupon according to his l ps. direction he have write and send a messenger of purpose to the lord of clanty a islander Scot to stir he up against james Mac Surley wrongful possess his rightful inheritance in those part of Ireland offer to join the queen force under his command to those power he shall bring for recovery of this his right so as he will after yield due tribute and obedience to her majesty but that upon the king of scot late proclamation that all bear arm shall be ready to attend the king on the 17 of july next follow in prosecution of the islander scot as be give out refuse to pay tribute he fear that this lord will be divert from embrace this business howsoever advantageous to he that he have receive Con Mac Neale the son of Neale Mac Brian and his horseman into her majesty pay and will short waste his father country whence Brian Mac art and some 400 bonnaghte or hire soldier be maintain and feed final that he think fit to rebuild olderfleete and leave some in Ward there because the haven be commodious to succour weather-beaten ship go to supply the garrison of Loughfoyle with necessary the 26 of May the lord deputy receive a letter from the lord in England with full answer to his late dispatch for the earl of ormonds detension they signify her majesty grief to be the great because any attempt make for his recovery be like to prove his ruin and that her majesty have write to the countefse to send the earl young daughter and heir into England for sir Arthur oneales demand upon his come in to serve her majesty in the first point concern religion her majesty bare with it because she take it to proceed of his ignorance not of presumption only wish the l. dep to let he see that her majesty pursue none in those part for religion and so to satisfy he but in no wise by any contract or condition next for his andothers suit for land and for entertainment because such overture be like daily to be make by such as submit themselves and protraction of send to and fro may lose many opportunity first touch the suit for land her majesty direction in particular case follow shall be a rule to the lord deputy for his grant of that kind and first for sir Arthur oneales demand tyrones estate that can not be grant he by reason Tyrone upon pretence of a old inquisition have extend the limit of his country and encroach far into the south and east but her majesty be please to give he tyrones principal seat reserve place for fort and land to maintain they and reserve all dependency of the vriaghte or neighbour lord also reserve land in Tyrone to reward the service of such gentleman as shall serve under sir Arthur in these war which they shall only hold of her majesty by letter patent for the rest sir Arthur oneale to be chief in Tyrone as well in superiority as in revenue touch Neale garue demand for o donnel estate her majesty pleasure be to reserve some port and castle and some land to reward the service of that country gentleman intend that these and more special the Mac swine shall depend only upon her majesty and have right to those land by her letter patent touch Mac guire country her majesty direct like reservation of land for fort and reward of service and general in all grant charge to reserve her majesty ancient right second touch suitor for entertainment in pay her maiestes pleasure be signify to allow one thousand pound a month so long as the lord deputy and the counsel there shall think fit to be employ that way according to the lord deputy discretion but their lordship advise wary to observe and know such as offer submission because it have always be the arch-traitor practice to let slip such as he can not defend that they may save their good and live upon her majesty without any intent to do her service last whereas the lord of Dunkellin by his letter in regard of some restriction whereby he be disable to serve her majesty as he desire have make offer to resign the government he have in the province of Connaght and forasmuch as the queen be always unwilling to employ any great lord in his own country yet find he place in that government by the earl of Essex have still continue he there only out of her special favour to he and for that of late some insolence have be offer to company of the english by the old earl of clanrieards soldier in her majesty pay their lordship signify that the queen pleasure be to accept the lord of dunkellins resignation in the fair manner and withal careful tender of his honour advise the lord deputy to invite he to accompany his lordship and serve in the army under he and sir Arthur savage than a colonel of the army and lie with his company at athlone be appoint provisional governor of the province of Connaght except the lord deputy know some sufficient cause to the contrary the lord deputy have attain his end of draw the army into the north by the safe land and settle of Loughfoyle garrison in the far north of Ireland on tyrones back his lordship the twenty eight of May hear that Tyrone have draw back his man two mile further into the fastness and be inform that the pace of the moyrye by reason of much wet late fall and the rebel break of the causey be hard to pass return by carlingford pace to dundalke and so to Dublin where he understand that the rebel have in his absence burn the pale though he leave for defence of it 2000 foot and 175 horse in lemster but the damage be not answerable to the clamour for many private man have in England sustain great loss by casual fire in time of peace than the whole pale have do by the enemy burn in war and many private man in England have in one year lose more cattle by a rot than the pale lose by this spoil of the rebel of which they lamentable complain beside that indeed this burn and spoil of the very pale do further the great end of finish the war no way so likely to be bring to a end as by a general famine give i leave to digress a little to one of the fatal period of Robert the noble earl of Essex his tragedy and the last but one which be his death whereof the follow relation be send into Ireland the five of june there assemble at yorke-house in London about the hear of my lord of Essex his cause eighteen commissioner viz. my lord of Canterbury lord keeper lord treasurer lord Admi lord of Worcester Shrewsbury Cumberland Huntingdon Derby and Zouch mast. controller master secretary sir John Fortescu lord popham chief justice lord Anderson chief justice of the common plea lord Perian chief baron of the exchequer justice gaudy and Walmesley they sit from eight of the clock in the morning till very near nine at night all at a long table in chair at the earl come in none of the commissioner stir cap or give any sign of courtesy he kneel at the upper end of the table and a good while without a cushion at length my lord of Canterbury move my lord treasurer and they joint my lord keeper and lord admiral that sit over against they than be he permit a cushion yet still be suffer to kneel till the queen sergeant speech be end when by the consent of the lord he be permit to stand up and after upon my lord of Canterbury motion to have a stool the manner of proceed be this my lord keeper first deliver the cause of the assembly and then will the queen counsel at law viz sergeant attorney solicitor and master Bacon to inform against he the sergeant begin and his speech be not long only a preface as it be to the accusation the sum of it be to declare the queen princely care and provision for the war of Ireland and also her gracious deal with the earl before he go in discharge ten thousand pound of his debt and give he almost so much more to buy he horse and provide himself and especial in her proceed in this cause when as after so great occasion of offence as the consumption of a royal army fruitless waste thirty hundred thousand (@) treasure contempt and disobedience to her express commandment she notwithstanding be content to be so merciful towards he as not to proceed against he in any of her court of justice but only in this private sort by way of mercy and favour after he the attorney begin who speech contain the body and substance of the accusation it be very sharp and sting for beside the man fault of contempt and disobedience where with he charge he he do also shrewd infer a dangerous disposition and purpose which be by many rhetorical amplification aggravate to the full he divide his speech into three part in the ingress he observe how large a commission he stand upon such a one as never any man have the like before namely that he may have authority to pardon all traitor of himself yea to pardon treason commit against her majesty own person and that he may manage the war by himself without be tie to the advice of the counsel of Ireland which clause he say be grant that he may at first proceed in the northern journey which the counsel of Ireland who land and live lay in the south may perhaps hinder and labour to divert he to the safeguard of themselves in the other two part of his speech be contain five special crime wherewith the earl be charge viz. his make the earl of Southampton general of the horse 2. his go to lemster and monster when he shall have go to Ulster 3. his make so many knight 4. his conference with Tyrone 5. his return out of Ireland contrary to her majesty command these all save the four be recite by the lord in their censure as the crime for which he be censure by they the first be amplify for that he do it contrary to her majesty mind plain signify unto he in England that he increase that offence by continue he in that office still when her majesty by letter have express command he to displace he and three for that he write a very bold presumptuous letter to her majesty in excuse of that offence which letter be afterward read the second point of his southern journey be aggravate in that it be make contrary to her majesty advise resolution agree upon by her counsel and approve by her martial man as the only mean to reduce Ireland and contrary to the earl own project yea and that without the advice of the counsel of Ireland also as appear by a letter of they under their hand though now the earl pretend their advice for his own xcuse whereupon follow the wing out and the weaken of the royal army that ever go out of England the waste of that huge expense and the overthrow of the whole action the three point viz. the make of knight be urge to have be contrary to her majesty express commandment a question be once make whether he shall have that authority or no because he have abuse it before yet the same be at the last grant with this limitation give he in charge that he shall make but few and those man of good ability whereas he make to the number of threescore and those some of his menial servant yea and that in a most unseasonable time when thing be at the worst which shall have be do upon victory and triumph only the four point namely his conference with the rebel be aggravate in that it be a equal and secret conference dishonourable to her majesty for he that sustain her royal person to confer in equal sort with the base and vile traitor that ever live a bush kerne and base son of a blacksmith suspicious also in that it be private and secret no man suffer to approach but especial no english man the end of the conference most shameful that the wretched traitor shall prescribe condition to his sovereign abominable and odious condition a public toleration of idolatrous religion pardon for himself and all the traitor in Ireland and full restitution of land and possession to all the sort of they it be add that before this parley a messenger go secret from the earl camp to the traitor viz. captain Thomas Leigh if not send by the earl at least by his connivency at least by the connivency of the marshal who the earl do not punish last the five point be urge to be intolerable presumptuous contrary to her majesty express commandment in writing under the seal of her privy signet charge he upon his duty not to return until he hear further from she that this his return be also exceed dangerous in that he leave the army divide unto two diverse man the earl of ormond and the lord chancellor man who himself have except against as unfit for such a trust and that he so leave this army as that if God his providence have not be the great the ruin and loss of the whole kingdom have ensue thereupon this be the sum of the accusation every part interlace with most sharp and bitter rhetorical amplification which I touch not nor be fit to write but the conclusion be whereby a taste of the same may be have that the ingress be proud and ambitious the progress disobedient and contemptuous the regress notorious and dangerous among other thing the lady rich her letter to the queen be press with very bitter and hard term my lady rich her letter he term a insolent saucy malapert action he propose also in the end a precedent for the earl punishment say he be fain to seek far for one gentle enough one William of britten earl of Richmond who refuse to come home out of France upon the king letter be adjudge to loose all his good land and chattel and to endure perpetual imprisonment master attorney particular say the follow word whereas the earl in his letter exclaim o for so I think he construe these word of he o hard destiny of i that I can serve the queen and please she too let i also say with the orator concern he in the end of his speech now faith he nothing remain but that we inquire all this be do before my lord go into Ireland he vaunt and boast that he will sight with none but the traitor himself he will pull he by the care out of his den he will make the earl tremble under he etc. but when he come thither than no such matter he go another way it appear plain he mean nothing less than to fight with Tyrone this be the effect of master attorney part master solicitor his speech follow which contain the unhappy success which ensue in Ireland after the earl departure whereby appear how little good the earl have do in that the traitor be grow much more confident more insolent and strongerthen ever he be before as appear principal by his declaration which he have give out since the earl departure vaunt that he be the upholder of the catholic faith and religion that whereas it be give out by some that he will follow the earl of Essex into England he will perhaps short appear in England little to England good many thing he add to that purpose after he sir Francis Bacon conclude the accusation with a very eloquent speech first by way of preface signify that he hope both the earl himself and all that hear he will consider that the particular bond of duty which he then do and ever will acknowledge to owe unto the earl be now to be sequester and lay aside then do he notable extol her majesty singular grace and mercy whereof he say the earl be a singular work in that upon his humble suit she be content not to prosecute he in her court of justice the star-chamber but according to his own earnest desire to remove that cup from he those he say be the earl own word in his letter and now to suffer his cause to be hear by way of mercy and favour only where no manner of disloyalty be lay to his charge for quoth he if that have be the question this have not be the place afterward pass along most eloquent through the earl journey into Ireland he come to charge he with two point not speak of before the first be a letter write by the earl unto my lord keeper very bold and presumptuous in derogation to her majesty which letter he also say be publish by the earl own friend the point of the letter which he stand upon be these no tempest to the passionate indignation of a prince as if her majesty be devoid of reason carry away with passion the only thing that join man and beast together her majesty heart be obdurate he will not say that the earl mean to compare she absolute to Pharaoh but in this particular only which must needs be very odious can princess err can subject suffer wrong as if her majesty have lose her virtue of judgement justice etc. far be it from i quoth he to attribute divine property to mortal prince yet this I must true say that by the common law of England a prince can do no wrong the last point of that letter be a distinction of the duty a subject owe to his prince that the duty of allegiance be the only indossolueble duty what then quoth he be the duty of gratitude what the duty of obedience etc. the second point of master Bacon accusation be that a certain dangerous seditious pamphlet be of late put forth into print concern the first year of the reign of Henry the four but indeed the end of Richard the second and who think fit to be patron of that book but my lord of Essex who after the book have be out a week write a cold formal letter to my lord of Canterbury to call it in again know belike that forbid thing be most seek after this be the effect of his speech the special point of the whole accusation be afterward prove by the earl own letter by some of her majesty letter and the counsel and by the letter of the earl of ormond and other of the counsel of Ireland open red by the clerk of the counsel the accusation end the earl kneel begin to speak for himself in effect thus much that ever since it please her gracious majesty to remove that cup from he which he acknowledge to have be at his humble suit and to change the course of proceed against he which be intend in the star-chamber he lay aside all thought of justify himself in any of his action and that therefore he have now resolve with himself never to make any contestation with his sovereign that he have make a divorce betwixt himself and the world if God and his sovereign will give he leave to hold it that the inward sorrow and affliction which he have lay upon his soul private betwixt God and his conscience for the great offence against her majesty be more than any outward cross or affliction that can possible befall he that he will never excuse himself neither a nor a from whatsoever crime of error negligence or inconsiderate rashness which his youth folly or manifold infirmity may lead he into only he must ever profess a loyal faithful unspotted heart unfeigned affection and desire ever to do her majesty the best service he can which rather than he will lose he will if Christianity and charity do permit first tear his heart out of his breast with his own hand but this always preserve untouched he be most willing to confess and acknowledge whatsoever error and fault it please her majesty to impute unto he the first part of his speech draw plenty of tear from the eye of many of the hearer for it be utter with great passion and the word excellent order and it may plain appear that he have intend to speak no more for himself but be touch as it seem with the oversharpe speech of his accuser he humble crave of their lordship that whereas he have perceive many rhetorical inference and insinuation give out by his accuser which may argue a disloyal malicious wicked and corrupt affection in he they will give he leave not in any sort to excuse himself but only by way of explanation to lay down unto they those false guide which have deceive he and lead he into all his error and so he enter into a kind of answer master attorney speech from point to point in order allege for the point of his large commission for pardon treason against her majesty person that it be a thing he have learn of master attorney himself only to meet with the rebel curiosity which have a opinion that all treason in Ireland may be interpret treason against her majesty person and therefore will trust no pardon without that clause that in make the earl of Southampton general of the horse the deceivable guide which mislead he be a opinion that her majesty may have be satisfy with those reason which move he as also with those reason which he have allege in his letter for continuance of he in the place but that after he perceive her majesty mind plain in her second letter he displace he the next day for his journey into monster he allege diverse thing principal that the time of the year will not serve for a Ulster journey and then the advice of the counsel there which he protest to allege not to excuse himself but rather to accuse his own error and the error of the counsellor in Ireland and whereas some of they to excuse themselves and charge he the deep have now write the contrary to the counsel he protest deep that therein they have deal most false and it seem say he that God his just revenge have overtake two of they already the earl of ormond by blindness and sir Warham st Leger by violent death for his make of knight he allege the necessity and straits he be drive unto that be the only way he have to retain the voluntary the strength and pride of the army that he make but two of his servant and those man of special desert and good ability that he think his service ought not to be any bar against they for the receive thereward of their desert but before he have thus wade through half his answer my lord keeper interrupt he and tell he that this be not the course that be like to do he good that he begin very well in submit himself unto her majesty mercy and pardon which he with the rest of the lord be glad to hear and no doubt but her princely and gracious nature be by that way most like to be incline to he that all extenuate of his oferice be but the extenuate of her majesty mercy in pardon that he with all the rest of the lord will clear he of all suspicion of disloyalty and therefore hemight do well to spare the rest of his speech and save time and commit himself to her majesty mercy and when the earl reply that it may appear by that hedge which he diligent put to all his answer that he speak nothing but only to clear himself from a malicious corrupt affection my lord keeper tell he again that if thereby he mean the crime of disloyalty it be that which he need not to fear he be not charge with it as the place and course take against he may warrant all that be now lay unto he be contempt and disobedience and if he intend to persuade they that he have disobey indeed but not with a purpose of disobey that be frivolous and absurd then my lord treasurer begin to speak and clear the earl from suspicion of disloyalty do very sound control diverse of his other excuse after he master secretary make a preface why he speak before his turn by reason of his place take the matter in hand and first notable clear the earl from all suspicion of disloyalty which he protest he do from his conscience and afterward often iterate the same and preserve it unto he entire he speak singular for the justify of her majesty special care and wisdom for the war in Ireland in provide whatsoever can be demand by the earl for that service before his go out with supply he afterward with whatsoever he can ask so it be possible to be give he in prescribe that course which have it be follow be the only way to have reduce that realm and which be forsake be the only ruin and loss of that royal army and as for all those excuse which the earl allege for himself he clear cut they off show that his excuse of follow the counsel of Ireland advice be nothing his commission be so large that he be not bind to follow they and if he have be yet be they a counsel at his command he may force they to say what he list his own letter which he allege may be provisionary write of purpose then to excuse he now to be short he great justify her majesty wisdom in manage that whole action as much as lay in she and lay the whole fault of the bad success in Ireland upon the earl ominous journey so he call it into monster and thus in the behalf of her majesty he full satisfy the auditor master secretary give the earl his right always and show more courtesy than any yet say he the earl in all his journey do nothing else but make as it be circle of error which be all bind up in the unhappy knot of his disobedient return also he give the earl free liberty to interrupt he at any time in his speech but the earl be content with the opinion of loyalty so clear reserve unto he be most willing to bear the whole burden of all the rest of the accusation and therefore never use any further reply only by reason of a question or two that be move by my lord of Canterbury and my lord admiral some little speech there be to and fro my lord of Canterbury question be concern the condition of yield unto Tyrone in toleration of religion the earl hearty thank he for move that doubt and then protest that it be a thing mention in deed but never yield unto by he nor yet stand upon by the traitor to who the earl have say plain hang thou up thou care for religion as much as my horse master secretary also clear the earl in that respect that he never yield to Tyrone in that foul condition though by reason of tyrones vaunt afterward it may have some show of probability by reason of my lord admiral question the earl speak somewhat of his return that he do it upon a false ground of hope that her majesty may pardon he as she do the earl of Leicester in the like case who return out of the low-countries contrary to her majesty express letter this I think with myself quoth the earl if Leicester be pardon who end be only to save himself why may not Essex be pardon who end be to save a kingdom but master secretary reply that upon his knowledge there never pass any letter from her majesty to forbid the earl of Leicester return judge Walmesley his speech be more blunt than bitter prisoner at our bar say he be more graceless they will not confess their fault again he compare my lord his come home and leave the army there to a shepherd that leave his flock to the keep of his dog in conclusion the earl protest that all he seek for be the opinion of a true and a loyal subject which may appear by the speech wherewith he hedge in all his answer namely that he intend only to show those false guide which mislead he whether they be his own error or the error of his counsellor who he follow that he yield himself whole to her majesty mercy and favour and be ready to offer up his poor carcase unto she he will not say to do for alas he have no faculty but to suffer whatsoever her majesty shall inflict upon he and so request they all to make a just honourable and favourable report of his disorder speech which have fall from he in such sort as his ache head and body weaken with sickness will give he leave this do they proceed to the censure my lord keeper begin with a good powerful and eloquent speech that by justice and clemency the throne be establish as for mercy her majesty have reserve it to herself but for the satisfy of her justice she have appoint they to inquire into the cause that they be to inquire only of those fault of contempt and disobedience lay unto the earl and to censure he according and for her mercy they have nothing to do with it only God be to work it in her princely breast in examine the earl fault he lay these for his ground that the two ground and foundation of the prince sceptre and estate be the reputation of a diligent and careful providence for the preservation of her estate and country and the obedience of her subject and he that shall take either of these from she shall take from she the crown and sceptre for the first he notable show at large how her matesty have deserve it in the whole course of the irish war for obedience he show the nature of it consist in precise follow the straight line of the prince commandment and upon that strain he amplify to the uttermost all the earl contempt and disobedience that her majesty great mercy may appear the more clear among the rest for he go through they all in order he answer thus to the pretence of Leicester precedent for excuse of the earl return in good thing the example be better than the imitation of another he that do well of his own head do best and he that do well by imitation do commendable in a less degree but in bad thing the proportion be otherwise the example be naught the imitation be worse therefore if my lord of Leicester do evil in come over contrary to the queen commandment my lord of Essex do worse in imitate my lord of Leicester and be so much the more to be punish for it in the end he come to the censure which be this if quoth he this cause have be hear in the star-chamber my sentence must have be so great a as ever be set upon any man head in that court and perpetual imprisonment in that place which belong to a man of his quality that be the tower but now that we be in another place and in a course of favour my censure be that he be not to execute the office of a counsellor nor to hold himself for a counsellor of estate nor to execute the office of earl Martial of England nor of the master of the ordinance and to return to his own house there to continue a prisoner as before till it shall please her majesty to release both this and all the rest after my lord keeper all the rest in order give their censure amplify her majesty clemency and the earl offence according to the manner in the star-chamber but all accord to this censure for so they call it and not a sentence master secretary say my censure be that the earl deserve etc. the great part of the day be spend in the lord censure who be many of they very long only the noble man not counsellor be short the earl of Worcester cite these two verse even for our fortune god may cast we down neither can chance excuse it a God frown the earl of Cumberland say if he think that censure shall stand he will crave long time for it seem unto he somewhat hard and heavy intimate how easy a general commander may incur the like but quoth he in confidence of her majesty mercy I agree with the rest the lord Zouch will give no other censure but that which he think the earl will lay upon himself that be that he will restrain himself from execute his office etc. and keep himself in his house till her majesty shall release all they all seem by their speech to conceive a sure hope of her majesty release this censure and the earl be reasonable cheerful only his body seem weak and distemper with sickness and now and then he show most manifest token of sorrow for his offence to her majesty by tear in his eye special in the first part of his own speech and when my lord keeper speak now I return to the irish affair Tyrone on the five of june write to the countess of ormond that he have write to owny mac rory request he to take pledge for the earl her husband and so to enlarge he conditional that he shall swear to do henceforward no hurt or hindrance to any in action with he and further that the young lady his mistress mean the earl daughter and heir shall in no sort be take for a pledge especial because it be give out that under that colour he seek to marry she to his elder son avow last that where it be say that the earl be treacherous surprise which can hardly have be so prove that Tyrone and his rebellious confederate shall have belceve it he will in that case not only take his favour from owny but procure the earl enlargement without any condition though by his release all Ireland shall be destroy to the same effect Tyrone write to the earl of ormond who letter he sauce with general complaint against the earl for the rigorous prosecution he have former make against he and his associate but this letter be permit to be send to Dublyn the say point can not be think void of that cunning wherein the writer excel a three letter he write at the same time to owny mac rory make owny himself judge whether he have treacherous take the earl or no advise he to take the best pledge he can the above name young lady except and for more security to send they to be keep in Tyrone if he concur with he in opinion that his so do will be more safe then if owny himself shall keep they in those part these letter he date forsooth from his camp near the newry so gallant be the gentleman now the lord deputy be return with his force into the pale who otherwise never appear in camp but hide himself and he in boggy wood and like fortify passage the eight of june the lord deputy write to master secretary concern the state of Connaght wherein nothing be sure the queen but athlone by a provident guard and Galloway by their own good disposition wish that the government of that province may be confer on the earl of Southampton to who the lord of Dunkellin will more willing resign and may do it with great reputation to himself in respect of the earl greatness rather than upon sir Arthur savage who notwithstanding upon the queen pleasure again signify be short after make governor of that province his lordship protest that it be such a place as he know the earl will not seek but only himself desire this because he know the earl aptness and willingness to do the queen service if he may receive such a token of her favour just commend his valour and wisdom as well in general as in the late particular service in the Moyry when the rear be leave naked he by a resolute charge with six horse upon Tyrone in the head of 220. horse drive he back a musket shoot and so assure the rear save the honour of the queen army to which purpose though not so ample his lordship also write to the queen at this time the county of Dublyn on the south of the river liffr be in effect whole overrun by the rebel the county of Kildare be likewise possess or waste by they the county of meath be waste as also the county of west meath except the barony of Deluin and the county of louth so that in the english pale the town have garrison and the land from drogheda or Tredagh to the navan and thence back to trim and so to Dublyn be only inhabit which be also like to grow waste if they be further charge with the soldier the fifteen of june the lord deputy write to sir Arthur chichester governor of Carickfergus that he shall not spare the subject late submit who protect the rebel good that he shall receive no more but such as will simple submit and give good pledge neither shall give pay to any except he know their service will be very beneficial to the queen that he shall continue to treat with the islander scot till advice come out of England what course shall be take with they that he shall take in shane oneale with promise of land and entertainment and promise that for prey he shall take of the rebel if the english assist he he shall have a three part and if he take they without the assistance of the english he shall have three part of four the nineteen of june the lord deputy advertise master secretary that he be more trouble to govern the friend then to suppress the enemy that find the army a mere chaos he have give it form that find it without spirit he have give it life that in all attempt he have preserve the whole body of it and every part from any blow restore the reputation of it and possess it with a disposition to undertake and a likelihood to effect great service that he have omit nothing which may be perform by this army in this estate during this time that the assurance the irish have receive of succour from Spain be the only fuel of the last blaze of this rebellion therefore pray that except master secretary have some certainty that Spain will not at that time assist the rebel the army may by all mean be strengthen which will be necessary if such assistance be send and will make a end of the war if none be send and howsoever that befall yet for prevention of munition and such supply to be furnish to the rebel from Spain advise that some few of the queen ship may lie on the west and somewhat towards the north of Ireland add that some little boat make both to row and to sail will bar the islander scot from supply the rebel with any munition and that his lordship to meet with the earl of ormond late set free by only mac rory who have take he prisoner that day take his journey towards Carlogh where he hope to sound the bottom of the condition of his delivery with the best course how to disentangle he and by his conference to make a shrewd guess how the earl stand affect in these doubtful time his lordship in his next letter advertise into England that he be not privy nor consent to the give of pledge at the earl of ormonds delivery but since they be give in regard of her majesty extraordinary care for the earl liberty he do not show any manifest dislike thereof and now conceive the earl do apprehend the indignity do to he by those base traitor and therefore have such a spleen against they as he have join with he in diverse plot as well to recover the pledge wherein the earl protest to spare no money if they be so to be redeem beside that he and their father protest that their danger shall not hinder they from do their uttermost service to the queen as also to work his revenge upon the rebel at this time Tyrone attend the garrison at Loughfoyle and Odonnel start through Connaght into Thomond and spoil both country sir Samuel bagnol draw out of the newry into monaghan where he take a prey and kill six commander and some sixty of the common rebel only three of his be stain and twenty hurt the subject of the pale fear belike to be complain on for the small assistance they give to the queen service send over the lord of howth and sir Patrick Barnewell to make first complaint after the irish manner of the wrong do they by the army never acquaint the lord deputy and counsel therewith and notwithstanding their former unwillingness to bear any charge for the queen service now they be content for these their deputy expense in England to cease every plough land at three shilling from the seven of july to the twelve sir Oliver Lambert with some troop lay encamp at the tough in Ophalia where he make a causey and build a fort and thereleft a guard to keep the passage always open for the victual of Phillipstowne fort in which service the earl of Southampton as a voluntary by his presence and valour much encourage our man at this time many of the rebel in lemster and the northern border make suit to the lord deputy to be receive to mercy with offer of large sum of money to the lord deputy for their pardon but his lordship refuse their offer till they have first do some service and have draw blood against some of their confederate thus much his lordship advertise into England the sixteen of july as likewise a good service present do and a great prey take in the fuse by sir Richard moryson the governor of dundalke the same twelve of july his lordship take his journey towards the border of the north upon hear that Tyrone be draw into those part there his lordship intend to spoil the corn as likewise in all other part when it shall be a little ripe Mac mahowne and Patrick mac art Moyle offer now to submit but neither can be receive without the other head but Oconnor roe mac gaire for good respect of service be at the same time receive to mercy his lordship hear that Tyrone contain himself in his fastness and be requite out of England to attempt something upon the lemster rebel leave the northern border strong guard against any invasion and leave order with the counsel to hasten the general host and make ready all provision for a journey into the north and leave Dublyn the twelve of August ride to the nasse and so march to the fort of Phillipstowne in Ophaly with five hundred sixty foot and sixty horse beside voluntary in his company in the way into Leax his lordship take a prey of two hundred cow seven hundred garron and five hundred sheep beside great store of small cattle the sixteen of August his lordship burn the country and spoil the corn march towards the passage one of the most dangerous in Ireland where sir Oliver Lambert with the force he have be to meet he both of they fight all the way and kill diverse rebel whereof the lord deputy leave fifteen dead in the place beside many hurt they meet together at noon the seventeen day the army march towards a fastness where the rebel have store great plenty of corn at the entry there be a Foard compass in with wood and a bog between they where the rebel let the vanguard of the horse pass but his lordship pass with a few gentleman and his own servant before the vanguard of the foot the rebel begin the skirmish with he and the foot wing be slow send out they come close up to he the traitor Tyrrel have appoint a hundred shot to wait on his lordship person with mark to know he in this skirmish we kill thirty five rebel and hurt seventy five on our part two only be kill and a few slight hurt captain masterson dangerous hurt in the knee and his lordship have a very good horse kill under he and another kill under master John chidley a gentleman of his lordship chamber but the best service at that time do be the kill of owny mac rory a bloody and bold young man who late have take the earl of ormond prisoner and have make great stir in monster he be the chief of the o mores sept. in leax and by his death they be so discourage that they never after hold up their head also a bold bloody rebel Callogh Walter be at the same time kill beside that his lordship stay in leax till the twenty three of August do many other way weaken they for during that time he fight almost every day with they and as often do beat they our captain and by their example for it be otherwise painful the common soldier do cut down with their sword all the rebel corn to the value of ten thousand pound and upward the only mean by which they be to live and to keep their bonaght or hire soldier it seem incredible that by so barbarous inhabitant the ground shall be so manure the field so orderly fence the town so frequent inhabit and the high way and path so well beat as the lord deputy here find they the reason whereof be that the queen force during these war never till then come among they the lord deputy in his return the first day pass into another part of the country with the foot alone for the horse not able to pass be send about so as the rebel have the advantage they most desire to fight with our foot without assistance of horse yet all the rebel of lemster here gather together and fight upon their natural ground have be so beat as that they suffer our man to pass without a blow that night eight head be bring to the lord deputy and with they one Lenagh a famous rebel take alive who be present hang on the same tree where he plot all his villainy sir Oliver Lambert with some troop march into donnel spagniahs country where he take 1000 cow 500 garon great store of sheep and kill twenty rebel at the first entry beside many kill in a fight which the rebel after maintain all the day and part of the night sir Arthur savage come out of Connaght to meet the lord deputy fight long with the rebel spoil the country and take a great prey but can not pass to his lordship in the lord deputy return out of leax redmond keating and the chief of the sept of the kelly and lalor be receive into her majesty protection upon condition to set at liberty the earl of ormonds pledge in their hand by this time his lordship have receive out of England gracious allowance of his former northern journey with her majesty promise to reinforce the army with two thousand foot and two hundred horse against the next journey into those part require he not to give any one man the command of both horse and foot and whereas all company be of two hundred or one hundred fifty advise to distribute some part into less number that more gentleman may be satisfy with command with the only increase of some chief officer pay and that his lordship will be spare to give passport for any to come into England to trouble her majesty with suit and most of all not to suffer able man to return out of Ireland as they daily do with their captain pasporte and to the end the commander may not be idle her majesty require that all service do by they may be certify monthly into England about this time the earl of Southampton leave the war of Ireland sail into England this summer service make it appear that journey with a great army do not so much good as garrison lie upon the rebel which upon any sudden service may easy be draw together in competent number and in the mean time keep the rebel at home from second one another the lord deputy by his letter during the foresay journey explain to the lord in England that he have be most careful not to increase her majesty charge in any thing the want whereof will not have make the rest of her great expense to be unprofitable and to the end the commander may not be think to lie idle beside the good fortune that none of they have receive any blow he particular remember many prey take and service do and for the chief garrison on the north border advertise that sir Arthur chichester have lay all the country waste within twenty mile of Carickfergus that sir Samuel Bagnol at the newry have do the like that sir Richard moryson at dundalke have banish Turlough Mac Henry out of the fuze into monaghan and yet the two last with most part of their garrison have be part of the army in all former journey the twenty six of August his lordship return from this journey of leax to Dublin and there receive advertisement that her majesty can not refuse to hear the complaint of the pale by the deputy former mention to be send over though she have sharp rebuke they that they do not first complain to the lord deputy which they excuse by experience that like complaint in Ireland have ever be vain the chief complaint be these that the force that shall lie upon the border near the rebel be lodge upon they that the fetch of one barrel of powder be often make a sufficient reason to spoil they by a company of horse and foot send to convoy it that the clarkeship of the counsel be sell and then execute by a deputy who for every small petition take great fee that the spiritual live be give to ignorant and idle person be the chief cause of this rebellion scarce any church stand for sixty mile between Dublin and athlone that they be spoil as much by the army as rebel no soldier nor captain be punish nor any order give for remedy take effect that private captain give pasporte to run aways and her majesty be deceive by false muster so as the force be weak to end the war and they be spoil as much as if the number be full require that some gentleman of the pale may be join with the commissary in take the muster of adjacent garrison in the same letter her majesty command the lord deputy to signify to sir Arthur o Neale that she purpose to create he earl of Tyrone and give he a portion of land fit for a earldom and for Tyrone that the lord deputy shall proclaim he traitor with promise of two thousand pound to any shall bring he alive and one thousand pound to he that shall bring his head to any of her majesty fort or garrison last her majesty give letter of favour to the deputy of the pale direct to the lord deputy to who the complaint be whole refer it be her majesty pleasure that only before he and by he they shall be hear and redress yet because the lord deputy be many way tax in these complaint he do expostulate in his next letter to master secretary that he shall be tax for those thing for which he expect approbation and thanks the wise counsel say he be uncertain and the wise man unperfect and what shall I look for when out of my weakness though free from wilfulness I shall happen to commit any error of consequence see I be now charge with so many matter and those nothing belong to i his lordship add that in his opinion nothing have make the affair of Ireland more unprosperous then that the state use to hear every man against and before the chief governor so as he be drive to let matter go as they will so as he may save himself another discontent letter be write to the same effect and to the same person but therein explain other grievance beside the former complaint and whereas the lord of the counsel have tax he for be rule by young counsel whereby he understand his three most familiar friend to be mean namely sir Henry Davers sir Richard moryson and sir William godolphin he bold answer that beside the counsellor of state he use the familiarity of none which be not old than Alexander the great when he conquer the World last he protest to master secretary that he take he for his chief friend and know that he have more power to do he good or hurt than any other yet as he will not dishonest lose he so he will not base keep he beseech he to use his power in mediate licence unto he that he may come over for a short time to kiss the queen hand for touch other favour concern the public he will never acknowledge any particular obligation to he or to any other since he make his demand as he think best for the service but the grant or deny thereof concern not he the great part of the army have neither armour nor morion neither be here mention the sick and hurt in other place beside dundalke nor yet the warder allow out of some of these company the fourteen of September his lordship begin another journey into the north and the fifteen encamp at the hill of Faghard three mile beyond dundalke and there his lordship lie till the nine of October in such extremity of weather as will have hinder his passage if the enemy have not withstand he his lordship tent be (@) wet and often blow down before his lordship come Tyrone with his uttermost strength have possess the Moyry be a strong fastness as any the rebel have but his lordship resolve to march over he if he stop his way and make he know that his kern can not keep the fortification against the queen force many skirmish fall out happy to we and two several day the rebel be beat out of their trench with great loss till at last upon the eight of October they leave the passage clear then after the army be a few day refresh at dundalke his lordship march the twenty one of October to the newry pass through the Moyry where he cause all the rebel trench to be lay flat to the ground and the wood to be cut down on both side of the pace at the newry for want of victual his lordship stay till the second of November when he set forward eight mile towards armagh and there encamp the rebel horseman show themselves upon a hill whereupon sir Samuel bagnols regiment have the rear and be not yet come into the camp be direct to march towards they there be a bog between we and they but the rogue quick draw to their fastness the next morning his lordship ride some quarter of a mile from the camp and view a place where sir John Norreys former intend to build a fort and like his choice set down there with the army to build the same the place be a hill like a promontory all environ with bog a river and great store of wood by it on the right hand over the river and a great bog be a little firm ground and then another bog and over that a fair country with house and much corn his lordship can by no mean send over any horse but four mile about wherefore he command a regiment of foot to advance to the first piece of firm ground and from thence to send over the next bog some few man to bring in the corn and timber of the house with direction to make their retreat to the gross if the enemy horse shall fall down that way on the left hand and before be a bog over the bog before a great wood that continue through all this fastness and over the bog on the left hand a hill where Tyrone all that day and most of the time that the army lie there do muster himself and his man this day most of his horse and foot fall over but far about on the right hand upon which our straggler that go out retire to the firm ground over the first bog and there begin between our foot and they a very good skirmish till our man do beat they off and bring with they great store of corn and wood and kill diverse of they in the mean time their scont on the other side be somewhat busy with we neal Oquin be take prisoner be the chief favourite unto Tyrone the next day we begin to work in the build of the fort and to impeach our work the rogue begin to skirmish with we on both side which be excellent maintain by some few of our man that we send out we see many of they kill and after understand they lose a great number whereof many be horseman of the best sort that have light to encourage their man to fight they be then so well beat as they will never after offer to meddle with we till our return by carlingford the nine of November the fort be finish his lordship call it mount Norreys in honour of his master so he term he under who he have serve his apprenticeship in the war and he leave therein four hundred foot under the command of captain Edward blaney with six week provision of victual the weather grow so extreme as it blow down all our tent and tear they in piece and kill many of our horse so that the ten day his lordship put all the army in arm with all the drum and trumpet and a great volley of shot proclaim tyrones head with promise of 2000 pound to he that bring he alive and 1000 pound to he that bring he dead which be do in the face of his own army and so his lordship march to the newry he have purpose to plant a garrison at armagh 8. mile beyond mount norrye but the rebel cow have eat up all the grass thereabouts which shall have feed our horse and the time of the year with the weather be now unseasonable for that purpose and whereas his lordship be resolve to return into the pale by carlingford to discern whether that way or the way of the Moyry be more safe that the army may not run so continual hazard this resolution be now confirm by carlingford fight necessity there be victual at carlingford and none at the newry or dundalke the twelve of November his lordship come with the army to the narrow water whence he send sir josias bodley with three hundred choice foot to possess a piece of ground and keep the enemy from hinder our passage over the water the stream whereof he find so exceed swift that it be like to be dangerous to venture our horse over the first that try be doctor Latware his lordship chaplain who only with his horse lead by the boat side and with some thirty foot go over but his lordship perceive so great difficulty by his passage that he pass the foot over as fast as may be send sir Henry Folliot to possess the pace of the fathom and make all the horse and our garron to go about that way in the mean time we may see the rebel force draw over the mountain towards the pace of carlingford and come close by our man that be first land yet they never offer any skirmish that night we encamp direct over the narrow water between the pace of the fathom and the pace of carlingford and have at midnight get over for our man some victual that come by water from carlingford his lordship cause the same to be deliver before day for the army have fast two day and after they have eat but a little biscuit and cheese or butter never man go on in a great jollity the thirteen of November we be to rise very early for otherwise we can not pass our carriage by the sea side as we have determine and by break of day the Scoutmaster bring word that Tyrone with all his army be lodge in the pace which be a exceed thick wood at the foot of a great mountain reach down to the sea side between which and the sea there be in most place as much space as seven may march in rank but in some place less and in some none at full water but only there be a narrow deep high way through the wood captain Thomas roper with the break company send out of the pale go on as a forlone hope and that day by course it fall out that captain Benjamin Berry with the lord deputy regiment under his command have the vanguard sir Christopher St Laurence have the rear of the vanguard sir Richard moryson have the vanguard of the rear and sir Samuel bagnol the rear of the rear so that we have but two body a vanguard and a rear thus subdivide captain trevor with as many as captain roper have in the point lead a forlorn rear out of all the regiment his lordship appoint three strong wing to go on the right hand for on the left hand be the sea command all by captain the first by captain Billings the second by captain esmond and the last by captain Constable the ground the rebel chiesely choose to make good be a little plain like a semicircle whereof the sea make the diameter and a thick wood the circumference at the next corner to we there run into the sea a river out of the wood be a Foard of good advantage to the enemy all along the circumference they have make diverse trench even close up to both the corner and at the further corner they have make a barricado reach a good way into the wood and down to the Sea at the first they show themselves horse and foot upon this plain but when his lordship command we to give on which they perform present and round their horse draw off into the wood and their foot into their trench and never shoot till the vanguard be draw over the river when from all part they pour upon we great volley of shot but present captain roper give on the far trench on the right hand of the corner captain Billings on the next with the wing he lead and captain Berry with the rest of the vanguard give upon the far corner where the barricado reach from the wood into the Sea in some of they they make good resistance and many of they lose their life with the pike and the Sword but the last trench where they make great show of opposition they do soon quit though it be strong for they and to great purpose to arrest we the reason his lordship conceive to be that in that place they be further from their retreat and fear the forlorn hope and wing lead by captain Billings may cut between they when we have gain the trench the vanguard make a stand in the rear of which to countenance they if there have be occasion his lordship stand with a troop of horse of voluntary gentleman and next to his lordship between the van and next body of foot sir Henry Davers and his lordship troop of of horse at this time they entertain skirmish with all part of our army but still fall towards the rear and at this time his lordship secretary master George Cranmer be kill between sir William godolphin and mast. Henry Barkely master ram his lord ps. chaplain horse be kill and a gentleman of his lordship chamber call master do that carry his cloak shoot through the leg and I will not forget one accident that may have prove of great consequence during this stand his lordship ride up to a little hill in the edge of the wood underneath which our man be in skirmish with the rebel beyond who somewhat more than a musket shoot off on the side of a hill by a few little house there stand in a troop some seven or eight horseman on foot with their horse by they at who his lordship cause his footman to shoot who always carry a long piece with he who as within two hour after it be tell his lordship by one that be at that time one of the number kill the next man to Tyrone on who shoulder at that time he lean sir Henry daner come unto his lordship and desire he may take twenty of his own horse to fall into the rear because he see all the enemy horse fall thitherward and that the irish horse only that day have the rear his lordship give he leave and withal send young james Blount with 100 shot out of the vanguard captain Caufeild and captain Constable with as many more out of sir Richard Morysons regiment to reinforce the rear with who the rogue continue a good skirmish almost for half a hour until their horse and foot come on a little plain somewhat far from the skirt of the wood sir hen. daner charge home and break they but in the begin of the charge he be shoot in the thigh after this charge they present draw off their foot by the mountain and their horse by the strand over against the narrow water in our rear captain Richard Hansard and captain trever be sore hurt and sir garret Moor ensign and Hugh hanlon kill and in all we lose not twenty but above threescore be hurt of the enemy as we hear then of certain there be fourscore kill outright but within two day after his lordship understand by maguire that they lose two hundred the marshal and the sergeant Maior be always in the van or rear as in either place the fight grow hot and general all the commander and soldier serve with extraordinary forwardness and alacrity to conclude by credible report the rebel lose in this journey above 800. and tyrones reputation who do all thing by his reputation be clean overthrow so that from all place they begin to seek pardon or protection on our part in the whole journey some two hundred be kill and dead of hurt and some 400. be hurt which short after recover give i leave to digress a little to continue the journal of my travel the writing whereof have occasion the relation of irish affair when the earl of Essex go lord lieutenant into Ireland the lord Mountioy be first name to that place whereupon by my brother sir Richard Morysons inwardness with he I than obtain his lordship promise to follow he into Ireland in the place of his chief secretary but this employment fail we both I retire myself into Lincolnshire where I live till his lordship be the last spring send over lord deputy and such be then my diffidence of vulgar report for I have no other knowledge of his lordship employment that I do not certain believe the change of the deputy till his lordship be ready to take his journey which be beside extraordinary hasten by the queen command for the necessity of her affair in that kingdom yet my letter swift than myself come to his lordship hand before his go and from he I receive this honourable answer that not know what be become of i he have already receive three secretary yet wish i to follow he for he will find out some fit and good employment for i the indisposition of my body by reason of a ague stay i some few month in that country but in july take my journey for Ireland I come to Cambridge whereas yet I be one of the fellow of Peter-house the master and fellow by special indulgence have continue unto i my place with leave to travel from the year 1589. to this present july in the year 1600. at which time be modest further to importune so love friend and have the foresay assurance of preferment in Ireland I yield up my fellowship which in my former absence have yield i some twenty pound yearly and the society to knit up their love course towards i give i aforehand the profit of my place for two year to come for which courtesy and for my education there I must ever acknowledge a strict bond of love and service to each of they in particular and to the whole body joint from thence I go to London and so to Westchester and while I stay there for a passage I receive another letter by which I do gather that his lordship purpose to employ i in the write of the history or journal of irish affair but it please God in his gracious providence which I may never leave unmention to dispose better of i for stay for a wind till the end of September one of his lordship three secretary either to avoid the trouble and danger of the war or for other reason best know to he come over and tell i that he have leave his lordship service thus with better hope of preferment I cross the sea in very tempestuous weather at our put to sea the carcase of a break ship swim by we and at our enter the port of Dublyn another ship be cast away in cross from one shore to another wherein a bishop and his whole family be drown after few day spend in Dublyn I take my journey to dundalke on the northern border where my brother sir Richard moryson be then governor and there I lodge till the lord deputy return with the army and the thirteen of November be the day of carlingford fight above mention while I walk in my brother garden I sensible hear by reverberation of the wall the sound of the volley of shot in that skirmish though the place be at least six mile distant in this fight the lord deputy his chief secretary George Cranmer as be above mention be kill and his lordship have now but only one secretary do receive i the next day at dundalke into cranmers place I return to the irish affair at Dundalk his lordship receive a letter from the lord admiral signify that he have earnest move her majesty to give he leave to come over for a short time who answer be that there live not any man that she will be more glad to see then his lordship but that now he have begin so worthy and all thing prosper under his work she will not give encouragement to the rebel by his absence who his presence have so daunt the list of the army and the distribution of the same into garrison in the end of November twelve colonel of the army the earl of Thomond lord Dunkellin sir Henry Dockowra sir Arthur chichester sir Henry power sir Charles
