inconvenience it may therefore please her (@) to have in a readiness six thousand of the train band of such country (@) best for transport into Ireland to be send over into monster upon the first (@) of any (@) power to be arrive there and some part of her navy in a readiness (@) a great portion of munition and artillery for we than otherwise this war (@) require we do hope to give her majesty a very good account of her kingdom and of ourselves until we shall have cause to sue for more relief and if it (@) (@) fall out that Spain will have war with England we shall be glad that the (@) of England may be make in Ireland and that we her poor servant shall have the happiness to strike the first blow for both her royal kingdom the which the (@) God preserve long unto she and she unto they and we etc. the same fourteen day from the say camp at the Fagher his lordship write to sir Henry Dockwra governor of loughsoyle first touch his lordship purpose that the general hoa be the last of june his lordship presume within (@) day after to be provide of all mean to put at the force in action according to their distribution the project whereof he send to he enclose and to be himself as high as armagh with such a power as Tyrone shall have good reason to think that he will do somewhat more and in the mean time he will god willing keep the field as near Tyrone as his mean will give he leave so as his lordship have plant at armagh and blackwater hope either by lie there to facilitate his plant of Ballishannon from Loughfoyle or to break into Tyrone and meet he there and if tyrones army shall break as his lordship expect he think to find no great difficulty herein but otherwise hold the passage to Dungannon not to be venture that way but touch the plant at Ballishannon that sir Henry Dockwra shall not build upon any supply from his lordship of victual munition or tool for artillery that he may use that he have at Loughfoyle and either bring it back by water or be after supply thereof from Dublin for munition tool and like necessary that his lordship can spare none and if he can yet have no speedy mean to send they for intelligence that he have send he a cipher which he may use safe in write to his lordship not care how the messenger speed so the letter be not understand that he have send the like cipher to sir Arthur chichester at Knockfergus with who he shall have often intelligence and may that way write most safe to his lordship that when he know his own mean and by his lordship project shall find he in readiness to answer his attempt he shall choose his (@) for plant Ballishannon and as near as he can send his lordship certain notice of the time and use all possible expedition but if he can not plant there (@) want or difficulty that then he shall agree with sir Arthur chichester that they may both at one time break into Tyrone where his lordship upon notice (@) will meet they in which journey beside all other effect of war they shall (@) all the dwelling and destroy the corn on the ground (@) may be (@) by encamp upon it and cut it down with sword and other way hold (@) best they shall spoil all the corn except that which he can gather wherein he shall not regard the (@) of the irish submitty in his campe. the (@) fourteen day we dislodge from the Fagher and leave the Moyry (@) with warder to guard it and the workman be to build a tower (@) keep of (@) we march eight mile and encamp at Carickbane a little beyond the Newrie the fifteen his lordship rise and march some fifteen mile encamp in Enagh the country of Mac Gennis the late rebel near dundalke be all submit his lordship have draw sir Richard moryson with his regiment from that government purpose to place he in lecayle near to the enemy and intend to march thither with the army the next day leave the rebel shall have leisure to burn the country and carry away the prey his lordship send sir Richard moryson this evening with six company of foot and one of horse to march all night into lecayle who come sudden on the rebel take all the prey and intake of downe Patrick the bishop seat one of the bradye be take and his head cut off the rest yield to mercy there and in all adjoin place here his lordship be advertise from the secretary of Ireland that the news of the spanish invasion this summer be second from diverse come from Cales the sixteen day his lordship march with the army through high mountain and wood and some dangerous pace seven mile to the Blackstaffe tiver near a strong castle call Dundrom lie on the north side of the pace where the plain country open into lecayle be a island compass on the west side with this river and on the three other side with the sea and two small arm thereof this night his lordship with some horse pass the Blackstaffe bridge and ride three mile into lecayle to view the country in the way Phelim Mac ever submit himself and yield to the queen his castle of Dundrom also Mac carty submit himself and draw his creaght or cattle servant and good into lecayle his lordship return to the camp and the next day ride to down Patrick and thence by saint Patrick well to arglasse be six mile in which town two castle yield to the queen and the warder up on their life save give up their arm a three castle there have be hold for the queen all the time of the rebellion by one jordane never come out of the same for three year past till now by his lordship come he be free and to he be give a reward from the queen by beside his lordship bounty of his private purse after dinner his lordship ride two mile to russel town and four mile to the camp at Blackstaffe the eighteen day Mat rory captain of Kilwarden adjoin be receive to the queen mercy upon his submission and sir Arthur chichester governor of Knockfergus all this day expect come in the evening to who his lordship that night impart his design in the present service and to the same effect send a packet by he to sir Henry Dockwra governor of Loughfoyle and to they both as also to sir Richard moryson be to be leave governor of lecayle his lordship give proclamation to be publish for establish the above mention new coin all this time Arthur Mac Gennis the chief of his name and Edmond boy Mac Gennis his uncle make mean to be receive to her majesty mercy but can not obtain the favour without first do some service this day his lordship and the counsel follow the army give thirty pound by to phelimy enter Mac Gennis for some special service and balinthor a strong castle be take by our man with diverse cow and other good six of the ward be kill and the rest swim awny his lordship have place sir Richard moryson with five hundred foot and fifty horse under his command to govern lecayle which have their residency at down do march back on the nineteen day eleven mile to five mile church near the newry pass one pace exceed strong by nature and plash with tree which lie at the end of the plain of lecaile and entrance into the woody mountain and before the entry of this pace sir Arthur chichester have receive two hundred foot to strengthen his garrison return back to Knockfergue the twenty day his lordship march with his force three mile to Carickbane lie north ward of the Newrie this day sir Henry Davers lie at mount norrye advertise his lordship that Tyrone lie in a fastness and his man never venture upon the plain the soldier leave under his command there can not in all this time get any occasion to fight with he whereof they show great desire only the horse often show themselves upon the hill have keep he beyond armagh where he with his creaght lay feed some thousand of cow whereupon because his lordship desire to preserve the grass near armagh for his horse troop as also to make store of hay there for the winter follow he send sir William godolphin with his lord ps. troop of horse under his command to second the force at mount Norreys in attempt some service upon Tyrone mean to draw present his whole force thither but in the mean time sir Francis staffords lieutenant of his horse send by sir Henry Davers to spy the rebel proceed have pass to the view of armagh and find that Tyrone have send back all his cow upon the hear of his lordship return out of lecayle for which cause and upon notice that Tyrone have take a day victual for his man as if he mean to attempt something his lordship recall sir William' godolphin with his troop the one and twenty day his lordship lie still in regard that for difficulty of get garrou that be carriage jades or by some negligence victual be not according to his former direction put into mount Norreys to which place he purpose to draw with his force this day three day bread come to his lordship force which in stead of other victual live upon and his lordship write to sir Henry Davers that according to his daily use of late day he shall the next morning early draw the force of mount Norreys towards armagh and shall on the sudden possess the abbey there and the town whether his lordship will also draw the army present for his second the two and twenty day his lordship have by extraordinary pay above the queen price get garron and carry victual with he for mount norrye and for the garrison he intend to plant at armagh march six mile near to mount norrye where sir Henry Davers with that garrison meet he have not be able for some difficulty to execute his lordship former direction from thence his lordship take with he the say garrison march forward seven mile and that night encamp a little beyond armagh where some few rebel show themselves brag but attempt nothing his lordship before his return from lecayle be purpose to leave such force at mount norrye as may plant the garrison at armagh when they find opportunity but lest they shall have be hinder by a great force his lordship rather than to return towards the pale for the attend there of the general host where his army shall have spend the same victual it now do be resolve himself in person to plant it imagine that Tyrone not look for he till the general host will not have his whole force with he nor by that reason and a opinion and fear that his lordship intend to march further into Tyrone will have any mind to follow his lordship or hinder his retreat when he shall have weaken his force by that plantation therefore the three and twenty day his lordship make a show to draw from his camp beyond armagh towards blackewater cause his force to make a stand for his retreat and so himself with his follower and servant ride more than a mile forward to view the way to blackewater fort and the place of the famous blackewater defeat under the marshal bagnols conduct and have pass a pace without one shoot make at his troop he return to his force and march back he leave a garrison of seven hundred fifty foot and one hundred horse at the abbey of armagh under the command of sir Henry Davers and that night march with the rest near to mount Norreys where he encamp have in this march from armagh view the Foard where general norry former be hurt make a stand with his horse to secure his foot distress by tyrones charge the four and twenty his lotdship leave at mount norry the foot and horse of that garrison march himself with 1250 foot and 150 horse six mile to (@) be two mile short of the newry this be a hill natural and artificial ofold sortify where in regard of the weakness of his force he encamp purpose there to attend and solicit the hasten to send to he from the pale all the mean he expect to furnish he for his intend journey to build the demolish fort of blackwater here his lordship army be muster and be by pole captain and officer 87. target 112. pike 291. musket 125. calliver 635. in all 1250. whereof beside captain and officer english 593. irish the rest want sword 191. the six and twenty day his lordship send victual to the garrison at mount Norreis and at armagh the twenty nine day his lordship receive aducrtisement that sir h. Davers draw out the garrison of armagh into the fastness where Brian be lay with his cattle have kill diverse of his man take many horse from he and spoil much of his baggage beside three hundred cow which he have take from Mac Gennis and the same day his lordship receive the examination of certain Waterford mariner who testify that be at the groin they be press there to serve the king of Spain in a fly boat of two hundred tun carry bread to Lisbon where there be a army of three thousand man to be ship with victual and munition for Ireland and there hear that tyrones agent lie at court importune aid to be send he present be not able to subsist any long without speedy aid and that the examinate demand if they be pilot for the irish coast and find they shall be employ that way have secret get ship to transport themselves into France and so return home the thirty day Arthur Mac Gennis chief of the name terrify by the plantation of the garrison in lecaile make humble suit for mercy and obtain her majesty protection for nine day conditional that he shall come the Saturday follow to submit himself in person to her majesty mercy and crave her gracious pardon at Dundalk where his lordship then purpose to be and rory Oge Mac Gennis obtain the like protection for one month the same day his lordship upon the lord precedent of monster his intercessory letter grant warrant for her majesty pardon to be pass for two hundred seventy artificer and husbandman of the county of kerry the first of july his lordship have purpose to rise from Dunanurey and to return himself to Dundakle but he stay that day in respect the weather be very foul and the rather to countenance the convoy go with biscuit up to mote Norreis and armagh hitherto his lordship have keep the field rather to make Tyrone keep his force together and so to weaken he then for purpose of any other service of moment but now hear from Dublin that the rise out for the general host come slow and not only victual be not yet arrive there but even the carriage and beef for the army be like in great part to fail the second day of july his lordship disperse his force into the say garrison front near upon the rebel and so with his follower and servant ride to dundalke lead with he of his army only three company of foot and one troop of horse the three day sir Francis Stafford governor of the newry bring Arthur Mac Gennis to dundalke who make his submission to her majesty kneel before the lord deputy and counsel then he make certain humble request first for his pardon which be grant second for land grant to his father by letter patent which his lordship promise to confirm except only the land of Glasny Mac Gennis on who he shall make no imposition that he may take in such tenant as will come from the rebel acquaint the governor of the newry therewith before he receive they which be grant four that he may retain and absolute command all his old tenant till alhollandtide next which be grant except glasny Mac Gennis five that he may enjoy the corn he have sow in lecaile which be sow on other man land can not be grant only favourable respect to he be promise six that his people may be free from all action of private wrong in the war which be grant upon a fine of three hundred cow present to be deliver for the army the same time Patrick mac mahowne nephew to the chief of that name be upon like humble submission receive to her majesty mercy with promise of his pardon the five day the lord President and counsel of monster by letter desire his lordship to recall his warrant of marshal law give to the lord bourke aswell because the lord abuse the same to draw follower to they and to revenge their private quarrel as because the whole province be peaceable and willing to be govern by judicial course and this warrant his lordship present recall this day sir Oliver saint john bring letter from the lord in England whereby her majesty give direction that the lord deputy shall public to all the army and private to the chief commander give thanks from her majesty to they for the zeal and duty they have show in her service and signify her gracious acceptance of their endeavour the six day his lordship stay at dundalke to hasten the supply of the general host which come in slow and to order the irish force of the same fit for defence of the pale this day captain Thomas roper with his company of foot according to his lordship former direction come from kells to serve in the army under his lordship and while his lordship lie here news come from armagh that sir Henry Davers have take some chief horse from tyrones camp and have enter Mac carty his country be one of the great fastness in Ireland and bring from thence a great prey his lordship find that the rise out of the general host will do little good in the army and they be willing to undertake their own defence which as their own peril his lordship think he may best commit to their trust the seven of july his lordship give order that the force of the general host for the county of Dublyn and lowth shall lie at lowth under the command of the lord of lowth and Mr garland of killencoule that those of meath shall lie at Kels under the command of the lord of Tremelstone and Mr dillon his deputy that those of westmeath shall be command by the lord of Deluin and any deputy his lordship shall choose so that his lordship or his deputy shall always in person be resident with they and keep they together ready to answer any service upon pain of a fine and imprisonment to such as shall disobey that those of Kildare shall under the earl of kildares command lie at Athy or else where at his lordship discretion and that the sheriff of the shire command they under his lordship the nine day his lordship march from dundalke towards the north and gather the force to he out of the adjoin garrison encamp at Latenbur beyond the newry where he lie still the ten day till the victual be in readiness to be carry to armagh the eleven day his lordship march some four mile to a hill little beyond mount Norreis and that day his lordship be advertise that sir Arthur chichester have take the sole castle hold in those part of Knockfergus by Brian mac art namely the Reagh and that sir Richard moryson in Lecale have take in two loughes or land in lake be all the fastness or place of strength which the say Brian art hold there the twelve day the army march early in the morning to armagh and there rest some hour march again after dinner a mile and a half beyond armagh and there upon a hill encamp the thirteen day of july the lord deputy with the army rise from the former camp and march one mile and a half to a hill on this side namely the southside of blackewater where he make a stand Tyrone and his horse and foot show themselves out of a wood beyond a meadow on the other side of the river and that with trumpet and diverse colour some win at the old defeat of the english in those part and with some drum rather for a brag ostentation then otherwise since they fight like thief upon dangerous passage use not to appear in such warlike manner and from the trench keep by the rebel on the other side of the water some volley of shot be pour upon we which sell down on every side dead on the ground by reason of the distance between we and do small or no hurt we have a rabinet and a falcon make from this hill some shot at the rebel troop far distant whereupon their puppet bravery sudden vanish and according to their wont manner they hide themselves in the wood present the lord deputy send three hundred foot to another hill on this side of the river adjoin to the old fort lie beyond the water and his lordship ride to that hill who many voluntary gentleman with his servant follow and in the way myself and some other light in a valley to refiesh ourselves by walk but find a enemy soil no place for recreation for out of the ditch and furrow many shot be make at we whereupon we take our horse one shot dangerous yet God be praise without hurt pass between my leg while one of my foot be in my stirrup and so we retire to the gross stand in more safety towards evening we encamp upon the above mention hill at which time we see fair off by a wood side Tyrone draw some horse over to our side of the water either as we imagine to assail Tirlogh Mac Henry of the fewes late submit and come after we to attend the lord deputy in this service or else to confer with he and his company but assoon as sir William godolphin commander of the lord deputy troop of horse by his lordship direction make towards Tyrone he with his horse present retire back that night we make gabyon to enter the rebel trench and send the rabinet and falcon to be plant on the other hill where our above mention three hundred foot lay all the night the rebel out of the trench shoot at our man while they be busy in work but the fourteen day very early at the dawn of the day upon our first discharge of the say great piece charge with musket bullet and after some three volley of our small shot the rebel quit their trench base run into the wood and our three hundred man pass the river under captain Thomas Williams his command possess the trench and the old ruin fort with the plain in which it lay the wood be almost musket shoot distance whether the rebel be flee and have by night carry their hurt and slay man present the lord deputy send one regiment to lie beyond the blackwater upon a hill where his lordship have make choice to build a new fort. upon view of the trench make upon every Foard his lordship find they be strong and artificial fortify wonder much that either they shall so laborious fortify they if they mean not to defend they of shall so cowardly quit such strong place and so sudden if they have former resolution to make they good in gain they we have some twenty man hurt and two slay and they have great loss especial in the go off though we can not true know it the fifteen day his lordship with a troop of horse and four hundred foot draw towards tyrones wood and view the pace in the sight of the rebel who run away with their cow only at his lordship retreat make some few shot at our man but hurt not one man in the evening captain trever and captain Constable with their company come to the camp send thither from lecayle to strengthen the army according to his lordship direction to sir Rechard moryson after the country be all take in and Mac Gennis the great neighbour rebel have submit himself the 16 day the l deputy draw out a regiment of irish command by sir christo. St. Laurence and pass the blackwater march to benburb the old house of shane o Neale lie on the left hand of our camp at the entrance of great wood there our man make a stand in a fair green meadow have our camp and the plain behind they and the wood on both side and before they the rebel draw in great multitude to these wood here we in the camp be ourselves in safety have the pleasure to have the full view of a hot and long skirmish our loose wing sometime beat the rebel on all side into the wood and sometime be drive by they back to our colour in the midst of the meadow where assoon as our horse charge the rebel present run back and this skirmish continue with like variety some three hour for the lord deputy as he see the number of the rebel increase so draw other regiment out of the camp to second the fight so that at last the rebel have draw all his man together and we have none but the by-guard leave to saveguard the camp all the rest be draw out doctor Latwar the lord deputy chaplain not content to see the fight with we in safety but as he have former do affect some singularity of forwardness more than his place require have pass into the meadow where our colour stand and there be mortal wound with a bullet in the head upon which he die the next day of the english not one more be slay only captain Thomas Williams his leg be break and two other hurt but of the irish on our side twenty six be slay and seventy five be hurt and those irish be such as have be rebel and be like upon the least discontent to turn rebel and such as be keep in pay rather to keep they from take part with the rebel than any service they can do we the death of those unpeaceable swordman though fall on our side yet be rather gain than loss to the commonwealth among the rebel tyrones secretary and one chief man of the ohagans and as we credible hear far more than two hundred kerne be slay and lest the disparity of loss often mention by i shall savour of a partial pen the reader must know that beside the fortune of the war turn on our side together with the courage of the rebel abate and our man hearten by success we have plenty of powder and spare not to shoot at random may well kill many more of they than they ill furnish of powder and command to spare it can kill of we these two last day our pioner have be busy in fortify and build a new fort at blackwater not far distant from the old fort demolish by the rebel and for some day follow his lordship special intend the furtherance and finish of this work so as many soldier be extraordinary hire to work therein as pioneer the nineteen day his lordship write to the lord in England that have not the irish submitty for the new coin now currant over all furnish the army with beef it have be in great distress since the victual of the new contract be not arrive and that of the old store consist principal of saltfish whereof the soldier can not feed especial in summer beside that by long keep it be of ill condition so as infection be fear in the army pray that in the next contract the soldier may be feed therewith only one day in the week that he have in his direction to sir Henry Dockwra give he choice either with the countenance of his lordship army on this side Tyrone to go forward with plant Ballishannon or concur with the governor of Knockfergus to enter into Tyrone where his lordship upon notice will meet they and in respect he since understand that he want tool for the plantation he think the second project will rather be follow by he that Tyrone lie with all his force to hinder his lordship from pass to Dungannon which he most fear and have no fastness but only this to stop it so as he doubt not to break in to meet sir Henry Dockwra if he can once be assure of his resolution further he beseech their lordship to give warrant for allowance to the captain for break arm upon bring the old because upon the break of piece the soldier be turn to serve with pike and our shot diminish daily and the pike be increase more than our use require the captain excuse himself that upon break of piece he be not able to provide other arm than pike for his man likewise he advertise to their lordship that since the last dispatch sir Henry Dockwra have take in Newtowne be some sixteen mile from Dungannon tyrones chief seat on the north side as he the lord deputy have plant at blackwater be some fifteen mile from Dungannon on the south side and that sir Henry Docwra have spoil and burn the country there about and have take some one thousand cow from the part near the lough of earn that sir John Barkley governor of the force at the Anneley have meet with tyrrel man as they pass towards Ophaly for which purpose that garrison be special lay there and have take from they three hundred cow and kill some of they and have stop they from trouble that country and that he the lord deputy with the army have destroy the rebel corn about armagh whereof he find great abundance and will destroy the rest this course cause famine be the only sure way to reduce or root out the rebel final pray their lordship as former to send one thousand shot for supply the strengthen of the english be the next way to diminish her majesty charge since the irish be keep in pay rather to prevent their fight against we then for confidence in their fight for we the same nineteen day of july the lord deputy write to sir Robert Cecil her majesty secretary that he find upon good consideration that the government of Connaght be not in his disposal and therefore be loath to exceed his commission he will only assure he that as it be requisite a man of experience and fit for the present service shall have that government so he conceive none to be fit than sir Oliver Lambert who have already deserve well in this service and will in his opinion be able to do her majesty as good service as any in that place who if it may stand with her majesty pleasure to give he warrant he be desirous to employ in those part fear it will be a great hindrance to his intend work if any shall be put into that government who may prove unfit or unable to make that war in which respect he have no other end but the advancement of her majesty service be bold to make it his humble suit that sir Oliver Lambert may be place in that government while the army lie at blackwater to build the new fort his lordship on the twenty of july draw out two regiment into the wood aswell to view the pace and provoke the rebel as to fetch some house thence for the build of our fort and to cut a field of corn lie on the skirt of the wood which be all perform the rebel on the further side of the blackwater only make a slight skirmish with our man upon their retreat on this side the river the two and twenty day we cut all the corn by the bog and wood side near our fort except that which our man have power to reap the three and twenty day captain Thomas Williams with his company be leave to govern the new fort who before the blackwater defeat do valiant defend the old fort there be after demolish by the rebel his Lp. with the army dislodge and at our rise a proclamation be make that how soever Tyrone vaunt that his pardon be offer he and he may have it at pleasure her majesty be not only resolve never to receive he to mercy but be please again to renew her gracious offer that whosoever bring he alive shall have 2000 li. and whosoever bring his head shall have 1000 li. for reward thence we march two little mile to a hill south westward in Henry Oges country where we encamp and cut down the corn on cucric side the seven and twenty day his lordship leave sir Henry follyots regiment to guard the camp draw out three regiment expect that the rebel will fight who show themselves on a hill near we with all their horse and foot and sound of trumpet yet our man not only cut down the corn close by they but enter the wood to cut corn there and burn many house in the skirt of the wood they be so patient as after one volley of shot they retire into the thick wood the same day the army dislodge march a mile or two more southward where we cut down great abundance of corn with our sword according to our fashion and here shane Mac Donnel groom tyrones marshal who corn this be upon humble submission be receive to her majesty mercy and come to his lordship in person the same night at our set down in our last camp whether we return the eight and twenty his lordship leave sir Christopher saint Laurence his regiment to guard the camp draw out three regiment both in the morning and evening to countenance two convoy of victual this day his lordship send a dispatch to sir Henry Dockwra about their concur in the present service and the like to sir Arthur chichester to the same purpose his lordship stay the long in these part to see the fort well victual and to cut down the corn whereof he find great store the nine and twenty day his lordship be advertise that sir Henry poor have scatter and break three hundred rebel in lease and have beggar they by diverse prey of cattle take from they and among they be of the sept of o have kill burn and hang forty at least and after have slay in fight o connor Mac lyre and most of his man and hurt many of those which escape and this day great store of victual for the fort come from the newry with a convoy safe to the campe. the thirty day his lordship rise with the army and march northward back to the new fort of blackwater and beyond it in all some three mile along the south side of the river and there his lordship encamp close upon the bog and the fastness or fortify passage in the wood. the same evening his lordship draw some choice man out of every regiment and some troop of horse and with they ride to view the country and woody pace more special that pace which lie right before we northward where the near and best passage be to enter Tyrone the arch-traytor chief house Dangannon be some ten mile distant to which after the passage of this pace and blackwater the plain country lie open yet be in some part boggy here tyrones man lay entrench on the other side of the river in such trench as he have make to impeach the build of our new fort at blackwater and cowardly quit they as be above mention the rebel bestow some volley of shot on his lordship troop but they return safe have only one man hurt and here one of the three trumpet in tyrones pay run from he to our campe. the one and thirty day his lordship draw out as before and that day and the first of August next follow cut down the wood to clear the say pace lie between we and the say passage over blackwater and this day the rebel attempt to cut off a guard which we have place on a remote hill to second the workman cut the wood but be by they and the workman stout receive and by our second beat back at the same time by accident we have almost lose all our best horse for at the alarm give the horse be fright with the skirmish and with diverse horseman hurry out to answer the alarm break their headstal and run back to armagh and some of the best as far as the newry whether our man follow do recover they all but have the rebel horseman follow they no doubt they may have catch they and defeat our man loose follow they and so by this advantage have do we more mischief than they can otherwise have do with their force double the second of August his lordship with the army rise and march back to armagh to the end he may shun all pace and from thence have a open passage into art Mac baron country we march six mile to armagh and three to Rawlagh where while we encamp sir Henry Davers with three hundred foot and forty horse be send into a fastness to burn some twenty fair timber house which he perform and about the time to set the watch he return towards the camp and at his retreat all tyrones force guard with three hundred horse skirmish with our man but they second out of the camp come off orderly the rebel follow they to our very camp into which they pour a volley of shot and by reason of the fastness adjoin and night approach retire in safety here his lordship be advertise that the earl of ormond have execute in the border of kilkenny and Tiperary nine and twenty rebel of which tybot Leyragh Butler and David bourk and vlicke bourke be the chief and that the company under his lordship command have slay eight and twenty of which two principal man of the omore one Okelly one captain Edmund roe bourke and one Richard bourke son to vlicke be the chief the three of August we rise and have march three mile back we encamp between the pace and armagh a little beyond armagh towards the north to the and our messenger and our convoy for victual may more safe pass which be the chief end of our return and that we may have better grass for our horse all the high country above be eat by the rebel creaghs or cattle and all the way we march the rebel in their fastness draw down closeby and follow we all the way be very strong here the commissary take a view of the army in field with his lordship regiment first of sir Benjamin Berry his lordship lieutenant of his foot in list 825 by pole present in the camp 490. 2. regiment of sir Oliver saint john in list 875 by pole 533. 3. regiment of sir hen. Folliot in list 500 by pole 305. 4. regiment of sir Christopher saint Laurence in list 750 by pole 400. Totall in list 2950 by pole 1728 herein be not contain either officer present or the sick or hurt or upon other occasion absent the four day some company be draw out to cut the pace of armagh and the rebel be in sight offer not to skirmish with they but towards night they draw down strong out of the wood to a hill under which we lay encamp in a fair meadow they come with cry and sound of drum and bagpipe as if they will attempt the camp and pour into it some two or three thousand shot hurt only two of our man but his lordship command that none in the camp shall stir have lodge in a trench some four hundred shot charge they not to shoot till the rebel approach near and after these our man have give they a volley in theirteeth they draw away and we hear no more of their drum or bagpipe but only mournful cry for many of their best man be slay and among the rest one horseman of great account and one pierce Lacie a archrebel of monster the next morning we find some dead body at the skirt of the wood and three scatter piece hence his lordship send direction to sir John Barkeley to bring with he to the camp a regiment from the annaly and Liseannon because Tyrone be grow strong by the come to he of his Mac Guire and his Mac mahownd and of cormocke mac baron come to he from the frontier of Loughfoyle the five day his lordship send again some shot with pioneer to cut the pace close by the rebel the six day his lordship purpose to rise and meet our convoy bring victual from the newry but be stay by ill weather send early some horse to stay the convoy till his lordship draw down towards those part this day his lordship give order to master treasurer that proportion of new money shall be send to all the market town to change the same for sterling and that proclamation shall be make in they to decry the old sterling money and only make the new to be currant the seven his lordship rise to draw towards the newry and march to mount Norreis encamp near the say fort. the eight day his lordship dispatch the earl of Clanrickard into Connaght to command the force in those part have send for sir John Barkeley to come with a regiment to the campe. here his lordship give warrant for the pass of her majesty pardon for land life and good to Arthus Mac Gennis chief of his sept with some 170 follower here his lordship receive letter out of England from Mr secretary signify that the lord precedent have send to her majesty diverse advertisement that the spaniard will present land in some part of monster from whence the lord deputy for necessity have late draw one thousand foot and fifty horse into Connaght that her majesty do well allow of his lordship care in draw those man to that service and not lose the present certainty for apprehension of the future not so assure that it be probable that the king of Spain will do something now at the upshot and though it be not credible that he will send ten or twelve thousand man into Ireland yet since he have from February last begin a foundation to provide force for the low-countries or Ireland as his affair shall require and since the low-country army be reinforce by land out of Italy her majesty think he may with ease transport four or five thousand man for Ireland and be like to do it and so he may for the time turn the state of Ireland will think they well bestow if he shall lose they all at the year end that in this respect her majesty have resolve to levy five thousand man to be in readiness and to send two thousand of they present for monster to arrive there by the ten of this month so as if the spaniard shall land the lord precedent may be enable to keep the provincial from revolt till he the lord deputy may come thither and more force may be send out of England and if they shall not invade Ireland than his lordship may keep the one thousand he have draw from monster to finish the work whereof he have lay a happy foundation hearty wish that his lordship may be the happy instrument to save Ireland to who he profess himself tie in most constant and honest friendship and pray his lordship to esteem these ready second beside the public duty to proceed much out of a extraordinary respect to his lordship that for bestow of the company to be send into monster as he who be go mean (@) (@) the earl of Essex make too great a monopoly in bestow all such place himself so now there be a great confusion every lord importune to prefer his friend and follower but that for his part he seek no man preferment herein but only advise that those may be first respect which come over with the lord deputy recommendation that the lord precedent have earnest move he and in good sort challenge to have hope in he for the procurement of some mean to gratify his follower and have by other friend obtain of her majesty that some of those company may be send over undisposed and leave to his disposal to which he the secretary have give second rather than that the place shall be bestow in England without any thank either to the lord deputy or lord President protest that how soeven he love the lord precedent he will not scant his due respect to his lordship wherein he think to give he the least discontent that he conceive the spaniard will not make difcent at Corcke which town be not guardable when they have it Noryet at Lymricke though fit by the situation because a enemy engage so far into the kingdom can not hope for supply when her majesty shall take due resolution to oppose they but rather judge Galloway a fit place for their discen give commodity to join with the northern rebel and seat in a country all out in rebellion or else Waterford in respect of the goodly river and the people affection to Spain (@) the fort of Dungannon shall careful be furnish with a commander man and necessary last that Ostend be obstinate besiege by the archduke with thirteen thousand foot and sixty piece of battery and howsoever the state have leave two thousand dutch there yet their army be at Bercke whence it will not be raise the town have be carry within ten day if sir Francis Vere have not throw himself into it with one thousand six hundred english to who her majesty send one thousand man and prepare to send 2000 more no succour the place because part of the army in Italy be come down to the arch duke the nine of August his lordship and the counsellor present in the camp write to the lord in England that the army have be employ in prepare her majesty fort and fit they for the winter war and in the present spoil of the rebel corn the only way to ruin they hope to keep the army in field till harvest be pass so that it be impossible to cut all their corn our garrison may have opportunity to gather the rest and the rebel may be hinder from gather any except it be tyrones corn near Dungannon whereunto the passage be so difficult as he Lp for so little think not good to hazard all especial since sir hen. Dockwra for want of match as he have write can not meet his lordship in Tyrone according to their former project whereof his lordship notwithstanding profess himself nothing sorry in regard that meeting will have give the arch rebel power to fling the dice again for recovery of their fortune and that upon a unequal hazard by set his rest upon either of they apart divide into three body under the lord deputy the say sir Henry Dockwra and sir Arthur chichester even with the whole force of his northern partaker though his other friend further off be keep from aid he by the disposal of our other force that beside the spoil of their corn his lordship by search have find a ensie way to paste to Dungannon which hitherto be never by any guide make know to we and have cut down a broad pace through a thick wood in two day labour and so come to the river where he purpose as soon as may be to build a fort with a bridge there be from thence to Dungannon less than four mile all in a plain that this will cut the arch-traytor throat for howsoever the name of oneale be so reverence in the north as none can be induce to hetray he upon the large reward set upon his head yet when the hope of assistance from Spain shall be take away they see their corn spoil and upon our expect supply see we enter Tyrone can not but see their apparent confusion that howsoever this summer few of their cow have be take which work be more proper to the winter war yet they have be force to feed they within their fastness which they use to keep for feed they in winter so as they must either starve they by keep they there in winter or hazard the take of they by our garrison if they feed they abroad that for the future service they beseech again to be enable by the send of one thousand shot out of England for supply and that the rather because Tyrone be very strong as appear by a note deliver by shane Mac Donnel groom who have be tyrones marshal be receive to (@) the seven and twenty of july beside our experience of their strength when few day since they pour three thousand shot into our camp and can hardly be keep from force it so as our army consist only of one thousand seven hundred ninety eight by pole and more than half of they be irish the speedy send of the supply be most necessary as also the send of munition and victual without which all this labour will be lose and the soldier force not only to leave his garrison but to live idle in the pale upon the subject last that the army by God grace have not have any disaster but burn their house in the very wood have still beat the rebel few or no english be lose together with these letter be send diverse note of victual and munition remain and new store to be send over and therewith be send the above mention note of tyrones force as follow a perfect note of such captain and company as be under the command of the traitor Tyrone within Tyrone not mention the chieftain as o Donnel o cane Mac gyre and the rest of the Ulster lord but only such as attend he in his country horse Tyrone for his guard 100 his son Hugh oneale 100 his brother Cormock 100 art Mac baron 20. Phelim ohanlons son 10. Tirlogh brasils son 50. tyrones base son 20. in all 400. his guard of foot lead by james Osheale a lemster man 200. lead by jenkyn Fitz Simon of lecayle 200. in all 400. other foot Hugh Mac Cawel and two other captain under Cormock tyrones brother 600. son to art Mac baron 100 Brian Mac art 200. tyrones base son 100 Mac can 100 Owen o can 100 donough aferadogh Ohagan 100 Owen Ohagan 100 Donnel oneale for Owen eualle o Neale 100 three brother Gillaspick Mac Owen 100 rory Mac Owen 100 Randal Mac Owen 100 Kedagh Mac Donnel 100 Owen o Quin 100 james o hagan 100 Phelym Oge o Neale 60. Tirlogh brasills son 200. Henry wragton 200. Henry Oge Mac Henry Mac shane 100 Tirlogh Con Mac baron son 100 art Ohagan son to Hugh 100 Hugh groom Ohagan and his cousin 100 Donnel groom Mac Edmund 100 Patrick Mac Phelim 100 Gilleduff Mac Donnel one of Connaght 100 in all 3260. Totall of horse and foot 4060. all these three hundred except have mean for themselves and their company within Tyrone and diverse of they have great force beside these which they keep in their own country for defence thereof this present nine of August the lord deputic write to sir Robert Cecyll the secretary this follow letter sir I receive your letter of the five and twenty of july the four of this month be in camp near the place where the marshal Bagnol be slay the news you receive from the precedent of monster of spanish succour I do find no way more confirm then by the assure confidence this people have thereof out of the which they be grow from the most humble beg of their peace to exceed pride and the traitor himself so strong as believe i sir he have many more fight man in his army than we and yet we do not omit any thing of our purpose but have since our come to this camp make that a fair way to blackwater which the marshal shun when he be overthrow and every day cut down either his wood or his corn in his sight only with some slight skirmish in all which God be thank we can say we have any disaster hitherto but in all he lose some of his best man I presume there can nothing be add to our cowsell of the distribution of her majesty force but whether the fault have be in the commander of the several party or in some impediment which they pretend I know not but we receive little assistance by any of they and the whole brunt of the war lie upon we but out of the present judgement we make of this country we have discover and direct sir Arthur chichester a course which if we may enable he to go forward in will I hope utter banish Tyrone and have find another way into his country that if we can but build a fort and make a passage over the river we shall make Dungannon a centre whether we may from all part draw together all her majesty force and as I presume before this winter end not leave a man in the country of Tyrone whatsoever other have undertake I beseech you sir to remembet that in all my dispatch I have declare that the uttermost you can look from we in this summer work shall be to plant such garrison as must take effect this next winter and that we shall proceed slow and come short of our purpose if we be not continual supply with mean and in time of victual and all kind of munition if you have not be inform in due time of our want I must excuse myself that be continual employ in the active prosecution of as busy a war as any be in the world and most common far from Dublin where our magazine be I be drive in all the several kind of our mean to appoint of the counsel commissioner who I have desire in my absence to inform you of the estate of these thing and to solicit our want have direct the particular minister of every nature to inform they of the remain and to deliver they to be send over estimate of what we shall want only in general I have in all my dispatch protest that without sufficient supply of these thing we shall be drive to sit still and make all the rest of her majesty charge unprofitable sir I can from a quiet judgement make you a large and perfect discourse at this time of our affair for I be continual full of the present business and have so little rest as sometime these rogue will keep we wake all night but in short I dare assure you I see a fair way to make Ulster one of the most quiet assure and profitable province if the Spaniard do not come if they do I can say what we shall first resolve on till by the event we see their purpose for to provide for all place that carry equal probability of their undertake we can neither can I put myself into any part of Ireland with my chief strength but I may happen to be as far from their descent as I shall be where now I be which make i the more loath to forgo my hold in these part and yet for all occasion you must not imagine i to be now in the head of a great army but of some sixteen hundred fight man of who there be not half english and upon the news of spanish succour I know few irish that I can reckon we with this army I must make my retreat which I resolve to have leave most part in garrison all this winter in these quarter and in truth sir I can at this present think of a better counsel then that we may go on with the war by these garrison against Tyrone as we be determine whether the spaniard come or no and to make head against they chief with mean out of England by this course they shall give each other little assistance and if we do but ruin and waste the traitor this winter it will be impossible for the spaniard to make this people live by which course I presume it be in her majesty power to give the king of Spain a great blow and to quit this country of they for ever if in the check the queen do not find the weakness of her army I disclaim from the fault for without a wise honest muster-master of good reputation to be still present in the army the queen in that kind will never be well serve and upon those officer that be I do continual call for their care in that matter if according to our desire you have send we one thousand supply of shot to the Newrie it have advance the service more than I can express but some you must needs send we to be able to leave those garrison strong in winter most part of these troop I have here be they that have strike all the blow for the recovery of the kingdom and be in continual action and therefore you must not wonder if they be weak if sir Henry Dockwra do not plant Ballishannon I think it fit that sir Arthur chichester have a thousand man of his list who I hope we will find mean to plant within four or five mile of Dungannon and by boat victual they commodious I do apprehend the consequence of that plantation to be great but till I hear from you again I will take no man from Loughfoyle because I be loath to meddle much with that garrison without direction but I besecch you sir by the next let i know your opinion I pray you sir give i leave to take it unkind of my l precedent to inform you that sir hen. Dockwra have have great favour in the nomination of captain than he for he never place but one who I displace after to have some leave to his nomination be more than I can obtain when the last supply come to i but since it be the queen pleasure I must bear this and as I do continual a great deal more with patience and though I be willing you shall know I have a just feel of these thing yet I beseech you sir to believe that my meaning be not to contest or to impute the fault unto you for by God sir where I profess my love in the same kind I have do to you they shall be great matter that shall remove i although they may and I desire that I may let you know when they do move i I do only impute this to my misfortune that I perceive argument too many of her majesty displeasure but while for she own sake she do use my service I will love whatsoever I suffer for she and love the sentence that I will force from the conscience of all and the mouth of the just that I have be and will be a honest and no unprofitable servant unto she I dare undertake we have rid my lord precedent of the most dangerous rebel of monster and the most likely man to have renew the rebellion for that night I receive your letter the rogue do power above three thousand shot into our camp at which time it be our good fortune to kill Peirce Lacie and some other of their principal man we be now pray for a good wind for we be at our last day bread if victual come in time we will not be idle sir if I have recommend any into England I be sure it be for no charge for I know none that have go from hence but there be many that continue here more worthy of preferment than they therefore I pray sir let they not be reckon i that there challenge any thing for i but whatsoever shall please their lordship I must be content withal and it shall not much trouble i for I mean not to make the war my occupation and do affect asmuch to have a great many follower as to be trouble with a kennel of hound but for the queen sake I will glad have she serve by such as I know to be honest man and unhappy be that general that must fight with weapon of other man choose and so sir be ashamed that I have trouble you so long I desire you to be assure that no man shall love you more honest and faithful than myself from the camp near mount Norreys this nine of August 1601 you sir most assure to do you service Mountioy touch the above mention distaste between the lord deputy and the lord precedent of monster his lordship short after write a letter to he resent himself in very high term of the wrong he conceive to be offer he as follow in his lordship letter my lord as I have hitherto bear you as much affection and as true as ever I do profess it unto you and I protest rejoice in all your good success as my own so must you give i leave since I presume I have so just cause to challenge you of unkindness and wrong in write into England that in prefer your follower sir Henry Dockwra have have more power from i then yourself and consequent to solicit the queen to have the nomination of some captain in this kingdom for the first I can have wish you will have be better advise because upon my honour he never without my special warrant do appoint but one who I after displace and I do not remember that ever since our come over I have deny any thing which you have recommend unto i with the mark of your own desire to obtain it and in your province I have not give any place as I think but at your instance for the other I think it be the first example that ever any under another general desire or obtain the like suit and although I will not speak injurious of your desert nor immodest of my own yet this disgrace can make i believe that I have deserve worse than any that have be general before i but since it be the queen pleasure I must endure it and you choose a fit time to obtain that or any thing else against i yet I will concur with you in the service as long as it shall please her majesty to employ we here but hereafter I doubt not but to give you satisfaction that I be not worthy of this wrong the counsel and myself upon occasion of extraordinary consequence send for some of the company of monster out of Connaght when we hear you be to be supply with two thousand out of England but we receive from they a flat denial to come and the copy of your letter to warrant they therein if you have any authority from the queen to countermand i you may very well justify it but it be more than you have vow to i to have when I before my come over protest unto you that if you have I will rather serve the queen in prison than here my lord these be great disgrace to i and so conceive and I think just by all that know it which be and will be very short all Ireland my allegiance and own honour be now engage with all my burden to go on in this work otherwise no fear shall make i suffer thus much and what I do it be only love do move i unto it for I know you be dear to one who I be bind to respect with extraordinary affection and so my lord I wish you well and will omit nothing while I be in this kingdom to give you ^he best contentment I can and continue as your assure friend Mountioy the lord precedent within few day not only with a calm noble and wise answer pacify his lordship anger but also by many good office between his lordship and master secretary with who as a most inward friend he have great power so combine their new begin love as he engage his lordship in a great bond of thankfulness to he the eleven of August his lordship write to the governor of the newry that to avoid the continual trouble of conuoye hereafter he shall present send up as great provision of victual to armagh as possible he can while his lordship lie in that part with the army for two day after by reason of much rain fall and the expect of these victual his lordship lie still in the camp near mount Norris the thirteen the victual come but not in such great proportion as be direct because the victuler have fail to bake great part of his meal and the cow expect from dundalke be not yet send by the submitty according to their promise the same day his lordship rose and encamp three mile short of armagh the fourteen his lordship rise with the army and put all the victual he have receive into the abbey of armagh and the fort of blackewater and return back to the same camp place the fifteen his lordship draw back to his former camp near mount Norreis and send out some company of horse and foot to the skirt of the wood near the fort to guard those that cut wood for make of carres to transport more victual to the say garrison the sixteen his lordship draw back to Carickbane near the newry to hasten the provision of victual in as great quantity as may be which be dispatch within few day the twenty three his lordship write the follow letter to Mr secretary sir I do ever foresee and have signify so much that any foreign succour will clean alter the state of this kingdom and the whole frame of our proceed and do find that the assurance that these people do now receive thereof do make they stand upon other term than they be wont and much divert our purpose which we have conceive with good reason and great hope of any but the english we have small assurance and of they the army be exceed weak the irish new submit and their waver faith hithetto we have use to great effect for we have waste they and the rebel by they but when we come to lay our force in remote garrison they fly the hardness of that life and do again betake themselves unto any head that be of power to spoil and with the best pay and prevail party they will ever be I be certain tell by sir John Barkely that some spaniard that arrive at sligo as they say to discover and with assurance of the present come of a great force do there fortify and as he have be more particular inform not in a compass only capable of themselves but in such sort as it will be able to lodge great number this and my be prevent to follow my purpose in these part as I will draw i into the pale to advise of the best assurance for the main and yet not to quit my purpose in such sort in these part but if the spaniard do not come I may again look this way with my former desire which be to beat the chief traitor clean out of his country for until that be do there will be ever leave a fire which upon all occasion will break out more and more violent when I have speak with the rest of the counsel and consider more near the disposition of these inward part I will more large relate unto you my opinion neither will I now much trouble you with my own estate although not only myself but I protest the service do feel the effect of a general conceive rumour of her majesty displeasure to i I be so near interest therein that I can speak much of that matter without the prejudice of a private respect to myself but only this I most humble desire her majesty for her own sake to use i no long here than she think i fit to be trust and grace for without both I shall but strive against the wind and tide and be fit for nothing but my own poor harbour unto the which I most humble desire to be speedy call with her gracious favour since my own conscience make i presume to desire so much that best do know with how untollerable labour of mind and body I have and do continual serve she and so sir I beseech God to send you as much contentment as I do want the 23 of August 1601. you sir to do you service Mountioy the fortify of the spaniard at sligo vanish with the rumour which be ground upon some arrive to bring the rebel certain news of present succour and present return and the brute or perhaps his lordship jealousy of her majesty displeasure arise from the confession of some examine about the rash attempt of the unfortunate earl of Essex who have accuse the lord deputy to be privy to that project his lordship purpose to draw into the pale or part near Dublyn leave his force in the north for those of Loughfoyle have not yet correspondency with these in this follow manner dispose at Carickefergus sir Arthur chichester governor 200. sir Foalke Conway 150. captain Billings 150. captain Phillip 150. captain Norton 100 captain Egerton 100 foot 850. sir Arthur chichester 25. captain John jephson 100 horse 125. at lecaile sir Richard moryson governor 150. captain Toby Cawfield 150. foot 300. these follow force when they shall be draw out for convoy of victual or otherwise be to be command in chief by sir Francis Stafford and be thus dispose in several garrison at the newry sir Francis Stafford governor 200. captain jostas bodley 150. sir William Warren 100 foot 450. sir Francis Stafford 50 horse at mount Norreis captain Edward blaney governor 150. captain atherton 150 sir Samuel bagnol 150. captain rotheram 150. foot 600. sir Samuel bagnol 50 horse at armagh sir Henry Davers covernour 150. sir h. Follyot 150. capt. guest 150. capt. Thomas roper 150. captain Francis roe 100 capt. trever 100 foot 800. sir Henry Davers 100 captain darcy 25. horse 125. at blackewater captain William governor 150. captain Constable 100 sir garret Moor 100 foot 350. the twenty four of August his lordship leave the field ride back to the newry from whence he send one w. a Englishman in bond to the lord in England for the reason follow sir Henry Davers after his elder brother perish in the late earl of Essex his attempt be desirous by active prosecution of the rebel to deserve her majesty good opinion and for this cause as for that he be enable to do great service aswell by his noble virtue as by the command he former and now have both of horse and foot his lordship in special love to he be most willing to give he all opportunity to attain this his desire appoint he governor of armagh advise he to be often stir with the force under his command and to practice what possible he can devise upon the person of the arch-traitor to he this Englishman make offer to kill Tyrone yet will not discover his plot for great secrecy as he pretend neither will he press he further since he require no assistance and so in the night he be suffer to go by the watch and pass to tyrones camp whence he be employ to the islander scot and come to sir Arthur Chithester hand be by he send back from Knockefergus to his lordship at the newry where be examine what he have do in tyrones camp he avow that once he have draw his sword to kill he though under pretence of brag what he will do for his service yet give he no good account of his action or purpose but behave himself in such sort as his lordship judge he frantic though not the less fit for such a purpose now because he have not perform that he undertake and give a ill account of himself in this action his lordship aswell for the discharge of sir h. Davers who employ he as of himself who consent thereunto and advise sir h. Davers so to do think good to send he prisoner to the lord that he may be there examine where by reason of his friend dwell in London they may be sufficient inform of the man quality the five and twenty his lordship and the counsel there present write from the newry this follow letter to the lord in England it may please your lordship since our last letter we have for the most part employ ourselves in put up as great quantity of victual as we can to armagh and the blackewater be loath to engage ourselves into any thing which we have further purpose until we shall see the issue of this assure expectation of the spaniard invasion or till we may by some mean better strengthen this army of the first we have reason to be jealous both by many argument of assure confidence in this people of present succour and by the arrival of a spanish ship in which the arch-traytor agent be return with assurance that he leave the spanish force ordain for his aid in a readiness to set out for the strengthen of our army we have good reason to be provident consider the weakness thereof and especial of the english and find by experience the rebel strength now when he have none but the force nourish in Tyrone to assist he wherefore hear that sir Henry Dockwra have plant a garrison at Dunnagall and have leave o Donnel possess in a manner of nothing in Tirconnel and that upon the late arrival of his munition he intend to be active in those part near Loughfoyle and understand by master secretary that about the twelve of August there be two thousand man to be supply for monster we resolve to send for some of the company in Connaght of the monster list and to put the rest into Galloway and thereabouts for the assurance of those part and upon the receive of that addition to our strength to have draw to monaghan and spoil the corn of that country be of exceed quantity or if we have see reason to have leave a garrison there and to have enable it to gather the most part of that corn for their better provision or otherwise to have continue the prosecution in these part until we shall hear of the spaniard land or by any assurance of their not come shall be at liberty to proceed in our former purpose but receive answer from the captain of monster that they have direction not to stir from Connaght upon any other order whatsoever then from the precedent of monster in regard of the present expectation of spaniard to land in those part and we thereby be not so well able to wade any further in our determination for the north and receive some probable intelligence that the place design for the spaniard land be sligo we resolve to leave the northern garrison very strong in foot and horse and as well provide with mean as we can and to draw ourselves with the rest of our force towards Connaght appoint the rest of the cowsel to meet we in the way at trim to advise with we of the best course to establish the heart of the pale and to answer the present expectation of spanish force and although by our sudden leave the north we have omit some thing which we conceive to be of great consequence to the service yet if it shall please your lordship to supply the foundation we have lay in those part with one thousand shot according to our former suit and with store of victual for the garrison in winter we hope you shall find no small effect of our summer labour but see we be persuade that if any spanish force arrive they will land at sligo where they have a fit place to fortify to be relieve by sea to unite themselves with all the rebel force and where they have a fair country to possess with a easy way by the rebel assistance into monster or the hart of the pale or especial to Loughfoyle where we can without great difficulty affront they have no magazine of victual or munition at athlone or Galloway and where it be unpossible for we to provide ourselves or if we can most difficult to carry they by land when we be so far in the country and have no mean for carriage therefore we most humble desire your lordship to send good store of victual and munition to Galloway and to Lymbrick which howsoever our expectation fall out will be most necessary for the prosecution of Connaght and that prosecution as necessary this winter since o Donnel have forsake his own country and betake himself to live in that province but because we do foresee it to be no ill counsel for the spaniard to land at sligo and think that Tyrone will present urge they to cut off our garrison at Loughfoyle whether from thence they have a fair way and secure from our opposition and may imagine that it will be no great difficulty for they with such royal provision as they will bring to force those slender fortification we beseech your lordship to send a large provision of victual and munition to carlingford and Knockfergus for we can by any other way than that relieve Loughfoyle if it be distress neither can her majesty hazard any loss in these great provision though we never use they for all kind of victual may be issue in this kingdom with great gain and especial corn which we chief desire and for munition it may be keep with providence as a store for all occasion thus howsoever it fall out we shall be enable to make such a prosecution this winter in Connaght and the north as in all reason will end these war it the spaniard come not and will leave this province of Ulster in far great subjection than ever any of her majesty progenitor have it and since we apprehend that Spain may make in this country a dangerous war for England we conceive that if not now yet with his first ability he will embrace it which make we to have the great desire if it be possible to prevent his foot here for ever and that by God help we hope to do before this next summer if we may be enable this winter to ruin Tyrone and o Donnel we have great need of one hundred northern horse for our horse here grow weak and ill and if your lordship please to afford we that number we will so handle the matter as it shall be no increase of the list if the spaniard come we must have at the least three hundred and if they be northern horse and northern rider we assure ourselves they will be much fit for this service than such as be usual send hither who come with purpose to get licence to return and yet be a great charge to her majesty but for the one thousand foot we desire by our former letter we find their come to be of that necessity as we must be most humble and earnest suitor to your lordship to send they present for our company be so exceed weak and now decay so fast by the extremity of the weather as a much great number will not supply we but that the check will be as great as now they be and they little be see among we which give we cause to wish now and humble to move your lordship to be please to send one thousand foot more soon after the report here be so uncertain as until we meet the rest of the counsel at trim we know not how the pale stand affect upon this assure confidence of the spaniard come only this we perceive many of they be waver yet the lord hereabout namely Mac Gennis Tirlogh Mac Henry ever Mac cooley and o hanlon keep with we notwithstanding that Tyrone have send they word that hereafter it will be too late for they to make their peace with he if they do it not now upon this occasion and they assute we as much as man can do that they will not fall again from their obedience though thereby their state be no better than horseboye but of this we can give your lordship no assurance neither in they have we any extraordinary confidence it may further please your lordship to be advertise that the lord of Dunsany have the command of a fort in the Brenny call Liscanon where we have place certain irish company as fit to spoil and waste the country thereabouts do late draw most of they into Mac mahow country for the take of a prey which they light on as be say to the number of some sixteen hundred cow but in their return be hardly lay unto as some of they say with very great number yet as we have hear by some that be present not above senenscore they do not only lose their prey but according to the manner of the irish who have no other kind of retreat fall to a flat run away to the fort so as poor captain esmond who have the command of the rear and very valiant with a few make good the place be sore hurt and afterward take prisoner and forty or fifty of our side slay we can learn that any english be among they so as we account our loss to be no more than the take of the captain neither do the rebel brag thereof both because they escape not free lose very near as many man as we do and for that they know they deal but with their countryman who as they do hold it no shame to run when they like not to fight though we mean to call some of we to account thereof and so we most humble take leave the eight and twenty of August his lordship receive two letter from the lord precedent of monster the first import that hear that his lordship have send into Connaght for part of the company of the monster list to come into the north he pray to be excuse that he have give contrary direction upon fear of the spaniard land and the knowledge of tirrel purpose to come with the banish monster man and aid of northern man out of Connaght present to disturb the province of monster and signify that now to manifest his precise obedience to his lordship command he have send they direction to march towards his lordship upon sight of his letter yet pray his lordship to send some part of they into Munster without which help he can neither keep the field against tirrel and the provincial fugitive at their first entry nor upon the spaniard arrival give any impediment to their dispose of such town as be recommend to his special care and assure his lordship that the spaniard have be see at sea and that in his judgement and by vulgar report it be likely they will make descent in some part of monster last advertise that he have send james the suggon pretend earl of Desmond and Florence Mac carty the chief practiser with the spaniard in those part into England the second letter import the lord president recommendation which by establish course be effectual to his lordship for the grant of her majesty pardon for land life and good to five hundred forty two inhabitant of Muskery and other part in the county of cork for which present warrant be according give the nine and twenty day his lordship come to trim where the counsellor come from Dublin meet he according to appointment here they consult of the public affair more particular how that part of the army within Lemster may be employ to prosecute tirrel send by Tyrone to disturb that province and yet to be ready upon any sudden occasion to make head against the foreign enemy and the advertisement be daily multiply that the spaniard be at sea it be conclude that in regard these force be not able to answer both or either the end aforesaid great part of the army in Ulster shall be draw down and both force join shall assail tirrel who come to insult over the subject and to draw they to rebellion but especial the late submitty who by many promise and threaten he have tempt to a relapse but prevail not with they and his lordship resolve by his presence to give a sharp edge to this service till either he shall be call to affront the spaniard land or to draw back into the north if they land not the three of September his lordship and the counsel here write unto the lord in England excuse that the extraordinary expense have far pass the limit sum of six thousand pound yearly which be far too little for the transportation of victual carriage of munition charge and imprest to victualler reward to messenger and for special service make of boat and thing of like necessity and the repair of castle house bridge fort and all building in which last charge they have not be able fit to repair athlone castle the key of Connaght nor the castle and bridge of Carlogh and laughlin and the fort of Phillipstowne and mary-burgh be of great consequence to curb the traitor and assure the subject and the decay whereof will give the rebel free passage into many country beside our dishonour to neglect those place which the wisdom of former time with great policy plant the great charge of repair whereof appear by the transmit certificate of commissioner appoint to view these place and for these reason they beseech her majesty warrant to leave this charge to their discretion for a time without any limitation promise not to enlarge the same in any thing which may be spare without apparent prejudice to her service and give their opinion that in this time of the new coin these place may be repair with small charge likewise they desire to have great store of munition and victual send over and that present to prevent the usual contrariety of wind after Michaelmas and all the winter season last they desire to have the one thousand shot present send over for which they have former write the army consist in great part of irish which can not be keep to live in garrison out of their own country and they advertise the lord that diverse of the horse at twelve penny have quit their pay be not able to live thereupon in those dear time this three of September likewise his lordship receive letter from her majesty give warrant for the pay of two thousand man send into monster be above the establishment the same day his lordship receive letter from sir Robert Cecyll secretary that the spaniard be discover near the silly and as he think they will land at Lymrick be forty five sail whereof seventeen be man of war whereof six be galleon the rest of one hundred or one hundred and fifty ton burden and have in they six thousand soldier pray his lordship to demand such supply as he think needful and upon the spaniard land to name the place whether the supply shall be send and assure his lordship that the two thousand man for monster be already embark the same time his lordship receive letter from the lord in England import her majesty acceptance of his service with her willingness and they to send he needful supply pray he to demand they timely because he can hardly receive they from England in six week after the demand the wind stand favourable likewise profess
