our own ship by the fourteen of this October at Rochester because they shall be secure in their transportation two thousand more shall be send by the twenty of this month to Bristol and Barstable and the five odd thousand shall be send to loughsoyle so as out leafy be now in all five thousand man the same day his lordship receive another letter from the queen of her own hand and sign below not above as the usual sign as follow since the brainsick humour of unadvised assault have seize on the heart of out causeless foe we doubt not but their gain will be their bane and glory their shame that ever they have the thought thereof and that your humour agree so sight with we we think it most fortunate happen in your rule to show the better who you be and what you be as your own hand write have tell we of late and do beseech the almighty power of the high so to guide your hand that nothing light in vain but to prosper your heed that nothing be leave behind that may avail your praise and that yourself in venture too far make not the foe a prey of you tell our army from we that they make full account that every hundred of they will beat a thousand and every thousand they double I be the bold to pronounce it in his name that ever have protect my righteous cause in which I bless they all and put you in the first place I end scribble in haste your love sovereign e. r. the same day his lordship receive letter from the lord in England signify that run ship of war set sail from Rochester with the first wind after the eight of October last to attend the coast of monster wherein be send two thousand foot for the army in monster under captain appoint that two thousand more be then levy to be send to the army by the way of Bristol and Barstable which be leave to his lordship to be dispose in supply or company as he think fit that one thousand foot more be send to supply Loughfoyle garrison that two hundred horse be send to his lordship for the army and fifty horse to Loughfoyle that they have send his lordship beside the former twenty last of powder thirty last more that they have send large provision of victual and that they great commend the lord president providence that he have make his soldier former lie live of their pay in money and so preserve the former store of victual in monster for this time without which the army can not have keep the field till the new provision arrive the last part of their l ps letter follow in these word hereunto we must add this as that whereof ourselves have be a good while both hearer and observer that no prince can apprehend with better acceptation your lordship proceed in that kingdom than her majesty do in so much as she use often this speech that she will not wish her army there nor the safety of her people in better hand then in you in who and so in other principal officer of her state and army as she do observe that all difficulty be well entertain with alacrity and resolution so we must let your lordship know that when her majesty have read a private letter of you to i the principal secretary write from kilkenny with your own hand assoon as you have hear the news of a foreign enemy it please her majesty to cause it be read to we all as be write in a style wherein she discern both the strong power of your own mind in promise to yourself all happy success against such a enemy and the lively affection you bear to her person for which you desire to be make a sacrifice wherein although you have not deceive her former expectation yet her majesty will have you know that she do not doubt but you shall live to do she many more service after you have make the province of monster serve for a sepulchre to these new conqueror of the four thousand man which now her majesty send into monster we send only two thousand under captain the rest we leave to conductor to be use as you shall please when they arrive and to displace any who we do send if you think they not sufficient now therefore till we hear further from you we have no more to say but that we account ourselves all in one ship with you that we will all concur to advance by our ministry whatsoever her majesty shall resolve to do for you all of we have one end and one desire to enable you as her majesty principal instrument to free that kingdom from the malicious attempt of foreign power and to redeem it out of the in ward misery by intestine rebellion the five of November four bark with munition and victual that be send from Dublin arrive in kinsale harbour and upon certain intelligence that Tyrone be come up with a great army to join with the Spaniard it be resolve by the counsel of state and the colonel of council at war that the next day the camp shall be fortify against Tyrone on the north side further from the towneward and that the next day follow the lord precedent with two regiment of foot consist of two thousand one hundred man in list and with three hundred twenty five horse shall draw to the border of the province to stop or at least hinder tyrones passage to which purpose the lord barry and the lord bourke with the force of the country have direction to attend the lord precedent the six day the camp be according fortify and the seven in the morning the lord precedent with the say horse and foot leave the camp at which time it be conclude by both counsel that we can attempt nothing against the town until either the lord precedent return or the new force and provision promise from England arrive it be judge a great work for we in the mean time to continue our lie before the town since the spaniard in the town be more in number than we who besiege they the same seven day his lordship and the counsel here write to the lord in England this follow letter it may please your lordship the first and second of this present month we receive her majesty and your lordship most comfortable letter of the four and six of the last and for the special care it please you to take of we do yield as we have just cause our most humble and hearty thanks protest that we will labour to deserve the same and the continuance which it please your lordship to promise thereof with the uttermost of our endeavour and service even to the sacrifice of our life and in the mean time humble pray your lordship both to accept in good part and favourable to report unto her majesty what hitherto we have be able to perform though nothing to that we do affect if our mean have answer our desire or that little we expect to come foam Dublin which we send for upon the spaniard first land here have by a more favourable wind arrive soon as we hope we beseech your lordship give we leave to refer you for your information in that point to the journal which herewithal we send for thereby we conceive will best appear both what we have do and be enable to do since the return of master Martial and other officer and commander send of purpose into the pale and the part northwards to draw the force thereabouts the more speedy hither to we and to hasten hither such other provision as (...) here we shall have need off and with your lordship favour license we to add that we can hardly proceed any further till our supply of man and munition come for we find it a work of great difficulty and assure loss of man and expense of all provision of war to undertake with these mean we have to force so many man out of any place although it be not great otherwise fortify but by the body of man only whereas this town of kinsale have a good wall and many strong castle in it we do look hourly for Tyrone esteem to be come with a great army of horse and foot select out of all the rebel in Ireland and from all other that he can seduce to his party at his come these provincial will discover themselves either against we or neutral as they be for better we do not expect from they except out supply do come before his arrival we shall hardly be master of the field but rather drive we fear to discontinue our siege yet if her majesty ship provision and supply do arrive in any time we hope to give she ere it be long a good account of this place though we desire your lordship to consider the difficulty we have to contend with in this country and season of the year beside the force and opposition of the enemy i the precedent do acknowledge the receipt of such a intelligence concern captain a. as it please your lordship to remember and since that time keep very good espial upon he and have have the sight of all his paper yet can find any thing give i cause to suspect he and therefore we all think it fit see his company be return hither among other to make use of his service here for which we find he very fit until there may be some apt occasion to dispose of he elsewhere without give he discontentment unless we have more particular and certain ground to charge he with which we must receive from thence yet in the mean time he shall be so narrow look unto as if he have the will which we doubt not he shall not have mean to hurt much the same seven day his lordship understand from master secretary by his letter date the nineteen of the last month and receive the second of this month that he find her majesty incline to make one governor over all Ulster and especial to like of sir Arthur chichester for that great charge whereupon he purpose to proceed therein if his lordship will explain himself how he will have that matter carry his lordship write his mind plain therein advise that sir Arthur chichester shall be make governor of all Ulster by what name it shall please her majesty to give he whereby he may direct all the part of that province and be resident where he shall think fit for the service command in chief where he come the manage of the war to be in general leave to himself except he receive particular direction upon special occasion from England or from the lord deputy add that from he the lord may be more speedy inform of what be do or fit to be require for the make of that war who of all other commander he think fit for that charge pray that the lord there will advise he and sir Henry Dockwra to hold a good correspondency for her majesty service since he conceive the war be to be chief make by their two join together for the scot that any number not exceed four thousand might in his conceit fit be entertain and leave to the disposal of sir Arthur chichester who judgement upon the state of thing there be fit to be follow for he can best choose apt place to lodge they till sir Henry Dockwra and he may draw into the field when those scot shall join with they and will be of very great use to spoil which be the best service can be do upon the irish. conclude that he have write to sir Arthur chichester that he shall send into England to he the secretary his opinion in this business which no doubt he will present do but this project of appoint a governor in Ulster take no effect by reason that Tyrone with most of his force be defeat short after in monster master secretary at the same time have send his lordship spanish news which in this his answer he confess be very likely yet think it will be very hard for they to make ready four thousand man more before Christmas he signify that they here be all of opinion that the necessity of the spanish force already in Ireland be more than be expect both by loss at sea and since their come hither and by the fail of the irish hitherto to join with they their supply will be hasten soon then be determine for so they be advertise by all the prisoner take and by such as do come unto we from they his lordship acknowledge himself very much bind unto master secretary for the good dispatch he procure with this last passage and not the least that her majesty be please to allow of their entertainment of the irish yet beseech he to believe that by this course they have prevent Tyrone of a great many man that otherwise will and must have serve he for entertainment have no other mean to live and yet hitherto all be do within the compass of the list and the white knight be one of they that before the come of this approbation be provide for so careful be he to give he good contentment for their outcry in the pale he answer that he do not marvel for by that which he have observe he do judge that the word pale have cost the queen a million and yet so ill be they dispose and so backward even in their own defence as they now suffer Tyrone with a few horse about the number of 60 to burn and prey they at his pleasure though they be able of themselves to have beat he and all his force and beside have the assistance of company in the queen pay be three thousand or two thousand at least yet if he will consider that four thousand spaniard for so the prisoner that we take deliver they to be upon his salvation with who all our prisoner relation and our intelligence do concur be possess of a towneful of strong house and wall about and help with many advantage of ground though command by some place he may easy conceive that it must be no small army can force they since our approach this winter be so difficult that the very trench we make be continual fill with water and the decay of our man be so great by continual labour sickness sword and bullet and therefore we have no reason to keep a great body of man in the pale to guard it till this dangerous war be fivish but to prevent this clamour of the pale it be mere unpossible though it will please her majesty to keep there ten thousand in she pay when they will not stir nor raise the cry but suffer themselves to be so use out of the malice of their own heart that they may have some colour of complaint be the worst sort of people in all the kingdom though he protest he have be as careful of they as if they have be his kindred or special friend know well their humour to be so clamerous for her majesty expense he beseech he to believe that no man do look upon it with more grief than himself who reap no commodity by it only be a painful and faithful distributer thereof according to the necessity of her own service but if he do not from his soul desire and with all his wit and endeavour seek to abridge it and to end both her war and charge than he desire no mercy of God nor favour from she and if he be not bind thereto by his public duty yet he protest that his private estate will urge he thereunto which he find unable any long to continue the expense at which he be force to live grow great by the mix coin as he ever think it will fall very heavy upon he by which reason he may value his entertainment to be less by the half than it be in the time of the old standard for whatsoever we buy with this new coin it be raise to the double price whereas it seem master secretary have be inform that all they which of late submit themselves to her majesty mercy and protection be now again revolt to the rebel he answer that it be true that some of they have make their peace with Tyrone and in truth except we can have give order for their defence against he he do never expect other from they and especial since the arrival of this foreign force he do think none in Ireland so sure but even here in monster they will do the like if our army do not hang over they yea he be sure that the lord precedent be of the same opinion but he be not move to preserve any thing which the world to his disadvantage may call he by neglect that which he know fit to be preserve for her majesty and her service touch these submitty while they be in rebellion he do spoil waste and kill many of they when they be receive to mercy he make many of they kill other in rebellion and lose their life for the queen service and now they be again revolt he doubt not but either to ruin they again or to force they to submission upon what condition he list if God please to send we a happy end of this war with the foreign enemy for the achievement whereof he hope he shall hear by the grace of the eternal God that they will adventure as far and in as good a fashion as may be expect from this army upon the arrival of the supply of man and munition till when he protest that we be at a stand because that he be most sure that without good number of man and store of all sort of munition this town so man as it be can not be force he add that hitherto God bethank we have in all our endeavour prosper against this proud enemy and that there never be army better dispose then this nor commander that continual show more sound judgement and brave resolution than we have do in particular that sir Oliver s. john have have great honour give he by the whole army for that which he do in their sight for he find no man come off from the skirmish mention in the journal which speak not of what he have extraordinary perform with his own hand and that in a far great measure than be therein relate that at the same time the lord Audley be hurt fight very gallant and if it shall please her majesty to take notice thereof it will be a great comfort to they and encouragement to the rest that he hope God will enable we short to send he relation of better service in the mean time and ever he pray the eternal God to preserve her majesty and her kingdom and send they her poor servant peace and quietness he add what he can wish it have please her majesty to have leave the horse to his disposal or at the least to have bestow they on some englishman for as 100 be confer though he hold the commander a worthy gentleman and faithful to her majesty service yet he be irish and in short time will make those horse irish so as he account to have receive only one hundred to serve his purpose for he do very much build on those horse not only to be his chief strength at this time but to have stand hereafter when her majesty do lessen the army to be employ for the absolute finish of this war and to this purpost now in all likelihood he shall not be able to make use of they which he confess do not a little trouble he he add that although there be but four thousand spaniard already land and they have no horse yet there be no doubt but they will avail themselves of great assistance in this country and that with a number much about this of natural spaniard their king have make the long continue great war in the low-countries beside he beseech he to remember that about two hundred spaniard hold a fort late build at Croyden in Brittany till sir John Norreis lose very near one thousand five hundred man before it that God bethank we have pluck one hundred fitfy spaniard by the ear out of Rincorran and we hope by the grace of God to do the like by they in kinsale yet except God shall please extraordinary to work for we this be not likely to be do without great loss of man and expense of all provision to that purpose for now they begin to work very hard about fortify of the town find as themselves say that they have other man to deal with than they expect etc. the eight of November certain ship to the number of thirteen be descry to pass by kinsale to the westward but it be not know whether they be english or spaniard the ten day we have news that the earl of Thomond be land with one thousand foot leave to the lord deputy disposal and with a hundred horse appoint in England to be command by the say earl and these be the thirteen ship discover to pass westward by this time the spaniard have get knowledge of the lord president departure from the camp with good part of our force and thereupon suppose we to be much weaken as in deed we be and inferior in body of man to they in the town they draw out this day about noon most part of their force and soon after send some sixty shot and pike to the foot of the hill close by our camp leave their trench very well line for their second some of we be present draw out to entertain skirmish with those that come up and another strong party be send but towards Ryncorran who from the bushy hill play in flank upon their trench and do beat they from the same so as they that be first send out close to out camp be beat back by our shot and think to find the second they leave behind they be disappoint by their quit of the trench and by that mean drive to follow the rest to the succour of the town our man follew with much fury hurt and kill diverse among who they bring off the body of a sergeant and possess the enemy trench the which the enemy be reinforce make many attempt to regain but be repulse and beat back into the town we hear by diverse that don jean commit the sergeant mayor who command then in chief present after the fight and threaten to take his head commend high the valour of our man and cry shame upon the cowardice of his own who he say have be the terror of all nation but now have lose that reputation and he give straight commandment upon pain of death which he cause to be set up on the town gate that from thenceforth no man shall come off from any service until he shall be fetch off by his officer though his powder be spend or his piece break but make good his place with his Sword captain Soto one of their best commander be that day slay for who they make very great moan and some twenty more beside those we hurt which can not but be many on our side only some ten be hurt and three kill among who master Hopton a gentleman of the lord deputy band be sore hurt and in few day die thereof if this skirmish have not be ready and resolute answer on our part the spaniard have then discover the smallness of our number and will no doubt have so ply we with continual sally as we shall hardly have be able to continue the siege the eleven day we have news that the one hundred horse and the thousand foot embark at bastable both which be leave to the lord deputy disposal the horse to be make new troop the foot to be disperse for supply or to raise new company as his lordship shall think fit be arrive at Waterford the twelve day sir Richard levison admiral of the queen fleet send into Ireland and sir Amias Preston vice-admiral be arrive with ten ship of war at cork wherein we have two thousand foot all under captain appoint in England beside other provision of artillery and munition and his lordship direct the admiral with all speed to bring the fleet into the harbour of kinsale the thirteen day his lordship write to master secretary this follow letter sir hear that our last packet be not yet go from cork by reason of the contrariety of the wind I have so good occasion to make this addition to our former dispatch that I have receive letter from my lord of Thomond Sr Anthony Cook and other from diverse place that all the supply appoint for this province be safe arrive at Waterford Yoghall cork and castle haven with no loss that I can hear of but of one victular although the weather have be extreme tempestuous and this last evening I be inform but by a rumour that the queen ship be discover about the haven of cork whereupon I present dispatch to sir Richard levyson to put into the haven of kinsale for otherwise it will be long ere we shall be able to avail ourselves of such artillery and munition as he bring for we from my lord precedent I hear that the rebel be draw down very strong whereupon I have direct sir Christopher s. Laurence that be come to the camp with most of the force of the pale to repair speedy to my lord precedent and I mean to send unto he all the horse that be now come out of England which I hope will be force sufficient to stop any power the rebel can make special since Tyrone as I hear himself will not be with they except they do steal by which will be hear to prevent if they come to force their passage I be confident that against so many horse as the lord precedent shall have they will never put themselves upon the plain for although they be as dangerous a enemy as any be in the world when we be drive to seek they in their strength or pass their fastness yet be they the worst and weak to force their own way either upon straits or plain so that except they steal their passage which I fear most I make no doubt but my lord precedent will give a very good account of they we here in the camp since our last letter have not have much to do only the enemy one day draw out I think most of his whole force upon opinion that the great part of our army be go from we to meet the rebel and begin a round fight with we close to our trench but we entertain they so well that we wait on they home to the wall of the town and make they leave some of their dead body behind they although we see they carry many off with they they have make within less they caliver shoot of our trench very good fight even from thence close to the town so that our man do follow they with great disadvantage yet we do beat they from one trench to another till I have much ado to make our soldier come off the great loss of our side fall to my share for I have one of my company kill and a very gallant gentleman that serve in that band call Mr. Hopton hurt I fear to death and I think there be not above 2 or 3 more that be kill in the camp over our head while we be in the skirmish then we make they so good a muster that they have think good to check we no more but within the town and without they do work very hard and have raise raveling and mount and we on the contrary side keep very good watch for if we shall receive but one blow of the spanish fencer all Ireland will take heart with it and we have no great reason to be very secure for believe i upon my honour I think the besiege be more in number than we that be the besieger at this time they do continual taste we but they find we so well at our ward that they still go away with the veny and now if the queen ship be come we will cast at all and I hope in God ere it be long win a fair game for the queen who money we play if any without consideration of the just circumstance of our present business to serve any private purpose tax i for be too negligent of other part of this kingdom and too large in my demand I beseech you sir to believe that I have good ground both for my precipitate draw hither and for the provision I have crave to strengthen myself by all mean while I be about this work for the first it be not my opinion only but my lord precedent that if I do not sudden make head to this force most of this province will have revolt and if we have suffer the force of Spain to have be master of the field but six day as easy they may have be if we have not seek well to prevent it I assure self fe that all the town of this province will have revolt and the current of that fortune will have run so violent through all Ireland that it will have be too late o have stop it for the second the difficulty of a winter siege in this country where by reason of the great number of the besiege we be force to keep strong and continual guard will soon waste a great army than we if God do not mighty bless we for the weather be so extreme that many time we bring our sentinel dead from the station and I protest even our chief commander who diligence I can but mighty commend do many of they look like spirit with toil and watch unto the which we be with good reason move since there be many example that where a enemy can sally out with two or three thousand man they have defeat army that have be treble our number but now beside these ordinary difficulty which in all winter siege do waste or make unprofitable the great part of a army when we be to make our near approach to force they we can do it without great loss for although the town be weak against the canon yet can we plant the canon no where but they have place that do absolute command it so that the town be weak to defend itself yet exceed strong to offend which be the best part that art can add to any fortification and this be so well provide by nature that from one hill they beat into any ground that we can lodge in near they all these difficulty and many more I do not allege as be any way diffident of the great favour that God be determine to show her majesty in this action but that you may in some measure guess that we be not so improvident in her majesty cause as to require a army and charge of great proportion than be fit for such a task the which when we have perform with that happiness that I hope the eternal God will blosse we with all I will then say and prove it unto you at large a and mirahilc (@) (@) sir if I shall write all unto you that I have a desire to let you know both for the public and my private I shall not end my letter before the time that I hope we shall beat the spaniard but have be up most of this night it grow now about four a clock in the morning at which time I light choose to visit our guard myself and be now go about that business in a morning as cold as a stone and as dark as pitch and I pray sir think whether this be a life that I take much delight in who heretofore in England when I have have a suit to the queen can not lie in a tent in the summer nor watch at night till she have sup but by God sir I will do for queen Elizabeth that which I will not do for myself and willing and be you my pledge that I will faithful serve she against all the world or any in the world or else I beseech God now I be go out that I may never return alive to my house of turf in the which I write this at her maieiste camp before kinsale this thirteen of November 1601. the thirteen day our fleet recover the mouth of kinsale harbour but can not get in the wind be strong against they the fourteen day the fleet with much difficulty warp in and recover the harbour whence the admiral and vice-admiral come to the lord deputy at the campe. this night and the next day the two thousand foot send under captain in the queen ship be land and come to the campe. and the fifteen day in the afternoon the lord deputy go aboard the ship whence return to the camp the enemy discern he ride in the head of a troop of horse and make a shot out of the town at he which graze so near he that it do beat the earth in his face in these ship be send unto we not only artillery and munition but also special officer to attend the same as five cannoneer two blacke-smith two wheelewrights and two carpenter this day the lord deputy be advertise that according to his former direction sir Christopher St Laurence be come out of the pale and the earl of Clanrickard out of Connaght to the lord president camp to who his lordship write that if the rebel shall slip by he he shall be careful to come up with his force to our camp so as he may arrive there to join with we before the rebel come up so far the queen ship after they have salute the lord deputy at his go aboard with thunder peal of ordinance have direction the next day to beat upon a castle in the island call castle nyparke which the lord deputy be resolve to make his next work and to beat the spaniard out of it and so to invest the town on that side this some of the ship perform and break the top of the castle but find that they do it no great hurt and that the weather be extreme stormy they cease shoot this day his lordship give direction that the hundred horse and one thousand foot which first land at castle haven and now be arrive from thence in the harbour of kinsale shall be conduct to cork to refresh themselves for be beat at sea and now land in extreme weather and in a winter camp where they have no mean to be refresh they begin to die and will have be lose or make unserviceable if this course have not be take to hearten they this day and for many day after diverse spaniard run from the town to we by who we understand that in the ten day skirmish the above name captain Soto a man of special account be slay the seventeen day the weather continue stormy so as neither that day nor the next we can land our ordinance or do any thing of moment yet because this be the day of her majesty coronation which his lordship purpose to solemnize with some extraordinary attempt if the weather will have suffer we to look abroad we send at night when the storm be some what appease the sergeant Maior and captain bodley with some four hundred foot to discover the ground about castle n. park and to see whether it may be carry with the pickaxe which be according attempt but the engine we have get to defend our man while they be to work be not so strong as it shall have be they within the castle have store of very great stone on the top rumble they down so fast as they break it so that our man return with the loss of two man and proceed no further in that course the eighteen day the lord deputy call a counsel both of the counsel of Ireland and of all the colonel and chief officer of the field and propound to they that since it have please her majesty so gracious to supply we with the matter and provision for the war it be our part to adulse of such a form as may be most likely to bring forth a effect not unworthy her princely care first our strength and mean to attempt the place or continue the siege be thorough consider and next the number and commodity of the enemy in the town and of their succour abroad the commodity and incommodity of proceed with expedition or by keep they from all relief be thorough dispute and in the end it be conclude that the sound course be to use all mean to invest they as speedy as we may by possess ourselves of all they hold without the town and next to mount our artillery in such place where it may annoy they most and by break down their house to expose they to the same extremity of cold and rain as we be expose to in the camp by which mean they may be reduce to a great weakness and then be force with much less hazard since when it come to the point of enter of a breach there be little or no difference between a strong town and a weak for the besiege in either do whole trust to their new and sudden work which the enemy within have as good opportunity to do in this place as in any other and have yet of our knowledge so many hand to fight as that the advantage will chief have be he the nineteen day a cannon be unship assoon as it be calm and place on this side of the water which play most part of the day upon the castle nyparke be a great relief to the besiege and break many place but make no breach that be assaultable in the night they of the town attempt to relieve the castle by boat but be repel by captain Tolkerne and captain Ward who lie with their pinnace between the island and the town hitherto nothing can possible be attempt against the town more than have be do for consider that the number of the defendant not only equal but by all report exceed the number of the besieger yea exceed they far after the lord precedent be send from the camp to meet Tyrone with two thousand one hundred foot and three hundred and twenty horse and consider that if we have undertake the carry of approach with a purpose to batter the whole army must either have be tire with watch night and day without shelter in tempestuous weather or disgraceful have forsake the work or to say the best incur the hazard of fight in place of disadvantage with a expert enemy and consider that the country stand upon such tickle term and so general ill affect to our side that almost the least blow which in the doubtful event of war may have light upon we will have drive they headlong into a general revolt and further that our army consist for a three part at the least of irish who be not fit to make good a entrench camp and much less fit to give upon a breach will without question either present have quit we or turn their weapon against we if the spaniard have have any hand over we and consider that in all sound judgement this little army which be to be the soul of that body that shall oppose itself against these invader and rebel be by all possible mean to be preserve as much as may be and not at all venture but with manifest assurance to prevail these thing with other like circumstance consider what can there be more do during the time that we want our supply and second but to assure our camp with careful watch against sally or surprise of the enemy and to invest they from succour or relief not omit in the mean time to provide whatsoever may be needful for the business in hand the meeting with all inconvenience and the take of all advantage upon the enemy guard without the town for which purpose diverse skirmish be make with very good success on our part the list of the army at kinsale the twenty of November the foot of the list the seven and twenty of October be 6900. the company draw since that list from other part of the kingdom to kinsale campe. sir Francis rush 150. captain masterson 150. captain Thomas Butler 100 sir Richard greame 100 captain Toby Cawfield 150. sir Christopher s. Laurence 150. sir Henry Harrington 100 sir Samuel bagnol 150. sir Edward harbert 100 sir William Warren 100 sir Edward Fitzgarret 100 sir tybbot dillon 100 sir garret Moor 100 captain Lionel guest 150. captain Malby 150. the earl of Clanrickard 150. sir Thomas bourke 150. captain Clare 150. captain Thomas bourke 100 captain Laurence esmond 150. sir George Bourcher 100 foot 2650. company send in the queen ship under captain viz. captain Sheffeild 100 captain Norton 100 captain Henry fortescue 100 captain Bret 100 captain lower 100 captain chatterton 100 captain Dorington 100 captain Crompton 100 captain Gilbert 100 captain wade 100 sir Anthonic Cook 150. sir Alexander Clifford 150. captain lane 100 captain Wadnol 100 captain Blandel 100 captain May 100 captain wynn 100 captain Kenricke 100 captain Butler 100 foot 2000 of the one thousand foot land at castle-haven with the earl of Thomond and the one thousand foot land at Waterford with sir Anthony Cook have no captain but be leave to the lord deputy disposal one thousand three hundred fifty be distribute among the captain to supply the deficient number in their several company and the rest be divide into these follow company increase the list sir garret haruye 150. captain Henry Barkeley 150. captain Roberts 150. captain boyse 100 captain Henslo for pioner 100 foot 650 Totall of foot 12200. hereof in the old list take out for a dead company keep for the earl of Desmond 100 take now out absent sir George Thorton in garrison at Kilmallock capt. Gawen haruye in garrison at Limricke and captain trever reckon before but not come hither who stay about the newry as I remember 300. so the Totall of foot be 11800. of these not distribute into regiment for attendance of the munition sir George Bourcher master of the ordinance 100 for pioner captain Hensloe 100 foot 200 foot distribute into eleven regiment under command of the lord deputy lord precedent and nine colonel under the lord deputy command by his lieutenant sir Benjamin Berry 1400. under the lord precedent 1100. under the earl of Clanrickard 1000 under the earl of Thomond 1000 under the lord Audley 900. sir Richard Percy 950. sir Richard moryson 1100. sir Charles Willmot 1000 sir Oliver saint john 1050. sir Henry follyet 1050. sir Christopher saint Laurence 1050. foot 11600. Totall of foot 11800. out of these regiment be raise a squadron or fly regiment which only be to answer alarm and to be free of all watch and to the same sir Henry power be appoint colonel and captain bostock his lieutenant the several company of this squadron be these out of the lord deputy regiment sir Richard Wingfield Martial 150. sir John Barkeley sergeant mayor 200. out of the lord president regiment captain Saxey 100 out of the lord Audley regiment the treasurer lieutenant 100 out of sir Charles willmots regiment captain Nuse 100 out of sir Henry follyots regiment captain josias bodley 150. out of sir Oliver saint john regiment captain bostock 100 out of the earl of clanrickards regiment captain Laurence esmond 150. foot 1050. horse in the army at kinsale take out of the list make the seven and twenty of October sir Edward harbert 12 and captain George greame twelve and thirty of master marshal otherwhere employ and now absent from the camp and the whole list be five hundred fifty seven horse call since that time from other part in the kingdom to the camp at kinsale the earl of Kildare 50. sir Oliver Lambert 25. sir garret more 25. horse 100 horse new send over and land at castle-haven and at Waterford the lord precedent add to his troop 50. the earl of Thomond a troop new erect 100 sir William godolphin who command the lord deputy troop have new erect to his own use 50. horse 200. Totall of horse 857. the twenty of November his lordship understand by letter from the lord in England that one thousand foot and fifty horse be send and already ship for Loughfoyle the same day the demi-canon plant the day before do again batter castle nyparke together with another cannon this day land and plant by it and with some ordinance also out of the ship though they serve to small purpose about noon one hundred man be send with captain York and captain Smith to view the breach and though they find it not assaultable yet the spaniard within be no long able to endure the fury of the shot hang out a sign of parley upon the first show of those man and offer to yield themselves and the castle upon promise of their life only which be accept they be bring present to the camp be in number sixtcene that be leave alive before the castle be yield the spaniard in the town make diverse shot at captain tolkerne pinnace with a piece of ordinance which they mount a day or two before close to the gate of the town but do no hurt at all unto the pinnace the same warp near to the other side under the hill and at last ride safe without danger of the shot the same day a platform be make upon a ground of advantage be a strong rath between the town and the camp that command one part of the town that under the favour thereof we may the better make our near approach though at that time we can hardly work by reason of the extreme frost and a demi cannon be mount upon it with which some shot be make at the town a sentinel take in the evening affirm that the first piece shoot off go through the house in which don jean lay and do otherwise great hurt the one and twenty the prisoner take in castle nyparke and some runaway be send to cork with direction to the mayor to send they and the former prisoner by the first ship into England keep of they still at cork only the sergeant Maior take in skirmish and the two commander of the castle of Rincorran and Nyparke this day the cannon and demi-cannon plant upon the platform do play into the town and this day the lord deputy go over into the hand to view how from thence the town may be best annoy and invest and the spaniard this day put out of the town great number of irish woman and child which come to the camp and be suffer to pass into the country to their friend the two and twenty day one james grace a Irishman obtain the lord deputy protection escape out of the town and give his lordship this intelligence follow six irish gentleman horseman come into the town of kinsale on Sunday the fifteen of November and one Owen Conde come the same day and they be all ready to go out again and father archer with they to put out the country if the bishop will suffer he don jean say private that the lord deputy be bear in a happy hour for he will have the town unless they be relieve from the north. they have nothing but ruske and water they have but four piece of artillery one small piece be at the churchyard one great and a small in james meaghe garden and the other big of all be at the watergate to play upon the ship and all four be mount the spaniard be five thousand by report at their set out from Spain they land at kinsal three thousand five hundred they be yet 3000 there be two hundred sick and hurt in the hospital they lose 100 at Rincorran and 17 and a boy at castle Nyparke they have nine slay when they offer to relieve the castle and five when captain Soto be slay they have four and thirty colour abroad when they shoot into the lord deputy camp and that be all they have and they have then two piece a great and a small and that day all the townsman be put out at the gate that they may do no hurt with the munition they fill the old abbey at the west gate with earth that they may mount a great piece there which they make account the ground where the english battery be plant at the north gate where the mount be raise yet it be not likely they will mount any ordinance there but rather keep it as a hold they have store of powder and munition which lie at John Fitz edmond castle but they mean to remove it present and put it in a seller within the town their treasure lie at the house where captain bostock lay they be much afraid the lord deputy will place some ordinance at castle nyparke or thereabouts which will much annoy they but most of all they fear the place of it at a place near the water side where some be send to seek rod not far from the place where the skirmish be when they sally for which call they raise their mount but especial fill up the old abbey from whence it be best command don jean lie at Philip roche a shot make from the english on Friday at night hit the house where don jean lay the townsman will stay no long there for fear of the shot and then the spaniard will be in great distress one go from don jean to Tyrone about nine day ago to hasten his come the man be blind of one eye the same day the lord deputy receive from her majesty direction that forasmuch as the county of Clare be of ancient time within the government or precinct of the province of monster until of late it be annex to the province of Connaght which her majesty understand be upon some untrue surmise make by commissioner for Connaght to the grievance and dislike of her subject of that county that the lord deputy and the counsel there shall speedy consider of this information and if they find it not evident a hindrance to her service than they speedy shall give order that by revocation of the former commissioner and letter patent for government of these several province and by grant new commissioner of like authority and effect and by all other way requisite in law the say county of Clare shall be reunite and annex unto the province of monster and be reduce under the order and government of the lord President and council of monster which her majesty be persuade will be for the advancement of her service and the good like of her love subject in those part the same two and twenty day four piece be plant by the cannon and demi cannon which altogether play into the town one of which shoot kill four man in the market place and strike off a captain leg call don John de saint John who after die of that hurt we likewise plant three culverin in the island beyond the water in which the foresay castle Nypark stand and from whence we hear that don John fear annoyance the three and twenty these do beat upon the old town with good effect and the same day our other six piece on the north-east side play upon the town and so continue till night in which time in all man judgement and by report of the prisoner we take they do great hurt to the town this day while the lord deputy the marshal and sergeant Maior be view the ground where the approach be intend a private soldier of sir John barkleys in their sight and in the face of the spanish guard attempt to steal a spanish sentenel as he have steal diverse before this sentenel be second by four that he see not he fight with they all five whereof one be the sergeant Maior who he have almost take and when he find he can do no good upon they all he come off without other hurt than the cut of his hand a little with the break of a thrust which one of they make at he and he hurt the sergeant Maior the night follow we begin certain near approach on the north-east side of the town on a hill which by the natural situation thereof be free from sudden sally by reason of a valley between it and the town so as it may be speedy second from the campe. there with much expedition be raise a fort and artillery plant to play into the town which with three or four company be easy gardable for in this business there seem nothing more to be respect then how to approach and infest the enemy in such sort as neither our old nor our new man may be overspend or over-harried with watch and work the time of the year and extremity of the weather consider nor the enemy may take any advantage to surprise our work by their sally without engage themselves in fight with our whole army for make those approach the lord deputy draw out one thousand foot continue the work all night and although the ground be extreme hard by reason of the frost and the night very light yet that night they bring the work to very good perfection the enemy play all the night upon they with great volley but hurt only three man either in the trench or in diverse sally they make in the one whereof a squadron of our new man do beat they back to the gate this day the lord precedent advertise that Odonnell by advantage of a frost so great as seldom have be see in Ireland have pass a mountain and so have steal by he into monster whereupon he purpose to return with the force he have to strengthen the campe. and in the evening sir Richard levison by the lord deputy direction draw the admiral and vice-admiral in between the island and kinsale whence the four and twenty day they shoot into the town the five and twenty day all the artillery still play upon the town but the shot from the ship do little hurt save only upon the base town the lord deputy give direction to spend few shot more except it be on the high town this night direction be give to make a platform for the artillery upon the trench which be make the three and twenty at night somewhat after midnight the spaniard make a sudden sally with purpose to force the trench but be soon beat back by sir Francis Barkeley who command the watch that night in that place the six and twenty the lord precedent with the two regiment of foot and with his horse he have lead out against Odonnell together with a Connaght regiment under the earl of Clanrickard and a regiment of the pale under sir Christopher saint Laurence which upon the way be command to join with the lord precedent come to the camp and these four regiment be that night quarter by themselves upon the westside of kinsale to invest the town more close and to keep Odonnell and the spaniard from join together which quarter or lesser camp be command by the earl of Thomond for the lord precedent remain ever near the lord deputy in the great camp and so do the earl of Clanrickard this day the three culverin be bring from the island beyond the water on the east-side and be plant on a hill in a point of land near the water on this side of the haven lie to the east of our fort new build there to which hill the town lie near and very open in the mean time the spaniard from the town play upon our ship with a demi-canon and shoot our admiral twice and our vide-admiral once while they ride as aforesaid close by the town but our ship within few shot exchange do dismount their demi-cannon so as they can make no more shoot with it and at the same shot hurt their chief gunner the seven and twenty day betimes in the morning our three piece plant the day before on the point of the hill near the water side play upon the town and do great hurt to the enemy by reason they be plant so near the town but the eight and twenty day fall out extreme windy and rainy we be enforce to cease our battery and spend the rest of the day in draw down to that place some other piece former plant upon the first platform the foresay eight and twenty in the morning we send a trumpet to summon kinsale who be not suffer to enter the town but receive his answer at the gate that they hold the town first for chhist and next for the king of Spain and so will defend it upon his return with this answer the lord deputy command to make battery with all our artillery plant all on the east side of the town which be present perform and continue till towards night break down great part of the east gate in the mean time the spaniard be retire in great number into their trench on the west side to escape the fury of our ordinance on the east side sir christopher s. Laurence be command to draw out from our new camp on the west side and to give upon they in their trench which he perform and do beat they out of the trench follow they to the very gate of the town kill many and hurt more of they and so return without loss of a man on our side have only some few hurt the nine and twenty all our artillery play upon the town and break down most part of the eastern gate and some part of a new work the enemy have make before the gate this day two spaniard write from kinsale to some of their friend prisoner in our camp who they style poor soldier when we know they to be man of account and withal send they such money as they want yet under the title of alm as if they have neither money of their own nor be of credit to be trust for any the last day of November sir Richard Wingfield the marshal take some fifty shot and go to the wall of the town to view the fit place for we to make a breach the spaniasd make a light skirmish with they and hurt some few the marshal when he have well view the wall draw the shot off and judge the wall close to the eastern gate on the right hand to be fit for the make of a breach he give present order that our artillery shall beat upon that place which be do without intermission and therewith we break down before night a great part of the wall which the enemy in the night attempt to make up again but be beat from it by our guard who play upon they with small shot most part of the night in the evening a Spaniard run away from kinsale to our camp who report to the lord deputy that our artillery have kill diverse captain and officer in the town beside many private soldier the first of December it be resolve in counsel of state and by the counsel of war namely the chief commander and colonel that some foot shall be draw out of the camp to give the Spaniard a bravado and to view if the breach we have make be assaultable and also to cause the spaniard to show themselves that our artillery may the better play upon they to this purpose two thousand foot command by sir John Barkeley the sergeant mayor and captain Edward blany be present put in arm and draw near the wall of the town who entertain a very hot skirmish with the spaniard who be lodge in a trench close to the breach without the town during this skirmish our artillery play upon those that show themselves either in the breach or in the trench and kill many of they beside such as be kill and hurt by our small shot among the rest on captain moryson a Spaniard of who as one of the pledge upon the composition we shall have cause to speak hereafter walk cross the breach animate his man and though Sr Richard Wingfield our marshal cause many both great and small shot to be make at he with promise of 20 pound to he that shall hit he or beat he off whereupon many great shot do beat the dirt in his face and story about his ear yet all the skirmish he continue walk in this brave manner without receive any hurt many think they best soldier who be often and dangerous hurt but it be a error for wound be badge of honour yet may befall the coward assoon as the valiant man and I have know most advenmrous man who never receive wound pardon this my digression not warrantable in a journal I will only add that brave soldier for the star have a kind of power in our birth be by some secret influence preserve when other intrude themselves into that course of life or drive to it by necessity of estate fall at the first alarm and to speak theological God preserve we but still in our way so as he who without call rush into another way then his own have no warrant of divine protection after a hour fight when we have take full view of the breach and find it not assaultable our man be draw off with little or no damage on our part only three of our man be hurt and captain guest horse be kill under he which captain first have kill two spaniard with his own hand the same day it be resolve in counsel to plant a fort on a rath on the west side of the town to lodge therein some foot for second to the guard of our artillery intend to be plant near the same and to this purpose in the night follow the marshal the sergeant mayor captain Edward blany and captain josias bodley trenchmaster the lord deputy be almost all night present with they draw out five and twenty of each company and entrench themselves on the say hill not half calliver shoot from the town begin to cast up a small fort. and though the spaniard perceive not their purpose yet many of they lie in a trench they possess close to the west gate do play very hot all night on our man guard the pioneer and we do no less on they so that diverse be hurt and kill on both side but the second day of December about nine in the morning when a great mist begin to break and they discover our work a yard high then from the say trench and more from the castle and high place in the town they ply we all the day with small shot notwithstanding which annoyance our man bring the work to very good perfection before night in the mean time a sergeant to captain blany draw out some seven or eight shot and sudden fall into a trench which some spaniard possess close by the town of who the sergeant kill two and each of the rest one with their own hand but when not content therewith they attempt another trench something distant from the first the sergeant in go on be shoot through the body and two of his company be hurt in bring he off and so return with this and no more loss this night the trench where the cannon be plant on the east side of the town be man with the lord deputy guard command by captain james Blount with sir Thomas bourkes company and sir Benjamin Berry company both command by their lieutenant by captain rotheram company command by himself by captain hobby company command by himself captain nuse command by his lieutenant and by captain Roger Haruy his company himself command in chief as captain of the watch there that night for as every colonel watch each three night so every captain watch in one place or other each second night also this night the fort on the west side near the town between the two camp which be cast up the day before be man by captain Flower command in chief and his company by captain Spencer and his company by captain dillon and his company and by the company of sir Arthur savage sir John dowdall captain masterson and sir William Warren command by their lieutenant together with certain squadron out of the earl of thomonds quarter in our second camp which stand in guard without the trench now within a hour after night and some two hour before the moon rise it be very dark and rainy the Spaniard impatient of the fort building the day before so close to the town west gate and resolve to attempt brave on our ordinance plant on the east side make a brave sally with some two thousand man and first give slight towards the trench on the west side but present with a gross and their chief strength fall upon the trench in which the artillery lie on the east side continue their resolution to force it with exceed fury have bring with they tool of diverse sort to pull down the gabbyon and the trench as also Spykes to cloy the ordinance the alarm be take in the camp the marshal and sergeant Maior sir Richard moryson sir William fortescue sir Francis rush and captain Roc sally present with some six hundred man towards the cannon and sir Benjamin Berry with some one hundred man fall direct towards the port of the town next to the camp and the lord deputy send out sir Oliver saint john with second upon the marshal arrival and charge the enemy break and our man do execution upon they sir Bentamin Berry fall direct upon the enemy second who he charge and break kill many of they and take the commander of that body be a ancient captain of great estimation with the enemy at the same time the enemy give upon our trench and fort build the day before on the west side and continue the attempt long with great fury till captain flower in heat and without direction sally out of the fort to follow part of their force discomfit the enemy enter the fort before he can return and possess themselves of our trench yet still our man continue the fight and sir William godolphin give many brave charge with his horse to countenance our man till the earl of Clanrickard be send to second they on this part with captain Skipwith captain Clare captain boise captain Thomas bourke and some threescore man for the rest of the regiment be not advance so far then his lordship and the rest charge the enemy gross be without the fort and break they and do execution upon they fall towards the town and so return thence enter the west fort again with little resistance for the enemy abandon it this fort his l p. and his company make good till he be relieve from the lord deputy in this sally in all the enemy leave in the field above one hundred and twenty dead body beside such as be kill near the town and can not next day be discern by we and we take thirteen prisoner among who be a captain Reformado as they term they for honour of antiquitic who be take by Henslo captain of our pioner and a sergeant and a drum after we hear by some of the town that they leave dead above two hundred of their best man among who be two captain two alferoe and the sergeant Maior be the second commander to don jean and don Carloes and that more than two hundred of they be hurt on our part captain Flower captain Skipwith and the earl of clanrickards lieutenant be hurt and captain Spencer and captain dillon and captain flower lieutenant be kill in the west fort who stay in the fort when captain fllower sally be there find dead in the place which they be command to make good and with their face to the enemy in as honourable manner as can be expect from any soldier the enemy sally on our fort guard our cannon cloy a demy-culuering of we which be a little craze be leave without the fort but the next morning it be make serviceable again some of they be kill upon the cannon and upon the powder and the trench about the cannon be in some place fill with dead body for in that particular attempt they leave seventy two body dead in the place and those of their best man whereof some be find have spike and hammer to cloy the cannon and in general among the dead body many be find to have spell character and hallow medal which they wear as preservation against death and most of they when they be strip be see to have scar of Venus warfare we take some forty shovel and as many mattock and much arm leave in the field which tool be so massy as they have great advantage of we therein and the sight of they will have put her majesty minister of the ordinance to shame who for private gain send sale ware to we unfit to be use in defend this fort of the cannon captain rotheram and james (@) ensign win great reputation by their valour and the courage they give to other because the earl of clanrickards company have watch the night before this sir henry Follyot be send with his regiment to guard the western fort till morning some hour before this skirmish the lord deputy be advertise by one Donnogh o driscoll that six spanish ship be put into castle haven and that six more be send with they from the groin but in the way be scatter from these by tempest and that since it be not know what become of they that in these six ship arrive be two thousand spaniard with great store of ordinance and munition and that by their report twenty thousand more be come present after they the three of December by reason of rany weather nothing can be do only upon relation of a french runaway that the enemy purpose to sally again some of our man be for a short time draw to arm but in vain the four day we receive a confirmation of the spaniard arrival at castlehaven whereupon it be resolve in counsel that our first camp shall be more strong fortify and that all our horse shall be draw into it and that the quarter or lesser camp on the west side consist now only of three regiment namely of the earl of Thomond sir Richard Percy and sir Christopher saint Laurence shall rise and sit down far off towards the south-gate have another regiment add to strengthen it draw out of the first camp on the north side of the town where the lord deputy lodge the lot whereof fall to sir Charles wilmott that our trench and fort on the east side for the guard of the cannon shall be commit to the continual guard of captain blany and our fort at the west gate to captain guest and that our battery shall cease till those storm of new spanish supply and the irish rebel draw near be over a drum be send to the town to offer don jean liberty to bury his dead which message he receive with due respect but pray we to bury they with promise to do the like for any of we happen to fall in his power and because our drum according to his direction expostulate with don jean that howsoever the spanish prisoner be well use by we yet his lordship hear that one of our man take in the last sally after he be hurt so long as he give himself out to be a irish man be keep in the hospital but after be discover to be a Englishman be draw out and kill for this cause don jean send back with he a spanish drum to the lord deputy entreat burial for his dead with the foresay promise to do the like for we and for the expostulation deny any such thing do to his knowledge with protestation to punish it high if he can discover any such thing to have be do the first his lordship promise to do as a christian like act though he know the inequality of the offer have so many of their body present in his power for the second his lordship rest satisfy yet he Lp do further expostulate with the drum that upon our summons of the town after martial manner they be not content to return a resolute answer but add scandalous word term we to which he answer protest that the speech be ill deliver by a harquebuzier who undertake to interpret it but can not do it right his lordship also except to a kind of challenge send by don jean that the question between England and Spain shall be try by combat between they two this trial be in neither of their power by commission nor in don jean will though he have the power beside that the council of Trent forbid the romanist to fight in Campo or combat in the field so as this message be rather quarrelsome then honourable which otherwise his lordship protest to be most willing to accept with thanks for the noble offer last his lordship remember that at our first set down he send a drum to don jean with this message that whereas his lordship understand certain lady and woman to be in the town he offer they before the play of our artillery free leave to depart or remain there still to command any provision for themselves which our camp afford and that don jean make a uncivil answer that he will not be his baud. to these exception he answer with a spanish shrug of the shouldier as have no knowledge nor commission to satisfy his lordship therein so his lordship protest that all the courtesy offer hitherto by he proceed out of that honourable respect which use to pass between honourable enemy and because he will ever be true to his own honour whatsoever other be to they but in case it be conceive to proceed of any respect of the greatness or power of the spanish nation or his own fear that he will hereafter show how much he disdain such ill interpretation of courtesy and so his lordship dismiss the Drum this night the spaniard attempt something by boat against our sentinel but be soon beat back again the five day sir Richard levison though the wind hinder the go out of kinsale harbour yet with tow get out the warspite the defiance the Swiftsure the Marline one merchant and a carvel and with they go to seek the spanish fleet new arrive at castlehaven the same day the four regiment above name do remove to the new camp place as be determine the day before the six day at ten in the morning our fleet arrive at castle haven and before four in the afternoon one spanish ship be sink the spanish admiral with nine foot water in hold drive to the shore upon the rock the vide-admiral with two other drive likewise a ground most of the spaniard quit their ship our fleet be force to stay there the next day by contrary wind and the spaniard have land some ordinance play upon our ship all the day but the night follow they warp out and the day after return to kinsale the six day likewise a scottish bark bring soldier from Spain and be one of the fleet new arrive at castlehaven but sever from they at sea by storm come into the harbour of kinsale and put the spaniard be fourscore into our hand who be bring to the camp and examine before the lord deputy David high of lie master and owner of the unicorn examine lay upon oath that he go from Waterford six week ago with good of Waterford for Rochel and so for Bordeaux but be drive through foul weather and a leak into the groin where within a hour after he be at anchor his ship be arrest and himself take by the governor call Conde but after they have unlade the bark and take away the sail he be set at liberty that Siriago with a part of the spanish fleet send for Ireland be then there and ready to embark again for Ireland have about one thousand four hundred land soldier place in nine ship whereof this examinate be one the admiral in which Syriago be be a Netherlander of one hundred fifty ton or thereabout the vide-admiral a Fleming of one hundred twenty ton or there about beside three french ship and three scot and a fliboat that they have great provision of powder pioner tool and twelve or 14 great piece mount for the field that the seven and twenty of the last they set sail at groin and have their direction as far as this examinate understand for kinsale that before their departure from the groin one jordan roche of kinsale bind for Bordeaux and from thence for South-Spaine come to a anchor at the groin be there take and force in the king name to be a pilot on this coast his ship be send on her voyage by who they understand that the castle of Ryncoran and Nyparke be take which he hear also by the report of a french man he meet at sea that aboard his ship there be embark about fourscore spaniard and five or six woman about five and twenty ton of bread and six butt of wine whereof the most part be spend in beverage but other munition than the soldier weapon they bring none aboard that Brittingdona be at Lisbon ready to transport two thousand soldier more for Ireland so soon as he can get ship that don Diego de Brochero in his return for Ireland land at Lisbon and from thence post to the court and after his arrival a present dispatch be make to hasten these supply for Ireland that in Spain they make no doubt but Ireland be already win and from thence the common bruit be they will for England then for Scotland and after will set upon the Turk that the five of December they make the land between cork and kinsale and the wind be scant turn all that day and night to come in and by seven of the clock this morning come into the harbour and at open of the day perceive our fleet which by the spaniard be conceive to be their fleet but by he as he say know to be her majesty and that of purpose he come to put the spaniard into our hand that before his ship come to anchor he get a boat and discover to sir amyas Preston the spaniard he have aboard whereupon sir amyas Preston man out his boat and tow in the ship whereunto the spaniard make no resistance last that he hear at the groin that the adilantado be then at port saint Marie do daily expect the come offoure thousand italian but for what purpose he know not the spaniard then examine on oath say that there be in the fleet with Siriago not above one thousand diverse of they take out of the jail and very poor and naked whereof one whole company of portugese be take out of prison that the admiral be lade with biscuit powder and match and two cannon for battery that the whole fleet consist of ten sail whereof the admiral and vide-admiral be hulk of three hundred ton as they esteem they the rest small bark of diverse nation that Siriago command the fleet and captain Alonzo Campo command the foot in chief be a captain of the terceres who have a old company and Sanedra have another old company but be himself a young soldier that they hear nothing that kinsale be besiege that Brittendona be at Lisbon and that they be gather supply but know not if they will be ready before the spring or no. that the adilantado be in South-Spaine and that a regiment of three thousand italian be to come for Ireland that the whole fleet be bind for kinsale and they think the queen fleet be their ship of Spain that all the ship be to be gather together at Lisbon against the spring and four thousand italian be come for England this six day of December all the ordinance be draw from the eastern and western platform into the first camp on the northside of the town where the lord deputy lodge that we may the better attend the service of the field have our artillery commodious place since we be advertise that Odonnel be join with those spaniard which land late at castle-haeven and that he together with Tyrone assist by all the rebel force in Ireland be draw up towards kinsale to relieve it and be come within few mile of the camp of all these news the spaniard in kinsale have knowledge and thereupon take heart again when they be otherwise ready to yield upon reasonable composition for this respect it be think enough for we to keep the ground we hold against all these enemy till we shall be further supply out of England since upon the least defeat or disaster befall we the whole kingdom will have be hazard if not lose by reason of the people inclination to a general revolt we fortify the foresay camp on the west or south-west side where the earl of Thomond lie with four regiment and it be resolve that two small fort shall be cast up and man between that camp and the water side southward the say fort and camp each one flanck the other thereby so to invest the town as all succour from the country may be cut off from it further it be resolve that the ditch of the lord deputy camp shall be deepn and the trench highthn and that the back part further from the town lie open hitherto shall now be close and make defensable against tyrones force as the side towards the town be make against the spaniard if they both at one time shall give upon we and that all the fort shall be barricado and by all possible art all the access to the town between our two camp be stop the seven day the lord deputy advertise master secretary in England of all these particular add that we daily hear very hot alarm of tyrones purpose to relieve the town who strengthen with the above name force be now lodge in wood and in accessable strength very near to our camp so as he hinder we from forage for our horse and from the help we former have out of the country for sustentation of our army and that his neighbourhood on the one side and the spaniard in kinsale on the other keep we at a bay from proceed in our aproche and battery beside that our last supply be in this short time incredible waste the new man die by dozen each night through the hardness of the winter siege whereunto they be not enure yet his lordship still make good his first hope of victory though it be defer and that which he think to have attempt with safety to the state and ease to the army be now to be do with hazard to the state inseparable from great action and great painfulness to the soldier to be endure with patience the eight day our artillery be place in the several place of our north side camp for the best defence thereof and a fort on the west side by the other camp according to the above mention resolution be cast up and almost finish where towards night we have a slight skirmish with the spaniard in which we have a ancient and some few man hurt in the evening the rebel horse be discover about tyrones force show themselves two mile off and after supper all our man be draw into arm upon notice give we by the scout that the rebel draw nigh but after a small time all save the watch be dismiss to rest this night sir Richard levyson return from castle haven with the queen ship into the harbour of kinsale and come to the camp to give the lord deputy account of the good service do there the nine ten and eleven day we spend in build the two sconce or fort as be former resolve in the six day counsel they be build on the west side of the town between the earl of thomonds quarter and the water southward and to invest the town round about we cast up trench between the fort and the earl of thomonds quarter be thirty score in length the fort and the camp slanck each other and we cast up trench from that side to the lord deputy camp to stop the passage of cow horse or any relief to the town the spaniard make two or three light sally to view our work on the west side as they do likewise the twelve day but they be beat back with ease and no loss on our part the thirteen day we draw three piece of artillery from the lord deputy camp and plant they on the west side near the other camp to play upon a abbey which flank that part where we intend to make a new breach the same day the spaniard take in the scot ship be send for England and sir Oliver s. john be dispatch for England and by he the lord deputy and the counsel write this follow letter to the lord in England it may please your lordship how we have proceed in the great business we have in hand here since ourla dispatch unto your lordship of the seven of the last month we have think fit to set down by way of journal enclose humble pray leave to refer your lordship thereunto to avoid needless repetition and if the service we have hitherto perform shall happy fall short of that which your lordship in this time have expect and ourselves we acknowledge hope we have make collection of the sundry difficulty and opposition that we have encounter since the first news of these spaniard discovery upon this coast to the end it may appear unto your lordship plain by the view thereof as we be confident it will that nothing have be want in our endeavour to bring this work to the desire conclusion but that a more slow proceed have be inavoidable occasion by the slow and untimely come to we of those mean and provision without which it be impossible to be effectual active and the arise of new accident and impediment in the mean time which make our work more difficult and therefore will not we hope be impute any fault of we since the arrival of the queen ship the force altillery and other provision out of England we have so annoy this town with battery in all part thereof as the breach be almost assaultable and the house in the town much beat down to the great weaken of the defendant in so much as we be not without hope to be offer it by composition or within a little more time to have enter it by force though that be hold a course of much hazard and loss in regard they within be very strong in body of man which we know to be most certain the Spaniard find how hardly he be lay to importune Tyrone and Odonnell with their force to come to relieve he they both be according come and encamp not far from the town and now one thousand more spaniard be arrive at castle haven with great store of munition and artillery and report that a great force be come after which do so bewitch this people as we make account all the country will now go out as most of they have do already as in our former letter we signify that we fear odonnel force be say to be four thousand and to be join with the spaniard that land at castle haven and tyrones as we hear general to be as many more and since his passage through the country hither Tyrrell with many
