other lemster rebel as it be say be join with he and come also hither by these mean we be induce to leave our battery for a time and to strengthen our camp that we may be able to endure all their fury as we hope we shall and keep the town still besiege and so invest as we be not out of hope in the end to carry it notwithstanding all that they can do yet since it be now most apparent that the king of Spain mean to make this place the seat of the war not only for the gain of this kingdom but from time to time to push for England if he shall get this for so some that we have take and examine do confess and that the whole strength of the irish be draw and draw hither to set up their rest to get that liberty as they call it that they have so long seek for we must earnest entreat your lordship to supply we and that speedy of all thing necessary for so great a war as this be like to be we hold it a matter of necessity that four thousand foot more be send we present without stay one for another to come together but as they can be levy and ship away and we desire good choice may be make both of the man and arm for in both the last be much defective those under captain be but ill body of man and the supply have very ill arm and weapon we conceive it will be fit for the service that I the deputy have liberty to put so many of they under captain as can at the first be use for supply for though our chief meaning be to fill up the band already here if so many be want at their come hither that her majesty may not unnecessary be charge with new band when the old be not full but much deficient yet a great part of our company be extreme sick through the exceed misery of this winter siege so as at this present there be but one three part of the last man that come over serviceable and able to do duty whereof happy a great part may recover it can therefore be determine until they be here what number will be necessary for supply and what company fit to be raise for that must grow out of a view here of such as continue still sick or be grow deficient by death or run away whereof of late there be very many notwithstanding the severe course we have take by execute some for a terror to the rest by make proclamation upon pain of death that none shall depart the camp without licence by give direction to the port town that they shall be stay and apprehend and last by send special man to cork Yoghall Waterford and wexford to see the same due put in execution for which purpose they have commission for martial law all which be well know to every private man in the camp and yet they steal away daily in such number as beside those that by device do get passage there be at this present take between this and Waterford at the least two hundred ready to be return though we confess the misery they endure be such as just deserve some compassion for diverse time some be find dead stand centinel or be upon their guard that when they go thither be very well and lusty so grievous be a winter siege in such a country for the sick and hurt man we have take the best course we can devise for at cork we have provide a guesthouse for they where they be most careful look unto and have their lend deliver in money to buy they what the market do afford with a increase of what be hold fit for they allow out of the surplusage of the entertainment for the preacher and cannoneer which we conceive your lordship have heretofore hear of and for those that be sick or sickly at the camp because we much desire to keep they well if it be possible we take this course first their own mean be allow they very due sir Robert Gardner be appoint a commissioner for that purpose that the soldier in all thing may have his right with proclamation that whosoever find himself in any want shall repair to he and second out of a general contribution from the officer and captain of the army there be fifty pound a week collect for they and bestow in provide warm broth meat and lodging so as a marvelous great number be thereby relieve and yet all this do not serve but that a great many be still unserviceable which we have here note at the great length that it may appear unto your lordship that it proceed not from want of care or providence in we but from keep the field in such a season where human wit can prevent their decay we must further earnest entreat your lordship that the fleet may remain upon this coast during the war with the spaniard and to furnish we with victual munition and money for easterly wind be rare at this time of the year and without every of these this action can be maintain but that the army will break and come to nothing neither will this country now afford we any thing no not so much as meat for our horse and therefore we must likewise be humble suitor that two thousand quarter of oat may speedy be send we without which undoubted our horse will be starve the particular of our want both of munition and victual be set down by the master of the ordinance and the Victualer for this province and we have make choice of sir Oliver s. john to solicit your lordship for a speedy and favourable dispatch as also to give satisfaction in all thing wherein it may please your lordship to require a more particular information in regard he be well acquaint with all thing that have pass here otherwise as we be unwilling to have spare he so we find he very unwilling to leave the service at this time have not I the deputy enjoin he to undertake this business know he can best satisfy your lordship in any particular that you may doubt off we have also hold it very fit humble to signify to your lordship that where we hear from the counsel of Dublyn and otherwise of her majesty purpose to send some scot that it will now very much advance the service for if four thousand scot which we think a convenient number may speedy be land there to join with the english at Loughfoyle and Carickfergus while Tyrone keep here with the force of that country they will no doubt in short time make so great a spoil there as he shall never be able to subsist to maintain a war any more and a great part both of the english force and of they after have sweep those country bare which they may quick do may draw hither to our assistance with great part of the prey which will abundant relieve both they and we with victual and so stop the passage behind he as he shall never be able to return but that the war both of Spain and Ireland may have a end together here whereof we can but wish your lordship to have due consideration and humble pray you to excuse we for make thus far bold which nothing shall have lead we unto but zeal and affection to the service last whereas the enemy fleet at Lysbone under the conduct of Bretandona be by intelligence from Spain assure intend for these part to bring supply to kinsale within a month or six week and whereas we find the great importance of this service depend on the countenance of her majesty fleet to have the same with we as well to guard the harbour and repel the enemy land as also to guard our magazine of munition and victual which must be keep in ship we have no other conveniency to keep they we have make humble bold to stay the fleet command by sir Richard levison and do in like sort beseech your lordship to victual they for three month long with all possible speed for they be now victual only till the twenty of january and because so great a quantity of victual as will serve they for that time can hardly be so soon provide we humble desire that this supply of their victual may be send unto they in part as it can be make ready and because this fleet by the opinion of the best experience in sea service who we for our part do believe must necessary be divide and yet be too small to serve in two part we humble pray that some such addition of ship as in your wisdom shall be think meet may be send hither to forbid the enemy to plant in other place as baltimore and Berre haven where it be very probable they mean to plant by which division of the fleet better service may be expect then otherwise can possible be perform for it be no doubt but many opportunity will be offer to fight with the enemy which otherwise can be look for this we humble submit to your lordship favourable consideration not forget as earnest as we may to recommend to your lordship good favour sir Richard levison admiral of this fleet who have show himself a most worthy gentleman both in perform of that service upon the spanish ship at castle-haven which in our I ournall be express as also in be himself painful careful wise and valiant in the whole course of all affair which your lordship commit to his charge and that in such measure as we think a more sufficient and gallant gentleman can not haud be choose for such a employment and so we most humble take leave etc. by the same dispatch the lord deputy write this follow letter to master secretary in England sir I know that all great action be accompany with many difficulty neither be they strange to i that have put on a mind to endure any thing for such a mistress in such a quarrel and with cheerfulness shall I suffer the extremity of hardness and adventure if it shall please her majesty so gracious to interpret our labour as to believe as it be true that our difficulty arise out of themselves and not from any defect of our counsel or endeavour for myself I protest that I do faithful propound unto myself whatsoever I presume be her majesty chief end to make a speedy safe and honourable conclusion of this war which to no private man will be more unsupportable then to myself be I not uphold by my duty and affection to her service I do conceive that it be apparent that the king of Spain be resolve to make a powerful war with England and that he have choose this country to be the seat thereof where we that be her majesty minister here must either mar or give way to this foundation if he be resolve to send continual supply and to fortify in several place the war be likely to grow long and difficult for first it be hard to force a warlike nation out of any strength without great number royal provision and long time and how hard our supply of either can be spare or send in time so far I fear the estate do already feel too much the necessity of make head to a enemy who have the heart of all this people shall have all their help if they dare do draw our army to endure all the incommodity of a miserable winter siege wherein without all prevention the great part of our strength will decay before we be ready in our chief design of force the town to use it and if otherwise we shall use in this work more than advise haste we may easy hazard the loss of this kingdom for little disaster to we will be conceive overthrow and believe sir that nothing contain even the best of this country inhabitant but the prosperity and reputation of our army so that although it may please God to enable we to cut off the thread of this war soon than we see reason to presume of yet because we have just reason to expect a grow enemy and in so great a cause it must please her majesty either to bend and maintain her royal power this way or by some attempt in his own country to divert his purpose for this for otherwise if he persist in his purpose for Ireland if he once grow of power to break the bank of our opposition he will sudden and not by degree overflow all hitherto it have please God to prosper we in all we have undertake or have be undertake against we we have win whatsoever the enemy be possess off without the town we have take above two hundred spanish prisoner there be as we be certain inform above one thousand dead and kill of they in the town the which we have now as thorough invest as may be but on the other side the whole force of Tyrone and Odonnell with all the strength of the rebel of Ireland do lie within six mile of we and to their assistance they have the spanish supply and that which be worst their munition and provision the whole province either be join with they or stand neutral and what use soever the enemy make of they I be sure we receive by they no manner of assistance notwithstanding all this I hope we shall give a good account of the besiege but we have reason to proceed with great caution have a desperate enemy before we and so many that be engage in the same fortune behind we for Tyrone and o donnel have quit their own country to recover they here or else to loose all now sir to enable we in this great war you must continual supply we with munition and victual it be true how incredible soever you think it that of two thousand man you send we you must account that we make use of little more than five hundred and yet we can well justify that there be nothing omit that human wit can provide for the preservation of such as we have I have much against his will and my own send over sir Oliver saint john because I presume he can satisfy you in many thing which by writing we can hardly do once more I think good to remember you that I protest before the eternal God that it grieve i to see her majesty so ill serve in her muster from the abuse whereof as I have do heretofore I do utter disclaim as not be in my power to reform for all the minister in that kind be but cypher or false number and it be beyond my power to descend into every particular care in such a active time wherein I spend all my meditation in make only of the war and wherein how much of the weight of every man burden do lie upon my shoulder I do better feel than I can express or make you believe I will discontinue the consideration of any thing that belong to my own private and have now only desire this bearer to negotiate for i as a public person and of public matter and I beseech God to send i the height of my ambition which be with the conscience of have do her majesty the service I desire to enjoy a quiet private life and that her majesty may never more have need of man of our profession you sir most assure to do you service Mountioy the fourteen day be so rainy and so tempestuous in wind as we can not stir out to proceed any thing in our business the fifteen our artillery plant by the camp on the westside do play upon the top of the castle in the town where the enemy shot be place that from thence they may annoy our man work in the trench and in the platform and attend our artillery our piece break down many of these castle and kill many of their shot lodge in they likewise in the night while our man be make new approach our ordinance play upon the town and many volley of small shot be exchange between we and the enemy the sixteen day the same ordinance play in like sort upon the castle in the town and do much hurt to the man there lodge the seventeen day be very tempestuous with rain and especial wind and so continue all night for which cause our artillery play but seldom upon the town and this night the spaniard sally and break down a platform which we have begin the day before with purpose to plant our artillery there whereupon a slight skirmish fall between we and they but with little or no hurt on either side the eighteen day our artillery continue to play upon the town and this day his lordship intercept this follow letter which he command i to translate out of spanish into English to the prince oneale and lord o donnel I think your excellency will have come at don Ricardo his go since he have order from you to say that upon the spaniard come to you from castle-haven you will do i that favour and so I beseech you now you will do it and come as speedy and well appoint as may be for I assure you that the enemy be tire and be very few and they can guard the three part of their trench which shall not avail they for resist their first fury all be end the manner of your come your excellency know better to take there than I to give it here for I will give they well to do this way be always watch to give the blow all that I can and with some resolution that your excellency fight as they do always I hope in God the victory shall be we without doubt because the cause be he and I more desire the victory for the interest of your excellency than my own and so there be nothing to be do but to bring your squadron come well appoint and close withal and be mingle with the enemy their fort will do as much harm to they as to we I commend myself to don Ricardo the lord keep your excellency from kinsale the eight and twenty the new style be the eighteen after the old style of December 1601 though you be not well fit I beseech your excellency to dislodge and come towards the enemy for expedition import it be needful that we all be on horseback (@) and the great haste the better sign by don I can Aguyla the nineteen day be so extreme rainy as we can do little or nothing the twenty in the morning be very fair our ordinance play and break down good part of the wall of the town and to the end we may sharpen Tyrone who lie so near do more annoy we by keep relief from we than he be like to hurt we by any attempt or if tyrones force come not up soon that we may proceed more round this day another great breach be make beneath the platform and though many shot be make to hinder we yet by the next morning the work be bring to good perfection the night be stormy with great lightning and terrible thunder to the wonder of all consider the season of the year and this night come certain intelligence that Tyrone draw on by don jean importunity determine present to set up his rest for the relief of the town and that the next night he will lodge within a mile and half of our campe. the one and twenty ourscout confirm the same and towards night Tyrone Tyrone show himself horse and foot show himself with all his horse and foot upon a hill within a mile of we in the way to cork whereupon two regiment of our foot and most of our horse be draw out of the camp make towards they but when they see our man resolute come forward they fall back to a fastness of wood and water where they encamp this night be light with continual flash of lightning the spaniard sally again and give upon a trench new make beneath our canon but be the soon repel because we keep very strong guard and every man be ready to be in arm by reason of tyrones be so near unto we the two and twenty tyrones horse and foot often show themselves from a hill beyond which they encamp in a wood yet our artillery still play upon the town break down the wall and some turret from whence the spaniard shoot annoy our man many intelligence confirm that Tyrone on the one side and the spaniard on the other have a purpose to force our campe. this night the spaniard sally and give upon a trench close to the westside of the town which the sergeant that keep it do quit but sir Christopher saint Laurence appoint to second he come up with some foot and do heat the spaniard into the town before they can do any great hurt save only a little deface it our artillery still play upon the town that they may see we go on with our business as if we care not for tyrones come but it be withal carry on in such a fashion as we have no meaning to make a breach because we think it not fit to offer to enter and so put all to hazard until we may better discover what Tyrone mean to do who strength be assure to be very great and we find by letter of don jean which we have intercept that he have advise Tyrone to set upon our camp tell he that it can not be choose but our man be much decay by the winter siege and so that we shall hardly be able to maintain so much ground as we have take when our strength be great if we be well put to on the one side by they and on the other side by he which he will not fail for his part to do resolute and it be most true that our man daily die by dozen so as the sick and runnaway consider we be grow as weak as at our first set down before our supply of four thousand foot the strength of our regiment the three and twenty of December the lord deputy regiment have able man 715. the lord president regiment able man 556. the earl of clanrickards regiment able man 529. the earl of thomonds regiment 572. the lord Audley his aegiment 370. sir Richard percy regiment 544. sir Richard Morysons regiment 541. sir Oliver saint john his regiment 515. sir Charles Wilmotts regiment 454. sir Henry follyots regiment 595. sir Christopher saint laurence regiment of irish 747. sir Henry power squadron or fly regiment draw out of the former regiment after the make of the list in November last 449. the Totall of foot able man beside runaway and hurt and sick lie aswell in the camp as at cork 6595. this evening one of the chief commander in tyrones army have some obligation to the lord precedent send a messenger to he for a bottle of Vsquebagh and Tyrone redue to assail our campe. by a letter wish he that the english army shall that night be well upon their guard for Tyrone mean to give upon one camp and the spaniard upon the other mean to spare no man life but the lord deputy and he don jean aguila after confess to the lord precedent that notwithstanding our sentinel he and Tyrone the night follow have three messenger the one from the other all the night be clear with lightning as in the former night be great lightning with thunder to the astonishment of many in respect of the season of the year and I have hear by many horseman of good credit and namely by captain pikeman cornet to the lord deputy troop a gentleman of good estimation in the army that this night our horseman set to watch to their seem do see lamp burn at the point of their staff or spear in the midst of these lightning flash tyrones guide miss the way so as he come not upto our camp by night as the spaniard ready in arm hourly expect but early about the break of the next day the four and twenty of December some half hour before day the lord deputy in his house sit at counsel with the lord President and master Martial as think the intend enterprise of the enemy by some accident to be break sudden one of the lord precedent horseman call he at the door and tell he that tyrones army be come up very near to our campe. and sir Richard greame have the scout that night when he discover that Tyrone with his force be on foot march towards the camp present advertise the lord deputy thereof and his lordship be always in readiness to entertain they seldom go to bed by night and at this time as I say set in counsel when he hear that they be advance within three quarter of a mile to our camp cause all our man to draw into arm in the quarter and himself with the marshal attend he advance towards our scout whence he send the martial sir Richard Wingfeild to take view of the enemy and he bring he word that they be in the same place former advertise upon his return the lord deputy leave for defence of the great camp on the northside his lordship own regiment under his lieutenant sir Benjamin Berry the lord president regiment who be in his lordship absence to command both camp in chief the earl of clanrickards regiment the lord Audlie his regiment and sir Richard moryson his regiment this do the lord Depuputie send a corporal of the field unto our lesser camp or quarter command by the earl of Thomond and give order there for dispose the four regiment of the earl of Thomond sir Richard Percy sir Charles Wilmot and sir Christopher saint Laurence and direct how to set all the company in their several guard in former note I find sir Richard percy regiment quarter in the earl of thomonds camp but here I find it reckon among they of the great camp and the earl of clanrickards regiment in this lesser camp whereof I remember not the certainty but be sure how soever they have be change that the regiment for number be at this time thus dispose in the two camp by this time the marshal with some the defeat of tyrones force four hundred horse and sir Henry power regiment be to answer alarm and so draw out at this time as they have be for three night before on like occasion be advance within twenty score of the enemy the ground rise so high between they and our man as they can not see one the other it be now the break of day whereas midnight be the time appoint for the rebel to meet with don isan force the Spaniard be to set upon our lesser camp or the earl of thomonds quarter and Tyrrell lead the rebel vanguard in which be the spaniard late land at castle-haven and Tyrone lead their battle and o donnel their rear be all to set upon our chief camp conceive themselves of sufficient strength to force both our camp at one instant and to make no great work of it the lord deputy with the lord precedent in his company be come up to our force lead out against Tyrone and resolve there to give he battle command sir John Barkeley sergeant mayor to draw out of the camp the two regiment of sir Oliver saint john and sir Henry Follyot upon their come up the enemy find we resolve to fight retire himself over a Foard and the marshal see they disorder in their retreat send word thereof by sir Froncis rush to the lord deputy desire leave to fight and his lordship by sir Samuel Bagnol give he leave to order that service according as he in his discretion shall find the disposition of the enemy and therewith send back sir George Carew lord precedent with three troop of horse to the great camp to command both camp in chief and to make head against the spaniard if they shall sally out of the town but the spaniard still expect the come up of the rebel according to their mutual project and never imagine that we with our small force can draw out sufficient band to meet and beat the rebel contain themselves within the town wall till as by the sequel shall appear their saily can little profit they after the say message send to the marshal present the earl of Clanrickard come up and exceed importune the marshal to fight whereupon the marshal draw a squadron of foot with their drum to the Foard and will sir Richard greame with his horse to march direct to the Foard then the enemy retire hasty with horse and foot over a boggy ground to firm land hope to keep that boggy passage against we then the marshal direct sir Henry Davers command the horse under he with his horse and sir Henry power with his regiment of foot to advance who present come over the foresay Foard unto he the lord deputy be upon the hill with two regiment of foot command the sergeant Maior there attend he to second our man with those foot so the marshal have the earl of Clanrickard and sir Henry Davers with he advance with some hundred horse and begin with a hundred harqubufier lead by lieutenant Cowel a valiant gentleman mark by a red cap he wear to be a special instrument in this fight to give occasion of skirmish on the bog side which the rebel with some loose shot entertain their three batallion stand firm on the one side of the bog and our fort on the otherside in this skirmish our foot be put up hard to our horse which the marshal perceive put forth more shoot which make the rebel retire towards their battle then the moshiall find a way through a Foard to the ground where the rebel stand he possess the same with some foot and present he pass over with the earl of Clanrickard sir Richard greame captain Tasse and captain Fleming and their horse and offer to charge one of the rebel battle of one thousand eight hundred man but find they stand firm our horse wheel about now sir Henry Davers with the rest of the horse sir William godolphin with the lord deputy and captain minshall with the lord precedent troop keep by the lord deputy to answer all accident and our sergeant mayor with two regiment send by the lord deputy to second the regiment of sir Henry power be with the marshal come all up whereupon the marshal with the horse charge home upon the rear of the battle and the irish not use to fight in plain ground and something amaze with the blow up of a gunpowder bag they have upon the like fright defeat the english of old at blackwater but most discourage to see their horse fly be all chief of sept and gentleman to the number of five or six hundred be sudden rout and our man follow the execution the other two battle that stand still now find this rout make haste to succour they whereupon the lord deputy send instant captain Francis roe with sir Oliver saint john regiment of which he be lieutenant colonel to charge on the flank of the vanguard which present retire disorderly be follow by our foot and horse but the spaniard land at castle-haven march there and be not so good of foot as the irish draw out by themselves yet be by sir William godolphin lead the lord deputy troop soon break and most of they kill the rest with their chief commander don Alonzo Campo be take prisoner namely two captain seven alfieroe and forty soldier whereof some be of good quality in the mean time many of the light foot irish of the van escape as do likewise almost all the rear by advantage of this execution do upon the spaniard and the main battle of which body far great than either of the other all be kill but only some sixty or there about thus the irish horse first leave the foot than two of the battalion be rout they all fall to fly for life our man do execution upon many in the place on our part sir Richard greame cornet be kill sir Henry Davers sir William godolphin captain Henry Crofts scout-master be slight hurt only six soldier hurt but many of our horse kill and more hurt the irish rebel leave one thousand two hundred body dead in the field beside those that be kill in two mile chase we take nine of their ensign all their drum and powder and get more than two thousand arm and have not our man be greedy of the spaniard spoil be very rich have not our foot be tire with continual watch long before in this hard winter siege have not our horse especial be spend by ill keep and want of all meat for many day before by reason of tyrones nearness so as the day before this battle it have be resolve in counsel to send the horse from the camp for want of mean to feed they and if Tyrone have lie still and not suffer himself to be draw to the plain ground by the spaniard importunity all our horse must needs have be send away or starve have not these impediment be we have then cut the throat of all the rebel there assemble for they never make head against they that follow the execution nor scarce ever look behind they but every man shift for himself cast of his arm and run for life in so much as Tyrone after confess himself to be overthrow by a six part of his number which he ascribe as we must and do to God great work beyond man capacity and withal acknowledge that he lose above one thousand in the field beside some eight hundred hurt this we understand by the faithful report of one who come from he some few day after and tell the l. deputy moreover that he torment himself exceed for this his overthrow after the battle the lord deputy in the midst of the dead body cause thanks to be give to God for this victory and there present knight the earl of Claurickard in the field who have many fair escape his garment be often pierce with shot and other weapon and with his own hand kill above twenty irish kern and cry out to spare no rebel the captive spanish commander Alonzo Campo avow that the rebel be six thousand foot and 500 horse whereas the lord deputy have but some one thousand two hundred foot and less than four hundred horse so before noon his lordship return to the camp where command vollia of shot for joy of the victory the spaniard perhaps mistake the cause and dream of the rebel approach present sally out but be soon beat into the town especial when they see our triumph and perceive our horseman from the hill on the west side to wave the colour we have take in the battle and among the rest especial the spanish colour for such most of they be the rebel in wood not use that martial bravery the same day a old write book be show to the lord deputy wherein be a prophecy name the soar and hill where this battle be give and foretell a great overthrow to befall the irish in that place a note give by one of tyrones follower of his loss at this overthrow Tirlogh Ohagan son to art Ohagan commander of five hundred slay himself with all his company except twenty whereof eleven be hurt and of they seven die the eighteen day after their return Kedagh Mac donnel captain of three hundred slay with all his man except threescore whereof there be hurt five and twenty donnel groom mac donnel captain of a hundred slay himself and his whole company rory donnel captain of a hundred slay himself and his company five of the Clancans captain of five hundred themselves slay and their company except threescore and eighteen whereof eighteen be hurt (@) (@) son have follower in number three hundred under the lead of captain Mulmore oheagarty all slay with the say Mulmore save one and thirty whereof twenty be hurt Colle Duff donnel captain of one hundred lose with all his company three of the neales captain of three hundred send by cormack mac Barron all lose save eighteen whereof there be nine hurt captain slay fourteen soldier slay 1995. soldier hurt 76. the five and twenty day be our Christmas day the spaniard in the afternoon make a flight sally but find we ready to entertain they present they (@) back yet to hinder our make a trench which woe then begin and which they find will do they much hurt they sally again strong at nine of the clock in the night and maintain the fight till eleven wherein the ensign to captain roper and captain guest with diverse other on our part be hurt hard by their wall but in short space after they be beat into the town with many of they hurt and so we perfect that work the six and twenty in the night the spaniard make another sally at the west gate as former upon a new trench we keep close to the town and that so hot as they enforce our man to quit it have the lieutenant of the guard and ten more of they shot but when the spaniard make upto our low four they be present with a volley of shot in their tooth which kill fearre and hurt eight of they and so they draw into the town the seven and twenty the lord deputy dispatch sir Henry Davers into England with the follow letter touch the happy overthrow of Tyrone from his lordship and the counsel here to the lord in England it may please your lordship in the last dispatch send by sir Oliver s. john which this time we hope be safe deliver unto your hand there be at large reveal unto you all our proceed at the siege and also the estate we be then in have before we in the town the spanish force and at our back Tyrone and Odonnell with the (@) army since who departure they dislodge from the place where they than in camp and lay in camp within less than two mile of we in the way towards cork whereby the passage from our camp to cork be block up so as no provision for our relief from thence can come unto we which unto the army be agreat annoyance and we in a manner be no better than besiege the enemy proud in their strength resolve to set up their rest account we in their opinion lose man unable to resist so great a power and therefore by a general consent they determine on all part to give upon our camp as well out of the town by the spaniard as by the irish on the other side and according to that resolution on Christmas even before day Tyrone with his army rise and with all his force of horse and foot by break of day present himself in order of battle but it please God in his goodness to give we a gracious victory with a handful of man in respect of his army the particular whereof be too long to insert in a letter we humble refer your lordship to the relation send with these wherein the same be at large and particular relate in which overthrow we gain many spanish letter of great consequence the most effectual of which we send unto your lordship herewith together with a brief abstract of those which we reserve here as unwilling to trouble your lordship with those of less by view of which intelligence and advice and the relation of such spanish prisoner of account and understanding as we have have conference with all the second and further preparation of the foreign enemy be more apparent discover then at the dispatch away of sir Oliver s. john and therefore we may not cease humble to importwe your lordship in regard of these threaten supply by sea and land and that the great and speedy use we have of the man munition victual and treasure which we former move your lordship for in the letter send by the say sir Oliver s. john will admit no long delay that your lordship will not only be please to supply we present therewith but that those proportion may be enlarge in every point as in your wisdom upon due regard have to the intelligence send herewith shall be think meet for in our understanding we be now by the occasion aforesaid enable to know more of the enemy dsigne than we do before all of every kind that be by we former write for will be too little and further in as much as the chief strength of our army consist in our horse troop who in this service be the principal mean and instrument of the overthrow give the enemy as well in give the first charge as in do and follow the execution for the preservation of they in strength we humble pray your lordship that the two thousand quarter of oat former write for may present be dispatch hither and in regard the whole country be so harry and waste that it can yield we any relief and that without corn our horse as they begin must daily weaken more and more and so our army be in hazard to perish for timely prevention thereof we humble pray your lordship that a addition of oat may be give to the say proportion and although not at once yet from time to time send hither as conveniency of ship will give leave for we dare assure your lordship if for want of they our horse have not be grow so feeble there have few of the enemy horse or foot escape and that your lordship will be further please to send away with the best expedition the munition and habiliment of war already write for and to send the same with all possible haste according to the demand send herewith and subscribe by the master of the ordinance without which all other charge and provision be to no purpose we also pray your lordship that you will be please to have consideration to send hither a compitent fleet to guard the coast for we be of opinion and so gather by the collection we have probable make that the king of Spain his fleet will be great which be grant the ship we have here will not be enough to supply all occasion consider how they must be disperse both to defend the coast and to prohibit the send and arrival of their second as also to answer all other service wherein ship be necessary to be employ and for as much as the end and deversion of this war rest chief in the weaken and disenable of Tyrone who reputation by this last overthrow be both with the foreign enemy and his own follower very much blemish and for that it may be apparent conceive that the Spaniard will no long maintain a tedious and chargeable war in this remote kingdom than he have a strong and powerful party and safe mean of land therein to disinable he from this assurance and hope here in our understanding the send of four thousand scot into Ulster will do excellent service and they be once join with her majesty force at Loughfoyle and Carickfergus will either absolute banish Tyrone from thence by possess all the hold and place of strength in Ulster or else constrain he to make his defence at home and keep he from give any help to the spaniard and so be utter reject from have either credit or aid hereafter from they the same time the lord deputy write to master secretary this follow letter sir we have write to the lord both of our estate and desire and do wish that it may please they to conceive that the material be great that must stop the breach that the king of Spain have already make in this country and a slender opposition will not resist but be carry away with a inundation if he send supply we have already miraculous overcome one dangerous brunt and God have give the queen the great victory that ever she obtain in this country but believe i sir that there be no one place that be defend with good man but will go near to break the army that do force it though it be carry we have force two place already hold by the Spaniard and now he remain possess and fortify in four several place more with great store of munition artillery and victual there be supply of horse and foot certain come unto they some say in great number we have endure I dare bold say the most miserable siege for extremity of weather and labour that in this age have be hear of if it please God to enable we to effect this it be impossible for this army to undertake in this season and those place as it be now or will be diminish any present service without rest believe i sir you must make peace or provide for a chargeable war for there be nothing that carry these place without royal provision if her majesty think she own occasion and not we her poor minister to be the cause of this huge expense I shall willing endure the purgatory I line in and sacrifice my time my life and my live to do she the best service I can but if you find that she conceive the worse of i because I be the cook to dress her diet here so chargable I beseech you sir if I may ever deserve your love use your uttermost power to rid i speedy of my office and I dare presume that I have make no evil way for my successor to tread after i I will fain write much unto you but with wet and heat in the last overthrow I have take some cold and my head do make i write in great pain I beseech you sir pardon i and esteem i your honest poor friend that be resolve to be so ever I be glad to send sir Henry Davers over with this good news who I assure you have take exceed pain and lose some of his blood in this last service and beside some necessity of his own have long desire such a opportunity to come over for a time and so sir I beseech God to send we peace for I be weary of the war from before kinsale the seven and twenty of December 1601 you sir most assure to do you service Mountioy the 28 day of December the lord deputy be advertise that Syrriago a principal commander of the spaniard land in the west part have receive news of tyrones overthrow be sudden go for Spain without acquaint any of the spaniard therewith and that he carry with he in the same ship Hugh Odonnell and thus be the old prophecy full accomplish which often before we have hear namely that monster shall be the destruction of the three great northern Hughes for Hugh Mac guyer lord of fermanagh and the first Robin-hood of this great rebellion be long since kill near the city of cork and Hugh Tyrone and Hugh o donnel be overthrow at kinsale whereof Hugh o donnel be now flee for Spain whence he never return and Hugh Tyrone draw faint his last breath in the north without hope of better live then as a wood-kerne here or as a fugitive abroad likewise Hugh mostian a famous rebel at this time flee with o donnel into Spain the nine and twenty day his lordship have advertisement from diverse place that Tyrone in his flight out of monster pass the blackwater lose many of his carriage and have some hundred and forty of his man drown fear make they so hasty as they can not attend the pass of their own fellow much less the fall of the water the last of December don jean general of the spaniard offer a parley send his drum mayor out of the town with a Alfiero bring a seal letter from the Spaniard parley he to the lord deputy by which he require as by the same send in the next dispatch by sir Richard moryson to the lord in England appear that some gentleman of special trust and sufficiency may be send from the lord deputy into the town for who pledge a spanish gentleman of like quality shall be send by don jean into the camp and upon conference he will acquaint the say gentleman with the condition upon which he stand this grant the lord deputy choose sir William godolphin to be employ in this important negotiation and send he into the town to confer with don John as he likewise send don Pedro Henrico to remain in the campe. his first conference with sir William godolphin tend to this that have find the lord deputy who he term viceroy though a sharp and powerful yet a honourable enemy and the irish not only weak and barbarous but as he fear perfidious friend he be so far in his affection reconcile to the one and distaste with the other as he be thereby induce to make a overture of such a composition as may be safe and profitable for the state of England with least prejudice to the crown of Spain by deliver into the viceroy power the town of kinsale with all other place hold by the spaniard in Ireland so as they may depart upon honourable rearme fit man of war not force by necessity to receive condition but willing induce for just respect to relinquish a people by who their king and master have be notorious abuse if not betray that if the viceroy like to entertain this parley he will please to understand he right and make such proposition as be suitable to man resolve rather to bury themselves alive then to give way to any accord that shall taste of dishonour be confident of their present strength and the royal second of Spain do not the former respect lead they to disengage their king of this enterprise sir William godolphin direct only to receive his demand return to the camp and relate they to the lord deputy and counsel the answer send back by he be this that howsoever the lord deputy have late defeat the irish and well understand their weakness and the unresistable difficulty that press they how ere they labour to cover the same yet know that her sacred majesty his mistress will in her merciful disposition repute her victory blemish by voluntary effusion of christian blood he be content to entertain this offer of agreement so it be upon honourable term fit the advantage her majesty have against they in the next conference the lord deputy require for the first article that don jean shall leave his treasure munition and artillery and the queen natural subject to be dispose at her majesty pleasure but don jean vow rather to endure the last of misery then to be guilty of so foul a treason against his king and the reputation of his procession though he be unable to subsist much more now when he have not only mean to sustain the war but hope by patience and constancy to attain the best end of his business add that he take it so ill to be misunderstand in have a article of this nature propound to he as if it be once more mention the viceroy shall from thenceforth use the advantage of his sword and not the benefit of his former proffer he further say that the viceroy have cause rather to judge two hundred thousand ducat well disburse by the queen to have the spaniard quit their possession of baltimore alone to say nothing of kinsale castle haven and beer haven which with all they perish yet baltimore may easy be keep for the arrival of the spanish fleet and all second that his master so deep engage shall please to send which may draw on a more powerful invasion this first be undertake upon false ground at the instance of a base and barbarous people who have discover their own weakness have arm his king and master to rely on his own strength be tie in honour to relieve his people thus engage this say he I speak in case the viceroy be able to force kinsale as I assure myself he can I have upon my honour two thousand able fight man old soldier beside the sick daily recover now better enure to the climate and endure of all hardness beside our convenient mean of food such as we spaniard can well live upon and our store of munition most import with confident assurance short to have new supply of all thing add that he preserve his strength to be able to front we in a breach which their heart not fail they have hand and breast to stop against treble our force though he will give the viceroy that right that his man be pass good yet spend and tire with a winter siege obstinate continue beyond his expectation but with such caution and so good guard as he have watch all advantage can never make a sally without loss to his part wherein he acknowledge himself much deceive that ground upon some error in our approach he have promise himself the defeat of one thousand man at least and at one blow but say he when we meet in the breach I be confident upon good reason to lay five hundred of your best man on the earth which loss will make a great hole in your army that have already suffer such extremity last he conclude that the king his master send he to assist the two count o Neale and o Donnel and he presume on their promise to join their force with he within few day have first long expect they in vain and sustain the viceroy army and at last have see they draw to the great head they can make lodge near kinsale reinforce with company of spaniard every hour promise he relief and at last break with a handful of man and blow asunder into diverse part of the world o donnel into Spain o Neale into the further north so as now find no such count to use his very word with who he be command to join he have move this accord the rather to disengage the king his master from assist a people so weak as he must bear all the burden of the war and so perfidious as perhaps in requital of his favour they may at last be win to betray he relation of this conference be make to the lord deputy and counsel they consider that the treasure that don jean bring be at first but one hundred thousand ducat whereof the great part can not but be spend in pay his soldier 4 month and other occasion of expense for which and other good reason they conclude not to stand upon the first article especial since many strong reason make the agreement as it be honourable so to seem very profitable to the state of England namely that our army be waste and tire with the winter siege that it be dangerous to attempt a breach defend with so many able man that if we shall lodge in the breach yet they have many strong castle in the town so much time may be spend ere we can carry it as our fleet for want of victual may be force to leave we that at this time our army be only provide for six day that we have not munition or artillery to make any more than one battery in one place at once five of our piece be craze that upon any disaster befall we the irish be like to revolt that beside the take of kinsale the other place hold by the spaniard as Baltymore custle haven and beare-haven will have make a long and dangerous war with infinite charge to the state of England they be strong fortify and well store with all provision of war and our army be so tire as it can not attempt they without be first refresh and then be supply with all necessary to the unsupportable charge of our sit must have be carry by sea to those place unaccessable by land last that in this time the king of Spain can not but send they powerful second be thus far engage in his honour beside that by this long war we shall be hinder from prosecution of the rebel who be now so break as in short time they must needs be bring to absolute subjection after many go to and fro certain article be agree upon the second of january towards the end of the year 1601 according to the english who end and begin the year at our lady day in Lent but the article bear date the twelve of januarie 1602 after the new style and according to the spanish manner to begin the year the first day of the same month the lord deputy give i the say article in english to be fair write that the copy thereof be sign by both the general may be send into England and likewise his lordship command i to translate the same article into the latin and italian tongue that two copy of each be sign by the general one of each may remain with the lord deputy and the other be send the condition of the spaniard yield kinsale and other place to the king of Spain these article follow word by word in english as they be sign by the lord deputy and the spanish general Mountioy in the town of kinsale in the kingdom of Ireland the twelve of the month of january 1602 between the noble lord the lord Mountioy lord deputy and general in the kingdom of Ireland for her majesty the queen of England and don jean captain and campe-master general and governor of the army of his majesty the king of Spain the say lord deputy be encamp and besiege the say town and the say don jean within it for just respect and to avoid shed of blood these condition follow be make between the say lord general and their camp with the article which follow 1 first that the say don jean shall quit the place which he hold in this kingdom as well of the town of kinsale as those which be hold by the soldier under his command in caste-haven baltimore and the castle at beere-haven and other part to the say lord deputy or to who he shall appoint give he safe transportation and sufficient for the say people of ship and victual with the which the say don jean with they may go for Spain if he can at one time if not in two 2 item that the soldier at this present be under the command of don jean in this kingdom shall not bear arm against her majesty the queen of England wheresoever supply shall come from Spain till the say soldier be unship in some of the port of Spain be dispatch assoon as may be by the lord deputy as he promise upon his faith and honour 3 for the accomplishment whereof the lord deputy offer to give free passport to the say don jean and his army as well spaniard as other nation whatsoever that be under his command and that he may depart with all the thing he have arm munition money ensign display artillery and other whatsoever provision of war and any kind of stuff as well that which be in castle-haven as kinsale and other part 4 item that they shall have ship and victual sufficient for their money according and at the price which here they use to give that all the people and the say thing may be ship if it be possible at one time if not at two and that to be within the time above name 5 item that if by contrary wind or by any other occasion there shall arrive at any port of these kingdom of Ireland or England any ship of these in which these man go they be entreat as friend and may ride safe in the harbour and be victual for their money and have moreover thing which they shall need to furnish they to their voyage 6 item during the time that they shall stay for ship victual shall be give to don jean people at just and reasonable rate 7 item that of both part shall be cessation of arm and security that no wrong be offer to any one 8 item that the ship in which they shall go for Spain may pass safe by any other ship whatsoever of her majesty the queen of England and so shall the ship of the say queen and her subject by those that shall go from hence and the say ship be arrive in Spain shall return so soon as they have unship their man without any impediment give they by his majesty the king of Spain or any other person in his name but rather they shall show they favour and help they if they need anything and for security of this that they shall give into the lord deputy hand three captain such as he shall choose 9 for the security of the performance of these article don jean offer that he will confirm and swear to accomplish this agreement and likewise some of the chief captain of his charge shall swear and confirm the same in a several writing 10. item that don jean in person shall abide in this kingdom where the lord deputy shall appoint till the last ship upon his lordship word and if it happen that his people be ship all at once the say don jean shall go in the same fleet without any impediment give he but rather the lord deputy shall give he a good ship in which he may go and if his say man be send in two shipping than he shall go in the last 11. and in like sort the say lord deputy shall swear and confirm and give his word on the behalf of her majesty the queen and his own to keep and accomplish this agreement and joint the lord precedent the marshal of the camp and the other of the counsel of state and the earl of Thomond and Clanrickard shall swear and confirm the same in a several writing I promise and swear to accomplish and keep these article of agreement and promise the same likewise on the behalf of his majesty the catholic king my master don jean aguila geo. Carew Clanrickard Thomond r. Wingfeild geo. Bourcher ro. Gardner ric. levison the date of this writing be after the new style don jean aguila Fynes moryson this agreement be assign by hand promise by honourable word and confirm the siege of kinsale raise by solemn oath on both part the lord deputy raise the siege upon the nine of januarie and his lordship with don jean aguila and some of the chief spanish captain in his company ride that day to cork whether our army march the same day the gross of the spaniard remain at kinsale after the lord deputy disperse the army through the town of monster to be lodge namely at cork Waterford Youghall Rosse callan Cashell Thomastowne kilkenny dungaruen and clommell the ten of january his lordship give order to the victualler to provide a month bisquit for three thousand five hundred spaniard after a pound and a half each day for a man and to provide for they as much beef and bear proportionable as can be get with speed his lordship give order that the ship shall be unlade in the port and make ready to transport the spaniard into Spain the eleven of january his lordship receive letter date the two and twenty of November from the lord in England advertise that the earl of Desmond be there late dead and therefore require that the company of foot keep in his name and for his maintenance shall be discharge reserve that part of entertainment which out of the same be allot to the lord bishop of Cashell and to the relief of the earl of desmonds sister Further advertise that eighteen hundred quarter of oat be send into monster for the horse troop which will with the transportation cost her majesty fifteen shilling the quarter and be to be issue to the troop at the same rate the same day his lordship receive letter date the four and twenty of December from the lord in England as follow after our right hearty commendation to your lordship we have now at last after long and great expectation receive your letter by sir Oliver s. john who arrive yesternight at the court by who although her majesty have not receive so much satisfaction as be hope for upon the former probability contain in your dispatch yet his relation have make a great alteration of that anxiety in which her majesty remain by those report which have be bring to this place to which in respect of your long and unexpected silence from those part we can no way give contradiction have not receive before now any particular advertisement since the arrival of sir Thomas savage and therefore no way able to make any judgement of your estate which be thus describe first that the irish rebel lodge close by you that your camp be full of all misery and penury to the great slander of this kingdom last that there be six thousand spaniard land of which last particular my lord of ormonds man be the relator for prevention of which uncertainty hereafter we be command in her majesty name to require you from hence forward to advertise we frequent from time to time of your proceed to the intent that her majesty may still have mean to provide for your support which you may not look to receive from hence in the time you shall use they except we may be daily inform beforehand from you of all such particular circumstance as fall out in that place to come therefore now to this present dispatch we have peruse your journal both of the service do and of the difficulty which have interrupt your proceed hitherto whereunto we mean to make no other reply then this that we that know your judgement and affection to her majesty service so well as we (@) must say thus much that we be no more doubtful that you have do as much as you can than you have reason we hope by the course that be take with you from hence not to believe and know that her majesty have in no sort neglect you for demonstration whereof you shall first understand that before the arrival of sir Oliver saint john her majesty have give order for four thousand man to be send into monster with such supply of munition and victual as her majesty think fit provisional to send though for lack of advertisement we can not make any other particular judgement what be too much or too little only this we know that if that body of spanish force which be now in that kingdom shall not be defeat before the like body of a army or a great arrive her majesty shall be put to such a war in the end as howsoever this state may undergo the excessive charge of continual levy and transportation which you will well consider to be of intolerable burden to this kingdom all circumstance consider yet such will be the extreme difficulty to maintain such a army in that realm where it must fight against foreign army and a universal rebellion and in a climate full of contagion and in a kingdom utter waste as we do well foresee that it will draw with it more pernicious consequent then ever this state be subject to for whosoever shall now behold the beginning of this malicious design of the king of Spain must well conclude although he have now begin his action upon a false ground to find a powerful party in that kingdom at his first descent wherein he have be in some measure deceive yet see he be now so deep engage and so well find his error that he will value his honour at too high a rate to suffer such a work to dissolve in the first foundation in consideration whereof her majesty like a provident prince resolve present to send a strong fleet to his own coast to prevent his new reinforcement not doubt if such a disaster shall happen that these force shall remain so long unremoved by you in Ireland which we can believe that her majesty fleet shall yet be in great possibility to defeat the new supply by the way for which purpose her majesty perceive how dangerous a thing it be for the fleet in Ireland to lie off at sea in this winter weather which they must do if it be intend that they shall hinder a descent and how superfluous a thing it be to maintain such a fleet only to lie in harbour her majesty be please to revoke the great part of her royal ship hither and to add to they a great proportion and send they all to the coast of Spain leave still such a competent number of ship there as may sufficient block up the harbour and give security and countenance to transportation to which end we have write a letter in her majesty name to revoke sir Richard levison and to leave sir Amias Preston with the charge of those ship contain in this note to who we have give direction in all thing to apply himself to those course which you shall think most expedient for that service you shall also understand that we have now direct sir Henry Dockwra to send eight hundred man by pole to Knockfergus to sir Arthur chichester and command he to make they up one thousand and so with all speed the say sir Arthur himself to march up with a thousand of the best man to your reinforcement in monster and thus have for the present little else to write unto you till we hear further we do conclude with our best wish unto you of all happy and speedy success and so remain etc. at the court at whitehall the four and twenty of December 1601. the same eleven day of january his lordship receive other letter from the lord date the seven and twenty of December signify that whereas his lordship have often move on the behalf of the captain that they may receive their full pay without deduction of the soldier apparel which they themselves will provide now her majesty be please to condescend thereunto I remember not whether his lordship have move this since or before the new mix coin be but sure this be great advantage to her majesty at this time have pay silver for the apparel and be to make the full pay in mix money the same eleven day of january his lordship receive from the lord in England letter date the five and twenty of November signify that a proportion of victual be provide at Plymouth for which he shall send four merchant ship of the queen fleer at kinsaile and require to be advertise upon what term the spaniard have yield which be then send over for England that they may be dispose according by the old date of this letter and another above mention of the two and twenty of November receive all on the eleven of january it may appear how necessary it be to have the magazine in Ireland well store and how dangerous it be that the army shall depend on sudden provision the same day his lordship receive letter from the lord in England that her majesty have make a leafy of four thousand foot whereof two thousand be now at the port to be embark for monster namely one thousand one hundred commit to the charge of eleven captain and nine hundred under the conduct of some of the say captain leave to his lordship disposal the fourteen his lordship lie at the bishop of cork house receive this follow letter from don jean lie in the town of cork translate out of spanish most excellent lord since they carry i to the city of cork certain merchant have tell i they think they shall find ship to carry i and my folk into Spain if your excellency will give they licence and passport of which I humble beseech your highness as also that of your great beniguitie your excellency will have pity of these his prisoner who here do expect the great mercy which so great a prince as your excellency use towards his servant and prisoner these poor prisoner suffer extreme want both with hunger and cold for there be no sustenance give they at all nor find they any alm I beseech your excellency will be please to have compassion of they there be one dead of hunger and other be ready to die of it God keep your excellency the year which we his servant wish his excellency from cork the four and twenty of january 1602 style and as they write your excellency servant don I can del' aguila the spanish prisoner be these take at Rincorran castle man and woman 90. take at castle Nyparke 16. take in the sally the second of December 13. take at tyrones over throw the four and twenty of December aswell principal as ordinary man one and forty prisoner in all one hundred sixty beside the runnaway during the siege be thirty and these together with many of the say prisoner have be send into England and the rest of who don lean write be still prisoner as (@) the four and twenty of january the lord deputy and council here write to the lord in England this letter follow may it please your lordship we have receive your letter of the four and twenty of December the eleven of january which be the first that we receive out of England since the arrival of sir Richard levison with our munition and supply and although we have upon every important revolution of our business dispatch unto your lordship both our estate and desire yet we humble desire your lordship pardon for the omission of our duty to inform you more often of our present estate the chief cause thereof be the respect and fear we have to possess you with such falsehood as it seem they do which undertake more liberal to advertise your lordship of the estate of our affair for in no place do all intelligence come apparel even to they that be near unto they in more deceivable mist until time and great observation discover the truth so that if we shall write unto your lordship often according to our best information we shall present to your reverent judgement such ridiculous contrariety as will give you occasion to confound your determination and to condemn we but in general we beseech your lordship to remember that as we have in all our dispache declare our hope to overcome all difficulty out of the confidence of our good cause and alacrity to serve her majesty so we have continual propound how great and difficult a war it be in which we be engage in that without God miraculous preservation the army in a winter siege will so decay as it must have please your lordship continual to supply it with man victual and munition also we propound that we hold it a matter of no small danger and great difficulty to force such and so many man in a place of the least advantage that we expect no less than a general revolt and a powerful combination of the rebel against we last again we humble desire your lordship to remember that we have promise nothing but the uttermost of our faithful counsel and endeavour to accomplish in that service her majesty purpose and therefore we be most hearty sorry that by our faithful and sincere counsel and our extreme endure in the execution thereof howsoever the event be not so speedy happy as we desire and labour for yet it be not our happiness that her majesty shall receive so much by sir Oliver saint john as we hope to have give she upon the former probability yet when it shall please your lordship thorough to consider our difficulty by the true relation thereof with all material circumstance we presume it will appear that we can have do no more and we must only attribute it unto God that we have do so much by sir Henry Davers your lordship have be acquaint at large with all our proceed until that present since which time the effect of that victory which it please God of his infinite goodness to give we against the traitor upon Christmas Eve have appear by great and unexpected good thereof insusue for the rebel be break and disperse o Donnel redmond bourke and Hugh mostyon all archrebel have embark themselves with sirriage for Spain and that without tyrones knowledge and contrary to his advice and will they have only leave behind they in monster with the provincial rebel Tyrrell and a small force with he be disperse by small company in Carbery beer Desmond kerry and the county of Lymrick Tyrone in great fear and with a speedy march haste out of the province of monster lose upon every Foard many of his foot but especial in pass the river of broadwater of may in Connolagh and at the abbey Owney in o malryans country at which foard the water be high as we be inform he lose above two hundred man and all the way as he go the weary foot cast away their arm which those of the country gather up and with all take some of their head but not so many as they may have do if they have regard their duty as they ought their tire horse be slay by their rider their hurt man which escape at the overthrow and be carry away upon garron die upon the way and four principal gentleman whereof we understand Tyrone himself be one and Mac mahow another the rest be not know yet be carry in litter since his departure from o mulryan country we hear nothing of he but we assure your lordship that the dismay in which they be and still be be incredible upon newyear Eve don jean send a letter unto i the deputy the copy whereof be here within send unto your lordship the next day sir William godolphin be send with instruction to receive from don jean the point whereof he desire to treat who discreet carriage in so weighty a cause wherein he perform as much as in discretion and judgement can be require be such as without do the gentleman wrong we may not omit to recommend he to your honourable favour he be as by experience we may true report wise valiant and of many extraordinary good part the copy of the article agree upon between we and don jean subscribe by either part your lordship with these shall likewise receive hope that in the same we have do nothing but that which shall be agreeable to your lordship and which as we suppose our present estate due consider vehement urge we to embrace now our great care be to hasten these spaniard away who be as don jean affirm no less by pole then three thousand five hundred the defect of ship be our chiafe want they and we be in equal pain for they be no less desirous to be go than we be to send they away the contempt and scorn in which the spaniard hold the irish and the distaste which the irish have of they be not to be believe by any but those who be present to see their behaviour and hear their speech insomuch as we may probable opniecture that by such time as don jean with his source be arrive in Spain it will be a difficult thing for the irish hereafter to procure aid out of Spain the copy of the contract for the rate which don jean must pay for tonnage and for victual for his man in their return your lordship with these shall also receive the nine of this month we dissolve the camp and bring hither with we don jean who remain hostage for the performance of the covenant between we the day follow capt. Roger Haruy and capt. Flower be dispatch westward to receive from the spaniard the castle of castle-haven of baltimore and of beere-haven the win of which place in our judgement although kinsale have be take by force will have be more difficult unto we then that of kinsale aswell in respect of the incommodity which we shall have find in keep a strong and furnish army in so remote place as in respect of the natural strength of those place and especial of baltimore which with a little art will be make of great strength since our come to cork I the deputy to ease her majesty great and unsupportable charger have discharge two thousand foot in list wherein without all respect of favour I cast those who have the weak company and assoon as we may be better secure that the spaniard purpose to forbear any further attempt for this realm which in a few month will appear as many as possible can be spare shall be in like sort casher to suppress the present rebellon in monster I the deputy have design four thousand foot and three hundred twenty five horse which be lay in such convenient place as we intend to lay they our hope be that in short time this rebellion will be extinguish but until we be better assure from the attempt of Spain for this kingdom the remainder of the army be disperse into the remote place of the province eastward of cork and I the deputy do purpose to remain here until I may be more secure that there will be no cause to draw the army back into these part Paul ivy the engineer with the best expedition that may be shall be send to the part of Baltymore and beerhaven to chuse-out fit ground to fortify the like must be do at kinsale and for the better hold of the city of this province in due obedience of who assurance in case the spaniard have prevail we have cause to doubt we think it under your lordship reformation very expedient that in every of they citadel be raise which guard with a few man and have some piece of artillery will everhave power to command they there place be thus strengthen there be no port forget that may be fit for the spaniard acommodate in any enterprise from hence upon England for all which lie in Desmond kerry or Connaght have too large a sea to pass for England which be subject to infinite inconvenience and as for the coast within saint George channel the danger of it be so infinite as there be no fear of those port notwithstanding it have please God to give we this happy success in free this realm of the spanish army under don jean yet fear that some second under another commander may be employ hither we humble beseech your lordship that you will be please to send the victual for which we write by sir Oliver saint john if the spanish supply come we shall have cause to expend they in this province if they come not than our care shall be such as they shall be preserve and dispense to the best use for her majesty service the like suit we make for the munition for which we then write but for the supply require of we in the dispatch we make by sir Henry Davers your lordship may please to make stay of they till a further occasion to use they only of one thousand thereof we have great need for the reinforcing of the company which be weak and therefore we desire that five hundred of they may land here at cork and the other five hundred at Waterford and that the rest may be in readiness if we have any new occasion to send for they till which time we be unwilling to charge her majesty or trouble your lordship or to draw any new force into these part which have make we give direction that sir Arthur chichester with the one thousand man which your lordship have command he to lead hither shall stay about the newry and make the war there aswell defend the pale as annoy Tyrone for sir Arthur be there shall be near enough to we if there shall fall out any occasion to draw those man hither sir Richard moryson be make the bearer of these our letter who we have choose to satisfy your lordship in such thing wherein happy you may doubt in particular we have acquaint he with the date of all our letter which we have send your lordship since the land of the spaniard so as he can inform your lordship of his own knowledge that we all or I the deputy have write thus often namely the three and twenty of September the first three and four and twenty of October the seven and the thirteen of November the seven twelve and seven and twenty of December and this present dispatch by himself if any of these have miscarry or find so slow passage as your lordship expectation be not satisfy in such time as for the service have be fit we beseech you be please to consider that the like may happen to such of you as be send hither and this may appear by your lordship letter which we last receive for the eleven of januarie as be note in the begin of this letter we receive your lordship of the four and twenty of December and with it another of the seven and twenty of that month touch the apparel a three of the two and twenty of December yea a four of the two and twenty of November we have license captain josias bodley to pass into England upon some private business import he and have address he to your lordship to receive your pleasure if you resolve upon any fortification in this kingdom the gentleman be very will experience and practise in that art and one who in all our work we have principal employ which he have with great hazard labour and sufficiency discharge we find all man here to embrace with much gladness her majesty resolution to leave the apparel of the soldier be much better content to have full pay without detain of any sum for their clothes and we hope it will be a mean to make the captain keep their company strong and as your lordship have direct upon notice of the decease of the earl of Desmond the company allow for he be discharge save what have please you to continue to his sister to the archbishop of Casbell and 10. power order be also take that the oat send over hither shall be issue at as high rate as we can but it have never be hitherto see that the price exceed ten shilling the quarter and we think they can be issue at a high rate for the soldier can live pay any more but will rather suffer his horse to starve which will be great inconueniency to the service then if the oat have not come at all though that way also they must have starve if the siege have continue the spaniard ship from hence to Plymoth where either such as have runneaway from the spaniard or such as be in Ryncorren and castle nyparke and yield upon promise of their life only and so much I the deputy signify by my letter to the gentleman of the part where they shall land of purpose to be make know to your lordship and that they may according be suffer to pass into France or some other country which be as much as they desire and so etc. from cork this fourteen of January 1601. the lord President be desirous to go over with this dispatch of please news to the
