court but the lord deputy be loath to spare he till the spaniard be go and because the relation of this business much concern his lordship honour he think it necessary to choose a messenger as in other part fit for the business so especial sound to he in affection and for such he choose sir Richard moryson who have be very inward with he till the death of the earl of Essex at which time his lordship begin to grow something strange towards he in regard that Mr secretary have conceive some displeasure against he about a passage of his dependency on the say earl yet his lord p ever profess to continue his love to he and promise at some fit time to make his peace with master secretary to which purpose his lordship choose this occasion concur with his own end only his lordship advise sir Richard moryson to entreat the l. president approbation of his carry this packet to the say end so much import he to which the lord precedent very noble give his consent and so he be dispatch with the lord deputy and the lord precedent letter to master secretary of especial recommendation on his behalf among his instruction he be direct at his first arrival to repair to sir Oliver saint john to learn of he the present estate of the lord deputy affair in court and that after they both shall communicate their proceed one with the other to a imaginary question why there be no use make of the first breach at the north east gate of kinsale he be direct to answer that the first battery be chief intend to annoy the spaniard by beat down the house and to take from they the use of some place whence they may annoy we that when by beat down the gate we have make a breach we find it not yet to be attempt but make near approach whereupon the spaniard make their strong sally both upon our approach and upon our Cannou and the next day we have intelligence that Tyrone Odonnell and all the rebel be encamp so near we as if we have engage ourselves in that work and in the guard of our cannon so far out of the camp they may in three hour have fall upon we on all side with great advantage which make we draw the cannon into the camp and to leave that work choose rather to invest they close on the west side which before lay open so as they may easy that way receive succour from the rebel and join with their force further the reason be set down which move the lord deputy and the counsel to make composition with den jean namely our weakness and the enemy strength since our army by sickness runaway and death be fall to be almost as weak as at the first sit down whereas the spaniard be more now then three thousand man by pole the suffering of a winter siege fall more upon we in the field then upon they in the town beside if we have take kinsale by force our army can not possible have march into the western part possess by other spaniard till it have be refresh and till we have new supply of victual and munition which can not easy arrive easterly wind in winter be very rare upon this coast. beside that ere we can have force the spaniard in the west in all likelihood new spanish supply will have arrive and the take of those remote place will have be more difficult and dangerous then that of kinsale and the king of Spain will have be engage in a long war which by this composition be like to be end beside our army consist much of irish unfit for such service as the enter of a breach so as therein we must of necessity have use our old english company where in all probability we must have lose great part they be esteem by the spaniard themselves as gallant fellow as ever they meet and such as in truth the loss of they will be formany year unrepairable and if we have be repulse with any blow give we we have reason to doubt that all the irish yea those of our army will have turn their sword against we yea if the breach have be enter the town of kinsale be build all of stone the spaniard in the house will have make we new work no less difficult than the former moreover six of our piece for battery be craze so as we can not make any more than one breach and the spaniard have so many hand and so large scope of ground within may easy have stop one gap against we and if we can have make diverse breach yet we have not powder and bullet sufficient for that purpose and for the small shot beside that our man be so waste as they can not guard diverse battery neither have we sufficient engineer for that purpose so that howsoever we stand upon term that don jean shall leave his munition and treasure to her majesty yet find he make obstinate opposition thereunto we be force for the above name reason and many like to make this present composition likewise among the instruction diverse reason be set down move don jean to make the say composition namely the malice he and the spaniard general have conceive against the irish in who aid they too late discover no confidence can judicial be place and for that they come to succour Tyrone and Odonnell can never see any such man say that they be not that be existent also for that don jean have instruction to keep the field and not to defend kinsale now since the overthrow of the irish have no hope to be able to come into the field moreover that his best man in this long siege be spend in continual watch and his new man grow weak with feed only upon ruske Further his desire to disengage the king his master from a war wherein he have little probability to prevail in respect of the small or no assistance which he conceive may be expect from the irish. also for that the treasure he bring be at the first but one hundred thousand ducat be in great part exhaust by pay the soldier six penny and the commander their entertainment so as by the remainder he have no hope to work any revolt among the irish or to give satisfaction to the covetous humour of those already in rebellion the nine and twenty of january the lord deputy and Counfell here write to the lord in England this follow letter may it please your lordship since our last dispatch by sir Richard moryson here have no extraordinary matter happen that may give we just cause now so soon to write again be it not that it please your lordship in your last to blame our slackness that of late we write too seldom and to command from hence a more frequent advertisement of our proceed which for your lordship satisfaction we be most careful to perform the chief point to which of late we have apply ourselves have be the dispatch away of the spaniard into their country because till we have free the country of they we may not safe leave this province to follow such service in other part as we desire next to undertake and we do the more earnest labour to end this for that we more and more observe such a settle hatred against this people both in don jean and the spanish in general as we gather upon many good ground that if they can be return before any new supply come from Spain the king will be easy induce to forbear any further to engage himself in this business and for that they wihall do protest to use their best endeavour to that effect which we hold to be unfeigned but the wind continue hitherto westerly and for the most part somewhat to the southward and withal very much stormy neither such of they as have ship and for a good space lie aboard to take the first occasion to be go can by any mean get out of the harbour nor such ship of we as be to come from the easterly part for transportation of the rest can find the mean to come about hither nor three other ship that have be long ready to go to the westward to take in those at castle haven baltimore and beerehaven can by any industry work thither for that purpose though we know they have with all care and earnestness endeavour it so as now we grow more doubtful than before that other force from Spain will arrive here ere these get home and therefore can but wish that for the prevention thereof her majesty will be please to continue her resolution for the send of her fleet to sea with as much expedition as may be and that the Tramontana and the moon be ship of small burden and so fit for the service on this coast may present be send hither and the rather for that here will be none leave but only the Swiftsure under the command of sir Amias Preston the two merchant ship appoint by your lordship to stay here be the one send away with sir Richard levison and the other employ for the transport of these spaniard by reason we have not other ship for that purpose and upon the same ground we most humble pray your lordship to send away the one thousand foot for supply and what else we desire in our former letter that we may not be unprovided for the worst that may happen although that we be in good hope ere it be long to have good reason to ease her majesty charge in this country without any danger to her service here we be further humble to beseech your lordship to procure we her majesty warrant to pass unto Neale garue o donnel the country of Tyreconnell in such sort as we promise the same unto he under our hand now almost a year ago so warrant by direction from her majesty he think the time long till he have his grant and in his late letter to i the deputy protest that he will do no more service until he have see i or that his grant be perform for which though I have blame he as I just may that be a unfit fashion of write for any subject yet the rudeness of his education and his forwardness and ability to do service consider we can but acknowledge that we hold it very meet that we shall perform unto he what we promise which to do we want warrant from her majesty and therefore desire to have it with your lordship next dispatch for we know he will earnest call upon it we do not hear of any head of importance or strength that remain gather together of the rebel so that we hope that if no more spanish succour do arrive their force will quick come to nothing but because we can assure the subject what further assistance the rebel shall receive out of Spain and for that if the king send any more we presume he will do it in a far great measure than heretofore as have experience not to trust in the force of the rebel and therefore reason to rely upon his own strength we most humble desire your lordship for a while to continue your honourable care of our affair and to enable we to withstand whatsoever shall be attempt by the foreign enemy and what you send we will employ with all sincerity and our best endeavour to the advancement of the service which her majesty do require and expect from we and so etc. the last of january the lord deputy be advertise by one come out of the west part that he be at beerhaven the thirteen of that month where the spaniard be in number sixty and Oswyllivan have some three hundred irish and the spaniard not know of don jean composition with his lordship do build a fort there with tree and earth near the castle and plant three small piece of ordinance therein whereof the great carry nine pound of powder that he come from thence to baltimore where he find one hundred spaniard which do not fortify but hear of don jean composition and have two ship with they have ship their ordinance and be ready to go to Sea about this time one Richard Owen come from Tyrone to mediate with the lord deputy her majesty mercy towards he whereupon his lordship give leave to a gentleman in the pale of tyrones old acquaintance to parley with he according to the follow instruction date the four of February when you speak with he you shall tell he that you understand that Richard Owen come from he to the lord deputy with commission from he to tell his lordship that he desire to be receive into the queen mercy if his life may be secure whereupon you find in he such conformity out of your ancient love which in former time you bear he be glad of the alteration and therefore as his friend do now undertake this long journey to persuade he to those course which may best answer his duty to his prince and repair his estate which in your opinion be desperate if you find he desirous to be receity to mercy you shall give he hope of it and promise he furtherance for the effect of it upon these condition that he shall in token of his penitency and according to the duty of a rebel to his natural prince first under his hand write a letter of submission to the lord deputy humble crave in the same her majesty mercy with promise to redeem his error pass by his future service that likewise he shall write a public submission to her majesty implore at her hand forgiveness of his fault and likewise promise amendment of his life with a willing desire to do her foam acceptable service in recompense of his transgression in the same protest to serve her majesty against all man either of Ireland or foreigner that shall endeavour the disturbance of this country that he shall put into her majesty hand his elder son for the assurance of his future loyalty and four principal gentleman of his blood as he former promise that he shall at his charge find workman to build such fort in the county of Tyrone and in such place as the lord deputy shall think fit that he shall permit throughout Tyrone her majesty officer of justice as the sheriff and other to have free liberty to execute their office as be accustom in other province and county of the realm and answer all other duty former agree upon that he shall only undertake for himself and his pledge to lie for no more than those that dwell upon that land only that be contain in his letter patent not any way undertake for the rest of Tyrone as Turlogh brassiloes son Mac Mahu o cane Macguire the two clandeboye and all of the east side of the ban. that if any of his neighbour shall continue in rebellion none of their people shall be harbour in Tyrone and likewise that none of Tyrone shall by his consent or knowledge succour any rebel or give assistance to they and if any such offender shall happen to be discover either by himself or any other her majesty officer upon knowledge thereof that he shall do his best endeavour to prosecute the party offend and either take they whereby they may be try by the law of the realm or kill they if they may not otherwise be have and shall assist her majesty officer in take to she use the good and chattel of the offender and their retinue that he shall not only true pay all her majesty rent and duty from this time forward due unto she out of Tyrone but also pay the arrearage that for many year have be by he detain that in respect of the great charge that he have put her majesty unto although it be not the thousand part of her disbursement which in all such great offence be accustom towards the victual of her majesty garrison he shall pay two thousand cow within six month that the county of Tyrone may be limit and no more by he to be possess then be contain in his letter patent that the territory of Tyrone may be divide into shire and have jail as he have former desire that he put at liberty the son of shane o Neale and all other prisoner english and irish. these thing you shall only propound as from yourself yet as conceive that they will be demand at his hand if he be receive and to draw as large a overture from he of what he will agree unto as you can persuade he tell he that the great assurance he do give the state of his loyalty the great will be his safety for we shall construe his good meaning by his free offer thereof and after we shall have the less reason to be lealous of he the fifteen of February the lord deputy and counsel here write to the lord in England this follow letter may it please your lordship the fourteen of this last month we disparch sir Richard moryson with our letter to your lordship from this place and the nine and twenty we write again by captain Butler yet to this day the wind nath continue still so westerly as since the departure of sir Richard no ship be (@) to we either out of England from your lordship as we desire or from Waterford wexford and those part as we direct to carry away the spaniard hence nor yet until Sunday the seven hereof can those ship stir that lay ready at kinsale to be send to baltimore castle haven and beer haven but now they be go we hope that the service to be do by they which be the possess of the castle and send away the spaniard in they will be present accomplish although the wind have serve they so scant as we fear they will hardly recover all the place whereunto they be direct there be only one scottish ship go from kinsale for Spain which carry one hundred sixety spaniard with part of the artillery but there lie now ready at the harbour for the first wind so much ship as will carry away one thousand five hundred more so as there will be yet remain in kinsale above one thousand spaniard which with the first ship that come from the other port shall be embark don jean stay to go last it appear by some letter intercept which we send herewithal unto your lordship that the king of Spain purpose to send a large supply hither with all expedition don jean assure we to do his best to stay they and if he arrive first in Spain he make no doubt to dissuade their come but if they shall come before his departure he promise to return they according to his covenant in the contract if they do not come under the command of some other that have a commission a part from he from the King the irish have of late receive letter from Odonnell to encourage the rebel to persevere in their rebellion assure they of present aid from Spain in the mean time the best of they all do but temporize be ready to assist they when they come especial if they come in any strength as it be to be think in all reason they will have find their first error her majesty must therefore be please to be at some charge to erect fortification at beer haven kinsale and this place the commodity and weakness of these place be as well know to the spaniard as to we and further with all speed to erect citadel at Lymbrick cork and Waterford though it be only to assure the town from revolt it appear by the king of Spain letter and so by the duke of (@) that his heart be very much set upon the enterprise of Ireland and therefore it be not unlike but that he may send more supply after or before don jean arrival in Spain either under he or some other commander which if he do it be also likely the same will be send short for prevention thereof if in your lordship wisdom it shall be think meet we do humble beseech that the four thousand supply heretofore desire and by your lordship intend may be present send hither whereof two thousand to be erect into company and their captain to be name here and the other 2000 for supply of the army which be exceed weak for our man die daily in great number than they die in the camp the infection be great and by some think a kind of plague for the people in the town die in far great number than the soldier though we hope the contrary and we do further desire that her majesty will be please to hasten her fleet to the coast of Spain which come timely will in our opinion hinder any enterprise for Ireland but least that fail we renew our former motion that the tramontama and the moon may be return to serve upon the coast of monster that the proportion of munition and victual desire in our former letter may speedy be dispatch hither and that victualler without impediment may come from all place to relieve we for already a very great dearth be begin and a famine must ensue the rate of all thing be incredible and the new money much repine at notwithstanding we do our uttermost endeavour to advance it but in a matter of so great importance we humble desire your lordship to give we leave to deliver our opinion free have so assure ground for it that if the king of Spain continue his war in this country it will be hard to preserve her majesty army and kingdom without the alter of the currant money so general be the dislike thereof and so insolent do they begin already to refuse it but if there come no foreign aid her majesty as we think may secure continue it as it be for all we that be of the army who it most concern in regard we live whole upon our entertainment will God will endure it for the advancement of the service though we be sensible of our loss by the excessive enhance of the price of all thing that we be to lieu upon which can be holpen so long as this new coin continue currant of Tyrone since his overthrow and departure we hitherto have hear little neither do we think he will be able to do any great harm without the aid of new supply from Spain and so we humble take leave etc. from cork etc. the same fifteenth-day the lord deputy write to master secretary in England this letter follow sir this strange continuance of the wind in the west and the south make i look back into the danger that both her majesty army and kingdom have pass for if sir Richard levison with her majesty fleet have not take the opportunity of that wind which do no more than bring he hither and give the rest of the supply with great difficulty their passage from other port to we no doubt by these contrary wind from that time to this day continue all the affair of her majesty here have be in a extreme hazard and when I consider first that in all likelihood we can expect no less than a powerful supply out of Spain and that the great the more the king shall find himself engage and his army stand in need of second except he may be in time advertise of this overture we have make here to disimbarke himself fair of a enterprise which I presume his minister here do believe and will persuade he to be unfit any long to embrace then that the wind have be such as have only serve to carry he the danger of his man here and not the peace which they have make for since his departure which be present after the overthrow don jean del' Aguyla be never able to send away any dispatch which we may hope to be arrive in Spaine and last that we have credible intelligence of the king resolution and forwardness to send his man here strong and speedy succour when I consider these thing I can but fear a heavy war to be towards we which as I do constant believe have be prevent if it have please God to send we a wind in any time to have send away these spaniard or at the least the assure relation of their estate thus the continuance of contrary wind in these part do make i apprehend the extreme peril wherewith her majesty shall be drive to make the war in this country with extreme charge if the spaniard persevere in their purpose for without huge magazine great waste and continual charge of ship and land carriage such a war can be make and I be persuade that her majesty be as good give over at the first the defence of this country as to intend a war without make those provision for it now as my love to she and to her service do make i as sensible I will bold protest as any man live of burden the state do feel so the same love shall make i suffer with alacrity the weight of my uneasy charge and the dangerous way wherein I walk if I do not perceive the poor ass to be the worse like that he do carry so much treasure from her coffer howsoever he do unwilling bear it away and feel nothing but the heavy burden thereof this and some inclination that I have find to measure my labour by the success not by my endeavour have I confess more discourage i than all the difficulty I ever pass or may expect and save the thankfulness which I can choose but yield unto God for the success which it have please he of late to give i I protest I be never accompany with more unquiet thought then since my last come to cork where I continue in a most noisome town full of infection see no end of my labour nor find any measure of they and yet fear that they be value of so little merit as they be rather likely to draw on dislike-wherefore as in my own heart I do utter distaste this unhappy profession with no further ambition then to set down in quietness under my own vine with the conscience of have be no unprofitable servant to her majesty so sir I vow before God I will acknowledge it a everlasting bond if you will be a mean to procure i that harmless fortune that I may as above all thing I desire serve her majesty henceforward with as pure as I will ever do with faithful devotion and make myself ready for another world for I thank God I do hate this blame i not I beseech you sir for apprehend my fortune with so much discomfort since I do not only perceive what enemy I have that be ingenious and industrious to urge all my proceed to my disadvantage but find that their malice do take such effect with her majesty as to move she to be unsatisfied with my endeavour wherein my own conscience can acknowledge any thing omit within my power or belong to my duty whatsoever the success have be sir as I never deserve any ill of they by deed except it be by do her majesty better service than they can or will do nor by word for I do not think or speak of they but when these token of their good will do force i unto it so I protest I do as much scorn their malice as the bark of so many whelp and will be little trouble with it but when I think that their false evidence do sway the opinion of my supreme judge in the title of her favour and my desert and do remember how doubtful the fortune of the war be I can but fear that one disaster shall be put into the ballence against all my labour and endeavour and therewithal conclude and confess that I covet no mortal fortune more then to be fair rid of the part which I play on so dangerous a stag before these serpent may find any advantage to hiss at i whereas otherwise if I have be secure of her majesty favour against these viper tongue I shall with confidence and alacrity go towards the great danger that can rise against i but as God have hitherto stop their mouth so I hope for her majesty good if not for i he will continue his favour who prosper i in all thing as I do sincere intend her service etc. the eighteen day the lord deputy receive letter from the lord in England signify that beside the two thousand last send the great part under captain the rest leave to his lordship disposal now upon a second levy two thousand more be appoint to be embark the six and twenty of the last month all which be leave to his lordship disposal except one company give to captain Thomas dutton upon his lordship letter of special recommendation the same day his lordship receive from the queen this follow letter Elizabeth right trusty and wellbeloved we greet you well the report which your letter by daver have bring we of the success it have please God to give you against our rebel and the spaniard combine with they be receive by we with such contentment as so great and happy a accident can afford wherefore although we as ever we have do in all other happiness which have befall we ascribe the high praise and thanks to his divine majesty yet forasmuch as we do account that they who be the servant of our state in like action be make participant in a second degree of his favour bestow upon we by their virtue and industry we can but hold they worthy of thanks from we as they have receive honour from he among who you be there the chief not only as chief put in trust by we but as we plain perceive in vigilancy in labour and in valour in this late action we can not forbear to let you see how sensible we be of this your merit it be true that before this good success upon the rebel we be in daily attention to have hear of some quick attempt upon the town than any be make both in respect that your own letter tend to such sense and especial because protraction of time bring with it apparent danger as well of access of new supply from our foreign enemy as of defection of a people so unconstant of disposition and so rebellious to government as those of that nation ever have be but we that time have understand by those journal which be commit to St john and daver some reason which have move you to the course you have take rather than to have use speed in attempt see all assault be accompany with loss and every loss in such a time multiply in rumour and whole convert by practice to the prejudice of the cause in question which be maintain now as thing do stand by the reputation of your army we do now conceive that all your work have have their foundation upon such reason as you think most advantageous for our service it remain therefore now and so we desire it may be make know to our army that have serve under you in such manner as you shall think best to express it that as we do know they have endure many incommodity in this siege which we will have be glad they can have avoid have make so good proof of their valour and loyalty as they have do at this time so as we rather seek to preserve they as the best treasure of a prince then to suffer they to waste if otherwise our kingdom can have be keep from danger of foreign conquest and intestine rebellion so we expect it at the hand of the better sort of our servitor there that it shall well be infuse into the mind of the rest that whatsoever either our own direction or expend of treasure can do for prevention of those difficulty which follow all army and be inseparable where the war be make in a climate so ill temper for a winter siege have be royal and provident afford they a matter of much more charge and uncertainty because all our care and direction have attend the wind and weather courtesy to conclude with answer to your demand for further supply of man although we hope that the time be so near of the final conclusion of your happy success against the remnant of the stranger in that poor town be press with so many want and with the despair which our late victory will add hereunto as that hardly any supply send from we can come before it have take effect yet because you may perceive how much we attribute to your judgement in any thing which for our affair be there desire we have as by our counsel have be signify unto you give order for four thousand man to be send thither out of hand with the full proportion of munition which you desire in which kind of provision we find so great consumption as we must require you to take some better order with they that have the distribution thereof for if it be observe what quantity have be daily send over and yet what daily want be pretend the expense will be find insupportable and so much the rather because all man know that whatsoever the irish company receive except now in this action be continual convert for money to the use of the rebel give under our signet at our palace at white-hall the 44 year of our reign the twelve of january 1601. in the begin of this letter above the queen hand sign these follow word be overwrite by the queen own hand viz. though for fear of worse end you do desire as we confess we once think to direct to end this work before either enemy or rebel can increase the peril of our honour yet we hope that no such adventure shall be more make but that their confusion be ere now light on their own head and let Clanrickard and Thomond know that we do most thankful accept their endeavour for yourself we can but acknowledge your diligence and dangerous adventure and cherish and judge of you as your careful sovereign the twenty of February twenty spanish captain with 1374 common soldier be before embark at kinsale in six english ship sail for Spain the seven and twenty day the lord deputy and counsel here write to the lord in England this follow letter may it please your loedship since our last dispatch the fifteen hereof 1400 of the spaniard that have lie aboard their ship in the harbour of kinsale to take advantage of the first favourable wind for Spain find the mean to set forth with a very scant wind upon Sunday last the twenty of this present so as now we have rid ourselves of above 1600 by pole reckonine those which we send from cork into England be some two hundred and these last number be true muster by one of our own commissary if our ship can get about from Waterford wexford and those eastern port from whence only two have yet recover kinsale though all the rest have be a good time ready and some of they at sea we doubt not within four or five day we shall be able to dispatch away all the rest though there remain yet as great a number the certainty whereof we do not know captain Haruy who be send into the western part to ship away the spaniard that be there and to take possession of the place be for certain possess of castle haven and upon Friday last set forward towards baltimore whereof by this time we make account he be possess also and that he be busy send away the spaniard with the first wind though we have not yet hear from he since his be there on Friday last be the eighteen we receive two packet from your lordship the one of the twelve the other of the 16 of the last month and at the same time there come into this harbour five company of foot whereof four be under captain namely Davys Holcroft Bradbery and Barker and the other one hundred under the conduct of the three last good store of ship come likewise in with victual that day and the next and on Sunday there arrive at kinsale about the time that the spaniard leave that port two bark lade with munition and artillery work tool and such other thing as we have write for and your lordship most careful have provide so as we have just cause with all thankfulness to acknowledge that it have please her majesty and your lordship to supply we as royal and plentiful as we desire and that as your lordship well note for our special comfort your providence and care of we be such as thing necessary be in readiness for our supply before you have notice from we that we have need of they we be resolve now that all those provision be come to keep a good force in the west part of this province which will both settle those that hitherto have stand waver and may beside prosecute those that have show themselves worse affect while such place as be needful may be fortify for since as we signify by our last upon the intercept of certain spanish letter there be great likelihood that a new supply from Spain will come speedy and strong if at all it behove we to be in readiness to entertain they by make the place know to they very strong and to curb the town by citadel to be erect otherwise they will revolt and fall to the spanish party when they shall see they come strong and well prepare and for those four thousand man which we perceive your lordship have appoint for we we humble leave it to your lordship consideration upon the sight of these letter out of Spain which we send whether you shall think fit to send they to we present or else have they there in such readiness as if any further supply shall arrive out of Spain we may in time make use of they here the company that be come be man well choose and well appoint their arm good and for aught yet we find the man neither change nor the arm or apparel embezzle and yet they have be very strict both view and muster before commissioner special appoint and the like course we mean to take with the rest of they if they do come as your lordship have appoint which yet we leave whole to your lordship who can best judge what number be meet for we by the intelligence we send you but especial by such as your lordship receive from Spain and as for those that in the conduction of the other man abuse themselves to her majesty so great loss we will do the best to find out the truth and ground thereof and inform you as your lordship have direct and further will most careful perform what else your lordship in these letter have command but where your lordship conceive a omission in i the deputy that so many arm be lose here I humble answer that be not able to look unto these thing myself I give commission to master Martial sir Robert gardener and sir Oliver s. john to take the particular care and charge thereof who be now not here can relate what they have do only we remember we have hear it allege when the captain be charge with the arm of their company that their answer be that some of the soldier run away with they and some other assay to make escape be strip both of their apparel and arm which ourselves see to be true in many that be find and return naked to the camp and the truth be such as be not lose that way nor spoil and break in the service as it can be deny but some be some part be put over with the supply to other company which be very few and the rest can not be recover from the casher captain who be in entertainment but a short time have nothing due whereout it may be defalk but must answer it before your lordship there in England for from hence they go soon after they be discharge the captain last come as other do before they desire that their warrant of entry may bear date that day that your lordship appoint they to be at the water side to receive their company and we acknowledge we think in reason they be to have it so or else their impress to be remit otherwise they have nothing to bear the charge of themselves and officer from that time to their land here which be often time six week or a month in which time their impress be usual spend and if afterward it be defalk from they they must all be so long without entertainment and so unable to live we humble desire to know your lordship pleasure herein as we do former upon the like occasion but hitherto have receive no answer from your lordship we be further to signify unto your lordship that the Victualer issue as he say by direction but one pound and a half of beef to a soldier which be too little for he to live upon and yet the rate in the victual nothing abate which be intolerable for he to bear and likewise the Victualer think that he may not issue the oat at a low rate than 15 s. the quarter which be seven shilling six penny the barrel be so high a rate as the horseman out of his entertainment can allow so much for his horse but by that mean both the horse will be starve and the oat will perish before they be spend in time of plenty the ordinary rate of oat in Ireland be but at twelve penny the barrel yet they be now well content to pay six shilling a barrel which be at the high rate the soldier can give of these particular we humble pray redress from your lordship and so etc. from cork etc. the first of march the lord deputy by letter from the lord in England be require to send over a lieutenant be one of the late cast company but still remain in Ireland to the end he may answer before their lordship certain complaint make against he for whereas many officer in the late levy of man have receive in the country able and sufficient man as well to serve under themselves as to be conduct over to be dispose by the lord deputy whereof they have for diverse sum of money dismiss many at the sea side pretend that they be lame or sick and that they have take better man in their place neither of these pretence be true their lordship purpose to inflict some exemplary punishment for this great offence and therefore require this lieutenant to be send over who be accuse among and above the rest the eight of March sir Oliver s. john who be send into England from kinsale with news of the good success in the take of Rincoran and Nyparke castle and the happy repulse of the spaniard sally upon our cannon return back to cork and bring from the queen this follow letter Elizabeth right trusty and well-beloved we greet you well by the genlemans relation who last you send unto we and by your letter we receive with much contentment the news of the rendition of kinsale and other place hold by the spaniard in that kingdom wherein although by compare the same with those report which be bring we by diverse that they be not only in misery for victual but in penury of man as not be five hundred strong we conceive that you may have give they strict law in their composition and so do now perceive how easy a matter it be for those that be near hand to the matter of war than we be to be mistake yet upon those consideration which we have observe in your journal last send over contain many important circumstance which do lead you to that course among which no one have so much move we as that assault will have shed the blood of our subject which be dear to we then any revenge or glory we do account it both in the success one of the most acceptable accident that have befall we and in your carriage thereof discern it to have be guide with as many part of a able and provident minister as any we have use in service of like nature and therefore hold it both just and necessary for we to yield you this testimony of our gracious acceptation of your endeavour which have be accompany with so much pain and peril it remain now see the state of all thing there and your own desire do require it that we speak something of those thing which be fit to be think of for the time to come whereof see this event have both already begin and be very like to work great alteration to our advantage that which we can wish you to aim at be in sum next to the safety of the kingdom to give all possible ease to our state by diminish that great consumption of treasure which of late year we have sustain and yet how to direct precise by what mean and parcel in every particular the same be to be do be very hard for we at this present especial until we shall receive from you and our counsel there further light by the information of the state of all thing now after these success together with your own opinion thereupon only as it be apparent to we already by your letter that in your own judgement have due sense of the infinite inconueniency which daily be multiply upon this kingdom by that occasion you do immediate after the rendition both cast some part of our army there and stay the supply come from hence so in that course we doubt not but you do and will continue as far forth as thing may bear it in take care that our army be not weaken by hold more small garrison than be necessary and this we may with very good reason say out of observation of that which have pass of latter year and agreeable to your own opinion that one charge there be very great to we and yet without any manner of ground of safety if there be cause of adventure and that be the entertainment of great number of irish wherein we will note unto you these two consideration first that when thing there be at most hazard for we your own spirit be doubtful of the service which may be reap by they second that heretofore when they have be use it have not be see that either they be entertain at the same rate of pay with our own nation or so mix in common with they in regiment but ever keep more apart both in company several and use in place and in service proper for they which course although this extraordinary danger of our kingdom have give occasion to dispense with yet doubt we not but in your own conceit you will think it meet with all convenient speed to reform and give beginning to it by such degree of dimunition and in such measure as you shall find to be most for the good of our service for the matter which have be move to you from the arch-trairor we commend your handle of the offer in that you have keep the dignity of the place you hold and therein we and yet we do not mislike that you do not so desperate reject he as to conclude he thereby from open the further scope of his desire and though till the next overture we have little more to write unto you yet we may say thus much in generality that the monstrousness of his fact stain with so many and deep spot of offence of several nature and degree though none more odious than his ingratitude and the quick sense we have always of the biemish of our honour do not permit we to hold any other way with he than the plain way of perdition and therefore do advise you to all course that may win we glory upon he and if our arm must be accompany with any part of mercy rather to employ the same in receive the secondary member and vriaght from he by who that life which be leave he stand then to make so much account of so vile a head as to think he worthy to be recover but rather that abandon of God and man he may be leave to feel the just reward of his foul demerit notwithstanding we will not mislike to hear from you again what you have further discover and guide our further resolution according to occasion hereupon we have think good to return this gentleman sir Oliver s. john to you with thus much of our mind upon your late letter and with such other matter as from our counsel he may have in charge to impart unto you be one of who good discretion and affection to our service we be very well persuade to the end that upon his arrival by which time much will be see of the event of your late happy success you may enter into some solid consideration of the form of government hereafter to be hold of the proportion of our army to be continue and of all thing that may be likely to settle that state in safety from foreign attempt and in a better obedience to we then heretofore when you have debate and resolve what seem good to you there upon all such point we can be then content that you send back this gentleman hither again instruct therewith and because it will be also needful for the furtherance of our resolution here to have good understanding of the civil part of that government as well as of the martial and that suit have be make unto we for sir Robert gardener our chief justice there to be license to come hither we shall like well that you send they both to the end that upon their report of your conceit there we may enter into more particular consideration of all thing incident which upon their arrival we shall be better able to do give under our signet at our palace of west minster the eight day of February 1601. in the four and forty year of our reign the same day sir Oliver s. john bring from the lord in England this follow letter to the lord deputy after our hearty commendation to your good lordship we have have in most of our late dispatch so little cause to fill our paper with any thing but with commendation of your lordship wise proceed and congratulation for her majesty happy success under you as at this time if any other we intend not to mix this acknowledgement of our extraordinary contentment for your late victory against the spaniard with any other particular direction especial see the change you have make in that country by free the same from foreign power howsoever infest still with a intestine rebellion must in all man knowledge that be acquaint with the affair of state have bring so many change as we can hardly tell what advice or direction to offer of new until we may receive from thence some further light of the present state of that kingdom from you who own eye and judgement be near and able to perform the same in which consideration see it have please her majesty by her own letter not only to give you notice of her royal and gracious acceptation of your so noble endeavour but to direct your lordship also to send over hither sir Robert gardener and this gentleman sir Oliver s. john with relation of all particular fit for her knowledge we will in expectation hereof forbear to enlarge our letter any further then with our best wish to your lordship of all perfect health and happiness as those that will ever be find etc. the same eight day don jean and the remain of the spaniard at kinsale be all embark ready to be go the next morning the lord deputy leave cork and take his journey towards Dublyn arrive that night at Yoghall and because the stormy weather and contrary wind keep the spaniard still in the port at kinsale his lordship be force to stay in that town some few day from whence he write to master secretary into England upon the twelve of march advertise he thereof and further give he notice that the other spaniard which be at beere-haven castle-haven and baltimore now be go for Spain that don jean have send to cork the pledge promise in the eight article of the agreement that five english company be late arrive at Waterford and last pray to be excuse to the rest of the lord of her majesty counsel that he forbear to write unto they till he come to Waterford where within few day he hope to meet the earl of ormond and some other of the counsel and upon conference with they to be better able to satisfy their lordship in some thing concern the present state of this kingdom according to her majesty pleasure late signify to he by her letter the pledge above mention be to lie for the safe return of our ship wherein the spaniard be embark these pledge be principal commander and among they be one captain moryson of who bold service mention be make in the sally upon the second of December this gentleman be invite by the lord deputy to accompany he to Dublin the rest of his fellow still remain at cork whether he be to return unto they and they together to be ship for Spain upon the safe return of our ship in which journey to Dublyn and during this gentleman abode there I have familiar conference with he for name sake and understand from he that his family in Spain be descend of a english gentleman who follow the emperor Charles the five in his war and after by his bounty be seat in Spain where at this day the chief of his name have good revenue the lord deputy be come to Waterford do write together with the rest of the counsel upon the eighteen of march this follow letter to the lord in England it may please your lordship the eight hereof we receive by sir Oliver s. john at cork the dispatch which it please your lordship to make by he and may not omit with all humble thankfulness to acknowledge the great comfort and contentment we have take in that it appear both thereby and by the relation of sir Oliver that her majesty and your lordship have most gracious and favourable accept and allow our poor endeavour we be most careful as you have direct to send sir Robert gardener and he unto your lordship so soon as I the deputy can get to Dublyn where sir Robert gardener now be and shall have consider and debate with the counsel there the business wherein your lordship look to be thorough inform in the mean space because that will ask some time we have think fit to acquaint your lordship how thing stand here since our last dispatch the spaniard for certain be all go from beere-haven castle-haven and baltimore and that day that sir Oliver s. john do arrive at cork we hear that all the spaniard at kinsale and last of all don jean himself be ship and in readiness to set sail but since we hear that until Saturday the thirteen hereof they can not get forth the harbour and be that night beat back on Sunday they be ready to set out again since which time we have not hear from thence more than that they lie aboard in the mouth of the harbour and our man be possess of the town and we have observe the wind since that time to be good for they so as we be in good hope they be all go the pledge according to agreement be come to cork be three captain of long continuance so as we have cause to think don jean have deal sincere with we and be not out of hope to be no more trouble with any spaniard yet to be provide for the worst that may happen so long as the spanish cloud hang over we we have divide both the victual and great part of the munition into sundry harbour along the sea coast of this province the more ready to answer all occasion as may more full appear by the note we send herewithin if by this mean her majesty charge grow great as we can but acknowledge it will and the service north ward go on slowly than it will otherwise if we may apply ourselves whole that way we beseech your lordship favourable to consider the necessity that lead we thereunto least leave any place unprovided for the facility shall invite a come thither and in that regard we have give out a intention to fortify in all the several place of beerhaven castle-haven baltimore and the creek and passage along that coast. whereas these spaniard be go as now God be thank they be we have no meaning so to do in regard we have no answer from your lordship touch that point and thereby conceive that her majesty will not undergo so great a charge though we continue still of this opinion that it be the safe course to fortify in those place and if the spaniard shall come again without strong fort and citadel upon the chief town who our late experience show we apparent to be waver we can neither have safety for retreat if any diaster shall befall we nor commodity for victual and munition but that altogether will be in danger and the whole hazard or lose at a instant which point we hold ourselves bind in duty to provide for and therefore if her majesty do not like to make citadel in these town and city which we note to be fit in our former letter we hold it of very great necessity that the harbour of cork and kinsale be yet well fortify which we have already begin to do at kinsale and have view the mouth of the harbour of cork where by raise one good fort at the entry and another upon a island in that river the harbour will very apt be secure and all victual and munition for our use most commodious defend against all enemy which we hold a matter of very great importance and if these work shall be erect ten culverin and ten demy-culuering of iron mount upon unshod wheel for platform to be place in the fort to be make in those two harbour must forth with be send with bullet for they either to cork or to kinsale our whole store of victual be divide into sundry part of this province as by this note appear we be in doubt we shall want when we enter into a prosecution northward unless your lordship be please to continue that course for our supply that we confess you have most provident hitherto afford we for where some conceine and as it seem have inform that we may be furnish here with victual it be to we most strange and past all belief and to make it more apparent unto your lordship it may please you to consider that it be impossible to make a end of this war without waste and spoil of the country this as we must do still we have of long time very earnest labour and effect in as great a measure as we can possible devise and then how can it in reason be think that we can starve the rebel and yet preserve victual in the country for the soldier so as we must conclude that if we fail once of our victual out of England upon hope to have it find in this realm by any man understanding the army will either be starve or drive to break upon a sudden when it will not be in our power to help it and this we beseech your lordship to believe if we have make any use of our experience here yet if there be any possibility thereof when we have confer what may be provide out of the pale and quiet part of monster we will further certify your lordship and beside we do apparent foresee now that the apparel of the soldier be leave unto the captain which yet best content all party that if the exchange of the new coin be not royal keep up the soldier will be in worse case than before for all thing here be already grow so dear and scarce since the new coin go currant as clothes be both excessive dear here and in any quantity not to be have for money but must necessary be provide in England and bring hither which can be if the exchange fail never so little for than will the soldier be unclothe which rather than he will endure he will run away though he be sure to be hang and this we fear will be likewise a mean for the break of the army the decay by sickness and otherwise be already so great notwithstanding all that we can do and yet we have not be want in our providency as we most humble crave to have supply send from time to time till the rebellion be break which if no foreign force arrive we hope will be in short time not under captain but conductor for we find by experience that the captain that be send hither with their company conceive that they shall not stand long either by negligence or corruption lose their man so that when they be turn over to supply other scarce ten of a hundred can be have of they where at the first come over with the conductor we can better call they to a strict account and find the man to fill up other company by dispose they to such as we know will best preserve they so as they need not reinforce their company with the irish as they will when they can come by english by which mean the company we confess be full of irish which till our supply come can well be holpen and whereas I the deputy have ever be as my duty be most desirous to diminish her majesty list and to that end not only have take all occasion by the death of captain to extinguish their entertainment but also have mere discharge above five thousand since November 1600. now the captain and man thus discharge think their fortune overthrow by i have never consideration of the necessity impose upon i to do it but only look upon their own loss and as they esteem it disgrace they become so many enemy to i and many of they clamorous against i and my proceed and whereas by some of your lordship letter it please you to let i know that your send many captain proceed from my recommend of many unto you I do humble assure your l ps. that almost all which come over be stranger to i and if the rest have have letter from i I write they at their request only to testify that they have behave themselves no otherwise then honest here which be the least I can afford they when I be force to take away their company but if her majesty expect a abatement of she list I beseech your l ps. to consider my hard condition for if I discharge such as you send over I do not only become odious unto they but offend many of your lordship by who favour they obtain that charge and if I discharge such old captain as I find here and of who sufficiency I have since have continual experience by their often adventure their blood and life I shall not only return unto her majesty importunate suitor arm with good justice to crave reward but myself shall incur the same and more just dislike of they and their friend but that which for her majesty service grieve i most be that I shall thereby disinable myself hereafter to do her majesty that service which heretofore I have do and next unto God must attribute to their valour and sufficiency for touch the irish by who discharge I mean to make no small abatement I have heretofore labour by unsensible degree to diminish that charge and I will choose a fit time full to effect it the sudden do whereof might cause rather an increase then decrease of her majesty charge we have late recommend some of the incorporate town here to your lordship and may happy have occasion to do the like to draw they if it may be to a more affectionate furtherance of the service at the least to hold they with some contentment though indeed they have not afford we that help that they both might and aught yet our meaning be not thereby to press your lordship to any enlargement of their franchise for which happy they will thereupon be suitor for we confess true to your lordship that we think these corporate town in general have already too great and too many privilege and immunity unless they better know or will more ready endeavour to deserve they which we think meet at this time to give your lordship a taste of lest they may otherwise make that use of our letter that we intend not Further we desire that your lordship will persuade her majesty to resolve present to make citadel in the chief of these town without which we shall never bring they to perform their duty and so etc. don jean whether with or without authority give he from Spain I know not have often discourse with the lord deputy during their abode together at cork that it be no unlikely or difficult work to make peace between England and Spain yea he go so far as to urge the lord deputy to deal therein but his lordship only make answer that he know her majesty to be gracious incline to hold good amity with all christian prince yet as she be confident in she own power so she be in all thing jealous of her honour and especial in that point wherein her royal meaning have not be entertain with the like by the state of Spain whence we have receive such ill measure in all our late treaty to that purpose as all man be discourage to be any more make instrument therein whereupon don jean swear unto his lordship that as he leave the state of Spain affect upon his knowledge it be then a thing easy to effect and a thing much desire of they to have firm peace between England and Spain and he further add that if upon his arrival in Spain find thing to stand in the same condition he do at the return of our ship thence give his lordship any inkling thereof then upon his reputation his lordship deal with the state of England in that matter shall loose no honour thereby the lord deputy hitherto have do no more than answer don jean proposition in civil term wherein he have speak no more then any private man may lawful have do if he have licence to confer with he yet lest he may be think to have exceed his commission in this nice discourse and have good reason to imagine that as God many time do work by unlikely yea by contrary mean so he and don jean out of then commission to make war one upon the other may prove commissioner for make a peace his lordship advertise thus much to master secretary in England pray to have further warrant and instruction if it be think fit he shall further proceed therein but by don jean silence from Spain this overture pass as a dream and take no effect as long as the queen live the four and twenty day of march be the last day after the english writing of the year 1601 the lord deputy and counsel be at kilkenny and entertain by the earl of ormond in his house write this follow letter to the lord in England it may please your lordship have certain intelligence since our come to this place that don jean with all the rest of the spaniard depart from kinsale on Tuesday the 16 hereof and that the wind since that time have serve they so well as we assure ourselves by this they be near the coast of Spain we think fit hereby to give your lordship notice thereof that you may know we be free now of they all since our be here there have be bring in a notorious rebel one William Mac hubbard late take in Vpperossery who of late have do great spoil and murder in these part more than any other so as we have cause he to be execute in this town to the great terror of many about the same time that he be execute a son of garret Mac mortaghe name moris Mac garret die of a hurt late give he in fight who be a most dangerous young man like to trouble all the country the death of these two rebel as also of a notorious rebel by birth of monster late slay call Dermot Mac awlye who be a inward man and a great practise instrument with Tyrone will great quiet all these part and your lordship can hardly think what a great change we find already by their so happy and timely cut off and as for sir Fynneen o Dryscoll o and the two son of sir Owen Mac carty they and their follower since their come in be grow very odious to the rebel of those part and be so well divide in faction among themselves as they be failen to prey and kill one another which we conceive will much avail to the quiet of these part i the deputy be this day go towards Dublin from whence your l ps shall hear from i according to the direction give i by your lordship and I the precedent be return into monster to attend my charge there we have be much importune by the army in general touch a abatement of half a pound of beef upon every flesh day from every particular soldier and of two hear every fish fish day and the horse troop likewise find themselves grieve that the victualler charge they with two shilling six penny increase in the issue of every barrel of oat without any other warrant then a private letter from m. wade clerk of the counsel which although we conceive m. wade have signify over upon some such purpose of your lord p2 or other good ground yet inregard of the importunity of the captain and to prevent a general mutiny of the army in regard the soldier be weak and much enfeeble by the late siege of kinsale and that the prize of all thing be increase above all measure by reason of the new standard coin and that the country be general much harry and waste and thereby great scarcity and want grow here we hold it meet and according give direction to the commissary of the victual to issue oat as former at six shilling the barrel and allow the soldier two pound of beef and eight herring a dav according as it be former accustom till your lordship resolution be return in that behalf which we humble pray and expect and so have no other matter at this time worthy the present to your lordship we most humble take leave etc. the lord President have accompany the lord deputy to kilkenny do from thence return to his charge in the province of monster at kilkenny the lord deputy begin to feel himself sickly have former complain of some distemper a likely effect of his watch and cold take during the hard winter-siege at kinsale and his lordship sickness so grow upon he as the next day he be carry in a horselitter and so all the journey till he come to Dublin where he ariive the eight and twenty of march in the begin of the year 1602 and his distemper still continue apply himself to take physic I will conclude the act of the year pass with this follow abstract of her majesty charge in the realm of Ireland from the first of April 1601 to the nine and twenty of march 1602. the Totall of all charge as well in the establishment as by other warrant extraordinary two hundred eighty three thousand six hundred seventy three pound nineteen shilling eleven penny half farthing viz. in the new coin mix ready money two hundred fifteen thousand eight hundred fifty pound nineteen shilling four penny half penny in apparel for the soldier provide in England with silver money sixty seven thousand eight hundred twenty three pound six penny half penny half farthing checqued by the muster-master in money fifteen thousand one hundred forty nine pound six shilling in apparel twenty two thousand four hundred fifty seven pound six shilling two penny half penny so her majesty whole charge be in the year 1601 two hundred forty six thousand eighty seven pound seven shilling eight penny half penny half farthing beside the concordatum bill impress upon account here the levy and transport of force pay in England the payment of work and the charge of the office of the ordinance for powder bullet etc. the three book chap. i. of the prosecution of the war by the lord Mountioy lord deputy against the rebel in the year 1602. in the beginning of the year 1602 for the latter part of march and good part of April the lord deputy indisposition of body above mention do still continue and his lordship for a short time attend nothing but the recovery of his health only on the one and thirty of march he signify to the lord in England that from the sixteen of march when the spaniard set sail from kinsale the wind have continue so favourable as he nothing doubt but they be arrive in Spain and his lordship advertise the state of his weak health and pray to be excuse that he can not as yet consider with the counsel here about the dispatch of sir Robert Gardner and sir Oliver s. john with the relation of this kingdom present estate according to the direction he have former receive to that purpose which he be careful to do so soon as health will permit he add that in the mean time the force be so dispose as they may be most active in the prosecution of Tyrone and his break partaker and the lord deputy have intelligence that after the knowledge of his and don jean agreement the preparation of second in Spain be divert and so not fear any interruption by foreign force be bold to give the lord in England confidence of his future endeavour in his charge whereof he hope to give her majesty a good account God please to restore his health so as he may proceed with that speed and alacrity which he intend the three of April his lordship receive letter from the lord in England signify that they concur with he in grief that by contrary wind stay the spaniard transport his lordship be force to stay in monster and slack the opportunity of prosecute Tyrone at his first return and flight out of monster when he be in such plight as he can feign no hope of safety to himself lie in some fastness or other and often change his abode for fear of some attempt against his person set at a price and still have neither powder nor lead but make infinite mean into Scotland to be furnish therewith so as if the northern garrison be not weak for want of supply and if some force can have be spare to strengthen they in all probability they may have ruin Tyrone ere this that our ship transport the spaniard be well use in Spain and upon their arrival the ship prepare at the groin for Ireland be present unfurnished so as her majesty fleet and some ship of the low country lie this summer on the coast of Spain they conceive all spanish aid for Ireland will for the present be divert that for citadel to be build in the town and port her majesty in general allow thereof leave the choice of most fit place and the manner of building to his lordship as also to certify a estimate of the charge and the best mean to raise it otherwise then out of her majesty coffer that her majesty commend the discreet intercept of the spanish letter by which the king earnestness to follow that enterprise apply but no doubt by the english fleet prepare for that coast will be divert that no supply shall be expect out of England where the leavyes have be so burdensome as for the present it be fit to forbear they that in all grant to the submit rebel they require his lordship to have care that they be not so absolute as they shall not be in awe of the state or be able to tyrannize over their neighbour and particular that any treason of the pattentee shall forfeit all the grant that irish company shall not be employ near their own home especial in any great number neither shall have any pay for apparel there be no reason that their pay shall be equal to that of the English last to the end the submitty may not abuse her majesty mercy to their temporize end as they have often do by revolt into rebellion after submission and protection their lordship require that as every chief rebel be take in so they shall be disarm but this last point be not effect for this consideration that by that mean every chief lord upon submission shall leave his country without defence and open to be spoil both by neighbour rebel and thievish subject it be true that after all the war full end a general disarm have be requisite but the event will show how that be after neglect in the proper time when the first act be cast the english force which now be press when there be no possibility to effect it the and the abovesaid force of Connaght be one hundred twelve horse and one thousand six hundred fifty foot hereof be leave to guard Galloway and athlone foot two hundred leave in garrison at the abbey of boil one thousand foot and sixty two horse which serve to further our new plantation at Ballishannon for there a garrison be new plant and sir Henry Follyot be make governor thereof the rest of the horse and foot be lay at the annaly and may fit join with the garrison dispose southward and northward upon lemster upon all occasion of service as more especial they may concur in stop the rebel for pass either on the south or north-side into Lemster as likewise the garrison southward may answer one another and these northward answer one another upon all occasion of service Garrysons in the Totall of horse by the list 1487. foot by the list 16950. the force be thus dispose for the summer service and the lord deputy have recover his health his first care be to obey her majesty direction in dispatch for England sir Robert gardener and sir Oliver s. john with a relation of the present state of this kingdom by they beside instruction of the present state his lordship send this follow letter to the lord in England date the five of May 1602. may it please your lordship although you have good reason to guess at the difficulty of the war of Ireland both by the long continuance and the exceed charge thereof before my time under which the rebel strength do ever grow as by the slow progress though still to the better that it have make I must confess under my government yet since I do conceive that none but we that be personal actor therein especial in these time wherein the fashion and force of this people be so much alter from that it be wont to be can thorough apprehend with how many impediment cross and opposition we undertake and proceed in all thing I humble desire your lordship to give i leave for your satisfaction and the discharge of my duty to open unto you some of the cause which I do better feel than I can express that have hinder so speedy a conclusion of this war as her majesty out of her great providence and large proportion of expense may happy expect at my first arrival I find the rebel more in number then at any time they have be since the conquest and those so far from be naked people as before time that they be general better arm than we know better the use of their weapon then our man and even exceed we in that discipline which be fit for the advantage of the natural strength of the country for that they be very many and expert shot and excel in footmanship all other nation do by that mean make better use of those strength both for offence and defence then can have be make of any squadron of pike or artificial fortification of town in regard whereof I presume that man wit can hardly find out any other course to overcome they but by famine which be to be wrought by several garrison plant in fit place and alter upon good occasion these plantation can not be make but by army which must first settle they and after remove they as the strength of the enemy require the time for those plantation not only of most conveniency but almost of necessity be to be in the sum and that for many eminent reason but especial in that mean may be provide for horse to live in the winter without which those garrison will prove of little effect now I beseech your lordship to remember that I receive this charge the eight and twenty of February in the year 1599 at which time I find the rebel in number and arm as I have say grow to the very height of pride and confidence by a continue line of their success and our misfortune of the subject the worst assist they open and almost the best lean to their fortune out of a despair of we the army discourage in themselves and believe i my lord for you will hardly believe much contemn by the rebel none of our garrison have stir abroad but they return beat the enemy be so far master of the field that Tyrone have measure the whole length of Ireland and be come back unfought with and with i they begin the war at the very suburb of Dublin at that time the choice of the whole army and even of every company that be leave behind be draw into monster by the earl of ormond howbeit I be desirous to loose no time nor opportunity present gather together that poor remnant be the refuse of the rest with a purpose to have fight with the traitor in his return between fercale and the ennye but he hasten his journey upon some intelligence of my design and I be the long stay by the difference of the counsel opinion from my intent it fall out that I come too late to try that fair fortune with he the rest of the spring I be enforce to attend the draw of diverse captain and company from remote and divide garrison that be to be employ for Loughfoyle and Ballishannon for by your lordship appointment I be to send one thousand other soldier from these part and to cast three thousand more in consideration of so many send thither out of England and to reduce the list from sixteen thousand to fourteen thousand which at that time be a proportion too little to undertake the war with all I be further to victual the fort of leax and Ophalye in those time account great and dangerous service and about the five of May 1600 I draw towards the north chief to divert Tyrone and his northern force from give opposition to the plantation at Loughfoyle but withal purpose if I find mean for victual and carriage to have leave a garrison at armagh the first I do thorough effect for I give way to those of Loughfoyle to land and settle quiet draw Tyrone with his chief force upon myself and in all the fight I have with he make he know that his fortune begin to turn and break those bound of his circuit whence he be wont to affront our great army for in that which be last before this call a northern journey when the army consist almost of double number of horse and foot they be by the traitor force and arrest within the confine of the pale at my return I find by observation in my journey wherewith the whole counsel do concur in opinion that the garrison of Loughfoyle will do little hurt to Tyrone except there be force leave at or about armagh since they may easy fly out of their reach and shall enjoy between Dungannoa and the pale one of the large and most fertile country of Ireland we become suitor to your lordship in june 1600 that with more man and more provision you will enable we to that plantation and in the mean time I intend the service in Ofalie and Leax the strength of the rebellion in lemster and most dangerous rebel of Ireland and whereas the last time the army pass through Leax be one of the great that have be at any time together in this kingdom it be encounter and almost distress by the only native of that country it please God that in all our conflict which be many we so prevail against they as though all the rebel in Lemster be then gather together yet by kill owny mac rory with many of the best man of both country and by utter spoil they that be exceed rich in all mean for life they have never since be able to make head any otherwise then to live disperse in little number as woodkerne and daily be consume and be away and further in that year we recover all the earl of ormonds pledge upon the arrival of the supply send by your lordship although the time of the year be far spend and indeed over-farre to plant garrison to any great purpose for the next ensue winter and that at that instant we be ill provide both of victual and money yet we set forward and the fifteen day of September come to dundalke and encamp two mile from thence within half a mile of the entrance of the Moyry we find that pace by which we be to pass be natural one of the most difficult passage of Ireland fortify with good art and with admirable industry the enemy have raise from mountain to mountain from wood to wood and from bog to bog long traverse with huge and high flanker of great stone mingle with turf and stake on both side with pallade wattle and possess with one of the great army that ever they be able to make but that which be our main impediment be the extremity of the weather and great rain which make the river unpassable how be it in the end the water somewhat fall after we have endure more than credible hardness and give many and those violent assault on their trench we win our passage and immediate plant a garrison within eight small mile of armagh now call mount Nerreys for at armagh the grass be so consume that we can not have live there with our horse while the place shall have be make tenable and this other garrison be think ncessary to be possess though armagh itself have be plant and in the mean time of little less effect and so far do we stretch out our victual and improve our time and all other provision that have tyrones army continual within little more than musket shoot of we within two day we make this
