fort guardable and leave therein all our baggage that with all our mean for carriage and even with many of our own horse we may from the newry convey as much victual thither as may be which we perform in so short a time and yet in so good time that we be force to fast two day in our return this garrison can not be to such effect as it shall be because we have not victual enough to leave a compitent proportion for a sufficient number of man neither can there any horse be leave for want of mean for they nevertheless that winter there be great good service do by those of that fort command by captain Edward blaney a very worthy and painful gentleman I do not repeat the manner of our fight nor the number of they both before and after in our return wherein the rebel seldom escape without a blow and namely in the pace of Carlingsord where he receive a notable overthrow neither set I down any thing in this to amplify our own do or endeavour but to give your lordship a account how this season be lose from make such plantation as by take their effect in the winter shall in short time have break the heart of the rebellion and to let it appear unto your lordship by the many difficulty and opposition we find in only bend this way how unpossible it have be for we at the same time with the number we have to have plant in other place which have be as necessary as this to have make a sudden end of the war and among other consideration your lordship may be please to conceive that albeit the list of the force here in Ireland be united consider may appear to be sufficient great yet divide the same into his part as three thousand in monster three thousand at Loughfoyle one thousand for Knockefergus and almost two thousand in Connaght the remainder whereof I have be only able to prevail myself and wherewith I have sustain the burden of the war both in Lemster and the north can hardly bear such diminution as (@) army be subject unto in their deficient man with so many subdivision as I be necessary constrain to make for the guard of important place as with the earl of ormond in leaxe Ophalia and diverse other part to defend the subject from the incursion of rebel and yet leave i a competent army to try a fortune with all the rebel of the north which we must be prepare for see no such diversion can be expect from the foresay garrison as be able to hinder the light footed kerne have flee their creaght into their fastness from join their utmost strength from the (@) part of their associate in less than three day warning whereas we on the contrary in case of present use can hope for no manner of assistance from our disjoin troop within the compass of as many week and beside these impediment and many other I do neither then nor at any time since meet with any more hurtful to my proceed (@) the restraint of our extraordinary and the want of all such kind of necessary as your lordship be persuade be only fit for a more royal war whereas the substance of extraordinary grow chief by huge provision of carriage to convey victual and waste therein by large proportion of pioneer and other workman tool with diverse other sort of engine and engineer for fortification and passage over river and other place otherwise unpassable by material for the same reward for spial and other service I will bold affirm what I presume I can prove to your lordship that there be no war in the world that to be effectual follow do require a more liberal expense for provision of all these thing than this and of all other extraordinary save the charge of great artillery whereof also in some measure though in a far less than other place we have great use and of many perchance more forcible to present unto your lordship orie these two reason a army be no where arrest with so many river and unpassable marsh as here second where the war be to be make to best effect we find no mean of victual or any other necessary provision but what we bring with we to redeem the loss of this opportunity for plantation to so good effect as it shall have be I undertake with a army no great than a reasonable garrison to make the war of lemster in the depth of winter and first I fall into the glinne the fast country of Ireland and till now of all the part of lemster only untouched where I first spoil all the country and make donnel Spantgah who before I have receive to her majesty mercy to join with i therein and after force Phelim Mac Feogh and all the tool the most pestilent infestor of the pale to submission who have since show more appearance of good subject then ever I know or hear of any of these rebel after go up and down as far as athlone I fall into fercale force Tyrrill out of a exceed great strength and banish he and in effect all the Oconnors out of Ophaly into the north. return towards the north I spoil all the Ferny with a journey where I be present and wherein beside many other be kill two of ever Mac cooley son I waste the fuse by sir Richard moryson plant a garrison above twenty mile from the pale in the Brenny by sir Oliver Lambert and return to drogheda by the general advice of the counsel I take in Turlogh Henry lord of the fuse and ever mac cooly farmer of the Ferny sir Ohy Ohanlon a northern lord and many of the macmahown and orelly who all beside their great oath give we such as be think their best pledge for their loyalty and to loose no part of this beginning year 1601 have settle the new submitty of lemster and the border of the north with as great assurance as I can I draw again into the north before the general host for that year can be in readiness and clear and assure the passage of the Moyry by cut down most part of the wood and build a fort there then I go into lecayle whole possess by Mac Gennis and take in all the castle in those part from thence I go to armagh and there place a garrison and albeit at this time the continual rumour we hear of preparation in Spain make we proceed somewhat more irresolute in our main course of plantation and make the war in Tyrone itself yet we go forward with a intent to draw sir Arthur chichester by Loughsidney into Tyrone to plant a garrison at the blackwater to force a passage somewhat beneath it to meet he and by build a fort and bridge upon the passage to have make Dungannon itself the centre whether without any great difficulty the garrison of Loughfoyle armagh Knockfergus mount Norreys and ell other of the north may at all time meet together to beat and absolute to banish the arch-traitor out of his own country and in the perform thereof to have spoil all the rebel corn save such as shall be within the command of those garrison who with the countenance of the army in the harvest time we resolve to enable to make large provision thereof for themselves and their horse and so far have we proceed in this course that we have force Tyrone from the blackwater where he lie with his army and have fortify and enreach there with great art we have clear the passage intend to Dungannon the make of the bridge only except which we mean to supply with a float and spoil most part of their stand corne. about which time the assure news be come unto we of the arrive of the spaniard which first stay and after clean divert our course we be drive by their come to bend our counsel to defend her majesty kingdom from foreign invasion that before be busy to recover it from inward rebellion and to that end to break off our work and to leave the further prosecution of that business the place already possess only preserve by this continue time wherein the army from the first be lead on in action endure all season and more fight then I think ever army do in so short time your lordship must not wonder if to make head against the spaniard we draw up weak company for beside deficient by sickness and death there be many company that have thirty and forty hurt man in they yet upon any uncertainty how probable soever I be loath to loose to her majesty the chief benefit of one whole year service till I be full assure of their land and therefore first draw myself only with a few horse into monster and never send for the force till the very last pinch of necessity and thus have your lordship also the reason how this second year be lose without lay the new foundation for root out of the rebel though God be thank it be win in defend her kingdom from a powerful and ambitious inuader to his dishonour and I hope also to the more sound and sudden subversion of the rebel I will speak nothing of the service at kinsale since to my great comfort I do find her majesty and your lordship so well satisfy therein but so behooveful for the public good I conceive it to make a clean riddance of they out of this country and as much as I may to assure in they the performance of their departure that it be necessary to keep the army in those part until we be quit of they and to give they the less advantage if they have purpose false I present convey the cannon into a island that do absolute command the haven of kinsale with a sufficient guard and begin a fortification there at that instant to maintain it I take order they shall have no more victual sell unto they than I presume will but serve they from day to day and for their provision of bread in effect they spend on their own store so that I can have be able at any time to invest they again on as ill or worse condition than I leave they but before the wind and other provision serve for their departure he winter be so far spend that we can not in the fit time return the company to their garrison nor otherwise can we have do it because the place be not store with victual nor any provision for horse and yet those little remnant that be leave to defend those place do many excellent and now I hope your lordship shall daily hear of more the whole force be return what course we have think on for this next summer I will not trouble your lordship with the repetition thereof be set down and deliver to sir Oliver s. john only this I beseech your lordship to give i leave to remember you of out of a public durie how much soever it may seem to taste of my private end that you continual enjoin i and I as much endeavour to decrease the list yet you still send over new captain and command i to bestow company on such as give they up in England to other recommend by they unto who to deal plain most of they do sell they and even of late I have receive your lordship letter for the increase of some particular man company when I cast the captain which your lordship send over I procure their hate and many of your lordship displeasure beside their own friend that favour they if I do not increase such as you commend I do incur the like if I cast those company and captain that in so many trial I do know to be best able to do her majesty service here I shall despair or at least be diffident hereafter of do any good and yet have they most reason to condemn i of injustice and to importune your lordship to be otherwise relieve that have spend most of they their moud and all of they their continual labour even in i aye for the recovery and defend of this kingdom I humble desire your lordship since heretofore it be my fortune to be hate of few that you will preserve i from become odious by do that which be fit for the service for I have already taste of their spleen who God know against my will I have be force to cashier though I have deal more favourable with some of they who be loath to harm I have rather commend when my only fault be that I do not punish they and since I hope God will so bless our work that ere it be long we shall much diminish the number of our labourer if in that great casher there be not mean to preserve the best captain I will be loath to be the man that shall undertake the conclusion of the war and now I do humble desire your lordship to pardon i if out of my great care to satisfy you in all thing I have trouble you with so long and I fear i so unworthy a letter of your lordship read etc. instruction be give to sir Robert Gardiner and sir Oliner saint john whereby they may satisfy her majesty in all point touch the present state of her affair in this kingdom the lord deputy with some commander diverse voluntary gentleman and his servant attend he ride to dundalke and while he there attend the come up of the force and the arrive of victual with other necessary that may enable he to take the field his lordship on the thirty of May receive from her majesty this letter follow Elizabeth right trusty and well-beloved we greet you well whereas the payment of our army in that kingdom have be of late year make partly in money by certain weekly lend and partly in apparel which course of payment be institute upon good consideration to prevent the fraud which diverse captain of evil disposition do exercise upon their company notwithstanding we have (@) by your letter and by the report of some person who have have credence from you to deliver the same to our counsel here that such manner of payment have not wrought that effect which be expect in cause our company to be keep full and yet be by reason of the late alteration of the standard of our money there more chargeable to we than the payment in ready money will be we have therefore think good to cease that manner of payment from henceforth and to revive the old manner of payment in money after the rate of eight penny by the day of the new standard to each soldier which course our pleasure be shall begin to take place from the first day of this month of April and to be continue by your warrant to our treasurer direct and shall be make from time to time by way of imprest to each captain for himself and his company at your disaretion according to the state of their company or to the necessity of our service until the day of full pay which we be please shall be make twice in every year viz. at the feast of saint Michael the archangel and the annunciation of the virgin Marie at which time our pleasure be that all our army shall be full and clear pay of their whole wage all defalcation due upon they be former deduct and for that purpose we will provide that against that time there shall be in our treasurer hand money sufficient to make full paid and whereas by your latter letter write since our council signify unto you that we be please to restore this kind of pay you do require that for the establish thereof with contentment of our army two thing may be (@) observe the one that our treasurer may have money in his hand sufficient from time to time for performance of this payment the other that the exchange be due maintain on this side without which you allege that there will arise inconueniency intolerable to the army we be please for your satisfaction herein to assure you that in both these I oint we will take such order that neither our treasurer there shall want money of the new standard for payment necessary in that realm nor the bank here sterling money to make good the exchange according as it be establish by our proclamation although in this point we can omit to let you know that we see no cause or such complaint as your letter do import of default in the exchange for that upon examination we do find that of three or four and fifty thousand pound return in this last year there be not unpay at this present above six thousand pound which consider our excessive charge in that year ought not to give to any much cause of offence these two point like as we be please to observe in such manner as we have write to the end that thereby our army and subject may perceive how great our care be that they shall receive contentment in thing due unto they so on the otherside for that a straight observation of the same on our part without a good correspondency of you and they to remedy some inconueniency which thereby may be cast upon we may prove very burden some to we we be to admonish you of the observation of two other point necessary on your part and they to be observe the first be that whereas heretofore when this manner of payment in money only which now be receive be in use through the corrupt disposition of some captain and for want of good discipline in our force great fraud be commit as well to we in not keep the full number by we allow as also to the soldier in detain their wage or part thereof from they which deceit without good caution now to be use may be again renew we do therefore expect that you shall establish so good a course of discipline for the order of our band in this point as that we shall not be hereafter abuse in decay of our number as heretofore we have be which you shall never so well prevent nor alien man mind from like fraud as by instict notorious and exemplary punishment upon captain and officer when their fault in this kind shall appear to be notorious not only by cast they out of our pay but by degrade and other note of ignominy which in military discipline be use to be just do to man who by their shameless action do not only bring shame to their profession but to the public service notable impediment and in a manner a evident treachery and as this first change of payment in apparel to be pay in money have his first motion from you our deputy and the principal captain and officer of the army in which you now note peril if the exchange be not maintain as it be true that that must be support by we and shall be so we know none must prevent the captain take of pay for their soldier apparel and not bestow it but yourself of who care and judgement we have great reason to assure ourselves both for your love to our service and your own honor. the second point which we recommend unto you be the due execution of our former proclamation touch this matter of the exchange and the assistance of the master of our exchange and his minister therein to the end that all fraud discover of late to have be use by merchant who abuse our princely intention therein for their private gain may be remedy and therein chief that the use of all money descry may be take away from the people of that country and withal sterling money may be bring into our exchange upon such condition as our proclamation contain for that we do find that our intent in the erection of this new coin can no way so soon take place as by withdraw all other money from they whereby the rebel may exercise traffic with foreign nation and by they be relieve wherefore you may add to the remedy in our say proclamation mention any other good mean that in your judgement shall be think meet to be be use and publish the same by proclamation in our name or advertise we of your conceit to the end you may have warrant from we to do that which we shall think meet to be do therein further we have think good to admonish you that forasmuch as the winter already deliver to the soldier will not be run out until the fourteen day of May inclusive and that it be likely see you know already that we purpose to take away the delivery of apparel you have furnish the company in Lieu thereof with some money by way of imprest whereby it may fall out that we shall be double charge therefore you our deputy shall take order with our treasurer that upon the half year full pay to be end at Michaelmas next defalcation be make of so much as any company shall have receive between the first of April and the fifteen of May above their weekly lend if so much shall be then over pay to any company give under our signet at our manor of Greenwich the eight and twenty of April in the four and forty year of our reign in the begin of june the lord deputy have gather the force together the lord deputy enter into Tyrone take the field and march up to blackewater to the passage which he have the last year discover to be most convenient to carry her majesty force that way into the heart of Tyrone at this passage lie some five mile eastward from the fort of blackewater his lordship encamp on the south side of the river have a small pace or skirt of wood between he and the river of which pace he have the year before cut down many tree so as at this time the passage be soon clear hence his lordship send sir Richard moryson with his regiment to possess the north side of the river for secure of the army passage against any attempt of the rebel thus the queen force be enter into Tyrone there encamp and his lordship spend some time in cause a bridge to be build over the river and a fort adjoin to guard the passage which of his own christian name be call Charlemount and leave captain Toby Cawfield with his company be one hundred and fifty to command the same from the camp the country be plain and open to Dungannon be distant some six mile and while these work be in hand we may see the town of Dungannon and tyrones chief house there seat to be set on fire whereby it be apparent that Tyrone with his force mean to fly and quit those part so as the lord deputy send Sr Richard moryson with his regiment to possess Dungannon whether his lordship soon after march with the rest of the force by this time sir Henry Dockwra governor of the force about Loughfoyle have plant many garrisous in those part have late plant a garrison at omy be some twelve mile distant from Dungannon whence he come with his force and meet the lord deputy at Dungannon thus the lord deputy have drive the archtraitor out of his own country as high as the castle Row upon the Ban send out some party to spoil and prey the country as far as Eniskillin upon lough erne then he take some of tyrones strong land namely one wherein he have a strong fort where we recover three piece of her majesty artillery and another island call Magherlowni which next Dungannon be the chief place of his abode and magazine for his war from Dungannon the lord deputy send sir Richard moryson with five hundred foot to meet sir Arthur chichester who come with his force from Carickfergus and be to pass Loughsidney and land within few mile of Dungannon where they be meet do according to the lord deputy direction begin to raise a fort. in the mean time the lord deputy have utter banish all partaker out of those part march five mile from Dungannon to Loughsidney where sir Arthur chichester lay with his force and his lordship encamp there till he have make the fort defensible to contain above one thousand foot and one hundred horse which be to be victual from Carickfergus by the way of the say lough this fort of his lordship barony he call Mountioy and make sir Bentamin Berry his lieutenant and now one of the colonel of the army governor of the same for the present service which be do the command of the fort be leave to captain Francis roe likewise for the present service sir Arthur chichester command in chief the force to be leave there which he may draw out upon all occasion of service as out of all other garrison in those part towards Carickfergus while his lordship encamp here upon Loughsidney he receive the eight of july letter from her majesty signify by her own hand that she be glad of his recovery of that sickness which do surprise he after his many care and labour both of body and mind in the siege of kinsale commend much his providence that notwithstanding his own state of body he do set on foot such preparation for the summer prosecution as nothing shall be want when the time shall serve that how soever her sensible feel of her subject burden cause she daily to call upon he and all other minister for the speedy and careful ease thereof yet he shall wrong both she and himself in believe that thereby any error be impute to himself who endeavour in that kingdom have much improve her opinion of he and shall rather conceive that thereby she will give he more occasion to call all other to a severe account who in place under he neglect her service and for private gain seek to prolong the war all other judgement of her valuation of his service make he guilty of his own grief and be far from her disposition towards he that since this summer he mean to lay the axe to the root of the tree by prosecute the arch-traitor who have nothing to bear he up but false rumour of spanlsh aid this summer if ever any be the time to end the war since by supply send to the state she have stop the currant of the spaniard progress in the siege of Ostend and have also set a chargeable fleet to sea to attend upon the coast of Spain and prevent the arrival of any his force in Ireland that the reduce the arch-traitor by her sword be the only agreeable satisfaction she can receive for the mischief fall upon her love subject by his iniquity she conceive the most ready mean for effect the same be to draw from he the chief captain of country to which purpose her pleasure be that the lord deputy shall receive to her mercy such of they as true and humby seek it wherein without prescribe he any particular course who best know all circumstance only she give this caution to provide against former mischief that whereas common the rebel fear to be spoil be wont to contract underhand with the arch-traitor to submit themselves thereby for the present to save their country and to give succour to the rebel creaght under hand and after the return of the army to revolt again now he shall consider the inward motive of their crave mercy and where he can not ruin they without spend more time and charge than the main action will permit there to deal with they in a more easy manner otherwise to give more sharp imposition in the condition of their submission and by waste their good to make their obedience more durable that she judge one condition necessary not to pardon any but upon service do not only upon those who particular they hate but upon any other as they shall be direct that as a argument of her confidence in he she give he power of war and peace only one thing she profess to see no cause to leave unexempt namely the pardon of the arch traitor a monster of ingratitude to she and the root of misery to her people think all other mercy then the proscription of he to all manner of prosecution mere incompatible with her justice and therefore command not to receive he upon any condition but upon simple submission to mercy for all thing life only except and to make this her pleasure know to all his complice persuade by he that he may be pardon at his pleasure and so fear to leave he lest after they shall be leave to his superiority and revenge concern fortification against foreign invasion her majesty give allowance to repair the fort at Waterferd and to build fort in the harbour of cork and Kinstle and to build a fort at Galloway and at carlingford but this last be not effect his lordship less fear the descent of foreign force within saint George channel and further to build such small fortification as he the lord deputy shall think mere aswell for the present plant of garrison in Tyrone as otherwhere employ therein captain bodley or captain Hansard be with the army or Paul Yuye be in monster or any who his lordship know fit to oversee and contrive these work concern Neale garue who hold part of odonnel country as yet by a her majesty give warrant to pass the same to he by letter patent yet in regard of his tickle disposition to make such restriction therein as he the lord deputy and the counsel here shall think meet and to pretend the same to be do by her majesty special direction the lord of Deluin upon succour give to the rebel and conference have with Tyrone at his come out of the north into monster in the doubtful time of the siege of kinsale have since be imprison in the castle of Dublin and now her majesty pleasure be that he shall be call to his trial before some of the counsel though it come to no effect he die in prison before the time prefix for his calling to answer there be matter enough to charge he with underhand favour the rebel howsoever peradventure there will be find no plain matter to question he for his life wherein her majesty profess no prince on earth less allow any proceed where the proof be not more clear than day light and so her majesty howsoever be dispose to forbear severity yet resolve to use correction of so ill a instrument the same eight day of july the lord deputy receive letter from the lord in England wherein after congratulate his lordship recovery as one to who both in respect of her majesty service wherein almighty God have extraordinary bless he and for their own particular affection they wish both health and honour their lordship at large signify that the ground of the summer service be so well lay as no man can disallow they that supply of man be send and those without captain that for the victual require her majesty think it a unsupportable charge to provide one year victual for fourteen thoulsand man yet they have so reconcile the demand and the provision as the demand be for one year and the whole number by pole the provision be answerable to the number but not for the whole time experience teach that the defect in the number will supply the abridgement in the time beside the help of many garrison take half victual and half money and that beef may be buy in every country with mix money whereas the victual in England be provide with sterling money that concern fortification he know her majesty pleasure pray he to commit that work to such discreet and honest person as her majesty may neither be put to unnecessary charge nor deceive in the disbursement that more than forty thousand pound have be pay to merchant in London for bill of exchange grant by the treasurer to irish merchant for satisfy their debt due in London before the alteration of the standard which absurd course he can never have hold if he have give no merchant any bill of exchange for any more money than he can bring good proof to have disburse or to owe for merchandise bring into the kingdom that whereas upon his lordship motion and the captain good like her majesty have be please to commit the apparel of the soldier to they against which resolution now many reason be produce their lordship find further impediment in that three hundred pound impress to each captain before hand be require and their sufficiency for the great part to answer such a sum be so doubt as it be think her majesty money shall run a hazard whereas former the contractor give her majesty time and beside put in good suretic to answer all defect beside that it be fear the captain will either let the soldier go naked or fill their company with irish who will require no apparel require to know his lordship judgement whether the old course be fit to be continue then this last that they conceive the king of Spain have not full abandon his purpose for Ireland yet be advertise that her majesty fleetly upon that coast make he for the present rather apply his counsel to stand upon defence esteem it dangerous to put to sea while her majesty ship be so ready to attend he so as they hope his lordship shall not be interrupt by any foreign power although no certainty can be give of such action of prince who have many design and for they many preparation may change mind at their pleasure in the same camp and the same eight of july the lord deputy receive the follow letter from master secretary Cecyll between who a firm combination of love or at least so firm as to such great person be incident have long be practise and now within few month have be final confirm my lord if I be now to begin the frame of our friendship I shall be curious to fasten it with all the ten nail that belong to architecture because in the begin man be curious to observe but I that know how straight the knot be tie of our affection by the mutual office of love and confidence do not tie myself to these compliment which be hold of great consequence but in vulgar and light friendship let that argument serve therefore for excuse of my long silence because I judge you by my own affection to speak of the subject of the general dispatch in this my private letter be but impertinent see I have join in the same and yet see I write there as a counsellor and here as a friend that have bind himself by election and not by compulsion I will tell you my opinion sincere of those thing which be most material in the same first my lord I do assure you that it be not in the compass of my judgement how to maintain that army at that height it be at long than the time of prosecution without extreme prejudice of this estate whereof though you be not the efficient cause or yet I must confess I have rather that lot may light upon any other then upon you because I will be loath your return shall not succeed a diminution thereof whereby you may receive the thanks for that effect by which this state feel victory more than by any other consequence whatsoever for this purpose I must confess I have endeavour to prepare her majesty mind to give you the power of compound with rebel both because you draw the sword which will best cut out the condition of submission and because for a while they shall rather hope for then feel any foreign succour for the traitor himself what you have you see and therefore I know that must be your warrant yet will I private say this unto you that if her majesty have not the prejudice in her own thought that he will insult when it come to the upshot and so her open herself in offer of a pardon will return unto she a double scorn I be confident persuade that when you have make trial and shall make it appear that there be no other impediment than her majesty acceptation you shall receive sufficient warrant for conclusion in the mean time lest you shall say you be put to do that which be tender to handle because my word can be no warrant or for that which be impossible to effect which be that he will trust his life in your hand by a personal submission first you have warrant to try it for receive he upon condition of his life so as therein you may fashion your own course as you list second for the point of his not dare to trust the state for his personal come in all other thing but that may be digest and that doubt send over hither whereby her majesty shall yet have the honour of refusal if God do so dispose her heart and not he which her majesty seek to avoid for the preparation in Spain I can say no more than I have do in the joint dispatch only the continuance of her majesty fleet upon the coast and this break out of byrones conspiracy in which Spain have give the french king occasion of offence may hinder any present invalion and so give you more time then when don jean arrive it be resolve and thus have I now of the public affair deliver you as much as I know to be worthy of advertisement my lord be somewhat trouble with a pain in my eye I presume to write the ordinary matter of my letter in a borrow hand reserve that which be of more privateness to my own self to assure you of the queen acceptation of your service and of the abolition of her former exception I vow before God that my heart do give i that warrant out of my poor judgement of her dispusition more than upon sudden speech sometime when the first apparition of new charge and likelihood to continue do present itself that I may say when I can by any occasion I bring sir Oliver saint john to her majesty because he may see how her majesty affection move to who report of her language now to he I do refer i but to come to the point my lord this I say the way to do your lordship good be to increase your merit and that can be do without increase your mean for the supply of mean therefore to prosecute I labour as far as I have credit to procure you they for the way to make a end by peace I think no man seek more than myself to enable you by persuade her majesty to give you that power wherein when lack of argument happen to work her majesty mind which in her princely indignation against that arch traitor be full of obstruction I do fall to the bind argument which of all thing most conclude and that be this that in short time the sword can end the war and long time the state of England can not well endure it what in the first be grant I leave to the thing themselves but for the second kind I confess that in case you have nothing that can produce good effect except there be more mortar send you to fasten the foundation which be yet to be lay upon dry stone for although I know that by this warrant you may safe give care and can cause he to be deal with yet that which you can do for he by this way will be by he contemn in which respect as I know your lordship have wisdom enough to conceal the latitude of your commission so believe i out of my judgement that if the queen may once perceive that it be only in she that he come not to reasonable condition and if she be sure that she shall not be scorn by offer that which he will not accept than such be her princely judgement and such be the mind of we all that be swear to give her majesty true counsel as I doubt not but by our humble importunity upon your advertisement what you find will be accept her majesty will be ready induce to do that which be so much for her majesty service be a matter which if my prayer to God can have otherwise bring to paste in her mind at this time I know full well how much it have be more advantageous then to have it send after occasion and fit opportunity and so much my lord for my comment upon the text of her majesty own letter for your return therefore I remain as I be that it can be of no other consequence to her majesty then for the great good of her service and to yourself infinite comfort and honour of which it be superfluous to speak till time give i more light and give you more opportunity in this present action to send i more ground then will I not fail to speak like a honest man that will neither halt nor practice with you in confidence whereof worthy lord believe i that I can be to any subject live I will be to you in which I must confess I be more fix because I see your moderation which all that follow you there have not and therefore in all great thing believe i by myself as I will do you for if you have not the faculty of distinction of every one humour that together with their general and honest affection to you have also as most flesh and blood have their own private end in which they be not ever so judicious as to consider whether all that be good for they be good for you I shall be often jealous that they will show i to you in many colour of openness or privacy as their appetite serve more or less of our french news I have entreat sir Oliver saint John to be my referendary be of opinion that Byrone and Awergne be both execute by this time though I know it not their practice doubtless be to have join with Spain by Sanoy for some greatness not fit for subject but whether so far as to execute any thing against the king person as it be divulge I can confident speak as yet because i think his own creature can not be such a monster in that kind for Spain I must still say that I conclude they will assail Ireland again and that they will have be there ere this time but for her majesty fleet which she have now return to tarry out till October to say where they will land be a strange speculation but I do assure you for my own part that I can be divert from my opinion that they will again come into monster I send you not the news of sir Richard levison take of the carrack at Lisbon because it come but to day by France but sure I hope it be true and the fleming have sure take one God send you all happiness and long life to do her majesty service of who I ask no more requital then that if you outlive i you remember that will Cecyll be son to a father and mother that love you the chief cause why the queen be so peremptory towards the traitor be that she have hear that after Blount go to he he brag that he be woo now I know not how true that be but sir Robert Gardner seem to think that the traitor in that point be belie sir Oliver s. John be very discreet and worthy your extraordinary favour he know well what be fit for every man your affectionate friend to do you service ro. Cecyll after the build of Mountioy fort the lord deputy purpose to follow Tyrone through his great fastness till he shall utter ruin he or drive he that way to the sea but by reason this degree of prosecution require long time and already the victual be waste which his lordship can with any conveniency bring with he and because the further the traitor shall be follow his lordship shall be the further from any mean to relieve his force he be enforce to alter his resolution and embrace the follow course of itself as good as the former first he dismiss sir Henry Dockwra to gather and provide for some good number of man at the omy and to prepare within twenty day to draw as far as dungeven in Ocanes country sufficient enable with mean to prosecute and fall upon Tyrone that way second his Lp give order to sir Arthur chichester to do the like by tomb and last he himself purpose to return within 20 day limit to make the war upon tirone by the way of Killetro lie next upon the border of tyrones country in the mean time on all side they put up as much victual as they can to such place as be most fit for the subsist of these several force during this prosecution he Lp be confident that the garrison of the omy under sir Henry Dockwra and the garrison of Mountioy upon Loughsidney where be leave 850 foot and 100 horse under sir Arthur chichesters command will restrain tirone from the plain into the fastness where now he be for the twenty day above limit in the mean time his lordship with the army intend to lie in such place as without great convoy he may put up victual for this purpose mean to employ the time in assure or waste all the country betwixt blackwater and the pale and with this purpose he march back towards monaghan and in the way take some land and strong place though in those and all the former service we have not lose five man of the army yet we have the disaster by a casual shot out of one of the land to lose sir John Barkley a worthy gentleman and sergeant Maior of the army and in another slight skirmish to lose capt. willis upon the death of sir John Barkley his Lp make sir Henry Davers sergeant mayor in his place and come into monaghan his Lp on the 19 of july advertise the lord in England of the former service as also that he have direct sir Henry Dockwra and sir Arthur chichester that in case any new spanish force shall land in Ireland they shall draw unto he with their principal force yet leave the garrison defensible as bridle to the submit late rebel and a diversion to the rest remain in rebellion that howsoever the number of those garrison seem to threaten the continuance of her majesty charge yet it be the most sure way to lessen the army and end the war in short time which only foreign invasion can hinder in which case it will be no long the war of Ireland but the war of England in Ireland and will require as royal supply as if a part of England be invade by so mighty a prince that the garrison upon tirone be leave so strong in number as that every of they a part may without apparent hazard not only withstand all the force wherewith Tyrone be able in any one place to make head against they but be stir with some party to seek out he and his creaght in their fastness and to keep they from feed or stir upon the plain which must necessary undo the rebel and this effect of the summer service will appear in the next winter for if in the mean time it be not his lordship hap according to his earnest endeavour to get tyrones head which be a work of difficulty not to be hope in so short a time yet he be confident to cut off so many of his member as he shall not be able to continue in any one place but shall be force to fly from bush to bush like a wood-kerne as now he do so long as the army keep the field which be dissolve he will soon grow to strength again except the garrison be keep strong so as he may not dare to appear himself or to feed his creaght upon the plain which can not be perform without have great store of victual to maintain the garrison that for the matter of fortification whereas their lordship note the sum demand to be excessive this demand proceed from a zeal to secure the kingdom speedy and by enter into a royal charge at once to cut off her majesty continue charge which be now above three hundred thousand pound yearly it seem good husbandry if by bestow one hundred thousand pound at once especial in the new mix coin her majesty may both secure the kingdom against foreign invasion and so bridle the town and country as half the say yearly charge may be present save and yet the army may be draw strong into the field then now it can be for it will be less charge to her majesty to keep twenty man in a castle cost five hundred pound the building then to keep one hundred man in a fort build for one hundred pound yet that he will conform himself to her majesty pleasure in that point employ the money allow to the best he possible can that whereas he the deputy have move that the captain may provide clothes for their company now upon better consideration he think the old course of clothe they by the merchant be of necessity to be continue that touch Neale garue his lordship find he to be of nature fiery and violent and with all extreme both proud and covetous and as sir Henry Dockwra have very well describe he to their lordship to be in his desire and demand most unreasonable and almost intolerable so as he that must contain he within any fit bound especial when he shall be deny any thing that he affect be enjoin to do any thing that he do not like must be of a infinite patience for at such time he use to break out in a fashion most hardly to be endure although his lordship profess that he take it rather to be want of breed and of knowledge to discern when he have good usage and when hard for through that defect he still think himself wrong and out of that conceit grow to that distemper than any want of good affection to the State for sir Henry Dockwra do acknowledge that upon all occasion of service that have not appear evident to prejudice he in he particular he show himself forward and very ready to the hazard of his own person very often in which respect his lordship hold he worth the cherish be beside well follow by all his country so as her majesty service receive very great furtherance both by he and they be therefore resolve according to her majesty warrant late receive to pass he the grant of his country his lordship further write that he receive many advertisement that the spaniard be ready to return into Ireland which though he for his part believe not in regard their lordship think the contrary yet the people here by many letter from their friend in Spain be make confident that they will make a new and strong invasion before Michaelmas day at the further that in this regard he make haste to draw the war to some good end no way so well to be do as by plant strong garrison upon tirone and by draw from he his strong partaker to which purpose he have send sir Henry Folliot to govern the garrison late plant at Ballishannon who have instruction to receive Mac guyre to mercy upon condition he will be content to have his country divide between o connor roe and himself and will deliver to her majesty use the castle of Eniskillin with the ordinance therein he Lp further signify that when the garrison of Ballishannon have effect the intend service he will leave it as a ward this same and all other fortification be so make as one small fort of very good strength be first frame guardable by a few to which be add a great fort of less strength and charge like to a bawne or yard wherein many upon occasion may be lodge so as if at any time one company be find sufficient the rest be draw away the loss of the big fort be command by the lesser will be of little moment and yet may be keep fit to receive great number if it be think fit to send they again at any time add that if the queen will be please to build a little castle in every one of the lesser fort it will great lessen her majesty charge in the number of man and yet be sufficient perpetual to bridle the irish. the nine and twenty of july the lord deputy be in monaghan receive letter from sir George Carew lord precedent of monster by the hand of sir Samuel Bagnol who the lord deputy have send into monster to bring from thence fifteen hundred foot which according he have perform these letter advertise certain expectation of the spaniard present invade monster with great force able to keep the field without any support from the irish rebel which expectation be ground upon the confession of many come out of Spain and by diverse letter send from thence by the irish but especial be confirm by the arrival of a spanish ship at Ardea bring a good proportion of munition to oswillyvan bear captain Tyrrell and other rebel in monster together with a good sum of money to be distribute among they for their encouragement to hold out in rebellion till the spanish succour shall arrive and the lord precedent signify his fear of a general defection upon the spaniard first arrival which he gather from the confidence of all the rebel in that province who have before seek for mercy in all humbleness and with promise to merit it by service now since the spanish ship arrive be grow proud call the king of Spain their king and their cease from rebellion to be the betray of their king and of the catholic cause yea sell nothing from this insolency though they have be some time beat by he many of their chief man kill and have lose the strong castle of Dunboy and the twenty of july the lord precedent advertise new intelligence of spanish force in great number lie ready at the groin either to be send for Ireland or the low country whereof 2000 be horse there be no probability that they shall be send by sea for the low country since they may more convenient be raise in these part wherefore he resolute believe they be intend for Ireland desire (@) for speed of intelligence a run post may again be establish between cork and Dublin the lord deputy by this time have plant a garrison in monaghan wherein he leave for the present sir Christopher s. Laurence with his 25 horse and 150 foot and under he captain esmond with his foot one hundred fifty this garrison lie fit to secure the pale from northern incursion and to prosecute those rebel which be like to stand out long this do his lordship take burn and spoil all the land in those part of great strength place ward in some of they and find Mac mahow chief of monaghan to stand upod proud term though otherwise make suit to be receive to mercy his lordship spoil and ransack all that country and by example thereof bring many chief of adjoin country to submit to mercy with as good show of duty and obedience as can be desire and more strict oath and pledge than have former be require so as now from the Bann to the dartcy include all Tyrone and from thence to Dublin the whole country be clear and the chief lord more assure than they be ever before his lordship place connor roe Mac guyre to who her majesty have late give the Chiefery of Fermannagh in the principal house of Mac mahow chief of monaghan lie within two mile of Fermannagh so as he may from thence easy plant and settle himself in his own country and so be able to do her majesty many good service in those part this do his lordship return to the newry mean there for a short time to refresh his weary force the 29 of july his lordship and the counsel with he make to the lord in England a relation of the past service which for brevity I omit and write further as follow upon such bruit as we hear of a new invasion out of Spain the l. precedent in a manner assure we that they will in that province invade present with a strong army of 15000 foot and 2000 horse we be much distract what next to do for if we shall draw that way to provide to entertain they we shall loose the advantage of this prosecution and spend another year unprofitable which we grieve to think upon and yet perhaps miss of their place of land if we proceed as we yet intend to draw this war to a speedy end which be that which we acknowledge we do more effect we shall be the less able to make that defensive stop to their invasion that we may if we attend that business only we do therefore most humble and earnest desire to be direct from your lordship who in likelihood best know the spaniard intention which of these course we shall most apply ourselves unto otherwise we be resolve whatsoever befall to prosecute the war northward with all earnestness out of the desire we have to draw the war to a end and ease her majesty of that excessive charge which to our exceed grief we observe she to be at which we doubt not to effect to her great contentment and ease her majesty speedy of a great part of her charge if we be not interrupt by the Spaniard for beside the good hold we have get of those that have a ready submit themselves which by all argument of sound and sincere meaning in they we take to be better and more assure than any that be take heretofore since her majesty and her ancestor enjoy this kingdom especial with the hold that we have plant among they we have set down such a plot for the prosecution of the rest upon all hand at one instant so soon as we take the field next which be agree upon the ten of the next month till which time we have think fit to refresh this army overtoiled and weary out with continual work upon the fort that we have make and with exceed great march which we be drive to for lack of mean to carry victual with we for a long time as we be very confident we shall in short time ruin or subdue all these rebel for we have leave no man in all the north that be able to make any very great resistance or that have not make mean to be receive to mercy o rourke only except who hitherto have be further off from feel the fury of our prosecution Tyrone be already beat out of his country and life in a part of o cane a place of incredible fastness where though it be impossible to do he any great hurt so long as he shall be able to keep any force about he the wales to he be unaccessible with a army yet by lie about he as we mean to do we shall in short time put he to his uttermost extremity and if not light upon his person yet force he to the the kingdom in the mean time we can assure your lordship thus much that from o cave country where now he live which be to the northward of his own country of Tyrone we have leave none to give we opposition nor of late have see any but dead carcase mere starve for want of meat of which kind we find many in diverse place as we pass the force which last we draw out of monster be fifteen hundred foot above the monster list which the lord precedent desire to retain there only till he have end his business at donboy be now under the command of sir Samuel Bagnol present upon their arrival to the border direct by the Annely to prosecute o rourke where most fit he may join with the force of Connaght and shall be meet withal by those of Ballishannon command by sir Henry Folliot all those will help we great to pen up the northern rebel on that side when we next attempt they as by the ten of August we mean to do from Loughfoyle and Carickfergus which sir Arthur Chithester from thence be now very well enable for by the mean of the garrison we last plant at Tyrone and upon lough Sidney both be on that side of the lough that lie next upon Tirene and as those force on Connaght side lie very fit to assist we for the speedy dispatch of the work so be they very ready to entertain the spaniard if they shall land in Connaght and not much unfit for monster if they shall arrive there we have direct they therefore several to apply and bend their endeavour to answer these sundry occasion and this in our providence be the best course that we can think upon for by the same if spaniard come not we shall go on very round with our business and we hope by the grace of God perform it to your great contentment and if they come which be the worst they will be able to make some good defensive war till we with the rest shall draw unto they and then we can hope to do any more until your lordship supply we royal out of England for if the Spaniard come so strong in horse and foot as be report and as it must needs be think he will find the error that the last time he commit it may not be expect at our hand with all the force we can draw to head leave some fort guard as we must needs do to bridle and keep in awe the country and to keep our former labour from be utter overthrow that we shall be able of ourselves to put they from any place that they have a mind to hold but must rather give they way till we be better furnish we be therefore humble to desire your lordship if the spaniard arrive or if you expect they certain then to think upon we favourable and to supply our want and that speedy especial man munition and victual for this kingdom will not be able to afford we any thing for such a war as than we must make which your lordship can but know far better than we can express for as we have note heretofore which we beseech you give we leave still to remember you of it will not then be any long the war of Ireland but the war of England in Ireland to the infinite danger and cumber of they both though for our part we will most cheerful undergo the toil and hazard thereof as it become we to conclude we must acquaint your lordship with a very great abuse creep in among the minister of the victual which do marvellous prejudice her majesty service here we can never know from any of they when the victual arrive in any part whether it be part of a old contract or of a new nor indeed whether it be for her majesty or for themselves by that mean we can never find how we be provide for nor what we may further expect and that which worse be the rebel get of the best victual that be send hither and yet we can call the victualler to account thereof for he affirm stifte that he be warrant by your lordship to sell it for his benefit and so as he sell it to the subject how ill affect soever it be no fault of he if the rebel afterward get it it be in vain for we by our extreme toil to spoil the rebel corn and waste their country the best way yet find to bring they to obedience if they can get that english victual for their money which we very think be provide for those that serve her majesty here and the best of it too when the poor soldier have that which be not worth the eat thus much we have of late discover which we leave to your lordship consideration not doubt but it will please you to provide remedy and so etc. the victualler above mention have obtain of the lord liberty to sell some victual upon pretence as it seem that the same will grow musty and must either be sell or lose but they abuse this liberty so far as the best victual be sell to the irish subject and by they to those that be in actual rebellion while they make bold to utter their musty provision to the queen army the seven of August the lord deputy write to sir Arthur chichester as likewise to sir Henry to make all thing in readiness against his take the field which he purpose to do within three day and his lordship project with they in case Tyrone shall go into fermanagh how to turn their face upon he that way or otherwise to draw into cormacke mac baron country for since her majesty will not be induce to show any mercy to Tyrone himself the only way to end the war be to force cormacke either present by fear of his country spoil or in short time by plant a garrison at the Cloher to submit himself some few day after his lordship receive from her majesty this follow letter Elizabeth right trusty and wellbeloved we greet you well although we have hear nothing from you direct since our last dispatch yet we impute it to no neglect of you have so great cause to judge the best of your action when every dispatch from other part of our kingdom report of great honour in the success of our army under you a matter special appear by those letter which we have see direct to our treasurer at war in Ireland contain the discourse of your march and abide in the heart of Tyrone and the recovery of that island and that ordinance of we which have be foul lose before in which respect we value the same so much the more acceptable we have also think good at this time to add this further that we be glad to find that you be join with Dockwra and chichester because that be the thing which have be long wish often attempt but never before effect be indeed the true consequence of our plantation with great expense both at Loughfoyle and in other part of Ulster so as when we perceive that now the time be come when you may make a universal prosecution and when we find that your own word give such hope that this ungrateful traitor shall never be able to hold up his head again if the Spaniard do not arrive we think it fit to touch these two thing follow first to assure you that we have send a fleet to the coast of Spain notwithstanding our former fleet return with the Caricke there to attend his coast and all such fleet as shall be prepare to annoy Vs. next we do require you even while the iron be hot so to strike as this may not only prove a good summer journey but may deserve the title of that action which be the war conclusion for furtherance whereof we have spare no charge even now again to send a magazine of victual and other necessary to those place by which you may best maintain those garrison with which you resolve to bridle those rebel we have hear likewise from Carew our precedent of monster that he have take the castle be hold by the rebel at beer haven and defend with the spanish ordinance in that province we find by he that there be constant expectation of spanish succour for which reason and consider what promise the king of Spain do make they and with what importunity they beg it at his hand beside one other craft they use to hide from he all fear which may divert he from that enterprise agree among themselves how great soever their misery be to conceal the same from he and his minister as appear well by a letter of odonnel own hand intercept of late by which he write to a rebel call o connor kerry desire he to advertise he of the state of Ireland but in no sort to deliver any bad report of their loss because he will be loath that the Spaniard shall know it we do require you very earnest to be very wary in take the submission of these rebel who ever make profit of their come in some let slip of purpose by the archtraitor other when they have compound for their own peace be notorious know to fill their country with more cattle than ever they have in seven year before which be a matter that most notorious discover that the great border traitor who country be seek to be lay waist do find a safe protection for their good under they a matter whereof we speak in no other sort then by way of caution know that no rule be so general either to leave or take which may not change in respect of circumstance give under our signet at our manor of Greenwich the fifteen day of july in the four and forty of our reign to this letter in the margin be add these word in her majesty own hand we con you many for have so near approach the villainous rebel and see no reason why so great force shall not end his day who wickedness have cut off so many and shall judge myself mad if we shall not change your authority for his life and so we do by this since neither Spaniard nor other accident be like to alter this mind as she that shall blush to receive such indignity after so royal prosecution we have forget to praise your humility that after have be a queen kitchen maid you have not disdain to be a traitor scullion God bless you with perseverance your sovereign e. r. at the same time his lordship receive this follow letter from master secretary my very good lord it must not seem strange to you to find this marginal cotation in her majesty letter whereby the last authority in pardon Tyrone be so absolute retrench for first as her majesty in these case may well out of experience of government assume more to her royal prudence than any of her counsel so God in heaven do know that even in these great cause she be please to proceed more absolute than ever according as she please to approve her will by the rule of her own princely judgement so as if you consider how little good the last authority which be give you to pardon no more but his life can have effect you will easy imagine that we think it to little purpose here to offend by contestation against this letter when in both the direction the difference be of so little consequence last the change that be proceed mere from the hope which your own letter to the treasurer have give of our opinion so to ruin he as he shall never be able again to stand whereof it be true that her majesty have take so good and comfortable hold the same be so just and agreeable to the difficulty of her own nature to forgive that offender as although in effect she have do little more than nothing before yet she think any mercy to he to be much it remain therefore that I resort to explain myself in my former advice when I and some other wish you though you have warrant to conclude for no more than life only that you shall notwithstanding see you have warrant sufficient to hear from he in no case forbear to discover what be his high or what will be his low for this be our intent as faithful counsellor if you find the state to be such as without his submission to be a subject all other come in will be idle and that to offer he nothing but life which he have already and will keep too long I fear i be the way to lack that good which by yield to more may have be effect that then and in such case you shall not forestall he so peremptory in your deal with he as to repel those overture which he will make for though you be not to conclude for more than you have warrant yet when you may send over what it be and what your opinion be thereupon it may be that when her majesty shall see what may have come she will be content for the good of her kingdom to descend from the greatness of her own heart full of just indignation against he these thing I touch out of the infinite caution whereunto the experience of my misfortune to be misjudge press i more violent than any other whereby I will secure myself against any doubt that I will practise upon you in any thing for any respect whatsoever which may be pernicious to you to who I have profess all honest friendship for I protest unto you howsoever it may be some man philosophy to conclude that all private consideration must be extinguish when there be question of the good of a man country because it challenge a part before wife child or friend yet do I not think it intend by that great rule that any honest man ought to betray a honest trust of a worthy friend for any respect whatsoever unless he know that friend who be confident in he false or wicked to his country to which he owe so much duty for that distinction make great odds in the question of this letter therefore when you shall examine the circumstance you will quick discern how little it ought to trouble you for if you have already treat you have warrant for it if you have conclude according to the authority of that warrant the new restraint come too late if not then be your lordship to obey this direction and in obey it to content your sovereign as then advise whereby you be justify to the world whatsoever come because you have obey and if that success succeed not which be hope for by a moderate measure of grace follow a orderly and sharp prosecution and never otherwise to be but at great length you may then resort to her majesty own self for asmuch as you shall come short of that to which you may have arrive if you have not be restrain for proof whereof it will be very fit that you do write of the condition particular which be all that either you or we can say where we be all bind first to give advice according to our conscience and then to yield obedience I do conclude that it be for his own particular a good speech of the cardinal Granuella who when he find the emperor grow more resolute daily against his counsel say he wish that from thence forward his counsel may never be follow for say he if it be bad and not receive I be glad for my country if good and not follow yet it must value i to my master and so much for that matter only this I have think convenient for both our particular see it be impossible that this dispatch can come so soon to you as the queen may expect that the secretary receive it from i and you from he for our discharge lest her majesty shall suspect that out of zeal to the cause howsoever we dare not contest yet that I have delay to send it or you pretend to have be long without it than you have be to which purpose I have write to he to take care of the send it to you with expedition and to advertise i both of his receipt from i and you from he and thus for this time I commit your lordship to God protection from the court at Greenwich this sixteen of july 1602. postscript pardon i for use another hand which I hope you will bear with be of no other subject then a honest secretary may set down because I be not sure whether you can perfect read her majesty hand I send you the same in a copy the latter part whereof be suitable with the former style of favour that be wont to pass between you grow by the occasion of your own postscript when you write to the treasurer that you have be a good while in oneales kitchen which you mean to warm so well as he shall keep the worse fire ever after God know I do asmuch desire to hear of your success as ever I do to hear of any thing because upon it judgement may be make which be likely to follow your come or tarry in which be assure that I will do you all the right to which religion honour and affection can tie i in the mean time take this comfort that her majesty do feel apprehend your virtue and begin now to speak and write in her wont style and therefore I grow daily more confident that we shall spend some year together in her service for myself I will now give over profession and so do you for neither of our heart have ever be accusable for baseness to our friend though in our kind we have several taste of ingratitude only take this still for assurance that I be your affectionate friend to command Robert Cecyll likewise at the same time the lord deputy receive this follow letter from the lord in England after our very hearty commendation to your lordship although her majesty have by her letter take notice of your lordship wise and happy proceed by a letter which have be send from Dublyn by the treasurer upon your take of the island in Tyrone yet we be move to express our sympathy with your good fortune both out of duty to the public and particular affection to yourself who God have make so happy a instrument of her majesty service we have likewise hear from the precedent of monster of his take in of beere-haven whereby we have well hope that province will have prove less troublesome than yet we can hope first because there be many provincial rebel out next because the hollowness of those that be repute subject appear many way though not so visible to all which be only maintain by the assurance they have of succour from Spain whereof as we have often say we can make no other judgement then that what he be able to do he have a will to do and what he have not do have only be hinder by the remain of her majesty fleet upon his coast and which she resolve to maintain till winter be well come on so as see here be do as much as her majesty can do and that many difficulty at home with himself and action of other abroad may make suspension if not divertion from that spanish invasion we think your lordship shall do well to take time while it serve in Ulster see we perceive you have now so order the matter as if he shall land in the north you be near he and if in the south you have mean now to draw most of the force of the kingdom to make head against he so as we have little else to say for the present but to commend your proceed and expect the success although we find your lordship have very good reason to draw away sir Samuel bagnol with those extraordinary company which you be content to spare till the take of the castle of donboy yet we have think it very convenient because it be worth your lordship knowledge to let your understand that if the spaniard shall attempt to land in Ireland with a army all our intelligence do confirm that it will be in monster or upon the near part of Conuaght and if this summer at all it will be between this and Bartholomew-tide in which respect we think it good to remember your lordship to have care for a month or two to send some force to strengthen that province above the list whereby at their first descent her majesty force may keep some such reputation as the provincial who eye will only be upon the army may not grow too insolent nor the governor there be force to leave the field for which purpose we think it not amiss that you shall present send some horse though you spare the few foot consider the weakness of those number that be there to answer any such occasion for the north although we doubt not but Odonnell be feed with the same hope that the monster rebel be yet we do not expect any great force to land in those part more northerly than the town of Galloway at the further we have now no more wherewith to hold you at this time then with the recommendation of all your action to God protection from the court at Greenwich this eighteen of july 1602. the army be refresh with these few day rest the lord deputy have determine to leave the newry and take the field again upon the ten of August but upon intelligence that Tyrone purpose to send Brian mac carty back into Killultagh to disturb those part and so to divert the prosecution of Tyrone as much as they can his lordship direct sir Arthur chichester from Masserine and sir Henry Davers from the newry to draw part of the force into that country and there to invest the fort of Enishlaghlin be the only hold the rebel have in those part in which for the strength thereof all the good of such as be flee into Tyrone be leave this resolution the lord deputy signify to sir Henry Dockwra who expect to meet his lordship in Tyrone about this time according to their former purpose and withal advertise he that he have send such a part of the army to Charlemount and the other fort near to the blackewater as will be in a manner to as good purpose for any service sir Henry Dockwra shall think fit to attempt upon Tyrone or for the stop of he from pass with his creaght from the fastness wherein he how lay as if his lordship be there present with the whole force for his lordship troop of horse be lodge at Charlemount with more than a thousand foot in list and sir Henry Davers his troop of horse be lodge at armagh with some seven hundred foot more in the fort adjoin a party of two hundred horse and a thousand four hundred foot may be draw by sir Henry Dockwra from thence upon any sudden occasion and these force together with the company at mount Norreys and at Mountioy fort be able to do great matter upon Tyrone if he can discover his purpose by any good espial yet his lordship offer himself with those force he have leave to draw present up into Tyrone if sir Henry Dockwra think it needful rather than any thing fit for the service shall be omit and whereas sir Henry Dockwra seem to doubt of Henry Oge his second his lordship be so confident of his sound meaning as he give assurance on his part that he will not only not do any thing to give the state offence but will advertise any thing he can learn to further the service and rather than fail will draw his whole force upon Tyrone to which also his lordship have by his letter earnest persuade he and touch Ocanes submission his lordship write to sir h. Dockwra that he will be glad to see he when he come into Tyrone which shall be present after the business at Enishlaghlan be dispatch and then he will be willing to give he all reasonable contentment last his lordship advise sir Henry Dockwra so to carry his business as he may be ready to perform his direction if the spaniard shall again land in Ireland as a strong rumour go that they short intend the ten of August the lord deputy write this follow letter to master secretary Cecyll sir I understand from sir Oliver Lambert that the fortification at Galloway be almost finish and that there will be needful for that place to have four demy-cannon and four whole culverin but I think it will be well if they be all culverin and of iron which he think will make it of very great use against the spaniard if they happen to land there as he suspect for that and other such great work I think we must necessary have some good great ordinance and therefore beseech you sir to be a mean that we may be speedy supply with a score of iron piece well furnish and fit to be place there and elsewhere in monster to the most advantage of the service whereby I be very confident that this whole country will much the better be secure and her majesty great ease in her continue charge though her present charge seem thereby much increase I protest I do apply with all carefulness my best endeavour so to husband this business as her majesty in a very short time shall find abatement of her charge here to her own contentment if I be not interrupt by the spaniard and if they do come I doubt not but these great work will keep the town near which they stand in so great awe as they will not sudden nor easy fall to their party as otherwise in all likelihood they will so as they show themselves any thing strong before any of they and then will it manifest appear that this cost be bestow to great good purpose for the keep of one town from revolt will very well countervail the whole charge that her majesty shall be at in plant of all those fortification and yet will they afterward be such bridle to the country all about they as they shall never be able to rebel again I conceive that by the place of a constable and a convenient ward in such of they as shall be think fit to be maintain her majesty shall be better and cheap serve then by lay of company or part of company in they that must upon occasion be remove for when one man only have the charge thereof who know that he must be accountable for it he will no doubt have much the great care and may more commodious be fit with all thing necessary but then must I in treat you sir to take order that these place be not bestow there at court upon such as will be suitor for they of which kind I make account there will be many since I partly know some of they already for I shall thereby be disable to do her majesty the service I affect and become mere a unprofitable officer for she in as much as it will be here soon observe that when these place be not leave to my disposal who shall best know both how every man deserve and for what each man serve here be fit it will be to little purpose for any of they to make i a eye witness of their well deserve when it be not in my power to reward they but will rather use mean to her majesty or the lord hope to get that they aim at by a much easy and short course which I confess to you sir I do with some grief observe in the recommend of many hither for captain place when some have give over their charge here by which mean I can neither lessen the queery charge as I will by cash of their company nor prefer other thereunto who I see daily to have very well deserve it and by this mean come in both sell of company a thing which I will otherwise never suffer and the place of such
