captain as those which we find here at the begin of this war who insufficiency have almost lose this kingdom I beseech you sir conceive that I have just cause to be grieve that must draw upon myself the hatred of a great many that I shall discharge in the great cash that I intend who will ever hold i the overthrow of they and all their fortune especial if I be not able to bestow upon some of the worthy of they such other place in this kingdom as have fall within the gift of my predecessor here although God be my witness this do nothing so much grieve i as that I shall thereby be disable to serve her majesty as I will to make a speedy end of the war that may be both safe and durable by leave such in all place as I know to be best able to serve she and such as if they do not employ their time in her continual service may more just than any other with their presence importune she for reward of their former service and so sir etc. at the same time the lord deputy write this follow letter to her majesty may it please your sacred majesty I have receive to my inestimable comfort your gracious letter of the fifteen of july for none of my endeavour do satisfy i in do you service until I find they approve by your majesty and when I have do all that I can the uttermost effect of my labour do appear so little to my own zeal to do more that I be often ashamed to present they unto your fair and royal eye which be the only cause that I do not more often presume to present your majesty with the account of my proceed lead on with a hope and restless desire to improve they unto some such degree as may be more worthy of your knowledge and whereas it please your majesty to restrain i from harken unto the arch-traitor Tyrone be it not in respect of my desire to cut off sudden the chargeable thread of this war there can nothing come more welcome unto i then to be his continual scourge and as by the favour of God he be already bring to a very low ebb so utter to cut he off or cast he out of this country and although I have great reason to presume that if he be not assist by any foreign power the ruin of his estate be certain yet how as a vagabond Woodkerne he may preserve his life and how long I know not and yet therein I humble desire your majesty to believe that I will omit nothing that be possible to be compass and for the caution your majesty do vouchsafe to give i about take in submitty to their advantage and to the abuse of your mercy I beseech your majesty to think that in a matter of so great importance my affection will not suffer i to commit so gross a fault against your service as to do any thing for the which I be not able to give you a very good account the which above all thing I desire to do at your own royal foot and that your service here may give i leave to fill my eye with their only dear and desire object I beseech God confound all your enemy and unfaithful subject and make my hand as happy as my heart be zealous to do you service your majesty true servant Mountioy the fort of Enishlanghen above mention the invest whereof make the lord deputy the take of the fell be seat in the midst of a great bog and no way accessable but through thick wood very hardly passable it have about it two deep ditch both compass with strong palisado a very high and thick rampeire of earth and timber and well flank with bulwark for defence of the place forty two and some twenty swordman be lodge in it but after that our force with very good industry have make their approach to the first ditch the besiege do yield the place to the queen and themselves absolute to her mercy so a ward of english be leave in the castle after the spoil thereof be take wherein be great store of plate and the chief good of the best man in the country be all flee to Tyrone and the man there take be bring bind to the newry and present upon the nineteen of August to the lord deputy the same day his lordship write this letter follow to master secretary Cecyll sir I have late write to you at large and I have now no more matter of importance to trouble you with only since my last we have take Enishlaghlen a place of great importance and the strong that I have hear of to be hold by any rebel in Ireland Henry oneale the elder son of shane oneale be break out of prison as his brother do the like long before and because they do cast themselves without all condition into her majesty protection I can but use they well but as thing stand now I do not see any great use to be make of they and I fear I shall be more trouble with they then if they be still where they be to morrow by the grace of God I be again go into the field as near as I can utter to waste the country of Tyrone and to prevail the garrison there of some corn to keep their horse in the winter which be do I will leave the garrison to take their effect which when they be well provide and aswell place will do more than a army and sir except thing fall out much contrary to that which we have good reason to expect I presume that if the queen keep these garrison strong and well provide all this winter she may before the next spring send into this kingdom sir Robert Gardner with some other good commonwealth man with her pleasure how much and how every man shall hold his land and what law she will have currant here and I be confident it will be obey neither be the reduce of this province to be too little regard for ill inhabit as it be with no industry and most part waste I can assure you the earl of Tyrone in the time of these war do raise upon Ulster above fourscore thousand pound by the year and to fall from that excess I think they may be bring to yield the queen willing much more than ever she expect present and in time more than I dare now promise and after this winter I think she may withdraw her garrison only leave ward in the place and if I be not much deceive you shall find that these man will be the last of all Ireland that will forsake the queen party and I presume after this winter do the queen good service against the spaniard if they come but if they come before I can tell what I may build on but I perceive by your last letter they be not likely to be many and then although it may stop yet I hope it shall not overthrow our work if there be no invasion here if I do not too much deceive myself I shall do the queen in her service of this country more advantage by my come over after Michaelmas than I can do here this gentleman sir Richard trever I perceive will settle himself in these part by the which he may do her majesty good service for it be unfit such land as he have give he shall lie waste and it will be much better to be order by man of his worth and quality and so sir I beseech God prosper all the queen affair and make we able to do she the service that her majesty do expect you sir to do you service Mountioy the next day be the twenty of August the lord deputy take the field and encamp in the mid way between the Newrie and armagh and there understand that Tyrone be flee into Fermannagh and thereupon conceive that the war be then chief to be make that way his lordship resolve first to spoil all the country of Tyrone and to banish all the inhabitant from thence enjoin such of they as will become subject to live on the southside of blackwater so that if Tyrone return he shall find nothing in the country but the queen garrison further to prevent his return and to make the war more convenient upon he and the remnant of the rebel his lordship resolve to plant a garrison at the Agher be cormock chief house seat near fermanagh and near monaghan whither sir Henry Dockura may draw the great part of his force and whether in one day march as the passage be now make most of the force in the northern garrison may be draw together the nine and twenty day of August his lordship pass the bridge at charlemount over the blackwater and that night encamp by Dungannon which his lordship make defensible leave a ward to keep the place to be a retreat for our man go out upon service and to preserve the oat grow there about for our horse in the winter there his lordship receive this follow letter from master secretary my very good lord your packet of the nineteen and nine and twenty of luly be yesterday deliver i and withal your private letter to her majesty date the ten of August all which be very well allow of as hereafter more particular you shall be advertise this be rather to advertise you of their receipt then that there be leave any extraordinary matter for i to write unto you which be not contain in the last dispatch from hence whereof at the write of your own I know you can not advertise the receipt one great cause of my writing this private letter be this that where I see how much it do distract your mind to think of Spain behind you and of the north before you fear to be divert from the conclusion of your labour you may perceive in what estate the preparation of Spain be now as I be certain advertise by one of my own who be new arrive from the port of Lisbon where he take ship the one and twenty of july last there be two great ship each of one thousand tun one call the saint Andrew and the other which shall be the vide-admiral the name forget beside there be but twelve ship of two hundred ton and downward in which it be resolve to send some fifteen hundred man to have relieve the siege at beer haven the news of the take whereof be first know there the ten of july be write from Watersord to Lisbon and not before of these fifteen hundred man eight hundred come from the groin be part of those that be transsport out of Ireland in the groin remain Odonnell and there be only the great saint Philip with ten small bark with which he mightny importune to be send into the north. if these have be send into beer haven in monster hope upon his arrival with some one thousand or fifteen hundred man to have raise the siege possess some part and make a beginning of a plantation hereof great benefit must needs have grow to the rebel for as those small number which shall have be land in monster with the bruit of the rest to follow which be always multiply will have make a distraction of the Ulster prosecution so any petty descent with he in Ulster will have raise the new hope of all those northern rebel and true my lord when it be consider how great a benefit it be to the king of Spain to consume the queen with charge in Ireland by his bestow only once a year some such forlorn company beside that he thereby keep up some kind of reputation in Europe in follow on his first design by send though a few which be add to that which same spread of great number fill the world with continual rumour of his undertake humour I can be secure but that he will still feed that fire with fuel although I know it a vanity to believe those other fond report of such mighty preparation and such army as he be no way able to embrace for your lordship may be assure whatsoever you hear of gather of number by land to this place or that they have be only for defence against such attempt as they do ignorant suspect from the queen and the low-countries and to continue obedience within his kingdom in the south part where the moor have be very apt to take arm and for the galley whereof we have hear so many to be bring out of holy they be intend for some enterprise upon so as the bruit that they shall have come for Ireland be idle only it be true that sir ki. levison defeat those 8 galley which be come as far to the norward as Lisbon under the conduct of Spinola who undertake to bring they into the low-countries for Sluse but of those eight two be sink a three spoil and never able togo to sea the other 5 be likewise so tear and the slave so mangle as we look not to hear of they in these quarter this summer so as I assure you though our carick prove nothing so much as we expect have be much pillage and many of the good take wet yet have sir Richard lonison in this service deserve a extraordinary repetation thus have I now deliver rather out of my private affection my private opinion that no great army be intend then that I mean to contest against the contrary opinion which be here continual multiply from thence of the great army the king of Spain amass to hinder any preparation which may come from hence whereby that kingdom may receive any comfort first because I know the very bruit of levy here must needs give help to your proceed next because I know what a folly it be in case which concern a kingdom to dissuade any manner of supply whereof the lack may prove perilous especial in this state which be so exhaust by that war of Ireland only as it be a easy work to divert all action of charge especial whensoever they may think to secure their opinion by maintain those ground to which I shall incline to who place it principal belong to give best judgement of foreign intelligence I will only therefore conclude with this I be sorry to find my sovereign heart so great and magnanimous though I must confess she have very just cause as not to be content to have make virtue of necessity and by her pardon of the great rebel to have dissolve the strength of the combination which be still unite with mind of despair will multiple still alienation whereof so potentan enemy as be the king of Spain will ever make his benefit where I be of opinion that if he be sure to be pardon and live in any security with the quality of any greatness such be his weariness of his misery and so of all the rest as he will be make one of the best instrument in that kingdom but I have now go on too far save that I be apt to take all occasion to exchange my thought with you by letter pray almighty God so to bless your endeavour as we may more enjoy each other company for the good of her majesty jeruice and so I commit you to God protection from the court at Hisham this seven of August 1602 your lordship love and assure friend to command ro. Cecyll the lord deputy spend some five day about Tullough Oge where the oneales be of old custom create and there he spoil the corn of all the country and tyrones own corn and break down the chair wherein the oneales be wont to be create be of stone plant in the open field sir Henry Dockwra only with some horse with he do meet the lord deputy here upon the thirty of August and bring with he Ocane a late submittie have leave the english foot at the omy where in like sort be the most part of o cane and young o donnel horse and foot victual at their own charge and ready to attend any service the lord deputy shall command they the same day his lordship understand that sir Arthur chichester be come towards he by Killetro and that Randoll Mac sorley have offer he to serve the queen in that journey with five hundred foot and forty horse upon his own charge whereupon the lord deputy resolve to march with the army to Dunnamore and thence to the Agher and in the second day march upon the six of September his lordship receive letter from the lord precedent of monster that four and twenty spanish ship be bruit to be arrive at beere-haven which news though his Lp keep secret yet the whole country be present full of it and from all part he receive the like alarm insomuch as among the irish it be constant believe that some spanish ship be arrive at carlingford notwithstanding his lordship leave a garrison at the Agher be a castle seat in a island and he entrench a large piece of ground for great force when sir Henry Dockwra shall draw they thither upon any service and from that castle his lordship bring away two brass piece Tyrone Brian Mac art Mac mahownd and cormack Mac Barron be flee into the bottom of a great fastness towards the end of lough erne who his lordship follow as far as he can possible carry the army yet come not within twelve mile of they beside they have a way from thence into orurkes country to which the army can not pass Mac guyre have late leave they and receive the queen protection from sir Henry Foliott upon condition to put in good pledge for his loialty and to give Oconnor Roc Mac guyre the land belong to he and to build up the castle of Eniskellin which he late break down deliver the same build at his own charge into the queen possession and Tyrone and his abovenamed confederay be all poor and all the rebel follow they be not above six hundred foot and sixty horse upon the seven of September his lordship understand by letter from sir Oliver Lambert that he be call back from the prosecution of Orurke by like news of the spaniard arrival the same day his lordship send back sir Henry Dockwra and direct he to draw most of his force with as much victual as he can put up to the omy and from thence to the Agher be twelve mile distant and fair way there to be rendent and to make the war till harvest be pass be always ready to follow his lordship further direction upon any land of spaniard likewise the next day his lordship send back sir Arthur chichester direct he to lie at Mountioy garrison clear the country of Tyrone of all inhabitant and to spoil all the corn which he can not preserve for the garrison and to desace all the land former take be ready to draw upon the rebel if they shall make any head yet with advile to be likewise ready to answer any new direction if the spaniard shall arrive so his lordship march back with his army and upon the nine of September divide all the waste land on the southside of blackwater towards the newry between hen. Mac shane and Con Mac shane son to shane o Neale only with leave to live there with their creaght and such follower as shall come unto they till her majesty pleasure be further know and enjoin they to sow their corn for the next year upon the plain thus his lordship bring back with he into the pale fourteen company of foot and one hundred horse come to the Newrie the eleven of September and the next day in he and the counsel letter to the lord in England after the relation of the former service write as follow we have take the best pledge we can of such as be become subject all of they have assist we with cow most of they with carriage with man and with their own presence so as if foreign force do not arrive we make no doubt of they nor to bring the rest to what term shall be fit for her majesty honour and profit we have think fit to suffer most of the native of Tyrone the rest be put over the river of the Bann to follow Henry and Gon Mac shane and perchance many of they will not quit they though the traitor shall return and grow strong but for all cuent we have spoil and mean to spoil their corn and in respect of the garrison and of the blackwater their creaght can hurd return but they will be still at our command if they shall prove false we have good bridle upon they and at any time their follower upon leave to dwell in Tyrone will easy forsake they these follower seem to desire nothing more then to hold their land of the queen without any dependency upon the oneales howsoever for the present we think good to hold they thus together partly for the good of the service and to give these two young man some lively hood who with the rest be utter reject may by some desperate course much prolong the war and partly we must confess free our of human commiseration have with our eye daily see the lamentable estate of that country wherein we find every where man dead of famine in so much that Ohagan protest unto we that between Tullogh Oge and tomb there lie unbury a thousand dead and that since our first draw this year to blackwater there be above three thousand starve in Tyrone and sure the poor people of those part never yet have the mean to know God or to acknowledge any other sovereign than the o neales which make i more comm serate they and hope better of they hereafter when we have confer with the rest of the counsel at Dublin and be better inform of the state of those part we will again presume to write to your lordship in the mean time we hasten thither find here all both subject and other as full possess of the spaniard come as if they be already arrive the like opinion as we hear be general over all Ireland upon what ground we know not for we have not hear any such substantial intelligence except the bruit advertise by the lord precedent and a general bruit bring hither by all ship of huge preparation in Spain and whether it be desire or fear that make this report believe it be strange to see how general and strong it be apprehend we must renew our former motion to your lordship that if the spaniard do land here we may speedy be supply with munition artillery and all thing else that we have write for or that your lordship in your wisdom shall find to be needful for we who can best judge what will be fit for a action of so great consequence and for that we shall be necessary enforce wheresoever they happen to arrive to draw all the force of the kingdom together to make head against they by which mean these northern part especial be leave bare such as have most ready and with best argument of sincere meaning submit themselves to her majesty mercy with intent to live dutiful as become subject will be leave again to the tyranny of the arch-rebel for want of defence we beseech your lordship to consider whether it be not more profitable for her majesty though for the present somewhat the more chargeable to enable we to make the war upon the spaniard and yet to go on too with this prosecution in some measure then for lack of a convenient force to be keep on foot to loose the benefit of our travel all this summer and the charge her majesty have be at in plant garrison which be keep will so bridle all these country as they shall neither have any benefit of the spaniard land nor the Spaniard any use of they nor of their victual all their country be command by some of the garrison that will keep they from stir to join with other for fear of lose all their own at home it be true that by the put of new man hither when we shall have draw out the old against the spanish army which will be a work of most necessity it can be expect that the service of those new man will work such effect as the old band will yet may it be hope the effect will be sufficient to keep the irish from join with the Spaniard see for the most part they shall live in garrison and shall not need to be draw into the field unless only for some short journey which will not last above two or three day at a time and if her majesty be not please to like of this motion or seem unwilling to disfurnish herself of so many man and arm we humble leave it to your lordship to consider whether it will not be fit for that purpose to entertain two or three thousand scot which we think may ready be send over to Loughfoyle or Carickfergus and be inland scot and not irish scot and good security have to serve her majesty faithful they will in all likelihood better endure the winter hardness and happy be sound fit for any service than such new man as come usual from England which yet we move no further then as a remembrance because we perceive the last year such a matter be think upon if the spaniard come not we doubt not but to give your lordship a very good account of all thing here to her majesty like yet must we desire the speedy send over of the victual contract for that shall have come in july and August last but no part yet land that we hear of for without those victual these garrison will be unfurnished which depend whole thereupon and we be further to move your lordship to cause to be send over a very great proportion of salt and bread for by prey-beeve get from the rebel and good number have of the protectee who we have cause to furnish we for our money we have use a great kind of spare of the victual in the store except bread only and we doubt not but many of the garrison will be able to help themselves many time with beef so as they may have salt to powder they while the season last and will not need to take from the victualler any thing but bread which now they will not deliver they alone unless they be so direct from your lordship the same twelve day of September his lordship write from the newry this follow letter to master secretary in England sir you will understand by our letter to my lord how hitherto we have bestow our time be return back to the newry I find all the world strange possess of the come of the spaniard although I can learn of any assure ground they have thereof upon the general concurrence from all part of such bruit I do employ two as likely instrument as any be to know the truth and to learn what assurance Tyrone himself have from Spain they undertake upon their life that all the intelligence he have receive of succour be only by letter out of the pale Tyrone have many way make importunate mean to be receive to mercy but I do still reject he and publish it that her majesty have command i not to hearken unto he yet still he continue to urge i to become a suitor to the queen for he it be true I have be ever loath to negotiate with he any otherwise then with my sword because I find it dangerous for myself consider the queen resolution but upon the receipt of my lord precedent letter of a new spanish invasion I adventure thus far to entertain his motion that if he will swear to submit himself absolute to her majesty mercy if it shall please she to receive he whatsoever succour he shall receive in the mean time I will only undertake to become a humble suitor unto her majesty for he so that notwithstanding till I know her pleasure I will not desist in my prosecution this day he send one to i agree to so much but with all propound certain article that he desire shall be grant whereupon mislike that he shall in any wise capitulate I command his messenger present to depart and forbid he to send any more to i and to cut off all hope to his party I have direct all the garrison anew to proclaim his head and the like to be do in the creaghte of such as be become subject in the mean time n. n. out of his own head and by that general authority that be give to all commander to parley with rebel have speak with Tyrone to the effect of his own letter which even now I receive and such as it be I send it you I protest before the eternal God it be without my privity but I must bear with he for great fault than this for he and all the irish be very irregular though he be fit enough for the charge I have give he which be only of irish company in a garrison which of all other I can worst victual and they will make best shift for themselves and great spoil upon the enemy I have command he to meddle no more with Tyrone for if I shall think it fit I will employ one better instruct for that purpose it be true sir that for many respect I have be fearful to deliver free my opinion what course I think fit to be take with Tyrone and so be I now but if it shall please her majesty to trust i with the authority to hearken unto he I will never use it but when I shall be sure to give she a very good account of my proceed therein for the low he be bring the more it will be for the queen honour to show he mercy and it be think he may be make a excellent instrument if Spain continue their purpose for this kingdom if the queen be resolve never to receive he it be most necessary that Ulster shall present be make a provincial government for this people seem to thirst for justice and by that mean the dependency upon the oneales will be soon extinguish if the queen be loath to augment her charge I think it be much better that the institution of Connaght be discontinue and the like officer to be transfer hither it be true that in all Ireland for the good of the general service there be no place so fit for the deputy to be resident as at athlone and if he be there Connaght will little need a governor I have here but little time and much (@) and therefore I can write to you of all thing so large as I will the which I purpose to do when I come to Dublyn only of this I pray you sir resolve i by your next we have here the worst intelligence of any instrument that any prince in Christendom do employ in so weighty a business I do know some that I do think be fit to be employ both in Spain and about the traitor here yet though I know myself to be honest they may prove knave if the queen be so confident of my faith that she will be please to make the best interpretation of what I shall do therein I shall be able to do she perchance some good service and give myself great light of all thing than now I have but if she mislike it I will only say about i with my sword though it be in the dark it be not a letter nor a reasonable book that can deliver all such conceit of i as I think necessary to let you know of this kingdom wherefore I despair to do it till I may have the happiness to see you if I have any certainty that the fear of Spain be pass I will make a great diminution of the list wherefore I pray you sir if you have in England any assurance thereof let i know it as soon as you can but here we look for they every hour and they say in the pale it be hold as sure as if they be already come if you show the enclose letter I pray you put your finger on the latter part or blot it out and yet if I think the queen will not be angry I will give the lady leave to come to her brother for I be loath to make war with woman especial since she be now great with child the same twelve day of September his lordship write from the newry to sir Oliver Lambert governor of Connaght this follow letter sir yesterday at my come to this town your messenger deliver i your letter contain a relation of your proceed since your go to the abbey of boil where and in your return thence I perceive you have have some knock on both side and the rebel be so many as you note I have good cause to be glad you speed so well and part with so good reputation to our side and so little loss withal which I doubt not proceed chief from your good command and manage of that business for which I may not omit to yield you many thanks neither will I be spare to testify so much where it may most redound to your due and well deserve commendation yet must I withal note that it somewhat grieve i to observe so great a alteration in those that of late seem desirous or at least not unwilling to receive her majesty mercy for that I have some reason to be doubtful that this sudden change proceed not altogether out of a certain expectation of spanish succour but out of some opinion they have conceive of a purpose you have to dispossess the principal man of their land and live and to get the same into her majesty hand by indictment and office to be find thereof and if they once entertain such a conceit they will assure put up all to any hazard and to their uttermost mean shun to be reclaim which I must acknowledge to you I do the rather doubt in that tybot long have grievous complain to i of the committal of his cousin davye bourke and some hard usage towards himself for which he seem fearful to come to you and therefore desire my licence to go for England which I have now send he with this purpose to give he contentment as much as may be and yet when he come to i I mean to school he and so I hope to hold he in good term for so at this time especial it do behoove we to bring our great work to the better conclusion I make no question but that both he and o connor sligo and the rest of they do all somewhat juggle and play on both hand to serve their own turn and therefore true deserve the less favour for they so do here for the most part and yet I wink at it but since it behon we so great to draw the war to a end to ease her majesty of that exceed charge and consumption of man and arm which her majesty and the state of England be grow very weary of and indeed unwilling to continue much long we that be here employ as chief instrument to effect what so earnest be desire must bear more for our country good than our own nature can well endure and therefore let i advise you with much earnestness to apply yourself unto it as the only and sole mean to make our do acceptable in England where we must be censure and by your next let i know certain I pray you whether you have do any thing already for the entitle of her majesty to any of their land in that province or whether you have any way attempt it or give they cause to suspect it I shall be well satisfy with your answer presume that you will do it sincere yet if any such thing be I pray you proceed no further in it but labour by all mean to win they both because I know it to be her majesty pleasure and that the multitude of subject be the glory of a prince and so every way it be fit to reclaim rather than destroy they if by any good mean it may be wrought o connor slige as you know be restrain of his liberty by the rebel and that I think upon a letter I send unto he so that he have a just pretence for his stand out so long and for any action into which he shall enter neither shall we be able to disprove his allegation though perhaps himself be not innocent neither at the beginning nor now you must therefore be content to think that what he do be by compulsion though indeed you do not think so for some reason apparent to yourself your stone work at Galloway about the bulwark will I fear prove chargeable and very long yet can I do no more than recommend it to your good husbandry and discretion who may best judge what be fit Tyrone be not yet go over the earn but lie between that and ruske where I have plant a garrison and another at the Agher hard by the clogh which lie both very fit to do service upon he to the former all the garrison near the blackewater and that at Mountioy and monaghan may fit draw upon all occasion and so I have lief order with sir Arthur chichester who have the chief care of all and to the latter and to omy which be but twelve mile from it sir Henry Dockwra have promise i to put up most of the force of Loughfoyle and to lie there about himself to Eniskillin or there about sir Henry Follyot have direction to draw his whole force leave a ward only at Ballishannon and beleeke which be already do but he have not his boat yet from you which be a great hindrance unto he and therefore I pray you send they with all speed possible if they be not go already touch your motion for master attorney I now return to Dublyn where if he cause it to be move at the table I will with the rest yield to any thing that be fit in the mean time you may use the chief justice in those business who have allowance for his diet and be of great experience and continuance in that Provinee so as thereby he may best know every man disposition I pray you let i hear stom you again with as much speed as you can touch the state of that province under your government and so hope for all these late bruit that we shall not this year be trouble by the spaniard or if we be that their number shall be small for so master secretary have confident write to i out of England I commend i right hearty to you from the newry this twelve of September 1602. the lord deputy be arrive at Dublyn and this summer service end since the compose of the irish trouble be henceforward to be wrought by the garrison plant in all part upon the rebel and the settle of the state to be manage by counsellor sir Henry Davers sergeant mayor of the army be the rather induce by the necessity of his private affair to discontinue his service in Ireland whereupon his place of sergeant Maior be void be confer upon sir Arthur chichester and because sir Richard moryson have a pretence to the place by former hope give he from the lord deputy his lordship to give he contentment raise his company of foot reduce late in a general cash to 150 to the former number of two hundred the seven and twenty of September the lord deputy at Dublyn teceive from the lord in England this follow letter direct to his lordship and the counsel of Ireland after our hearty commendation to your good lordship and the rest of the counsel there whereas your lordship in your late letter of the twenty nine of the last july do advertise we of a great abuse creep in among the minister of the victual in that realm namely that you can know from any of they when the victual arrive in any port nor whether it be of a old contract or a new nor whether it be for her majesty or themselves and by that mean you can never find how you be provide for nor what you may further expect and which be worse that the rebel do get of the best victual that be send thither and you can call the Victualer to account thereof because he affirm stiff that he be warrant by we to sell it for his benefit and so as he sell it to the subject how ill soever affect it be no fault of he if the rebel afterward get it upon this information from your lordship we have not only call all the victualler to account how this great abuse be commit but have peruse our former order take this time 2 year when we contract with they to appoint commissary there for the keep and issue of victual by who default be their minister it shall seem these lewd part be play therefore for your lordship satisfaction in the first point the answer of tolles and Cockain will suffice who do absolute affirm that they send no other victual thither then such as by contract they be tie to send and though they send often a great proportion than will serve to furnish any contract yet that do always appear unto we upon the certificate of the inuoye and we that undergo the principal care of those matter here have enjoin they to have always a good proportion in store to serve upon all occasion whereof they send as they find convenient ship often time a large quantity before hand more than by contract they be bind unto for if they shall not make provision before we contract with they it will be hard for they to perform the same in so short a time and by such day as we must of necessity limit unto they upon diversity of accident and demand from you and the overplus do always pass into the next contract but in that your lordship do find fault that you can never be inform upon what contract the victual be provide which do arrive there we must remember unto you the order we have always observe here which will easy reconcile the difficulty in this matter first therefore your lordship be to call to mind that we here for the most part in the make of our contract both for the quantity of the victual the time to have it there and the place of unlade the same be direct by such letter as we receive from your lordship and you of the counsel and by such certificate as be send we from the surveyor of the victual from thence which surveyor ordinary have certify unto we the quantity that arrive there then so soon as any contract be make with the undertaker we send a abstract thereof unto your lordship both of the several kind and for how many man and for what time the same be provide and to what place the same be to be transport by which your lordship may know what you be to expect and for more assurance we have require of your lordship by diverse letter to appoint certain discreet person at the port where the same be to be land and unlade to see and visit those victual and to take knowledge both of the quantity kind and goodness and that with good usage the same be likely to continue the space of five month from the land thereof which direction if it be due observe and perform your lordship need no other certificate to satisfy you in all respect and we have usual receive from master Newcomen the surveyor of the victual very exact certificate of the quantity of victual that arrive there as also of the goodness and upon what contract they be send for until the contract be full all the victual send thither be to satisfy the same and if there be any overplus it be reserve for the next contract for the undertaker absolute deny the send of any victual thither but such as be to serve the soldier and to perform the contract so as all the victual be to be account for her majesty provision therefore it be far easy for your lordship to have the like certificate there thus as we have remember unto you the direction we have give unto your lordship so we have think good to acquaint you with the rest of the course we hold here which you shall understand to be in this manner upon every contract we make we give the victualler a impress before hand to provide those victual they contract for but the whole sum that they be to have by that agreement they never receive until they do produce true certificate from the officer of the port that the full proportion which they be tie to contract and provide be lade on shipboard and ready to be transport of good and well condition victual according to the contract and therefore if your lordship will be as strict to call for the certificate of the unlade there as we do cause the inuoy to be peruse there can be no abuse in that case consider all the victual send thither come to the magnzine and how much soever it be all be for the use of the soldier for the other point your lordship do complain of that the minister there do sell the victual and their peremptory untrue and respectless answer unto you it be the desire of the victualler as this information be general that your lordship will not only be inform of the particular abuse commit by any officer or commissary there but to take a severe course to see they exemplary punish for we have give no other licence for the sell of victual than your lordship have be acquaint with all in the contract make with the victualler in August be two year which be in these word and because it be not possible in so great a mass of provision with all the care that may be use by reason of transport and toss they to and fro but some will perish and decay in this case their lordship be please that the foresay commissary depute now by the merchant shall be permit to utter and sell to the poor in the market town where they reside and near unto they such victual as be decaiable and unfit to be utter to the soldier and by the instruction give to the commissary there be a clause whereby they be restrain that if there shall fall out any waist in the provision either by leak moisture or other casualty and accident by transportation by land or by sea or by any unfit or inconvenient room to place or house the same victual than they be to acquaint the chief commander colonel or counsel therewith and procure some sufficient testimony for the manifest of the truth and of the quantity that be decay surprise by any ambush of the enemy or by fire or other inevitable danger become unfit to be use that it may appear that happen not by their negligence and default the which be certify her majesty in that case to bear the loss and waist thereof therefore we marvel your lordship will endure so insolent a answer to be make by inferior officer know we always do send your lordship the copy of those agreement and article we make with any victualler and in this article this consideration only do lead we that if by long keep often remove or other casualty the victual shall not be fit for the soldier than your lordship or the provincial governor or such as you shall appoint be acquaint therewithal we think it more fit and now do so and not otherwise that the same may be sell to the poor in the next market then that the loss shall light on her majesty nevertheless in those case we refer it to your lordship judgement and discretion who her majesty do trust with so great a government to order they as you shall see cause to conclude therefore if that caution we take for the good of the soldier that no evil victual shall be thrust upon he shall be abuse and pervert to supply the need of the rebel and the soldier the worse serve we think this matter worthy of strict examination and as severe punishment as may be inflict upon offender in so high a degree which we earnest require may be do and that in all these and like case your lordship will consider that we of her majesty counsel here that be absent from thence do proceed general in these matter as reason do direct we and as we receive light from you and the counsel there but your lordship that do know the state of the kingdom and see the particular course and disposition of thing there and what be fit and not fit may easy reform those inconvenience and abuse that happen and be commit and inform we of those particular that be not within our knowledge that these matter may be carry with more perfect order and rule for the good of the soldier who welfare be only intend and seek herein to her majesty excessive charge and our great trouble this be another great abuse and though it be not pertain to this matter yet by reason of late we have discover the same to be very common we have think good to inform your lordship thereof and earnest to pray you and the rest of the counsel to give strict order it may be avoid the matter be this we find by diverse examination take that it be a common practice that if any servingman country-fellow or labouring-man happen to be maim or grievous hurt by any mischance or disorder if he be in any of the country near to the seaside or any port where ship do go for Ireland he will find passage there and follow the army in some company or other and do such service as he be able and when he have get some knowledge of the country and of the captain and chief officer than he procure a passport from some captain or other or else he get his name to be insert in some general passport as a man by reason of hurt receive unfit for service and so upon his come over hither again he be recommend into the country for a pension by which mean the country be burden with this kind of man and the poor soldier that be indeed maim in the war be disappoint and hinder of that pension he ought to have this be to be reform by two mean first that order be take no maim man that have receive their hurt in service be send away in a general pass because the statute do require a particular certificate from their general certify the hurt and maim the soldier have receive next the remedy be that no such passport be give but by subscription of your lordship the provincial governor or chief colonel under who he do serve express the hurt and maim the soldier have receive and in what service and under what captain and the office and place he hold in that company this by general direction be observe will avoid the number of counterfeit soldier and give due way to the relief of the maim soldier who indeed without such particular certificate ought not by the statute to be relieve and the want thereof have be great hindrance to many poor man therefore nothing doubt but your lordship will take order herein according we bid your lordship hearty farewell from the court at oatelands the 29 of August 1602. the eight of October the lord deputy write to the lord precedent of monster that howsoever by reason of carmacke escape he can not for the present spare the horse and the foot which be with he above the monster list under the command of sir Samuel bagnol yet he will not fail to send fifty horse present into Connaght whether he purpose short to take his journey be now busy about preparation for the same and further his lordship pray he to send a good proportion of victual from the store in Limricke by water to athlone without which the force can not be keep together in that province and according his lordship give order that boat shall be send from athlone to Limrick to fetch the same and whereas his lordship have write to the sheriff in monster to levy certain beef for the army he pray the lord precedent to give they assistance for take they up and send they into Connaght with all convenient speed the fifteen of October the lord deputy be advertise from the lord precedent that Carmock Mac Dermott late escape have make suit to he to be reconcile to the state and that upon his answer that if he will justify himself to be innocent he shall have a just trial or if he will confess his fault than he will be a suitor to the l. deputy for his pardon he the say Carmock have make choice to sue for pardon rather than to stand to his trial and that in the mean time all the castle in his country be possess for her majesty and his follower dispose under other lord upon good assurance beside the faction which be nourish against he among his near kinsman which thing make he unable to do any hurt so as if his lordship have any occasion to employ in Connaght the force which be in monster above the list of the province his lordship may dispose of they at his pleasure further the lord precedent advertise that he understand by one james black late come out of Spain that o donnel be dead there and that this news be most certain the twenty of October the lord deputy dispatch captain blany from Dublin with commission to govern the garrison of ruske and monaghan recall sir Christopher s. Laurence and captain esmond from that command to repair to Lublin that his Lp may hear and compose the difference rise between they he have further order to leave captain atherton to govern the fort of mount Norreys whereof he have former the command likewise to put up good store of victual from the newry to those garrison and to deliver letter to sir Arthur chichester with who he be to concur in the intend service for those part he be direct to know from Mac mahow his resolute answer whether he will submit himself upon the condition send unto he and if he refuse they then to give he no further time of protection but to prosecute he present by all mean to give o Gonnor roe Mac gayre his best assistance to establish he in his country and for the other Mac guyre in rebellion not to accept any submission from he except he undertake some extraordinary good service because he have late abuse the savour offer he by sir Henry Folliot governor of Ballishannon last whereas some of tyrones captain offer to come from he to serve the queen he be direct to entertain those offer and either to draw they from he or to make any use of those offer and treat with they which he in his discretion shall think fit the one and thirty of October the lord deputy receive from her majesty this follow letter direct to his lordship and the rest of the counsel here Elizabeth right trusty and well-beloved and trusty and well-beloved we greet you well we have be acquaint with your dispatch to our counsel of the fourteen and to our secretary Cecyll of the thirteen in the first whereof we have perceive the success of your last prosecution wherein as we have cause so we do bless God that he have so prosper you and our force under your charge as our enemy be not so hasty to attempt we anew or our rebel able to interrupt your proceed which letter be for the most part but a journal require no other answer but this that we do yield unto yourself and to our faithful and love subject that have assist you both praise and thanks for your good service and now because we know your affection be so well mix with understanding of the state we stand in both here and there as you can well consider of what importance it be to we to ease our kingdom of those great or rather infinite charge which we have thus long sustain which still continue in that height will take away the true feel of our victory we have think good to deliver you our pleasure in that behalf for it be almost as good for we to lack a great part of their reduction as to be drive to that charge in keep they which our crown of England can endure without the extreme diminution of the greatness and felicity thereof and alienation of our people mind from we consider that for these only rebellion in Ireland we have be force to part with many of our ancient possession which be part of our flower of our crown and to draw from our subject a thing contrary to our nature those great payment which but for the hope they have that the same shall serve to work their future ease and respiration they will not so willing have bear nor we so just can have impose upon they in all which consideration though it be true that the authority and direction may be expect from we to who almighty God have only give the supreme power to warrant your proceed in matter of such importance yet because in this one point of abate of our force now from the proportion whereat they stand you have always in your own judgement the certain idea thereof as a thing that you resolve to do so soon as the expectation of the spanish army be pass over we rather think if you have observe the judgement which you have make by your last letter of the improbability of their come in those time with any power that these our letter shall serve more to approve your action or at the least your resolution to abridge all those charge which be either mere raise in that only expectation or be only necessary to be tolerate until you have plant those garrison in this prosecution then that you be now to begin it which if it be so we do then command you to proceed with it with all possible speed both because the preservation of our treasure and the very true reason of state do require it and for that there be neither appearance of any foreign army neither be the rebel by your own description of any force to make any dangerous head against you so as if now our charge shall still continue we know not in what time it shall be a bare to conclude therefore in that point we do refer the number the place and person to be order by you whole and by the advice of our counsel there and we will for the present only lay before you in general some thing out of square in that kingdom which will be look unto first we find diverse officer and minister entertain with many other charge which be superfluous whereof the error grow upon a conceit that our charge be easy in respect of the mix coin whereof your own judgement can best inform you all circumstance consider we do also find lack in the manage of diverse possession which be win unto we as well as in collect and answer former duty all which will both by your discretion and your authority be think of and distribute to person to order with time according to their several office to the intent the world may see the end of our prosecution be to reform the kingdom to revive justice and order and not still to suffer these licention expense or loose cost in many thing by many inferior person unremedy although while the kingdom be invade by a powerful enemy and tear in piece with a domestical rebellion we know the time can not admit such reformation as may be use in great calm and we must say to you our treasurer that when we remember your own word concern the point of the exchange in the first alteration of our coin how you tell we still that in continuance of time every second issue will so recompense the first return as the exchange may well enough be bear we little think we shall have see so many bill subscribe with your own hand and your minister to be pay here in sterling for as it be not our meaning nor ever shall be whensoever in we publish any thing to make it the instrument of deceit so we ever repose so much in your judgement as when you shall see a abuse or a plain corrupt traffic make of that liberty of exchange which we only vouch save to erect for the use of those that shall not make it a trade than you will either have find the mean to discover their fraud and so whole have reject their bill and punish the fault or else if you can not have hit the very bird of their deceit in the eye you will take it concesse that a fault there be have take some such exception against they as may have serve so to have suspend you allowance as they shall not have have from you so good warrant to demand that just of we which be so excessive a burden unto we the rather see merchant shall by all constitution bestow the money they receive for ware utter in the natural commodity of the country which imposition will have bridle they very much and be not unjust see it can be deny but that there be some commodity as hide tallow yarn and many other thing whereon they may have bestow some money and not have make over such great sum where you may perceive that to all those which serve the army and for the bill due to the officer and soldier we have make no difficulty although we know if it be examine that in their exchange over so much money as some do there be some indirect deal we will not have you think who we know to be honest diligent and a sufficient servant that we do touch you in the least degree with lack of sincerity or desire to do we service for of both these we have great reason to take gracious recognition but rather to let you to know how sensible we be of the clamour how negligent your deputy have be in many place to charge we here how great portion of treasure be due by your certificate and how necessary it be see but by this coin those expense can be bear that all supper fluous charge be cut off and this matter of the exchange correct in some degree or other before like a canker it consume our treasure which be the sinew of our crown we pray you therefore as we do mean here to do with our privy counsel call to you some three or four of that counsel of the best understanding and think among you of some better caution for this matter of exchange wherein see we do see the state of that kingdom principal with your eye we shall be content to incline most to those course which you shall find convenient and because no day go over our head wherein we be sure there be not some increase to that load we do hereby give you our deputy authority without tarry to advertise we of your opinion one way or other to publish a proclamation or order either for toleration or explanation of the form of our former institution which shall seem best to you with consideration of all due circumstance not doubt but you shall find many thing which be fit for we to suffer in the beginning which by change of circumstance may now be alter and because in these matter of payment which flow like blood out of the vein time be precious if there be any thing which you and that counsel shall find fit to be do for prevention of some part of this abundant consumption grow by the freedom of all person to have the exchange see the distance of place to be pass by sea be subject to length and uncertainty we be content provisional to warrant your proceed in any thinh you do or publish in our name it remain now that we do satisfy you our deputy of some other thing contain in your private letter to the secretary first for your doubt you may not use your best mean to explore the practice of Spain and the inward treason of the rebel because your minister may often prove unhonest we have no more to say then this that if you consider that we have trust you as absolute with that kingdom as ever we do deputy you need make small doubt of any other interpretation of your action in that point towards you for when soever the great curiosity shall censure our action it shall never appear where we see faith and duty only intentional in that we will be willing to censure the action of our minister according to the success and therefore as we do leave liberty here in England to our secretary of state to employ such person as be like to discover practice though we allow not any immediate treaty or corespondency with subject of other province but where we be particular acquaint with it so do we give you warrant by such ordinary mean as may be take by opportunity of person to pass to and fro under colour of trade or traffic to foreign part to inform yourself of the enemy preparation in such thing as belong thereunto that you may be able from time to time to fashion your own present proceed according to such advertisement as may be bring you see you may sometime receive they with more expedition and more freedom for thing concern that realm than they can be usual convey to our minister here from those who they employ for our service from whence you have see what judgement we have make all this year of their distraction and weakness though we stick not in some measure to provide for you though far short of that which we must have do if we have credit those bruit which be bring into that kingdom by such as we be persuade if it be well observe come out of Spain full of device and practice to blow that abroad which they believe not next concern your opinion for the order of some provincial governement and make your residence at athlove for the place we know it be situate very well to answer all service and as thing stand Ulster have need of good distribution now as much as Connaght wherein as we can very well like that you do prepare for residence in that place whereby the limit of our pale may be enlarge so we wish you that both in Ulster and in Connaght you do fashion the commandment in such sort as one governor have not too much country to rule for where man be more absolute than ordinary they be common apt to use thing with less care and moderation so as in that matter we confess to you that we have rather have many good subject employ in many place than any one to manage too much for the matter of charge of your residence because we know not what charge belong unto it to make the place capable nor how it may stand with the state of the town of Dublyn which be a port and not to be overthrow stand so commodious for passage out of England we will have all circumstance well consider and then you shall receive more of our direction last for Tyrone we do so much mislike to give he any grace that have be the only author of so much effusion of blood and the most ungrateful viper to we that raise he and one that have so often deceive we both when he have crave his pardon and when he have receive it of we as when we consider how much the world will impute to we of weakness to show favour to he now as if without that we can not give a end to this rebellion we still remain determine not to give he grace in any kind and see it be unsafe for any prince to make all fault appear venial because every offender will thereby become insolent and see in common reason the cut off so many associate must needs have leave he a body without limb and so not worthy our respect we do very well allow of your late reject he for when we look on his manner of seek mercy at all time we do still certain conclude that it be do upon some practice to serve some present turn see one day he only desire simple mercy for his life and another day fall to capitulate neither can we see why so much depend upon his reduction when for aught we know no man can advise we if he shall come in and be at liberty hereafter out of our hand how we can be so assure of he but that still we must be in doubt of he and at the same charge we be at to contain he and he notwithstanding their former reduction which if we must account to be at whosoever or whatsoever we recover we shall take small contentment in that victory whereof the end will be worse than the beginning and therefore mistake not this our earnestness in this point as if it proceed from any opinion that you have demean yourself otherwise then become you in the charge commit to you for we will have you know for your comfort that we approve all the course you have hold since you take the sword in your whole government to have be accompany with diligence wisdom and good success and so we accept the same at your hand for n. n. his proceed with the traitor although we do not allow of his boldness to do such a thing without your direction yet we have so little reason by the course of his service to doubt of his affection as we do dispense with that error in which we assure ourselves he mean no harm give under our signet at our manor of Richmond the nine day of October 1602 in the four and forty year of our reign the first of November rory o donnel brother to o donnel late dead in Spain who in the summer follow you shall see create earl of Terconnell have make humble suit to the lord deputy for her majesty mercy and find that his lordship make doubt of his true meaning do by his letter date the first of November earnest protest that he do desire from his heart and have long desire to become a subject though for the present he have the command of all his brother force and have receive promise of help to be send this next summer from Spain or Rome and to obtain this mercy he plead that his brother go out upon his private discontent against the will of his father and himself be enable by stranger to force they that his grandfather by Henry the eight for his good service to the crown be knight and have his country give he and his heir by letter patent that his predecessor have long serve the crown against the (@) that himself have often seek to become a subject and for the same have be imprison by his brother with many danger of his life last that now assoon as he have make himself master of his brother force he humble offer to submit himself and of late have suffer the governor of Connaght to pass by he with the queen force lest by fight with they he shall incense the state against himself and his people and touch Neale garue whereas he have a grant of part of his brother land he offer to prove that he have since that grant agree with his brother to join with he against the english force as soon as the spaniard shall arrive in Ireland and be able to keep the field hereupon the lord deputy send he her majesty protection by captain gore with direction to come unto his lordship so soon as he shall be in Connaght whether he intend short to take his journey about this time the lord deputy employ sir garret Moor of english race into the Brenny where all the rebel submit and glad receive her majesty protection among they one of the o rely come with one hundred man and Mac gaurons son with fifty man and some one thousand cow from Ororke be the great strength he have and another of the o rely be refuse pardon undertake to bring in Mac guyre head so he may have her majesty pardon which Mac guyre have abuse her majesty protection into which the governor of Ballishannon have former receive he at the same time Tyrone send to the lord deputy by sir garret Moor a absolute submission to the queen mercy and write to the lord deputy that howsoever the show of this submission may hinder he from receive the aid he expect out of Spain yet he have send it in confidence that his lordship will deal honourable with he this submission be in these word right honourable lord your worthy endeavour in her majesty service and process of time have sufficient teach i how improvident I have perscuer in action whereas heretofore upon my submission I may have hope her majesty will have receive i to her mercy unto which action I be enforce for saveguard of my life only which be indirect seek for by her majesty officer as be know to sundry person of credit here and her subject and that before I be proclaim traitor I never send letter into Spain or receive any fronthence though afterward I be animate to continue in action upon hope of spanish aid and promise of many confederate of both which be disappoint my estate be great decrease and though I may perhaps be able to hold out for a time yet be I weary of the course I hold and do much repent i of the same most humble and with a penitent heart desire and wish to be reconcile to my prince and to be receive to her majesty mercy who I be right sorry I have so much offend and provoke and yet I know that her majesty mercy be great than my offence the rather that at the first I do not wilful oppose myself against her majesty but for safety of my life be drive into action as aforesaid and for my continuance therein I submit myself to her majesty mercy and grace acknowledge her majesty alone to be my natural prince and myself her unworthy subject but if her highness will vouchsafe of her accustom grace to grant not only pardon to i my kinsman and follower and unto my adherent in their own name and upon their several submission but also to restore i and they to our pristinate blood and possession I will from henceforth both renounce all other prince for she and serve her highness the residue of my life humble request even of your honour now that you have bring i so low to remember I be a nobleman and to take compassion on i that the overthrow of my house and posterity may be prevent by your good mean and honourable care towards her majesty for i which with all humility I desire and will accept and for the better do hereof if your lordship do mislike any of the article which I do send unto you I pray your lordship to appoint either some of the privy counsel or some gentleman of worth to confer with i and your lordship shall find i conformable to reform they the twelve of November 1602. subscribe Hugh Tyrone the thirteen day the lord deputy be advertise that captain Tyrrell upon a mutiny between he and the rebel of kerry have leave monster and have some six hundred man with he be come back into Lenoster and it be think that Tyrone will send he to orowrke to assist he in the defence of his country about the eighteen of November his lordship begin his intend journey into Connaght and by the way this follow letter from Tyrone to Oconnor sligo be intercept be send to his lordship we commend we unto you Oconnor sligo we have receive your letter and as former we have write unto you we have remain in Fermannagh wellnigh this quarter of a year and have often write unto you and to odonnel son and request you to come and see we near logh earn concern our counsel either for peace or war and neither of you come thither to meet we we think that you and o donnel son and Ororke and o connor roe and ourselves as many of we as be of our faction will have maintain war for a great time and to that end we come to these part and have forgo so many of our own people as have not rise with we but see that o Rorke if it be true and connor have receive protection and that every one do make peace for himself we may all easy be deem man break and not substantial in war but concern our counsel and advice which you write for our advice unto you be neither to make peace nor cessation but that peace or cessation which shall be make by all our consent and agreement and if you do otherwise stand to the hazard yourselves for you shall not have my consent thereunto subscribe oneale the lord deputy take the foresay journey into Connaght as well to take order with the rebel in action which have send messenger to Dublyn in their name to crave the queen mercy as also to view the town of Galloway and to consider how the descent of foreign enemy may best be prevent by build of a fort upon the haven before his lordship come sir Oliver Lambert the governor with the force under he have make a journey wherein he quite banish Mac William out of the county maio. his lordship have make some stay in the pale come to athlone the second of December and lay in the castle be very strong and divide from the town by a bridge over the river shannon where the governor and the counsel for that province make their residence here the fourteen of December o connor sligo and Rowry Odonnell brother to the traitor o donnel late dead in Spain two rebel of great power in those part come to his lordship and make their humble submission to her majesty o connor sligo allege many thing in his own excuse as the manner of o donnel take he and keep he in prison and submit himself to her majesty mercy Rowry o donnel albeit he have under he all his brother follower and creaght yet do he both simple and absolute submit himself to her majesty grace without stand upon any condition but signify his readiness to deliver such pledge as shall be demand of he all such castle as ballymote and other in the county of sligo which he have get into his possession and to do any thing that he may receive her majesty favour allege further that his father and grandfather have be true servitor that he himself with the privity of sir Coniers Clifford than governor have resolve to have serve her majesty against his brother but upon the discovery of his purpose he be keep in iron a matter well know to be true and now most frank offer his service if he may be receive either here or beyond the sea wheresoever her majesty will be please to employ he which manner of carriage proceed from a man of good spirit active and wise induce the l. deputy to receive he and do in some sort move all the counsel to pity his case that he do no soon submit himself and the rather because they do foresee how noteable a instrument he may be make to bridle the insolency of sir Neale garue which be grow intolerable of who they think he may be the best curb that can be devise and therefore they resolve at their come to Dublin to send for the say sir Neale and this competitor and with the advice of the rest of the counsel serious to consider how to provide for and to dispose of they both wherein albeit they purpose to give unto sir Neale the benefit of her majesty gracious promise yet do they think it a thing very expedient for her service and the settle of Tirconnell that some competent portion in Tirconnel shall be allot to this gentleman in which point they by letter humble pray the lord in England to move her sacred majesty to send unto they her highness warrant for take such a final order between they as by the general advice and consent of this counsel shall be think fit for her majesty service the lord deputy proceed on his journey to Galloway keep his Christmas there and in that town all the rebel of that province the flahertye the Mac Dermotts of the courlewe connor roe and diverse other submit themselves and be receive and so for the present this province be bring to quietness only the proud insolent faithless Bryan Ororke notwithstanding his former humble message send to the l. deputy touch his desire to be receive to mercy absent himself and have draw unto he tyrones Mac guyre who for his deceitful and treacherous deal the lord deputy have banish out of Fermannagh and expose to prosecution and the traitor Terrell late come out of monster and trust to the fastness of his country persist in his rebellion and therefore albeit his lordship do foresee the manifold difficulty which must grow in his prosecution yet do he hold it very necessary to take the present opportunity to scourge he several way before the spring and before his foreign hope may give he any further encouragement and for this end as he have appoint a proportion of victual and other necessary to be present bring from Lymrick to athlone so now he resolve to furnish sir Oliver Lambert with a army to surprise his country Leytrim and to take it in to her majesty hand sir Henry Follyot also with the assistance of rowrie o donnel who already have do some service against o Rorke be appoint from sligo and those part to enter into his country and his lordship intend present to raise a three army to be send from the pale to annoy he by which course his lordship hope this rebel shall not be able long to subsist in his pride and contempt the submission of the foresay rebel be make by each of they in writing and in these word follow first I do acknowledge Elizabeth by the grace of God queen of England France and Ireland to be the only true absolute and sovereign lady of this realm of Ireland and of every part and of all the people thereof unto who gracious mercy I do humble submit myself my land and good and withal faithful repentance for my unnatural disobedience unto her royal majesty do most earnest implore her mercy and pardon for myself and such of my follower as with i have be seduce to this wicked rebellion further I do renounce all and any manner of obedience unto any other power or potentate which I owe only to my say dread sovereign Elizabeth and utter abjure any dependency and adherence to any of her enemy whatsoever or disloyal subject and do promise swear and vow from henceforth to live in her subjection in all duty and obedience and to use my best endeavour to the uttermost of my power to withstand and confound any enemy either foreign or domestical that shall attempt any thing against the sacred person or estate of her majesty or to the hurt of her faithful and obedient subject and especial and namely I do renounce as before and promise my endeavour as aforesaid against the king of Spain and the arch-traitor the earl of Tyrone all this as I do upon my salvation swear to perform sincere so if I do herein break my oath I do acknowledge myself not only to be worthy of all infamy and extreme punishment but to be ever after account unworthy the name of a christian or the society of man to the which as I have unfeigned swear so I do now in witness hereof set to my hand in Christmas holiday his lordship view the town of Galloway and judge it a place of great importance to be preserve from be possess by any foreign enemy he give present direction to finish that fort the building whereof be already begin in a place well choose both to command the haven and to defend the town from foreign invasion during his lordship abode in Galloway he receive this follow letter from the lord in England after our right hearty commendation to your good lordship her majesty have hear of your whole letter bring by sir Henry Davers bear date the ten of November in answer to one of her majesty of the nine of October wherein you do prevent in some thing such direction as be digest into a dispatch ready to be sign and send you as namely in the matter of exchange for one point and so of some other wherein her majesty have hear the apology of you the treasurer be as she be ever ready to interpret gracious those action which be well mean though they succeed not always as be wish though she have give we charge to write much more particular in that matter yet now have be please to command we only to let you know master treasurer that although you shall never need to excuse your integrity because she never doubt it yet she can not free you and your minister from such oversight as have increase the burden of this exchange for though one part of your answer be that new thing must be settle with plausible circumstance at the entry yet these be she own word that you shall not have think it new long ago to have stop that current when you see it carry so much contrary to the true meaning of the proclamation even now that the institution have be on foot almost two year and that such a sum of money be return back again by you when the issue of the new coin have be no great see that except which spend in the lend of the army a small quantity have be otherwise disperse in the kingdom so as sure it may not be deny but many of these bily may have be keep from the bank if your deputy have be as careful as they ought for although captain and soldier and merchant carry and deliver necessary for the army be by her majesty institution intend to be relieve in their reasonable and just demand yet who can ever think that her majesty ever intend that merchant shall have be suffer to sell those merchandise for 300l there which cost they but one 100l here and then turn that 300l upon the exchange or that captain and officer will make over four time more money by one bill than their whole entertainment come to in a year and therefore foresight hereof by the experience dear buy make her majesty now resolve in her last order that she will not allow any exchange but only to the army and such as do relieve the same and that she mean from henceforth shall be just answer how soever it may be that some seditious person mislike that these unreasonable bill have be look into and so some suspense of payment make may speak scandalous either of the past or future when already there be deliver to your deputy master treasurer ten or twelve thousand pound and more shall follow after towards the discharge and now to come to that which follow and do require expedition your Lp shall understand that the greatness of her majesty army be such
