as she can bear it up without extreme prejudice to her state and kingdom she have command we hereby to let you know that she be not satisfy with this abatement of 1000 but do command you with all possible expedition to reduce the army to 12000 foot and 1000 horse a matter which she find good to do in her wisdom not only for the necessity of the expense but out of this judgement that she shall be sufficient able to provide for the defence against foreign power before the storm shall fall without which occasion consider your own relation of the weakness and desperate estate of the rebel with the desire of the rest to come from he she persuade herself that this list well compound out of the band that be casher will be strong enough against any home rebellion and so much the rather if you make it as much english as you can by rid as many of the irish as you think meet in all which for the person and place she refer it whole to your own best judgement and now that we be speak of the army we think it fit to remember unto you that it be a great error in the officer of the minister of that kingdom that the master which be one of the principal key of her majesty charge be no better order the fraud whereof do evident appear see that army which be keep upon such a height in list and payment be know and confess even by the captain themselves to be so extreme defective in their number for although we be not ignorant that multiplicity of garrison hinder the possibility of exact muster yet there be difference between tolerable imperfection and those gross negligence which be use by the minister of that service whereof the world be apt sometime to accuse those that shall both here and there censure the offender as those that do commit the fault punish herein we entreat your Lp and that counsel to make our case your own and then to consider whether we can well discharge our duty to her majesty when in time of so great a expense we be not able to show she any manner of account of her army by any authentical certificate more than every captain or passenger can relate unto we no not for the expense of eight or ten month time in which point of the muster we think it not amiss to say something to you what we conceive concern the upon apparel first the order set down very careful and at large for the manner of the delivery and the to be raise upon the apparel be not observe the due observation whereof will advantage the checque of the apparel very much for such soldier as be send from we thorough apparel oftentimes have apparel there again at the time of their delivery give they especial those that be disperse into band to fill they up whereby we see her majesty double charge though special warning have be give thereof from hence and daily able man be suffer to come over hither as of late ten of those that be set out of the county of Lincoln in the late leafy and by the examination take here late it be prove that money be give to procure they passport and none of those soldier of the late levy do stand her majesty and the country before they arrive there in less than six pound a man the order be not observe that have be prescribe that no soldier shall be discharge but by pass from the lord deputy principal governor or chief commander for daily diverse come over with pass of their captain and diverse be send away together in one pass sometime to the number of thirty and few of they maim and those bring no other pass then from the mayor where they be set on land here in all which and other particular duty though the muster master controller and such other officer may be think to be only responsable in such case wherewith they be trust yet the authority which your lordship have and the mean you have to distinguish how and when thing of that nature may be in some good sort reform and the person offend therein be in your eye to behold both they and their do and in your power to rule and punish they as you find cause do impose upon you thus much either to call upon they and to chastise they or else to deliver your own judgement which when we shall receive from you and thereof inform her majesty it will include your care and we to have it otherwise and will satisfy the expectation and discourse of this time when her majesty subject be bite with accidental charge of the war begin now to think that much of that which her majesty impose here at home be not necessary but rather voluntary or for lack of care and providence in the expense unprofitable waste especial now they hear of nothing but victory and improbabillity of foreign power and yet find no difference between the present state of her majesty charge and that which she be at when there be a spanish army in that kingdom we have also think good to let your lordship know how great confusion it breed in her majesty expense that those of her counsel there while your lordship have be whole conversant in military cause have not call to their answer nor according to their desert have punish such commissary of victual there as have make private gain to themselves by sale of the victual commit to their charge without warrant from we or direction from the purueor here their superior whereof from you we have receive both advertisement and mislike but especial for that you of the counsel there have never call upon nor strict charge the commissary to bring in their account in so much as there remain yet unaccount for for any thing we know the whole charge of those victual which we have careful send over and whereof we have receive certificate of the safe arrival for the space of almost two year of which mass if the same be reckon your lordship shall find it little less than the value of one hundred threescore thousand pound whereof your lordship may thus conclude that either the sum defalk be great which remain in the treasurer hand or else the remain in be so great with the commissary as her majesty may have spare the provision late send over by which the treasure have be so mighty exhaust or else the waste must be such as be not only to be reject in the account but the author thereof severe to be punish a matter wherein we write the more earnest to your lordship from who we will have all exception take that we assure ourselves you deserve not because we hear that a great part of the waste shall be excuse sometime by the avow that it have be cast on their hand because the soldier have not be enjoin to take it and that some other time they have be command to remove it and carry it from place to place by which they pretend great loss and which of all thing be most subject to suspicion in they that it be take for a good warrant in Ireland to allow of any waste for which a commissary can produce a certifficate from a poor mayor sovereign or bailiff of a town or port which how easy it be obtain your lordship own wisdom can best judge who be so well acquaint with the poverty and condition of that place further because no one thing be more heavy to the queen and the realm than the matter of victual wherein it be no way possible for we to do more here and that we perceive by your letter of the two and twenty of September among other thing that your lordship be not well satisfy in diverse thing concern the victual we think it not amiss to handle some particular point of that matter which we wish all those of the counsel and all other interest in the care of those thing may well understand because you may the better another time answer those argument with which they seek to satisfy you when in truth if they examine themselves whatsoever they lack it proceed most from their own default first whereas in the letter aforesaid you note how slow such victual do arrive there as have be provide here upon request make by your lordship we answer that Newcomen who you send over be able to give you satisfaction that present upon his come over hither and perusal of your lordship demand there be no delay use by we to satisfy you in such sort as may be to your lordship contentment and to serve the army and garrison and because Newcomen be not only make privy but do both think the provision appoint to be sufficient to answer your lordship desire and the use of the army and garrison and be one of the undertaker for those provision of victual he be much to blame if he have not particular discharge that trust true it be that we conceive you have cause to marvel that in so great distance of time so small quantity of so great a mass as be provide have arrive there which if it have happen by contrariety of wind only then must your lordship be satisfy and we excuse but howsoever it be by the copy of the contract (@) unto your lordship by we with the charge the victual do amount unto we doubt not but you rest thorough satisfy of our care and leave we rather cause to suspect that our former letter write to your lordship concern the victual cause have not come to your hand or that the content of they be out of your remembrance for in they namely that of the four of August last we do not only send your lordship as former we do of all the rest a copy of the contract make by the victualler but do satisfy you in diverse thing whereof we do find you do complain which by our former letter we deliver and signify at large and we both remember well the thing you note and the course we hold for your satisfaction to which we add nothing more but do repeat unto you that we then do say in that point that we find it a great fault in the commissary of the victual there that they do never inform you of the arrival of victual in those part nor upon what contract they be provide which will well become both the commissary and of the victual for by many letter send from he to we we be particular certify both of the victual that arrive there and upon what contract they be furnish beside the victualler here do protest that they do provide no victual at all but for the use of the army and to furnish the contract so as what quantity soever be send thither the same be to be take for her majesty use and to be account to furnish the contract until they be complete and then the overplus be to pass to serve the next contract for it may fall out that such victual as be send to one place may by contrariety of wind arrive in another province or port which now as the purveyor do inform we have of late happen to one of their bark drive into cork and there stay by the governor which shall have come to Galloway and so that town thereby disfurnish and those that be send soon from hence may arrive late and sometime miscarry but the chief matter that do breed scruple doubt and matter of abuse be that there be not appoint there in the usual port where there be magizine as in Dublin carlingford cork Lymricke Carickfergus Loughfoyle and Galloway some sufficient person who with the mayor and officer of the port may oversee the unlade of the victual from time to time and take knowledge of the goodness of the victual and the quantity of the same and to charge the surveyor of the victual to perform their duty likewise and to be inform of the fame and certify you thereof wherein or in any other sort if any abuse be commit by the commissary it be both the earnest request of the undertaker and our absolute desire and that which your lordship place do require to see some exemplary punishment inflict upon they for their evil carriage which may and aught to be reform so as for a answer to that letter we must still refer you to our former letter namely those of the four of August last forasmuch as be to be perform by we here who see and hear with other eye and ear in that place and not our own and where we do understand by your late letter also that the commissary and agent for the undertaker do refuse to take beeue at twenty shilling a piece the victualler here do not only deny the same to be do by their privity but earnest beseech we as often they have do that they may have they at that price and in our letter send by necowman at their entreaty we do require that your lordship will be please to take order they may have at reasonable rate such beef as be take from the enemy which suit they do renew and do assure we they will be glad to receive they at that rate and forasmuch as many great and heavy account be to be take before either reckon can be clear or fault clear distinguish her majesty have resolve immediate after Christmas to send over some well choose commissioner both for integrity and experience in all thing in this nature to examine and survey the state of her majesty receipt and issue to who as she nothing doubt but your lordship who zeal and care appear so great in her majesty service will give the best support which you can possible afford they so her majesty require your lordship now upon conference with the counsel there to appoint a day for all those inferior person who have any thing to do with the matter of account receipt and expense to come to Dublin to the intent that those which shall be send over may not loose their time by attend their repair from remote place nor your lordship who eye and judgement will give great light to that commission may be otherwise distract by any new journey or prosecution to which the grow on of the year may invite you for the present desire you have that some commissioner shall be send over for the pass of some land to the submitty with such reservation as be fit for her majesty she mean present to send over authority according like very well among othing that you intend to cut off all dependency upon the irish lord which be one very necessary consideration last because your Lp and the counsel may know that although it be not to be look for at the hand of any prince that they shall unnecessary keep company in pay for the relief of any captain yet because her majesty in her own disposition intend nothing less then to neglect those servitor of she who you shall testify to have deserve extraordinary be like to suffer penury by this cashering she have will we to let you know that she be please to continue to every such captain and so many other officer as you think necessary their ordinary pay whereby they may be enable to maintain themselves there about you for many good purpose until some other occasion offer itself to employ they elsewhere or some company there fall within your gift to confer it upon they which be do that entertainment may cease and now that you perceive her majesty resolution whereof she have much hasten the send away unto you in which respect we can so particular touch all thing as we will we must now conclude that howsoever her majesty pleasure be that those error of subordinate minister in these matter of account and reckon shall be thus mention to your lordship and that counsel that be only to make it appear how serious a reformation be expect from you and though she see how fearful your lordship be without a more particular commandment what number to diminish to have make so large a abatement as she command you not know how soon you may have cause to use they for her service yet her majesty be so far from imputiug it to any error in judgement or lack of zeal in you to her service as she confess she have be too blame for not command it and not you for not do it who care toil and peril as they have be more than any governor have undergo before you so may your lordship take this comfort also that the success which it have please God to give you do make yourself a acceptable minister to your prince and country and make all we very glad both for the public to which we owe our first duty and for your own private who shall ever find it in all thing fit for we to perform towards you from the court at white-hall the 22 of November 1602. a list of the army as it stand the first of januarie 1602. horse the lord deputy 100 the lord precedent 100 the earl of Thomond 50. the earl of Dennis dale 50. leave in ward at Enishcorthy 20. foot 12 370. the four of january sir Henry Dockwra governor of loughsoyle force write to the lord deputy this follow letter right hon. and my very good lord the argument of this my letter shall be only the testification of my private duty and a little discourse touch Neale garue and those irish who I must free confess I be more to seek in what sort to govern and use to the benefit of her majesty service and discharge of my own duty then in any other point of the whole business neales first come without compulsion his bring we to liffer his service many time commendable perform in his own person the furtherance he give we for plant at Dunagall the help he give our man in their great want when o donnel besiege they the loss of his brother in that service the trial of his fidelity by stand firm in so dangerous a time and last the good he forsake of his own to serve the queen for half pay be argument neither untrue gather nor unjust allege to make much in his favour on the otherside his extreme pride ambition and infatiable covetousness his want of any knowledge when he be well deal with all his importunity in all thing right or wrong his continual beg and unprofitable waste of whatsoever he get his aptness to desperate and unspeakable discontent for trifle of no worth his facility to be mislike by man of best quality and his underhand juggle which be too apparent by many with the rebel he be true to be charge with all as the other good service be to be acknowledge and yet to discountenance he and challenge he of those fault be to raise a new war and to drive the whole country in a unseasonable time to a obdurate alienation of mind from all english government to discharge his people we shall find a singular want for espial of many thing which they give we light of so shall we likewise for gather of prey whensoever we go a journey and beside the arm they have get among we and the charge they have put her majesty unto the one will hardly be recover out of their hand and the other not unworthy think to be a ill piece of service to make utter lose again on the other side they give continual advertisement aswell from we to the rebel as from they to we fort or place of strength alone by themselves they neither will nor dare abide in without help to make their peace with o Donnel they shall for find difficulty to join with the spaniard if any come near to those part they may if they will and will undoubted if the be never so little discontent in these inconvenience on the one side and necessity on the other I see not in my judgement any better course to be hold then to temporize somewhat to feed their humour a while though it be chargeable to the queen and to mingle lenity and severity so as some be punish for these notorious abuse when they be apparent prove let he rage and storm while he will and other wink at who fault be apparent and yet more close carry from direct and manifest proof by testimony of witness and therewithal to get what by fair mean and by force as I see best occasion to temper they the best pledge he have for himself and the best of his people into my hand and be once possess of they to keep they till I see great cause of assurance of his fidelity or at the least a less occasion of suspicion which course I be already enter into under a good colour as have take his second son the elder be at Dublin together with two more of the chief man about he with his own consent in the name of pledge for other but in truth most of all for himself I be not ignorant but he will grievous complain against i for those course and many of our own nation will whet he forward some for want of knowledge of the truth some blind with private malice against my own person and whatsoever shall happen amiss upon cause of his discontentment will be impute to i and the corruption of my deal but I fly to your honour for succour and glad submit myself to a better course if I may be instruct for such be the state of the business between i and he without partiality or malice both upon the duty of my allegiance and peril of my soul together with this letter sir henry Dockwra send to his lordship the copy of the follow letter he have late write to the lord in England most honourable lord the journey mention in my former letter and intend upon Ocane I set forward on before captain Vaughan depart the river have first ship all necessary provision for plant a garrison at Colrane and see they down the lough with a fair wind to carry they thither before I set forth how be it have pass through the country and effect in a manner all thing to my own desire be come unto the place I find not the ship nor any appearance of news what be become of she which the master excuse but so as I leave to your lordship to judge whether sufficient or no this bearer be instruct with the full state of the cause the sum and effect of that journey be that notwithstanding this I send down captain orme with two hundred english and the irish of Enishlowen to pass over at the green castle by water to the end that he enter at one end of the country and I at the other the prey may the more assure be take or at least the more spoil do myself go overland pass two pace without resistance enter the three beat they from defence of it set fire on their camp contain 30 great house all full of corn take Ocanes brother prisoner that have before perfidious revolt from the queen service who I sacrifice in the place and so pass by not through the wood because it be no ordinary passage and a fair way do lie by the sea side hard by so come into the plain and heart of the country burn and spoil till I meet that night with captain orme at a place of strength agree upon between we who come a way least look for light upon the kill of some few of the people and a small prey of fifty cow and five hundred sheep for with the rest for all his sudden come they make away and get to the mountain for four day space together afterward I divide the force into three body and traverse first about and then through the country spoil and burn such a quantity of corn and number of house as I shall hardly have believe so small a circuit of ground can have afford if I have not see it and because I fail of meet the ship I hold my course towards Tyrone intend all under one journey to have waste and spoil as high as Dungannon but that I be prevent by a sudden thaw of weather after a long frost and snow which raise the river that with much difficulty I can recover home but be return I meet with letter from Dunagal advertise i of their great want of victual by reason that the ship which I have a month before dispatch away with all provision be not then arrive whereupon have diverse other reason also to draw i that way I resolve to make my next journey thither and to settle and establish the garrison of ballyshannon so with one hundred gartons load of biscuit and munition I pass unto they and happy relieve their great want in a most seasonable time at my come I find there be a ship from Galloway arrive within the harbour of Calbeg and during the time I be there the other that I have send from hence come also into the same harbour but by extremity of weather which I be ancie witness unto neither of they both be able to put in either to Ballishannon or Dunnagall all the time I be there which be twenty day so that the further fruit of my come thither consist only in this that I cause Neale garue to make a cut upon the country for Cowes wherewith the garrison be plentiful relieve go to ashrawe and there leave four company of foot and fifty horse which I carry from thence beside those that be at Dunagall before settle then there get in a sufficient quantity of corn to feed the horse near all the winter long fetch in turf and old house for fuel by the commodity of the garrou which I carry with i see the situation of Bundroise and dulike and all that part of the country and so return to Dunagall where I take in Mac swine Fannaght and some other of the country for who Neale garue have undertake and deliver in pledge of his own such as in truth I make choice of more to bridle himself then for any great assurance I think they be for the other howbeit the state of thing stand in such term chief by reason of the extreme foulness of weather that I be not altogether unwilling draw to accept of their subjection upon slender assurance who have the time serve to compel to other condition I shall hardly have deal withal or give care unto in any sort but this be the advantage which I think myself to have gain by take they in that Neale garue importunity be satisfy who if his humour be altogether restrain will undoubted prove a desperate rebel himself settle in full possession of his own country if he can keep it furnish with mean to feed his people of his own which before I can not be rid of but he will wring the queen store and beside be always complain as he do still of his half pay in which notwithstanding he be now less to be hearken unto for that the just and reasonable colour which he have before of be banish from all private mean of his own to maintain they by be clear take away and further a opportunity be gain that those man which have play false before be return home with all their good think themselves safe and sure under protection may upon very justifiable reason hereafter be look into and seize on when they least expect and can worst avoid it who good by any other mean will never be get but conceal or do away among the rebel where we shall never find they what other benefit be have by settle that garrison as it be your lordship may easy gather out of your own knowledge to which the bearer be able to add somewhat as have live a good space of time thereabouts and go furnish with many instruction and remembrance for that purpose who will also lay down unto your lordship the state of the army as be a commissary and the necessary requisite to that place as be a man special choose by the captain and in that kind of business request to labour and solicit in their behalf the castle of Ballishannon I can not take by reason the piece of artillery be not come nor any manner of provision so much as a board to be have for the purpose but all thing be now send away which the wind serve fit to bring thither that business will be easy effect but your lordship must understand that the bar at the come in be so shallow whatsoever some vain man will talk to the contrary and the ride so open without be cover with any manner of land as by mere necessity the ship that shall be employ in bring any thing to it must be of very small burden if they get in and yet if any weather arise force whether they be great or small to make the place of their unlade at Dunagall from whence it must be carry to the other place by land so that both the charge and trouble thereof will be much more than be expect or then I can ever be right inform of till I see it I have now assemble the force to make another journey into Tyrone but in so unfit a season of weather as it yet hold as I shall be force to suspend it till some alteration make the river and high way more passable the next after that which I mean God will without question to undertake be to accomplish my first intent of settle at Colraine which I know myself able to perform though the whole force of the rebel shall be return before I undertake it but then shall our force be so far extend and divide asunder as more than the bare keep of those hold we be possess of we shall not be able to do till a new supply of man arrive which in my opinion shall come most seasonable towards the end of the next month in that the cow which now their corn be go be their only relief be then easy to be fetch away or spoil and for any impeachment we shall have by the spaniard though we be daily threaten by many thunder rumour yet I see no great reason to suspect their come hither nor shall in that respect rely myself further then upon your lordship better intelligence and most honourable care according to the occasion shall be offer only this I must put your lordship in mind of that by a army able to master we in field come furnish with artillery the river and all our provision both of victual and munition be easy to be take from we which notwithstanding we may much prevent if our fort at this place be make up in time which be almost do already and a couple of good ship of war lodge at Cullmore for that straight whilst I be myself at Ballishannon I must advertise your lordship that I give charge to captain wiles lie at lyffer that with those force I leave behind he shall make a journey upon sleught art a people in Tyrone who before my go make many offer of their subjection but so as in conclusion I must stand to their courtesy how long they will continue in that state and therefore reject they utter he fall according to my direction upon they bring away three hundred cow and burn most of their house and corne. they offer again a new parley but because I be resolve to take in none of Tyrone leave their number to feed upon their own hungry store shall be diminish I do still refuse they and will do all other of that country except I see some apparent extraordinary and special cause to the contrary so I most humble recommend my duty and the best service I be able to perform to your lordship command from the derry the second of january 1602. the lord deputy be return from Galloway to athlone and be advertise from Rowry o donnel that he have late do some service against o rowrke do by his letter of the six of january give he thanks for the same encourage he to drive o rowrke out of his country wherein a army of four thousand english be then ready to assist he with assurance that her majesty be so incense by o-rowrkes contempt as she be resolve never to pardon he and with promise of that country to he and his heir if he join his force to expel o rowrke Further his lordship assure he that this shall be no bar but rather a furtherance to his hope of have his brother land for as he will never take from sir Neale garue any thing for mer give or promise to he so if he can prove that since his submission he have commit any treason wherein the other can not by a honourable trial justify himself than he shall be sure that his lordship will satisfy his best expectation the nine of january his lordship in he and the counsel letter to the lord in England after relation of the present affair write further as follow in this journey I the deputy receive her majesty express direction for the reduce of her highness force to twelve thousand foot and one thousand horse which I do most willing obey and for performance thereof I take present order which now be full put in execution though upon the sudden it can not be do the army be divide in the remote part of this kingdom and albeit I have give straight charge that out of the new casher company the band subsist shall be make strong yet must I make know to your lordship the difficulty I find to perform this direction by reason the soldier be once casher do use notwithstanding any care that we can take to wander to and fro and sometime fall into the rebel hand which use to strip they of their arm and clothes sometime into the hand of border subject which deal no better with they and so they become most miserable creature so hard a thing be it to keep they together to be turn over to other company when once they know of their casher as I remember your lordship have note the like difficulty in your letter to keep together and to send over the whole number by you appoint to come into this kingdom and in this casher of company according to her majesty express pleasure which her highness prescribe to be of the irish company that the english may subsist and be make strong although I will willing perform this direction and to that end have discharge a number of irish company heretofore and now yet see the arch-rebel do yet hold out albeit I have direct such a course for his prosecution by sir Arthur chichester who be in pursuit of he as before the spring I hope he shall be quite break and that this rebel o rowrke have draw such a head together I think it not amiss yet to continue some of the irish company for a time hope with God good favour that ere it be long I may reduce the army to a lesser number and then with more conveniency and less danger may discharge the irish company which in the mean time I will so exercise and employ as they shall not be idle but shall be still expose to endure the brunt of the service upon all occasion and touch the continue of this service against the rebel orowrke and his confederate which we be of opinion may not be delay for the prevention of further danger as also for the uphold of her majesty army in regard of the general scarcity of all sort of victual in all part of this kingdom we humble pray your lordship to remember that a proportion of victual be send to Galloway Ballishannon and Lymrick with all speed without the which we see no mean how this service can be follow or the army in general may be preserve from perish for where it be expect by your lordship that some great number of beef and other victual may be get from the rebel we have already so impoverish they by prosecution as they be ready to starve and among the subject of the pale their harvest be so unseasonable and their corn be so destroy by the weather as number of subject will undoubted die of famine and we see no mean for her majesty army in this kingdom to subsist especial for this present year but upon provision to be send out of England which in discharge of our bind duty we think meet plain to signify unto your lordship and do humble leave it to your careful providence for such abuse as have be commit in dispose of the victual we shall be ready upon the arrival of such commissioner as be purpose to be send to yield unto they our best assistance and in the mean season to have all thing in readiness against their come the sixteen of january the lord deputy receive the follow letter direct from her majesty to his lordship and the rest of the counsel for Ireland Elizabeth right trusty and well-beloved we greet you well the abuse which by the fraud of merchant do daily multiply in the course of exchange do cast upon we so great burden and we find they to be so impossible to be prevent by any caution that can be devise whereof we have sufficient proof in the like fruit that follow of the restraint make since the first proclamation publish as we can find no other way to remedy those inconvenience but by take from the merchant all benefit of exchange other then hereunder be mention in the form of a proclamation which we think good to be publish in that kingdom to make know to all man in what manner we intend to allow of the exchange from the day of the publish thereof which therefore you shall cause to be do immediate upon the receipt of these our letter and for that our intent be by this proclamation as you may perceive by the tenor thereof to explain all former proclamation and order touch this matter of the exchange and that from the day of the publish of this new declaration of our pleasure the same only be take for the rule of the exchange and no benefit of our former proclamation to be allow to any yet because in some of they there be some clause meet for we to be continue we have here under make a short note of those clause out of the say proclamation which clause our pleasure be that you cause to be take verbatim and insert into this proclamation when you shall publish it or else to express the substance of they in such word as you shall think fit or to alter or omit any of they or to add to this new direction for our advantage require you in your consideration of this our purpose to cast aside all private respect and only to aim at the ease of our great charge so far forth as it may be do without inconueniency of great moment to our state there than our charge be to Vs. the proclamation upon the alteration of the standard of our money in this realm whereunto we be lead aswell by example of our progenitor who have ever make a difference between the money of this realm and our realm of England as also by a necessary providence of keep the sterling money both from the hand of our rebel here and also from transportation into foreign country which chief by the say rebel and their factor be do we do erect a exchange for the use of all sort of our subject and other use intercourse between these 2 realm for convert of money of the new standard of this realm into sterling money in England and of english money into those of this realm reciprocal hope that the honest and upright carriage of merchant in a equal exercise of traffic between the two realm will have cause in the say exchange a indifferent and mutual commodity both to the merchant for his trade and to we for our payment and both their and our intention have concur in preserve the sterling money from the rebel and from transportation into foreign country but in this little time of experience which we have make thereof be not yet two year past we have find our expectation great deceive and the scope give the first institution of the exchange exceed abuse by the slight and cunning of merchant which though we do immediate upon the begin of the exchange discover to be breed yet do we not think that the same will ever have grow to such a height as since we have perceive wherefore we do by some restriction and limitation seek to contain those fraud within reasonable bound but it fall out that the remedy propose have be so far from the ease of the grief as whatsoever have be by we prescribe for the redress have but serve for a ground and pretext of new invention of deceit for that by the cunning craft of some merchant the scope give by our proclamation to the say exchange be so abuse as that some merchant who have bring commodity into that kingdom from hence have not be content to sell the same for reasonable gain but have raise his price of the same commodity to so much in the new money as do in their true value of silver almost countervail the sterling he pay for it here viz. that which cost he ten shilling sterling to thirty shilling irish after that rate that which cost he 100 pound to 300 pound he have return to our exchange the same 300 pound which be answer he here in sterling yield he profit of three for one which be so great a gain as no adventure of any merchant into the further most part of traffic do yield and to we such a burden as if the same shall be permit be nothing else in effect but to make our exchequer a mart for the cunning of merchant to work upon beside many of they have of purpose to make profit by the say exchange buy up old bill of debt from diverse person to who payment have upon just consideration be defer and compound for the same for small sum of money of the new standard return the whole upon we by exchange whereby they have make a exceed profit conrrary to the true meaning of our proclamation intend for the use and benefit of such as exercise a honest and direct course of merchandise by which fraud there be ever a great quanrity of money of the new standard return upon we for sterling money in this realm but neither be there any proportionable quantity of sterling money bring in here into the exchange nor deliver into the bank to be convert into new money there and consequent there do grow upon we a intolerable burden in continual payment of sterling money and yet the two mischief which be the chief cause of alteration of our standard not remedy that be the preserve of the sterling money from the rebel and from transportation into foreign country for little of it be bring in by merchant of this country and the same be not currant to be use here among our good subject we find it partly transport and partly fall into the hand of the rebel wherewith they have be the better enable to continue in their wicked course wherefore for redress of so great abuse daily practise by merchant we do hereby publish that our meaning be that from the day of the publish hereof the place of exchange of money shall be only at Dublyn for this our realm of Ireland and at London for our realm of England for all such as use the trade of merchandise but for other that be in our pay and have wage of we as be of our army or otherwise there shall be a bank maintain at cork as heretofore it be to receive their bill but the bill receive there shall be paiable only at London and for the use of passenger and soldier depart out of our realm into England there shall be likewise exchange at Bristol and Chester so as no such soldier or passenger do bring thither any bill contain above the sum pound but for merchant there shall not be at the say place of Chester and Bristol any payment of bill return but only at our city of London in such manner as be hereafter express and further our pleasure and meaning be that the say exchange shall extend only to such as now be or hereafter from time to time shall be in our pay here serve we in the field or in ward or garrison and to all officer of government of justice of our revenue or of the exchange and to such other as be contain in our establishment to all and every of who we be please to allow the benefit of exchange money of the new standard of this realm into monies currant in England want only twelve penny sterling in the pound viz. yearly to each of they rateable in his degree for so much as he do save above his expense of that which he do receive yearly of we or aught to receive clear for his pay all deduction and defalcation be foreprise and so as there be no fraud use by any of they in abuse this our liberality and favonr conttary to our true meaning and for other use trade of merchandise although they deserve no favour in regard of the fraud wherewith many of they have abuse our gracious meaning in the institution of our exchange intend and in regard of the excessive raise of the price of all ware whereby both our subject be extreme burden here and we intolerable charge in the exchange in England yet in regard of the present poverty of this our realm whereby we conceive that there want as yet for a time sufficient commodity of the growth or manufaction of this kingdom wherewith to maintain traffic we be please to maintain for their use a exchange in this manner that every such person not be of those that belong to our establishment but a merchant who shall deliver to the master of the exchange or his deputy in this realm one hundred pound whereof forty pound shall be of the standard of sterling money of silver or of gold and threescore pound in mix money of the new standard of this realm shall receive of the say master of the exchange or his deputy a bill direct to the bank of exchange in England where the same be playable whereby he shall receive for each hundred pound deliver here in that manner one hundred pound in monies currant of England want only twelve penny in the pound for each pound of the mix money deliver and for the starling no defalcation to be make as heretofore have be ordain and after that rate for more or less in quantity and to the end that the fraud use by some merchant may be better prevent and the master of the exchange or his deputy understand that he deal true in bring his money to the exchange our pleasure be that every such merchant resort to the exchange shall bring a certificate from the officer of our customhouse where his good be enter what good he have enter there and at what time to the end that it may thereby be discern that he seek nothing but the return of his own money and be not a cullourer of other man and sor that diverse noble man and gentleman of this realm have cause many time to repair into England either for suit or other necessary cause and some have child there either at the university or at the inn of court or chancery or in our service at court who shall have cause for those purpose to use sterling money and to have the money of this realm convert into money currant in England we be please that all such have the benefit of the exchange in such manner as for those of our army be above limit for such yearly sum of money as our deputy and counsel there for the time be shall think good to allow to any of they upon their demand and the warrant of our say deputy and counsel shall be sufficient warrant to the master of our exchange or his deputy for the receive of all such bill as they shall require he to admit for any such nobleman or gentleman and now have explain some part of the abuse offer to we in the exchange and declare our pleasure for the reformation of they we do not doubt but that as upon the former restriction by we propose to the same end so now many ill mind person will not stick to flander our do as though there be not in we a honourable meaning to perform what here we have promise whereof although our proceed shall by their true and just effect manifest the contrary yet because evil tongue accustom to calumniate the action of prince be sometime the instrument of alteration of people mind from their dutiful opinion of their sovereign where there be to we nothing so dear as the conservation of the love of our subject we do for prevent of any such malicious purpose require all magistrate and officer who have any charge in the government of that our kingdom to have a care to such evil rumour and to the spreader of they and such as they shall find to be author or instrument of divulge any slanderous speech touch this matter of the exchange to make they a example for other to be admonish by and to assure all man that this institution of base money in this kingdom have have his chief ground upon hope we have thereby to weaken the rebel of this kingdom who by the use of sterling money have and have mean to provide themselves from foreign part of all thing necessary to maintain their evil course and that the same be by this way partly and partly by power of our army once suppress we shall have just cause to restore the money of this realm to such estate as our progenitor have accustom to use here give under our signet at our palace of Westminster the four and twenty day of December 1602 be of our reign the five and forty year the clause of former proclamation touch the exchange meet now to be continue the use of sterling money or of any other than these new money prohibit upon penalty of imprisonment and fine all officer have power give they to seize the say money put in use and every informer allow the moiety of so much as he shall discover to allow for all sterling money of silver bring into the exchange with purpose to receive new money for the same gain of two shilling in the pound of new money for gold two shilling six penny gain of new money to allow ten in the hundred profit for all base silver money bring into the exchange counterfetter to be severe look to and punish all passenger come into Ireland to be search or put to their oath what sterling money they carry with they the same day his lordship and the counsel here receive this follow letter from the lord in England after our very hearty commendation to your lordship we have receive your letter of the seven of this instant together with a several note or abstract of some material point and doubt to be consider and resolve concern the last prescribe form of the exchange and as both your letter and abstract be address together for answer of her majesty letter late send unto you with a form of a proclamation thereunto annex so now you shall again receive the resolution of her majesty and we of her counsel touch the same matter only and the doubt by you propound which according to your desire we send with as much speed as a business of that importance reduce to a new deliberation can be dispatch for the liberty that her majesty do give you either of proceed or of respite and suspense to publish the proclamation according to the judgement you shall make of it upon consideration of any very dangerous effect that you shall find apparent or likely to ensue you have right acknowledge her majesty gracious respect unto you in who as the chief minister of that state she repose special confidence both for your care and wisdom and for the opportunity you have by the present contemplation of all thing near at hand and under your eye to discern and discover any inconueniency and to apply the medicine according and therefore although it please she to take that resolution together with we of her counsel which be set down by the say proclamation hope that it will be a mean to cure and prevent the intolerable fraud and enormity in the practice of the exchange which be intend and institute for the ease of her excessive charge and for the good of her subject there yet for as much as you have show so great a distrust and fear of dangerous consequence if you shall forthwith have proceed to the publish of that proclamation and upon advise consultation as her majesty assure herself have propound these point of doubtfulness that accompany your letter she be well please to give such credit to your opinion as that she have upon a new deliberation with we of her counsel cause a temper and moderation to be set down with the chief point whereof you doubt as may appear unto you by a form of a proclamation differ from the former and now send unto you wherein because you may ready observe the particular alteration from the former proclamation by compare both together we need not make rehearsal of they here for satisfaction of your doubt only we have think good to say somewhat concern the six and seven article in your abstract in which you make question what course be best to be hold for the discovery of the fraud use by merchant and other in their exchange and what mean be to be use that her majesty be not overburden in the exchange upon which question and your own opinion thereof deliver we can omit to make two observation the one that yourselves do acknowledge the intolerable fraud of merchant and other use in the exchange whereby not only her majesty gracious intention and meaning of the exchange have be extraordinary abuse but her subject in that realm by the excessive rate in the sale of all commodity have be unconscionable overcharge and therefore yourselves can deny but that it be very dangerous for the exchange to be uphold without remedy of these fraud the second that for asmuch as there can be any certain rule and order prescribe to avoid these fraud that shall be free from the evasion of cunning and deceitful person and the only remedy do consist in the careful and diligent oversight of her majesty minister to who that trust be commit her majesty think that as yourselves do true find the fault and abuse so none can better provide for their remedy than you that be there present and especial you the treasurer by who minister error her majesty have be so much prejudice and whereas especial cause of these fraud be impute to the multiplicity of the bill of exchange we shall most willing be of that mind to reduce all unto one place at Dublyn be it not that we find you the treasurer to vary in your opinion have signify heretofore by your particular letter to some of we that there be no possible way of remedy but by reduce all the bank to one place and yet by this letter joint with the rest of the counsel deliver your opinion for the establish of two place unto which opinion because we conceive you be win upon the consultation of that counsel we have apply our consent thereunto and to the end it may plain appear unto you how the merchant and other abuse the exchange do most fraudulent serve their turn both upon her majesty subject there if it be true as have be inform to we by person of good credit come from thence that they improve their commodity to a treble price and more in respect of that coin and likewise upon her majesty excessive loss by return of their money upon the exchange we have think good to send you a estimate or calculation of the gain that one of they may make and as it be to be suppose do make in this course upon the expense but of one hundred pound utter there in commodity make and raise thereupon but two for one whereby you may judge how unreasonable advantage may be further make upon the profit of three or four for one if the merchant be so ill dispose or can find the mean of a corrupt minister under the treasurer to combine with he and so we bid you right hearty well to fare from the court at white-hall the 24 of December 1602. a computation send over enclose in the former letter of the gain which a merchant may make by the exchange bring to the exchange in each one hundred pound forty pound sterling and suppose the merchant to be without sterling money in his store or without credit and to use the exchange direct if he convert one hundred pound sterling into ware and sell the same in Ireland at the rate of two for one viz. for two hundred pound irish he do thereby follow to have the benefit of the exchange he must have fourscore pound sterling which suppose that he buy at five shilling irish each twenty shilling sterling his fourscore pound sterling do cost he one hundred pound irish. then come he to the exchange with one hundred pound irish and fourscore pound sterling for both which the minister give he a bill to receive in England one hundred seventy five pound sterling for he must loose five pound of the exchange of the one hundred irish. then have he in his purse in England one hundred seventy five pound defalk his first stock which be one hundred pound rest clear to he seventy five pound and this he may do upon as many return as he make in a year if it be object that he can buy sterling money at so low a rate as for five and twenty shilling irish but that he do pay thirty shilling irish for twenty shilling sterling then be his gain the less by nineteen pound and yet shall he gain six and fifty pound but suppose such (@) merchant as be not in necessity to by sterling money with irish but that he they borrow it here of friend though he pay twenty pound in the hundred for (@) be his gain in this manner his hundred pound sterling convert into ware and sell in Ireland for two hundred pound irish he bring to the exchange one hundred and twenty pound irish and fourscore pound sterling borrow and receive a bill to be pay in England one hundred fourscore and fourteen pound lose six pound for the return of one hundred and twenty pound irish. so have he in his purse in England one hundred fourscore and fourteen pound out of which deduct one hundred pound which be the first stock rest to he fourscore and fourteen pound out of which gain allow he fourscore pound to pay for so much borrow by he yet rest to he fourteen pound and further he have remain in his hand in Ireland fourscore pound irish remain of his two hundred irish whereof he bring only one hundred and twenty pound to the exchange to have which fourscore pound return by the exchange he must borrow two and thirty pound sterling and so shall he have a bill to be pay in England one hundred and eight pound for he lose four pound for exchange of the fourscore pound irish out of which one hundred and eight pound abate the two and thirty pound borrow there rest gain seventy six pound whereunto add the fourteen pound above mention than the whole gain be fourscore and ten pound from whence take for the interest of one hundred and twelve pound borrow for three month after twenty in the hundred for a year which be for three month six pound twelve shilling and then his clear gain be towards his freight custom forbear the money and other charge fourscore and four pound eight shilling about the end of january the lord deputy return from Connaght to Dublyn and by the way receive letter from Rowry o donnel who now have undertake the prosecution of o Rorke and signify his determination to make a road present into his country and to leave some of his man to lie upon he in some place of convenient strength but his lordship be come to Dublyn receive another letter from the say rory o donnel upon the eighteen of February signify that O rorke strength be much increase by the repair of many chief rebel into his country so as for the present he be neither able to attempt o Rorke nor to defend himself from his attempt till the english force shall draw up to assist he the hasten whereof he pray and that he may have leave to put up his creaghte for a time towards Ballishannon for his better safety the five and twenty of February the lord deputy write this follow letter to the lord in England and send it by the hand of the lord precedent of monster at this time go for England may it please your lordship although I be unwilling to inform you often of the present estate of this kingdom or of any particular accident or service because the one be subject to so much alteration and the other light deliver unto all that be not present with such uncertainty and that I be loath to make any project unto your lordship either of my request to you or my own resolution here since so many thing fall sudden out which may alter the ground of either yet since I do write now by one that can so sufficient supply the defect of a letter I have presume at this time to impart unto your lordship that I think fit to be remember or do determine on most humble desire your l p. that if I err in the one or hereafter alter the other you will not impute it to my want of sincerity or constancy but to the nature of the subject whereof I must treat or of the matter whereon I work and first to present unto your lordship the outward face of the four province and after to guess as near as I can at their disposition monster by the good government and industry of the lord precedent be clear of any force in rebellion except some few unable to make any forcible head in Lemster there be not one declare rebel in Connaght there be none but in o rorke country in Ulster none but Tyrone and Brian Mac art who be never lord of any country and now do with a body of loose man and some creaght continue in glancomkynes or near the border thereof Connogh macgayre sometime lord of Fermannagh be banish out of the country who litre with o. Rorke and at this time o connor macgayre be possess of it by the queen and hold it for she I believe that general the lord of the country that be reclaim desire a peace though they will be waver till their land and estate be assure unto they from her majesty and as long as they see a party in rebellion to subsist that be of power to ruin they if they continue subject or otherwise shall be doubtful of our defence all that be out do seek formercy except o Rorke and o who be now with o Rorke and these be obstinate only one of their diffidence to be safe in any forgiveness the loose man and such as be only captain of bonnaghte as Tyrrill and Brian Mac art will nourish the war as long as they see any possibility to subsist and like ill humour have recourse to any part that be unsound the nobility town and english-irish be for the most part as weary of the war as any but unwilling to have it end general for fear that upon a peace will ensue a severe reformation of religion and in particular many border gentleman that be make poor by their own fault or by rebel (@) continue their spleen to they now they be become subject and have use to help themselves by stealth do never more use they nor better prevail in they than now that these submitty have lay aside their own defence and betake themselves to the protection and justice of the state and many of they have taste so much sweet in entertainment that they rather desire a war to continue they than a quiet harvest that may arise out of their own honest labour so that I do find none more pernicious instrument of a new war than some of these in the mean time Tyrone while he shall live will blow every spark of discontent or new hope that shall lie hide in any corner of the kingdom and before he shall be utter extinguish make many blaze and sometime set on fire or consume the next subject unto he I be persuade that his combination be already break and it be apparent that his mean to subsist in any power be overthrow but how long he may live as a wood-kerne and what new accident may fall out while he do live I know not ifit be impute to my fault that notwithstanding her majesty great force he do still live I beseech your lordship to remember how secure the bandito of Italy do live between the power of the king of Spain and the pope how many man of all country of several time have in such sort preserve themselves long from the great power of prince but especial in this country where there be so many difficulty to carry a army in most place so many unaccessable strength for they to fly unto and then to be please to consider the great work that first I have to break this main rebellion to defend the kingdom from a dangerous invasion of a mighty foreign prince with so strong a party in the country and now the difficulty to root out scatter troop that have so many unaccessible den to lurk in which as they be by nature of extreme strength and peril to be attempt so be it unpossible for any people natural and by art to make great use of they and though with infinite danger we do beat they out of one yet be there no possibility for we to follow they with such agility as they will fly to another and it be most sure that never traitor know better how to keep his own head than this nor any subject have a more dreadful awe to lay violent hand on their sacred prince than these people have to touch the person of their neales and he that have as pestilent a judgement as ever any have to nourish and to spread his own infection have the ancient swell and desire of liberty in a conquer nation to work upon their fear to be root out or to have their old fault punish upon all particular discontent and general over all the kingdom the fear of a persecution for religion the debase of the coin which be grievous unto all sort and a dearth and famine which be already begin and must of necessity grow short to extremity the least of which alone have be many time sufficient motive to drive the best and most quiet estate into sudden confusion these will keep all spirit from settle breed new combination and I fear even stir the town themselves to solicit foreign aid with promise to cast themselves into their protection and although it be true that if it have please her majesty to have long continue her army in great strength I shall the better have provide for what these cloud do threaten and soon and more easy either have make this country a raze table wherein she may have write she own law or have tie the ill dispose and rebellious hand till I have sure plant such a government as will have overgrow and kill any weed that shall have rise under it yet since the necessity of the state do so urge a diminution of this great expense I will not despair to go on with this great work through all these difficulty if we be not interrupt by foreign force although perchance we may be encounter with some new eruption and by often adventure with some diaster and it may be your lordship shall sometime hear of some spoil do upon the subject from the which it be impossible to preserve they in all place with far great force than ever yet be keep in this kingdom and although it have be seldom hear that any army have be carry on with so continual action and endure without any intermission of winter breathe and that the difficulty at this time to keep any force in the place where we must make the war but especial our horse be almost beyond any hope to prevent yet with the favour of God and her majesty fortune I do determine myself to draw into the field as soon as I have receive her majesty commandment by the commissioner who it have please she to fond over and in the mean time I hope by my own presence or direction to set every party on work that do adjoin or may be draw against any force that now do remain in rebellion in which journey the success must be in the hand of God but I will confident promise to omit nothing that be possible by we to be do to give the last blow unto the Rebe lion but as all pain and anguish impatient of the present do use change for a remedy so will it be impossible for we to settle the mind of this people unto a peace or reduce they unto order while they feel the smart of these sensible and apparent fear which I have remember to your lordship without some hope of redress or security therefore I will presume how unworthy soever I be since it concern the province her majesty have give i with all humbleness to lay before your grave judgement some few thing which I think necessary to be consider of and first whereas the alteration of the coin and take away of the exchange in such measure as it be first promise have breed a general grievance unto man of all quality and so many incommodity to all sort that it be beyond the judgement of any that I can hear to prevent a confusion in this estate by the conunuance thereof that at the least it will please your lordship to put this people in some certain hope that upon the end of the war this new standard shall be abolish or ease and that in the mean time the army may be favourable deal with in the exchange since by the last proclamation your lordship send over they do conceive their case will be more hard than any other for if they have allow they nothing but indefinite as much as they shall mere gaint out of their entertainment that will prove nothing to the great part for the only possibility to make they to live upon their entertainment will be to allow they exchange for the great part thereof since now they do not only pay excessive price for all thing but can hardly get any thing for this money and although we have presume to alter in show though not effect the proclamation in that point by retain a power in ourselves to proportion their allowance for exchange yet be it with a mind to conform our proceed therein according to your lordship next direction and therefore do humble desire to know your pleasure therein for our opinion of the last project it please your lordship to send we I do humble leave it to our general letter only as from myself I make overture to the counsel of the other you send direct only to myself and because I find they general to concur that it will prove as dangerous as the first I do not think it fit any otherwise to declare your lordship pleasure therein and whereas it please your lordship in your last letter to command we to deal moderate in the great matter of religion I have before the receipt of your lordship letter presume to advise such as deal in it for a time to hold a more restrain hand therein and we be both think ourselves what course to take in the revocation of what be already do with least encouragement to they and other since the fear that this course begin in Dublin will fall upon the rest be apprehend over all the kingdom so that I think your lordship direction be to great purpose and the other course may have overthrow the mean to our own end of reformation of religion not that I think too great preciseness can be use in the reform of ourselves the abuse of our own clergy church-living or discipline nor that the truth of the gospel can with too great vehemency or industry be set forward in all place and by all ordinary mean most proper unto itself that be first set forth and spread in meekness nor that I think any corporal prosecution or punishment can be too severe for such as shall be find seditious instrument of foreign or inward practice nor that I think it fit that any principal magistrate shall be choose without take the oath of obedience nor tolerate in absent themselves from public divine service but that we may be advise how we do punish in their body or good any such only for religion as do profess to be faithful subject to her majesty and against who the contrary can not be prove and since if the irish be utter root out there be much less likelihood that this country can be thereby in any time plant by the english since they be so far from inhabit well any part of that they have already and that more than be likely to be inhabit may be easy choose out and reserve in such place by the seaside or upon great river as may be plant to great purpose for a future absolute reducement of this country I think it will asmuch avail the speedy settle of this country as any thing that it will please her majesty to deal liberal with the irish lord of country or such as now be of great reputation among they in the distribution of such land as they have former possess or the state here can make little use of for her majesty if they continue as they ought to do and yield the queen as much commodity as she may otherwise expect she have make a good purchase of such subject for such land if any of they hereafter be disobedient to her law or break forth in rebellion she may when they shall be more divide ruin they more easy for example unto other and if it be think fit may plant english or other irish in their country for although there ever have be and hereafter may be small eruption in some place which at the first may easy be suppress yet the suffer they to grow to that general head and combination do questionless proceed from great error in the judgement here and may be easy as I think prevent hereafter and further it may please her majesty to ground her resolution for the time and number of the next abatement of the list of her army somewhat upon our poor advice from hence and to believe that we will not so far corrupt our judgement with any private respect as without necessity to continue her charge see we do thorough conceive how grievous it be unto her estate and that we may not be precise tie to a establishment that shall conclude the payment of the treasurer since it have ever be think fit to be otherwise till the come over of the earl of Essex and some such extraordinary occasion may fall out that it will be dangerous to attend your lordship resolution and when it will be safe to diminish the army here that there may be some course think of by some other employment to disburden this country of the idle swordman in who I find a inclination apt enough to be carry elsewhere either by some of this country of best reputation among they or in company as now they stand under english captain who may be reinforce with the great part of irish. that it may be leave to our discretion to make passage and bridge into country otherwise unaccessible and to build little pile of stone in such garrison as shall be think fit to be continual bridle upon the people by the commodity of which we may at any time draw the great part of the army together to make a head against any part that shall first break out and yet reserve the place only with a ward to put in great force as occasion shall require which I be persuade will prove great pledge upon this country that upon any urgent cause the queen may safe draw the great part of her army here out of the kingdom to be employ at least for a time elsewhere wherein I beseech your lordship to consider what a strength so many experience captain and soldier will be to any army of new man erect in England against a invasion or send abroad in any offensive war but until these place be build I can conceive how her majesty with any safety can make any great diminution of her army last I do humble desire your lordship to receive the further explanation of my meaning and confirmation of the reason that do induce i unto these proposition from the lord precedent of monster who as he have be a very worthy actor in the reducement and defence of this kingdom so do I think he to be best able to give you through account of the present estate and future providence for the preservation thereof wherein it may please your l p. to require his opinion of the hazard this kingdom be like to run if it shall by any mighty power be invade and how hard it will be for we in any measure to provide for the present defence if any such be intend and withal to go on with the suppression of these that be leave in rebellion so that we must either adventure the new kindle of this fire that be almost extinguish or intend only that leave the other to exceed peril and thus have remember to your lordship the most material point as I conceive that be fit for the present to be consider of I do humble recommend myself and they to your lordship favour from her majesty castle of Dublin this six and twenty of February 1602. at the same time the lord deputy write to the lord in England about his private affair wherein he signify that all manner of provision necessary for the maintenance of a household be of late especial buy at such excessive rate aswell in regard of the famine grow daily great in Ireland by the continual spoil of the country and the army cut down of the rebel corn for these last two year as also in regard of the disualuation of the mix coin now currant after the take away of exchange whereof each shilling have no more than two penny halfpenny silver in it and that the price of the say provision daily so increase as sour time the entertainment allow he by her majesty for his maintenance will not answer his ordinary expense except it will please their lordship to allow he exchange for the most part of his entertainment that thereby he may be enable to make his provision out of England in the begin of march the lord deputy understand that Brian Mac art have secret steal into Killoltagh with some five hundred man under his lead as he have late do the like but be soon drive out again by sir Arthur chichester whereupon his lordship send sir Richard moryson from Dublyn up to his garrison in lecayle and give he his lordship guard and three other company of foot to lead with he that he may assist sir Arthur chichester in the prosecution of this rebel who be soon drive out of Killoltagh by those force now because I have often make mention former of our destroy the rebel corn and use all mean to famish they let i by two or three example show the miserable estate to which the rebel be thereby bring sir Arthur chichester sir Richard moryson and the other commander of the force send against Brian Mac art aforesaid in their return homeward see a most horrible spectacle of three child whereof the elder be not above ten year old all eat and gnaw with their tooth the entrail of their dead mother upon who flesh they have feed twenty day past and have eat all from the foot upward to the bare bone roast it continual by a slow fire be now come to the eat of her say entrails in like sort roast yet not divide from the body be as yet raw former mention have be make in the lord deputy letter of carcase scatter in many place all dead of famine and no doubt the famine be so great as the rebel soldier take all the common people have to feed upon and hardly live thereupon so as they beside feed not only on hawk kite and unsavoury bird of prey but on horseflesh and other thing unfit for man feed the common sort of the rebel be drive to unspeakable extremity beyond the record of most history that ever I do read in that kind the ample relate whereof be a infinite task yet will I not pass it over without add some few instance captain trevor and many honest gentleman lie in the newry can witness that some old woman of those part use to make a fire in the field and diverse little child drive out the cattle in the cold morning and come thither to warm they be by they surprise kill and eat which at last be discover by a great girl break from they by strength of her body and captain trenor send out soldier to know the truth they find the child skull and bone and apprehend the old woman who be execute for the fact the captain of Carickfergus and the adjacent garrison of the northern part can witness that upon the make of peace and receive the rebel to mercy it be a common practice among the common sort of they I mean such as be not swordman to thrust long needle into the horse of our english troop and they die thereupon to be ready to tear out one another throat for a share of they and no spectacle be more frequent in the ditch of town and especial in waste country then to see multitude of these poor people dead with their mouth all colour green by eat nettle dock and all thing they can rend up above ground these and very many like lamentable effect follow their rebellion and no doubt the rebel have be utter destroy by famine have not a general peace short follow tyrones submission beside mercy former extend to many other by which the rebel have liberty to seek relief among the subject of Ireland and to be transport into England and France where great multitude of they live for some year after the peace make the four of march the lord deputy receive letter from sir Henry Dockwra advertise many vehement suspicion of sir Neale garue disloial purpose namely his underhand put Mac swine to go again into rebellion and to take a island of he which be a fit place to set up a new rebellion and also his make a storchouse of arm with extraordinary provision of they further he advertise that himself use all mean to keep Tyrone in the glynne where he now be till his lordship come up which journey he advise to be in the begin of the next month but understand that within few day he will remove towards fermanagh howsoever the english there at that time be weak yet he will lie for the arch-rebel on his way to omy or Agher not doubt but in the passage of those plain he shall have some opportunity of fight with he and at the least to take good part of his prey from he last he advertise that he have raze hen. ouingtons castle and Mac hugbe island which both have be nest and start hole for thief the fifteen of march the lord deputy leave Dublin and ride towards the northern border where his lordship with his rotinue lie to and fro the remain few day of this year and part of the begin of the next till Tyrone be receive to mercy and the war end to the end his lordship be in those part may give life to the present service as well of the force send to prosecute Ororke as of the garrison lie in wait for all advantage upon Tyrone himself and his break partaker touch monster affair in the year 1602 the lord deputy at his come from the affair of monster cork cause sir ric. Percy to be swear counsellor for the province of monster and in his journal towards Cillkenny knight three irish man John Fitz edmond and two citizen of Watterford Edward Gough and richard aylward the lord precedent at Killkenny take his leave of the lord deputy and make short journey by reason he be sickly come not to cork till the three of April 1602. when the spaniard by composition be to render the castle in the west o swillivan bear have surprise his castle of donboy in beerehaven from the spaniard where of some be kill in the surprisal which free they from suspicion to have yield it voluntary contrary to the composition this strong castle upon a excellent haven o keep for the king of Spain have sixty warder with he at first and three piece of spanish ordinance the lord precedent meaning to take this castle take the field the 23 of April and after many attempt upon the rebel in which some of they be kill and some take and execute and many prey take by party send out it be resolve the fourteen of may to pass the force over to a island call the great island that way to march to beerehaven the way thither by land be unpassable for the victual and carriage beside many place of advantage in the mountain where the rebel though few in number may distress a great army and easy forbid their passage here by the sea side the foot stay for the ship carry the victual munition and ordinance which be detain by contrary wind till the last of may. the six of june the force be ferry over to the land near castle Dermot where they encamp and though they land in another
