part then the rebel expect who lie there to hinder and impeach their land yet the rebel haste to they to begin the skirmish with they when they be in good order and almost have all pass the ferry so as the rebel have no advantage in this fight they leave 28 dead in the place and have more than 30 wound whereof captain tirrel be one be slight hurt in the belly and some be take prisoner whereas on our part only seven be hurt the ten of june our force have land their ordinance encamp within musket shoot of the castle of donboy but not within the sight of the castle a rise ground lie between the camp and the castle so as the great shot from the castle fly over the camp without do any hurt the twelve a fort within the hand of dorse keep by the rebel be surprise by the english and all the rebel kill or hang and therein be take three iron piece of spanish ordinance the 17 of june after two day battery the english assault the breach and possess part of the castle Dunboy the rebel keep and defend the rest all that day and night and great part of the next at which time the english be by force make full master of it the rebel defend it be 134 select soldier and all of they be kill in the castle or seek to fly or be prisoner be execute in the camp except twelve man of chief account and most esteem by Tyrrell which be keep to be examine upon torture or to work some good for the service with Tyrrell by the save of their life of spanish ordinance there be take one culverin two saker and one falcon of brass and two saker five minion and one falcon of iron the gunner be italian and spaniard who perish with the rest nine barrel of powder take in the castle be employ to blow it up leave any spaniard or rebel may after make use of it this castle take the lord precedent return to cork where sir Samuel bagnol attend his come with letter from the lord deputy and according to his lordship direction the lord precedent send by he 1500 foot be above the old mounsterlyst who come with these force to the lord deputy the 29 of july and bring letter from the lord precedent advertise the above mention confident expectation of a second spanish invasion at the same time sir Edward Wingfield be land at cork bring to the lord President 500 foot for supply of the weak company sir Charles wilmott governor of kerry wherein be many provincial rebel beside 1000 stranger to help they have before the siege of dunboy prosecute Mac morris clear kerry of all rebel and prosecute they into Desmond take castle and great prey of cow and bring the knight of kerry on his knee and this do he march towards the lord precedent in his way to dunboy and unite his force to the army after the take of that castle he be now again send into kerry with direction that all garrison shall burn the corn they can not gather and that he shall remove the irish inhabitant with their good to a country near Lymricke that the spaniard again expect may make no use of they in August the lord precedent be advertise that many in Carbery revolt and that upon a ship from Spain not long before arrive with money to distribute among the most active rebel Donnogh Mac carty and Finnen his brother who have attend the lord precedent at the siege of dunboy be now revolt and have take impress money from the king of Spain whereupon the two captain Roger and Gawen Haruy lie there in garrison have take many prey from they and spoil the country and yet by daily intelligence the lord precedent understand that the news of the take of Dunboy come into Spain the king have command to stay all his provision for Ireland till his pleasure be further signify and no doubt the queen fleet lie at this time upon the coast of Spain most of all discourage he from any new attempt in succour of the irish rebel about the end of August it be general divulge in monster that a spanish fleet be discover upon the coast whereupon the irish post up and down the country with great sign of joy so as at the lord president suit sir Samuel Bagnol be send back to he with the force he have former lead out of monster to the lord deputy the second of September the lord precedent receive this follow gracious letter write from the queen with her own hand your sovereign e. r. my faithful George how ioy we be that so good event have follow so troublesome endeavour laborious care and heedful travel you may guess but we best can witness and do protest that your safety have equal the most thereof and so God even bless you in all your action about this time the lord precedent have receive manifest proof that Cormock Dermod lord of Muskery have late commit many act of treason cause he to be apprehend and commit prisoner to the gentleman porter and hear his follower practise his escape give the say gentleman porter charge to keep he safe upon his danger to answer for he in the mean time seize all his castle into her majesty hand and like wise cause his wife and child to be bring prisoner to cork notwithstanding cormocke escape out of a window the nine and twenty of September yet be hearten to rebellion by captain Tyrrell and Oswillivan bear he consider that his castle be all in the queen power his elder son late student in Oxford be now keep prisoner in the tower that his young son his wife and daughter and many of his chief follower be now prisoner at cork and that the rebel desire to join with he be hunger-starved and will live upon his country already waste and therefore he wise choose to submit himself to her majesty mercy and upon the two and twenty of October this his submission be accept about this time the lord precedent hear that o donnel be dead in Spain the three and twenty of October sir Samuel bagnol with the regiment send back from the lord deputy fall by night upon tyrrel camp lie in Muskery to expect cormock return kill eighty of his man make he fly away in his shirt take all his cattle be more than one thousand with sixty horse and hackney beside thing unseeve in irish spoil as velvet outlandish apparel spanish coin and all the money Tyrrell have get of the proportion send from Spain and make Tyrrell fly into the mountain of Desmond in November sir Charles wilmott break by night into the quarter of the knight of kerry kill forty of his man take five hundred cow two hundred garron and two month provision of meal the rebel Tyrrell burke o swillivan and Mac morris be daily assault by the english and spoil of their cattle the rest of this month and the follow of December and have many of their best man kill sudden fall into disputation and after to controversy and so the stranger resolve to steal away as they do with great amazement leave the fastness they have hold to the ransack of the english first Tyrrell than William bourke who lead 1500 man march towards the pale sir Charles wilmott have first in another conflict with they kill many of the most forward kerne take all their baggage and prey of cattle be 2000 cow 4000 sheep and 1000 garron in December the lord precedent leave sir Charles wilmott to command in chief all the force have beside the lord barry with 1600 provincial under he to attend such service as he shall direct leave the province of monster to meet the lord deputy at Galloway in Connaght in the mean time the say rebel flee towards the pale as break man some resolve to join with Tyrone and some to return into Connaght their own country wherewith the monster rebel be so daunt as they daily come in to sir Charles wilmott in great number and with much cattle to submit themselves to mercy the lord precedent before his journey into Connaght take order that o bear country shall be so waste as neither spaniard nor rebel shall find relief there about this time captain Taaffe command our irish man in Carbery assail a band of rebel lead by a priest the pope nuntio kill he with most of his man and get all their cattle and now in the absence of o flee away his country be waste and his castle all take the foresay priest be a man of special authority so as upon his death the Mac carty and all Carbery submit to mercy and have power over all spiritual live in Ireland so as all priest depend upon he the lord precedent return into monster in january from Connaght and have send sir Edward Wingfeild with certain company of foot into Connaght according to the lord deputy direction and leave sir Charles wilmott and sir g. thornton commissioner to govern monster himself in the begin of February ride to Dublyn leave no rebel in monster but Mac morris the knight of the Glan Thomas Oge and connor o driscoll not able joint to make two hundred man whereof Mac morris in few day be well beat and spoil of all he have by sir char. wilmott and in the begin of march the l. President sail into England from Dublin chap. 2 of tyrones take to mercy whereby the war be full end and of a new mutiny of the city of monster for establish the public exercise of the roman religion with the appease thereof in the begin of the year 1603. together with the lord deputy recall into England and the reward there give he for his service in the begin of the year 1603 with mention of his untimely death within few year after and a word of the state of Ireland some ten year after the five and twenty of march in the begin of the year 1603 the lord deputy write this follow letter from Mellifant sir garret Moor house to master secretary in England sir I have receive by captain hayes her majesty letter of the six of February wherein I be direct to send for Tyrone with promise of security for his life only and upon his arrival without further assurance to make stay of he till her pleasure shall be further know and at the same time I receive another from her majesty of the seventeen of February wherein it please she to enlarge the authority give unto i to assure he of his life liberty and pardon upon some condition remember therein and withal I receive a letter from yourself of the eighteen of February recommend to i your own advice to fulfil as far as I possible can the meaning of her majesty first letter and signify her pleasure that I shall seek by all the best mean I can to promise he his pardon by some other name then earl of Tyrone and rather by the name of baron of Dungannon or if it needs must be by the name of some other earl second to deliver he his country in less quantity and with less power than before he have it and last to force he to clear his pace and passage make difficult by he against any entry into his country and now since it have please her majesty by so great a trust to give i so comfortable argument of her favour I be encourage the more free to presume to declare myself in this great matter which I call great because the consequence be great and dangerous to be deal in without the warrant of her gracious interpretation and though my opinion herein shall proceed from a long and advise consideration describe with large and many circumstance and confirm with strong and judicial reason yet because I think it fit to hasten away this messenger I will write of these thing somewhat though on the sudden and commit the rest to the sufficient judgement and relation of the lord precedent now in his journey towards you and the rather because I find he to concur with i in the apprehension of this cause and of the state of all other thing of this kingdom and first for her majesty first letter I pray you sir believe i that I have omit nothing both by power and policy to ruin he and utter to cut he off and if by either I may procure his head before I have engage her royal word for his safety I do protest I will do it and much more be ready to possess myself of his person if by only promise of life or by any other mean whereby I shall not direct scandal the majesty of public faith I can procure he to put himself into my power but to speak my opinion free I think that he or any man in his case will hardly adventure his liberty to preserve only his life which he know how so well to secure by many other way for if he fly into Spain that be the least whereof he can be assure and most man but especial he do make little difference between the value of their life and liberty and to deceive he I think it will be hard for though wise man than he may be overreach yet he have so many eye of jealousy awake that it will be unpossible to charm they and I do upon assure ground believe that it be nothing but fear of his safety that of a long time especial of late have keep he from conformity to the state and if any thing do keep he now from accept the low condition and from settle himself and his hart to a constant serve of her majesty it will be fear of a absolute forgiveness or the want of such a estate as may in any measure content he the danger of his subsist as he do be either if there come no foreign force to maintain still a loose head of rebellion which will be better able to offend any such as be become subject than we can be if we be a thousand time more to defend they at all time and in all place to stir up and to maintain all humour and to be a wound remain open unto which they may have recourse and upon all accident be ready to swell or to infect the whole body of this kingdom otherwise if there shall be any invasion to be a powerful and politic head to draw this country to their assistance if there come no foreign force and that he shall be cut off yet be it likely some other in the nature of a spoil outlaw will arise up in his place as ill as himself and if he be keep prisoner the like effect will arise as if he be dead if he be cut off or keep prisoner and the spaniard shall arrive most of the swordman will flock unto they for advantage of pay and the discontentment of lord of country will be as great or great then if he be among they and therefore they as likely to fall then as now to the spanish party but if it be possible to make he a good subject the use her majesty may make of he must be among these people since during his life and liberty none will aspire to that place of o Neale which do carry with it so great a interest in the north and what interest he have he may be lead to employ to suppress and settle the mind of the people to government and have once declare himself to be a dutiful subject it will be first a great discouragement for the spaniard to come and if they do come if he continue honest his presence and interest will sway the north from give they assistance or annoy the subject if we withdraw our garrison and make the rest of Ireland more advise how they declare themselves against the State sir to conclude because I can short express my own mind herein I think it best if it please her majesty to receive he to her mercy so that first his submission be make in as humble sort and as much for her majesty honour as can be devise and then that she assure he of absolute forgiveness and forget of his fault and as much honour and profit as he have before provide that we take from he as much as possible we may those lock wherein his chief strength lie otherwise I be persuade either the queen shall not serve she own turn by he if she keep he prisoner or he will serve his turn if he live at liberty and ever have a affection to relapse how I be resolve to proceed in this business you shall know by the lord precedent which notwithstanding many thing may alter but for the substance I do think we shall be able to compass as much as by her majesty last letter be require and by you write after that except that point of the take from he the title of the earldom of Tyrone for the which I think there be many reason that it shall not be much stand upon beside what I have write before of give he contentment which may be apply to this first you do but give he a title which he do shake of as a mark of his bondage and that which he fall from to accept this he do asmuch prefer before this as the estate of a absolute prince before the condition of a subject and it be the name of o Neale with the which he have do so much mischief that be fatal and odious and not the name of Tyrone which he be sane to leave before he can have power to become a rebel for believe i out of my experience the title of our honour do rather weaken than strengthen they in this country and if you give he the same degree but with another name it may be think a condition rather by he obtain then by we impose especial if he enjoy his country and last if you make he only baron of Dungannon you leave in he a spur to discontentment without any great bridle from do hurt for his power will be never the less and yet he that do not sit easy will ever think of another seat and his own title will the more run in his mind the more he be unsatisfied with this new notwithstanding all my opinion of these thing I will run as near as I can to the straight line of her majesty pleasure and I presume I will so handle this matter that I will be sure her majesty honour shall not be endanger I mean by the authority she have give i which any man shall hardly take notice of till I be assure upon what term I shall find he and if his request be not as humble as become he or as by her majesty be require he shall make little use of any negotiation that shall be with he and so sir etc. touch the receive of Tyrone to mercy no man shall take from i the reputation the earl of Tyrone receive to mercy such as it be to have be the instrumental cause of do this honour to my decease sovereign and my nation and of give this disgraceful blow to the arch-traitor Tyrone that he humble submit himself to queen Elizabeth find mercy at her royal foot who he have proud offend and who sole power in despite of his domestical associate and foreign support have bring he on his knee and that the victory be full achieve by the sole sword of the english nation and well affect english-irish who blood he have spill and that so the arch-traitor lose the mean long to subsist in rebellion by the advantage of England unsettle estate or at least the advantage and the vainglory to fasten merit on the sacred majesty of king james the say queen happy successor by submit to his royal mercy and so hide the extreme misery in which he be plunge to have make this his action seem altogether voluntary and every way noble in he to which he be force by the high constraint and in the most base manner that can be imagine now as no man know the circumstance of this action better than myself so I will brief and true relate they queen Elizabeth have be sick for more than a month space and of some apparent danger of her death the lord deputy have be advertise and at this time she be dead depart the four and twenty of march the last day of the year pass though it be not know ne to the lord deputy till the seven and twenty of march in the night nor public or to Tyrone himself till the five of April after his humble submission make before the lord deputy to the queen as then live though indeed she be dead this business pass in manner follow there be a gentleman among the voluntary follower of the lord deputy who have long be earnest ambitious of the honour of knighthood which by no endeavour of service expense of money or assistance of friend he can hitherto attain now a servant of his post from London and get a happy passage at sea come upon the 27 of march late in the night to Mellifant where the lord deputy then lay and bring with he the first news of the queen death which when he have relate to his master he have be long please to take my advice in his affair advertise i of these news and bring his servant to confirm the same in my hear whereupon I require his servant not to speak a word thereof to any man threaten he with the lord deputy displeasure and severe punishment if any such rumour be spread by he then I be bold to give his master confidence of receive the honour he desire if he will follow my advice which be this that he shall go to the lord deputy and tell he this report of the queen death bring by his servant and the strict charge he have give unto he for the conceal thereof till his lordship shall think fit to make it know and withal to make tender of himself and all his mean to follow his lordship fortune in this doubtful time for such it be in expectation though most happy in event the gentleman do as I advise he and for his particular it take the same effect which I expect as I will show when I have first set down how his lordship hereupon proceed with Tyrone the lord deputy be warrant by the queen letter above write to receive tirone to her majesty mercy have upon the five and twenty of march send sir William godolphin and sir garret Moor to treat with he for which they have a commission in these word Mountioy whereas the earl of Tyrone have make humble suit unto we that upon his penitent submission to her majesty mercy we will be please to send some gentleman to who he may make know his humble petition and impart somewhat to they that do much concern her majesty service for the great trust we repose in you and the good opinion we conceive of your discreet judgement we have make choice of you to be employ herein and do by these present give you both joint and several our absolute warrant and authority upon this occasion of her majesty service to parley and confer with he or any of his adherent or follower provide that of this your conference you shall with all convenient speed give we knowledge in all particular and of all his and your proceed herein to the end you may receive our further direction and for so do this shall be your sufficient warrant give at Tredagh the four and twenty of march 1602. to our trusly and well belove sir William godolphin and sir garret more knight when I have write this commission his lordship command i to write this follow protection Mountioy whereas upon the humble suit and submission of Hugh earl of Tyrone and his penitent contrition for his former offence by many message and letter signify unto we we have think good to receive into her majesty most gracious protection his own person and such as shall come in his company with safety to he and they and the rest of his follower whatsoever dwell in the county of Tyrone or now abide with he aswell in their body as good for and during the space of three week to the end he may repair unto we to let we more full understand his humble petition these be straight to charge and command all and every her majesty officer minister and subject to permit and suffer he and they peaceable to enjoy the benefit thereof without any restraint molestation or hostile act against he or he in their body or good during the time above limit so as in the mean time and they continue of good and dutiful behaviour towards her majesty and this State give at Tredagh the four and twenty of march 1602. to all commander of horse and foot and to all other her majesty officer and subject to who it may appertain likewise his lordship command i to write several letter to the governor of garrison require they to give Tyrone and his follower full benefit of this protection and these write be all sign by the lord deputy be deliver to sir William godolphin with charge that when Tyrone be in his company and on the way to come to his lordship than and not before he shall deliver he the protection and likewise the letter to be send to the several garrison by his own messenger these commissioner on the six and twenty of march send one bath from armagh to Tyrone to prepare the way of their meeting the seven and twenty both the commissioner come to Charlemont where sir William godolphin stay for his troop of horse but sir garret Moor ride that night to tullough-oge where he speak with Tyrone the eight and twenty sir garret Moor write to sir William that Tyrone be full resolve to obey the lord deputy commandment and will meet he the next morning at nine of the clock to ride forward in his company to the lord deputy and Henry hagan who bring this letter give sir William confident assurance of tyrones performance the same eight and twenty day the l. deputy be at Mellifant and there have the foresay notice of the queen death and consider that this rumour be no good ground for a new treaty with Tyrone yet break out be it true or false may cause new combustion in Ireland most apt to relapse into new tumult as appear by the ensue mutiny of the very city and corporate town as also that if it be true than he have no power from the succeed king to receive Tyrone to mercy yea that in case it shall prove false than such treaty with the archtraitor in any other then queen Elizabeth name may prove very dangerous to he for these reason he resolve speedy to strike up the former treaty with Tyrone and so present dispatch a horseman to sir William godolphin to advertise he thereof and to require he to hasten tyrones come by remembrance to he that his former delay in treaty have much incense the queen and by threaten he that if he make the least delay of his submission his power to do he good may be easy restrain and then he shall expect nothing from he but a sharp prosecution to his utter ruin sir William have receive these his lordship and sir garret aforesaid letter think it no time to stand nice upon term of equality which may argue his distrust of Tyrone and awaken in he his old jealousy of our meaning to he and therefore leave order that his troop shall follow he do ride from Charlemont and meet Tyrone on the nine and twenty of march at nine of the clock in the morning at Toker a place lie five mile beyond Dungannon where show he the lord deputy protection he most humble and thankful accept thereof and so commit himself to the commissioner to ride in their company to the lord deputy by the way they deliver his protection to his own hand and likewise the letter which he be to send to the several governor by his own messenger on the thirty of march 1603. they come all together to Mellifant in the afternoon where Tyrone be admit to the lord deputy chamber kneel at the door humble on his knee for a long space make his penitent submission to her majesty and after be require to come near to the lord deputy perform the same ceremony in all humbleness the space of one hour or there about the next day he also make a most humble submission in writing sign with his own hand in manner and form follow as appear upon record i Hugh oneale by the queen of England France and Ireland her most gracious favour create earl of Tyrone do with all true and humble penitency prostrate myself at her royal foot and absolute submit myself unto her mercy most sorrowful implore her gracious commiseration and appeal only to her princely clemency without presume to justify my unloyall proceed against her sacred majesty only most sorrowful and earnest desire that it may please her majesty rather in some measure to mitigate her just indignation against i in that I do religious vow that the first motive of my unnatural rebellion be neither practice malice nor ambition but that I be induce first by fear of my life which I conceive be seek by my enemy practice to stand upon my guard and after most unhappy lead to make good that fault with more heinous offence the which in themselves I do acknowledge deserve no forgiveness and that it be impossible for i in respect of their greatness in any proportion even with my life to make satisfaction I do most humble desire her majesty to pardon they that as I have be already a sufficient argument of her royal power have little leave but my life to preserve itself so that it may now please her majesty to make i a example of her princely clemency the chief ornament of her high dignity and that I may be the better able hereafter with the uttermost service of my life to redeem the foulness of my fault I do most humble sue unto her majesty that she will vouchsafe to restore i to my former dignity and live in which estate of a subject I do religious vow to continue for ever hereafter loyal in all true obedience to her royal person crown prerogative and law and to be in all thing as far and as dutiful conformable thereunto as I or any other nobleman of this realm be bind by the duty of a subject to his sovereign or by the law of this realm utter renounce and abiut the name and title of o Neale or any other authority or claim which have not be grant or confirm unto i by her majesty and that otherwise by the law of this realm I may not pretend inst interest unto and I do religious swear to perform so much as be above mention and the rest of these article subscribe by my own hand as far as shall any way lie in my power and to deliver such pledge for the performance thereof as shall be nominate unto i by the lord deputy I do renounce and abjure all foreign power whatsoever and all kind of dependency upon any other potentate but her majesty the queen of England France and Ireland and do vow to serve she faithful against any foreign power invade her kingdom and to discover true any practice that I do or shall know against her royal person or crown and namely and especial I do abjure and renounce all manner of dependency upon the king or estate of Spain or treaty with he or any of his confederate and shall be ready with the uttermost of my ability to serve her majesty against he or any of his force or confederate I do absolute renounce all challenge or intermeddle with the vriaght or softer with they or other neighbour lord or gentleman out of my country or exact any black rent of any vriaght or border lord I do resign all claim and title to any land but such as shall be now grant unto i by her majesty letter patent last as the only be a subject do include all the duty of a subject so will I be content to be inform and advise by her magistrate here and will be conformable and assist unto they in any thing that may tend to the advancement of her service and the peaceable government of this kingdom as namely for the abolish of all barbarous custom contrary to the law be the seed of all incivility and for the clear of difficult passage and place which be the nursery of rebellion wherein I will employ the labour of the people of my country in such sort and in such place as I shall be direct by her majesty or the lord deputy and counsel in her name and will endeavour for myself and the people of my country to erect civil habitation and such as shall be of great effect to preserve we against thief and any force but the power of the state by the which we must rest assure to be preserve as long as we continue in our duty this submission be present by the earl of Tyrone kneel on his knee before the lord deputy and counsel and in the presence of a great assembly at the same time the earl promise to write unto the king of Spain for the recall of his son from thence into Ireland and to do the same at such time and in such word as the lord deputy shall direct likewise he vow to discover how farre he have proceed with the king of Spain or any other foreign or domestical enemy for past or future help and combination then the lord deputy in the queen name promise to the earl for himself and his follower her majesty gracious pardon and to himself the restore of his dignity of the earldom of Tyrone and of his blood and likewise new letter patent for all his land which in his former letter have be grant to he before his rebellion except only the country possess by Henry Oge oneale and the fues possess by Turlogh Mac Henry to both which at their submission the lord deputy have former promise that they shall hold the same immediate from the queen to which end this exemption and reservation be now make of these country and the dispose of they leave to her majesty power and likewise except and reserve three hundred acre of land to be lay to the fort of Mountioy and three hundred more to the fort of Charlemont during her majesty pleasure to hold any garrison in the say fort to these exemption of Henry Oge and Turlogh Mac Henry their country and themselves from the earl right or power he give his full consent as likewise to the reservation of the land lay to the say fort he promise to reduce his country to pay her majesty like composition as Connaght now do and for long time have pay and to answer rise out of soldier and all charge for advance her majesty service the three of April the lord deputy have the earl of Tyrone in his company ride to Tredagh and from thence upon the four day to Dublyn the next day a english ship arrive in that haven in which come sir Henry Davers who bring with he letter from the lord in England advertise the queen death and that james the first be proclaim king of England Scotland France and king james proclaim Ireland the copy of which proclamation they send to the end it shall here be publish in like sort also in the same ship come one master Liegh kinsman to the lord deputy who bring his lordship a favourable letter from the king out of Scotland this master Liegh his lordship present grace with the honour of knighthood and concern the gentleman former speak of who servant bring the first news of the queen death I be not deceive in the honour I do ominate to he as I have former write for after he have follow my advice in the manner of his impart that important news to the lord deputy his lordship conceive so good a opinion of he for his discretion and for the particular affection he have express towards he by the tender of his service in follow his fortune this doubtful time as his lordship do not only by the way from meltfant to Dublyn extraordinary grace he and often call he not without some admiration of the better sort of his train to ride by his side talk familiar with he but now upon his arrival to Dublyn upon this occasion of honour his cousin Leigh do also knight he in the mean time according to the lord deputy commandment the counsellor of the state the nobleman knight and chief commander of the army then be at Dublyn assemble together in the castle to who his lordship make know the queen death and the king proclamation which he first than all in course sign and present take horse with joyful acclamation publish the same through the chief street of Dublyn I can omit to mention that the earl of Tyrone upon the first hear the lord deputy relation of the queen death can not contain himself from shed of tear in such quantity as it can not well be conceal especial in he upon whole face all man eye be cast himself be content to insinuate that a tender sorrow for loss of his sovereign mistress cause this passion in he but every dull understanding may easy conceive that thereby his heart may rather be more ease of many and continual jealousy and fear which the guilt of his offence can not but daily present he after the great security of pardon and there need no Oedipus to find out the true cause of his tear for no doubt the most humble submission he make to the queen he have so high and proud offend much eclipse the vain glory his action may have carry if he have hold out till her death beside that by his come in as it be between two reign he lose a fair advantage for by England estate for the present unsettle to have subsist long in rebellion if he have any such end or at least a ample occasion of fasten great merit on the new king if at first and with free will he have submit to his mercy which he will have pretend to do only of a honourable affection to his new prince and many will in all likelihood have believe so much especial they to who his present misery and ruin estate be not at all or not full know the six of April the earl of Tyrone make a new submission to the king in the same form he have do to the queen the name only change he also write this follow letter to the king of Spain it may please your most excellent majesty have since the first time that ever I receive letter from your highness father and your majesty or write letter unto you perform to the uttermost of my power whatsoever I promise insomuch as in the expectation of your assistance since the repair of o donnel to your majesty I continue in action until all my near kinsman and follower have forsake i I be enforce as my duty be to submit myself to my lord and sovereign the beginning of this instant month of April in who service and obedience I will continue during my life therefore and for that grow old myself I will glad see my son settle in my life time I have think good give your majesty all thanks for your princely usage of my son Henry during his be in Spain most humble to desire you to send he unto i and for the poucrtie whereunto I be drive I have in sundry letter both in irish and other language so signify the same as it be inconvenient herein to make relation thereof and so I most humble take my leave from Dublin etc. your highness poor friend that be Hugh Tyrone together with the same he write another letter to his son Henry to hasten his come from Spain into Ireland but without any effect last the lord deputic renew to the earl of Tyrone his majesty protection for a long time till he can sue out his pardon and send he back into his country to settle the same and to keep his friend and former confederate in better order upon this change of the State sir Henry Davers who late bring letter to the lord deputy from the lord in England return back with purpose to repair present unto the king whereupon the lord deputy commend to his relation the follow instruction sign with his lordship hand wherein you must note that his lordship omit the news of the queen death receive by the servant of a gentleman as aforesaid the same be only a private inteliigence whereupon he can not safe build his late proceed and that his lordship only insist upon letter from the state which can only give warrant to the same the instrustion be these you be to inform the king majesty that at your come over hither the five hereof with the letter from the lord in England signify the decease of my late scueraigne mistress you find with i here at Dublin the earl of Tyrone new come in upon protection and by that mean the rcalme for the present general quiet all expect that upon a conclusion with he which then every one conceive to be likely in as much as he put himself into my hand which till that time he will never do to any the country will in short time be thorough settle so that every one that find himself in danger do press i in a manner hourly for his pardon foresee that he that stay out long be sure to be make the example of the justice of the state where such as can soon make their way by assure their future loyalty and service be hopeful to lay hold upon their sovereign mercy now to the end you may acquaint his majesty how far forth I have proceed with the earl of Tyrone and upon what warrant you shall be hereby thus remember he have often make great mean to be receive to mercy which as often I have deny he prosecute he to the uttermost of my ability be ever confident in opinion that until I have bring he very low and drive he out of his own country as I do the last summer and leave garrison upon he that take most of the creaght and spoil the rest of his good he will not be make fit to crauc mercy in that humble manner that be beseem so great a offender in December last when I be at Galloway he importune i by many message and letter and by some that he trust very well vow much sincerity if he may be hearken unto there and at that time he send i a submission frame in as humble manner as I can reasonable require to that I send he this answer that I will recom it to her majesty but until I have further direction from she I will still prosecute he as I do before and get his head if I can and that be all the comfort I give he yet cease he not to continue a suitor with all the earnestness that he can devise hope in the end to obtain that he desire in the month of march I receive letter from her majesty of the sixteen and of February whereby I be authorize to give he my word for his come and go safe and to pardon he so as he will come parsonal where I shall assign he to receive it and yield to some other condition in the last of those two letter contain and withal I be special require above all thing to drive he to some issue present because her majesty then conceive that contrariety of success here or change of accident in other part may turn very much to her disadvantage for which she be still apt to believe that he lie in wait and will spin out all thing further than be requisite with delay and shift if I shall not abridge he short after the earl renew his former suit with very great earnestness and in most humble manner as may appear by his letter in march send i to Tredagh whether I be then draw upon special occasion of sevice I think it fit to entertain the offer of his submission and to draw on the speedy conclusion of so important a business both for that the daily intelligence out of Spain threaten danger unto this kingdom and for that I have then receive advertisement from the counsel in England of her majesty dangerous sickness the least of which accident may have reviue his hope add new life unto his languish partisan and utter change the whole frame of my proceed to this end I sign his protection for three week with several warrant to the border garrison of forbearance from do any hostile act either upon his person and the person of his follower or upon their good during the term aforesaid appoint sir garret Moor a gentleman well deserve of the state and out of ancient acquaintance with the earl much respect by he to repair unto he and to give he knowledge that if simple and plain according to the tenor of his humble request he be resolve without any delay to present his petition unto i in his own person where I assign his appearance he shall then receive a protection for his safe come and return with assurance for his people and good during his absence by the hand of sir William godolphin who I have purposely send into those part with a sufficient guard to attend his resolution and to bring he safe unto i these condition though at first seem somewhat hard as both taste of too great a humbleness and not utter free from danger of his person who head be set to sale by a public act and private man not bind to take knowledge of the present proceed find easy acceptance than almost any man will have imagine the earl peremtory command that none of he upon what pretence soever shall presume to dissuade he from obey this summons see no way of mediation be leave unto he save only this vow in the presence of a great many that although the deputy heavy hand have almost bring he to the height of misery yet shall no mortal power have extort from he a submission of this nature but that out of long and earnest observation of his proceed he have find reason to hope that when his lordship shall discover the unfeigned penitency of his heart for his forepast misdeed with a firm resolution to redeem his offence by faithful serve her majesty and well deserve of the state during the whole remainder of his life that he shall then find from he as great commiseration of his present suffering and as charitable a repair against the threaten ruin of his house posterity and poor distress country as he have tat bitterness in the whole course of his former prosecution thus persuade he leave direction for settle his country the best he may on such a sudden and with a guard of 50 horse under the lead of sir William godolphin make great march until he come unto i within three mile of Tredagh fall there down on his knee before a great assembly confess his unworthiness yet humble crave her majesty mercy which as above all earthly thing he protest to desire so he vow with the uttermost of his power to deserve the same it be too long to set down all that pass in this first interview he strive to express in all his speech and gesture the low degree of humbleness to i that be to value and to maintain the greatness of her state and place who he so high have offend the next morning I send for he the treasurer at war be only present with i and make he see how well I understand his present condition how unpossible it be for he to subsist even in the poor and most contemptible fashion of a Woodkerne if her majesty be but please to employ the present instrument of his ruin final find he most sensible both of his estate and the queen high favour in remit his crime I promise he her gracious pardon on those condition mention in the memorial send by your hand from thence he attend i to Tredagh and so to Dublin the four of April where the next day I receive letter from the nobility in England signify the death of our late sovereign whereupon I call together the counsel and such of the nobility as be in town and acquaint they with the content thereof I propound also the present proclaim of his majesty whereunto all most willing agree and among they the earl of Tyrone and when they have set their hand to the proclamation all together do accompany i the deputy to the publish thereof in the city since that time I think fit to dismiss the earl of Tyrone into his own country the better to retain his people and partisan in good order but first we take from he a new submission to his majesty sign by his hand which now I send by you also you shall inform his majesty that now there be no rebel in Ireland who have not sue to be receive to the king mercy and that I think fit to yield the same to most of they leave only some few to be prosecute to utter ruin for a example and terror to other ill affect subject wherein I desire to know his majesty pleasure last you be to present my humble suit unto his majesty to be discharge of this government or if it shall please his majesty to employ i further herein yet that he will vouchsafe i leave to kiss his royal hand which I desire not only out of my particular affection to have the happiness to see he but also out of my desire to inform he thorough of the present estate of this kingdom wherein I presume that I shall be able to do his majesty very good service and if it shall not please his majesty to resolve for the present on some other man to undertake this government but only to leave the authority to some fit man hand during my absence and if he be resolve to make choice among those that be here present and therein shall require my opinion you shall say that although I will not presume to recommend any to his majesty yet I do think sir George Cary treasurer at war to be most fit for that place who have already be lord justice of this kingdom and howsoever he be no soldier yet be well acquaint with the business of the war wherein he have be ever very industrious to advance the service at the same time the lord deputy send over master Richard Cook one of his secretary to negotiate his affair in Court and because his lordship desire to retain the superintendency of this government with title of lord lieutenant and with two three part of the lord deputy allowance in regard no man be able to support the place of lord deputy with the other three part of that allowance except he have other great fee and place of commodity in this kingdom his lordship nominate as before sir George Cary to be most fit for that place some other counsellor be in this one point join with he namely to sign all such warrant as shall be sign for the disburse of the treasure the instruction give to master Cook be these to procure a new patent to the lord Mountioy with title of lord lieutenant and with authority to leave sir George Carey treasurer at war to be lord deputy and so his lordship to come present over 2. to procure new patent for ward let of the king land compound the king debt etc. as before 3. to solicit for victual munition and money 4. to move the change of the base coin now currant 5. to advertise the news from Spain 6. to solicit the send of new seal namely the great seal signet counsel seal for the state monster and Connaght for the king bench common plea and exchequer 7. to procure authority to pass estate to the irish lord after king james his proclamation at Dublin the lord deputy send like proclamation to all governor magistrate and officer of province city and country to be in like sort publish and with all make know to they several his majesty pleasure signify in his letter direct to the lord in England to continue all governor magistrate and officer and all his majesty minister as well martial as civil of both the kingdom of England and Ireland in as absolute authority and jurisdiction of their place as before the decease of the late queen Elizabeth of famous memory they enjoy and exercise the same as also to continue and establish all the law and statute of both kingdom in their former force and validity till such time as his majesty shall please to take full knowledge and resolve for the public good of any alteration not intend but upon some special and weighty cause and shall please to give notice of his pleasure Further his lordship advise they to concur with he in the vigilant care to present all thing in the best estate may be to the first view of so worthy and mighty a sovereign the twelve of April the lord deputy receive letter from sir Charles wilmott and sir George Thorneton appoint commissioner with joint authority for govern the province of monster in the absence of sir George Carew lord precedent late go for England advertise that they have block up Mac Morrish in the castle of Billingarry belong to the lord fitz-morrice and hope by the take thereof to clear the province of all open rebel the fifteen of April his lordship receive a letter from Ororke humble implore the queen mercy and the same day after his hear of the queen death another in like humbleness crave the king mercy the sixteen day his lordship receive letter from the mayor of cork advertise the mutiny of the city in monster about religion that he have receive the king proclamation the eleven of April and have defer the publish thereof to this day only to the end it may be dove with more solemnity humble pray that in regard the fort build for defence of the harbour of cork from foreign invasion be not keep by a commander sufficient to secure the same for the crown his lordship will accept the offer of he the mayor and there of the corporation of the say city to keep the same for his majesty at their own peril last complain that the soldier now keep the fort do shoot at the fisherman and at the boat send out of the town for provision use they at their pleasure the same sixteen day his lordship be advertise by several letter first that the citizen of Waterford have break up the door of the hospital and have admit one doctor white to preach at saint Patrick church and have take from the sexton the key of the cathedral church of themselves mutinous set up the public celebration of the mass and do many insolence in that kind second that Edward raghter a dominican Friar of kilkenny assist by some of the town come to the blackfriar use for a session-house and break the door pull down the bench and seat of justice build a altar in the place of they and command one bishop dwell in part of the abbey to deliver he the key of his house who be to take possession of the whole abbey in the name and right of the friar his brother the eighteen day his lordship be advertise from the commissioner of monster that the citizen of cork have not only refuse to join with they in publish the proclamation of king james but have draw themselves all into arm and keep strong guard at their port and have absolute forbid the commissioner to publish the same with such contemptuous word and action as will have raise a mutiny if they have not use great temper that the townsman have make stay of boat load with the king victual and munition for the fort of Haleholin say that the fort be build within their franchise without their consent and be meet to be in the custody of the city whereupon they the say commissioner accompany with the lord roche and some 800 person of the country all express muchioy but none of the citizen assist or express any joy do publish the proclamation upon a hill near the town with as much solemnity as may be and have furnish the fort with victual and munition from kinsale and they beseech his lordship speedy to reestablish by new letter patent the magistrate authority because the cease thereof by the queen death have especial embolden these citizen to be thus insolent the same day one Edward Gough a merchant of Dublyn new come out of Spain and examine upon oath say that at Cales he see the ordinance ship to s. Lucas for forty sail as he hear there ready to go for Lisbon where be a fleet of 140 ship prepare as some say for Ireland or as other say for Flaunders but he hear no general name only hear that don jean be again receive to the king favour the 22 day his lordship write to the soneraigne of kilkenny that howsoever he have no purpose violent to reform religion in this kingdom but rather pray for their better understanding yet he can not permit yea most severe punish in that town and otherwhere the seditious and mutinous set up of the public exercise of popish religion without public authority and likewise with prejudice do to those of the procession establish by God and by the law of both the realm require that he and they shall desist from such mutinous disorder apprehend the chief author and if they want power to suppress the sedition of a few priest and friar his Lp offer to assist they with the king force for he will not fail to give life to the law and obedience due to his majesty the four and twenty day his lordship be advertise that the citizen of Lymrick have with their priest enter into all the church of the city and there erect altar have use the rite of the romish church the 25. day his lordship write this letter to the citizen of Waterford your letter of the three and twenty of this instant come this day to my hand and have due consider the content of the same I find that they return a double excuse of the course you have use first for your delay of time to proclaim the king most excellent majesty according to such direction as be send unto you from the earl of ormond by a counsellor of this State and the next for such disorder as be report to be commit by the public breach of his highness law in matter of religion to the which we return you this answer follow first albeit we will have wish that you have have a more careful regard to have perform such direction as you receive from to noble a peer of this realm by so reverend a messenger as you may assure yourselves in such a matter dare not abuse you his highness sole and undoubted right concur also with your own knowledge and conscience yet we will not condemn you for that omission of the time see afterward you do obey our direction in that behalf and give so public a testimony of your joyful allowance and consent to his majesty right and lawful title proclaim among you but as in this part you have give unto we a kind of contentment so in the last point we can forbear to let you understand the just mislike we do conceive that you be citizen of wisdom and good experience and the law of the realm continue in force will be draw either by your priest or any like practice to commit any public breach of the law and the rather because out of that unspotted duty which you profess you have ever carry to the crown you will not in reason conceive that the example of your offence in such a cause and in so great and populous a city can not but in itself be very dangerous in these disorder time wherein example do carry man astray which in discharge of our duty to the king highness we may not suffer and therefore have resolve to make our speedy repair unto those part for none other purpose but to establish his majesty law that no public nor contemptious breach be make of they wherein we wish you have be more wary content yourselves with the long and favourable toleration you enjoy during the late queen reign rather than in this sort to have prescribe law to yourselves whereby in wisdom you may perceive how much you have prejudice the very obtain of your own desire by the course you have take as we be credible inform and yet because it may be that the report of your behaviour have be make more heinous than there be cause we be well please to suspend our give credit to such particular information until upon due examination the truth may appear wherein we hope and shall be glad that you can acquit yourselves so of these imputation now lay upon you or otherwise that you conform yourselves now at last in such sort to the obedience you owe to his majesty and his law as we be not enforce to take severe notice of your contrary action the same day his lordship be advertise from the mayor of Galloway that howsoever he find no seditious inclination in the citizen yet to prevent disorder in these mutinous time the governor of the fort have give he some of his soldier to assist his authority who he to that purpose have place in the strong castle of the city the same day his lordship receive letter from the mayor of cork signify that the thirteen day of this month he have publish in the city the proclamation of the king with the great solemnity he can and complain that the soldier in the king fort offer many abuse to the town with offer from the corporation to undertake the safe keep of that fort for his majesty the 26 day his lordship write to the sovereign of wexford that whereas they excuse their erect of popish rite by the report they hear of his majesty be a roman catholic he can not but marvel at their simplicity to be seduce by lie priest to such a opinion since it be apparent to the world that his majesty profess the true religion of the gospel and ever with careful sincerity maintain it in his kingdom of Scotland charge he and those of wexford upon their wexford to his majesty to desist from the disorder course they have take in celebrate public the idolatrous mass lest he at his come up into those part shall have cause severe to punish their contempt show to his majesty and the law of his kingdom the same day his lordship be advertise from the commissioner of moun r that the citizen of cork grow daily more and more insolent deface place of scripture write on the wall of the church to the end they may wash and paint over the old picture and that one term a legate from the pope with many priest have go in solemn procession hallow the church and sing mass therein public the townsman have place guard of arm man set at the church door and at the porch yea bury their dead with all papistical ceremony and take the sacrament in like sort to spend their life and good in defence of the romish religion and thereupon take boldness to offer wrong to the english and to practise the get of the king fort into their hand yea refuse to sell any thing to the english for the new mix money and not suffer the king victual to be issue out of the store till they have assurance that the soldier shall be send out of the liberty of cork the 27 day his lordship write to the sovereign of clemmell commend he and the rest of that city that they have proclaim the king with great joy and gladness but charge they upon their uttermost peril to cease from the public exercise of the romish religion which they of themselves have mutinous establish the same day his lordship write this follow letter to the sovereign of kilkenny after my hearty commendation I have receive your letter of the 25 and 26 of this month and be glad to understand thereby that you be somewhat conformable to my direction be willing to have cause to interpret your action to the best but though I mean not to search into your conscience yet I must needs take knowledge of the public breach of his majesty law and whereas you let i understand that the inhabitant be willing to withdraw themselves for their spiritual exercise to privacy content only with the use of the ruinous abbey that be a public place I can but take notice thereof and marvel how you dare presume to dispose at your pleasure of the abbey or any thing belong to his majesty and therefore again charge you upon your allegiance to forbear any public exercise of that religion prohibit by the law of this realm and full to reform these disorder according to my direction upon your extreme peril the same day his lordship write this follow letter to the mayor of cork after my very hearty commendation I do first receive some mutual complaint and information from the commissioner of monster and you whereof so far as they concern your particular I will take notice and be glad to hear you both or your agent for you and reform what I shall find amiss in either but of public offence or error I must take public knowledge and first for the proclamation of the king wherein I be inform that you be not only yourselves slow and backward but make resistance to those who be governor in that province in our late sovereign time and have our direction be not like to abuse or deceive you and offer with due forwardness and obedience after your unfitting and dangerous delay to have publish the same whereof I can but marvel and think you much to be blame in so undoubtful a right and with direction receive from those in authority to make such needless consultation and much more to offer violent resistance to those who better understand their duty and be ever ready in so much loyalty to perform it yet in regard of your solemn and joyful publication thereof I be willing to interpret your action to the best and take your good performance for a excuse but I be further give to understand that you have suffer the public celebration of the mass to be set up in your city of your own fancy and without public authority both against the law of this realm and I assure you contrary to that religion which his majesty zealous profess whereof I can but take public notice as you have public offend the king and his law and as I have do before so again I charge you upon your allegiance to desist from such seditious insolence and to apprehend the chief author thereof which if you do not present obey I shall be force against my will to use his majesty sword and power to suppress the same further you have by your letter make suit unto i to have the king fort Halebolin commit to your custody and I be inform that you have proceed in that insolency as to stay his majesty munition and victual and artillery which upon special trust of your loyalty be keep in your city from be transport from thence to the relief of the king fort. it may be you have rash and unadvised do this upon some opinion of the cease of authority in the public government upon the death of our late sovereign which be somewhat more though no way in true and severe judgement excusable and I think otherwise you will never have be so foolish to run into so great danger but since as it have please his majesty to renew and confirm unto i by his royal letter and letter patent under his scale the place of his deputy in this kingdom and to signify his gracious pleasure to continue all other his officer and magistrate aswell martial as civil in their former authority and jurisdiction so by virtue thereof and power give i from his majesty I have renew the lord president patent and grant a new commission to sir Charles wilmott and sir George thornton with charge and authority to govern the king force fort and place of strength and to defeud the town from foreign invasion and intestine mutinic or rebellion and further to govern that province according to his majesty direction in which command of they there be no derogation from your civil government and limit authority if you right understand the one and the other therefore as you shall at first have concur with they put in so great place of trust over you by your late queen and mistress especial in dangerous time of change for the peaceable government of all under both your charge that you may have deserve his majesty gracious acceptance of your service by present all thing in the best state you can to his highness first view so now I require you upon your allegiannce to be assist and obedient to they in all thing touch his majesty service and not to presume to interrupt the conveyance of the king victusl munition or artillery unto such place as shall be think fit by they for the furnish of his maicstics fort or force wheresoever they think convenient this if you shall perform I shall be glad to interpret your past action to the best find your endeavour to redeem what you have do amiss and not be force against my will to take notice of the height of your offence or error and use his majesty power to redress they I have since the write hereof see a letter present i by master Meade in denial and excuse of these information and if I shall find you conformable and obedient to these my direction I will be glad to have occasion to interpret all thing pass in the better part and take as little notice as I can thereof and so etc. the same day the lord deputy write this follow letter to the mayor of Lymricke after my hearty commendation I have not write unto you that I remember since I send you direction for the proclamation of the king which because I understand you publish according to your duty with all due solemnity and sign of joy and continue in dutiful sort not be seduce unto disorder as some of the town of that province be I think rather to have cause to commend you and give you encouragement in your loyal proceed than any way to blame you but I have since be inform that you have take example of other city seduce by their priest and against his majesty law and I assure you contrary to the religion he zealous profess upon your own fancy without authority set up the public celebration of the mass whereof I can but take public notice as you have public offend the king and his law and therefore I charge you upon your allegiance to desist from such seditious insolence and to apprehend the chief author thereof which if you do not present obey I shall be force against my will to take more severe notice thereof then willing I will and so hope that in a matter of so great consequence you will be better advise I expect to have answer from you the eight and twenty day his lordship write this follow letter to the commissioner of monster after my hearty commendation I have already send you your commission renew for continuance of your authority and sign the king letter patent to the lord precedent for his government and as former I advise you so again I pray you to transport as great a proportion of victual and munition as you can out of the city of cork into the fort of Halebolyn and the castle of shandon and if you may by fair mean you shall do well to endeavour to draw some company into the town which if you can effect yet I will have you not to slack the carry of victual and munition I have draw together some 5000 man and shall be able to employ they in reduce and settle the town of those part and if the citizen of cork upon the renew of your authority and my late direction prove more conformable than they be you shall do well to govern all without violence but if they continue obstinate in their former insolence I advise you to set guard upon the store of victual and munition and to leave the town the same day his lordship write this follow letter to sir Charles Wilmot one of the say commissioner sir charles I have receive your letter of the twenty of April and be glad of the good success you have in take the castle in kerry and for your man of cork I have hear of their insolence and I believe they and for any thing I know all the town in monster stand upon little better term assoon as I can possible for I have no force in all lemster I have gather together 5000 man and be come towards you and have so dispose of all thing in the north that if need be I will draw the whole army of Ireland ere it be long into monster some few except to guard the garrison with Waterford I think to begin for they give the first example but it be true that if they hold against i I be ill provide to force they for at Dublyn we be ill store of all thing but we will do aswell as we may I do like your course well to draw as many as you can to one head and I think it fit that it be about cork if your munition and victual be in the power of the town I know not what to say but I have first write to the town not to interrupt you in the dispose of the king munition and victual and upon my commandment if they deny it it be treason therefore I think they will be advise therein if you may therefore as sudden as you can convey as great a proportion of victual but especial and first of munition out of the town than I will command they to receive you with such force as you shall appoint into the town which if they deny it be treason too and if you have any store out of the town and your force be gather together and they continue obstinate it be good some little guard though it be but seven or eight man be put into the castle where the munition and victual be and for all the rest of the english to withdraw themselves out of the town by little and little and then if they continue obstinate still in not receive the king force my desire be that you shall present invest the town which I presume may be do with some 1000 man if you put two or 300 man into the fort next to kinsale gate which with so many man will be easy guardable and with the rest of your foot entrench near to the gate next towards shandon and with some 100 horse beat the way when you be in this forwardness if you think this way feasable I will send you either more man if with those you have you think not fit to engage the cannon or if I be loose myself from be engage in any other place I will come to you but if Waterford hold out I shall for the time have my hand full let i hear from you at large of all thing and in the mean time it be fit you put the best arlillery you have into Halebolin fort. I have send this by one who I think to be trusty and I pray you to send he back speedy to i and to impart this project to as few as you think good write to i how Lymricke and the other city do stand and so etc. the thirty day his lordship receive letter from the mayor of Corcke and his brother signify that the commissioner have by direction charge they to suffer his majesty minister to pass through their port with eight and forty barrel of powder and lead and match proportionable to be bring from his majesty store in that city to the fort of Halebolyn and that in regard they wonder so great a proportion shall be carry to the fort where no artillery be yet plant especial the quantity former issue being not yet spend nor any service be in hand they fear the commissioner purpose to assault the town or at least to starve they be enforce thereby to make stay of the say munition till his lordship pleasure be further know renew their suit to have the custody of the fort commit to the corporation that they do all they can to cause the mix money of the new standard to pass currant but it be with such grief and loss to the poor town as they hope his Lp will be a mean to his majesty for alter the same that they have receive rebuke from his lordship concern certain insolence but can not call to mind any particular wherein they have offend the state except that be a offence after many abuse and wrong do they to keep watch and ward to preserve themselves and keep the city for the king majesty in those doubtful time as they term they that touch the point of religion they only exercise now public that which ever before they have be suffer to exercise private and as their public prayer give public testimony of their faithful heart to the king royal majesty so they be tie to be no less careful to manifest their duty to almighty God in which they will never be dissemble temporisor thus they foolish rush into apparent treason by make stay of the king munition and presume to excuse their mutinous and insolent establish the public exercise of the romish religion and that upon their own head without any direction yea in opposition of public authority the lord deputy now be ready to take his journey for monster and purpose first to attempt Waterford write to the mayor thereof the first of May to this effect because it seem by your neglect of my direction and your impertinent answer that you do not know or have forget both my authority and yourselves I think good to let you understand that it have please the most mighty prince king james the first our sovereign by his letter patent under his great seal of England to make i his deputy and chief governor under himself of this kingdom and further to command i by his letter sign with his royal hand to cherish his good subject and to suppress the rebellious the which difference of good and rebellion I be no otherwise to distinguish in you but by that obedience which I have require and do now require of you to his majesty law and royal pleasure and as in my duty to God and my king I shall rejoice to find you in the number of those that I be bind to cherish and preserve so shall I be hearty sorry to find you such as I must be force to correct or to ruin and although it be none of my purpose to enter into your conscience yet if the effect of your conscience be to disturb the peace of this kingdom to violate the law thereof and by force to set up your own religion it be my duty to use the king power to suppress such insolence and therefore my purpose be to repair myself to the king city whereof you be one of his magistrate to see his peace and obedience maintain the which if both I and you and that corporation do not as our duty be intend we have a king that be not only able to call we his poor servant to a account but to revenge the wrong of the great monarch of the World the same day his lordship write to the mayor of cork this follow letter after my hearty commendation I have receive two letter from you the one not sign with any hand the other sign by you the mayor and two of your brother and for the first concern the stay you have make of the king munition and victual I marvel great at your presumption to limit and take account what proportion the commissioner authorize by his majesty think good to issue for his service and wish you in matter of so great consequence to be well advise not do any thing rash nor consult or deliberate after your fancy of thing not belong any way to your consideration but submit your own judgement to be rule by those place in authority over you I mean sir Charles Wilmot and sir George Thorneton who commission to govern that province I have late by order from his majesty renew for the answer of your second letter I refer myself to those I send you by master Mead which I think ere this time be come to your hand and as by they you shall more particular perceive so I assure you that I expect better satisfaction from you for the late insolent disorder commit or permit by you in that city then by these I have receive charge you again as former to desist from the violate of his majesty law by public celebration of the mass set up of your own fancy without superior authority in which if I find you not conformable and obedient to my direction I must conceive of your loyalty and affection to his majesty service as I find by the effect testify in your public action and be force to take more severe notice thereof than I willing will in regard of the good opinion I have heretofore have of your proceed and for that point in your last letter touch the fort of Halebolin whereof you desire to have the keep to the king use I will deal plain with you that since I find you so little able to govern the inhabitant of your town in due obedience to his majesty and his law and so easy seduce by your priest and friar to the prejudice thereof and the hazard of yourselves and have always find by experience the true integrity and forward resolution of the king soldier to advance his service till I shall see a better reformation of these your proceed I must needs think they fit than you to have place of so great trust commit to their guard and custody the same day his lordship understand by letter from the mayor of cork that the king force lie near the town and their arm man of the city have proceed to act of hostility some have be kill on both side whereof they crave relief from his lordship make their contumacy against the commissioner authority a private quarrel to the person of one of they as be their enemy and seek their utter ruin likewise the bishop of cork advertise that a most seditious sermon be preach at cork by a popish priest teach that he can not be a lawful king who be not place by the pope and swear to maintain the roman religion also that one of his man go to the port of the town be hurt by one of the guard who wish he have the traitor his master there with threat of death to he last that the citizen by resolution take in a public counsel with their priest have write to all the town and city to assist they in the defence of the catholic faith and have not only stay the king munition but lay it up in their own storehouse and imprison the clerk who keep it the three of may his lordship be on the way towards monster be advertise from justice synot that the citizen of wexford have conform themselves to his lordship pleasure and have redeliver the church to the hand of the english minister the four of May his lordship in camp at Gracedea near the city of Waterford and summon the mayor and his brother to open their port and to receive he and the king force the same day his lordship write this follow letter to the mayor of cork for the dispatch of your messenger and not to omit any thing that heath in i to make you understand your dutiful obedience to his majesty and the great error and insolent demenor you have of self will or malice enter into I be content to write unto you though I know not well in what sort to write for by your course I can take you for subject and out of my desire to interpret your action to the best I can wish not to have cause to repute you rebel to deal plain with you for any thing that you self inform or I can otherwise understand I see not that sir Charles Willmot have do but as in his duty he be tie to do but I be present draw down to the city of cork and have reserve one care for any your just complaint will judge of your proceed as I shall find they I have let you understand my pleasure by my letter one send by master Meade which if he have not deliver he be more to blame and I assure myself some of they come to your hand and in all I have charge you upon your allegiance as again by these I do to desist from public breach of his majesty law in the celebration of the mass prohibit by the same and to yield due obedience to his magistrate and especial upon your extreme peril not to presume to make any stay of his majesty victual and munition leave upon special trust on your fidelity within the wall of that city but to suffer it by his majesty commissioner of that province to be issue to the fort or where they shall think meet for the present service I shall be glad to find that you conform yourselves to due obedience in all these and other duty of good subject if otherwise you obstinate persist in the contrary I must needs hold you for enemy to the king and the peace of these his realm and as such think you fit to be prosecute by the revenge sword of these and other his majesty force from the camp at Gracedea near Waterford the four of May 1603. the five of may his lordship be advertise that the governor of Loughfoyle upon sir Neale Garnes late insolence and disloyal practice have by force take from he his cow horse and all his substance and that he himself have give pledge to come to his lordship the disloyalty prove against he be these that he obstinate carry himself in all
