thing concern the service that he forbid his people to yield any relief to the english garrison that he restrain his man from building or plough that they may assist he to do any mischief that be threaten to set fire on the liff that he refuse to admit any sheriff in his country that he have long be upon a word with the rebel that he swear he will go into rebellion rather than any english man shall enjoy a foot of church land in his country which notwithstanding be reserve in his patent that he have create himself o Donnel and that he have murder a honest subject who will not follow he in those course with many like insolence and hereby the lord deputy and state be set at liberty for the promise of Tirconnell make unto he so as his lordship have now good occasion to give Rowry o donnel contentment to which end some good portion of land be assign to sir Neale garue his Lp procure the rest of the late o donnel country to be give his brother Rowry who afterward in England he procure to be create earl of Tyrconnel thereby extinguish the name of o donnel the citizen of Waterford at first refuse to receive any force into the town but only his lordship and his retinue yet the chief of they attend his lordship in the camp and for their proceed in the point of religion his lordship require they to bring unto he one doctor white a jesuite the chief seducer of they by seditious sermon they be ready upon safe conduct or protection to bring he to his lordship presence which be grant they by word of mouth the say doctor white accompany by a young dominican friar come into the camp but when they foolish carry a crucifix open show the same the soldier be hardly keep from offer they violence and when they put up the crucifix in their pocket yet can hardly endure the sight of their habit which each wear according to his order doctor white wear a black gown and corner cap and the friar wear a white woollen frock white be come into his lordship tent be bold to maintain erroneous and dangerous position for maintenance of that which the citizen have do in the reform of religion without public authority all which his lordship do as no lay man I think can better do most learned confute and when white cite a place in saint Austin for his proof his lordship have the book in his tent show all the company that he have false cite that father for howsoever his very word be find there yet they be set down by way of a assertion which saint Austin confute in the discourse follow at this surprisal white be somewhat out of countenance and the citizen ashamed but in conclusion when those of the town allege king John his charter for privilege why they shall not receive the king force into the town his lordship reply that no king can give that privilege to his subject whereby his successor shall be prejudice in the due obedience they be to expect from they and in a word tell they that if they do not present open their port to he and the force with he he will cut king john charter in piece with king james his sword and if he enter the town by force will ruin it and strew salt upon the ruin whereupon they better consider of the business receive his lordship and the force into the town and the soldier be so well discipline that howsoever they be not a little grieve by this yield to have the hope of rich pillage take from they yet no wrong worth the speak of be do to any one in the town his lordship here suppress all public exercise of romish religion and restore the church to the english minister and take from the chief citizen a oath of allegiance to his majesty with a abjuration of all dependency on any foreign potentate have hold the like course of reformation in his passage hither through kilkenny the citizen whereof have in like sort offend but his lordship forbear any way to censure their past sedition leave they to the king mercy for charter life and good if thereby they have endanger they only his lordship leave one thou sand man in garrison at Waterford and make choice of a place of advantage where they shall lie till such time as a fort may there be build to command the town and bridle it from run into like insolence hereafter and because the north be now quiet there be no further use of any garrison in lecayle his lordship appoint sir Richard morryson former governor of lecayle to command this garrison leave at Waterford and to govern the county of wexford certain head whereupon some Waterford man be examine a part one from the the other and in a careless manner yet so as the answer be write out of sight 1. what preparation be devise to rescue Waterford if the army have lay siege thereunto 2. who be the instrument that be appoint and be assemble they 3. who be their leader both in town and country and when and where they shall meet 4. whether any thing be publish in writing and set upon the church door and other place to seduce the people and what the effect thereof be or by who it be set up or whether it be set up in Clomell only or in more place 5. whether any of they do know sir mahowne o Dullany priest to come to Clomell the five of this month report that sir donnel o Carol have fifteen hundred man ready to the rescue of Waterford 6. whether any of they know Humfery Arthur of Lymrick or any other messenger from thence to come to Clomell to report that five thousand be in readiness to the rescue of Waterford also or whether he be still at Clomell yea or no. 7. whether they know which quarter of the country be appoint for father Mulrony to take the charge thereof to be assemble to the rescue of Waterford 8. whether they know father leinagh haunt likewise so of father ractor and the rest who name they be not to seek of themselves 9 whether they have or can tell certain that any intend yet to draw these rescue to cork or any other head to prevent the army 10. whether themselves be swear to live and die in the quarrel or what noble man or lawyer be swear also 11 whether they be able themselves to deliver any of these seducer to the lord deputy yea or no by what reason they shall not if they deny it be conversant with they daily 12. whether they know any messenger go for Spain or elsewhere to procure help to those confederate who be go when they go and what they be or from whence and what be their message or how charge be collect for they from Waterford his lordship by small journey in regard of the impediment by the slackness and fail of supply of garron and beef from the country march to the city of cork and come thither upon the ten of May be without any contradiction receive into the town with all the force he bring with he though sir Charles Willmott have invest the town and at the same time with the force of the monster list lie before it the eleven day his lordship to make it apparent to they and all the world how willing he be to give they gentle audience in their just complaint first admit they to speak what they can of any offence they have receive or just suspect before they be call in any question for their own disorder but their accusation for the most part be such as if they have be prove which be not do the proof as less important be defer to a more convenient time yet import rather imputation of want of discretion in rash speech than any just pretext for their proceed and therefore be lay a part as impertinent to the main cause then to be handle and for the rest of their more select accusation they be judge to have in they no important excuse for their seditious carriage but be such for the great part as his lordship be force to justify without call the adverse party to his answer as be do either by his lordship direction or out of duty impose upon the commissioner of this province by virtue of the place of authority commit to their charge thus the townsman labour to divert their public offence by a colourable excuse of private spleen and some grudge against one of the commissioner and in regard the earl of ormond come that night to cork the lord deputy be desirous not only to have his lordship but as many of the nobility and man of the best rank as he can to be witness of their heinous offence and of the mild proceed against they do defer till next day the receive of the townsman answer in justification of their own action at which time many breach of his majesty law and their duty be object against they first in the public erection of the romish religion against the law and the abolish that profession which be allow by the same second in their maintain these action by force and arm man three in their attempt to demolish the king fort at the south gate of the city four in stay the issue of the king munition and victual with the seize of they into their own hand and the imprison of the king officer and minister to who charge they be commit last in bear arm and do all act of hostility against his majesty force wherein their insolent proceed be so far follow as they have kill a grave and learned preacher walk upon the hill adjoin to their wall and have batter shandon castle wherein lie the lady Carew wife to the lord precedent then absent in England after due examination take of all these point his lord p resolve as he have former do at Waterford to leave the censure to his majesty pleasure that he upon view thereof may use his royal mercy or justice in remit or punish and reform the same only his lordship take notice of some few of the principal offender and ringleader who offence be apparent and sever from the common action and they his lordship command to be hang for example and terror to other some his lordship leave in prison to be try by course of law as master Meade the recorder who be a most principal offender but he may as well have forgive he for no man that know Ireland do imagine that a irish jury will condemn he the chief citizen of cork take the above mention oath of allegiance to his majesty abjure all dependency upon any foreign potentate from cork his Lp write to the earl of Tyrone to meet he at Dublyn in readidinesse to bear he company into England this do his lordship lest a strong garrison of soldier in the town of cork and so upon the fifteen of May match towards Lymrick and the citizen thereof have proceed to no further disorder than the public celebration of mass be soon reduce to order and willing take the oath of allegiance with abjuration of dependency upon any foreign potentate as the other city have do the sixteen of may his lordship receive letter from the earl of Tyrone whereby he give he many thanks that he have procnr out of England authority to proceed with he according to the instruction he have former from the late queen promise to be ready at Dublyn to attend his lordship into England and touch a complaint of shane neales son for some cow his man have take from they promise to make restitution and because he think many complaint will be make against his people by reason of their poverty he beseech his lordship not to give credit to they till he may repair to his lordship to satisfy he protest that he will be ready at all time to come unto his lordship and to do all duty of a faithful subject the lord deputy have give order to fortify the castle of Lymrick and have from thence write to the mayor of cork to assist the commissioner in build the fort at their south gate take his journey towards Dublyn the nineteen of May and come to Cashell the twenty of May where he reform the town as he have do the rest and take the like oath of allegiance from the townsman there he understand that a priest command all the people have tie a goldsmith of our religion to a tree threaten to burn he and his heretical book at which time he burn some of our book which he so term but that upon a townesmans admonition the priest set the say goldsmith free after he have stand so bind to a tree some six hour before all the people of the town in continual fear to be burn the lord deputy have quiet settle all the town and city in monster return to Dublyn and because upon the first settle of peace many petition be exhibit against the late rebel for restitution of good which they have take in time of rebellion and be not now able to restore so as the exact thereof be like to produce new trouble rather than any satisfaction to the plaintiff a authentical act of oblivion for all like grievance be publish and send to the governor in all part of the kingdom in this late monster journey his lordship receive letter from the king whereby sir George Carey leave l. deputy by the lord Mountioy l. lieutenant return into England he be choose to be one of his majesty privy counsel in England and be make lord lieutenant of Ireland with two three part of the deputy allowance assign to he be license to come over into England and have authority to leave sir George Carey the king deputy during his lordship absence have the other three part of the deputy allowance and his own entertainment as treasurer at war for his support and captain floyd lie now in the harbour with the king pinnace call the Tramontana ready to transport he his lordship with the earl of Tyrone in his company together with his household servant and some knight and gentleman his follower take ship in the afternoon and the next morning early we discover the desire land of England the weather be very but within one hour the sky be over cast with a thick fog and we bear all sail we fall sudden upon the skerrye a hideous great black rock where after so many danger escape in the war it please God miraculous to deliver we from be cast away as it be in the very haven for certain bird call gull see our ship ready to rush upon they and their desert habitation with full sail rise cry and flutter round about we whereat the governor of the pinnace be amaze look out and behold that terrible spectacle cry to the steareman aloof for life which fearful voice may have daunt he as it do most in the ship but he stout do his work answer helm aboard which do the ship by force of the stern and by the help of the tide come in between it and the rock turn about with strange swiftness and swim along by the rock so near to it as the beat hang at the stern dash against it neither be the most expert man in the ship for a long time free of this fear know that such great rock have usual small pinnacle adjoin to they the least whereof have be as dangerous to we as the main rock but the ship by God merciful providence pass on safe that day by noon we come into the bay of Beaumarris and be set on shore by the boat the earl of Tyrone ride from thence to London in the lord Mountioy his company and howsoever his lordship happy victory against this traitor make he gracious in the eye of the people yet no respect to he can contain many woman in those part who have lose husband and child in the irish war from fling dirt and stone at the earl as he pass and from revile he with bitter word yea when the earl have be at court and there obtain his majesty direction for his pardon and performance of all condition promise he by the lord Mountioy be about September to return he dare not pass by those part without direction to the shiriffes to convey he with troop of horse from place to place till he be safe embark and put to the sea for Ireland the lord Mountioy come to court be honour of all man and gracious receive of the king be present swear one of his majesty privy counsel and for further reward of his service short after the king make he master of the ordinance give he two hundred pound yearly old rent of assize out of the exchequer and as much more out of the duchy to he and his heir for ever beside the country of Lecale in Ireland together with other land in the pale there which after the decease of the lady Mabell countess of Kildare be to fall to the crown for want of heir male of her body he have the full superintendency over all irish affair no dispatch pass to and from the lord deputy but through his hand as lord lieutenant and his majesty likewise create he earl of Devonshire which dignity be to descend to the heir of his body lawful beget but it die with he and he enjoy the rest of this worldly happiness but few year for he be surprise with a burn fever whereof the first fit be very violent he call the death of the lord Mountioy create earl of Devonshire to he his most familiar friend and tell they that he have ever by experience and by presage mind be teach to repute a burn fever his fatal enemy desire they upon instruction then give they to make his will and then he say let death look never so ugly he will meet he smile which he noble perform for I never see a brave spirit part more mild from the old mansion than his do depart most peaceable after nine day sickness upon the three of April in the begin of the year 1606. this most worthy lord cure Ireland from the most desperate estate in which it ever have be and bring it to the most absolute subjection in which it have ever be since the first conquest thereof by our nation yet he leave this great work unperfect and subject to relapse except his successor shall finish the building who foundation he have lay and shall polish the stone which he have only rough hew and because he know this relapse will be most dangerous have observe every rebellion in Ireland to be more dangerous than the former and that none can be more dangerous than this last without the loss of the kingdom therefore he be most careful to prevent all future mischief to which end whatsoever effect his design have sure I be that he do meditate these wholesome prciect first to establish garrison in the city of monster and in the renew of their forfeit charter to cut of many exorbitant privilege grant to their first english progenitor from who they be so degenerate as the very speak of english be by they forbid to their wife and child then by the exchange of land and by the dispose of the new grant of land to be make to the irish to draw they all to inhabit the inland country and to plant the english upon the haven seacoast and river last because he know all endeavour will be in vain if civil magistrate shall think by fair mean without the sword to reduce the irish to due obedience they have be conquer by the sword and that maxim be infallible that all kingdom must be preserve by the same mean by which they be first gain especial with the irish by their nature pliable to a hard hand and jadish when upon the least prick of provender the bridle be let loose unto they therefore his Lp purpose to persuade that the army shall stand in some convenient strength till the king revenue be increase and establish so as Ireland may be a nursery to maintain some convenient number of old soldier without any charge to England and till the reformation of religion and due obedience to the magistrate be at least in some good measure settle in Ireland and especial in the foresay city a list of the army as begin the first of April and end the last of march beside concordatum munition and other extraordidary two hundred fourscore ten thousand seven hundred thirty three pound eight shilling nine penny half penny farthing half farthing the charge of the irish war from the first of October 1598. to the last of march 1603 be four year and a half beside great concordatum great charge of munition and other great extraordinary eleven hundred fourscore eighteen thousand seven hundred seventeen pound nineteen shilling one penny the charge of the army as be abovesaid forecast for the year follow begin the first of April 1603 and to the last of march 1604 the horse stand as in the former list but the foot to be reduce to 8000 amount to one hundred sixty three thousand three hundred fifteen pound eighteen shilling three penny farthing half farthing in the year 1613 by the entreaty of my brother sir Richard moryson vicepresident of monster and out of my desire to see his child God have give he in Ireland 1613 beside some occasion of my private estate I be draw over again into Ireland where we land the nine of September miraculous preserve from shipwreck for at nine of the night be dark at that time of the year we fall upon the coast of Ireland and not well know the coast but imagine it to be Yoghall port we tack about to beat out at sea the night follow but have some hour before spring a leak and our pump be foul so as they will not work we have no hope to live so long at sea and again not know the coast we dare not venture to put in upon it beside that in case it be Yoghall harbour our best fortune be to enter a bar haven by night in this distress by divine providence we be preserve the moon break out through the disperse cloud and shine so bright as our best marrine easy discover the harbour of Yoghall and the tide serve happy we pass the bar into the same and the next morning we may see the danger we have escape most apparent for our ship be so far unable to endure the wave of the sea with her great leak and the foulness of the pump if we have be force to keep a board till the next day light may make we know the coast as the same night she have sink in the quiet harbour if the mariner have not choose rather to drive she on ground at this time I find the state of Ireland much change for by the flight of the earl of Tyrone and the earl of Tirconnell with some chief of country in the north and the suppression and death of sir Cabier Odogherty their confederate in make new trouble all the north be possess by new colony of english but especial of scot the mere irish in the north and overall Ireland continue still in absolute subjection be powerful in no part of the kingdom except only Connaght where their chief strength be yet little to be fear if the english-irish there have sound heart to the State but the english irish in all part and especial in the pale either by our too much cherish they since the last rebellion in which we find many of they false heart or by the king religious course to reform they in their obstinate adiction to popery even in those point which oppugn his majesty temporal power or by the fullness of bread in time of peace whereof no nation soon surfeit then the irish be grow so wanton so incense and so high in the instep as they have of late mutinous break of a parliament call for the public good and reformation of the kingdom and from that time continue to make many clamourous complaint against the english governor especial those of the pale against the worthy lord deputy and his minister through their side wound the royal authority yea in all part the churl be grow rich and the gentleman and swordman needy and so apt to make a prey of other man good the citizen of monster have long since obtain the renew of their old charter with all their exorbitant privilege and be now grow most refractory to all due obedience especial for matter of religion in which part the very number of the priest swarm among they and be active man yea contrary to their profession bloody in handle the sword far exceed the number of the king soldier reduce to very small or no strength and many loose man flock into that province out of the low-countries who be train there in the irish regiment with the archduke daily send over new man to be in like sort train there and themselves lie disperse and hide in all corner with heart no doubt apt to embrace mischievous enterprise and howsoever the english lawyer come over after the last war vaunt Ireland to be reduce to full obedience by their itinerary circuit scarce mention with honour the sword that make way to they yet they be therein deceive that the irish in their clamorous and litigious nature fly to they with many complaint do it only to get countenance to their cause from they who be stranger to they perhaps against former judgement of the governor who better know they and so to oppose one magistrate against another not as they may perhaps think in sincere affection to be rule by the law yea those chief of country who use to wait on they to the limit of their country do it rather to keep the people by their awful presence from exhibit complaint against themselves than as the judge thought out of their dutiful respect to they or to the State for otherwise even among the english-irish in the inferior person from the counstable to the justice of peace and so upward justice have not his due course which can never have life but in the move of all the member with due correspondency and many outrage be by the english-irish and mere irish do against the english late plant there so as now when Ireland shall have enjoy the fruit of the last war in the due subjection of the mere irish these time threaten the next combustion from our degenerate english irish. only the lover of peace be erect to good hope by a general confidence that our sovereign will apply his royal power severe justice most available in Ireland and other his heroic virtue to the timely prevention of any mischievous issue as not long after his majesty happy begin with bring those his subject to conformity of make wholesome law for the public good by common consent of that kingdom three estate assemble in his royal court of parliament at Dublyn in the year 1614 to which work and all his royal counsel God give happy success the list captain Craford 50. captain John Vaughan 50. captain Arthur Basset 50. in all 1400. the Totall number 1947. the discourse upon several head through the say several dominion of travel in general part 3 book i. chap. i. that the visit of foreign country be good and profitable but to who and how far since the best and most generous wit most affect the see of foreign country and there can hardly be find a man so blockish so idle or so malicious as to discourage those that thirst after knowledge from so do I may seem to undertake a vain and needless task if I shall persuade thereunto wherefore I pass over the abundant fruit it yield I will not speak of the experience thereby attain which instruct the most dull and simple as the sun by his beam colour the passenger intend nothing less than to be so colour and which neither by hear nor any sense can so easy be gain as by the eye for since nothing be in the understanding which have not first be in some of the sense sure among the sense which be as it be our sentinel and watchman to spy out all danger and conduct we through the thorny labyrinth of this life pilgrimage not any one be so vigilant so nimble so wary nor by many degree so trusty as the sight according to the say of the poet less do it move the mind that beat the ear than what before the faithful eye appear this ground of my discourse be grant yet I be not so blind affect to this course of travel as I will thrust all into this warfare without difference or choice first woman for suspicion of chastity be most unfit for this course how soever the masculine woman of the low country use to make voyage for traffic not only to their own city but even to hamburg in Germany and more remote place neither will I advise Angelica if she be alive in these day to trust herself alone and in desert place to the protection of wander knight lest she shall mere with more strong encounter than be that of the weak hermit nor yet will I herein give unlimited liberty to marry man hold alexius unexcusable who leave his Bside upon the very marriage day yet after a due time of conversation to combine love why shall he not in summer season follow the war at his prince command yea upon his free will since we owe ourselves to our country as to our wife yea why shall he not search after politic wisdom by short excursion into foreign part since we permit merchant and mariner though marry to take long voyage for gain neither can gentleman more enrich themselves then by the knowledge of military and political affair and indeed the civil law permit man to travel after the espousal always provide that this industry rather increase then diminish our estate except our country be in question in which case all respect to our private family whether of love of frugality or whatsoever private good must be cast behind our back since the commonwealth contain each private man estate and a part must be put to hazard for the preservation of the whole body let Plato the divine philosopher have patience with i though I be not of his opinion who in his twelve book of law assign to this course the last period of life from the age of fifty year to threescore it be true which he say of that age to be most able to discern between good and unprofitable law and that it be less subject to infection from corrupt custom yet as some young man once free of the tutor awe be prone and apt to run into vice so many old man always compare like disposition have forcible restrain themselves from natural inclination for fear of shame this cause of restraint once take away while among stranger they be at more liberty do often return to their own nature even in vice most improper to that age and in that case their dotage be more slanderous both to themselves and their country now that old man may dote in this sort one example of Tiberius the emperor may serve for plane prove thereof who in his youth and the year of his strength have dissemble his wicked inclination at last in his old age give his nature the reins and retire himself as it be out of the sight of the senate and people of Rome into the island Caprea there he shameless give himself over to all beastly lust think himself safe from the censure of the roman though his wickedness be no less know in Rome then if it have be do in their market place according to the italian proverb Amer' e and nonson' cieche love be stark blind and see nought do amiss but other people be not blind iwis and though we shall grant that which Plato attribute to old man yet they who custom be grow to another nature shall never be able to endure the frequent change of diet and air which young man can bear without prejudice to their health except it be by little and little and as it be by insensible degree not to speak of the weakness proper to old age which make they so sickly as they be not only unfit for conversation abroad but also have small hope to return and relate their observation at home old man be indeed most fit for ambassage in neighbour country for in this employment they live in the sight of their countryman and may use their accustom diet and a little change of air do but little hurt which they may prevent or mitigate by the advice of physician neither be child unripe year fit for this course howsoever they be more to be excuse who send they with discreet tutor to guide they with who eye and judgement they may see and observe thus the roman as Snetonius write permit the visit of foreign country to the son of senator under the government of one who have bear office in the city for the charge of such child be not to be credit to all of full age without choice child like parrot soon learn foreign language and soon forget the same yea and their mother tongue also a familiar friend of i late send his son to Paris who after two year return home refuse to ask his father blessing after the manner of England say France it be not the french fashion thus while like ape they imitate strange fashion they forget their own which be just as if a man shall seek his perdition to gain a cloak for ornament likewise sickly man be unfit for this course of life who in regard of their weak health want the mean to make benefit thereof last they shall do well to keep they selnes at home who have a scrupulous conscience and think themselves so wise as they will not follow the advice of experience man but as in all action they be happy that hold the mean so middle age be most fit to visit foreign part and to make use thereof have first lay a good foundation of art and science in general and special those which they mean to profess and be of soripe discretion as they can distinguish between good and evil and since we must not only respect their age but most of all their disposition I will add that it must be diligent observe in each man whether he natural affect this course or no for it be most certain that the nature of man can with good success beforce to any course it like not according to the old say of the poet furc a though nature be repel by force yet still it turn to have his course I know that second cause can do nothing without the first and it be no less true that the first do in natural thing work by the second which make i of opinion that the position of the star in each man nativity be not altogether vain this grant it be not just that the commonwealth parent or friend shall wrest any man nature to course contrary thereunto not leave each man liberty to show and follow his own inclination to conclude I think with Plato that before any man take this course he must obtain leave of the magistrate as the custom be in England where none but merchant may without leave go out of the island to the end that suspect person may be keep at home left being not well instruct in the true religion they shall be seduce by papist and I take parent consent for grant without which the sacred power give they by God shall wicked be violate my purpose be thus slight to have pass over the worthy praise due to the knowledge of foreign state wherewith I begin lest with the vaive rhetorician I shall seem to praise Hercules who no man dispraise but when I consider that so many head so many wit and remember that myself have sometime hear though a man shall be last to hear the detraction of his own course some pleasant other witty and some malicious to scoff at this kind of industry to gain knowledge it will not be amiss so far to take in hand again the task I have almost finish as according to the end of my discourse I may encourage the indifferent reader by full answer their idle objection in the first front be the pleasant man who remember the italian stale proverb Asino Asino if a ass at Rome do sojourn a ass he shall from thence return the very ass stray into barren pasture do after learn more willing to stay at home with plentiful feed and very dull spirit man at who this bitter proverb allegorical aim howsoever by this industry they do not sudden become catce yet I will be bold to say they be by nothing more or with great ease instruct according to their capacity so they travail not alone as Socrates laconical say for if by the way they find good companion they must needs in some measure be partner of their wit and observation neither be the wise observer of human pilgrimage ignorant that grave university man and as they say sharp sight in the school be often repute idiot in the practice of worldly affair as on the contrary blockish man and to speak with the italian very ass by continual practice in grave employment gain the wisdom of they who affair they manage and the more they seek to know the world the more they converse with those who know more than themselves so much be they more inflame with sweet emulation such as (@) sleep from Themistocles read the triumph of Miltiades second the witty detractor object that aglaus Psophidius be judge wise by the oracle of Apollo because he never go out of the grange wherein he be bear according to that of the poet Claudian happy in native soil his life who spend who one house birth one house a gray head lend but what if passenger shall come to a stately palace of a great king be he more happy who be lead only into the kitchen and there have a fat mess of brewis present he or rather he who not only dine at the king table but also with honour be conduct through all the court and chamber to behold the stately building precious furniture vessel of gold and heap of treasure and jewel now such and no other be the theatre of this world in which the almighty maker have manifest his unspeakable glory he that sail in the deep see the wonder of God and no less by land be these wonder daily present to the eye of the beholder and since the admirablevariety thereof represent to we the incomprehensible majesty of God no doubt we be the more happy the more full we contemplate the same thou will say he have live well who have spend his time retire from the world who know be least have live best this may be true in woman and thus among many roman gentleman when one praise Fulvia another Claudia a three with good judgement prefer a senator unknown wife to both these and many other several commend because she be no less good and fair yet be know to few or none not unlike to that matron who think all man breath do stink as her husband do but it be the part of a industrious man to act their affair in the world though sluggard lie by the fire of a man parent challenge a part friend a part and the commonwealth a part and he be judge a unprofitable servant by the most true oracle of God who hide his talon neither do the verse of the lyrical poet object in the three place any whit move i abroad forget thou at home forget by thou and in another place Samos and Chios and rhodos in Samos Rhodes and Chios what may please let we at Rome commend sit at ease o poor effeminate Horace let his genius pardon i a very hog of the epicure hear and to use his own word bear to consume corn be any mutual remembrance of kinsman more please then that which be cause by mention of their virtue if a man shall offer to show thou all the strange miracle of africa will thou answer go your way and paint they which do I will glad see they or if one will show thou paradise and the infinite flower and fruit thereof will thou rather snort in a chimney corner and not shake off the least drowsiness for the very possession of that happy inheritance but if he will go and paint they then promise perhaps and at leisure to view they yet as Socrates for argument sake be wont to defend the contrary to all disputer so I rather judge that Horace thus write in the person of the epicure after the manner of poet or for pastime and spend of vacant time then that it be his own judgement there want not some who discourage man from this course by the manifold difficulty and danger thereof but as the poet say virtue climb craggy rock venerable science with his gray head be never find on a soft feather bed many more there be who tremble at the very thought of danger of death to which this course of life be subject behold salomous sluggard who say there be a lion by the way a lion be in the street fear thou the sea because it swallow many why fear thou not a bed in which more have breathe there last but a great fear distract even the mind of the wise sort that they see many return from foreign part corrupt with vice proper to they according to the fleming proverb that no man be ever make more healthful by a dangerous sickness or come home better from a long voyage and because it be hard to learn virtue and more hard not to learn vice or to depose they once learned from hence they say it be that so many homebred angel return from Italy no better than courtly devil sure this be just as if a man shall say I have a ground overgrow with bush which I fear to root up because nettle or darnel will more easy spring then grass or corn field be make fruitful by tillage so be man make more wise by knowledge of foreign kingdom the italian say excellent ' lasciar' spin sweet rose gather make a end and leave the prickle that offend we must observe virtue for imitation and vice that we may abhor they contrary be manifest by contrary beauty shine more be set with a deform foil and the glory of virtue be more eminent by the object of base vice yet that which be say of the athenian may true be say of the best wit of all sex and condition if they be good they be best if ill worst thus history witness that woman by nature most witty have often give extraordinary testimony of excellent goodness or monstrous badness thus Swetonius say of caesar. great virtue of the mind with like vice be combine thus the english proverb say no knave to the learned knave I confess that steep mountain yield most violent stream but it be a point of art to lead these stream in a fair bed till they fall into the Sea this be the scope of all I say that by this course the good become best the bad prove worst sure out of the holy scripture the atheist gather most profane argument as the godly reader suck the eternal food of his soul bee and toad draw honey and poison out of the same herb to conclude the ill humour of the body grow predominant turn the best meat to their nourishment yet none but a mad man for the abuse of that which be of itself good will forbid to read the most holy scripture to gather sweet flower or to eat the best meat write of a certain earl who after long absence return to his home and no less to his former vice as a dog to his vomit and be for the same reprove do answer that his tailor also return to his occupation but Cato the young be of a more virtuous mind who regular morality can not be corrupt by wanton Asia but make his familiar friend Curio a false prophet therein far great be the virtue of Scipio who in Spain leave a fair virgin untouched and yet will see she that he may excel Alexander the great in the rare emulation of continency who dare not behold the captive daughter of Darius lest he shall be subdue by woman who have conquer man live with good man seek out honest not licentious house to lodge in for the italian say well quel' tu fai. tell i with who thou do converse and I will soon thy deed rehearse restrain the vice to which thou be natural most incline by force thyself to exercise contrary virtue for the poet say true be sure to rule thy mind in all thou do if it obey not than it rule the roast let the incontinent man restrain his unbridled lust by the remembrance of his chaste mistress at home which will make he fear the miserable disease incident to the unchaste as seaman tremble to strike upon a rock let he that be give to excessive drink of wine while yet he be sober behold the ridiculous gesture the dull brain the shickle memory the oppress stomach with each weakness and deformity of the drunkard so by one vice remedy let he learn to cure all the rest there be no danger to so wary mind either of lose their own virtue or of learning foreign vice beside that sweet virtue be of such power as they forcible draw even negligent man stand least upon their guard to admire love and exercise they yea let these strict censurer of manner tell i if they have so cleanse the pump of their own commonwealth as there be less danger of this corruption at home then abroad sure many fall into vice abroad but more at home many return from foreign part after they have abroad satisfy their disordinate appetite by give youth his swinge as the proverbeis do at home cast off their vice and return to the old bound of shamefastness which at home they never violate add to their old virtue the lustre of foreign ornament on the contrary most man at home wrap in the habit of vice by custom take away both the shame and sense of sin never reform their debauch life nor forsake vice till in age or death they be for saken by they but among other vice dissemble lie and jealousy be by popular voice deem proper to traveller the first imputation I will refer to the precept of the next chapter show how far it be fit for a wise man to dissemble and the second to the last precept of the same chapter show how a traveller shall govern his tongue and in this place by the way I will brief discuss the reproach of jealousy which the tumultuous flock of woman injurious thrust upon we just as if they shall say that by experience and the industrious search of wisdom we shall be endue with vice not with virtue or that those who can see far of yet can discern that which be before their eye and foot or that those who be skilful in politic affair be ignorant in domestical government and that those who abhor litigious suit at law ready make peace with their neighbour will nourish household dissension most odious to a wise man not know the term of love wherewith they be in duty bind to entertain their dear friend their sweet wife and child sweet send from above and nephew dear pledge of love but to say truth most honourable title of old be by abuse grow most reproachful as the name of sophister by their continual brawl about moon shine in the water the name of elenche by intricate fallacy the name of tyrant by unlimited cruelty and the name of jealousy by affect extremity as that jealousy be most hateful which grow from malice of nature from a mind guilty of wickedness or from conscience of any defect in a man own body or mind so the virtue of jealousy deer not only to traveller but to all wise man be in my opinion approve of all man that be in their right wit our very God be in a good sense say to be jealous he that have a jewel of price be not willing to have it snatch out of his hand much less out of his sight reason like a severe schoolmaster shall overlook all thy own action and why shove it not have like authority in direct the behaviour of those who honour or shame can be divide from thou and of this judgement so virtue decline not to the neighbour vice between which there go nothing but a pair of shear as the proverb be you shall ever find the chaste woman desire a husband virtuous jealous for other who have rather not be love then have their action observe leave they to be their husband executioner and to the rack of their own conscience only pray they to take this warning that their husband love for which perhaps they little care begin then to freeze or rather vanish away when they have once cast away all care of their good name since only virtue combine true love but the slanderer of this industry press we more near object that vice ill custom and very heresy be spread through the world by traveller let i ask they how we shall have know the diverse elevation of the pole who will have teach we geography represent paint world unto we how shall we have learn all other art yea how shall we have discover new world or rather the unknown region of the old have not this industry of traveller be yea to use the most bind and conclude argument the save knowledge of the holy gospel have be unrevealed to we have not the apostle excel in this industry of make the whole world their country to preach the same when we have thus compare the great and manifest profit of travel with the light and doubtful inconvenience object against it we must confess that they who discourage the affecter of these great reward by such small danger be not unlike the sophister who persuade that blindness deafness and the privation of other sense be not to be number among evil because we see many unpleasing thing often hear that which offend the ear and for one good smell draw in twenty ill savour for my part let they dispute never so subtile though they convince i yet shall they never persuade i to pull out my eye stop my ear or cut of my nose nor yet to prefer sloth to a active life but we must give ear to parent friend and as well private as public commonwealths-man who not unjust seem to fear lest young man by this course shall be pervert from true religion and by this fear dissuade pass into foreign part as the chief cause of this mischief sure if the vessel be new that take a ill savour it will stick much fast thereunto but I have already profess that I will have a traveller of ripe year have first lay good ground of all art and most especial of religion let i speak of myself when I behold the mimical gesture and cerimoniall show of holiness of the roman priest under the shadow whereof they abuse the superstition of layman with unspeakable art to their own ambition and covetousness and on the contrary observe their corrupt manner I be so far from be seduce by they as I can not refrain from laughter and wonder as Cicero speak of soothsayer that one priest do not laugh when he see another his fellow priest if you meet a fair paint face the more near and narrow you behold it the less it please but if the beauty be true then there be danger lest you or your son swallow the bait search corrupt ware narrow and they will stink at thy nose which far off seem to be odorifferous such be the paint of the papist the english and those of the reform church who esteem their argument as spear of bulrush permit their book to be read in our university but they perhaps guilty of error will in no sort give liberty to read our book we appeal to all that be skilful in language for the truth of our translation they by statute of the jesuit college admit no young scholar to study language till he have take a oath that he will use that skill to the defence of the roman translation with tooth and nail wherein if they offend their conscience they have the pope power of dispensation on their side therefore let the papist fear to give their follower leave to hear we in our school or church lest they be chain with the force of truth and let we secure permit our man to pass into the heart of Italy so they be first of ripe year and well instruct upon my word they run no other danger then the escape the snare of the inquisition of which discretion I shall speak at large in the four and twenty precept of dissimulation in the next chapter if any man object that some of our young scholar have pass into their seminary beyond the sea let he consider that they be not seduce abroad but first infect at home in their parent house and our university which mischief parent and magistrate ought to prevent by keep the suspect at home for the rest there be no danger but behold when I think to have finish my task carper consume with envy who bark at traveller as dog at the moon and think to gain reputation by other man disgrace they be not ashamed to say that vagabond Cain be the first traveller old writer I confess sometime use the word of peregrination for banishment but God be praise here be no question make of banish or curse man drive out from the sight of God they which spend the great part of their year in foreign place as it be in voluntary banishment may more just be compare to Cain and be not unlike to rude stage player who to the offence of the beholder spend more time in put on their apparel then in act their comedy for life be compare to a stage and our parent and kinsman expect our proof to the beholder therefore it be fit to restrain this course within due limit to which the roman as Suetonius write prescribe perhaps too strict three year in the last place they that detract from traveller to the end they may choke we with our own disdain if not with argument send out their spy in their last skirmish to cast this dart at we after so many danger and trouble how many of you after your return be prefer in the commonwealth to what purpose do you tire yourselves in attain so many virtue be it to exercise they lean on a ploughman or shepherd staff I shall enter a most spacious field of common grief if I shall search the cause why in our age great part of the counselor of state and peer of realm rather desire to have dull and slothful companion than those that be wise and ambitious and so in like sort rather base and expert ready servant than those that be free and learned knowledge puff up and I remember of late a learned physician who be send for by a great lord and he be offend at his long stay free and bold answer that knowledge can not dance attendance hence be our calamity to omit the more curious search of this evil who first encounter astonish i though I be not ignorant of the cause but let he that cast this dart tell i whether this ill be vot common to all man of virtue and if it be so he must at least confess with the poet partner in grief do solace give and let all rare man in any kind of virtue when they be despise or neglect comfort themselves with this philosophical precept of Aristotle that virtue be desire for itself not for any thing else so I say the fruit of travel be travel itself have thus retort our enemy weapon upon their own breast because the common sort be more move with example than argument it remain that in the last place I shall adorn the triumph of this virtuous industry with some few and special example many have be find who have pass into remote part of the world only to gain health far great be the number of they who as the poet say Garamant as and Indos who further run to shun base poverty then garamant and indian doe and great be the number of they who follow the standard of ambition have pierce to the very gate of hell with sound of drum and trumpet to conclude as diligent merchant gather precious ware into one storehouse so phylopher have from the first age of the world pass by flock into foreign part to gain knowledge as the egyptian into Chaldea the greek into Egypt and the roman into Greece Pythagorus walk far and near not only to learn but also in diverse place to get disciple who he may teach for the poet say well to know avail thou no whit if no man know thou know it to be brief if we will credit old monument which I confess to suspect he come in person and sow the precept of his philosophy even among the Britain divide from all the World Plato have write somewhat too severe against traveller perhaps like Alexander the great who be angry with his master Aristotle because he have publish the (@) (@) (@) (@) (@) which he have read unto he thereby leave he nothing wherein he may excel other so Plato have get the name of divine by his very travel will forbid or limit the same to other that he may shine among the philosopher as the bright moon among the lesser star it be most certain that he be not only industrious but even curious in this course so as he sail into Sicily the entrance of which island be upon pain of death forbid to stranger only that he may see the burn of the mountain Aetna Apelles by draw of a most subtle line at Rhodes be make know to protogenes Homer be blind yet cease not to travel in our age they which be renown at home for any art be not content therewith except they may pass into foreign court to make know their skill the most ancient lawgiver get the experience by which they have rule in their city not by secure study at home but by adventurous travel abroad as the poet say adversity do often whet our wit Moses Orpheus Draco Solon mine Rhadamanthus Lycurgus and almost all the consul of Rome themselves have be in foreign part and grant ample privilege to stranger among physician we read that Esculapius and Hypocrates travel and that galene be at Smyrna Corinth Alexandria in Palestine at Lemnos Cyprus and at Rome and Auicenna boast that he have pass through the whole World I know that many in our university become learned physician but no doubt they will have be more learned if they have pass into foreign part one land yield not all thing a man shall hardly learn at home the diverse nature of herb and other thing or the diverse disposition of one and the same body according to the difference of the clime air and diet this the Spaniard witty observe who have get the french pox sail into America and do there learn the cure of that disease from those who first infect the spaniard therewith we praise physician especial for experience as lawyer for diligence desire to have a old physician and a young lawyer to give we counsel and follow our business but experience be of several thing disperse through the universal World it be write that of old the egyptians have several physician for each several disease who will not have return more learned from their lecture also they lay up approve remedy of disease in the temple of Isis and Vulcan what scholar then return from Egypt shall not have gain great reputation to his skill and we know that opinion many time avail with the sick even more than the medicine in this sort ambitious man of old by the only opinion of their experience by see the world do obtain to be number among the god jupiter of Creta in Italy Bacchus in the further east Hercules in the most remote part of Africa towards the west plant monument of their travel the voyage of the argonaut the wander of Ulysses and Aeneas be sing by all ballad-seller Alexander the great pass the monument of Bacchus in the east it be infinite only to name the roman emperor who excel in this industry for as the wiseman say that he be a citizen of the world so the roman by give remote prince the privilege to be citizen of Rome and by send roman citizen in colony to inhabit remote place use the whole world for a city neither do they ever admit any to the high dignity in the city of Rome nor yet to the inferior magistrate thereof who have not first bear rule or office in some remote province one julius Caesar come see and overcome with his army among the cimbrian german spaniard Britan grecian african and those of Asia the very western emperor of late time have be inflame with the same desire Charles the great make happy war beyond the Pyrenean mountain against the sarrasen beyond the Alps against the Lombard and in Germany against the saxon who have not hear of the european prince like so many flood carry army into the east to omit all other for I desire to be brief charles the five inferior to none of his predecessor and emulous of Hercules himself pass his pillar at the further strait of the mediterranean sea and add to his arm the Mot of no far beyond this only leave out the first word because he have pass the limit of Hercules as Alexander the great have do those of Bacchus whereby notwithstanding some think that he do rather blemish then increase the fame of his travel since that part of Africa be so near adjoin to his kingdom of Spain but in the mean time they forget that he be bear at Gaunt in Flaunders methinks I have say enough and too much in so clear a cause therefore I will only add some choice example of the holy scripture and so conclude Abraham leave his country at God command and go to the land of promise call also the land of pilgrimage jacob serve for his wife in Mesopotamia and in his old age pass into Egypt the israelite be bring by large circuit from the servitude of Egypt into the land of promise that the protection of God may by adversity be more imprint in their mind and they be more stir up to keep the law of so gracious a God the example of a woman the queen of sheba be famous who come to jerusalem to be a eye and ear witness of Salomon wisdom to be brief Christ himself live in the flesh as a pilgrim choose no set place of abode when he be in the cradle three wiseman come from the east to worship he and himself be a infant be carry into Egypt to shun Herod tyranny he command his apostle to preach the gospel throughout the World among the father saint Augustine wish to have see three thing Christ in the flesh Paul in the pulpit and Rome in the flower in our age the turk and papist so mad affect pilgrimage as they superstitious think the same available to the salvation of their soul with which extreme lest I shall seem to know no mean I will conclude these example for my part I think variety to be the most please thing in the world and the best life to be neither contemplative alone nor active altogether but mix of both God will have make eternal spring have he not know that the divers-season will be not only most profitable to the work of nature but also most pleasant to his creature while the cold winter make the temperate spring more wish such be the delight of visit foreign country charm all our sense with most sweet variety they seem to i most unhappy and no better than prisoner who from the cradle to old age still behold the same wall face orchard pasture and object of the eye and still hear the same voice and sound beat in their ear not the song of the cuckoo nor the crake of the crow nor the howl of wolf nor the bellow of ox nor the bley of sheep no nor the sweet voice of lark and nightingale if they be shut up in a cage do so much please we at home as the variety of all compose of diverse tune delight we in the field abroad in like sort it be manifest that all the other sense be not so much please with any thing as variety they be in some measure happy who have but one house yet have change of chamber to remove as the season of the year change but I judge lawyer and officer more happy who have their term to live in the city and their vacation to return into the country so often as it be renew their marriage day and of all I judge the nomads most happy the comparison hold in other thing who live in tent and so by remove not only escape the heat of summer the cold of winter the want of pasture all disease and all unpleasing thing but at their pleasure enjoy all commodity of all place let we imitate the stork swallow and crane which like the nomads yearly fetch their circuit and follow the sun without suffer any distemper of the season the fix star have not such power over inferior body as the wander planet run water be sweet but stand pool stink take away idleness and the bait of all vice be take away man be create to move as bird to fly what they learn by nature that reason join to nature teach we nothing can be add to the worthy praise of he as the poet suith who many man manner have see and have in many city be in one word I will say what can be say upon this subject every soil be to a valiant man his own country as the sea to the fish we be citizen of the whole world yea not of this world but of that to come all our life be a pilgrimage God for his only beget son sake the true Mercury of traveller bring we that be here stranger safe into our true country chap. 2 of precept for traveller which may instruct the unexperienced I will follow my purpose and give precept not to expert man as Phormio do to Hannibal in military affair but only to the unexperienced and that not curious as if I will prescribe they every step they shall go but such as may whet the wit and memory of other man well know that many thing may be add which be slip out of my memory and which other may daily find out 1 in the first place as every man in any course of life so most of all a traveller who be subject to many danger must by his daily prayer solicit God for his gracious protection all our action must take beginning from God the fountain of all good if we desire with the israelite to have God cloud and pillar of fire protect we in our journey and abode let he daily commend himself to God protection and even in his journey daily at morning and evening not while he siumber in his bed but in private withdraw from company either kneel as before his father or stand as before his master and lord make his prayer though never so short to his almighty and most merciful God and let no man take this for a needless precept for I free profess that when I be most devout in this kind I find myself hedge about with the good angel as on the contrary when I neglect the same I often observe by some manifest accident that I be leave to error and danger 2 let each traveller forecast with himself his own purpose and end for they which be unskilful in the art of paint carve and building can never worthy praise nor well imitate the rare work they shall see of these kind experience teathe that no action be wise undertake whereof the end be not forecast in the first place howsoever it be last put in execution but since it be infinite to apply my precept to the several end of several man and no more possible then for a physician to cure the patient not know the cause and the progress of his sickness I profess to write especial in this place to the humanist I mean he that affect the knowledge of state affair history cosmography and the like and out of that I write let other man apply to their use what they judge fit for they and if the humanist judge many thing I shall write less necessary for he let he know that as a orator and poet must have some skill in all science so the humanist must have some knowledge of all thing which fall into practice and discourse 3 let a traveller observe the underwrite thing and of they some curious some slight as he shall judge they fit for his purpose he shall observe the fruitfulness of each country and the thing wherewith it abound as the mine of metal and precious stone the chief law and custom of the worker in those mine also batch and the quality of the water with the disease for the cure whereof it be most proper the name spring and course of river the pleasant fountain the abundance or rarity of pasture grove wood corn and fruit the rare and precious plant the rare and proper beast the price of necessary thing and what he daily spend in his diet and horsemeat and in hire horse or coach the soil of every day journey the plenty of fish or flesh the kind of meat or drink with the sauce and the rare manner of dress meat the country expense in apparel with their constancy or fickleness in wear it the race of horse as the giannets of Spain the courser of Naples and the heavy horse of Freesland and how they manage and feed these horse the situation of city and province the healthfulness of the air the chorography the building the riches the magnificence of citizen their household stuff and in general all special thing as statue colossus sepulcher with the inscription library with the most rare book theatre arch bridge fort armoury treasury monastery church public house university with their founder revenue and disputation to conclude let he visit the most learned man and those that excel in military art or any virtue and let he confer with they as his end require thus do I visit Beza at Geneva thus do I visit Belarmine at Rome be ready to take horse and in the habit and person of a Frenchman thus in my return do I glad see Henry the four of Bourbon king of France famous for the feat of arm and wisdom only Lipsius who I love for his book of constancy and much desire to see for his universal learning do bereave i of this hope when I come into the low-countries by his inconstant flight to the spaniard the traveller shall further observe the policy of each state and therein the court of each king or prince with the courtier entertainment fee or office the stature of the prince their revenue the form of the commonwealth whether the prince be a tyrant or belove of the people what force he have by sea or land the military discipline the manner of the people their vice virtue industry in manual art the constitution of their body the history of the kingdom and since the soul of each man be the man and the soul of the commonwealth be religion he shall observe the disposition of the people whether it be religious superstitious or profane and the opinion of religion differ from his and the most rare ceremony thereof he shall also observe the traffic of merchant and therein the commodity which they carry out and most want the haven and road for ship their skill in navigation and whether they use subject or stranger for their mariner last the value of the coin in each country and the several currant piece and whatsoever he shall think meet to add hereunto 4 and because the memory be weak and those who write much be many time like the clerk that carry their learning in their book not in their brain let he constant observe this that whatsoever he see or hear he apply it to his use and by discourse though force make it his own thus student of rhetoric at first seek matter for word rather than word for matter at last attain a easy style flow like a still river and lay aside the affectation of word let nothing worth the knowledge pass his eye or ear which he draw not to his own possession in this sort in the mean time though he trust not to his paper yet for the weakness of memory let he careful note all rare observation for he less offend that write many toy than he that omit one serious thing and after when his judgement be more ripe he shall distil gold as the proverb be out of this dung of Ennius let he write these note each day at morn and at even in his inn within write table carry about he and after at leisure into a paper book that many year after he may look over they at his pleasure but great caution must be have especial in place of danger how he carry about he these paper the subject whereof can but in many place be offensive and perhaps dangerous if once upon suspicion he chance to be search therefore as he send his book and heavy thing for carriage half yearly either into his own country or to some place in the way by which he be to return there to be keep for he so he shall do well to send these paper book therewith and for abundant caution lest any thing he note by the way shall in any place upon mischance prejudice he he shall do well to write such thing in cypher and unknown character be also ready to give a feign interpretation of they to any magistrate if need be 5 also I advise he to leave a trusty friend at home who will keep good credit with the merchant that furnish he with money abroad leave his friend ill payment lose he his credit and so drive he to disgraceful want for if his friend pay ready nothing shall be want to he but merchant will never trust those which break their day and for his exchange and other way of receive money at fit time and place as also for the value of gold and silver coin let he read the former last chapter of the first part treat thereof fifty or sixty pound sterling yearly be sufficient at the time when I be beyond sea to bear the charge of a traveller diet necessary apparel and two journey yearly in the spring and autumn and also to serve he for moderate expense of pleasure so that he imitate not the german who drink and banquet as much abroad as at home nor the italian who live they among christian or pagan yet can restrain their incontinency nor the polonian who be perhaps the son of castellani I mean such as have the keep of castle or like entertainment from the king only for their life common spend more prodigal in Italy and like place then at home so as many time they spend their whole patrimony abroad in which kind I can but commend our countryman who how soever at home they may have spend prodigal yet go beyond sea rather dispose their expense to repair this former prodigality then otherwise and practice the rule of the poet manner each man his cote to fit as his cloth will permit but I return to the purpose and since it be convenient for he that travel to make two journey yearly in the spring and autumn and since in these journey his expense will be great than when he abide in city as well for the hire of coach and horse as for his diet in common inn he must be careful to take the opportunity to moderate his expense when he settle himself to abide some month in any place they which have servant to attend they must make account that each servant shall spend as much for his diet as himself especial in Germany where passenger of all sort sit at the same table and pay the like shot for the danger or security of carry money about he in all part I shall speak at large in the three and twenty precept of this chapter in general he must be wary not to show any quantity of money about he since thief have their spy common in all inn to inquire after the condition of passenger if his journey be long let he not tell no not to his companion in his journey the further end thereof but rather from city to city profess that he intend to go no further suppose he be at Paris if he profess his journey be from thence to Rome it be all one as if he show his purse since all man know he must have great store of money for that journey so as it be more fit he shall profess only to go for Strassburg in Germany or for lion in France and when he shall come thither he shall either perhaps have new consort of his journey or else may profess to his former consort that there he meet with letter which force he to go further 6 that he may the better premeditate those thing which former I advise to be observe by he and the like he shall do well before he set forth to get some skill at least superficial in the art of cosmography for if he be altogether ignorant thereof he shall like a blind fold man not know where he be or which foot first to set forward no man can read with profit and pleasure the voyage of other man old history and the march of army except he have some skill in this art how much less shall he be able himself to perform such action if he have no skill therein I contain under this art Corography and the knowledge of those kingdom which he be to pass they be most necessary for his use also it be fit for he aswell to observe the old as the new name of each place which give great light in the read of old writer 7 for the precept before he set forth in the last place I advise he to make his will which no wise man stay at home will have unmake in which I commend our progenitor who make their will if they take a journey but from York to London the same be much more necessary in this our age when we undertake sit more dangerous journey and that the rather for that there be many difficulty in understand the law and manner of make will in foreign part and the force they will bear at home beside that very death and that in a strange place be like too much to distract the mind though it be not afflict in that sad hour with worldly cogitation 8 when he have once begin his journey since at the first step the ignorance of language do most oppress he and hinder the fruit he shall reap by his journey while he be as it be deaf and doumb and astonish with this babylonian confusion of tongue can neither ask unknown thing nor understand other man speech by which he may learn much my advice be that in each kingdom which he desire most to know and the language whereof be of most use in his own country he go direct to the best city for the purity of language namely in Germany to leipzig strasburg or Heidelberg and in France to Orleans etc. where have learn the language at least as much as be necessary to understand and to be understand he shall make his next journey more profitable by discourse and in the same make his language more perfect hear much increase knowledge what can thou learn if at least thou understand not those who shall instruct thou howsoever thou be hardly or not at all understand by they except thou have a interpreter with thou which a man of small mean can maintain and yet in that case do the rich only borrow his knowledge and take it at the second hand I confess that rich man have such consort and make good use of they may with more case attain knowledge while they have the help of other man eye ear foot and understand and may suck from they the quintessence of their observation but poor Cleanthes while in the day time he draw water to gain his live do by night more earnest thirst after knowledge and gain it which all rich man do not who wit use to be corrupt with their fortune and I will think that even for these rich man it be more honourable and safe to be able to use their own sense and understand than other man since we see that prince ambassador and peer of other realm be more welcome and esteem and less subject to contempt if they do but only get the form of salute and call for necessary in the language of the country as if they will not seem stranger and first in the learning of a language labour to know the grammar rule thereof that thyself may know whether thou speak right or no. I mean not the curious search of those rule but at least so much as may make thou able to distinguish number case and mood merchant woman and child neglect these rule and rush into the rash practice of language do many time pronounce the tongue and speak common speech more graceful than other but they seldom write the tongue well and always forget it in short time want the practice on the contrary they who learn the rule while they be attentive to the congruity of speak perhaps do less graceful pronounce the tongue but in the mean time they both speak and write pure language and never so forget it as they may not with small labour and practice recover it again in the next place I advise he to gather the choice phrase that he may speak and write more eloquent and let he use himself not to the translate form of speech but to the proper phrase of the tongue for every language in this kind have certain property of speak which will be most absurd be literal translate into another tongue to this end the stranger must read those book which be best for speech in familiar conference in which kind as also for the instruction of his soul I will commend unto he the holy scripture but that among the papist they be not to be have in the vulgar tongue neither be the read of they permit to layman and be not the phrase so know to those who use to read they as they will be understand by discretion without knowledge therefore to this purpose he shall seek out the best familiar epistle for his writing and I think no book better for his discourse then Amadis of Gaul for the knight errant and the lady of court do therein exchange courtly speech and these book be in all language translate by the master of eloquence in the three place I advise he to profess pythagorical silence and to the end he may learn true pronunciation and the property of each language not to be attain but by long observation and practice that he for a time listen to other before he adventure to speak as in general to all live in foreign part and desire to search out the knowledge of they not to be have so well from any as from the inhabitant so particular to he that will learn the language my counsel be that he shun for the time the conversation of his own countryman only visit they in their lodging and that not often nor long but that he live not in the house with any of they for the dutch traveller converse drink and lodging with their own countryman hardly attain any small skill and never the perfect use of any foreign language be it never so easy so as myself remember one of they who be reprehend that have be thirty year in Italy he can not speak the language he do merry answer in dutch ah be man jaher alas good sir what can a man learn in thirty year but the true cause of his not speak the tongue be his perpetual converling with his countryman I profess free that I never observe any to live less together in foreign part than the english nor any who make more profit of their travel than they but I return to the purpose when he that desire to learn any language have observe the former rule then let he hire some skilful man to teach he and to reprove his error not pass by any his least omission and let he not take it ill that any man shall laugh at he for that will more stir he up to endeavour to learn the tongue more perfect to which end he must converse with woman child and the most talkative people and he must cast of all clownish bashfulness for no man be bear a master in any Art I say not that he himself shall rash speak for in the beginning he shall easy take ill form of speak and hardly forget they once take the very artificer of (@) can speak latin but most rude and false and I speak of experience that the scholar of our university conversant in read creero howsoever they seldom speak latin but only in disputation yea and shun the occasion of speak it yet when they come abroad and be force necessary to make use of the latin tongue they do perhaps at first speak it less ready but in short practice they speak it more eloquent and more easy then the say polake or any other abroad who have practise the tongue from their young year and so they may speak ready neither cite for the quantity of syllable nor the purity of phrase nor the strict keep of grammar rule moreover I speak by experience that it be commendable at home before set forth to learn the read and understand of a language and the writing thereof yet can then be profitable to practise the speak of the tongue (@) he can have the foresay commodity in that part where it
