be natural and for this cause I prescribe pythagorical silence and to listen to other before the practice of speech and to take more care to speak well then much but at last the learner must begin and he that never do a thing ill can never do it well but whereas many boast and have the same to speak many tongue aswell as their mother tongue I do not think but know that it be false the french have a good proverb Roys among the blind the poor blind be the king and thus they which have no skill in tongue will bold say that this or that man do perfect and without stamer speak many tong but howsoever a stranger live some six or more year in any foreign part may perhaps speak that tongue as perfect as his own yet he that travel in few year through many kingdom and learn many language shall never speak all nor many of they with natural pronunciation and without error and some stamer and slowness in speech yea he that learn one tongue alone and that with many year practice shall more hardly attain the perfect property and elegancy thereof than a unskilful man will think for Theophrastus have live many year at Athens be know to be a stranger of a old woman sell herb only by the pronunciation of one syllable for my own particular I remember that I pass from Genoa to Milan on foot in a disguise habit and that in a inn not far from Pavia I meet a Englishman we satdowne to supper where he voluntary and unasked do rash profess himself to be a Dutchman whereupon I salute he in dutch familiar till he betray manifest his ignorance in that language and excuse himself that he be no Dutchman but bear upon the confine of France where they speak altogether French then I likewise speak to he in french till he be out of countenance for his want of skill in that language so as myself be a man in his case dissemble my country and quality cease further to trouble he and we after discourse in the italian tongue he chance to speak these word io whereas a italian will have say io and by this little difference of add the syllable I know he to be a Englishman for I have find before that he be no Frenchman which nation together with the english add that syllable thus when supper be end I take he aside and speak english to he whereat he rejoice and embrace i swear that he have be in the stable and command his man to make ready his horse and will present have rid away if I have not discover myself to he and so we lodge in one chamber and bed see how small a thing will make it manifest that we be not that country man whereof we speak the language the knowledge of tongue have ever be high esteem Aulus Gellius write that Mithridates speak the language of two and twenty province which be subject to he so as he never speak with any subject by a interpreter Themistocles in one year get so much knowledge in the persian tongue as he be able to speak with Artaxerxes without a interpreter Ennius say he have three heart mean three language Claudius the emperor put a grecian prince from be a judge because he can not speak latin and send he into Italy to learn the tongue as Suetonius write to conclude who have not hear the worthy fame of that heroical woman Elizabeth late queen of England among who rare virtue her skill in language be not the least be able to confer with most ambassador or prince in their own tongue and whereas some king think it a base thing to speak in a strange tongue and take it for honour if they can induce any ambassador to speak their tongue they seem to i like unto those who be poor and proud speak much against rich apparel and extol stuff and furniture of small price that they may seem to do that of election and judgement which they do only for want yet I will not be so understand as if I think fit that one ambassador at a treaty shall consent to have the same write in the language of another ambassador but rather that it shall be write in a three tongue equal know to they both as in the italian tongue the treaty be between England and France but in the mean time I think it honourable to the most mighty king to be able to entertain familiar speech with any ambassador or prince of neighbour kingdom though less potent than his own let we be encourage by these noble foresay example to labour diligent that we may attain this rich jewel of speak tongue in the last place I advise the traveller that if he can have leisure he join for great ornament the learning to write the hand of each nation with the knowledge of each tongue especial of that which be most of use in his country 9 for englishman they shall do well at their first set forth to pass into Germany and there spend some time for since we use too much the help of our servant so as we will scarce make ourselves ready and since we use to despise the company of mean people at bed or board there we may learn to serve ourselves where he that come into a shoemaker shop must find out the shoe will fit he and put they on himself there we may learn to admit the company of mean man where many time poor fellow yea very coachman shall be thrust to be our bedfellow and that when they be drink and like man will often sit by we at the table and in some place as most part of Low-Germany they drink always round so as we shall be sure to pledge like man and drink to they in the same cup and if we have a servant of our own will rather have he sit next we than any other there we may learn to feed on homely meat and to lie in a poor bed there among many other thing we may learn to moderate our aptness to quarrel whereof I will speak more in the proper place to conclude all in general that pass Germany as stranger be free among that honest people from all colinage and deceit to which in other part they be subject above other especial unexperienced 10 as it be good before his set forth to be reconcile with his enemy that they may practise no ill against he or his friend in his absence and that his mind may be more religious compose against all event so while he be abroad let he often write to his friend of his health which precept if Theseus have not forget he have not be author of his most dear father death by bear the false sign of a black sail and this be no less good to himself then to his friend since he that write often shall often receive letter for answer for one hand wash another and the poet say well who will belove be that thou love see for indeed there can hardly be give a more certain sign of love or contempt then the frequent rare or no writing or especial answer of letter whereof the italian have a proverb chiscrive a respond o ha who write to he that answer not again he be a fool or need do he constrain 11 when he will observe the situation of any city let he if he may without jealousy of the inhabitant first climb one of the high steeple where have take the general situation of the city he shall better remember in order the particular thing to be see in the city to which end let he carry about he a dial which may show he the north south east and west which know he shall less err in the description of the city and this he may observe public only with his eye for avoid of jealousy and after be retire into his inn may draw it in paper if he think good and lest for the want of a guide to show the markable thing in each city he shall omit any thing worth sight let he confident visit some chief doctor or man of principal account especial in Germany where they be most affable for if he shall say that he come to see they as the live monument of that city I will be bold to promise that they will give he a guide to show he any thing worth sight and to instruct he in such thing as be fit for he to know for as woman easy believe such as tell they that they be fair though indeed they be deform so man of best quality will easy believe that their name be know among stranger and they take these visitation for honour do unto they yea many especial in some place be vicious proud that their neighbour shall see stranger thus visit they 12 many desire to have their countryman and friend to be their companion in these their journey and it be well say a pleasant consort by the way be like a coach that glide away but why shall he not rather desire consort of the same nation of who he may learn the language and all other thing worthy to be observe myself can never see any profitable spend their time abroad who flock together with their own countryman neither do I attribute the little proficiency of the german and their give themselves to drink even among the sober italian to any thing more then to their live together in foreign part for a italian converse abroad with italian shall never learn bashful chastity how shall any man cast off a vice proper to his nation it he do not disuse it by little and little which he shall hardly do among his countryman incline thereunto neither be there danger of learning foreign vice by leave to converse with his country man so he propound to himself the foresay end to learn virtue and cast off vice and if he bend himself whole to attain that end moreover in place of danger for difference of religion or proclaim war whosoever have his countryman or friend for his companion do much increase his danger aswell for the confession of his companion if they chance to be apprehend as for other accident since he shall be accountable and draw into danger aswell by his companion word or deed as by his own and sure there happen many danger and cross by the way many be of such intemperate affection as they not only diminish the comfort they shall have from this consort but even as dog hurt by a stone bite he that be next not he that cast the stone so they may perhaps out of these cross grow to bitterness of word between themselves yea sometime filthy end their old friendship with new injury if not in single combat beside if this dear friend and consort shall happen to die by the way and if other ill accident shall increase this evil whereof many may be imagine as namely if by die among enemy or pagan he can have so much as the honour of a grave sure I speak by experience nothing can be add to this calamity this grief threaten sickness unto thou and to how many ill that state be subject in foreign part I shall show in the precept of preserve health and this event will take from thou all the pleasure of remember thy danger past after thou return home yea will make that bitter unto thou which use to be most comfortable to other therefore I commend the english who withdraw themselves from consort with their countryman abroad not shun they unnatural out of hate but only lodging in diverse house and only spend some hour of the week in their company to nourish acquaintance that they may bestow the rest of the time among those of the same country wherein they live and so better their language and learn the state of the country for my part if I be to suffer ill I have rather be alone then have a friend partner with i howsoever the poet say the miserable man do grieve the less if he have partner in his sad distress which be to be understand of enemy or unknown partner for I can think that my torment can be assuage by the like misery of my friend other object that it be the unspeakable comfort of marriage that man and wife like well pair heifer bear all burden together sure if other kind of ill can be divide into equal part as burden may I may be of their opinion but many kind of ill be like the soul which be all in the whole body and all in every part thereof neither be the torment of the soul ease by the body suffer with it therefore if I be to suffer poverty banishment or torment I have rather be a single man then marry since the compassion of my wife and child suffering with i will infinite increase my misery these thing be grant I confess it follow for of contrary the consequence be contrary that the traveller be to impart his good success to his friend whereof Cicero in the dream of Scipio so dispute as if a man see all the pleasure of paradise can take no delight therein if he be alone and have no man with who he may communicate they but in conclusion since traveller meet with more danger than pleasure it be most fit for they to take such consort abroad as the way yield and to defer the impart of their good success to their friend till their happy return home at which time as their absence have sharpen their friend desire to see they so the discourse of these pleasant accident may sweeten their conversation 13 in stead of a companion let the traveller have always with he some good book in his pocket as we read that Alexander the great lay Homer under his pillow and let this book be either such as fit his end or study or such as contain precept or sentence which by daily use he desire to make familiar unto he always beware that it treat not of the commonwealth the religion thereof or any subject that may be dangerous to he by this companion he shall make the solitude of the inn and many irksome thing less unpleasing to he 14 as we read that Alexander the great set on fire with his own hand the wane of carriage take from Darius and that by his example all the macedonian cast away the spoil they have take from the persian lest they shall hinder they in their expedition against India so the traveller compare small thing with great must carry only most necessary thing with he especial in such place as the low-countries where boat and wagon be change many time in one day journey and where as also in Italy they bring he not to his inn but only to the water side or to the gate of the city for in such place heavy carriage will be a great burden or charge to he 15 let he inquire after the best inn especial in Germany and also at night in Italy for he may take a short dinner in any inn of Italy so he lodge safe at night in the best inn with moderate and ordinary expense he shall avoid the fraud and injury of knave and shall sleep safe both for his person and the good he have with he in all inn but especial in suspect place let he bolt or lock the door of his chamber let he take heed of his chamber fellow and always have his sword by his side or by his bed side let he lay his purse under his pillow but always fold with his garter or some thing he first use in the morning lest he forget to put it up before he go out of his chamber and to the end he may leave nothing behind he in his inn let the visit of his chamber and gather his thing together be the last thing he do before he put his foot into the stirrup 16 some advice that a traveller shall learn to swim but I think that skill be more for pleasure at home then of use abroad and yield small comfort or help in a storm at Sea let other man have their free opinion as I have i yet I know that Caesar deliver himself and his commentary from perish by his skill in swim but neither be all as fortunate as Caesar neither be all sea like that of Africa myself have know many excellent swimmer whereof some in the sight of the wish land have perish by the rage of the sea wave and other have sink by the weight of their fearful companion know their skill and so take hold of they while at the same time other have not the least skill in swim but trust to the hold of break part of the ship or light chest have escape that danger and come safe to shore but if any man put his trust in swim let he conceal his skill lest other trust therein take hold of he and make he perish with they 17 in like manner some persuade a traveller to use himself first to hardness as abstain from wine fast eat gross meat and go journey on foot but in my opinion they shall better bear these thing when necessity force who cherish their body while they may neither do I commend they who in foreign part take journey on foot especial for any long way let they stay at home and behold the world in a map who have not mean for honest expense for such man while they base spare cost do so blemish their estimation as they can enjoy no company but that of such poor fellow as go on foot with they who can no way instruct they or better their understanding beside that by weary their body they be apt to fall into sickness and base expose themselves to the danger of wild beast thief and their poor companion I dare be bold to say that all murder in Germany by the high way be commit upon footman for they who be well bring up when they be weary by go on foot will spend more to cherish themselves in their inn and make long stay therein by which mean they not only spend almost as much as if they have hire horse or coach but also bewray their plenty of money to their foot companion who be needy it often happen even among the german otherwise of honest disposition that they plot mischief against they which once intend the vast solitude of the wood in Germany offer many opportunity to put their wicked purpose in practice and it be a hard remedy to be prescribe to one of good education that after his weary journey he shall also suffer in his inn moreover the german account of stranger according to their outward habit and their bold or deject countenance and do altogether despise passenger on foot to conclude the solitude of the way by reason of few town or village make a journey on foot most tedious in Germany but in Italy if any where this go on foot may be bear with by reason of the pleasant and fruitful field the frequent city town and village the safety from thief except it be upon the confine of prince where hoseman and footman be in like danger and by reason of the italian opinion who respect a man behaviour not his habit always provide that these journey be short and sweeten with a pleasant companion but for my part I think the best go on foot be according to the french proverb when a man lead his horse in his hand and may mount he at pleasure and I must confess that I have observe some of our country man to err in this kind of who though few undertake these foot journey yet they general think that it be a point of frugality to suffer in foreign part as if our abode there shall be less profitable unto we except we shall like menedemus vex ourselves with unnecessary suffering of ill 18 the traveller must have great care to preserve his health neither be it the last point of wisdom to follow the advice of Cicero who bid he be a old man quick that desire to be a old man long but most of all be this care necessary for a traveller for those that be sick by the way suffer many discommodity in all place and our country man in Italy and Spain run high danger where howsoever be in health they may discreet shun the snare of the inquisition yet when they be sick confession the sacrament in one kind and the adoration thereof as change into the body of Christ and extreme unction at the point of death be thrust upon they by the priest man ready to die can ill dissemble neither be any weight so heavy as that of a wound conscience wherewith if the sick man be so affect as he profess himself to be of the reform religion than the physician and the apothecary be forbid to help he and very kitchen physic be deny he by the priest command and if he recover he shall be sure to be bring into the inquisition but if he die his body shall be bury in the highway not in any churchyard of which event and the example thereof I shall speak more large in the treaty of religion in Italy former I have show that sickly man be unfit for this course of life now the preservation of health consist in the use of six thing namely of air diet purge exercise sleep and accident or passion of the mind to shun the incommodity of the air he must respect the season of the year fit for journey and the change of diverse clime the spring and autumn be the most fit season for journey and he shall do well to go first to cold clime in summer time and to hot clime in the winter that he may use his body by little and little to these change they who take journey in country continual cover with snow use to wear some green thing before their eye to comfort the sight and to carry hot odour to comfort the brain in Moscony subject to great cold man cover their neck ear and vital part with fur and in time of snow wear a cot or cover for their nose and also rub their nose and face with snow before they enter into the hot stove lest sudden heat shall putrify the same as man of good credit report on the contrary in hot region to avoid the beam of the sun in some place as in Italy they carry vmbrel or thing like a little canopy over their head but a learned physician tell i that the use of they be dangerous because they gather the heat into a pyramidal point and thence cast it down perpendicular upon the head except they know how to carry they for avoid that danger also in the hot clime of Turkey they be thick garment but lose and a thick Tulbant upon their head but hollow and bear up from their head and they shave their head all to make the sunbeam to have less power upon their body touch the change of diet as also of the air a young man may change they by little and little but to old man the least change of they be dangerous therefore let the traveller use himself before his journey to these change by little and little but in no extremity which he have better endure only for the time when necessity force they use the best remedy as antidote against poison namely warm clothes against cold and the like and in this he must use moderation for little ill do little hurt in the morning before he take his journey let he take a small breakfast that ill smell may not offend he let he dine spare lest his afternoon motion hinder digestion for the precept to make a light supper be for those that stay at home in his dinner often drink and sup warm broth help the purge faculty the season of the year and the nature of the clime be to be respect in diet as well as in the change of air in winter and cold region let he take hot comfortable thing but in summer and hot region let he take thing that cool the blood it be dangerous to drink when his body be heat except he first make water and wash his mouth and when he be heat let he not sudden expose himself to cold in his inn let he have care to dry his foot and neck if they be wet the rule of health be infinite therefore let he take the physician advice according to the state of his body I will only add that some very curious think the art of cookery necessary for a traveller it be not amiss that he have the skill to make a Cawdell or dress some dish he like Homer bring in Achilles dress his meat in the camp and we read that king Antigonus do see the poet antagoras seethe a conger in the camp and say to he that Homer of Agamemnon spend not his time in dress conger who answer that Agamemnon use not to go about the camp to observe who dress conger and indeed this art be more necessary for a soldier then a traveller for the traveller use not to go into barbarous region but to civil place where for the most part he find minister for this purpose but the war waste all country and carry desolation with it touch the purge of the body as all repletion be ill and Socrates well advise to take heed of those meat which invite man to eat when they be not hungry so when the humour be grow through intemperancy it be good to purge they he that feel any change in his body let he not neglect it but take physic which do he may with a small remedy prevent great sickness and keep his body in health afterward not oppress himself with meat nor enflame his blood with violent motion this I speak of experience for myself thus take physic once or twice have my health in foreign part for seven year after which time at last care which bring gray heir have almost kill i by grief conceive for the death of my most dear brother in Asia in the morning and at noon let he offer thus to purge natural in which nature for the most part yield to custom nothing be a more certain sign of sickness grow than the obstruction of the body against which in Italy I take each morning while I be so dispose a spoonful of the syrup of corinthian currant Damascus prune boil and other moist thing as butter and hony be good for this purpose as a german physician write who I follow in this point and since myself God be thank be never sickly neither have the art of physic and since I profess in the begin of this book not only to relate thing observe but also those I have gather by read I trust I may without offence add the say doctor advice for traveller instruction to my observation my experience have teach i that it be most dangerous to stop the flux of the body which experience I dear buy by the loss of my foresay brother and there be no better remedy for it than rest but if it continue many day and too much weaken the body rice well boil hard egg water temper with steel red and sowrish wine and marmalate be good to bind the body touch exercise since it must be gentle and only till we raise colour into our face not till we sweat it may seem ridiculous to prescribe the same to traveller who be almost continual in motion therefore I will only admonish the traveller to avoid extremity therein and that he neither drink when he be hot nor sudden expose himself to cold and that when he be extreme cold he likewise warm himself by little and little not sudden at a great fire or in a hot stove and that after dinner he rest a while touch sleep breed by excess raw humour and watch that dry the body they be happy who keep the mean and they be the physician friend who delight in extreme and to their counsel I commend they in the last place touch accident or motion of the mind I will only say that mirth be a great preserver of health and sadness a very plague thereunto the body follow the temper of the mind as the temper of the mind follow that of the body myself have be twice sick to death in foreign part (@) when I lose my dear brother Henry in Asia who death I must ever lament with the same passion as David do that of Absalon who wish to redeem his life with his own death and sure I free profess his life have be more profitable than i both to our friend and to the commonwealth the second time I be sick to death at home in England upon a less just but like cause namely grief thus be at the gate of death twice for grief I find the poet to say most true that care make gray head and Seneca no less true that he who have escape Styx and the infernal hag to he in care he will show hell itself to speak something of preserve health by sea he that will not vomit at all let he some day before he take ship and after at sea diminish his accustom meat and especial drink and let he take the follow remedy against ill smell and weakness of stomach some advice that he shall drink sea water mingle with his wine and some more spare that he drink sea water alone which dry cold humour and shut the orifice of the belly and stomach but I think they do ill who altogether restrain vomit for no doubt that work of the sea be very healthful therefore I will rather advise he to use he accustom diet till he have sail one day or two into the main or till he feel his body weak and think it enough purge then let he take meat agreeable to the sea in small proportion as powder beef neates-tongue dry and like salt meat and after eat let he seal his stomach with marmalate let he often eat pomegranate quince coriander prepare and such meat as be sharp and comfort the stomach and let he drink strong wine and sometime hot water but spare and let he dip a piece of biscuit in his wine and to restrain the extremity of vomit till he be somewhat use to the sea let he forbear to look upon the wave of the sea or much to lift up his head to avoid the ill smell of the ship he may in summer carry red rose or the dry leaf thereof lemon orange and like thing of good odour and in winter he may carry the root or leaf of Angelica clove rosemary and the foresay lemon orange and Rose leave to conclude if there be no physician in the ship let he that be sickly take counsel of the physician at home for the remedy of that weakness to which himself be most subject and of disease most proper to seaman especial if he take any long voyage 19 to teach the traveller how to behave himself in foreign part be a large and intricate precept whereof I will handle many branch in this and the next follow precept it be a old say be at Rome the roman manner use and otherwhere each place custom choose sure a traveller must live after other man fashion not his own always avoid extremity by discretion according to the italian proverb Paese dove the country where thou go use thou as do the most now in this so great variety of fashion in all nation it seem unpossible to give any set rule since the french say well tant payis tant'de guise as many nation so many fashion and since no man be able to number these diverse event first I advise the traveller in general to be so wary as he adventure not to do any new thing till the example of other give he confidence let he reprove nothing in another man house much less in a strange common wealth in which kind it be not amiss to seem dumb or tongue-tied so he diligent employ his eye and ear to observe all profitable thing let he be courteous even somewhat to ward the vice of courtesy to his host the child and his fellow sojourner in the house I do not advise he to imitate they who will put off their hat to a very dog for in all action baseness must be shun and decency embrace but it be venial somewhat to offend in the better part apply ourselves to the diverse nature of man if he shall apply himself to their manner tongue apparel and diet with who he live he shall catch their love as it be with a fish-hooke for diet he need less care but for apparel he must sit it to their like for it be a good precept aswell at home as abroad to eat according to our own appetite but to be apparel to other man like I have observe the german and french in Italy to live and converse most with their own countryman disdain to apply themselves to the italian language apparel and diet and the english above all other to subject themselves to the law custom language and apparel of other nation and hence it be that the conversation of the english abroad be wonderful please unto stranger only because they be force to dissemble their country among papist I have find by experience that other nation who habit and name they take have reap the commendation of this their virtue and it be certain that the german who the english do often personate have thereupon be often praise in foreign part for their temperance and other virtue less proper to they in the mean time the english who be thus please for this virtue while they dissemble their country be by other accident less agreeable to the like of stranger in diverse place when they confess what countryman they be as in Italy for the difference of religion in the low-countries for that many of they have go away in their debt in France and Scotland for the old hatred of both nation and in the Hans or seabordering city for the many injury they pretend to have receive from english man of war at Sea perhaps severe and froward censor may judge it a apish vice thus to imitate other nation but in my opinion this obsequiousness of conversation make we become all thing to all man deserve the opinion of a wise man and one that be not subject to pride but he must always shun extremity lest while he affect to be affable he incur the infamy of a slatterer 20 he must be humble so it be with decency and without balenes yet I think in Germany he shall do well to seem but not to be proud where they will take a man to be of base condition if he be courteous and officious as in reach any thing to another or do like office of service and where they respect especial the outward habit esteem a frown proud countenance for grave and generous last where they that sit last at table pay the same shot with the first who have the best meat the clean bed and best bed fellow which myself experience when I do travel from Stoade to the low-countries in the disguise habit of a servant in the mean time a stranger may not in any place altogether use the same boldness as one of the same nation may on the contrary I will rather offend in humility among the italian who respect nothing less than the apparel and outward habit and be ready to observe with knee and cap a proud stranger though they scoff at he behind his back and the host will not fail to put their observance and reverence into the reckon make he pay for his pride and from hence it be that the polonian gentleman as I say before the sum of governor of castle for life be of their own nature proud do in the space of one or two year spend all their patrimony among these officious and flatter italian which they do not among the german though many of they live long in their university and give themselves to drink as much as the german though not so daily and that because the german can in their nature so observe they and nourish their pride in general he shall do best that keep a mean littus not always bear to the main nor while to shun storm thou do strain beat too much on shore again liberal modesty be decent but clownish bashfulness disgraceful 21 and because the youth of our age as they hold civil behaviour to consist in bold speak and proud look so they place the opinion of wisdom in the volubility of the tongue I must remember the traveller of two god italian proverb in keep close lip and never fear any fly shall enter there so mafa rompere the tongue be bonelesse yet do make the break backbone oft to ache it be a old say sometime it repent to have speak never to have hold thy peace therefore let he have a flow tongue let his mind be lock up but his forehead be clear and cheerful let he speak spare and seldom speak of his own commonwealth private estate or good quality which otherwise know will give he more grace than his own boast nothing do more preserve a traveller from fall into danger or soon deliver he in any danger than the moderate discreet use of his tongue it be a old proverb that man go to Rome by ask the way of those they meet but I may say that the way to go thither and to return safe thence be silence the italian say well Assaisa tacer' who know no thing yet know his fill to hold his peace if he have skill there be great art to shun talkative companion or not to seem to hear their question two thing be necessary to be observe that he have a cheerful countenance as a argument of innocency to free he from suspicion of any wickedness or of be a spy the other that he shun vicious silence aswell as clownish bashfulness he may spare and as it be careless inquire after thing worthy to be observe and what he learn in this kind let he diligent note in his itinearie 22. curiosity to see the burn of the mountain Vesunius now call make Pliny perish and the like curiosity to see the burn of Aetna breed like mischief to Empedocles likewise the inquire after the secret of religion and desire to be present at those rite have make many perish therefore he must be wary and discreet in this point yet I know not how as Cicero praise some affectation of speech in a young orator so I can but allow some curiosity in a traveller and think the same to be of great use to he since he run through the observation of many thing in short time the infiniteness whereof no memory can comprehend neither be he like ever to see they again therefore in my opinion let he be so he seem not to be curious 23 some dissuade man from be patient in their conversation say that he invite a new injury who bear the old patient according to the italian proverb fa the man who make himself a sheep the wolf will eat while he do sleep but howsoever I may grant that in thy own country thou shall be so much esteem of other as thou esteem thyself yet he that live in foreign part must with Diogenes beg a alm of a image that he may learn patience the thunderbolt strike not that which yield I advise young man to moderate their aptness to quarrel lest they perish with it we be not all like amades or rinalldo to encounter a host of man we have not the enchant body against wound which Aehilles and Orlando have we shall not find abroad the same judge or judgement which we may have at home nor the same indulgence or approve custom of single fight in Italy twelve or more arm man will assault one enemy unarm and perhaps sleep in his bed for the italian in our age have for the most part place (@) their happiness in the earthly paradise of Italy and the pleasure of this world be not give in their nature to undergo dangerous and equal combat howsoever honourable and since the father in the council of Trent lest they shall seem to have do nothing do strict forbid these combat unto they they willing obey therein to shaddow-their want of dare yet can they not put off their natural pride and desire of revenge but according to the nature of proud man be apt to take revenge upon all dishonourable advantage of number or arm and that with strange cruelty so as at this day more perish there by these treason than ever perish before the council of Trent by single combat when they have a quarrel they present arm all their body and as they vulgar say their very shinbone and hinder part with male of iron and then compass with their friend servant and hire fencer call will not stick to fall upon their enemy in this sort though he be a unwary stranger want friend and when they have do a murder they fly without any impediment to the confine of neighbour prince live there as banish man for a time upon robery till they can obtain pardon which escape a stranger can so easy make but if they have a quarrel with italian use like practice it be a thing most ridiculous to see with what proud brag they thus walk arm and guard and with what wariness and foolish tumult the contrary part thus walk about the city keep as far the one from the other as be possible till bythe intercession of friend or authority of the magistrate they be make friend which must be do with infinite ceremony and caution of honour no way blemish but by themselves whereas a stranger in Italy may not without licence from the magistrate wear a sword in their city no nor so much as a dagger either in the city or highway of the pope State how much less will it be permit to any stranger thus to arm himself if he will since we be of opinion that it be better once to die than always to fear death even in our private chamber and to be continual so load with iron arm as a man can hardly walk or breath therefore a stranger must be very wary not to have a quarrel and if any be thrust upon he he must be no less wary to shun the danger by leave the place or city in Italy neither will I advise a stranger to sight for his money if he be assault by thief call Banditi in Italy except the way from Rome to Naples where he have a guard of soldier to join with since they be man of desperate fortune and when they assail the passenger have not only their body arm as aforesaid but carry musket and have ready mean of escape ever lie upon the confine of prince but in my opinion he shall do better to carry letter of credit for receive money in great city as he pass and willing to yield they that which he have about he especial since they use not to kill any not resist be content with the spoil of they yet in general for Italy I remember not that ever I live in any place where few wrong and cause of quarrel be offer then there for they have a proverb tuttie not hauer' paur a give good respect to all fear neither great nor small so as the italian offer mutual honour more than be due and nothing be more easy then to abstain from word of reproach which a civil man shall hate aswell in respect of himself as other the chief cause of quarrel there be either make love to other man private concubine or the keep of a private concubine to a man self for it be proverbial say e may and who drive a ass and lead a whore have toil and sorrow evermore and the stranger who will entangle himself in this mischief seem worthy to bear the punishment since there be plenty of grass in the open field though a man never break into enclose pasture as in Italy so in Germany Bohemia the low-countries and Denmark the magistrate never pardon any murder nor manslaughter upon hot blood nor he that kill in single combat upon those term which some call honourable neither be there any way to escape punishment but by flight and this be common to all these nation that only the officer of justice either stop or lay hand upon a murderer or any offender against the law and this make great respect of person for a poor man have kill one that have rich friend shall be pursue with light horse while either not at all or slow they follow other and give way to their escape let a stranger consider how difficult his flight will be in a strange country and how hot he be like to be pursue the german be apt to quarrel and sometime they fight after their fashion which be a slash or two with the edge of the sword and if one of their finger be hurt they straight shake hand and go to the tavern to drink but to stab or make a thrust be vulgar call cin schelemstucke that be the act of a villain and the very judge esteem it a most abominable act it be ridiculous that he which be wound never so flight though it be at the first encounter straight shake his adversary by the hand and both return again to the city where he that be hurt pay the wine to the other (@) new or renew league of friendship in Germany Bohemia and Denmark no man will part a quarrel nor put himself between they that be at variance neither will they do it in disputation that I may mingle jest with cusn where the argument be seldom or never take up by the moderator for in truth they be not so fierce in any of these kind but that they can compose the matter themselves the little danger in their manner of fight make their quarrel very frequent in these place as every where it believe a traveller with his best judgement to shun quarrel and if he must needs adventure his body yet to forecast mean of escape after victory beside the lie and such word as we account most disgraceful with many in Germany be make familiar speech and clounish rudeness esteem for the neighbour virtue for the coachman when they be drink will easy give ill word especial to a stranger and they will not stay a minute for he either in the inn if he be not ready to take coach or by the way if he have any necessary cause to light herewith thou be incense think he worthy to be strike but the magistrate think not so and will rather bear with he and his partaker if they tumultious revenge thy wrong who will not with silence and feign deafness slip his neck out of such base and dangerous brawl a stranger need not fear thief in Germany for they be most rare but if any such assault he let he defend himself the best he can for they always kill those who they rob either out of their nature apt to insult upon the conquer or because their punishment be most cruel by the law neither be there any pardon for capital crime the sweitzer for the most part soldier and stiff drinker yet seldom or never have any quarrel because the law impose great penalty upon those that offer injury and the severe magistrate never spare they there be through all city and village with most wise and religious carefulness officer appoint who particular intend the execution of this justice theene or murderer be very seldom or never hear of among they aswell for the severity of the law and the serious execution thereof as because they be industrious at home and to shun poverty be more incline to serve in foreign war then to live by infamous course at home in Poland the gentleman be most prone to quarrel come bat and murder especial if they be heat with drink as many time they be and that because of the unfit privilege they have above other and because they have power of life and death in their own territory neither can be call in question for criminal matter but in the public parliament hold once in three year or there about where they be also try by gentleman who for consanguinity friendship or the common cause be like to be favourable to they and they care not great upon what unequal term they offer violence nor how many they be that set upon their adversary some gentleman who have be in foreign part be much more civil than the rest but in general there be no place where a stranger aught more to avoid quarrel especial if he stand not upon equal term as not have one or more gentleman on his part in the mean time all that can here offer violence be gentleman to who the rest be slave either for fear of infamy or for the abundance they have of all thing for life robber by the highway be very rare in Poland and a passenger may safe carry ready money about he especial if he conceal it it be in vain to give any precept for quarrel in Turkey where a christian not only may not quarrel but not so much as carry a sword no nor look a Turk in the face without a bastinado for the turk among themselves they seldom or never fight a combat the citizen and man of inferior degree stand in as humble awe of their soldier as the christian do of they neither dare lift up the head or hand against a common soldier though they be one hundred against one and the soldier howsoever they brawl among themselves like butter-wive yet they never strike one another the law be most severe in the punishment thereof thief be less to be fear there because passenger neither go nor ride alone but in caravan that be a multitude of man and load camel yet the christian commend to the protection of those that lead the caravans not only by friend but by bribe and chance to meet by the way any janissary shall be force to give they such victual as they carry especial wine except they have a janisary to protect they whereof one will serve to defend they against the injury of a thousand chance to meet they but they seldom do the christian passenger any other wrong than this consume of their provision howsoever in all event I will advise no christian of the better sort have mean for fit expense to go any journey without a janissary to protect he especial since at Constantinople from one christian ambassador or other he may easy obtain a janizaric to attend he faithful and at a very easy rate at which city it be most fit for a Christian to begin his journey into other part of Turkey howsoever he may likewise obtain such a janizaric of some christian consul either at Halepo in or at Cayro call also Babylon in Egypt and at other frequent place upon the sea const. and this janizaric for some eight asper a day wage will faithful help the christian of who he be hire not as a companion but rather as a free kind of servant englishman especial be young and unexperienced be apt to take all thing in snuff of old when they be sense with buckler as with a rampire nothing be more common with they then to fight about take the right or left hand or the wall or upon any unpleasing countenance clash of sword be then daily music in every street and they do notione fight combat but care not to set upon their enemy upon advantage and unequal term but at this day when no nation labour more than the english as well by travail into fortaine kingdom as by the study of good letter and by other mean to enrich their mind with all virtue I say in these day they scorn such man and esteem they of a idle brain who for ridiculous or trifle cause run the trial of single fight and howsoever they behave themselves stout therein yet they repute they to have lose as much opinion of wisdom as they have gain of dare much more do they despise they who quarrel and fight in the street public and do not rather make private trial of their difference as also those who make quarrel with man of base condition yea they think they in famous who with disparity of number do many assail one man and for this beastly quality compare they to hog whereof when one grunt all the herd come to help he they think they worthy of any punishment beside that upon kill any man mercy be seldom or never show they howsoever in other fair combat the prince mercy have many time give life to the manslayer and the cause why single fight be more rare in England in these time be the dangerous fight at single rapier together with the confiscation of man-slayer good so as I be of opinion contrary to the vulgar and think they worthy of praise who invent dangerous weapon as rapiet pistol gun and gunpowder since the invention whereof much small number of man have perish by single fight or open war then in former time and conquest and such inundation of barbarous people as be those of the goth hun and longobard be much less to be fear nothing do in old time more animate strong tyrant and giant to oppress weak man than the huge weight of their club and of their arm where with Goliath have easy quell David if God have not put in his mind to fight against he with a new kind of weapon more suitable to his strength I return to the purpose and do free profess that in case of single fight in England the magistrate do favour a wrong stranger more than one of the same nation howsoever the law favour neither and that a stranger so fight need fear no treason by any disparity or otherwise but in the mean time here and in all place happy be the peaceable let i add one thing of corrupt custom in England that those who be not gown man never have the opinion of valour till in their youth they have gain it with some single fight which do they shall after live more free from quarrel but it be to be wish that a better way be find to preserve reputation than this of single fight aswell contrary to the law of God as a capital crime by the law of man thief in England be more common then in any other place so far as I have observe or hear but have take purse by the high way they seldom or never kill those they rob the true man have strength arm and courage may cheerful resist they have the law magistrate people and all passenger together with a good cause on his side but this be peculiar to the english that not only the officer of justice but all private man present or meet he by chance be bind to apprehend a murderer or any thief and that the next constable or under officer be bind to pursue they by hue and cry from village to village and city to city and howsoever the english be for a great part descend of the french and so partake with they nature and manner have also like custom more special in quarrel and single fight yet in France they have not this custom to pursue and apprehend malefactor only they have marshal in several province to pursue malefactor with light horse but otherwise only the officer of justice use to apprehend they in city and of late to repress the malice of man after a long civil war break out into single fight and murder they have make severe law and impose great penalty upon those that quarrel especial if any blood be shed whereas in England only man slaier be call in capital question and small or no punishment be inflict upon one that light wound another for the rest the french and english have the same aptness to quarrel and the same bravery in these single fight also the scot be therein like the english save that the scot will take part and assail a enemy with disparity of number and arm wherein also the northern english be not at this day full reform in this kind the barbarous irish do offend in Ireland but the english and irish-english there have the custom of the English and in time of peace few or no thief rob by the high way of Ireland but the stealth of cow horse and sheep be frequent all I have say of this subject be only to this purpose that the traveller be inform of the condition of justice combat and roberies in foreign part may better learn to apply himself to patience and to use moderation according to the necessity and danger more proper to he than other in diverse place 24 be to write of simulation I be at a stay and grope for passage as in a dark labyrinth for the voice of the vulgar esteem the vice of dissimulation proper to a traveller and high do reproach he there with shall we then say that he who know so to live with italian spaniard and very barbarous pagan as he can gain their well-wishing will be at home and among his friend subject to the odious vice of dissimulation the very plague of true friendship sure by travel the good become better in all kind of virtue and the ill more wicked in all vice but let the indifferent judge tell i if the great part of clown under their rugged coat and most lawyer and merchant under the shadow of fair word and sometime wicked perjury have not more skill to dissemble if that be to deceive then any Travaler whatsoever not except Plato himself no doubt simulation in fit place and time be a virtue he that can dissemble can live but he that so dissemble as he be account a dissembler indeed have not the skill to dissemble but be note with that infamy so as another shall better be believe upon his word than he upon his oath Cicero commend the say of Epicharmus remember to distrust and call it the sinew of wisdom and the italian have a proverb Dio: from he I trust God help i at my need of he I trust not myself will take heed Antigonus pray God to defend he from his friend let i speak of my own experience myself be never deceive by the italian who I suspect but by a german which nation have a clear countenance and general reputation of honesty I be at Lindaw strip and cozen for a time of all the gold I carry about i therefore it be a point of art for a traveller to know how to avoid deceit and how to dissemble honesty I mean to save himself not to deceive other let he have a clear countenance to all man and a open breast to his friend but when there be question of his country good of his enemy lie in wait for he of his own credit or life let he shut his bosom close from his inward friend that counsel thou will have another keep first keep it thyself a traveller must dissemble his long journey yet only in dangerous place and among suspect person myself have observe some too wary in this kind who in most safe way use gross caution to hide from their near friend the purpose of their journey and sometime in city will conceal where and what hour they dine and sup in like sort a traveller must sometime hide his money change his habit dissemble his country and fair conceal his religion but this he must do only when necessity force let i insist upon some example which be most proper to manifest the truth in a dark argument myself in Italy many time pass for a german and then consort myself with germane faithful companion as they be all in general hater of drunkenness as some of they be either drink altogether water or use the french diet and of the same religion with i as those be of the palatinate of Rhine and in some other province then I covenant with these my consort that when any man speak dutch to i though I have some skill in that language especial for vulgar speech and most of all if we be in any long discourse one of they shall take the answer out of my mouth as be slow of speech though it be do somewhat unmannerly second that if I be discover in any dangerous place not to be a German as I profess they shall say that I be unknown to they and by the way fall into their company and so withdraw themselves out of danger by leave the place shall leave to i the care of myself and with these consort I go to Naples and there confident though less wise in respect of the war between England and Spain I enter to view the strong fort keep by the spaniard and after go to Milan another time under the name of a polonian I go to the duke of Lorraine his court at Nanzi where be curious sift by the guard at the city-gate and be ask many question about the king and queen and state of Poland I so satisfy they as they admit i into the city but when at the very entrance they bid i hold up my hand which ceremony the french use in take of oath I be much affray lest they shall put i to my oath for my country but when they have ask i if I come not from any place infect with the plague and I have answer no upon my oath they let i pass into the city there be great art for a traveller to conceal his religion in Italy and Spain with due wisdom and without offend his conscience for if a man will seem as I may say a Puritan Papist which sort they call piachia petty that be brest-beater there be danger to fall into the suspicion of a hypocrite for the italian well know te carezzapin che far' not o ba o i gabbar' who more than he be wont do court and woe he have deceive thou or fain will so do and they have often read that of Tacitus the more any do dissemble the more to do they be nimble also the traveller must beware not to fall into such error as I observe two of my familiar friend yet in a safe place and free of danger gross to fall into of which one be a German and live in the state of Florence when he return after dinner to his lodging and his hostess ask where he have be make answer that he come from hear of a mass whereas mass be only sing in the morning and when the priest be fast the other be a Englishman and go to Rome in a disguise habit do wear apparel of so many colour and so strange fashion as by the same be most strange and uncomely not only in the sight of his own countryman but also of the italian he draw the eye of all jesuit and roman upon he so as they begin to inquire after he and he hardly escape thence by speedy flight and when they pursue he have fall into their snare if he have not be forewarn of his danger by a italian friend to these I will add a three who be a Englishman and by freedom of speech voluntary profess himself a Frenchman be discover by i at that time also disguise and by chance fall into his company but he learning at that time that nothing be more safe than silence afterward escape danger into which otherwise he may easy have fall myself live in Italy and for the space of one year never hear a mass but daily I go out of my chamber in the morning as if I have go to the mass at my very first come into Italy I present go to Rome and Naples and so at my first entrance pass my great danger that have satisfy my curiosity if perhaps in my return I shall happen to fear any danger I may more content and speedy escape away for they who stay at Paduoa some month and after go to Rome may be sure that the jesuit and priest there be first by their spy advertise not only of their come but also of their condition and the most manifest sign of their body whereby they may be know moreover I be at Rome in Lent time it happen that some few day before easter a priest come to our lodging and take our name in writing to the end as he tell we that we may receive the sacrament with our host family therefore I go from Rome upon Tuseday before Easter and come to Sienna upon good Friday and upon easter-even pretend great business take my journey to Florence where I stay only easter day and from thence go to Pisa and before the end of Easter week return in haste to Sienna where I have a chamber which I keep when I be at Rome and where I mean now to abide for a time thus by often change place I avoid the priest inquire after i which be most dangerous about easter time when all man receive the sacrament yet indeed there be less danger of the inquisition in the state of Florence than other where as there be no danger thereof at all in the state of Venice to he that can hold his peace and behave himself modest one thing I can omit that some few day before easter when I be ready to come from Rome I adventure to visit Bellarmine and that in the lesuite college profess myself to be a Frenchman and wear italian clothes and that after their manner which be a matter of no small moment for if I have not be wary therein the crafty spy of Rome will easy have know i by some gesture or fashion of wear my clothes which they know to be proper to the english as the muffle a man face with his cloak or the like but especial I take heed not to gaze on the college wall a manifest sign of a stranger nor to look steadfast in the face of any Englishman chance to meet i whereof some be like to have know i in the university of Cambridge lest by such behold of they I may draw their eye to look earnest on i for one look invite another and with these caution I do happy satisfy this my curiosity also upon good judgement I make myself know to cardinal Allan when I first come from Naples to Rome and when he have promise i his protection hold my peace and abstain from public offence I rest thereupon for the worst event yet withal to avoid the conversation and familiarity of priest and englishman yea even of those that be of the cardinal family I first leave the common inn then change my hire chamber take another in a poor house close under the pope palace as a place least like to be search I do not commend the curiosity to be present at see the rite of a contrary religion which be the death of two young man and give occasion to the first macedonian war the people of Rome assist the murderer and the king of Macedon desire to revenge the death of the two young man informertime and now to this day the turk use to fling stone at the christian who they call unwashed dog because they use not bath when they come near to their mosque or their sepulcher the papist do no less persecute the reform church with fire and sword and howsoever one of the reform profession may live in Italy and yet never communicate with they in their rite by the foresay govern of the tongue by go out of his chamber each morning as if he go to mass for the italian general think they be not safe till in the morning they have worship the at the elevation thereof which their devotion be do in a moment and by change place of abode with like discreet carriage yet since it be dangerous to see their rite yea perhaps sinful why shall he not restrain his curiosity to hear their mass and see their ceremony especial all the monument of the church be to be see at another time of the day but if any will needs be present at their mass either to please his companion or for his own pleasure as go to see a stage-play or for curiosity wherewith many be lead of two evil he must choose the least namely rather to sign himself with the cross or negligent to make offer as if he dip his hand or his glove upon it as their manner be into the holy water-box rather than by omit these common ceremony to fall into suspicion and be call into question either be drive to deny his religion under his hand write or be burn with fire let they stay at home who be so zealous as they will pull the or sacrament out of the priest hand they shall do better to avoid the adore thereof by slip out of the way or restrain their curious walk for inordinate desire of martyrdom be not approvable for the avoid whereof and all snare we be bid join the serpent wisdom to the dove simplicity saint Paul be not so furious for he do not cast down the altar in Athens but take occasion by the altar which epimenides erect in the time of a plague to a unknown God he preach Christ peaceable unto they though he be a apostle and so have great authority than the blind zealous of our time for my part I know no reason why one of the reform church may not say his prayer in the church of papist and I know the great part of the mass I mean the divine office be good but the chief mischief be the adoration or communication of the ismenius a ambassador of the theban be will to adore the persian king let fall his ring from his finger and take it up make a show to adore the king yet be not judge to have offend against the freedom of the greek nation the papist at the tinkle of a little bell lift up the consecrate bread to be adore for the true body of Christ at which time all that be present fall on their knee and mumble a short prayer and only the more devout strike their breast but all papist believe Christ to be there corporal present no doubt they err in that thought but the question be of the outward reverence exhibit how far that may offend the conscience of the stranger who otherwise know the truth of that point and believe it the lutheran though they do not believe transubstantiation yet they believe consubstantiation which be a corporal presence yet I neither read nor hear any follower of doctrine who have positive forbid one of their profession and live among lutheran to communicate in prayer and sacrament with they if he may not with his own and be sure that with common consent they confess the lutheran to have true sacrament William Perkins a late writer of singular learning and piety do reach that the preach of the word and the administration of the sacrament differ not in substance and that the word preach by heretic be the true word of God as their sacrament also be true he teach that the pharisee though in part heretic and apostate yet by Christ command be to be hear as sit in the chair of Moses so man take heed of their false doctrine he conclude that the word have his power among heretic then so have the sacrament which himself say do not differ in substance from the word he teach that some of the levite be heretic and do teach after a sort the breach of the moral law and believe justification by work and yet that the circumcision administer by they be true he teach that judas be a hypocrite and be call a devil by Christ yet that he true preach and baptize from all which point he collect that infant be not to be rebaptize because the sacrament be true the right form be use which be administer by papist last he conclude that howsoever the church of Rome be no true church yet it have true sacrament because in that church the true church be though it lie hide to which these sacrament only belong yet he deny that it follow thereupon that it be lawful to communicate the supper of the lord with papist I will only add one position more of this godly man in another discourse of he namely that in the external worship of God the particular gesture be not prescribe by the word of God so they be do decent and modest according to the laudable custom of each church either stand sit kneel or lie prostrate some may infer from this discourse and the last position thereof that the god of the gentile be idol to papist protestant and to all man but howsoever the papist be a idol to they who think erroneous of it yet of itself and to other right judge of it that it be a true sacrament and so be to have avereverence according to the custom of the church wherein a man do live that in like sort a Papist pray before the image of Christ or of the apostle do sin because he bend his knee to they and think they to be worship but that if another abhor from such idolatry shall pray in a chamber or church where such picture be and shall kneel before they yet he shall not sin have no mind to worship they or kneel to they that God will be worship with holiness not with faction that some honest kind of dissemble religion within due limit be tolerable yea that the outward gesture and reverence use in the church of papist even to the sacrament elevate be if not lawful yet not impious for my part God be my witness that I abhor from deny my faith or my bless saviour in any point of my faith and will not for a world employ my tongue or pen to give encouragement to any wickedness yet not to leave the conscience of such as sojourn among foreign papist altogether upon the rack give i leave to say that the former position be grant I can but think that there be great difference between those who superstitious worship one true god in three person which article of the trinity can be deny to be hold by papist and the heathen idolator worship imaginary god yea very devil that we be not tie to write our faith in our forehead and thrust ourselves into the hand of inquisitor but may with godly wisdom avoid their snare yea that I can condemn the bare kneel and pray or outward reverence in the church of papist as simple impious no other circumstance concur to aggravate such action especial they be do in foreign part where no offence be give to weak brother which the apostle 1 cor. 8. 13 bid we avoid but rather the offence of those be avoid who be christian howsoever superstitious and this I be the rather induce to think because none of our teacher have to my knowledge ever dogmattical forbid we to hear a papist sermon at which if we may be present without sin no doubt we may not without sin omit the reverence in our gesture due to the word of God from the chair of Moses howsoever speak by the mouth of Pharyses but for the main question of hear a mass of adore the therein and of communicate that sacrament with they I must confess that I hold the hear of a mass be always join with kneel to the to be questionless a sin which the godly must bewail who by curiosity or any vain affection have be seduce thereunto because thereby they have communicate in prayer to saint without precept or promise and so without faith and have further exhibit the reverence of kneel to the which howsoever it be due to God at the receive of the sacrament yet to beholder only of the elevation where the priest only receive it no pretence be leave for such outward reverence or worship also I must confess that whatsoever may be conclude out of the former and like position to prove that the papist have a true sacrament of baptism yet in the lord supper they want the true form require by master Perkins to make a true sacrament and in my opinion this one thing alone make it most unlawful for we to communicate the lord supper with they namely that they mangle it and make it lame by give it only in one kind of bread without the other kind of wine contrary to the institution and so much may suffice for this kind of simulation moreover I advise no man to believe that which some by woeful experience have of late find most false namely that man of any religion may free come to Rome in the year of jubily for the privilege of that year belong to man banish and indebt not to heretic as they term we in sum they who fear God from their heart who see before their eye the misery of they that be infect with the french disease and who know the chaste pleasure of marriage they may with honest dissemble and little art keep their chastity in Italy where a stranger be not less esteem for not be vicious in that kind as they common be so he be not a austere and bitter reprover thereof to those with who he live yea rather he shall avoid many danger by not be rival to any of they in their love in like sort when he live in other country nothing be more easy then by honest dissimulation to avoid the communicate with they in the proper vice of the country only the german be like fire which convert all into his own element for singular art must be use by he that will preserve himself from drunkenness among they and let no man wonder that I say it be easy to preserve chastity in Italy and most hard to be sober in Germany since the first be a solitary vice and hate the rival but the second be communicative and require the emulation of companion wherein they strive for victory as in game for the wager of the art to shun drink I shall treat in the follow chapter of the german diet by which it will appear that some dissimulation be honest and virtuous the vice whereof be to be avoid both at home and abroad and can just be impute to a traveller of this kind to conclude he that will safe live abroad and so return home must carry himself wise and wary so shall he not only avoid vice without the blot of rude austerity and shall without danger both converse with papist and even be present at their rite if he be so affect but also with Ulysses shall have the skill even to steal away the image of Pallas from the trojan his enemy without fear of surprisal 25 it remain that to a traveller return home with experience I shall not give precept as to a novice but friendly admonition as to a fellow soldier first his discourse must not be general and continual in dispraise of other nation for so he shall be wray want of judgement except he add some good reason for all general and several imputation thus the italian err who come into England and see the familiar conversation of our woman do repute they for harlot who be much chaste than their woman will be have like liberty as we have thus stranger may easy judge amiss of the woman in Freesland give kiss to each man to who they drink and take kiss of each who they pledge as also of the virgin in Holland who hand in hand with young man slide upon the ice far from their father house and there lodge in a strange town or village for these old custom of particular place be no certain sign of unchastity the italian Sansovinus gross err in this kind be otherwise a man of great wit and judgement who affirm that parent in England take the pillow from the head of their child ready to die out of tender pity and charity to put they out of their pain because perhaps he hear that some woman hire to keep some that be sick of contagious disease and therefore send with they into solitary place have sometime commit this notable villainy what can he have say more if he have live among the indian who eat their parent that the worm may not eat they I have hear some complain of England for the dear rate of diet and for the people in humanity to stranger because they have be ill use at Gravesend where the very english be rude and ill serve and by some obscure host of London who use to entertain and wrong stranger have otherwise never visit the citizen of London the scholar of the university gentleman or learned man nor have ever go further than London into the country which if they have do they shall have find these man and the very country people not only courteous but too much give to admire stranger so they can make themselves understand or have with they a guide skilful of the language and fashion other I have hear speak very ill of Italy whereas there be no country in the world more commodious to he as they say that be who know to do his own business we betray our ignorance or ourself love when we dispraise foreign thing without true judgement or prefer our own country before other without show good reason thereof they err no less who like critic or the poet Aristarchus omit the virtue of other nation discourse only of all their vice again it be no less unfit to praise foreign thing without good judgement as I have hear some beyond measure extol the bridge of prage in Bohemia the monument of saint Dennis in France and poor antiquity of Rome this argue a vulgar man since the vulgar praise small thing admire mean thing and have altogether no feeling or apprehension of great thing 26 also I admonish he after his return home to renew his old friendship and as soldier in a good commonwealth when the war be end return to the work of their call like the follower of Mercury aswell as of Mars so that he return home lay aside the spoon and fork of Italy the affect gesture of France and all strange apparel yea even those manner which with good judgement he allow if they be disagreeable to his countryman for we be not all bear reformer of the World dance teach good carriage of the body yet we must not always dance so diverse strange manner teach we good behaviour yet we must not use inconstancy of manner thou do wise forbear abroad to offend stranger with who thou do live but from day to day either with thy apparel or diet or aulterity of thy country manner and why shall not thou much more forbear at home to offend thy own countryman with who thou be to spend all the rest of thy life or provoke they to scoff at thou for the foresay vanity disagreeable to they Alexander the great himself though he be a most powerful king and most gracious in his subject love can not use the persian apparel and fashion who he have overcome without the great offence and repine of his macedonian many at the first sight may judge i to err in that I persuade the traveller when he return home not to use those manner which in good judgement he allow in case they be not approve and use by his countryman but this be good in my judgement for confirmation whereof I will only yield one example the italian be a great and somewhat vicious curious observer of ceremonious compliment when he have salute one and begin to entertain he with speech if he chance to espy another man with who he have very great business yet will he not leave the first man without a solemn excuse but a Englishman discourse with any man I mean in the house or any chamber of presence not in the street if he spy another man with who he have serious occasion to speak will sudden without any excuse turn from the first man and go to confer with the other and with like negligence will leave and take new man for discourse which a italian will take in ill part as a argument of disrespect this fashion and the like curiosity I will have a Englishman to leave when he return out of Italy as taste of affect niceness and not in use except he be of such place authority and grace at home as he may have confidence to bring any good foreign custom or manner into use and fashion in his own country for we seldom commend or follow any man of mean sort take upon he to bring in new word into our language or new manner into practice or clothes into wear and except he shall leave the foresay curiosity his company be like to be shun as of a nice observer of man action and manner and most man will think that he do not so much out of judgement allow foreign thing as out of pride despise his own yet I do not give this admonition so much that he may not offend other as that he may not be offend himself by other for I will be bold to maintain this position against the vulgar opinion namely that sharp sense subtle wit curious behaviour and like nice property savour of either extreme be to be account among the owner calamity and that a certain dulness in some mean not in extremity do give the owner great ease and quietness for since all the object of human life be more often accompany with noisomeness than pleasure it come to pass that he who have a quick smell be trouble with more stink than he be refresh with sweet odour that a eye offend with any the least error in building with the very household stuff never so little dispose out of order with negligent attire though it be not slovenly or sluttish and with like unplease sight be more often offend and sometime redeem the please of his eye with extraordinary charge to the purse that one of a nice taste find few thing that like his appetite seldom be please with any meat cook or host while in the mean time they who have more dull sense use many thing with pleasure and be more rare offend that a subtle wit by all argument and mean enlarge each ill accident make they seem unsupportable and therewith be drive into frenzy while other man of more dull apprehension though indeed they be oppress with worse fortune yet be not able to search the bottom of their distaster by blockish oblivion give their soul much rest for discuss hereof out of one particular learn to resolve of all as honour be not in he that receive it but in he that give it so love be not the excellency of the party belove but the fancy of the lover hence it come that witty man wound themselves with the edge of their own wit while in the mean time they snort in sound sleep which be more dull than that love or like fancy can disturb their rest yea since self love be the very root of love this blind love of all that ourselves do or think make the Ovidian enamour person fain in their wind imagination a graceful comeliness in mere deformity as they who see a aethiopian woman black yet love she upon the conceit of her white tooth and soft skin and so they repute their mistress much fair and more virtuous than indeed she be and the more they feed and
