nourish these fiction of their own brain the more they love she for they even to desperate madness and mere idolatry while in the mean time the dull lover more trust to their eye then to their wit can find out these subtle argument to deceive themselves and so make they love a black more a squint-eyed lame or deform mistress hence it be also that howsoever there be but one true beauty yet diverse fancy find several beauty in each complexion or rather imagine they such to please their own fancy to conclude hence it be that he who at home tie himself to the curious manner of Italy and find that other observe not the like towards he be often enrage as if he be disrespect and so take every thing for a injury be distract with these false conceive offence which be take by he but not give by they who observe not the same nice rule of behaviour as he do and myself though never very ceremonious speak this of experience which have confirm my judgement that a traveller must cast away all custom smell of unpleasing curiosity and howsoever he do well to observe curious all foreign custom and ceremony thereby to enrich his knowledge and confirm his judgement yet he must use they spare in his conversation at home 27 last I advise he that after his return he spare and not without entreaty relate his journey and observation such must a traveller be as may be hire with a crust of bread to hold his peace or to speak how little then do it become he to be so talkative as he will hire one to hear he myself have hear many who have scarce see the Lyone of the tower and the bear of paris-garden as I may well say in comparison of their small journey and experience with other man so engross all the talk of the table in relate their adventure as if they have pass the pillar of Hercules nothing can be ask which they can not resolve of their own knowledge have well learned the precept of Ovid to Loviers refer what thou know not bold relate as if thou know thereof the state and this they do with great applause of the ignorant and no less derision of experience man who in their discourse have often find they liar and well know that as many hasten out at one gate pass more slow so vessel full of good liquor sound not so much as the empty and they who understand much be not so free in impart it and these be the man who have brand traveller with the title of liar but a wise man ought to distinguish such sponge from praiseworthy traveller for in all art profession and course of life some take upon they the skill and faculty of the best who be common most ignorant and impotent therein and it be great injustice to ascribe the weaken effe of the one to any defect in the other or in the art and course itself therefore Taur be let mariner of the wind force and plowman of their bull discourse but I will have a traveller after his return like a orator or poet so well instruct in all subject of discourse as nothing shall be altogether strange to he yet so discreet also as he shall not but upon some fair occasion speak of those thing whereof he can discourse most eloquent and judicial and since stale harlot by this art make their putrify ware saleable how much more shall traveller who discourse more please in the stomach then in the mouth make the very stone and insensible creature to dance and hang upon their mouth as they be say to have be move by the eloquence and music of Ulysses and Orpheus chap. 3 of the opinion of old writer and some proverb which I observe in firraigne part by read or discourse to be use either of Travellert themselves or of diverse nation and province old writer affirm that the northern man in respect of their heat keep in by the cold be general great eater then southern man thus they prove it because all man have a better stomach in winter then in summer because northern man pass towards the south daily lose their appetite and because both man and beast of the south be more lean than those of the north. this opinion be of itself true but the argument for proof admit some exception for the turk towards the south be fat general than our man of the north not that they eat more but that they be eunuch and give to idleness I say therefore that the opinion be general true but by many accident prove false namely in place which suffer not the extremity of cold in the north or of heat in the south and compare barren pasture in the north with fertile pasture in the south and upon like accident hindt the true effect the fortitude of the mind and the strength of the body for the same reason they attribute to northern man and show by history that hereupon they be fortitude and strength ever conqueror as the mede against the assyrian the assyrian against the chaldean the greek against the persian the parthian against the greek the roman against the carthaginian the goth against the roman the turk against the arabian the tartar against the turk the english against the french even in France though the french call in by the english can never conquer they last they conclude that the scythian be most valiant and the best soldier of the world the truth be that the roman be overrun by barbarous people of the north yet not for their want of valour but by their dissension and the vastness of their empire fall with his own weight yet the same roman subdue and long hold in subjection many nation of the north as France the low-countries and Britanny and no doubt the hope of spoil not valour or strength make the barbarous people overrun the roman who may have be quiet from they if they have be poor no man will fish with a golden hook for a half penny fish again the riches of the roman make they effeminate which likewise encourage the barbarous people to assail they but it be fit to say that wisdom and wit rather than heat or cold make man to be valiant for no man contemn death or have due respect of honour but he with who reason prevail more than nature nature have his force as the eagle beget not a dove but reason rather than nature be the cause that when common soldier run away yet gentleman choose rather to die then escape by flight not so much because they be bear of a noble race as because they will not be a reproach to themselves and their race not because gentleman die with less pain than the common sort but because they better understand that the soul be immortal that he die in a good cause who fight for his country and that a honourable death be to be prefer before a disgraceful life in all great empire valour and learning flourish together and decay together with the ruin of the empire follow their decay as in those of the assyrian persian mede and the empire more know to we by history of the greek and roman therefore howsoever strength and a innated boldness be propagate and come by nature yet true fortitude be not find in the north nor in the south nor proceed from nature but where learning flourish and cowardice be repute baseness and where the word of God teach the immortality of the soul and the vanity of mortal life most reign there man be most valiant also they affirm that the southern man be more witty and more wise then northern because the barbarous goth and northern people when they get wit and wisdom great victory yet can not make true use of they but lose province for want of wit and wisdom in as short a time as they get they by their valour and strength sure variable fortune do exercise and toss part of the goth and vandal yet other part of the goth and the longobard subdue the plain country of Italy and these settle a long last kingdom call it Lombardie and though Hannibal be a southern man yet of he after the field win by he at can it be first say Hannibal thou know how to overcome but thou know not how to make use of thy victory beside that wit and wisdom can general be thus join in one subject except we will grant that woman common most witty be also common most wise there be a mediocrity require in wisdom be not too wise cven as Solomon advise not to be too lust mean in outward appearance for the inward man can be too just but mediocrity perhaps will be judge rather to be find in the inhabiter of the world middle region again howsoever we may grant that the italian in the sound of their empire by valiant act and learned write leave notable mark and evidence of their wisdom yet in our age they may perhaps be prefer to other for some endowment of nature but must yield the pre-eminence of valour and learning to some other nation the vain wisdom of man tire itself in vain while it attribute so many and so great change in the world to this or that clime or star or any natural cause rather than to look back to the first mover of all human thing and acknowledge his finger in the dispose of they they affirm that the northern people be most cruel and Tacitus accuse the germane transsilnanians and old briton of cruelty bodine dispute witty cruelty against this opinion first because fat man as the northern be better man than those who be lean as the southern man be and he call Caesar to witness who be not afraid of the fat man Anthony and dolabella but of the lean man Brutus and in my opinion fat man who heat be decay be thereby less bold for any great enterprise only I admire Luther who alone and weak do wonderful oppose himself to the great multitude of papist and power of the pope but I remember that melancton a lean man and skilful in the greek and hebrew tongue and universal learn do assist he yea the german say that melancton be more learned and Luther more bold so as according to the course of the world it be likely that melancton do much in that great work whereof Luther bear the name beside that we must attribute the happy and wonderful reformation of religion to high cause than those under the moon namely not to the natural heat of man but to the boldness proceed from the divine heat of the holy spirit bodine affirm that northern man because they be fat be less prone to the extremity of good or evil and so conclude they to be less cruel which he show by example in that the carthaginian and other people of the south use to pull out eye to pull off the skin to burn with a slow fire and to impale or set upon stake and to use like cruelty towards condemn man and that those of America use to smear their child with the blood of their enemy whereas on the contrary the roman be merciful first behead condemn man then by the law of percin forbid citizen to be beat with rod after punish by starve and by banishment in my opinion he may have add the lenity of the Britan be more northward where the great offence be punish with hang except (@) (@) (@) draw and quarter be add yet the severity thereof be common (@) by let they hang till they be dead bodine add that northern man do always assail with open force and be soon pacify whereas southern man assule with foxlike craft and be cruel to those that submit to they but in the end while he confess that the german be northern break the bone of condemn man upon the wheel and that the greek be southerly put to death by (@) of the hemlock and that in Chios they mingle water to make they die without pain by these contradiction he rather obscure than illustrate that which he will prove for my part while I consider these and like example so contrary in both kind together with the great change of the world in diverse time so as they who one age be cruel become in another age merciful while I consider the old in egrity of the roman when they reproach the carthaginian to be breaker of faith and find they after to become great breaker thereof conclude that no faith be to be keep with heretic with which note they brand any enemy at pleasure and bring in devilish equivocation the plague of integrity which take away all faith among man and last prove these thing not with word but with fire and sword upon these consideration I be induce to conclude not only for cruelty but for all vice and virtue that southern man as more witty if they be good prove best if ill prove worst and that the degree of good or ill proceed not from wit but from the application of it to good or ill therefore not the north nor the south but philosophical precept godly law and the knowledge of God word or otherwise the want thereof make man good or ill and where knowledge religion and good law flourish there virtue be practise but among barbarous and superstitious people live in cimmerian darkness all vice have ever and will for ever flourish Abraham conceive just fear lest for his wife beauty he shall suffer violence and death only because the fear of God be not in those place where he sojourn for this fear of God in himself merciful and so command his child to be merciful do restrain the most fierce nature from offer any wrong to their neighbour also philosophy do keep the very heathen within limit of honesty and justice which as the poet say do soften manner with remorse and keep they from a furious course in like sort old writer affirm that northern man be most perfidious but nothing perfidiousness be more easy then in all sort of man to find example of perfidiousness (@) witness that the old egyptian be natural most perfidious yet be they most southerly in like sort the southern carthaginian be of old most infamous for treacherous act on the contrary many history tax northern man for break league also the northern goth and southern spaniard object mutual breach of faith one to the other therefore as I say knowledge and religion be the cause of all virtue as ignorance and atheism or superstition be the cause of all vice neither be these cause hereditary to any clime or nation but be disperse through the world by supernal distribution diverse at diverse time they write that southern man be rather spare and frugal then covetous and that northern man be prodigal and give to rapine but the egyptian Cleopatra covetousness and prodigality pass the roman and all other in luxury and at this day nothing can be add to the rapacity and covetousness of the turk and more special of those most towards the south daily exercise both against christian and among themselves and this seem to be attribute to their corrupt and tyrannical form of government and to their ignorance of religion as also of liberal and manual art not to the situation of the province I confess that in general southern man be now more frugal in diet and apparel then northern but the jew and southern man be and ever have be great usurer extortioner and amasser of treasure so as they must also be repute covetous and as the italian be most frugal so have the roman in their riches be monster for luxury so as the clime can be the cause but indeed riches be cause of pride and luxury as the example of all time and nation do teach and the same riches be cause of covetousness according to the poet. as money grow so grow avatice prodigality at this day not for the clime sake but for some other cavy may just be impute to northern man yet this vice disperse treasure vicious be not so great a vice as that of rapine and covetousness hide those treasure and bury they from use old writer tax southern man most for jealousy no doubt the most sharp usie sight be sometime dim and so for what cause so ever it must be confess that the sharp witted southern man be to this day mad jealous always torment themselves with this restless passion and use their wife like slave yet no whit more free thereby from fatal horn though to preserve their wife chastity they permit the stew and that because they live among man who no less vex themselves in find mean to enjoy these forbid love than the other be vex in the course to prevent their enjoy thereof and because their wife so watch think themselves to bewray simplicity and ignorant folly if they omit any occasion of offend this way though it be with never so base a man have tax the witty southern man with jealousy yet they in general conclude that northern man be most suspicious and that upon a contrary cause namely suspicion the defect of wit no doubt they who be most guilty of their own defect take in worst part the whisper and private laughter of those that be in their company yea I will say of experience that I find the italian nothing nice to show their strong fort to i and other stranger and that in northern part the same be not to be see by stranger or at least with great difficulty by which and like argument easy to be bring I be induce to think that want of true judgement be the cause of suspicion but not the sole nor yet the chief cause thereof to omit many other cause sometime a ill conscience make man suspicious as we read that our tyrant Richard the three upon the least shadow or shake of a leaf have his hand upon his dagger again the best and wise man be just suspicious when they live among wicked man or have necessary affair with they therefore let southern man consider whether they use not more to wound their own conscience with guiltiness of wicked deed then northern man use to do and whether they be not more just to be accuse of treachery poison and like high crime than the other for no doubt the jealous southern man by guiltiness of these crime in spite of their wit and wisdom shall become in all kind most suspicious old writer affirm that southern man be more prone to madness then the northern and they report that infinite number of mad man be find in Africa madness where many almshouse be build only to receive the sick of this kind and that the south part of Spain do abound with distract man and this be agreeable to nature and the rule of natural philosophy for howsoever the situation of place can proper be the cause of any virtue or vice yet it be probable that it may cause disease or health bodine against the judgement of Hipocrates prove that northern man be more venereous than southern first because our body have great inward heat in venery winter then in summer and so in winter be more apt for the act of generation the same reason be of a northern and southern body as of winter and summer I shall think that the hot and dry southern man be most prone to venery but that the cold and moist northern man be most potent therein again Aristotle say that they who ride most be most venereous which bodine also object against Hipocrates who false hold that the northern man ride make they less fruitful in generation it be most certain by our and all man experience that great part of Asia and especial the southern province lie at this day waste or little inhabit though polygamy be permit among they I mean the have of many wife for one man and that all Europe on the contrary be wonderful populous and especial in the most northern part though no man have more than one wife allow he by this one argument it be most manifest that the northern man be most potent for generation and it be no less manifest that southern man have more desire by the multitude of their wife their libidinous use the love of boy and all man consent so general as it need no further proof yea man of experience say that northern man only travel towards the south be more and more trouble with this restless desire bodine dispute that southern man be long live then northern contrary to the opinion of Pliny first because elephant who as Aristotle say have the long (@) (@) life of all other be only find in the south I remember that the turk at this day repute they old woman or past the age of love who be come to the age of 25 year and that myself do see few or no man in Asia who have gray beard and it any have grey hair it be not for the number of their year but because they grow old soon than northern man I can so well speak of other nation where I live a short time and as a stranger but I remember that in benerly a town of Holdernes in England there live in our age one jemings a carpenter who the man of those part report to have live 120 year and that he marry a young woman some few year before his death by who be of good fame he have four child and that his elder son by his first wife then live be 100 year old or thereabouts but be so decrepit as he be rather take for the father then the son and lest I shall seem by one swallow to make summer as the proverb be the man of hereford-shire can witness that such example be not rare in England where in the reign of king james they make a morris-dance of fifteen person all bear in the same county or within the compass of 24 mile who make 1500 year between they some be little less than 100 year old and some fair pass that age many such example be not want in England and Ireland to prove that northern man be long live myself have know some and have credible hear of many more woman of one 100 year age in these kingdom the irish report and will swear it that towards the west they have a island wherein the inhabitant live so long as when they be weary and burden with life their child in charity bring they to die upon the shore of Ireland as if their island will not permit they to die in ourtime the irish countess of Desmond live to the age of about 140 year be able to go on foot four or five mile to the market town and use weekly so to do in her last year and not many year before she die she have all her tooth renew again bodine may best be confute with his own argument for as he say that sortherne man be long live so he confess that they be most give to venery whereas they that be like the cock sparrow can be long live and whereas old writer affirm that the inhabitant of the middle region be of short life because southern man use to great heat and northern man use to great cold can easy bear they both but the inhabitant of the middle region be oppress both with cold and heat be subject to these change of the air which breed disease and old age this seem to i as if they shall say that custom make extreme thing but not temperate thing to be tolerable since those of the middle region be no less use to the change of their temperate air than the other be to the extremity and their change give i leave to say contrary to the vulgar opinion that the pureness or any property of the air do not so much cause long or short life as the change of air by long journey or by remove man dwelling from one air to another which change be more powerful the more violent they be and that to man of all clime whether they journey or remove dwell from the north to the south or from the south towards the north. this experience teach by many example first of great traveller whereof infinite number in youth die before they return home second of those that dwell in the fen of Lincolnshire and of Essex in England where they that be bear and live all their day in those fen and in that unhealthful air live to be of very old age and with good health but it they remove dwell to a pure air soon die as likewise they that be bear in pure air and come after to dwell in those fen live very short time this in general I say because many very age people be find in those fen but particular I be confirm in this opinion as by many other so by one pleasant example of a husbandman who myself do see in Essex who dwell in the fen not far off be threescore year old healthful and like to live long and within few year past have marry and bury eight wife all which he have bring to his house in the fen upon one nag of some forty shilling price for these woman bear in pure air soon die after they come to dwell with he in the fen many prove that southern man be most religious by their sumptuous church in which it be a great trespass so much as to sper by the very prince of Africa enter religion the profession of monk by their fast frequent pray whip of themselves law make against irreligious person and the pontificial habit of their king on the contrary they affirm that northern man as woman and child soon make and as soon break league of amity do soon and greedy embrace any religion and no less speedy cast it off again as the ostrogothe and visigothe be drive from their seat become christian under the reign of the emperor Valens and soon after terrify with burn fall from the christian Faith and the goth in Italy first become christian than arrian yea gotland soon receive the christian faith and present return to their idolatry and the turk soon fall to the arabian religion as also tartar be easy draw on both hand for the point of religion and last the german tax the papal fraud together with their neighbour do of their own accord fall from the pope obedience without any force or violent constrain but on the contrary that southern man ever do slow embrace any new religion and howsoever they be often divide into sect yet can never be draw to change their religion without miracle and force of arm so as Antiochus by no torment can draw any one of seven brother or their mother that exhort they to be constant so much as to taste swine flesh it be easy to oppose example and argument to the foresaid example and argument if we behold the temple monastery bell and other old ornament or religious vesture of our northern island England no doubt they far paste those of the south where neither the present church building nor the ruin of like old building show the like if any magniffcence yea rather the sepulcher than the mosque or church of the south may be think sumptuous neither want we example of northern king as of the saxon in England and goth in Italy who put on monk habit nor yet of nation in Europe who have violent with much suffer maintain their rite of religion moreover see how these man omit to distinguish superstition from religion they confess that the northern man first discover the papal fraud yet they will also have they more simple whence it follow that the sharp-witted southern man do first see these fraud and cover they for fear of the pope persecution or because they esteem religion only a state policy and know the truth yet abstain from reformation sure Petrarch Dantes and other free wit of Italy do see the papal fraud before the german and though fearful yet plain pronounce Rome to be Babylox but our northern Luther when at Rome he have see the licentious roman and their criminal fraud can not abstain but he must needs diuulge these imposture of religion and be weak for his defence yet can not but oppose himself to most powerful enemy northern man be soon draw with the love of religion the of fear due to God they like fox may command over lion which our good Epimethei at last by the event perceive do so much abhor all hypocrisy and whorish paint of religion as by no danger they can be fright from profess truth who constancy in suffer persecution for the same be abundant witness by the multitude of they burn in France as Sleyden write of his own sight and by the more violent at least more last persecution of they by fire under Marie late queen of England therefore let we say that northern man be easy draw to the true religion and by no terror can be keep from reform corruption grow therein for in that without torment and of free will they be draw to profess the truth this prove they most religious and in that southern man still hold their old opinion this prove that they prefer the peace of the world before the peace of conscience and in that the northern magistrate I speak of our reform church seek rather to reach and persuade religion then to force it by fire and sword this prove that they be more godly and mild than those of the south who tyrannical persecute their own subject differ from they in religion shall I attribute it to the constancy of the spaniard that they dare not lift up a finger against the tyranny of their clergy or rather to the baseness of their mind and the unspeakable tyranny of the inquisition punish innocent and nocent without distinction if they never so little across the ambitious pride and covetousness of the clergyman they who live in the king and general approve religion of their country enjoy all privilege these in this particular may rather be think secure then religious and they who dare profess the religion which they judge to be true though it be do against their king edict and with loss of good and life of which kind we have notable example in northern part these man do more fear God then man and true deserve to be call religious beside it must not be grant that all northern people have thus voluntary and speedy yield to the profession of the reform religion for in Ireland far towards the north few or none be find of the native who in this so clear light of the gospel have cast of the roman religion yet be not this to be attribute to their constancy in religion but rather to the lenity of the reform profession which think that the conscience must not be force or perhaps to the magistrate fault have take small or no good order to have the people instruct in the truth or last and most of all to the ignorant blindness of this nation which do not willing receive any rule and law of civil life or religious reformation or rather froward oppose the same and so as child prize their babble they more obstinate retain the outward pharisaical holiness of the south in burn wax light have image and external pomp of cope and procession and like human tradition last if we shall consider well the above mention position of old writer namely that northern man be more blockish and ignorant by that reason it will follow that northern man be more hardly draw from any receive opinion for ignorant man be also suspicious and hardly admit any that bring innovation the rather because they be less capable of their persuade argument so the philosopher say of a city in Greece that the citizen be so blockish as they can not be lead by eloquence in like sort the professor of alchumy seldom deceive any ignorant person who will answer as Cicero speak of soothsayer thou promise i kingdom and yet beg a groat of i but we daily see that witty man and learned be often catch and draw to expense by their abstruse dark argument therefore in my opinion it shall be say that the religion of southern man by the outward ceremony be like sepulcher sumptuous without but that the religion of the northern man be glorious within or if set human experience aside we will weight this by the holy scale of the sanctuary we shall find undoubted that we can neither in north nor south come to God or fly from he at our pleasure but that God in what country soever impart his light to his choose child which he hide from potent kingdom and man more wise and nightie in their own sight old writer affirm that northern man have soft skin then southern softness of skin man as woman have they in general soft than man whereupon bodine say that the transulpine man come into Italy be much trouble with fleae and bite fly but in my opinion howsoever we may true say that northern man have white skin and many time by the accident of fatness soft than the common sort there yet the ethiopian and other people of Africa dwell near the extreme south on this side the line as they have proper white tooth so be their skin howsoever tan far soft than the skin of any nation whatsoever by the common consent of all man they account southern man to be most clean and neat of body as well for their frequent use of bathe and continual wash as for that they will not endure cleanliness any the least filth or spot upon their apparel especial the turk upon their tulbent or white linen wear upon their head and keep their house in like sort from all filth but above all be curious in keep their church in which it be no small trespass so much as to spit which in common conversation they take for a offence as if he that spit be weary of their company and in deed by the nature of the clime and continual bathe in hot bath they take away the rheum so as they be not trouble with cough and spit it any man object that the german use bathe if not so continual as the turk yet very often sure the german use it not for cleanliness but to dry up the gross humour which they get by intemperate drink and they be otherwise more slovenly in their apparel in their stove and all manner of linen if they be compare with southern nation and nothing less than clean if they be compare with other northerly nation of Europe they write that of old the southern man pass all other in luxury as well of apparel as diet and they allege that the roman have oyster out of Brittany and luxury that Anthony himself be a roman and much give to this luxury yet be far surpass therein by Cleopatra a more southerly queen of Egypt but at this day it may be confident say that they be nothing less than luxurious in either kind as shall be show more at large in this volume and in the chapter treat of the diverse diet and apparel of soverall nation the french in Europe and the syrian in Asia but most especial the greek in Europe be all inhabiter of middle region be by old writer tax with levity levity bodine confess that the french be more quick and nimble and as inhabiter of a middle region also more cheerful since the northern man by gross humour and the southern man by melancholy be make more slow and in this sense he be content levity shall be impute to they for otherwise he bring many argument why northern man shall be light or inconstant southern man shall be obstinate and the inhabiter of middle region among who he contain the french shall be constant but how soever he can have the patience to have levity that be inconstancy in word and deed attribute to the french yet no doubt by the general consent of nation the french in apparel gesture in counsel of peace and war and more special in the raise of civil water be nothing lesie then constant and grave if they be compare with the wise italian and dilatory spaniard beside that levity which they put in swiftness and nimbleness must be attribute to the irish above any nation of the middle or whatsoever region the old writer affirm that the southern nation be more fortunate which fortunateness they prove by many argument yet among those who they judge thus fortunate the egyptian be ever slave to more northern nation except those age wherein their Pharaoh piolemyes and sultan rebel to the emperor rule over they and for the most part southern man have have the fortune to serve other and no doubt slavery be no badge of good fortune if they object the old say that all ill come from the north it be true that northern man have common conquer the south and so they bring ill to other rather than have it themselves and ill may be say to come from the north rather than to be in the north. but if they judge the south happy because it first have the knowledge of God word the art military learning policy civility and empire some be of opinion that these and all good or ill thing be circular carry through the world and communicate to all nation at diverse time and perhaps justus Lipsius in our age observe this by the read of history do thereupon prophesy of a empire from the west as if the spaniard with their west-indians shall have the Empireo the world and all good thing that follow it which hope of they though former fear our age have break I undertake not to know future thing as he do but for the old writer opinion this I know that the above name fortunate thing come from the east rather than the south and if they will needs have they to come from the south yet as it first have they so it first lose they neither be it so great happiness to have have any good as it be misery to have lose it so as the south may be say to have be fortunate of old but to be miserable now and the north now enjoy these thing to be fortunate for the present yet not to have be miserable of old in the want of they which then they know not and so never desire they for as the poet say unknown undesired last old writer affirm that few or no devil be in the south by reason of the plentiful light which be think to drive they away and the subtleness of the devil and the possess with devil air which can not bear they and that whole army of devil and witch be in the north. they be very tender heart to the devil torment with fire in give they the cold north to in habit yet of old they say the gate of hell be at the lake of Avernus in the territory of Naples have the roman bishop possess this gate with their squadron so as northern man can no more pass that way have wickedness increase or be they more unkind to we then the heathen roman that now in the age of the world they force northern man to find out a new gate at the mountain Heela in Iseland but to speak serious the platonist and some of the christian father follow they do give body both to good and ill angel Aristotle and his peripatetike and our schoolman follow they hold that angel be simple and abstract intelligence and substance altogether without body Zanchius compare all their argument conclude that angel may take body and be not in many place at once but in one place and move with these body as swift as the wind so as nothing can hinder their motion be make in time unperceiveable by we by which it may be gather that their substance not take any body be so subtle as they need not to be sustain by the air and if they imagine that airy devil be sustain by thick air yet watery and earthy devil for such body they also give they be not hinder by any subtleness of air to compass the earth if southern man have such store of light as they write let i merry ask they why the southern as well papist as turk burn so many candle by daylight which northern man use not in these day nor ever use but as receive from they but I grant that the light of the gospel not any other light drive away devil since the devil be the prince of this world but not of the cstildren of light and the want of the gospel light make so many witch in Lapland upon the baltike sea which be most rare in England so far in the north as the old roman say they be divide from the world before the preach of the gospel the druid in France the heathen priest pronounce oracle and the german woman foretell thing to come be as I think witch but all these witchcraft cease after the come of Christ for witch and conjurer I will not deny they to be here in the north as in all the world for the devil be every where ready to devour his prey but profane history and even the holy scripture witness that the egyptian and chaldean be of old most famous sorcerer yea the very northern woman which go about and tell fortune be for the reputation of their art call gypse and have their face tan that they may seem to be surtherne woman which sort be in Italy call (@) in Tripoli of Syria at Haleppo and in the city of Cilicia they show place at this day where frog and the very sand be enchant with pillar erect over the place lest they shall grow italian love a fearful wench that often fly from Venus sport to she that at the drink bench challenge love the dutch resort in love the german be say to be ambitious the french light the spaniard impatient the italian jealous to their great torment the german gesture or behaviour be say to be fencerlike the Frenchman soft and gentle the italian grave the spaniard proud the german be say to walk like a dunghill cock the french in haste speedy the italian somewhat slow the spaniard disdainful the german look or countenance be say to be fierce the french man flatter the italian constant the spaniard lofty the german speech be say to be manly the Frenchman sweet and fluent the italian win the auditor the spaniard flexible the german manner be say to be rustical the Frenchman light the italian gracious the spaniard magnifical the german apparel be say to be constant without any curiosity the Frenchman luxurious and careless the italian neat the spaniard decent i think their hose and ruff be nothing less than comely for sing art the german be say to howl the fleming to sing the spaniard to sob the french to deskant the italian to bleat or otherwise the italian to lament the german to cry the french to sing or otherwise the spaniard weep the italian sigh the english bleat like goat the german bellow the french sing in speech the german be say to be simple the french ready the italian subtle the spaniard brag towards stranger the german be say to be unhospitall I think otherwise the french gentle the spaniard flatter the italian officious no doubt if you respect outward office in conversation the german be say to be imperious and intolerable I shall say they be peaceable when they be sober and diverse affect according to their several nature when they be drunken the french mild I will rather say trifle and ceremonious the spaniard wary the italian wise in hatred the german be say to be revengeful I shall grant that they be cruel upon victory the french threaten sure they depose hatred when the blood be cold the spaniard obstinate the italian secret no doubt and both they and the spaniard great revenger upon any unequal term in business the german be say to be industrious the french careful the spaniard vigilant the italian circumspect the german be say to be singular in manual art the french in civility the spaniard in navigation italian in learning but with favour I shall think the italian be of old more famous for learning then now they be and howsoever the spaniard have the fortune to find out the new world yet they must yield to the Britain and fleming in the art or at least the practice of navigation the italian and spaniard be say to be wise before the act the french in the act the german after the act otherwise it be say in the italian tongue I Spanuoli I Francesi l'italiani I Perrtughesi we that be the spaniard seem wise and be fool the french seem fool but be wise the italian seem and be wife the portugal neither seem nor be wise in France the king treasurer in England duke be say to be fatal miserable the german be say to invade their enemy land like louse that be slow the french like fleae now bite now drive away the spaniard like Crablise stick fast the italian woman be say to be give to the study of humanity the french to the learning of language the fleming especial to the skill of language the german to household affair in apparel the italian woman be say to be neat and grave only the venetian show their neck and breast naked the french light and variable the spaniard proud the german foolish perhaps because they wear extreme straight sleeve on their arm and guard one and the same gown with many and diverse colour guard the fleming fine no doubt they and especial the brabander excel for white and fine linen and for general comeliness of their garment the italian woman be say to be sharp witted the spanish blunt I shall hardly think it the french simple I shall rather say most crafty as most woman be every where the german good mother of family yea exceed good the spanish woman be say to be paint the italian somewhat less paint the french seldom paint and sometime the german virgin never that I observe except those of Prussen have perhaps borrow this vice of the moscovite their neighbour she be say to be a fair woman that have the face of a english woman the body from the neck to the navel of the french the other part of the flemish to this purpose be the verse in latin she must have thirty thing that fair be count in which they say fair Helena surmount etc. the rest I omit for the wantonness of they the italian say in their tongue quest proverbial speech of traveller in general Falcone veder' lontany l'orcechie Asino vdir' bone all la hocea mangiar ' spalle di Camelo portar' some di ' eun ' sacchone pien' perch ha that be in english these thing be require in a traveller the eye of a hawk to see far off the ear of a asle to hear the least whisper the face of a ape to be ready to laugh in soothe the mouth of a hog to eat whatsoever be set before he the back of a camel to bear burden patient the leg of a hart to fly from danger a huge great purse top full of gold because he that have money be call lord we in England vulgar say that a traveller to Rome must have the back of a ass the belly of a hog and a conscience as broad as the king high way the italian say hore vn' set vn' studiante nove a traveller five hour do crave for sleep a student seven will have and nine sleep every idle knave the italian advise a traveller that he shall meat and dress his horse like a friend but ride he like a enemy the italian traveller say and from a new host and a old harlot God deliver we of the city in Germany they say in the vulgar tongue ulm die reich Augspurg proverbial speech in particular of Germany Bohemia and Sweitzerland die hoffertige Trier die elt nurnberg die witzige strasburg die edle that be vlm the rich augsburg the proud Trier the elder nurnberg the witty strasburg the noble that all Germany be blind only nurnberg have one eye of the bishopric upon the Rhine that Chur be the high because it be seat upon the high Alps near the spring head of the Rhine Costnetz the ample Basil the sweet or pleasant strasburg the noble because no man be a canon of that church which be not a earl or a baron of seventeen descent spire the most religious metz the most venerable as the chief among the elector clergyman Colen the rich Trier the most ancient the italian courtesan say that a German make love like a clown do that work like a ass and pay like a prince which the german also say in the same word of their vulgar tongue bulet fuch wie esel wie-ein furst the german say that Suenia alone have whore franconia robber and beggar Banaria pilfer thief Helvetia hangman and bawd Frasia and Westphalia perjure person and Saxony drunkard more than enough to serve all long and broad Germany that the student of Gena ply the cittern those of the pot and those of leipzig the play at card that the citizen of Lubeck be lord those of Luneburg gentleman those of hamburg clown and of the hamburger these verse be vulgar hamburger be like sword we see sharp to dandle have war they will yet have not skill sword to handle that westphalians devour gammon of bacon and have poor inn vulgar arm wirish iusen brown bread vulgar crank broat that be sick broad thin drink vulgar din bier and long mile vulgar and to this proverbial speech be add in the vulgar tongue gloub night that be if thou believe not walk thither to try it of Hessen land they have this vulgar rhyme hohe and less shall grobe speisse uberall hart bet and will land hessen high mount and valley deep with gross meat all annoide sour wine hard bed for sleep who will not Hessen land avoid the silesian in reproach be call Esellfrasser that be devourer of ass and the silesian she ass be call the mother of hare and if any german will put the upon another cunning he will say that the other be never in Stlesia the sweitzer be by the german in reproach call (@) that be cow milker because the man use to milk Cowes Bohemia I pass with speed and be unskilful in the language so as I never observe any proverbial speech among they of this kind neither have it be my chance since that time to read any such speech in approve author among the city of netherlands Harlam be call great though at that time the proverbial speech of netherlands or the low-countries design building of Amsterdam make it much great in circuit Leyden be call fair Delft rich catholic those of bruxelles be call devourer of pullin or capon-eacers the hollander rude vulgar plump and the netherlander in general hasen that be hare-head and because they feed much on butter they be call butter mouth and because daily pass to and fro in ship they use for avoid of great expense in inn to carry with they box of butter they be also call butter-box by the English as I pass hasty through Denmark and Poland be in the language of Denmark and Poland I do never hear any such proverbial speech among they neither have I ever the hap to find any such in approve author touch the city of Italy it be proverbial say among they Romala santa paduoa proverbial speech of Italy la Milano Bologna (@) l'antica napols gentile that be Rome the holy paduoa the learned Venice the rich florence the beautiful Milan the great Bologna the fat Ravenna the ancient Naples the gentile geneve the proud in general the italian be say to be mad twice in the year at shrovetide for their unspeakable luxury in meat wantonness and all pleasure and in Lent for the no less wonderful superstition of that time for speech the hetrutians be say to be elegant the Milanesi voluble the venetian slow the Genoesi and Bergmaschi ridiculous the Milanesi be say to excel in the study of the civil law the florentine in natural philosophy the calabrian in the greek tongue the neapolitan in the hetrurian or tuscan tongue those of Lucca in divinity those of Bologna in the mathematics the venetian in music those of ferraria Paduoa and salernum in physic those of Sienna in logic those of Perusium in the canon law of the pope and those of in sophistry they say that the merchant of Florence be crafty those of Lucca covetous the venetian most bold hazard all in one ship those of Milan honest so as if they be put to it they will confess that the house they sell be infect with the plague the calabrian be lay to be officious to stranger in hope of gain the neapolitan to love all that excel in any virtue those of Lucca to reverence stranger and to defend they from wrong the florentine to be officious towards they without hope of gain and to communicate their fortune and counsel to they if they stay long there the venetian to be officious in word those of Padnoa to be in hospital and likewise those of Genoa even towards those who have deserve well of they the Bergomaschi to be so inhuman as to hate stranger the Milanesi to be officious towards they even to their own damage those of Parma to be tourtous but within short time to be weary of they and those of ferraria to be secret towards a stranger though they have eat a bushel of salt with he the Milanesi be say to be little jealous and to hate fat woman the mantuan to love woman that can dance the florentine to love a modest woman and one that love home the neapolitan to love a stately high-minded woman those of Lucca be say to love constant the venetian contrary and to desire fat woman with great dug but to love without choice and to force they if other have enjoy the ferrarian to hate their rival those of Sienna to be rapt with fair face those of Perusium to love they white tooth and grey eye those of padona to love woman with little breast which make their woman use the juice of plantain to keep they from grow those of Genoa to love fervent and to desire fat woman to be temperate in the use of they and to hate those that smell of perfume those of Piacenza to be jealous and once offend to practise the murder of their mistress and rival those of Cremona to love one skilful in musical instrument those of Ravenna one rule at a beck the Pisanes a plain deal open heart wench those of Crotona to think themselves never belove enough and to kill themselves if they can enjoy their love those of Pistoia seldom be jealous but to hate the very wife of he that be of another faction and those of viterbo when their lust be satisfy to set their mistress to sale for profit the neapolitan be say to wooc by horsemanship and tilt those of Milan with feast and by slander the woman that refuse they those of ferraria by praise those of Mantuoa by mask or dance those of Perusium by threaten the venetian by boast and magnificence those of Bologna by gift the florentine by sonnet those of Sienna by feign tear those of Lucca by obstinate love those of Verona by obsequiousness the roman and Aretine by witty jest in diet the neapolitan be say to be gorgeous or honourable spend more sugar than bread the florentine spare but wonderful cleanly those of Lucca therein as in all thing to keep the golden mean the Tyberine pear and Martiolane cheese be account dainty those of Genoa to be of a most slender diet and no cleanliness the mantuan to feed on base pease those of ferraria to be in hospital those of Paduoa to sup with a halfpenny worth of fish the venetian to spend little for meat those of Sienna to keep a honourable diet and to hold kid flesh and fresh cheese for great dainty those of Milan to have a plentiful diet and to invite appetite with sharp thing the novocomians to eat without end and drink stout those of Piedmont to eat after the french manner and those of Ancona to live most base for their diet the woman of Lucca be account honest and skilful in cookery those of Sienna fair and well affect to poet the florentine delicate and good at the needle and matter of the family the pistoians deceiver the perusian delicate hand and delight much in fish the novocomians to bless their husband with the ram horn the roman to seem religious the cayetans to be paint and lustful the capuan proud and excellent in nothing the neapolitan delight in flock of maid servant and lover the beneventant to be of rude beauty the picenes desirous to be observe by their husband the bononian to be somewhat brown colour with a body full of juice and love sigh and tear of their suitor the ferrarian robber of those they love those of Vrbino seem of a rude nature but none be more gentle those of Parma covetous and hard those of Piedmont ever beg those of Genoa lecherous talkative fair bountiful to their lover and always adorn with sweet flower those of paint to the very grave the venetian of a variable disposition very jealous and some of they give the use of their body free without reward but I shall think there be few such for they pleasant scoft'at our english woman that they give the fruit of love to their lover for charity vulgar amor' as they give alm to beggar and affect to have yellow hair white skin and cherry cheek all by art and to have short leg and of the venetian most famous courtesan it be proverbial say son grass rise they be fat with clout white with chalk red with paint and high with shoe in general it be say of the italian woman ale sante in chiesa Siren they be magpye at the door saint in the church goat in the garden devil in the house angel in the street and siren at the window of Rome this verse be proverbial particular of the italian city the court of Rome can abide to have a sheep with a shear hide the old roman by these letter s. p. q. r. do signify the senate and people of Rome and they be grave upon the gate of the city one do pleasant thus interpret they that be foolish people seek Rome these verse also be proverbial vale (@) Macus ere Rome farewell I have thou see well for i and then I will return again to thou when lecher jester ingle bawdelle be of Genoa it be say Mar' senza (@) Mortbianchi Genoa that be the sea without fish mountain without wood man without faith woman without shame white Moor Genoa the proud Sienna be say to abound with fountain tower and fair woman and they say in the vulgar tongue Sienna torre e campane scolarie putane becchie Sienna be full of six thing tower and bell scholar and whore cuckold and bawd they of Sienna have a proverb that a stranger once drink of the fountain Brando must needs stay there as surprise with the love of the city but the florentine in their reproach say di Brando who of the fountain Brando drink become a foole. the florentine be say stout to revenge the breach of faith and to be mindful of benefit to have arm of iron for their industry and the body of ant for their spare dict it be vulgar say of they Fiorentino a die a Revederci he that speak of the florentine say well that they give plenty of three thing God keep you health till we meet again will you any thing with i it be vulgar say I Bergomaschi parlar' gross and ill far' sottile those of bergame be gross in speech but subtle in action again bianco and ill Nero cioe and cottone black and white that be pepper and bombast have make Venice rich again Podesta Senigallia the mayor of Senigallia command and then do it himself in reproach of Modena it be vulgar say Mener Orsia Modena to lead bear at Modena again Dala Marca Asinie the marchians have famous ass and schoolmaster and they say vulgar that a great lie be Bugia Marchiana that be a Marchian lie as I hasty pass the vast empire of the turk of Turkey and be ignorant of the vulgar language I never hear any such proverb neither think that the unlearued turk trouble their brain with these laconical sentence only among the italian I hear this proverb in their tongue in Cipro tree a buon' mercato sale Zuccaro e putane in Cyprus three thing be good cheap salt Sugar and whore in France they say proverbial picard hart norman pour norman ' Covard picard who make a picard make a rope say the norman of France to hang the coward norman say the picard again Francois ilseit larron he that make a Frenchman make a courteous man he that make a britain make a thief the french in reproach to the norman salute they with this counterfeit or make word fla-va-gou-la-man as if they shall call he Flateur larron Menteur that be flatterer bragger glutton thief liar they vulgar say gascon gascon green head as rash in anger sale the salt burgundian which reproach grow of a strange cruelty use by the citizen of Aiguesmortes against a garrison of burgundian who in the reign of Charles the seven and in the year 1422 by a sudden conspiracy cast out and kill the burgundian lie there in garrison and salt their dead body in a vessel of stone which they show to this day they name three jurisdiction or commonalty which it seem of old have foolish and great privilege la basoche de Paris cornards de Roven la mere de Dijon of the city they say Paris the great Roven la rich the rich Orleans la the fair Dijon la Folle the foolish Angers base haut clocher riches putaine low city high steeple rich whore poor scholar they say vulgar badaut the fool or as we say cockney di Paris cornards the foresay society of horn Diego Roven gue spin the word have some reference to a latin word signify those that carry dead body to be bury by night but the french can hardly give a true signification of it Copienx the copious for their art in jest the Flesche fanx tosmoing the false witness quatorze vn'fromage fourteen for a cheese Manz (@) the novice or simple man the solongne s'abusent toussiour a profit which be abuse always to their own profit as if under colour of simplicity they be most crafty of three city of champagne granier the garner as full of corn the challons cave the cellar as full of wine the Reims bourse the purse as full of money the troyes they say vulgar a blue Monmartre a Paris Paris a Monmartre this speech a be ambiguous or of diverse signification vulgar it shall be thus understand it be further from Monmartre to Paris then from Paris to Monmartre but in this place it be thus mean there be more of Monmartre within Paris then of Paris upon Monmartre because almost all the house of Paris be plaster over and the matter of this plaster be daily bring into Paris from Monmartre also a Monmartre Ily a putain vache oste Nonnains Ily putain at Monmartre there be more whore than cow but take away the nun and there will be more cow than whore also fromage cheese do Awergne Angelot a kind of cheese the Brie andoville entrails or tripe the Troy saveiss sausage Pont chapon capon mans. mustard mustard the Dijon Pruneaux prune the Tours matrons great chestnut Lyon pain spice bread the Reims rave rape root the limosin Pesches peach the Corheil pain bread the genesse the say that the manner of the french be di fare legger' come Scrivotio e not to speak that they will do not to read as they write not to sing as they prick England in general be say to be the hell of horse the purgatory of servant and England the paradise of woman the londoner pronounce woe to he that buy a horse in smythfield that take a servant in pauls-church that marry a wife out of Westminster londoner and all within the sound of bow-bell be in reproach call cockney and eater of butter toste the kentish man of old be say to have ray because traffic in the low-countries they never pay full payment of what they do owe but still leave some part unpaid Essex man be call calf because they abound there Lancashire egge-py and to be win by a apple with a red side norsorlke wile for crafty litigiousnes Essex style so many as make walk tedious kentish mile of the length northumberland man exercise in road upon the scot be account best light horseman cornish man best horse rider and wrestler and most active man Lincolnshire bell and bag pipe Devonshire whitepot Tewksbery mustard Banberry cake kings-norten cheese Sheffeld knife Derby ale be proverbial speak of I hasty pass through part of Scotland border upon England and have no skill Poland and Ireland in the irish tongue so as I observe no such proverb in those kingdom the second book chap. i. of the fit mean to travel and to hire coach and horse hereof I must entreat brief and howsoever the subject be large yet I will compendious restrain this my discourse Germany and Sweitzerland and Boemerland pray he that desire more plain instruction in any particular to read the follow discourse in this three part of these nation diet in general and expense in their inn as also to gather particular observation for his use out of my daily journey relate in the first part. the great part of Germany be a plain country with few hill and almost no mountain but it be full of vast wood and the soil be either sandy or for the most part dry and little subject to dirt so as they use common coach for their journey which be easy to be find in any city neither shall a passenger long stay for companion to fill up the coach so as by this constant manner of travel he shall not be put to any extraordinary charge from hamburg to nurnberg be nine day journey I remember that six of we in company hire a coach for fifty dollar that five of we for two day journey pay five dollar that four of we for three day journey pay seven dollar for our Coach but in our journey from hamburg to nurnberg we pay for our coachman diet himself pay for his horse meat whereas in the other journey he pay also for his own diet the ordinary coach hold six consort but those of nurnberg receive eight bear two in each boot on the side but if companion be not ready find to fill the coach the passenger shall do better in go forward with such company as they have and the coachman will rather go for less than stay in the inn and spend more in expect the full number the top of the coach be make with round hoop cover with leather or for the most part with black cloth which be buckle together in the midst when it rain or the weather be cold for otherwise the hoop fasten with staple of iron to the body of the coach fall backward to the end so as the passenger may sit in the open air in low Germany a passenger common pay about four Lubeck shilling for each meal in middle Germany he shall pay about four Batzen and in high Germany he shall pay about six or seven batzen each meal and all passenger sit at one common table at the foot of the Alps where the fall of water make the way dirty they use to ride on horseback Sweitzerland consist of hill and mountain so as they likewise travel on horseback and there the passenger shall common pay some five or six batzen each meal the horse in both place be to be hire for six or seven batzen by the day but the traveller expense be double by pay as much for the day in which the horse return empty beside that he must hire a footman to bring back his horse and must also bear his charge by the way which great increase his expense in these country yield wine the footman be as good or better duaker than the horseman in upper Germany a horseman shall pay daily about thirty creitzer for oat and about six for Hey in low Germany about (@) (@) shilling for oat and almost the foresay value for Hey this I write more particular because he that travel for Italy can take a more frugal course then to buy a horse in Germany which he may sell for gain in (@) especial if he sell he by the way be within few day of his journey end whence he may pass by hire coach or horse to Padova for it he bring his horse thither those that be to buy he be such crafty knave and will so conspire together against he as he shall be force to sell his horse under hand be make weary with the great charge of his meat but this frugalty have some difficulty if the passenger have no skill in the tongue in which case he must hire a interpreter and if he have not horseman to accompany he because they pass alloy coach yet if this horseman will follow the coach he may with a small gift induce the coachman to teach he the mean to provide for his horse but this (@) be take away when he once come as high as nurnberg to which place it be more easy though dear to pass by Coach it be a matter of small moment yet not unfit to be observe that the (@) coach man of Germany have this custom that the coach come down from the upper part give the way to those that come up all man speak strange thing of the Alps and such as will hardly be believe by the Alps those that have not see they the way from Vienna to Padova be plain yet lie between high mountain and fetch many compass so as it be sit for horseman but I pass that way in the company of a coach which go slow in the slony way and be hardly by force of man shoulder keep from fail the way from augsburg to Padova be like this and of both thoseiourney I have speak at large in the first part. myself weary of expect companion and violent carry with the desire to return into my country do all a one without any one in my company which I think few or none ever do pass over the Alps from bergame to Chur save that sometime I hire a footman to run by my horse and to guide i for a mile or two in this journey through the grisone country I sometime not without horror ascend very steep passage of mountain lie with my face on my horse neck who bridle I leave free to he hold myself with one hand on his main and the other on the saddle woe to i if any mare have then pass that way at the sight whereof my horse use to be so surious as many time at straight passage and steep fall of the mountain into low valley I be force to light from he and on foot to hold his bridle and yet sometime he be so fierce as he plunge out of the way up to the saddle skirt in snow so as I can hardly recover he the passage over the Alps towards Geneva and saucy especial the mountain call Farca and Gothardo be most dangerous of all other the fit time to pass the Alps be the winter month when no snow be new fall and the old snow be hard congeal or else the month of sune july and August when the snow near the high way be altogether melt for the middle month be very unfit either by fall of new snow or by melt of the old neither can any man pass before the officer appoint to have care of the way have open the same myself pass over the more easy Alps in the month of june do often hear mountain of melt snow fall into near valley with as violent rush as if whole city have fall by a earthquake over the Alps towards Geneva and Savoy passenger be sometime carry upon sledge sometime with glove and shoe full of nail they creep over they on hand and foot and in both these kind their guide ever warn they to turn their eye from behold the steep fall of the mountain into most deep valley for sometime it happen that in a turn or wind way the sledge whereon the passenger sit be cast out of the way and hang down into a most deep valley with the passenger head downward and his heel upward woe be to he then if he let his hold go or the harness tie the sledge to the horse shall break yea very mule and ass go most slow and so most firm yet sometime fall into deep valley and so perish in the snow myself in my journey from padona to augsburg be a less difficult passage do see a horse boggle at a casual noise tumble half a mile into a valley with his heavy load upon his back yet have no harm the snow be so congeal as it bear he up so as his master by a large circuit bring he safe again up to the rest of his horse on the moutaine top Bohemia be all plain but often rise into low and fruitful hill so as there as in Germany they journey most in coach a passenger in this kingdom shall pay some five bohemian grosh or upon the confine towards nurnberg some eightteene or twenty creitzer for each meal meat yet at prage in most inn the bohemian themselves use to diet by the meal but upon account the host have little or nothing for diet in the house to sell but buy most thing without door for the guest in the low-countries traveller pass most in long narrow country wagon low-countries the side thereof be like rack for horse and across over they short and somewhat narrow board be fasten for passenger to sit upon two in a rank so as they hold some eight or ten passenger and they have goodly mare to draw these wagon use their horse for the troop in their army or export they as sometime their mare also to sell in foreign part I do never see the mean of passage so ready in any place as these wagon here at all time be before the door of the waggoner inn nor consort so ready find to all place whereof the number be infinite pass both by wagon and boat neither do I ever see traveller pass at so easy rate I mean for their passage not for the inn so they have not heavy luggage for in that case the wagon be leave and take at the gate of the city as I think not to wear the brick pavement with their wheel and the wagon be often change in each day journey this carriage to his inn and from it so often must needs be a great burden to his shoulder or charge to his purse the waggoner be common drunken drive their mare like mad man yet without danger of turn over their wagon because the way be most plain fair and sandy from Delft to hage be two hour journey with consort I pay two stiver for my wagon and alone I pay seven the way lie between ditch and be plain and safe the country people continual repair it for otherwise the way in this low watery soil can not be so dry and sandy as they be and because they cast up sand upon the passenger some curious man use spectacle of glass to preserve their eye on all side from city to city they have ditch cut upon which boat pass almost every hour to and fro and give passage at a low rate and the wind be fair they bear sail otherwise they be draw by horse or by mariner with a rope fasten upon a pole set up in the hinder part of the boat and the mariner be common drink through their too much haste and negligence it often happen that the rope wherewith the boat be draw catch hold on some post and stake by the way or chance to be entangle with the horse or rope of other boat meet they and so overturn they in the water with no small danger to the passenger the rate of passage by boat be diverse but ever small myself have pass three mile for four stiver seven mile for six stiver and fourteen mile as from Amsterdam to Harlingen for eight stiver the mariner use not to deceive stranger in the rate neither can they easy do it they be vulgar know to every child every day and at a set hour the boat must go away with those passenger they have and may not stay for more and if at any time some few passenger or any one alone will pay the whole freight of the boat than they must without any delay transport those passenger or that one man this I will illustrate with one example a bark must every day at a set hour set sail from Harlingen a city in Freesland to Amsterdam a city in Holland and like be the custom of other city for mutual traffic neither may the covetous mariner stay one minute after the hour and after it be never so little loose from the strand it may not come back to the shore though never so many passenger shall come sudden and desire to be receive into it but these new passenger must hire another bark the price whereof be vulgar know and that be offer by they or any one passenger the mariner may (@) to go present away sometime it happen that one bark receive so many passenger as the owner get ten flemish gulden for one freight for if great number of passenger come before the appoint hour that turn to the mariner profit but if one man alone or few man do after the appoint hour offer to give five gulden for the say passage they may not reruse present to transport he or they the like custom be keep in other city for small boat and short passage namely that ten comfort which be most readthe find shall pay each man three stiver for his passage and if one or two be in haste will pay these thirty stiver the boat without delay must carry he or they I can deny but these rate of hire bark or boat be subject to change for in the passage from Harlingen to Amsterdam myself pay eight stiver for my transport which of old be but five stiver a man as likewise for small boat we then pay three stiver for a passage which of old be but one blank but in the mean time these increase be not raise by the mariner covetousness after their pleasure but by the public authority of the magistrate in lawful and decent manner in the public inn a passenger pay some ten or fourteen stiver (@) but if he drink wine that will cost as much more by reason of the great imposition upon the wine beside that the fleming his consort drink beer stiff especial if they light upon english beer and drink be put into the common reckon of the company a stranger shall pay for their intemperancy the dane have such and the same wagon as the fleming which a man alone Denmark or few or more consort may hire some four mile be a short day journey for 20 or 24 Lubeck shilling or about that rate traveller use not to pass on horseback the coachman pay for their own diet and their horse meat myself common pay each meal for my diet six Lubeck shilling reckon three for beer apart and in some place joint for both four Lubeck that be eight danish shilling last I be carry in a boat four mile by the sea-coast for eight Lubeck shilling Poland for the most part or almost all be a plain country fit for the pass of coach which may be hire in city and a (@) to those of Germany from Dantzke to Poland Crakaw be ten day journey a coach may be hire for some 44 german gulden myself pay there for my part six gulden leave the coach after four day journey because the horse be tire and for my diet two of those day upon our guide reckon my part come to two gulden but I be sure he deceive we in one city by the way five of we pay 2 dollar for one supper but myself after pass alone common dine in village for 2 or 3 grosh and sup for 4 or 6 grosh they use to carry a bed in the coach and to sit upon it in the day time for otherwise no bed be to be find but only in great city which be very rare and they who will have wine must also carry it with they for it be not to be have but only in great city our horse as I say be tire we leave our coach and by the king letter or warrant grant to one of our company we take up horse and that for small price namely one or two grosh for a polish or dutch mile but the polonian for the most part ride on horseback and the most convenient and frugal course for passenger be to buy horse and sell they in Italy after their journey neither shall they want horseman to bear they company from city to city but he that be a horseman can carry his bed and so must have patience to rest upon a bench till he shall find bed upon the confine of Germany in the mean time his long horsmans coat which the polonian and hungarian general use may with straw make his logding more convenient especial if it be line with wolf skin or like fur for the winter time neither shall he need to fear any cold since the polonian use hot stove as the german have and do also lodge all the family therein at night upon straw and bench horsemeate will cost some two or three grosh at noon and some four or five at night in Italy they use few or no coach but only in the state of Venice where from Treviso to Padova be twelve mile myself and my companion hire a coach for eighteen venetian lyre for other part of Italy Traveliers for journey use Italy horse or mare in Lombary and otherwhere upon hilly country and in the plain towards Naples they use mule and ass much more common than horse and the same beast in like sort be use for all carriage post-horse be to be hire in every city and for one they common pay a silver crown that be seven venetian lyre for ten mile but I do not remember to have see any use post-horse as we do for gallop the italian use to ride a slow pace and if any passenger think this rate dear he may take a more frugal course by enquit after post-horse of return so call because they must return home empty if they find no passenger by chance to use they for these horse may be have at a low rate and if the passenger find they not ready at every stage it will not be unpleasant for he to walk on foot to the next stage where or by the way he shall common find such horse so as the want of consort or heavy baggage make not his walk on foot unpleasant for otherwise the field and way be most pleasant and the city town or dwelling most frequent but if he ride upon a post-horse of return he must take heed that he light from his horse at the towne-gate for if he ride to the inn the postmaster will force he to take a new post-horse or else to go away on foot whereas if he come to the inn either on foot or upon a hackney it be free for the horse-letter to furnish he and his company with hackney horse these horse lever be call and let their horse at a low rate especial if the passenger have two or three consort and they will send a servant on foot to seed the horse by the way and to bring they back except their journey be short as of one or two day in which case they will let a horse to a passenger be alone with caution that he shall meet he by the way and at the journey end leave he at the inn which they appoint without further cure of his meat or return yea if the passenger will ride his horse beyond that inn the host or his servant know the horse will stay he and take the horse into their keep also ordinary carrier from city to city use to let horse and lead passenger with they myself hire a horse of return from Bologna to rimini be thirty five mile for ten poali from Sienna to Lucca be forty five mile a vetturine horse for fourteen or from Lucca to Pisa be run mile for two from Pisa to (@) be forty one mile for or silver crown and from Milan to Cremona be fifty two mile a carrier horse for five lyre beside horse-meat he that have his own horse or a vetturine horse leave to his keep as I say for a short journey shall pay for horse-meat after these rate at Lucca in the state of Florence he shall pay each night some twelve creitzer for oat and eight creitzer for the stable that be hay straw and stable room and at Vicenza and in the state of Venice some eighteen soldi for oat and twenty for the stable the italian ride upon most hard saddle have common a leather cushian of their own to fasten upon the pummel of the saddle touch the charge of diet by the way a passenger in the state of Venice shall have his supper and bed for forty and in the upper part of Italy for three and he may dine convenient for one giulio upon reckon or at most for a giulio and a half but if he will eat at the ordinary he shall pay three many agree with the vetturine not only for horse and horsemeate but also for their own diet but for my part I will rather live at my own charge and discretion then at they except the journey from Rome to Naples in which for the great haste especial at dinner time and for the familiarity which the vetturine have with the host all man use to agree with they aswell for diet as for horse and horse meat which if they shall not do they shall spend more and hardly be use so well in Italy the passenger must be content with a hard flock bed for by reason of the heat of the country they use no
