feather bed he shall have clean sheet at least if he be curious to demand they but because the bed be suspect for filthiness of the Venerean disease passenger use to wear breech of their own it be good to lodge in the best inn especial in Italy for in they he shall be best use and shall be most safe from danger for other particular let the passenger read the more full handle of they in my daily journey through Italy in the first part and the follow chapter of the italian diet in this three part. in the turkish empire they travel not as we do sometime one man alone sometime two three or more consort at pleasure but as thief there go in troop to Turkey spoil so merchant for their security join together till they have some two or three hundred camel load with good and a convenient number of man to attend they and this company be call vulgar a carravan to which passenger join themselves for their better safety this company to avoid the heat of the sun use to begin their journey in the evening and to continue the same till two hour after sunne-rise rest all the day in tent and every man carry his own meat or provide it by the way signify one that lead merchant good and Muccaro signify he that look to the beast and to the load of they and these man let camel horse and mule to passenger for the whole journey at reasonable rate and do wait upon the passenger to feed the bealt and to load they as also to buy and dress meat for the men. myself and my brother Henry in our journey from Tripoli in Syria to Haleppo pay nine Pyastri for two ass to ride upon and for they meat and for some tribute vulgar cafar of twenty meidine or thereabouts due by the way and in our journey from Haleppo to Constantinople we pay to our Muccaro bear the charge of the beast meat seventy and one for a horse and a mule to ride upon and for a camel to carry our provision of biscuit wine damask prune and some such comfortable thing for we pitch our tent near village or city from whence we buy egg hen and rice as we need they and sometime have opportunity to supply that which we have consume of those provision which we carry with we one camel will bear a passenger and good store of necessary provision with he but the pace thereof be very hard and shake the body of the rider the hinder part of the camel be high than the fore part the horse either go a foot pace or gallop but be not teach the pace of amble or trot yet in regard that in these journey the passenger go slow follow load camel their horse be easy enough to ride upon the mule natural have easy and slow pace and be most commodious especial for sick man beside these caravans a passenger may light upon other commodity of take his journey namely when bashaes or other turkish governor be recall from their governement and return with their family to Constantinople for these magistrate be often change in Turkey and so these commodity of passage be frequent only the passenger must be commend to the protection of this bassa or governor which upon a small present or gift he will easy undertake and swear by his head touch it as their manner be to swear that he will bring he safe to his iournye end and the passenger who together with this caution have a janizare to protect and guide he shall need to fear no danger so as he receive this janizare from a ambassador consul or christian merchant who will ask account of the passenger safety from the janizare at his return they have a three commodious way for journey in the company of a troop of horse vulgar call Cassenda which often carry the turk treasure up to Constantinople and not only the chief of this troop upon a small gift will protect any passenger but also this course of all other be most commodious for journey because they ride a good pace be not trouble with load camel and so come speedy unto their journey end the french seldom use coach for journey but at Paris he that will hire a coach about the city shall pay seven or eight rial by the day likewise at Paris France very notary and ordinary man hire horse and foot clothes to ride about the city and they pay ten sou for the horse and five for the foot-cloth by the day but for journey the french have three sort of horse the first be of post-horse let for a stage of some three mile short or long for which a french man shall pay fifteen perhaps a stranger twenty sou and as much for a guide beside some five sou of free-gift the second sort be call Chevaux that be horse to be leave at a place and for the hire of these for a like distance of mile a passenger pay some ten sou and he need no guide to bring back the horse because he be to leave he in a place appoint at the end of the stage but he may not ride these a false gallop as they use to ride post-horse for if he that receive the horse can find either by the note deliver he or by the swet of the horse that he have ride a extraordinary pace he shall pay ten sou the ordinary penalty for that wrong the three sort be call Chevaux de lovage that be hire horse and these a passenger may hire to what place he will for some fifteen or twenty sou by the day for so many day till the horse may be send back and at the journey end he deliver the horse with a note to some friend of the owner and by the way he pay for his meat some five sou at noon and ten at night I will add one example of my own experience from Roan to deep be twelve or fourteen mile to be ride in some four hour but the horse be weak passenger use to bate at a village call Totes in the half way and howsoever before the civil war a horse of hire for this short journey be let for ten sou yet at the time when I pass that way they demand and take thirty sou reckon three day for the journey and the send back of the horse he that hire this horse from Roan to deep and back again to Roan pay no more so he return within three day but if his occasion be to make any stay at deep or to sail from thence to England or to journey from thence to any other place than he pay the foresay thirty sou and leave the horse with a host still pay for his meat some eight or ten sou by the day till he can be send back yet passenger be so frequent there as the host do not only soon free the passenger of this charge for the horse meat but easy gain himself some ten or more sou from he that undertake to carry the horse back to Roan in general the traveller must be content with hard saddle and sometime with a rope for a bridle who have they not of his own the french in their inn use to eat at a ordinary table together for the most part in Totes the foresay village of Normandy before the great civil war assoon as a traveller light from his horse they bring he water to wash and present set bread and wine before he for without some refection the french have not patience to stay for supper then at supper common they give mutton a capon or pullet and a partridge or like meat with some banquet dish as apple cherry grape in summer and chestnut rice dry grape and fruit in winter then they bring clean sheet to be air before the passenger at the fire last in the morning they give he his breakfast as butter toast or some morsel of flesh and for all this and his horsemeat he be wont to pay no more than some 12 sou as likewise for his dinner and horsemeat some 10 sou but after the civil war when I pass these part sometime I pay 12 sometime 15 sou for each meal and for my horse-meat at noon five sou at night ten sou but a horseman pay nothing for his bed and if any desire to break his fast he pay for it apart but a small matter neither at this time be there any ordinary table which they call table the host table for since frenchman use not to leave it where any such be I shall otherwise have see it beside that they pay not apart for breakfast where ordinary table be hold as we do in these inn the horseman have his bed free the footman pay some three sou a night in some place as upon the confine of France towards Flanders the host only give linen bread and wine and when the guest will eat cook bring in tray of diverse meat ready for dress and when the guest have choose their meat and agree for the price they carry it back and after it be prepare bring it in warm with sauce be the passenger have a horse of his own which he may buy here to sell with good gain in Italy he shall pay for his meat as I say five sou at noon and some ten sou at night the french have also long wagon cover with cloth such as our english carrier use wherein woman and such as can endure the slowness thereof use to travel from city to city myself after I have be rob in champagne pass in such a wagon from challons to Paris and pay two gold crown of the sun for my passage always it be to be understand that in most kingdom and place a stranger shall pay somewhat more than one that be bear in the country in England towards the south and in the west part and from London to Barnick England upon the confine of Scotland post-horse be establish at every ten mile or thereabouts which they ride a false gallop after some ten mile a hour sometime and that make their hire the great for with a commission from the chief postmaster or chief lord of the council give either upon public business or at least pretence thereof a passenger shall pay two penny halfpenny each mile for his horse and as much for his guide horse but one guide will serve the whole company though many ride together who may easy bring back the horse drive they before he who know the way aswell as a beggar know his dish they which have no such commission pay three penny for each mile this extraordinary charge of horse hire may well be recompense with the speed of the journey whereby great expense in the inn be avoid all the difficulty be to have a body able to endure the toil for these (@) the passenger be at no charge to give they meat only at the ten mile end the boy that carry they back will expect some few penny in gift some nobleman have the office of chief postmaster be a place of such account as common he be one of the king counsel and not only he but other lord of the council according to the quality of their office use to give the foresay commission sign with their hand joint or several but their hand be less regard than the postmaster except they be favourite and of the high office or the business be important in the inn man of inferior condition use to eat at the host table and pay some six penny a meal but gentleman have their chamber and eat alone except perhaps they have consort and friend in their company and of their acquaintance if they be accompany perhaps their reckon may common come to some two shilling a man and one that eat alone in his own chamber with one or two servant attend he perhaps upon reckon may spend some five or six shilling for supper and breakfast but in the northern part when I pass towards Scotland gentleman themselves do not use to keep their chamber but to eat at a ordinary table together where they have great plenty of good meat and especial of choice kind of fish and each man pay no more than six penny and sometime but four penny a meal one horse meat will come to twelve penny or eighteen penny the night for hay Oates and straw and in summer time common they put the horse to grass after the rate of three penny each horse though some who ride long journey will either keep they in the stable at hard meat as they do in winter or else give they a little oat in the morning when they be bring up from grass english passenger take any journey seldom dine especial not in winter and withal ride long journey but there be no place in the world where passenger may so free command as in the english inn and be attend for themselves and their horse as well as if they be at home and perhaps better each servant be ready at call in hope of a small reward in the morning neither do I ever see inn so well furnish with household stuff coach be not to be hire any where but only at London and howsoever England be for the most part plain or consist of little pleasant hill yet the way far from London be so dirty as hire coachman do not ordinary take any long journey but only for one or two day any way from London the way so far be sandy and very fair and continual keep so by labour of hand and for a day journey a coach with two horse use to be let for some ten shilling the day or the way be short for some eight shilling so as the passenger pay for the horse meat or some fifteen shilling a day for three horse the coachman pay for his horse meat sixty or seventy year ago coach be very rare in England but at this day pride be so far increase at there be few gentleman of any account I mean elder brother who have not their coach so as the street of London be almost stop up with they yea they who only respect comeliness and profit and be think free from pride yet have coach because they find the keep thereof more commodious and profirable then of horse since two or three coach-porses will draw four or five person beside the commodity of carry many necessary in a Coach for the most part englishman especial in long journey use to ride upon their own horse but if any will hire a horse at London they use to pay two shilling the first day and twelve or perhaps eighteen penny a day for as many day as they keep he till the horse be bring home to the owner and the passenger must either bring he back or pay for the send of he and find he meat both go and come in other part of England a man may hire a horse for twelve penny the day find he meat and bring or send he back and if the journey be long he may hire he at a convenient rate for a month or two likewise carrier let horse from city to city with caution that the passenger must lodge in their inn that they may look to the feed of their horse and so they will for some five or six day journey let he a horse and find the horse meat themselves form so i twenty shilling last these carrier have long cover wagon in which they carry passenger from city to city but this kind of journey be so tedious by reason they must take waggon very early and come very late to their inn as none but woman and people of inferior condition or stranger as Fleming with their wife and servant use to travel in this sort in Ireland since the end of the civil war some lord and knight have bring Ireland in coach to Dublin but they be not general use neither be there any to be hire though the way be most plain and general good for coach they ride for the most part upon their own horse but they be also to be hire for some twelve penny or eighteen penny the day find the horse meat which in the stable will cost some twelve penny each night and at grass little or nothing in every city there be some know house where a ordinary be keep for diet and bed may be have and the ordinary be common twelve penny each meal by the way in poor hamlet at this time of peace there be english house where be good lodging and diet and where no such be passenger must go to the house of nobleman gentleman and husbandman english and irish-english where they can want entertainment in some good measure these inhabitant much love hospitality but all other house be full of filth and barbarousness but there be not any inn in the very city which hang out bush or any sign only some citizen be know who will give stable and meat for horse and keep a table where passenger eat at a ordinary and some citizen have cellar wherein they draw wine if not all the year yet as long as their wine last but they have no tavern with ivy bush or sign hang cut save only some few at Dublin in Scotland a horse may be hire for two shilling the first day and eight penny the day until he be bring home and the horse letter use to send a footman to bring Scotland back the horse they have no such inn as be in England but in all place some house be know where passenger may have meat and lodging but they have no bush or sign hang out and for the horse they be common set up in stable in some out-lane not in the same house be the passenger lie and if any man be acquaint with a townsman he will go free to his house for most of they will entertain a stranger for his money a horseman shall pay for oat and straw for hay be rare in those part some eight penny day and night and he shall pay no less in summer for grass whereof they have no great store himself at a common table shall pay about six penny for his supper or dinner and shall have his bed freesand if he will eat alone in his chamber he may have meat at a reasonable rate some twenty or thirty year ago the first use of coach come into Scotland yet be they rare even at Edinburgh at this day since the kingdom of England and Scotland be unite many scot by the king favour have be promote both in dignity and estate and the use of coach become more frequent yet nothing so common as in England but the use of horse-litter have be very ancient in Scotland as in England for sickly man and woman of quality chap. 2 of the sepulcher monument and building in general for I have speak particular of they in the first part write of my daily journey among all the sepulcher that I have see in Europe or in Turkey that in Westminster erect to Henry the seven sepulcher in general king of England of copper mettle adorn with vulgar precious stone be the fair especial consider the stately chapel build over it the next to that in my opinion be the sepulchre at Winsore make of the same mettle curious carve at the charge of cardinal wolfye have he not leave it unperfect so as none have yet be bury under it the next place I will give to the sepulcher of the turkish ottoman whereof the fair be the monument with the Mosche or chapel build over it for sultan soloman at Constantinople the other monument of the sultan be build more low with a little round Mosche over they all of the best marble the top be a round globe of brass or lead and for the better show they be common set upon hill the inside be round and lightsome with window and in the very midst lie the sultan with his son round about he which according to their custom be strangle by the command of their elder brother assoon as the father be dead and his sultana be lay by his side when she die these be all lay in chest of cypress lift up from the ground with their tulbent over their head which live they wear upon their head with some jewel at the crown and these chest be compass with a grate of iron without which be a round gallery or walk place spread with tapestry upon which the zantones or priest that keep the sepulchre continual sit as if the sultan will not be leave alone without attendance when they be dead I speak not of the turk common sepulcher which have no beauty be in common field with three stone erect at the head the breast and the foot neither do I see any other stately monument erect to the turkish vizier and bashaes in the next place be the monument of the saxon elector mauritius at Friburg in Germany be of black marble three degree high with fair statue and the monument of english nobleman in Westminster and saint Paul church at London of great magnificence and number than I have see any otherwhere in the next place be the sepulcher of the french king at saint dennys near Paris and of the palatine at Heydelberg in Germany I speak not of the prince of Orange his sepulchre at Delft in Holland which be a poor monument far unfit for so worthy a prince who deserve so well of the low-country man but they have few or no stately monument nor almost any ordinary sepulcher erect to the dead of the same degree with the french king sepulcher or rather to be prefer before the most of they be the sepulcher of Italy but they be of another kind some of they at Rome and that of the king of Arragon at Naples and some few other be stately and beautiful the rest be erect little from the ground and sometime pyramidal but the altar build over they be adorn with rare picture Porphery marble and lydian sione and upon these altar they sing mass and prayer the dead lie under they as I say that all the turk except the sultan or emperor be bury in the open field so I have see in Germany some field without the city compass with fair square wall of stone wherein citizen be bury of these the fair be at leipzig the wall whereof be build with arch cloister under which the chief citizen be bury by family the common sort only lie in the open part of the field and at one corner of the wall there be a terrace cover above but open on the two side towards the field and pave on the ground wherein stand a pulpit this place be call acre that be that acre or field of God the like bury place I have see at Geneva without the wall of the city at Pisa in Italy they have a public place of burial like in fashion to these but within the wall of the city and the building compass it be very stately and much to be prefer before that of leipzig this place be call Campo santo that be the holy field either of the use or because the emperor Frederick Barbarossa return from the holy land and take that earth for ballast of his ship do after lay the same in this place the exchange or public place for the meeting of merchant and for the sell public building for merchant to meet of small or rich ware at London be build of freestone by sir Thomas Gresham knight and merchant be the most stare build in that kind that I have see in Europe or Turkey for that of Antwerp in imitation whereof this be say to have be build be never see by i and I have hear many prefer this before that Ancona in Italy have such a place much less but very beautiful and that of hamburg in Germany be much to be commend that of Constantinople call Besensteine be markable rather for the precious ware and jewel then for the building which be only of brick and therein also they sell captive for slave for public house build for the meeting of the senate there be many in Germany senate-house which be very stare and among they that of hamburg seem to i the fair the german low-countriman and sweitzer have in each city public house public place for recreation where the citizen meet to walk and for exercise as shoot in the piece and crossbow and where they also drink and feast together those of Sweitzerland be less sumptuous for building but much more pleasant have tree within the bough whereof they have room or arbour build upon the body of the tree whereof diverse contain three or four table beside that water be draw up by pipe to the top of the tree for wash of glass and like use among the stable of prince that of the duke of Saxony at dresden in Germany be stable the fair of all other and a rare build for that purpose for clock that of strasburg in Germany be most artificial and to be wonder at and be much to be prefer before that of Lubeck or any other in the clock World in Italy there be many wonderful ruin of old theatre water conduit and theatre and water conduit like monument in many place but these ruin belong not proper to this discourse and there be few like build of late only the water counduit at Rome new build by pope sixtus be a kingly work and the theatre at Vicenza now stand and in use for comedy be fair and stare the theatre at London in England for stageplay be more remarkable for the number and for the capacity then for the build the bridge at London be worthy to be number among the miracle of the world Bridges if man respect the building and foundation lay artificial and stately over a ebb and flow water upon 21 pile of stone with 20 arch under which bark may pass the low foundation be as they say pack of will most durable against the force of the water and not to be repair but upon great fall of the water and by artificial turn or stop the recourse of they or if man respect the house build upon the bridge as great and high as those of the firm land so as a man can know that he pass a bridge but will judge himself to be in the street save that the house on both side be combine in the top make the passage somewhat dark and that in some few open place the river of Thames may be see on both side in the second rank be the bridge of our lady at Paris in France the next place belong to the bridge of Venice call Rialto consist of one but a high arch and build partly of marble partly of freestone and to be ascend by many stair on both side and have low shop upon the ascent and on the top and for the build of the whole bridge be more stately than that of Paris in the four rank be the bridge of Florence over the river Arno and for bare bridge have no building upon they the bridge of dresden in Germany and many other bridge in England in my opinion may be prefer before that of Florence have it not those house build upon it but the bridge of Rome call Sant' Angelo be not worth the name howsoever the italian after their brag manner have make the same appear famous to man that never see it as likewise they have spread the fame of the roman river Tyberis which at Rome and run under this bridge deserve rather to be account a little brook or a ditch than a river and as for the inundation thereof they be rather to be attribute to the narrowness of the bed than the greatness of the river only increase upon the fall of rain or melt snow from the neighbour mountain and make no haven at the very mouth where it enter into the Sea sure howsoever the italian be of old very magnificent in many thing yet by obtrude their latin tongue to all nation they conquer they do likewise make they conceive of every spring brook and porch as of the world miracle and there be some among they at this day who in their sleeve laugh at traveller curious search after these antiquity thus make famous by old writer and who take pleasure to delude this their simplicity for such they esteem it howsoever we do but by the way search after they have more profitable end of our journey into Italy there be some other bridge of exceed length as those of strasburg and Wien more know by the name of Vienna in Germany but they be only build of timber and lay over with loose piece of timber that upon danger of army and inundation they may be more easy break and build up again and so be nothing less than durable I do only here mention they the goldsmith shop at London in England be in diverse street but especial that call cheapc-side be exceed rich furnish continual with gold and silver goldesmiths shop plate and jewel the goldsmith shop upon the bridge at Florence and Paris have perhaps sometime be as rich or better furnish for the time or some nuptial feast of the prince or like occasion with plate and jewel borrow of private person for that purpose but I may lawful say set all love of my country apart that I do never see any such daily show any thing so sumptuous in any place of the world as in London I will not speak in this place of church and academical college but refer they to their proper place where in the last part I shall treat of religion and university church and college only here it shall suffice in a word to say that the church in England and college in the university thereof consider due circumstance be to be much admire and prefer before any in the World Munster affirm that the roman build in Germany beyond the river Danow and Rhine more stately city then be to be see in other part thereof but for my part building in Germany I will say that nurnberg dresden Brestawe the chief city of silesia and the city upon the sea coast towards the north call Hans stetten that be free city be much fair and far more magnificent build than those that he name the city within land except Ertford those of Hessen and some like which be build of timber clay and plaster or of dirt and cover with tile of wood be build of great polish and carve free stone four or five story high with a high roof bear window and cover with tile some of the foresay city on the sea coast be build of free stone but for the most part of brick and in the low before room be common large they have great door like gate towards the street which be open by day expose to the eye of passenger the bright dish and other like vessel of tin and brass which use to be set forth in the upper end of that room and not only the private house but the public street be notable for cleanliness uniformity and beauty the house and building upon the alps except some few city which be equal to the foresay build within land as insprucke lie in the way between augsburg and Padova be build much low most common of freestone and cover with tile of wood and for three part of the year they be continual cover with snow and as through all Germany the casement of the window be little so in these part they be much less so as a man can hardly put his head out of they and the window on the inside be all to be shut close with wooden window they use all possible art to keep the cold out and to retain the heat of the stoave within nobleman castle in Germany be for the most part answerable to the build of the city within land but they show more antiquity and be more build for strength then beauty and the more stately palace of city and these castle in some place as myself observe at augsburg but very rare be cover with copper which Germany yield but never with lead whereof they have none of their own the building of house in the town be for matter like to that of the city but low and the village for the most part be build of timber clay and plaster or dirt and cover with tile of wood or the worst of they with straw in general at the most frequent door of every house they have rope that man without by the ring of a bell may be let in and those within may open the door by the pull of a rope without go to they and the door likewise by weight be make to shut of themselves at the heel of he that come in without the help of any hand the window in general be of a thick glass with little casement close in wood not in iron which they seldom open that the stoave may be keep warm to conclude Germany yield to no place in the world for the number or stately build of city yet so as respect the greatness thereof it have not so frequent and little distant city as netherlands and other less dominion have among the city of Sweitzerland the build of bern be most uniform and fair be of freestone with arch cloister towards the street as in some part of Sweitzerland of Italy under which man walk dry foot after the great rain but most of the city as of the town and village be fair build with timber clay and plaster and common be seat on the decline side of mountain and hill of the last sort be the house of Boemerland save that the wall and foundation of Boemerland be make of whole body of tree as they be cut down even cover with the bark and join together with clay and mortar and these house be general cover with tile of wood not with slate in the unite province the house be most of brick aswell in city as in village and so uniform as if they have all be build at a time and by the same workman low. country the front of they towards the street be common narrow except some few that be broad and high and so be build inward in length with narrow window and nothing at all cast out from the wall and the roof have window for use of the room be cover with tile or piece of wood in the same form at Leyden and Delft the house be build very high of many story and the roof be steep and have also window for use at the hage be a most pleasant village where the state keep their residence the building be like but the castle in which count Maurice dwell and some few gentleman house be build of freestone and in some outlane many house be cover with straw the ware of merchant the vessel of tin and brass be keep most clean and like ornament lie open in the low and great room by large door to passenger view as I former say of the city on the sea coast of Germany they build with very slender timber so as the new build threaten fall and upon little force totter and shake the floare be pave with plaster board be more costly and apt to take fire and these floare be daily cast over with sand to keep they from dirt only by sweep away the sand at night in the kingdom of Denmark there be only one city compass with wall call of Denmark Kopenhagen in which as in the other little city townss and village the house of the citizen be for the most part of timber clay and plaster seldom of freestone the king castle there be build of free stone but with no magnificence his second castle at elsenure be very stately build of freestone and also strong fortify to command one side of the straight sea where great tribute be exact of all ship enter or go out of the sound in Poland there be but few city for so large a kingdom but the house of they of Poland be build of free stone much like to the inland city of Germany save that in some place the house be arch towards the street like the cloister of monastery some of the house in the village be of timber clay and plaster but the great part of mere dirt and cover with straw few of the noble man house be of free stone but the great part of timber clay and plaster the italian be exceed rich in their own commodity and by traffic of Italy which the gentleman and prince scorn not to use and they spend little in housekeeping or apparel all their pride be to build stately house water counduit fountain and to have rich jewel and like permanent thing all the building have thrasonical inscription either engrave or paint upon they of the founder praise and almost of his pedigree the house of citizen and of the common sort be of unpolished stone and common of little flint low build with a roof almost lie flat without window and cover with tile the gentleman palace be most frequent and be build as well in city as in the country of carve freestone and marble and most of they altogether of diverse colour marble for they have many quarry of marble so as the same be not far fetch the magnificence of their building be rather in show then in charge to be prefer before our building of polish freestone many of their palace seem fit to receive a king with his court for the stately building but not for the capacity the italian family be small and solitary the building of they be in such fashion as the first upper chamber be large and as long as a gallery fit to entertain great company with feast and dance the window be great and lie open to admit air and cover with arras to lean upon and have on the inside window of wood to close by night the rest of the chamber be on each side of this gallery rich furnish with hang bed embroider and sumptuous furniture and tapestry spread under foot where the chair stand but the room be narrow and have little window cover but not with glass for the window of all Italy be cover with linen cloth or paper only the city of Venice boast of the singularity that the window thereof be common glaze yet so be also the window of some few palace in other part the italian more willing spend their money in building wherein they delight to have cool chamber with open terrace lie upon water and shade on the side of the house where the sun lest come and likewise in adorn fountain with shade seat and image in make cave under the earth and water-conduit then in any earthly thing their mistress always except and because they can endure labour for their disease joint or the heat of the country they strive to make their stair very easy and by much compass to raise the ground by little and little wherein they use so great art as in some place a horseman may with ease and almost unsensible ascent ride up to the top of high tower the floare of their upper chamber be not board but pave with plaster or with a matter make of lime and tile some city as Bologna padona and other in the dukedom of Mantua be build with arch towards the street like cloister of monastery and how soever the street be dirty yet under they be pleasant and dry walk even in rainy weather for other city the street whereof lie open at Rome they be pave convenient with flint at Sienna beautiful with brick at Florence Pistoia Lucca and Naples stately with freestone very broad and easy touch Tarky in the greek land in Asia and Africa common the house of Turkey be build of unpolished stone and flint only one roof high and many time with arch towards the street always with window not glaze or cover but open and the floor on the top of house be beat plain with plaster and compass with a wall indent on the top some yard high and here in the open air they eat and walk by day and lodge at night so as a man may see all his neighbour in bed about he for they be seldom some once or twice in the year about September trouble with rain particular at jerusalem for their lock the key be make of wood not of iron at Constantinople and in the province adjoin the house be common of impolish stone and flint or of timber and clay plaster or of earth form like brick but not harden by fire and be build some two story high and with a roof almost lie flat without window and cover with tile much after the common build of Italy in general the house have large window not close with glass or other thing but altogether open only by night to be close with wooden window after the italian manner the street of Constantinople be narrow raise on each side for the passage of man and woman but there also narrow the middle part of the street be so broad as one ass or like beast load may pass for they use no coach or cart the sultan or emperor palace vulgar call and by the italian seraglio be of some three square courtyard build round about of freestone and piece of marble with arch towards the yard like cloister which be pave with broad stone and this palace have a very spacious garden round compass with a stone wall some few palace of the vizier or bashaes in this city be build of polish freestone two roof high with the high roof almost flat after the manner of the italian palace and it be worth the observation that each Mosche or turkish church rise in the top with diverse globe and they be cover with brass or copper they make a beautiful show especial the sun shine and more especial because they be seat upon hill the turkish bashacs laugh at they who tell how beautiful and stately the christian palace be builton the outside as if they only respect the inward magnificence but christian do also this inward beauty some more some less and that the turk neglect outward beauty the desire of inward beauty be not the true reason thereof for as they place all religion in the show and outward thing so no doubt they will in this also great respect the outside be it not that they live under such a tyrannical government which make all the empire full of desolation so as they do not preserve the house they conquer from christian in their wont magnificence much less themselves erect such stately building ' yea dare not have any rich householdstuff at least to be vulgar see lest their riches shall make they a prey to their magistrate or soldier find nothing so safe unto they as the sordid show of poverty and this be the cause that many of they hide and bury their treasure under the earth whereof the free use will prove dangerous to they and as the poet say what do riches profit i who to use they be not free but the turk and especial the christian subject to they be bear and from infancy breed under the yoke of perpetual slavery and have never taste the sweetness of liberty do bear with ease this burden which we think unsupportable in France the house of Paris be of unpolished great stone cover over with plaster and be build stately three or four sometime six roof high with the of France high roof which have window and they be cover with tile the building of other city be common of timber clay and plaster sometime of freestone and four or five roof high whereof each as it be high so be more project into the street much darken the same and cause the rain to fall into the midst thereof the street be no broad than for two cart to meet and pass one by the other almost under every house be a cellar to lay up wine Perry cider and all kind of drink and few of the window be glaze which be also darken with grate of wood the rest be altogether open to be shut by night with window of wood the building of the village be like we in England common of timber and clay and thatch over the gentleman house be build like those in the city whereof I have speak but the palace of great lord for the most part be stately build of free stone yet more beautiful and stately be the king palace common of free stone curious carve with pillar of marble and sometime of brick with of marble in the part most open to the eye among these palace of the king that of fontainebleau be the most stately and magnificent that I do see and most pleasant for the garden and sweet air Caesar in his commentary say that building of England be then like those of France now at London the house of the citizen especial in the chief street of England be very narrow in the front towards the street but be build five or six roof high common of timber and clay with plaster and be very neat and ommodious within and the build of citizen house in other city be not much unlike this but withal understand that in London many stately palace build by nobleman upon the river Thames do make a very great show to they that pass by water and that there be many more like palace also build towards land but scatter and great part of they in back lane and street which if they be join to the first in good order as other city be build uniform they will make not only fair street but even a beautiful city to which few may just be prefer for the magnificence of the build beside that the alderman and chief citizen house howsoever they be stately for building yet be build all inward that the whole room towards the street may be reserve for shop of tradesman make no show outward so as in truth all the magnificence of London building be hide from the view of stranger at the first sight till they have more particular view thereof by long abode there and then they will prefer the building of this famous city to many that appear more stately at the first sight great part of the town and village be build like the citizen house in London save that they be not so many story high nor so narrow in the front towards the street other of they be build in like sort of unpolished small stone and some of the village in Lincolnshire and some other country be of mere clay and cover with thatch yet even these house be more commodious within for cleanliness lodging and diet than any stranger will think they to be most of the house in city and town have cellar under they where for coolness they lay beer and wine gentleman house for the most part be build like those in the city but very many of gentleman and nobleman palace aswell near London as in other country be stately build of brick and free stone whereof many yield not in magnificence to like building of other kingdom as Homby build by Sr Christopher hatton Tybals late belong to the earl of Salisbury seat near London and the earl of Exeter his house near Stamford by which palace lie near the high way a stranger may judge of many other like stately building in other part the king palace be of such magnificent building so curious art and such pleasure and beauty for garden and fountain and be so many in number as England need not envy any other kingdom therein among they be many a stranger may see near London the king palace of Hampton court of richmond of Greenwich of Nonsuch of otelands of Schene of Winsore and in London the palace of white Hall in Scotland the city Edinburgh be fair build of unpolished stone but the gallery of timber build upon the front of the house do rather obscure then adorn they of Scotland and the king palace at one end and the fortify castle at the other end of the city be more stately build then the rest but all the beauty of the city confir of one large street the by lane be few and full of beggary the house in village and scatter in the country be like to those in England but the gentleman and nobleman house be nothing so frequent nor so stately build as the better sort of the English neither be their I own and city in number building or pleasantness comparable to those in England last the village of clay cover with straw be much more frequent than in England and far less commodious within among the king palace that at edenburg and that of sterling for the building and Fawkland for the pleasure of hunt be the chief the house of the irish city as cork galoway and Lymrick the fair of they for building be of unwrought free stone or flint or unpolished stone build some two of Ireland story high and cover with tile the house of Dublin and Waterford be for the most part of timber clay and plaster yet be the street beautiful and the house commodious within even among the irish if you pardon they a little slovenlinesse proper to the nation in general the house very seldom keep out rain the timber be not well season and the wall be general combine with clay only not with mortar of lime temper the irish have some quarry of marble but only some few lord and gentleman bestow the cost to polish it many gentleman have castle build of free stone unpolished and of flint or little stone and they be build strong for defence in time of rebellion for which cause they have narrow stair and little window and common they have a spacious hall join to the castle and build of timber and clay wherein they eat with their family neither be many of these gentle man house void of filth and slovenlinesse for other irish dwelling it may be say of they as Caesar say of the old brittane house they call it a town when they have compass a skirt of wood with tree cut down whether they may retire themselves and their cattle for the mere barbarous irish either sleep under the canopy of heaven or in cabbine wattle and cover with turf the german long enjoy settle peace the french and the nitherlander for of fort in general many year distract with war have many city strong fortify with ditch and earthen wall the turk polonian sweitzer bohemian dane english scot and irish have few fort or fortify place the king of England have cause such to be dismantle and pull down as encouragement to the noble to contemn their authority only at Barwick against the border scot and at (@) against the neighbour french they have maintain fortification to hinder incursion the turk neither fortify themselves nor maintain the strong place they have conquer from christian in Denmark the city Kopenhagen and in Poland Crakaw and warsaw be in some sort fortify and in Ireland the english of late have make small fort upon some few haven to prevent foreign invasion and in some inland territory to suppress the rebellious inclination of some irish lord otherwise in the say kingdom if any city be compass with wall they be ready to fall for age and be rather fit to resist the first fury of ill arm mutineer then to endure a sharp siege or the very sight of the cannon like be most of the city in Italy only at Naples and at Milan there be two strong fort and at Rome a strong castle and in Lombardy and especial in the state of Venice many city and some fort make as strong as huge charge and exquisite art can make they chap. 3 of Germany Boemerland and Sweitzerland touch the geographical description the situation the fertility the traffic and the diet the geographer search out the greatness of the globe and of all the part in the superficies thereof by the help in general of geography of the celestial circle fit to the Conuex or bend of the earth the circle of heaven be of two sort the great and the less the great be six in number the zodiac two Colun meridian and horizon of which the geographer in the description of the world only make use of the and meridian the compass the middle swell of the (@) sphere between both the pole of the world and the great convexity or bend thereof from the east towards the west to which circle when the sun be come by his proper motion in each year twice it make two equinoctial that be day and night of equal length one in the spring the other at the fall of the leaf the circle in the convex or bend superficies of the earth that be direct and perpendicular under the say be call the of the earth and compass the earth from the east to the west divide it into two hemisphere that be half sphere the northern and the southern the meridian circle be draw through the pole of the heaven in which the Meridian meet and through the vertical point that be the meridian point right over head of each place whether the sun be come by his accidental motion in each day it make noon above the horizon and midnight under the horizon or with the antipode the circle in the convex or bend of the earth direct and perpendicular under this circle meridian pass by the extreme point of the earth that be under the pole and by any appoint place in the superficies or upper face of the earth be call a meridian of the earth and because there be no certain number of particular place on the earth it follow that the Meridian be innumerable so as every place distant from another towards the east or west have his own peculiar meridian diverse from the meridian of another place yet for make of map and like use the geographer appoint one hundred eighty Meridian namely ninty easterly and ninety westerly the lesser circle be call parallel that be equal distant because have relation parallel one to the other or to any of the great circle they be in all part equal distant for all lesser circle have relation to one of the great and be call the parallel of this or that great circle but here only mention be make of the parallel refer to the which be lesser circle draw near the from east towards west or contrary by the vertical point of several place in heaven or by the place themselves in the upper face of the earth and they be the great the near they be to the the lesser as they be more distant from the same towards either pole and the geographeis call they northern parallel which be near the in the northern hemisphere and southern parallel which be so draw in the southern hemisphere also as there be no certain number of particular place so the parallel be innumerable in so much as each place upon the upper face of the earth distant from another towards the north or south have his pecular vertical parallel yet usual the geographer number 180 parallel namely ninty northern and ninty southern of this number be the four parallel which include the four zone or the five zone girdle by which the upper face of the earth be distinguish into clime and the in the midst of they and great of they be join to they and make the five zone the whole circle of the or meridian contain 360 degree whereof each degree consist of 60 minute about 500 make a degree 125 pace make a stadium a italian mile make 8 a french mile 12 a german mile 32 so as 1 degree contain 62 italian mile and a half or 15 common german mile and a half and half quarter although the earth be convex or bend and spherical orround yet in a certain respect they give to the same from west to east or contrary a longitude in longitude and latitude the and parallel and like wise from the south to the north or contrary a latitude in the Meridian and howsocuer the earth in his upper face by nature have neither beginning nor end yet they appoint the artificial beginning of the longitude in the meridian circle draw by the fortunate or canary land and therefore call it the first meridian and so proceed from it towards the west or the east they reckon the longitude of the earth for example two Meridian be draw the first by the canary land the second by any place who situation be inquire as many degree as be sound in the parallel circle proper to the say place from the first meridian to the proper meridian of the place of so many degree be the longitude of that place say to be in like sort the circle and the parallel circle of the place who situation be inquire be draw as many degree as be include in the meridian circle of that place from the to the parallel of the place of so many degree be the latitude of that place say to be as the parallel be of two sort so be the latitude namely northern from the towards the northern pole and southern from the same towards the southern Pole also the longitude in like sort but imaginarily be say to be easterly and westerly begin at the first meridian the place situate under the be say to have no latitude and the place under the first meridian no longitude the zone compass the earth zone like girdle according to the longitude thereof divide it by the and four parallel into five part whereof two be temperate and three intemperate one of the intemperate be the middle lie under the between the two parallel call and this be call the torride or burn zone because it be under the ecliptic line of the sun yearly course be continual burn with the beam thereof this zone in the superficies or upper part of the earth contain the great part of Africa towards the south yea almost all africa except Egypt and Mauritania towards the northern pole and the further part of Afirick towards the southern pole and it contain the chief land of the east indies next to this middle torrid zone towards the north lie one of the temperate zone seat between the two parallel call the tropic of cancer and the arctic circle and it contain the great part of America the northern part of Africa and almost all Europe and Asia the other temperate zone lie by the middle torrid zone on the other side of the towards the south seat between the two parallel call the tropic of Capricorn and the antarctic circle and contain the part of America call Peru and the extreme southern part of Africa and great part of the southern world as yet undiscovered next to these temperate zone lie the other two zone call intemperate for cold as the first be for heat and one of they lie under the northern pole of the world contain Noruegia and the part of Tartary lie within the arctic circle the other lie under the southern pole which part of the world be not yet discover climb be tract compass the earth circular from the west to the east and they be much more narrow than the zone and not of equal latitude among themthemselue climb but as zone be the great the near they be to the and the narrow the more they be distant from the towards either of the pole so be the climb the latitude of each clime be so great as from the begin to the end of it the great solstitial day may increase half a hour and because this variation of the day in part most remote from the happen in short distance of the earth therefore the climb also most remote from the be make more and more narrow in our age wherein great part of the world be discover which be of old unknown this distribution of the earth from the arctic circle to the antarctic may be make into 23 clime the equinoctial clime not be number but this property must ever be observe that the solsitiall day of the follow clime be ever half a hour long than the solstitial day of the forego clime the first clime aswell from the towards the north as from it towards the south be place where the great day contain 12 hour and a half and that be next to the on either side the second where the great day contain 13 hour the three where it contain 13 hour and a half the four where it contain 14 hour and so forward till you have number the 23 clime make the day of 23 hour and a half and so come to one of the say circle arctic towards the north or antarctic towards the south where in the solstitial day of the one half of the year the sun shine 24 hour above the horizon and the night be but a moment and on the contrary in the solstitial day of the other half of the year the sun be hide 24 hour under the horizon and the day be but a moment but beyond these circle this distribution of the earth into clime cease because after the day be no more increase by half hour but the oblique horizon on both side hide certain portion of the ecliptic about the solstitial point which be perpetual appear or hide and when the sun pass they it make continual day for some week yea for some month or the like continual night till you come to one of the pole under which there be continual day for the six summer month and likewise continual night for the six winter month the earth be divide into five part Asia africa Europe America and or Southland not yet discover now I must speak of the part of Europe part of the World and Asia seat in the temperate zone towards the north and under the northern latitude and easterly longitude which must always be observe for the understanding of the description now follow the oriental longitude namely from the first meridian towards the east of Germany with Sweitzerland and Boemerland from the 23 degree of Germany Sweitzerland and Bohemia to the 46 degree extend itself 23 degree the northern latitude namely from the equinoctial to the north of the same country from the parallel of 45 degree and a half to the parallel of 55 degree and a half extend itself 10 degree Germany be divide into the upper and the low the upper lie upon the Alps and near the river Danow be subdivide into 11 province Austria styria Carinthia athesis Rhetia vindelicia Bavaria Sucuia Helvetia or Sweitzerland alsatia and the tract upon the river Rhein to Metz. 1 Austria be of old call the upper Pannonia of the bridge or of upper Germany contain Sweitzerland the Peones come out of Greece to inhabit it and also Auaria now it be vulgar call Oestreich that be the easterly kingdom Danow the great river of Europe which go on the course be call Isther run through it and divide it into Austria on this side and on the far side of Danow it have many ancient and famous city whereof the chief be Uienna vulgar Wien build upon the bank of Danow famous not so much for the university and the traffic of the place as for that it be most strong fortify to keep out the turk and it be subject to the emperor as he be archduke of Austria 2 styria of old call Valesia and japidia be a small region in the midst of the Alps and be at first only a marquisate whereupon it be vulgar call Stoirmark but after by the emperor Frederick Barbarossa be raise to a dukedom and be at this time subject to a prince of the house of Austria by division of inheritance the city thereof be Volenburg Hal and griet the chief city it have two river mour and Draw 3 the inhabitant of Carinthia be call carni vulgar kerntheine the easterly and southerly part thereof be call Carniola vulgar Krein and the inhabitant thereof be of old call japides here be the spring head of the river Draws and Saws in the midst of the Alps the city Philac and Clagefort be of small moment 4. the Athesine province lie under Banaria towards the Alps between Carinthia and Helvetia or Sweitzerland and have the name of the river athesis vulgar call Etsch which run into Italy by Trent and Verona and be there call l'adice and so fall into the river po. this province be common call the county of tyrol the city whereof be brixia and AEnipons vulgar inspruck a fair city 5. the name of Rhetia vindelicia and Norira in these day be out of use and the limit of they be often confound that be proper Rhetia which lie between the lake of or Costnetz towards the north and the high top of the Alps towards the south who chief city be bregant and the inhabitant of these Alps be vulgar call grison 6 vindelicia be the other part of that tract lie between the Danow and the Alps which have fair city as Augusta vindelicorum vulgar augsburg a famous city Vlme Ingolstad Ratisbona vulgar Regenspurg and Passaw observe that the old limit of Rhetia do reach to Verona and como in Italy but now great part of it be lay to Suevia in Germany as namely the city augsburg and Vlme aforesaid 7 the country of Bavaria and of the bishopric of Saltzburg be of old call vindelicia Noricum and the inhabitant thereof taurisci and it have these city scherdung Saltzburg and lintz 8 Suevia stretch into old vindelicia and that which at this day be so call contain the great part of Rhetia and vindelicia the suevians vulgar Schwaben of old forsake their dwell upon the river Elue and invade upper Rhetia which to this day they hold the city thereof be Nerlingen Gepingen and the foresay ulme and augsburg 9 Helvetia or Sweitzerland be of old part of Gallia belgica now be reckon as part of Germany the head spring of the Rhine the second river of Germany next in greatness to the Danow be in the high Alps of Helvetia where it rise in two head and the northerly head fall from the mountain and Gotardo be call the fore Rhine and the southerly head fall from the lepontine mountain be call the hinder Rhine both which run towards the east be unite at Chur and then with the name of Rhine it fall towards the north violent from the mountain Helvetia have many and very famous city namely Schaffhusen as the house of boat or ship Constantia vulgar Costnetz Tigurum vulgar zurech Solodurum or solothurn bern Lucern and Geneva with Losanna which two last of old be reckon in Savoy but now be confederate with the sweitzer the inhabitant of Helvetia be common call Sweitzers and among themselves they will be call eidgenossin that be partaker of the swear league the part of Helvetia between the Rhine and the lake of Constantia be call Brisgoia vulgar Brisgaw Bris signify a price and gaw a meadow and therein be the springhead of the river Danow and the town thereof be Rotwill Brisach Friburg a university Basil a famous university of old belong to alsatia now confederate with the sweitzer 10 alsatia so call of the river run through it be divide into the upper and the low the upper from Basil to strasburg be call singaw and the inhabitant of old be call Tribocchi and Tribotes some hold strasburg of old to have be the chief city thereof but it have now three city Basil Selestade and Rusach the low lie above strasburg to the mount have these city Haganaw and Sabern 11. for the tract upon the Rhine first above alsatia towards metz the nemetes who chief city be Spira and the vangiones who chief city be worm possess the west side of the Rhine the tract adjoin be call vetus Hannonia vulgar alt-henegaw something further from the Rhine towards the dukedom of Luxenburg be these province the county sweybrucken also call in latin of two bridge and the city be sweybrucken and sarbrucken second Austrachia vulgar vestreich as a vast kingdom three the territory of the elector bishop of Trier whereof the chief city be vulgar trier on the other side of the Rhine towards the east the marquisate of baden lie next to Helvetia who inhabitant of old be call Vespi next lie the dukedom of Wirtenburg the city whereof be Tubinga and Sturcardia whereof the former be a university then follow the palatinate of Rhine the inhabitant whereof be of old call Intuergi and and be now call Phaltzer and Heidelberg seat upon the river Neccar be the chief city and the seat of the palatine 19 province of low Germany among which Bohemia be reckon elector the low Germany be divide into nineteen province franconia Bohemia moravia silesia Saxonia Lusatia Misnia Turingia Marchia the dukedom of Branswicke the dukedom of Meckleburg Hassia juliacum Clivia Westphalia Frisia orientalis Pemerania Borussia and Linonia for I omit Gallia belgica to be handle in his proper place 1 franconia be a ancient and noble nation the inhabitant whereof drive the roman out of Gallia possess the same and give the name of France to that kingdom this province have old and fair city namely bamberg a bishop seat Rotenburg francfort famous for the yearly mart or fair Wirtzberg a bishop seat Mentz or metz the seat of the chief elector bishop and nurnberg a famous city which some hold to be in Bavaria but the citizen do more willing acknowledge themselves to be francke all the province except the free city and the three city belong to bishop be subject to the margrave of Brandeburg 2 Bohemia have a language proper to itself and have two province belong to it moravia have his proper language and silesia use the dutch tongue and these three make a kingdom which be subject to the emperor and it be join by geographer to the province of Germany because the same compass it almost round about Bohemia be not divide into county but according to the territory belong to the king or to noble man and gentleman this be call the king land that the land of the baron of rosenberg or the land of the popell and so of the rest the chief city and seat of the emperor their king be prage the river blue have his head spring in Bohemia be the three river of Germany and it run through Saxony to hamburg and after fall into the sea the inhabitant of Bohemia come out of Dalmatia as their language witness 3 moravia be of old inhabit by the marcomanni and have subject to it Bohemia silesia and Polonia but at this day it be only a marquisate subject to Bohemia and have the name of the river morava the chief city thereof be Bromia vulgar prim. 4 the inhabitant of silesia be of old call Dantuli and Cogni the river Viadrus or Odera run through it into pomerania and so fall into the sea silesia be annex to Bohemia and so be likewise subject to the emperor as king of Bohemia and the chief city thereof be Vratislavia vulgar Bressell and the inhabitant of this province be germane as well in language as manner 5 Saxony contain all that lie between hassia silesia Polonia Bohemia and the baltic sea so as at this day Lusatia Misnia Turingia both the mark and the dukedom of Brunswick and of Meckleburg be contain therein 6 Lusatia be a little region annex to the kingdom of Bohemia in the upper part be the city Gurlitz a university and Pautsan and Siltania in the low soravick and Cotwick and the river Sprea run through they both 7 Misnia be of old inhabit by the and Sorabi of the sclavonian nation it be a fertile region and therein begin the mountain which ptolemy call Suditi in which be mine of metal and especial of silver the city thereof be Misnia vulgar mizzen Torg leipzig and Witteberg two university Fryburg the field whereof have rich mine of silver dresden the seat of the saxon elector Remnitz and Suicania 8 the province of Turingia be say of old to have be inhabit by the goth because the chief city be call Gota the metropolitan city be Erford be large and ancient and one of the free city of the empire this province be subject to the duke of Saxony with the title of Langrave as Misnia be also with the title of marquis 9 the river Odera have his head spring in Marchia and run through it divide it into the new mark and the old the chief city of the old be Frankford upon the Odera so call in difference of the more know Frankford upon the Maene the new mark have these city berlin the seat of the elector and Brandeburg of which the elector of Brandeburg have that style and both the new and old be subject to the say elector 10 Brunswick give the name to that dukedom and have the name of bruno that build it and be a free city of the empire strong fortify and not any way subject to the duke of Brunswick though upon some old title he have the name thereof and possess the rest of the dukedom hold his court at wolfenbriten not far distant from Brunswick 11 the dukedom of Meckelburg be of old inhabit by the as Ptolemy write it have two city both on the seaside wismar and Rostoch a university 12 Hassia be a mountainous country in which Ptolemy place for old inhabitant the longobardi the chatti the Teucteri and the at this day it be subject to the family of the landgrave of Hassia it have these city casseit the chief seat of the elder brother of that family hersphild and Marpurg a university the tract upon the river Lovia be divide into the county of Nassaw whereof the chief town be Dillenberg and the county of Catzmelbogen so call of the chatti inhabitant and melibot a famous mountain the bishopric of Colen give title to one of the clergy elector and be of old inhabit by the vbij of who the chief city be first call Vbiopolis which Marcus repair and call it Agrippina Augusta but Marcomirus king of the franc or french conquer it call it colonia it be a small country and the bishop elector have most part of his revenue from other place 13 juliacum be a little region and have title of a dukedom 14 the dukedom of Cleve be of old inhabit by the Vstpetes and the city Cleve be the seat of the duke 15 Westphalia be a large region inhabit by the cherusci Teucteri bructeri and the vigenove and it have these city Padeborn Munster which the anabaptist hold in time of Luther breme a free city of the empire fair build upon the river visurgis and Mindawe 16 easterly Freesland lie upon the river vulgar Emms and be a county subject to the count of Emden who have his name of the chief city Emden but of late upon some difference he be for a time drive out of that city so as it seem he have not absolute power over it 17 pomerania be of old inhabit by the hermiones and lie upon the baltike sea or Oest sea and be subject to the duke thereof it have these town Stetin both on the seaside Sund stutgard and Grippwalt which lie also on the sea and be a old university but have few or no student 18 Bornssia or Prussia be at this day subject to the king of Polonia by agreement make between the polonian and the knight of the tentonick order but the inhabitant be german both in speech and manner the chief city be these dantzk a famous city acknowledge the king of Poland for tribute yet so as they will not receive he into the city but with such a train as they like another city be Konigsperg the seat of the duke of Prussen who be of the family of the elector of Brandeburg but have the dukedom in fee from the king of Poland to who it fall in want of heir male the other city be Marieburg elbing and Thorn which lie upon the confine of Poland and witty Copernicus be bear there 19 Ltuonia be a part of Germany but have neither the speech nor the manner thereof it be subdue some two hundred year past and be bring from the worship of idol and devil to christian religion yet in the village they have not at this day full leave their old idolatry it be inhabit by the old saxon and have these city Refalia on the seaside derbt within land and the metropolitan city riga on the seaside which the duke of Moscony have often but in vain attempt to subdue old writer affirm as Munster witness that the german have perpetual winter and know not harvest for want of fruit this opinion no doubt proceed the situation of Germany rather from their neglect or ignorance of tillage and husbandry then from the indisposition of the air or soil yet I confess that they have far great cold than England lie more northerly especial in low Germany and the province lie upon the baltic or Oest sea more especial in Prussen part of that shore which the more it reach towards the east do also more bend towards the north where in September myself do feel our winter cold and since the baltic sea be little subject to ebb and flow and the water thereof be not much move except it be upon a storm it be daily see that in winter upon a north or north-west wind this sea for a good distance from the land be freeze with hard ice to which the inland Rivers be much more subject which argue the extreme cold that this part of Germany suffer also near the Alps though southerly that part of Germany have the say mountain interpose between it and the sun and feel the cold wind that blow from those mountain perpetual cover with snow do much less partake the heat of the sun than other under the same parallel have not the say accident upon these alps whereof I have former speak in this book the snow lie very deep and cover all the ground for nine month of the year yet notwithstanding the valley and descent of they lie open to the south sun and take life from the heat thereof be very fruitful last in general through all Germany the abundance of lake and mountain do increase this cold of the air in diverse place except they be something defend from the same by wood adjoin and in some place as namely at Heidelberg where the city be almost full enclose with mountain the cold wind in winter do more rage break in on that side the mountain lie open the more they be restrain and resist on the other side as likewise by accident the sun beam in summer reflect against those mountain though in a cold region be so violent hot as the city at that time be much annoy with multitude of fly which not only vex man but so trouble the horse as they be force to cover they with clothes from this annoyance the foresay intemperateness of cold press great part of Germany in stead of fire they use hot stove for remedy thereof which be certain chamber or room have a earthen oven cast into they which may be heat with a little quantity of wood so as it will make they hot who come out of the cold and incline they to swet if they come near the oven and as well to keep out cold as to retain the heat they keep the door and window close shut so as they use not only to receive gentleman into these stove but even to permit rammish clown to stand by the oven till their wet clothes be dry and themselves sweat yea to endure their little child to sit upon their close stool and ease themselves within this close and hot stove let the reader pardon my rude speech as I bear with the bad smell it must needs be that these ill smell never purge by the admit of any fresh air shall dull the brain and almost choke the spirit of those who frequent the stove when myself first enter into one of they this unwonted heat do so wind about my leg as if a snake have twine about they and make my head dull and heavy but after I have use they custom become another nature for I never enjoy my health in any place better than there this intemperateness of cold be the cause that a laurel tree be hardly to be find in Germany and that in the low part towards Lubeck they keep rosemary within the house in eartherne pitcher fill with earth as other where man preserve the choice fruit of the south yet can they not keep this rosemary when it prosper best above three year from wither for this cause also they have no italian fruit in Germany only at prage I do see some few orange tree preserve in pitcher full of earth by set they four in the heat of the summer day and after draw they into house where they be cherish by artificial heat and the like fruit I do see at Heidelberg in the palatine elector garden grow open in summer but in winter a house be build over they with a oven like a stove and yet these tree yield not any ripe fruit when as at London and many part of England more northerly than those part of Germany we have musk melon and plenty of apricot grow in garden which for quantity and goodness be not much inferior to the fruit in Italy also this cold be the cause that in mizzen where they plant vine and in the high part of Germany on this side the Alps where they make wine thereof the grape and the wine be exceed sour only the wine upon Neccar and those upon the west side of the Rhine be in their kind good but harsh and of little heat in the stomach the cherry call Zawerkersen be reasonable great but sour and the other kind call Wildkersen be little and sweet but have a black juice unpleasing to the taste they have little store of pear or apple and those they have be little and of small pleasantness only the muskadel pear be very delicate especial when it be dry and the german make good use of those fruit they have not so much for pleasure when they be green as for furnish the table in winter for their pear and apple they pare they and dry they under the oven of the stove and then dress they very favor with cinnamon and butter in like sort they long preserve their cherry dry without sugar and the great part of their cherry they boil in a brass cauldron full of hole in the bottom out of which the juice fall into another vessel which be keep grow like marmalade and make a delicate sauce for all roast meat and will last very long as they use it the italian have a proverb that be God give clothes according to the cold as to the cold muscovite he have give futte to the english wool for cloth to the french diverse light stuff and to southerly people store of silk that all nation abound in some thing and want other may be teach that they have need of one another help and so be stir up to mutual love which God have thus plant between mankind by mutual traffic for this must be understand not only of clother but also of all other thing necessary for human life Germany do abound with many thing necessary for life and many commodity the fertility of Germany to be transport for great city and city within land of which Germany have store those argue plenty of commodity to be transport and these plenty of food to nourish much people and since that paradox of Cicero be most true that small cause of expense rather then great revenue make man rich sure by this reason the german shall be most rich they never play at dice seldom at card and that for small wager they seldom feast and spare need no sumptuary Law to restrain the number or costliness of dish or sauce they be apparel with homely stuff and wear their clothes to the uttermost of their last their household stuff be poor in gift they be most spare and only be prodigal in expense for drink with which a man may soon burst then spend his patrimony they have corn sufficient for their use and the merchant in the city upon the sea coast export corn into Spain aswell of their own as especial of that they buy at Dantzke they want not cattle of all kind but they be common lean and little so be their horse many in number and little in stature only in Bohemia they have goodly horse or at least great and heavy like those in Friesland but I remember not to have see much cattle or great herd thereof in the field of any town the reason whereof may be gather out of the follow discourse of the german diet their sheep be very little bear a course wool and common black which they export not but make course clothe thereof for the poor sort the gentleman and for the most part the citizen wear english cloth the liberty of hunt common reserve to prince and absolute lord and they have great store of red dear feed in open wood which the prince kill by hundred at a time and send they to their Castlas to be salt use they in stead of beef for the feed of their family they have no fallow dear except some wild kind upon the Alps they have great store of fresh fish in lake pond and river among which the lake of Sweitzerland be most commend at hamburg they catch such plenty of sallmon as it be a common report that the servant make covenant with their master not to be feed therewith more than two meal in the week and from thence great plenty of sturgeon be export either the cold drive away bird or else they labour not to take they for I do seldom see they serve at the table but only sparrow and some few little bird in all their river I do never see any swan yet they say that at Lubeck and about private castle of gentleman they have some few they say that they have some mine of gold but sure they abound with mine of silver above all Europe and all metal where so ever find be by a law of the golden bull appropriate to the emperor and to the elector in their several dominion also they abound with copper and brass where with they cover many church but within forty year past the english have bring they lead which they use to that and other purpose also they have great plenty of iron and they have fountain yield most white salt in city far within the land which city be common call Holla Austria beyond the Danow yield excellent saffron and at judiburg in styria grow store of Celtica as the latin herbalist call it in the season of the year yellow amber be plentiful gather upon the sea coast of Prusfia and pomerania the german export into
