foreign part and there sell many curious and well prise work of manual Art and it be worth the consideration that the citizen of nurnberg dwell in a sandy and baeren soil by their industry and more special by their skill in these manual art live plentiful and attain great riches while on the contrary the inhabitant of alsatia the most fruitful province of all Germany neglect these art and content to enjoy the fatness of their soil in slothful rest be the poor of all other german moreover the upper part of Germany abound with wood of fir which tree as the laurel be green all winter and it have many Okes also upon the Alps and not else where and low Germany especial towards the baltic sea abound with wood of oak they convey great store of wood from the Alps into the low part by the river Rhine cut down whole tree and when they be mark cast they one by one into the river to be carry down with the violent stream thereof or otherwise bind many together to float down with man stand upon they to guide they and at many city and village they have servant which know the tree by the mark and gather they up in place where they may best be sold. the city that be one the sea-coast on the north side of Germany have very great of the traffic of Germany ship but more fit for take in great burden then for sail or fight which the netherlander more common fraught with their commodity than the german themselves neither be the german mariner much to be commend the german sea in good part and the baltic sea altogether be free from pirate which be the cause that their ship be little or not at all arm only some few that trade into Spain carry great ordinance but be general make large in the rib rather fit for burden then fight at Sea I never observe they to have any common prayer morning or evening as our english ship have while they be at sea but the mariner of their own accord use continual to sing psalm and they be punish by the purse who swear or so much as once name the devil from which they abhor and herein they deserve to be praise above the hollander in who ship a man shall hear no mention of God or his worship the say free city of Germany lie on the sea-coast be call hansen-stetten that be free city because they have of old in all neighbour kingdom great privilege of buy any ware as well of stranger as citizen and of sell or exchange their own ware to either sort at pleasure and to bring in or carry out all commodity by their own ship with like immunity equal to citizen in all the say dominion and no less prejudicial to they then advantageous to themselves in England they be wont to dwell together at London in the house call the Stilyard and there to enjoy these liberty which long since have lie dead the german seldom bring aught in their ship into England and the english have now long time find it more commodious to use their own ship and just complain that the english have not the like privilege in the say free city for which cause the privilege of the german be lay dead in England though not full take away Caesar witness that the Schwaben inhabit Suenia then contain great part of Germany admit merchant not to buy any thing themselves but only to sell the spoil they get in war but Munster a German write that these suevians or be now the only forestaller of all thing sell in fair or market and that for this cause they be exclude from buy any thing through Germany except it be sell in their own town of traffic in general the german do apply themselves industrious to all traffic by land which only the free city on the sea-coast exercise somewhat cold by sea at home the german among themselves spend and export a unspeakable quantity of beer with great gain which yield great profit to private citizen and to the prince or public senate in free city there be no merchandise of the world that more easy find a buyer in Germany than this for the german traffic with stranger I will omit small commodity which be often sell though in less quantity yet with more gain than great and in this place I will only speak of the commodity of great moment aswell those that the country afford as those that buy in foreign part to be transport in their own ship the german export into Italy linen clothes corn wax fetch from dantzk and those part and coin silver of their own which they also exchange uncoined with some quantity of gold into England they export board iron course linen clothes and of that kind one sort call diaper wrought in mizzen and bombast or cotton into Spain they export linen cloth wax brass copper cordage mast for ship gunpowder bombast or cotton and nurnberg ware so they call small ware again they receive all kind of silk from Italy whereof they use little quantity for their own apparel but send great store over land to those city on the sea-coast where the english merchant reside to be sell unto they for the english merchant have their staple first at Emden the count whereof use they well yet in the war between England and Spain this place grow dangerous for they for the enemy often take their good and make they prisoner at the very mouth of the harbour whereupon they remove to hamburg where be oppress with new imposition and be deny the public exercise of their religion they go from thence and settle their staple at Stoade in like sort the english merchant trade for Poland and those part first have their staple at dantzk in Prussen by staple I mean their residence in a city give they privilege to stop any foreign ware intend to be carry further and to force the merchant to sell they there except he have rather return with they to the place from whence he come but when the dantzker under pretence of the suevian war exact of they a dolor for each woollen cloth and asmuch for a last of any other good and after when the war be end will remit nothing of this imposition and further when they forbid the english by a law to dwell in Poland the commodity whereof be only sell there leave they shall learn the language and find the mystery of the trade and last when they exact as much weekly of a Englishman dwell in the city as they do of a jew dwell there the english make agreement with the senate of Meluin for eleven year to pay they six grosh for each cloth or other last of good and to pay as much more in the city kettle to the duke of Prussen for his give they free passage to Meluin and so they settle their staple there whereupon the dantzker be offend with the citizen of Meluin and the hamburger no less with those of Stoade procure the free city by a public writing to outlaw not only Meluin and Stoade for receive the english to the common prejudice of the rest but also Koingsperg the seat of the duke of Prussen and the free city Lubeck for favour the english in this course and permit they be stranger to sell their good to any other than the citizen of each several city but I will return to the traffic of Germany I former say that the german receive all kind of silk stuff from Italy from the english they receive woollen clothes lead and such like thing from Spain they bring in their own ship wine fruit oil salt wool and more common coin silver and because the trade of Prussen a german province but late annex to Poland be of great importance with all stranger I will add this that the english bring thither great quantity of tin and woollen clothes with copper and like thing and that they bring from thence pitch liquid pitch hemp flax cable mast for ship board and timber for building linen cloate wax mineral salt which in Poland they dig out of pir like great stone and the same be put to the fire be make pure and be black his colour be more durable and less subject to give again than our boil salt also they bring from thence pine ash for make of soap and great quantity of corne. yet the english seldom have need of their corn for the use of England which many time of their own they transport to other nation but they buy it as the free city do to transport it to other and the low-country man buy it as well for themselves as to serve Spain therewith so as great quantity thereof be distract into all part of Europe the amber that be bring from these part be not gather at Meluin or Dantzke but on the sea side of Konigsperg where the duke of Prussen hold his court and all along the coast of curland where howsoever it lie in great quantity scatter on the sand of the sea yet be it as safe as if it be in warehouse since it be death to take away the least piece thereof when it be first gather it be all cover over with dross but after it be polish become transparent bright at Dantzke I do see two polish piece thereof which be esteem at a great price one include a frog with each part clear to be see for which the king of Poland then beiyg there offer five hundred dollar the other include a newt but not so transparent as the former some think this amber to be a gum distil from tree and by these piece fall upon frog and like thing this opinion shall seem true but those tree from which they hold this amber to distil abound in Germany yet amber be only find upon this coast of the baltic sea other think rather that amber be generate by the sea and it be most certain that mariner sound far from the land often find sand of amber stick to their plummet whereof myself be a eye witness and Munster hold they to be deceive who think amber to be a gum distil from tree and because it be fat and burn be put to the fire conclude it to be a fat clay or bituminous matter affirm that it be not only find upon the sea coast but often catch at sea in net and he add that be liquid it often fall upon and include little beast which grow with it to the hardness of stone and that it smell of myrrh the diet of the german be simple and very modest if you set aside their intemperate drink for as they be nothing sumptuous but rather spare in their apparel the german diet and household stuff so they be content with a morsel of flesh and bread so they have store of drink and want not wood to keep their stoave warm and in general since they affect not foreign commodity but be content with their own commodity and be singular as well in the art as industry of make manual work they easy draw to they and retain with they foreign coin the free city use to have always a year provision of victual lay up in public house to serve for homely food for the people in case the city shall happen to be besiege they common serve to the table sour cabbage which they call crawt and beer or wine for a dainty boil with bread which they call Swoope in upper Germany they moreover give veal or beef in little quantity but in low Germany they supply the meal with bacon and great dry pudding which pudding be savoury and so pleasant as in their kind of mirth they wish proverbial for kurtz predigen lange worsten that be short sermon and long pudding sometime they also give dry fish and apple or pear first dry then prepare with cinnamon and butter very savoury they use many sauce and common sharp and such as comfort the stomach offend with excessive drink for which cause in upper Germany the first draught common be of wormwood wine and the first dish of little lamprey which they call i augen as have nine eye serve with white vinegar and those that take any journey common in the morning drink a little brant wein that be their aquavita and eat a piece of Pfeffer kuchen that be gingerbread which use to be sell at the gate of the city they have a most delicate sauce in my opinion for roast meat of cherry sod and bruise the juice whereof become hard like marmalade but when it be to be serve to the table they dissolve it with a little wine or like moisture and as they have abundance of fresh fish in their pond and river so they desire not to eat they except they see they alive in the kitchen and they prepare the same very savourly common use anniseed to that purpose especial the little fish whereof they have one most delicate kind call Smerling which in Prussen I do eat first choke then sodden in wine and they be very little yet sixty of they be sell for nineteen grosh the foresay sauce of cherry they thus prepare and keep they gather a dark or blackish kind of cherry and cast away the stalk put they into a great cauldron of brass set upon the fire till they begin to be hot than they put they into a less cauldron full of hole in the bottom and press they with their hand so as the stone and skin remain in this cauldron but the juice by the foresay hole do fall into another vessel then again they set this juice upon the fire continual stir it lest it shall cleave to the bottom and after two hour space they mingle with it the best kind of pear they have first cut into very small piece and so long they boil it and continual stir it till it wax hard and notwithstanding the stir begin to cleave to the vessel this juice thus make like a marmalade may long be preserve from mould in this sort they which desire to have it sweet mix sugar with it and other other thing according to the taste they desire it shall have then they put it into earthen pitcher and if it begin at any time to wax mouldy they put these pot into the oven after the bread be bake and take out also these pitcher must be close stop that no air may enter and must be set where no sun or continual heat come last when they will make ready this sauce they cut out a piece of the say juice and mingle with it a little wine to dissolve it with vinegar or sugar or spice according to their several appetite and so boil it again some half hour in Saxony mizzen and those part they sometime serve to the table a calf head whole and undivided into part which to we stranger at the first sight seem a terrible dish gape with the tooth like the head of a monster but they so prepare it as I never remember to have eat any thing that more please my taste they use not for common diet any thing that come from the cow neither have I observe they to have any butter in Saxony or the low part of Germany but they use a certain white matter call smalt in stead of it not taste like our butter they do not common eat any cheese neither remember I that I ever taste good cheese there except one kind of little cheese make of goat milk which be pleasant to eat but salt and strong cheese they sometime use to provoke drink for which purpose the least crumb be sufficient these cheese they compass round with thread or twig and they begin they in the midst of the broad side make a round hole there into which hole when the cheese be to be set up they put some few drop of wine that it may putrify against the next time when they eat the mouldy piece and very creep maggot for dainty morsel and at last the cheese become so rot and so full of these worm that if the say bind that compass it chance to break the cheese fall into a million of crumb no big than moat they have a kind of bread brownish and sourish and make with anniseed which seem very savoury to i they serve in stead of a banquet a kind of light bread like our fritter save that it be long round and a little more solid which they call fastnacht kuchen shrovetide bake because then and upon s. martini day and some like feast they use to make it they use not in any place almost to offend in the great number of dish only some few inn of chief city give plentiful meal and for the saxon they for the most part set on the pot or roast meat once for the whole week yet in the golden bull they have a law that host shall not serve in more than four dish the price of they to be set by the magistrate and that they shall not gain in the reckon more than the four or at most the three penny and that the guest shall pay several for their drink the german drink so large as it be unpossible to prescribe the rate thereof it be to be wish by stranger that not only drink shovid be pay for a part from meat but that each man shall pay the share himself drink and no more so the charge of sober passenger in Germany have all thing reasonable cheap will not in such measure increase as otherwise they do through their companion intemperancy the say saxon set the dish on the table one by one for the most part gross meat whereupon I have hear some merry compare they to the tyrant of siry of who one be dead still a more terrible monster succeed he here and in these part of the low Germany they use to serve in sour crawt or cabbage upon a void circle of carve iron stand on three foot under which they serve in one large dish roast flesh and pullet and pudding and whatsoever they have prepare which dish a country man of i do not unproper compare to the ark of Noah contain all kind of creature also in Saxony for the first dish they serve in stew cherry or prune than toast or sodden pullet or other flesh and last of all bar to fill his belly that have not enough almost all their table be round and of so great a compass as each dish be serve one by one not as we use to have the table full furnish with meat they that sit at the corner of the table be force to stand on their foot as often as they cut any meat the german seldom break their fast except it be in iournic with a little gingerbread and aquavity they sit long at table and even in the inn as they take iournic dine very large neither will they rise from dinner or supper till though slow yet full they have consume all that be set before they and they can speak more reprochsul of any host then to say ith night sit gefressen that be I do not eat my belly full there yea at berne a city of Sweitzerland they have a law that in feast they shall not sit more than five hour at the table and at Basell when doctor and master take their degree they be forbid by a statute to sit long at table then from ten of the clock in the morning to six in the evening yet when that time be past they have a trick to cozen this law be it never so in dulgent to they for than they retire out of the public hall into private chamber where they be content with any kind of meat so it be such as provoke drink in which they have no measure so long as they can stand or fit let the german pardon i to speak free that in my opinion they be no less excessive in eat then drink save that they only protract the two ordinary meal of each day till they have consume all that be set before they but to their drink they can prescribe no mean nor end I speak of their ordinary diet especial at inn by the way as they travel in feast their provision be rather full then sumptuous at leipzig for mere curiosity I procure myself to be invite to a marriage feast in one of the chief citizen house the marriage be in the afternoon and at supper they serve in a piece of roast beef hot and another cold with a sauce make with sugar and sweet wine than they serve in a carp fry than mutton roast than dry pear prepare with butter and cinnamon and therewith a piece of broil salmon then bloat herring broil and last a kind of bread like our fritter save that it be make in long roll and more dric which they call fastnacht kuchen that be shrovetide bake together with cheese and thus with seven dish a senator nuptial feast be end without any flock of foul or change of fish or banquet stuff which other nation use only their be endless drink whole barrel of wine be bring into the stoave and set by we upon a table which we so ply as after two hour no man in the company be in case to give account next morning what he do say or see after that time to nourish this drink they use to eat salt meat which be upon ill disposition of my body once displease and unwholesome for i and I complain thereof to my host he between jest and earnest reply that the use of salt be commend in scripture allege that text let speech be season with salt and then say he much more shall our meat be thus season salt thus please their palate because it make the same dry and provoke the appetite of drink for which cause also when they meet to drink as they dine with dry pork and beef heavy salt together with cheese sharp like that of Parma so when the cloth be take away they have set before they raw bean waternut wstich I do see only in Saxony and a loaf of bread cut into shives all sprinkle with salt and pepper the least bit whereof will invite he to drink that have least need and to say truth pork dry or Bacon be so esteem of the german as they seem to have much great care of their hog then of their sheep or other cattle for in the morning when they turn they forth they scratch they with their finger as barber do man head and bless they that they may safe return and in the evening when they be to come back with the herd a servant be command to attend they who wash the dust from they as they pass by the fountain and so follow they till they come home of their own accord without any beat or drive the price of a fat sow be at least five sometime fourteen gulden yea at Heidelberg it be credible tell i that a sow be so fat as she can not at one feed eat a raw egg all her entrails be close up with fat have late be sell for fifty gulden with this fat they lord many roast and broil meat aswell flesh as fish and they never eat any pig but nourish they to full growth so as myself and some of my countryman at Wittenberg desire to eat a pig hardly buy one for half a dolor and be ourselves force to kill dress and roast it the servant abhor from such a strange work neither can we entreat any one to eat the least bit thereof when they roast a shoulder of mutton they beat the upper part thereof with the backside of a hatchet or like instrument before they put it on the spit to make that part tender which they carve as the most dainty part yet use they seldom to carve any man lest they shall seem to desire that morsel themselves for they hold it a point of civility not to take that be carve but to force it upon the carver they dip their bread in sauce but think it ill manner to dip meat therein as likewise to reach bread with the point of a knife and not rather to call for it by hand last when the table be to be take away they think to offer he courtesy who trencher they offer to take up and put into the voider and will in courtesy strive to do it he that will abide in any city may easy obtain to be entertain for bed and board at a convenientrate by some chief citizen or doctor as I have former say now something must be say of inn by the high way faith that the inn keeper of Germany be sordid that be base or slovenly but I will rather save they be churlish and rude proud or rather grave and surly when you come in you must salute the host and happy you if he salute you again you must drink with he and observe he in all thing for your carriage you must lay it in the common eat room yet there it shall be most safe and if you will put off your boot you must do it in the same room and there lay they aside you must expect the hour of eat for they nothing regard he that desire either to hasten or protract it you must take in good part what be set before you demand nothing for your own appetite the shot demand must be pay without expostulation for the host seldom deceive stranger or other and never remit one half penny of that they demand above the table hang a bell especial through all low Germany by sound whereof they call the servant to attend and at nurnberg there hang such a little bell under the table which they find if any man speak immodest of love matter or any like subject and though it be do in sport yet it serve to remember a wise man of his error in low Germany after supper they lead the guest into a chamber of many bed and if any man have no companion they give he a bedfellow last all thing must be desire and entreat as if the guest be entertain of free cost for the host think you behold to he for your entertainment without any obligation on his part through all Germany they lodge between two featherbed except Sweitzerland where they use one bed under they and be cover with woollen blanket and these featherbed for softness and lightness be very commodious for every winter night the servant be call into the warm stove whereof such feather as be reserve they pull the feather from the quill use only the soft of they for make of bed the bed lie under be great and large and that above be narrow and more soft between which they sleep aswell in summer as winter this kind of lodging be not incommodious in winter if a man do lie alone but since by the high way they force man to have bedfellow one side lie open to the cold by reason that the upper bed be narrow so as it can fall round about two but leave one side of they both open to the wind and weather but in summer time this kind of lodging be unpleasant keep a man in a continual sweat from head to foot yet in country village and many part of Saxony passenger have no cause to complain of this annoyance since all without exception rich and poor drunken and sober take up their lodging among the cow in straw where sometime it happen that he who lie down have a pillow of straw under his head when he awake find the same either scatter or eat by the cow yea where they have bed I will advise the passenger to wear his own linen breech for their sheet be seldom or never clean they advise well who wish passenger to offer the servant drink money that he may show they the best bed yet when that be do this best bed will prove far unfit to be enter naked though perhaps the servant will judge it very pure and clean this by experience I often find once with extreme laughter observe the servant special courtesy to i who take my reward bring i to a bed with clean sheet as he call they wherein he swear deep that no body have lie but his own mother which be a old trot of 90 year age these servant in inn expect as it be of duty drink money from all passenger and bold demand it as if it be their right whether the passenger will or no which they do rude in the low part of Germany by offer they a pot to drink at part and more civil in the upper part the maid servant offer a nosegay to each several guest this be peculiar to the german none serve or attend more rude none more bold challenge reward I have former advise english traveller first to pass by Germany that they may there learn patience by serve themselves for if you come to a shop to buy shoe the master bid you to find out yourself those that will fit you and then to put they on yourself which do he ask the price whereof he will not bate one half penny and when you have pay his ask than the prentice challenge drink money as of duty and the like manner be observe in all other shop wherein you buy any thing in the mean time if in your inn you bid the servant reach any thing to you the same man that when you take horse will in this sort exact drink money of you will not reach that you call for but mumble that you have as many foot and hand as he will go away as if he hear not or regard not what you say the german of Prussia near Poland be much to be praise for hospitality who not only entertain stranger at a good rate and which much cleanliness and good fare and lodging wherein they give clean sheet and if the passenger stay long change they often as once each week which in so cold a clime may seem less requisite but also have in custom I speak of the city of Meluin and Dantzke to give their guest weekly a bath to wash their foot and as often beside as they return from any journey which courtesy I never remember to have be offer unto i but once in Germany at Lubeck the inn of Germany hang out no sign at their gate but they be vulgar know and so may be easy find out beside that many of they may be know by than arm of nobleman and gentleman for they hold it a point of reputation to pass other inn in the number of these arm fix on the front of their inn and upon the wall of the common eat room so as I have number three hundred or four hundred such arm in one inn howsoever Germany abound with all necessary for life yet the expense by the way be great by reason of the Dutchman large drink in low Germany where they drink beer a passenger shall pay each meal common three or four or about 4 lubeck shilling in upper Germany where they drink wine he shall pay common six or seven batzen each meal and if he have a servant he shall pay asmuch for he as for himself I pass from Stoade to Emden in the disguise habit of a servant where I first by experience find that he who vilify himself do not thereby save one penny since poor fellow sit at the same table with gentleman and pay to the uttermost farthing as much as they howsoever they sit low and aswell at board as for bed be more coarse handle yet I say not but such a man may save the poor expense of drink money which the servant perhaps will not expect from man of base condition all dutch consort drink stiff and assoon as ever the cloth be take away after supper except you present rise before they set the pot of schlaffdruncke or sleep cup on the table and if you do but slip one drop you shall beside your ordinary pay equal share with those that drink all night till they be drink and sober again and to say truth the german be in high excess subject to this vice of drink scarce note with any other national vice so that as their doctor and artisan affect the knowledge only of one science or manual art do become excellent therein so this nation in general and every part or member thereof practise night and day the faculty of drink become strong and invincible professor therein in Saxony when the gate of the city be to be shut while they that dwell in the suburb pass out do reel from one side of the street to the other as if it be too narrow for they to walk in while they stumble and fall in the dirt while they by straddle with their leg as if a cart shall pass between they do for the most part bear up themselves from fall yet jostle every post pillar and passenger by the way while the gate of the city seem not wide enough for they to pass except the wall also be pull down friend sdmit to behold from laughter can you then withhold for howsoever the rich sort hide this intemperance for the most part by keep at home sure the vulgar yield this daily spectacle yet in truth it be no shame especial in Saxony even to spew at the table in their next fellow bosom or to piss under the table and afterward in their bed and I know not how the fellowship of drunkard be so please to they as a man shall with no other quality make so many friend as with this so as he that will be welcome in their company or desire to learn their language must needs practise this excess in some measure when they drink if any man chance to come in and sit in the room though he be a stranger of another nation they do not only conjure he to pledge they by the bond of friendship of his father nobility and his mother chaflity but if need be compel he by force thereunto vulgar cry kanstunight fiessen so if thou can not swill and devour thou canft serve no master well in the mean time they like not to drink great draught wherein our country man put they down but they will spend a age in swope and sip their coachman be in this kind so tender heart to their horse that out of a fellow feel of thirst they will suffer they to drink in stand water scarce cover their shoe when they sweat by the high way the german repute it such honour to they to have abundance of wine as the very prince strive as for a princely pre-eminence who shall have the huge and most capable vessel in his cellar some of these vessel contain more than a thousand measure each of seventy can or pot and be ascend by twenty or thirty stair out of this vessel they daily draw wine and be half empty they fill it up again but at the birth of a child or any like feast they turn this monster loose for all comer to tame it and drink it out to the bottom passenger in the inn of low Germany so make their reckon at dinner as they reserve a proportion to drink before they take Coach once I observe that myself and seven consort after dinner upon a full gorge have sixteen great pot to drink at part at which time one of our consort be a horseman and not fit to ride be take into our coach and sit by i now laugh then weep and often knock his head against i at last defile i by cast his stomach in my bosom with no reproach to himself among his countryman but unspeakable offence to i when they be sit down to drink if any man come in by chance each one at the table salute he with a cup all which garauss he must drink as for a fine before he can be admit into their number for they be very jealous that any man be sober shall behold their quaff so as a man have better fall among the thick of his enemy fight then into the company of his friend drink he that read this will think that they drink sweet nectar at the least or some like drink invite excess but in low Germany sometime and rare they drink rhenish wine common beer and that so thick and ill smell and sometime medicinal as a stranger will think it more fit to be eat or cast into the sink then to be drink whereof a drop once fall on my hand seem to i foul puddle water their wine in general be sharp and those of the Rhine small which be to be have in their city and when I first pass to leipzeg and be ignorant of the language be force to commit myself to a conductor and after my covenant with he for my diet desire he to carry some glass bottle of wine in our coach yet he can not in the way use it temperate but either will allow we no wine at all or at one meal drink off a whole great bottle as if he think it a shame to taste it and not drink all out at once thus as often it fall out in prince court that a stranger may die of thirst but he that be acquaint in court shall hardly escape sober so he give i either no wine or too much in upper Germany for the most part they drink wine and that with some less excess then be use in the low part yet so as in this vice they degenerate not from their countryman the german of Prussia former praise by i must pardon i if I task they with this vice as much as the rest when I pass from Meluin to Dantzke my companion by the way show i a tower call groske where certain husbandman be upon a wager to drink twelve measure of wine which we call last and use for proportion of merchant ware not for wine or beer do roast upon a spit one of their consort because he leave they before the task be perform and to save their life for this murder pay their prince as many silver grosh as can lie between that tower and the city of Dantzke in general the german want not many exemplary punishment and effect of this vice for many quarrel in drink be kill and he that kill never escape if he be take I remember that a gentleman of Brunswick ride from Hamburge to his home when he be extreme drunken be next day find tear in many piece by the strike of his horse when he fall out of the saddle which be a miserable and exemplary kind of death and the like mischief befall another while I be at Torge in mizzen and a physician a familiar friend of i tell i that many german die sudden upon excess of drink be ordinary for hide of the shame give out to die of the fall sickness in their drink they use no mirth and little discourse but sad ply the business sometime cry one to the other seyte frolich be merry drink drink out and as according to the proverb every psalm end in so every speech of they end in ich bring I drink to you for frolic they pinch and that very rude their next neighbour arm or thigh which go round about the table so for equality they drink round especial in Saxony except in courtesy they sometime drink out of course to a guest and this equal manner of drink they say have his first original from a pleasant or rather wicked act of a undutiful son who receive a box of the care from his father and dare not strike he again do notwithstanding strike his next neighbour as hard a blow as he receive desire he to pass it round about the table as a frolic in these word (@) let it go round so my father shall have it in his course and so more modest or less wicked he revenge himself while all drink in this manner circular out of one and the same pot they scoff at he that drink the last remainder say proverbial that he shall marry a old trot at Nurneberg and some other inn of high Germany each guest have his peculiar drink glass set by his trencher which when he have drink out if he set it down with the mouth upward it be present fill again in which fill the servant use a singular dexterity stand in great distance from it but if he turn the mouth downward they expect till in sign of thirst it be turn upward for they be such master in this art of drink as they be serve by dumb sign without speak a word in Saxony two use to begin a pot to two and when each receive the pot or give it to his fellow they curious look upon certain peg or mark set within of purpose that they may divide the drink by the equal balance of Iustice sometime they take three glass at once upon 3 finger and beginning to another drink they all of at once which kind of karauss they call the crown of the emperor if you begin to any man you must fill the cup for he with your own hand or at least deliver it to he yourself or otherwise for a panelty you must drink it again and some do willing make these error that they may seem to be compel to this please penalty when they be extraordinary merry they use a kind of garauss call kurlemurlebuff wherein they use certain touch of the glass the beard some part of the body and of the table together with certain whistle and phillipping of the finger with like rule so curious dispose in order as it be a labour of Hercules to observe they yet he that err in the least point of ceremony must drink the cup of again for penalty they hold it a point of reputation if themselves have sense and memory can send their guest home void of sense or reason or full as they more gentle call drunkenness and the better to perform this they will now and then go out of the warm stove to ease their stomach by cast which use make easy and familiar to they they seldom or never drink with their hat on for sit in a warm stove bear head they find their head more speedy ease of the vapour that arise from drink many of the german go to sleep do by the advice of the physician put little stone into their mouth to keep they open for as a boil pot better secthe the meat if it be cover so the fire be moderate but if it be extraordinary great and hot the potlid must be take off lest it boil over so it be good to help a man concoetion if he sleep with his mouth shut so his diet be spare or moderate but in such excess as the german use not only the mouth but if it may be the very breast be to be open that the heat of the inward part may have vent the german spare and rare give any gift to those with who they drink but if they do than contrary to the custom of the turk and polonian they willing make they good when they be sober and for the most part merchant and all traffiquer of business make all their contract of buy and sell and otherwise with the counsel of the pot likewise when they sell house or land they bring a tun of beer or vessel of wine into the strecte and seal the bargain by drink with their neighbour in like sort conclude all their contract which agree upon when they be half drunken yet be confirm by they when they be sober at prage I remember the german do scoff at a polake gentleman to who a dutch abbot give a gold ring in his cup the polake in requital give he his horse of price and though he do earnest refuse so great a gift by instance force he to accept it yet in the morning be sober send for his horse again to conclude hardly any man will give his daughter in marriage to a man who he have not see drunken by which in a moment they be confident to conjecture what life she shall lead with he since in drink man lively bewray their disposition which they can cloak and dissemble when they be sober and they find by experience that in drink cholerikeman be prone to quarrel sanguine man to dance and embrace man possess with melancholy to tear and complaint and they who be phlegmatic to dull astonishment and spew the trade of brew be more commodious among the german than any other traffic so as at Torg where the best beer be brew and from thence distract to other city only the senate have the privilege to sell the same by small measure as also to sell wine and in the rest of low Germany as only the senate buy and sell wine so the chief citizen by turn brew bear admit troop of poor people into their house to drink it out as the gain of brew be great so prince raise great imposition from it and the most rich citizen or alderman as I say not only disdain not to brew but even greedy expect their turn at which time they also sell it by can and have their low room full of drink table for the common people where every man pay for his drink before he can be fill that at least their purse may teach they measure which otherwise they can observe yea myself not without wonder have see in a senator house poor soul pawn their clothes for drink and go home half naked yet sufficient arm with drink against the great cold the beer of Torge be most esteem in high Saxony and the most part at leipzig drink no other yet for their servant brew a small beer call beer of the covent and a kind of most small beer which the student call that be rake their be a imperial law in the golden bull against host mariner and carter who either in cellar or carriage by the highway mingle brimstone or water with wine wherein notwithstanding they daily offend put in brimstone to make it heady strong and water to fill up the measure there be in the same golden bull many law make against drunkenness at such time as the german have war with the turk begin to look into themselves for reform of notorious vice wherein it be decree that courtier give to this vice shall be expel the court of prince and that all magistrate shall search out drunkard and severe punish they but give i one prince free of this vice who may thus punish his courtier myself be at a great duke funeral do see a prince his near cousin drink so stiff to expel sorrow as all his sense and almost his spirit be suffocate there with and of many prince there present pardon i to speak truth I do not see one sober at this funeral feast what will these prince have do at a marriage prince have a custom to drink by attorney when they be sickly or ill dispose and many time they reward this substitute strong bear much drink as for a good service to the common wealth yet except they be very sick few be find which will not in person perform their own task give i one magistrate of so many thousand who with his own innocency be arm with boldness to punish other give i one I be ashamed to say it but truth be truth I say give i one minister of God word who preach against excess of drink myself have hear some hundreth of their sermon yet never hear any invective against this vice come the teacher needs must be ashamed who for the same offence be blame only the woman of Germany be most temperate in eat and drink and of all I do ever see most modest in all kind of virtue yet the woman of Bohemia use as great or little less excess in drink as man not without a stain to their reputation of chastity the woman of Germany have a custom to help their husband or friend by sip of the cup but I do never see any chaste woman as most of they be drink large much less to be drunken but for man of all sort whatsoever peccant if the prince smite as oft as they offend his sword and arm will fail he ere the end thus howsoever the german be honest deceive neither stranger nor country man and have abundance of all thing to sustain life yet stranger by reason of the general in temperance of the nation be either allure to participate this vice of drink with they or at least by ill custom be draw to partake their punishment in pay of the shot and through their churlish rusticity be ill entertain and yet force to reward the servant who attendance deserve nothing less it remain that I shall inform passenger how to apply themselves to the german in this drink custom so as at least with less hurt or offence they may pass bring their territory for those who pass sudden through the same without long abode in any place nothing be more easy then to shun all participation of this nice by consort themselves with fit companion in their journey so as they be the great part as well in the coach as at the table may rather draw the lesser part in sobriety then be induce by they to excess but they who desire to converse with the (@) and to learn their language can possible keep within the bound of (@) and must use art to shun great or daily excess such a passenger sit do (@) table must not present drink of all the cup begin to he from other for the german be so exceed charitable to all man as they will furnish he present with new cup on all hand for fear that he shall suffer thirst he shall do better to set the cup in order before his trencher and first to drink of those of lesser quantity but ever to keep one or two of the great to return in exchange to he that drink to he for this kind of revenge as I may term it the german fear more than the irish do great gun and to avoid the same will forbear to provoke he with garauss for they love not health in great measure which they call but have much rather sip then swallow in this kind I remember a pleasant french gentleman much distaste they who invite to a feast and admonish that he can not possible return sober do at the very beginning of supper drink great garausles of himself call for they beside the small health commend to he from other which unwonted kind of skirmish when they dislike he present reply why shall we lose time since we must be drunken let we do it quick the soon the better and therewith he so tire those at the table as he find no man will in that kind contend with he but to the purpose if the cup set about his trencher increase in number he may easy find occasion as when his consort go out to make water either to convey some of they to their trencher or to give they to the servant to let away after supper he may nod and sleep as if he be drunken for sometime the fool to play be wisdom great they say and so he shall be lead to a bed which they have in all their stove and call the Faulbett that be the slothful bed otherwise he may fain headache or fear of a ague or if these excuse prevail not as seldom they do while he state in the room because they can endure to have a sober man behold they drink then as if he go out to make water or speak with some friend he shall do best to steal away and howsoever he have confident promise to return yet to come no more that night no not to fetch his cloak or hat which be always lay up safe for he especial if he foresee the skirmish like to be hot but above all let he take heed of the old fashion to take leave of his companion and bid they good night for the german upon no entreaty or excuse will suffer any man to go to bed so sober if there be music and dance their dance be of no art and small toil he have much better dance with the woman till midnight then return to the table among the drinker for one of these four he must do drink sleep dance or steal away no five course remain last let he wary choose his companion of that nation with good trial of their honest disposition but with stranger as english french and Polakes let he careful eschew excess of drink for these and especial the english when they be heat with drink be observe to be mad in take exception and in the ill effect of fury be more prone to quarrel then the dutch and have no mean in imitate foreign vice or virtue but with Brutus that they will they will too much for Bohemia and Switzerland that seat in the centre of Germany this on the Boemerland and Sweitzerland northwest side of the Alps I have contain their geohraphicall description in that of Germany and have speak something of they in this discourse of Germany it remain to add something of they touch the particular subject of this chapter the bohemian drink the wine of Hungary be much better than those of Germany and have much better beer in regard they have great plenof corn and the sweitzer drink the delicate wine of Italy neither of their of both in general trafficke be comparable to that of Germany because Bohemia be far within land and have no great commodity to be export and Sweitzerland be addict to the mercenary service of foreign prince in their war change their cattle for the wine of Italy and content with their own so they want not plenty of good drink some cantones of the sweitzer make great gain of spin wool whereof they Sweitz particular make piece of cloth some 134 else long and lest covetousness of private man may prejudice the common good they appoint overseer to this trade who punish all fraud severe and some capital for food they abound with honey butter and milk and have plenty of venison find in the wild Alps and especial of excellent sort of fish by reason of their frequent lake in public inn a meal be give for six or seven batzen they be hospital towards stranger and among themselves they have public house where they meet and shoot with crossbow and musket with like exercise there they sometime eat together and invite guest to these house as to a tavern and to the end all thing may there be do with more modesty the table of the magistrate and all other sort of man be in one and the same room in meat they use moderation and for drink use far less excess than the saxon somewhat less than they of upper Germany they have strict law to imprison drunkard for a year and at solemn feast the vulgar sort be admonish to behave themselves modest yet drunkenness have such patronage among the best sort as it can be banish they brag of their ancient temperance and say that excess come into the commonwealth together with the accept of military stipend from foreign prince Bohemia abound with corn cattle fish as plenty of salmon wood good horse but heavy like those of Freesland and with which it be death to carry out Dohemerland particular yet for gain of fifty in the hundred there want not who hazard that danger I say it have the commodity and also produce wine but very sharp and unpleasant and have some mine of metal howsoever it be much more southerly than England yet the italian fruit as fig be there most rare which in winter they keep in cellar and only in summer time set they abroad in garden and in like sort but with great difficulty they preserve rosemary but they have no laurel at all the man drink if it be possible more than the german and be much more subject to gluttony and their woman swill wine and beer daily and in great excess which to the german be most reproachful in the inn they give large diet for some five bohemish a meal and upon the confine of Germany towards nurnberg for some twenty creitzer a meal but the bohemian eat often in the day and sit almost continual at the table and since at prage and in many other place all thing be sell out of the inn after the manner of Poland the bohemian seldom eat at a ordinary but demand what meat they will upon a reckon for the rest Boemerland and Sweitzerland little differ from Germany for the diet the host the inn excess of drink or any like thing chap. 4 of the unite province in netherlands and of Denmark and Poland touch the say subject of the precedent three chapter the longitude of netherlands lie or extend seven degree and a half from the meridian of twenty two degree and a half to that of thirty degree and the latitude lie or extend five degree from the parallel of forty eight degree and a half to that of fifty three degree and a half it be call netherlands as a country lie low and the people for language and manner have great affinity with the german both be call dutchmen by a common name of old this country be a part of Gallia transalpina that be beyond the Alps from Italy which be subdivide into and Narbonensis and again so call of the people long hair be subdivide into and Celtica or lugdunensis and belgica this part call belgica contain the lowcountry who dominion have be by marriage derive from the Burgundian family to that of Austria and some divide this country into seventeen province whereof some still remain subject to the king of Spain other of which I be to speak be unite in league have recover their liberty by the sword and at this time do make war with the Spaniard about the same but some map among these seventeen province reckon the county of valkenburg which be part of the dukedom of Limburg other map make Mechlin and Antwerp to be province which be both contain in the dukedom of Brabant therefore I better approve those who divide the whole country into fifteen province namely the county of Artois of Flanders of Hanaw of Zealand of Holland of zutphan and of namur and the dukedom of Luxenburg of Brabant of Limburg and of Gelderland and the territory of west-freesland of groan of Vtrecht and of Transisola 1 the county of Artois have many fair city whereof arras be the chief give the name to the province and to those rich hang wherewith our great man adorn their palace 2 Flanders be the large county the chief city whereof be Ghant where the emperor Charles the five be bear and Bruges whether great concourse of merchant be make of old so as the stranger hear no other name but Flanders do by custom impose the name of Flanders on all the fifteen province and the name of fleming on all the inhabitant Flanders have other city namely Calleis Dunkirk Ostend and Sleuse all lie on the sea coast whereof Sleuse be a municipal village of Bruges but at this time be itself strong fortify and Ostend take from the spaniard by the state of the unite province be at this time commit by they and under their pay to the custody of a english garrison under sir Edward Norreys knight this province yield plenty of corn and flax and be very rich with make linen and woollen clothes it have excellent pasture and be enrich with cheese butter oil make of rape-roote salt and the fish of herring but it yield no wine the famous wood arduenna lie in the confine thereof towards land where it abound with wood but towards the sea they burn turf make of earth and also burn cow dung 3 the county of Hanaw have the principality of Arscot unite to the dukedom of Brabant by which the duke son have the title of prince the chief city of this county be and Valinciennes it have mine yield lead and marble of many colour and a good kind of coal 4 the county of Zealand be by situation the first of the unite province consist of many land whereof seven be principal and the chief be walcherne the chief city whereof be Midleburg famous for traffic and the staple for spanish and french wine near that be the city Vlishing strong fortify be the chief of the fort then engage to the crown of England and keep by a english garrison under the command of sir Robert Sidney knight for the second fort engage to England lie in another island and be call Brill be then keep by a english garrison under the command of the lord barrow all these land be fertile and yield excellent corn more plentiful than any other province so as one acre thereof be say to yield double to a acre of Brabant but they have no sweet water nor good air and for want of wood burn turf they take plenty of sea-fish which they salt and carry into other country mad for die of wool grow there plentiful which likewise they export and grow rich by sell these commodity as likewise Spanish and french salt and like traffic 5 the county of Holland call of old Battavia and inhabit by the chatti as Tacitus write be in situation the second of the unite province but the first in dignity the city whereof be Amstelrodam famous for traffic Rhoterodam where Erasmus be bear Leyden a university Harlem Dort the staple for the rhenish wine and Delft all very fair city and I may not omit the most pleasant village of the hage call Gravenhage because the count court be there and it be now the seat of the unite state want only wall to make it number among the most pleasant city be no doubt a village yield to none for the pleasant seat this province do so abound with lake pool of water and artificial ditch as it give passage by water as well as by land to every city and poor village which be infinite in number and these ditch itow for the most part to the river Rhine for the Rhine of old run towards Leyden do fall a little below it into the sea but at this day by reason the land be low and subject to overflow it have change the bed and at in the dukedom of Cleve divide itself into many branch the first run to arnheim a city of Gelderland then to Vava Rena and Battovodurum where Lecca receive his water and take away the name from the Rhine the first branch of Rhine yet so as a little branch thereof still hold the name of Rhine which run to Mastricht there divide into two one whereof fall into vecta and so into a arm of the sea near munda the other run by Woerden and after a long course necre Leyden be divide into five little branch whereof three fall into a lake and the four turn to Renoburg and lose itself in mountain of sand near the village Catwicke I remember that the water fall through Leyden be call Rhine so as I think it probable that all the stand water lie between the several pasture there come from the Rhine after it have lose the name I say that the Rhine at Battovodurum be call Lecca which run to Culenburg and to viana where in a ditch be the fountain of Isala which run to (@) thus to omit the little branch at Battovodurum the first branch of the Rhine be lose in the Rivers Lecca and Isala the second branch bend from Lobecum to and fall into the brook take the name of the second branch the old family of king among the gall where be a old castle compass with the brook and of the same name then run to Dort in Holland it receive the foresay Lecca and Isala and so near Rhoterodame fall into the mosa and under that name fall near Brill into the german Sea the three branch of the Rhine run from Lobecum within two mile of Arnheime fall into the ditch of Drusus or rather of the three branch Germanicus and so run to dewsborow the city of Drusus where it receive the old Isala spring in Westphalia and by the name of Isala or Isell run to zutphane and then to Deventry fall into at Amstelrodame and by a arm of the sea be carry to west-freesland and so fall into the german sea near the island flye 5 to return to my purpose Holland be little in circuit but abound with people and dwelling and be poor of itself be most rich by industry and want both wine and corn yet furnish many nation with both neither wool nor flax grow there but of both bring in to they they make linen clothes much prise and also woollen both carry to the very indies I need not speak of Holland cheese so vulgar know and much esteem last Holland be famous for the traffic of all commodity and the roman so high esteem the fortitude and faithfulness of the old Battani as they have a band of they for their Guard 6 the county of Zutphane be account part of Gelderland and subdue by the state be join to the unite province in the year 1591. 7 the county of namure so call of the cheese city have mine of iron and plenty of stony coal contrary to all other coal in that it be quench by the infusion of oil it have also a ill smell which they take away by the sprinkle of salt and it burn more clear have water cast upon it this county have also quarry of freestone and of marble of diverse colour 8 the dukedom of Luxenburg have the name of the chief city and the inhabitant of the upper part be germane but they of the low part be like the french in language and manner 9 the dukedom of Brabant have fair city namely Antwerp most famous before the civil war because Maximilian of Austria bring thither from Bruges in Flanders the famous traffic of all nation by a ditch draw to sluice only to be fail upon at the flow of the sea tide at this day forsake of merchant it lie overgrow with grass and the say traffic enrich Holland and the unite province the next city be Brissell of old the seat of the duke and now of the spanish governor then Lovan a famous university then Mechlin subject to the unite state then bergenapzome a fortify city at this time commit to the custody of sir Thomas Morgan knight with a english garrison the inhabitant of this dukedom be of old call tungri 10 the dukedom of Limburg have Mastricht for the chief city and the bishopric of licge pertain to it wherein the city of liege be the bishop seat and the territory thereof yield a little quantity of a small wine and have mine yield a little iron some lead and brimstone and a very little quantity of good gold the mountain yield a black alabaster with marble and other stone especial stony coal in great quantity which be there find at first be now call general liege coal 11 the dukedom of Gelderland be of old inhabit by the menappij and Sieambri and abound with excellent pasture and meadow so as great herd of cattle bring thither out of Denmark to be sell be for great part fat there the chief city be Nimmengen the second harduike a fortify city subject to the unite state and the three arnheim also subject to they 12 the territory of west-freessand be divide as Holland with artificial ditch and abound with eocellent pasture for fat of the great herd of cattle and yield itself all kind of cattle of extraordinary bigness as horse of Freesland vulgar know it have many city where of the chief be Lewerden Dockam Fronikar a university and Harlingen not to speak of nine other town fortify with wall and ditcbe this territory be subject or associate to the unite state 13 the territory of groningen make part of Freesland by cosmagrapher be also subject to the state and have the name of the chief city strong fortify and seat in a fenny soil 14 the territory of Vtrecht be also associate under the same unite state who chief and very pleasant city be call Vtrecht 15 the territory of Transisole vulgar call land over Ysseli the land beyond Yssell be also associate to the unite state whereof the chief city be Deventry which besiege by the state army in the year 1591. be then subdue and it lie near to the Sea it have another city call swoll the unite province of netherlands through which only I do pass have a most intemperate air the winter cold be excessive and the summer heat far exceed the situation the ordinary heat of that clime the reason of the cold be that the northern wind of themselves ordinary cold do here in a long course on all side glide upon the german sea thereby gather far great cold and so rush into those plain province no where stop either by mountain or wood there be no mountain scarce any hill no wood scarce any grove to hinder they from violent passage with their uttermost force like reason may be give for the heat for the same open plain no way shadow from the beam of the sun by opposition of wood or mountain must needs in summer be subject to the heat of the sun and wind from land add that in winter the frequent river lake and pool or stand water in finite increase the coldness of the air these water aswell run as stand be almost all winter freeze over with a thick ice so as they will bear some hundreth of young man and woman slide upon they with patten according to their custom yea the arm of the sea call Zwidersea lie within land between Holland and Freseland though it be large and deep have only two flat or shoal yet be compass with land and the continent be many time in winter so freeze over as victualler erect tent in the midst of it have beer and wine and fire make upon iron furnace to refresh such as pass upon sledge or slide upon iron pattern from one shore to the other this cold be the cause why their sheep and cattle be keep in stable to bring forth their young and howsoever the same be do in Italy subject to great heat yet it be not of necessity as here but out of the too great tenderness of the italian towards the few cattle they have and this be the cause that how soever they use not hot stoave as the german do yet the woman as well at home as in the church to drive away cold put under they little pan of fire cover with box of wood bore full of hole in the top and this sordid remedy they carry with they by the high way in wagon which the dane or mosconite use not though oppress with great cold only some of the more noble woman dislike this remedy choose rather to wear breech to defend they from the cold in this distemper of air it can be expect that there shall be plenty of flower the fertility of the unite province and summer fruit no doubt in regard of the fatness of the soil water with frequent ditch and through the foresay heat of the summer they may have plenty of flower and fruit be it not impossible or very difficult to preserve they from perish by the winter cold and be not the inhabitant careless of such dainty though in late time as they have admit foreign manner so luxury have more power with they then former it have I have oft see one apple sell for a blank and those great cherry which be bring into England grow not here but in Flaunders and the territory within Land they have abundance of butter cheese and root and howsoever they have not of their own full sufficiency of other thing to maintain life yet they abound with the same bring from other part some province as the bishopric of Vtrecht yield corn to be transport but in general the unite province of which only I discourse in this place have not sufficient corn for their own use yet by traffic at Dantzke they furnish themselves and many other nation therewith they have little plenty of river fish except only eales but in the mosa as it fall from Dort to the sea they have plenty of salmon and other fish which fish do of old yield great profit to the prince and merchant and for sea fish salt and dry they make great traffic therewith myself lie for a passage in the island fly do see great quantity of shellfish sell at a very low rate great herd of ox and calf be yearly bring into these part out of the dukedom of holst unite to the kingdom of Denmark in which part they feed most on dry and salt meat and these herd be fat in the rich pasture of Gelderland and Freesland there be great abundance of sea fowl especial in west-freesland and they want not land fowl they careful nourish stork as presage happiness to a aristocratical government make they nest on the top of public house and punish any that drive they away or trouble they in which kind also they preserve heron make nest in those grove which be only in few city they have a race of heavy horse and strong which they sell in sorraigne part use only their mare to draw wagon and for like use at home the province on the sea coast as I former say burn their own earth by the frequent dig whereof they say the sea or lake at Harlem be first make and of these tnrffe they make fierce both clear and of good smell without smoke and commodious to dress meat to starch linen and like use they be notable mariner yet in that to be blame that be at sea they use no public prayer that ever I hear and several city have great number of ship the traffic wherein they trade with such industry and subtlety as they be in that point envy of till nation the very italian who in foresee wisdom will be account Promethei be by they make Epimethei wise after the deed too late repent that when they come first to settie their trade in Flaunders they take yowg youth of that nation to be their cassier who by write letter for they learn the secret of that trade and after to the italian great prejudice exercise it themselves some three fleming brother or partner use to settle themselves in as many city of great trade where they keep such correspondency as by buy all thing at the well head where they be cheap and transport they far off where they be dear and especial by live spare both in diet and apparel and not shame to retail any commodity in small part which great merchant disdain to sell otherwise then by whole sale they have attain the high knowledge and riches of trade thus they buy raw silk of the turk and weave the same into diverse stuss in Italy which they sell not there but transport they into England and the northern part where they bear high price and there retail they by the small proportion they have of their own very fine linen and woollen clothes of diverse kind and many clothes of Cotton arras hang plenty of hops aswell on the sea-coast of as in the east part of Holland and great store of butter cheese and fish salt and dry all which they transport again they bring from Dantzke store of hemp whereof themselves make rope and cable neither transport they any rude matter but by work it at home enrich many populous city also from Dantzke they bring corn all kind of pitch and other commodity of that place and from Italy many kind of silk stuffy also by the diligent fish especial of herting on the sea-coast of England they grow rich sell the same to all nation and to the very english who be not so industrious in that trade last they draw the commodity of all nation to they and fetch they from the very indies and in like sort they transport they to the remote part where they yield most gain it be not amiss to add the very word of Marchantius write of the old trade of burges in Flanders since what I have write be only to be understand of the unite province thus he say Lodovicus Crassus in the year 1323. grant a staple to Bruges which his son confirm the staple be a privilege of stay foreign commodity in the place except the seller and bringer choose rather to return whence they come Bruges have a market place with a house for the meeting of merchant at noon and evening which house be call the burse of the house of the extinct family bursa bear three purse for their arm engraveu upon their house the merchant of England Scotland France Castilia Portugal Arragon Nanar Catnlania Biscaia the Hans city of Germany namely Lubeck Hamberg Rostoch Dantzk eRiga Renel and diverse other city the merchant of Venice Florence Genoa Luca and Milan namely fifteen nation have each their college or house here the italian bring chamblet and grogram in make of goat hair in Galatia a province of the lesser Asia they bring hide thread of silk of silver and of gold and clothes make of they they bring jewel wine of Candia alum brimstone oil spice apothecary ware mithridate rhubarb mummy Sena Cassia and the soil of brass the french bring salt red and white wine oil and paper the english bring wool lead tin beer woollen clothes especial those so make veil for the low country woman the scot bring skin of sheep conney and other and course woollen clothes the spaniard and portugal bring grain for scarlet dye gold silver raw silk thread of silk the wood Guiacum Salsaparilla unicorn horn and spice the german with the dane and polake bring honey wax corn Salt-Peter wool glass rich fur quicksilver arm rhenish wine timber for building again they export out of Flanders fair and great horse fat beene butter diverse kind of cheese pickle and fume herting diverse sea-fish salt woollen and linen clothes tapestry of great variety and beauty rare picture and all manuary work thus Flanders give the name to all netherlands Bruges (@) in the year 1414. get a privilege that they who be free of that city by birth gift buy or marriage shall be free from all confiscation of their good which exceed the privilege of any other city in netherlands for those of Ypre have the like yet lose it upon any force offer to the prince the trade at Bruges begin to decay in the year 1485 partly for the narrowness and unsafety of the port of sluice and the river lead from thence to Bruges partly by the fame of the large and commodious river scaldis at Antwerp and partly by the civil war for first the portugal have take calicut in the east indies carry their famous spice to the fair of Antwerp in the year 1503. and contract with that city draw the Fuggari and Welfari German merchant thither and after the merchant of Florence Lucca and the Spinolae of and those of other nation except part of the spaniard leave Bruges seat themselves at Antwerp about the year 1516. and they be invite thither by the privilege of marriage dowry which become shadow to many fraud for when husband either break in life time or be find banckerouts at death the wife be prefer to all debtor in the recovery of their dowry notwithstanding Bruges at this day by the three general tax of Flaunders yet in use pay something more than Ghant for public use these be the word of jacobus Marchantius the foresay trade of the unite province have at home much commodity and increase by the river as the Rhine bring down the commodity of Germany and by the stand or little move water which be most frequent and by channel or ditch wrought by hand and bear at least little boat for passage to each city and village but these water for the most part end in stand pool by reason they fall into a low ground near the sea the air be unwholesome the water be neither of good smell nor taste neither do they drive Mils as run water do elsewhere of which kind they have few or none myself in a dark rainy day pass one of these say narrow channel number a hundred little boat at least which pass by we and be hire at a low rate whereby the great trade and singular industry of the inhabitant may be conjecture add that beside the german sea lie upon diverse of these province they have many arm of the sea that run far within Land all the river fall from Germany which in this low soil often overflow have change their old bed and fall into ditch make by hand do no more run with their wont force but as I have say in the description of Holland do end as it be in lake by reason of the foresay industry of the people inhabit the unite province the number of their ship and the commodity of their sea and water howsoever they want of their own many thing for necessity and delight yet there be no where great abundance of all thing neither can any nation endow with the great riches by nature have so long bear as they have do a civil war and intolerable exaction and tribute much less can they by this mischief have grow rich as this people have do one thing not use in any other country be here most common that while the husband snort idle at home the woman especial of Holland for traffic sail to hamburg and manage most part of the business at home and in neighbour city in the shop they sell all they take all account and it be no teproch to the man to be never inquit after about these affair who take money of their wife for daily expense glad pass their time in idleness touch this people diet butter be the first and last dish at the table whereof they make all sauce especial for fish and thereupon by stranger they be merry Dict. call butter-mouth they be much delight with white meat and the bawer drink milk in stead of beer and as well man as woman pass in boat from city to city for trade carry with they cheese and box of butter for their food whereupon in like sort stranger call they butter box and nothing be more ordinary then for citizen of good account and wealth to sit at their door even dwell in the market place hold in their hand and eat a great lump of bread and butter with a lunchen of cheese they use to seethe little piece of flesh in pipkin with root and gobbet of fat mingle therewith without any cutiosity and this they often see again set it each meal of the week on the table new heat and with some addition of flesh root or fat morsel as they think needful and this dish be vulgar call hutspot they feed much upon root which the boy of rich man devour raw with a morsel of bread as they run play in the street they use most common fresh meat and seldom set any salt meat on the board except it beat feast to provoke drink they use no spit to roast meat but bake they in a earthen pipkin as in a oven and so likewise see they and these meat be cold they often heat and serve to the table so as I have come into a inn and be in the kitchen can see nothing ready for supper yet present call to supper have see a long table furnish with these often heat meat which smoke on the outside yet be cold on the inside this people be proverbial say to excel in bake meat especial in bake of venison yet to my knowledge they have no red dear in these province neither have they any enclose park for fallow dear nor any connygree only count Mauritz have of late have out of England some buck and do of fallow dear which run in the grove at the hage and there be some coney near Leyden upon the sandy bank of the sea which be not sufficient to serve the inhabitant of those part but be account good and pleasant to eat neither in foreign part do they much desire to feed on coney either because they be rare or because the flesh be not savoury they use to eat early in the morning even before day and the cloth be lay four time in the day for very servant but two of these time they set before they nothing but cheese and butter they see all their meat in water fall of rain and keep in Cesternes they eat mushroom and the binder part of frog for great dainty which frog young man use to catch and present they to their mistress for dainty I have see a hundred of oyster in diverse city sell sometime for eight or twelve yea for twenty or thirty stiver they dress fresh water fish with butter more then enough and salt fish savourly with butter and mustard where they eat not at a ordinary but upon reckon as they do in village and poor inn there they weigh the cheese when it be set on table and take away be pay by the weight and I have know some waggish soldier who put a leaden bullet into the cheese make it thereby weigh little less than at first sit down and so deceive their host but in the chief inn a man shall eat at a ordinary and there gentleman and other of inferior condition sit at the same table and at the same rate the inn be commodious enough and the city be frequent scarce some eight mile distant one from the other commodity of lodging be as frequent yea they hang out sign at the door which fashion be not in use in many city of Germany in Denmark Poland Scotland and Ireland where the inn be only know by fame and this make i marvel that notwithstanding this sign oblige they to lodge stranger myself though well apparel have diverse time be refuse lodging in many of those inn which seem to i a scorn and flat injury at the fair city of Leyden not want many fair inn I be refuse lodging in six of they and hardly get it in the seven which make i gather that they do not willing entertain englishman neither do I attribute this to their in hospital nature but to the licentiousness of our soldier who perhaps have deserve ill of they thereby or perhaps by ill payment for which I can blame the english in that case butrather the unequal law of England give all to the elder brother lie sluggish at home and thrust the young brother into the war and all desperate hazard and that in penury which forcible drive the most ingenious disposition to do unfit thing by reason of the huge imposition especial upon wine the passenger expense be much increase for the exaction often equal or pass the value of the thing for which they be pay and though a man drink beer subject to less imposition and less dear than wine yet he must understand that his companion drink large and be he never so sober in diet yet his purse must pay a share for their intemperance after supper passenger use to sit by the fire and pass some time in mirth drink upon the common charge and to warm their beer till it have a froth yet do they not use these night drink so frequent nor with such excess as the german do I remember that have be at sea in a great
