storm of wind thunder and lightning about the month of November when such storm be rare and be very weary and sad I land at Dockam in west-freesland where at that time some young gentlewoman of that country pass through that city towards groan according to the fashion of those part we do eat at a ordinary table and after supper sit down by the fire drink one to the other where after our storm at sea the custom of Freesland do somewhat recreate we for if a woman drink to a man the custom be that she must bring he the cup and kiss he he not move his foot nor scarce his head to meet she and man drink to they be tie to the like by custom a stranger will at first sight marvel at this custom and more special that their very husband shall take it for a disgrace and be apt to quarrel with a man for omit this ceremony towards their wife yet they interpret this (@) (@) as if they judge their wife to be so foul or infamous or at least (@) as they (@) they unworthy of that courtesy in the first book of this three part and in the journal of the first part I have particular set down the rate of expense for (@) through those part they great esteem english beer either for the (@) wine or indeed the goodness thereof and I have observe some in their cup thus to magnify it english beer english verstant english bear make a english wit so in the sea town of England they sing this english rhyme (@) of mutton and english beer make the fleming tarry here they say that there be (@) brewer at (@) and there they imitate the english beer and call that kind Delphs English but with no cost can they ever make as good as the english be though they provide to have english brewer either by reason of the difference of the water or rather as by experience I have find because our beer carry over sea whereby it work a new and go a better favour do drink much better than that we have at home they say that of old there be more than 700 brewer at Torgaw till upon the water (@) or corrupt they forsake that place it be not lawful to sell rhenish wine and french white wine in the same tavern lest they shall be mix but one man may sell french (@) wine and rhenish wine which can well be mix without be (@) (@) (@) and for the same cause they may not sell in one place diverse land of the same country wine and of the same colour the netherlander use less excess in drink (@) (@) saxon and more than other german and if you ask a woman for (@) (@) she take it for a honest excuse to say he be drunken and sleep but I will true say that for every day drink though it be far from soberness yet it be not with so great excess as the saxon use neither in tavern where they and special the common sort most meet and in private feast at home do they use so great excess as the saxon neither do drunken man reel in the street of netherlands so frequent as they do in those of Saxony only I do once see not without astonishment a man of honourable condition as it seem by his apparel of velvet and many ring on his finger who lie grove on the ground close by the cart rut of the highway with two servant distend his cloak between the sun and he and when we light from our waggon to behold more near this spectacle think the man to be kill or sore wound his servant make sign unto we that we shall not trouble he who be only drunken and will be well assoon as he have sleep a little at this we much wonder and go on our journey at feast they have a fashion to put a capon rump in the saltseller and to contend who shall deserve it by drink most for it the best sort at feast for a frolic will change hat whereby it happen that gallant shall wear a burgher cap and a burger a hat with a feather cry tousfoiz a mode liege all fool after the fashion of liege some want companion to drink lay down their hat or cloak for a companion so play themselves both part of drink to and pledge till they have no more sense or use of reason then the cloak or (@) have last all bargain contract and solemnity whatsoever be do in (@) cap the longitude of Denmark and Norway extend 8 degree and a (@) from the meridian Denmark of 27 degree and a half to that of 36 degree and the latitude extend 10 degree from the parallel of 58 degree to that of 68 degree the kingdom of Denmark be divide into six part Finmark Norway Gothia scandia Seland and jutland 1 Finmark reach towards the north beyond the arctic circle to the castle Warthouse and therefore must needs be desert and barren 2 Norway in the german tongue signify the way to the north and it be so large as of old it have and still retain the name of a kingdom and towards Finmark it reach to the arctic circle the city be name (@) not far from the narrow sea call and Nidrosia former call Trondia lie upon the same sea and bergis the seat of a bishop and Solzburg a city of traffic in Norway they catch great store of stockfish which they beat with cudgel and dry with cold and great store of a fish from the greek word call Plaise for the breadth thereof and they sell great quantity of this fish to the german city upon the sea which they keep to feed the people in case the city shall be besiege 3 the island Gothia be annex to the crown of Denmark yet the succians take it in our time but the dane recover it again history report that the goth come out of this island yet old writer under the name of scandia contain all the tract of the neck of land lie from the hyperborean sea between the northern ocean and the Bodick gulf from whence it be more probable that the goth come out then only from this little island who after seat themselves upon the euxine sea and the bank of Danow and from thence make incursion upon the roman empire and hereupon the say tract contain not only scandia and Gothia but all Norway and Suecia be by old writer call the shop and sheath of nation the island Gothia yield the rich fur call sabels 4 the island scandia be also call Scondia and scandinavia and Schonlandia that be fair land the beauty whereof the dane high extol and for the firtiltie prefer it to sealand though it pass the same in the building of the king court and other house the city thereof be Helsenburg Lanscron and London the metropolitan city 5 the island of Seland whence they hold the zelander of netherlands to have come into those part be beautify with the city copenhagen that be the haven of merchant where the king have his court and there be a university it have also the strong castle Cronemburg build in the village Elsenar and the city Roschild so call of a fountain be the seat of a bishop where the king be bury between the castle Cronembirg in Seland and the castle Helsenburg in scandia be the famous straight of the sea call by which the ship enter into the baltic sea and return from dantzk and Righa lade with precious commodity pay great tribute to the king of Denmark both at the entry and go (@) of that straight 6 jutland signify a good land in the german language be the northern part of the cimbrian chersonesus that be neck of land whence the cimbri come who make war upon the roman and this jutland with the foresay seland be proper call Denmark the other part be peculiar region at diverse time annex to that crown the chief town of jutland be Aleburg Nicopia and Wiburg the rest of the tract of the cimbrian chersonesus contain the dukedom of holst vulgar call holstein which of old be part of Saxony but so as the dane often force it to the pay of tribute and at last about the year 1465 they full subdue it part of this dukedom lie upon the Briton sea between the brook and the river Elue be call Ditmarcia all fenny so as by cast down certain bank they may drown all the country and by this strength the inhabitant keep their enemy out long preserve a rude or rural liberty but at last in our time Fredrick king of Denmark upon advantage of a great frost in those fen sudden assail and subdue they join that country to the say dukedom of holst of which dukedom the chief town be Flensburg slesuick where of old the duke hold their court be seat on the sea towards the east and Gottorp and Meldorp in Ditmarcia upon the sea towards the west upon the confine of holst lie the fair imperial free city Lubeck and hamburg to the freedom whereof the duke of holst be great and near enemy challenge the same to be build in their soil for which cause the king of Denmark possess that dukedom be much suspect by these city who they more and more fear as their power more increase some reckon the island orcades for part of Denmark and they say that the inhabitant speak the goth language but history witness that howsoever of old they belong to the dane yet they have long be subject to the kingdom of Scotland Denmark lie near the arctic circle must needs be subject to great cold howsoever the misty air cause by the frequent isle do in some sort mitigate the extremity the situation thereof in regard of the clime it can be expect that fruit shall grow here which be only ripen by the heat of the sun they have corn sufficient for their own the fertilty use and plenty thereof as of all other commodity be bring to they from danizk and all other part by reason of the frequent concourse of merchant into the sounat which they enjoy at good rate and with much ease the dane exchange great plenty of dry and salt fish and of other small commodity the traffake for necessary to clothe and feed they and be in both these kind frugal and spare as the german be they also attain to some small riches by this poor traffic and since they feed for the most part on dry fish bacon and salt meat and little use fresh meat as veal and mutton they carry great herd of ox and calf out of holst into netherlands last since they have no other commodity of their own to transport and merchant that pass the baltike sea of necessity land at Elsenar bring they all necessary from foreign part and also take of they such commodity as they can spare sure howsoever the ship of Denmark be in strength sail and last next to the english yet their merchant seldom make any other voyage then towards the northern isle to take fish in diet they be much like the (@) the german and especial the neighbour saxon their dainty be bacon and salt meat but the common people feed much on diverse kind of dry fish which at the first view of they a strangermay well perceive by their lean and wither face and they likewise feed on bread very black heavy and windy I do see no common inn at copenhagen Elsenar or rosehilde but some be there license to keep tavern for sell of wine where the common table for that purpose be always ready cover with linen but passenger must obtain diet and lodging with some citizen and in their house they shall find honest manner moderate diet and clean bed and sheet to conclude the dane pass if it be possible their neighbour saxon in the excess of their drink Poland have the name of pole in that language signify a plain and be a vast kingdom Poland the longitude thereof extend 16 degree from the meridian of 38 degree to that of 54 degree and the latitude extend 9 degree from the parallel of 47 degree to that of 56 degree it be divide into the great and the less 1 of the great Poland these be the chief town Bosnan seat on the brook Barta and Genesua and Ladistavia seat upon the river vistula or Wexel 2 the lesser Poland lie towards the south wherein be Craconia vulgar Crakaw the seat of the king the inhabitant come of the scythian and the manner of the common people at this day little differ from the old sarmatian this region be fenny and great part thereof be woody but it so abound with corn and pasture as it supply all Europe with corn and the neighbour country with herd of cattle it have no vine but it yield plenty of pit-coale and much wax and honey and it no less abound with many kind of the say cattle aswell wild as tame other province be annex to this kingdom namely Samogitia Massovia Lithuania Volhinia Russia and Podolia for I omit Borussia though subject to this kingdom under a free yoke because I former say that it be number among the province of Germany the inhabitant be germane in language and manner and because I have in that place former describe the same 3 Samogitia have no wall town but the people live in cottage and be rude and of great stature only apply themselves to the plough and feed of cattle not know any use of money scarce the service of God 4 the metropolitan city of Massovia be Warsovia vulgar warsaw where the parliament of the kingdom be hold 5 Lithuania give the title of great duke and be a most large province fenny and woody so as in summer there be no passage into it but in winter when the fen be freeze merchant trade with the inhabitant Vilna be the metropolitan city and seat of the bishop it have very few town and the village be common distant 20 german mile one from the other they have plenty of honey wax a kind of beast like a ox call Alce wild beast and rich fur but they scarce know the use of money 6 Volhinia be the most fertile province of that kingdom and full of fair town and castle 7 Russia or Reutenia have many town whereof the most know be Leopolis vulgar Leimpurg and it be famous for swift and good horse not to speak of the rich fur and other commodity 8 last Podolia abound with excellent pasture but have few city or town in general Poland be subject to as great cold as the low part of Germany lie under the situation the same parallel and the country as they lie more northerly so they suffer more cold for the coast of the baltike sea the more it lie towards the east the more it still bend to the north beside that the plainness of the country and the frequency of lake and fen do more increase the cold they use stove heat with earthen oven for remedy against cold as the german do the revenue of the king and gentleman be moderate scarce sufficient to maintain a plentiful table and to exchange with merchant for wine and spice which the fertilty they much use especial in dress of fish and for foreign stuff and clothes of silk and wool Poland abound with beast aswell wild as tame and yield excellent horse not great but quick and stir neither do the gentleman more delight in any thing then in their horse so as they hang gold chain and lewel at their ear and paint they half over with exquisite colour but in that uncomely that they be not natural for horse as the Carnatian colour and their hinder part they adorn with rich fur and skin of lion and leopard and the like aswell to terrify their enemy as to adorn and beautify their horse Poland likewise abound with flesh whitemeate bird fresh-water-fish it be far within land and all kind of pulse as pease and the like it have some but very few mine of gold and silver towards the Carpatian mountain of Hungary and of iron and brimstone it abound with honey which they find in hollow tree and cave of the earth beside the husbandman hive it yield great quantity of wax flax linen clothes make thereof hemp pitch of both kind maft for ship bear and timber rich fur salt dig out of pit amber soape-ash and all kind of grain especial rye which have make Daniske famous for relieve all nation therewith in time of dearth no marvel then if merchant bring unto they silk of Italy cloth of England wine of Spain and the very spice of India with most remote commodity since the traffic they not only sell they at what price they list but also bring from thence such precious foresay commodity Poland be all far within land except Borussia vulgar prussen which with immunity be subject to this kingdom though I have describe it among the province of Germany because the people be germane in language and manner and the very inhabitant of Borussia have but few ship use stranger to export their commodity Poland abound with the aforesaid most necessary commodity and the people live content with their own yet be they not rich because they want the foresay foreign commodity far bring and so dear and they have so little gold and silver as despise all in respect of it they sell all commodity at a most low rate especial those which be for daily food and unfit to be export and in truth myself have in Poland and Ireland find a strange cheapness of the dyot all such necessary in respect they want and so more esteem silver this observation make i of a opinion much contrary to the vulgar that there be no more certain sign of a fluorish and rich commonwealth than the dear price of these thing except the year of famine nor any great argument of a poor and weak state than the cheap price of they and it make i confident to conclude that old wife snare with papistical superstition do foolish attribute the late dear price to the change of religion in our time while they ignorant extol former time wherein twenty four egg be sell for a penny for in our age our king have more royal tribute our noble far great revenue our merchant much great wealth than ever our progenitor have and this be the cause that all thing for diet and appartell and our very wanton desire be sell at much high price then in former age because our riches make we not able to want any thing to serve our appetite at what price soever it be set again for Italy it have no great store of flesh bird fish and like thing for food in regard of the populousness thereof yet the inhabitant hold it no disgrace to be spare in diet and modest in apparel so it be cleanly in regard of this general temperance and that the nobility disdain not to weave silk and trade for they be the sinew of that country howsoever all thing be sell there at most dear price yet no prince consider thing to be consider no gentleman no merchant of the universal world have great treasure and wealth than those of Italy I have say that Poland do abound with all kind of flesh whitmeate freshwater fish and all thing necessary for food and that it yield no wine which the inhabitant seldom drink but in place thereof they use beer which they of dantzk brew very strong and good and they make a drink of honey which they esteem almost as much as wine and the best composition thereof be make in the province of Masjovia they have such store of butter as I have see they anoint cart wheel therewith but it be more white and less savoury than we this kingdom have few city and if a stranger will for a time sojourn in any of they he shall easy find a german or Netherlander to be his host who will entertain he more commodious than any of that nation though perhaps at extraordinary rate as myself find abide with a Netherlander at Crakaw the inn in the chief city afford convenient bed and plenty of flesh and freshwater fish and these fish they dress with pepper and spice more then enough for which kind of cookery the polonian be praise above the german or any other nation yet the spite be far bring and dear sell make the sauce far more costly than the fish itself there be scarce any gentleman who have not the skill and do not use to dress fish for his own eat in village and small city by the highway a passenger shall find no bed but he may carry a bed in his coach and fit upon it conucnicnt other use to sleep upon straw lap with a fur horsemans coat which they use to wear and if they have no such coat they must be content to sleep upon clean straw and all the passenger lie together in the warm stoave with those of the family both man and woman neither shall they find in such place any wine or choice meat which they use to bring from city in their coach for the inn in such place be poor naked house have nothing to sell but close by they be the shambles the baker and brewer house where the passenger buy beer and such meat as they like and bring it to the inn which a poor hostess will dress afford they only fire and a course tablecloth and it seem to i that the lord of the place use to impose upon some vassal this charge to entertain stranger for the hostess will give her labour for nothing except in courtesy you desire she to eat with you and if you free give she a small reward as three penny for the whole company she will think you deal bountiful with she but she will ask you nothing also you may free carry away in your coach flesh bread wine or any thing that remain which I have see do many time no country in Europe afford victual at'a low rate myself and a companion do in a country town invite two guest and our dinner for four person come but to four grosh and a half I have former set down the ordinary expense general in a chapter treat of that subject in the first book of this three part and particular in the journey through Poland in the first part. now I will only add that in the village and little city by the high way I have buy ten egg for one grosh a goose for three a partridge for two a loin of mutton for two a pig for three grosh and all like thing at a very small price so as if a passenger have a cook in his company or have himself any skill to dress meat for his own appetite I think he shall there want nothing for necessary or delicate food but he may not expect that a country hostess shall seek out or curious dress any dainty for he last the polonian be as stout drinker as the german and pass they all except only the saxon yea above all that ever I observe give to this vice they seem to i to be mad in this kind that in drink they be prone to quarrel brawl and fight give i leave to add one observation which to i seem very strange at Meluin and Dantzke in Prussen between Michaelmas and Christmas the country people bring in sledge lade with dead hare all foozen over which be so preserve aswell and better then if they be powder with salt till our lady day in Lent about which time the frost begin first to break and if they will eat a hare in the mean time they thaw it at the fire or the oven of the warm stove or by cast it into water and so they present set it to the fire either to be roast or boil in like sort they preserve pheasant or any kind of flesh be freeze over aswell as if they be salt and if any man think this a traveller fiction let he know that a most credible person tell i of his certain knowledge and experience that the moscovite in Russia bring the dead body of man in winter thus freeze over and so lay they on heap in the Bellfrees of the church where they lie without rot or ill smell till about our lady day in Lent the snow begin to thaw and the earth to be fit for dig for till that time the earth be cover with deep and hard snow and if it be not so cover yet be so hard by continual frost as it can be dig and at that time each family take the body of their dead and take care to bury they chap. v. of Italy touch all the subject of the three chapter go before the longitude of Italy extend fourteen degree and a half from the meridian of twenty seven degree and a Italy half to that of forty two degree and the latitude extend eight degree from the parallel of thirty eight degree to that of forty six degree Italy of old be call Saturnia janicula oenotria and ausonia and last it have the name of Italy it be call Saturnia of Saturn who banish from his country teach the inhabitant of this country the art of husbandry as poet fable and be account the first king of this people then call aborigines as bear there not come from any foreign part to inhabit there it be call janicula of janus or Noha who they affirm to have come thither after the deluge and to have teach they the art to plant vine and sow corn and to have build the city now call Genoa it be call oenotria either of the excellent wine or of Oenotrius king of the sabine as likewise it have the name (@) of Ausonius the son of Ulysses last it be call Italy of italus king of Sicily or of a old greek word signify ox and show the inhabitant to have be herdsman Ptolemy describe it in the form of a chersonesus that be neck of land or Peninsula that be almost a island and Pliny in the form of a oaken leaf but other more apt compare it to a man leg from the thigh to the sole of the foot old writer divide Italy from Gallia cisalpina or enclose Gallia with a line draw from the river Varus beyond Genoa by the apennine mountain to the brook rubico where it fall into the sea near Ravenna and this line be oblique stretch from the east to the west and so they divide it from Italy and make Picmount the dukedom of Milan the dukedom of Ferrara the Trevisan marquisate and all histria to be a peculiar part of Europe but these province be at this time part of Italy it be better enclose and confine by a line draw from the head of the brook Varus through the coccian Alps to the mountain adula which lie upon the Alps of or mount gothard and so through the rhetian Alps towards the east to the brook arsia confine histria and the rest of Italy be compass with the Sea also the mount Appenine derive from the Alps run all the length of Italy in the form of a fish back bone and almost in the midst divide it into two tract one lie towards the upper or adriaticke sea the other towards the neither of tyrrhene Sea for howsoever the Apennine about Ancona seem to bend towards the adriaticke sea and there to end yet after it turn from thence and divide the rest of Italy till it end upon the straight of the sicilian Sea Italy worthy call the queen of nation can never be sufficient praise be most happy in the sweet air the most fruitful and pleasant field warm sunny hus (@) thicket shadow grove haven of the sea water brook bath wine and oil for delight and most safe fort or defence as well of the sea as of the Alps neither be any part of Europe more inhabit more adorn with city and castle or to be compare thereunto for tillage and husbandry the province thereof be number 14. first beyond the apennine towards the tyrrhene sea lie five province Liguria tuscia Campania suodevide into Vmbria Latium and Campania the happy lucania vulgar (@) and Calabria the upper and the low also on this side the apennine towards the adriaticke sea lie five province go back from the east to the west Salentinum apulia samnium vulgar Abrotzo vulgar Marca ancomtana and flaminia vulgar Romandiola whereof part beyond the brook rubico reach into Gallia cisalpina again in the part call of old Gallia cisalpina be four province Lombard Marca Trenisana forum and histria 1 the chief city in Liguria be Genoa a free city or at least have the show of liberty to which all this province be subject which heath all upon the Tyrrbene sea and be now vulgar call Genoa be of all Italy the most rocky and barren tract yet whether by husbandman art and labour or by lie upon the south sun I know not but sure I be and well remember that pass that way in the begin of winter I take great pleasure in the plenty and goodness of the fruit thereof beside that all man extol the fertility of mount Ferrate a part of Liguria enclose and water by the Rivers Tanoro and Po 2 tuscia have the name of frankincense which they use for incense and be former call Hetruria at this day name toscana it be a old dukedom erect by the longobard and after be divide into many territory of free city and commonwealth the liberty whereof namely of Florence Pisa and Sienna the family of Medici invade in the time of the emperor Charles the five and now possess all Toscany with title of great duke only the city of Lucca still preserve the old liberty of that commonwealth it have very many city of which these be the chief Florence Pisa a university Sienna and Lucca which still be free city 3 Campania vulgar campagna be subdivide as I say into Latium Vmbria and Campania the happy Latium have the name of the fable of Saturn lurk there in banishment and it be the fountain of the famous latin tongue and the head city thereof be Rome which city together with the whole province be at this day subject to the pope and the province be vulgar call campagna the second part be Vmbria which be hold part of Latium and lie beyond Rome amid the mount apennine of who shadow it have the name of Vmbria but be now call the dukedom of Spoleto to which dignity it be raise of old by the Lombard and it be subject to the pope of Rome the city thereof be Assisium Spoletum Perusium and Otricoli the three part be Campania the happy vulgar call Terra have the name of the most fertile plain of Capua seat upon the bank of the river Volturnus and to that city it be of old subject but at this day it be the chief province of the neapolitan kingdom the head city whereof be Naples of old call Parthenope and Dysiarchia now adorn with stately palace of duke earl and gentleman especial those of the duke of Gravina and the prince of salerno these noble man dwell there the great part of the year the capuan delight corrupt the army of Hannibal be know to all the World this province be a earthly paradise where Bacchus and Ceres strive for principality I pass over Cuma of old a famous city and Linternum famous for the banishment and sepulchre of Scipio the african since at this day only remain some ruin of Cuma and scarce any memory of Linternum necre suessa be the mountain Valerius or falernus famous for the wine it yield and the famous mountain gaurus massicus and Vesunius the mountain Vesunius be now call out of the top whereof of old great flame break out burn the neighbour place in which flame Pliny live in the time of trajan be choke and perish while he curious search the cause of those flame in our age this mountain burn and now daily fire break out of it here the beauty of all the world be gather as it be into a bundle here be the famous dwelling of the roman in the territory of napy here be the acherusian fen the lake of Avernus the ditch of Nero the bridge of Caligula and other wonder celebrate by poet the kingdom of Naples be subject to the king of Spain which together with the dukedom of Milan also subject to he be think to contain more than half Italy beside the island of Sicily annex to this kingdom whereof this be the chief province and the bound of it reach to the sea and so lie backward on this side the apennine towards samnium where it be confine and divide from the mark of Ancona by the brook Truento 4 lucania vulgar Basilicata be a small territory the city whereof be and Laina 5 Calabria a province of this kingdom be divide into the upper and the low the upper be call great be of old inhabit by the greek and use still that language corrupt with the italian the city thereof famous of old be where Ennius be bear Croto where Milo be bear who carry a ox Tarentum now the chief city and Locris the low Calabria be call the chief city whereof be Reghio so call because Sicily be say to have be there divide from Italy by a earthquake 6 Salentinum vulgar Terra Ottranto have the city Ottranto and Brundisium 7 apulia vulgar Puglia be divide into Peucetia and daunia in Pencetia or Mesapia vulgar call Terra barri be the city and Bitonto in daunia vulgar call Puglia Piana be the city Mansfredonia beneventum make a dukedom by the lumbards asculum and the village Cannae famous by the old defeat of the roman and here be the mountain garganus vulgar call Sant' Angelo 8 samnium of old call at this day have the name of Abrozzo where be sulmo in which oxid be bear and here the kingdom of Naples be confine on this side the apennine mountain 9 vulgar Marca Anconitana be subject to the pope and have the name of the chief city Ancona so call of the crookedness of the haven which be hold the best haven of Italy Pesaro belong to this province 10 flaminia or Romandiola vulgar Romagna have fair city Vrbinum subject to the duke thereof which some make part of rimini Bologna subject to the pope and ancient Ravenna which with the great part of this province be subject to the pope who erect Urbine from a county to a dukedom with covenant of vassalage which the pope seldom omit yet some part of the province be subject to the venetian 11 Lombardy of old be part of Gallia cisalpina which the river Padus vulgar Po and of old call Eridanus divide into Cispadan on this side the Po and Transpadan beyond the po. Cispadan of old call Emilia now vulgar del' Po contain Pigmont so call as seat at the foot of the mountain whereof the chief city be Turin of old call Augusta taurinorum and this province be subject to the duke of Savoy also it contain the territory of Parma subject to the duke thereof wherein be the city Parma and Piacenza Transpadane vulgar del' Po contain the dukedom of Milan the chief city whereof be Milano and it have other city namely como where both plime be bear seat on the most pleasant lake (@) vulgar di como abound with excellent fish also tic. numb vulgar (@) where the french king Francis the first be take prisoner by the army of Charles the five last Cremona among other thing famous for the Tower this dukedom be the large and rich of all other as Flaunders be among the county and it be subject to the king of Spain 12 also Transpadane Lombardy contain the dukedom of Mantua subject to the duke thereof and Marca Trevisana or Trivigiana subject to the state of Venice Mantua be the chief city of the dukedom and Marca Trevisana have the famous city Venice Padua (@) Verona Vicenza brescia and Bergamo the (@) of old inhabit all cisalpina gailia who give the name to the jyrrhene sea and be expel by the gall and of they the insubres inhabit the Transpadan part and there build Milano and the senones inhabit the Cispadane part 13 histria be divide into forum and histria proper so call unlgar and because the venetian acknowledge they come from thence be a dukedom erect by the Lombard the chief city whereof be the most ancient adorn with the title of a Patriarchate which at this day be almost fall to the ground near that city be a town in which they write that s. mark pen his gospel now the chief city be Frioli the confine of this region lie upon Marca Trenisana and all the province to the river (@) be subject to the state of Venice the other part be subject to the archduke (@) Austria here grow the wine pucinum now call Prosecho much celebrate by (@) 14 histria proper so call be almost in the form of a peninsule almost a island and the chief city be justinopolis unlgar Capo and all the province be subject to the state of Venice Italy in winter time namely the month of December January and February have the situation a temperate cold with little or no frost or ice and howsoever myself do see not only the river of the state of Venice but the very inland sea of Venice freeze and cover with thick ice for the space of three week yet the venetiaos find it be a rare accident in summer the heat be excessive and the dew fall by night be very unwholesome as also thunder and lightning be frequent which do great hurt both to man and beast then abroad as sad experience often show they but in the dog-day no man be so hardy as to put his head out of his door or to go out of the city for they proverbial say that be when the sun lodge in the sign of the lion he that preserve his health gain enough this excess of heat they careful avoid by inhabit upon the side of the mountain and hill towards the sea which cool the wind and by retire into vault under ground or open terrace lie upon river and free from the sun yea some have find the mean by a artificial mill to draw wind into a vault and from thence to disperie it into any room of the house all Italy be divide with the mount Apennine as a back be with the bone and upon both side thereof as well towards the north as south the hill and plain extend towards the tirrhene and adriatic sea in so narrow compass as many time a man may at once see both the sea from the top of the mountain so as the fresh wind blow from each sea do not a little mitigate the heat of the clime for the sea wind blow from any quarter whatsoever while they gather cold by long glide on the water must needs refresh where they blow as on the contrary wind sweep upon the earth increase the heat thus in the west part of Sicily when the south east wind blow and sweep upon the plain parch by the sun it bring excessive heat yet the same wind yea the very south wind in his nature most hot when they sweep upon the sea and after beat upon the mountain of Liguria do bring a pleasant coolness with they touch the fertility of Italy before I speak of it give i leave to remember the (@) that Jerome Turler write of travel into foreign part relate that a prince of Naples have a kinsman to his pupil who desire much to see foreign kingdom he can not deny he so just a request but only wish he first to see Rome whether he go and after his return the prince take a account of he what he have see and find he spare to relate his observation in that place he make this answer to his request cousin you have see at Rome fair meadow plain mountain wood grove fountain river village castle city bath amphitheatre playhouse temple pillar statue colossus triumphal arke pyramid academy garden water-conduit man good and ill learned and unlearned more you can see in the universal world then be content and stay at home and so he restrain the young man in his desire to travel wherein perhaps he rather seek to get liberty then experience this I write to show that the italian be so ravish with the beauty of their own country as have by sharpness of wit more than the true value of thing magnify and propound to stranger admiration each brook for a river each vice for the neighbour virtue and each poor thing as if it be to be extol above the moon they have thereby more wrong themselves then we for we pass through Italy though we find ourselves deceive in the fame of thing yet still we hear and see many thing worthy to be observe but of the italian hold Italy for a paradise very few sharpen their wit with any long voyage and great part of they have not see the village and city within ten mile of their dwelling hence it be that great part of the italian have nothing to boast of but their natural wit while our nation beyond their Alps beside natural gift have wisdom gain by experience Italy be most populous so as gentleman palace and land belong to they be common confine within some few enclosure the castle city village and palace be most frequent whence it be that the land be narrow and not well capable of so much people they plant and sow in the very ditch of the high way in the furrow of land upon the wall and ditch of city and castle yea to the very door of private house fit each least corner as well to profit as beauty only Lombardy have large and open field with pastufe to feed sheep and cow and with plenty of whitmeat for they have delicate butter which be not otherwhere to be find except in the valley of Pisa or of the river Arno all other place use oil in stead of it near Parma and Piacenza it yield excellent cheese much prize of very prince in foreign part whether great quantity thereof be transport and great extract into other part of Italy Lombardy also afford sheep to Toscany and other part of Italy as Sicily do corn whereby of old it deserve to be call the garner of Rome Italy have great store of goat the milk whereof be so nutritive as they give it to the weak body for a restorative great herd of cattle be bring into Italy out of Hungary and from diverse country of the Alps but the hungarian ox grow lean with drive far and find in Italy no pasture wherein they may be fat this make italian base to esteem of beef out of Lombardy the italian have few or no catle all Italy be like a most pleasant garden and have few pasture and this make the italian so tender towards the few cattle they have as for fear of cold forsooth in that hot clime they lead they into stable when they be to bring forth their young in the plain country of Lombardy they use horse and especial mare of a exceed little race to ride upon and for bear of burden and ox to draw cart and sometime caroche vulgar Carozzi but in the mountain and hilly country they use ass and mule seldom horse to ride upon and for burden in the roman territory I have see mavy beast call buffol like ox but great and more deform have great horn with foul nostril cast up into the air it be a slow and dull beast yet be provoke have malice enough and the back thereof be common bare of hair and ever almost gall they eat not the flesh thereof but trade with the hide as with those of ox and this beast be hold commodious for husbandry and patient of labour they have no race of horse for beauty or service but only in the kingdom of Naples ass be common sell for 10 crown a piece and a mule for 50 or 60 gold crown which beast be only use in all Italy except only Lombardy of the mule I observe that he will go under a heavy burden from daybreak in summer to dark night without any bate or rest by the way only his meat be tie in a net before his mouth so as he eat while he go and his pace be slow and when his burden be take off at night he tumble and rub his back in the dust to cool it and be thereby more refresh from weariness than a horse can be with lie half the night otherwise he lie not down in the stable scarce once in six month a mule be beget between a horse (@) and a she ass but a mule mount a she mule a ass or any beast whatsoever do never in gender of they and the heat of his seed be yield for cause thereof narrow Italy can bear red or fallow dear only the wood of Toscany yield some few wild boar which be preserve for the great duke game otherwise a few wild beast may soon make great spoil in so rich and well till field as be these of Italy the hill and mountain thereof lie upon the south sun be in general most fertile or fruitful of all other such be the field and hill or the neapolitan territory such be the mountain and hill of Liguria lie upon the tyrrhene sea such be the territory about the lake of vulgar lie at the foot of the southside of the Alps the field of Lombardy be less happy in yield fruit but give excellent pasture and corn where the husbandman make use of the very furrow between the acre for as in the acre he sow corn so in the furrow he plant elm tree the lop whereof serve he to burn and likewise plant vine which shoot up in height upon the body of those tree but these vine yield but a small wine by reason they grow so high and in a plain country in the upper part of Italy they plant in one and the same field olive and almond tree and under they sow corn and in the furrow plant vine which shoot up rest upon short stake and yield strong wine of diverse sort because they grow not high and the ground be hilly have more benefit from the sun beat upon it the soil of Toscany be hilly and stony seem to i at the first sight to be barren but after I find it not only to yield fruit plentiful but also good increase of corn as of one measure sow common eight or ten measure often fourteen and sometime twenty five neither do they give the ground rest by lay it fallow as we do but each second year they sow part of it with bean and pulse yield plentiful increase and then bury the stubble to rot in the ground make it thereby fat to bear wheat again myself observe that at the foot of the southside of the Alps they gather wheat and rye in the month of june and then sow the same field with light kind of grain which they gather in the month of October yet by reason of the multitude of the people and the narrowness of the land the italian not only carry not any grain into foreign part but also the merchant bring grain to they be cherish by the prince with fair word and reward that they may come again more special by the duke of Florence who take care to provide for his country not only grain from Sicily and all other part but also sheep out of Lombardy which he divide among his subject at what price he list take this charge upon he to see that his people want not victual as well for the public good as his own great gain Italy yield plenty of orange which tree be most pleasant to behold yield fruit three time each year and bear at one time ripe and green orange and bud they have like plenty of citron Limon and cedar tree which in Lombardy grow upon the brick wall of garden as vine do with we and be keep in earthen vessel but upon the mountain and hill of upper Italy the field abound with these tree which both in body and fruit be as big as our appletree and they transport great store of these fruit into foreign part there be many wood of chestnut which they little esteem only poor people eat they and with the rest they feed hog as with acorn the chestnut tree be not unlike the oak tree but that it be more small high and straight there be some wood of pine tree which be high without any bough or leaf to the very top where they have a round tuft and they bear at one time the fruit of three veere one pine apple round and sharp at the top have some hundred or more knob like hazel nut in which knob the kernel be of little bigness but of such virtue to provoke wantonness as they serve it at all feast all the field be full of figtree not small as with we but as big in the body as some appel-tree and they have broad leaf the fruit have the form of a long (@) and a black skin and a red juice be to be suck like sugar in taste neither do I think any fruit to be more pleasant than this pull from the tree I say pull from the tree because the dry fig export be not in taste comparable thereunto in the field of upper Italy be great plenty of almond tree so as you will say that a whole province be but one garden like plenty have they of Olive tree which yield a sweet oil use by they in stead of butter and in sorraigne part for wholesomeness yet I can think that it can be whok some when it be heat as the italian use it to fry meat they have some but not to great plenty of pomegranate which tree be not unlike that of the white role but the leaf be little and the flower and the bud of a red colour the husbandman make ditch about the root of all these fruit tree and the inhabitant of pleasant taly be notable in all kind of husbandry the cypress poplar and oak tree grow in many place but be little esteem as bear no fruit Italy upon the hill and mountain lie towards the sun yield rich wine and very nourish yet some out of experience say they be not wholesome for fat man as cause obstruction and hinder the passage of the urine and other cuacuation but I be sure they be more pleasant in taste than any other wine whatsoever bring into England that ever I taste but of all the kind of wine to be name in my follow discourse I have speak more at large in the first part write my journal through Italy I have see pease artichoke cloved gilly flower and other flower of the best kind sell in the marketplace of saint mark in Venice all the month of February but they have not the odoriferous smell of summer-flower also at Genoa in the month of December I do see the same flower and fruit sell and many of they for one yea the flower be odoriferous in smell and new gather which make i think that those I do see at Venice be preserve by art and not new gather and they of Genoa acknowledge that they learn the art to make flower grow in winter of cow by chance nipping-offsome bud in summer which they observe to bud and put forth again in winter for the garener upon this observation do themselves nip offsome bud new put forth in summer and forbear to water that root all summer time do upon approach of winter dig about the rout and sow clove about it to make the winter-flower have the better (@) and then cover the root with earth begin daily to water it and with this art soon or late use they make the earth yield rose or any flower in what month of the year they will to that the ground lie upon the south sun and fence from cold wind the gulf of Venice afford fish to serve that city in good plenty the sea of Rome afford less and that of Genoa none at all but in the sea of Genoa near the island Sardinia and Corsica they fish cora sell at Genoa for three lyre the ounce in the market at Venice they have great ouster but in no great plenty and diverse kind of shellfish as cockle scalop and razor call in the italian tongue cape tonde round cape cape sante holy cape and cape long long cape and these they have in more plenty then in most part of England but the oyster be very dear some twenty for a lyre and I do not remember to have see shelfish in any other city of Italy but only in neither have the italian any store of freshwater fish so as most of their market be furnish in very lent-time with salt and dry fish or at least new dead which the german can endure use to see they alive before they will eat they they have at certain time of the year reasonable plenty of bird but not great in number or variety of kind but hen and especial those of Turkey or the indies seem more plentiful serve in the market because the common sort feed only upon root diverse kind of pulse herb and small meat dry or salt I remember not to have see any stork in Italy no not in the free city and state where fabulous writer say they most willing live as under more just lord and governor the italian gentleman much delight in the art to catch bird and in garden fit to that purpose with net bush and glade spare no cost or industry in that kind not only the gentleman but even the prince of Italy open profess to be merchant which our man with leave may I say foolish (@) and only permit the the traffic retail of their good to man of inferior sort keep all trade in grosle or whole sale to themselves or at least by their treasure common great and authority such as it be draw the chief profit thereof into their own purse and by this course they keep the patrimony descend from their ancestor and daily increase they while our gentleman prodigal in expense and ashamed to make honest gain destroy their family but of all trade they be most enrich by silk and clothes make of it especial they of Florence and Lucca where the gentleman for exercise of this trade keep open shop the silkworm be vulgar call Farfalli which enfold themselves in a piece silk worm of silk they weave of a oval form and yellow colour and some of they so enfold be let out for preservation of the kind by clip that piece of silk they weave the other piece be set in the sun that the enfold worm may die whereby (@) (@) be make excellent which otherwise will be little worth they feed on the leaf of mulberry tree and the duke of Florence plant these tree in the ditch upon the highway from which if any passenger pull a bough he pay a great penalty wonder at this make of silk and the art to weave the same after I return into england by acquaintance with some that for experience keep these worm I find that about the month of August they cast seed upon paper or linen cloth whereon they be lay and soon after die that this seed lay aside all winter be set forth in the sun the next may or assoon as the mulberry tree yield leaf to feed the worm that by the sun heat the worm take life of that seed in the form of a horse hair at the first after grow to a strange bigness feed greedy upon those leaf that they begin then to be sick and grow of a yellow colour leave their feed that they be then put into a place fit for their work with corner little distant one from the other that they then weave and enfold themselves in their web as I say that part of the web be lay aside to preserve the worm out of which they eat out their way and come forth wing like butterfly but little use the wing and these web yield no silk thread but be dress and sever do serve for base use that the rest of the web be put into a oven to choke the worm which web yield excellent silk dislolue itself into small thread last that the worm preserve by spoil their web out of which they eat their way do as I say cast a seed or glutinous matter upon a paper or linen cloth and then die and that all these thing namely to come to life to be feed up to weave their web to leave seed for generation and to die be finish in the space of four month after take my journey into Turkey I do see infinite number of these worm in the great and lesser Asia where I find that these worm grow to full bigness from the month of February to the month of May then leave their meat then have house make of reed for they of purpose like the hive for bee but more large where as I say they involve themselves in their own web which be set in the sun the worm die within they and the web moisten with hot water be with wheel draw into small thread that some of these web for preservation of the kind be lay aside within the house where the worm eat out their way and come forth wing be lay upon a linen cloth upon which they leave the foresay seed or glutinous matter and so die that these clothes be lay aside in winter but in the month of February next follow be set out in the sun or more frequent carry in the bosom of country people both man and woman by which heat the seed yield worm at first no great than a grain of mustard seed but after grow to the length of a man hair as he usual wear it on his head and to the thickness of a man little finger former in the chapter of proverb I say that proverbial the merchant of Florence be call crafty those of Lucca greedy those of Venice bold venture all in one vessel the traffic those of Milan faithful profess if need be that the plague be in the house they desire to sell. and I there mention this proverb of the venetian traffic ilbianco and Nero and cottone black and white that be pepper and cotton have make Venice rich english merchant bring into Italy tin lead herring especial dry which they esteem among dainty Conny skin veche kersey and sometime english corne. they also bring thither diverse commodity from dantzk as cordage hemp Caviale tallow wax indian hide and like commodite of Poland and Moscovy the netherlander bring into Italy dry fish and the commodity of all nation with which they trade both here and in all place into England netherlands and over part the italian send velvet of Genoa taffeta of Florence and Lucca satin of Bologna and other city stuff of Milan as fustian and diverse kind of silk weave and in thread gold and silver clothes of gold and silver Alom and like commodity bring to Venice out of Turkey from the land of the mediterranean sea subject to Venice they send to we malmsy and Muskedine wine of Candia corrand of Zant and Cephalonia the ship of our part which bring corn or any victual into Italy be receive with all courtesy especial by the duke of Florence in the haven of Ligorno and even by the pope in the haven of civita Vecchia the italian have great traffic with the turk in the mediterranean sea whereof we shall speak more hereafter but out of the straits of that sea they trade little in our age or nothing at all except sometime into Spain with their own ship and for navigation whereof I must speak in the chapter of commonwealth they have small skill in that art their ship be of great but then to receive commodity and well furnish with ordinance against the turkish pirate but they be slow and not easy turn so as sometime the turk less dare at sea yet take some great venetian ship with their small bark or galley for the italian and turk make their naval fight with galley and no other ship whereof the venetian and Spaniard have great number in this Sea we read that the pope make league with the venetian and spaniard for bear some charge of war against the turk and it be likely he have some galley in that one sole haven of civita Vecchia belong to he but I never chance to see any of the pope galley the duke of Florence at this time have ready arm to spoil the turk some 5 or 6 galley the other prince have none at al. these galley be much different in bigness and have their name of the number of the oar row they as trtremes of three oar on each side of fifteen oar and the mediterranean sea be subject to small ebb or flow of tide and little subject to storm these galley safe row between the neighbour shore and every night put into some haven or bay the italian be so much enamour of their own land as they desire to see no other soil and abhor from venture themselves at sea so as they seldom prove expert in navigation never bold the venetian have a law that every ship shall carry a young gentleman of Venice in it allow he diet and a stipend and also shall bring up a venetian boy in (@) thus their wise progenitor take care that neither gentleman nor the inferior sort shall be ignorant in navigation but the gentleman at this day so they may have the benefit of the law by receive the stipend and the value of their diet for the voyage care not for the experience and rather desire to stay at home then trouble the ship any further and for this cause the venetian altogether use greek aswell for common mariner as for officer and master of their ship and these greek as I have often find by experience except they can see the shore which by reason of the narrowness of the sea and frequent isle may often be see be often in doubt sometime ignorant where they be and the least storm arise make such a noise and confusion as they bewray their ignorance and want of courage our english ship come forth of the harbour of Venice together with a venetian ship will sail into Syria and return back again before the venetian ship can come thither whereof two reason may be give one that the english mariner be pay by the voyage not by the day or month of absence contrary the greek be pay by the italian after the day of absence not after the voyage the other reason be that not only the italian ship be huge and great and slow of sail but also the master upon the first change of wind or foresee of ill weather either for fear or because they be pay by the day not by the voyage present put into some haven whence common they can come forth but with one or very few wind whereas the english on the contrary have not only nimble swift ship but themselves be so expert and bold as in regard their loss be the great the long they be from home they either sail if the wind be any whit favourable or lie at hull if the wind be full contrary and so be reedy to take the first blast of wind serve their turn concern the diverse kind of diet in diverse italian city I have before relate their diet these thing proverbial say the neapolitan be magnifical spend more sugar than bread the florentine be of spare diet but wonderful cleanliness those of Lucca keep golden mediocritic in all thing the Tyberine pear and Martioline cheese be great dainty those of Genoa be of most spare diet and no cleanliness the mantuan feed on base bean the ferrarian be in hospital the padoan sup with half a pennyworth of fish the venetian live spare the Siennesi magnifical and their dainty be goat flesh and fresh cheese the Milanesi live plentiful and provoke appetite with sharp sauce the Novocomenses eat without end and drink stout those of Piedmont diet after the french manner and those of Ancona base and these thing may perhaps be true say if the italian city be compare one with the other but many thing may seem less apt say if general they be compare with the city of foreign part the italian general compare with english or french be most spare in their diet general they require small preparation or furniture of their table they eat neat and modest but as they be not like the spaniard who be say to eat spare at their own cost large at other man table so howsoever they be not so great flesh-eater as the northern man yet if the bread be weigh which one of they eat at a meal with a great charger full of herb and a little oil mix therein believe i they have no cause to accuse northern man for great eater they seldom make feast but if they make any than out of their innated pride disdain to be surpass by any they make they sumptnous and that much more than we always make the comparison equal of one degree against the other and this be most certain that they infinite pass we in the expense about their garden in fit place for bird in draw water to they and adorn the conduit head with imagery in chapel and other building of which thing some yield they fruit the other last perpetual for they bestow their money in stable thing to serve their posterity where as our great expense end in the cast out of excrement which make i less commend our expense in great provision of meat as well at feast as daily diet and give i leave to hold this paradox or opinion against that of the common sort that the english be never more idle never more ignorant in manual art never more factious in follow the party of prince or their landlord never more base as I may say trencher slave then in that age wherein great man keep open house for all comer and goer and that in our age wherein we have better learn each man to live of his own and great man keep not such troop of idle servant not only the english be become very industrious and skilful in manual art but also the tyranny of lord and gentleman be abate whereby they nourish private dissension and civil war with the destruction of the common people neither be I move with the vulgar opinion prefer old time to we because it be apparent that the cloister of monk who spoil all that they may be beneficial to few and gentleman house who nourish a rabble of servant in idleness and in rob by the high way lie open to all idle people for meat and drink be cause of great ill than good to the commonwealth yet I will not be so understand as if I will have the poor shut cut of door for I rather desire that great work of charity shall be exercise towards they to which we shall be more enable by honest frugality then by foolish prodigality I call it foolish and think the vulgar sort of prodigal worthy of all ignominy who with huge expense keep many kennel of dog and cast of hawk and entertain great number of stranger sometime not know by name often scoff at the entertainer always ingrateful and so not only use they to live unlawful without labour or sweat of their brow but also in the mean time themselves will have a brother for their butler and be so niggardly towards their kinsman yea child and wife as they provide not necessary for they and have no care of their advancement education and mean to live but prefer vainglory before these religious care how much better be it for these prodigal man to lay aside some good part of their revenue to nourish learned man to procure good preacher for their companion and guide to relieve virtuous man in their want and to spend the same to like noble and princely end but I return to my purpose a stranger may live in Italy with less expense then in Germany where he must bear the charge of his consort excessive drink and if any object the dearth of victual and wickedness of host in Italy he shall find that this be his own want not any ill of the country and when he have experience to do his own affair there he will be of my opinion the italian have small money of brass and for the least of they a man may buy bread little paper of spice or any such thing that be to be sold. these small money the abundance of people in a narrow land and the common people poverty but most of all their innated pride such as they have rather starve for want then beg these thing make they do any service for a stranger for a small reward and make the passage of river or channel as at Venice and all necessary to be afford for a small piece of money neither be it a small commodity of these little brass money that it make the mean sort more ready to give alm this benefit the english may well know by the want of like money whereby the hire of porter all reward and each alm be give in silver money and the small piece thereof be rare all expense be much increase the woman of Italy know not the price of any thing or ever go to market scarce be allow to go to church neither do they trust their servant to make their market but the rich of all Italy and most noble especial in Venice daily buy their own victual and other necessary and in all market-place stand little boy with basket to carry any thing that be buy to their house which they easy find know all street and ally and never fail to perform this honest though the buyer leave they and according to their custom go about his other affair for if they shall fail they can escape punishment be easy to be find in the market where they use daily to stand and well know by face and name yet in truth the italian diet be so spare as almost stranger alone use these little porter and the very gentleman of Venice which notwithstanding arrogate to themselves a pre-eminence above all gentleman of Italy with the singular title of carry home what they buy to eat either in the sleeve of their gown or in a clean handkerchief they spend much bread and oil and the very porter feed on most pure white bread almost without any other meat except it be some root and those that be rich do for the most part feed on bread neither remember I to have ever see brown bread in Italy only they eat sailet of herb with their bread and mingle they with oil and I remember that I see a barrel of oil sell for twenty lyre and a bushel of wheat contain forty eight measure call by the latin and use by the hebrew for 120 lyre but the very gentleman buy their bread of the baker many time especial in short day of winter they will break their fast with a bit of cakebread or sweet bread call vulgar and general Gentilezze and a cup of sweet wine and so abstain from dinner for the most part at table they use black or bay salt which the venetian have of their own for bid the use of any white salt bring into the territory so as it be only sell by stealth and with danger of penalty the magistrate daily use to set the price of flesh and all thing sell in the market especial in the upper part of Italy and namely at Sienna where stranger live very commodious and by this custom a stranger can no more be deceive than one of the country in the state of and especial at Sienna a stranger may live more commodious then in any other part of Italy because the inhabitant be most courteous so as at Sienna they admit stranger to converse and dance with the chief gentlewoman of the city and because the language especial at Sienna be hold the most pure as also for that be very cheap and stranger need not stand in fear of be murder as in Lombardy they do in the state of Milan there be plenty of all kind of flesh especial of mutton and abundance of whittmeate be commend above all other part of Italy for delicate butter not to be have otherwhere except in some few large valley and excellent cheese whereof great quantity be transport into foreign part and they no less than the netherlander serve in butter and cheese every meal for the first and last dish in the market place of Venice there be plenty of mutton and veal sell in little portion and by weight there as in all Italy and there be also plenty of fish hen egg Turkey hen and some store of bird with great abundance of red herring and pickle herring sardelle anchone and like pickle fish of Caviale a salt liquor make of fish and botargo as I think the rone of a fish of piacentine cheese and cheese of Parma of mushroom snail the hinder part of frog all hold for great dainty and these thing be to be have in more abundance because the common sort eat little or no flesh or fish or bird but only herb pulse snail and root with white bread I have speak former of their fish at sea and their shell-fish which they much esteem and sea-fish be indeed rare find but only at Venice also they have little store of freshwater fish only there be great abundance of eel where the river Po end in a lake near the adriatic sea in the dukedom of the upper part of Italy yield the same thing but in a far less quantity and in Toscany they frequent eat young goat flesh which be very good and savoury and sometime there will be wild boar to be sell and they delight much in fresh curd new press and make into little cheese the italian sell all kind of flesh in little piece and all thing for diet in little portion that the mean sort if they list may at least taste the great dainty the inner part of goat vulgar and the stone of ram and Regles vulgar Granella be esteem great dainty especial in Toscany which we cast away be very good meat fry and because the land be more populous than plentiful in victual they eat lay and other bird which we esteem unwholesome in general the italian and more special the florentine be most near at the table and in their inn from morning to night the table be spread with white clothes strew with flower and fig leave with ingestar or glass of diverse colour wine set upon they and delicate fruit which will invite a man to eat and drink who otherwise have no appetite be all open to the sight of passenger as they ride by the high way through their great unglase window at the table they touch no meat with the hand but with a fork of silver or other mettle each man be serve with his fork and spoon and glass to drink and as they serve small piece of flesh not whole joint as with we so these piece be cut into small bit to be take up with the fork and they see the flesh till it be very tender in summer time they set a broad earthen vessel full of water upon the table wherein little glass fill with wine do swim for coolness they use no spit to roast flesh but common stew the same in earthen pipkin and they feed much upon little fish and flesh cut and fry with oil they have no skill in the art of cookery and the meat be serve to the table in white glister and paint dish of earth whereof the fine be much esteem with we they be not willing invite to eat with other man esteem base of those who live at other man trencher call they vulgar shifter for meal and the reason hereof be that they will not be tie to invite other again which in their pride they will do if they shall be invite to they and this be the chief cause that make they nice to converse with stranger of the florentine though most courteous yet spare other italian jest say that when they meet a man about dinner time they ask ' signoria ha sir have you dine and if he answer I they reply as if they will have invite he to dinner but if he answer no they reply signor go sir for it be high time to dine they think it best to cherish and increase friendship by meeting in market place and garden but hold the table and bed unfit for conversation where man shall come to eat quick and sleep sound thus not provoke appetite with variety of meat or eat with other for good fellowship they must needs be more temperate than other entice by these mean to eat beyond hunger in city where many take chamber in one house they eat at a common table but each man have his own meat provide the hostess dress it and serve each man with his own napkin glass fork spoon knife and ingestar or glass of wine which after meat be several and neat lay up by the hostess and at the table perhaps one man have a hen another a piece of flesh the three poach egg and each man several meat after his diet but it be no courtesy for one to offer another part of his meat which they rather take to be do in pride as if he think that he that have a salad or egg can not have a hen or flesh if he list for want of money to conclude they hold it no honour or disgrace to live plentiful or spare so they live of their own and be not in debt for in that case they be esteem slave thus live of their own they give due honour to superior so they return due respect to they otherwise they despise he that be rich say in scorn let he dine twice a day and wear two goune if he will it be enough for i to have convenient diet and apparel they have a very delicate sauce for roast meat call savore make of slice of bread steep in broth with as many walnut and some few leaf of marjoram beat in a mortar and mingle therewith together with the juice of gooseberry or some sharp liquor put in when it be set on the table in some city and university especial for the german sojourn there and unwilling to buy their own meat they have ordinary table to be pay by the week or month at the rate of some eight or ten crown the month which live they call a that be by dosen or by the great but it be much more commodious for he that have some experience and skill in the tongue to buy his own meat since in Camere locande that be hire chamber the hostess at a reasonable rate of the chamber be tie to dress his meat and give he napkin with like necessary and there want not good commodity to buy all thing he want and to live cheap as I have show in the expense of my journey through Italy the italian host be notable in fawn and crouch for gain so as they meet passenger at the city gate and emulous invite they to their house with promise of all dainty as if they will give they for nought but when they be once come into the house all thing threaten famine and for that meat they have if the passenger first agree not for the price they extort so unreasonable as nothing can be add to their perfidiousness and covetousness the german say these be fair-spoken and most obsequious man in all thing till they come to the shot for if any man love honourable title cap bend of knee and a humble look they will observe he to the full but in the end the shot will be intolerable and he shall pay for their feign courtesy and lowliness and this extortion be not to be avoid by the best experience if they stand not continual upon their guard with these fencer especial in both the mark or Marquisates where they be not host but devourer of passenger and howsoever the italian host be more excusable in their extortion because the prince grant to keep these inn do not shear but indeed devour they and he that buy must needs sell yet the marchians inhospitall nature be singular and above all other for the florentine oppress with like or great exaction yet use stranger much more courteous I will advise the unexperienced passenger that there be in these italian inn two ordinary course of eat one that be upon reckon the other all that be by the meal at a set rate seldom exceed three the passenger for cheapness shall take his breakfast upon reckon or carry about he some almond fig dry or raisin that dine upon reckon in case the host set a excessive price on i it for woe to he that eat without first know the price he may seem content to eat of his own take only bread and wine whereof the price be know and ordinary and so may contain their rapacity within some reasonable bound but at night because of his bed he shall do well to sup at the ordinary and before supper to know his bed and get clean sheet yet he must not expect a feather bed which that clime bear not as too hot for the (@) but a hard mattress only he shall have clean sheet at least if he curious demand they howsoever against the worst event he shall do well to carry linen breech and to wear they in the ordinary sheet for the italian if they have no kind of the french pox yet for the most (@) be trouble with a itch witness the frequent cry in their street of (@) ointment for the Itch. I former say that a passenger needs have no care of his horse by the way for it be the custom to agree for their meat as well as hire with the vetturine so they call those that let horse and go on foot or send a servant to meat they but since the same vetturine will also offer a passenger to agree with he in like fort for his own diet sure as I have say in the chapter of the manner to take journey the passenger be in ill case that be diet by they neither will I advise any so to do except only in the way from Rome to Naples and back again where a passenger in such a tumultuary journey and by reason of that old custom shall otherwise be worse entreat last a passenger shall do wise especial at nigh to go to the best inn and of most fame that he may be more safe from the loss of his money or hazard of his life the italian hold it a great shame to be drunken they sometime salute one another with a cup in manner of a health but leave it to his pleasure when he will pledge they and then he salute he that drink to he as well as he to who he drink say sir I pledge you and I drink to you Sir the word brindisi come of the dutch phrase i bring each I will bring it to you use when they drink to any man and this show the custom be borrow from the german and use by the obsequious italian to please they yet abhor from drunkenness so please to the german Italy yield excellent wine and the common red wine be hold very nourish so as the fair woman will dive with the same and a sop of bread dip in it think it will make they fat which kind of woman the venetian most love all thing else be equal yea and more fair so as they proverbial say chibeve branco bianco a he that drink white piss white he that drink red gain the colour these be the most famous wine of Italy (@) Christ the tear of Christ and like wine near cinqueterre in Liguria la (@) and the white muskadine especial that of Montefiaschoni in Toscany and in the kingdom of Naples and prosecho in histria in general the grape that grow high upon elme-tree in the plain as in Lombardy and especial the grape of Modena yield very small wine but those that grow upon hill and mountain rest on short stake yield very rich wine in the shop where they sell muskadines there be continual boy attend with little wig of sweet bread and junket which the italian dip in the wine and have thus break their fast in winter time they common eat no more till supper the three book chap. i. of the geographical description of Turkey the situation fertility traffic and Diet. the longitude of Turkey extend fifty five degree and a half from the meridian of forty four degree and a half to that of a hundred degree and the latitude extend forty degree from the parallel of ten degree to that of fifty degree the province of this empire in Europe be thus number Illyris Albania Epirus Gracia Macedonia Thessalia Thracia Mysia dacia or I Hungaria and the land under he that lie in Europe 1 Illyris a part of Sclavonia be subject partlv to the turk partly to the house of Austria the chief city whereof be zara which together with the territory thereof the turk take from the venetian the rest of the province be still subject to the house of Austria and Scordona lie upon the sea as do the former city and all the province also Croatia vulgar cranaten and of old call liburnia belong to this province 2 Albania have these know city Dirachium vulgar Dorazzo of old call Epidaurus and Vallona 3 Epirus have these town chimaera Meiandria Butrinto and Nicopolis of old part of Epirus be call acarnania of the royal blood of this province be Alexander Scanderbag who bring up in the great Turk court and upon occasion fall from he do so much trouble that vast empire 4 Gracia be of old divide into and Helles of old call Aiggealia and Pelasgia be at this day name Morea and it be a that be a neck of land almost a island only join to the continent with a isthmus that be a narrow piece of Land the rest be compass with the sea and be of old divide into which have the city sution and Carinthus Argo'is which have the city Argos and neapolis Achaia or Elis whereof the chief city be Elis and Arcadia who chief town be Psofis and and here the river Emaus or Erimanthus spring and join with the brook Alpheus fall into the gulf of Arcadia also the river Inachus spring in the mountain Parthenius and fall into the gulf of neapolis moreover have a five province call Lacedemonta or Laconia whereof the chief city be Lacedaemon or Sparta most famous of old the six province be Messena in which be the city Metona now call Modon the straight neck of land join to the continent be against the turk fortify with a wall by the christian but the turk cast down the wall and take all the province Helles or Achaia the second province of Greece contain Attica megaris Boetia Phocis and AEtolis Attica be more famous than the rest in which be the famous city Athens megaris be a small region the chief city whereof be Megaria in which Euclides be bear Boetia be a very large region so call of a ox lead Cadmus thither who build the boetian Thebes so call for difference from nine other city call Thebes the mountain Thermopula derive
