from the mountain lie upon Epirus divide Greece from the west to the east as the apenninedivide Italy and the famous mountain otris Pelion and Ossa be part thereof of old Aulis be a famous city of Boetia in which iphigenia daughter to Agamemnon be sacrifice Phocis be a small region the town whereof be Elatea and Delphis seat at the foot of the mountain Parnassus have the temple of Apollo not in the town but upon a rock of the mountain where spring the castalian fountain sacred to the muse and the mount Helicon lie near the same the region of the locri be small and the chief city be now call Lepanto of old a people call peer come out of Thrace dwell under of who it be call the Pierian mountain and the muse be call pierides Doris pertain to the region of the locri and the chief city be Doricum whence come the doric dialect the last province of Helles and of all Greece be AEtolia divide from by the river Achelon fall from the mount Pindus and the chief town thereof be naupactus now call Lepanto near the gulf whereof the christian navy under the commaud of don juan of Austria give a famous overthrow to the turkish navy in our age. the other city be call Chaledon whence be the Chaledonian boar sing of the poet 5 the five province of Turkey be Macedonia of old call Migdonia and Emathea the chief city whereof be thessalonica vulgar now call Saloniche to the citizen whereof s. Paul write his epistle the mountain of this province Olympus Pelion Ossa be famous by the fable of the giant and Athos be feign to pass the cloud with his top 6. the low part of Macedonia be call Thessalia or AEmonia of Thessalus the son of AEmon or as other say of jason the chief town whereof be Pharsalos who field be famous by the victory of Caesar against Pompey 7 Thracia have fair city trimontium of old call Poneropolis and philippopolis Adrianopolis and the head city constantinopolis of old call Bysantium now stambol seat upon the Bosphorus of Thracia it have famous mountain Rhodope Mela and ismarus upon propontis the thracian chersonesus or neck of land lie upon the hellespont in which be the town Sesto and Callipolis 8 the upper Mista be divide into three part Bosnia and Servia and the low Misia into three part Bulgaria wallachia and Moldavia in Bulgaria the river Danubius begin to be call Isther which fall into the euxine sea with four strong and three lesser channel 9 dacia or Transiluania be of old possess by the saxon who there build seven city or castle of which the province be call septem-castrensis vulgar Sieben burgeon and of old it belong to the kingdom of Hungary but at this day be tributary to the turk 10 Hungaria so call of the people hunnt be of old call Fannonia the low and of right belong to the german emperor but of late the turk have subdue the great part thereof it have many and strong fortify city as Varadinum Segedinum vulgar seget castrum take by the turk Strigonium vulgar (@) take by the turk in the year 1543 Alba at that time also take by they the feat of the bishop Buda seat upon the Danow twice or thrice take and regain on both side of old the king seat call vulgar Offen and Pesta seat on the other side of Danow vulgar call new offen the hungarian nation yield to none in strength and courage not unlike the scythian in language and manner 11 the land of Europe in the ionian sea be these Corcira vulgar corfu Cephalania and Zaintos in latin Zacinthus vulgar now call zante all three subject to the venetian all the land in the aegean sea be subject to the Turk be innumerable among they be the cyclades so call because they lie round together the chief whereof be Cytnos Cyphnos vulgar Sifano parus now call Paris famous for the marble tenos now call Tenasa naxus Andros and Delos the chief of all where Apollo and Diana be bear next they be the sporades so call of lie disperse among which be Melos lera Nicaria AEgina and Lemnos vulgar Stalemine whether they fable that Vulcan be cast down the island Ibea now vulgar Negroponte be attribute to Greece be separate from the continent with so little distance as it can hardly be name a island and it lie close to the city of Athens the Turk have great part of Asia divide into Asia the lesser and Asia the great the lesser be now call Anatolia or Anatolia of a greek word signify the east be a kind of isthmus or neck of land lie between two sea the euxine towards the north and the mediterranean towards the south as it have the thracian Bosphorus as passable by a ox swim and propontis as lie before the sea and Hellespont and the aegean sea towards the west and be confine with the river Euphrates towards the east this lesser Asia be all subject to the Turk and have 16. province Bithynia Paphlagonia Capadocia Gallatia the great and lesser Misia jonia Charia Lydia Pamphilia Lacaonia Licia Cilicia the lesser Armenia and Chomagena 1 Bithynia be at this day call Migtonia and the chief city thereof be Nicaea the metropolitan city famous for the council in the year 314. of 318. bishop meeting to beat down the arrian heresy and there make the nicene creed Lybissa where Hannibal be bury Chalcedo where one of the eight old counsel be hold by 530 bishop Heraclia Nicomedia Phrasso where Esculapius be bear and bursa seat over against Constantinople where some turkish emperor lie bury and thither the great Turk elder son be send to govern or in a kind of exile for he never see his father more till he be dead and thither he be send assoon as he be circumcise 2. some account Pontus for part of Bithynia 3. Paphlagonia be the three province 4. Capadocia the four so call of the river Capadocis and the chief city be now call Genech and here the amazon be say to have live from the destruction of Troy to the time of Alexander the great 5 the lesser be call of son to the king of Thebes fly with his sister from his stepmother who mount on a Ramm with a golden fleece perhaps a ship so call his sister be drown and give the name to Hellespont and he come himself to this part of Asia which at this day be call and yield a most excellent wine and in this province be Ilium or Troy the mountain Ida and Tinolus and the river Pactolus the nine Sibylla that prophesy of Christ be a frigian and here reign king Tantalus by covetousness lose the use of his good of who the poet so fable the great be within Land 6 the chief city of Misia be Traianopolis build by trajan and whereof mentiou be make in the seventeen chapter of the act of the apostle and where Gallene be bear who live 140 year 7 Gallatia or Gallogrecia be possess by the gall under Brennus whereof the chief city be Laodicia and to this province belong Pisidia the chief city whereof be Antiochia 8 the chief city of jonia be Ephesus where be the temple of Diana which erostrate a Gothe do burn to be famous Miletum where Thales and anaximines be bear and Smyrna 9 the chief city of Charia be of old Halicarnassus in which be the sepulchre of the king hold for one of the seven miracle of the world 10 the chief city of Lydia be Sardis where Croesus reign 11 the chief city of Pamphilia be Zelotia and in this province be the mountain chimaera upon the wild top whereof lion be find as in the middle part yield grass goat do feed and in the bottom be serpent whereof come the fiction of the monster 12 in Lacaonia of old be these city Iconium metrapolitan and lysire where Timothy saint Paul disciple be bear and the river Xanthus run through this province 13 Licia lie upon the sea between Pamphilia and Charia 14 Cylicia lie under the mountain Taurus upon the further bosom of the mediterranean sea or iccian gulf where Alexander the great overcome Darius and there be Tarsus now call bias in which town saint Paul be bear 15 Armenia the lesser be think by some to be the land ararat upon the mountain whereof the ark of Noah rest after the deluge under this province some comhrehend 16 Chomagena be full of mountain and confine upon Asia the lesser towards the east the geographer divide Asia the great into five part according unto five empire the first of the duke of Moscovia the second of great cham over the tartar the three of the persian king the four of diverse indian king the five of ottoman over the turk and this last only belong to my purpose therefore omit the rest I will speak of it the great Turk have these province in Asia the great namely Syria Arabia Babylonia Chaldea Assyria and diverse land Syria be vulgar call Sorya and contain Palestina Phaenitia Caelosyria Damaseena Sirophaenitia and as some account Mesopotamia Palestina of old call Canaan be subdivide into idumaea judaea Samarta Galilea Idumea of old call edom be not unfertil and abound with palmtree but where it confine upon Arabia be say to be barren and there be the mountain call sur in scripture it have these chief city of old Maresa Ascalon judea be the second province of Palestina so call of the tribe of and jerusalem the chief city thereof be at this day call Chutz the most famous place thereof be Bethlehem bethania mount Olivet joppe where s. Peter raise Dorcas from the dead Lydda where he heal the man sicko of the palsy Arimathia where joseph be bear and Hebron where the wife of Abraham and four patriarch lie bury the hebrew say that the valley call Sittim by Moses be most (@) where now be the lake Asphaltis and in this valley stand Sodom and Gomorrah beyond jordan be the desert of Betabora where John baptize and the land Gilliad where the amonite and moabite dwell of old Samaria the three province of Palestine have these city Sichim capernaum seat upon the lake Geneseret Nahim where Christ raise the widow son betzaida and Coratzen and beyond the lake be Samaria the chief city of the ten tribe that fall from juda Galilea the last province of Palestine be divide into the upper and the low the upper call Gallile of the gentile contain the kingdom basin and have these city Chana the great Caesarea Philippi Seleutia and Gaulon and this province have the title of tetrarch the low have the same title and contain the region of decapolis and Traconitis beyond jordan the chief city thereof be Betsaida Chana the lesser and Nazaret and here be the mount tabor where Christ be transfigure and the river jordan run through all Palestine have two head jar and dan upon the mount antilibanus and run thence into the lake Asphaltis by the way make two lake samachonitis upon the confine divide the two galily and Genezaret or upon the confine between the low Galily and Samaria phenicia the second part of Syria lie upon the sea and reach to Serophenicia from the city dora upon the sea to the mount carmelus where it be confine with the mount Lybanus the chief city thereof be dora Ptolemais Acon Tyrus Sarcpta and Sydon Selosiria the three part of Syria so call as crooked or hollow have of old the title of tetrarch in which be the city Damascus which give the name to the four part of Syria and here alabaster grow of which they make vessel Damascus be think to be build by the servant of Abraham and near the city be a place where Christ appear to saint Paul and the sepulchre of Zachartas be say to be there and they show a place where Cain be say to have kill Abel the soil be most fertile so as writer and the consent of all man witness that grape grow there all the year and that there be plenty of quince fig almond and Damascus prune Sirophenitia the five part of Syria have these city of old call happy julia Biblus Iripolis Landicia Antiochia of old call Reblatha which after it have be decay by a great earthquake be rebuild by the emperor justinian and call theopolis a famous city in which the professor former call disciple first have the name of christian and history testify that saint Peter be the first bishop thereof Mesopotamia the last part of Syria be so call as lie between two river swift Tigris so call of the swiftness Tigris in the mede tongue signify a arrow and Euphrates and by the yearly overflow of these river after the solstice as Egypt by that of Nilus the soil be make most fertile whereof writer report wonder namely that one measure sow yield fifty and in some place sixty measure and that plant perpetual flourish there yet that the inner part want water so as the inhabitant find a spring use to keep it secret that it may not be know to their enemy at this day the turk call this province Diarbecke the city whereof be edessa and Carra which Moses in the twelve chapter of genesis call Haram where Abraham dwell when he come out of Chaldea 2 Arabia be the second part of the turkish empire in Asia the great which be subdivide into Petrea rocky desert and happy Arabia the israelite live forty year in rocky Arabia be full of mountain and barren whereof proceed their murmur there be famous mount Sinai upon which god law be publish and over against it mount Horeb. in Sinai be the region Nabathea and the city thereof Petra after call Arech be in the scripture call Petra of the desert and near it lie the region agra or Agara the inhabitant whereof be call agaren as come of Hagar concubine to Abraham desert Arabia be barren destitute of water and cover with deep sand the inhabitant whereof do live in tent have no certain abide but near Euphrates some dwelling be where be the famous town after call amphipolis happy Arabia lie almost in the form of a chersonesus or neck of land between the two great gulf of the sea the arabian gulf and the persian and it yield cinnamon frankincense myrrh the gum laudanum and other precious odour and abound with honey wax and all kind of cattle except swine only it be say that grain of gold as big as Acorres be find here among the clod of the earth it have the bird phoenix of which kind there be never more than one only which by strike of stone together kindle a fire and burn herself in her nest of myrrh and of the ash come a worm which become a bird and so the phoenix live again they fish pearl in the arabian gulf and jewel be find upon the sea shore the nation of the sabaean be more famous than any other in this province who region call Saba be celebrate for plenty of frankincense and it have wood of tree which be cut yield a frothy humour that turn into that odour the city of happy Arabia be (@) Tolnaby that be the city of the prophet because Mahomet be say to have write his Alcorane there and Mecha famous by Mahomet sepulchre the kingdom of Ormus be part of happy Arabia have a peculiar king but tributary to the king of Spain as he be king of Portugal the metropolitan city whereof rich in trade be call Ormus 3 Babilonia the three part of the turkish empire in Asia have the metropolitan city of old call by the same name but in these day call Bagdet 4 Chaldea the four part lie on the east side of Babilonia whereof the chief city be call vhrr in the scripture from whence Abraham upon God commandment go to Haran a city of Mesapitania 5 Assiria the five part be so call of Assur the son of Shem whereof the chief city be Ninus call ninive in the scripture the old seat of the king build by Assur and Aruela famous by the victory of Alexander the great against Darius the persian King 6 the land of Asia be the six part of the turkish empire in Asia the great and they lie either in the mediterranean sea or in the Archipelagus or in the indian sea in the mediterranean sea lie Candia of old call Creta famous of old for have one hundred city and by the labyrinth of Daedalus and it be call Creta of the earth whiteness from whence great quantity of muskadine wine be export into diverse part of Europe and it be subject to the state of Venice Rhodes lie in the same sea and be of old famous for the residence of the knight of Jerusalem but at this day be possess by the turk drive out those knight who now have their residence in Malta a island near that of Sicily Cyprus be a island in the same sea and be most fertile yield cane of honey whence sugar be make and rich wine and aboundiug with many thing require for life and for pleasure and this island the turk in the last age take from the venetian by force of arm the chief city whereof be Famagosta and Nicosia the Archipelagus have innumerable land whereof the principal and most fruitful be Tenedos small in circuit but famous by the navy of the greek harbour there at the siege of Troy Lesbos Lemnos Mitelene at this day call Metalon of the chief city Samnus of old call Sicania where Hypocrates be bear and Chios now call Zio more esteem than any of the rest for the marble malmsey wine mastic the juice or gum of the tree call and no less for the many rich commodity it yield then for the goodness and largeness of the soil the land of the indian sea belong not to the turk and therefore I will omit they the turkish empire stretch itself yet far contain great part of Africa which by the grecian be call Libya and the word in the greek tongue signify horror and cold give the name to Africa as be void of cold the mountain Atlas in Africa as Taurus in Asia which in some part be call Caucasus and imaus as the mountain of Europe be general call the Alps do divide this country into many part stretch itself towards the east and so forward to Nilus which part or province be know by these name Mauritanta Africa the lesser Syrenaica marmarica AEgyptus Lybia AEthiopia the region under the mountain of Luna and the land 1 Mauritania Tingitana contain two kingdom whereof the king of Spain hold some part and morcco subject to the turk of old it have these town tingis metropolitan and Luxon near which be the garden hesperide which the poet fable to have apple and tree of gold at this day the two chief city be call and Morocco at the straight sea between Spain and Africa the mountain abila in Africa and calpa in Spain be of that form as man will judge they be once join whereupon the poet fable that Hercules divide they and do let in the ocean and so make the mediterranean sea and for this cause the straight be call the narrow sea of Hercules and the pillar of Hercules be erect on Africa side which the emperor Charles the five add to his coat of arm Mauritania caesariensis be also call Numidia for the people be rich in cattle and dwell in tent and when they have eat the grass of one place then remove to another be of their pasture call Nomades and after change a letter become to be name Numidae 2 Africa the less a most fertile region of old be at this day call the kingdom of Tunis and the chief be Hippon metropolitan where saint Austin be bishop Utica renown for have Cato a citizen Carthage where Tertullian be bear Tunis at this day chief madaura where Lucius Apuleius be bear and jacapa where the vine be say to yield grape twice in the year the brook rubricatus be famous for the serpent kill there by Attilius Regulus in the time of the first punic war the quicksand or shoal of the sea adjoin be much fear of mariner lie sometime deep sometime shallow as the sand be drive into diverse part by diverse wind blow and storm and they be two the less not far from Carthage the great towards Syrenaica at this day all this sea-coast be call Barbary and be subject to the turkish Ottoman 3 have the name of the chief city Siren which of old have emulation for greatness with Carthage and therein be bear Aristippus the philosopher Calimachus the poet and Eratostines the mathematician and assome say Simon who carry the cross of Christ 4 marmarica be sandy and of old therein be the temple of jupiter call Hammon of the sand and these two province be annex to Egypt 5 Egypt be most fertile the very garner of the universal world and famous for the antiquity of the kingdom the upper part thereof be call Thebais the low towards the mediterranean sea be call of the letter delta the city thereof no less famous in these day then of old be these Alexandria build by Alexander the great at the mouth of the river Nilus who body there bury be see by Augustus and here Ptolemy be bear who do gather in this city the famous library of seven hundred thousand volume which be all consume by fire the next chief city be Canopus where stand the temple of Syrapis or Osiris then Pelusium at this day call Damiata seat upon the mouth of Nilus call Pelusium last the chief city of all be Babylon build by the babylonian permit to dwell there which at this day be huge increase and be call Alcaiero that be this caiero from whence some forty distant towards the north lie the three famous pyramid three day journey towards the east in a garden call Matarta be well fortify of old grow and still grow the herb balsamum sweat balsam out of the bough and be cut with a knife yield the more precious opobalsamum and at this day the same be find even at Caiero in the garden of the rich sort they say also that coral be find in the red Sea I have almost omit the city Arsinoe also call the city of the crocodile because the crocodile be there worship Nilus fall into the mediterranean sea in seven great arm which have the name of the adjacent town namely Heracleoticum or and Pelusiacum the first and the last whereof be one hundred and seventy mile distant one from the other the Nilus do yearly overflow and thereby give incredible fertility to the ground and the snow melt upon the mountain of Luna or the constellation of the moon and Mercury be think to be cause of this overflow and the same happen to be great or less than usual or come late or soon than usual be a sign of dearth to they whereof Pliny say that Egypt in twelve cubit height of the flood feel famine atthirteene cubit be hungry but that fourteen make they merry fifteen safe and sixteen bring plenty and dainty it be strange that all other river eat and consume their bank Nilus rather increase they by bring with it a mud that cover the sand and do as it be dung the field to make they more fertile in sixty day after the flood the field be clear of water the flood increase from the summer solstice to the sun enter into Libra and after the water retire into his own bed about the twelve of October they sow their field and in May follow reap their harvest Egypt with the province belong to it have long be subdue by the turk 6 Lybia have diverse province Biledurgeret that be the region of date be inhabit by the black Getuli from thence towards the river Niger lie the desert of Lybia waste and full of lion pard and other fierce and venomous beast whereof come the fiction of Medusa and pursue the inhabitant of Atsanaga be of a colour between tawny and black at the promontory call the white cape be the city call argen where the arabian and portugal trade together at the promontory call the green cape the river Niger fall into the atlantic ocean and the inhabitant be call nigrite this tract contain many kingdom namely Senige Gambrey Tambot Guangara where the garamantes dwell of old two kingdom of Nubia and other kingdom which I omit as subject to their king or to Pretz jan and so not belong to our purpose 7 AEthiopia be divide by Nilus into inward and outward inwad AEthiopia be divide by old writer into AEthiopia proper so call and Barbaria and in the mid thereof be the island Meroe make by Nilus in which be a city call Meroe the seat of the old king after call Saba whence be the queen which come to Solomon and the eunuch of queen Candaces who Philip baptize the troglodyte live in cave of the earth and their kingdom be at this day call adel. Barbaria extend eight degree beyond the from the promontory call Capo (@) to the gulf of Barbary and be so call of old the outward AEthiopia be call AEgisimba by Ptolemy and contain the kingdom of Amatzen and of Vangue seat under the equinoctial line all AEthiopia and part of Libya be say to be subject to Pretz jan therefore I say no more of they nor of the kingdom under the mountain of Luna as pertain not to my purpose 8 only of the many province under the mountain of Luna beyond the equinoctial line I will add that the inhabitant of Capo the cape of good hope be exceed black and nothing different from the aethiopian and lybian though they have a great latitude by thirty degree towards the south equal to the latitude of the far part of Spain and live under the temperate zone 9 the great island of Africa call Madagascar by the inhabitant and saint Laurence by the spaniard be of the mahometan religion and be say to abound with the medicinal wood Santalum with amber and elephant the canary land call of old the fortunate land be six or more as some write in number whereof canaria the great give the name to the rest which be subject to the king of Spain as be likewise the hesperide little land seat over against the green cape the turkish emperor have to my knowledge no other i will of Africa under he the turkish empire be so vast and contain great part of Europe Asia and Africa the temper of the air can not be otherwise describe then by particular part the situation thereof but out of the description of this empire in the journal of the first part and by compare the particular province with other of the same longitude and latitude and by the fruit and export commodity here to be mention the temper of the air may be know or at least conjecture more easy to this purpose I will only add that I land in Palestine about the end of May find their wheat harvest almost in and in the haven of joppa buy about a thousand apricot for six asper and the year follow when I sail from Constantinople towards Italy that about the midst of march I do eat pease and other pulse in the greek land last in Palestine Cyprus and those part partly I understand by other partly I find by experience that it seldom reins and that about September and October only and not often at that time but so violent for the time as if it will beat down the very house fall as it be by palefull at once and that the field be water with night dew at the fall whereof no man stir out of door but with his head well cover for danger of sickness all man use to keep in the house till the dew be dry while in the mean time by day the heat be so excessive as a man can hardly endure his apparel though it be of linen or silk if it hang not loose but be close about he the fertility of the soil general through this empire be exceed great and the goodness and variety of the fruit equal and in some place pass Italy the fortility the wine of Greece of mount Libanus and especial of Palormo in Anatolia be exceed rich and good yet have the turk less plenty of all thing than Europe for they very spare and only to serve necessity either set plant or sow great part of the people be waste with war and they that remain have not free fruition of their own good in the great tyranny under which they live aswell of the emporour as of undergovernour change at least once a year and the general rapacity and licentiousness of the soldier hence it be that there be vast solitude and untilled desert on all side where yet the ground of itself bring forth diverse wild fruit without tillage they have diverse kind of grain wheat the grain call Milet barley oat Rye pease and all kind of pulse which for the kind be like those of Europe but the wheat for the bigness of the grain and so the rest be to be prefer before they there be great abundance of rice flax and cotton grow in the field they have good plenty of all kind of cattle yet be no more industrious in graze and feed herd then in sow or plant and so they have egg hen Rice hony which in a composition they drink fruit and bread for daily food they desire no other dainty or great riches since they can neither enjoy their good while they live nor yet bequeath they at death and nothing be more dangerous then to be account rich the or greek monk in Candia with who I abide for a time show i sield which the year past have yield they ninety five measure of grain for one sow but Candia though it lie in the compass of the turkish empire almost on all side yet be subject to the state of Venice the island Chios vulgar Zio be suby to the turk and be famous for the pleasantness as also for the fertility yield Mastic the fruit of the tree and have abundance of partridge and of all kind of foul I have in my jornal of the first part speak of the most fertile land Cyprus and Mettilene in Syria they have sheep of strange bigness whereof many have tail weigh twenty and some thirty pound bear wool and be wreathe to their heel more than the horn of ram be and let no man think this incredible since the same be report of sheep in Africa and this be confirm by consent of all who have be in these part mule be somewhat rare but they have innumerable camel a beast most apt to carry burden and lie patient down to receive they and most able to bear hunger and especial thirst when the male and female engender they lie down on their belly with tail to tail and their head many eile distant one from the other and in the time of the year when they be natural prone to generation they be fierce with a kind of madness so as their master then take heed of any violence they may do they the turk also have many dromedaty a kind of beast not unlike the camel but far pass horse in swiftness and very camel in patience of labour their horse be rather fair then strong and they make their skin shine by lay they upon their own dung dry these horse either run which often they put they to for spurt and in bravery or go a foot pace as they use to follow lade camel in journey but they be not teach either to trot or amble as we be and be good for short iournic but not able to endure so long journey as we do therefore the turkish cavaltery for war be of more swiftness than strength and the german horse be heavy they easy overtake they fly and as easy fly when they be beat the turk have great plenty of sea and fresh water fish and of bird and all foul and for christian buyer whereof be great multitude especial at Constantinople they furnish their market therewith and in truth at Constantinople there be as great variety and goodness of these kind as can be wish only the oystor though pleatifull yet have not the delicate salt taste that we have the mediterranean sea be nothing so salt as the ocean but in general the turk by reason of the foresay tyranny and of their temperance in diet do little use fish or fowl or any like exercise yea by reason of the same tyranny of the emperor governor and soldier the traffic the turk careless and cold exercise traffic with merchant I grant that they trade in Anatolia and other part of their own empire after a cold manner but they make no voyage by sea into foreign part except some few that come to Venice for they do not labour in any kind more than necessity force and be so far from the insatiable desire of riches as they avoid nothing more than the opinion to be rich so as the jew the greek subject to the turk and other confederate christian export their commodity they themselves have very few ship the emperor only have some twelve great ship well arm to bring he necessary from Egypt to Constantinople in like sort they have few marrine and those unexperienced and fearful use the greek their vassal and other slave take in ward to that purpose and they much esteem that be gentle treat captive skilful in navigation some town keep at their private charge a few small galley and bark to rob the christian and the great Turk navy consist all of galley nothing comparable to those of Venice and they winter at Constantinople and another haurn in Greece whereof I shall write more large in the discourse of the turk commonwealth among other city of trade they have two very famous one in Asia the other in Africa that of Asia be call Haleppo and it be within-land the port thereof be call by the turk and Alexandretta by the christian whence the commodity of merchant be carry upon camel and the five day arrive at Haleppo whether the commodity of Persia be bring by the river Euphrates and upon camel back from the city Taurus of old subject to the persian but in our age subdue by the turk the indian commodity be bring thither by the red sea and the gulf of Arabia the famous city for trade in Africa be call Babylon and now be name Alcatero whence the commodity of India Egypt and all Africa be export moreover upon the mouth of the great arm of the river Nilus the city Alexandria be seat upon the sea some few day sail from Aleaiero the venetian bring into Turkey woollen clothes which they call broad be die scarlet Violet and of all colour and they be so strong and well make as they will last very long so as the turk prefer they before out english clothes and because the venetian furnish they in great quantity they use few other clothes of that kind also the venetian bring to they satin and damask make in Italy of dalmatian silk and great quantity of gold and silver to buy the precious commoditte of Turkey whence they carry out raw silk for by reason of the foresay tyranny as the turk be negligent in husbandry and trade so be they in manual art not draw their silk into thread nor weave the same into clothes and howsoever they have infinite number of silkworm especial at Tripoli and in most part of Asia which make great quantity of silk as I former say in the discourse of Italy yet they sell this silk raw and and buy of the venetian the foresay clothes make of their own silk so as the silkworm may well be say to be more diligent and more to promote the public good than the inhabitant for they swarm in all garden diligent finish their web while the idle inhabitant yield the commodity thereof to stranger the venetian also export from Turkey spice and apothecary ware and great quantity of the dye call indigo they export gall Cotten wool cotton thread chamlet or grogram make of the fine hair of goat not shear but pull off from their back and weave in Galatia a province of the lesser Asia they export Turkey carpet goat skin wrought and die into diverse colour the english bring to the turk kersey wrought and die of diverse colour and kind but they bring little broadcloth wherewith they be abundant furnish from Venice they also bring to they tin and black conni-skinne in such quantitic as the turk admire the same a Frenchman merry tax our woman affability say that in England there be such plenty of Connyes and they so tame as they be take in the tavern the english export from they spice and apothecary ware for the trade into the east indies be not then set up they also export the foresay commodity raw silk indigo and other precious die of Scarlet purple and the like gall Mastic grow only in the island Zio Cotton and the thread thereof Turkey carpet for table chamlet grogram of goat hair the merchant come to Constantinople hardly find there any commodity to export therefore the english ship have unlade there sail empty to Alexandretta and there receive the commodity of Haleppo again the italian who bring much gold and silver to Haleppo for the commodity there to be sell do again receive gold and silver for such commodity as they bring to Constantinople and carry the same back to Venice the english lie at the land of Zant and Cephalonia subject to the venetian and at Petrasso seat in the gulf of Corinth and subject to the great Turk export corrand other from Algiers a port of Barbary export sugar other from the island Candia subject to the venetian export muskadines and other from diverse land export earthen dish and vessel paint which for the pureness be much esteem and use in Italy and in our part northward the sword of Damascus be famous for the mettle pierce iron and cut a nail in piece but the exportation of they be forbid though out christian supply the turk with all warlike munition which they may shame to have particular name in this discourse of traffic the precious oriental commodity of Persia and the east indies have make the trade of turkish city to be famous namely their spice and rich die and jewel which notwithstanding the turk have in part of their own for I former say that Arabia yield frankincense myrrh cinnamon and jewel and Egypt yield balsam and opobalsam the more precious gum of the balmetree in great quantity omit many commodity which beside they have of these kind I speak not of thessalonica a city of Macedonia now call Saloniche nor other haven and city of traffic in Greece as be of less moment all the precious traffic of Turkey by reason of the inhabitant slothfulness be in the hand of Jew and of christian and be long in the sole hand of the venetian but the french in the age past and the english in our age have have as I may say a traffickiug league with the turk and so partake that trade and these three state only not to speak of the german who at this time have war with the turk and never sail so far to exercise traffic among so many state of christian have their ambassador at the turkish Court and if any other christian arrive in that empire as the fleming often do they use at this time to come under the banner of one of these three nation the reader must understand that when I be in Turkey the english and Fleming have not as yet begin their traffic in the east indies which be like to destroy the traffic in Turkey bring many rich commodity from the well head for their diet the turk live spare I have say slovenly but that I remember their diet their frequent bathe and wash and the curious cleanliness of the linen and all other clothes which they wear but I will be bold to say they feed negligent and without any pomp or magnificence the rich sort do fit at meat like tailor with their knee bend upon carpet or upon the grass when they eat by rivers side and in garden as they do more frequent then in the house and their table be so low as they may well reach to it sit upon the ground about this table they cast a long towel to wipe their hand but passenger by the highway and general the ordinary sort of turk use grass in stead of this towel other carry about a table of leather colour red or yellow which table shut and open like a purse and upon it they can set but one dish at once it hang hollow upon certain buckel common they eat by the highway upon the ground and always with their knee bend like our tailor they see their meat till it be very tender so as they may break it with their finger for they have no knife neither have they variety of dish set before they but all sit in a circle fall upon one dish take meat they all together say a short prayer or grace and talk not while they eat but silent fall hard to their work they have abundance of all thing for food aswell of flesh except swines-flesh as of bird and other meat but they abstain from fish they have plenty of corn at least sufficient for their temperate diet which be exceed good and far big than we they be ignorant of the art of bird foul hunt or cookery and have no lascivicus appetite provoke they to gluttony be content with simple meat their sobriety in this kind can sufficient be commend and since their great man can be content to feed on rice and drink water it be no marvel that with ease they keep great army in the field all the turkish householdstuff be contain in one poor pot to seethe meat in one spoon of wood one cup of leather or wood to drink in a poor bed or yea often a single coverl alone and the earth serve they for bedstead table and stool they have no need of a troop of cook and scullion to dress meat and make clean dish they willing eat curd turn sour and mingle with bread and water common call mishmish and fresh cheese or curd and have plenty of milk aswell of cow as of goat in stead of bread they eat unleavened cake bake on the cinder which common be mingle with a kind of seed they feed common on hen and rice either sod alone or with a hen or mutton in a vessel full of hole without any liquor put in when it be set on the fire so as there be no other juice but that of the meat the rice be make very thick within these narrow bound be their most costly feed restrain in time of the year they feed much upon fruit and keep grape all winter so as you will judge they fresh they abhor from swine flesh as the jew do for the rest I do never see nor hear by relation of other that the rich of they do affect any other variety of meat than I have name and I have often see man of the better sort eat out of the seethe pot without any dish set before they the above name flesh of mutton be very savoury and the sheep of Syria and the adjoin part of Asia be of such greatness as many time a tail of they hang to their heel and very woolly and fat and close weave in many plight do weigh thirty or more pound they have also Venyson for in the wood there be many wild goat disperse and I have see a kind of fallow dear in Syria call Gazelle of which kind I have see some bring out of Barbary into England and they much delight in fruit have excellent of many kind and in great quantity namely apricot and musk Melones and diverse kind of pumpion whereof one call Angouria as big as our pumpion be exceed full of a very cold juice be most pleasant for the coolness in any great heat which coolness though I take to be unwholesome for one sick of a ague yet myself almost waste with the burn of that disease do vehement desire to eat of this fruit and find it nothing hurtful or rather healthful to i in the haven of Alexandretta or Scanderona a grecian the master of a venetian ship give i a present of four or five apple which he call as they vulgar do the apple of Adam and I never in my life taste so delicete a-fruite it be of form like a long pear or rather for the crookedness like a cucumber of the lesser sort and it have a most thin skin of colour like a peeche skin the least part whereof be open the juice be easy to be suck out which be very pleasant and not much unlike to the juice of a fig new pull from the tree if I shall particularize all the kind of pleasant fruit I may be infinite therein the turk when they have eat not while they eat go like good fellow together and like horse at once drink for that meal as greedy as if the water be turn into wine which kind of drink those that be zealous of their law and those that journey by the high way more special and all turk in general most common use for which cause those that journey use to pitch their tent upon the bank of pure fountain or run water which they no less know or as curious search out as we do the best inn or tavern beside common they have a cup if I may so call it be a purse of leather that open or shut with string hang at their horse saddle pommel which as they sit on horseback they put down into the fountain and draw water to drink not omit to taste a good spring of water no more than we will a piece of rare wine their water especial in province lie near the sun be in this property contrary to we that it loosen the body no less than the rice bind it in city diverse kind of drink be to be sell some esteem as much as wine with we one kind I remember present unto we in Palestine by the sobasha of ramma which be make of medicinal herb to purify and cool the blood and they drink it hot so as it seem a very physical potion they drink sugar or honey mingle with water and water sod with grape rose-water and honey and they have whole ton of the juice of citron and lemon which they willing drink and all these kind be to be sell in their ctty wine be forbideen by Mahomet law which permit aquavite vulgar call Harech which aquavite they often drink even to drunkenness and whether it be out of the common error of mankind to desire forbid thing or out of the licentiousness of soldier which every day grow great than other howsoever in idleness they obey their law in not plant vine yet not only the janissary but even the religious man will drink wine large even to drunkenness with christian as well ambassador as other yea if christian passenger carry wine by the way for their own drink and have a janisary to protect they yet they will familiar come to drink with they and if they have no protector they will take their wine and whatsoever they have else at their pleasure so as their false prophet have only provoke vice by forbid it many province yield rich wine the chief whereof be the greek wine which notwithstanding seem to i for the most part to be corrasive fret the stomach and entrail and as well the white as red wine of mount Lybanus and antilibanus which be carry to Tripoli and as far as Haleppo the wine about jerusalem be sharp and small but the best wine of all be the white wine of palorme in Anatolia which be like the spanish sack but more pleasant to the taste be not so sweet as the canary wine nor so harsh and strong as the sherry sack this wine be carry to Constantinople where also good wine grow plant by christian there but this be most esteem for only christian plant vine and make wine howsoever the turk be content to take part of they at the christian charge the turkish soldier be to fight if they can find no wine drink the juice of black poppy call opium to raise their spirit to a kind of fury think they selnes make more valiant thereby for howsoever we think this herb especial take large to be dangerous for the health yet there be not a Turk from the high to the low who do not as it be daily use it nothing be more frequent sow nothing more plentiful grow especial in Anatolia nothing more easy find a buyer yea if their camel and dromidary fail by the way or upon necessity must go further than they use to journey as sometime it fall out in army and other journey than they give they this herb by which they report their spirit so to be stir up as they will go till they fall down dead in this vast empire I do see no june no not in their city and a man shall rare find any bed among christian and if he do yet the sheet be make of cotton intolerable for heat for in Turkey general they lie upon tapestry carpet and sometime in city upon a mattress with a quilt to cover they and by the high way they lie upon straw hay or grass and in all place near Palestine they either by night lie upon the house top on a plaster floor or in yard upon the earth and in open air have the spangle heaven for their canopy and not only passenger but all turk daily wear linen breech so as in these province not subject to cold a man may better endure this poor kind of lodging but the turkish passenger in stead of inn have certsine hospital build of stone with cloister after the manner of monastery where by charitable legacy of alm all passenger may have meat for certain meal or day especial the pilgrim towards Mecha for who sake they be especial found and these house be vulgar call kawne or as other pronounce Cain and the cover cloister of they build after their manner but one roof high be common as well to turk as any other passenger to lodge in open and like good fellow altogether upon such mattress as they carry or upon the bare ground if straw be not to be have for christian passenger carry such mattress and necessary victual which fail they supply they in city and every day in village may buy fresh meat but they must dress their own meat neither be the art of cookery great in Turkey then with we in Wales for toasting of cheese in Wales and seethe of rice in Turkey will enable a man free to profess the art of cookery no stranger use to travel without a janisary or some other to guide he who know the place where most commodious lodging be to be have but passenger by the way use not to go into city but only to buy fresh meat which do they return to the tent of their Carravan which use to be pitch in some field adjoin in hot clime near the sun as I have say in the first part write my journey through Turkey the turk there dwell use to begin their journey towards the evening and to end they two or three hour after the sun rise rest in their tent all the heat of the day Christian passenger shall do well to go to the italian friar at jerusalem and to merchant their countryman or at least to christian in city of traffic and to the ambassador or merchant of their own country at Constantinople who be themselves stranger and not ignorant of the evil incident to stranger will no doubt in courtesy direct they to get convenient lodging and other necessary chap. 2 of France touch the particular subject of the first chapter the longitude of France extend thirteen degree from the meridian of sixteen degree to that of twenty nine degree and the latitude extend eight degree from the parallel of forty two degree to that of fifty degree France of old be divide into cisalpina and transalpina in the description of Italy I have former speak of cisalpina which be also call of gown the inhabitant wear and because they have short hair 1 transalpina be subdivide into and so call of their long hair be again subdivide into belgica of which I have speak former (@) (@) in the description of netherlands into and Celtica or lugdunensis the second part of be of old call aremorica lie upon the mountain and they differ in language from the french be more like to tie spaniard next to the Pyreni dwell the or call vulgar guascon come from Spain their chief city be Tolouse where be a famous university and the parliament of that province another city call Bourdeaux have also a university but be more famous by the general concourse of merchant trade for french wine beyond the river garumna run through the midst of Aquitania dwell the santones a ancient people who country be call Santoigne next lie the pictones or pictavi upon the river Loyer who country be call Posctou abound with fish fowl and all game for hunt and hawk it have three chief city all seat of bishop Poitiers Lusson and Maillezais the neck of land adjoin be call aulone and the land and nosire dame de Bovin etc. yield great quantity of salt to be transport the country of the Bituriger be call Berry and the chief city burges of old call Auaricum be a university and the citizen at six fair in the year sell great quantity of woollen cloth for the country have rich pasture feed many flock of sheep of who wool this cloth be make beside that it abound also with wine corn and all kind of cattle the city be within land and be call in latin Biturigum of two tower next the same lie the dukedom Burbonois and other small territory Celtica or lugdunensis another part of contain the part of transalpina that lie between the river Loyer and seyne beyond which last river France of old extend and include good part of netherlands first towards the west lie the dukedom Bretaigne which have three language in itself all differ from the French the first be of the people call breton Bretonnant come from the english or cornish briton the first inhabitant and the chief city be saint Paul and treguier the second people be call breton galot be of language near the french and the chief city be Rhein where be the parliament of the whole dukedom and Dol and saint Malo the three be mix of the two former and the city thereof call Nantes be the duke seat and chief city of the dukedom from the sea coast thereof great quantity of salt make by the heat of the sun be transport and there by mine of iron and lead towards the east lie Normandy so call of man of the north namely the cimbri there inhabit and the chief city be Roan within land lie turroyne upon the Loyer and the chief city be Orleans next lie the little country of France like a island between two river so call of the francke a people of Germany conquer and give that name to the whole kingdom the chief city and seat of the king be Paris Picardy lie towards the north and the chief city be Amiens upon France within land towards the east lie the province champagne next to it lie the dukedom of Lorraine the duke whereof bear their arm a arm arm break out of cloud and hold a naked sword to signify that the duke have supreme power from God alone and the chief city of the dukedom be Nancy the seat of the duke and toul and Neufchastell the next country of old esteem part of Lorraine be inhabit by the lingones and by the mediomatrice and the chief city by the latin call Mediomatricum and metis be now vulgar call metz which city the king of France take in the year 1551 from the empire in the time of the emperor Charles the five who besiege the same long but in vain the king of France still hold it the dukedom of Burgundy belong of old to the empire but be now subject to the king of France the chief city whereof be Dijon where the parliament of the whole dukedom be hold it have other city namely beaulue Challon Chastillon Noyres and a place call bourgougne which give the name to the dukedom yet other write that it have the name of Bourges that be town the county of Burgundy belong of old to the empire but be now subject to the king of Spain who progenitor marry the daughter and heir of the duke of Burgundy at which time the king of France take the foresay dukedom from the say daughter and heir and this county be vulgar call franche Conte as free from tribute it have two free city dole a university and Besancon 2 the second part of transalpina Gallia be Narbonensis which only at this day yet not all may true be call Gallia it be of old call braccata of the inhabitant apparel and be call Narbonensis of the chief city Narbona lie upon the river athesis near the mediterranean sea which Strabo witness to have be of old a famous city for traffic the river Rhodanus run through it which fall from the Alps and increase by Araxis but still retain the first name fall into the mediterranean Sea this part call Narbonensis by the benefit of the air and sun yield fig grape citron peach pomegranate chessenut rich wine and all delicate fruit and all the field be make odoriferous by wild rosemary myrtel palmetree and many sweet herb and the inhabitant have late plant cane of sugar to conclude the province be very pleasant and plentiful in all thing on the west side of Rhodanus the tectosages dwell of old in the province call Languadoc have that name because the inhabitant use oc for the french the chief city thereof be Narbona aforesaid Mompeliers of old a famous university and Clermont the dukedom of Savoy lie in a corner from the alp to the mediterranean sea of old inhabit by the and it lie on the same side of the Alps with France be reckon a part thereof but the duke thereof be a absolute prince and the chief city be chambery the province be very fertile and where it be more barren yet afford excellent fruit and all thing for food at a convenient price dolphiny lie between the river Rhodanus and the dukedom of Savoy and give the name of dolphin to the french king elder son Provence be a most sweet territory and have the city Marseile famous by trade with the turk Arles and Auignon subject to the pope for when many pope be at one time John the two and twenty do long sit in this city give by joane queen of Naples to the pope in the time of Clement the six alienate from the kingdom of Naples by she and annex to the patrimony of saint Peter in the year 1360. the principality of Orange be a absolute dominion have the chief city of the same name and seat between Languedoc dolphiny and the pope territory of Auignon the air of the northern part of France be pure than that of England and be not cover with cloud draw out of the sea as England be for that cause in winter become the situation more cold and in summer more hot and far less annoy with mist and rainy weather but on the other side more and less according to the clime the part of France lie towards the mountain Pirenei and near to the equinoctial line be subject to intemperate heat yet often allay by the wind blow from the sea and by the shadow of the mountain this southerly part yield all the fruit of Italy and in the northerly part as in Normandy they have abundance of apple and pear tree of which they make great quantity of cider and Perry and this part as towards the sea it yield also plenty of corn so within land it afford the like of wine and in the very northerly island call France they have plenty of grape upon pleasant hill water with sweet river but the wine make of they be small and sharp all France be most pleasant and not only about Narbona but in many other territory according to the commodity of the clime it yield great plenty of red and white wine export in great quantity which be hold excellent to be drink the white in the moan and the red with meat which red be otherwise repute unwholesome as provoke and cause rheum France abound with all thing necessary for food as well corn as cattle red dear foul and also with all kind of fish by reason it be partly compass with the sea and upon all side be water with sweet river for fire they use wood and coal yet have they no pit coal or sea coal but have their sea eoale out of England for their smith forge and where they have less store of wood within land there they burn straw fur and other kind of stubble they have good race of horse which the great part use in the war who be not able to buy neapolitan courser spanish lanet or english courser breed of the neapolitan horse and english mare but for their journey they have no geldings or amble nag as we have but common use trot and stone nag the gentleman do not meddle with traffic either because it be of old forbid the fertility and traffic to great lord and gentleman lest the king imposition shall thereby suffer damage they be by singular privilege exempt and free from all such burden or because in deed they think such traffic ignoble and base and so unfit for they which error the french no less dear buy then the english as I have show in the discourse of Italy and shall again prove in that of England in general the french be less studious of navigation or industrious in that kind because they abound almost with all thing for plentiful food and rich attire and if they want any thing stranger glad bring it to they and exchange it for their wine salt and course linen clothes neither have I hear or read that they ever do any brave exploit by sea they have in time of war some few man of war for piracy and some few ship to export their commodity but they sail only to neighbour country as out of Normandy and Bretaigne into England Ireland and the low-countries and only those of marseile to Tripoli in Syria as for the colony which in our age they have lead into the west indies their unhappy success therein have discourage they from like new attempt and whosoever see their rich city within land witness that their wealth consist in native commodity more than trade by sea may easy guess that they be not much addict to navigation the french have many commodity by which they draw foreign coin to they but four especial wine salt linen course cloth and corn which in that respect some call the loadestone of France neither be it a matter of small moment that they have many riner give commodity to the mutual traffic of their city they have plenty of flax and hemp whereof they make canvas sail rope and cable neither want they wool whereof they make cloth little inferior to the english cloth but not in quantity to be export Bourdeaux be a famous city for exportation of wine as Rochel and the neighbour port be no less for Salt France yield saffron and woad for die which they call Du Pastell and many small commodity to be export as card pin paper and the like yea they export into Spain linen clothes make thin with wear and sell they there for a good price the spaniard bring into France some quantity of wool raisin olive oil citron and other fruit whereof France need no great quantity and Cochenillo for die the fortingal bring into France holy thistle a herb like a white thorn have leaf like cotton on they and sugar and diverse kind of indian wood as (@) Schomache Fustocke and logwood and a small quantity of date and these carry out of France great quantity of linen cloth which we call white roane and great quantity of vittree canvas and paper some woollen cloth much corn especial wheat good quantity of wax and card and the like commodity the english bring into France great quantity of woollen-cloath call kersey and cotton lead tin english vitriol or shoemaker black sheep skin and by stealth other hide forbid to be export great quantity of hearring and new find land fish dry of wool though forbid to be export oil soap tun soap ash old wear cloak and I know not to what use very old shoe with other native and foreign commodity and they bring from thence linen clothes call white roane and Vitree canvas paper white and red wine in great quantity thread saffron wax and from Paris gold and silver the hollander bring into France two or three kind of their linen clothes copper feather and wier and they carry thence the foresay linen clothes wine prune paper and the above name commodity the french carry into Italy tin lead dry fish call poor John bring to they by the english and their own above name commodity and they bring out of Italy silk clothes and other italian commodity among the french only those of Marseile traffic with the turk and their great trade be only at Tripoli in Syria who carry into Turkey spanish silver and french linen clothes and bring from thence raw silk spice gall cotton and indigo for die old writer relate that the gall use to lie on the ground to feed on milk and their Diet. swine flesh and to be give to gluttony at this day none eat less bacon or dry flesh for ordinary diet than the french yet I can commend their temperance since all as well man as woman beside dinner and supper use breakfast and bever which they call collation and gouster so eat four time in the day all France abound with necessary for food as well all kind of cattle as fruit not inferior in some place to those of Italy and wild boar and red dear for they have no fallow deer and bird and fowl and all kind of fish afford by the sea and their many pleasant river but their beef be neither very good nor much use their sheep be less than we in England but the flesh of they be sweet and savoury in the inn they have great plenty of partridge and diverse kind of bird because the country people neither do nor may eat they and the gentleman be general spare in their ordinary diet so as great plenty of these dainty be bring to the chief inn howsoever England be happy in all abundance and have some dainty for food proper to itself as God wit and some other kind of sea fowl and especial fallow dear and brawn though it pass France general in plenty of sea fowl and as well the variety as plenty of sea fish yet have it not such abundance as France have of land fowl or such as haunt the wood and field as partridge feasant woodcock and the like or at least by reason of the common sort not feed thereon and the say spare ordinary diet of the gentleman France seem much more to abound with they be common in all the chief inn I speak of England in general for in some place they so abound with we as they bear little or no price the french be commend and say to excel other in boil meat sauce and make dish vulgar call but in my opinion the lard of their meat be not commendable whereby they take away all variety of taste make all meat savour of pork and the french alone delight in mortify meat they use not much whitmeate nor have I taste there any good butter which our ambassador cause to be bring unto they out of England and they have only one good kind of cheese call Angelot please more for a kind of sharpness in taste then for the goodness as well the gentleman as citizen live more spare then the english in their ordinary private diet and have not their table so furnish with variety and number of dish they dine most with sodden and liquid meat and sup with roast meat each have his several sauce but their feast be more sumptuous than we and consist for the most part of make fantastical meat and salad and sumptuous composition rather than of flesh or bird and the cook be most esteem who have best invention in new make and compound meat and as in all thing the french be cheerful and nimble so the italian observe that they eat or swallow their meat swift and add that they be also slovenly at meat but I will rather say they be negligent or careless and little curious in their feed and to this purpose I remember a accident that happen to a Frenchman eat with we at the master table in a venetian ship govern by greek and sail from Venice to Ilierusalem who turn her foul trencher to lay meat on the clean side do so offend the master and all the mariner as well the best as common sort as they hardly refrain from offer he violence for mariner in general but especial the greek be so superstitious as they take this his negligence in turn his trencher be of like opinion for the turn of any thing in the ship upside down as if it have be a ominous sign that the ship shall be cast away in a village of Normandy half way between Roan and deep call Totes and in like sort in all the inn of those part before the civil war assoon as passenger light from their horse the host give they water to wash and bread and wine for the french have not the patience to expect their supper without some refection then at supper the table be serve with mutton a capon or pullet partridge and like meat with a kind of banquet as in summer apple cherry and grape and in winter Chessenuts Rice raisin and stew prune then they give their guest clean sheet dry they at the fire in their presence and in the morning give they for breakfast some butter toste or motsell of meat and for all this together with horsemeate each man pay some twenty two or twenty five sou as likewise the bate at noon for horse and man cost each some ten sou after the civil war I pass through these part and common each meal pay twelve or fifteen sou with worse interertainment and for breakfast pay several but no great rate towards the confine of Flanders the host only cover the table and a side table upon which every passenger have his glass for the french be curious not to drink in another man cup and the host be only to be pay for this service otherwise at time of eat they call the cook dwell near the inn who bring the best meat they have and when the guest have choose their meat and agree for the price they carry it back to dress it and so send it warm with sauce in general through the city of France passenger seldom dine at their inn but with some companion go to the tavern or cook shop but at night they must eat with the host that give they bed where they shall have clean sheet and see they dry before their face but they be of course cloth and very few chamber be private but most have three or four bed wherein they lie not single but for the most part with bedfellow also the guest as well merchant and gentleman as those of common sort eat at a ordinary table and for supper common large with diverse roast meat each man pay some fifteen sou he that hier a chamber in city which he may have well furnish at Paris for some two crown a month he must buy his meat at cook shop which be frequent and very clean neither be it any disgrace as with we to buy a morsel of meat there and to agree for the price before it be eat and they that high chamber can have no better conveniency for diet either at Paris or in other city but he that stay long in a city may agree in a citizen house or a inn for his diet and lodging by the year which he may have at Paris in extraordinary sort for some one hundred fifty crown yearly and ordinary for less and at Rone for one hundred twenty or one hundred crown and in many city for eighty crown and in many good inn for sixty crown yearly drunkenness be reproachful among the french and the great part drink water mingle with wine and always french wine not sack or spanish wine which be sell as physic only by apothecary or other foreign wine whereof I remember not to have see any in the northern part of France yet mariner soldier and many of the common sort use to drink Perry and cider to very drunkenness yea I have see many drink wine with like intemperance and when these kind of man sit at drink they use much mirth and sing in which be they take great delight as the french in general be by nature cheerful and lively woman for the most part and virgin always except by stealth they offend against the custom use to drink water except it be in the province yield Perry and cider which all sort use to drink without exception and at Paris I remember to have see a poor woman to beg a cup of water which be give she she drink it off and go away merry as if she have receive a good alm chap. 3 of England touch the particular subject of the first chapter the longitude of England extend nine degree and a half from the meridian of thirteen degree and a half England to that of twenty three degree and the latitude extend fix degree from the parallel of fifty degree and a half to that of fifty six degree and a half learned Camden who I glad follow in this description of England make the circuit of all Brittany to be one thousand eight hundred thirty six mile this be the most famous island of all the world and be divide into two kingdom that of England and that of Scotland England be subevide into diverse county or shire and land 1 in the description whereof I will first begin with cornmall of old inhabit by the it be for the most part a mountainous country but the soil be not unfirtile beside that the people incredible fatten the same with lay upon it the owe of the sea call Orwood and a certain mud the sea coast as Camden write who I follow be beautify with very many town which have much ship the inward part abound with a rich vain of metal where wonderful quantity of most pure tin be dig up and not only tin but gold and silver with it and diamond form into angle by nature itself which we call cornish diamond Eringo grow plentiful all along the sea side and with great labour of the husbandman they have such abundance of corn as great quantity of wheat be yearly export thence into Spain also the inhabitant make great gain by the fish of pilchard which they salt and dry in the smoke and export a huge multitude of they yearly into Spain and Italy here be the famous mount Michael of old call Dinsol and by the inhabitant the rock Cana. this rock be somewhat high and craggy upon the top whereof be a chapel dedicate to Michael the archangel the town Falemouth have a fair haven capable of very many ship and most safe from storm where the rock do fortify two castle build by Henry the eight and this haven be by Ptolemy call ostium 2 Devonshire likewise inhabit by the have fair haven be no less rich in the vein of tin and beautify with frequent town in no part of England the ground require more expense for in many place it be barren till it be fat with the owe or sand of the sea which make it wonderful fruitful but in the remote part from the sea this sand be dear buy the river Plimus give the name to the town Plymouth of old call Sutton which grow from a fisher village to a fair town by the commodity of the haven be most safe even for great ship as well in the say river as in another call Tamera not far from thence be the place where they fable that Coryneus wrestle with Gogmagog and in this town be bear sir Francis Drake knight the chief glory of our age for navigation who for two year space do with continual victory as it be besiege the gulf of Mexico and in the year 15 enter the straight of Magellan compass the world in two year and ten month with many change and hazard of fortune the town Dortmouth be much frequent with merchant and strong ship for the commodity of the haven fortify with two castle the city Excester call isen by Ptolemy and of old call Monketon of the monk be the chief city of the county and the seat of the bishop 3 Dorsetshire be of old inhabit by the durotriges the town weymouth have a castle build by Henry the eight to fortify the haven dorchester be the chief town of the county but neither great nor fair 4 Sommersetshire be of old inhabit by the netherlander and be a large and rich county happy in the fruitful soil rich pasture multitude of inhabitant and commodity of haven the chief town Bridgewater have the name of the bridge and the water in the island Auallon so call in the Britan tongue of the apple which the latin call Glasconia flourish the monastery Glastenbury of great antiquity derive from joseph of Arimathta Dunstan cast out the ancient monk bring thither the benedictine of a late institution and himself be the first abbot over a great multitude of monk endow with kingly revenue in the church yard of this monastery they say that the great worthy of the Britan prince Arthur have his sepulchre the episcopal little city call well of the well or fountain have a stately bishop palace the city bath be famous for the medicinal bath whereof three fountain spring in the very city which be wholesome for body numb with ill humour but be shut up certain hour of the day that no man shall enter they till by their sluice they be purge of all filth the bishop of welles buy this city of Henry the first remove his episcopal seat thither yet still keep the old name of bishop of welles and there build a new cathedral church the city Bristol be compass with a double wall and have so fair building as well public as private house as next to London and York it be prefer to all other city of England 5 Wiltshire be also inhabit by the belgae or netherlander and lie all within land rich in all part with pasture and corn malmesbury be a fair town famous for the woollen clothes the town Wilton of old the chief of this county be now a little village beautify with the stately palace of the earl of Penbroke the city of Salisbury be make pleasant with water run through the street and be beautify with a stately cathedral church and the college of the dean and prebend have rich inhabitant in so pleasant a seat yet no way more famous then by have John jewel a late worthy bishop bear there some six mile from Salisbury be a place in the field where huge stone be erect whereof some be eight and twenty foot high and seven broad stand in three row after the form of a crown upon which other stone be so lay across as it seem a work hang in the air whereupon it be call Stoneheng vulgar and be repute among miracle as place there by Merlin there be scarce any stone for ordinary build in the territory adjoin 6 Hamshire of old be inhabit within land by the belgae or netherlander and upon the sea coast by the William the norman conqueror make here a forest for dear destroy town and holy building for some thirty mile compass which ground now well inhabit yet serve for the same use we call new-forest Southampton a fair little city lie upon the Sea Wintchester of old call venta of the belgae be a famous city in the time of the roman and in these day it be well inhabit water with a pleasant brook and pleasant seat and have a old castle wherein there hang against the wall a table of a round form vulgar call prince Arthur round table but Gamden think it to have be make long after his time it have a cathedral church and large bishop palace and a famous college found for train up young scholar in learning whence many learned man have be first send to the university and so into the church and commonwealth in the town or port of Portsmouth lie a garrison of soldier to defend those part from the incursion of the french by Sea 7 Berkshire be of old inhabit by the Atrebatij Newberry a famous town enrich by woollen clothes have his beginning of the ancient town spina Windsor be famous by the king castle neither can a king seat be in a more pleasant situation which draw the king often to retire thither and Edward the three keep at one time John king of France and David king of Scotland captive in this castle the same Edward the three build here a stately church and dedicate it to the bless virgin Mary and to s. George the capadocian and first institute the order of knight call of the garter as a happy omen of victory in war happy succeed who wear under the left knee a watchet garter buckle have this mot in the french tongue grave in letter of gold hony and the ceremony of this order he institute to be keep in this church 8 the county of surry be of old inhabit by the otelands be beautify with the king very fair and pleasant house as richmond be with the king stately palace 9 the county of Sussex of old inhabit by the regni have the fair city Chichesler and the haven Rhie know by be the most frequent passage into France 10 the county of Kent be rich in meadow pasture and pleasant grove and wonderful abound with apple and cherry it have most frequent town and safe harbour for ship and some vein of iron William the norman conqueror after the manner of the roman institute a warden of the five port Hastings Dover rumney and Sandwiche to which Winchelsey and rye the chief haven and other town be join as member which have great privilege because they be tie to serve in the war and the warden of they be always one of the great lord who within his jurisdiction have in most thing the authority of admiral and other right Detford town be well know where the king ship be build and repair and there be a notable armoury or storehouse for the king navy not far from thence upon the shore lie the break rib of the ship in which sir Francis Drake sail round about the world reserve for a monument of that great action Greenwich be beautify with the king palace Eltham another house of the king be not far distant the town Gravesend be a know road the city Rochester be the seat of a bishop and have a stately cathedral church Canterbery be a very ancient city the seat of a archbishop who in the hierarchy of the roman bishop be style the pope legate but the pope authority be banish out of England it be decree in a synod hold the year 1534 that the archbishop lay aside that title shall be call the and metrapolitan of all England before the road of Margat lie the dangerous shelf or flat of sand whereof the great be call Goodwin sand Dover be a port of old very commodious but now less safe only it be more famous for the short cut to Calais in France the town rumney one of the five port in our grandfather time lie close upon the sea but now be almost two mile distant from the same 11 Glocestershire be of old inhabit by the dobuni William of malmesbury write that this county be so fertile in corn and fruit as in some place it yield a hundred measure of grain for one sow but Camden affirm this to be false the same writer affirm that the very high way be full of Appell tree not plant but grow by the nature of the soil and that the fruit so grow be better than other plant both in beauty taste and last be to be keep a whole year from rot he add that it yield in his time plenty of vine abound with grape of a pleasant taste so as the wine make thereof be not sharp but almost as pleasant as the fench wine which Camden think probable there be many place still call vineyard and attribute it rather to the inhabitant slothfulness then to the fault of the air or soil that it yield not wine at this day Tewksbury be a large and fair town have three bridge over three river and be famous for make of woollen cloth for excellent mustard and a fair monastery in which the earl of Gloucester have their sepulcher the city of Gloucester be the chief of the county through which the Severne run and here be the famous hill of Cotsall upon which great flock of sheep do feed yield most white wool much esteem of all nation Circester be a ancient city the largeness whereof in old time appear by the ruin of the wall the river Onse spring in this county which after yield the name to the famous river Thames fall into it 12 Oxfordshire also be inhabit by the dobuni a fertile county the plain whereof be beautify with meadow and grove the hill with wood and not only it abound with corn but with all manner of cattle and game for hunt and hawk and with many river full of fish Woodstock town be famous for the king house and large park compass with a stone wall which be say to
