have be the first park in England but our progenitor be so delight with hunt as the park be now grow infinite in number and be think to contain more fallow deer than all the christian world beside history affirm that Henry the second for his mistress Rosamond of the clifford house do build in his house here a labyrinth unpassable by any without a thread to guide they but no ruin thereof now remain the town itself have nothing to boast but that jeffry Chancer the english Homer be bear there godstowe of old a nunnery be not far distant where Rosamond be bury Oxford be a famous university give the name to the county and be so call of the ford for ox or of the ford and the river 13 buckinghamshire be of old inhabit by the which Camden think to be the Cassei and it have a large and pleasant town call Ailsbury which give the name to the valley adjoin the city Buckingham be the chief of the county and the town of Stonystratford be well know for the fair inn and stately bridge of stone 14 Bedfordshire have the same old inhabitant and have the name of Bedford the chief town 15 hertfordshire have the same old inhabitant and the chief town be Hertford in this county be the stately house thibauld for building garden and walk saint Albon be a pleasant town full of fair inn 16 Midlesex county be of old inhabit by the trinobant call in the time of the saxon king in this county be the king stately palace Hamptencourt have many courtyard compass with sumptuous building London the seat of the Britain empire and the chamber of the king of England be so famous as it need not be praise it have college for the study of the municiple law wherein live many young gentleman student of the same the little city Westminster of old more than a mile distant be now by fair building join to London and be famous for the church wherein the king and noble have stately sepulcher and for the court of justice at Westminster hall where the parliament be extraordinary hold and ordinary the chancery and king bench with like court also it have the king stately palace call Whitehall to which be join the park and house of saint james. the city of London have the sumptuous church of saint Paul beautify with rich sepulcher and the burse or exchange a stately house build for the meeting of merchant a very sumptuous and wonderful bridge build over the Thames rich shop of goldsmith in Cheapside and innumerable stately palace whereof great part lie scatter in unfrequented lane 17 Essex county have of old the same inhabitant and it be a large territory yield much corn and saffron enrich by the ocean and with pleasant river for fish with grove and many other pleasure it have a large forest for hunt call Waltham Forrest Chensford be a large and fair town near which be new-hall the stately palace of the rateliffe earl of Sussex Colchester be a fair city pleasant seat well inhabit and beautify with fifteen church which great flourish in the time of the roman Harewich be a safe haven for ship saffron Walden be a fair town the field whereof yield plenty of saffron whereof it have part of the name 18 the county of Suffolk be of old inhabit by the iceni and it be large the soil fertile pleasant in grove and rich in pasture to fat cattle where great quantity of cheese be make and thence export saint Edmondsberry vulgar call Berry be a fair town and so be Ipswich have stately build church and house and a commodious haven 19 the county of Norfolk have of old the same inhabitant and it be a large almost all champion country very rich and abound with sheep and especial with coney fruitful and most populous the city Norwich chief of the county deserve to be number among the chief city of England for the riches populousness beauty of the house and the fair building of the church Yarmouth be a most fair town fortify by nature and diligent art and have a very fair haven upon the bay which Ptolemy name metaris vulgar call the washes lie the large town of linne famous for the safety of the haven most easy to be enter for the concourse of merchant and the fair building 20 cambridgeshire have of old the same inhabitant and consist all of open corn field except some place yield saffron and it give excellent barley of which steep till it spring again they make great quantity of malt to brew beer in such quantity as the beer be much export even into foreign part and there high esteem Cambridge be a famous university seat upon the river grant by other call Came of which and the bridge over the same it be call Cambridge the northern part of this county consist of land green and pleasant in summer but all cover with water in the winter whereof the chief call Ely give the name to all the rest call as if they be but one island the i will of Ely the chief town whereof call also Ely be famous for be the seat of a bishop 21 hunt donshire have of old the same inhabitant the chief town whereof be Huntingdon 22 Northamptonshire be of old inhabit by the coritani and be a country most painful till and full of inhabitant Northampton be the chief city large and wall Peterborow be the seat of a bishop near Stamford be the stately palace burleigh build by William the first lord burleigh 23 Leycestershire have of old the same inhabitant a champion country and fruitful in bear corne. in lutterworth a little town of trade John Wickliff be pastor or minister Leicester the chief city have more antiquity than beauty 24 Rutlandshire have of old the same inhabitant and be the least county of England and have the name of the red earth the town of Vppingham deserve no other mention then that it be the chief town of the county 25 Linconshire have of old the same inhabitant and be a very large county rich in corn and pasture and abound with fowl and fish and all thing necessary for food the great washes of Holland when the sea flow be cover with water but when it ebb the ground be discover to be pass but not without danger and with a good guide Lincoln the chief city be of old one of the most populous city of England and one that have great trade and have a sumptuous cathedral church 26 have of old the same inhabitant the chief city whereof be Nottingham pleasant seat in the western part be the wood call Shirewood feed infinite number of fallow and red dear whether the king of old be wont to retire for hunt 27 Darbyshire have of old the same inhabitant the chief town whereof be Derby fair and well inhabit the ayle whereof be for goodness proverbial prefer before that kind of drink in any other town the western part have high mountain call Peake yield lead which they make into sow and in his proper vein be there find likewise there millstone be out out and there be the old castle call the castle in the Peake near which be a great hole or cave in the mountain gape wide and have many inward cave and this hole with reveuerence be it speak be vulgar call the devil arse at peuke of which many fable be tell and the place be account among the miracle of England the like fable be tell of (@) hole not far distant very steep and deep 28 Warwickshire be of old inhabit by the wherein be Coventry a large fair and wall city so call of the covent of monk and at this day it be the fair city within-land whereof the chief trade of old be make round cap of wool but the same be now very little use the trade be decay Warwick be the chief city of the county and near the same upon the hill Blacklow Peter of Gaveston be behead by the lord of the kingdom not far thence be a transparent and pleasant but little wood and there be clear fountain which place yield sweet solitude for the muse and there they report that the famous worthy Guy of Warwick after many adventure achieve do first live a Heremites life and be after death bury 29 worcestershire have of old the same inhabitant which after in the time of beda be call either of wic signify a corner or bay or of wyche signify (@) in the saxon tongue and there be excellent salt-pit or brook and new fountain of salt be daily find the country be happy in the healthful air tertility of soil and sweet river but especial yield abundance of pear of which they the neighbour welshman where Henry Percy the young with his force be overthrow by Henry the four 32 Cheshire be a great county of gentleman no other county have so many knight house Westchester be a pasture and send great quantity of cheese to London I know that Worcester cheese be most esteem but there be not such quantity to transport they I know that Suffolk and the fen of Essex yield huge cheese in great number to be export but they be not so please to the taste as these I know that in all the county some quantity of very good cheese be make for private man use but not in proportion to be export whereas Cheshire yield great quantity of very good cheese comparable to those of Holland serve the great part of London therewith and export the same into other part when the heir male of this county face Henry the three add this large patrimony to the crown so as the king elder son shall be earl of Cheshire and Richard the second of a county make it a principality and himself be call prince of Cheshire but Henry the four reduce it again to a county Palatine and at this day it have palatine jurisdiction administer by a chamberlain a special judge two exchequer baron three sergeant at law a sheriff a attorney a escheator etc. 33 Herefordshire be of old inhabit by the silures and it so much abeund with all thing necessary for the life of man as it be not content in that respect people feed on oaten bread in all part be multitude of cattle but it want wood and for fire use a kind of turf Russia which of the castle we call castle-towne be the chief town and have a garrison of soldier but Douglas be the most frequent and best inhabit town because it have a excellent haven easy to be enter in the western part balacuri be the seat of the bishop under the primacy of the archbishop of York and there be the fort call the pile wherein a garrison of soldier be keep upon the southern promontory lie a little island call the calf of man which abound with sea bird call puffin and a kind of duck engender of rot wood which the english call barnacle in general the inhabitant have their proper tongue and law and have their proper coin they abhor from steal and from beg and be wonderful religious general and most ready conform themselves at this day to the church of England and the people in the northern part speak like scot and in the southern part like irish. Edwin king of Northumberland subdue the northern people and subject they to the crown of England yet with many change of fortune this island long have their own king even since the norman conquer England and since the time that John king of England pass into Ireland by the way subdue this island about the year 1210 till the kingdom come to the scot in the year 1266. after that time marry the daughter of Reginald the last lay claim to the island before the king of England as supreme lord of Scotland and when sheecould not prevail William Montague her kinsman take the island of man by force which his heir sell for a great sum of money in the year 1393 to William Scroop who be behead for treason the island fall by right to Henry the four king of England who assign the same to Henry Percy earl of Northumberland with proviso that he and his heir at the coronation of the king of England shall carry the sword vulgar call Lancaster sword before the king but the same persey be also kill in civil war the king give that island to Stanlye from who discend the earl of Derby who keep the same till Ferdinand earl of Derby die without heir male and the earldom fall to his brother but this island to his daughter as heir general queen Elizabeth think it unfit that woman shall be set over her soldier there in garrison give the keep thereof to sir Thomas Gerrard but king james the fourteen of August in the five year of his reign grant by letter patent this island with all thing thereunto appertain to Henry earl of Northampton and Robert earl of Saltsbury their heir and assign for ever they upon do homage for the same present his majesty with two falcon and his heir and successor at their coronation in like sort with two falcon and howsoever no use or intent of this grant be mention in these letter patent yet no doubt the grant be make to the use of those upon who humble petition to his majesty the letter patent be grant as therein be express declare namely of William lord Stanley earl of Derby heir male to John lord Stanley and of Elizabeth countess of Huntingdon Anne wife to the lord Chandois and Francis wife to sir John Egerton knight be the heir general of the say John lord Stanley the famous river Thames fall into the german ocean over against Zealand and before it fall into the same make the 14 island canuey upon the coast of Essex so low as it be often overflow all but some high hill to which the sheep retire be some four thousand in number the flesh whereof be of delicate taste and they be milk by young man near that be the 15 island Sheppey so call of the sheep wherein be Quinborrough a most fair castle keep by a constable without the mouth of Thames lie the shelf or sand dangerous to sea man which of the great be all call Goodwin sand where they say a island the patrimony of the same earl Goodwinn be devour by the sea in the year 1097. in the britain sea lie the 16 i will of wight have in the sea most plentiful fish and the land be so fruitful as they export corn beside that in all part it have plenty of coney hare partridge and Feasanes and have also two park of fallow dear also the sheep feed there upon the pleasant hill yield wool in goodness next to the fleece of lemster and Cotsall flock it have six and thirty town and castle and the ecclesiastical jurisdiction thereof belong to the bishop of Wintchester towards the west lie other land pretend to be french but subject to England namely 17 Gerzey whither condemn man be of old banish and 18 Garnsey neither so great nor so fruitful but have a more commodious haven upon which lie the town of saint Peter both land burn a weed of the sea or sea coal bring out of England and both speak the french language I omit the seven isle call Siadae and other adjoin and will only add that the land lie near Cornwall which the greek call hesperide the english call silly and the netherlander call sorling be in number some 145 more or less whereof some yield wheat all abound with coney crane swan hirnshawe and other sea bird the great of they be call saint Mary and have a castle wherein soldier lie in garrison commit in our time to the keep of sir Francis godolphin and after to his son sir William godolphin be of a noble family in Cornwall also many of the say land have vein of tin and from hence be lead first carry into Greece and the roman emperor banish condemn man hither to work in the mine of mettle the air of England be temperate but thick cloudy and misty and Caesar witness the situation that the cold be not so pierce in England as in France for the sun draw up the vapour of the sea which compass the island and distil they upon the earth in frequent shower of rain so that frost be somewhat rare and howsoever snow may often fall in the winter time yet in the southern part especial it seldom lie long on the ground also the cool blast of sea wind mitigate the heat of summer by reason of this temper laurel and rosemary flourish all winter especial in the fertility and traffic the southern part and in summer time England yield apricot plentiful musk melon in good quantity and fig in some place all which ripen well and happy imitate the taste and goodness of the same fruit in Italy and by the same reason all beast bring forth their young in the open field even in the time of winter and England have such abundance of apple pear cherry and plum such variety of they and so good in all respect as no country yield more or better for which the italian will glad exchange their citron and orange but upon the sea coast the wind many time blast the fraite in the very flower the english be so natural incline to pleasure as there be no country wherein the gentleman and lord have so many and large park only reserve for the pleasure of hunt or where all sort of man allot so much ground about their house for pleasure of garden and orchard the very grape especial towards the south and of a pleasant taste and I have say that in some country as in Glostershire they make wine of old which no doubt many part will yield at this day but that the inhabitant forbear to plant vine aswell because they be serve plentiful and at a good rate with french wine as for that the hill most fit to bear grape yield more commodity by feed of sheep and cattle Caesar write in his commentary that Brittany yield white lead within land and iron upon the seacoast no doubt England have unexhaustible vein of both and also of tin and yield great quantity of brass and of Allom and iron and abound with quarry of freestone and fountain of most pure salt and I former say that it yield some quantity of silver and that the tin and lead be mingle with silver but so as it do not large quit the cost of the labour in separate or try it two city yield medicinal bath namely Buxstone and bath and the water of bath especial have great virtue in many disease England abound with sea-coal upon the sea-coast and with pit coal within land but the wood at this day be rather frequent and pleasant then vast be exhaust for fire and with iron-mille so as the quantity of wood and charcoal for fire be much diminish in respect of the old abundance and in some place as in the fen they burn turf and the very dung of Cowes yet in the mean time England export great quantity of sea-coal to foreign part in like sort England have infinite quantity as of metal so of wool and of woollen clothes to be export the english beer be famous in netherlands and low Germany which be make of barley and hops for England yield plenty of hops howsoever they also use flemish hop the city of low Germany upon the sea forbid the public sell of english beer to satisfy their own brewer yet private swallow it like nectar but in netherlands great and incredible quantity thereof be spend England abound with corn which they may transport when a quarter in some place contain six in other eight bushel be sell for twenty shilling or under and this corn not only serve England but also serve the english army in the civil war of Ireland at which time they also export great quantity thereof into foreign part and by God mercy England scarce once in ten year needs supply of foreign corn which want common proceed of the covetousness of private man export or hide it yet I must confess that daily this plenty of corn decrease by reason that private man find great commodity in feed of sheep and cattle then in the plough require the hand of many servant can by no law be restrain from turn corn field into enclose pasture especial since great man be the first to break these law England abound with all kind of foul aswell of the sea as of the land and have more tame swan swim in the river than I do see in any other part it have multitude of hurtful bird as crow raven and kite and they labour not to destroy the crow consume great quantity of corn because they feed on worm and other thing hurt the corne. and in great city it be forbid to kill kite or raven because they devour the filth of the street England have very great plenty of sea and river fish especial above all other part abundance of oyster makrell and herring and the english be very industrous in fish though nothing comparable to the fleming therein the english export into Italy great quantity of red herring with gain of two or three for one not to speak in this place of other commodity which they export with great gain and in this fish they be very industrious as well in the sea upon the coast as in the northern land to conclude they export in great quantity all kind of salt fish and those dry in the smoke and pickle as pilchard poor John Caviale botargo and the like which they sell in Italy and those part at a dear rate England abound with pulse of all kind and yield great quantity of saffron and of flax whereof they have also great quantity from Dantzke whence also they have like plenty of pitch and of fir tree for mast of ship which two thing if England want not I dare say that this island or part of a island abound with all thing necessary for honest clothing large and dainty feed and for war by land and sea as for war it have not only the aforesaid metal but also great quantity of saltpetre beside the famous broad cloth it yield for clothe many stuff whereof great quantity be also export and I will not omit that howsoever it have silk from foreign part yet the english silk stocking be much to be prefer before those of Italy Spain or any part in the World England abound in cattle of all kind and particular have very great ox the flesh whereof be so tender as no meat be more desire the cow be also great with large udder yield plenty of whitmeate no part in the world yield great variety nor better of that kind and the hide of ox be contrary to the common good export in great quantity by uniustifiable though strict forbid by many statute the flesh of hog and swine be more savoury then in any other part except the bacon of Westphalia and of the southern land where they common feed on root and chestnut the goodness of the sheep may be conjecture by the excellency of the wool and woollen clothes which sheep be subject to rot when they feed on low wet ground except the marsh overflow by the sea which for the saltness be hold very wholesome for they and these rot often destroy whole stock for they seldom drink but be moisten by the dew fall in the night and the feed of sheep upon like accident of discase often undo the ownet in his estate but more common preserve from that ill they enrich many so as it be proverbial say he who sheep stand and wife die the husband gain their dowry must needs be rich the king forest have innumerable herd of red dear and all part have such plenty of fallow dear as every gentleman of five hundred or a thousand pound rend by the year have a park for they enclose with pale of wood for two or three mile compass yet this prodigal age have so force gentleman to improve their revenue as many of these ground be by they dispark and convert to feed cattle last without offence be it speak I will bold say that England yea perhaps one county thereof have more fallow dear than all Europe that I have see no kingdom in the world have so many dove-house I former say that the wolf be altogether destroy in England and Wales so as the sheep feed free in the field and mountain England have much more dog aswell for the several kind as the number of each kind than any other territory of like compass in the world not only little dog for beauty but hunt and water-dogge whereof the bloodhound and some other have admirable quality it have infinite number of coney whereof the skin especial black and silver haired be much prise and in great quantity transport especial into Turkey the nag and geldings be singular for the gentle amble pace and for strength to perform great journey so be the hunt horse of exceed swiftness much esteem in foreign part especial in France and Scotland and of both kind the number be infinite the great horse for service and to draw coach and cart be of like number and goodness and one kind for service call the corser as breed of the neapolitan corser and english mare yield not for bravery of race to the neapolitan corser or spanish gennet I say that they be all strong and the horse for journey in defatigable for the english especial northern man ride from day break to the evening without draw bit neither spare their hory nor (@) whence be the proverb that England be the hell of horse the purgatory of servant and the paradise of woman because they ride horse without measure and use their servant imperious and their woman obsequious the gentleman disdain traffic think it to abase gentry but in Italy with grave counsel the very prince disdain not to be merchant by the great and hardly leave the retail commodity to man of inseriour sort and by this course they preserve the dignity and patrimony of their progenitor suffer not the sinew of the commonwealth upon any pretence to be wrest out of their hand on the contrary the english and french perhaps think it unjust to leave the common sort no mean to be enrich by their industry and judge it equal that gentleman shall live of their revenue citizen by traffic and the common sort by the plough and manual art as diverse member of one body do in this course daily sell their patrimony and the buyer except lawyer be for the most part citizen and vulgar man and the daily feel of this mischief make the error apparent whether it be the prodigality of the gentry great than in any other nation or age or their too charitable regard to the inferior sort or rashness or slothfulness which cause they to neglect and despise traffic which in some commonwealth and namely in England pass all other commodity and be the very sinew of the kingdom I have at large relate in this book treat of Poland the english traffic in the baltic sea and treat of Germany their traffic with the Hans city and so treat of other several state the english traffic with each of they so as it be lose labour to repair it again only for Spain whereof I have no cause to speak touch their traffic with England I will add that the english carry into Spain woollen clothes saffron wax and corn and bring from thence oil fruit sack and sweet wine indian spice with God and silver and in general I will observe that England abound with rich commodity of their own and export they with their own ship from very Iseland and Moscovye to both the indies and at this day buy not so much of the turk as they be wont but by long navigation fetch spice and like commodity from the far east indies so as the ship of England must needs be very great in number and strength but of England naval glory I must speak at large in the discourse of that commonwealth in the mean time I free profess that in my opinion the english mariner be more dare then any other nation in storm of wind rage of sea and thunder of ordinance in naval fight and if any stranger take i of too much boast in this point I desire he to consider of Martin furbusher attempt in the freeze sea of sir Francis drake and sir Thomas candish dangerous navigation round about the world and if these thing shall not move he the worst I wish he be that in person he may experience their courage and art in a fight upon equal term Caesar in the four chapter and five book of his commentary write thus of their diet the Britan diet it be unlawful for they to taste hare goose or hen yet they keep they all for their pleasure and the inward part sow no corn but live upon milk and flesh at this day the english inhabitant eat almost no flesh more common than hen and for goose they eat they in two season when they be fat upon the stubble after harvest and when they be green about Whitsuntide at which time they be hold for dainty and howsoever hare be think to nourish melancholy yet they be eat as venison both roast and boil they have also great plenty of coney the flesh whereof be fat tender and much more delicate than any I have eat in other part so as they be in England prefer before hare at which the german wonder who have no venison the prince keep it proper to themselves and the hunt of hare be proper to the gentleman in most part they esteem hare as venison and seldom eat coney be there somewhat rare and more like roast cat then the english coney the english husbandman eat barley and Rye brown bread and preserre it to white bread as abide long in the stomach and not so soon digest with their labour but citizen and gentleman care most pure white bread England yield as I have say all kind of corn in plenty I have former say that the english have abundance of whitmeate of all kind of flesh fowl and fish and of all thing good for food and in the ducourle of the french diet I have show that the english have some proper daintics not know in other part which I will in a word repeat the oyster of England be of old carry as far as Rome be more plentiful and lavorie then in any other part England have abundance of godwit and many sea-fowle which be rare or altogether unknown elsewhere in the season of the year the english eat fallow dear plentiful as buck in summer and do in winter which they bake in pasty and this venison pastry be a dainty rare find in any other kingdom likewise brawn be a proper meat to the english and not know to other they have strange variety of whitmeate and likewise of preserve banquet stuff in which preserve France only may compare with they it be needless to repeat the rest and I shall be tedions if I shall search particular like dainty which the english have only or in great abundance than other nation in general the art of cookery be much esteem in England neither do any soon find a master than man of that profession and howsoever they be most esteem which for all kind be most exquisite in that art yet the english cook in comparison with other nation be most commend for roast meat as abundance of all thing make they cheap so riches prefer a gluttonous appetite before gold and the prodigality of gentleman who have this singular folly to offer more than thing be worth as if it be a point of dignity to pay more than other and last the great money of silver and the not have small coin or brasle money to pay for small matter these thing I say in this great plenty make we poor and great increase the price of all thing also the say abundance and the riches vulgar increase and the old custom of the english make our table plentiful furnish whereupon other nation esteem we glutton and devourer of flesh yet the english table be not furnish with many dish all for one man diet but several for many man appetite and not only prepare for the family but for stranger and relief of the poor I confess that in such plenty and variety of meat every man can use moderation nor understand that these several meat be not for one man but for several appetite that each may take what he like and I confess that the english custom first to serve gross meat on which hunger spare not to seed and then to serve dainty which invite to eat without hunger as likewise the long sit and discourse at table which make man unaware eat more than the italian can do at their solitary table these thing I say give we just cause to cry with Socrates God deliver i from meat that invite to eat beyond hunger but the italian Sansovine be much deceive write that in general the english care and cover the table at least four time in the day for howsoever those that journey and some sickly man stay at home may perhaps take a small breakfast yet in general the english eat but two meal of dinner and supper each day and I can never see he that use to eat four time in the day and I will profess for myself and other englishman pass through Italy so famous for temperance that we often obserae that howsoever we may have a pullet and some flesh prepare for we eat it with a moderate proportion of bread the italian at the same time with a charger full of herb for a salad and with root and like meat of small price will each of they eat two or three pennyworth of bread and since all fullness be ill and that of bread worst I think we be more temperate in our diet though eat more flesh than they eat so much more bread than we do it be true that the english prepare large for ordinary diet for themselves and their friend come by chance and at feast for invite friend be so excessive in the number of dish as the table be not think well furnish except they stand one upon another neither use they to set drink on the table for which no room be leave but the cup and glass be serve in upon a side table drink be offer to none till they call for it that the old english hospitality be I will bold say a mere vice I have former show in the discourse of the italian diet which let he read who shall think this as dissonant from truth as it be from the vulgar opinion if any strange desire to abide long in a city or university he may have his table with some citizen of the better sort at a convenient rate according to his quality from ten pound to twenty pound yearly I have hear some german complain of the english inn by the high way as well for dearness as for that they have only roast meat but these german land at Granesend perhaps be injure by those knave that flock thither only to deceive stranger and use englishman no better and after go from thence to London and be there entertain by some ordinary host of stranger return home little acquaint with english custom but if these stranger have know the english tongue or have have a honest guide in their journey and have know to live at Rome after the roman fashion which they seldom do use rather dutch inn and companion sure they shall have find that the world afford not such inn as England have either for good and cheap entertainment after the guest own pleasure or for humble attendance on passenger yea even in very poor village where if Curculio of Plautus shall see the thatch house he will fall into a faint of his spirit but if he shall smell the variety of meat his starveling look will be much cheer for assoon as a passenger come to a inn the servant run to he and one take his horse and walk he till he be cold then rub he and give he meat yet I must say that they be not much to be trust in this last point without the eye of the master or his servant to oversee they another servant give the passenger his private chamber and kindle his fire the three pull of his boot and make they clean then the host or hostess visit he and if he will eat with the host or at a common table with other his meal will cost he six penny or in some place but four penny yet this course be less honourable and not use by gentleman but if he will eat in his chamber he command what meat he will according to his appetite and as much as he think fit for he and his company yea the kitchen be open to he to command the meat to be dress as he best like and when he sit at table the host or hostess will accompany he or if they have many guest will at least visit he take it for courtesy to be bid sit down while he eat if he have company especial he shall be offer music which he may free take or refuse and if he be solitary the musician will give he the good day with music in the morning it be the custom and no way disgraceful to set up part of supper for his breakfast in the evening or in the morning after breakfast for the common sort use not to dine but ride from breakfast to supper time yet come early to the inn for better rest of their horse he shall have a reckon in writing and if it seem unreasonable the host will satisfy he either for the due price or by abate part especial if the servant deceive he any way which one of experience will soon find have former speak of ordinary expense by the high way aswell in the particular journal of the first part as in a chapter of this part purposely treat thereof I will now only add that a gentleman and his man shall spend as much as if he be accompany with another gentleman and his man and if gentleman will in such sort join together to eat at one table the expense will be much diminish last a man can more free command at home in his own house than he may do in his inn and at part if he give some few penny to the chamberlain and ostler they wish he a happy journey England have three public feast of great expense and pompous solemnity namely the coronation of the king the feast of s. George as well upon his day yearly as at all time when any knight of the order be install and the three when sergeant at the law be call the lord mayor of the city of London upon the day when he be swear and enter his office keep a solemn feast with public show of great magnificence beside that he and the sheriff of the city daily keep well furnish table to entertain any gentleman or stranger that will come to they to the great honour of the city in this particular pass all other city of the world know to we for the point of drink the english at a feast will drink two or three health in remembrance of special friend or respect honourable person and in our time some gentleman and commander from the war of netherlands bring in the custom of the german large garauss but this custom be in our time also in good measure leave likewise in some private gentleman house and with some captain and soldier and with the vulgar sort of citizen and artisan large and intemperate drink be use but in general the great and better part of the english hold all excess blame worthy and drunkenness a reproachful vice clown and vulgar man only use large drink of beer or ale how much soever it be esteem excellent drink even among stranger but gentleman garrawse only in wine with which many mix sugar which I never observe in any other place or kingdom to be use for that purpose and because the taste of the english be thus delight with sweetness the wine in tavern for I speak not of merchant or gentleman cellar be common mix at the fill thereof to make they pleasant and the same delight in sweetness have make the use of corand of Corinth so frequent in all place and with all person in England as the very greek that sell they wonder what we do with such great quantity thereof and know not how we shall spend they except we use they for die or to feed hog chap. 4 of Scotland touch the subject contain in the first chapter the longitude of Scotland extend five degree from the Scotland meridian of sixteen degree to that of one and twenty degree and the latitude extend four degree from the parallel of fifty six degree and a half to that of sixty degree and a half in the geographical description whereof I will brief follow the very word of Camden as near as I can be a author without exception 1 the gadeni of Scotland be of old next neighbour to the Ottadini of Northumberland in England and inhabit the country now call Teyfidale wherein be nothing memorable but the monastery of mailor 2 in merch so call as a border country the castle Hume be the old possession of the lord of Hume near which be kelso the ancient dwell of the earl of bothwell which be long by inheritance admiral of Scotland and the merch be mention in history for nothrng more than the valour of the say earl 3 Laudania of old call Pictland shoot out from merch towards the scottish narrow sea call the Frith and be full of mountain but have few wood in this country be these little city or town Dunbarre haddington and Musleborrow place wherein have be see the warlike virtue of the english and scot somewhat low and near to the foresay Frith lie Edinburgh which Ptolemy call castrum a rich city of old compass with wall and the seat of the king whole palace be at the east end in a valley over which hang a mountain call the chair of Arthur our britain prince and from this palace be a easy ascent to the west end where the length of the city end in a steep rock upon which be build a most strong castle call the maiden castle the same which Ptolemy call this city be long under the english saxon and about the year 960 England be invade by the dane it become subject to the scot Leth be a mile distant and be a most commodious haven upon the narrow scottish gulf vulgar call Edinburgh Frith 4 towards the west lie the upon another gulf run between England and Scotland vulgar call solway Frith of the say inhabit the country call Eskedale annandale and Nidtsdale in which be the little town dunfrise 5 next lay the in the valley where galway and Whitterne which city Ptolemy call Leucopibia be seat 6 in the little country Caricta have good pasture be the little town gergeny which Ptolemy call rerigonium 7 more inward lay the damnij where now Sterling mert and Claidsdale be seat here the river cluyde run by Hamelton the seat of the hameltons family of english race of which the three earl of Arran live in our day and after by Glascow the seat of a archbishop and a little university here be the territory call lennox whereof the steward have long time be earl of which family the late king of Scotland be descend and namely james the six who raise this earledom to a dukedom give that title to the lord and these serve in the french and neapolitan war be honour by the king of France with addition of buckles or in a field Gueules to their ancient coat of arm with this inscription that be distant thing I join Sterling or Strivelin lie not far off a little city of the king have a most strong castle upon the brow of a steep rock 8 next these towards the north lie the caledonij somewhat more barbarous than the rest as common they be more rude towards the north where not only the air be cold but the country waste and mountainous and here be the Caledonian wood so know to the roman writer as it be by they take for all Brittany and the wood thereof at this day this region be call by the scot allibawne and by the latin Albania and contain the bishopric Dunkeledon and the territory Argile so call as near the irish of which the Cambellan family have the title of earl of Argile who be the general justice of Scotland by right of inheritance and great master of the king household 9 towards the west lie the inhabit a waist and fenny country now call cantire that be a corner of land and next lie Assinshire 10 next lay the creones which region be now call strathuaern 11 next lay the at the promontory hay 12 on the east-side of the caledonian lie the Vernicones in the fruitful little region call Fife where be the town of saint Andrew metropolitan of all Scotland 13 the little region athol be fertile of which the steward of the family of lorne have the title of earl here be Strathbolgy the seat of the earl of huntly of the family of the seaton who take the name of gordan by the authority of a parliament 14 next lie goury have fruitful field of wheat whereof John lord Rethuen be of late make earl but arrell in this region have long give the title of earl to the family of hayes 15 under Fife lie angush where be Scone famous for the king consecration montrose have his earl of the family of the Grahames but the douglass earl of angush of a honourable eamily be make governor by Robert the three of this region and these earl be esteem the chief and principal earl of all Scotland and it be say that they have right to carry the king crown at the solemn assembly of the kingdom 16. 17 next lie the two region of Marnia and Marria upon the sea where be dunetyre the chief seat of the family of the keythe who by warlike virtue have deserve to be the marshal of the kingdom and Aberdene that be the mouth of the be a famous university and queen Mary create John Ereskin earl of marre who late be the regent of Scotland and be by inheritance sheriff of the county of Sterling 18 next lie the Taizeli where now Buquhan be seat 19 then towards Murrey Frith the five ocomagi of old inhabit Rosse murray and nesseland 20 more innerly be the gulf Vararis right over against the town inuernesse 21 the cantae possess the corner of land shoot towards the sea where be the most safe haven Cromer 22. 23 yet more inward where bean Rosse and southerland be seat the and Mertae of old inhabit thus far Edward the first king of England subdue all with his victorious army have beat the scot on all side in southerland be mountain of white marble a very miracle in this cold clime but of no use the excess and magnificence in building have not yet reach into these remote part 24 Further near catnesse the Catni of old inhabit the earl of which country be of the ancient and noble family of the sint-cleres 25 Vrdehead be think the remote promontory of all Brittany where the cornabij of old inhabit 26 I will in one word mention the land in the gulf Glotta or dunbritten Frith the land lie the island Glotta call Arran by the scot give the title to a earl next that lie Rothesia now call buthe whence be the steward king of scot as they say then Hellan the island of the saint without the foresay gulf many land lie thick together vulgar call the western land and number forty four be of old call by some hebrides by other Inchades and Leucades and by many as Ptolemy Ebudae Ina one of these land have a monastery famous for the burial of the king of Scotland and for the habitation of many holy man among which be Columbus the apostle of the pict of who cell the island be also name columbkill the scot buy all these land of the norwegian as a great strength to the kingdom though yield very little profit the old inhabitant whether scot or irish be of dare and impatient of be subject to any law near these lie the orcades vulgar orkney about thirty in number yield competent quantity of barley but no wheat or tree the chief whereof be Pomonia well know by the episcopal seat and yield both tin and lead these orcades land be subject to the dane and the inhabitant speak the goth language but Christiern king of the dane sell his right to the king of Scotland five day and night sail from the orcades be the island Thule so often mention by poet to express the further corner of the world whereupon Virgil say that be the further Thule shall thou serve many have think that Iseland be this Thule condemn to cold air and perpetual winter but Camden think rather that Schotland be Thule which the mariner now call Thilensall be subject to the king of Scotland in the german sea towards the coast of Brittany be few land save only in edenburg Frith where these be find May k and inche-colme that be the island of Columbus Scotland reach so far into the north must needs be subject to excessive cold yet the the same be in some sort mitigate by the thickness of the cloudy air and sea vapour and as in the northern part of England they have small pleasantness goodness or abundance of fruit and flower so in Scotland they have much less or none at all and I remember that come to Barwick in the month of May we have great storm and feel great cold when for two month before the pleasant spring have smile on we at London on the west side of Scotland be many wood mountain and lake on the fertility the east side towards the sea I pass Fife a pleasant little territory of open field without enclosure fruitful in corn as be all the part near Barwick save that they yield little wheat and much barley and Oates and all a plain country but it have no wood at all only the gentleman dwelling be shadow with some little grove pleasant to the view Scotland abound with fish and have plenty of all cattle yet not so big as we and their horse be full of spirit and patient of labour but very little so as the scot then will give any price for one of our english geldings which notwithstanding in queen Elizabethe time may not upon great penalty be sell unto they the navy or ship of Scotland be of small strength in the memory of our age neither be their mariner of greet experience but to make they more diligent the traffic merchant their king have former lay small or no imposition or custom on they and while the english have war with the spaniard the scot as neutral by carry of english commodity into Spain and by have their ship for more security lade by english merchant grow somewhat rich and more experience in navigation and have better and strong ship then in former time and sure since the scot be very dare I can see why their mariner shall not be bold and courageous howsoever they have not hitherto make any long voyage rather for want of riches then for slothfulness or want of courage the inhabitant of the western part of Scotland carry into Ireland and neighbour place red and pickele herring sea coal and aquavitae with like commodity and bring out of Ireland yarn and Cowes hide or silver the eastern scot carry into France course clothes both linen and woollen which be narrow and shrink in the wet they also carry thither wool skin of goat wether and of coney and diverse kind of fish take in the scottish sea and near other northern land and after smoke or otherwise dry and salt and they bring from thence salt and wine but the cheese traffic of the scot be in four place namely at camphire in Zealand whether they carry salt the skin of wether otter badger and martens and bring from thence corne. and at Bordeaux in France whether they carry clothes and the same skin and bring from thence wine prune walnut and chessenut three within the baltic sea whether they carry the say clothes and skin and bring thence flax hemp iron pitch and tar and last in England whether they carry linen clothes yarn and salt and bring thence wheat oat beanes and like thing the scot have no staple in any foreign city but trade in France upon the league of the nation and in Denmark have privilege by the affinity of the king and stock in great number into Poland abound in all thing for food and yield many commodity and in these kingdom they live at this time in great multitude rather for the poverty of their own kingdom then for any great traffic they exercise there deal rather for small fardel then for great quantity of rich ware touch their diet they eat much red Colewort and cabbage but little fresh meat use to salt mutton and goose which make i more wonder that they the diet use to eat beef without salt the gentleman reckon their revenue not by rent of money but by chauldron of victual and keep many people in their family yet live most on corn and rootes not spend any great quantity of flesh myself be at a knight house who have many servant to attend he that bring in his meat with their head cover with blue cap the table be more than half furnish with great platter of porridge each have a little piece of sodden meat and when the table be serve the servant do sit down with we but the upper mess in steed of porridge have a pullet with some prune in the broth and I observe no art of cookery or furniture of household stuff but rather rude neglect of both though myself and my companion send from the governor of Berwick about border affair be entertain after their best manner the scot live then in faction use to keep many follower and so consume their revenue of victual live in some want of money they vulgar eat hearth cake of oat but in city have also wheaten bread which for the most part be buy by courtier gentleman and the best sort of citizen when I live at Berwick the scot weekly upon the market day obtain leave in write of the governor to buy pease and beanes whereof as also of wheat their merchant at this day send great quantity from London into Scotland they drink pure wine not with sugar as the english yet at feast they put comfit in the wine after the french manner but they have not our vintner fraud to mix their wine I do never see nor hear that they have any public inn with sign hang out but the better sort of citizen brew ale their usual drink which will distemper a stranger body and the same citizen will entertain passenger upon acquaintance or entreaty their bedstead be then like cubbard in the wall with door to be open and shut at pleasure so as we climb up to our bed they use but one sheet open at the side and top but close at the foot and so double passenger do seek a stable for their horse in some other place and do there buy horse-meat and if perhaps the same house yield a stable yet the payment for the horse do not make they have bed free as in England I omit to speak of the inn and expense therein have delate the same in the itinerary of the first part and a chapter in this part express treat thereof when passenger go to bed their custom be to present they with a sleep cup of wine at part the country people and merchant use to drink large the gentleman somewhat more spare yet the very courtier at fcast by night meeting and entertain any stranger use to drink health not without excess and to speak truth without offence the excess of drink be then far great in general among the scot then the English myself be at the court invite by some gentleman to supper and be forewarn to fear this excess will not promise to sup with thembut upon condition that my inuiter will be my protection from large drink which I be many time force to invoke be courteous entertain and much provoke to garauss and so for that time avoid any great intemperance remember this and have since observe in my conversation at the english court with the scot of the better sort that they spend great part of the night in drink not only wine but even beer as myself will not accuse they of great intemperance so I can altogether free they from the imputation of excess wherewith the popular voice charge they chap. v. of Ireland touch the particular subject of the first chapter the longitude of Ireland extend four degree from the Ireland meridian of eleven degree and a half to that of fifteen and a half and the latitude extend also four degree from the parallel of fifty four degree to that of fifty eight degree in the geographical description I will follow Camden as former this famous island in the virginian sea be by old writer call jerna Inuerna and Iris by the old inhabitant Eryn by the old Britan yuerdhen by the english at this day Ireland and by the irish bard at this day in which sense of the irish word Avicen call it the holy island beside Plutarch of old call it Ogigia and after he Isidore name it scotia this Ireland according to the inhabitant be divide into two part the wild irish and the english irish live in the english pale but of the old kingdom five in number it be divide into five part 1 the fast be by the irish call mow by the english monster and be subdivide into six county of kerry of Limricke of corcke of tipperary of the holy cross and of Waterford to which the seven county of Desmond be now add the a scithean people come into Spain and from thence into Ireland inhabit the county of kerry full of woody mountain in which the earl of Desmond have the dignity of palatine have their house in trail a little town now almost uninhabited not far thence lie saint Mary Wic vulgar call smerwicke where the lord Arthur Grace be lord deputy happy overthrow the aid troop send to the earl of Desmond from the pope and the king of Spain on the south side of kerry lie the county of Desmond of old inhabit by three kind of people the be spaniard the Velabri so call of their seat upon the sea water or marsh and the ibern call the upper irish inhabit about beerehaven and baltimore two haven well know by the plentiful fish of herring and the late invasion of the spaniard in the year 1601. next to these be the county of Mec Carti more of irish race who as enemy to the fitz-geralds queen Elizabeth make earl of Glencar in the year 1556. for of the fitz-geralds of the family of the earl of Kildare the earl of Desmond descend who be by birth english and create earl by king Edward the three become hateful rebel in our time the three county have the name of the city cork consist almost all of one long street but well know and frequent which be so compass with rebellious neighbour as they of old not dare to marry their daughter to they the custom grow and continue to this day that by mutual marriage one with another all the citizen be of kin in some degree of affinity not far thence be Yoghall have a safe haven near which the viscount of barry of english race be seat in the four county of tipperary nothing be memorable but that it be a palatinate the little town holy-cresse in the county of the same name have many great privilege the six county have the name of the city Limerike the seat of a bishop wherein be a strong castle build by king John not far thence be awue the seat of a bishop and the low Ossery give the title of a earl to the butler and the town thurles give they also the title of viscount and there be Cassiles now a poor city but the seat of a archbishop the seven county have the name of the city Watersord which the irish call Porthlargi of the commodious haven a rich and well inhabit city esteem the second to Dublyn and because the inhabitant long faithful help the english in subdue Ireland our king give they excessive privilege but they rash fail in their obedience at king james his come to the crown can not in long time obtain the confirmation of their old charter 2 lemster the second part of Ireland be fertile and yield plenty of corn and have a most temperate mild air be divide into ten county of Catterlogh kilkenny wexford Dublyn Kildare the king county the queen county the county of longford of Fernes and of wickle the Cariondi of old inhabit Caterlogh or Carloo county and they also inhabit great part of kilkenny of upper Ossery and of ormond which have nothing memorable but the earl of ormond of the great family of the butler inferior to no earl in Ireland not to speak of Fitz pairic baron of upper Ossery it be redicnious which some irish who will be believe as man of credit report of man in these part yearly turn into wolf except the abundance of melancholy humour transport they to imagine that they be so transform kilkenny give name to the second county be a pleasant town the chief of the town within land memorable for the civility of the inhabitant for the husbandman labour and the pleasant orchard I pass over the wall town Thomastowne and the ancient city rheban now a poor village with a castle yet of old give the title of baronet I pass over the village and strong castle of Leighlin with the country adjoin usurp by the sept of the cavanaghs now surname omore also I omit kosse of old a large city at this day of no moment the three county of wexford call by the irish county Reogh be of old inhabit by the menappij where at the town call banna the english make their first descent into Ireland and upon that coast be very dangerous flat in the sea which they vulgar call ground the city Weshford Weisford or wexford be the chief of the county not great but deserve praise for their faithfulness towards the english and frequent inhabit by man of english race the cauci a seaborder nation of Germany and the menappij aforesaid of old inhabit the territory now possess by the omore and Ohirns also they inhabit the four county of Kildare a fruitful soil have the cheese town of the same name great honour in the infancy of the church by saint Bridget king Edward the second create the giralds earl of Kildare the Eblani of old inhabit the territory of Dublin the five county have a fertile soil and rich pasture but want wood so as they burn turf or sea-coal bring out of England the city Dublyn call divelin by the english and Balacleigh as seat upon hurdle by the irish be the chief city of the kingdom and seat of justice fair build frequent inhabit and adorn with a strong castle fifteen church a episcopal seat and a fair college a happy foundation of a university lay in our age and endow with many privilege but the haven be bar and make less commodious by those hill of sand the adjoin promontory hoth-head give the title of a baron to the family of saint Laurence and towards the north lie Fengall a little territory as it be the garner of the kingdom which be environ by the sea and great river and this situation have defend it from the incursion of rebel in former civil war I omit the king and queen county namely Ophaly and Leax inhabit by the Oconnors and Omores as likewise the county of longford Fernes and wicklo as less afford memorable thing 3 the three part of Ireland be Midia or Media call by the english Methe in our father memory divide into Eastmeath and Westmeath in Eastmeath be drogheda vulgar call Tredagh a fair and well inhabit town trim be a little town upon the confine of Ulster have a stately castle but now much ruinate and it be more notable for be the ancient as it be barony of the lacy Westmeath have the town Deluin give the title of baron to the english family of the nugent and Westmeath be also inhabit by many great irish sept as the Omaddens the Magoghigans and MacCoghlans which seem barbarous name Shamon be a great river in a long course make many and great lake as the large lake or lough Regith and yield plentiful fish as do the frequent river and all the sea of Ireland upon this river lie the town have a very fair bridge of stone the work of sir Henry Sidney lord deputy and a strong fair castle 4 Connaght be the four part of Ireland a fruitful province but have many bog and thick wood and it be divide into six county of Clare of Letrim of Galloway of of maio and of the county of Clare or Thowmond have his enrle of Thowmond of the family of the obrenes the old king of Connaght and toam be the seat of a archbishop only part but the great of this county be call Clare of Phomas Clare earl of Gloucester the adjoin territory clan Richard the land of Richard son have his earl call Clanricard of the land but be of the english family burgo vulgar Burck and both these earl be first create by Henry the eight in the same territory be the barony Atterith belong to the baron of the english family Bermingham of old very warlike but their posterity have degenerate to the irish barbarism the city galway give name to the county lie upon the sea be frequent inhabit with civil people and fair build the northern part of Connaght be inhabit by these irish sept o o Rorke and Mac Diarmod upon the western coast lie the island Arran famous for the fabulous long life of the inhabitant 5 Ulster the five part of Ireland be a large province woody fenny in some part fertile in other part barren but in all part green and pleasant to behold and exceed store with cattle the next part to the pale and to England be divide into three county lowth down and antrimme the rest contain seven county monaghan Tyrone Armach Colrane donergall fermanagh and cavon lowth be inhabit by english-irish down and antrimme be contain under the same name and the baron thereof be of the berminghams family and remain love to the English monaghan be inhabit by the english family Fitzursi and these be become degenerate and barbarous and in the sense of that name be in the irish tongue call Mac mahon that be the son of bear I forbear to speak of Tyrone and the earl thereof infamous for his rebellion which I have at large handle in the second part of this work Armach be the seat of a archbishop and the metropolitan city of the whole island but in time of the rebellion be altogether ruinate the other county have not many memorable thing therefore it shall suffice to speak of they brief the neck of land call Lecale be a pleasant little territory fertile and abound with fish and all thing for food and therein be down at this time a ruin town but the seat of a bishop and famous for the burial of s Patrick s. Bridget and s. columb the town of Carickfergus be well know by the safe haven the river Bann run through the lake Euagh into the sea be famous for the fish of salmon the water be most clear wherein the salmon much delight the great family or sept of Ulster be thus name o Neale o Donnel whereof the chief be late create earl of Tirconnel o buil Mac guyre o cane o Dogharty Mac mahow Mac Gennis Mac Sorleigh etc. the lake Ern compass with thick wood have such plenty of fish as the fisherman fear the break of their net rather than want of fish towards the north in the midst of vast wood and as I think in the county donergall be a lake and therein a island in which be a cave famous for the apparition of spirit which the inhabitant call Ellanui frugadory that be the island of purgatory and they call it saint Patrick purgatory fable that he obtain of God by prayer that the irish see the pain of the damn may more careful avoid sin the land of Ireland be uneven mountainous soft watery woody and open to wind and flood of rain and so fenny as it have bogges upon the very top of mountain the funation not bear man or beast but dangerous to pass and such bog be frequent over all Ireland our matriner observe the sail into Ireland to be more dangerous not only because many tide meet make the sea apt to swell upon any storm but especial because they ever find the coast of Ireland cover with mist whereas the coast of England be common clear and to be see far off the air of Ireland be unapt to ripen seed yet as Mela witness the earth be luxurion in yield fair and sweet herb Ireland be little trouble with thunder lightning or earthquake yet I know not upon what presage in the year 1601 and in the month of November almost end at the siege of kinsale and few day before the famous battle in which the rebel be happy overthrow we do nightly hear and see great thunder and lightning not without some astonishment what they shall presage the field be not only most apt to feed cattle but yield also great increase of corn I will free say that I observe the winter cold to be far more mild than it be in England so as the irish pasture be more green and so likewise the garden all winter time but that in summer by reason of the cloudy air and watery soil the heat of the sun have not such power to ripen corn and fruit so as their harvest be much late than in England also I observe that the best sort of flower and fruit be much rare in Ireland then in England which notwithstanding be more to be attribute to the inhabitant then to the air for Ireland be oft trouble with rebellion and the rebel not only be idle themselves but in natural malice destroy the labour of other man and cut up the very tree of fruit for the same cause or else to burn they for these reason the inhabitant take less pleasure to till their ground or plant tree content to live for the day in continual fear of like mischief yet be not Ireland altogether destitute of these flower and fruit wherewith the county of kilkenny seem to abound more than any other part and the say humility of air and land make the fruit for food more raw and moist hereupon the inhabitant and stranger be trouble with looseness of body the country disease yet for the rawness they have a excellentremedy by their aquavitae vulgar call Vsqucbagh which bind the belly and dry up moisture more than our aquavitae yet in flame not so much also inhabitant aswell as stranger be trouble there with a ague which they call the irish. ague and they who be sick thereof upon a receive custom do not use the help of the physician but give themselves to the keep of irish woman who starve the ague give the sick man no meat who take nothing but milk and some vulgar know remedy at their hand Ireland after much blood spill in the civil war become less populous the fertility and traffic and aswell great lord of country as other inferior gentleman labour more to get new possession for inheritance then by husbandry and people of their old land to increase their revenue so as I then observe much grass wherewith the island so much abound to have perish without use and either to have rot or in the next spring-time to be burn lest it shall hinder the come of new grass this plenty of grass make the irish have infinite multitude of cattle and in the heat of the last rebellion the very vagabond rebel have great multitude of cow which they still like the nomads drive with they whither soever themselves be drive and fight for they as for their altar and family by this abundance of cattle the irish have a frequent though somewhat poor traffic for their hide the cattle be in general very little and only the man and the greyhound of great statute neither can the cattle possible be great since they eat only by day and then be bring at evening within the bawne of castle where they stand or lie all night in a dirty yard without so much as a lock of hay whereof they make little for sluggishness and that little they altogether keep for their horse and they be thus bring in by night for fear of thief the irish use almost no other kind of theft or else for fear of wolf the destruction whereof be neglect by the inhabitant oppress with great mischief they be so much grow in number as sometime in winter night they will come to prey in village and the subburbe of city the earl of ormond in monster and the earl of Kildare in Lemster have each of they a small park enclose for fallow dear and I have not see any other park in Ireland nor have hear that they have any other at that time yet in many wood they have many red dear loose scatter which seem more plentiful because the inhabitant use not then to hunt they but only the governor and commander have they sometime kill with the piece they have also about Ophalia and wexford and in some part of monster some fallow dear scatter in the wood yet in the time of the war I do never see any venison serve at the table but only in the house of the say earl and of the english commander Ireland have great plenty of bird and fowl but by reason of their natural sloth they have little delight or skill in bird or fowl but Ireland have neither sing nightingale nor chatter pie nor undermine mole nor black crow but only crow of mingle colour such as we call Royston crow they have such plenty of pheasant as I have know sixty serve at one feast and abound much more with rail but partridge be somewhat rare there be very many eagle and great plenty of hare coney hawk call gosse-hawke much esteem with we and also of bee as well in hive at home as in hollow tree abroad and in cave of the earth they abound in flock of sheep which they shear twice in the year but their wool be course and merchant may not export it forbid by a law make on behalf of the poor that they may be nourish by work it into cloth namely rug whereof the best be make at Waterford and mantle general wear by man and woman and export in great quantity Ireland yield much flax which the inhabitant work into yarn and export the same in great quantity and of old they have such plenty of linen cloth as the wild irish use to wear 30 or 40 else in a shirt all gather and wrinkle and wash in saffron because they never put they off till they be wear out their horse call hobby be much commend for their amble pace and beauty but Ireland yield few horse good for service in war and the say hobby be much inferior to our gelding in strength to endure long journey and be breed in the fenny soft ground of Ireland be soon lame when they be bring into England the hawk of Ireland call goss-hawk be as I say much esteem in England and they be seek out by money and all mean to be transport thither Ireland yield excellent marble near Dublin Killkenny and cork and I be of their opinion who dare venture all they be worth that the mountain will yield abundance of metal if this public good be not hinder by the inhabitant barbarousness make they apt to sedition and so unwilling to enrich their prince and country and by their slothfulness which be so singular as they hold it baseness to labour and by their poverty not able to bear the charge of such work beside that the wise sort think their poverty best for the public good make they peaceable as nothing make they soon kick against authority than riches Ireland have in all part pleasant river safe and lange haven and no less frequent lake of great circuit yield great plenty of fish and the sea on all side yield like plenty of excellent fish as salmond oyster which be prefer before the english and shellfish with all other kind of sea-fish so as the irish might in all part have abundance of excellent sea and freshwater fish if the fisher man be not so possess with the natural fault of slothfulness as no hope of gain scarce the fear of authority can in many place make they come out of their house and put to sea hence it be that in many place they use scot for fisherman and they together with the english make profit of the inhabitant sluggishness and no doubt if the irish be industrious in fish they may export salt and dry fish with great gain in time of peace the irish transport good quantity of corn yet they may not transport it without licence leave upon any sudden rebellion the king force and his good subject shall want corne. Ulster and the western part of monster yield vast wood in which the rebel cut up tree and cast they on heap use to stop the passage and therein as also upon fenny and boggy place to fight with the English but I confess myself to have be deceive in the common fame that all Ireland be woody have find in my long journey from Armah to kinsale few or no wood by the way except the great wood of Ophalia and some low shrubby place which they call glinne also I do observe many boggy and fenny place whereof great part may be dry by good and painful husbandry I may not omit the opinion common receive that the earth of Ireland will not suffer a snake or venomous beast to live and that the irish wood transport for building be free of spider and their web myself have see some but very few spider which the in habitant deny to have any poison but I have hear some english of good credit affirm by experience the contrary the irish have in most part great wood or low shrub and thicket do use the same for fire but in other part they burn turf and sea coal bring out of England they export great quantity of wood to make barrel call pipe-stave and make great gain thereby they be not permit to build great ship for war but they have small ship in some sort arm to resist pirate for transport of commodity into Spain and France yet no great number of they therefore since the irish have small skill in navigation as I can praise they for this art so I be confident that the nation be bold and warlike will no doubt prove brave seaman if they shall practice navigation and can possible be industrious therein I free profess that Ireland in general will yield abundance of all thing to civil and industrious inhabitant and when it lay waste by the late rebellion I do see it after the come of the lord Montioy daily more and more to flourish and in short time after the rebellion appease like the new spring to put on the wont beauty touch the irish diet some lord and knight and gentleman of the english-irish the diet and all the english there abide have competent mean use the english diet but some more some less clean few or none curious and no doubt they have as great and for (@) part great plenty than the english of flesh foul fish and all thing for food if they will use like art of cookery alwase I except the fruit venison and some dainty proper to England and rare in Ireland and we must conceive that venison and fowl seem to be more plentiful in Ireland because they neither so general affect dainty food nor so diligent search it as the english do many of the english-irish have by little and little be infect with the irish filthiness and that in the very city except Dublyn and some of the better sort in Waterford where the english continual lodging in their house they more retain the english diet the english-irish after our manner serve to the table joint of flesh cut after our fashion with goose pullet pig and like roast meat but their ordinary food for the common sort be of whitmeate and they eat cake of oat for bread and drink not english beer make of malt and hop but Ale at Corck I have see with these eye young maid stark naked grind of corn with certain stone to make cake thereof and strike of into the tub of meal such relic thereof as stick on their belly thigh and more unseemly part and for the cheese or butter common make by the english irish and english man will not touch it with his lip though he be half starve yet many english inhabitant make very good of both kind in city they have such bread as we but of a sharp favour and some mingle with annisseed and bake like cake and that only in the house of the better sort at Dublyn and in some other city they have tavern wherein spanish and french wine be sell but more common the merchant sell they by pint and quart in their own cellar the irish aquavitae vulgar call Vsquebagh be hold the best in the world of that kind which be make also in England but nothing so good as that which be bring out of Ireland and the Vsquebagh be prefer before our aquavita because the mingle of raisin Fennell seed and other thing mitigate the heat and make the taste pleasant make it less in name and yet refresh the weak stomach with moderate heat and a good relish these drink the english-irish drink large and in many family especial at feast both man and woman use excess therein and since I have inpart see and often hear from other experience that some gentlewoman be so free in this excess as they will kneel upon the knee and otherwise health after health with man not to speak of the wife of irish lord or to refer it to the due place who often drink tell they be drunken or at least till they void urine in full assembly of man I can though unwilling but note the irish woman more special with this fault which I have observe in no other part to be a woman vice but only in Bohemia yet so as accuse they I mean not to excuse the man and will also confess that I have see virgin as well gentlewoman as citizen command by their mother to retire after they have in courtesy pledge one or two health in city passenger may have featherbed soft and good but most common lousy especial in the high way whether that come by their be force to lodge common soldier or from the nasty filthiness of the nation in general for even in the best city as at Corck I have observe that my own and other english man chamber hire of the citizen be scarce sweep once in the week and the dust then lay in a corner be perhaps cast out once in a month or two I do never see any public inn with sign hang out among the english or english-irish but the officer of city and village appoint lodging to the passenger and perhaps in each city they shall find one or two house where they will dress meat and these be common house of englishman seldom of the irish so as these house have no sign hang out a passenger can challenge right to be entertain in they but must have it of courtesy and by entreaty the wild and as I may say mere irish inhabit many and large province be barbarous and most filthy in their diet they scum the seethe pot with a handful of straw and strain their milk take from the cow through a like handful of straw none of the clean and so cleanse or rather more defile the pot and milk they deu our great morsel of beef unsalted and they eat common swine flesh seldom mutton and all these piece of flesh as also the entrails of beast unwashed they see in a hollow tree lap in a raw cow hide and so set over the fire and therewith swallow whole lump of filthy butter yea which be more contrary to nature they will feed on horse die of themselves not only upon small want of flesh but even for pleasure for I remember a accident in the army when the lord Mountiey the lord deputy ride to take the air out of the camp find the buttock of dead horse cut off and suspect that some soldier have eat that flesh out of necessity be defraud of the victual allow they command the man to be search out among who a common soldier and that of the english-irish not of the mere irish be bring to the lord deputy and ask why he have eat the flesh of dead horse thus free answer your lordship may please to eat pheasant and Partridge and much good do it you that best like your taste and I hope it be lawful for i without offence to eat this flesh that like i better than beef whereupon the lord deputy perceive himself to be deceive and further understanding that he have receive his ordinary victual the detain whereof he suspect and purpose to punish for example give the soldier a piece of gold to drink in Vsquebagh for better digestion and so dismiss he the foresay wild irish do not thresh their oat but burn they from the straw and so make cake
