thereof yet they seldom eat this bread much less any better kind especial in the time of war whereof a bohemian baron complain who have see the court of England and Scotland will needs out of his curiosity return through Ireland in the heat of the rebellion and have letter from the king of scot to the irish lord then in rebellion first land among they in the further north where for eight day space he have find no bread not so much as a cake of oat till he come to eat with the earl of Tyrone and after obtain the lord deputy pass to come into our army relate this then want of bread to we for a miracle who nothing wonder thereat yea the wild irish in time of great peace impute covetousness and base birth to he that have any corn after Christmas as if it be a point of nobility to consume all within those festival day they willing eat the herb schamrock be of a sharp taste which as they run and be chase to a fro they snatch like beast out of the ditch neither have they any beer make of malt and (@) nor yet any ale no not the chief lord except it be very rare but they drink milk like nectar warm with a stone first cast into the fire or else beefe-broath mingle with milk but when they come to any market town to sell a cow or a horse they never return home till they have drink the price in spanish wine which they call the king of Spain daughter or in irish Vsqueboagh and till they have outsleep two or three day drunkenness and not only the common sort but even the lord and their wife the more they want this drink at home the more they swallow it when they come to it till they be as drink as beggar many of these wild irish eat no flesh but that which die of disease or otherwise of itself neither can it escape they for stink they desire no broth nor have any use of a spoon they can neither seethe artichoke nor eat they when they be sodden it be strange and ridiculous but most true that some of our carriage horse fall into their hand when they find soap and starch carry for the use of our laundress they think they to be some dainty meat do eat they greedy and when they stick in their tooth curse bitter the gluttony of we english churl for so they term we they feed most on whitmeate and esteem for a great dainty sour curd vulgar call by they bonaclabbe and for this cause they watchful keep their cow and fight for they as for religion and life and when they be almost starve yet they will not kill a cow except it be old and yield no milk yet will they upon hunger in time of war open a vain of the cow and drink the blood but in no case kill or much weaken it a man will think these man to be scythian who let their horse blood under the ear and for nourishment drink their blood and indeed as I have former say some of the irish be of the race of scythian come into Spain and from thence into Ireland the wild irish as I say seldom kill a cow to eat and if perhaps they kill one for that purpose they distribute it all to be devour at one time for they approve not the orderly eat at meal but so they may eat enough when they be hungry they care not to fast long and I have know some of these irish footman serve in England where they be nothing less than spare in the food of their family to lay meat aside for many meal to devour it all at one time these wild irish assoon as their cow have calue take the calf from they and thereof feed some with milk to rear for breed some of the rest they (@) and see they in a filthy poke and so eat they be nothing but froth and send they for a present one to another but the great part of these calf they cast out to be eat by crow and wolf that themselves may have more abundance of milk and the calf be take away the cow be so mad among they as they will give no milk till the skin of the calf be stuff and set before they that they may smell the odor of their own belly yea when these cow thus mad deny their milk the woman wash their hand in cow dung and so gentle strike their dug yea put their hand into the cow tail and with their mouth blow into their tail that with this manner as it be of enchantment they may draw milk from they yea these cow seem as rebellious to their owner as the people be to their king for many time they will not be milk but of some one old woman only and of no other these wild irish never set any candle upon table what do I speak of table since indeed they have no table but set their meat upon a bundle of grass and use the same grass for napkin to wipe their hand but I mean that they do not set candle upon any high place to give light to the house but place a great candle make of recde and butter upon the flower in the midst of a great room and in like sort the chief man (@) in their house make fierce in the midst of the room the smoke whereof go out at a hole in the top thereof a italian friar come of old into Ireland and see at Armach this their diet and nakedness of the woman whereof I shall speak in the next book of this part and the second chapter thereof be say to have cry out Armachana carnes nude vain Armach city I do thou pity thy meatesrawne and woman nakedness I trust no man expect among these gallant any bed much less featherbed and sheet who like the nomads remove their dwelling according to the commodity of pasture for their cow sleep under the canopy of heaven or in a poor house of clay or in a cabin make of the bough of tree and cover with turf for such be the dwelling of the very lord among they and in such place they make a fire in the midst of the room and round about it they sleep upon the ground without straw or other thing under they lie all in a circle about the fire with their foot towards it and their body be naked they cover their head and upper part with their mantle which they first make very wet steep they in water of purpose for they find that when their body have once warm the wet mantle the smoke of they keep their body in temperate heat all the night follow and this manner of lodging not only the mere irish lord and their follower use but even some of the english irish lord and their follower when after the old but tyrannical and prohibit manner vulgar call cosher they go as it be on progress to live upon their tenant till they have consume all the victual that the poor man have or can get to conclude not only in lodging passenger not at all or most rude but even in their inhospitality towards they these wild irish be not much unlike to wild beast in who cave a beast pass that way may perhaps find meat but not without danger to be ill entertain perhaps devour of his insatiable host. the four book chap. 1. of the german bohemian sweitzer netherlander dane polonian and italian apparel one thing in general must be remember touch the diverse apparel of diverse nation that it be daily subject to change as each commonwealth by little and little decline from the best constitution to the worst and old manner be daily more and more corrupt with new vice or as each commonwealth be by due remedy purge and reform the most rich among the german as old writer do Germany witness use of old straight apparel express to life the lineament of the whole body which kind of apparel the Schwaben or use at this day and the woman be apparel as man of which wicked custom we find at this day no remainder except the soldier wife follow the camp may perhaps somewhat offend that way they add that the under which name the roman comprehend all the german use of old to be clad in skin no doubt the german as they (@) so be at this day in their apparel constant and modest and I have almost say slovenly sure if a man observe the time they spend in brush their apparel and take out the least spot aswell at home as abroad when they come to their inn they will seem clean but if we behold their apparel so wear to proof as the nap of the cloth and that somewhat course be wear off the ground plain appear and spot with grease and wear especial the sleeve which they wear large and at table not without cause lift up with one hand while they take meat with the other lest they shall fall into the dish no doubt without offence be it speak they be somewhat slovenly and for this imputation of old lay on the german I appeal to Tacitus write to this purpose in the latin tongue the slovenly and naked german live in the same house among the same beast and he that at this day look upon their schwartz reytern that be black horseman must confess that to make their horse and boot shine they make themselves as black as collier these horseman wear black clothes and poor though they be yet spend no small time in brush they the most of they have black horse which while they painful dress and as I say delight to have their boot and shoe shine with black stuff their hand and face become black and thereof they have their foresay name yea I have hear germane say that they do thus make themselves all black to seem more terrible to their enemy I have often hear their preacher declaim against the common inconstancy in apparel but they do herein according to the art of lift which be ever most please when it task man with vice whereof they be not guilty but never with those that may be true impute for drunkenness the famous yet almost sole vice of the german be in the mean time silent pass over by they in their pulpit or else out of a guilty conscience slight reprove no doubt the german be of all other famous and great nation least expenseful in apparel whether a man consider the small price of the garment or their long last by a imperial law husbandman be forbid to wear any stuff that cost more than half a Gulden the ell and man that live by their art and hand be forbid to wear any thing that cost more than the three part of a Gulden the ell and servant to wear any ornament of gold silver or silk and gentleman to wear any gold or more than two ounce of silver and doctor of the civil law who have many privilege from the herald and be much respect in Germany and likewise knight not to wear more than two ounce of gold upon their apparel and last citizen be permit to face their garment with silk or velvet but be forbid to wear any gold or silver by the same imperall statute enact in the year 1548 noble woman that be gentlewoman be permit to wear a chain of gold worth two hundred gulden and ornament of the head worth forty gulden and the doctor of the civil law be permit to wear like chain and their wife have the same privilege with noblewoman in the statute of the year 1530 citizen wife be permit to wear gold chain of fifty gulden and silver girdle of thirty gulden and their daughter to wear ornament of ten gulden upon their head and these law be wise make to restrain that nation though by nature and custom most modest in apparel because the rich thing they be to wear be not make in the empire but to be buy with money they have not so much as woollen or linen cloth of their own but such as be course which make they that wear silk or velvet as well as other wear shirt of course cloth I do see the emperor when he mourn for his sister apparel in english black cloth who otherwise use to wear for the most part the same cloth of a watchet or some light colour seldom wear any rich apparel and the scabbard of his sword be of leather not of velvet as we use the man in Germany wear shirt band of course linen short and thick only in Prussia I observe they to wear long ruff with rebato of wire to bear they up such as our woman use which seem to i less comely because they be seldom make of fine cloth as cambric or lawn but of their own course linen such as I have often see the spaniard to wear their handkerchief be very large and wrought with silk of diverse light colour with great letter signify word as for example d. h. i. m. t. signify der her Trost that be the lord be my comfort so as they seem more like wrought saddle clothes than handkerchief many of the saxon wear thrum hat which be call Brunswick hat as most use in those part be so stiff as a sword will hardly pierce they especial with the brass hatband they wear about they and be so heavy as they lie upon the ear and make they hand down with small comeliness few wear feather in their hat yet the doctor of the civil law have the privilege to wear they and myself have see many student in the university and most coachman of Germany wear feather cost each some twelve or sixteen batzen the mention of the say shirt band use in Prussia make i remember that the citizen of Dantzke seat in that province do general wear more rich apparel than any other german and I remember that their say shirt band or ruff be little less than a quarter of a ell long and hang upon their shoulder notwithstanding they have stay to bear they up which mad fashion but not so long the english use of old and have long since lay aside the man in these part common wear silk and velvet without any decent distinction of degree and the woman seem much proud in apparel then the man I have see marry woman not of the rich sort daily wear hat of velvet though some wear also feel hat and other to wear frontlet of velvet and other wear hat have their hatband all set with pearl and many of their daughter do wear chain of pearl worth three hundred gulden yea some of these virgin have show i their chain of five hundred gulden value be the daughter of citizen and merchant as well marry as unmarried woman in the chief city of that province do wear short cloak and for the great part of silk or satin the use whereof be vulgar among they and that of changeable or light colour with petticoat and apron of like colour but not so frequent of silk and I have see virgin of ordinary rank in those city daily wear silk stocking but I return to the general discourse of the german apparel citizen and man of inferior rank wear course cloth of Germany and only the rich sort use english cloth and this cloth be common of a black or dark colour and they think themselves very fine if their cloak have a narrow face of silk or velvet the gentleman delight in light colour and when I persuade a familiar friend that black and dark colour be more comely he answer i that the variety of colour show the variety of God work and the gentleman wear italian silk and velvet of these colour but most common english cloth for the most part of yellow or green colour the saxon in stead of sword carry hatchet in their hand be very skilful in the use of they so as they will hit any small mark therewith and they wear hang dagger with massy sheath of silver or iron the gentleman and other that have the privilege to wear sword as the doctor of civil law have plain pommel to they never guild and the scabbard not except the emperor be always of leather many of the german in steed of hat wear cap line with fur and they use large stomacher of fur or lambeskinne keep their stomach very warm either for the coldness of the clime or rather because their stomach need more cherish in regard they be often oppress with excess in drink most of they wear great large breech except the vulgar Schawbon who wear such straight breech as our old man use with stocking of the same cloth fasten to they and general their doublet be make straight to the body upon which in winter time they wear jerkin line with fur few man or woman wear gold ring pearl or jewel but Bohemia yield false stone like the oriental precious stone yet of small or no value and I have see some gentleman wear these false stone and brass ring guild over the wear whereof be hold disgraceful with we at magdeburg I do see a young-gentleman have all his finger load with ring which I think to be of gold till myself see he buy a ring of three hoop for some fifteen penny in english money and so find his foolish pride the statute as I former say permit noblewdman that be gentlewoman to wear chain of gold which notwithstanding they wear very seldom and in like sort their earl vulgar call grave and their knight sometime wear gold chain make of extraordinary great link and not go more than once about the neck nor hang down further than the middle button of the doublet the german in great part measure a stranger dignity by the richness of his apparel and by his grave or to speak plain proud look citizen wife in some place wear upon their head little cap in the form of a oyster-shell and they wear short cloak reach no further than their elbow citizen daughter and virgin of inferior sort wear nothing upon their head but their hair weave with lace and so gather on the forepart of the head with the forehead stroke up plain and upon the forepart of the head the gentlewoman wear a border of pearl and all other from the high to the low common wear garland of rose which they call Crantzes for they keep rose all winter in little pot of earth whereof they open one each Saturday at night and distribute the rose among the woman of the house to the very kitchen maid other keepethem all in one pot and weekly take as many roll as they need and cover the rest keep they fresh till the next summer and the common sort mingle guild nutmeg with these rose and make garland there of only woman wear these garland in winter but in summer time man of the better sort wear they within door and man of the common sort wear they go abroad they keep rose all winter in this sort they choose the close and thick bud of all kind of rose but the damask rose best keep the smell and other kind the colour then they take a pot of earth and sprinkle some bay salt in the bottom and lay these bud several not very close one to the other in two row one above the other which do they sprinkle the same and wet all the bud with two little glass of rhenish wine and again sprinkle they with bay salt in great quantity yet such as it may not eat the leaf in like sort they put up each two row of bud till the pot be full which they cover with wood or lead so as no air can enter and then lie it up in a cold cellar where no sun come when they take out the bud they dip they in luke warm water or put they into the oven when the bread be take out which make the leaf open with the turn of the bud between two finger than they dip a feather in rhenish wine and wipe the leaf there with to refresh the colour and some do the like with rose water to renew the smell give i leave by the way to relate though out of due course that I observe woman at leipzig in like sort to keep cherry all winter after this manner they enclose some cherry in a glass so as no air can enter and then fasten the glass to some low shrub or bough of a tree so as the glass may hang in a brook run gentle now I return to my former discourse many of the say virgin have their neck-band set with spangle such as some child with we wear the marry woman wear their gown close about the breast and neck with a very short ruff about their neck such as man also wear set with poke stick as small as reed and they wear little hat upon their head the virgin in general wear linen sleeve about their arm as close as they can be make for they esteem it the great grace to have the small arm and their petticoat be guard with some ten or more fringe or lace of silk or velvet each fringe be of a different colour one from the other make the skirt thereof as variable in colour as the rainbow citizen wife put off their ruff when they go out of the house cover their neck and mouth with a linen cloth for fear of cold and they wear great heavy purse by their side with great bunch of key hang by chain of brass or silver and all general aswell marry woman as virgin go with bare leg and I have see a virgin in Saxony refuse a pair of silk stocking offer she of gift and the maid servant and marry woman of the inferior sort wear no shoe except they go out of the house and great part go also abroad bear foot the marry woman hide their naked foot with long gown but the maid servant wear short gown and gird they up into a roll some handful under the waist about their hip especial in the low part of Germany many time offend chaste eye with show their nakedness especial when they stoop for any thing to the ground and in those part of Germany the citizen wife like our little child wear red and yellow shoe and guild at the toe in general it be disgraceful to marry woman or virgin except at augsburg and some few other city to go out of door without a cloak which common be of some light stuff as grogram or the like face with some fur and at Hidelberg they never go abroad without a little basket in their hand as if they go to buy something except they will be repute dishonest the marry woman always have their head cover in some city with a piece of velvet other where with little cap of velvet silk or feel or with some like fashion according to the use of the country and very many wear such crosse-clothe or forehead clothes as our woman use when they be sick in many place the ordinary citizen wife have their gown make with long train which be pin up in the house and bear up by maid servant when they go abroad which fashion of old only great nobleman use with we and in many city aswell the marry as unmarried woman wear long farthingale hang about their foot like hoop which our woman use of old but have now change to short farthingale about their hip the bohemian be apparel much like the german and delight in green yellow and light colour but more frequent wear silk and velvet than the german Bohmerland and also false jewel of their own and many time they wear black cloth with many lace or fringe of light colour each fringe differ in colour one from the other and in respect of foreign ambassador come from all part to prage and of italian merchant frequent there the bohemian be more infect with foreign fashion than the german the marry gentlwoman attire their head like our virgin and in like sort bear up their hair on the forehead with a wire they use with the german to make their gown with train or to bear they out with long farthingale and to wear short cloak citizen wife wear upon their head large gray cap rugged like gray connie skin and form like the hive of bee or little cap of velvet close to the head of a dun colour with the hinder skirt or hinder part cut off and open and upon their leg they wear white buskin wrought with velvet at the toe but upon their arm they wear large sleeve and contrary to the german think they to be most comely the sweitzer be citizen for their nobility be long sinceroot out by popular sedition wear large round cap such as be use by our prentice and student Sweitzerland in the inn of court and together with they they wear cloak whereas with we they be only use with gown yea and sword also which seem strange to be wear with cap they wear great large puff breech gather close above the knee and each puff make of a diverse light colour but their doublet be make close to the body the marry woman cover their head with a linen coif and upon it wear such cap as the man use which be broad than we use in England and common wear a linen crossecloth upon the forehead to be brief the virgin go bare head with their baire weave up and use short cloak and aswell marry as unmarried woman as also the man be apparel like the german and affect nothing less than pride in their attire in the unite province the inhabitant be for the most part merchant and citizen the man use modest attire of grave colour and little beautify with lace or netherlands other ornament they wear short cloak of english cloth with one small lace to cover the seam and a narrow face of silk or velvet their doublet be make close to the body their breech large and fasten under the knee common of woollen cloth or else of foam light stuff or of silk or velvet they use very little lace no imbrodery yet the hollander of old account the most rude of the other province at this day increase in wealth and reputation of the state do by little and little admit luxury and their son apply themselves both to the apparel and manner of the english and French woman aswell marry as unmarried cover their head with a coif of fine Holland linen cloth and they wear gown common of some flight stuff and for the most part of black colour with little or no lace or guard and their neck ruff be little or short but of very fine linen for aswell man as woman for their body and for all use of the family use very fine linen and I think that no clown in the world wear such fine shirt as they in Holland do some of the chief woman not able to abide the extreme cold and loath to put fire under they for heat as the common use be because it cause wrinkle and spot on their body do use to wear breech of linen or silk all woman in general when they go out of the house put on a hoyke or veil which cover their head and hang down upon their back to their leg and this veil in Holland be of a light stuff or kersie and have a kind of horn rise over the forehead not much unlike the old pummel of our woman saddle and they gather the veil with their hand to cover all their face but only the eye but the woman of Flanders and Brabant wear veil altogether of some light fine stuff and fasten they about the hinder part and side of their cap so as they hang loose not close to the body and leave their face open to view and these cap be round large and flat to the head and of velvet or atleast guard therewith and be in form like our potlid use to cover pot in the kitchen and these woman aswell for these veil as their modest garment with gown close at the breast and neck and for their pure and fine linen seem to i more fair than any other netherlander as indeed they be general more beautiful I do see the king of Denmark enter a day journey in his progress towards Holsatia vulgar holst and he wear a loose gippoe of black velvet spare adorn Denmark with gold lace and in the town he wear a large broad brim felt hat with the brim in part button up but in his coach he wear a rough Brunswick hat use in the low part of Germany and have a large chain of gold hang under one arm so low as it be fold about his girdle and when he walk abroad he carry his sword upon his shoulder with the point in his hand and the hilt hang down behind he his chief courtier and his young brother be all attire in a english cloth which they call kentish cloth we call motley but much fine than that whereof we make cloakebag and of purpose make for they cost some two dollar the ell they wear gold chain so short as they reach not further than the six or seven button of their doublet but the link be great and they have a tablet of gold annex to they they carry their sword as the king do with the hilt hang over the shoulder and they wear dagger with heavy sheath of silver like those use in Saxony the king guard wear huge breech puff and of diverse colour like the sweitzer hose in general the dane be apparel like the german and especial like the saxon constant and modest and they so abhor from strange fashion as the king father late decease be report to have give the strange apparel of certain gentleman new return from foreign part to the infamous hangman that they may be despise of the gentry gentlewoman virgin go with their head bare and their hair weave and adorn with row of pearl and the marry gentlewoman go with their head cover with a fine linen coif and wear upon their forehead a french shadow of velvet to defend they from the sun which our gentlewoman of old borrow of the french and call they bonegrace now altogether out of use with we and they adorn their head with border of Gold woman as well marry as unmarried noble and of inferior condition wear thin band about their neck yet not fall but erect with the upper body of their outward garment of velvet but with short skirt and go out of the house they have the german custom to wear cloak they also wear a chain of gold like a breastplate and girdle of silver and guild at dermind the haven of Dantzke in Prussen I do see the king of Poland ready to sail into Suevia or Suecia his father kingdom of inheritance for who late the polonian dead he then wear mourn apparel namely a long black cloak of woollen cloth and a cap or low hat of black silk with narrow brim with a fall band about his neck a black doublet close to his body and large breech fasten under the knee the queen be of the house of Austria be attire like the noblewoman of Germany and be then ready to take ship her head be cover with a coif of fine linen and upon her forehead she wear a crossecloth almost down to the nose the king courtier wear two long coat the upper coat or cloak with sleeve be long than the other the skirt whereof on the right side be so fasten on the shoulder with silver button and so cast upon the left shoulder as they have their right arm altogether free and this upper coat be of english cloth face before with silk the low or inner coat be of silk or some light stuff hang down on one side to the knee on the other side double and fasten to the girdle and both coat be of light colour but without any lace of gold or silver or other ornament whatsoever they wear breech and stocking of the same cloth like those of our old man or the truse of Ireland and their shirt be of much fine linen than the german use and they wear a fine and very large linen handkerchief fasten to their girdle behind but they have no ruff nor any band of linen about their neck which be only use by some few gentleman who have live in foreign part but the (@) of their coat wear raise with a peak behind to keep the neck warm they wear extraordinary little cap hardly cover the crown of the head and in they wear some six feather not of mix or light colour nor broad as we wear they but white and narrow such or the same as be pull from capon tail the polonian shave all their head close except the hair of the forehead which they nourish very long and cast back to the hinder part of the head they carry for arm a turkish Cemeter and wear shoe of leather and also of wood both paint and both shod under the heel and toe with piece of iron make great noise as they go the gentleman wear chain of gold fold about their girdle and carry in their hand a little hammer of silver and perhaps guild and these of inferior sort one of iron the hungarian in their attire differ little from the polonian but no hungarian may wear a feather except he have do some noble act and according to the number of his brave action so many feather he may wear to witness his valour at Crakaw I do see the castellani that be keeper of castle and many gentleman ride to the court and other place in the city and the gentleman attend they go on foot before their horse with feather in their little cap and the vulgar attendant follow their horse the buttock of their horse be cover with cloth of gold or the skin of some wild beast or some like ornament and about many of their horse ear hang chain of gold or silver their bridle be guild and set with button of gold and the horseman not only wear sword by their side but also every horseman especial ride in the highway or be in foreign part upon any ambassage or in like pomp have another and some a three sword or scimitar fasten to their saddle and girth beside that both on foot and on horseback they carry a hammer in their hand the gentlewoman after the netherlander fashion cover the head with a coif of fine linen and wear a crosscloth upon the forehead and as the man so they wear no ruff or linen band about the neck but many have about their neck chain of pearl worth two hundred yea five hundred dollar and some line the collar of the upper body of their gown with fur and so cover the nakedness of the neck behind the unmarried woman wear apron of fine linen and go with bare head have their hair weave as our woman use with a narrow piece of velvet cross the midst of their head and go out of the house they cast a white veil upon their head and back but show their face open the mean sort of marry woman either wrap their head and mouth with a narrow long piece of linen or only cover their mouth with linen and wear a low hat cast a veil from it to cover the hinder part of the head and all the back and they wear loose kirtle over their other apparel which be tie behind with string the common sort of country woman cover themselves all over with linen cloth or skin of beast the prussian be tributary to the king of Poland and upon free condition acknowledge themselves subject to the crown of Poland and their attire as also language little differ from the german save that their apparel be more sumptuous but of they I have former speak in the discourse of the german attire of the italian it be proverbial say that the venetian be gown yet by night Italy go to visit their mistress wear short spanish cloak that those of Terrara and Mantua be proud in their attire with their cap set with gold button that the florentine be ridiculous yet I observe none more modest attire that those of Genoa be neat and comely in attire but wear no gown nor lace nor guard that those of Milan be decent and the neopolitan be glitter and sumptuous sure the italian in general respect the conveniency more than ornament of their apparel when they take journey they wear large boot that they may fling off be untie but such as keep they dry in all weather and to the same end they wear thick feel hat and shortfelt cloak which no rain can pierce respect the health not the ornament of their body and howsoever their apparel be soft and delicate yet they only wear cloth and stuff make at home not any bring from foreign part their garment be common of silk but seldom embroider and never lay with gold or silver lace and common of black colour and howsoever all those mix colour which we so high esteem come from thence yet be they not invent by the italian but by the factor of our merchant who lie there of purpose to feed the fantastical pride of our youth in new stuff or at least new colour and name the citizen of Genoa wear gold chain and may seem proud garment of velvet save that we must remember that they be not only merchant but gentleman and some of they prince the venetian by reason of their strict law from all antiquity restrain excess in apparel howsoever many time they wear sumptuous garment yet be they hide under their gown not to be see but by their mistress at night they make woollencloth of such last as they bequeath their gown by their last testament all the gentleman not one except wear black cloth gown button close at the neck with the sleeve put on over their doublet aswell young as old man but some under this civil gown we be rich fur and embroider garment and the senator doctor and knight we be scarlet gown with large sleeve line in winter with rich fur and their senate be no less or more glorious in public pomp than the roman senate be of old and the gentleman constant wear these gown either in singular pride to be know from other for no citizen nor any gentleman of other city wear gown or for obedience to the law or out of a old custom which the most wise magistrate permit not to be break and for the same cause all the gentleman none except wear little cap of frieze or cloth hardly cover the crown or the forepart of the head all other italian in general wear stuff cloak and common of silk in summer and cloth in winter and light feel hat with narrow brim and large breech sometime wide and open at the knee after the spanish fashion but more common tie under the knee and a loose coat or gippo but not wide and a doublet close to the body both of silk and line with silk and silk stocking also many wear jewel but as it be hide to be see only by chance last in great wisdom they care not to have rich apparel but hold it honourable to live of their own they make no fine linen and therefore use course linen both for shirt and other use of the family and common wear little fall band and many time ruff of Flanders linen sometime wrought with italian cutwork much use with we but their ruff be not so great as we and they have little skill in wash starch or smooth linen they wear very short hair as all nation do that live in hot clime the contrary vice of wear long hair be proper to the french english and scot but especial to the irish. the italian clothe very little child with doublet and breech but their breech be open behind with the shirt hang out that they may case themselves without help among other prince of Italy I do see Ferdinand the three duke of Florence who do wear a cloak of english cloth with one little lace and breech of veluct without any ornament and stocking of leather and a leather scabbard to his sword and his coach be line with old green veluct and the horse seem take out of the plough the woman in general be delight with mix and light colour the woman of Venice wear chopine or shoe three or four hand-bredths high so as the low of they seem high than the tall man and for this cause they can go in the street without lean upon the shoulder of a old woman they have another old woman to bear up the train of their gown and they be not attend with any man but only with old woman in other part of Italy they wear low shoe yet somewhat raise and be attend by old woman but go without any help of lead the woman of Venice wear gown leave all the neck and breast bare and they be close before with a lace so open as a man may see the linen which they lap about their body to make they seem fat the italian most love fat woman they show their naked neck and breast and likewise their dug bind up and swell with linen and all make white by art they wear large fall band and their hair be common yellow make so by the sun and art and they raise up their hair on the forehead in two knot horn and deck their head and uncover hair with flower of silk and with pearl in great part counterfeit and they cast a black veil from the head to the shoulder through which the nakedness of their shoulder and neck and breast may easy be see for this attire the woman of Venice be proverbial say to be grande gross rise that be tall with wood fat with rag red with paint and white with chalk the woman of Genoa be attire much after the french fashion and by reason of neighbourhood borrow diverse manner from France which be also to be understand of other city lie under the french Alps and they go abroad either alone or attend by man not by woman as in other part yea by night and early in the morning to the church which suspect fashion other italian can endure they wear net and black veil cover there with their face contrary to the french manner without which the poor woman go not abroad in general the woman of Italy for diverse city have some fashion differ from other most common but especial the wife of shopkeeper wear gown of silk and light stuff yea weave with gold and those close at the breast and neck with a stand collar and little ruff close up to the very chin and show no part naked and gentlewoman in general wear gown lose behind with a close collar hide all nakedness and with train bear up by waitingmaide and sometime with open hang sleeve the marry woman wear their head bare or cover with a fine linen coif and a hat and a veil hang down from the hinder part of the head to the back the unmarried have their head bare with their hair knot like snake and tie with gold and silver lace or else they be cover with a gold net cawl and they wear also gold chain the marry woman wear chain of pearl about the head and neck which in some place be forbid to virgin and these pearl be many time especial at Venice counterfeit and make of glass but very beautiful to the eye widow and woman that mourn cover all their head and shoulder with a black veil and upon the forehead they wear a shadow or and about their neck a white veil hang down before to their foot the country wench wear upon their head gold and silver cawle or at least seem such and straw hat and guild girdle and for the rest as other woman be delight with light colour the city virgin and especial gentlewoman cover their head face and back with a veil that they may not be see pass the street and in many place wear silk or linen breech under their gown also I have see honourable woman aswell marry as virgin ride by the high way in prince train apparel like man in a doublet close to the body and large breech open at the knee after the spanish fashion both of carnation silk or satin and likewise ride astride like man upon horse or muse but their head be attire like woman with bare hair knot or else cover with gold net cawle and a hat with a feather and many time in the city as at padua I have see courtesan in plain english whore in the time of shrove apparel like man in carnation or light colour doublet and breech and so play with the racket at tennis with young man at which time of shrove the woman no less than man and that honourable woman in honourable company go mask and apparel like man all the afternoon about the street even from Christmas holiday to the first day in Lent the woman wear man breech have they open all before and most part behind only button with gold or silver button and the courtesan make all the forepart of their gown in like manner open to avoid wrinkle last the italian use to tie themselves upon a vow for recovery of health or like cause to wear certain apparel for a time or for life and if the vow be in repentance of sin the colour be ash colour vulgar Beretino which I have see some wear for long time constant with purpose to wear they during life chap. 2 of the turk french english scottish and irish apparel the turk shave their head but only in the very crown where they leave a tuffr of hair and they do not now as Turkey of old only nourish the hair of the upper lip but all the beard grow round they cover their head thus shave with a close cap of scarlet and above it wear some twelve or twenty else of fine white cotton cloth weave into a round globe which in their tongue be call a tulbent and by some Tsalma neither do they ever uncover their head in honour to any man but salute by bend the body and lay their left hand on their right side this cap or this head as they call it be hollow and so admit air be bear up by little hoop and so cool the head yet be thick keep out the sun from pierce it and be of most fine linen be much light than our hat all the order or degree among the turk be know by the ornament of the head or by their head as they speak the azimoglane wear pyramidal cap like sugar-loves of a mingle colour and light stuff the janizares wear the say tulbent but have also a cap peculiar to their order vulgar call Zarcola which they wear go abroad into the city be a stand cap plain at the top with a hood hang down behind like that part of our french hood with a guild horn of brass upright above the forehead the janizares that be courtier wear a feather hang down from the hinder part of the head to the very heel the chauss and all degree upward to the very emperor wear the say tulbent or Cap. with a little piece of red velvet appear at the very crown upon which they set jewel and feather whereby these high order and degree in the war be distinguish like white tulbent but altogether plain be wear by inferior turk that be not soldier and they can be more provoke then by cast any spot upon their white head which they wear as a holy badge of their religion place the purity of the foul for a great part in the outward purity of the body Tulbent and garment all these tulbent be of pure white but the greek and other christian aswell subject as stranger wear shass that be stripe linen common white and blue wound about the skirt of a little cap. such a shasse myself do wear cost fifteen mcidines the persian wear such tulbent for the form but the cloth be of green colour and the turk as I think call Seriffi and by other call Hemir namely the kindred or race of Mahomet who make great show of hereditary holiness and be of singular reputation do not only wear green tulbent but all garment of the same colour yet some of they wear garment of other colour with a green mark to be know from other they say that Mahomet use to wear green garment whereupon in superstition they only permit this colour to his race and if any chance to wear a shoestring or garter of that colour by ignorance of this rite they will fly upon he and beat he with cudgel and if he still wear they will punish he more severe myself ignorant of this rite pass most part of Turkey with my doublet line with green taffata but sleep by night in my doublet and hide the silk lest they shall think i rich by great chance this error of i be never detect till I come to Constantinople where our ambassador observe it and tell i the great cruelty they use towards such as wear any green thing do much astonish i yet do I still wear the same be safe in the privilege of the ambassador house till I go into a venetian ship to sail into Italy beside these hypocrite of Mahomet race for that cause so much respect as the witness of one of they avail more than of ten common turk they have other order of religious man whereof the chief and as it be metropolitan bishop be call mophty who the emperor high respect and take counsel of he when he go to war also the cady be a chief judge of ecclesiastical cause and all these wear silk gown of sky colour blue which colour be esteem next green and proper to some such order and these religious man wear their gown long to the ground with close sleeve and their tulbent be large but flatter than other turk wear neither man nor woman of the turk wear any neck band or collar but their gown be cut close to the low part of the neck and there make fast so as all the neck be naked and the gown of man and woman little differ save that the man have they large the woman close at the breast they hate the black colour as infernal and much use by christian in general the man wear a long coat to the knee and upon it a long gown with gather sleeve hang to the calf of the leg and button at the breast and a three long gown hang behind to the ground with sleeve close to the arm they wear a girdle of silk or linen twice or thrice about the waste or of fine leather with plate of gold and silver their breech and stocking be of one piece of kersey like irish trouser but large the stocking hang loose without any garter they wear their shirt hang over their breech under which they have linen breech which they wear also by night in stead of sheet and they pull out their shirt by day lest they shall be spot by their privy part make it a point of religion to keep their garment clean last they wear red and yellow shoe of most thin leather point sharp at the toe and two finger high at the heel with piece of iron under the sole or else leather buskin and both these they put off within door sit upon the ground spread with carpet cross their naked foot like our tailor their upper gown and breech be common of english or venetian cloth and many time of satin or damask or some light stuff and their coat be loose and common line with black coney skin bring out of England and much esteem by they as be soft and cool and keep out the sun in a loose garment and also warm in a close garment thus they wear the fine cloth silk and stuff but not one be find so prodigal or ridiculous as to wear any lace and much less to cut any stuff all wear they plain and laugh at our contrary fashion they have no gloove and I remember that myself in Syria be poor attire yet be take for a great man only for wear glove they wear very large handkerchief and wrought all over with silk of light colour which they hang by their side about the girdle they use linen cloth or cotton cloth very thin and fine but of brown colour for thinness not unlike our bolt clothes but most pure and clean in which they be curious for all thing wear about the body the chief pride of the turk be in have the pummel of their cemeter or short and broad sword set with jewel which be many time counterfeit and common of small value and likewise in have good horse with bridle and saddle rich and set with like jewel I never observe any turk to wear gold ring or jewel on their finger except only some soldier in Syria who I have see wear great ring of white bone upon their thumb but the great man high esteem Christian jeweller not to wear the jewel but rather to have their treasure portable and easy to be hide the turk wear no sword in the city but only in the camp or in journey for janissary and other soldier have such authority without arm as no man dare resist they so as carry only a long and heavy cudgel in their hand one of they will therewith beat multitude of turk like so many dog yet the janissary in Syria wear at their girdle short and heavy knife like dagger the turkish woman wear smock of which fashion also the man shirt be of fine linen wrought with silk at the wrist upon the sleeve and at the skirt and a long cote of silk wrought with needlework and edge with sleeve close to the arm and at the breast with their neck naked the woman gown be much like those of the man for cloth and fashion and in like sort without lace and plain without cut and open before so as the smock be see and they wear linen breech as man by day and night or else such breech of cloth as man wear and both these open at the knee and as the man so likewise the woman have no collar of any garment but their neck be naked and the woman have pearl hang in their ear but they seldom wear shoe or flock like man but common buskin of light colour adorn with gold and silver or with jewel if they be of the rich sort or wine of great man and these they wear only abroad for at home their foot be naked and as man so they sit cross-legged upon carpet they weave up their hair in curious knot and so let they hang at length and deck the hair with pearl and button of gold and with jewel and flower of silk wrought with the needle the woman in Syria cover their head with little piece of coin money join together with thread instead of a linen coif no turkish woman that ever I observe in that vast empire at any time go forth to buy any thing or for any business of the family but when upon other occasion they go forth than they cover their head and forehead with a white veil their eye with a black ciper and muffle their mouth and neck with white linen and hide their very hand under their veil though their hand be all paint over with a red colour make of a herb which in the eastern part be hold a great ornament so as the very man in some place paint their hand also the woman over their garment be they costly or poor wear a gown of a dark colour cloth which both rich and mean woman all general use of the same kind of cloth and the same colour whensoever they go out of the door so as thus muffle and cover they can be distinguish in condition or beauty neither go they abroad in any pomp to be see nor without the leave of their husband to who and to no other at any time they show their face open and their hand unpainted except they will by immodesty procure their own danger under the neck of this gown cover all their apparel they thrust the end of their white veil hang down from the hinder part of the head yet the greekish woman wear this veil loose over that gown and this singular modesty be attribute to these woman that they blush to come into market place or public meeting or great company and be not displease to be strict keep at home last in respect of their frequent bathe and their face cover when they go abroad and so never open to the sun wind or any ill weather the turkish and greekish woman have most delicate body and long preserve their beauty the french if we respect the time of these late civil war wear light stuff and woollen cloth with a doublet close to the body and large easy breech and all thing France rather commodious for use then brave for ornament and scoff at those who come rich attire to the camp or wear long hair but if we consider their apparel before the misery of the say civil war we shall find they author to we english of wear long hair doublet with long belly to the navel ruff hang down to the shoulder and breech puff as big as a ton with all like wanton levities in time of peace gentleman wear mix and light colour and silk garment lay with silk lace and satin common race and stocking of silk or of some light stuff but never woollen or worsted which only merchant wear and embroider garment with great inconstancy in the fashion and negligent or careless which the german call slovenly because they many time go without hatband and garter with their point untrust and their doubtless unbutn the sumptuary law forbid gentleman to wear cloth or lace of gold and silver but when the king proclaim a honourable war against any fortaine prince he permit any bravery to his soldier yet so as the war end after a fit time to wear out that apparel they must return to their former attire except the king be so weak as he can give life to these law aswell man as woman common wear course linen and gentleman or servant ruffle in plain rag in general man and woman except courtier and some of the gentry wear light stuff and rather delicate then sumptuous garment and howsoever the law forbid to wear silk lace upon silk stuff yet the execution of the law be neglect they ever offend more or less according to the liberty of the time against this old law never yet abolish but rather in time wear out of respect merchant wear black garment of cloth or light stuff of silk common after a modest fashion the senator wear cloak and hat not gown and cap as we use and only the precedent and counsellor of parliament wear scarlet gown and that only at solemn time as the first day that the court sit and all the procurator daily wear gown the country people common use to wear blue cloth in loose coat and close breech with stocking hang over their shoe but they have leave this fashion and now for the most part wear close doublet and large breech with a large coat hang down to the knee all of light stuff make at home and stocking of course wool and their wife in like sort attire have their head all over-wrapped in linen in general the woman marry cover their head with a coif or net cawl the gentlewoman bear up their hair on the forehead with a wire and upon the back part of the head wear a cap of other hair than their own over their cawl and above that they wear a coif of silk line with velvet and have a peak down the forehead or else the gentlewoman and wife of rich merchant with small difference of degree wear upon their head a black veil of ciper peak at the forehead with a velvet hood hang down behind only the gentlewoman wear this hood gather and the merchant wife plain woman of inferior sort wear like hood of cloth and sometime of silk or a light stuff and some merchant wife and woman of ordinary condition wear a white coif of linen fine or course according to their condition with certain high and not very comely horn wreathe up on the forehead both man and woman late use fall band which the better sort starch and raise up with wire show their neck and breast naked but now both more common and especial in winter wear thick ruff gentlewoman and citizen wife when they go out of door wear upon their face little mask of silk line with fine leather which they always unpin and show their face to any that salute they and they use a strange badge of pride to wear little look glass at their girdle common they go in the street lean upon a man arm they wear very light gown common black and hang loose at the back and under it a upper-body close at the breast with a kirtle of a mix or light colour and of some light stuff lay with many guard in which sort the woman general be attire they wear sleeve to their gown bear out with whalebone and of a differ colour from the gown which beside have other loose hang sleeve cast backward and aswell the upperbody as the kirtle differ from the gown in colour and stuff and they say that the sleeve bear up with whalebone be first invent to avoid man familiar touch of their arm for it be relate unto i I know not how credible that by physician advice the french make issue in their arm for better health as the italian use to make they under the knee cover with a close garter of brass in France as well man as woman use rich to be adorn with jewel the man wear ring of diamond and broad jewel in their hat place upon the root of their feather the lady wear their jewel common at the breast or upon the left arm and many other way for who can contain the mutable french in one and the same fashion and they common wear chain of pearl yea the very wife of merchant wear ring of diamond but most common chain of bugell and like toy of black colour the gentleman have no plate of silver but some spoon and a salt much less have they any plate of gold but the great lord or prince eat in silver dish and use bason and ewer of silver and no other kind of plate use always to drink in glass and each several man to have a glass by himself Caesar report that the old Britan be apparel in skin and wear long hair England with the beard all shave but the upper lip now the english in their apparel be become more light than the light french and more sumptuous than the proud persian more light I say then the french because with singular inconstancy they have in this one age wear out all the fashion of France and all the nation of Europe and tire their own invention which be no less busy in find out new and ridiculous fashion then in scrape up money for such idle expense yea the tailor and shopkeeper daily invent fantastical fashion for hat and like new fashion and name for stuff some may think that I play the poet in relate wonderful but incredible thing but man of experience know that I write with historical truth that the english by God goodness abound at home with great variety of thing to be wear be not only not content therewith and not only seek new garment from the further east but be beside so light and vain as they suffer themselves to be abuse by the english merchant who nourish this general folly of their countryman to their own gain daily in foreign part cause such new colour and stuff to be make as their master send paint out of England to they teach stranger to serve our lightness with such invention as themselves never know before for this cause the english of great modesty in apparel be force to cast off garment before they be wear since it be the law of nature that every man may eat after his own appetite but must wear his apparel after the vulgar fashion except he will look like a old picture in cloth of arras I have hear a pleasant fable that jupiter send a shower wherein whosoever be wet become a fool and that all the people be wet in this shower except one philosopher who keep his study but in the evening come forth into the market place and find that all the people mock he as a fool who be only wise be force to pray for another like shower that he may become a fool and so live quiet among fool rather than bear the envy of his wisdom this happen to many wise man in our age who wear apparel of old and good fashion be by other so mock for proud and obstinate fool till at last they be force to be foolish with the fool of their time the english I say be more sumptuous than the persian because despise the golden mean they affect all extremity for either they will be attire in plain cloth and light stuff always provide that every day without difference their hat be of beaver their shirt and band of the fine linen their dagger and sword guild their garter and shoe rose of silk with gold or silver lace their stocking of silk wrought in the seam with silk or gold and their cloak in summer of silk in winter at least all line with velvet or else they daily wear sumptuous doublet and breech of silk or velvet or cloth of gold or silver so lay over with lace of gold or silk as the stuff though of themselves rich can hardly be see the english and french have one peculiar fashion which I never observe in any other part namely to wear scabbard and sheath of velvet upon their rapier and dagger for in France very notary use they in the city and ride upon their footecloath or in coach both hire and in England man of mean sort use they in the time of queen Elizabeth the courtier delight much in dark colour both simple and mix and do often wear plain black stuff yet that be a brave time of war they together with our commander many time wear light colour rich lace and embroider but the better sort of gentleman then esteem simple light colour to be less comely as red and yellow only white except which be then much wear in Court now in this time of king james his reign those simple light colour have be much use if I shall begin to set down the variety of fashion and foreign stuff bring into England in these time I may seem to number the star of heaven and sand of the Sea I will only add that the english in great excess affect the wear of jewel and diamond ring scorn to wear plain gold ring or chain of gold the man seldom or never wear any chain and the better sort of woman common wear rich chain of pearl or else the light chain of France and all these jewel must be oriental and precious it be disgraceful to wear any that be counterfeit in like manner among the better sort of gentleman and merchant few be sound who have not cupboard of silver and gold plate to the value of two hundred pound at the least and if a feast last long than one day they seldom use the same plate of silver or guild yea not only the great lord but the better sort of knight and gentleman use to eat in silver dish and whereas the french and italian use to drink in glass and have few vessel no pot or bole of silver and the german drink in pewter or stone pot have little or no plate most of the householder in England of any reasonable condition drink in silver yet howsoever the gentleman be serve with pot and bole of silver they rather delight to drink in glass of Venice only the common sort use other kind of glass in the general pride of England there be no fit difference make of degree for very bankrupt player and cutpurse go apparel like gentleman many good law have be make against this babylonian confusion but either the merchant buy out the penalty or the magistrate not inflict punishment have make the multitude of law hitherto unprofitable like (@) be the excess of all age and sex but God be thank not of all particular order for only the merchant and student of the university with great comeliness and no less neatness be apparel in light stuff or silk or cloth of grave colour and much keep their old fashion or at least be not curious addict to new the wife of merchant though little yield to other in pride or expense yet have long use and still retain a decent attire with little or no inconstancy in the fashion they wear a gown of some light stuff or silk gather in the back and gird to the body with a girdle and deck with many guard at the skirt with which they wear a apron before they of some silk or stuff or fine linen they wear upon their head a coif of fine linen with their hair raise a little at the forehead and a cap of silk or a little hat of beaver yet without fit difference of estate or condition and some wear light french chain and necklace of pearl the grave sort of citizen wear gown and cap other wear hat and cloak and their prentice cloak and cap no citizen wear any sword in the city at public meeting the alderman of London wear scarlet gown and their wife a close gown of scarlet lay with guard of black velvet husbandman wear garment of course cloth make at home and their wife wear gown of the same cloth kirtle of some light stuff with linen apron and cover their head with a linen coif and a high feel hat and in general their linen be course and make at home gentlewoman virgin wear gown close to the body and apron of fine linen and go bareheaded with their hair curious knot and raise at the forehead but many against the cold as they say wear cap of hair that be not their own deck their head with button of gold pearl and flower of silk or knot of they wear fine linen and common fall band and often ruff both starch and chain of pearl about the neck with their breast naked the grave sort of marry woman use to cover their head with a french-hood of velvet set with a border of gold button and pearl but this fashion be now leave and they most common wear a coif of linen and a little hat of beaver or felt with their hair somewhat raise at the forehead young marry gentlewoman sometime go bare head as virgin deck their hair with jewel and silk but more common they use the foresay linen coif and hat all in general wear gown hang loose at the back with a kittle and close upper-body of silk or light stuff but have late leave the french sleeve bear out with hoop of whalebone and the young marry gentlewoman no less than the virgin show their breast naked the servant of gentleman be wont to wear blue coat with their master badge of silver on the left sleeve but now they most common wear cloak guard with lace all the servant of one family wear the same livery for colour and ornament and for the rest be apparel with no less pride and inconstancy of fashion then other degree the husbandman in Scotland the servant and almost all in the country do wear course cloth make at home of gray or sky colour and flat blue cap very broad the merchant in city be attire in english or french cloth of pale colour or mingle black and blue the gentleman do wear english cloth or silk or light stuff little or nothing adorn with silk lace much less with lace of silver or gold and all follow at this time the french fashion especial in Court gentlewoman marry do wear close upper body after the german manner with large whalebone sleeve after the french manner short cloak like the german french hood and large fall band about their neck the unmarried of all sort do go bareheaded and wear short cloak with most closelinnen sleeve on their arm like the virgin of Germany the inferior sort of citizen wife and the woman of the country do wear cloak make of a course stuff of two or three colour in chequer work vulgar call Plodan to conclude in general they will not at this time be attire after the english fashion in any sort but the man especial at court follow the french fashion and the woman both in court and city as well in cloak as naked head and close sleeve on the arm and all other garment follow the fashion of the woman in Germany in Ireland the english and the english irish be attire after the english manner Ireland for the most part yet not with such pride and inconstancy perhaps for want of mean yet the english irish forget their own country be somewhat infect with the irish rudeness and with they be delight in simple light colour as red and yellow and in like sort the degenerate citizen be somewhat infect with the irish filthiness as well in lousy bed foul sheet and all linen as in many other particular but as well in diet as apparel the citizen of Dublyn most of all other and the citizen of Waterford and Galloway in some good measure retain the english cleanliness touch the mere or wild irish it may true be say of they which of old be speak of the german namely that they wander slovenly and naked and lodge in the same house if it may be call a house with their beast among they the gentleman or lord of country wear close breech and stocking of the same piece of cloth of red or such light colour and a loose coat and a cloak or three corner mantle common of course light stuff make at home and their linen be course and slovenly I say slovenly because they seldom put off a shirt till it be wear and these shirt in our memory before the last rebellion be make of some twenty or thirty else fold in wrinkle and colour with saffron to avoid lousiness incident to the wear of foul linen and let no man wonder that they be lousy for never any barbarous people be find in all kind more slovenly than they be and nothing be more common among they then for the man to lie upon the woman lap on green hill till they kill their louse with a strange nimbleness proper to that nation their say breech be so close as they expose to full view not only the noble but also the shameful part yea they stuff their shirt about their privy part to expose they more to the view their wife live among the english be attire in a sluttish gown to be fasten at the breast with a lace and in a more sluttish mantel and more sluttish linen and their head be cover after the turkish manner with many else of linen only the turkish head or tulbent be round in the top but the attire of the irish woman head be more flat in the top and broad on the side not much unlike a cheese if it have a hole to put in the head for the rest in the remote part where the english law and manner be unknown the very chief of the irish as well man as woman go naked in very winter time only have their privy part cover with a rag of linen and their body with a loose mantel so as it will turn a man stomach to see a old woman in the morning before breakfast this I speak of my own experience yet remember that the foresay bohemian baron come out of Scotland to we by the north part of the wild irish tell i in great earnestness when I attend he at the lord deputy command that he come to the house of Ocane a great lord among they be meet at the door with sixteen woman all naked except their loose mantle whereof eight or ten be very fair and two seem very nymph with which strange sight his eye be dazzle they lead he into the house and there sit down by the fire with cross leg like tailor and so low as can not but offend chaste eye desire he to set down with they soon after Ocane the lord of the country come in all naked except a loose mantle and shoe which he put off assoon as he come in and entertain the baron after his best manner in the latin tongue desire he to put off his apparel which he think to be a burden to he and to sit naked by the fire with this naked company but the baron when he come to himself after some astonishment at this strange right profess that he be so inflame therewith as for shame he dare not put off his apparel these rogue in summer thus naked bear their arm gird their sword to they by a with in stead of a girdle to conclude man and woman at night go to sleep lie thus naked in a round circle about the fire with their foot towards it and as I former say treat of their diet they fold their head and upper part in their woollen mantle first steep in water to keep they warm for they say that woollen cloth wet preserve heat as linen wet preserve cold when the smoke of their body have warm the woollen cloth chap. 3 of the german and bohemian commonwealth under which title I contain a historical introduction the prince pedigree and court the present state of thing the tribute and revenue the military state for horse foot and navy the court of justice rare law more special the law of inheritance and of woman dowry the capital judgement and the diversity of degree in family and commonwealth COnstantine the great make emperor about the year 306 the historocall introduction remove his seat from Rome to Constantinople and at his death divide the empire among his child and howsoever the empire be after sometime unite in the person of one prince for his reign yet it can never be again establish in one body but be most common divide into the eastern and western empire in the time of augustulus emperor of the west the remote country of the empire recover their liberty by the sword and barbarous nation in great army invade the empire till they possess Italy so as this emperor be force to depose his imperial dignity about the year 476. and thus the western empire cease till Charles the great king of France about the year 774 subdue the Lombard and be at Rome salute emperor of the west by pope Leo the three and the prince of Italy from which time the empire of the east and west of old divide by inheritance among brother and kinsman have no more any mutual right of succession but begin to be several govern history write that Charles the great king of France be descend of the german and that all Gallia transalpina that be beyond the Alps and upper Germany as far as Hungary be by a common name call France only divide into eastern and western France and the diverse nation of Germany former govern by their king and duke be at this time first unite under this Charles the great about the year 911. Conrade the first jon to the duke of franconia a large province of Germany be first out of the race of Charles the great salute emperor of the west by the prince of Germany though Charles the Simple and other of the race of Charles the great still reign France to the year 988 yet with less reputation than their progenitor have and trouble with many confusion thus Germany divide itself from France draw to itself the empire of the west whereof in our age it retain rather the shadow then the old glory four duke of Saxony succeed Conrade in this empire and in the time of Otho the three duke of Saxony and emperor contrary to the former custom whereby the emperor succeed by right of blood or the last testament of the decease emperor or by the consent of the prince of Germany the election of the emperor be in the year 984 establish hereditary to seven prince of Germany call elector by a law make by the emperor and the pope from that time the empire have remain in Germany with free election yet so as they most common therein respect the right of blood in which respect the house of Austria have long continue in the possession of the empire and the emperor of Germany for many age by this right govern Italy and receive their crown at Rome till weary and wear out by the treachery of the pope and force to bear the public burden upon their private revenue they be make unable to support their former dignity for these cause of Habsburg of the house of Austria choose emperor in the year 1273 first lay aside all care of foreign matter then the riches of the emperor daily decrease and the riches of inferior prince no less increase the emperor in process of time for great sum of money sell liberty and absolute power to the prince and duke of Italy and Germany yea their very right of invest to the prince of Italy most of the city in netherlands and all the canton of the sweitzer be of old subject to the german emperor till by the dissension between they and the pope they find mean to gain their liberty of old ninety six great city thus make free still acknowledge the emperor in some sort but after many of they league with the sweitzer and netherlander quite forsake the emperor many of the rest and many less city either pawn to prince for money borrow or give to prince for their good service to the emperor in their war become subject to diverse prince by the emperor consent so as at this day there be only sixty city all seat in Germany which be call free and imperial city have absolute power within themselves and howsoever these in a sort acknowledge the emperor their chief lord yet they little or not at all fear or respect his weak power hitherto the roman bishop not endure a superior lord first cast the emperor of the east out of Italy and after by all mean weaken their power till Mahumet the second emperor of the turk about the year 1453 swallow that empire within his foul jaw hitherto the say bishop that they may reign alone sometime bewitch the barbarous king which have destroy the empire of the west and then reign in Italy for religion sake to promote the church of Rome and at other time oppress they with open treachery till they have confer the kingdom of Lombardy and the empire of the west upon Charles the great king of France hitherto the same bishop for the same cause have trouble the empire of the west with civil dissension till at last Italy as I say have buy liberty of the emperor and the say german emperor contain themselves at home for no emperor after the say of Habsburg but only Lodowick the bavarian do ever lead any army into Italy they now think good to rage no more against this deject empire but rather to cherish it convert themselves whole to bring all christian king under their yoke and now the turkish emperor begin to threaten ruin to the german empire and in very Germany the pope stage where they have play their bloody part by continual raise of civil war the reformation of religion begin fresh to spring and to pull the borrow plume of the pope therefore the emperor from that time to this our age have be whole busy in resist the turk and compose the domestical difference of religion and from the same time forward the court of Rome be continual distract with the faction of France and Spain till the pope skilful to use the ambitious discussion of prince to their own profit and greatness make they all subject to the roman yoke and the king on the contrary labour nothing more then to have the pope on their party at who beck all Christendom be govern to which end they give large bribe to the cardinal who have now assume to themselves the election of the pope to conclude the pope to make their own power transcendent keep the power of the prince in equal balance by sow dissension among they and favour now one now the other party till for scare of the reform religion now also spring in France they can no long keep this equality but be force to forsake the king of France distract with civil war and to advance the king of Spain as protector of the church who client at last get the power to govern all thing in Rome at their pleasure and the Spaniard at this time distract abroad with the french and english war and besiege at home with the power of the jesuit and religious man seem less to be fear by the roman in that respect as likewise the king of Spain doubt not to maintain the awful authority of the pope which they know must always be favourable to their design as well for the protection which they give to the roman church against the reform religion as for that the massy gold of Spain bear so great sway in the colleague of the cardinal that by strange success the pope less incline to the spanish faction be soon take away by untimely death to omit many other I will only mention pope sixtus who live happy in that chair so long as he favour Spain but assoon as he be think to decline from that faction and when he see a white mule present he for the tribute of the neapolitan kingdom be say to weep that so little a mule shall be give for so great a kingdom he live not long after but sudden vanish away at Rome be two image call Pasquin and upon which libel use to be fix and of late when the pope by the mediation of the king of France have make peace with the venetian contrary to the like of the king of Spain a white sheet of paper be fix on Pasquin and another demand what that paper mean be fix on and a three paper be fix on Pasquin answer that the clean paper be for the pope to make his last will and testament as if he can not live long have offend the spanish faction yet in our age the king of France after the civil war appease begin to recover their former power in the roman court but I leave these thing as somewhat stray from my purpose and return to the affair of Germany in the say family of Austria the western empire have grow old and weak the house of Austria by little and little from that time to this our age for howsoever the emperor Charles the five of the say family heir to eight and twenty kingdom in respect he be bear at gant in netherlands and so repute a german be choose emperor in the year 1519 by the elector reject the king of France Francis the first as a stranger and at that time the power of this emperor seem fearful to the italian at the first blush yet the pope of Rome in the triumuirall war of England France and Spain do with such be support the weak part and by contrary motion in one and the same cause so favour now one now the other side and so dispense with the break of oath on the
