spare not the inhabitant more than stranger yea in some case favour stranger more than the inhabitant as in debt which a stranger can stay to recover by long process myself have a suit for money at Lindaw my advocate will by no mean take any fee of i and the judge give i right with great expedition in criminal offence they never have any pardon from court which be common in foreign kingdom but the punishment be know by the fact so the malefactor be apprehend for all hope of safety be in flight yet I deny not that favour be often do in the pursuit for since only the sergeant can apprehend there be no place where more malefactor escape by flight in the city of Lubeck most honour for justice the common report be that the very judge and senator have late wink at a gentleman break of prison and flight with his keeper who be imprison for a murder they can neither execute without great offend the king of Denmark nor otherwise set free without scandal of Iustice a man suspect of any crime or accuse by one witness be draw to torture yet be never condemn upon any probability till himself confess the fact which confession be easy extort because most man have rather die then endure torment so as many time innocent man have be after know to have perish by their own confession as with we sometime innocent man have be know to die be find guilty by a jury of twelve swear man and because it can be that the judgement of man shall not often err hence it be that the civil lawyer have a strange yet good say that a mischief be better than a inconueniency namely that it be better one innocent man shall die by trial than many nocent person shall escape for want of trial in Germany not only man but woman also be accuse be put to torture and for diverse great crime the law judge they to death with exquisite torment and because they can hardly be endure with christian patience lest the condemn shall fall into despair the very preacher when they have hear their confession and settle their mind in true faith by rare example of too great charity permit and advise that they be make drunken to stupefy their sense so as thus arm they come forth with more bold than holy mind and look and seem not to feel unsufferable torment of death near Lindaw I do see a malefactor hang in iron chain on the gallows with a mastiff dog hang on each side by the heel so as be starve they may eat the flesh of the malefactor before himself die by famine and at Frankford I do see the like spectacle of a jew hang alive in chain after the same manner the condemn in Germany lose not their good but only in case of treason against their absolute lord but in Bohemia the good of the condemn fall to the emperor as he be king of Bohemia in the territory belong to the king and to the prince and gentleman in the territory whereof they be absolute lord as they be all in their own land in Germany courtier and student of university have their proper judge and prison so as by singular privilege they may not be try in any other court. and of old the student of many university have such privilege at this day not full allow as for murder they can not be punish further then with expulsion in Germany they have a custom to give a condemn man to a virgin that desire he for her husband but according to the circumstance of the crime they grant or deny the same the office of the hangman be hereditary so as the son can refuse to succeed his father and of late the hangman son of hamburg be a student and learned if not a graduate in the university of Basil after his father death be call home by the senate of hamburg and force to do his father office which be most ignominious but of great profit for the german hold it reproachful to take off the skin of any beast die of itself so as the hangman do that office have the skin for his labour the german be so superstitious as they think it a great reproach to touch the head or body of any put to death and think it most ridiculous for any man to salute the hangman or speak courteous to he and esteem it a foul fault to eat or drink with they or any of his family therefore the hangman and those of their family who help they in their office and succeed they have no child do all wear a green cap or some apparent mark by which they may be know or at least be tie to profess their quality when they come into any company leave any man shall offend in the former kind and in public tavern they have table proper to they at which the base body will not sit for any reward if they perform not their office with dexterity they fear to be stone by the people who rage many time in that case they have hardly escape but be expert in do their office and have most sharp sword they common show great dexterity in behead many at one time and as it be in a moment they be common thirsty of blood so as the common report be that the hangman of Torge behead some of his companion with the sword of justice because they will not pledge he when they be so full drunken as they can no more whereupon the sword be take from he and be to this day keep in the senate-house and only deliver to he at time of execution and that this rascal can not live a week without drink the blood of some beast beside at breme not long before this time forty freebooting soldier be behead at one time and the hangman have fail in give a foul wound to the first man execute and have with much difficulty appease the people anger for the same he present drink some of the man blood that be dead and after he have fetch a frisk or two behead all the rest with strange dexterity as it be in a moment of old among the german manslaughter be punish by a mulct of cattle but no man escape death for adultery at this day as after will appear they punish mankiller more severe and adultery in most place be death and in no part of Germany free from severe punishment not only the free city of the empire have the privilege of the sword or capital justice grant to they by emperor but also many city subject to inferior prince have that privilege grant by some of their lord and those city that have it not yet upon accident of capital offence obtain it for the time by petitionary letter at court so as the prince permit justice the city give sentence and see execution do in the place where the crime be commit and present after the fact never use as we do to send judge from county to county at set time of the year for casual manslaughter or by chance medley as we term it the civil law give arbitrary punishment but the law of Saxony punish it with a certain and express mulct namely of one Wehrgeld and by the civil law not only the principal but every one that be accessary pay the whole mulct whereas by the saxon law if it be not know which of they kill he all joint pay but one mulct kill in sudden anger which we call manslaughter be punish with behead through all Germany and Bohemia and that without delay for if the offender be apprehend he shall within few hour or next day be behead and put in the same coffin with he that he kill and so both be bury with one funeral pomp and in the same grave and if upon escape the manslayer live within the confine of the empire whensoever his fact be know he shall be send back to the place where he commit it contrary to the custom of Italy where the prince cherish or at least give safe abode to the banish man of the next country only I must except the lord and gentleman of Bohemia who upon capital offence be not present judge but be refer to the next parliament in free city I have observe this form of judgement and execution the judge sit before the tribunal cover with black cloth and the senator and consul sit upon a bench above he and this place of judgement be common in a porch or under the senate-house have one side all open towards the market place then the crier who carry the sword before the judge call out the accuser and the hangman come forth accuse the malefactor which do the crier lead the malefactor before the tribunal where he be again accuse and confess the fact according to his confession former make either in torture or before the senator appoint to examine he then the judge give sentence and break his white rod this do the hangman repeat the sentence in the market place and present the malefactor be bring forth to be behead this manslaughter in sudden sury be very frequent among the german by reason of their excessive drink in the city of hamburg I observe thirty seven to be thus kill in the space of six week and only three of the manslayer to be behead the rest escape by flight and at prage in Bohemia I observe fifteen servant of the polonian ambassador whereof many be gentleman and thirteen bohemian and german to be wound to death in their cup within the space of three week all the manslayer escape except one poor clown who be execute it be true that post-horse be keep for the sergeant to pursue malefactor yet they slow follow gentleman or those that have good friend howsoever they will soon apprehend a stranger or a poor offender neither use they earnest to pursue any except they be hire by the friend of he that be kill or be otherwise terrify by the magistrate for combat in Germany read the precept of patience in the chapter of precept be the second chapter of this 3 part. here I will only say that in combat very few or no germane be kill few hurt and that light which I rather attribute to their peaceable nature not apt to take thing in reproach then to their severe law I have say that manslayer die without hope of pardon if they be apprehend but otherwise the german have no severe law to punish injury in which justice the sweitzer of all nation excel without which law no capital punishment can keep man from revenge especial in a warlike nation and unpatient of reproach only at augsburg I remember severe law make to keep the garrison soldier from combat where they have a plat of ground to which they call one another to fight upon injury but it serve more to make show than proof of their valour for a soldier wound another pay four gulden he that draw his sword though he draw no blood pay two gulden he that upon challenge and the great provocation kill another be banish and the magistrate give such real satisfaction to the wrong by deed or word as they may with reputation forbear revenge yea he that do a wrong be bind under great penalty that he himself shall present make it know to the magistrate crave pardon and submit himself to punishment howsoever the wrong never complain at prage in Bohemia manslaughter commit by gentleman against stranger and those of mean condition be much more frequent because gentleman can only be judge in parliament which be not often call and be then try by gentleman who be partial in the common cause and common acquit they or delude justice by delay otherwise the bohemian punish manslaughter murder robbery and like crime as the german punish they by the civil law the punishment of a boy for manslaughter be arbitrary but he be not subject to the cornelian law or capital punishment except he be capable of malice by the law of Saxony a boy for manslaughter be punish by the foresay mulct if he be capable of malice otherwise he be subject to no punishment and in like sort if he deprive one of the use of any member but in custom if he be seventeen year old he may be and be common put to death by the civil law the punishment of real and verbal injury be arbitrary and as many wound as be give so many be the punishment but by the law of Saxony he that strike another so as he lose the use of a member be punish by a mulct of money uncertain which be give always to he that be maim and if he die not a thousand wound or maim be punish only with one mulct except they be do at diverse time and place in which case several mulct be inflict always understand that these judgement be give where the offender be civil accuse for if these wound be give of set malice and if he be capital accuse he shall die according to the circumstance which the german much regard thus at Lubeck a man be behead for strike a citizen in his own house and in the way from Stoade to breme I do see a sad monument of a wicked son who hand first and then his head be cut off for strike his father he that kill a man of set malice and like heinous murderer have all their bone break upon a wheel and in some case their flesh be pinch off with hot burn pincer and they that kill by the highway be in like sort punish and many time for great crime the malefactor some few day before the execution of judgement be nail by the ear to a post in a public place that the people may see they after the execution the bone and member of the malefactor be gather together and lay upon the wheel which be set up in the place of execution common where the crime be commit for eternal memory of his wickedness with so many bone hang on the side of the wheel as he commit murder or like crime and myself have number sometime eighteen often fourteen bone thus hang for memory of so many murder or like crime commit by one man these mark long remain and cross set up in place where murder be commit though the murderer escape by flight make passenger think these crime to be frequent in Germany yet the highway be most safe and the nature of the people abhor from such act which be never commit by gentleman but only by rascal against footman in the highway and those that dwell in solitary house yet the severity of punishment and the more fierce nature of the german retain some kind of fierceness from their old progenitor make such as be thus give over to wickdnes to be more barbarous and unmerciful and when they have once do ill to affect extremity therein to conclude I have say that the law of Saxony condemn a man to death who threaten to kill another though he never do the act by the civil law difference be make between a day and a night thief because we may not kill he that steal by day but may kill he that rob by night if we can spare he without danger to ourselves by the law of Saxony he that by night steal so much as a little wood shall be hang but steal that or like good by day shall only be beat with rod in the civil law it be doubtful whether theft be to be punish with death or no and most common it conclude that only theft deserve not death if it be not accompany with other crime but the law of Saxony express condemn a thief to be hang if he steal above the value of five hungarian ducat of gold or under that value to be beat with rod and to be mark with a burn iron in the ear or cheek and forehead and so to be banish and howsoever general a thief may not be hang by the civil law yet in some case it condemn he to be hang by the law of Saxony the thing steal must be restore to the owner and may not be detain by the magistrate and they who witting receive steal good or give any help to thief be subject no less than the thief to the punishment of hang in Germany there be very few robbery do by the highway and those only upon footman for they that pass by coach or horse carry long pistol or carbiner and be well accompany but if any robber affaile in respect of the severe punishment they common kill in Germany they who be hang for simple theft hang in iron chain upon the gallows till they rot and consume to nothing but in Bohemia after three day they be cut down and bury I do see one that have steal less than five gold gulden whip about the town one that consent be lead by his side for ignominy but not whip I do see another small offender lead to the gallows with a condemn man that he may beware by that example and I have see other for steal under the say value put in a basket and thrice duck in the river for a warn upon the first fault and I have often hear they tax our english justice for hang those that steal above the value of thirten penny halfpenny which will hardly buy a rope by the civil law he that find any thing and for gain keep it be guilty of theft for he ought to make it public know and to restore it be own or other wise if he be poor to keep it if he be rich to distribute it among the poor by the law of Saxony it be a thievish thing not to make public know any thing that be find but he that so do shall not suffer death or any corporal punishment because he do not of purpose take it away but if he that lose it do cry it in the church or marketplace then if it be more than the value of five shilling he be think worthy to be beat with rod or to endure such arbitrary punishment according to the value of the thing find by the civil law he that cut down tree secret shall pay the double value but by the law of Saxony the mulct be according to the value by the civil law they that steal the necessary belong to husbandry shall restore four fold and also incur infamy but one law of Saxony condemn they to have their bone break with wheel and another law make the punishment arbitrary the civil law confiscate good for which custom be not pay but the law of Saxony impose fine aswell upon those which pay not custom and duty as upon those that pass not the beat way where they be pay but go some by way to defraud the prince by the civil law sacrilegious person be behead but by the law of Saxony their bone be break upon the wheel and mark be set up according to the number of their offence in that kind by the civil law no offender may be burn in the forehead because the face may not be dissigure as create to the similitude of God but in Saxony those which be beat with rod or banish be also many time mark by be burn in the hand or by cut off their ear or by pull out their eye or by be burn in the cheek so as the hair may not cover the mark but it may be manifest to stranger in foreign part yet the interpreter of that law think at this day that offender can not be so punish by that law and that a thief ought not so to be mark by the civil law witch do any act whereupon a man die be to be behead but by the law of Saxony they be to be burn yet by a late statute of the elector they be sometime behead for you must understand that in all place the provincial law be daily increase by new statute of prince and by the law of Saxony a witch have do no hurt by that art be punish arbitrary and the german credible report that there be many witch in the country lie upon the baltic sea and especial upon the northern side thereof as in Lapland be part of the kingdom of Suetia and that in those place they have general meeting and college of witch who will tell any man what his friend do at anytime in the remore part one of they fall down as in ecstasy and when he come to himself relate the particular thereof and that they ordinary sell wind to the mariner to carry they out of the haven to the main sea in Germany those that set house on fire either hire thereunto or of there ownemalice and also witch use to be burn or if their crime be heinous use to be put to death with a (@) iron on spit thrust into their hinder part coin of counterfeit money be by the law to die in boil lead by the civil law the good of a banish man may be seize to repair any loss but it be not lawful for any man to kill he neither be he infamous but by the law of Saxony he that be banish by the empire may be kill because he break the peace and after a year banishment he be infamous always understand that he be lawful banish by the civil law a traitor to his country be to be burn to death but by the law of Saxony his bone be brokn upon the wheel and by custom many torment be in some crime add to this punishment by the civil law he that steal a virgin widow or nun and all that help he in that rape be behead but by the law of Saxony beside the behead of the offender the place be to be lay waste where the force be offer and the beast to be kill that help to do the force as the horse which carry they away yet this be not observe but in practice only he be put to death that offer the force of old the woman of Germany be wont to purge themselves from suspicion of adultery by the combat of champion or by tread on share of hot burn iron with their naked foot with out take any harm and this purgation shall still be observe neither be it abrogate in Saxony but only be vanish by difuse and the german have not only of old be severe punisher of breach in wedlock so as it be lawful for the husband to expel his adulterous wife out of his house before all his neighbour with her body naked and her hair shear and so to beat she with rod through the street but also even to this day the chastity of wife through the severity of the law against the incontinent be no where so preserve as in Germany if a marry person lie with one that be unmarried aswell on the man as the woman side the marry party be put to death and the unmarried be punish by the purse and with ignominy and if both party be marry boh die and our age have see two notable example of this justice in Germany one of a duchess who by authority of her husband and of her own brother be for this crime force to drink poison secret for preserve of all their honour the other of another duchess who be brick up in a most narrow room have a hole in the wall by which she receive her meat to prolong her miserable life while her husband have another wife and live with she in the same castle in which she thus languish in most place of Germany this sin be punish no less than with death yet in some place and upon some circumstance as of a man have a old and barren wife the delinquent sometime escape with a mulct of money and otherwhere the judgement be draw out with delay of the suit to spare the party without manifest breach of the Law in Bohemia adultery be also punish with death in Germany I do see a poor knave hang and rot on the gallows be condemn to that death for have two wife at one time in two several city and I do see another behead for lie withhi wife sister in civil cause I observe these laudable custom in Germany namely that in many court they that go to law lie down a caution or pledge which he lose civil judgement who in the end of the trial be find rash and unjust to have sue the other that the fee of lawyer be limit and that ica or impertinent speech be punish and they be tie to speak nothing that be not to the purpose of old no beauty age nor riches help a deflower virgin to get any husband at any time and no doubt virgin to this day be no where so careful of their good name as in Germany no where virgin more modest behave themselves no where virgin live to so ripe year before they be marry as in Germany at Wittenberg I do see harlot punish by stand at the altar with a torch light in their hand and by be whip with rod while many drum be beat and bason tinckle about they at Heidelberg I do see a harlot put in a basket and so duck into the river Neccar and because she whoop and hollow as in triumph when she rise out of the water she be for that impudence duck the second time at prage in Bohemia howsoever harlot be there as common as in Italy and dwell in street together where they stand at the door and by wanton sign allure passenger to they yet I do see some man and woman of the common sort who for simple fornication be yoke in cart and therewith draw out of the city the filth of the street but while the bohemian thus chasten the pooter sort I fear the great fly escape their web in Germany at the time of public fair after the sound of a bell it be free for debtor harlot and banish people to enter the city but they must have care to be out of the territory before the same bell sound again at the end of the fair they be otherwise subject then to the Law at leipzig I do see a harlot take after this second sound of the bell who have be former banish with two of her fore-finger cut off and she not for incontinency but by the law of banishment be next day behead while I live in the same city it happen that a virgin of the better sort be with child and cunning conceal it be surprise with the time of birth in the church upon a Sunday and silent bring forth the child in her pew or seat cover it with rush be dead which be unknown to all in the body of the church only some young man sit in a rood or loft with the musician perceive the fact and accuse she for murder the child in the mean time she go home from the church in the company of the other virgin without any show of such weakness and after upon the say accusation be imprison the report be that she shall be judge to death after the old law mention by the poet Propertius namely be sew in a steke with a live cat in steed of a ape and a live cock snake and dog and so drown in the river with they but delay be use in the judgement and her honourable friend make intercession for she and the murder of her child be not prove when I leave the city after six month she remain in prison and it be not know what will become of she aswell in Germany as Bohemia bastard be exclude from public profession of liberal or mechanical art only they may exercise they in the house of private gentleman in which course of life as servant they common live but never in open shop all graduate in university take a oath that they be beget in lawful matrimony and if any man ignorant shall marry a woman great with child howsoever the child be bear in marriage yet it shall inherit nothing from the husband bastard can be surety for any imprison or delinquent man nor enjoy the extraordinary benefit of the law and be commone name of the city or town where they be bear for a mark of ignominy not after any man surname but the public notary by privilege grant to they from emperor and pope have power to make their posterity legitimate in the low part of Germany which be all name Saxony of old a debtor shall not be receive into prison except the creditor allow the jailor two penny by the day to give he bread and water and after a year imprisonment if the debtor take his oath that he be not able to pay he shall be set free yet the creditor have still his right reserve upon his yearly wage for his labour and upon his gain by any art or trade and upon any good whatsoever he shall after possess and before any debtor be imprison the magistrate give he eighteen week time to pay his debt and commit he not till that time be past and in some place the debtor line at his own expense and shall be tie to pay his creditor charge if he be able to do it in some place especial at Lubeck I have observe that stranger be creditor have more favour than the creditor of the same city against a citizen debtor because stranger by reason of their traffic and have homeward can well expect the delay of suit in which respect their debtor shall present be imprison whereas mutual among the citizen they give the foresay or like time of payment before they will imprison they debt without specialty be try by oath in Bohemia the debtor be imprison present and maintain themselves not be release till the creditor be satisfy in Germany if any man draw other man money into his hand and be able yet pay not his debt he be guilty of capital punishment but that ingenious and honest nation have few or no such bankrupt by the law of Saxony he that deceive by false weight and measure be to be whip with rod the emperor of old grant the privilege of coin money to many prince and free city and the emperor in the diet or parliament of the year 1500 command all prince person and body so privilege to send their counsellor to he at nurnberg and that in the mean time all coin shall cease under the penalty to lose the privilege of coin in the same place the year 1559 many law be make for coin money whereof I will relate some few first the weight and purity of the mettle be prescribe together with the inscription to be set upon the coin then it be decree that after six month no strange money shall be currant whereof many be particular name that all foreign gold shall after the same time be forbid except the spanish single and donble ducat the Portugal crown with the short cross the crown of Burgundy netherlands France Spain and Italy to each of which piece a certain value be set moreover it be decree that counterfeit coin or melt shall be punish according to the quality of the offence that uncoyned gold and silver shall be deliver by each man into the mint of his own prince that it shall be lawful to goldsmith for exercise of their trade and no more to melt gold and silver and to divide it into part so as they export none of it that no man shall sell or pawn the privilege of coin heretofore grant he from the emperor and that like privilege hereafter to be grant shall be of no force without certain condition there prescribe other law of coin I have former set down in the chapter of coin Germany have few or no beggar the nation be general industrious except leprous man who live in almshouse and stand far off beg of passenger with the sound of a bell or of a wooden clapper but no man deny alm to he that beg they have small brass money of little value the law forbid any to beg but those that be lame and charge magistrate to bring up their child in manual Arts. the law wise provide against all fraud in manual art and in trade and since no trade can do more hurt then the apothecary for the preservation of health or the less hurt of the sick the law provide that their shop be yearly visit and purge of all corrupt drug which the visiter see burn the german free permit usury to the jew who at Franckfort at prage in the province of moravia and in many place under prince of the papacy have city or at least street to dwell in where they live separate from christian and grind the face of the poor with unsatiable avarice for they take fifty in the hundred by the year with a pawn of gold or silver and one hundred in the hundred by the year with a pawn of apparel or household stuff never lend any thing without a good pawn but the german among themselves can by the law take more than five or fix in the hundred for a year use yet among christian there want not some who use both the name and help of the jew to put out their money with great gain of old among the german with out respect to last will and testament the the law of inheritance son lawful beget succeed alone in the inheritance and for want of they first brother then uncle Caesar in his commentary write that the field be yearly divide by the magistrate no man have fee or inheritance proper to he lest husbandry shall take away their warlike dispotion or they shall become covetous and add that they love vast solitude upon their confine as if no people dare dwell near they or at least to the end they may live more safe from sudden incursion of enemy at this day all barbarousness be abolish they succeed according to the law in the fee and inheritance of their parent and kinsman and affect peace as much as any other nation but they trust not so much to solitude or the naked breast for defence from their enemy as in strong fort and well fortify city by the civil law as the son so the nephew or son son represent his father succeed in land grant by fee by the law of Saxony only the son succeed exclude the nephew and if there be no son the fee return to the Lord but howsoever the old interpreter have so determine yet the late interpreter judge it most unequal so to exclude the nephew so interpret the statute of the saxonicall law as they make the fee grant to the father and his child to extend to the nephew or the son of any his son so as the son can exclude they by the feudatory civil law brother and collateral cousin succeed in the fee of the father sometime to the seven degree sometime infinite for the interpreter extend the succession of the right line without end but the succession of the collateral line only to the seven degree but in the law of Saxony collateral kinsman have no right of succession in the fee except they have it by right of joint investiture these law differ in number the degree for the saxon make the first degree in cousin-germans by the father side namely the son of two brother and the second degree in the son of two cousin-germans whereas in the civil law cosengerman be in the four degree of consanguinity by the civil law brother divide a fee prejudice not themselves in mutual succession so as two brother divide and after one of they die without a son the part of he that be dead shall return to he that live but by the law of Saxony the succession depend upon use it in common from which if they depart they be judge to have renounce the mutual right of succession so as one brother die after the division the other have no right to his part therefore by custom one brother use to retain the fee and to satisfy his brother in money and good common with condition that this money and good shall be bestow in get another fee by the civil law if the vassal have build house or bestow money in better the old house the lord of the fee shall either satisfy the heir according to the estimation of the expense or shall suffer he to carry away the house but by the law of Saxony the fee lie open to the lord with all the house build one case except by the civil law if the vassal die without heir male before the month of march the fruit of that year pertain to the lord but if he die after the calends of march before the month of August the fruit pertain to the heir but by the law of Saxony if the vassal live past the day when the rent be due the heir shall enjoy the fruit of his labour by the civil law if the fee upon the death of the lord fall to all his son either equal or otherwise the investiture must be desire of all but by the law of Saxony it suffice to ask it of one son of the dead Lord by the civil law a servant or a clown may be invest in a fee which do the clown become a gentleman if the nature of the fee require it but by the law of Saxony only he that be bear of the knightly order by father and mother be capable of a fee though custom prevail to the contrary by the civil law if the vassal leave a heir he can refuse the inheritance and retain the fee but must hold or refuse both but by the law of Saxony he may retain the fee leave the inheritance and in that case be not bind to satisfy creditor by the civil law a man may give or sell his land to a prince or prelate and take it again of he in fee but by the law of Saxony except the prince or any buyer whatsoever retain the land a year and a day before he grant it back in fee he that give or sell it or his heir have right to recover the land by the civil law if the vassal have lose his horse or arm in war he have no remedy against the lord because he be tie by duty to help he but by the law of Saxony the vassal be not tie to serve the lord any long except he repair his loss and the lord be tie to pay a certain ransom for his captive vassal by the civil law the lord or the father of the vassal be dead the vassal be bind to ask investiture within a year and a month but by the law of Saxony either of they be dead he must ask it without delay by the civil law the vassal must serve the lord at his own charge but by the law of Saxony he be only tie to serve he six week and by custom the lord must feed he and his horse or give he a competent allowance by the civil law the pupil be excuse from the lord service but by the law of Saxony the tutor must serve in his place by the civil law a fee fall to a monk belong to the monastery during his life but by the law of Saxony it return to the Lord and touch the succession of monk in any inheritance whatsoever though by the civil law they be account dead yet the same law admit they to succeed with the child of the intestate father but by the law of Saxony they be not capable of any inheritance yet this law seem unjust to the pope it be correct so as their succession be give to the monastery but in our age the judge have pronounce a monk himself to be capable of inheritance notwithstanding the papal law give his inheritance to the monastery and that because the monkish vow be against the word of God the person of monk be free to take inheritance by the civil law the vassal be bind to accompany his lord when he go with the king of the roman to take the crown of the empire at Rome but by the law of Saxony he may redeem this service with pay the ten part of his yearly rent and since the golden bulla have restrain this service to twenty thousand foot and four thousand horse and the payment of they have since be equal divide through Germany allow a horseman twelve gulden and a footman four gulden by the civil law he forfeit his fee who cut down fruitful tree or pull up vine but by the law of Saxony it be free to the possessor to make the land or house of the fee better or worse at his pleasure by the civil law if the lord deny investiture it must be ask often and humble but by the law of Saxony if the vassal ask it thrice and have witness that the lord deny his service afterward so he have good witness thereof he and his heir shall possess the fee without any bond of service and his heir be not bind to ask investiture by the civil law if two lord of one vassal shall both at one time require his service he be bind to serve the most ancient lord but by the law of Saxony the person of the vassal must serve the lord that first call he and he be to pay a sum of money as the ten pound to the other by the aforesaid law and daily practice it appear that the territory of prince according to the old feudatory law either fall to the elder son who give his brother yearly pension or according to his inheritance recompense they with money or other land or else be equal divide among the brother yet some fee be also feminine and fall to the daughter and their husband and some may be give by testament but other as those of the elector for want of heir male be in the emperor power who with the consent of the prince of the empire common give they to the husband of the daughter or to the next heir by affinity if there be none of consanguinity I have hear of credible man that the dukedom of Austria first fall to the son then to the cousin and for want of they to the daughter the duke of Wineberg and the duke of coburg son to Frederick duke of Saxony and elector but deprive of his electorship by the emperor Charles the five for his religion do equal divide their father inheritance the electorship be give away the inheritance whereof can not be divide but I do observe that the brother to that duke of coburgs son be unmarried have no inheritance subdivide to he which be say shall be do when he take a wife the count Palatine of the Rhine not long before this time decease do divide all the inheritance with his brother duke Casimere except the palatinate which with the style and dignity of elector belong to the elder son but they say that many time the knight and chief man of the province will not for the public good lest the prince power shall be weaken permit this division among their prince but force the young brother to take money or yearly pension for the part of his inheritance and that this division be also many time forbid by the die father last testament and they seem to do this not without just cause since the great number of child often oppress diverse principality thus 17 brother all prince of anhalt for the title be common to all the young brother with the elder even where the patrimony be not divide divide their father estate between they be say to have each of they ten thousand gold gulden by the year and if all these brother shall have child it be probable that the principality can not bear so many heir I remember that I do see one of they at dresden in the court of christian elector and duke of Saxony who receive of he a pension to maintain certain horse and be one of his courtier the like happen in our time to the count of Mansfeild whereof twenty seven live at one time and some of they follow the war of netherlands the revenue of so narrow a county suffice not to bear up the dignity of their birth howsoever it yield mine of silver which be at that time pawn for money to the of augsburg I observe that the young son of protestant prince who fee can not be divide yea and the elder son during his father life enjoy the revenue of bishopric as administrator be so call beside money and pension and some land of inheritance and otherwise for better maintenance follow the war in this sort when the elector Christian duke of Saxony die his three son be yet under age enjoy three bishopric namely those of mizzen Nauberg and Mersberg though the emperor and the gentleman of those part in a provincial meeting be instant to have three bishop choose and the emperor desire that dignity for one of his brother the same three prince yet be under age I do see coin of gold and silver bear the image of all three but when they come to age the electorship and the inheritance belong to it fall to the elder son the young retain the say bishopric for life and their part of other land that may be divide for inheritance to they and their child the fee of prince be give by the emperor and the fee of many gentleman and of some earl be give by prince but I return to the law of succession by the civil law they that disscend of the right line have the first place in succession all which without respect of sex or fatherly power do succeed equal the son by the pole the nephew to their part namely to the part which their father shall have have if he have be then live so as it seem that four or more nephew the son of a three brother dead divide with two brother live all the nephew shall only have a three part belong to their father be dead and each of the two live brother shall have another three part the law of Saxony change nothing touch the person but differ in the succession of good for the daughter shall by privilege have their mother apparel and other ornament with all utensil or household stuff so as they shall be value to they in their due part and the niece bear of one of the sister be dead have the same right with the other sister for her mother part but none can have these utensil save the woman on the mother side vulgar call Spieimagen for the brother daughter have no right to they and I have hear of learned man that these utensil can be alienate by the last testament namely vessel of brass but not of pewter linen bed except the heir of innkeeper who chief wealth common consist in such furniture also sheep goose jewel of gold and like ornament of the mother except the seal ring of gold and pearl and other jewel which man use to wear as well as woman by a law make in the dukedom of Meckelburg because the woman in the year 1388 redeem theeir captive prince with their jewel many privilege of succession be grant to woman by the law of Saxony as the utensil belong to the daughter so beside the decree of the civil law in the knightly order all good of expedition as arm and the like belong to the son and the sword be always give to the elder son but these thing be not observe among those of common or plebeian rank except custom have make they as law so as the daughter by custom have the utensil and the elder son have the chief horse for the plough I have former say that by the law of Saxony the nephew be exclude from succeed in a fee with his uncle on the father side that be his father brother but that in our day the nephew be admit according to the civil law I have say that in the succession of movable good the son succeed the father by the pole but the nephew or son of another son decease succeed their grandfather only in the part belong to their father I have say that the law of Saxony change nothing touch the person but only differ in the succession to some good as the utensil now I add further that the nephew succession and equal division with his father brother be decree by a imperial law abrogate all contrary custom by the civil law brother on both side and together with they the child of their dead brother and sister be then first call to inheritance when the decease have no heir in the right line descend or ascend but without any respect to the imperiallisaid law as speak of custom not write law or to the last civil law the law of Saxony decree and of old custom it be observe among the saxon that in the succession of collateral the live brother exclude the child of his dead brother I say in freehold not in fee and the brother on both side exclude the brother on the one side only in the three degree and the brother on both side exclude the child of his dead brother in the three degree but I have observe that this law be thus practise among the saxon as imagine there be three brother thomas John and Andrew and it happen that Thomas first die leave a son and then John die unmarried or without issue the good of John at his death shall not fall to the son of Thomas his elder brother but to his brother Andrew yet live and Andrew die last as well his own as his brother john good fall to his own son but if he have no son than they fall to the son of Thomas and again put the case that Thomas and John be both dead and each of they have leave a son or son if Andrew die without a son the son of Thomas succeed he without any respect to the son of John by the civil law the uncle of the decease by the father side be not only exclude by the brother of the decease but also by the brother child but by the law of Saxony since the right of representation simple have no place and these person be in the same degree namely in the three degree they be call together to the inheritance yet the scabines or judge of leipzig have pronounce the contrary to this judgement of the judge in the high court of the duke of Saxony rather follow the civil law which prefer the brother son before the uncle on the father side by the civil law in the succession of collateral the brother of both side be for a double bond prefer to the brother by one parent only so as the privilege be not extend to thing in fee but to thing in freehold because in fee the bond on the mother side be not regard by the law of Saxony a brother on both side exclude a brother by one parent as near by one degree by the civil law bastard be admit to the inheritance of the mother and the brother lawful beget be call to the inheritance of a dead bastard brother by the say mother but by the law of Saxony as a bastard can be admit to inherit with one lawful beget so he that be lawful beget can succeed a bastard that be not legitimate and by the law of Saxony a mother have a bastard daughter and die without any other child can leave her utensil good to that daughter yet in all case concern bastard the judge leave the law of Saxony as unequal and judge after the civil law so as in Saxony bastard both succeed and be succeed unto and always part of the good be give if not by law yet by equity to maintain the bastard and the interpreter will have the law of Saxony understand of those that be bear in incest who have not the benefit of legitimation by the civil law he that be bear in the seven month after marriage be repute lawful beget but by the law of Saxony he be repute a bastard that be bear before the due time yet because physician agree that the seven month may be call due time in custom and practice the law of Saxony agree with the civil law by the civil law the testament be break by the birth of a Posthumus that be a son bear after his father death if it give no part to this child so the birth be prove by two witness but by the law of Saxony four man by hearsay and two woman by sight must testify the birth in the civil law it be controvert how son of brother shall succeed the uncle by the father side and the great part say that they succeed to the part of the brother so as one child of a brother shall have as much as two or more child of another brother but by the law of Saxony when the inheritance fall to any that be not brother and sister they succeed by pole so as one brother have many child each of they shall have equal part with the only child of another brother and if they be further off in degree those that be equal in degree have equal portion but both these law be make to agree by a statute of the emperor Charles the five in the year 1539 whereby it be determine that the son of brother shall not succeed to part but by pole to the uncle by the father side not withstand any statute or custom to the contrary by the civil law the division of inheritance must be make by lot and if the part be not so make equal the judge must determine it but by the law of Saxony if there be only two person the elder divide and the young choose and if there be more person then according to the civil law the inheritance be divide equal and they cast lot for their part in this division I have observe such equity among the saxon as if one son of a citizen have be bring up in the university or instruct in any art or science at the father charge something shall be take from his part and give to the other brother want like education or be tender in year and the german be less apt to disagreement seldom go to law about inheritance and if any difference happen a arbiter be appoint and the magistrate determine it with expedition by the civil law the son of a banish man be deprive of his father inheritance but by the law of Saxony he shall enjoy it by the civil law the degree of consanguinity end in the ten degree except baron and noble person who die without heir the kinsman succeed though it be in the hundred degree and if all the family of a king shall die and leave no man near than one of the old blood remove a thousand degree yet he shall succeed in the kingdom the degree of consanguinity by the law of Saxony end in the seven degree for that be the ten by the civil law the son of two brother be by the law of Saxony in the first degree who by the civil law be in the four degree by the civil law city howsoever privilege can possess the vacant good of man die without heir but they fall to the emperor but by the law of Saxony city that have absolute power confiscate these good by custom so as the good of a stranger or any die without heir be bring to the judge of the place who keep they for one whole year yea they challenge unmoveable good but with prescription of year and these good use to be convert to godly use and I have observe some to be deep fine for fraudulent detain these good by the civil law he that be of age so he be in his wit and no prodigal person may free sell give or by any course alienate his good but by the law of Saxony this power be restrain for no man without the consent of the next heir can alienate unmoveable good get by his progenitor vulgar call stamgutter but only for godly use or dowry give upon marriage for contract of dowry be of force for use and property without consent of the heir though make after the marriage if the gift be confirm by the giver death but if any man will sell his progenitor good first by the civil law he must offer they to be buy to the next heir and they refuse to buy they he may then free sell they to any man and if they be never offer to the heir notwithstanding the possession be transfer but the heir have a action for their interest by the civil law weakness as of old age do not make the gift of less force but by the law of Saxony a man or woman sick to death can without the consent of the heir give any good above the value of five shilling so as a certain solemnity be require among the sick and also those that be healthful in the gift of any movable or unmoveable good for among the sick or healthful he that will give any good if he be of knightly order he must be of that strength as arm with his sword and target he can upon a stone or block a ell high mount his horse and his servant be admit also to hold his stirrup if he be a citizen he must beable to walk in the way to draw his sword and to stand up right before the judge while the gift be make and a clown must be able to follow the blow one morning last a woman must be of that strength as she can go to the church of a certain distance and there stand so long till the gift be make but these thing be understand of gift among the live not of gift upon death by the civil law gift be of force though make out of the place where the good be seat but by the law of Saxony for unmoveable good the gift must be make in the place and before the judge of the place where the good be seat only some case except by the civil law the heir that make no inventory be tie to the creditor above the good of inheritance but by the law of Saxony he be neither tie to make a inventory nor to pay further than the good of the decease extend by the civil law within ten day and by the law of Saxony within thirty day after the death of he that die the heir may not be trouble by the creditor a imperial statute decree that he who make a testament must be in his right mind so as he speak to the purpose and must have witness who have no profit by his testament and such as themselves have power to make a testament he that disinherite the next heir be bind to give he a lawful legacy according to his good by the civil law leprous person and bear unperfect be not exclude from inherit but by the law of Saxony the lame dumb blind leprous and the like be not capable of inheritance or see yet if any man after his succession shall become leprous he shall enjoy the inheritance by the law of Saxony tutorage belong only to the kinsman by the father side and not as by the civil law to all in the same degree but ever to the next and if many be in the same next degree then to the elder of they only yet so as the danger of tutorage belong to all together thus christian duke of Saxony die the duke of Winberg only not the duke of coburg in the same degree with he be tutor to his child though the electorship be take from their grandfather and give to this family with great and just envy by the emperor Charles the five in common judgement especial respect such case the kinsman on the mother side seem more fit to be tutor who have no profit but rather loss by the death of the pupil whereas the kinsman on the father side be heir to he yet the common practice to the contrary as in this particular example produce no tragical event among the german be of a good and peaceable nature by the civil law a pupil be say to be in minority till he be five and twenty year old and the tutorage cease and the pupil be capable of investiture at ripe age namely the male at fourteen the female at twelve year age but by the law of Saxony the pupil be say to be in minority till he be 21 year old and the male be capable to be invest in his fee when he be 13 year and six week old for the saxon make difference between these two thing binnen jahren unnd binnen that be under year and under day for the pupil be hold under year for inheritance till he be fourteen year old and for fee till he be thirteen year and six week old but he be hold under day or in minority till he be twenty one year old the imperial law of the golden bulla not withstand make the elector son to be of ripe age and free from tutor at eighteen year age I have observe that tutor in Saxony allow the pupil five in the hundred for all their money which they have in their hand female be under tutor till they marry and they can marry without their consent but refuse to give consent they be bind to yield a reason thereof before the judge lest they shall fraudulent deny consent by the civil law the tutor be not bind to give account till the tutorage be end but the administrator may yearly be call to account and the elder brother must give account to the young of the inheritance which he administer undivided but by the law of Saxony if the tutor be not heir to the pupil as the kinsman by the father side while the mother live who exclude he from succession he be tie yearly to give account but if he be heir to the pupil he be not bind to give account which notwithstanding be restrain to parent and brother who for reverence of the blood and natural affection be free from suspicion of fraud or fault especial where the administration be of good which either they possess with the pupil undivided or in which they have right of succession also by the law of Saxony the elder brother when his brother have no mother live as heir to his brother be not tie to give account to his brother or to his joint heir for the administration of a common and undivided inheritance in like sort by the civil law the tutor be bind to give surety or sufficient caution for preserve the pupil good but by the law of Saxony as former if the tutor be heir to the pupil or joint heir with he in undivided inheritance he be not tie thereunto by the civil law whatsoever fall to the son in the power of the father of his mother good either by testament of the mother or from her die intestate the father shall have the use and full administration thereof for his life and for the confidence and reverence of a father he be not tie to give surety or caution for use or restore those good to his son as other have like use thereof be bind to do yet so as in regard of this use for life the father be bind according to his power to give a gift in marriage to his son leave he but the mother have no right to the use of her son good by the law of Saxony the use be so long grant to the father till his child depart from he but the lawyer so interpret this if the father because of the separation for if the son will depart of his own motion except he be out of minority and will take upon he the care of a family the father shall retain the use and be bind after to restore these good except they perish by mistortune without his fault and the same law be for the mother also touch the good of her son when the father be dead but the law concern the father must be understand of the unmoveable good fall unto the son from the mother by the civil law the father and mother or other in the ascend line succeed the son or daughter die in equal portion with the brother and sister but by the law of Saxony the parent of the son dead or if they be dead the grandfather and grandmother or any ascendant whatsoever exclude brother and sister by both parent and collateral whatsoever and indeed by the law the father alone succeed the die son or daughter exclude the mother except the utensil good in which the mother be prefer but by the late statute of the elector this law be change so as the father and mother succeed together yet these thing must be understand of the good in freehold for in fee they of the ascendant line succeed not the descendant but as every stranger may succeed by contract express in the investiture by the civil law the father can make a gift to the son be under his power but by the law of Saxony he may yet the son receive the gift be bind to acknowledge it when his father die and to abate so much of his portion in the division with his brother if it be of any value and not give to supply his want at that time and by both law the gift be good from the father to the son go to warfare by the civil law the wife in time of marriage may have good in which the husband have no right either to alienate or to administer they as those good which she bring to her husband above her dowry and never give they to he but by the law of Saxony the man and wife have all good in common so as all be say to be the husband and the wife can call nothing her own and the husband have the use of all without exception even while they live together for the burden he bear yet he have not the property of these good only they both possess they undivided so long as they live together the husband at marriage take his wife and all her good into his tuition but this tuition be only understand for the use which end when the wife die but the wife have not like use in her husband good and the husband in administer the good of his wife must deal honest and neither sell nor engage they because he be only her tutor by the civil law the wife have power without the presence or consent of her husband to give or alinate her movable or unmoveable good only during the marriage she can give away her dowry to the prejudice of her husband without his consent but by the law of Saxony the wife can give her unmoveable good nor sell or alienate any good without her husband consent because she be under his power as her tutor yea the wife can give her good to her husband because he be her tutor can be actor to his own profit but if before the magistrate she choose another tutor by who authority the gift be make than it be of force for in all case in which a gift between man and wife be of force by the civil law in the same case at this day by custom it be of force among the saxon so as the former manner be observe but all these thing of the wife gift to her husband and of alienate her good by contract which she can make without the consent of the husband her tutor be not understand of the alienation by her last will and testament for by the law of Saxony it be controvert whether the wife may give a gift to her husband at her death without the authority of the foresay tutor choose by she and if it be give without the same whether after the death of the wife according to the civil law this gift be confirm and some interpreter say that the same authority of a choose tutor and the same solemnity be require as in a gift between the live other determine that the gift at death without a tutor be of force so it be make before the judge because it be not a simple give but participate some thing of the last Will and testament and for that cause five witness be require to it or that it be register which do the gift be of force because favour be to be give to the last testament which must not be captious but free also because he that be of ripe age but in minority though he can give or contract without the authority of his tutor yet he may give for death and so it be conclude that in doubtful case the gift must be favour that it may subsist rather than be make void last the law of Saxony in this consent with the civil law that a wife may make a will and for death give her unmovable good to any other but her husband without the consent of the husband her tutor but by the statute of the elector the gift of utensil good make to the husband in prejudice of her next kinswoman be of so little force as with death it be not confirm except it be remuneratory yet among the live this gift of stuff as some restrain it so it be not to the husband be of force if it be make before a notary and with witness by the civil law the husband may not have the care of his wife good lest she upon affection shall remit his ill administration and so shall be in danger to loose the good of her dowry but by the law of Saxony present upon marriage the husband be lawful tutor to his wife by the civil law the dowry of the wife give by her father upon the death of the wife return to the father except it be covenant to the contrary in the contract of the dowry but by the law of Saxony the husband upon his wife death gain all movable good and so much of the dowry as be in ready money except it be express covenant to the contrary in the contract of the dowry and all the good of the wife above that she bring in dowry fall to the husband nothing except but only the utensil good yet this law be not extend to the perpetual and yearly rent of the wife which be repute unmoveable good by the civil law if either the man or the wife marry the second time the party may in no case give more to the second husband or wife then to the child of the first marriage but among the saxon this law be abolish by contrary custom so as not only the step mother use to have much more of the husband good than the child of the first marriage but on the other side also the second husband upon the death of the second wife be to have all her movable good except the utensil common gain more than her child of her first marriage by the civil law a widow retain the dwell house honour and dignity of her husband decease till she marry to another and by the law of Saxony the dead husband leave his widow the right of his family and blood and custom so interpret this law as all privilege and dignity be thereby grant as by the civil law widow and virgin by the law of Saxony if they be of such age as they have no tutor may give or alienate their good which a wife can do be under the tutorage of her husband yet the interpreter restrain this to movable good be otherwise in unmovable good but by last will and testament they may dispose of both by the civil law if there be no letter of dowry or jointure the husband die the wife must have the four part of his good but in some part of Saxony the custom be that the wife be a widow shall have the three part of her husband good as it be in all mizzen but in other part as in Thuring the civil law be observe and she have the four part if the husband leave but 3 or four child but if he have more than the widow have only a equal part with each of they but in mizzen the wife have not the utensil good which use nor to be give to woman have a three part and moreover the widow be tie not only to leave she own good but her part of good get in marriage by her husband and whatsoever her friend give to she in the life of her husband or she any way gain to their child at her death whether she give they to her husband in time of his life or no for it be always presume that she get these thing out of her husband good and if in any place there be no custom to determine this than the widow beside her four or equal part have also the utensil good and in case the husband leave no child than the widow have her choice whether she will receive the three part or renounce the same will retain utensil good and all other her own good movable or unmovable together with her dowry but if the husband leave child the widow have not this choice but must renounce all the rest and stick to her three part and by custom of the country her dowry and gift for marriage be double so as she that bring one thousand gulden for her dowry shall have two thousand gulden in the division of her husband inheritance and the right which marry party by statute have in one another good can be take from they by last will and testament discourse with man of experience I hear that the widow of prince while they remain widow possess all their husband estate except the electorship which the next kinsman by the father side administer by his right during the minority of the son and enjoy also the tutorage of their child but if they marry again the country free itself from they with give they a tun of gold for dowry and that the daughter of prince have dowry from the subject by subsidy collect and use to swear before the chancellor that their husband be dead or upon any accident whatsoever they will not return to burden the country that the daughter of gentleman never marry to any of inferior degree than gentleman which be constant keep by both sex and be common bestow with a small dowry and since by the law they can succeed in fee have at the parent death only a part of their movable good with the utensil proper to they and one sister die her portion go not to the brother or their child as also the marry sister die and leave no daughter her portion go not to her own son except live and in health she bequeath it to they in her testament but to the niece on the mother side last that in case the good of a dead woman be neither give by her last testament nor any kinswoman to she on the mother side can be find her good go not to her own son or male-kinsman but be confilcate to the prince or in free city to the commonwealth it be say that the roman emperor caracalla be wont to say that only that nation know how to rule their wife which add the feminine article to the sun the degree in family and the masculine to the moon as the german do say die Sonn (@) der mont. and no doubt the german be very churlish to their wife and keep they seruy at home so as myself in Saxony have see many wife of honest condition and good first the wife estate to dress meat in the kitchen and scarce once in the week to eat with their husband but apart with the maid and after the meal to come and take away their husband table and if they come to sit with he at table yet to sit down at the low end at least under all the man myself have see husband of like quality to chide their wife bitter till they weep abundant and the same wife of good rank very soon after to bring a chair to the husband and serve he with a trencher and other necessary the man be invite to friend house or any solemn feast never go in company with their wife who go alone with their face cover it be no novelty for a husband to give a box on the ear to his wife and they scoff at the law in nurnberg which fine the husband three or four dollar for strike his wife as a most unjust Law it be ridiculous to see the wife of german foote-soldier go to the war lade with burden like she-ass while the man carry not so much as their own cloak but cast they also upon the woman shoulder and I shall hardly believe that the german can love their wife since love be gain by loveliness as the poet say he that for love do thirst let he be love first but they while they command all thing imperious in the mean time neither for dulness court they with any pleasant speech nor in courtesy grace they in public so much as with a kiss it be a common say I take a dowry with my wife and lose the freedom of my life but howsoever the german have great dowry in marriage and their wife have power to make a testament for dispose their good with many like privilege and howsoever they be also provoke with these injury yet the man keep they within term of duty may not we then just marvel that englishman have great power over their wife so as they can neither give any thing in life nor have power to make a will at death nor can call any thing their own no not so much be their garter yea the law I must confess too severe permit the husband in some case to beat his wife and yet the husband notwithstanding all their privilege use their wife with all respect and give they the chief seat with all honour and preeminence so as for the most part they will carry burden go on foot fast and suffer any thing so their wife may have ease ride feast and suffer nothing notwithstanding no people in the world that ever I do see bear more scorn indignity and injury from the pamper sort of woman than they do sure either these our woman want the modesty of the wife or else our man have not I will not say the severity which I less approve but rather the gravity and constancy of the husband in Germany but while the german thus use their wife like servant they behave themselves of servant as companion towards their servant who bring in meat to the table with their head cover and continual talk with their master without any reverence of the cap or like duty the german be neither too indulgent nor too stern to their son and daughter of son and daughter yet they give they no tender education but as they bring their child naked into the hot stoave so they expose they naked to frost and snow neither do they exact any humility or respect from their child who in all place be familiar with their parent and never stir their hat when they speak to they and when they go to bed they ask not blessing on their knee as our child do but shake hand with they which be a sign of familiarity among friend in Germany as in most other place a gentleman never so poor will not marry the rich merchant daughter nor the degree in commonwealth a gentlewoman upon any condition any other than a gentleman neither be there any just cause of disinherit then base marriage which pollution of blood the kinsman will not suffer as in our age have be see by notable example one in the house of Austria whereof the arch duke of inspruch marry the daughter of gentleman a citizen in augsburg which his kinsman will not suffer till he condition that her child shall not succeed he in his fee as they do not though at this time they be live the other of a earl who marry the daughter of a citizen in nurnberg be cast in prison by his kinsman till he leave she he be not account a gentleman who be not so by four descent at the least both by the father and the mother side and I remember that the monk of Luneburg by statute may not admit any man into their number who have not eight degree on both side yea the german be so superstitious in this kind as a gentleman may have a action against he who say he be no gentleman for the better conjecture of gentleman estimation in Germany I remember one of the chief call von of Shulenburg who I do see and he be say to have fourteen thousand gold gulden yearly rend and never to ride without forty or fifty horse to attend he but I can sufficient marvel that the gentleman howsoever sometime learned yet proud despise graduate of the university no less or more than merchant which I find not only by common practice but also by my private experience for convert with a gentleman he perceive that I speak Latin better than he think become a gentleman ask i how long I do study in the university and when I say that I be master of art which degree our best gentleman disdain not I find that he do after esteem i as a pedant whereupon find by discourse with other that gentleman despise these degree I sorbore after to make this my degree know to any and it seem more strange to i that gentleman first rise by learning warfare and traffic they only judge warfare worthy to raise and continue gentleman but indeed the traffic of Germany be poor be chief of thing wrought by manual artist which they have some pretence to disdain whereas in Italy traffic be the sinew of the commonwealth which the most noble disdain not and it be to be wish that in England where traffic be no less noble the practice thereof be no stain to gentry when I tell a english gentleman the pride of the gentleman in Germany despise degree of learning and he hear that the gentleman be vulgar call edelman he pleasant say that they be so call of the english word idle men. the gentleman of Germany bear the arm of their mother though she be no heir as well as of their father and common they join to they in steed of a mot or sentence certain great letter that signify word as d. h. i. m. t. signify der be it Trost that be the lord be my comfort and likewise f. s. v. signify that be trust but beware also citizen and artist bear arm of their own invention and trick out full as the arm of gentleman only the helmet be close which gentleman bear open among the general order of knight into which gentleman of all nation be the general order of knight admit the templary in the year 1124 be confirm by pope honorius be so call of the temple at jerusalem in part whereof they dwell history report that pope Gregory the nine incite they to do great damage by their treachery to the emperor Frederick make the holy war in Asia at last the induce of heathenish religion all kind of lust and intemperance and the suspicion of their conspire with the turk or the fear of their too great power make pope Clement the five a Frenchman and reside at Auignon first to extinguish the order in France then in all Christendom in the year 1312 the second order of the johanite or saint John be institute by balduine the second king of jerusalem then in the year 1308 they take the i will of Rhodes and be call the knight of Rhodes till they be expel thence by the turk in the year 1522 and then possess the island of Malta they be to this day call the knight of Malta and great part of the templary rent be give to this order into which of old none but gentleman be admit the three order of the teutonike that be german be institute in the year 1190 in the time of the emperor Henry the six they be the order of the german knight call hospital of the hospital which they keep near the sepulchre of Christ to entertain pilgrim at last all christian be drive out of Palestine they remove their seat to Venice whence be call by the duke of Moscovy against the prussian they seat themselves in Prussia livonia and Curlandia they be all bear of noble parent and do wear a white cloak with a black cross the polonian in the year 1410 kill the master of the order and many thousand of the knigh when many city under the protection of the king of Poland seek their liberty in the year 1450 and this order have win a battle against the king at last because the citizen refuse to pay the soldier the knight themselves betray their city to the polonian and after much blood shed on both part at last in the year 1466 peace be make with covenant that the king of Poland shall have Pomerella with other castle and town and that the order shall retain Kingspurg and final in the year 1547 this order be total extinguish the master upon the dissolution of this order the duke of Prussia be create thereof be as they say force to these condition namely that Albert marquis of Brandeburg be of the e'ector family than master of the order shall become vassal to the king of Poland and shall possess Konigspurg with title of a duke to he and his brother of the same venture and their heir male for ever in which dukedom be fifty four castle and eighty six town moreover that the say duke shall take new arm and a dukall habit and when he come to do his homage at Crakaw in Poland shall have his seat by the king side but that upon male heir fail the dukedom shall fall to the kingdom of Poland which be to provide for the daughter and heir according to her degree and to appoint no other governor of the province than a german have inheritance in Prussia in the time of my be at Dantzke it be say that duke Albert be grow into a frenzy by a poison cup give he at his marriage with the daughter of the duke of Cleve and the common speech be that the elder son to the elector of Brandeburg be daily expect in the duke court to marry the daughter and heir to the sickly duke to who himself be next of kin by the father side and heir and it be a common speech that the say sickly duke have late lend forty thousand gulden to the king of Poland and that the elector of Brandeburg have offer seven ton of gold to the king of Poland that his grandchild may succeed in the dukedom of Prussia but that it be flat refuse by the senate of Poland so as it be diverse think according to man diverse judgement what will become of the dukedom after the say sickly duke death some judge that the king of Poland will keep the dukedom fall to he other that the powerful family of Brandeburg will extort the possession thereof by force of money or of arm I omit the military order of knight in England France and netherlands to be mention in their due place among the german I can not observe any ordinary degree of knight confer the ordinaery degree of knighthood in Germany in honour upon such as deserve well in civil and warlike affair such as the king of England give to their subject with the title of sir to distinguish they from inferior gentleman but in our age we have see master Arundel a english gentleman create earl of the empire for his acceptable service to the emperor Christian elector of Saxony decease do institute a military order of knight like to the teutonike order save that it be no religious order and he call it die gulden geselschaft that be the golden fellowship by which bond he tie his near friend to he and the badge of the order be a jewel hang in a chain of gold have on each side of the jewel engrave a heart pierce with a sword and a shaft and upon one side near the heart be the image of faith hold a crucifix with these word grave about the heart that be for love of virtue upon the other side near the heart be the image of constancy hold a anchor with these word grave about the heart that be he that to the end shall be save last about the circle of the jewel these great letter be engrave f. s. v that be namely in english trust but beware the province of the reform religion
