and they pay tribute for every head of cattle feed therein as two stiver weekly for each cow for the pail the great number whereof may be conjecture by the plenty of cheese export out of Holland and the infinite quantity of cheese and butter they spend at home be the most common food of all the people for ox horse sheep and other beast sell in market the twelve part at least of the price be pay for tribute and be they never so often by the year sell to and fro the new master still pay as much they pay five stiver for every bushel of their own wheat which they use to grind in public mill and since they give tribute of half in half for food and most necessary thing common pay as much for tribute as the price of the thing sell the imposition must needs be think great lay upon foreign commodity serve for pleasure pride and luxury beside that these tribute be ordinary and no doubt upon any necessity of the commonwealth will be increase french wine at middleburg the staple thereof and rhenish wine at Dort the staple thereof be sell by privilege without any imposition but in all other place man pay as much for the impost as for the wine only in the camp all thing for food be sell without any imposition lay upon they and some but very few eminent man have the privilege to pay no imposition for like thing of food each student in the vnivertie have eighty measure of wine vulgar call stoop allow he free from imposition and for six barrel of beer only pay one Gulden and a quarter that be two shilling six penny english be altogether free from all other tribute which privilege the citizen enjoy in the name of the student diet with they and no doubt the rector and professor of the university have great immunity in these kind one thing be hardly to be understand how these province thus oppress with tribute and make war against a most powerful king yet at this time in the heat of the war which use to waste most flourish kingdom and make province desolate have far great riches than any most peaceable country of their neighbour or then ever themselves former attain in their great peace and prosperity whether it be for that according to the poet adversity oft whet the wit so as by war they be grow more witty and industrious or for that Flanders and Antwerp the famous city in former time so draw all traffic and rich merchant to they as all the neighbour province be thereby impoverish all which trade by the war fall to Holland most strong in ship or for that the unite province have such commodity by the sea and water run to all town and by the strength of their city as in the heat of war they be free from the enemy incursion or any impediment of their traffic and seem rather to carry the war to their confine then to have it in their bosom in which point it be not unpleasant to remember how the hollander mock the spaniard as if not acquaint with the northern sea and the ebb and flow thereof they think they may at pleasure come into any haven and lead their army into any of those province and that when the spaniard first enter Holland with their army and they cut the bank of the sea drown their country the spaniard be therewith astonish and give gold chain money and the most precious thing they have to the country people on condition they will bring they out of those watery place to firm land if any man require true and great reason of these province grow rich by war let he make curious search thereof for it be beside my purpose no doubt the frequent arm of the sea within land pass by their city the innumerable water though for the most part stand or little move which by make ditch carry boat and bark to all their city be there more frequent than in any other part of the world and to all their village and compass almost all their pasture yield no small commodity to their commonwealth for they have little of their own to export and want corn wood or coal and many necessary for their use yet by this only benefit and their singular industry not only most abundant enjoy all commodity of all nation for their own use but by transport they from place to place with their own ship whereof they have a unspeakable number make very great gain be delight in navigation by nature as bear and breed in the midst of sea and water and have by war heat their phlegmatic humour attain to such skill therein as for traffic they sail to the most remote coast of the world and in process of time be grow so bold seaman as they will scarce yield in this art to the english for many former year excel therein so as their tribute impose on merchant commodity must needs be of exceed great moment and not to weary myself with the curious search thereof I will only add for conjecture of the general one particular relate to i by credible man that in time when Italy suffer dearth and be supply with corn from these part the tribute of one city Amsterdam in one week exceed the sum of ten thousand pound sterling whence the revenue of all tribute in all the haven and city may be conjecture to be excessive great so as add the imposition upon domestical thing and the great contribution pay by the enemy subject upon the confine in time of war to purchase the safety of their person and good with freedom to till their ground from the rapine of freybooting soldier a man may well say that the unite province be no less able than they have be dare to do great thing this commonwealth be govern by particular law and custom of diverse the law place and by the public edict upon diverse new occasion make by the state of the province and these want by the civil law the particular city be govern after the manner above name and particular at Leyden myself have observe the inhabitant of village call by write set upon post in the public street to have their controversy judge by the magistrate of the city not at any set time of the year but according to the occasion of other affair at the judge pleasure high injury and maim of any member be punish by the law which pass over light injury not give such ample satisfaction to the wrong even by word as the constitution of the sweitzer give so as with they no less than in England quarrel and brawl be frequent and often break out into manslaughter wherein those who will revenge themselves by force first agree between themselves whether they will strike or stab and then draw out long knife which they ordinary wear they wound one another by course according to their agreement either by slash or stab which they call and they common allow money to be put out to use and to the end poor man upon pawn may borrow small sum for a short time they admit a italian or Lombard vulgar so call in each city who take a pawn lend a gulden for a brass coin call a doigt by the week but this Lombard in the french church there be not admit to receive the communion the pawn use to be worth a three part more than the money lend and one year and a day be pass after the money be due the usurer have the pawn to himself but before that time the debtor at his pleasure may at any time have his pawn first pay the borrow money with the use to the day of payment and the common report than be that the state will take this as a public office into their own hand to help the poor not able to pay by sell the pawn to the owner best profit touch inheritance upon the mother death the child may compel their father to divide his good with they lest perhaps he shall consume or waste the same and the wife that bring a dowry be her husband grow never so rich by his trade may when she die give not only her dowry but half her husband good get in marriage to her own kinsman after his death if she have no child by he and if she bring no dowry yet she have the same right to dispose of half her husband good get in marriage and as be suppose by their mutual labour a son may not be disinherit but upon cause approve by the law for the father be bind to give a three part of his estate among his child and only have power to dispose of the rest or any part thereof in legacy at his pleasure the wife of Holland buy and sell all thing at home and use to sail to hamburg the wine and into England for exercise of traffic I hear from credible man that the citizen of Enchusen within thirty year then past use to marry a wife and put she away at the year end if they like she not which barbarous custom civility and religion have since abolish and at Delft I do see two example of man who have bury their wife do after marry their wife sister it be no rare thing for blow to happen between man and wife and I credible hear that they have slight punishment for that fault and myself do hear the crier summon a man to answer the beat of his wife before the magistrate the multitude of woman be far great than of man which I not only former hear from other but myself observe to be true by the daily meeting of both sex where a man may see sixty or more woman slide upon the ice and otherwise recreate themselves with five or six or much few man but the reason thereof be not easy yield since we can say that the man be much consume by the civil war their army consist altogether of stranger and few or no hollander except some willing serve for otherwise they can be press by authority but only for the defence of the city or town wherein they dwell excpt these reason thereof may be approve that the watery province breed phlegmatic humour which together with the man excessive drink may disable they to beget male or that the woman as I have hear some hollander confess not easy find a husband in respect of this disparity of the sex in number common live unmarried till they be thirty year old and as common take husband of twenty year age which must needs make the woman more powerful in generation and the woman not only take young man to their husband but those also which be most simple and tractable so as by the foresay privilege of wife to dispose good by their last will and by the contract in respect of their dowry which to the same end use to be wary draw they keep their husband in a kind of awe and almost alone without their husband intermeddle not only keep their shop at home but exercise traffic abroad myself have hear a wise make answer to one ask for her husband that he be not at home but have new ask her leave to go abroad nothing be more frequent then for little girl to insult over their brother much big than they reprove their do and call they great lubber whereof when I talk with some scholar my companion as a fashion seem strange to i they be so far from wonder thereat as they tell i it be a common thing for wife to drive their husband and their friend out of the door with scold as if they consume the good wherein they have a property with their husband I shall be too credulous if I shall think all family to be sick of this disease and I must confess that in few other nation all family be altogether free from like accident but I may bold say that the woman of these part be above all other true tax with this unnatural domineer over their husband the nobility or gentry have long be root out by the people as witness the gentleman and experience show after the example of the sweitzer especial in Holland and Zealand for in Friesland they have many and within land as frequent family of gentleman as otherwhere I can not hear of more than some three family of gentleman in Holland and Zealand for the lord of Nassaw be stranger and these gentleman live after the plebeian manner of the other inhabitant so as it be in vain to seek for any order of knighthood among they neither be these gentleman as those of Germany curious to marry among themselves for those who come to great honour in this commonwealth be either advocate of the law or son of merchant myself do once in the high way meet a gentleman wagon to who our waggoner give the way and the custom in Holland be that the wagon set forth give the way to all they meet till they have go half the way and after in like sort the way be give to they and our wagon have then pass more than half the way the plebeian hollander my companion be much offend with our waggoner that contrary to the custom he have do that honour to the gentleman the vulgar sort so despise gentleman or any superior if he affect greatness as upon like occasion they proverbial use to say if he be rich let he dine twice as if they despise rich man of who they stand not in need be content with their own after the manner of the italian save that the italian do it out of pride these out of clownishness and affect of equality touch capital judgement where the offence be heinous and such as former capital judgement age have not know the judge inflict exquisite punishment and torment upon the malefactor in such sort with strange torment the wicked person be put to death who kill the prince of orange with a pistol thief and pirate be put to death by hang and of all other offender they never pardon pirate upon any intercession as destroyer of traffic upon which their commonwealth and private estate depend the manslayer be behead and bury in the same coffin with the man he kill and if perhaps he can be apprehend but escape into some foreign part he may perhaps but very rare obtain pardon if he can first be reconcile with the friend of the man slay by he but in case he be apprehend they can or at least use not to scandal justice by pardon but wilful murderer according to the circumstance of the person kill or of the more or less wicked manner of the act be put to death with more or less torment and hang in iron chain till the body rot for terror to other coiner of money have their bone break upon the wheel a death more usual in Germany for heinous crime but in all torment they common mitigate the severity of the law more than the german do for I have see some execute in this manner who be first hang and so have no feel of the pain he that burn private and much more public house and he that purpose or threaten to burn they though he never do the act be himself burn by fire with a mark upon his head if the act be do upon his breast if it be only purpose or upon his mouth if it be only threaten a offender escape by flight howsoever he live long in foreign part yet if he ever return be the distance of time never so great he escape not unpunished myself have see a manslayer who have live six year in foreign part and then for love of his country return home be then behead as if the crime have be new commit no man will apprehend any malefactor nor hinder his flight but rather think it a point of humanity to help he only the hangman and base fellow appoint for that office lay hold upon capital offender so as very many escape by flight neither can any so base or poor man be find except the hangman and his say companion who for any reward will be hire to do the office of a executioner both these action be infamous here as in Germany among the apprehender the chief be call provost and they of old have power to hang vagabond till abuse it to revenge and rapine it be take from they upon the rumour of any crime commit these man with their servant arm be send out into the country to apprehend the malefactor it be credible tell i that the emperor Charles the five have sudden command a man to be hang who after by a other confession of the fact be find guiltle upon this error make a that no hangman shall ever live at the hage or near the court than Harlam to the end he be not at hand the magistrate may less offend in deliberate or protract judgement for as in upper Germany so in netherlands there be little or no distance of time between the offence commit and the execution of judgement whereas in England these judgement be exercise at London once in six week and for the country at two or four set time in the year no man be put to death without confession of the fact neither do they as in Germany force confession by torture but they condemn upon one witness where probable conjecture concur to prove the malefactor guilty I think save the judgement of the better experience in these affair that the of their (@) in general military discipline of the state army be very commendable for since those commonwealth be most happy where reward and punishment be most just give sure the state neither detain nor delay the payment due to the soldier nor leave unpunished their insolence nor yet their wanton injury either towards the subject or the enemy yield upon condition in the camp all thing for food be free from all imposition so as a man may there live more plentiful or more frugal then in any of their city and beside the soldier pay due give they all sick and wound person be send to their hospital vulgar call Gaslhausen that be house for guest where all thing for health food and cleanliness of the body be phisical plentiful and neat minister to they of which kind of house fair and stately build they have one in each city also when they be recover of their sickness and wound they be present send back to the camp or their winter garrison they who be maim in the war and make thereby unfit for service have from they a pension for life or the value of the pension in ready money on the other side they so punish the breaker of martial discipline as when band of soldier be conduct to any service or garrison through the midst of their city or village not one of they be so hardy as to leave his rank to do the least wrong to any passenger or to take so much as a chicken or crust of bread from the subject by force and while myself be in those part I remember that upon the give up of a castle into the state hand after proclamation make that no soldier shall do the least injury to any of those who have yield the same a soldier wanton take one of their hat away or change his hat with one of they be present hang up for this small insolency for war by land they have no great power I speak particular of the unite their foot and horse province not of netherlands in general which we read to have of old raise a army of eighty thousand man for since the subject can be press to the war but when their own city or town be besiege and in that case their magistrate go before they and lead they to the wall and since the number of they be very small who willing follow that profession hereupon almost all their army consist of stranger and long experience have conclude mercenary soldier to be unfit for great conquest so as wise man think for this reason that the common wealth of the state be more fit by due observe of their league and amity with confederate and neighbour to defend their own then ambitious to extend their empire by invade other they have heavy Friesland horse more fit to endure the enemy charge then to pursue he fly I speak not of Flanders and the other province yield good light horse but all the way and passage be fence in with ditch of water they have at home less use of horse which make they common sell these horse in foreign part use only mare to draw their wagon and for other service of peace which mare be very beautiful and good the inhabitant of these province by nature education and art be most fit for of their (@) power navigation and as in the exercise of all art they be no less witty than industrious so particular they have great skill in cast great ordinance in make gunpowder cable anchor and in build ship of all which thing and whatsoever be necessary to navalll war they have great abundance the matter be buy in foreign part but wrought by their own man at home so as they be most powerful at sea neither have any king a navy superior or equal to they except only the king of England and for conjecture of their general power at sea I will be bold to add what I have credible hear that one city of Amsterdam at this time have some hundred ship for the war or man of war and some four hundred ship of merchant well arm for defence beside as they say some ten thousand bark or without all doubt a uncredible number therefore if perhaps the unite province forget their old league with England and our late merit in defend their liberty shall at any time resolve to have war with England which for the good of both nation God forbid then be such bloody fight at sea like to happen as former age never know yet the course of those time whereof I write give small probability of any such event like to happen for many reason combine our mind together first the happy amity that have be time out of mind between our nation next the bond of love on our part towards those we have preserve from bondage and the like bond of their thankfulness towards we which howsoever ambition may neglect or despise yet never any nation be more oblige to another in that kind and so long as the memory thereof can live it must needs quench all malice between we beside that they be not able to raise a army of their own man by land aswell for want of man as because it must consist altogether of voluntary no man be bind to serve in the war except his town be besiege and his own magistrate lead he to the wall they have hitherto happy use and may ever so use our man for soldier wherein Brittany abound above all other nation neither do they by much so esteem the auxiliary band of any other nation as of we last in that they want many necessary of their own and yet abound in all thing by traffic can long subsist without the freedom thereof and nothing be so powerful to diminish their wealth and to raise civil discord among they as the bar of this freedom which then seem more easy or less difficult to the king of England then to any other neighbour prince or I will bold say to all other neighbour prince join together against they they have strength of their own to maintain that freedom by sea and be able with the only support of Brittany to defend themselves by land against all other enemy for they have only three passage to sea one by Vlishing in Zealand another by Brill upon the south-west coast of Holland and a three narrow passage by the island fly to the tassel on the north-east side of Holland whereof the two first be guard by the say two strong city with the fort belong to they all keep by garrison of english soldier and the stop or restrain of the three seem less difficult to the navy of Brittany then to the power of any other enemy at this time when I pass through these part of which time I write the unite province much complain of the english for take their good at sea and hinder their free traffic wherein they shall have consider that they cause the war with Spain which we bear only to second they and if our merchant be force to leave the traffic of Spain where they have great freedom and amity only for their sake how can they think it just and equal that they shall free supply Spain with food and necessary for war so as the very commodity of England can not then be vent into Spain but only by flemish and some few scottish ship and mariner except they desire to make the war eternal by which they only grow rich in which case our project be more just who for a time make war that we may after live in peace and whereas they then complain that not only prohibit ware carry to prohibit place but also other their commodity carry to friend be spoil by our man of war which perhaps through the insolency of captain and soldier may sometime happen no doubt these injury be rare and never bear with by the queen or inferior magistrate and they can not be ignorant how hardly the insolency of soldier can be restrain by land and much more by sea for all good englishman I may profess that they abstain from prohibit traffic no good englishman wish good success or impunity to any english ship exercise piracy especial against so near confederate these complaint I well remember to have be at that time frequent in those part I know not how since appease or continue and because the q. of England have disburse much treasure for their safety which they be bind to repay at the end of the war and threaten to deduct these spoil out of the same many then fear lest this difference may in process of time breed discord between England and those province also because the town and fort give to the queen as pledge for money disburse be then keep with weak garrison overtop in number by the very citizen it be then think that the state may take they by force if our governor have not watchful eye upon their design and change of counsel in general good man on both side be to wish the continuance of peace between England and these province by which both commonwealth have long have and may still have unspeakable benefit and that the rather because we never yet have war but perpetual amity together neither can any war prove more bloody or mischievous to either part then that between ourselves to conclude happy be the maker curse the breaker of our peace
